assessments.title: The Music in Words: Understanding Meter

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Hidden Beat Inside a Poem

Loud and Soft: The Sounds Inside Words

TY-ger, TY-ger, BURN-ing BRIGHT. A thumping pattern, almost like a drum, hiding inside the syllables. The English poet William Blake built it there on purpose, back in the 1790s, and it still pounds today.

Every word you speak has syllables that are louder and syllables that are softer. Try saying the word "hello." You naturally say it "hel-LO," pushing harder on the second part. Now try saying it "HEL-lo," with the stress on the first part instead. Sounds strange, right? That's because your voice already knows which syllables are stressed — said louder and stronger — and which are unstressed — said more softly and quickly. Stressed and unstressed syllables are like the high and low notes in a song. They give language its shape.
stimulus.content_text: The Hidden Beat Inside a PoemLoud and Soft: The Sounds Inside Words TY-ger, TY-ger, BURN-ing BRIGHT. A thumping pattern, almost like a drum, hiding inside the syllables. The English poet William Blake built it there on purpose, back in the 1790s, and it still pounds today. Every word you speak has syllables that are louder and syllables that are softer. Try saying the word "hello." You naturally say it "hel-LO," pushing harder on the second part. Now try saying it "HEL-lo," with the stress on the first part instead. Sounds strange, right? That's because your voice already knows which syllables are stressed — said louder and stronger — and which are unstressed — said more softly and quickly. Stressed and unstressed syllables are like the high and low notes in a song. They give language its shape.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the difference between stressed and unstressed syllables?

choices.text: Stressed syllables are first, while unstressed syllables are last.
choices.feedback: The passage shows that stressed syllables can come in different positions, like the second syllable in 'hel-LO,' so they are not always first.

choices.text: Stressed syllables are sung, while unstressed syllables are spoken.
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions singing syllables — it only talks about how loudly or softly we say them when we speak.

choices.text: Stressed syllables are louder, while unstressed syllables are softer.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly defines stressed syllables as 'said louder and stronger' and unstressed syllables as 'said more softly and quickly.'

choices.text: Stressed syllables are shorter, while unstressed syllables are longer.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says unstressed syllables are said more quickly, making them shorter, not longer.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Meter Actually Is

Meter is a pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables that repeats across a line of poetry, creating rhythm — like a heartbeat that keeps going, beat after beat. You might think meter just means counting how many syllables a line has, the way you count syllables in a haiku. Actually, meter is different. A haiku cares about the number of syllables. Meter cares about the order — which syllables are loud and which are soft, and how that pattern repeats.

Poets measure meter in small units called feet. A foot is a group of two or three syllables arranged in a set pattern — think of it like a single step when you walk. One step by itself is just a step. But when you take step after step at the same pace, your footsteps create a rhythm. Poetic feet work the same way. When the same foot repeats across a line, you hear a steady beat pulling you forward.

Here is something that surprises many readers: meter and rhyme are not the same thing. Rhyme is about matching sounds at the ends of lines, like "bright" and "night." Meter is about the pattern of loud and soft syllables within a line. A poem can have a strong, pounding meter without a single rhyme anywhere. They are two separate tools a poet can choose to use — or choose to skip.

A poem can have a strong, pounding meter without a single rhyme anywhere. They are two separate tools a poet can choose to use — or choose to skip.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why do repeated poetic feet create rhythm in a line of poetry?

choices.text: Because repeated feet turn every poem into a haiku
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that haikus count syllables, while meter is about the order of stressed and unstressed syllables — they are different things.

choices.text: Because repeated feet add more syllables to each line
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says meter is about which syllables are loud or soft and how that pattern repeats, not about adding more syllables.

choices.text: Because repeated feet make the ends of lines sound alike
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because making the ends of lines sound alike is rhyme, and the passage clearly says meter and rhyme are two separate tools.

choices.text: Because repeated feet keep the same stress pattern going
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says when the same foot — the same stress pattern — repeats across a line, it creates a steady beat, which is rhythm.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Two Famous Patterns: Trochaic and Iambic

Let's go back to William Blake's "The Tyger." Read the opening line one more time and clap on the loud syllables: "TY-ger, TY-ger, BURN-ing BRIGHT." Notice how each pair of syllables starts with the louder sound: LOUD-soft, LOUD-soft. This pattern is called trochaic meter. The word "trochaic" comes from a Greek word meaning "to run," and you can almost feel the poem sprinting when you read it. The effect is fierce and powerful, like the tiger Blake is describing.

Now compare that with a different poet. Rudyard Kipling was a British writer born in India in 1865. He became famous around the world for his poems and stories. His poem "If" uses the opposite pattern, called iambic meter. In iambic meter, the unstressed syllable comes first, then the stressed one: soft-LOUD, soft-LOUD. If trochaic sounds like "DUM-da, DUM-da," then iambic sounds like "da-DUM, da-DUM."

Read this line from "If" and listen carefully: "If YOU can KEEP your HEAD when ALL aBOUT you." Can you hear the softer syllables rising gently into the louder ones? That slow, steady rise gives the poem a calm, thoughtful feeling. It matches the wise advice Kipling is offering. He chose iambic meter on purpose, the same way a musician picks a tempo — the speed of a song — to match the mood of the music.

Read this line from "If" and listen carefully: "If YOU can KEEP your HEAD when ALL aBOUT you." Can you hear the softer syllables rising gently into the louder ones? That slow, steady rise gives the poem a calm, thoughtful feeling. It matches the wise advice Kipling is offering. He chose iambic meter on purpose, the same way a musician picks a tempo — the speed of a song — to match the mood of the music.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the order of syllable sounds in iambic meter?

choices.text: Two soft syllables come before a loud one
choices.feedback: The passage does not describe any pattern with two soft syllables before a loud one — iambic meter is simply one soft syllable followed by one loud syllable.

choices.text: Two loud syllables come one after the other
choices.feedback: The passage never describes two loud syllables in a row — iambic meter always alternates between a soft syllable and a loud syllable.

choices.text: A loud syllable comes first, then a soft one
choices.feedback: This is actually the trochaic pattern — the passage says trochaic goes 'LOUD-soft, LOUD-soft,' which is the opposite of iambic.

choices.text: A soft syllable comes first, then a loud one
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly states that in iambic meter the unstressed (soft) syllable comes first, followed by the stressed (loud) one: soft-LOUD, soft-LOUD.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Poets Choose Their Beat

A fast, thumping meter like Blake's makes a poem feel urgent and wild. A smooth, rolling meter like Kipling's makes a poem feel steady and serious. You feel the mood in your body first — in the rhythm of your voice as you read aloud.

Not every poem uses meter. Some poets write in free verse, poetry with no fixed pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables. Free verse has its own kind of rhythm, but it doesn't repeat the same foot over and over. You will discover more about free verse in the next article. For now, knowing that meter exists helps you see it as a deliberate decision. When a poet uses meter, they are choosing to add music to their words.

Don't read it in a flat, even voice where every syllable sounds the same. Instead, let your voice rise and fall naturally. If you find a repeating pattern — soft-LOUD or LOUD-soft — you have found the meter. You are hearing something the poet planted there, a rhythm that has been waiting, sometimes for hundreds of years, for your voice to bring it to life.

You will discover more about free verse in the next article. For now, knowing that meter exists helps you see it as a deliberate decision. You are hearing something the poet planted there, a rhythm that has been waiting, sometimes for hundreds of years, for your voice to bring it to life.

choices.text: To build a repeating beat or rhythm

choices.text: To sing the poem with a real melody
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions singing or a real melody; 'music' here is a comparison to rhythm, not actual singing.

choices.text: Free verse builds a calm feeling, but trochaic meter builds a thoughtful mood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article connects calm and thoughtful feelings to iambic meter, not to free verse or trochaic meter.

choices.text: Free verse uses only soft sounds, but trochaic meter uses only loud sounds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because free verse isn't limited to soft sounds — it simply doesn't repeat the same stressed and unstressed pattern.

choices.text: Free verse lacks a fixed pattern, but trochaic meter repeats a LOUD-soft beat.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly says free verse has no fixed pattern, while trochaic meter repeats a LOUD-soft beat across a line.

choices.text: Free verse matches ending sounds, but trochaic meter counts the total syllables.

choices.text: The choice to write the poem without any fixed pattern of beats.

choices.text: The total number of syllables counted in each line of the poem.

choices.text: The matching sounds that poets place at the very ends of the lines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because matching sounds at the ends of lines is rhyme, and the passage clearly states that meter and rhyme are two separate tools — rhyme is not what creates the fierce or calm feeling.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows that spoken words naturally have loud and soft syllables, and meter is what happens when a poet organizes those sounds into a repeating pattern across a line.

assessments.title: Free as a Bird: Poems Without Rules

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Free Verse: Poetry That Makes Its Own Rules

Six Lines About Fog and What Free Verse Means

The fog comes / on little cat feet. That is the opening of a poem by Carl Sandburg, and in just six lines, it is finished. No rhyme. No thumping beat you can clap along to. Just fog, creeping in like a cat, sitting quietly over the harbor — the sheltered stretch of water where ships rest — and the city, then moving on. The poem feels soft and slow, exactly the way real fog feels against your skin. Sandburg wrote it this way on purpose. He chose a kind of poetry called free verse, and it gave him the freedom to make the poem sound like the very thing it described.

Free verse is poetry that does not follow a set pattern of rhyme or meter. Meter is the steady beat inside a line of poetry — think of it like a drumbeat that repeats again and again. In a metered poem, you can tap your foot to the rhythm. Free verse has no fixed drumbeat. Instead, the poet controls the rhythm by choosing where each line ends, where to pause, and which words land next to each other.

No rhyme to worry about, no beat to keep — just write whatever you want, right? In a metered, rhyming poem, the pattern does some of the work for you. It tells you how long each line should be and which sounds to aim for. In free verse, the poet makes every one of those decisions alone. Every word, every pause, every line break matters.

choices.text: Free verse is easier to write, but a metered poem is harder to write.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says free verse is harder to write, since the poet must make every decision alone without a pattern to guide them.

choices.text: Free verse has no rhyme, but a metered poem must always rhyme.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say metered poems must always rhyme — it describes meter (beat) as the key difference, not rhyme.

choices.text: Free verse has a steady beat, but a metered poem has no fixed beat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two types switched around — it's the metered poem that has the steady beat, not free verse.

choices.text: Free verse has no fixed beat, but a metered poem has a steady beat.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that free verse has no fixed drumbeat, while meter is described as a steady, repeating beat you can tap your foot to.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How a Line Break Changes Everything

Imagine these words as a plain sentence: "The cat sat still and watched the bird fly away." Now picture a poet breaking that sentence into lines:

The cat sat still / and watched / the bird / fly away.

You linger on "watched." You feel the stillness. Then "fly away" lands at the end, and the bird is gone. The words are the same, but the line breaks — the places where the poet drops to a new line — change how you experience them. That is one of the most powerful tools a free verse poet has.

Now picture a poet breaking that sentence into lines: The cat sat still / and watched / the bird / fly away. You linger on "watched." You feel the stillness. Then "fly away" lands at the end, and the bird is gone. The words are the same, but the line breaks — the places where the poet drops to a new line — change how you experience them. That is one of the most powerful tools a free verse poet has.

prompt._: According to the passage, what causes the reader to experience the words differently in the poem version?

choices.text: The shorter amount of time it takes to read the poem.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about how much time it takes to read the poem.

choices.text: The places where the poet drops down to a new line.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says 'the line breaks — the places where the poet drops to a new line — change how you experience them.'

choices.text: The new and descriptive words the poet decides to use.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states 'the words are the same' in both versions, so no new words are added.

choices.text: The special punctuation marks the poet puts at the end.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions punctuation marks as a reason for the different experience.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Carl Sandburg and Langston Hughes: Two Masters of Free Verse

Carl Sandburg was born in Illinois in 1878. He loved writing about ordinary American life, from roaring cities to the wide, flat prairies — the big, open grasslands that stretch across the middle of the country like a green ocean. His poem "Fog" is only six lines long, yet readers have remembered it for more than a hundred years. Sandburg did not need rhyme to make it stick. He used imagery — words that paint pictures in your mind — to compare fog to a cat. You can see those little cat feet. You can feel the silence. That single image does all the work.

Another master of free verse was Langston Hughes. Hughes was an African American poet who grew up during the Harlem Renaissance, an exciting burst of art, music, and writing created by Black artists in the Harlem neighborhood of New York City during the 1920s and 1930s. Jazz musicians were bending musical rules all around him, and Hughes did the same thing with poetry.

His lines were often short and direct, like someone looking you in the eye and telling you something true. Hughes wanted his poems to sound like real people talking, like a jazz musician playing a solo — following feeling instead of a fixed score. A strict rhyme pattern would have boxed in those natural rhythms.

prompt._: According to the text, when did the Harlem Renaissance take place?

choices.text: During the 1920s and 1930s
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states the Harlem Renaissance took place 'during the 1920s and 1930s.'

choices.text: During the 1940s and the 1950s
choices.feedback: The 1940s and 1950s are never mentioned anywhere in the passage, so this cannot be the right answer.

choices.text: During the past one hundred years
choices.feedback: The phrase 'more than a hundred years' in the passage describes how long people have remembered Sandburg's poem 'Fog,' not when the Harlem Renaissance happened.

choices.text: During the time around 1878
choices.feedback: 1878 is the year Carl Sandburg was born in Illinois — it has nothing to do with the Harlem Renaissance.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Not Everyone Was Convinced — and What Free Verse Really Demands

Free verse became popular in the twentieth century, but not everyone welcomed it. For hundreds of years, most English-language poetry had followed strict rules about meter and rhyme. When poets started writing without those patterns, some readers complained. They said free verse was not real poetry at all — just regular sentences chopped into short lines. Over time, though, more people came to see the skill involved. A free verse poet still picks every word with care, still builds images that stay in your memory, and still shapes rhythm — just a rhythm that belongs to that one poem and no other.

Listen to where the lines break and where the silence falls. They are the poet's choices — dozens of tiny decisions stacked together to make you feel something specific. Free verse does not mean "anything goes." It means the poet built the rules from scratch, the way an architect designs a building that has never existed before. It just looks like nothing you have seen yet.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows both Sandburg and Hughes chose free verse so their poems could sound and feel like the very things they were writing about — fog moving softly or jazz music flowing freely.

choices.text: It forced them to use a steady drumbeat that readers could easily tap along to.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly explains that free verse has no fixed drumbeat, which is the opposite of what this answer says.

choices.text: It proved to complaining readers that regular sentences chopped into lines were real poetry.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the complaining readers came after free verse was already being written — the poets didn't choose free verse to answer those complaints.

choices.text: It helped them write shorter poems without having to worry about finding rhyming words.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says either poet picked free verse to write shorter poems or to avoid the trouble of finding rhyming words.

prompt._: How does the author organize the ideas in the article from beginning to end?

choices.text: By describing the history of traditional poetry, then explaining the rules of strict meter, and finally introducing the new idea of free verse.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article begins by introducing free verse, not by describing the history of traditional poetry or explaining strict meter rules first.

choices.text: By showing why people disliked free verse, then explaining how to write a new poem, and finally listing the most famous poems ever.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never teaches you how to write a poem or provides a list of the most famous poems ever written.

choices.text: By comparing free verse poetry to jazz music, then explaining the use of line breaks, and finally describing Carl Sandburg's early life story.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while jazz is briefly mentioned when talking about Hughes, the whole article is not organized around comparing free verse to jazz music.

choices.text: By explaining the features of free verse, then sharing examples of famous poets, and finally showing how readers' opinions changed over time.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article first explains free verse features, then gives examples of Sandburg and Hughes, and finally shows how readers' opinions changed over time.

prompt._: The main idea of the article is that writing free verse requires great skill. Which similarity between free verse and traditional poetry supports this main idea?

choices.text: Both became highly popular during the Harlem Renaissance in the 1920s.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only links the Harlem Renaissance to Langston Hughes and free verse poetry, not to traditional poetry.

choices.text: Both use regular sentences chopped into short lines to tell stories.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage presents 'sentences chopped into short lines' as a criticism some people made about free verse, not as something true about both kinds of poetry.

choices.text: Both rely on a steady drumbeat to help readers feel the emotion.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a steady drumbeat, or meter, belongs only to traditional poetry — the passage clearly says free verse has no fixed drumbeat.

choices.text: Both require the poet to carefully shape rhythm and pick words.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says free verse poets still pick every word carefully and shape rhythm, just like traditional poets do — both require real skill with words and rhythm.

syllabus_metadata.title: Free as a Bird: Poems Without Rules

assessments.title: Songs of the Heart: Lyric Poetry

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Lyric Poetry: A Window into Someone's Heart

A Quiet Room Full of Secrets

In 1886, in a small Massachusetts town, a woman named Lavinia Dickinson opened a drawer in her dead sister's bedroom. Inside, she found roughly forty handbound booklets stitched together by hand. Each one was packed with poems — over 1,700 in all. Emily Dickinson had written them alone, in secret, for years. Those tiny booklets held some of the most powerful lyric poetry ever written in English.

But what exactly is a lyric poem, and why did Dickinson fill drawer after drawer with them?
stimulus.content_text: Lyric Poetry: A Window into Someone's HeartA Quiet Room Full of Secrets In 1886, in a small Massachusetts town, a woman named Lavinia Dickinson opened a drawer in her dead sister's bedroom. Inside, she found roughly forty handbound booklets stitched together by hand. Each one was packed with poems — over 1,700 in all. Emily Dickinson had written them alone, in secret, for years. Those tiny booklets held some of the most powerful lyric poetry ever written in English. But what exactly is a lyric poem, and why did Dickinson fill drawer after drawer with them?

prompt._: According to the text, which contrast is true about Lavinia Dickinson and Emily Dickinson?

choices.text: Lavinia hid the poems, while Emily found the drawer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the sisters switched — the passage says Lavinia found the drawer and Emily wrote the poems, not the other way around.

choices.text: Lavinia published the poems, while Emily read them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Lavinia publishing the poems or Emily reading them — those details simply aren't in the text.

choices.text: Lavinia found the poems, while Emily wrote them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that Lavinia found the booklets in the drawer, while Emily was the one who wrote over 1,700 poems in secret.

choices.text: Lavinia wrote the booklets, while Emily stitched them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Emily both wrote the poems and stitched the booklets together herself — Lavinia didn't make them.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Lyric Poetry Is — and What It Isn't

A lyric poem expresses the personal emotions or thoughts of a speaker rather than telling a story. Think of it this way: if a narrative poem is a movie with characters and action, a lyric poem is a photograph of a single feeling. It freezes one moment and asks, What does this feel like inside?

The word lyric comes from the lyre, a small stringed instrument — shaped a bit like a wooden U with strings stretched across it — played in ancient Greece. Thousands of years ago, Greek poets sang their poems while plucking the lyre, blending music and feeling together. Today, lyric poems are usually read on a page, not sung aloud. They still carry a musical quality, though. Poets create that music through rhythm — the beat or pattern of stressed sounds in a line — and through repeated words or sounds that echo in your ear.

Actually, many lyric poems do not rhyme at all. What makes a poem a lyric poem is its focus on personal feelings, not whether the last words in each line match.
stimulus.content_text: What Lyric Poetry Is — and What It Isn't A lyric poem expresses the personal emotions or thoughts of a speaker rather than telling a story. Think of it this way: if a narrative poem is a movie with characters and action, a lyric poem is a photograph of a single feeling. It freezes one moment and asks, What does this feel like inside? The word lyric comes from the lyre, a small stringed instrument — shaped a bit like a wooden U with strings stretched across it — played in ancient Greece. Thousands of years ago, Greek poets sang their poems while plucking the lyre, blending music and feeling together. Today, lyric poems are usually read on a page, not sung aloud. They still carry a musical quality, though. Poets create that music through rhythm — the beat or pattern of stressed sounds in a line — and through repeated words or sounds that echo in your ear. Actually, many lyric poems do not rhyme at all. What makes a poem a lyric poem is its focus on personal feelings, not whether the last words in each line match.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why are many lyric poems still musical even when they are not sung?

choices.text: Because they tell a full story with characters and action
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because telling a full story with characters and action describes a narrative poem, which the passage compares to a movie — the opposite of a lyric poem.

choices.text: Because poets use rhythm and repeated sounds
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that poets create a musical quality through rhythm and repeated words or sounds that echo in your ear.

choices.text: Because the last words in each line always rhyme
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says that many lyric poems do not rhyme at all, so rhyming is not what makes them musical.

choices.text: Because Greek poets played them on a lyre
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Greek poets playing the lyre explains the history of the word 'lyric,' not why poems read on a page today still have a musical feel.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Emily Dickinson: Big Feelings in Small Spaces

Emily Dickinson lived most of her life in Amherst, Massachusetts, in a quiet house where she spent her days reading, baking, gardening, and writing. She rarely left home and did not seek fame. Yet inside that quiet life, her mind blazed with ideas about nature, death, and wonder.

Look at the opening of one of her most famous poems:

"I'm Nobody! Who are you? / Are you — Nobody — Too?"

In just two lines, you can feel the speaker's loneliness — and also a spark of excitement at finding someone who understands. Notice the word speaker here. The speaker is the voice inside a poem. A poet can invent a voice, the same way an author creates a character in a story. When you read a lyric poem, ask yourself: Who is talking, and what are they feeling?
stimulus.content_text: Emily Dickinson: Big Feelings in Small Spaces Emily Dickinson lived most of her life in Amherst, Massachusetts, in a quiet house where she spent her days reading, baking, gardening, and writing. She rarely left home and did not seek fame. Yet inside that quiet life, her mind blazed with ideas about nature, death, and wonder. Look at the opening of one of her most famous poems: "I'm Nobody! Who are you? / Are you — Nobody — Too?" In just two lines, you can feel the speaker's loneliness — and also a spark of excitement at finding someone who understands. Notice the word speaker here. The speaker is the voice inside a poem. A poet can invent a voice, the same way an author creates a character in a story. When you read a lyric poem, ask yourself: Who is talking, and what are they feeling?

prompt._: According to the text, what is the correct order of steps a person should take when reading a lyric poem?

choices.text: First read the poem, then ask who is talking and what they feel.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says to ask 'Who is talking, and what are they feeling?' after you read a lyric poem.

choices.text: First read the poem, then learn about the poet and where they lived.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage does mention where Emily Dickinson lived, it never says learning about the poet's life is a step for reading a lyric poem.

choices.text: First read the poem, then look for excitement and find a friend.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never tells readers to look for excitement or find a friend as steps for reading a lyric poem.

choices.text: First read the poem, then invent a voice and create a character.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because inventing a voice and creating a character is something the poet does when writing the poem, not something the reader does.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Langston Hughes: Dreams You Can See

Langston Hughes wrote during the early and middle 1900s and became one of the most important voices in American literature. His lyric poem "Dreams" urges readers to hold tight to their hopes.

"Hold fast to dreams / For if dreams die / Life is a broken-winged bird / That cannot fly."

Hughes also compares a life without dreams to a barren field — a place so empty and lifeless that nothing grows, like a parking lot covered in snow. These vivid pictures help you feel why dreams matter, not just understand it. That is exactly what lyric poetry does best. It uses images and comparisons to put emotions right into your body.

Hughes poured his own hopes and experiences into his poems. Readers of all backgrounds have found comfort and inspiration in his words for nearly a hundred years.
stimulus.content_text: Langston Hughes: Dreams You Can See Langston Hughes wrote during the early and middle 1900s and became one of the most important voices in American literature. His lyric poem "Dreams" urges readers to hold tight to their hopes. "Hold fast to dreams / For if dreams die / Life is a broken-winged bird / That cannot fly." Hughes also compares a life without dreams to a barren field — a place so empty and lifeless that nothing grows, like a parking lot covered in snow. These vivid pictures help you feel why dreams matter, not just understand it. That is exactly what lyric poetry does best. It uses images and comparisons to put emotions right into your body. Hughes poured his own hopes and experiences into his poems. Readers of all backgrounds have found comfort and inspiration in his words for nearly a hundred years.

prompt._: In the poem 'Dreams,' how is a life without dreams similar to a broken-winged bird?

choices.text: Both are unable to move forward or rise up
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because just like a broken-winged bird cannot fly, a life without dreams cannot move forward or rise up — that's exactly the comparison Hughes makes in the poem.

choices.text: Both are things that Langston Hughes experienced
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the poem is using the broken-winged bird as a comparison to explain what life feels like without dreams, not to describe something Hughes personally went through.

choices.text: Both are sad things that people write poems about
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Hughes isn't saying both things are sad subjects for poetry — he's comparing them because neither one can move forward or rise up.

choices.text: Both are found outdoors during cold weather
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cold weather and being outdoors are not part of the comparison — that detail comes from a different example in the passage about a barren field.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
How to Spot a Lyric Poem

When you read a poem, look for clues. Does the poem describe a battle, a journey, or a series of events? Does it instead focus on one feeling, one thought, or one moment seen from the inside? Look for emotion words, strong images, and a speaker sharing something personal.

Read the poem out loud. Do certain sounds repeat? Does the rhythm pull you along like a drumbeat? Those patterns are the echo of that ancient lyre, still humming inside the words.
stimulus.content_text: How to Spot a Lyric Poem When you read a poem, look for clues. Does the poem describe a battle, a journey, or a series of events? Does it instead focus on one feeling, one thought, or one moment seen from the inside? Look for emotion words, strong images, and a speaker sharing something personal. Read the poem out loud. Do certain sounds repeat? Does the rhythm pull you along like a drumbeat? Those patterns are the echo of that ancient lyre, still humming inside the words.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a lyric poem?

choices.text: a poem with many events told in order
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a poem with many events told in order is the opposite of a lyric poem, which zooms in on just one feeling or moment.

choices.text: a poem with only repeated sounds and rhythm
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because repeated sounds and rhythm are clues that help you recognize a lyric poem, but they don't define what a lyric poem is about.

choices.text: a poem about a battle or a journey
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says poems about battles or journeys are NOT lyric poems — those are the kinds of poems you should look past to find a lyric poem.

choices.text: a poem about one feeling or one moment
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a lyric poem focuses on 'one feeling, one thought, or one moment seen from the inside.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Lyrics All Around You

Here is something you already know without realizing it: song lyrics are a modern form of lyric poetry. That is why we call them lyrics. The difference is that song lyrics are written to go with music, while lyric poems on the page create their own music through rhythm and word choice alone. But the goal is the same — to make you feel something deep.

The next time a song gives you chills or makes your eyes sting, pay attention. You are experiencing the same kind of art that Greek poets performed with their lyres, that Dickinson hid in her drawer, and that Hughes offered to the world. A few well-chosen words can carry a feeling across thousands of years — and land it right in your chest.
stimulus.content_text: Lyrics All Around You Here is something you already know without realizing it: song lyrics are a modern form of lyric poetry. That is why we call them lyrics. The difference is that song lyrics are written to go with music, while lyric poems on the page create their own music through rhythm and word choice alone. But the goal is the same — to make you feel something deep. The next time a song gives you chills or makes your eyes sting, pay attention. You are experiencing the same kind of art that Greek poets performed with their lyres, that Dickinson hid in her drawer, and that Hughes offered to the world. A few well-chosen words can carry a feeling across thousands of years — and land it right in your chest.

prompt._: Which detail from "Lyrics All Around You" best supports the idea that song lyrics and lyric poems have the same purpose?

choices.text: They both try to make people feel strong emotions.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that both song lyrics and lyric poems share 'the same goal — to make you feel something deep,' which means making people feel strong emotions.

choices.text: They were first performed by Greek poets with lyres.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Greek poets with lyres is just a history fact about where lyric poetry came from, not about the shared purpose of songs and poems.

choices.text: They are always written to be sung with music.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually explains that lyric poems are NOT written with music — only song lyrics are, so this is a difference, not something they share.

choices.text: They both use exactly the same rhythm and words.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says songs and poems use the exact same rhythm and words — in fact, it says poems create their own music through rhythm and word choice on their own.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is one major difference between Emily Dickinson and Langston Hughes?

choices.text: Dickinson played a stringed instrument while reading, while Hughes used rhythm to create music.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because neither Dickinson nor Hughes is described as playing a stringed instrument; the lyre is only mentioned as something ancient Greek poets used thousands of years ago.

choices.text: Dickinson wrote about characters and exciting action, while Hughes wrote about personal feelings.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both Dickinson and Hughes wrote lyric poetry about personal feelings — writing about characters and exciting action describes narrative poetry, which is the opposite of what either poet did.

choices.text: Dickinson kept her poetry hidden away in secret, while Hughes shared his work with the world.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly shows Dickinson hid her poems in secret drawers and never sought fame, while Hughes published his work and became one of the most celebrated voices in American literature.

choices.text: Dickinson wrote her poems to be sung out loud, while Hughes wrote his poems to be read silently.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says lyric poems today are usually read on a page rather than sung aloud, and there is no evidence that either Dickinson or Hughes wrote their poems to be sung.

prompt._: How does the author organize the text to show the effect that lyric poetry has on its readers?

choices.text: By comparing narrative poems to action movies and then comparing lyric poems to single photographs.
choices.feedback: Answer C is incorrect because the movie and photograph comparison explains what lyric poetry *is*, not how it affects readers.

choices.text: By sharing specific lines of poetry and then describing the strong feelings those words create.
choices.feedback: Answer B is correct because the author quotes specific lines of poetry from Dickinson and Hughes and then describes the strong feelings those words create in the reader.

choices.text: By explaining how ancient Greeks played the lyre and then showing how modern songs are made.
choices.feedback: Answer A is incorrect because while the article does mention the ancient Greeks and modern songs, that is not the main way the author shows the effect poetry has on readers.

choices.text: By describing the problem of hidden poems and then showing how Lavinia Dickinson solved it.
choices.feedback: Answer D is incorrect because the story of Lavinia finding the poems is just the opening hook — it is not about solving a problem, and it does not show the effect poetry has on readers.

prompt._: According to the article, what is the correct sequence of how lyric poetry has been shared from ancient times to today?

choices.text: First it was sung with a stringed instrument, later it was completely forgotten by most readers, and today it is only studied in ancient history books.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says lyric poetry was forgotten — it shows lyric poetry has been important and widely read all the way from ancient Greece to today.

choices.text: First it was sung with a stringed instrument, later it was written to be read on a page, and today it is also experienced as modern song lyrics.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows the three stages in this exact order: sung with a lyre in ancient Greece, then written to be read on a page, and today also enjoyed as modern song lyrics.

choices.text: First it was written to tell long stories about characters, later it was sung with a stringed instrument, and today it is mostly read on a page.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because telling long stories with characters describes narrative poetry, not lyric poetry, and the article says lyric poetry has always been about personal feelings, not stories.

choices.text: First it was experienced as modern song lyrics, later it was written to be read on a page, and today it is sung with a stringed instrument.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the real order — modern song lyrics are the newest form, not the oldest, and ancient Greek lyre-singing came first, not last.

prompt._: Which statement best compares how Emily Dickinson and Langston Hughes are used in the article to explain lyric poetry?

choices.text: Both poets are shown as writers who used poems to share strong inner feelings.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Dickinson expressing loneliness and wonder, and Hughes expressing his feelings about dreams, proving both poets used lyric poetry to share strong inner feelings.

choices.text: Both poets are shown as writers who sang their poems with a lyre for listeners.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says ancient Greek poets sang with lyres — Dickinson and Hughes wrote poems on paper, not performed with instruments.

choices.text: Both poets are shown as writers who told long stories with many events.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that lyric poems focus on one feeling or moment, not on telling long stories with lots of events — that would be a narrative poem.

choices.text: Both poets are shown as writers who cared most about making every line rhyme.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that many lyric poems do not rhyme, and what makes a poem lyric is its focus on personal feelings, not matching end words.

syllabus_metadata.title: Songs of the Heart: Lyric Poetry

assessments.title: Poems That Tell Stories: Narrative Poetry

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Stories That Sing: The Power of Narrative Poetry

A Story Chanted Aloud

According to legend, a blind poet sat before a crowd in ancient Greece nearly three thousand years ago. He could not read from a page — instead, he chanted a story from memory: warriors clashing on a battlefield, a hero lost at sea for ten long years. The audience leaned in. The rhythm of his words kept them spellbound. That poet was Homer, and his poems were among the earliest stories ever recorded. They were not written in paragraphs. They were spoken aloud in verse — language arranged in lines with a beat, the way lyrics in a song follow a rhythm instead of running across the page like regular sentences.
stimulus.content_text: Stories That Sing: The Power of Narrative PoetryA Story Chanted Aloud According to legend, a blind poet sat before a crowd in ancient Greece nearly three thousand years ago. He could not read from a page — instead, he chanted a story from memory: warriors clashing on a battlefield, a hero lost at sea for ten long years. The audience leaned in. The rhythm of his words kept them spellbound. That poet was Homer, and his poems were among the earliest stories ever recorded. They were not written in paragraphs. They were spoken aloud in verse — language arranged in lines with a beat, the way lyrics in a song follow a rhythm instead of running across the page like regular sentences.

prompt._: According to the text, how is verse different from regular sentences?

choices.text: Verse is read silently instead of aloud.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says verse was chanted and spoken aloud, which is the opposite of reading silently.

choices.text: Verse is written in paragraphs on a page.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says Homer's poems were NOT written in paragraphs, so paragraphs describe regular writing, not verse.

choices.text: Verse is arranged in lines with a beat.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly defines verse as 'language arranged in lines with a beat,' just like song lyrics follow a rhythm.

choices.text: Verse is made only for ancient crowds.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage mentions ancient crowds to tell Homer's story, but it never says verse can only be for ancient audiences.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Ballads: Songs That Traveled

One special kind of narrative poem is called a ballad — a story-poem originally meant to be sung aloud and passed from person to person, the way you might pass along a funny video today. Singers once carried ballads from village to village across Europe during the Middle Ages, roughly between the years 500 and 1500, sharing tales of heroes, outlaws, and lost love. Their simple, repeating patterns made them easy to memorize, even for people who had never learned to read. A classic example is "Barbara Allen," a ballad that spread across Britain and America and has been recorded in over 90 different versions. Ballads still echo in modern music. Many country and folk songs follow the same pattern: short stanzas — groups of lines bundled together like paragraphs in a story — that repeat a familiar rhythm.
stimulus.content_text: Ballads: Songs That Traveled One special kind of narrative poem is called a ballad — a story-poem originally meant to be sung aloud and passed from person to person, the way you might pass along a funny video today. Singers once carried ballads from village to village across Europe during the Middle Ages, roughly between the years 500 and 1500, sharing tales of heroes, outlaws, and lost love. Their simple, repeating patterns made them easy to memorize, even for people who had never learned to read. A classic example is "Barbara Allen," a ballad that spread across Britain and America and has been recorded in over 90 different versions. Ballads still echo in modern music. Many country and folk songs follow the same pattern: short stanzas — groups of lines bundled together like paragraphs in a story — that repeat a familiar rhythm.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why were ballads easy for people to memorize even if they could not read?

choices.text: because they were recorded in over 90 different versions
choices.feedback: Having 90 different versions shows how popular ballads were, but it has nothing to do with why they were easy to memorize.

choices.text: because they told stories about heroes and outlaws
choices.feedback: Stories about heroes and outlaws made ballads interesting, but the passage never says exciting topics made them easier to memorize.

choices.text: because singers traveled from village to village sharing them
choices.feedback: Singers traveling from village to village explains how ballads *spread*, but not why they were easy to memorize.

choices.text: because they used simple, repeating patterns
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says their 'simple, repeating patterns' made ballads easy to memorize, even for people who couldn't read.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Robert Frost and the Tricky Fork in the Road

One of the most famous narrative poems in English is "The Road Not Taken" by Robert Frost. Frost was an American poet born in 1874 who won four Pulitzer Prizes — one of the highest honors a writer in the United States can receive, like a gold medal for literature. He often wrote about the farms, forests, and people of rural New England, using plain words that hid surprising ideas underneath.

In "The Road Not Taken," published in 1916, a traveler walks through a yellow wood and reaches a fork in the path.
stimulus.content_text: Robert Frost and the Tricky Fork in the Road One of the most famous narrative poems in English is "The Road Not Taken" by Robert Frost. Frost was an American poet born in 1874 who won four Pulitzer Prizes — one of the highest honors a writer in the United States can receive, like a gold medal for literature. He often wrote about the farms, forests, and people of rural New England, using plain words that hid surprising ideas underneath. In "The Road Not Taken," published in 1916, a traveler walks through a yellow wood and reaches a fork in the path.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the traveler do after walking through a yellow wood?

choices.text: He visits a farm in the forest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions farms when describing Frost's writing topics, not as something the traveler visits in the poem.

choices.text: He finds a surprising hidden idea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'surprising hidden ideas' describes Frost's writing style in general, not an action the traveler takes in the poem.

choices.text: He meets people from New England.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because meeting people from New England is never mentioned as part of what happens in the poem's story.

choices.text: He reaches a fork in the path.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the traveler 'walks through a yellow wood and reaches a fork in the path' — those are the exact words used!

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Rudyard Kipling: Galloping Rhythms

Rudyard Kipling was another master of narrative poetry. Born in India in 1865, when India was part of the British Empire — the vast collection of lands around the world controlled by Britain — Kipling grew up surrounded by the sights, sounds, and stories of two very different cultures. In 1907, he became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. He wrote narrative poems about soldiers, sailors, and animals, many set in India or at sea. His poem "Gunga Din" tells the story of a brave water-bearer on a battlefield, and its rhythms pound like drumbeats, pulling you from one scene to the next. That beat is called rhythm — the pattern of strong and soft sounds in a line, like the steady thump of your heart or the bounce of a basketball on pavement. Kipling could make you feel the heat of an Indian afternoon just by choosing the right words and the right beat.
