Born in India in 1865, when India was part of the British Empire — the vast collection of lands around the world controlled by Britain — Kipling grew up surrounded by the sights, sounds, and stories of two very different cultures. In 1907, he became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. He wrote narrative poems about soldiers, sailors, and animals, many set in India or at sea. His poem "Gunga Din" tells the story of a brave water-bearer on a battlefield, and its rhythms pound like drumbeats, pulling you from one scene to the next. That beat is called rhythm — the pattern of strong and soft sounds in a line, like the steady thump of your heart or the bounce of a basketball on pavement. Kipling could make you feel the heat of an Indian afternoon just by choosing the right words and the right beat.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares rhythm to 'the steady thump of your heart or the bounce of a basketball on pavement.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about the meaning of rhythm?

choices.text: Rhythm is a repeating pattern of strong and soft sounds
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines rhythm as 'the pattern of strong and soft sounds,' and the comparisons to a heartbeat and bouncing ball both show a steady, repeating pattern.

choices.text: Rhythm is the speed at which a poem is read aloud
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes rhythm as a pattern of sounds, not the speed at which something is read.

choices.text: Rhythm is a type of music played during a battle scene
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rhythm is described as a feature of poetry in general, not a kind of music connected to battle scenes.

choices.text: Rhythm is a loud and surprising sound in a poem
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a heartbeat and a bouncing basketball are steady and predictable sounds, not loud or surprising ones.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Makes a Poem a Poem?

So what makes narrative poetry different from a regular story? The answer is form. A story written in prose — ordinary writing that fills up sentences and paragraphs, like this article — flows across the page. A narrative poem arranges words into lines and stanzas, with rhythm and sometimes rhyme. That structure gives the story a musical quality. It makes certain moments hit harder and certain feelings ring louder.

Actually, many narrative poems do not rhyme at all. What makes them poems is the way they use line breaks, rhythm, and stanzas to control how you hear and feel each word. Rhyme is just one tool in the poet's toolbox, not a requirement. A stanza of four lines is called a quatrain, and it is one of the most common building blocks in narrative poetry around the world.
stimulus.content_text: What Makes a Poem a Poem? So what makes narrative poetry different from a regular story? The answer is form. A story written in prose — ordinary writing that fills up sentences and paragraphs, like this article — flows across the page. A narrative poem arranges words into lines and stanzas, with rhythm and sometimes rhyme. That structure gives the story a musical quality. It makes certain moments hit harder and certain feelings ring louder. Actually, many narrative poems do not rhyme at all. What makes them poems is the way they use line breaks, rhythm, and stanzas to control how you hear and feel each word. Rhyme is just one tool in the poet's toolbox, not a requirement. A stanza of four lines is called a quatrain, and it is one of the most common building blocks in narrative poetry around the world.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a quatrain?

choices.text: A tool to control word rhythm
choices.feedback: While the passage does talk about tools poets use to control rhythm, a quatrain is defined by its four-line structure, not as a rhythm tool.

choices.text: A poem that does not use rhyme
choices.feedback: The passage does mention poems that don't rhyme, but that idea has nothing to do with the definition of a quatrain.

choices.text: A stanza made of four lines
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage states clearly, 'A stanza of four lines is called a quatrain' — that's the exact definition given in the text.

choices.text: A story written in sentences
choices.feedback: This describes prose, which the passage says is 'ordinary writing that fills up sentences and paragraphs,' not a quatrain.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Narrative Poetry Still Matters

Poems are not just pretty words on a page. They are the original movies, the earliest way humans found to make a story stick inside someone's mind and never leave. Today, narrative poems are read in classrooms in more than 100 countries, and new ones are written every year in hundreds of languages. The tradition Homer began nearly 3,000 years ago is still very much alive.
stimulus.content_text: Why Narrative Poetry Still Matters Poems are not just pretty words on a page. They are the original movies, the earliest way humans found to make a story stick inside someone's mind and never leave. Today, narrative poems are read in classrooms in more than 100 countries, and new ones are written every year in hundreds of languages. The tradition Homer began nearly 3,000 years ago is still very much alive.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that narrative poetry is still important today?

choices.text: Narrative poems help stories stick inside someone's mind.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it explains why poems are memorable, but it doesn't give any evidence that people are still reading or writing them today.

choices.text: Narrative poems began with Homer nearly 3,000 years ago.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it tells us when narrative poetry began (3,000 years ago), which is a historical fact, not proof that it is still important in the present.

choices.text: Narrative poems are the earliest way humans told stories.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it describes something from the past (the earliest way humans told stories), not evidence that narrative poetry is still important today.

choices.text: Narrative poems are read in classrooms in more than 100 countries.
choices.feedback: This is correct because it gives a present-day fact — poems being read in over 100 countries right now — which directly supports the idea that narrative poetry is still important today.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the narrative poems of Robert Frost and Rudyard Kipling different?

choices.text: Frost wrote about rural farms and forests, while Kipling wrote about soldiers and sailors.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Frost wrote about rural farms and forests, while Kipling wrote about soldiers and sailors.

choices.text: Frost wrote poems without any rhythm at all, while Kipling wrote poems with a steady beat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Frost avoided rhythm — rhythm is a key part of all narrative poetry described in the passage.

choices.text: Frost created ancient ballads meant to be sung, while Kipling created modern stories meant to be read.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither poet created ancient ballads; ballads were passed around during the Middle Ages, hundreds of years before Frost or Kipling lived.

choices.text: Frost used pounding and galloping rhythms, while Kipling used plain words and hidden ideas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps the two poets — Kipling had the galloping, pounding rhythms, and Frost was the one who used plain words hiding surprising ideas.

prompt._: Based on the article, why did narrative poetry help stories survive for thousands of years?

choices.text: because stories were only shared in one country at a time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ballads traveled across many countries — 'Barbara Allen' spread across both Britain and America in over 90 versions.

choices.text: because poets wrote their stories in paragraphs and prose
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that narrative poetry is different from prose — poems use lines, stanzas, and rhythm, not paragraphs.

choices.text: because audiences preferred reading poems over hearing them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says poems were chanted and sung aloud, not read — that's how they were shared and remembered for so long.

choices.text: because rhythm and patterns made stories easy to remember
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says ballads had simple, repeating patterns that made them easy to memorize, helping stories survive even before people could read.

prompt._: Which order of events best summarizes the history of narrative poetry presented in the article?

choices.text: It began with ancient chants, changed into sung ballads, was written by famous poets, and is still read today.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's order: ancient chanting, then sung ballads, then famous poets, then still read today.

choices.text: It began with Greek warrior battles, moved to travelers in woods, ended with brave soldiers, and is read today.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it lists topics from individual poems (warriors, a traveler, soldiers) rather than summarizing the history of narrative poetry as a whole.

choices.text: It began with ordinary prose, changed into short stanzas, became rhyming lines, and is now used in new movies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because narrative poetry began with spoken chants, not ordinary prose, and the article never says poems turned into movies.

choices.text: It began with famous written poems, changed into sung ballads, turned into ancient chants, and is read today.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — ancient chants came first, thousands of years before famous written poems.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are Homer's ancient poems and the ballads of the Middle Ages similar?

choices.text: Both were used to tell stories about rural farms and deep forests.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because writing about rural farms and forests describes Robert Frost, not Homer or the ballad singers of the Middle Ages.

choices.text: Both were created by famous writers who won the highest literary prizes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Homer won any prizes — prizes like the Pulitzer and Nobel are only mentioned for Frost and Kipling.

choices.text: Both were shared aloud from memory instead of being read from a page.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Homer chanted his stories from memory and ballads were sung aloud and passed from person to person, so both were shared out loud instead of read from a page.

choices.text: Both were written down in paragraphs to make them easier to read.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither Homer's poems nor ballads were written in paragraphs — they were performed aloud, and the passage says Homer 'could not read from a page.'

syllabus_metadata.title: Poems That Tell Stories: Narrative Poetry

assessments.title: Gulliver Among the Little People

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Gulliver's Travels: The Tiny World with a Giant Message

Waking Up in Ropes

Imagine waking up and not being able to move a single muscle. You try to lift your arm — nothing. You try to sit up — impossible. And when you finally manage to look down, you see why: hundreds of tiny ropes are pinning you to the ground. Now here's the wild part. The people tying you down are only six inches tall, about the height of a pencil!
stimulus.content_text: Gulliver's Travels: The Tiny World with a Giant MessageWaking Up in Ropes Imagine waking up and not being able to move a single muscle. You try to lift your arm — nothing. You try to sit up — impossible. And when you finally manage to look down, you see why: hundreds of tiny ropes are pinning you to the ground. Now here's the wild part. The people tying you down are only six inches tall, about the height of a pencil!

prompt._: Even though you are completely pinned to the ground, what does the text say about the people tying you down?

choices.text: They are hiding in the green grass.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions grass or says anything about the people hiding.

choices.text: They are only six inches tall.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the people are 'only six inches tall, about the height of a pencil' — that's a direct quote from the text!

choices.text: They are using thick metal chains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says 'hundreds of tiny ropes' were used, not thick metal chains.

choices.text: They are as strong as giants.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never describes the people as strong or giant-like — in fact, they are described as very tiny.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Giant Among Tiny People

In the story, Gulliver works as a ship's surgeon — a doctor who treats sick and injured sailors at sea. On his first voyage, a terrible storm wrecks his ship. Gulliver swims to shore, collapses from exhaustion, and falls into a deep sleep. When he wakes, he finds himself tied down by hundreds of tiny threads. He has landed on the island of Lilliput.

I attempted to rise, but was not able to stir: for, as I happened to lie on my back, I found my arms and legs were strongly fastened on each side to the ground; and my hair, which was long and thick, tied down in the same manner. I likewise felt several slender ligatures across my body, from my arm-pits to my thighs. I could only look upwards; the sun began to grow hot, and the light offended my eyes. I heard a confused noise about me; but in the posture I lay, could see nothing except the sky. In a little time I felt something alive moving on my left leg, which advancing gently forward over my breast, came almost up to my chin; when, bending my eyes downward as much as I could, I perceived it to be a human creature not six inches high, with a bow and arrow in his hands, and a quiver at his back.
stimulus.content_text: A Giant Among Tiny People In the story, Gulliver works as a ship's surgeon — a doctor who treats sick and injured sailors at sea. On his first voyage, a terrible storm wrecks his ship. Gulliver swims to shore, collapses from exhaustion, and falls into a deep sleep. When he wakes, he finds himself tied down by hundreds of tiny threads. He has landed on the island of Lilliput. I attempted to rise, but was not able to stir: for, as I happened to lie on my back, I found my arms and legs were strongly fastened on each side to the ground; and my hair, which was long and thick, tied down in the same manner. I likewise felt several slender ligatures across my body, from my arm-pits to my thighs. I could only look upwards; the sun began to grow hot, and the light offended my eyes. I heard a confused noise about me; but in the posture I lay, could see nothing except the sky. In a little time I felt something alive moving on my left leg, which advancing gently forward over my breast, came almost up to my chin; when, bending my eyes downward as much as I could, I perceived it to be a human creature not six inches high, with a bow and arrow in his hands, and a quiver at his back.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why was Gulliver unable to rise when he woke up on the island?

choices.text: He was injured during the terrible storm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Gulliver being hurt or injured during the storm.

choices.text: He was blinded by the hot sun in his eyes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the sun did bother Gulliver's eyes, the passage says the reason he couldn't rise was that he was tied down.

choices.text: He was still too tired from swimming to shore.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Gulliver couldn't rise due to being fastened to the ground, not because he was still tired from swimming.

choices.text: He was tied down by many tiny threads.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states his arms, legs, and body were 'strongly fastened' to the ground by threads and ligatures.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A War Over Eggs

As Gulliver learns the Lilliputian language, he discovers something surprising. Lilliput is at war with a neighboring island called Blefuscu. Are you ready for the reason? The two sides cannot agree on which end of a boiled egg you should crack open first. Lilliput insists on the little end. Blefuscu insists on the big end. Soldiers have died — all over a breakfast egg.

You might be thinking, "That is the silliest thing I have ever heard!" And that is exactly what Swift wanted you to think. By making the Lilliputians fight over something as small as an egg, Swift was poking fun at European leaders who dragged their countries into wars over equally foolish disagreements. To do this, Swift used a technique called satire — a type of writing that uses humor and exaggeration (stretching the truth way past what is real, like a funhouse mirror stretching your reflection) to criticize real problems. Swift was one of the funniest writers of his time — but he always had something important to say.
stimulus.content_text: A War Over Eggs As Gulliver learns the Lilliputian language, he discovers something surprising. Lilliput is at war with a neighboring island called Blefuscu. Are you ready for the reason? The two sides cannot agree on which end of a boiled egg you should crack open first. Lilliput insists on the little end. Blefuscu insists on the big end. Soldiers have died — all over a breakfast egg. You might be thinking, "That is the silliest thing I have ever heard!" And that is exactly what Swift wanted you to think. By making the Lilliputians fight over something as small as an egg, Swift was poking fun at European leaders who dragged their countries into wars over equally foolish disagreements. To do this, Swift used a technique called satire — a type of writing that uses humor and exaggeration (stretching the truth way past what is real, like a funhouse mirror stretching your reflection) to criticize real problems. Swift was one of the funniest writers of his time — but he always had something important to say.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after Gulliver learned the Lilliputian language?

choices.text: He discovered that Blefuscu agreed with Lilliput about eggs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Blefuscu and Lilliput did NOT agree — they were actually at war because they disagreed about which end of an egg to crack.

choices.text: He discovered that Swift was one of the funniest writers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Swift being a funny writer is something the passage's author tells the reader, not something Gulliver found out.

choices.text: He discovered that Lilliput was at war with Blefuscu.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says right after Gulliver learned the language, he discovered that Lilliput was at war with Blefuscu.

choices.text: He discovered that soldiers had died in many battles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions soldiers dying, but it doesn't say that is what Gulliver specifically discovered after learning the language.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Hero Who Is Not Welcome

Here is where the story takes a surprising turn. Gulliver eventually helps Lilliput win the war. He wades out into Blefuscu's harbor and drags the entire enemy fleet — their whole group of warships, like a parking lot full of boats — back to Lilliput using ropes. You would think that makes him a hero forever, right? But even after this heroic act, the Lilliputians grow nervous about his size and strength. Gulliver must escape the island to save his own life. Even a giant is not safe when small people feel threatened.
stimulus.content_text: A Hero Who Is Not Welcome Here is where the story takes a surprising turn. Gulliver eventually helps Lilliput win the war. He wades out into Blefuscu's harbor and drags the entire enemy fleet — their whole group of warships, like a parking lot full of boats — back to Lilliput using ropes. You would think that makes him a hero forever, right? But even after this heroic act, the Lilliputians grow nervous about his size and strength. Gulliver must escape the island to save his own life. Even a giant is not safe when small people feel threatened.

prompt._: In the sentence, "He wades out into Blefuscu's harbor and drags the entire enemy fleet — their whole group of warships, like a parking lot full of boats — back to Lilliput using ropes," what does the comparison "like a parking lot full of boats" help the reader understand about the word "fleet"?

choices.text: a place where boats are repaired
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a parking lot is used to show many things grouped together, not a place where boats are repaired.

choices.text: a large group of boats together
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the definition in the passage and the parking lot comparison show a fleet is a large group of boats all together.

choices.text: a line of boats moving quickly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a parking lot shows things sitting together in one place, not moving quickly.

choices.text: a single boat used in war
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says a fleet is their 'whole group' of warships, not just one single boat.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Four Voyages, Not Just One

Here is something that might surprise you — Lilliput is only the first of Gulliver's four voyages! In his second voyage, he visits Brobdingnag, a land of giants where he becomes the tiny one. Each voyage lets Swift hold up a mirror to a different part of human behavior — greed, pride, cruelty, and foolishness.
stimulus.content_text: Four Voyages, Not Just One Here is something that might surprise you — Lilliput is only the first of Gulliver's four voyages! In his second voyage, he visits Brobdingnag, a land of giants where he becomes the tiny one. Each voyage lets Swift hold up a mirror to a different part of human behavior — greed, pride, cruelty, and foolishness.

prompt._: According to the text, what is Brobdingnag?

choices.text: a land of tiny people where Gulliver is big
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it gets it backwards — in Brobdingnag, Gulliver is the tiny one, not the big one.

choices.text: a land of four voyages that Gulliver takes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Brobdingnag is one specific place Gulliver visits, not a name for all four of his voyages.

choices.text: a land of mirrors that Swift holds up
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'mirror' in the passage is a figure of speech meaning Swift shows us human behavior — it is not describing a real place.

choices.text: a land of giants where Gulliver is tiny
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Brobdingnag is 'a land of giants where he becomes the tiny one.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Story That Works Twice

This is what makes Gulliver's Travels so unusual, and why you might want to read it yourself someday. It works on two levels at the same time. Read it one way, and you get a thrilling adventure full of strange lands and impossible creatures. Read it another way, and you get a clever criticism of how real people and real governments behave. Children have loved the book for its wild imagination. Adults have admired it for its sharp wit. Both readers are right — they are just reading different layers of the same story.

Jonathan Swift figured out nearly three centuries ago that the best way to get people to see their own mistakes is to make them smile first. Not a bad trick, is it?
stimulus.content_text: A Story That Works Twice This is what makes Gulliver's Travels so unusual, and why you might want to read it yourself someday. It works on two levels at the same time. Read it one way, and you get a thrilling adventure full of strange lands and impossible creatures. Read it another way, and you get a clever criticism of how real people and real governments behave. Children have loved the book for its wild imagination. Adults have admired it for its sharp wit. Both readers are right — they are just reading different layers of the same story. Jonathan Swift figured out nearly three centuries ago that the best way to get people to see their own mistakes is to make them smile first. Not a bad trick, is it?

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that both children and adults can enjoy Gulliver's Travels?

choices.text: Jonathan Swift wrote the book nearly three centuries ago.
choices.feedback: When the book was written (three centuries ago) doesn't help explain why both children and adults can enjoy it today.

choices.text: The book is unusual compared to other stories of its time.
choices.feedback: The book being unusual compared to other stories doesn't give us any details about why both children and adults would enjoy reading it.

choices.text: Swift wanted to get people to see their own mistakes.
choices.feedback: Swift wanting people to see their mistakes is only about one layer of the story — the adult layer — so it doesn't show that both kids and adults can enjoy it.

choices.text: The story has thrilling adventures and clever criticism.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says children love the thrilling adventures and adults love the clever criticism, showing both groups find something to enjoy.

prompt._: Based on the article, how does Gulliver's experience in Lilliput contrast with his experience in Brobdingnag?

choices.text: In Lilliput he works as a ship's doctor, but in Brobdingnag he works as a brave soldier.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gulliver is a ship's surgeon before his travels, and the article never mentions him becoming a soldier in Brobdingnag.

choices.text: In Lilliput he is welcomed as a great hero, but in Brobdingnag he is feared as a dangerous enemy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was in Lilliput — not Brobdingnag — where Gulliver was eventually feared and had to escape to save his life.

choices.text: In Lilliput he fights in a war over eggs, but in Brobdingnag he fights in a war over ships.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the egg war only takes place in Lilliput, and the article says nothing about a war involving ships in Brobdingnag.

choices.text: In Lilliput he is a giant among tiny people, but in Brobdingnag he is a tiny person among giants.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states Gulliver is a giant in Lilliput but 'becomes the tiny one' in Brobdingnag, showing a clear contrast between the two voyages.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the main reason Jonathan Swift wrote about silly events like a war over boiled eggs?

choices.text: To write a true history book about real European naval battles.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Swift invented these exaggerated, silly events on purpose as satire — the article makes clear he was criticizing real behavior, not recording real history.

choices.text: To use humor to help people see their own foolish mistakes.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly states that Swift used humor and exaggeration (satire) to help people recognize their own foolish behavior, like fighting wars over unimportant things.

choices.text: To teach sailors how to prepare their breakfast eggs properly.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the egg war is not about cooking instructions — it's a joke meant to show how silly real arguments between countries can be.

choices.text: To show that giant travelers are dangerous to small island towns.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the story's deeper message is about human pride and foolishness, not a warning about giant travelers being a threat.

prompt._: Which sequence best summarizes the main events of Gulliver's time on the island of Lilliput?

choices.text: He wakes up tied down, learns about a silly war, captures the enemy ships, and then escapes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it lists the events in the exact order they happen in the passage: waking up tied down, learning about the egg war, capturing the ships, and then escaping.

choices.text: He wakes up tied down, starts a war over eggs, destroys the enemy ships, and then escapes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gulliver did not start the egg war — it was already going on when he arrived on the island.

choices.text: He captures the enemy ships, wakes up tied down, learns about a silly war, and then escapes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the events are out of order — Gulliver wakes up tied down first, not after capturing the ships.

choices.text: He wakes up tied down, travels to a land of giants, fights over boiled eggs, and then escapes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because visiting the land of giants happens on Gulliver's second voyage, not during his time on Lilliput.

prompt._: Which comparison best shows how the article explains that Gulliver's Travels can be enjoyed in two different ways?

choices.text: It compares Gulliver's job as a surgeon with his escape from Lilliput to show he changes careers in the story.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gulliver's career as a surgeon and his escape from Lilliput are just plot details, not a comparison that explains the two different ways readers can enjoy the book.

choices.text: It compares the exciting adventures with the hidden lessons about people's behavior to show the book has two layers.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly says the book works on two levels: one as a thrilling adventure and one as a clever criticism of real human behavior, which matches exactly what B describes.

choices.text: It compares Gulliver's storm at sea with the war over eggs to show that both are equally realistic.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never compares the storm and the egg war as equally realistic — the egg war is actually meant to seem ridiculous to make a point about foolish real-world conflicts.

choices.text: It compares the tiny people of Lilliput with the giants of Brobdingnag to show every voyage teaches the exact same lesson.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says each of Gulliver's four voyages explores a *different* part of human behavior like greed, pride, or foolishness — not the exact same lesson each time.

syllabus_metadata.title: Gulliver Among the Little People

assessments.title: Gulliver in the Land of Giants

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Gulliver Among Giants: The Land of Brobdingnag

Everything Flips Upside Down

Have you ever felt tiny — like when you stand next to a tall adult, or look up at a skyscraper? Now imagine a whole world where everything is that enormous. That's exactly what happens in Part Two of Gulliver's Travels, a book written by Jonathan Swift and published in 1726. In Part One, Gulliver visited Lilliput, where the people were only about six inches tall. In Part Two, he sails to a land called Brobdingnag, where the people are about sixty feet tall — roughly twelve times the height of a normal person. That means a Brobdingnagian is as tall as a six-story building! Swift placed Brobdingnag on the west coast of North America, far from any land that European explorers had mapped at the time.
stimulus.content_text: Gulliver Among Giants: The Land of BrobdingnagEverything Flips Upside Down Have you ever felt tiny — like when you stand next to a tall adult, or look up at a skyscraper? Now imagine a whole world where everything is that enormous. That's exactly what happens in Part Two of Gulliver's Travels, a book written by Jonathan Swift and published in 1726. In Part One, Gulliver visited Lilliput, where the people were only about six inches tall. In Part Two, he sails to a land called Brobdingnag, where the people are about sixty feet tall — roughly twelve times the height of a normal person. That means a Brobdingnagian is as tall as a six-story building! Swift placed Brobdingnag on the west coast of North America, far from any land that European explorers had mapped at the time.

prompt._: According to the text, how does the height of the people in Brobdingnag contrast with the height of a normal person?

choices.text: They are exactly six stories shorter than a normal person.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says a Brobdingnagian is as tall as a six-story building, meaning they are giant, not shorter than one.

choices.text: They are exactly six inches taller than a normal person.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because six inches is the height of the tiny Lilliput people, not a measurement of how tall the Brobdingnagians are.

choices.text: They are twelve times the height of a normal person.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states the Brobdingnagians are 'roughly twelve times the height of a normal person.'

choices.text: They are about twelve times shorter than a normal person.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it gets it backwards — the Brobdingnagians are twelve times taller than a normal person, not shorter.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Stranded Among Giants

Gulliver arrives in Brobdingnag after his ship lands on an unknown coast. He goes ashore to explore, but his crewmates row away in a panic when they spot a giant chasing them. Gulliver is left behind — stranded in a land where everything could crush him.

A farmer who is about seventy feet tall finds Gulliver hiding in a field of barley. The farmer picks him up and carries him home. At the farmhouse, the farmer's baby grabs him like a toy, and a cat three times the size of an ox nearly attacks him.

I fell into a high road, for so I took it to be, though it served to the inhabitants only as a footpath through a field of barley. Here I walked on for some time, but could see little on either side, it being now near harvest, and the corn rising at least forty foot. I was an hour walking to the end of this field, which was fenced in with a hedge of at least one hundred and twenty foot high, and the trees so lofty that I could make no computation of their altitude. There was a stile to pass from this field into the next. It had four steps, and a stone to cross over when you came to the uppermost. It was impossible for me to climb this stile, because every step was six foot high, and the upper stone above twenty. I was endeavouring to find some gap in the hedge, when I discovered one of the inhabitants in the next field, advancing towards the stile, of the same size with him whom I saw in the sea pursuing our boat. He appeared as tall as an ordinary spire-steeple.
stimulus.content_text: Stranded Among Giants Gulliver arrives in Brobdingnag after his ship lands on an unknown coast. He goes ashore to explore, but his crewmates row away in a panic when they spot a giant chasing them. Gulliver is left behind — stranded in a land where everything could crush him. A farmer who is about seventy feet tall finds Gulliver hiding in a field of barley. The farmer picks him up and carries him home. At the farmhouse, the farmer's baby grabs him like a toy, and a cat three times the size of an ox nearly attacks him. I fell into a high road, for so I took it to be, though it served to the inhabitants only as a footpath through a field of barley. It was impossible for me to climb this stile, because every step was six foot high, and the upper stone above twenty. I was endeavouring to find some gap in the hedge, when I discovered one of the inhabitants in the next field, advancing towards the stile, of the same size with him whom I saw in the sea pursuing our boat. He appeared as tall as an ordinary spire-steeple.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what causes Gulliver to be left behind on the unknown coast?

choices.text: His crewmates lose him in the tall barley.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Gulliver walks through the tall barley after he is already stranded — it is not the reason his crewmates left.

choices.text: His crewmates want to explore the island.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the crewmates were not exploring — they were fleeing in fear from a giant.

choices.text: His crewmates are frightened by a large cat.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the large cat appears later at the farmer's house, not on the coast where Gulliver is left behind.

choices.text: His crewmates row away to escape a giant.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states the crewmates rowed away in a panic after spotting a giant chasing them, which is why Gulliver was left behind.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
From Sideshow to Royal Court

The farmer discovers he can make money from Gulliver. He carries Gulliver to towns and markets, charging people to watch the tiny man perform tricks. Gulliver gives shows for about ten weeks, performing up to twelve times a day, until he becomes weak and thin from exhaustion.

Soon, the Queen of Brobdingnag wants to meet him. She buys him from the farmer for one thousand pieces of gold and brings him to live at the royal palace. The farmer's nine-year-old daughter, Glumdalclitch, comes along to take care of Gulliver, and Gulliver calls her his "little nurse."

The most important moments happen between Gulliver and the King. Gulliver proudly talks about Europe — its wars, its leaders, its weapons, and its laws. He spends five audiences, each lasting several hours, describing how the English government works. But the King asks tough questions and thinks European ways are foolish and cruel. When Gulliver explains gunpowder and cannons, the King is shocked. He calls the people of Europe "the most pernicious race of little odious vermin." Pernicious means causing great harm. Odious means deeply disgusting. Vermin are pests like rats or cockroaches — creatures people want to get rid of.

Swift wants you to notice that the King has a point. Swift was holding up a mirror to real England in the 1720s, showing his readers how their wars and weapons might look to someone wiser.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happened right after the Queen bought Gulliver from the farmer?

choices.text: Gulliver became weak and thin from exhaustion
choices.feedback: Gulliver became weak and thin during the market performances, which took place before the Queen bought him.

choices.text: Gulliver was brought to live at the royal palace
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says right after the Queen bought Gulliver, she brought him to live at the royal palace.

choices.text: Gulliver explained gunpowder and cannons to the King
choices.feedback: Gulliver's conversations with the King happened later, after he had already been living at the palace for some time.

choices.text: Gulliver performed tricks at markets for ten weeks
choices.feedback: This happened before the Queen bought Gulliver — the market shows were how the farmer made money earlier in the story.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Size Really Means

So what is Swift trying to tell you? In Lilliput, Gulliver looked down on tiny people and their tiny problems. In Brobdingnag, someone bigger and wiser looks down on Gulliver — and on all of European civilization, meaning the laws, customs, and beliefs that a society builds over time. Swift's message is that size does not make you better. What matters is wisdom, kindness, and being able to see your own faults.

At the end of Part Two, a giant eagle grabs Gulliver's traveling box and drops it into the sea. A passing English ship saves him, and he returns home after being away for about three years. When Gulliver first arrives home, he keeps bending down to walk through doorways because he still expects everything to be giant-sized.

This change in perspective — the way things look different when seen from a new angle — is exactly what Swift wanted for his readers, including you. The next time you see people arguing over something small, ask yourself: would a giant think this argument matters?

prompt._: How are Gulliver's experiences in Lilliput and Brobdingnag similar?

choices.text: In both places, Gulliver learns to speak a new language quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Gulliver learning to speak a new language in either Lilliput or Brobdingnag.

choices.text: In both places, someone bigger looks down on someone smaller.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that in Lilliput, Gulliver (the bigger one) looks down on tiny people, and in Brobdingnag, someone bigger looks down on Gulliver — in both cases, a bigger person looks down on a smaller one.

choices.text: In both places, Gulliver is rescued by a passing English ship.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the English ship rescue only happens once, at the end of Gulliver's time in Brobdingnag, not in both places.

choices.text: In both places, Gulliver stays for exactly three years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Gulliver was away for about three years total, not three years in each separate place.

prompt._: According to the article, how does Gulliver's experience in Lilliput contrast with his experience in Brobdingnag?

choices.text: In Lilliput he performs tricks for the local farmers, but in Brobdingnag he lives safely in the royal palace.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — it's actually in Brobdingnag (not Lilliput) where Gulliver performs tricks for farmers, and he ends up at the royal palace later in Brobdingnag too.

choices.text: In Lilliput he is dropped into the deep sea, but in Brobdingnag he is rescued by a passing ship.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — being dropped into the sea and rescued by a ship both happen at the end of his time in Brobdingnag, not in Lilliput.

choices.text: In Lilliput he is much larger than the people, but in Brobdingnag he is much smaller than them.
choices.feedback: This is correct — in Lilliput, Gulliver towers over the tiny six-inch people, but in Brobdingnag, he is the tiny one next to sixty-foot giants.

choices.text: In Lilliput he thinks European ways are very wise, but in Brobdingnag he thinks they are completely foolish.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — it is the King of Brobdingnag (not Gulliver) who thinks European ways are foolish, and the article doesn't say Gulliver himself changes his mind about Europe.

prompt._: According to the article, why did Jonathan Swift include the giant King's shocked reaction to European weapons?

choices.text: To help readers see their own society's faults from a new angle.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states Swift used the King's reaction like a mirror to show readers how their own society's wars and weapons might look foolish or cruel to someone wiser.

choices.text: To prove that giant kings are much more dangerous than normal humans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says giant kings are more dangerous than regular humans — the King's reaction is about judging European behavior, not showing off his own power.

choices.text: To show that Gulliver was a very brave and powerful world explorer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the King's shocked reaction actually makes Gulliver and Europeans look bad, not brave or powerful.

choices.text: To explain how gunpowder and cannons were invented in the 1720s.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never explains how gunpowder or cannons were invented — Gulliver just describes them to the King, and the King is horrified by them.

prompt._: Which choice shows the events from Gulliver's time in Brobdingnag in the correct order?

choices.text: The farmer finds Gulliver, an English ship saves him, and the Queen buys him.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it leaves out the Queen buying Gulliver and places the ship rescue in the wrong place in the order.

choices.text: The farmer finds Gulliver, the Queen buys him, and an English ship saves him.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the exact order in the passage: the farmer finds Gulliver, the Queen buys him, and then an English ship saves him at the very end.

choices.text: The Queen buys Gulliver, the farmer finds him, and an English ship saves him.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the farmer has to find Gulliver first before the Queen can ever buy him — the order is backwards.

choices.text: An English ship saves Gulliver, the farmer finds him, and the Queen buys him.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the English ship saves Gulliver at the end of the story, not at the beginning.

prompt._: How does the author support the central message that "size does not make you better"?

choices.text: By explaining how Gulliver survives being attacked by a giant cat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the cat attack is just a scary moment in the story, not evidence that supports the central message about size and worth.

choices.text: By describing the exact height of the people and the crops in Brobdingnag.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the exact heights and measurements help you imagine Brobdingnag, but they don't say anything about whether being bigger makes you a better person.

choices.text: By showing that the giant farmer is greedy and the giant King is wise.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the greedy farmer and the wise King are both giants, showing that being big doesn't automatically make you good or smart — it's your choices and wisdom that matter.

choices.text: By showing how Gulliver bends down to walk through doorways at home.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gulliver bending down at doorways shows how his perspective changed, but it doesn't directly support the idea that size doesn't determine someone's value.

assessments.title: Robinson Crusoe: Alone on an Island

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Robinson Crusoe: Twenty-Eight Years on a Desert Island

A Man Alone on the Shore

Published in 1719 by the English writer Daniel Defoe, it tells the story of a young man stranded on a deserted island near South America for twenty-eight years. Before the shipwreck, Crusoe was a young Englishman who ignored his father's warnings and chose a life at sea. Then a violent storm off the coast of South America smashed his ship to pieces and washed him ashore alone. This is how Robinson Crusoe's real story begins — not with dreams of adventure, but with sand in his teeth and terror in his chest.
stimulus.content_text: Robinson Crusoe: Twenty-Eight Years on a Desert IslandA Man Alone on the Shore Published in 1719 by the English writer Daniel Defoe, it tells the story of a young man stranded on a deserted island near South America for twenty-eight years. Before the shipwreck, Crusoe was a young Englishman who ignored his father's warnings and chose a life at sea. Then a violent storm off the coast of South America smashed his ship to pieces and washed him ashore alone. This is how Robinson Crusoe's real story begins — not with dreams of adventure, but with sand in his teeth and terror in his chest.

prompt._: According to the text, how was the real start of Robinson Crusoe's story different from dreams of adventure?

choices.text: It began with many years on the island.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the twenty-eight years on the island came after the shipwreck, not at the very beginning of his story.

choices.text: It began with his father's warnings at home.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because his father's warnings happened before the story really began, not at the true start of his adventure.

choices.text: It began with a calm trip across the sea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a violent storm smashed his ship to pieces — that is the opposite of a calm trip.

choices.text: It began with fear and pain on the shore.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says his story began with 'sand in his teeth and terror in his chest,' which means fear and pain on the shore.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Salvaging a Life from Wreckage

Crusoe was resourceful — good at figuring out solutions with whatever he had, the way you might build a fort from couch cushions when you have no real building materials. Over several trips back to the wrecked ship, he brought back knives, saws, axes, rope, cloth, gunpowder, seeds, and even a Bible.
stimulus.content_text: Salvaging a Life from Wreckage Crusoe was resourceful — good at figuring out solutions with whatever he had, the way you might build a fort from couch cushions when you have no real building materials. Over several trips back to the wrecked ship, he brought back knives, saws, axes, rope, cloth, gunpowder, seeds, and even a Bible.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Crusoe go back to the wrecked ship several times?

choices.text: To gather useful supplies for survival
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage lists practical survival items like tools, rope, seeds, and cloth that Crusoe brought back, showing his trips were all about gathering useful supplies.

choices.text: To look for other people from the ship
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage only talks about Crusoe collecting objects and supplies, and never mentions him searching for other people.

choices.text: To repair the broken ship for sailing
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Crusoe trying to fix or repair the ship — he was taking things FROM the wreck, not working on it.

choices.text: To collect treasures from the wreck
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the items listed — like saws, rope, and seeds — are practical survival tools, not treasures like gold or jewels.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Building a World from Scratch

With his rescued tools, Crusoe got to work. He built a shelter against a rocky hillside and surrounded it with a tall fence of sharpened wooden stakes. He shaped pots from clay and baked them hard in a fire he built. He planted barley — a grain crop similar to wheat that people use to make bread and soup — along with rice from the seeds he had saved. He caught and raised wild goats for milk and meat. Day by day, he turned a patch of wild land into a small working farm.

But staying alive meant more than growing food. Crusoe had to survive loneliness, too. He kept a journal and scratched marks into a wooden post to track the passing days. He read the Bible he had found on the ship. He thought deeply about his life and the choices that had brought him here. Slowly, through those quiet, difficult years, the reckless young man who had ignored his father grew into someone patient and thoughtful.

For many years, Crusoe believed he was completely alone on the island. Then one day, everything changed.

It happened one day, about noon, going towards my boat, I was exceedingly surprised with the print of a man's naked foot on the shore, which was very plain to be seen on the sand. I stood like one thunderstruck, or as if I had seen an apparition. I listened, I looked round me, but I could hear nothing, nor see anything; I went up to a rising ground to look farther; I went up the shore and down the shore, but it was all one; I could see no other impression but that one. I went to it again to see if there were any more, and to observe if it might not be my fancy; but there was no room for that, for there was exactly the very print of a foot — toes, heel, and every part of a foot. How it came thither I knew not, nor could I in the least imagine; but after innumerable fluttering thoughts, like a man perfectly confused and out of myself, I came home to my fortification, not feeling, as we say, the ground I went on.

That footprint eventually leads Crusoe to a man he names Friday. Friday becomes Crusoe's companion, and their story shifts from pure survival into something new — a story about friendship between two people from different worlds.

prompt._: According to the passage, which sequence of actions describes how Crusoe reacted when he first discovered the footprint on the shore?

choices.text: He followed the footprints into the forest, found Friday, and brought him back to camp
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Crusoe did not follow the prints into the forest or find Friday that day — the passage says meeting Friday happened much later.

choices.text: He stood frozen in shock, searched all around for more prints, then hurried home to his fortification
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Crusoe froze in shock, searched all around for more footprints, and then hurried home to his fortification — in exactly that order.

choices.text: He called out for help, ran to his boat, and sailed away from the island
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Crusoe never called out for help or sailed away — the passage says he searched the shore and then went home to his fortification.

choices.text: He drew a picture of the footprint, wrote about it in his journal, then went back to farming
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Crusoe drawing the footprint or returning to farming right after he discovered it.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Novel Built on a True Story

Many novels are pure invention, but some blend real events with imagined ones. Defoe based his story partly on the real experience of Alexander Selkirk, a Scottish sailor who was marooned — left behind and stranded, like being dropped off in the middle of nowhere with no ride home — on an island in the Pacific Ocean. Selkirk survived for four years by hunting wild goats and eating wild turnips. A passing ship finally rescued him. Defoe read about Selkirk's adventure and used it as a starting point, then stretched the story far beyond what really happened. He also made a clever choice: he wrote the entire novel using first-person narration — writing where the main character says "I" and speaks directly to the reader, like reading someone's personal diary. He created Crusoe's world from research, imagination, and one distinctive true story, and this made it feel startlingly real.
stimulus.content_text: A Novel Built on a True Story Many novels are pure invention, but some blend real events with imagined ones. Defoe based his story partly on the real experience of Alexander Selkirk, a Scottish sailor who was marooned — left behind and stranded, like being dropped off in the middle of nowhere with no ride home — on an island in the Pacific Ocean. Selkirk survived for four years by hunting wild goats and eating wild turnips. A passing ship finally rescued him. Defoe read about Selkirk's adventure and used it as a starting point, then stretched the story far beyond what really happened. He also made a clever choice: he wrote the entire novel using first-person narration — writing where the main character says "I" and speaks directly to the reader, like reading someone's personal diary. He created Crusoe's world from research, imagination, and one distinctive true story, and this made it feel startlingly real.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are Alexander Selkirk's real life and the story of Robinson Crusoe similar?

choices.text: Both are about a man stranded on an island.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Selkirk was marooned on a real island, and Defoe based Robinson Crusoe on that same idea of a man stranded on an island.

choices.text: Both are about a sailor writing a personal diary.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because first-person narration was a writing choice Defoe made for the novel — the passage never says Selkirk wrote a diary.

choices.text: Both are about a person who rescued wild goats.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Selkirk hunted wild goats to survive, which is the opposite of rescuing them.

choices.text: Both are about events that were completely made up.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that Selkirk's experience was a real event, not something made up.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why This Story Still Matters

Three hundred years after Defoe wrote it, people still read Robinson Crusoe. Stripped of every comfort, Crusoe cannot buy what he needs at a shop or call someone to fix what breaks. His story challenges readers to ask what they would do — and who they would become — if they had to start from nothing.
stimulus.content_text: Why This Story Still Matters Three hundred years after Defoe wrote it, people still read Robinson Crusoe. Stripped of every comfort, Crusoe cannot buy what he needs at a shop or call someone to fix what breaks. His story challenges readers to ask what they would do — and who they would become — if they had to start from nothing.

prompt._: Based on the text, what does the phrase "stripped of every comfort" mean for Robinson Crusoe?

choices.text: He cannot buy what he needs or call someone to fix what breaks.
