choices.text: He cannot remember what he did before he arrived on the island.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Crusoe forgetting anything about his life before the island.

choices.text: He cannot read the famous book written three hundred years ago.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about Crusoe being unable to read a book.

choices.text: He cannot figure out who he will become later in his long life.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Crusoe has trouble figuring out his future — that idea simply isn't there.

prompt._: How is the person Crusoe becomes on the island different from the person he was before the shipwreck?

choices.text: He changed from someone who loved reading into someone who preferred working
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows Crusoe both reading the Bible and working hard to survive — he didn't give up reading for working.

choices.text: He changed from a poor farmer into a skilled and wealthy sailor
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Crusoe was already a sailor before the shipwreck, and the passage never mentions him becoming wealthy.

choices.text: He changed from a lonely person into someone with many friends
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Crusoe was alone for many years and only found one friend, Friday — he didn't go from lonely to having many friends.

choices.text: He changed from a reckless young man into someone patient and thoughtful
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Crusoe started out reckless and ignoring his father's advice, but years on the island turned him into someone patient and thoughtful.

prompt._: Based on the text, how did the cause of Robinson Crusoe's stranding differ from the cause of Alexander Selkirk's stranding?

choices.text: A search for a companion brought Crusoe there, while Selkirk wanted to build a farm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says either man went to the island looking for a companion or to start a farm — those details are simply made up.

choices.text: A desire for adventure led Crusoe there, while Selkirk was forced to stay behind.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though Crusoe loved adventure, the passage says it was the violent storm — not his desire for adventure — that actually caused him to be stranded.

choices.text: A passing ship dropped Crusoe off, while Selkirk was trapped by a violent storm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two men switched around — it was Crusoe who was wrecked by a storm, not Selkirk, and no passing ship dropped Crusoe off.

choices.text: A storm wrecked Crusoe's ship, while Selkirk was marooned and left behind.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states a storm wrecked Crusoe's ship, while Selkirk was marooned, meaning he was deliberately left behind on his island.

prompt._: Which event happened after Crusoe saved supplies from the wreck but before he found the footprint on the shore?

choices.text: He built a farm and kept track of the days.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Crusoe building his farm and tracking the days after saving supplies but before he discovered the footprint.

choices.text: He was washed onto the island after a storm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being washed ashore by the storm happened at the very beginning, before Crusoe saved any supplies from the wreck.

choices.text: He met Friday and began a friendship.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because meeting Friday happened after the footprint — the passage says the footprint eventually led Crusoe to Friday.

choices.text: He was rescued by a passing ship.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being rescued by a passing ship is what happened to the real sailor Alexander Selkirk, not to Robinson Crusoe in the story.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the difference between Robinson Crusoe's physical survival and his emotional survival?

choices.text: Physical survival required tracking the days, while emotional survival required planting crop seeds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the two types — tracking days helped with emotional survival, and planting seeds was part of physical survival, so these are switched around.

choices.text: Physical survival required building a camp, while emotional survival required finding a human friend.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows Crusoe handled loneliness through journaling and reflection long before he ever met Friday.

choices.text: Physical survival required growing his food, while emotional survival required overcoming loneliness.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that growing food handled physical survival, and that Crusoe also had to 'survive loneliness' through journaling, reading, and reflection.

choices.text: Physical survival required gathering tools, while emotional survival required fighting wild animals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions fighting wild animals — Crusoe actually caught and raised goats rather than fighting them.

syllabus_metadata.title: Robinson Crusoe: Alone on an Island

assessments.title: Treasure Island: The Map and the Voyage

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Treasure Island: How a Rainy-Day Map Became the Greatest Pirate Story Ever Told

A Stranger at the Door

Before long, danger comes knocking at the door, and that boy's life will never be the same.

This scene opens Treasure Island, one of the most famous adventure novels ever written. A novel is a long work of fiction — a made-up story with enough room for many characters, settings, and twists. Robert Louis Stevenson, a Scottish author, published Treasure Island in 1883.
stimulus.content_text: Treasure Island: How a Rainy-Day Map Became the Greatest Pirate Story Ever ToldA Stranger at the Door Before long, danger comes knocking at the door, and that boy's life will never be the same. This scene opens Treasure Island, one of the most famous adventure novels ever written. A novel is a long work of fiction — a made-up story with enough room for many characters, settings, and twists. Robert Louis Stevenson, a Scottish author, published Treasure Island in 1883.

prompt._: According to the text, how is a novel different from a short, true account?

choices.text: A novel is a long work of made-up fiction
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that a novel is 'a long work of fiction — a made-up story.'

choices.text: A novel is a short work based on real events
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a novel is long and made-up (fiction), not short and based on real events.

choices.text: A novel is a true story written by one author
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a novel is fiction, meaning it is made up, not a true story.

choices.text: A novel is a brief tale with only one character
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a novel has room for 'many characters,' not just one character.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From a Drawing to a Story

On a rainy afternoon, Stevenson sat down with his stepson, Lloyd, and drew a map of an imaginary island. He sketched a coastline, colored in hills and harbors, and wrote the words "Treasure Island" across the top.
stimulus.content_text: From a Drawing to a Story On a rainy afternoon, Stevenson sat down with his stepson, Lloyd, and drew a map of an imaginary island. He sketched a coastline, colored in hills and harbors, and wrote the words "Treasure Island" across the top.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what most likely caused Stevenson to create the map of Treasure Island?

choices.text: He was finishing a story he had started
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage suggests the map was the starting point, not something made to finish an already-begun story.

choices.text: He needed a map for an upcoming voyage
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions any real voyage or travel plans.

choices.text: He wanted to find real gold on an island
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly says the island was imaginary, so Stevenson wasn't looking for real gold.

choices.text: He was spending time indoors with Lloyd
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows it was a rainy afternoon and Stevenson was spending time indoors with Lloyd, which most likely inspired them to draw the map together.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Jim Hawkins and the Dead Pirate's Secret

The hero of the story is Jim Hawkins, a boy who lives with his parents at a small inn called the Admiral Benbow. One day, a rough, scarred sailor named Billy Bones arrives and takes a room. Billy Bones drinks heavily and tells terrifying tales about life at sea. When Billy Bones suddenly dies, Jim and his mother search the old pirate's sea chest for money he owes them. Inside, Jim finds something far more valuable than coins — a treasure map showing where a fortune in gold lies buried on a distant island.

Stevenson tells this entire story through Jim's own voice. This is called first-person point of view — like reading someone's private diary. You experience every shock and scare exactly as Jim does, which pulls you right into the action. The novel opens with Jim looking back on the moment Billy Bones first appeared:

I remember him as if it were yesterday, as he came plodding to the inn door, his sea-chest following behind him in a hand-barrow; a tall, strong, heavy, nut-brown man; his tarry pigtail falling over the shoulders of his soiled blue coat; his hands ragged and scarred, with black, broken nails; and the sabre cut across one cheek, a dirty, livid white. I remember him looking round the cove and whistling to himself as he did so, and then breaking out in that old sea-song that he sang so often afterwards:

"Fifteen men on the dead man's chest —
Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum!"

in the high, old tottering voice that seemed to have been tuned and broken at the capstan bars. Then he rapped on the door with a bit of stick like a handspike that he carried, and when my father appeared, called roughly for a glass of rum.

Notice how Jim describes every detail — the tarry pigtail, the scarred hands, the broken nails. This is Stevenson showing readers that a good first-person narrator makes you feel as if you were standing right there beside him.
stimulus.content_text: Jim Hawkins and the Dead Pirate's Secret The hero of the story is Jim Hawkins, a boy who lives with his parents at a small inn called the Admiral Benbow. One day, a rough, scarred sailor named Billy Bones arrives and takes a room. Billy Bones drinks heavily and tells terrifying tales about life at sea. When Billy Bones suddenly dies, Jim and his mother search the old pirate's sea chest for money he owes them. Inside, Jim finds something far more valuable than coins — a treasure map showing where a fortune in gold lies buried on a distant island. Stevenson tells this entire story through Jim's own voice. This is called first-person point of view — like reading someone's private diary. You experience every shock and scare exactly as Jim does, which pulls you right into the action. The novel opens with Jim looking back on the moment Billy Bones first appeared: I remember him as if it were yesterday, as he came plodding to the inn door, his sea-chest following behind him in a hand-barrow; a tall, strong, heavy, nut-brown man; his tarry pigtail falling over the shoulders of his soiled blue coat; his hands ragged and scarred, with black, broken nails; and the sabre cut across one cheek, a dirty, livid white. I remember him looking round the cove and whistling to himself as he did so, and then breaking out in that old sea-song that he sang so often afterwards: "Fifteen men on the dead man's chest — Yo-ho-ho, and a bottle of rum!" in the high, old tottering voice that seemed to have been tuned and broken at the capstan bars. Then he rapped on the door with a bit of stick like a handspike that he carried, and when my father appeared, called roughly for a glass of rum. Notice how Jim describes every detail — the tarry pigtail, the scarred hands, the broken nails. This is Stevenson showing readers that a good first-person narrator makes you feel as if you were standing right there beside him.

prompt._: According to the passage, which event happened right after Billy Bones died?

choices.text: Jim and his mother searched the pirate's sea chest.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that Jim and his mother searched the sea chest right after Billy Bones died.

choices.text: Jim read the treasure map and planned a voyage.
choices.feedback: The passage only says Jim found the treasure map — it never mentions him planning a voyage after finding it.

choices.text: Billy Bones arrived at the inn and took a room.
choices.feedback: This happened at the start of the story, before Billy Bones died — not after.

choices.text: Billy Bones sang a song about fifteen men.
choices.feedback: Billy Bones sang the sea-song when he first arrived at the inn, which was before he died, not after.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Charming Villain

Jim brings the map to two local gentlemen: a squire — a wealthy landowner, like the richest farmer in a small town — named Trelawney, and a doctor named Livesey. The squire buys a ship called the Hispaniola and hires a crew in the port of Bristol. Among the new sailors is a friendly, one-legged cook named Long John Silver.

A parrot sits on his shoulder and squawks, "Pieces of eight! Pieces of eight!" Jim especially looks up to him. But Silver is secretly the leader of a band of pirates plotting mutiny — a rebellion where a crew turns against its captain, like students locking a teacher out of the classroom and taking over. Silver has planted his pirate friends among the crew, all waiting for the right moment to seize the ship and steal the treasure.
stimulus.content_text: The Charming Villain Jim brings the map to two local gentlemen: a squire — a wealthy landowner, like the richest farmer in a small town — named Trelawney, and a doctor named Livesey. The squire buys a ship called the Hispaniola and hires a crew in the port of Bristol. Among the new sailors is a friendly, one-legged cook named Long John Silver. A parrot sits on his shoulder and squawks, "Pieces of eight! Pieces of eight!" Jim especially looks up to him. But Silver is secretly the leader of a band of pirates plotting mutiny — a rebellion where a crew turns against its captain, like students locking a teacher out of the classroom and taking over. Silver has planted his pirate friends among the crew, all waiting for the right moment to seize the ship and steal the treasure.

prompt._: The passage compares mutiny to 'students locking a teacher out of the classroom and taking over.' Based on this comparison, what does the word mutiny most likely mean?

choices.text: a secret plan to hide treasure from others
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mutiny is about taking over leadership, not hiding treasure from others.

choices.text: a rebellion where a group turns against its leader
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines mutiny as 'a rebellion where a crew turns against its captain,' and the classroom comparison shows a group turning against their leader.

choices.text: a punishment given by a captain to a crew
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mutiny is when the crew rebels against the captain, not when the captain punishes the crew — it goes the other direction.

choices.text: a voyage taken by sailors across the ocean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mutiny describes a rebellion on a ship, not the act of sailing across the ocean.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Apple Barrel

But one night, Jim climbs inside an apple barrel on deck to grab a snack. From inside the barrel, he overhears Silver laying out the mutiny plan to another sailor.

He waits until Silver leaves, then races to tell the captain, the doctor, and the squire. The honest men are outnumbered, but they hold one advantage: they know the attack is coming, and the pirates do not know they have been discovered. The story fills with suspense — that tight, nervous feeling you get when danger is close but hasn't hit yet, like watching a glass teeter on the edge of a table. From here, the conflict explodes onto the island itself, with battles, betrayals, and a desperate hunt for gold.
stimulus.content_text: The Apple Barrel But one night, Jim climbs inside an apple barrel on deck to grab a snack. From inside the barrel, he overhears Silver laying out the mutiny plan to another sailor. He waits until Silver leaves, then races to tell the captain, the doctor, and the squire. The honest men are outnumbered, but they hold one advantage: they know the attack is coming, and the pirates do not know they have been discovered. The story fills with suspense — that tight, nervous feeling you get when danger is close but hasn't hit yet, like watching a glass teeter on the edge of a table. From here, the conflict explodes onto the island itself, with battles, betrayals, and a desperate hunt for gold.

prompt._: In the sentence, “The story fills with suspense — that tight, nervous feeling you get when danger is close but hasn't hit yet,” what does suspense mean?

choices.text: a nervous feeling when danger is near
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines suspense right in the sentence as 'that tight, nervous feeling you get when danger is close but hasn't hit yet.'

choices.text: a happy feeling when a trip begins
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because suspense is not a happy feeling — the passage clearly describes it as a tight, nervous feeling.

choices.text: a secret plan to attack the ship
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a secret plan to attack the ship describes the mutiny that Silver is planning, not the meaning of suspense.

choices.text: a quick race to warn the captain
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim racing to warn the captain is an action that happens in the story, not the definition of the word suspense.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Story That Invented Pirates

Stevenson created these images for his story, and they were so vivid that the whole world adopted them as fact.

Every novel you open is an invitation to see through someone else's eyes — even the eyes of a boy who never existed, standing in the dark inside an apple barrel, listening to pirates whisper.
stimulus.content_text: The Story That Invented Pirates Stevenson created these images for his story, and they were so vivid that the whole world adopted them as fact. Every novel you open is an invitation to see through someone else's eyes — even the eyes of a boy who never existed, standing in the dark inside an apple barrel, listening to pirates whisper.

prompt._: Which detail from the text gives an example of how a novel lets you see through someone else's eyes?

choices.text: The author created vivid images for a story.
choices.feedback: This describes what the author did, but it doesn't give an example of experiencing a story from a character's point of view.

choices.text: The whole world adopted the images as fact.
choices.feedback: This tells us people believed the images were real, but it doesn't show an example of seeing through a character's eyes.

choices.text: The story invented the idea of real pirates.
choices.feedback: The passage says Stevenson created vivid pirate images, but it never says he invented the idea of real pirates.

choices.text: A boy listens to pirates in a barrel.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the boy hiding in the apple barrel as the specific example of what it feels like to see through someone else's eyes in a novel.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is Long John Silver different from Billy Bones when they are first introduced?

choices.text: Silver hides in an apple barrel, while Bones hides in a large sea chest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim is the one who hides inside the apple barrel, and Billy Bones's sea chest contains a treasure map, not Billy Bones himself.

choices.text: Silver is a wealthy landowner, while Bones is a poor and lonely sailor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Silver is a ship's cook, not a wealthy landowner — the passage says Squire Trelawney is the wealthy landowner.

choices.text: Silver seems friendly and likable, while Bones is rough and terrifying.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly calls Billy Bones rough and terrifying, while Long John Silver is described as friendly and someone Jim looks up to.

choices.text: Silver wants to buy a new ship, while Bones wants to steal the treasure.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Silver wants to buy a ship or that Bones wants to steal the treasure when they are first introduced.

prompt._: Why does the article say readers feel close to the danger in Treasure Island?

choices.text: Because the map shows the island clearly and labels its hills and harbors
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the map is what inspired Stevenson to write the story, not the reason readers feel close to the danger.

choices.text: Because the squire and doctor explain the voyage before it begins
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the squire and doctor are characters in the story, and the article never says they explain the voyage to readers.

choices.text: Because Jim tells the story himself and describes what he sees and hears
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly says that Jim's first-person narration makes readers experience every shock and scare exactly as Jim does, pulling them into the action.

choices.text: Because the pirates know Jim has discovered their mutiny plan
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the pirates do NOT know Jim discovered their plan — that's actually the good guys' secret advantage.

prompt._: Which event happens after the ship is prepared for the voyage but before the fighting on the island begins?

choices.text: The pirates battle for gold on the island
choices.feedback: This is what happens after the apple barrel scene, so it comes too late in the story to be the right answer.

choices.text: Jim finds the treasure map in Billy Bones's chest
choices.feedback: This happened earlier in the story, back at the inn before the ship was ever bought or prepared for the voyage.

choices.text: Jim overhears the mutiny plan in the apple barrel
choices.feedback: This is correct! Jim overhears the mutiny plan in the apple barrel after the ship is ready but before the battles on the island begin.

choices.text: Stevenson draws a map with Lloyd on a rainy day
choices.feedback: This happened before the story even began — it's when Stevenson the author drew a map with his stepson, not an event inside the story.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the beginning and ending of the article to show its main idea?

choices.text: Both focus on how doctors and squires help Jim solve problems.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the doctor and squire are minor characters mentioned briefly, and helping Jim is not what connects the beginning and ending of the article.

choices.text: Both prove that treasure maps are the most important part of any novel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the treasure map is just one part of the plot, and the article never claims maps are the most important part of any novel.

choices.text: Both show how one made-up pirate story can pull readers into adventure.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the opening and closing of the article highlight how this one made-up pirate story draws readers into adventure, which is the article's main idea.

choices.text: Both explain how real sailors taught Stevenson to write true history.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Stevenson invented the story from an imaginary map he drew — the article never says real sailors taught him true history.

syllabus_metadata.title: Treasure Island: The Map and the Voyage

assessments.title: Treasure Island: Battle for the Treasure

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Treasure Island: How an Ordinary Boy Outwitted the Pirates

A Boy Alone on a Drifting Ship

Can you imagine sailing toward a dangerous island, knowing that half the crew wants to betray you? That is exactly the situation facing Jim Hawkins — an innkeeper's son who, just weeks ago, had never left his small English village.
stimulus.content_text: Treasure Island: How an Ordinary Boy Outwitted the PiratesA Boy Alone on a Drifting Ship Can you imagine sailing toward a dangerous island, knowing that half the crew wants to betray you? That is exactly the situation facing Jim Hawkins — an innkeeper's son who, just weeks ago, had never left his small English village.

prompt._: According to the text, what was Jim's life like just weeks ago, in contrast to his current dangerous situation?

choices.text: He had never left his small English village.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses these exact words: Jim had 'never left his small English village' just weeks ago, showing his life used to be simple and safe.

choices.text: He had worked as the captain of a large ship.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Jim is described as an innkeeper's son, not a ship's captain — there is no mention of him ever commanding a ship.

choices.text: He had sailed on a ship with a friendly crew.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says half the crew wants to betray him, so the crew is not friendly.

choices.text: He had already explored the dangerous island.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Jim is sailing toward the dangerous island now — he hasn't explored it yet.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Stockade and a Daring Escape

As the crew went ashore, the tension between the two groups finally snapped. Silver and his pirates showed their true colors. Fighting broke out. Jim and his allies retreated to an old log stockade — a small wooden fort with a fence of tall logs and a cabin inside, like a ring of sharpened telephone poles protecting a shed.

But Jim did not stay put for long. He found a tiny round boat called a coracle hidden on the beach — so small and light that one person can carry it, like a giant walnut shell with room for a single passenger. He cut the ship's anchor rope so the pirates could not sail away, climbed aboard the drifting vessel, and fought the pirate guarding it.

Stevenson describes the desperate struggle aboard the Hispaniola in Jim's own words — and notice how honestly Jim admits his fear:

I was no sooner certain of this than I began to feel sick, faint, and terrified. The hot blood was running over my back and chest. The dirk, where it had pinned my shoulder to the mast, seemed to burn like a hot iron; yet it was not so much these real sufferings that distressed me, for these, it seemed to me, I could bear without a groan; it was the horror I had upon my mind of falling from the cross-trees into that still green water beside the body of the coxswain. I clung with both hands till my nails ached, and I shut my eyes as if to cover up the peril.
stimulus.content_text: The Stockade and a Daring Escape As the crew went ashore, the tension between the two groups finally snapped. Silver and his pirates showed their true colors. Fighting broke out. Jim and his allies retreated to an old log stockade — a small wooden fort with a fence of tall logs and a cabin inside, like a ring of sharpened telephone poles protecting a shed. But Jim did not stay put for long. He found a tiny round boat called a coracle hidden on the beach — so small and light that one person can carry it, like a giant walnut shell with room for a single passenger. He cut the ship's anchor rope so the pirates could not sail away, climbed aboard the drifting vessel, and fought the pirate guarding it. Stevenson describes the desperate struggle aboard the Hispaniola in Jim's own words — and notice how honestly Jim admits his fear: I was no sooner certain of this than I began to feel sick, faint, and terrified. The hot blood was running over my back and chest. The dirk, where it had pinned my shoulder to the mast, seemed to burn like a hot iron; yet it was not so much these real sufferings that distressed me, for these, it seemed to me, I could bear without a groan; it was the horror I had upon my mind of falling from the cross-trees into that still green water beside the body of the coxswain. I clung with both hands till my nails ached, and I shut my eyes as if to cover up the peril.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Jim cut the ship's anchor rope?

choices.text: to make it easier to reach the stockade
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never connects cutting the anchor rope to reaching the stockade.

choices.text: to keep the pirates from sailing away
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Jim cut the rope 'so the pirates could not sail away' — that's the exact reason given.

choices.text: to free himself from the fight on board
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Jim cut the rope before he even boarded the ship, so it wasn't to escape a fight.

choices.text: to help the ship drift toward the beach
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Jim wanting the ship to drift toward the beach.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Man Who Already Found the Gold

Now here is a twist you might not see coming! On the island, Jim met someone unexpected. Ben Gunn was a marooned pirate — marooned meaning left behind on purpose, abandoned on a wild, lonely island by his own crewmates three years earlier. He had once sailed with the feared Captain Flint, the very pirate who buried the treasure in the first place. Imagine being stuck alone on an island for three whole years!

Left alone all that time, Ben Gunn had done something — he had already dug up the treasure himself! He agreed to help Jim's side in exchange for passage back to England. So when Silver and his pirates finally followed the map to the spot marked with an X, they found nothing but an empty hole. Can you picture their faces? The pirates were furious. But Doctor Livesey, Ben Gunn, and the others were hiding nearby. They ambushed the confused pirates — surprised them with a sudden attack, the way a cat leaps from behind a curtain. Silver's men scattered into the jungle.
stimulus.content_text: The Man Who Already Found the Gold Now here is a twist you might not see coming! On the island, Jim met someone unexpected. Ben Gunn was a marooned pirate — marooned meaning left behind on purpose, abandoned on a wild, lonely island by his own crewmates three years earlier. He had once sailed with the feared Captain Flint, the very pirate who buried the treasure in the first place. Imagine being stuck alone on an island for three whole years! Left alone all that time, Ben Gunn had done something — he had already dug up the treasure himself! He agreed to help Jim's side in exchange for passage back to England. So when Silver and his pirates finally followed the map to the spot marked with an X, they found nothing but an empty hole. Can you picture their faces? The pirates were furious. But Doctor Livesey, Ben Gunn, and the others were hiding nearby. They ambushed the confused pirates — surprised them with a sudden attack, the way a cat leaps from behind a curtain. Silver's men scattered into the jungle.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Ben Gunn do before Silver and his pirates followed the map?

choices.text: He sailed away back to England.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Ben Gunn had not sailed back to England yet — he was still on the island and agreed to help Jim's side in exchange for a trip back to England.

choices.text: He dug up the buried treasure.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly tells us Ben Gunn 'had already dug up the treasure himself' before Silver's crew followed the map.

choices.text: He hid the gold in the jungle.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Ben Gunn hid the gold in the jungle — it only tells us he dug it up, not where he put it.

choices.text: He ambushed the confused pirates.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was Doctor Livesey, Ben Gunn, and the others together who ambushed the pirates — and that happened after the pirates found the empty hole, not before.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Silver's Last Trick

With the pirates defeated, Jim and his friends loaded the treasure onto the Hispaniola. But what about Long John Silver — do you think he faced justice?

Long John Silver, ever the survivor, managed to talk his way out of punishment. Silver was a villain, but he had also been kind to Jim in his own way. What do you think — did Jim make the right call letting him go?
stimulus.content_text: Silver's Last Trick With the pirates defeated, Jim and his friends loaded the treasure onto the Hispaniola. But what about Long John Silver — do you think he faced justice? Long John Silver, ever the survivor, managed to talk his way out of punishment. Silver was a villain, but he had also been kind to Jim in his own way. What do you think — did Jim make the right call letting him go?

prompt._: The text asks if Silver 'faced justice,' and then compares this idea to how he 'managed to talk his way out of punishment.' Based on this comparison, what does the phrase 'faced justice' mean?

choices.text: Receiving a hidden treasure
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hidden treasure is about finding something valuable, and has nothing to do with punishing someone for their actions.

choices.text: Receiving a quick rescue
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a quick rescue means being saved from danger, which is the opposite of being held responsible for wrongdoing.

choices.text: Receiving a fair reward
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a reward is something positive given for good behavior, but Silver was a villain who did wrong things — not someone who deserved a reward.

choices.text: Receiving a strict penalty
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares 'faced justice' with 'talked his way out of punishment,' showing that facing justice means actually receiving a strict penalty for what you did wrong.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Just a Story About Gold

Robert Louis Stevenson wrote Treasure Island in 1883. He invented Jim Hawkins as a fictional narrator — the character who tells the story in first person, using "I" and "me." Stevenson's novel gave the world many of its most famous pirate ideas: treasure maps with an X marking the spot, buried chests of gold, and colorful one-legged rogues. You have probably seen these ideas in movies and cartoons without even realizing they all started here!

But here is the most important thing to take away: the heart of Treasure Island is not really about gold. It is about character development — the way a person in a story changes on the inside, not just what happens to them on the outside. At the start, Jim is a quiet boy helping his mother run an inn. By the end, he has faced killers, outsmarted pirates, and sailed a ship by himself. That inner change is what separates a great novel from a simple list of adventures. Think about it this way — the treasure Jim carries home is not just gold. It is the knowledge of who he became when everything was on the line. And that is something no pirate could ever steal.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Just a Story About Gold Robert Louis Stevenson wrote Treasure Island in 1883. He invented Jim Hawkins as a fictional narrator — the character who tells the story in first person, using "I" and "me." Stevenson's novel gave the world many of its most famous pirate ideas: treasure maps with an X marking the spot, buried chests of gold, and colorful one-legged rogues. You have probably seen these ideas in movies and cartoons without even realizing they all started here! But here is the most important thing to take away: the heart of Treasure Island is not really about gold. It is about character development — the way a person in a story changes on the inside, not just what happens to them on the outside. At the start, Jim is a quiet boy helping his mother run an inn. By the end, he has faced killers, outsmarted pirates, and sailed a ship by himself. That inner change is what separates a great novel from a simple list of adventures. Think about it this way — the treasure Jim carries home is not just gold. It is the knowledge of who he became when everything was on the line. And that is something no pirate could ever steal.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the term 'character development' mean?

choices.text: the list of adventures that happen in a novel
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually says a great novel is different FROM a simple list of adventures, so adventures alone are not what character development means.

choices.text: the events that take place on the outside of a story
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says character development is about changes on the INSIDE, not what happens on the outside.

choices.text: the way a person in a story changes on the inside
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines 'character development' as 'the way a person in a story changes on the inside, not just what happens to them on the outside.'

choices.text: the way an author creates a treasure map for readers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because treasure maps are mentioned as a famous pirate idea from the book, not as a definition of character development.

prompt._: Which statement best shows the contrast between Jim at the beginning of the article and Jim by the end?

choices.text: He starts as a boy searching for treasure and ends as a man who buries the gold again.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim never buries the gold again — the passage says he and his friends loaded the treasure onto the ship to take home.

choices.text: He starts as a sailor with years of experience and ends as a pirate leader with a crew.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim starts as an innkeeper's son with no sailing experience, not as an experienced sailor, and he never becomes a pirate leader.

choices.text: He starts as a frightened child on the island and ends as someone who never feels fear.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows Jim feeling fear during the ship battle, proving he still gets scared — he just acts bravely anyway.

choices.text: He starts as a quiet innkeeper's son and ends as a brave boy who outsmarts pirates.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly tells us Jim starts as a quiet innkeeper's son and ends having outsmarted pirates and sailed a ship alone — showing his growth.

prompt._: Why were Silver's pirates left confused and furious when they reached the spot marked on the treasure map?

choices.text: Because Doctor Livesey and his allies had hidden the gold inside the stockade
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Ben Gunn dug up the treasure, not that Doctor Livesey hid it inside the stockade.

choices.text: Because Ben Gunn had already dug up the treasure during his three years alone
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that Ben Gunn had already dug up the treasure during his three years alone on the island, leaving nothing but an empty hole for the pirates to find.

choices.text: Because Jim had already moved the gold onto the Hispaniola by himself
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim did not move the gold by himself — it was Ben Gunn who had already dug it up during his three years alone on the island.

choices.text: Because the treasure map was a fake that Captain Flint drew to trick people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the map was fake — the treasure was real, but Ben Gunn had already removed it from the spot marked with an X.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the correct order of events during Jim's adventure?

choices.text: Jim cuts the anchor rope, hides in the stockade, and meets a marooned pirate.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim hid in the stockade before he cut the anchor rope, not after.

choices.text: Jim hides in the stockade, meets a marooned pirate, and cuts the anchor rope.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim met the marooned pirate after cutting the anchor rope, not before.

choices.text: Jim hides in the stockade, cuts the anchor rope, and meets a marooned pirate.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Jim first hiding in the stockade, then cutting the anchor rope to strand the pirates, and finally meeting Ben Gunn on the island.

choices.text: Jim meets a marooned pirate, cuts the anchor rope, and hides in the stockade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jim didn't meet the marooned pirate first — hiding in the stockade happened before he met Ben Gunn.

prompt._: The author's main idea is that the true meaning of the story is not about finding gold. Which comparison from the text best supports this main idea?

choices.text: Comparing the tall wooden stockade to the tiny round boat on the beach.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because comparing the stockade to the coracle is just describing two objects in the story and has nothing to do with the main idea about character development.

choices.text: Comparing the dangerous pirate adventures to the quiet life in the small village.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because comparing Jim's adventures to his quiet village life describes outside events, not the inner change the author says is the true meaning.

choices.text: Comparing the gold the pirates want to the brave young person Jim becomes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the real treasure is who Jim became, directly comparing the gold the pirates want to the brave, changed person Jim grows into on the inside.

choices.text: Comparing the heavy chests of gold to the empty hole in the dirt.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because comparing the gold chests to the empty hole is just about the treasure hunt, not about Jim's inner growth.

syllabus_metadata.title: Treasure Island: Battle for the Treasure

assessments.title: The Sword in the Stone

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Boy Who Pulled a Sword from Stone

A Sword in a Churchyard

A gleaming sword stood locked in a block of stone in a London churchyard. Golden letters on the stone spelled out a challenge: "Whoever pulls this sword from this stone is the rightful King of England." Knights rode in from every corner of the land. Not one of them moved it even a hair's width. The sword stayed fixed in the stone as if it had grown roots.

This is one of the most famous stories ever told. But before you picture it as something that really happened, you should know that this story is a legend — a traditional tale about a human hero that may have been inspired by real people or events but is not a factual record of history. You might think legends and myths are the same thing. Actually, they are different. A myth usually involves gods or explains how the natural world works, like why the sun rises. A legend centers on a human hero and often connects to a real time or place. The story of King Arthur is a legend.

There is no single "correct" version of this story. Writers have been retelling Arthur's legend for over eight hundred years, and each one changed details along the way. Some versions say the sword was in an anvil on top of a stone. The version you are about to read is one of the most well-known, but it is not the only one.

prompt._: According to the text, how is a legend different from a myth?

choices.text: A legend centers on a human hero, but a myth usually involves gods.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that a legend centers on a human hero while a myth usually involves gods.

choices.text: A legend usually involves gods, but a myth centers on a human hero.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it has the definitions switched around — it's myths that involve gods and legends that center on human heroes, not the other way around.

choices.text: A legend explains how the sun rises, but a myth tells true events.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says myths explain things like why the sun rises, not legends — and neither one tells true events.

choices.text: A legend is a factual record, but a myth is a traditional story.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly says a legend is NOT a factual record of history.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Secret Hidden Since Birth

While knights struggled with the sword in London, a boy named Arthur was growing up in the quiet countryside. He lived with Sir Ector, a kind and loyal knight, and Ector's son, Kay. Arthur believed he was simply Ector's younger boy. He had no idea that his real father was King Uther Pendragon, the king who had ruled England before him.

When Uther died, the kingdom fell into chaos — total confusion and disorder, like a classroom where every student shouts at once and no one listens. Powerful lords fought each other for control. Common people suffered. But before Uther died, he had given his baby son to a powerful wizard named Merlin. Merlin knew Arthur was destined — meant by fate — to become a great king someday. The time was not yet right, though. So Merlin placed baby Arthur with Sir Ector, who raised the boy as his own.

Arthur grew up learning to ride horses, polish armor, and serve as Kay's squire. A squire was a knight's personal assistant, like a teammate who carries the equipment bag before the big game. A squire handled a knight's weapons and shield and helped him prepare for battle.

He had no idea that his real father was King Uther Pendragon, the king who had ruled England before him. When Uther died, the kingdom fell into chaos — total confusion and disorder, like a classroom where every student shouts at once and no one listens. Powerful lords fought each other for control. Common people suffered. But before Uther died, he had given his baby son to a powerful wizard named Merlin. Merlin knew Arthur was destined — meant by fate — to become a great king someday. A squire was a knight's personal assistant, like a teammate who carries the equipment bag before the big game. A squire handled a knight's weapons and shield and helped him prepare for battle.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Merlin place baby Arthur with Sir Ector?

choices.text: Because King Uther wanted his son to live in London.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions King Uther wanting Arthur in London — Arthur actually grew up in the quiet countryside, far from London.

choices.text: Because Sir Ector needed a squire to polish his armor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur polished armor as a squire for Kay, Sir Ector's son — not for Sir Ector himself.

choices.text: Because the powerful lords were fighting each other for control.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the lords fighting each other describes the chaos in the kingdom, but it is not the reason Merlin chose to place Arthur with Sir Ector.

choices.text: Because it was not yet time for Arthur to rule.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Merlin knew Arthur was destined to be king but 'the time was not yet right,' so he placed Arthur with Sir Ector until he was ready.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Moment Everything Changed

One day, Sir Ector and Kay traveled to London for a grand tournament — a competition where knights fought in contests of skill, similar to a sports championship for warriors. Arthur came along as Kay's squire. On the morning of the first contest, Kay discovered a problem. He had left his sword back at the inn where they were staying.

Arthur raced through the streets, reached the inn, and found it locked tight. Then Arthur noticed the churchyard nearby and the strange sword sticking out of its stone. He did not know that the strongest men in England had already failed to move it. He simply walked up, wrapped his fingers around the handle, and pulled.

The sword slid free as smoothly as a knife through soft butter.

Arthur brought the sword to Kay. Kay's eyes went wide — he recognized it immediately. Sir Ector dropped to his knees. He knew what this meant. With a trembling voice, Ector told Arthur the truth about his royal birth. Arthur was stunned. He was not a simple squire. He was the heir to the throne — the person next in line to become king, the way an oldest child might inherit a family farm.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened immediately after Arthur found the inn locked?

choices.text: He pulled the weapon from the rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pulling the sword from the stone came after Arthur noticed it, so it was not the immediate next step after finding the inn locked.

choices.text: He handed the missing sword to Kay.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because handing the sword to Kay was one of the last things Arthur did, after he had already noticed and pulled the sword from the stone.

choices.text: He traveled to the grand tournament.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur had already traveled to the tournament before any of this happened — that's what brought him to London in the first place.

choices.text: He saw the sword in the stone.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses the word 'then' to show that noticing the sword in the stone was the very next thing that happened after Arthur found the inn locked.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A King No One Expected

How could a boy with no army and no lands rule over them? Arthur answered their doubts without a single word. He placed the sword back into the stone. Then, with everyone watching, he pulled it out again.

Arthur was crowned King of England. With Merlin as his advisor, he built a kingdom based on justice and honor. He gathered the bravest knights and seated them around the Round Table — a circular table with no head, so that every knight who sat there was equal. No seat was more important than another. That table became a symbol of fairness itself.

People have been telling and retelling Arthur's legend for centuries because it carries an idea that still matters today: greatness does not always announce itself with trumpets and armor.

Then, with everyone watching, he pulled it out again. Arthur was crowned King of England. With Merlin as his advisor, he built a kingdom based on justice and honor. He gathered the bravest knights and seated them around the Round Table — a circular table with no head, so that every knight who sat there was equal. No seat was more important than another. That table became a symbol of fairness itself. People have been telling and retelling Arthur's legend for centuries because it carries an idea that still matters today: greatness does not always announce itself with trumpets and armor.

prompt._: In the sentence "He gathered the bravest knights and seated them around the Round Table — a circular table with no head," what does the phrase "no head" show by comparing the seats at the table?

choices.text: that one seat was saved for the king
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a seat being saved or set aside for King Arthur.

choices.text: that the largest seat belonged to Merlin
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Merlin had a largest or most important seat at the table.

choices.text: that every seat was the same in importance
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says 'every knight who sat there was equal' and 'no seat was more important than another,' showing that 'no head' means all seats were the same in importance.

choices.text: that the seats were placed in straight rows
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the table was described as circular, not arranged in straight rows.

prompt._: According to the text, how does Arthur's belief about who he is contrast with the truth?

choices.text: He thought he was a powerful wizard, but he was actually a normal boy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur never thought he was a wizard — Merlin was the wizard in the story, not Arthur.

choices.text: He thought he was Sir Ector's son, but he was actually the king's son.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Arthur believed he was Sir Ector's son, but his real father was King Uther Pendragon.

choices.text: He thought he was the rightful king, but he was actually a simple squire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the truth — Arthur actually thought he was a simple squire, but he turned out to be the rightful king.

choices.text: He thought he was a famous knight, but he was actually a poor farmer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Arthur was a squire, and there is no mention of him thinking he was a famous knight or a poor farmer.

prompt._: Based on information from across the article, why was Arthur able to pull the sword from the stone when the strongest knights could not?

choices.text: Because Arthur was stronger than all the knights who tried before him
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Arthur was stronger than the knights — in fact, the whole point of the story is that strength alone wasn't what made the difference.

choices.text: Because Arthur had trained as a squire and knew how to handle weapons
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because squire training helped Arthur handle weapons in battle, but it had nothing to do with the magical challenge of pulling the sword from the stone.

choices.text: Because Merlin used magic to make the sword easy to pull out
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Merlin cast a spell on the sword to make it easier to pull out — Merlin's role was to protect Arthur and guide him, not to secretly help him with the sword.

choices.text: Because Arthur was the true heir to the throne, destined to be king
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the sword's inscription said only the rightful King of England could pull it out, and Arthur was destined by fate to be that king, which is why he succeeded where all the powerful knights failed.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best summarizes Arthur's story in the article?

choices.text: He pulls the sword from the stone, then trains as a squire, and becomes a great king.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Arthur was already a squire before he ever pulled the sword from the stone.

choices.text: He travels to a grand tournament, finds a locked inn, and brings a lost sword to Kay.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only describes one small part of the story (the tournament morning) and leaves out the most important events, like Arthur becoming king.

choices.text: He fights in a grand tournament, pulls the sword from the stone, and rules with an army.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur never fought in the tournament himself, and the passage says he ruled through justice and honor, not with an army.

choices.text: He trains as a squire, pulls the sword from the stone, and becomes a great king.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Arthur trained as a squire first, then pulled the sword from the stone, and then became a great and fair king — exactly that order.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are the author's explanations of a "squire" and an "heir" similar?

choices.text: Both are shown as positions of great wealth and power.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither explanation mentions wealth or power; both use simple, relatable comparisons from ordinary life.

choices.text: Both are described as important jobs for young knights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'heir' is not a job for a young knight — it means the person next in line to become king.

choices.text: Both are defined as magical characters from old myths.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both words are explained with real-life examples, and neither one is connected to magic or myths.

choices.text: Both are explained by comparing them to ordinary life.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author explains 'squire' using a sports team analogy and 'heir' using a family farm analogy — both are everyday comparisons kids can understand.
