assessments.title: Knights of the Round Table

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Round Table: King Arthur and the Dream of a Perfect Kingdom

A Table with No Head

Picture a massive table, perfectly round, with seats for over a hundred knights. No seat sits at the head, because there is no head. According to legend, King Arthur built this table on purpose. He wanted his knights to understand one idea right away: courage and character matter more than rank or wealth.

But here is something you should know before we go further. Historians are not sure a real King Arthur ever existed. His story is a legend — a tale passed down through centuries, rooted in a culture's values and possibly based on a real person, but shaped and reshaped by storytellers over time. You might think legends are just like fairy tales, but they are different. Fairy tales are fictional stories meant to entertain or teach a simple lesson. Legends connect to a people's history, beliefs, and hopes. The legend of Arthur has been told in Britain and France for over 800 years, and writers keep retelling it because its ideas still feel alive.
stimulus.content_text: The Round Table: King Arthur and the Dream of a Perfect KingdomA Table with No Head Picture a massive table, perfectly round, with seats for over a hundred knights. No seat sits at the head, because there is no head. According to legend, King Arthur built this table on purpose. He wanted his knights to understand one idea right away: courage and character matter more than rank or wealth. But here is something you should know before we go further. Historians are not sure a real King Arthur ever existed. His story is a legend — a tale passed down through centuries, rooted in a culture's values and possibly based on a real person, but shaped and reshaped by storytellers over time. You might think legends are just like fairy tales, but they are different. Fairy tales are fictional stories meant to entertain or teach a simple lesson. Legends connect to a people's history, beliefs, and hopes. The legend of Arthur has been told in Britain and France for over 800 years, and writers keep retelling it because its ideas still feel alive.

prompt._: According to the text, how is a fairy tale different from a legend?

choices.text: It is meant to entertain or teach a simple lesson.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says fairy tales are 'fictional stories meant to entertain or teach a simple lesson,' which is exactly what answer A says.

choices.text: It is told by writers for over eight hundred years.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — being told for over 800 years describes the legend of King Arthur, not fairy tales.

choices.text: It is a true story about a real historical king.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says historians aren't sure King Arthur was even real, and fairy tales are fictional, not true historical stories.

choices.text: It connects to a people's history and beliefs.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says legends (not fairy tales) are the ones that connect to a people's history and beliefs.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Teenage King in a Divided Land

The story begins after young Arthur pulls a magical sword from a stone, proving he is the rightful king of England. But pulling out a sword is one thing. England was full of quarreling lords who refused to follow a teenager. Arthur had to earn their respect, stop their fighting, and build something brand new: a court where justice and honor came first.

In the legend, Camelot was a gleaming castle on green hills, its towers catching the sunlight. Inside those walls, Arthur gathered the bravest knights in the land and seated them at his famous Round Table.
stimulus.content_text: A Teenage King in a Divided Land The story begins after young Arthur pulls a magical sword from a stone, proving he is the rightful king of England. But pulling out a sword is one thing. England was full of quarreling lords who refused to follow a teenager. Arthur had to earn their respect, stop their fighting, and build something brand new: a court where justice and honor came first. In the legend, Camelot was a gleaming castle on green hills, its towers catching the sunlight. Inside those walls, Arthur gathered the bravest knights in the land and seated them at his famous Round Table.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Arthur create a brand new court?

choices.text: To give the quarreling lords a safe place to fight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur's goal was to stop the lords from quarreling, not to give them a place to continue fighting.

choices.text: To show off his gleaming new castle on the hills.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the description of the gleaming castle just helps us picture Camelot — the passage never says Arthur built the court to show it off.

choices.text: To keep the magical sword safe from the angry lords.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about keeping the sword safe from the lords.

choices.text: To bring fairness and honor to a divided country.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Arthur wanted to build a court where justice and honor came first, which means fairness for a divided land.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Code of Chivalry

The Knights of the Round Table followed a code of chivalry — a set of rules for how knights should behave, like a school's honor code but for warriors. A knight who followed chivalry never attacked someone who could not fight back. He kept his promises, told the truth, and defended the helpless. He treated women with respect and showed mercy to defeated enemies. Chivalry was not just about fighting well. It was about living well.

Now, you might think real knights in the Middle Ages actually followed all these rules. Most did not. Real medieval knights could be greedy, violent, and cruel. The chivalry described in legends was an ideal — a picture of the best possible behavior, the way a dream shows you a perfect version of something you hope for. Storytellers made knights sound nobler than they actually were because they wanted to inspire people to aim higher.
stimulus.content_text: The Code of Chivalry The Knights of the Round Table followed a code of chivalry — a set of rules for how knights should behave, like a school's honor code but for warriors. A knight who followed chivalry never attacked someone who could not fight back. He kept his promises, told the truth, and defended the helpless. He treated women with respect and showed mercy to defeated enemies. Chivalry was not just about fighting well. It was about living well. Now, you might think real knights in the Middle Ages actually followed all these rules. Most did not. Real medieval knights could be greedy, violent, and cruel. The chivalry described in legends was an ideal — a picture of the best possible behavior, the way a dream shows you a perfect version of something you hope for. Storytellers made knights sound nobler than they actually were because they wanted to inspire people to aim higher.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did storytellers do after real knights failed to follow the rules of chivalry?

choices.text: They stopped writing stories about knights altogether.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because storytellers did the opposite — they kept writing stories about knights, they just made them sound more heroic than real knights actually were.

choices.text: They punished knights who broke the rules of chivalry.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says storytellers had any power to punish knights or that they tried to do so.

choices.text: They made knights sound nobler to inspire people.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says storytellers 'made knights sound nobler than they actually were because they wanted to inspire people to aim higher.'

choices.text: They created new rules for knights to follow in battle.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not mention storytellers creating new rules for battle — their goal was to inspire people, not to change the rules.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Famous Knights and a Dangerous Secret

Many famous knights earned seats at the Round Table. Sir Gawain was known for his courtesy and courage. Sir Percival was pure-hearted and devoted. Sir Galahad was said to be the most virtuous knight — the one whose goodness ran deepest, like a well that never runs dry. Galahad would later lead the quest for the Holy Grail, a sacred cup that many knights searched for but few could find. In the legend, only the worthiest knight could reach it.

But the most celebrated knight was Sir Lancelot. He was the strongest fighter, the most skilled with a sword, and the most daring in battle. No enemy could defeat him in single combat — a one-on-one fight between two warriors. Yet Lancelot carried a secret that would eventually tear Camelot apart.

Lancelot loved Queen Guinevere, Arthur's wife. Their love was not wicked, but it was a betrayal of the king who trusted them both. When the truth finally came to light, it split the knights into rival groups. War broke out inside the very court that was supposed to stand for peace.
stimulus.content_text: Famous Knights and a Dangerous Secret Many famous knights earned seats at the Round Table. Sir Gawain was known for his courtesy and courage. Sir Percival was pure-hearted and devoted. Sir Galahad was said to be the most virtuous knight — the one whose goodness ran deepest, like a well that never runs dry. Galahad would later lead the quest for the Holy Grail, a sacred cup that many knights searched for but few could find. In the legend, only the worthiest knight could reach it. But the most celebrated knight was Sir Lancelot. He was the strongest fighter, the most skilled with a sword, and the most daring in battle. No enemy could defeat him in single combat — a one-on-one fight between two warriors. Yet Lancelot carried a secret that would eventually tear Camelot apart. Lancelot loved Queen Guinevere, Arthur's wife. Their love was not wicked, but it was a betrayal of the king who trusted them both. When the truth finally came to light, it split the knights into rival groups. War broke out inside the very court that was supposed to stand for peace.

prompt._: The passage says Galahad's goodness was 'like a well that never runs dry.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word 'virtuous'?

choices.text: That his goodness was common among all knights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Galahad was the MOST virtuous knight, which means his goodness was special and greater than the other knights, not common to all of them.

choices.text: That his goodness came from drinking sacred water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the well is just a comparison to help describe his goodness — it doesn't mean he actually drank any special water.

choices.text: That his goodness was deep and never ran out
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the 'well that never runs dry' comparison shows that Galahad's goodness was both very deep and endless, which is exactly what 'virtuous' means here.

choices.text: That his goodness was hidden and hard to find
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a well that never runs dry means goodness that is always available and flowing, not hidden or hard to find.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why the Story Still Matters

This is what makes the legend of King Arthur so different from a simple adventure story. Arthur tried to build a world where honor ruled. Camelot shone as an example of what people could achieve when they held themselves to a high standard. But human flaws — love, jealousy, misplaced trust — cracked the dream apart. Arthur's own goodness made it hard for him to see the danger until it was too late.

People have been retelling this story for over eight centuries, in poems, books, and films. They keep coming back to it not because it has a happy ending, but because it asks a question you will face your whole life: Is it still worth trying to do the right thing, even when you know you might fail? Every time someone sits down at a table and treats the person across from them as an equal, a small piece of Camelot comes back to life.
stimulus.content_text: Why the Story Still Matters This is what makes the legend of King Arthur so different from a simple adventure story. Arthur tried to build a world where honor ruled. Camelot shone as an example of what people could achieve when they held themselves to a high standard. But human flaws — love, jealousy, misplaced trust — cracked the dream apart. Arthur's own goodness made it hard for him to see the danger until it was too late. People have been retelling this story for over eight centuries, in poems, books, and films. They keep coming back to it not because it has a happy ending, but because it asks a question you will face your whole life: Is it still worth trying to do the right thing, even when you know you might fail? Every time someone sits down at a table and treats the person across from them as an equal, a small piece of Camelot comes back to life.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the author mean by 'human flaws' in the story of King Arthur?

choices.text: love, jealousy, and misplaced trust
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'human flaws — love, jealousy, misplaced trust — cracked the dream apart,' giving us the exact definition.

choices.text: poems, books, and films about Arthur
choices.feedback: Poems, books, and films are how people have retold the Arthur story over the centuries, not the human flaws the author is describing.

choices.text: honor, courage, and high standards
choices.feedback: Honor, courage, and high standards are the positive goals Arthur was trying to achieve, which is the opposite of flaws.

choices.text: battles, quests, and magical powers
choices.feedback: Battles, quests, and magical powers are parts of adventure stories, but the passage never calls these 'human flaws.'

prompt._: Based on the passage 'The Round Table: King Arthur and the Dream of a Perfect Kingdom,' how does King Arthur's original vision for his court contrast with what happens to it in the end?

choices.text: He built a court for real warriors, but they proved to be greedy and cruel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says real medieval knights were greedy and cruel, but that describes knights in history — not what happened inside Arthur's court.

choices.text: He built a court for justice, but it was destroyed by secrets and war.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Arthur's goal was justice and honor, but secrets and war between his own knights destroyed that dream in the end.

choices.text: He built a court of equal members, but he eventually claimed the highest seat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur never claimed a highest seat; the whole purpose of the Round Table was that every seat was equal with no head.

choices.text: He built a court to find the Grail, but only one knight was pure enough.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the quest for the Holy Grail was just one part of the story, not the reason Arthur built his court or what caused its downfall.

prompt._: What most likely caused storytellers to make the Knights of the Round Table seem more noble than real knights?

choices.text: A wish to make battles sound more exciting than real life
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions storytellers wanting to make battles sound more exciting — their goal was to inspire better behavior, not more thrilling fights.

choices.text: A wish to prove that every knight in history followed chivalry
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually tells us the opposite — it says most real medieval knights did not follow chivalry at all.

choices.text: A wish to explain why Arthur was the youngest king in England
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never describes Arthur as the youngest king in England, and his age has nothing to do with why storytellers described chivalry the way they did.

choices.text: A wish to show people an ideal of brave and honorable behavior
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says storytellers made knights sound nobler 'because they wanted to inspire people to aim higher,' which means they were showing an ideal of brave and honorable behavior.

prompt._: Which sequence best describes how the article organizes the main events and ideas in the legend of King Arthur?

choices.text: Arthur creates the Round Table, knights follow chivalry perfectly, and Camelot lasts forever.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us knights did NOT follow chivalry perfectly and Camelot did NOT last forever — it was destroyed by betrayal and war.

choices.text: Arthur becomes king, builds a court based on honor, and then human flaws destroy it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's order: Arthur becomes king, builds an honorable court, and then human flaws like Lancelot's betrayal bring Camelot down.

choices.text: Storytellers invent the legend, Arthur gathers knights, and the code of chivalry begins.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article mentions storytellers as background information to help us understand legends, not as a main event in the sequence of Arthur's story.

choices.text: Lancelot betrays Arthur, Arthur pulls the sword from the stone, and knights go on quests.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the events in the wrong order — Arthur pulls the sword from the stone at the beginning of the story, long before Lancelot's betrayal happens.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the beginning and ending of the legend to show the article's main idea?

choices.text: Camelot begins as a place of secrets and ends as a place where Arthur trusts no one.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Camelot starts as a place built on trust and honor, and Arthur's flaw was trusting too much, not becoming someone who trusted no one.

choices.text: Camelot begins as a place of war and ends as a place where everyone follows chivalry.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Camelot actually begins after fighting among lords, and the story never shows a happy ending where everyone follows chivalry.

choices.text: Camelot begins as a place of magic and ends as a place ruled by stronger knights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Camelot doesn't end with stronger knights ruling — it falls apart because of betrayal between people who were supposed to trust each other.

choices.text: Camelot begins as a place of equal honor and ends as a place broken by human weakness.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows Camelot starting with the equal Round Table and ending when human flaws like love and betrayal tore it apart, which matches the article's main idea.

syllabus_metadata.title: Knights of the Round Table

assessments.title: The Quest for the Holy Grail

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Quest for the Holy Grail

A Light in the Great Hall

A golden cup appeared out of nowhere, floating above the dinner table, glowing so brightly that every knight in the hall shielded his eyes. Then it vanished. The Knights of the Round Table sat in their chairs at Camelot, unable to speak. They had just seen the Holy Grail — and every single one of them wanted to find it.

This is one of the most famous stories from the legends of King Arthur. A legend is a traditional story about human heroes that may be rooted in real history. Legends are different from myths, which are stories about gods and how the world was created. You might think legends are completely made up, but many scholars believe a real British war leader from the 400s or 500s CE may have inspired King Arthur. The legend grew from something real.
stimulus.content_text: The Quest for the Holy GrailA Light in the Great Hall A golden cup appeared out of nowhere, floating above the dinner table, glowing so brightly that every knight in the hall shielded his eyes. Then it vanished. The Knights of the Round Table sat in their chairs at Camelot, unable to speak. They had just seen the Holy Grail — and every single one of them wanted to find it. This is one of the most famous stories from the legends of King Arthur. A legend is a traditional story about human heroes that may be rooted in real history. Legends are different from myths, which are stories about gods and how the world was created. You might think legends are completely made up, but many scholars believe a real British war leader from the 400s or 500s CE may have inspired King Arthur. The legend grew from something real.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the difference between legends and myths?

choices.text: Legends are stories from Camelot, while myths are stories from Britain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says myths are from Britain or that legends only come from Camelot.

choices.text: Legends are stories about human heroes, while myths are stories about gods.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches exactly what the passage says: legends are about human heroes and may be rooted in history, while myths are about gods and creation.

choices.text: Legends are stories about gods, while myths are stories about heroes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the definitions switched — the passage says legends are about human heroes and myths are about gods, not the other way around.

choices.text: Legends are stories that are always true, while myths are never true.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says legends are always true or myths are never true — it only says legends 'may be rooted in real history.'

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Cup That Could Heal the World

According to Christian tradition, the Holy Grail was the cup Jesus used at the Last Supper — the final meal he shared with his disciples, his closest followers and students, before his death. Over the centuries, storytellers gave the Grail miraculous powers. They said it could heal the sick, produce endless food, and even grant eternal life. But only someone with a pure heart could ever reach it.

Here's something that surprises many readers: there is no single "correct" version of this story. A French poet named Chrétien de Troyes wrote one of the earliest versions around 1190. Later, other authors changed the details, added new characters, and gave the story different endings. That's how legends work. They shift and grow as different people retell them across the centuries.
stimulus.content_text: The Cup That Could Heal the World According to Christian tradition, the Holy Grail was the cup Jesus used at the Last Supper — the final meal he shared with his disciples, his closest followers and students, before his death. Over the centuries, storytellers gave the Grail miraculous powers. They said it could heal the sick, produce endless food, and even grant eternal life. But only someone with a pure heart could ever reach it. Here's something that surprises many readers: there is no single "correct" version of this story. A French poet named Chrétien de Troyes wrote one of the earliest versions around 1190. Later, other authors changed the details, added new characters, and gave the story different endings. That's how legends work. They shift and grow as different people retell them across the centuries.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is there no single "correct" version of the Holy Grail story?

choices.text: Because the original story was lost and no one could remember it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the original story being lost or forgotten.

choices.text: Because different people retold and changed the story over the centuries
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that other authors changed details and added new characters over the centuries, which is why no single version is 'correct.'

choices.text: Because the story was only told in one country and one language
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the story being retold by many different people over many centuries, not limited to one country or language.

choices.text: Because Chrétien de Troyes did not want others to copy his version
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage gives no information about Chrétien de Troyes trying to protect or control his version of the story.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Round Table Breaks Apart

King Arthur's Round Table was not just a piece of furniture. It was a symbol. Because the table had no head and no foot, every knight who sat at it was equal — like players sitting in a circle where no one gets the best seat. Arthur built the Round Table to keep his knights united. The Grail quest shattered that unity.

One by one, the knights rode out of Camelot. They pushed through dark forests, crossed flooding rivers, and faced terrifying tests. Some reached enchanted castles where mysterious voices asked them riddles. Others fought monstrous creatures and rival warriors. Many never came home. The quest scattered Arthur's greatest fighters across the earth.
stimulus.content_text: The Round Table Breaks Apart King Arthur's Round Table was not just a piece of furniture. It was a symbol. Because the table had no head and no foot, every knight who sat at it was equal — like players sitting in a circle where no one gets the best seat. Arthur built the Round Table to keep his knights united. The Grail quest shattered that unity. One by one, the knights rode out of Camelot. They pushed through dark forests, crossed flooding rivers, and faced terrifying tests. Some reached enchanted castles where mysterious voices asked them riddles. Others fought monstrous creatures and rival warriors. Many never came home. The quest scattered Arthur's greatest fighters across the earth.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the knights do after they rode out of Camelot?

choices.text: They sat together in a large circle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the quest scattered the knights far apart from each other, not together in a circle.

choices.text: They traveled back to their own homes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says many knights never came home, so they did not travel back to their own homes.

choices.text: They pushed through dark forests.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the knights 'pushed through dark forests' after leaving Camelot.

choices.text: They built a brand new round table.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the knights building a new round table — that never happened in the story.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Strongest Knight Falls Short

Sir Lancelot was the mightiest fighter at the Round Table. He traveled farther and battled harder than almost anyone on the quest. But Lancelot carried a secret that weighed on his conscience — that inner voice that tells you whether your actions are right or wrong, like an alarm that rings when you know you've done something you shouldn't have. Lancelot was secretly in love with Queen Guinevere, Arthur's wife. No matter how bravely he fought, his divided heart kept the Grail out of reach.

He came close once. He actually saw the Grail glowing before him. But a powerful force struck him down into a deep sleep. When he woke, the cup was gone. Lancelot rode back to Camelot heartbroken. All his strength and skill meant nothing because his heart was split in two.
stimulus.content_text: The Strongest Knight Falls Short Sir Lancelot was the mightiest fighter at the Round Table. He traveled farther and battled harder than almost anyone on the quest. But Lancelot carried a secret that weighed on his conscience — that inner voice that tells you whether your actions are right or wrong, like an alarm that rings when you know you've done something you shouldn't have. Lancelot was secretly in love with Queen Guinevere, Arthur's wife. No matter how bravely he fought, his divided heart kept the Grail out of reach. He came close once. He actually saw the Grail glowing before him. But a powerful force struck him down into a deep sleep. When he woke, the cup was gone. Lancelot rode back to Camelot heartbroken. All his strength and skill meant nothing because his heart was split in two.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares a conscience to 'an alarm that rings' — what does this comparison help the reader understand about the word conscience?

choices.text: an inner warning that tells you when something is wrong
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a conscience is an inner voice that tells you if your actions are right or wrong, just like an alarm warning you when something is wrong.

choices.text: a powerful force that puts you into a deep sleep
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the powerful force that put Lancelot to sleep is a different part of the story and has nothing to do with the meaning of conscience.

choices.text: a feeling of courage that pushes you to fight harder
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a conscience is not about feeling brave or fighting harder — it's a warning voice about right and wrong.

choices.text: a secret thought that you hide from other people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a conscience is not about hiding secrets from others — it's an inner warning you feel inside yourself.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Purest Knight Succeeds

Sir Galahad, Lancelot's own son, was the knight who finally reached the Holy Grail — at least in most versions of the legend. Galahad had none of his father's inner conflict. He had no secret sins, no divided loyalties — no promises pulling him in opposite directions, the way a rope pulled from both ends will eventually snap. He was gentle, honest, and completely devoted to doing right.

When Galahad reached the Grail at last, he looked inside the cup and saw a vision of divine glory — something sacred and heavenly. The vision was so overwhelming that Galahad's soul rose up to heaven. He had achieved what no other knight could.
stimulus.content_text: The Purest Knight Succeeds Sir Galahad, Lancelot's own son, was the knight who finally reached the Holy Grail — at least in most versions of the legend. Galahad had none of his father's inner conflict. He had no secret sins, no divided loyalties — no promises pulling him in opposite directions, the way a rope pulled from both ends will eventually snap. He was gentle, honest, and completely devoted to doing right. When Galahad reached the Grail at last, he looked inside the cup and saw a vision of divine glory — something sacred and heavenly. The vision was so overwhelming that Galahad's soul rose up to heaven. He had achieved what no other knight could.

prompt._: Read this sentence from the text: "When Galahad reached the Grail at last, he looked inside the cup and saw a vision of divine glory — something sacred and heavenly." Based on this sentence, what is the definition of the word "divine"?

choices.text: Something pulling opposite ways
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'pulling opposite ways' describes divided loyalties, not the word 'divine.'

choices.text: Something sacred and heavenly
choices.feedback: This is correct because the sentence uses a dash to define 'divine' right away — 'something sacred and heavenly' is the author's own explanation of the word.

choices.text: Something rising up to the sky
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because rising up to the sky describes what happened to Galahad's soul after he saw the vision, not what 'divine' means.

choices.text: Something snapping like a rope
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because snapping like a rope is part of the metaphor about Lancelot's conflicts, not a definition of 'divine.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What the Grail Still Means

The quest destroyed Camelot. So many knights died or disappeared that the Round Table never recovered. Arthur's dream of a united kingdom began to crack apart.

But the story refused to die. Painters, authors, and filmmakers have retold it for over eight hundred years. The Grail legend carries a sharp idea: the strongest person in the room can still fail if their heart is at war with itself, and a quiet, honest person can succeed where warriors cannot. People still use the phrase "holy grail" today — to describe any goal worth a lifetime of effort. Every time you hear someone say it, you're hearing an echo of those knights riding out from Camelot, chasing a golden light they saw for only a moment.

Every time you hear someone say it, you're hearing an echo of those knights riding out from Camelot, chasing a golden light they saw for only a moment.

prompt._: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that the Holy Grail story is still important today?

choices.text: Arthur's dream of a united kingdom began to crack.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arthur's kingdom cracking apart is an event from the old legend, not proof that the story still has meaning in the world today.

choices.text: Knights died or disappeared during the long quest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because knights dying during the quest is something that happened in the old story, not evidence that the story is still important today.

choices.text: The strongest person in the room can sometimes fail.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it shares a lesson or idea from the story, but it doesn't show us that people are still using or connecting with the story today.

choices.text: People use the phrase to describe a lifelong goal.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says people still use the phrase 'holy grail' today to mean a goal worth a lifetime of effort, which shows the story is still alive in our everyday language.

prompt._: Which statement best shows the contrast between Sir Lancelot and Sir Galahad in the quest for the Holy Grail?

choices.text: Lancelot wanted to heal the sick but could not, while Galahad wanted endless food but received it.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because neither Lancelot nor Galahad had goals of healing the sick or finding endless food — those were powers the Grail was said to have, not what the knights were personally seeking.

choices.text: Lancelot searched for many years but gave up, while Galahad searched for one day but kept going.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Lancelot gave up or that Galahad only searched for one day — those details are not in the story.

choices.text: Lancelot fought with great strength but failed, while Galahad lived with a pure heart but succeeded.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says Lancelot's strength wasn't enough due to his divided heart, while Galahad's pure and honest heart led him to succeed.

choices.text: Lancelot sat at the Round Table but stayed in Camelot, while Galahad left the castle but never saw the Grail.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Lancelot did leave Camelot and travel far on the quest, and Galahad did see and reach the Grail.

prompt._: Based on the article, why did the Grail quest cause the end of King Arthur's united kingdom?

choices.text: because the quest scattered and killed too many knights
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly states that many knights never came home and the quest scattered Arthur's greatest fighters, leaving the Round Table too weak to survive.

choices.text: because the Grail made Arthur's knights fight each other
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the knights didn't turn on each other — they each rode off separately into the world on their own quests.

choices.text: because Arthur sent his knights away as a punishment
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Arthur never sent his knights away as a punishment — they chose on their own to go search for the Grail.

choices.text: because finding the Grail proved Arthur was a weak king
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says finding the Grail made Arthur look weak; the problem was simply that too many of his knights were lost.

prompt._: Which choice shows the order of important events in the article?

choices.text: Galahad reaches the Grail, the knights leave Camelot, and Camelot is united again.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Camelot is never united again — the article clearly says the quest destroyed Camelot and the Round Table never recovered.

choices.text: The knights begin the quest, the Grail appears in the hall, and Galahad reaches it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the order wrong — the knights can't start the quest before the Grail appears, since seeing the Grail is what inspires them to go looking for it.

choices.text: The Grail appears in the hall, the knights leave Camelot, and Galahad reaches it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the article: the Grail appears in the hall first, then the knights leave Camelot on the quest, and finally Galahad reaches the Grail.

choices.text: The knights leave Camelot, Galahad reaches the Grail, and the Grail appears in the hall.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the knights leaving Camelot before the Grail appears, but the article shows the Grail appearing first is what causes the knights to leave.

prompt._: How does the comparison between Sir Lancelot and Sir Galahad help explain the main idea of the article?

choices.text: By showing that older warriors know more than younger knights.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says older warriors know more; in fact, the younger knight Galahad succeeds where the experienced Lancelot fails.

choices.text: By showing that finding treasures matters more than ruling kingdoms.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never suggests finding treasures is more important than ruling kingdoms — the Grail quest is about inner purity, not treasure hunting.

choices.text: By showing that physical strength matters less than a pure heart.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly shows that Lancelot's great strength wasn't enough — only Galahad's pure, honest heart could reach the Grail.

choices.text: By showing that fighting monsters takes more skill than answering riddles.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never compares fighting monsters to answering riddles or says one takes more skill than the other.

assessments.title: Anansi the Spider: African Folktales

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Anansi the Spider: The Tiny Trickster Whose Stories Crossed Oceans

A Spider Outsmarted Them All

A tiny spider once stood before the most powerful god in the sky and made a deal. He would capture a python, a leopard, and a swarm of hornets — or he would own nothing. Everyone laughed. But the spider won.

His name is Anansi, and he comes from the folklore — the collection of traditional stories, songs, and beliefs passed down through generations — of the Ashanti people of Ghana, in western Africa. Folklore is like a community's shared storybook that no one wrote down at first. People told these tales out loud, parent to child, for hundreds of years. The Ashanti's favorite character was a spider.
stimulus.content_text: Anansi the Spider: The Tiny Trickster Whose Stories Crossed OceansA Spider Outsmarted Them All A tiny spider once stood before the most powerful god in the sky and made a deal. He would capture a python, a leopard, and a swarm of hornets — or he would own nothing. Everyone laughed. But the spider won. His name is Anansi, and he comes from the folklore — the collection of traditional stories, songs, and beliefs passed down through generations — of the Ashanti people of Ghana, in western Africa. Folklore is like a community's shared storybook that no one wrote down at first. People told these tales out loud, parent to child, for hundreds of years. The Ashanti's favorite character was a spider.

prompt._: According to the text, how were Anansi stories first shared, unlike a written storybook?

choices.text: They were painted on walls by Ashanti artists
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about painting stories on walls.

choices.text: They were written down by the god of the sky
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says no one wrote the stories down at first, and the god of the sky is never described as writing anything.

choices.text: They were told out loud from parent to child
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that people 'told these tales out loud, parent to child, for hundreds of years' instead of writing them down.

choices.text: They were sung only during special celebrations
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes stories being shared from parent to child in everyday life, not only during special celebrations.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Trickster Who Used Brains, Not Muscles

Anansi is a trickster — a character in folklore who survives by outsmarting others rather than overpowering them. In some stories, Anansi looks like an ordinary spider. In others, he walks and talks like a man but keeps his spiderlike habits, spinning webs of clever schemes. No matter what shape he takes, Anansi is always hungry, always scheming, and always looking for a way to come out on top.

But tricksters are more complicated than that. Anansi shows that a small, weak creature can defeat powerful ones through quick thinking. At the same time, his tricks sometimes backfire. When Anansi acts greedy or selfish, the story punishes him. He ends up embarrassed or empty-handed. Listeners cheer for his cleverness, but they also learn where cleverness without kindness leads.

The most famous Anansi story shows exactly how this works. Anansi wanted to own every story in the world. He went to Nyame, the sky god, and asked for them. Nyame agreed — but set a price that seemed impossible. Anansi had to capture three dangerous creatures: a python, a leopard, and a nest of hornets. Nobody believed a spider could do it. But Anansi used his wits. He tricked the python into stretching along a long branch to measure himself, then tied him to it. He dug a pit, covered it with leaves, and waited for the leopard to fall in. He sprinkled water on the hornets so they thought it was raining, then offered them a dry gourd to shelter in — and sealed it shut. Anansi delivered all three creatures to Nyame and won ownership of every story ever told. That is why, according to the Ashanti people, all stories are called "spider stories."

prompt._: Based on the section, why do Anansi stories often end with him being punished when he uses tricks the wrong way?

choices.text: to show that small animals should avoid big challenges
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Anansi takes on very big challenges like capturing a python, a leopard, and hornets — and he succeeds!

choices.text: to show that strength matters more than smart thinking
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — Anansi proves that a small, weak creature can win through quick thinking, not muscles.

choices.text: to show that Nyame wants to keep stories for himself
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Nyame actually agrees to give Anansi the stories once he completes the tasks, so Nyame isn't trying to keep them.

choices.text: to show that cleverness without kindness leads to trouble
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says listeners learn 'where cleverness without kindness leads,' meaning tricks used selfishly lead to trouble.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Lessons Hidden Inside Adventures

Each Anansi tale ends with a moral — a lesson about right and wrong woven into the story, like a secret message hidden inside a gift. Sometimes the moral teaches you to use your brain instead of your fists. Other times it warns against greed, laziness, or dishonesty. You may have heard of Aesop's fables, the ancient Greek stories where animals like tortoises and hares teach lessons about life. Anansi stories work the same way. The adventure pulls you in, but the lesson stays with you after the story ends.

Actually, for the Ashanti, Anansi stories taught children how to behave, explained why the world works the way it does, and held an entire culture's wisdom inside a spider's adventures.
stimulus.content_text: Lessons Hidden Inside Adventures Each Anansi tale ends with a moral — a lesson about right and wrong woven into the story, like a secret message hidden inside a gift. Sometimes the moral teaches you to use your brain instead of your fists. Other times it warns against greed, laziness, or dishonesty. You may have heard of Aesop's fables, the ancient Greek stories where animals like tortoises and hares teach lessons about life. Anansi stories work the same way. The adventure pulls you in, but the lesson stays with you after the story ends. Actually, for the Ashanti, Anansi stories taught children how to behave, explained why the world works the way it does, and held an entire culture's wisdom inside a spider's adventures.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the order in which Anansi stories affected Ashanti children?

choices.text: Children heard adventures, then carried lessons with them
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the adventure 'pulls you in' first, and then 'the lesson stays with you after the story ends,' which matches this order perfectly.

choices.text: Children memorized morals, then listened to tales
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says children memorized morals before listening to the tales — the exciting adventure always comes first.

choices.text: Children learned behavior, then heard the stories
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows children heard the stories first, and the lessons about behavior came from those stories — not before them.

choices.text: Children told their own stories, then learned wisdom
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Ashanti children telling their own stories as a step in how they learned wisdom.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Stories That Survived the Unthinkable

Anansi did not stay in Ghana. His stories crossed the Atlantic Ocean under the worst conditions imaginable. When enslaved Africans were torn from their homes and forced onto ships bound for the Caribbean islands and the American South, they could carry almost nothing. But they carried their stories.

In their new and terrible circumstances, Anansi became a symbol of hope and resistance — the act of fighting back against unfair power, the way a tree bends in a storm but refuses to break. If a tiny spider could defeat a leopard through cleverness, then perhaps an enslaved person could find ways to survive and even triumph, meaning to win against great odds.

As the stories traveled, they changed. In Jamaica, Anansi became "Anancy." In the American South, his tales blended with other traditions and helped inspire the character of Br'er Rabbit — another small, clever animal who outsmarts bigger creatures. Across the Caribbean today, children still grow up hearing Anansi tales. Storytellers still begin with the traditional opening: "Once upon a time, there was and there was not..."

Folklore does not sit still. It moves with the people who tell it, changing shape like Anansi himself — sometimes a spider, sometimes a man, but always carrying the same sharp wit and hard-won wisdom inside.

prompt._: The author compares resistance to 'the way a tree bends in a storm but refuses to break.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word resistance?

choices.text: It means running away from something dangerous
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither the tree nor the idea of resistance is about running away — both are about facing something difficult and holding on.

choices.text: It means growing stronger after every problem
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison only shows surviving without breaking, not becoming stronger after every problem.

choices.text: It means fighting back without being destroyed
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines resistance as fighting back against unfair power, and the tree bending but not breaking shows you can face something hard without being destroyed.

choices.text: It means staying perfectly still no matter what happens
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the tree in the comparison actually bends and moves, which is the opposite of staying perfectly still.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Web That Reaches You

Stories are survival tools disguised as entertainment. Anansi knew that before anyone else — which is exactly why he wanted to own every single one.
stimulus.content_text: A Web That Reaches You Stories are survival tools disguised as entertainment. Anansi knew that before anyone else — which is exactly why he wanted to own every single one.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by calling stories 'survival tools'?

choices.text: things that belong only to Anansi
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Anansi wanted to own stories, not that stories already belong only to him.

choices.text: things that help people live and learn
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage calls stories 'survival tools,' meaning they help people live and learn, even though they look like entertainment on the outside.

choices.text: things that are meant just for fun
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says stories are 'disguised as' entertainment, meaning fun is just their cover — their real job is to help people survive and learn.

choices.text: things that are boring and serious
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says stories are disguised as entertainment, which means they are enjoyable and fun to experience.

prompt._: According to the article, how did the purpose of Anansi stories change when they crossed the ocean?

choices.text: In Africa they were written down in large storybooks, while in the Americas they were only shared out loud.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the opposite — in Africa, stories were told out loud and passed down by speaking, not written in storybooks.

choices.text: In Africa they featured a small and clever spider, while in the Americas they featured a large and strong leopard.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anansi remained a small, clever spider in both Africa and the Americas — the leopard was never the hero of the stories.

choices.text: In Africa they taught young children to be very greedy, while in the Americas they taught them to be kind.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the African stories actually warned against greed and punished Anansi when he acted greedy, so they never taught children to be greedy.

choices.text: In Africa they explained how the world works, while in the Americas they became a symbol of fighting back.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that in Africa the stories explained the world and taught behavior, while in the Americas they became a symbol of hope and resistance against unfair power.

prompt._: Why did Anansi become an important character in stories that were told for many generations?

choices.text: Because he taught lessons by using clever plans
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage explains that Anansi uses clever tricks and his stories teach important lessons, which is why people kept sharing them for generations.

choices.text: Because he lived only in stories from Jamaica
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Anansi's stories began in Ghana and traveled to many places, including Jamaica, the American South, and across the Caribbean.

choices.text: Because he won every story from Nyame by luck
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Anansi won the stories through careful, smart plans — like tricking the python with a branch and trapping the hornets in a gourd — not by luck.

choices.text: Because he was the strongest animal in the stories
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Anansi is described as small and weak — he wins by being clever, not by being the strongest.

prompt._: According to the article, which sequence shows how Anansi stories spread and changed over time?

choices.text: They started in western Africa, crossed the ocean, and blended into new traditions.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the stories started with the Ashanti in Ghana, crossed the Atlantic Ocean, and then blended into new traditions like Br'er Rabbit in the American South.

choices.text: They were written in large storybooks, given to the sky god, and taught to the Greeks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anansi stories were passed down by speaking out loud, not written in storybooks, and the article never connects them to the Greeks or the sky god in this way.

choices.text: They blended into new traditions, crossed the ocean, and started in western Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the steps in the wrong order — the stories started in Africa first, before they crossed the ocean or blended into new traditions.

choices.text: They started in the Caribbean, crossed the ocean, and moved into western Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the stories did not start in the Caribbean — they began in western Africa with the Ashanti people.

prompt._: The author compares Anansi to the animals in Aesop's fables and to Br'er Rabbit. How do these comparisons support the main idea of the article?

choices.text: By explaining that Anansi is a greedy spider who travels across the ocean to collect new stories.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anansi didn't travel across the ocean — it was enslaved Africans who carried his stories with them, and the comparisons are about teaching lessons, not collecting stories.

choices.text: By showing that Anansi is a small animal who uses his brain to teach lessons and win.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the comparisons to Aesop's fables and Br'er Rabbit both highlight that Anansi is a small, clever creature who uses his brain to outsmart others and teach lessons — which is exactly the main idea of the article.

choices.text: By proving that Anansi is a strong creature who uses his muscles to defeat his many enemies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states Anansi is a trickster who wins by outsmarting others, not by using physical strength or muscles.

choices.text: By describing how Anansi is a Greek character who changes his shape to hide from great danger.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anansi is from West Africa, not Greece, and the comparisons to Aesop's fables and Br'er Rabbit are about teaching lessons through clever animal characters, not about hiding from danger.

syllabus_metadata.title: Anansi the Spider: African Folktales

assessments.title: How Mountains Are Born

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
World Mountains: Built from Below, Torn Down from Above

A Peak Still Growing and the Planet's Cracked Shell

Right now, as you read this sentence, Mount Everest is rising. The tallest mountain on Earth pushes about half an inch higher every single year. That sounds tiny, but over millions of years, those half-inches stacked up to create a peak 29,032 feet above sea level — so high that jet planes fly at the same altitude. Mountains are not permanent fixtures that have always looked the way they do today. They are born, they grow, and they wear away. Every mountain on Earth has a story, and that story starts deep underground.

Earth's outer layer is called the crust — the thin, solid rock we live on. But the crust is not one smooth piece. It is broken into enormous slabs called tectonic plates, which fit together like the cracked shell of a hard-boiled egg. These plates rest on a layer of extremely hot, slow-flowing rock beneath them. Think of a cracker floating on top of thick honey. The plates drift constantly, but so slowly — just a few inches per year — that you will never feel them move. When these massive plates push, pull, or slide against each other, they reshape Earth's surface in dramatic ways.
stimulus.content_text: World Mountains: Built from Below, Torn Down from AboveA Peak Still Growing and the Planet's Cracked Shell Right now, as you read this sentence, Mount Everest is rising. The tallest mountain on Earth pushes about half an inch higher every single year. That sounds tiny, but over millions of years, those half-inches stacked up to create a peak 29,032 feet above sea level — so high that jet planes fly at the same altitude. Mountains are not permanent fixtures that have always looked the way they do today. They are born, they grow, and they wear away. Every mountain on Earth has a story, and that story starts deep underground. Earth's outer layer is called the crust — the thin, solid rock we live on. But the crust is not one smooth piece. It is broken into enormous slabs called tectonic plates, which fit together like the cracked shell of a hard-boiled egg. These plates rest on a layer of extremely hot, slow-flowing rock beneath them. Think of a cracker floating on top of thick honey. The plates drift constantly, but so slowly — just a few inches per year — that you will never feel them move. When these massive plates push, pull, or slide against each other, they reshape Earth's surface in dramatic ways.

prompt._: According to the text, how are tectonic plates different from one smooth piece of crust?

choices.text: They fit together without any cracks or gaps
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage compares the crust to a cracked eggshell, meaning it does have cracks and gaps between the plates.

choices.text: They stay perfectly still deep under the ground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the plates 'drift constantly,' meaning they are always moving, not staying still.

choices.text: They are made of extremely hot, slow-flowing rock
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because extremely hot, slow-flowing rock describes the layer underneath the tectonic plates, not the plates themselves.

choices.text: They are broken into enormous slabs of rock
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the crust 'is broken into enormous slabs called tectonic plates,' which is exactly what makes it different from one smooth piece.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Collision Mountains and Volcanic Mountains: Two Ways Peaks Are Built

When plates collide, the rock between them has nowhere to go but up. It crumples and folds like a rug bunching up when you push it against a wall. Over millions of years, that crumpled rock rises into towering peaks.

The Himalaya mountain range in Asia is the most dramatic example. About 50 million years ago, the Indian plate — once a separate landmass surrounded by ocean — drifted northward and slammed into the Eurasian plate. That slow-motion collision pushed Earth's crust upward, building the tallest mountains on the planet. The Indian plate is still pushing today, which is why Everest keeps growing taller.

You might think every mountain forms from plates crashing together, but some mountains are built in an entirely different way. Volcanic mountains form when magma — melted rock trapped deep underground — forces its way through cracks in the crust and erupts onto the surface as lava. Each eruption adds a new layer of hardened rock, like dripping candle wax that builds up into a thick mound. Over thousands of eruptions, a cone-shaped mountain appears.

Mount Kilimanjaro, the tallest mountain in Africa, formed exactly this way. It is actually a dormant volcano — meaning it is not erupting right now but still could wake up someday.

It crumples and folds like a rug bunching up when you push it against a wall. Over millions of years, that crumpled rock rises into towering peaks. The Himalaya mountain range in Asia is the most dramatic example. About 50 million years ago, the Indian plate — once a separate landmass surrounded by ocean — drifted northward and slammed into the Eurasian plate. That slow-motion collision pushed Earth's crust upward, building the tallest mountains on the planet. The Indian plate is still pushing today, which is why Everest keeps growing taller. You might think every mountain forms from plates crashing together, but some mountains are built in an entirely different way. Volcanic mountains form when magma — melted rock trapped deep underground — forces its way through cracks in the crust and erupts onto the surface as lava. Each eruption adds a new layer of hardened rock, like dripping candle wax that builds up into a thick mound. Over thousands of eruptions, a cone-shaped mountain appears. Mount Kilimanjaro, the tallest mountain in Africa, formed exactly this way. It is actually a dormant volcano — meaning it is not erupting right now but still could wake up someday.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that the movement of Earth's plates causes mountains to form?

choices.text: Magma forces its way through deep cracks and erupts onto the surface as lava.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes how volcanic mountains form from magma, not how plate movement builds mountains.

choices.text: The Indian plate was once a separate landmass surrounded by the ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only tells us the Indian plate was once surrounded by ocean — it doesn't explain how mountains formed.

choices.text: A slow-motion collision pushed the crust upward to build the tallest mountains.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it directly shows that a plate collision pushed the crust upward to build mountains, which is the main idea.

choices.text: Mount Kilimanjaro is a dormant volcano that is not erupting right now.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes Kilimanjaro's current volcanic status, which has nothing to do with plate movement causing mountains.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Fault-Block Mountains: Snapped and Tilted

A third type of mountain forms along faults, which are deep cracks in Earth's crust. Imagine breaking a chocolate bar into three pieces, then pushing the middle piece upward while the two outer pieces stay flat. That is how fault-block mountains work. Enormous blocks of rock shift upward along a crack, creating a steep cliff on one side and a gentler slope on the other. The Sierra Nevada range in California formed this way — one giant slab of rock tilted skyward along a fault line millions of years ago.
stimulus.content_text: Fault-Block Mountains: Snapped and Tilted A third type of mountain forms along faults, which are deep cracks in Earth's crust. Imagine breaking a chocolate bar into three pieces, then pushing the middle piece upward while the two outer pieces stay flat. That is how fault-block mountains work. Enormous blocks of rock shift upward along a crack, creating a steep cliff on one side and a gentler slope on the other. The Sierra Nevada range in California formed this way — one giant slab of rock tilted skyward along a fault line millions of years ago.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happens to blocks of rock along a fault before a steep cliff and gentle slope are created?

choices.text: The blocks of rock shift upward along a crack
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 'enormous blocks of rock shift upward along a crack,' which is what creates the steep cliff and gentle slope.

choices.text: The blocks of rock fold over each other slowly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because folding over is not mentioned in this passage — that might describe a different type of mountain, but not fault-block mountains.

choices.text: The blocks of rock sink downward into the ground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the rocks shift upward, not downward — sinking is the opposite of what actually happens.

choices.text: The blocks of rock melt and then harden again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the rocks melt or harden — that's not how fault-block mountains form.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Mountains That Shrink and Mountains You Cannot See

Here is something surprising: mountains do not just grow. They also shrink. Wind, rain, ice, and flowing rivers slowly wear mountains down in a process called erosion — the gradual scraping and dissolving of rock, the way a bar of soap gets smaller every time you use it. The Appalachian Mountains in the eastern United States were once as tall as the Himalayas. Hundreds of millions of years of erosion ground them down to the rounded, gentle peaks you see today. Every mountain is in a constant tug-of-war between the forces that push it up and the forces that wear it down.

It is easy to assume mountains only exist on land, but the longest mountain range on Earth is actually underwater. The Mid-Ocean Ridge stretches more than 40,000 miles along the ocean floor, where tectonic plates pull apart and magma rises to fill the gap, building underwater peaks.
