Wind, rain, ice, and flowing rivers slowly wear mountains down in a process called erosion — the gradual scraping and dissolving of rock, the way a bar of soap gets smaller every time you use it. The Appalachian Mountains in the eastern United States were once as tall as the Himalayas. Hundreds of millions of years of erosion ground them down to the rounded, gentle peaks you see today. Every mountain is in a constant tug-of-war between the forces that push it up and the forces that wear it down. It is easy to assume mountains only exist on land, but the longest mountain range on Earth is actually underwater. The Mid-Ocean Ridge stretches more than 40,000 miles along the ocean floor, where tectonic plates pull apart and magma rises to fill the gap, building underwater peaks.

prompt._: The author explains the word 'erosion' by comparing it to using a bar of soap. Based on this comparison, what do erosion and using soap both do?

choices.text: Both cause an object to slowly get smaller.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly compares erosion to a bar of soap getting smaller over time, showing that both slowly reduce the size of something.

choices.text: Both cause an object to quickly split apart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes erosion as gradual and slow, not quick, and neither process causes something to split apart.

choices.text: Both cause an object to quickly become clean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison is about getting smaller, not about cleaning — the passage never mentions anything becoming clean.

choices.text: Both cause an object to slowly grow taller.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both erosion and soap use make things smaller, which is the opposite of growing taller.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Scenery

Mountains do far more than look impressive. They block weather systems and force clouds to drop rain, which is why one side of a mountain range can be green while the other side is dry desert. Rivers born from mountain snow carry fresh water to billions of people. Unique plants and animals live at high elevations and nowhere else on Earth. So the next time you see a mountain, you are not just looking at a pile of rock. You are looking at a machine that shapes weather, feeds rivers, and shelters life, all while being slowly built from below and torn apart from above.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Scenery Mountains do far more than look impressive. They block weather systems and force clouds to drop rain, which is why one side of a mountain range can be green while the other side is dry desert. Rivers born from mountain snow carry fresh water to billions of people. Unique plants and animals live at high elevations and nowhere else on Earth. So the next time you see a mountain, you are not just looking at a pile of rock. You are looking at a machine that shapes weather, feeds rivers, and shelters life, all while being slowly built from below and torn apart from above.

prompt._: In the section 'More Than Scenery,' the author describes a mountain as being 'a machine that shapes weather, feeds rivers, and shelters life.' Based on this description, what does the author mean by calling a mountain a 'machine'?

choices.text: something that does important jobs in nature
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage lists three important jobs mountains do — shaping weather, feeding rivers, and sheltering life — which is why the author compares them to a machine.

choices.text: something that is made entirely of water and snow
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mountains are made of rock, not entirely of water and snow, even though snow on mountains does help feed rivers.

choices.text: something that moves quickly across the land
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mountains don't move across the land; in fact, the passage says they are slowly built up and worn down, not that they travel anywhere.

choices.text: something that is built by people using metal parts
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says mountains are built by people or made of metal — that's the everyday meaning of 'machine,' but the author is using the word in a special, creative way.

prompt._: According to the article, how is the Himalaya mountain range different from the Appalachian Mountains?

choices.text: The Himalayas are slowly growing taller, while the Appalachians are slowly wearing down.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly states that the Himalayas are still rising due to plate collision, while the Appalachians have been worn down by erosion over millions of years.

choices.text: The Himalayas were built by erupting magma, while the Appalachians were built by deep faults.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Himalayas were built by two plates colliding, not by erupting magma, and the article does not say the Appalachians were built by faults either.

choices.text: The Himalayas are made of solid rock plates, while the Appalachians are made of melted lava.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article does not describe the Himalayas as solid rock plates or the Appalachians as made of melted lava — those details simply aren't in the passage.

choices.text: The Himalayas are located deep underwater, while the Appalachians are located on dry land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says the Himalayas are underwater — they are in Asia, and it is the Mid-Ocean Ridge that is found on the ocean floor.

prompt._: Based on the article, how do cracks in Earth's crust cause mountains to form?

choices.text: They help wind and rain to scrape away rock into gentle slopes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wind and rain causing erosion is what wears mountains down, not what builds them up through cracks.

choices.text: They allow melted rock to escape and giant rock blocks to rise.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that cracks called faults let giant rock blocks rise up, and cracks in the crust also let magma escape to the surface and build volcanic mountains.

choices.text: They cause tectonic plates to crash and crumple into tall peaks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because plate collisions happen when plates crash into each other, which is a different process from what cracks in the crust do.

choices.text: They force hot magma to stay underground and cool into flat rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says magma forces its way through cracks and erupts onto the surface — it does not stay underground and cool into flat rock.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence best describes the order of events that shaped the Appalachian Mountains from their beginning to today?

choices.text: Magma erupted through cracks in the crust, then wind and rain rounded the peaks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Appalachians were built by plates colliding and pushing rock upward, not by magma erupting through cracks in the crust.

choices.text: Faults cracked the crust into blocks, then the blocks tilted to form gentle slopes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tilting rock blocks along a fault describes how fault-block mountains like the Sierra Nevada formed, not how the Appalachians formed.

choices.text: Plates collided and pushed rock upward, then erosion wore the peaks down over time
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the Appalachians were once as tall as the Himalayas (built by plate collisions pushing rock up), and then erosion wore them down into the gentle peaks we see today — exactly that order.

choices.text: Erosion wore the mountains down first, then plate collisions pushed them back up
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the events backwards — the mountains were pushed up by plate collisions first, and erosion wore them down afterward, not the other way around.

prompt._: How does the article contrast the forces that build mountains with the forces that wear them down?

choices.text: Building forces push mountains up on dry land, while wearing-down forces tear them down underwater.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because erosion happens to mountains on land, not underwater — the article never says wearing-down forces work underwater.

choices.text: Building forces push mountains up very quickly, while wearing-down forces tear them down slowly.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article explains that building forces are actually very slow — taking millions of years — and erosion is also a gradual process, not a fast one.

choices.text: Building forces pushed mountains up in the past, while wearing-down forces tear them down right now.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both forces are happening at the same time right now — Everest is still growing today while erosion is also currently wearing mountains down.

choices.text: Building forces push mountains up from below, while wearing-down forces tear them down from above.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article's title and content clearly show that building forces (like magma and plate collisions) push up from below, while erosion forces like wind and rain tear mountains down from above.

syllabus_metadata.title: How Mountains Are Born

assessments.title: Great Ranges of the Americas

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Two Great Spines of the Americas

A Raindrop's Split Decision

A single raindrop lands on a rocky ridge high in the mountains of Colorado. It rolls a few inches to the left and begins a journey toward the Pacific Ocean. Another drop, falling just two feet to the right, will travel thousands of miles in the opposite direction — all the way to the Atlantic. That ridge is part of the Rocky Mountains, and it sits along something called the continental divide — an invisible line where water flowing down one side of the mountains ends up in a completely different ocean than water on the other side. Think of it like the peak of your roof at home: rain on the front slides to the street, while rain on the back drips into the yard.

The Rockies are one of two enormous mountain ranges that run like spines down the western edges of North and South America. The other is the Andes, far to the south.
stimulus.content_text: The Two Great Spines of the AmericasA Raindrop's Split Decision A single raindrop lands on a rocky ridge high in the mountains of Colorado. It rolls a few inches to the left and begins a journey toward the Pacific Ocean. Another drop, falling just two feet to the right, will travel thousands of miles in the opposite direction — all the way to the Atlantic. That ridge is part of the Rocky Mountains, and it sits along something called the continental divide — an invisible line where water flowing down one side of the mountains ends up in a completely different ocean than water on the other side. Think of it like the peak of your roof at home: rain on the front slides to the street, while rain on the back drips into the yard. The Rockies are one of two enormous mountain ranges that run like spines down the western edges of North and South America. The other is the Andes, far to the south.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the contrast between raindrops that fall on different sides of the continental divide?

choices.text: They travel toward two completely different oceans.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says one drop heads to the Pacific and the other heads to the Atlantic, which are two completely different oceans.

choices.text: They travel different distances before reaching land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never compares how far each raindrop travels before reaching land — that detail isn't mentioned at all.

choices.text: They follow the same path to the Pacific Ocean.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the two raindrops travel in completely different directions, not the same path to the Pacific.

choices.text: They flow at different speeds down the mountains.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never talks about how fast or slow the raindrops flow down the mountains.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Rockies: A Wall of Stone and Snow

The Rocky Mountains stretch more than 3,000 miles from New Mexico in the United States to British Columbia in Canada. They pass through six American states and two Canadian provinces. Mount Elbert in Colorado stands as the tallest peak at 14,440 feet, surrounded by deep canyons and crystal-clear rivers.

You might think mountains like these were built by volcanoes. Some mountains are. But the Rockies formed when huge slabs of Earth's outer shell, called tectonic plates, crashed together over millions of years — imagine pushing two thick rugs together on a smooth floor until they crumple and fold upward.

Grizzly bears roam the Rockies' northern forests. Bighorn sheep scramble along cliffs that would make your knees wobble. Elk, moose, and mountain lions fill the high valleys. The mountains provide homes for all of them because different elevations — heights above sea level, like floors in a tall building — create different climates stacked on top of each other.
stimulus.content_text: The Rockies: A Wall of Stone and Snow The Rocky Mountains stretch more than 3,000 miles from New Mexico in the United States to British Columbia in Canada. They pass through six American states and two Canadian provinces. Mount Elbert in Colorado stands as the tallest peak at 14,440 feet, surrounded by deep canyons and crystal-clear rivers. You might think mountains like these were built by volcanoes. Some mountains are. But the Rockies formed when huge slabs of Earth's outer shell, called tectonic plates, crashed together over millions of years — imagine pushing two thick rugs together on a smooth floor until they crumple and fold upward. Grizzly bears roam the Rockies' northern forests. Bighorn sheep scramble along cliffs that would make your knees wobble. Elk, moose, and mountain lions fill the high valleys. The mountains provide homes for all of them because different elevations — heights above sea level, like floors in a tall building — create different climates stacked on top of each other.

prompt._: Which key detail explains why the Rocky Mountains can provide homes for so many different animals?

choices.text: The deep canyons are filled with crystal-clear rivers.
choices.feedback: The crystal-clear rivers are a detail describing the mountains, but the passage never says rivers are the reason so many different animals can live there.

choices.text: The mountains pass through six American states.
choices.feedback: The number of states the mountains pass through tells us how big they are, but it doesn't explain why so many different animals are able to live there.

choices.text: The tectonic plates crashed into each other.
choices.feedback: Tectonic plates explain how the Rocky Mountains were built over millions of years, not why they can support so many different animals.

choices.text: The different heights create different climates.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says that different elevations create different climates, which is the key detail explaining why so many animals can call the Rockies home.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Andes: The Longest Chain on Earth

Far to the south, the Andes Mountains run about 4,500 miles along the entire western edge of South America. They pass through seven countries: Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina. Many peaks rise above 20,000 feet. The highest, Mount Aconcagua in Argentina, reaches 22,841 feet — nearly three miles above the sea.

Like the Rockies, the Andes formed when tectonic plates collided. But the Andes also include active volcanoes, where melted rock from deep underground still erupts through the surface. The Andes are still growing — the plates beneath South America keep pressing together, pushing the mountains higher each year. Meanwhile, the older Rockies are slowly being worn down by wind, ice, and rain.
stimulus.content_text: The Andes: The Longest Chain on Earth Far to the south, the Andes Mountains run about 4,500 miles along the entire western edge of South America. They pass through seven countries: Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina. Many peaks rise above 20,000 feet. The highest, Mount Aconcagua in Argentina, reaches 22,841 feet — nearly three miles above the sea. Like the Rockies, the Andes formed when tectonic plates collided. But the Andes also include active volcanoes, where melted rock from deep underground still erupts through the surface. The Andes are still growing — the plates beneath South America keep pressing together, pushing the mountains higher each year. Meanwhile, the older Rockies are slowly being worn down by wind, ice, and rain.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after tectonic plates collide to form the Andes?

choices.text: The Andes stop changing and stay the same height.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly says the Andes are still growing, which means they are definitely still changing.

choices.text: The volcanoes form first and then the plates begin to collide.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the plates collide to form the mountains first, and the volcanoes are simply part of what makes up the Andes — they don't come before the collision.

choices.text: The Andes are slowly worn down by wind, ice, and rain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because wind, ice, and rain wearing down the mountains is what the passage says is happening to the older Rockies, not the Andes.

choices.text: The plates keep pressing together and push the mountains higher.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that the plates beneath South America keep pressing together and push the Andes higher each year.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
People of the High Peaks

The Andes have been home to human civilizations for thousands of years. The most famous was the Inca Empire, which thrived in the 1400s and early 1500s. The Inca built the city of Machu Picchu on a narrow ridge between two peaks, nearly 8,000 feet above sea level. Clouds swirl around its steep green slopes even today. The Inca carved terraces into the mountainsides — wide, flat steps cut into the slope, like a giant staircase — so they could grow crops on land that would otherwise be too steep to farm. They also built roads connecting their empire across some of the roughest ground on Earth.

It is easy to assume that living at high altitude just means dealing with cold weather. But the bigger challenge is the air itself. At extreme heights, the air holds less oxygen. Over many generations, Andean peoples developed larger lungs and blood cells that carry oxygen more efficiently. They also domesticated — trained and raised over generations from wild animals — llamas and alpacas. Llamas carry heavy loads along steep trails. Alpacas provide soft, warm wool for clothing. Both animals thrive in thin mountain air.
stimulus.content_text: People of the High Peaks The Andes have been home to human civilizations for thousands of years. The most famous was the Inca Empire, which thrived in the 1400s and early 1500s. The Inca built the city of Machu Picchu on a narrow ridge between two peaks, nearly 8,000 feet above sea level. Clouds swirl around its steep green slopes even today. The Inca carved terraces into the mountainsides — wide, flat steps cut into the slope, like a giant staircase — so they could grow crops on land that would otherwise be too steep to farm. They also built roads connecting their empire across some of the roughest ground on Earth. It is easy to assume that living at high altitude just means dealing with cold weather. But the bigger challenge is the air itself. At extreme heights, the air holds less oxygen. Over many generations, Andean peoples developed larger lungs and blood cells that carry oxygen more efficiently. They also domesticated — trained and raised over generations from wild animals — llamas and alpacas. Llamas carry heavy loads along steep trails. Alpacas provide soft, warm wool for clothing. Both animals thrive in thin mountain air.

prompt._: The passage compares terraces to 'a giant staircase.' Based on this comparison, what does the word 'terraces' most likely mean?

choices.text: deep holes dug into the ground for planting
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because terraces are flat platforms built into the side of a slope, not holes dug into the ground.

choices.text: flat steps cut into a steep mountainside
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes terraces as 'wide, flat steps cut into the slope, like a giant staircase,' which matches this answer exactly.

choices.text: tall fences used to protect mountain crops
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions fences at all — terraces are about shaping the land for farming, not protecting it with barriers.

choices.text: narrow roads built to connect distant cities
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage talks about roads separately — roads connected the empire, while terraces were used for farming.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Mountains That Shape the Weather

Both the Rockies and the Andes control weather patterns across their continents. When moist air blows toward a mountain range, it rises and cools. Cool air cannot hold as much moisture, so rain or snow falls on the windward side — the side facing the incoming wind. By the time the air crosses the peaks, most of its moisture is gone. The far side, called the rain shadow, stays dry. Picture wringing out a wet towel over one side of a wall — the other side gets almost nothing. This effect has created thick forests on one side of these ranges and dry deserts on the other.
stimulus.content_text: Mountains That Shape the Weather Both the Rockies and the Andes control weather patterns across their continents. When moist air blows toward a mountain range, it rises and cools. Cool air cannot hold as much moisture, so rain or snow falls on the windward side — the side facing the incoming wind. By the time the air crosses the peaks, most of its moisture is gone. The far side, called the rain shadow, stays dry. Picture wringing out a wet towel over one side of a wall — the other side gets almost nothing. This effect has created thick forests on one side of these ranges and dry deserts on the other.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a rain shadow?

choices.text: the dry side of a mountain range away from the wind
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the far side, called the rain shadow, stays dry because the air has lost most of its moisture by the time it crosses the peaks.

choices.text: the side of a mountain that faces the incoming wind
choices.feedback: This is actually the definition of the windward side — the side facing the incoming wind — not the rain shadow.

choices.text: a thick forest that grows on the side of a mountain
choices.feedback: Thick forests grow on the wet, windward side of the mountain, not on the dry rain shadow side.

choices.text: a cloud that forms when moist air rises over peaks
choices.feedback: A rain shadow is not a cloud — it is the dry area of land on the far side of a mountain range where very little rain falls.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Never Finished

They decide where rivers flow, where rain falls, what animals survive, and how entire civilizations live. They are the machinery of continents — and they never stop moving.
stimulus.content_text: Never Finished They decide where rivers flow, where rain falls, what animals survive, and how entire civilizations live. They are the machinery of continents — and they never stop moving.

prompt._: What evidence does the author give to support the point that mountains are the "machinery of continents"?

choices.text: They help people build new machines for cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions people, cities, or building machines of any kind.

choices.text: They are built using very large metal machines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions metal machines or anything being built — 'machinery' is used as a comparison to show how powerful mountains are, not that they are made of metal.

choices.text: They finally stop moving after many long years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — it clearly states that mountains 'never stop moving.'

choices.text: They decide where rivers flow and animals live.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that mountains decide where rivers flow and where animals survive, which is the evidence the author gives to support calling them the 'machinery of continents.'

prompt._: Based on the article, what is one major difference between the Andes and the Rocky Mountains?

choices.text: The Andes were formed by plates, while the Rockies were formed by volcanoes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says both the Rockies and the Andes were formed by tectonic plates colliding — the Rockies were not formed by volcanoes.

choices.text: The Andes create dry rain shadows, while the Rockies create wet rain shadows.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that both mountain ranges create rain shadows on their dry sides, so this is not a difference between them.

choices.text: The Andes are longer and taller, while the Rockies are shorter and lower.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states the Andes are about 4,500 miles long with peaks over 20,000 feet, while the Rockies are about 3,000 miles long with a tallest peak of 14,440 feet.

choices.text: The Andes have one main climate, while the Rockies have many stacked climates.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article describes the Rockies as having many stacked climates at different elevations, and does not say the Andes have only one climate.

prompt._: Based on the article, how do the mountains help cause their own older peaks to slowly wear down?

choices.text: By creating dry rain shadows that block wind from reaching the highest valleys.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the rain shadow is the dry side of the mountain that gets very little rain, so it would not cause the heavy rain and ice that wear peaks down.

choices.text: By pushing tectonic plates together until the earth's outer shell crumples and folds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tectonic plates crashing together is what builds mountains up, not what wears them down.

choices.text: By splitting falling raindrops so they flow away into two completely different oceans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because splitting raindrops toward different oceans is about the continental divide, not about erosion or wearing down mountain peaks.

choices.text: By forcing wet air to rise and drop heavy rain and ice on the rocks.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that mountains force moist air to rise, causing rain and snow to fall on them, and that same wind, ice, and rain is what slowly wears the older Rockies down.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence best describes the order in which the Andes Mountains developed over time?

choices.text: People settled first, then plates collided, then mountains formed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the mountains formed millions of years before any people, like the Inca, ever lived there.

choices.text: Volcanoes erupted, then plates collided, then peaks grew taller
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article shows that plate collisions came first and caused the mountains to form, not volcanoes erupting before that.

choices.text: Peaks rose high, then plates began colliding, then volcanoes appeared
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the peaks couldn't rise before the plates collided — the colliding plates are what pushed the mountains up in the first place.

choices.text: Plates collided, then volcanoes formed, then peaks kept rising
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article describes plates colliding to form the Andes, active volcanoes as part of that mountain range, and the plates still pushing the peaks higher today — in exactly that order.

prompt._: By comparing the Rockies and the Andes throughout the article, what main idea does the author develop about both mountain ranges?

choices.text: Both ranges were formed by volcanoes that are still active today
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says the Rockies formed from tectonic plates crashing together, not volcanoes, and only the Andes have active volcanoes.

choices.text: Both ranges provide homes mainly for large grazing animals
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both ranges are home to many kinds of animals, including predators like grizzly bears and mountain lions, not mainly large grazing animals.

choices.text: Both ranges are slowly getting taller as plates push together
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the Andes are still growing taller — the article says the older Rockies are slowly being worn down by wind, ice, and rain.

choices.text: Both ranges shape the land, weather, and life around them
choices.feedback: This is correct because the entire article shows how both mountain ranges shape weather, rivers, animal habitats, and human civilizations across their continents.

syllabus_metadata.title: Great Ranges of the Americas

assessments.title: Mountains of Europe, Asia, and Africa

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Mountains That Move: Four Great Ranges Across the World

Elephants in the Snow

More than two thousand years ago, a general named Hannibal led 37 war elephants up icy mountain paths, through blinding snowstorms, and over cliffs so steep that soldiers slipped to their deaths. He was crossing the Alps — a wall of rock and ice stretching 750 miles across Europe. Hannibal wanted to attack Rome, and the Alps stood in his way. Those mountains still stand today, but they are not the same mountains Hannibal crossed. They have grown taller. Mountains rise, shrink, and change — just too slowly for anyone to notice in a single lifetime.
stimulus.content_text: Mountains That Move: Four Great Ranges Across the WorldElephants in the Snow More than two thousand years ago, a general named Hannibal led 37 war elephants up icy mountain paths, through blinding snowstorms, and over cliffs so steep that soldiers slipped to their deaths. He was crossing the Alps — a wall of rock and ice stretching 750 miles across Europe. Hannibal wanted to attack Rome, and the Alps stood in his way. Those mountains still stand today, but they are not the same mountains Hannibal crossed. They have grown taller. Mountains rise, shrink, and change — just too slowly for anyone to notice in a single lifetime.

prompt._: According to the text, how are the Alps today different from the Alps that Hannibal crossed?

choices.text: They have grown taller over time.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states the Alps 'have grown taller' since Hannibal crossed them.

choices.text: They are covered with more snow and ice.
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about the Alps having more snow or ice today than in Hannibal's time.

choices.text: They stretch across a longer distance.
choices.feedback: The passage tells us the Alps stretch 750 miles across Europe, but never says they stretch across a longer distance than before.

choices.text: They are shorter than they used to be.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — the Alps grew taller, not shorter.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Alps: Europe's Great Wall of Rock

The Alps curve across eight European countries, including France, Switzerland, Italy, Austria, and Germany. They separate northern Europe from the warm Mediterranean south. Their tallest peak, Mont Blanc, rises 15,781 feet — nearly three miles into the sky. For centuries, these mountains blocked trade and travel. Roman armies struggled to cross them. But today, engineers have drilled tunnels straight through the rock for trains and cars. Millions of skiers and hikers visit the Alps every year, drawn by snowy peaks and green valleys that Hannibal's soldiers never had time to admire.
stimulus.content_text: The Alps: Europe's Great Wall of Rock The Alps curve across eight European countries, including France, Switzerland, Italy, Austria, and Germany. They separate northern Europe from the warm Mediterranean south. Their tallest peak, Mont Blanc, rises 15,781 feet — nearly three miles into the sky. For centuries, these mountains blocked trade and travel. Roman armies struggled to cross them. But today, engineers have drilled tunnels straight through the rock for trains and cars. Millions of skiers and hikers visit the Alps every year, drawn by snowy peaks and green valleys that Hannibal's soldiers never had time to admire.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did engineers drill tunnels through the Alps?

choices.text: because skiers needed a faster way to reach snowy peaks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions skiers as visitors to the Alps, but never says the tunnels were built to help them reach the slopes.

choices.text: because the mountains blocked trade and travel for centuries
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the mountains 'blocked trade and travel for centuries,' and then explains that engineers drilled tunnels to allow trains and cars to pass through.

choices.text: because the tunnels would make Mont Blanc easier to climb
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects the tunnels to climbing Mont Blanc in any way.

choices.text: because Roman armies wanted to move soldiers more quickly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Roman armies lived long before the tunnels were built, and the passage says they 'struggled' to cross — it never says tunnels were made for them.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Himalayas: Still Growing

Now look east, to the mightiest mountain range on Earth. The Himalayas stretch across five countries: India, Nepal, Bhutan, China, and Pakistan. Mount Everest, at 29,032 feet above sea level, is the highest point on the planet. Jet airplanes sometimes fly below its peak.

You might think mountains this big were built by volcanoes, but the Himalayas were made by tectonic plates — enormous slabs of Earth's outer shell that fit together like a cracked eggshell and drift very slowly across the surface. Millions of years ago, the plate carrying India crashed into the plate carrying Asia. The collision crumpled the ground upward, the way a rug bunches up when you push it against a wall. That collision is still happening. The Himalayas grow about half an inch taller each year — roughly as fast as your fingernails grow.

These mountains are so massive that they control the weather across an entire continent. They block freezing winds from Central Asia, keeping India warmer. They also push moist ocean air upward, creating monsoons — seasonal storms that dump enormous amounts of rain. Millions of farmers in India and Bangladesh depend on monsoon rains to water their crops. Without the Himalayas, those rains might never come.

Three of the world's greatest rivers — the Ganges, the Indus, and the Brahmaputra — begin as melting snow and ice high in these peaks. They provide water to hundreds of millions of people on the plains below.

You might think mountains this big were built by volcanoes, but the Himalayas were made by tectonic plates — enormous slabs of Earth's outer shell that fit together like a cracked eggshell and drift very slowly across the surface. Millions of years ago, the plate carrying India crashed into the plate carrying Asia. The collision crumpled the ground upward, the way a rug bunches up when you push it against a wall. That collision is still happening. The Himalayas grow about half an inch taller each year — roughly as fast as your fingernails grow. They also push moist ocean air upward, creating monsoons — seasonal storms that dump enormous amounts of rain. Millions of farmers in India and Bangladesh depend on monsoon rains to water their crops. Without the Himalayas, those rains might never come. Three of the world's greatest rivers — the Ganges, the Indus, and the Brahmaputra — begin as melting snow and ice high in these peaks. They provide water to hundreds of millions of people on the plains below.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened right after the plate carrying India crashed into the plate carrying Asia?

choices.text: The snow formed rivers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rivers forming from melting snow is a separate fact about the mountains, not what happened right after the collision.

choices.text: The volcanoes built peaks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the Himalayas were NOT built by volcanoes, but by tectonic plates colliding.

choices.text: The ground crumpled upward.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the collision 'crumpled the ground upward' — that was the immediate result of the two plates crashing together.

choices.text: The monsoons brought rain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because monsoons are an effect of the mountains blocking moist ocean air, which is unrelated to what happened immediately after the plates crashed.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Urals: Ancient and Worn

Between Europe and Asia lies a much older, quieter range. The Ural Mountains run through Russia for about 1,550 miles, from the Arctic Ocean south to the steppes — the wide, flat grasslands — of Kazakhstan. Their highest peak reaches only 6,217 feet. Compare that to Everest, and the Urals seem small. But they are more than 250 million years old. Erosion — the slow grinding of wind, water, and ice against rock, like sandpaper wearing down wood — has been shrinking them for longer than dinosaurs have existed. Despite their modest height, the Urals hold rich deposits of iron, copper, gold, and precious gems like emeralds. Russia's mining industry has depended on these mountains for centuries.
stimulus.content_text: The Urals: Ancient and Worn Between Europe and Asia lies a much older, quieter range. The Ural Mountains run through Russia for about 1,550 miles, from the Arctic Ocean south to the steppes — the wide, flat grasslands — of Kazakhstan. Their highest peak reaches only 6,217 feet. Compare that to Everest, and the Urals seem small. But they are more than 250 million years old. Erosion — the slow grinding of wind, water, and ice against rock, like sandpaper wearing down wood — has been shrinking them for longer than dinosaurs have existed. Despite their modest height, the Urals hold rich deposits of iron, copper, gold, and precious gems like emeralds. Russia's mining industry has depended on these mountains for centuries.

prompt._: Based on the comparison to Mount Everest, what does the word "modest" mean when describing the height of the Ural Mountains?

choices.text: Incredibly ancient
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'incredibly ancient' describes the Urals' age, not their height, which is what 'modest' is describing in this sentence.

choices.text: Relatively small
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares the Urals to Everest and says they 'seem small,' which means their height is relatively small, or modest.

choices.text: Completely flat
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the Urals are flat — in fact, they are mountains with peaks, just not very tall ones.

choices.text: Highly valuable
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'highly valuable' refers to the minerals and gems found in the Urals, not to how tall they are.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Atlas Mountains: A Wall Against the Desert

In North Africa, the Atlas Mountains stretch across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia. They act as a barrier between the fertile coast and the vast Sahara Desert to the south. The mountains trap moisture from the Mediterranean Sea and the Atlantic Ocean. On their northern slopes, you would find green valleys and thick forests. Walk to the southern side, and the land drops into dry, empty desert. The Berber people have lived in these mountains for thousands of years. They built villages on steep hillsides and carved terraced fields — flat steps cut into the mountainside like a giant staircase — so they could grow crops on land too steep to farm any other way.
stimulus.content_text: The Atlas Mountains: A Wall Against the Desert In North Africa, the Atlas Mountains stretch across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia. They act as a barrier between the fertile coast and the vast Sahara Desert to the south. The mountains trap moisture from the Mediterranean Sea and the Atlantic Ocean. On their northern slopes, you would find green valleys and thick forests. Walk to the southern side, and the land drops into dry, empty desert. The Berber people have lived in these mountains for thousands of years. They built villages on steep hillsides and carved terraced fields — flat steps cut into the mountainside like a giant staircase — so they could grow crops on land too steep to farm any other way.

prompt._: According to the passage, what are terraced fields?

choices.text: barriers that trap moisture from the sea and the ocean
choices.feedback: The passage describes the Atlas Mountains as a barrier that traps moisture from the sea, so this describes the mountains, not terraced fields.

choices.text: steep hillsides where villages are built by the Berber people
choices.feedback: Steep hillsides are where the Berber people built their villages, which is a different detail from the passage — not what terraced fields are.

choices.text: flat steps cut into a mountainside for growing crops
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly defines terraced fields as 'flat steps cut into the mountainside like a giant staircase' used for growing crops.

choices.text: green valleys found on the northern slopes of mountains
choices.feedback: Green valleys are mentioned in the passage as something found on the northern slopes of the mountains, not as the definition of terraced fields.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Rock That Shapes Everything

The Alps and Himalayas are young and still rising. The Urals are ancient and wearing away. The Atlas Mountains are a mix of old and new. But every one of these ranges directs rivers, controls weather, and shapes the lives of the people living nearby. The next time you see a mountain, remember: it is not just sitting there. It is either growing or shrinking, rising or crumbling — a slow-motion event that started millions of years before you were born and will continue millions of years after.
stimulus.content_text: Rock That Shapes Everything The Alps and Himalayas are young and still rising. The Urals are ancient and wearing away. The Atlas Mountains are a mix of old and new. But every one of these ranges directs rivers, controls weather, and shapes the lives of the people living nearby. The next time you see a mountain, remember: it is not just sitting there. It is either growing or shrinking, rising or crumbling — a slow-motion event that started millions of years before you were born and will continue millions of years after.

prompt._: Which detail from the text supports the author's point that mountains are a "slow-motion event"?

choices.text: They direct rivers and control the weather.
choices.feedback: This tells us what mountains do to rivers and weather, but it has nothing to do with how slowly mountains change over millions of years.

choices.text: They shape the lives of nearby people.
choices.feedback: This tells us how mountains affect people nearby, but it doesn't give any evidence about mountains being a slow, long-lasting event.

choices.text: They keep changing for millions of years.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says mountains have been changing for millions of years before us and will keep changing for millions of years after — that's the 'slow-motion event' the author is talking about.

choices.text: They are a mix of old and new.
choices.feedback: This describes only the Atlas Mountains and what they look like, not how slowly all mountains change over a very long time.

prompt._: Based on the text, how are the Himalayas different from the Ural Mountains?

choices.text: The Himalayas are growing taller, but the Urals are shrinking.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the Himalayas grow taller each year, while erosion has been shrinking the Urals for millions of years.

choices.text: The Himalayas are growing taller, but the Urals are staying the same.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Urals are not staying the same — the passage explains that erosion has been slowly shrinking them for a very long time.

choices.text: The Himalayas are shrinking, but the Urals are growing taller.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two mountain ranges switched — it's the Himalayas that are growing, not the Urals.

choices.text: The Himalayas are staying the same, but the Urals are shrinking.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Himalayas are not staying the same — the passage says they grow about half an inch taller every year.

prompt._: According to the text, what causes the land near both the Himalayas and the Atlas Mountains to receive enough water for growing plants?

choices.text: The mountains form when tectonic plates crash together.
choices.feedback: This describes how the Himalayas were formed by tectonic plates, but it doesn't explain how water reaches the land for growing plants.

choices.text: The mountains trap moist air coming from the oceans.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says both the Himalayas and the Atlas Mountains trap moist ocean air, which brings rain and water to the land nearby.

choices.text: The mountains have flat steps carved into their sides.
choices.feedback: Terraced fields are steps the Berber people carved to farm on steep slopes, but they don't explain where the water for growing plants actually comes from.

choices.text: The mountains wear down from wind and ice over time.
choices.feedback: This describes erosion, which is how the Ural Mountains wear down over time — it has nothing to do with bringing water to farmland.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the correct sequence of events that happens as mountain ranges form and age?

choices.text: The ground crumples upward, erosion slowly shrinks the rock, and tectonic plates crash together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ground can't crumple upward on its own first — it needs the tectonic plates to crash together to make that happen.

choices.text: Erosion slowly shrinks the rock, tectonic plates crash together, and the ground crumples upward.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because erosion happens after mountains are already built — it can't shrink mountains before they even exist.

choices.text: Tectonic plates crash together, the ground crumples upward, and erosion slowly shrinks the rock.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows plates crashing first, then the ground pushing up, and then erosion wearing the mountains down over millions of years.

choices.text: Tectonic plates crash together, erosion slowly shrinks the rock, and the ground crumples upward.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because erosion doesn't happen before the ground crumples up — the mountains have to form first before wind and water can start wearing them down.

prompt._: Which statement best explains the main idea the author supports by comparing the four mountain ranges?

choices.text: Even though mountains are made of rock and ice, they all act as walls that block out deserts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because acting as a wall against the desert is only described for the Atlas Mountains, so it doesn't apply to all four ranges in the article.

choices.text: Although mountains differ in age and size, they all change slowly and shape the environment.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows all four ranges change slowly over time and shape the environment around them, which is exactly what the author says in the final section.

choices.text: While mountains block human travel and trade, they all provide rich minerals for deep mining.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blocking travel and providing minerals are only details about some mountains, not a point the author makes about all four ranges.

choices.text: Because mountains are built by shifting plates, they all grow taller and create huge storms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because not all four mountains are built by tectonic plates, and only the Himalayas are described as creating monsoon storms.

assessments.title: Living with Mountains: Climate, Culture, and Challenge

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Life Among the Peaks: How People Survive on the World's Mountains

A Staircase Carved into the Sky

High in the Andes Mountains of Peru, a farmer steps onto a narrow strip of flat earth cut into a steep hillside. People have grown food on steps like these for thousands of years — and that is just one of the clever ways mountain communities have learned to thrive in some of Earth's most difficult places.

Mountains do not just sit there looking impressive. They actually change the weather around them. When wind carries moist air toward a mountain, the air has nowhere to go but up. As it rises, it cools. The water vapor inside it condenses — meaning it changes from an invisible gas into tiny water droplets, the same way cold air turns your breath into a little cloud on a winter morning. Those droplets form clouds, and soon rain or snow falls on the windward side of the mountain.

Here is something that surprises many people. As you climb higher, you move closer to the sun — yet the temperature drops. The sun is so far away that a few thousand feet makes no difference. What matters is the air. At higher elevations — heights above sea level — the air is thinner. Thin air holds less heat, the way a thin blanket keeps you less warm than a thick one. Temperature falls about 3.5 degrees Fahrenheit for every 1,000 feet you climb.

People have grown food on steps like these for thousands of years — and that is just one of the clever ways mountain communities have learned to thrive in some of Earth's most difficult places. Mountains do not just sit there looking impressive. They actually change the weather around them. When wind carries moist air toward a mountain, the air has nowhere to go but up. As it rises, it cools. The water vapor inside it condenses — meaning it changes from an invisible gas into tiny water droplets, the same way cold air turns your breath into a little cloud on a winter morning. Those droplets form clouds, and soon rain or snow falls on the windward side of the mountain. Here is something that surprises many people. As you climb higher, you move closer to the sun — yet the temperature drops. The sun is so far away that a few thousand feet makes no difference. What matters is the air. At higher elevations — heights above sea level — the air is thinner. Thin air holds less heat, the way a thin blanket keeps you less warm than a thick one. Temperature falls about 3.5 degrees Fahrenheit for every 1,000 feet you climb.

prompt._: According to the text, what is surprising about climbing higher on a mountain even though you move closer to the sun?

choices.text: The temperature drops because the air is thinner
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that thinner air at higher elevations holds less heat, causing the temperature to drop.

choices.text: The sun warms you more as you get closer to it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains the sun is so far away that climbing a few thousand feet closer makes no difference in how much it warms you.

choices.text: The wind blows harder and brings warmer weather
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say wind brings warmer weather at higher elevations — in fact, higher elevations are colder.

choices.text: The air becomes thicker and holds more heat
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says air gets thinner at higher elevations, not thicker, and thin air holds less heat.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Farming and Food on the Mountain Slopes

Flat land is rare in the mountains. So mountain communities invented terrace farming — the practice of cutting flat steps into a steep slope so crops have level ground to grow on. Water flows gently from one terrace down to the next, watering the plants along the way. The Inca civilization of South America built more than one million hectares of terraces across the Andes, many of which are still used today. Farmers in the Andes grow potatoes on terraces — and Peru alone has over 3,000 varieties of potato. In Southeast Asia, terraces hold flooded rice paddies that shimmer like mirrors. This method has fed mountain families for at least 2,000 years.
stimulus.content_text: Farming and Food on the Mountain Slopes Flat land is rare in the mountains. So mountain communities invented terrace farming — the practice of cutting flat steps into a steep slope so crops have level ground to grow on. Water flows gently from one terrace down to the next, watering the plants along the way. The Inca civilization of South America built more than one million hectares of terraces across the Andes, many of which are still used today. Farmers in the Andes grow potatoes on terraces — and Peru alone has over 3,000 varieties of potato. In Southeast Asia, terraces hold flooded rice paddies that shimmer like mirrors. This method has fed mountain families for at least 2,000 years.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did mountain communities invent terrace farming?

choices.text: Because they wanted to grow many potato varieties.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because growing thousands of potato varieties is something Andean farmers do, but the passage never says that's why terraces were created.

choices.text: Because they needed level ground to grow crops.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says flat land is rare in mountains, so farmers invented terraces to create level ground for their crops.

choices.text: Because water flows gently down the steep slopes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because water flowing gently from terrace to terrace is a helpful feature of the design, not the reason terraces were invented.

choices.text: Because the weather is too cold for normal farming.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions cold weather as a problem — it only talks about the lack of flat land in the mountains.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Bodies Built for the Heights

The Sherpas of Nepal have lived in the Himalaya Mountains for centuries, many of them above 12,000 feet. Over many generations, their bodies adapted to thin air. Scientific studies show that Sherpas carry genetic traits — features passed down from parents to children through DNA — that help their blood deliver oxygen more efficiently. Their muscles perform better where lowland visitors gasp for breath. Today, Sherpas serve as expert guides and porters for mountaineers climbing Everest, which at 29,032 feet is the tallest mountain on Earth. Tenzing Norgay, a Sherpa, was one of the first two people to reach Everest's summit, in 1953.
stimulus.content_text: Bodies Built for the Heights The Sherpas of Nepal have lived in the Himalaya Mountains for centuries, many of them above 12,000 feet. Over many generations, their bodies adapted to thin air. Scientific studies show that Sherpas carry genetic traits — features passed down from parents to children through DNA — that help their blood deliver oxygen more efficiently. Their muscles perform better where lowland visitors gasp for breath. Today, Sherpas serve as expert guides and porters for mountaineers climbing Everest, which at 29,032 feet is the tallest mountain on Earth. Tenzing Norgay, a Sherpa, was one of the first two people to reach Everest's summit, in 1953.

prompt._: According to the passage, which of the following shows the correct order of events?

choices.text: Sherpas reached Everest's summit, then their bodies adapted to thin air over generations.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — the passage says Sherpas adapted over many generations first, and the summit climb by Tenzing Norgay happened much later in 1953.

choices.text: Sherpas' bodies adapted over generations, then they became expert guides for climbers.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that Sherpas adapted to thin air over many generations, and then describes how they serve as expert guides and porters for climbers today.

choices.text: Tenzing Norgay became a guide, then Sherpas first settled above 12,000 feet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Sherpas had been living above 12,000 feet for centuries before Tenzing Norgay was born, so their settlement came first.

choices.text: Scientists studied Sherpas' DNA, then Sherpas first moved to the Himalaya Mountains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the scientific studies happened after Sherpas had already been living in the mountains for centuries, not before they moved there.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Mountain Cultures Around the World

Mountains shape more than bodies. They shape entire ways of life. In the Swiss Alps, communities have herded dairy cattle in high pastures for centuries, producing famous cheeses such as Gruyère, which has been made in Switzerland since at least 1115 CE. In Morocco's Atlas Mountains, Berber villagers weave colorful rugs and grow saffron, a spice so valuable it can cost more than $5,000 per pound. In the Ethiopian highlands, people grow a tiny grain called teff and use it to make a spongy flatbread that forms the base of nearly every Ethiopian meal. Ethiopia produces about 70 percent of the world's teff supply.
stimulus.content_text: Mountain Cultures Around the World Mountains shape more than bodies. They shape entire ways of life. In the Swiss Alps, communities have herded dairy cattle in high pastures for centuries, producing famous cheeses such as Gruyère, which has been made in Switzerland since at least 1115 CE. In Morocco's Atlas Mountains, Berber villagers weave colorful rugs and grow saffron, a spice so valuable it can cost more than $5,000 per pound. In the Ethiopian highlands, people grow a tiny grain called teff and use it to make a spongy flatbread that forms the base of nearly every Ethiopian meal. Ethiopia produces about 70 percent of the world's teff supply.

prompt._: Based on the text, how does the amount of teff Ethiopia produces compare to the rest of the world?

choices.text: It divides its teff equally among all of the other countries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Ethiopia divides or shares its teff equally with other countries.

choices.text: It produces more teff than all other countries combined.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because 70 percent means Ethiopia grows more teff than all other countries combined, since the rest of the world only produces the remaining 30 percent.

choices.text: It produces much less teff than all other countries combined.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 70 percent is more than half, meaning Ethiopia produces far more teff than the rest of the world, not less.

choices.text: It buys almost all of its teff from other nearby countries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Ethiopia produces teff — it never mentions Ethiopia buying teff from other countries.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Danger, Geology, and Why Mountains Matter to Everyone

Mountain life also means constant risk. Avalanches send walls of snow crashing downhill at speeds of up to 200 miles per hour. Landslides carry mud and rock without warning. Flash floods roar through narrow valleys. Earthquakes strike mountain regions more often than flat ones. Most of the world's great mountain ranges were pushed up when massive slabs of Earth's outer shell — called tectonic plates — collided and crumpled upward. The Himalayas formed when the Indian Plate crashed into the Eurasian Plate about 50 million years ago. Those plates are still pressing against each other today, making the ground beneath mountain communities unstable.

Mountains are not just scenery in the distance. They are working parts of the world you live in every day. About half of the world's population depends on freshwater that flows down from mountain snowpack and glaciers. More than 1 billion people live in mountain regions across every continent except Antarctica. Understanding how mountains shape weather, land, and human life helps us appreciate how important these towering landscapes are.
