Avalanches send walls of snow crashing downhill at speeds of up to 200 miles per hour. Landslides carry mud and rock without warning. Flash floods roar through narrow valleys. Earthquakes strike mountain regions more often than flat ones. Most of the world's great mountain ranges were pushed up when massive slabs of Earth's outer shell — called tectonic plates — collided and crumpled upward. The Himalayas formed when the Indian Plate crashed into the Eurasian Plate about 50 million years ago. Those plates are still pressing against each other today, making the ground beneath mountain communities unstable. Mountains are not just scenery in the distance. They are working parts of the world you live in every day. About half of the world's population depends on freshwater that flows down from mountain snowpack and glaciers. More than 1 billion people live in mountain regions across every continent except Antarctica. Understanding how mountains shape weather, land, and human life helps us appreciate how important these towering landscapes are.

prompt._: According to the text, what are tectonic plates?

choices.text: large rocks carried downhill by landslides
choices.feedback: Large rocks carried downhill describe a landslide, which is a different mountain danger mentioned in the passage, not tectonic plates.

choices.text: layers of snow that build up on mountain peaks
choices.feedback: Layers of snow on mountain peaks are called snowpack, not tectonic plates — snow is not part of Earth's outer shell.

choices.text: massive slabs of Earth's outer shell
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that tectonic plates are 'massive slabs of Earth's outer shell,' giving us the definition right in the text.

choices.text: narrow valleys where flash floods happen
choices.feedback: Narrow valleys are where flash floods happen, but they have nothing to do with tectonic plates, which are deep underground.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the way Sherpas survive in the mountains different from how farmers in the Andes survive?

choices.text: Sherpas survive by growing crops on steep slopes, while Andes farmers guide climbers to the top.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps the two groups — it's Sherpas who guide climbers and Andes farmers who grow crops on steep slopes, not the other way around.

choices.text: Sherpas survive by herding cattle in high pastures, while Andes farmers build steps for water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because herding dairy cattle in high pastures is something people in the Swiss Alps do, not Sherpas.

choices.text: Sherpas survive by moving to lower elevations, while Andes farmers stay high up to plant crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says Sherpas live high up in the Himalayas, often above 12,000 feet, and never mentions them moving to lower elevations.

choices.text: Sherpas survive by relying on changes inside their bodies, while Andes farmers change the land.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Sherpas adapted through genetic changes inside their bodies, while Andes farmers adapted by building terraces to change the land.

prompt._: According to the text, how do mountains cause changes to the earth's water supply?

choices.text: They block heavy winds from reaching the ocean and absorb all the freshwater into their dry soil.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says mountains block winds from the ocean or absorb freshwater — in fact, the text says water flows down from mountains to supply people below.

choices.text: They push tectonic plates apart to create deep oceans and melt glaciers to cause heavy flooding.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says tectonic plates colliding push mountains upward, not apart to create oceans, and nothing in the text connects glaciers to heavy flooding.

choices.text: They heat up water vapor until it becomes a gas and trap the falling rain inside the thick clouds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that rising air cools down (not heats up) to condense water vapor into droplets, and the text never mentions rain being trapped inside clouds.

choices.text: They force moist air up to create rain and store frozen water that flows down to the valleys.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that mountains push moist air upward causing rain and snow, and that mountain snowpack and glaciers supply freshwater to about half the world's population.

prompt._: According to the article, in what order did these events happen in the Himalaya region?

choices.text: Tectonic plates collided, Sherpas adapted to the thin air, and the mountains formed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Sherpas could not have adapted to thin mountain air before the mountains were fully formed and people began living in them.

choices.text: The mountains formed, Sherpas adapted to the thin air, and tectonic plates collided.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the mountains could not have formed before the tectonic plates collided — the collision is what caused the mountains to be pushed up.

choices.text: Tectonic plates collided, the mountains formed, and Sherpas adapted to the thin air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage tells us plates collided 50 million years ago to form the Himalayas, and only after the mountains existed could the Sherpas live there and adapt over many generations.

choices.text: Sherpas adapted to the thin air, tectonic plates collided, and the mountains formed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Sherpas could not have adapted to mountain air before the tectonic plates even collided or the mountains were formed.

prompt._: Which statement best compares mountain communities in a way that matches the main idea of the article?

choices.text: They all make the same kinds of food from crops grown on terraces.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mountain communities around the world grow very different foods, such as potatoes, rice, cheese, saffron, and teff — not the same crops on terraces.

choices.text: They all depend on mountain rivers more than on farming or animals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article describes communities relying on terrace farming, herding cattle, and growing special crops — not mainly on mountain rivers.

choices.text: They all live safely because mountains protect them from danger.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that mountain life includes constant dangers like avalanches, landslides, flash floods, and earthquakes.

choices.text: They all find ways to adjust their lives to mountain conditions.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because every example in the article — Sherpa bodies, Inca terraces, Swiss herding, Ethiopian teff — shows people adjusting their lives to fit mountain conditions.

syllabus_metadata.title: Living with Mountains: Climate, Culture, and Challenge

assessments.title: After Rome Fell: A New Order in Europe

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
When Rome Fell: How Feudalism Held Europe Together

The Roads Go Silent

Imagine a stone road stretching for miles, cracked and choked with weeds. No soldiers march on it. No merchants roll their carts along it. In the year AD 476, the Roman Empire collapsed, and roads like this one went quiet across Europe. The powerful government that had built them was gone. Trade slowed. Cities shrank as people fled to the countryside. Schools and libraries disappeared, and literacy — the ability to read and write — faded like footprints in rain. A new era had begun. Historians call it the Middle Ages, or the medieval period. It stretched from about AD 500 to AD 1500 — nearly a thousand years.
stimulus.content_text: When Rome Fell: How Feudalism Held Europe TogetherThe Roads Go Silent Imagine a stone road stretching for miles, cracked and choked with weeds. No soldiers march on it. No merchants roll their carts along it. In the year AD 476, the Roman Empire collapsed, and roads like this one went quiet across Europe. The powerful government that had built them was gone. Trade slowed. Cities shrank as people fled to the countryside. Schools and libraries disappeared, and literacy — the ability to read and write — faded like footprints in rain. A new era had begun. Historians call it the Middle Ages, or the medieval period. It stretched from about AD 500 to AD 1500 — nearly a thousand years.

prompt._: According to the text, how were Roman roads different before and after the empire collapsed in AD 476?

choices.text: Before, roads connected small villages; after, they linked large cities
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about roads connecting villages or cities — it only describes who used the roads, not what places they connected.

choices.text: Before, roads were made of dirt; after, they were paved with stone
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the roads as stone roads the whole time — it never says they changed from dirt to stone.

choices.text: Before, soldiers and merchants used the roads; after, they sat empty
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says soldiers and merchants used the roads before the empire fell, and then the roads 'went quiet' and empty afterward.

choices.text: Before, roads were short and narrow; after, they stretched for miles
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the roads as stretching for miles before the empire fell, not after — and it never says the roads changed in size.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Broken Land

Without Rome's army and laws, Europe split into many small kingdoms. Germanic tribes — groups of warriors and families from northern and central Europe — now controlled different regions. The Franks held much of what is now France. The Angles and Saxons settled in England. The Visigoths ruled Spain. These groups fought each other for land and power. Bandits roamed the countryside. Vikings, fierce raiders from the north, attacked villages without warning. Ordinary people needed protection badly.
stimulus.content_text: A Broken Land Without Rome's army and laws, Europe split into many small kingdoms. Germanic tribes — groups of warriors and families from northern and central Europe — now controlled different regions. The Franks held much of what is now France. The Angles and Saxons settled in England. The Visigoths ruled Spain. These groups fought each other for land and power. Bandits roamed the countryside. Vikings, fierce raiders from the north, attacked villages without warning. Ordinary people needed protection badly.

prompt._: According to the passage, what caused Europe to split into many small kingdoms?

choices.text: The need for ordinary people to find safe protection.
choices.feedback: People needing protection is a result of Europe becoming dangerous and broken, not the cause of the split itself.

choices.text: The loss of Rome's powerful army and strict laws.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that losing Rome's army and laws is what caused Europe to break into many small kingdoms.

choices.text: The constant fighting between different Germanic tribes.
choices.feedback: Germanic tribes fighting each other is something that happened after Europe split apart, not the reason it split in the first place.

choices.text: The sudden attacks on villages by fierce Viking raiders.
choices.feedback: Viking attacks are mentioned in the passage, but they were a problem that happened after the split — not the cause of it.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Deal for Safety: Feudalism

Out of this chaos — this scary disorder — a new system grew. It was called feudalism. A king owned all the land in his kingdom, but he could not guard it alone. So he gave large pieces of land to powerful nobles, people from wealthy and important families. These nobles became lords. In return, each lord promised to fight for the king and bring soldiers when called.

The lords then split their land among smaller lords or knights, trained warriors on horseback. These knights made the same promises. Everyone owed loyalty to the person above them, like links in a chain. Each link held up the next.

You might think kings had total power over everyone. Actually, kings depended on their lords. A king who angered his lords could lose their soldiers and even his throne.
stimulus.content_text: A Deal for Safety: Feudalism Out of this chaos — this scary disorder — a new system grew. It was called feudalism. A king owned all the land in his kingdom, but he could not guard it alone. So he gave large pieces of land to powerful nobles, people from wealthy and important families. These nobles became lords. In return, each lord promised to fight for the king and bring soldiers when called. The lords then split their land among smaller lords or knights, trained warriors on horseback. These knights made the same promises. Everyone owed loyalty to the person above them, like links in a chain. Each link held up the next. You might think kings had total power over everyone. Actually, kings depended on their lords. A king who angered his lords could lose their soldiers and even his throne.

prompt._: According to the text, why did the king give large pieces of land to powerful nobles?

choices.text: Because the nobles paid the king gold for the land
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says nobles paid the king gold — they gave loyalty and soldiers, not money.

choices.text: Because the knights refused to fight without land
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the text never says knights refused to fight without land — that detail simply isn't in the passage.

choices.text: Because the nobles had already taken the land by force
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows the king willingly gave the land to nobles as part of a deal, not because they took it by force.

choices.text: Because the king could not guard all his land alone
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the king 'could not guard it alone,' which is exactly why he gave land to the nobles.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Life on the Manor

Most people in medieval Europe never saw a king. They lived on a manor, a large estate with a lord's house, farmland, a church, and a small village. At the bottom of the feudal system were the serfs, peasants who worked the lord's fields from sunrise to sunset. In return, the lord was supposed to protect their families from bandits and invaders.

It is easy to assume serfs were the same as enslaved people. They were not. Serfs had certain rights. They could keep some of the food they grew. A lord could not sell them the way enslaved people were sold. But serfs could not leave the manor without permission. They were not free. It was a hard bargain — they did most of the work but held very little power.
stimulus.content_text: Life on the Manor Most people in medieval Europe never saw a king. They lived on a manor, a large estate with a lord's house, farmland, a church, and a small village. At the bottom of the feudal system were the serfs, peasants who worked the lord's fields from sunrise to sunset. In return, the lord was supposed to protect their families from bandits and invaders. It is easy to assume serfs were the same as enslaved people. They were not. Serfs had certain rights. They could keep some of the food they grew. A lord could not sell them the way enslaved people were sold. But serfs could not leave the manor without permission. They were not free. It was a hard bargain — they did most of the work but held very little power.

prompt._: Based on the passage, which detail helps explain the meaning of the word 'serf' by showing how they were similar to enslaved people?

choices.text: A serf lived inside the lord's house.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says serfs lived in the lord's house — they lived in the village on the manor.

choices.text: A serf could keep some of the crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because keeping some crops was a right that serfs had but enslaved people did not, making it a difference, not a similarity.

choices.text: A serf could not be sold by a lord.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because not being able to be sold is something that made serfs different from enslaved people, not similar.

choices.text: A serf did not have complete freedom.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says serfs could not leave without permission and were not free, which is a way they were similar to enslaved people.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Not All Darkness

You might picture the Middle Ages as a time of nothing but misery. But people still created beautiful things. Builders raised stone castles and tall cathedrals. The Christian Church became the one thing that connected people across all the scattered kingdoms. Churches ran the few schools that existed. Christianity gave Europeans a shared set of beliefs and holidays, holding their culture together even when governments could not.

Latin, the old language of Rome, slowly changed into new languages — French, Spanish, Italian. Knights replaced Roman soldiers. Castles replaced Roman forts. Old ways faded, and new traditions grew in their place.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Feudalism Still Matters

Feudalism was not fair. It kept most people poor and stuck in one place for life. But the next time you see a castle in a movie or read about a knight in a story, you are looking at this system's fingerprints. The deal was simple — land for loyalty, work for protection — and it held Europe together when nothing else could.

assessments.title: Lords of the Land: Manors and the Feudal Pyramid

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Life on the Manor: How Medieval Europe Really Worked

A Tiny World Behind Wooden Walls

Smoke rises from a row of small, one-room cottages made of mud and straw. A blacksmith hammers a glowing piece of iron into a horseshoe. Nearby, a woman spins sheep's wool into thread while her children chase chickens through the dirt. Welcome to a medieval manor — an estate, or large piece of land, owned by a lord. Think of it like a small town that belonged to one powerful family.

A manor included farmland, a village, a church, and the lord's house or castle. You might picture everyone in medieval times living inside a castle. Actually, only the lord and his family lived there. Most people lived in those tiny cottages with dirt floors and no windows. Almost everything people needed — food, clothing, tools — was made right there on the manor. The manor was self-sufficient, meaning it could take care of itself, like a lunch box packed with everything you need for the whole day.

Every person on the manor had a role in keeping it self-sufficient. Farmers grew wheat, barley, and vegetables. Shepherds raised sheep for wool. Blacksmiths forged — heated and shaped metal into — tools and horseshoes. Millers ground grain into flour. Bakers turned flour into bread. Very few things came from outside. That mattered because roads were rough, and bandits hid in the forests.

That mattered because roads were rough, and bandits hid in the forests.

prompt._: According to the text, what is one difference between where the lord's family lived and where most people lived?

choices.text: The lord's family lived in the church, while most people lived in the lord's house.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anyone lived in the church — it was just one part of the manor.

choices.text: The lord's family lived in one-room cottages, while most people lived on farmland.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the lord's family lived in cottages or that most people lived on farmland.

choices.text: The lord's family lived in the castle, while most people lived in tiny cottages.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the lord's family lived in the castle while most people lived in tiny cottages with dirt floors and no windows.

choices.text: The lord's family lived in the village, while most people lived in the castle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says the lord lived in the castle, not the village, and most people lived in cottages, not the castle.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Feudal Pyramid

Medieval society had a strict order called the feudal system — a set of rules about who owed what to whom. Picture a pyramid. The king sat at the top. Below him were the great nobles, or lords, who controlled large areas of land. Below the lords were the knights, soldiers who protected the land. At the very bottom, making up the biggest group, were the peasants and serfs who farmed.

Each level had a job. The king provided laws and led the kingdom. Lords managed their manors and sent soldiers when the king needed an army. Knights trained for battle. Peasants and serfs did the hard, daily work of growing food and building things. But the king was not all-powerful — he needed his lords' armies and support, so power flowed up and down, not just from the top.

The feudal system was not fair. But it gave people order during a dangerous, turbulent time — a time full of conflict and confusion, like a river churning after a storm. Everyone knew their role. The lord protected the serfs. The serfs fed the lord. This arrangement kept medieval society running for hundreds of years.

At the very bottom, making up the biggest group, were the peasants and serfs who farmed. Each level had a job. The king provided laws and led the kingdom. Lords managed their manors and sent soldiers when the king needed an army. Knights trained for battle. Peasants and serfs did the hard, daily work of growing food and building things. But the king was not all-powerful — he needed his lords' armies and support, so power flowed up and down, not just from the top. The feudal system was not fair. The serfs fed the lord. This arrangement kept medieval society running for hundreds of years.

prompt._: A main idea of the text is that the different levels of the feudal system relied on each other. Which detail best explains why the king relied on the lords?

choices.text: Because he provided laws for the whole kingdom.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because providing laws was the king's job that he did for the kingdom, not a reason he needed help from the lords.

choices.text: Because he sat at the very top of the pyramid.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being at the top of the pyramid just describes where the king ranked, not why he depended on the lords.

choices.text: Because he gave people order during a conflict.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because giving people order describes what the whole feudal system did, not a specific reason the king needed the lords.

choices.text: Because he needed their armies and support.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the king 'needed his lords' armies and support,' which is direct evidence that he relied on them.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Hard Life of a Serf

Life was hardest at the bottom. Serfs were workers tied to the lord's land. They could not leave the manor without permission. If a serf grew ten bushels of grain, he might owe three or four to the lord. Serfs also worked several days each week on the lord's own fields, leaving less time for their own small gardens. They even paid fees to use the lord's mill and the lord's oven.

You might think serfs were the same as enslaved people. They were not. Serfs could not be bought or sold like property. They had rights — they kept a share of their crops, and the lord was expected to protect them. Still, almost every part of a serf's day was controlled by someone else.

They even paid fees to use the lord's mill and the lord's oven. You might think serfs were the same as enslaved people. They were not. Serfs could not be bought or sold like property. They had rights — they kept a share of their crops, and the lord was expected to protect them. Still, almost every part of a serf's day was controlled by someone else.

choices.feedback: This is wrong because serfs only gave the lord three or four bushels out of ten, not all ten bushels.

choices.text: get permission from the lord to leave the manor
choices.feedback: Getting the lord's permission was about leaving the manor entirely, not about when a serf could work in his own garden on the manor.

choices.feedback: Paying fees to use the lord's mill and oven is mentioned as a separate hardship, not as something a serf had to do before working his own garden.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Lord and Lady

The lord lived in the largest building on the manor, often a stone house or castle. He held court in his great hall, solving villagers' problems and making rules. The lord's wife, called the lady of the manor, managed the household and watched over the servants. When the lord rode off to war, she ran the entire manor by herself.

The village church also shaped daily life alongside the lord's authority. A priest led prayers, marked holidays, and helped settle disputes — arguments or disagreements — between neighbors. Together, the lord, the lady, and the church gave the manor its structure and order.

He held court in his great hall, solving villagers' problems and making rules. The lord's wife, called the lady of the manor, managed the household and watched over the servants. When the lord rode off to war, she ran the entire manor by herself. The village church also shaped daily life alongside the lord's authority. A priest led prayers, marked holidays, and helped settle disputes — arguments or disagreements — between neighbors. Together, the lord, the lady, and the church gave the manor its structure and order.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the lady stayed home to run the manor when the lord went to war, and the church is never mentioned going to war.

choices.text: They all helped keep order on the manor.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that together, the lord, the lady, and the church gave the manor its structure and order.

choices.text: They all worked only with servants.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the lord held court for villagers, the priest helped neighbors settle arguments, and the lady watched over servants — they did not all work only with servants.

choices.text: They all lived in the largest building.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the lord lived in the largest building — the lady and the church are not described as living there.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why the Manor Still Matters

Look around your neighborhood. Someone grows or delivers your food. Someone keeps you safe. Someone fixes what breaks. A medieval manor worked the same way — except nobody could leave. The freedom to choose where you live and what you do? That is something a medieval serf could only dream about.

assessments.title: Walls of Stone: How Medieval Castles Were Built

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Built to Survive: The Story of Medieval Castles

From Dirt and Wood

Picture a hill of packed earth as tall as a house. On top sits a wooden tower. Around the base, a rough wooden fence circles a yard full of chickens, grain stores, and nervous villagers. This was the first kind of castle in medieval Europe — and it could be built in just a few weeks.

These early castles were called motte-and-bailey castles. The motte was the big mound of dirt. The bailey was the fenced yard below it, where people kept animals, stored food, and gathered when danger came. You might think all castles were made of stone, but the earliest ones were built entirely from wood and earth. They worked well enough — until someone brought a torch. Wood burns. One fire could destroy years of work in a single night.
stimulus.content_text: Built to Survive: The Story of Medieval CastlesFrom Dirt and Wood Picture a hill of packed earth as tall as a house. On top sits a wooden tower. Around the base, a rough wooden fence circles a yard full of chickens, grain stores, and nervous villagers. This was the first kind of castle in medieval Europe — and it could be built in just a few weeks. These early castles were called motte-and-bailey castles. The motte was the big mound of dirt. The bailey was the fenced yard below it, where people kept animals, stored food, and gathered when danger came. You might think all castles were made of stone, but the earliest ones were built entirely from wood and earth. They worked well enough — until someone brought a torch. Wood burns. One fire could destroy years of work in a single night.

prompt._: According to the passage, how were early motte-and-bailey castles different from what most people imagine castles to be?

choices.text: They were used to store food instead of protect people
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — storing food was just one thing the bailey was used for, but protecting people was also a key purpose of the castle.

choices.text: They were made of wood and earth instead of stone
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says most people might think castles were made of stone, but these early ones were built entirely from wood and earth.

choices.text: They took many years to build instead of a few weeks
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says these castles could be built in just a few weeks, which is actually very fast, not many years.

choices.text: They were built on flat ground instead of on hills
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage describes a hill of packed earth (the motte), so the castle was built on a mound, not flat ground.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Walls That Could Not Burn

Castle builders needed something better. They found it in stone. Over time, thick stone walls replaced wooden fences. These walls could stretch ten feet thick. Stone could not catch fire. It could not be chopped down with axes. But stone castles cost a fortune and took many years to finish.

Hundreds of workers showed up for the job: stonecutters who shaped heavy blocks, carpenters who built wooden frames, and blacksmiths who made iron tools. These builders worked year after year to raise a single castle.
stimulus.content_text: Walls That Could Not Burn Castle builders needed something better. They found it in stone. Over time, thick stone walls replaced wooden fences. These walls could stretch ten feet thick. Stone could not catch fire. It could not be chopped down with axes. But stone castles cost a fortune and took many years to finish. Hundreds of workers showed up for the job: stonecutters who shaped heavy blocks, carpenters who built wooden frames, and blacksmiths who made iron tools. These builders worked year after year to raise a single castle.

prompt._: Based on the text, why did builders choose to make castle walls out of stone?

choices.text: Because stone walls took less time to finish.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — stone castles actually took many years to finish.

choices.text: Because stone walls provided jobs for workers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says workers were needed to build stone castles, but providing jobs was not the reason builders chose stone.

choices.text: Because stone walls could not catch on fire.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states 'stone could not catch fire,' which was a key reason builders chose stone over wood.

choices.text: Because stone walls were built by blacksmiths.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blacksmiths made iron tools used in construction, not the stone walls themselves.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Every Stone Had a Purpose

Nothing in a castle was there by accident. A moat — a wide ditch filled with water, like a river wrapped around the walls — kept attackers from reaching the stone. A drawbridge could be pulled up, cutting off the only path across.

Tall towers gave defenders a high view of the land. From up there, soldiers could spot enemies early and shoot arrows downward. Builders also carved narrow slits into the walls called arrow slits. These openings were just wide enough for an arrow to fly out but too thin for an enemy's arrow to fly in. Think of peeking through a mail slot — you can see out, but no one can easily reach you.

The strongest part of any castle was the keep — the tallest, toughest tower at the center. It was the last safe place if enemies broke through the outer walls. The keep had the thickest walls, the heaviest doors, and enough stored food and water to last for weeks.
stimulus.content_text: Every Stone Had a Purpose Nothing in a castle was there by accident. A moat — a wide ditch filled with water, like a river wrapped around the walls — kept attackers from reaching the stone. A drawbridge could be pulled up, cutting off the only path across. Tall towers gave defenders a high view of the land. From up there, soldiers could spot enemies early and shoot arrows downward. Builders also carved narrow slits into the walls called arrow slits. These openings were just wide enough for an arrow to fly out but too thin for an enemy's arrow to fly in. Think of peeking through a mail slot — you can see out, but no one can easily reach you. The strongest part of any castle was the keep — the tallest, toughest tower at the center. It was the last safe place if enemies broke through the outer walls. The keep had the thickest walls, the heaviest doors, and enough stored food and water to last for weeks.

prompt._: According to the text, what did soldiers do from the tall towers after they spotted enemies?

choices.text: They hid inside the keep.
choices.feedback: Hiding in the keep was a last resort if enemies broke through the outer walls, not what soldiers did from the towers when they first spotted enemies.

choices.text: They filled the wide moat.
choices.feedback: The moat was already filled with water as a permanent defense — soldiers didn't fill it, and filling it isn't mentioned as something tower soldiers did at all.

choices.text: They shot arrows downward.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says soldiers used the tall towers to spot enemies early and then shoot arrows downward at them.

choices.text: They pulled the bridge up.
choices.feedback: Pulling up the drawbridge was something defenders could do, but the passage doesn't connect that action to soldiers in the towers — it's a separate defense feature.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Cold Stones and Everyday Life

It's easy to imagine castles as the sparkling palaces you see in movies. Actually, most castles were cold, dark, and drafty — full of chilly air leaking through gaps in the stone. Stone kept enemies out, but it also kept warmth out.

Still, life inside looked a lot like a small village. The lord lived there with his family, servants, and soldiers. There were kitchens, stables, workshops, a chapel, and a great hall for feasts and meetings. A castle was not just a weapon. It was a home and a community all squeezed behind one set of walls.
stimulus.content_text: Cold Stones and Everyday Life It's easy to imagine castles as the sparkling palaces you see in movies. Actually, most castles were cold, dark, and drafty — full of chilly air leaking through gaps in the stone. Stone kept enemies out, but it also kept warmth out. Still, life inside looked a lot like a small village. The lord lived there with his family, servants, and soldiers. There were kitchens, stables, workshops, a chapel, and a great hall for feasts and meetings. A castle was not just a weapon. It was a home and a community all squeezed behind one set of walls.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was a castle similar to a small village?

choices.text: Both were powerful weapons created to fight enemies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a castle was more than a weapon — it was also a home and a community.

choices.text: Both were drafty structures constructed from cold stones.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being drafty and made of stone describes how castles felt, not what made them similar to villages.

choices.text: Both were sparkling palaces designed for royal families.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says castles were cold and dark, not sparkling palaces — that's just a movie idea.

choices.text: Both were active communities filled with living spaces.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes castles as communities with many living and working spaces, just like a small village.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Invention That Ended It All

For centuries, builders made their castles stronger after every attack. Then, in the 1400s, armies began using cannons powered by gunpowder. A heavy iron cannonball could smash through even the thickest stone wall. Suddenly, the strongest castle in the world could be broken open in days.

Castles stopped being safe. Many were left empty. Their ruins still stand across Europe today — crumbling towers on green hills, arrow slits staring out at nothing. Next time you see a picture of one, look closely at the walls. Every stone was placed by hand, by someone who believed those walls would protect them forever.
stimulus.content_text: The Invention That Ended It All For centuries, builders made their castles stronger after every attack. Then, in the 1400s, armies began using cannons powered by gunpowder. A heavy iron cannonball could smash through even the thickest stone wall. Suddenly, the strongest castle in the world could be broken open in days. Castles stopped being safe. Many were left empty. Their ruins still stand across Europe today — crumbling towers on green hills, arrow slits staring out at nothing. Next time you see a picture of one, look closely at the walls. Every stone was placed by hand, by someone who believed those walls would protect them forever.

prompt._: According to the text, what is gunpowder?

choices.text: a tool used to place heavy stones
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions any tool for placing stones — that detail simply isn't in the text.

choices.text: a space left empty in castles
choices.feedback: The passage does say castles were left empty, but it never uses the word 'gunpowder' to describe empty spaces.

choices.text: a kind of stone used in walls
choices.feedback: Stone is the material used to build castle walls, not something that powers cannons.

choices.text: a material used to power cannons
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says cannons were 'powered by gunpowder,' so gunpowder is the material that made cannons work.

prompt._: According to the article, what was one major difference between early wooden castles and later stone castles?

choices.text: Wooden castles were built quickly, but stone castles took many years to complete.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states wooden castles could be built in 'a few weeks,' while stone castles took 'many years to finish.'

choices.text: Wooden castles were built by blacksmiths, but stone castles were built by villagers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says blacksmiths helped build stone castles by making iron tools, and villagers gathered inside wooden castles for safety — neither built the other type.

choices.text: Wooden castles took years to build, but stone castles were finished in a few weeks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the facts backwards — the article says wooden castles were built in weeks and stone castles took many years, not the other way around.

choices.text: Wooden castles were used as homes, but stone castles were only used as weapons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says castles were both homes and communities, not just weapons — this was true for stone castles too.

prompt._: Based on information from across the article, why did castle designs keep changing over the centuries?

choices.text: Stone became cheaper and easier to find as the years went on
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says stone castles 'cost a fortune,' which means stone was expensive, not cheaper over time.

choices.text: Each new threat revealed a weakness that builders tried to fix
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows builders changing designs each time a new threat exposed a weakness, from fire destroying wood to cannons smashing stone.

choices.text: Kings ordered builders to copy castle designs from other countries
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions kings giving orders to copy castle designs from other countries.

choices.text: Builders wanted castles to look more beautiful and impressive over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says castles were cold, dark, and drafty — not beautiful — and never mentions builders trying to improve their looks.

prompt._: Based on the text, which list shows the correct order of events in the history of castle building?

choices.text: Builders switched to stone, builders used dirt and wood, armies attacked with cannons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says builders switched to stone before using dirt and wood, but the passage clearly shows wood and earth castles came first.

choices.text: Builders used dirt and wood, armies attacked with cannons, builders switched to stone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it places cannon attacks in the middle of the timeline, but the passage says cannons didn't appear until the 1400s, which was the very end of the castle-building era.

choices.text: Builders used dirt and wood, builders switched to stone, armies attacked with cannons.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes dirt-and-wood castles first, then the switch to stone, and finally the arrival of cannons in the 1400s — exactly this order.

choices.text: Armies attacked with cannons, builders switched to stone, builders used dirt and wood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts cannon attacks first, but cannons came last in history — dirt-and-wood castles and stone castles both existed long before cannons were invented.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: While armies used powerful cannons to break through stone walls, builders quickly invented stronger wooden fences to stop the attacks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly says cannons destroyed castles and builders did not invent stronger wooden fences — castles were simply abandoned after cannons arrived.

choices.text: While castles looked like sparkling palaces from the outside, they were actually dark and empty buildings on the inside.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says castles were cold and drafty inside but full of life, not empty, and it never says they looked like sparkling palaces from the outside.

choices.text: While castles were built as strong fortresses to stop enemies, they also served as busy homes for large communities.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it covers the whole article's two main points: castles were strong fortresses built to stop enemies, and they were also busy communities where many people lived.

choices.text: While early castles were made from simple dirt and wood, later builders used heavy stone blocks to stop dangerous fires.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only covers one detail from the beginning of the article — the change from wood to stone — and ignores most of what the article is about.

assessments.title: From Page to Knight: The Code of Chivalry

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
From Boy to Knight: Fourteen Years of Training

A Seven-Year-Old Leaves Home

A boy stands in a castle doorway, seven years old, watching his mother's horse disappear down a dirt road. He will not go home for years. His new life as a page — a young servant training inside a lord's castle — has just begun.

You might think any boy in the Middle Ages could grow up to be a knight. Actually, only boys from noble families got this chance. A peasant family needed their sons to work the farm. They could never afford to send a boy away for training. Knighthood belonged to the wealthy.

As a page, the boy learned manners, how to serve food at the lord's table, and how to ride a horse. He practiced fighting with wooden swords. He studied reading and simple math. Every day was a lesson in how to act, speak, and move like a future knight.
stimulus.content_text: From Boy to Knight: Fourteen Years of TrainingA Seven-Year-Old Leaves Home A boy stands in a castle doorway, seven years old, watching his mother's horse disappear down a dirt road. He will not go home for years. His new life as a page — a young servant training inside a lord's castle — has just begun. You might think any boy in the Middle Ages could grow up to be a knight. Actually, only boys from noble families got this chance. A peasant family needed their sons to work the farm. They could never afford to send a boy away for training. Knighthood belonged to the wealthy. As a page, the boy learned manners, how to serve food at the lord's table, and how to ride a horse. He practiced fighting with wooden swords. He studied reading and simple math. Every day was a lesson in how to act, speak, and move like a future knight.

prompt._: According to the text, what is one difference between boys from noble families and boys from peasant families?

choices.text: Peasant boys trained with wooden swords at home
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says pages practiced with wooden swords at the lord's castle, not at home.

choices.text: Noble boys stayed home to help work on the farm
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was peasant boys, not noble boys, who stayed home to help work on the farm.

choices.text: All boys in the Middle Ages could become knights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — not all boys could become knights, only boys from wealthy, noble families.

choices.text: Only noble boys had the chance to train as knights
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states 'only boys from noble families got this chance,' while peasant boys had to stay home and work the farm.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Squire's Hard Work

Around age fourteen, a page became a squire — a knight's personal assistant, like a teammate who never leaves your side. A squire followed his knight everywhere. He fed and brushed the knight's horse. He polished the knight's armor until it gleamed. He carried the knight's weapons.

Training grew harder. Squires fought with real swords and heavy lances, which were long wooden poles with pointed tips, like giant spears meant for use on horseback. They charged at targets while wearing heavy armor. On the battlefield, a squire stayed close to his knight, ready to pass him a fresh weapon or help him if he fell.

A full suit of armor could weigh forty to sixty pounds. That is about as heavy as a large dog sitting on your shoulders. You might picture armor as one solid metal shell, like a turtle's back. But it was actually made of many separate metal pieces shaped to fit each part of the body. The pieces connected at joints so a knight could still bend his arms, swing a sword, and climb onto a horse. Putting it all on took help. A squire buckled and strapped each section into place, piece by piece. Dressing a knight for battle could take half an hour.

A squire followed his knight everywhere. He fed and brushed the knight's horse. He polished the knight's armor until it gleamed. He carried the knight's weapons. Training grew harder. Squires fought with real swords and heavy lances, which were long wooden poles with pointed tips, like giant spears meant for use on horseback. They charged at targets while wearing heavy armor. On the battlefield, a squire stayed close to his knight, ready to pass him a fresh weapon or help him if he fell. A full suit of armor could weigh forty to sixty pounds. That is about as heavy as a large dog sitting on your shoulders. You might picture armor as one solid metal shell, like a turtle's back. But it was actually made of many separate metal pieces shaped to fit each part of the body. The pieces connected at joints so a knight could still bend his arms, swing a sword, and climb onto a horse. Putting it all on took help. A squire buckled and strapped each section into place, piece by piece. Dressing a knight for battle could take half an hour.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why was a knight's armor made of many separate pieces connected at the joints?

choices.text: To let the knight bend his arms and swing a sword.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly states the pieces connected at joints so the knight could bend his arms, swing a sword, and climb onto a horse.

choices.text: To make the heavy metal armor shine in the bright sun.
choices.feedback: The passage never says the pieces were connected to make the armor shine — shining was the squire's job of polishing, which is a separate idea.

choices.text: To help the young squire practice using heavy weapons.
choices.feedback: The separate pieces helped the knight move in battle, not to give the squire practice with weapons — those are two different topics in the passage.

choices.text: To keep the metal suit from looking like a turtle shell.
choices.feedback: The turtle shell comparison just helps us picture what armor looks like, but it has nothing to do with why the armor was built with separate connected pieces.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Night Before and the Big Moment

If a squire proved himself brave and skilled, he could be knighted around age twenty-one. The night before the ceremony, many squires spent hours alone in a church, praying and thinking about the life ahead of them. Religion was woven deeply into a knight's duties.

The next day, the young man knelt before a lord or king. A sword tapped each of his shoulders. He rose as a knight. It was one of the proudest moments of a medieval man's life — fourteen years of training, finished in a few seconds.
stimulus.content_text: The Night Before and the Big Moment If a squire proved himself brave and skilled, he could be knighted around age twenty-one. The night before the ceremony, many squires spent hours alone in a church, praying and thinking about the life ahead of them. Religion was woven deeply into a knight's duties. The next day, the young man knelt before a lord or king. A sword tapped each of his shoulders. He rose as a knight. It was one of the proudest moments of a medieval man's life — fourteen years of training, finished in a few seconds.

prompt._: According to the text, what did a squire do right before the knighting ceremony the next day?

choices.text: He knelt before a lord to begin his training
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because kneeling before a lord happened during the ceremony, not before it, and it was not part of beginning his training.

choices.text: He spent hours praying alone in a church
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that squires spent hours alone in a church praying the night before the ceremony.

choices.text: He received a sword tap on each shoulder
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sword tap on the shoulders was part of the knighting ceremony itself, not what happened the night before.

choices.text: He practiced sword fighting with other squires
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says squires practiced sword fighting the night before the ceremony.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Code Knights Promised to Follow

Every new knight swore to follow the code of chivalry — a set of rules for honorable behavior, like a promise to be the best version of yourself. Chivalry meant being brave in battle, loyal to your lord, generous to the poor, and showing courtesy, or polite respect, to everyone around you.

But not every knight kept that promise. Some knights burned villages or robbed travelers on the road. The code was an ideal, not always real life.
stimulus.content_text: The Code Knights Promised to Follow Every new knight swore to follow the code of chivalry — a set of rules for honorable behavior, like a promise to be the best version of yourself. Chivalry meant being brave in battle, loyal to your lord, generous to the poor, and showing courtesy, or polite respect, to everyone around you. But not every knight kept that promise. Some knights burned villages or robbed travelers on the road. The code was an ideal, not always real life.

prompt._: Based on the passage about the code of chivalry, how is the code of chivalry similar to a personal promise someone might make?

choices.text: Both were given as orders by a lord
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the code was something knights personally swore to follow, not a set of orders handed down by a lord.

choices.text: Both are rules that everyone always follows
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us that some knights broke the code by burning villages and robbing travelers, so it was NOT always followed.

choices.text: Both were created to prepare for battle
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because chivalry included being loyal, generous, and courteous — not just preparing for battle.

choices.text: Both describe how a person hopes to behave
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares chivalry to a personal promise and calls it an 'ideal,' meaning it describes how someone hopes or tries to behave.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Tournaments: The Sports Events of the Middle Ages

Knights proved their skills at tournaments — big fighting competitions that drew huge, cheering crowds, much like a soccer match today. The most exciting event was jousting, a contest where two armored knights galloped toward each other on horseback and tried to knock each other off with long lances. A knight who lost could have his horse and armor taken by the winner. A knight who won became famous.

A knight's life meant privilege, but it also meant duty. He managed his lord's lands, served in court, and protected the people around him. That boy who once stood alone in a castle doorway had spent fourteen years learning one idea: strength means nothing unless you use it to guard someone weaker than yourself.
stimulus.content_text: Tournaments: The Sports Events of the Middle Ages Knights proved their skills at tournaments — big fighting competitions that drew huge, cheering crowds, much like a soccer match today. The most exciting event was jousting, a contest where two armored knights galloped toward each other on horseback and tried to knock each other off with long lances. A knight who lost could have his horse and armor taken by the winner. A knight who won became famous. A knight's life meant privilege, but it also meant duty. He managed his lord's lands, served in court, and protected the people around him. That boy who once stood alone in a castle doorway had spent fourteen years learning one idea: strength means nothing unless you use it to guard someone weaker than yourself.

prompt._: In the article "From Page to Knight: The Code of Chivalry," what is the meaning of the word jousting?

choices.text: A contest on horseback that involves knights knocking each other off.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says jousting is a contest where knights on horseback try to knock each other off, which is exactly what B says.

choices.text: A modern sporting event that is very similar to a regular soccer match.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because jousting is an old Middle Ages event, not a modern sport, and the passage only compares the crowd excitement to soccer — not jousting itself.

choices.text: A large fighting competition that draws huge, cheering crowds to watch.
choices.feedback: This describes a tournament — the big competition — not jousting, which is just one event inside a tournament.

choices.text: A fourteen-year training program that teaches boys to guard weak people.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the fourteen-year training program is about becoming a knight, not the definition of jousting.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was a page's training different from a squire's training?

choices.text: A page fought in tournaments, while a squire managed the lord's lands.
choices.feedback: The article never says pages fought in tournaments, and managing the lord's lands was something a full knight did, not a squire.

choices.text: A page cared for horses, while a squire studied reading and math.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says pages studied reading and math, while squires were the ones who fed and brushed the knight's horse.

choices.text: A page wore heavy armor, while a squire dressed in simple clothes.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — it was the squire who trained in heavy armor, and the passage never mentions pages wearing simple clothes.

choices.text: A page used wooden swords, while a squire trained with real weapons.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly states pages used wooden swords, while squires trained with real swords and heavy lances.

prompt._: Based on the article, why was learning manners an important part of a page's early training?

choices.text: It helped them entertain the large crowds that gathered to watch tournaments.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never links manners to entertaining crowds at tournaments.

choices.text: It convinced wealthy lords to choose them over peasant boys for knighthood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says only boys from noble families could become knights, so peasant boys were never an option regardless of manners.

choices.text: It prepared them to follow the rules of chivalry by showing polite respect.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article connects learning manners as a page directly to chivalry's rule of showing courtesy, or polite respect, to others.

choices.text: It allowed them to avoid the hard physical work of carrying heavy weapons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pages still trained hard with wooden swords and did physical work — manners didn't replace any of that.

prompt._: Which step happened last in the full journey from page to knight?

choices.text: The squire learned to put on a knight's heavy armor.
choices.feedback: Learning to put on armor was part of being a squire, which came in the middle of the journey, not at the end.

choices.text: The boy practiced fighting with wooden swords at the castle.
choices.feedback: This happened early in the journey, when the boy was still a young page learning the basics at the castle.

choices.text: The squire spent a night praying alone in a church.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the night of praying in the church happened right before the knighting ceremony, making it the last step before becoming a knight.

choices.text: The boy left home at age seven to live with a lord.
choices.feedback: This happened at the very beginning of the journey, when the boy was only seven years old.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how a page's early training compares to a knight's final duties?

choices.text: A page works hard on a peasant farm, while a knight lives a wealthy life inside a castle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pages did not work on peasant farms — the passage says only boys from noble families could become pages and train inside a castle.

choices.text: A page fights wearing heavy metal armor, while a knight fights using long wooden lances.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it gets the details backwards — squires and knights wore heavy armor and used lances, not pages, and pages practiced with wooden swords, not metal armor.

choices.text: A page practices basic skills and manners, while a knight uses them to protect weaker people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows pages learning basic skills and manners, and knights using those skills to protect weaker people, just as the last paragraph explains.

choices.text: A page learns to ride horses for fun, while a knight rides to win famous tournaments.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says a page rides horses just for fun — riding was serious training, not a hobby.

assessments.title: Medieval Towns Come Alive: Markets, Guilds, and Trade

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Where Town Air Made You Free

A Muddy Street Full of Noise

Picture a street barely wide enough for two donkeys to pass. A blacksmith hammers a glowing horseshoe. A baker shouts the price of fresh bread. Chickens scatter between the legs of travelers. Welcome to a medieval town.

You might think these towns looked like modern cities, with wide roads and thousands of buildings. Actually, most medieval towns were tiny. A few hundred people might live in the whole place. But small as they were, these towns changed how people in Europe lived.
stimulus.content_text: Where Town Air Made You FreeA Muddy Street Full of Noise Picture a street barely wide enough for two donkeys to pass. A blacksmith hammers a glowing horseshoe. A baker shouts the price of fresh bread. Chickens scatter between the legs of travelers. Welcome to a medieval town. You might think these towns looked like modern cities, with wide roads and thousands of buildings. Actually, most medieval towns were tiny. A few hundred people might live in the whole place. But small as they were, these towns changed how people in Europe lived.

prompt._: According to the text, how were most medieval towns different from modern cities?

choices.text: They had wide roads and many buildings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says modern cities have wide roads and many buildings — that's the opposite of what medieval towns were like.

choices.text: They had noisy shops on every street.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because noisy shops are part of the opening description of one street, and the passage never says every street had them or calls this the main difference.

choices.text: They were full of travelers and chickens.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because travelers and chickens are used to paint a picture of the street scene, not to explain how medieval towns differed from modern cities.

choices.text: They were small places with few people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that most medieval towns were tiny, with only a few hundred people living there.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Towns Got Started

For most of the early Middle Ages, people lived on manors — large farming estates owned by lords, like little kingdoms with fields, cottages, and a big house in the center. But wherever roads crossed, rivers were easy to wade across, or castles drew crowds, people gathered to trade. A few market stalls became a row of shops. Shops attracted inns. Inns attracted more travelers. Soon, a town was born.

A market square sat at the heart of every town. Farmers hauled in vegetables and grain. Traders spread out cloth, tools, and spices. Merchants opened permanent shops. Craftsmen set up workshops. These merchants and craftsmen were not kings or nobles. They were not poor serfs — workers who were legally tied to a lord's land, the way a book belongs to a library and cannot just walk away. These townspeople were something new: a growing middle class, earning a living through skill and trade.
stimulus.content_text: How Towns Got Started For most of the early Middle Ages, people lived on manors — large farming estates owned by lords, like little kingdoms with fields, cottages, and a big house in the center. But wherever roads crossed, rivers were easy to wade across, or castles drew crowds, people gathered to trade. A few market stalls became a row of shops. Shops attracted inns. Inns attracted more travelers. Soon, a town was born. A market square sat at the heart of every town. Farmers hauled in vegetables and grain. Traders spread out cloth, tools, and spices. Merchants opened permanent shops. Craftsmen set up workshops. These merchants and craftsmen were not kings or nobles. They were not poor serfs — workers who were legally tied to a lord's land, the way a book belongs to a library and cannot just walk away. These townspeople were something new: a growing middle class, earning a living through skill and trade.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what was the main reason many medieval towns began?

choices.text: Travelers came after towns were already built.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage shows travelers were drawn to places that were already growing into towns, so the towns came first, not the other way around.

choices.text: People wanted to live closer to lords' big houses.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says people gathered where trade was easy, not because they wanted to be near a lord's big house.

choices.text: Lords moved serfs into towns to open shops.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says townspeople were a new middle class separate from serfs, and there is no mention of lords moving serfs into towns.

choices.text: People gathered in places where trade was easy.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly shows towns grew in spots where trading was convenient, like road crossings and river fords, starting with just a few market stalls.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Guilds: The Rules of the Trade

As towns grew, craftsmen formed groups called guilds — organizations that controlled a single trade, like a club with strict rules about who could join and how the work should be done. There were guilds for bakers, weavers, blacksmiths, shoemakers, and many others. If you wanted to sell bread in town, you had to belong to the bakers' guild. The guild checked every loaf. This protected buyers from bad bread.

Joining a guild took years. First, a young person — sometimes only twelve years old — became an apprentice. An apprentice lived with a master craftsman and learned the trade from scratch. The apprentice earned no money but received food, a place to sleep, and training.

After several years, the apprentice became a journeyman. That word comes from the French word journée, meaning "a day's work." A journeyman earned wages and traveled from town to town, working for different masters to sharpen his skills. When he felt ready, he created a masterpiece — his very best piece of work — and showed it to the guild. If the guild members approved, he became a master craftsman and could open his own shop.

There were guilds for bakers, weavers, blacksmiths, shoemakers, and many others. If you wanted to sell bread in town, you had to belong to the bakers' guild. The guild checked every loaf. This protected buyers from bad bread. Joining a guild took years. First, a young person — sometimes only twelve years old — became an apprentice. An apprentice lived with a master craftsman and learned the trade from scratch. The apprentice earned no money but received food, a place to sleep, and training. After several years, the apprentice became a journeyman. That word comes from the French word journée, meaning "a day's work." A journeyman earned wages and traveled from town to town, working for different masters to sharpen his skills. When he felt ready, he created a masterpiece — his very best piece of work — and showed it to the guild. If the guild members approved, he became a master craftsman and could open his own shop.

prompt._: According to the text, what did a person become right after finishing time as an apprentice?

choices.text: a master craftsman with a shop
choices.feedback: Incorrect — opening your own shop as a master craftsman happens later, after the journeyman stage, not right after being an apprentice.

choices.text: a journeyman who earned wages
choices.feedback: Correct — the passage says 'After several years, the apprentice became a journeyman' who earned wages, which is exactly what happened next.

choices.text: a young person who joined a guild
choices.feedback: Incorrect — joining the guild as a young person is the very beginning of the process, not the step that comes after being an apprentice.

choices.text: a guild member who checked loaves
choices.feedback: Incorrect — checking loaves is something the guild does to protect buyers, not a role given to someone who just finished being an apprentice.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Town Air Makes You Free

Towns were noisy, crowded, and smelly. Narrow streets turned to mud when it rained. But towns offered something powerful: freedom.

Kings or lords gave towns special documents called charters — written agreements that listed the rights townspeople had. A charter might let townspeople own property, run their own courts, or choose their own work. Serfs on manors had none of these rights. They could not even leave without their lord's permission.

Here's the surprising part. In many places, a serf who escaped to a town and survived there for one year and one day earned freedom. The charter protected them. No lord could drag them back. People repeated a saying across Europe: "Town air makes you free."
stimulus.content_text: Town Air Makes You Free Towns were noisy, crowded, and smelly. Narrow streets turned to mud when it rained. But towns offered something powerful: freedom. Kings or lords gave towns special documents called charters — written agreements that listed the rights townspeople had. A charter might let townspeople own property, run their own courts, or choose their own work. Serfs on manors had none of these rights. They could not even leave without their lord's permission. Here's the surprising part. In many places, a serf who escaped to a town and survived there for one year and one day earned freedom. The charter protected them. No lord could drag them back. People repeated a saying across Europe: "Town air makes you free."

prompt._: According to the passage, how are owning property and choosing your own work similar?

choices.text: They are both rights granted by a town charter.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly lists owning property and choosing your own work as examples of rights a charter could give townspeople.

choices.text: They are both reasons why the towns were noisy.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says towns were noisy because they were crowded, not because of property ownership or work choices.

choices.text: They are both strict rules for serfs to follow.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because owning property and choosing your own work are freedoms, not strict rules — and they applied to townspeople, not serfs.

choices.text: They are both tasks controlled by a local lord.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says serfs were the ones controlled by lords — townspeople with charters had the freedom to own property and choose their work without a lord deciding for them.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than a Market

Those cramped, muddy streets held something bigger than shops. They held new ideas — that ordinary people could build better lives through their own hard work. A baker's apprentice could one day own a bakery. A weaver's son could become a master. That idea did not stay inside the town walls. It spread, and it still matters every time you imagine what you might become.
stimulus.content_text: More Than a Market Those cramped, muddy streets held something bigger than shops. They held new ideas — that ordinary people could build better lives through their own hard work. A baker's apprentice could one day own a bakery. A weaver's son could become a master. That idea did not stay inside the town walls. It spread, and it still matters every time you imagine what you might become.

prompt._: According to the text, which statement defines the "new idea" found in the towns?

choices.text: Ordinary people could clean up the cramped, muddy streets.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while the passage mentions muddy streets, it never says cleaning them up was the new idea.

choices.text: Ordinary people could travel outside the town walls safely.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions ordinary people traveling outside the town walls safely.

choices.text: Ordinary people could buy many things from the large shops.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not describe the new idea as being able to buy things from shops.

choices.text: Ordinary people could build better lives through hard work.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses almost these exact words: 'ordinary people could build better lives through their own hard work.'

prompt._: Based on the article, how was a craftsman in a town different from a serf on a manor?

choices.text: A craftsman belonged to a wealthy noble family, but a serf joined the middle class.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says craftsmen were part of the new middle class, not noble families, and serfs were the ones without freedom or rights.

choices.text: A craftsman followed strict guild rules, but a serf traveled from town to town.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the journeyman (a stage of becoming a craftsman) who traveled from town to town, and serfs didn't travel at all.

choices.text: A craftsman farmed on a large country estate, but a serf sold goods in a shop.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it completely backwards — craftsmen worked in town shops and serfs farmed on large estates, not the other way around.

choices.text: A craftsman could choose his own daily work, but a serf was tied to the land.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says craftsmen had rights like choosing their own work, while serfs were tied to the lord's land and couldn't even leave without permission.

prompt._: According to the article, what made it possible for ordinary people to build better lives in medieval towns?

choices.text: They could build large modern cities and pave wide roads for travel.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article actually says medieval towns were tiny with narrow, muddy streets — not large modern cities with wide roads.

choices.text: They could travel to different manors and bake bread for the king.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions traveling to different manors to bake bread for a king — that never happens in the passage at all.

choices.text: They could learn a trade in a guild and gain rights from a charter.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly says guilds taught people trades and charters gave townspeople rights, and both helped ordinary people build better lives.

choices.text: They could live on large farming estates and work for a noble lord.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because living on large farming estates and working for a noble lord describes life on a manor, which is exactly what limited people's freedom — not what helped them build better lives.

prompt._: Which choice shows the correct order of how a young worker could become the owner of a shop in a medieval town?

choices.text: become an apprentice, work as a journeyman, become a master, open a shop
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly describes this exact order: apprentice → journeyman → master → open a shop.

choices.text: open a shop, join a guild, become an apprentice, become a master
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because opening a shop comes last, not first — you have to earn that right by completing all the steps.

choices.text: work as a journeyman, become an apprentice, make a masterpiece, open a shop
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because you must be an apprentice before becoming a journeyman, not the other way around.

choices.text: become a master, make a masterpiece, work as a journeyman, open a shop
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the order backwards — you make your masterpiece in order to become a master, not after.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the main idea of the article by comparing life in medieval towns to life on manors?

choices.text: Towns gave people freedom through trade, while manors kept people tied to the land.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows that towns offered freedom through trade and charters, while serfs on manors were legally tied to the land and had no such rights.

choices.text: Towns forced people to work for zero wages, while manors allowed people to earn money.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was only apprentices who earned no wages while learning a trade, and it was serfs on manors — not manor workers — who lacked the freedom to earn their own living.

choices.text: Towns were strictly ruled by master craftsmen, while manors were ruled by the kings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because towns were not ruled by master craftsmen, and manors were controlled by lords, not kings.

choices.text: Towns featured wide roads and large buildings, while manors had small muddy fields.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says towns actually had narrow, muddy streets — not wide roads and large buildings like modern cities.
