assessments.title: The Church at the Center: Religion in Medieval Life

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Church at the Center of Everything

Bells Before Sunrise

A heavy bronze bell rings out across a small village in the year 1200. It is still dark outside. Every person in every house hears it. They know what it means: time to pray. That bell will ring again at noon and again at sunset. From the moment a baby was baptized to the day a person was buried, the Roman Catholic Church shaped almost every part of life in medieval Europe.

You might think people chose to follow the Church the way you might choose a sport or a club. Actually, there was no choice at all. Nearly every baby was baptized within days of being born. From that moment on, you belonged to the Church. You followed its rules. You attended its services. Almost no one imagined doing anything different.
stimulus.content_text: The Church at the Center of EverythingBells Before Sunrise A heavy bronze bell rings out across a small village in the year 1200. It is still dark outside. Every person in every house hears it. They know what it means: time to pray. That bell will ring again at noon and again at sunset. From the moment a baby was baptized to the day a person was buried, the Roman Catholic Church shaped almost every part of life in medieval Europe. You might think people chose to follow the Church the way you might choose a sport or a club. Actually, there was no choice at all. Nearly every baby was baptized within days of being born. From that moment on, you belonged to the Church. You followed its rules. You attended its services. Almost no one imagined doing anything different.

prompt._: According to the text, what was different about belonging to the Church compared with joining a sport or club?

choices.text: People joined the Church only when they were old enough.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says babies were baptized within days of being born — so people didn't wait until they were older to join.

choices.text: People did not have a choice about belonging to the Church.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'there was no choice at all' — babies were baptized and became part of the Church automatically.

choices.text: People chose the Church the same way they chose a club.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — it tells us people did NOT choose the Church the same way they might choose a sport or club.

choices.text: People could leave the Church if they changed their minds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says almost no one imagined doing anything different, meaning leaving the Church wasn't really an option people considered.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Power of the Pope

At the top of the Church stood the Pope, the leader of all Catholics, based in the city of Rome. He did not command armies, yet kings feared him. His power came from something people cared about even more than land or gold — their souls.

The Pope could excommunicate a ruler on religious matters. To excommunicate someone means to cut that person off from the Church completely — like being locked outside a door that everyone else walks through. In 1077, a powerful emperor named Henry IV made the Pope so angry that the Pope excommunicated him. Henry stood barefoot in the snow for three days, begging to be let back in. That is how much the Church's power mattered.
stimulus.content_text: The Power of the Pope At the top of the Church stood the Pope, the leader of all Catholics, based in the city of Rome. He did not command armies, yet kings feared him. His power came from something people cared about even more than land or gold — their souls. The Pope could excommunicate a ruler on religious matters. To excommunicate someone means to cut that person off from the Church completely — like being locked outside a door that everyone else walks through. In 1077, a powerful emperor named Henry IV made the Pope so angry that the Pope excommunicated him. Henry stood barefoot in the snow for three days, begging to be let back in. That is how much the Church's power mattered.

prompt._: Based on the section, what is the main reason kings feared the Pope even though he did not command armies?

choices.text: He could send them to stand in the snow
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Henry chose to stand in the snow himself to show he was sorry; the Pope did not send him there as a punishment.

choices.text: He could replace them with a new ruler
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Pope having the power to replace one ruler with another.

choices.text: He could take away their land and gold
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says people cared about their souls even MORE than land or gold, and it never says the Pope could take those things away.

choices.text: He could cut them off from the Church
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the Pope could excommunicate rulers — cutting them off from the Church — and that this power made even powerful emperors beg to be forgiven.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Building Like No Other

Every village had a parish church — a local church that served the people living nearby, the way your neighborhood school serves your street. It was usually the biggest building in town. Stained glass windows glowed red, blue, and gold when sunlight hit them. Painted walls and carved stone figures told Bible stories to people who could not read. Walking inside felt like stepping into a giant picture book.

People gathered at the parish church for baptisms, weddings, and funerals. Priests gave sermons — speeches that taught lessons from the Bible, like a teacher giving a lesson but about God.
stimulus.content_text: A Building Like No Other Every village had a parish church — a local church that served the people living nearby, the way your neighborhood school serves your street. It was usually the biggest building in town. Stained glass windows glowed red, blue, and gold when sunlight hit them. Painted walls and carved stone figures told Bible stories to people who could not read. Walking inside felt like stepping into a giant picture book. People gathered at the parish church for baptisms, weddings, and funerals. Priests gave sermons — speeches that taught lessons from the Bible, like a teacher giving a lesson but about God.

prompt._: According to the passage, why did medieval churches have painted walls and carved stone figures?

choices.text: to tell Bible stories to people who could not read
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that 'painted walls and carved stone figures told Bible stories to people who could not read.'

choices.text: to help priests practice their sermons each week
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes sermons as speeches by priests, but never says the artwork was used to help priests practice them.

choices.text: to celebrate weddings and baptisms with pictures
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while weddings and baptisms did happen at the church, the passage never says the paintings or carvings were there to celebrate those events.

choices.text: to make the church the biggest building in the village
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the church was the biggest building in town, but that's a separate fact — it has nothing to do with why the walls were painted or the stone figures were carved.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Schools, Hospitals, and Monks

The Church ran the only schools and hospitals in most of Europe. Priests and monks were among the very few people who could read and write. Monks lived together in monasteries — quiet, walled communities where they prayed, copied books by hand, and grew food. Church hospitals cared for the sick and the poor. Without the Church, most people would have had no doctor and no teacher.
stimulus.content_text: Schools, Hospitals, and Monks The Church ran the only schools and hospitals in most of Europe. Priests and monks were among the very few people who could read and write. Monks lived together in monasteries — quiet, walled communities where they prayed, copied books by hand, and grew food. Church hospitals cared for the sick and the poor. Without the Church, most people would have had no doctor and no teacher.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how were priests and monks different from most other people in medieval Europe?

choices.text: They lived in busy, open cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says monks lived in monasteries, which were described as quiet, walled communities — the opposite of busy, open cities.

choices.text: They traveled to help sick people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes monks staying in their monasteries to pray, copy books, and grow food, not traveling to help sick people.

choices.text: They needed doctors and teachers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because priests and monks were the ones providing care through Church hospitals — they were the helpers, not the people needing doctors and teachers.

choices.text: They knew how to read and write.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that priests and monks were among the very few people who could read and write, making them different from most others.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Paying the Tithe

The Church collected a tax called a tithe. A tithe was one-tenth of everything a family earned or grew — imagine giving away one out of every ten apples you picked. This money paid for priests, kept church buildings standing, and helped feed the poor. Some families resented the tithe, meaning they felt angry or bitter about paying it. But most people paid willingly. They believed giving to the Church was a duty to God.
stimulus.content_text: Paying the Tithe The Church collected a tax called a tithe. A tithe was one-tenth of everything a family earned or grew — imagine giving away one out of every ten apples you picked. This money paid for priests, kept church buildings standing, and helped feed the poor. Some families resented the tithe, meaning they felt angry or bitter about paying it. But most people paid willingly. They believed giving to the Church was a duty to God.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'tithe' mean?

choices.text: a tax of one-tenth of earnings
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says a tithe was 'one-tenth of everything a family earned or grew,' which means it was a tax of one-tenth of earnings.

choices.text: a donation to build new churches
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while some tithe money did help keep church buildings standing, the passage never says it was specifically a donation to build new churches.

choices.text: a payment made once a year
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the tithe was paid once a year — it only explains what fraction of earnings it was.

choices.text: a gift given only to the poor
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the tithe wasn't a gift only to the poor — it paid for priests and church buildings too, not just the poor.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Days of Rest and Celebration

Life in medieval Europe was full of hard, exhausting work. But religious holidays gave people a break. Christmas, Easter, and special days honoring saints brought feasts, fairs, and music. The Church gave the calendar its shape. It told people when to work and when to rest.

In medieval Europe, the Church was the school, the hospital, the government, and the gathering place, all at once. For the people of the Middle Ages, that single bell ringing before sunrise was not just a sound. It was the heartbeat of their entire world.
stimulus.content_text: Days of Rest and Celebration Life in medieval Europe was full of hard, exhausting work. But religious holidays gave people a break. Christmas, Easter, and special days honoring saints brought feasts, fairs, and music. The Church gave the calendar its shape. It told people when to work and when to rest. In medieval Europe, the Church was the school, the hospital, the government, and the gathering place, all at once. For the people of the Middle Ages, that single bell ringing before sunrise was not just a sound. It was the heartbeat of their entire world.

prompt._: The author states that religious holidays gave people a break from hard work. Which detail from the passage supports this idea?

choices.text: Special days brought feasts, fairs, and music.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says special days brought feasts, fairs, and music, which are all signs of celebration and rest from hard work.

choices.text: The people did hard and exhausting daily work.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes the hard work people did every day, which is the problem the holidays solved, not the support for the break itself.

choices.text: The Church acted as a school and hospital.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Church acting as a school and hospital is about its role in the community, not about giving people a rest from work.

choices.text: A single bell rang before the sun rose.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the bell ringing before sunrise is about the start of a regular day, not about holidays or taking a break.

prompt._: Based on the article, what was a major difference between priests and most of the people who attended the local church?

choices.text: Priests paid the yearly tithe tax, while most regular people collected the money.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says families paid the tithe tax, and that money was used to pay for priests — so it was the other way around.

choices.text: Priests were able to read and write, while most regular people could not.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article clearly states that priests and monks were among the very few people who could read and write, while most regular people could not.

choices.text: Priests lived far away in Rome, while most regular people stayed in villages.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because priests lived in local village churches called parishes, not in Rome — Rome is where the Pope lived, not regular priests.

choices.text: Priests did hard and exhausting work, while most regular people lived quiet lives.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says regular people's lives were full of 'hard, exhausting work,' not priests — monks and priests spent their time praying, copying books, and caring for the sick.

prompt._: Why did the Church have so much influence over people's everyday lives in medieval Europe?

choices.text: Because the Church provided essential services and shaped daily routines
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows the Church ran schools, hospitals, collected taxes, performed life ceremonies, and shaped the calendar, making it central to everyday life.

choices.text: Because kings collected the tithe and gave the money to Church leaders
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the Church collected the tithe, not kings — the money paid for priests, church buildings, and the poor.

choices.text: Because the Pope commanded large armies that forced people to obey Church rules
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage specifically says the Pope 'did not command armies' — his power came from people's belief in their souls, not military force.

choices.text: Because most people could read the Bible and agreed with Church teachings
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says priests and monks were among the very few people who could read and write, meaning most people could NOT read the Bible.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence best describes how the Church was involved in a person's life from beginning to end?

choices.text: A person chose to join the Church, then was excommunicated, then returned to the Church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says there was 'no choice at all' about joining the Church — people didn't choose to join, and most people were never excommunicated.

choices.text: A person was baptized as a baby, attended services throughout life, then was buried by the Church.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states the Church was involved 'from the moment a baby was baptized to the day a person was buried,' matching this sequence perfectly.

choices.text: A person built a parish church, then became a monk, then rang the bells at sunset.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never describes building a parish church and becoming a monk as steps in a typical person's life journey.

choices.text: A person paid tithes, then attended school, then was baptized at death.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states baptism happened within days of being born, not at death, and the sequence is completely backwards.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how the medieval Church compared to everyday groups and services we know today?

choices.text: It acted as a school, hospital, and government combined rather than just a place of worship.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the Church acted as the school, hospital, government, and gathering place all at once, making it much more than just a place of worship.

choices.text: It functioned like a neighborhood sports club that families could choose to join for fun.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states there was no choice — nearly every baby was baptized and automatically belonged to the Church, so it was nothing like a voluntary sports club.

choices.text: It served as a powerful military army that forced kings and emperors to follow its rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the Pope 'did not command armies,' meaning the Church's power came from people's beliefs about their souls, not from military force.

choices.text: It operated like a giant picture book that only taught villagers how to read and write.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'giant picture book' comparison in the passage only describes the stained glass and painted walls, and the Church actually ran schools rather than just teaching people to read through pictures.

assessments.title: Behind Monastery Walls: Monks, Nuns, and Learning

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Monks Who Saved the World's Books

A Single Page Could Take Days

Behind high stone walls, in a quiet room lit by candles, a monk dipped a quill pen — a writing tool cut from a large bird feather — into dark ink. He pressed it against a sheet of parchment, a smooth material made from animal skin, not paper. Paper as you know it barely existed in medieval Europe, so monks wrote on stretched and dried sheepskin or goatskin instead. One letter at a time, he copied an ancient book. His hand ached. A single page could take an entire day.

Here's the surprising part: most people in the Middle Ages could not read or write. Monks and nuns were some of the only people in Europe who could, making them the keepers of the world's knowledge.
stimulus.content_text: The Monks Who Saved the World's BooksA Single Page Could Take Days Behind high stone walls, in a quiet room lit by candles, a monk dipped a quill pen — a writing tool cut from a large bird feather — into dark ink. He pressed it against a sheet of parchment, a smooth material made from animal skin, not paper. Paper as you know it barely existed in medieval Europe, so monks wrote on stretched and dried sheepskin or goatskin instead. One letter at a time, he copied an ancient book. His hand ached. A single page could take an entire day. Here's the surprising part: most people in the Middle Ages could not read or write. Monks and nuns were some of the only people in Europe who could, making them the keepers of the world's knowledge.

prompt._: According to the text, how were monks and nuns different from most people in the Middle Ages?

choices.text: They were able to read and write
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly states that most people couldn't read or write, but monks and nuns could, making them the keepers of knowledge.

choices.text: They used candles to light their homes
choices.feedback: The passage only mentions candles lighting one monk's room where he worked, not as something special that set monks apart from everyone else.

choices.text: They lived in towns instead of on farms
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about monks living in towns or most people living on farms, so there's no evidence for this.

choices.text: They made paper from animal skin
choices.feedback: This is tricky, but monks wrote *on* parchment made from animal skin — they didn't make paper, and the passage says paper barely existed in medieval Europe.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Prayer

Monks lived in communities called monasteries. Nuns lived in similar communities called convents. You might think monks spent every hour praying. They did pray a lot — seven or eight times a day, starting before sunrise. But between prayers, they farmed land, cooked meals, tended herb gardens, and made medicine from plants. Monasteries were busy, working places — like a school, hospital, farm, and church combined. Travelers could knock on the door for a hot meal and a bed. Poor families came for food. Sick people came for healing.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Prayer Monks lived in communities called monasteries. Nuns lived in similar communities called convents. You might think monks spent every hour praying. They did pray a lot — seven or eight times a day, starting before sunrise. But between prayers, they farmed land, cooked meals, tended herb gardens, and made medicine from plants. Monasteries were busy, working places — like a school, hospital, farm, and church combined. Travelers could knock on the door for a hot meal and a bed. Poor families came for food. Sick people came for healing.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why were monasteries able to help sick people who came for healing?

choices.text: because monks spent every hour studying diseases
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions monks studying diseases — this detail was made up and doesn't appear anywhere in the text.

choices.text: because monks made medicine from plants they grew
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says monks grew herb gardens and made medicine from plants, which is exactly what would help sick people who came for healing.

choices.text: because monks prayed seven or eight times each day
choices.feedback: Praying many times a day was part of the monks' religious routine, but prayer isn't what the passage connects to healing sick people.

choices.text: because monks let travelers sleep in their beds
choices.feedback: Offering beds helped travelers who needed a place to sleep, not sick people who needed medical care.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Room Where Books Were Born

One of the most important rooms in any monastery was the scriptorium — a special writing room, like a workshop just for making books. Monks sat at wooden desks for hours, copying ancient texts by hand. The printing press did not exist yet. Every single copy of every single book had to be written out by a real person.

Some monks added beautiful artwork called illumination — picture a glowing page covered in tiny paintings and bright colors. They pressed thin sheets of real gold, called gold leaf, onto the pages and painted swirling vines, animals, and little scenes. One book could take months or even years to finish.

Many great works by ancient Greek and Roman writers — like Aristotle, Virgil, and Cicero — survived only because monks copied them. Without those aching hands and flickering candles, those words would have vanished.

choices.text: Because ancient writers asked monks to save their work

choices.text: Because the pages needed bright colors and tiny paintings

choices.text: Because the printing press did not exist yet
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly states the printing press didn't exist yet, so every book had to be written out by a real person.

choices.text: Because gold leaf had to be pressed onto each page

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Rule That Brought Order

Around the year AD 530, an Italian monk named Benedict wrote a set of guidelines called the Rule of Saint Benedict. Before his rule, different monasteries ran in different ways, and some were disorganized. Benedict's rule divided each day into clear periods of prayer, work, and rest. It required monks to own nothing, to obey the leader of the monastery (called an abbot), and to stay in one monastery for life. Monasteries across Europe followed this rule for hundreds of years.
stimulus.content_text: The Rule That Brought Order Around the year AD 530, an Italian monk named Benedict wrote a set of guidelines called the Rule of Saint Benedict. Before his rule, different monasteries ran in different ways, and some were disorganized. Benedict's rule divided each day into clear periods of prayer, work, and rest. It required monks to own nothing, to obey the leader of the monastery (called an abbot), and to stay in one monastery for life. Monasteries across Europe followed this rule for hundreds of years.

prompt._: How were monasteries across Europe most alike after they followed Benedict's rule?

choices.text: They all moved from monastery to monastery.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Benedict's rule required monks to stay in one monastery for life, which is the opposite of moving around.

choices.text: They all chose a new abbot each day.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says abbots were chosen each day — monks simply had to obey whoever the abbot was.

choices.text: They all followed the same daily order.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Benedict's rule gave every monastery the same clear daily schedule of prayer, work, and rest, and monasteries across Europe followed it for hundreds of years.

choices.text: They all had different daily plans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because having different daily plans describes how monasteries worked *before* Benedict's rule, not after they followed it.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Nuns Who Led and Learned

Nuns followed a similar schedule of prayer, work, and study. Even though women had fewer chances in medieval society, convents gave them something rare — a place to read, write, and lead. One famous nun, Hildegard of Bingen, became a brilliant writer, musician, and scientist. She wrote books about medicine, nature, and music that people still study today.

choices.text: A famous writer, musician, and scientist
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a famous writer, musician, and scientist describes Hildegard of Bingen herself, not what a convent is.

choices.text: A place to read, write, and lead
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the phrase 'a place to read, write, and lead' right after mentioning convents, which tells us exactly what a convent is.

choices.text: A daily schedule of prayer and study
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a daily schedule of prayer and study describes how nuns spent their time, not the meaning of the word convent.

choices.text: A book about nature and medicine
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a book about nature and medicine describes what Hildegard of Bingen wrote, not what a convent is.

stimulus.content_html:
Words That Outlasted Empires

Kingdoms rose and fell. Wars burned villages to the ground. But inside monastery walls, the books survived. The next time you open a book — any book — think about this: someone, somewhere, once sat by candlelight and copied it by hand so that you could read it.

assessments.title: Charlemagne: The Father of Europe

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Charlemagne: The King Who Became Emperor

A Giant of a Man

A tall man with a thick neck and piercing eyes sat up in bed, reaching for the book on his pillow. He was trying, again, to teach himself to write. His fingers could grip a sword with ease. But holding a pen? That was harder. This man was Charlemagne, ruler of the largest empire Europe had seen in over three hundred years. His name means "Charles the Great," and he earned it.

Charlemagne was king of the Franks. The Franks were a Germanic people — meaning they originally came from the same region as many German-speaking groups. You might think the Franks were French, since France is named after them. Actually, the Franks lived across a wide area that today includes parts of France, Germany, and beyond. Their kingdom was not the same as any single country on a modern map.
stimulus.content_text: Charlemagne: The King Who Became EmperorA Giant of a Man A tall man with a thick neck and piercing eyes sat up in bed, reaching for the book on his pillow. He was trying, again, to teach himself to write. His fingers could grip a sword with ease. But holding a pen? That was harder. This man was Charlemagne, ruler of the largest empire Europe had seen in over three hundred years. His name means "Charles the Great," and he earned it. Charlemagne was king of the Franks. The Franks were a Germanic people — meaning they originally came from the same region as many German-speaking groups. You might think the Franks were French, since France is named after them. Actually, the Franks lived across a wide area that today includes parts of France, Germany, and beyond. Their kingdom was not the same as any single country on a modern map.

prompt._: According to the text, what is true about the Franks, even though France is named after them?

choices.text: They lived across parts of many countries.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the Franks lived across a wide area that includes parts of France, Germany, and more.

choices.text: They ruled the largest empire in the world.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Charlemagne ruled the largest empire Europe had seen in 300 years, not that the Franks ruled the largest empire in the world.

choices.text: They lived only in the country of Germany.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Franks lived across parts of France, Germany, and beyond — not only in Germany.

choices.text: They spoke many different modern languages.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about the Franks speaking many different modern languages.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Building an Empire

Charlemagne was born around AD 742 and became king in AD 768. He was strong, full of energy, and a brilliant military leader. He believed God had chosen him to spread Christianity and bring order to Europe. Through years of military campaigns — long, planned wars fought in different places — he conquered neighboring kingdoms in Italy, Germany, Austria, and beyond.

By the end of his reign — the time a ruler holds power — his empire stretched from the Atlantic Ocean to central Europe. It reached from the North Sea down into central Italy.

He believed God had chosen him to spread Christianity and bring order to Europe. Through years of military campaigns — long, planned wars fought in different places — he conquered neighboring kingdoms in Italy, Germany, Austria, and beyond. By the end of his reign — the time a ruler holds power — his empire stretched from the Atlantic Ocean to central Europe. It reached from the North Sea down into central Italy.

He believed God had chosen him to spread Christianity and bring order to Europe. Through years of military campaigns — long, planned wars fought in different places — he conquered neighboring kingdoms in Italy, Germany, Austria, and beyond. By the end of his reign — the time a ruler holds power — his empire stretched from the Atlantic Ocean to central Europe. It reached from the North Sea down into central Italy.' Which sentence best summarizes the main idea of this passage?

choices.text: Charlemagne's strong religious beliefs led him to conquer lands and build a large empire.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it combines both key ideas from the passage: Charlemagne's religious beliefs and his military conquests that built a large empire.

choices.text: Charlemagne's birth in the year AD 742 led him to become a brilliant military leader.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the year Charlemagne was born didn't cause him to become a military leader — the passage never makes that connection.

choices.text: Charlemagne's long time holding power led him to travel from the Atlantic Ocean to Italy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only mentions details about where his empire reached, and makes it sound like he was traveling instead of building an empire through conquest.

choices.text: Charlemagne's planned military campaigns led him to be chosen to spread his religion.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the cause and effect — the passage says he was chosen to spread religion first, and then launched military campaigns, not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Crown That Meant More Than Gold

On Christmas Day in AD 800, Charlemagne stood inside St. Peter's Basilica in Rome — a basilica is a grand, important church. He had come to help Pope Leo III, the leader of the Christian Church, who was in danger from powerful enemies. During the service, the Pope placed a golden crown on Charlemagne's head and declared him Emperor of the Romans.

Now, Charlemagne was already a king. So why did this matter? A king rules one kingdom. An emperor rules over many kingdoms and peoples. No one had held the title of Roman Emperor in western Europe for over three hundred years. This moment tied Charlemagne's power to the memory of ancient Rome and gave him the blessing of the Church.
stimulus.content_text: A Crown That Meant More Than Gold On Christmas Day in AD 800, Charlemagne stood inside St. Peter's Basilica in Rome — a basilica is a grand, important church. He had come to help Pope Leo III, the leader of the Christian Church, who was in danger from powerful enemies. During the service, the Pope placed a golden crown on Charlemagne's head and declared him Emperor of the Romans. Now, Charlemagne was already a king. So why did this matter? A king rules one kingdom. An emperor rules over many kingdoms and peoples. No one had held the title of Roman Emperor in western Europe for over three hundred years. This moment tied Charlemagne's power to the memory of ancient Rome and gave him the blessing of the Church.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the Pope do right after placing a golden crown on Charlemagne's head?

choices.text: He led Charlemagne inside St. Peter's grand basilica.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Charlemagne was already inside St. Peter's Basilica when the crowning happened — the Pope didn't lead him inside afterward.

choices.text: He declared Charlemagne the Emperor of the Romans.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the Pope placed the crown on Charlemagne's head AND declared him Emperor of the Romans — those two actions happened together.

choices.text: He asked Charlemagne to fight his powerful enemies.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Charlemagne came to Rome to help the Pope fight his enemies — the Pope didn't ask Charlemagne to fight after the crowning.

choices.text: He named Charlemagne the king of a single kingdom.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Pope declared Charlemagne an emperor, not a king of one kingdom — the passage even explains that an emperor rules over many kingdoms.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Schools, Books, and a Love of Learning

People sometimes call the centuries after Rome's fall the "Dark Ages." But learning never disappeared completely. Monks in monasteries — quiet communities where religious men lived, worked, and prayed together — had been copying books and studying for generations. Charlemagne built on their work. He noticed that many priests could barely read. This troubled him deeply.

So he opened schools in churches and monasteries across his empire. He invited the best scholars to his court. He ordered monks to copy ancient books and teach Latin, reading, writing, and arithmetic. His legacy — the lasting impact a person leaves behind, like footprints pressed into wet cement — earned him the nickname "the Father of Europe."
stimulus.content_text: Schools, Books, and a Love of Learning People sometimes call the centuries after Rome's fall the "Dark Ages." But learning never disappeared completely. Monks in monasteries — quiet communities where religious men lived, worked, and prayed together — had been copying books and studying for generations. Charlemagne built on their work. He noticed that many priests could barely read. This troubled him deeply. So he opened schools in churches and monasteries across his empire. He invited the best scholars to his court. He ordered monks to copy ancient books and teach Latin, reading, writing, and arithmetic. His legacy — the lasting impact a person leaves behind, like footprints pressed into wet cement — earned him the nickname "the Father of Europe."

prompt._: The author compares a "legacy" to "footprints pressed into wet cement" to show that a legacy is something that...

choices.text: stays behind for a long time
choices.feedback: This is correct because both the definition ('lasting impact') and the cement image show something that stays behind for a long time.

choices.text: helps workers build new roads
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the cement is just used as a picture to explain what 'lasting' means — the passage isn't actually talking about building roads.

choices.text: leads people to a new place
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because footprints in cement show something left behind, not a path that guides people somewhere new.

choices.text: requires a lot of hard work
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the footprint image is about something that stays, not about how much effort it took to make it.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
An Empire Falls, but Ideas Survive

Charlemagne died in AD 814. His empire did not last long after that. His grandsons fought over it, split it apart, and within a few decades, the great empire was gone.

But here is what survived: the schools kept teaching. The copied books preserved ancient knowledge that might have been lost forever. And Charlemagne's idea — that a ruler should care about education, not just war — echoed through the centuries. Today, every time you sit in a classroom and open a book, you are doing something Charlemagne fought to make possible. He never fully mastered writing. But he made sure millions of people after him could.

His grandsons fought over it, split it apart, and within a few decades, the great empire was gone. But here is what survived: the schools kept teaching. The copied books preserved ancient knowledge that might have been lost forever. And Charlemagne's idea — that a ruler should care about education, not just war — echoed through the centuries. Today, every time you sit in a classroom and open a book, you are doing something Charlemagne fought to make possible. He never fully mastered writing. But he made sure millions of people after him could.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the idea continued without stopping, not that it was forgotten and later remembered.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'echoed' here is used figuratively — the passage is talking about an idea, not a real sound bouncing off walls.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Charlemagne already ruled a large, powerful empire before he was crowned emperor — not a small kingdom.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says schools kept teaching and books were preserved even after Charlemagne's empire fell apart.

assessments.title: 1066: The Norman Conquest of England

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Invasion That Remade England

A King Falls, and Rivals Rise

Hundreds of wooden ships rocked in the cold waves of the English Channel. Horses stamped nervously on the decks. Thousands of soldiers gripped their weapons and stared at the gray coast of England. It was the fall of 1066, and William, Duke of Normandy, had come to take a kingdom.

The trouble started months earlier. In January 1066, King Edward of England died without an heir — a person next in line to receive the throne, like the oldest child who inherits a family farm. Several powerful men wanted to be king. Harold Godwinson, an English nobleman — a lord who owned land and had power — was crowned first. But across the water in Normandy, a region in northern France, William was furious.

William believed Edward had promised him the throne years before. He also said Harold had sworn an oath, a serious promise, to support William's claim. When Harold took the crown anyway, William decided to fight for it.
stimulus.content_text: The Invasion That Remade EnglandA King Falls, and Rivals Rise Hundreds of wooden ships rocked in the cold waves of the English Channel. Horses stamped nervously on the decks. Thousands of soldiers gripped their weapons and stared at the gray coast of England. It was the fall of 1066, and William, Duke of Normandy, had come to take a kingdom. The trouble started months earlier. In January 1066, King Edward of England died without an heir — a person next in line to receive the throne, like the oldest child who inherits a family farm. Several powerful men wanted to be king. Harold Godwinson, an English nobleman — a lord who owned land and had power — was crowned first. But across the water in Normandy, a region in northern France, William was furious. William believed Edward had promised him the throne years before. He also said Harold had sworn an oath, a serious promise, to support William's claim. When Harold took the crown anyway, William decided to fight for it.

prompt._: According to the text, how did Harold Godwinson's actions differ from what William believed Harold had promised to do?

choices.text: Harold took the crown instead of supporting William's claim
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Harold swore an oath to support William's claim, but then Harold took the crown for himself instead.

choices.text: Harold asked King Edward to choose a different heir
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Harold asking King Edward to choose a different heir.

choices.text: Harold refused to travel to Normandy to meet with William
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about Harold refusing to travel to Normandy.

choices.text: Harold sent his soldiers to attack William's ships at sea
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Harold attacked William's ships — it was actually William who sailed his ships toward England.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Battle of Hastings

William gathered a huge army. You might think his soldiers all came from one country, the way a modern army does. Actually, soldiers in 1066 were loyal to individual lords, not to nations. William's fleet — a large group of ships sailing together — crossed the Channel and landed on England's southern coast. Harold rushed south to meet him.

On October 14, 1066, the two armies clashed near the town of Hastings. Harold's soldiers held the top of a hill behind a wall of shields. William's Norman knights charged uphill again and again on horseback. The fighting lasted all day. Near the end, Harold was killed. Without their king, the English army broke apart and fled.
stimulus.content_text: The Battle of Hastings William gathered a huge army. You might think his soldiers all came from one country, the way a modern army does. Actually, soldiers in 1066 were loyal to individual lords, not to nations. William's fleet — a large group of ships sailing together — crossed the Channel and landed on England's southern coast. Harold rushed south to meet him. On October 14, 1066, the two armies clashed near the town of Hastings. Harold's soldiers held the top of a hill behind a wall of shields. William's Norman knights charged uphill again and again on horseback. The fighting lasted all day. Near the end, Harold was killed. Without their king, the English army broke apart and fled.

prompt._: In the section 'The Battle of Hastings,' which detail best supports the main idea that the English army lost the battle because of what happened to their leader?

choices.text: The Norman knights charged uphill again and again.
choices.feedback: This tells us what William's soldiers did during the battle, but it doesn't explain why the English army lost because of their leader.

choices.text: The English army broke apart without their king.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly shows that Harold being killed caused the English army to break apart and flee, which is exactly why they lost.

choices.text: The fighting between the two armies lasted all day.
choices.feedback: This just tells us the battle was long, but it doesn't connect the loss to anything that happened to Harold, the English leader.

choices.text: The two armies clashed near the town of Hastings.
choices.feedback: This only tells us the location of the battle, which has nothing to do with why the English army lost.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
William the Conqueror Takes Control

William was crowned King of England on Christmas Day, 1066. People call him William the Conqueror. He built castles all across England. You might picture these as fancy palaces with golden rooms. But they were nothing like that. William's castles were fortresses with thick walls, built to watch over the English people and stop rebellions — uprisings where people fight back against a ruler.

William took land away from English lords and gave it to his Norman followers. A new ruling class took power, and a new system of government took root. He also ordered a great survey of all the property in England — a careful count of everything people owned, like a teacher taking attendance of every farm, cow, and field. This record was called the Domesday Book. It told William exactly what his new kingdom was worth.
stimulus.content_text: William the Conqueror Takes Control William was crowned King of England on Christmas Day, 1066. People call him William the Conqueror. He built castles all across England. You might picture these as fancy palaces with golden rooms. But they were nothing like that. William's castles were fortresses with thick walls, built to watch over the English people and stop rebellions — uprisings where people fight back against a ruler. William took land away from English lords and gave it to his Norman followers. A new ruling class took power, and a new system of government took root. He also ordered a great survey of all the property in England — a careful count of everything people owned, like a teacher taking attendance of every farm, cow, and field. This record was called the Domesday Book. It told William exactly what his new kingdom was worth.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after William took land away from English lords?

choices.text: English lords gave land to William.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the story backwards — William took land away from English lords, not the other way around.

choices.text: He was crowned King of England.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because William was crowned king on Christmas Day 1066, which happened before he took land from the English lords.

choices.text: A new ruling class took power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that after William took land from English lords and gave it to his Norman followers, 'a new ruling class took power.'

choices.text: He built castles across England.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because building castles is mentioned earlier in the passage and is not described as something that happened as a result of taking the land.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Story Stitched in Thread

One astonishing record of these events still survives. The Bayeux Embroidery is a cloth 230 feet long. Artists embroidered it, meaning they sewed pictures and Latin words into the fabric with colored thread. It shows Harold's oath, William's ships, the battle, and Harold's death. It is one of the most important artifacts — objects made by people long ago — from the entire Middle Ages.
stimulus.content_text: A Story Stitched in Thread One astonishing record of these events still survives. The Bayeux Embroidery is a cloth 230 feet long. Artists embroidered it, meaning they sewed pictures and Latin words into the fabric with colored thread. It shows Harold's oath, William's ships, the battle, and Harold's death. It is one of the most important artifacts — objects made by people long ago — from the entire Middle Ages.

prompt._: Based on the text, how are the pictures and the Latin words on the Bayeux Embroidery similar?

choices.text: Both were made by King Harold before the battle.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says King Harold made the embroidery — it says artists made it.

choices.text: Both were found on many different Middle Ages artifacts.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage only talks about the Bayeux Embroidery, not about pictures and words appearing on many other artifacts.

choices.text: Both were used to teach people how to embroider.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about teaching people how to embroider.

choices.text: Both were sewn into the fabric with colored thread.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says artists sewed both the pictures and the Latin words into the fabric with colored thread.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Two Languages Become One

The Norman rulers spoke French. You might think English disappeared after the conquest. Here's the surprising part: ordinary English people kept right on speaking English. The two languages existed side by side for hundreds of years. Slowly, French words mixed in. Words you use every day — "beef," "court," "justice," "government" — all came from French. Over centuries, the blending created the English you speak right now.

Next time you say the word "justice" or walk past a stone castle, you are touching something that began with those wooden ships rocking in the Channel nearly a thousand years ago. The Norman Conquest didn't just change who wore the crown. It changed the very words in your mouth.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the phrase 'the two languages existed side by side' mean?

choices.text: French and English were written in the same alphabet
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions alphabets or how the languages were written down.

choices.text: French and English were both spoken at the same time
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says English people kept speaking English while the Norman rulers spoke French, meaning both languages were used at the same time.

choices.text: French and English combined into one new language right away
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the blending happened slowly over centuries, not all at once.

choices.text: French replaced English as the main language people used
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ordinary people 'kept right on speaking English,' so English was not replaced.

prompt._: According to the article, how was the ruler of England in January 1066 different from the ruler on Christmas Day 1066?

choices.text: The January ruler was a Norman duke, but the Christmas ruler was an English nobleman.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two rulers switched — Harold the English nobleman ruled in January, and William the Norman duke ruled on Christmas Day, not the other way around.

choices.text: The January ruler was an English soldier, but the Christmas ruler was a French knight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Harold is described as a nobleman, not just a soldier, and William is called a duke, not simply a French knight.

choices.text: The January ruler was an English nobleman, but the Christmas ruler was a Norman duke.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states Harold Godwinson, an English nobleman, was crowned first in January 1066, and then William, the Duke of Normandy, became king on Christmas Day 1066.

choices.text: The January ruler was a French artist, but the Christmas ruler was an English farmer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither ruler was a French artist or an English farmer — the article never describes either man that way.

prompt._: According to the article, why do people today use French words like "beef" and "justice" when speaking English?

choices.text: Because King Edward died without leaving an heir to rule.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because King Edward dying without an heir caused the fight over who would be king, not the mixing of French and English words.

choices.text: Because English people decided to stop speaking their own language.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that ordinary English people kept right on speaking English — they never gave up their own language.

choices.text: Because artists sewed Latin words into a long historical cloth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Bayeux Embroidery is about a historical record stitched in thread, and it has nothing to do with how French words became part of the English language.

choices.text: Because Norman rulers took power in England and spoke French.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly says Norman rulers spoke French, and their language slowly blended into English over hundreds of years, creating words we still use today.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information about the Norman Conquest across the first three sections of the article?

choices.text: By describing the events of the Norman Conquest in the order they happened.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the first three sections follow a clear timeline: Edward's death and the rivalry, the battle in October 1066, and then William being crowned on Christmas Day 1066.

choices.text: By comparing the English and Norman armies to show which was much stronger.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article describes both armies briefly but never compares them to argue which one was stronger.

choices.text: By listing the changes William made to the country from greatest to least.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article doesn't rank William's changes from greatest to least — it describes them as part of the story of what he did after taking power.

choices.text: By explaining the problem of having no king and offering several different solutions.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article doesn't offer different solutions to the problem of no king — it simply tells what happened after Edward died.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the section about William taking control with the main idea of the section about two languages becoming one?

choices.text: One shows William changing where battles were fought, while the other shows the conquest changing what people wore.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither section talks about where battles were fought or what people wore.

choices.text: One shows William changing who ruled England, while the other shows the conquest changing how people spoke.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the first section is about William changing who ruled England, and the second section is about how the conquest changed the words people speak — both supported by specific details in the passage.

choices.text: One shows William changing how the Bayeux Embroidery was made, while the other shows the conquest changing castle walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Bayeux Embroidery is discussed in a completely different section, not in the two sections this question is asking about.

choices.text: One shows William changing why Harold wanted the crown, while the other shows the conquest changing ship travel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither section talks about why Harold wanted the crown or how ship travel changed.

assessments.title: Life on the Land: What It Was Like to Be a Medieval Peasant

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
A Day in the Life of a Medieval Peasant

Waking Up and Eating Breakfast in a Peasant Home

Smoke stings your eyes. You cough and roll off a straw mattress onto a cold dirt floor. A fire glows in the center of your one-room house, but there is no chimney. The smoke just drifts up through a thatched roof — a roof woven from dried straw, layered like a thick, scratchy blanket over your head. There are no windows. On the other side of a thin wall, your family's cow breathes heavily in the dark. She sleeps in the same building you do. Her body heat helps warm the house.

This is morning for a peasant child in the Middle Ages, roughly 800 years ago.

Your mother hands you a chunk of dark bread and a cup of ale — a mild drink made from grain, like a flat, bitter juice. There is no sugar. No tea. No oranges or bananas shipped from faraway places. For the main meal later, she stirs a thick porridge of grain and vegetables over the fire. Meat is rare. Your family might taste it at Christmas or Easter, but almost never on an ordinary day.
stimulus.content_text: A Day in the Life of a Medieval PeasantWaking Up and Eating Breakfast in a Peasant Home Smoke stings your eyes. You cough and roll off a straw mattress onto a cold dirt floor. A fire glows in the center of your one-room house, but there is no chimney. The smoke just drifts up through a thatched roof — a roof woven from dried straw, layered like a thick, scratchy blanket over your head. There are no windows. On the other side of a thin wall, your family's cow breathes heavily in the dark. She sleeps in the same building you do. Her body heat helps warm the house. This is morning for a peasant child in the Middle Ages, roughly 800 years ago. Your mother hands you a chunk of dark bread and a cup of ale — a mild drink made from grain, like a flat, bitter juice. There is no sugar. No tea. No oranges or bananas shipped from faraway places. For the main meal later, she stirs a thick porridge of grain and vegetables over the fire. Meat is rare. Your family might taste it at Christmas or Easter, but almost never on an ordinary day.

prompt._: According to the text, how was meat different from other foods for a peasant family?

choices.text: It was eaten only at special times, not on ordinary days.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states meat was rare and only eaten at special times like Christmas or Easter, not on ordinary days.

choices.text: It was cooked in a porridge with grain and vegetables every day.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the porridge was made from grain and vegetables, not meat.

choices.text: It was served at breakfast instead of at the main meal.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because breakfast in the passage was dark bread and ale — meat is never mentioned as a breakfast food.

choices.text: It was shipped from faraway places unlike bread and ale.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says oranges and bananas were shipped from faraway places, not meat.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Working the Fields and the Role of Peasants in Medieval Society

After eating, you head outside. Your father is already guiding a wooden plow behind the family ox. You scatter seeds across the freshly turned soil. Your mother bends over the small garden near your house, pulling weeds around rows of onions, beans, and cabbages.

You might think peasants were slaves who had no choices at all. Actually, peasant families were not slaves. They owed work and a share of their crops to a lord — a wealthy landowner who controlled the village. But peasants kept their own homes and raised their own children. They were the largest group in medieval society, doing the work that fed everyone.
stimulus.content_text: Working the Fields and the Role of Peasants in Medieval Society After eating, you head outside. Your father is already guiding a wooden plow behind the family ox. You scatter seeds across the freshly turned soil. Your mother bends over the small garden near your house, pulling weeds around rows of onions, beans, and cabbages. You might think peasants were slaves who had no choices at all. Actually, peasant families were not slaves. They owed work and a share of their crops to a lord — a wealthy landowner who controlled the village. But peasants kept their own homes and raised their own children. They were the largest group in medieval society, doing the work that fed everyone.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why were peasants the most important group for keeping medieval society running?

choices.text: because they did the work that fed everyone
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says peasants were 'the largest group in medieval society, doing the work that fed everyone.'

choices.text: because they owned the land and controlled the villages
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the lord was the wealthy landowner who controlled the village, not the peasants.

choices.text: because they told the lord what crops to grow
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions peasants giving instructions to the lord; the lord was the one in charge.

choices.text: because they were forced to work as slaves
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage specifically says peasants were NOT slaves — they kept their own homes and raised their own children.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Three-Field System and the Changing Seasons

Look out across the village fields, and you see something interesting. The land is split into three big sections. One grows wheat or rye. Another grows barley or oats. The third sits empty. That empty field is fallow — resting, the way your legs rest after a long run. Each year, the crops rotate to a different field. The field that grew wheat last year grows barley this year. The field that grew barley goes fallow.

It's easy to think medieval people weren't very smart because they had no machines or computers. But this three-field system was a brilliant invention. Without any modern science, farmers figured out that soil gets tired, just like people do. Letting a field rest brought back its strength.

Work fills every season. Spring means plowing and planting. Summer means weeding under a hot sun. Autumn is the busiest time — every family rushes to harvest crops before cold weather ruins them. Winter is quieter, but there are still animals to feed, fences to mend, and tools to repair. Your hands grow rough and calloused — hardened with thick, tough skin, like the sole of an old shoe.
stimulus.content_text: The Three-Field System and the Changing Seasons Look out across the village fields, and you see something interesting. The land is split into three big sections. One grows wheat or rye. Another grows barley or oats. The third sits empty. That empty field is fallow — resting, the way your legs rest after a long run. Each year, the crops rotate to a different field. The field that grew wheat last year grows barley this year. The field that grew barley goes fallow. It's easy to think medieval people weren't very smart because they had no machines or computers. But this three-field system was a brilliant invention. Without any modern science, farmers figured out that soil gets tired, just like people do. Letting a field rest brought back its strength. Work fills every season. Spring means plowing and planting. Summer means weeding under a hot sun. Autumn is the busiest time — every family rushes to harvest crops before cold weather ruins them. Winter is quieter, but there are still animals to feed, fences to mend, and tools to repair. Your hands grow rough and calloused — hardened with thick, tough skin, like the sole of an old shoe.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens to the field after it grows barley?

choices.text: It grows wheat next year.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the field that grew wheat grows barley next — not the other way around after barley.

choices.text: It stays empty forever.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because fallow means the field is resting for one year, not staying empty forever.

choices.text: It goes fallow next year.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states, 'The field that grew barley goes fallow.'

choices.text: It grows oats next year.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oats and barley are grown in the same field together, not one after the other.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Holidays, Community, and Why Their Story Still Matters

Religious holidays saved the year from being nothing but work. On those special days, the whole village stopped. Families walked to the stone church at the center of the village. The priest led prayers and services. Afterward, neighbors shared better-than-usual meals, played games, and danced together. Christmas and Easter were the biggest celebrations of all.

The church was more than a place to pray. It was where people gathered and felt like a community. Each family owed a tithe — a payment of one-tenth of their crops, like sharing one slice of every ten you baked.

Next time you eat a piece of bread, think about this: someone planted that grain, harvested it, and ground it into flour. Eight hundred years ago, a peasant child your age did that same work with bare, calloused hands. The food on your table still starts in a field. The difference is who bends down to do the work — and what they get in return.

Families walked to the stone church at the center of the village. The priest led prayers and services. Afterward, neighbors shared better-than-usual meals, played games, and danced together. Christmas and Easter were the biggest celebrations of all. The church was more than a place to pray. It was where people gathered and felt like a community. Each family owed a tithe — a payment of one-tenth of their crops, like sharing one slice of every ten you baked. Next time you eat a piece of bread, think about this: someone planted that grain, harvested it, and ground it into flour. Eight hundred years ago, a peasant child your age did that same work with bare, calloused hands. The food on your table still starts in a field. The difference is who bends down to do the work — and what they get in return.

prompt._: Based on the comparison in the passage, what does the phrase "like sharing one slice of every ten you baked" help the reader understand about a tithe?

choices.text: It was a meal that neighbors divided into ten equal pieces.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the bread slice comparison is used to explain a payment amount, not a meal that neighbors split into pieces.

choices.text: It was a celebration that happened one time in ten days.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says a tithe had anything to do with a ten-day schedule or a celebration happening every ten days.

choices.text: It was a payment that took one part out of every ten.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says a tithe is one-tenth of crops, and the bread slice comparison shows exactly that — one out of every ten parts.

choices.text: It was a gift that families could choose to give or keep.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says families 'owed' a tithe, meaning it was required — not something they could choose to give or keep.

prompt._: How does the author contrast what people might assume about medieval peasants with what was actually true?

choices.text: by correcting wrong ideas that peasants were slaves or unintelligent
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author directly states two wrong assumptions — that peasants were slaves and that they weren't smart — and then uses evidence from the passage to show the truth.

choices.text: by proving that peasants had machines but chose not to use them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says peasants had machines — in fact, it points out they had no machines or computers at all.

choices.text: by explaining that peasants ate meat every day but preferred bread
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says meat was rare and peasants almost never ate it on ordinary days, so there's no contrast about eating meat every day.

choices.text: by showing peasants worked only in summer but actually rested in winter
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says people assumed peasants only worked in summer — it actually says work filled every season, including winter.

prompt._: How does the author support the main idea that medieval peasants lived a very difficult life?

choices.text: By describing how the freezing winter weather caused them to lose their harvested crops every single year.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says winter was 'quieter,' not that cold weather destroyed their crops every single year.

choices.text: By explaining how a lack of machines and duties to a lord caused them to work constantly.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly shows that having no machines meant constant hand labor every season, and owing work and crops to a lord added even more to their burden.

choices.text: By detailing how the smoky, windowless houses caused them to become too sick to farm the land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while the passage does describe smoky, windowless houses, it never says the smoke made peasants too sick to farm.

choices.text: By showing how their strict status as slaves caused them to lose their homes and their children.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually tells us peasants were NOT slaves — they kept their own homes and raised their own children.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the correct order of events in a peasant child's morning?

choices.text: Wake up in a smoky house, eat dark bread, and scatter seeds in the field.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly shows waking up in the smoky house first, then eating dark bread, then going outside to scatter seeds, in that exact order.

choices.text: Mend broken fences, wake up in a smoky house, and eat dark bread.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because mending fences is a winter chore mentioned much later in the article, not something that happens in the peasant child's morning.

choices.text: Feed the family cow, stir a thick porridge, and pull weeds in the garden.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the cow is never fed in the morning section, stirring porridge is described as something for the main meal later in the day, and pulling weeds is the mother's job, not the child's morning task.

choices.text: Scatter seeds in the field, eat dark bread, and guide a wooden plow.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it puts scattering seeds before eating breakfast, but the passage says the child eats first and then heads outside to work.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is a fallow field similar to a peasant family on a religious holiday?

choices.text: Both move to a new section of the farmland.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is the crops that rotate to different fields each year, not the peasant families moving to a new section of land.

choices.text: Both take a rest from their usual hard work.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says a fallow field is 'resting' and that religious holidays let the whole village stop working — both get a break from hard work.

choices.text: Both grow extra food for the village lord.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a fallow field grows no crops at all, and religious holidays are about rest and celebration, not producing extra food for the lord.

choices.text: Both give a share of crops to the local church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because paying a tithe (one-tenth of crops) to the church is something peasant families do, and it has nothing to do with what a fallow field is.
