assessments.title: The Prophet Muhammad and the Birth of Islam

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Medieval Islamic Civilization

A Cave in the Mountains

A man sat alone inside a dark cave on a rocky mountain near the city of Mecca. His name was Muhammad, and he was about forty years old. It was around the year AD 610. According to Muslim belief, the angel Gabriel appeared to him there and began sharing God's words. That moment in the cave would give birth to Islam, one of the world's great religions. The word "Islam" means "submission to God," and "Muslim" means "one who submits." Today, nearly two billion people follow this faith.
stimulus.content_text: Medieval Islamic CivilizationA Cave in the Mountains A man sat alone inside a dark cave on a rocky mountain near the city of Mecca. His name was Muhammad, and he was about forty years old. It was around the year AD 610. According to Muslim belief, the angel Gabriel appeared to him there and began sharing God's words. That moment in the cave would give birth to Islam, one of the world's great religions. The word "Islam" means "submission to God," and "Muslim" means "one who submits." Today, nearly two billion people follow this faith.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the difference between the meaning of the word "Islam" and the word "Muslim"?

choices.text: "Islam" means submission to God, while "Muslim" means one who submits.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage states exactly this: the word Islam means submission to God, and Muslim means one who submits. You can find this information near the end of the passage.

choices.text: "Islam" means God's spoken words, while "Muslim" means the angel Gabriel.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. The passage never says Islam means spoken words or that Muslim means the angel Gabriel. Those details appear in the story but are not the meanings of the words.

choices.text: "Islam" means the city of Mecca, while "Muslim" means a man in a cave.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. The passage never says Islam means the city of Mecca or that Muslim means a man in a cave. Those are details from the story, not the meanings of the words.

choices.text: "Islam" means one who submits, while "Muslim" means submission to God.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. This answer has the two meanings switched around. The passage says Islam means submission to God and Muslim means one who submits, not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Growing Up in Mecca

Muhammad was born around AD 570 in Mecca, a busy trading city on the Arabian Peninsula — a piece of land surrounded by water on three sides, like a thumb sticking out into the ocean. This peninsula is in the area we now call Saudi Arabia. Muhammad's parents died when he was young. His grandfather and then his uncle raised him. He grew up to be a merchant, a person who buys and sells goods for a living, like a shopkeeper who travels from town to town. People trusted him so much they called him "al-Amin," meaning "the trustworthy one."
stimulus.content_text: Growing Up in Mecca Muhammad was born around AD 570 in Mecca, a busy trading city on the Arabian Peninsula — a piece of land surrounded by water on three sides, like a thumb sticking out into the ocean. This peninsula is in the area we now call Saudi Arabia. Muhammad's parents died when he was young. His grandfather and then his uncle raised him. He grew up to be a merchant, a person who buys and sells goods for a living, like a shopkeeper who travels from town to town. People trusted him so much they called him "al-Amin," meaning "the trustworthy one."

prompt._: Based on the section about Muhammad growing up in Mecca, which key detail best supports the idea that Muhammad's honest character caused people to respect him?

choices.text: He was born around AD 570 in a busy trading city.
choices.feedback: This tells us when and where Muhammad was born, but it says nothing about his honest character or why people respected him.

choices.text: He grew up to be a merchant who traveled to trade.
choices.feedback: This tells us Muhammad's job, but just being a traveling merchant doesn't explain why people respected his honest character.

choices.text: His grandfather and then his uncle raised him.
choices.feedback: This tells us who raised Muhammad after his parents died, but it doesn't give us any clue about his honesty or character.

choices.text: People trusted him and called him 'the trustworthy one.'
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says people trusted him so much they called him 'the trustworthy one,' which directly shows his honest character earned their respect.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A New Message

Muslims believe the angel Gabriel's visits continued for many years. The messages Muhammad received were later written down in the Quran, the holy book of Islam. The Quran contains prayers, rules for daily life, and stories about earlier prophets — people chosen by God to share His messages, like messengers carrying important letters.

Islam is a monotheistic religion. That means Muslims worship only one God, called Allah in Arabic. You might think "Allah" means a different God from the one in Christianity or Judaism. Actually, "Allah" is simply the Arabic word for God — the same God. Muhammad taught that he was the last in a long line of prophets that included Abraham, Moses, and Jesus.
stimulus.content_text: A New Message Muslims believe the angel Gabriel's visits continued for many years. The messages Muhammad received were later written down in the Quran, the holy book of Islam. The Quran contains prayers, rules for daily life, and stories about earlier prophets — people chosen by God to share His messages, like messengers carrying important letters. Islam is a monotheistic religion. That means Muslims worship only one God, called Allah in Arabic. You might think "Allah" means a different God from the one in Christianity or Judaism. Actually, "Allah" is simply the Arabic word for God — the same God. Muhammad taught that he was the last in a long line of prophets that included Abraham, Moses, and Jesus.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened to the messages after Muhammad received them?

choices.text: They were given to earlier prophets.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the earlier prophets like Abraham and Moses came before Muhammad, so they couldn't have received his messages.

choices.text: They were hidden away for many years.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the messages were written down in the Quran and shared with people, not hidden away.

choices.text: They were changed into Arabic words.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says the messages were changed or turned into Arabic words.

choices.text: They were written down in the Quran.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that the messages Muhammad received were later written down in the Quran.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Rejected in Mecca

Before Islam, the people of Arabia followed many different religions. Some worshipped many gods. Some were Christian or Jewish. So when Muhammad began sharing his message of one God, not everyone wanted to listen. Many powerful people in Mecca rejected him. Muhammad and his followers faced persecution — being treated cruelly and unfairly because of their beliefs.
stimulus.content_text: Rejected in Mecca Before Islam, the people of Arabia followed many different religions. Some worshipped many gods. Some were Christian or Jewish. So when Muhammad began sharing his message of one God, not everyone wanted to listen. Many powerful people in Mecca rejected him. Muhammad and his followers faced persecution — being treated cruelly and unfairly because of their beliefs.

prompt._: Based on the passage, which phrase is most similar in meaning to the word 'persecution'?

choices.text: being asked to follow many gods
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because worshipping many gods is something that happened before Islam in Arabia, and has nothing to do with the meaning of persecution.

choices.text: being rejected from a religion
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because persecution describes how Muhammad and his followers were treated badly, not the act of being turned away from a religion.

choices.text: being treated cruelly and unfairly
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines persecution as 'being treated cruelly and unfairly because of their beliefs,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: being ignored and forgotten by others
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says persecution means being treated cruelly and unfairly, which is much worse than just being ignored or forgotten.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Journey to Medina

In AD 622, Muhammad and his followers left Mecca and traveled about 200 miles north to the city of Medina. This journey is called the Hijra, and it was so important that it marks year one of the Islamic calendar. In Medina, people welcomed Muhammad. He became both a religious and political leader, helping settle disputes — disagreements between people, like arguments that need a fair judge.

His message of one God, equality, and care for the poor drew many people to Islam. Muhammad's teachings transformed a divided land of rival tribes into a unified community bound together by a shared faith. Eventually, he returned to Mecca with a large group of followers. The city surrendered peacefully. By the time Muhammad died in AD 632, most of the Arabian Peninsula had become Muslim.
stimulus.content_text: The Journey to Medina In AD 622, Muhammad and his followers left Mecca and traveled about 200 miles north to the city of Medina. This journey is called the Hijra, and it was so important that it marks year one of the Islamic calendar. In Medina, people welcomed Muhammad. He became both a religious and political leader, helping settle disputes — disagreements between people, like arguments that need a fair judge. His message of one God, equality, and care for the poor drew many people to Islam. Muhammad's teachings transformed a divided land of rival tribes into a unified community bound together by a shared faith. Eventually, he returned to Mecca with a large group of followers. The city surrendered peacefully. By the time Muhammad died in AD 632, most of the Arabian Peninsula had become Muslim.

prompt._: In the section "The Journey to Medina," what is the meaning of the word disputes?

choices.text: rules for shared faith
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'rules for shared faith' connects to Muhammad's teachings that united people, not to the meaning of disputes.

choices.text: disagreements between people
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines disputes right after the word, saying they are 'disagreements between people, like arguments that need a fair judge.'

choices.text: groups of rival tribes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'groups of rival tribes' describes the divided people of Arabia, not the meaning of the word disputes.

choices.text: journeys to new cities
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'journeys to new cities' describes the Hijra, which is the trip Muhammad took to Medina, not what disputes means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Civilization of Ideas

Islam did not stop at religion. In the centuries after Muhammad, Muslim scholars built one of the most advanced civilizations on Earth. In Baghdad, they created a huge library called the House of Wisdom, where scholars translated Greek, Persian, and Indian books into Arabic, saving knowledge that might have been lost forever. Muslim mathematicians developed algebra. Muslim doctors wrote medical books that European doctors studied for hundreds of years. Muslim astronomers mapped the stars.

Think about that the next time you use the word "algebra" in math class. It comes from the Arabic word al-jabr. A religion that began with one man in a mountain cave didn't just spread across continents — it built hospitals, libraries, and observatories that pushed human knowledge forward for everyone.
stimulus.content_text: A Civilization of Ideas Islam did not stop at religion. In the centuries after Muhammad, Muslim scholars built one of the most advanced civilizations on Earth. In Baghdad, they created a huge library called the House of Wisdom, where scholars translated Greek, Persian, and Indian books into Arabic, saving knowledge that might have been lost forever. Muslim mathematicians developed algebra. Muslim doctors wrote medical books that European doctors studied for hundreds of years. Muslim astronomers mapped the stars. Think about that the next time you use the word "algebra" in math class. It comes from the Arabic word al-jabr. A religion that began with one man in a mountain cave didn't just spread across continents — it built hospitals, libraries, and observatories that pushed human knowledge forward for everyone.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that Muslim scholars helped save ancient knowledge?

choices.text: They translated older books into Arabic.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says translating older books into Arabic saved knowledge 'that might have been lost forever,' which is exactly about preserving ancient ideas.

choices.text: They wrote medical books for Europeans.
choices.feedback: Writing medical books for Europeans was a new contribution Muslim doctors made, not an example of saving knowledge from the past.

choices.text: They mapped the stars in the night sky.
choices.feedback: Mapping the stars was something Muslim astronomers created on their own — it doesn't show them saving knowledge that already existed.

choices.text: They developed a new math called algebra.
choices.feedback: Developing algebra was a brand-new idea Muslim mathematicians came up with, so it shows creating knowledge, not saving it.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between how Muhammad was treated in Mecca and in Medina?

choices.text: In Mecca he taught about many gods, but in Medina he taught about one God.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Muhammad taught about one God the whole time — his message about one God never changed between Mecca and Medina.

choices.text: In Mecca he lived in a cave, but in Medina he lived on a mountain.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the cave was just where Muhammad first received God's message, and the passage never says he lived on a mountain or in a cave in either city.

choices.text: In Mecca he was a political leader, but in Medina he was a merchant.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says Muhammad was a merchant before his journey and became a political leader in Medina, not Mecca.

choices.text: In Mecca many people rejected him, but in Medina people welcomed him.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states people in Mecca rejected and persecuted Muhammad, while people in Medina welcomed him.

prompt._: Which statement best describes the main cause-and-effect structure the author uses to organize the whole article?

choices.text: It explains how disagreements over ancient books caused scholars to build a giant new library.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the House of Wisdom was built to collect and translate knowledge, not because scholars disagreed over ancient books.

choices.text: It explains how one man's time in a cave led to the creation of a large, advanced society.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the entire article shows how one man's experience in a cave led step by step to a great civilization with libraries, new math, and medical discoveries.

choices.text: It explains how the invention of new math rules caused doctors to write better medical books.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article presents algebra and medical books as separate achievements of Muslim scholars, not as one causing the other.

choices.text: It explains how the harsh desert environment caused people to travel to new cities for trade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions the desert environment as a cause for people traveling to new cities.

prompt._: Which summary best shows the main events in the order they happened in Muhammad's life and the growth of Islam?

choices.text: Muhammad became a leader in Medina, received messages in a cave, returned to Mecca, and then Islam spread across Arabia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts Muhammad becoming a leader in Medina first, but the passage clearly says the cave experience in AD 610 happened before the move to Medina in AD 622.

choices.text: Muhammad grew up in Mecca, returned peacefully to Mecca, moved to Medina, and then Islam spread across Arabia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says Muhammad returned to Mecca before moving to Medina, but the passage tells us he moved to Medina first and returned to Mecca later.

choices.text: Muhammad received messages in a cave, moved to Medina, returned peacefully to Mecca, and then Islam spread across Arabia.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the passage: cave messages (AD 610), move to Medina (AD 622), peaceful return to Mecca, and then Islam spreading across Arabia by AD 632.

choices.text: Muhammad received messages in a cave, spread Islam across Arabia, moved to Medina, and then returned to Mecca peacefully.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says Islam spread across Arabia right after the cave, but the passage shows the spread happened much later, after the move to Medina and the return to Mecca.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Muhammad's reputation as a young merchant in Mecca similar to his later role as a leader in Medina?

choices.text: In both roles, he traveled across the Arabian Peninsula to buy and sell goods.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because traveling to buy and sell goods describes his work as a merchant, not what he did as a leader in Medina.

choices.text: In both roles, he translated old books to save important knowledge for other people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because translating books was done by Muslim scholars at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, which happened long after Muhammad's time.

choices.text: In both roles, he was rejected by powerful people who disagreed with his beliefs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Muhammad was rejected in Mecca, but in Medina people welcomed him — so rejection did not happen in both places.

choices.text: In both roles, he acted as someone who was fair and trusted by others.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows Muhammad was trusted as a merchant ('al-Amin') and also trusted as a fair leader in Medina who helped settle disagreements between people.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Prophet Muhammad and the Birth of Islam

assessments.title: The Five Pillars: Foundation of the Islamic Faith

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Five Pillars of Islam

A Voice Rings Out at Dawn

Before the sun rises over Jakarta, Indonesia, a voice echoes from a tall tower. In a village in Nigeria, a man calls out across the rooftops. All around the world, Muslims are waking up to pray. You might think Islam belongs to just one part of the world, but actually, Muslims live on every continent. From Texas to Turkey to Tanzania, they share five duties called the Five Pillars of Islam.

Think of five roots holding up a giant tree. Each root does something different, but together they keep the tree standing strong. The Five Pillars work the same way.
stimulus.content_text: The Five Pillars of IslamA Voice Rings Out at Dawn Before the sun rises over Jakarta, Indonesia, a voice echoes from a tall tower. In a village in Nigeria, a man calls out across the rooftops. All around the world, Muslims are waking up to pray. You might think Islam belongs to just one part of the world, but actually, Muslims live on every continent. From Texas to Turkey to Tanzania, they share five duties called the Five Pillars of Islam. Think of five roots holding up a giant tree. Each root does something different, but together they keep the tree standing strong. The Five Pillars work the same way.

prompt._: In the passage, the author says some people might think Islam belongs to just one part of the world. What detail does the author give to show this idea is wrong?

choices.text: Muslims live on every continent in the world
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses 'Muslims live on every continent' as the direct proof that Islam is not limited to just one part of the world.

choices.text: Muslims share five duties called the Five Pillars
choices.feedback: The Five Pillars are duties Muslims share, but that detail doesn't show that Muslims live in many different places.

choices.text: Muslims wake up early to pray each morning
choices.feedback: Waking up early to pray tells us about a Muslim habit, not about how widely Muslims are spread around the world.

choices.text: Muslims listen to a voice from a tall tower
choices.feedback: The tall tower is part of the story's opening scene, but it doesn't prove that Muslims live all over the world.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Saying What You Believe

The first pillar is the Shahada, a declaration of faith. A declaration is a statement you say out loud and truly mean — like placing your hand on your heart and making a promise. The Shahada says: "There is no god but God, and Muhammad is his prophet." Muhammad was a man born in the city of Mecca over 1,400 years ago. Muslims believe God chose Muhammad to deliver his message to the world. When a person says the Shahada and truly believes it, that person becomes a Muslim. These words are whispered into a newborn baby's ear and repeated in daily prayers.
stimulus.content_text: Saying What You Believe The first pillar is the Shahada, a declaration of faith. A declaration is a statement you say out loud and truly mean — like placing your hand on your heart and making a promise. The Shahada says: "There is no god but God, and Muhammad is his prophet." Muhammad was a man born in the city of Mecca over 1,400 years ago. Muslims believe God chose Muhammad to deliver his message to the world. When a person says the Shahada and truly believes it, that person becomes a Muslim. These words are whispered into a newborn baby's ear and repeated in daily prayers.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does a person become a Muslim?

choices.text: because the person whispers to a baby
choices.feedback: Whispering the Shahada into a newborn's ear is a tradition, but it is not what makes a person become a Muslim.

choices.text: because the person accepts the Shahada
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that saying the Shahada and truly believing it is what makes a person a Muslim.

choices.text: because the person is born in Mecca
choices.feedback: Being born in Mecca is where Muhammad was born, but the passage never says you have to be born there to become a Muslim.

choices.text: because the person hears daily prayers
choices.feedback: The passage mentions daily prayers, but it never says that hearing them is what makes someone a Muslim.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Praying Five Times a Day

The second pillar is Salat, or prayer. Muslims pray five times each day — at dawn, midday, afternoon, sunset, and evening. They face the direction of Mecca, a holy city in present-day Saudi Arabia. Before praying, Muslims wash their hands, face, and feet in a ritual called wudu. A ritual is a set of steps you follow the same way each time, like a team's handshake before every game. Prayer can happen at home, at school, or in a mosque, a building where Muslims gather to worship.
stimulus.content_text: Praying Five Times a Day The second pillar is Salat, or prayer. Muslims pray five times each day — at dawn, midday, afternoon, sunset, and evening. They face the direction of Mecca, a holy city in present-day Saudi Arabia. Before praying, Muslims wash their hands, face, and feet in a ritual called wudu. A ritual is a set of steps you follow the same way each time, like a team's handshake before every game. Prayer can happen at home, at school, or in a mosque, a building where Muslims gather to worship.

prompt._: According to the text, what do Muslims do before they pray?

choices.text: They travel to the holy city of Mecca.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Muslims face toward Mecca during prayer, but the text never says they travel there before each prayer.

choices.text: They wash their hands, face, and feet.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that before praying, Muslims wash their hands, face, and feet in a ritual called wudu.

choices.text: They gather with friends in a mosque.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Muslims can pray in a mosque, the passage says prayer can also happen at home or school, so gathering in a mosque is not what they do before praying.

choices.text: They perform a special team handshake.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the team handshake is only mentioned as a helpful example to explain what the word 'ritual' means, not as something Muslims do.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Giving and Fasting

The third pillar is Zakat, which means giving to the poor. Muslims who have enough money are expected to share a portion of it with people who need help. Zakat is not just a nice thing to do. It is a duty. It reminds Muslims that their wealth is a gift from God.

The fourth pillar is Sawm, or fasting during the holy month of Ramadan. During Ramadan, Muslims do not eat or drink during daylight hours. You might think fasting means not eating at all for a whole month, but every night brings a warm meal called iftar and time with family. Fasting teaches patience, self-control, and compassion — a deep care for people who do not have enough food, like feeling someone else's hunger in your own stomach. Children, elderly people, and those who are sick do not have to fast. When Ramadan ends, Muslims celebrate a holiday called Eid al-Fitr with prayers, feasts, and gifts.
stimulus.content_text: Giving and Fasting The third pillar is Zakat, which means giving to the poor. Muslims who have enough money are expected to share a portion of it with people who need help. Zakat is not just a nice thing to do. It is a duty. It reminds Muslims that their wealth is a gift from God. The fourth pillar is Sawm, or fasting during the holy month of Ramadan. During Ramadan, Muslims do not eat or drink during daylight hours. You might think fasting means not eating at all for a whole month, but every night brings a warm meal called iftar and time with family. Fasting teaches patience, self-control, and compassion — a deep care for people who do not have enough food, like feeling someone else's hunger in your own stomach. Children, elderly people, and those who are sick do not have to fast. When Ramadan ends, Muslims celebrate a holiday called Eid al-Fitr with prayers, feasts, and gifts.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares compassion to 'feeling someone else's hunger in your own stomach.' Based on this comparison, what does the word compassion most likely mean?

choices.text: a basic need that everyone has for meals
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because needing food is a basic human need everyone shares, but compassion is specifically about caring deeply for others who are struggling.

choices.text: a deep understanding of what others suffer
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the comparison 'feeling someone else's hunger in your own stomach' shows you deeply understand what another person is suffering, which matches 'a deep understanding of what others suffer.'

choices.text: a strong wish to eat food after a long day
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wanting to eat after a long day is about your own feelings, not about caring for or understanding someone else.

choices.text: a personal feeling of being sick or weak
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because feeling sick or weak describes your own personal experience, not a feeling of care or understanding toward another person.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Journey to Mecca

The fifth pillar is the Hajj, a pilgrimage to Mecca. A pilgrimage is a long journey to a place that is sacred to you — imagine traveling across an ocean just to stand in one special spot. Every Muslim who is healthy enough and can afford the trip should make this journey at least once. During the Hajj, millions of people walk together around a large stone building called the Kaaba. Pilgrims wear simple white garments — clothing with no fancy designs or labels. A king looks the same as a farmer. Everyone is equal before God.
stimulus.content_text: The Journey to Mecca The fifth pillar is the Hajj, a pilgrimage to Mecca. A pilgrimage is a long journey to a place that is sacred to you — imagine traveling across an ocean just to stand in one special spot. Every Muslim who is healthy enough and can afford the trip should make this journey at least once. During the Hajj, millions of people walk together around a large stone building called the Kaaba. Pilgrims wear simple white garments — clothing with no fancy designs or labels. A king looks the same as a farmer. Everyone is equal before God.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a pilgrimage?

choices.text: a special gathering of millions of people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because millions of people gathering is a detail about what happens during the Hajj, not the definition of a pilgrimage.

choices.text: a large stone building in the city of Mecca
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the large stone building in Mecca is called the Kaaba, not a pilgrimage.

choices.text: a long journey to a place that is sacred
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines a pilgrimage as 'a long journey to a place that is sacred to you.'

choices.text: a simple white outfit worn during prayer
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because simple white clothing describes the garments pilgrims wear during the Hajj, not what a pilgrimage means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Five Duties, One Billion People

A girl in Malaysia whispers the Shahada. A man in Egypt kneels to pray. A family in Brazil shares their earnings with neighbors. These five practices do not just organize a Muslim's day. They connect a person in one country to a stranger in another — through the same words, the same prayers, the same open hands.
stimulus.content_text: Five Duties, One Billion People A girl in Malaysia whispers the Shahada. A man in Egypt kneels to pray. A family in Brazil shares their earnings with neighbors. These five practices do not just organize a Muslim's day. They connect a person in one country to a stranger in another — through the same words, the same prayers, the same open hands.

prompt._: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that the Five Pillars connect Muslims around the world?

choices.text: A family in Brazil shares their earnings with others
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it only describes one example of one practice, not the bigger idea of Muslims worldwide being connected through shared traditions.

choices.text: People in different countries share the same prayers
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says people in different countries share the same words and prayers, which is exactly what connects them.

choices.text: The five practices help organize a Muslim's daily life
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because organizing daily life is a different idea — the question asks about connecting Muslims around the world, not about scheduling their day.

choices.text: About one billion people follow the Islamic faith
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the number one billion only appears in the title, not in the passage itself, and it doesn't explain how people are connected.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is Salat (prayer) different from the Hajj (pilgrimage)?

choices.text: Salat involves wearing simple white clothing, while the Hajj involves washing hands and feet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the details — simple white garments belong to the Hajj, and washing hands and feet (wudu) belongs to Salat.

choices.text: Salat is performed by walking around a building, while the Hajj is performed by facing Mecca.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps the two practices — facing Mecca is part of Salat, and walking around the Kaaba building is part of the Hajj.

choices.text: Salat is practiced only during a holy month, while the Hajj is practiced five times every day.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Salat is practiced five times every day, not during a holy month — fasting during a holy month describes Sawm (Ramadan), not Salat or the Hajj.

choices.text: Salat happens in many everyday places, while the Hajj requires a journey to one specific city.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Salat can happen at home, school, or a mosque, while the Hajj is a journey specifically to the city of Mecca.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a cause-and-effect structure the author uses to explain why the Five Pillars unite Muslims around the world?

choices.text: Giving money to the poor causes families to celebrate Eid al-Fitr together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never links giving money to the poor (Zakat) with celebrating Eid al-Fitr — those are two separate pillars and ideas.

choices.text: Praying at dawn causes Islam to spread to every continent on Earth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says that praying at dawn is what caused Islam to spread to every continent.

choices.text: Traveling to Mecca causes all Muslims to practice every pillar the same way.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says that traveling to Mecca causes all Muslims to practice every pillar the same way.

choices.text: Sharing one set of duties causes Muslims to feel linked across countries.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that sharing the same five duties is the cause that leads to the effect of Muslims feeling connected across the world.

prompt._: Based on the passage, which of these practices happens first in a Muslim's life?

choices.text: Fasting for the month of Ramadan
choices.feedback: Fasting during Ramadan happens later in life because the passage says children do not have to fast.

choices.text: Traveling to the city of Mecca
choices.feedback: Traveling to Mecca is something only healthy adults who can afford the trip are expected to do, so it happens much later in life, not first.

choices.text: Hearing the words of the Shahada
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the Shahada is whispered into a newborn baby's ear, making it the very first practice a Muslim experiences.

choices.text: Sharing money with people in need
choices.feedback: Sharing money with the poor requires having enough money first, so this cannot be the first practice in a Muslim's life.

prompt._: The author compares the Five Pillars to the roots of a tree. How does this comparison help explain the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: It shows that the religion began in one small city and slowly grew across the world.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the tree comparison is about how the pillars support the faith as a whole, not about Islam starting small and spreading to new places.

choices.text: It shows that praying five times a day is the single strongest part of the faith.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the tree comparison is about all five roots working together, not about one pillar being more important than the others.

choices.text: It shows that the five different practices work together to keep the faith strong.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says each root does something different but together they keep the tree strong, which matches the idea that all five pillars work together to support the faith.

choices.text: It shows that followers must plant giant trees to celebrate their many holidays.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions planting trees or any holiday that involves trees — the tree is just used as a comparison to explain the pillars.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Five Pillars: Foundation of the Islamic Faith

assessments.title: From Desert to Empire: The Spread of Islam

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
From Desert to Three Continents: The Spread of Islam

A Faith Crosses an Ocean of Sand

Picture a map stretching from Spain all the way to India. Now color it in. In the year 632, when Muhammad died, Islam existed in just one small corner of that map — the Arabian Peninsula, a huge piece of land nearly surrounded by water on three sides. One hundred years later, Islam had spread across that entire map. Three continents. Thousands of cities. Millions of people. How?
stimulus.content_text: From Desert to Three Continents: The Spread of IslamA Faith Crosses an Ocean of Sand Picture a map stretching from Spain all the way to India. Now color it in. In the year 632, when Muhammad died, Islam existed in just one small corner of that map — the Arabian Peninsula, a huge piece of land nearly surrounded by water on three sides. One hundred years later, Islam had spread across that entire map. Three continents. Thousands of cities. Millions of people. How?

prompt._: According to the text, how did the size of Islam in 632 contrast with its size one hundred years later?

choices.text: It started on three continents and then shrank to one peninsula.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has things backwards — the passage says Islam started in one small area and grew bigger, not the other way around.

choices.text: It started in one small area and then spread across three continents.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Islam began in just the Arabian Peninsula in 632 and had spread across three continents one hundred years later.

choices.text: It started in Spain and then moved only to India over time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses Spain and India only to describe the size of the map, not to say Islam moved only from one to the other.

choices.text: It started with millions of people and then lost most of them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Islam grew to include millions of people, meaning it gained followers rather than losing them.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Caliphs Take the Lead

After Muhammad died, new leaders called caliphs took charge. A caliph was a political leader — like a president or a king — who guided the Muslim community. You might think caliphs were prophets like Muhammad, but they were not. They did not receive messages from God. They were organizers, decision-makers, and commanders. The first caliphs had been Muhammad's closest friends. Under their leadership, Muslim armies moved outward from Arabia. Soldiers were driven by faith, by a desire to share Islam, and by the chance to gain new lands and wealth.
stimulus.content_text: Caliphs Take the Lead After Muhammad died, new leaders called caliphs took charge. A caliph was a political leader — like a president or a king — who guided the Muslim community. You might think caliphs were prophets like Muhammad, but they were not. They did not receive messages from God. They were organizers, decision-makers, and commanders. The first caliphs had been Muhammad's closest friends. Under their leadership, Muslim armies moved outward from Arabia. Soldiers were driven by faith, by a desire to share Islam, and by the chance to gain new lands and wealth.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Muslim armies move outward from Arabia?

choices.text: They wanted to share their religion and gain new lands.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says soldiers were driven by a desire to share Islam and to gain new lands and wealth.

choices.text: They wanted to help the new caliphs become the next prophets.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that caliphs were not prophets and were never meant to become prophets.

choices.text: They hoped to deliver the messages that caliphs got from God.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that caliphs did NOT receive messages from God, so there were no messages to deliver.

choices.text: They needed to find new political leaders after Muhammad died.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the caliphs were already in charge as the new leaders — the armies weren't looking for new ones.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Swords

Here's the surprising part. Muslim armies conquered many places, but they usually did not force people to convert — to give up their old religion and accept a new one. Many conquered people kept praying the way they always had. Over time, though, large numbers chose Islam freely. Why? Islam taught that all believers were equal before God. It did not matter where you came from or what language you spoke. That message felt powerful, especially to people whose own rulers treated them unfairly.

Merchants — people who buy and sell goods — also carried Islam along trade routes. They loaded camels with spices, silk, and perfume. They traveled roads that connected cities across Asia, Africa, and Europe. Everywhere they stopped to trade, they shared their beliefs. Faith traveled alongside cinnamon and cloth.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Swords Here's the surprising part. Muslim armies conquered many places, but they usually did not force people to convert — to give up their old religion and accept a new one. Many conquered people kept praying the way they always had. Over time, though, large numbers chose Islam freely. Why? Islam taught that all believers were equal before God. It did not matter where you came from or what language you spoke. That message felt powerful, especially to people whose own rulers treated them unfairly. Merchants — people who buy and sell goods — also carried Islam along trade routes. They loaded camels with spices, silk, and perfume. They traveled roads that connected cities across Asia, Africa, and Europe. Everywhere they stopped to trade, they shared their beliefs. Faith traveled alongside cinnamon and cloth.

prompt._: According to the text, what did many conquered people do before large numbers chose Islam freely?

choices.text: They loaded camels with silk and spices.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because loading camels with silk and spices is something merchants did, not something conquered people did before choosing Islam.

choices.text: They traveled far to buy and sell goods.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because traveling to buy and sell goods describes merchants, not conquered people staying in their homes.

choices.text: They kept praying as they always had.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that 'Many conquered people kept praying the way they always had' before large numbers later chose Islam freely.

choices.text: They forced their rulers to act fairly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the text says people were drawn to Islam because their rulers treated them unfairly — there is no mention of them forcing rulers to change.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Umayyad Empire

One of the mightiest Muslim empires was the Umayyad Caliphate — a caliphate is a government run by a caliph. By the early 700s, the Umayyads ruled from their capital city of Damascus in present-day Syria. Their empire stretched from the Atlantic coast of North Africa to the borders of China. The Umayyads built roads, organized their government, and made Arabic the official language across all their lands.

You might imagine the Islamic empire stayed as one united country the whole time. Actually, Muslims sometimes disagreed about who should lead. Over the years, the empire split into separate kingdoms, each with its own rulers and traditions.
stimulus.content_text: The Umayyad Empire One of the mightiest Muslim empires was the Umayyad Caliphate — a caliphate is a government run by a caliph. By the early 700s, the Umayyads ruled from their capital city of Damascus in present-day Syria. Their empire stretched from the Atlantic coast of North Africa to the borders of China. The Umayyads built roads, organized their government, and made Arabic the official language across all their lands. You might imagine the Islamic empire stayed as one united country the whole time. Actually, Muslims sometimes disagreed about who should lead. Over the years, the empire split into separate kingdoms, each with its own rulers and traditions.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the word 'caliphate' similar in meaning to the word 'empire'?

choices.text: Both words describe a road connecting two capital cities
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because roads are something the Umayyads built, but neither 'caliphate' nor 'empire' means a road connecting cities.

choices.text: Both words describe a type of religion practiced by many people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'caliphate' and 'empire' both describe systems of government and rule, not a religion — Islam is the religion mentioned in the passage.

choices.text: Both words describe a system of rule over a large area
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that a caliphate is a government run by a caliph, and an empire is also a large territory under one rule, so both words describe ruling over a large area.

choices.text: Both words describe a language spoken across many lands
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arabic is the official language mentioned in the passage, but neither 'caliphate' nor 'empire' has anything to do with describing a language.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Three Faiths, One City

In 711, Muslim armies crossed from North Africa into Spain. For nearly eight hundred years, parts of Spain lived under Muslim rule. In cities like Cordoba and Toledo, something unusual happened. Muslims, Christians, and Jewish people lived side by side. They shared markets, streets, and ideas. In Cordoba, a giant mosque held thousands of worshippers under rows of red-and-white stone arches. Nearby, scholars from all three faiths studied astronomy and poetry together.
stimulus.content_text: Three Faiths, One City In 711, Muslim armies crossed from North Africa into Spain. For nearly eight hundred years, parts of Spain lived under Muslim rule. In cities like Cordoba and Toledo, something unusual happened. Muslims, Christians, and Jewish people lived side by side. They shared markets, streets, and ideas. In Cordoba, a giant mosque held thousands of worshippers under rows of red-and-white stone arches. Nearby, scholars from all three faiths studied astronomy and poetry together.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a scholar?

choices.text: a person who studies subjects
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly links scholars to studying subjects like astronomy and poetry.

choices.text: a person who sells goods in a market
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions markets where people shared goods, but scholars are shown studying, not selling.

choices.text: a person who leads an army
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions armies crossing into Spain, but scholars are never connected to leading armies.

choices.text: a person who prays in a mosque
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions people praying in the mosque, but that describes worshippers, not scholars.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Civilization Built by Many Hands

The spread of Islam carried more than prayer. It carried the Arabic language, new styles of building with domes and arches, discoveries in science, and beautiful art made from swirling patterns and bright tiles. People from very different backgrounds — Arab, Persian, African, Spanish — all added their own ideas to this growing civilization.

Today, when you use the numbers on your math worksheet, you are using a system Muslim scholars passed along to Europe. The world Islam built is still part of your world right now — hiding in plain sight on every page you count.
stimulus.content_text: A Civilization Built by Many Hands The spread of Islam carried more than prayer. It carried the Arabic language, new styles of building with domes and arches, discoveries in science, and beautiful art made from swirling patterns and bright tiles. People from very different backgrounds — Arab, Persian, African, Spanish — all added their own ideas to this growing civilization. Today, when you use the numbers on your math worksheet, you are using a system Muslim scholars passed along to Europe. choices.text: By explaining that new styles of building included beautiful domes and tall arches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because domes and arches describe old building styles from the past, not a direct example of how that world is still part of your everyday life.

choices.text: By explaining that people from many backgrounds added their own special new ideas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because people from many backgrounds adding ideas explains how the civilization grew, not how it connects to our lives today.

choices.text: By explaining that students use a number system passed along by Muslim scholars.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author uses the number system on your math worksheet as a specific, real-life example to show that Muslim scholars' work is still part of your world right now.

choices.text: By explaining that the spread of Islam carried the Arabic language far away.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows armies spread Islam by conquering places, while merchants spread it by sharing their beliefs along trade routes as they traveled and traded goods.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the beginning and ending of the article?

syllabus_metadata.title: From Desert to Empire: The Spread of Islam

assessments.title: The House of Wisdom: Baghdad's Golden Age of Learning

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Baghdad's House of Wisdom

A Round City Rises from the Desert

Picture a city shaped like a giant circle. Thick walls surround it. At the very center sits a golden palace. This was Baghdad, built in AD 762 along the Tigris River in what is now Iraq. The Abbasid Caliphs — the powerful rulers of the Islamic Empire — moved their capital from Damascus to this new city. A caliph was like a king, the leader of millions of people across a huge empire.

Baghdad grew fast. Within a few decades, it became the largest city in the world. At its peak, over a million people lived there. Artisans — skilled workers who made pottery, cloth, and metalwork — filled its workshops. Merchants sold spices, silk, and books. Travelers arrived from Africa, China, India, and Europe.
stimulus.content_text: Baghdad's House of WisdomA Round City Rises from the Desert Picture a city shaped like a giant circle. Thick walls surround it. At the very center sits a golden palace. This was Baghdad, built in AD 762 along the Tigris River in what is now Iraq. The Abbasid Caliphs — the powerful rulers of the Islamic Empire — moved their capital from Damascus to this new city. A caliph was like a king, the leader of millions of people across a huge empire. Baghdad grew fast. Within a few decades, it became the largest city in the world. At its peak, over a million people lived there. Artisans — skilled workers who made pottery, cloth, and metalwork — filled its workshops. Merchants sold spices, silk, and books. Travelers arrived from Africa, China, India, and Europe.

prompt._: According to the text, what did artisans do that was different from what merchants did?

choices.text: They traveled from places like Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because traveling from places like Africa describes visitors who came to Baghdad, not artisans.

choices.text: They ruled over the huge Islamic Empire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ruling the Islamic Empire was the job of the Abbasid Caliphs, not artisans.

choices.text: They sold things like spices and silk.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because selling spices and silk was what merchants did, not artisans.

choices.text: They made things like pottery and cloth.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says artisans were skilled workers who made things like pottery, cloth, and metalwork.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Library Like No Other

The most surprising place in Baghdad was a great library and research center called the House of Wisdom. It opened in the early 800s. The caliph wanted to collect every important book in the world. He sent agents to distant lands to find them.

Workers gathered manuscripts — books written carefully by hand, since printing machines did not exist yet. These manuscripts came from Greek, Persian, and Indian civilizations. Scholars translated them into Arabic. The ideas of Greek thinkers like Aristotle, Plato, and Euclid — a mathematician who wrote the first great geometry textbook — were all studied. Indian texts about math and the stars were brought to Baghdad too. Without these translations, many of those ancient writings might have disappeared forever.
stimulus.content_text: A Library Like No Other The most surprising place in Baghdad was a great library and research center called the House of Wisdom. It opened in the early 800s. The caliph wanted to collect every important book in the world. He sent agents to distant lands to find them. Workers gathered manuscripts — books written carefully by hand, since printing machines did not exist yet. These manuscripts came from Greek, Persian, and Indian civilizations. Scholars translated them into Arabic. The ideas of Greek thinkers like Aristotle, Plato, and Euclid — a mathematician who wrote the first great geometry textbook — were all studied. Indian texts about math and the stars were brought to Baghdad too. Without these translations, many of those ancient writings might have disappeared forever.

prompt._: The main idea of this section is that the House of Wisdom collected and saved important knowledge. Which detail explains why many ancient writings were kept from disappearing?

choices.text: Because Euclid wrote a great geometry textbook.
choices.feedback: Euclid's geometry textbook is just one example of a book that was studied at the House of Wisdom, not a reason why ancient writings survived.

choices.text: Because scholars translated them into Arabic.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says that without the Arabic translations, many ancient writings might have disappeared forever.

choices.text: Because the caliph opened the library in the 800s.
choices.feedback: When the library opened is a fact about its history, but it doesn't explain how ancient writings were saved.

choices.text: Because workers wrote the books carefully by hand.
choices.feedback: Writing books by hand describes what manuscripts are, but it doesn't explain why ancient writings were kept from disappearing.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Copying Old Books

You might think the scholars just copied what other people had already figured out. Actually, they did something much bigger. They used that old knowledge as a starting point and made their own discoveries.

A scholar named al-Khwarizmi helped develop algebra — a kind of math where you solve puzzles with missing numbers, like figuring out what fills the blank in 3 + ___ = 7. Scholars also learned about the number zero from Indian mathematicians and spread it across the empire. They built better tools for studying the stars. They wrote encyclopedias — huge books that organize knowledge on many subjects, covering topics from medicine to science to history all in one place. They drew maps of the known world more accurately than anyone had before.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Copying Old Books You might think the scholars just copied what other people had already figured out. Actually, they did something much bigger. They used that old knowledge as a starting point and made their own discoveries. A scholar named al-Khwarizmi helped develop algebra — a kind of math where you solve puzzles with missing numbers, like figuring out what fills the blank in 3 + ___ = 7. Scholars also learned about the number zero from Indian mathematicians and spread it across the empire. They built better tools for studying the stars. They wrote encyclopedias — huge books that organize knowledge on many subjects, covering topics from medicine to science to history all in one place. They drew maps of the known world more accurately than anyone had before.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did scholars at the House of Wisdom do with old knowledge before making their own discoveries?

choices.text: They stored it without reading it
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because scholars actively read and used the old knowledge — that's exactly how they were able to develop new ideas like algebra.

choices.text: They sent it back to other countries
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions scholars sending knowledge back to other countries.

choices.text: They used it as a starting point
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that scholars 'used that old knowledge as a starting point' before going on to make their own discoveries.

choices.text: They threw it away and started over
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says scholars used old knowledge as a starting point, meaning they kept and built on it rather than throwing it away.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Place for Everyone

When we say "Islamic civilization," it is easy to assume that means everyone was Muslim. Here is the surprising part: the House of Wisdom welcomed scholars of many faiths. Muslim, Christian, and Jewish thinkers worked side by side. "Islamic civilization" describes the empire and its culture, not just one religion.

Their ideas spread far beyond Baghdad. In cities like Cairo and Cordoba (in what is now Spain), European scholars translated Arabic texts into Latin and carried the knowledge back to their own countries. This is how algebra, medical encyclopedias, and ancient Greek ideas reached Europe.
stimulus.content_text: A Place for Everyone When we say "Islamic civilization," it is easy to assume that means everyone was Muslim. Here is the surprising part: the House of Wisdom welcomed scholars of many faiths. Muslim, Christian, and Jewish thinkers worked side by side. "Islamic civilization" describes the empire and its culture, not just one religion. Their ideas spread far beyond Baghdad. In cities like Cairo and Cordoba (in what is now Spain), European scholars translated Arabic texts into Latin and carried the knowledge back to their own countries. This is how algebra, medical encyclopedias, and ancient Greek ideas reached Europe.

prompt._: How were Muslim, Christian, and Jewish scholars alike at the House of Wisdom?

choices.text: They all came from cities in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the scholars at the House of Wisdom were not all from European cities — the House of Wisdom was in Baghdad.

choices.text: They all ruled the empire together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage talks about scholars sharing ideas, not ruling the empire together.

choices.text: They all worked and studied together.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Muslim, Christian, and Jewish thinkers 'worked side by side' at the House of Wisdom.

choices.text: They all translated Latin into Arabic.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says European scholars translated Arabic texts into Latin, not Latin into Arabic.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Day the River Ran Black

This golden age of learning lasted from roughly the 700s to the 1300s. Then, in 1258, the Mongol army — fierce warriors from Central Asia — invaded Baghdad. They destroyed the House of Wisdom. According to legend, soldiers threw so many books into the Tigris River that the water turned black with ink.

But here is what the Mongols could not destroy: the ideas had already traveled. Algebra was already being studied in distant classrooms. Medical knowledge was already saving lives in other cities. The books were gone, but the thinking lived on — in every equation you solve, in every map you read, in the zero sitting quietly between the numbers on your ruler.
stimulus.content_text: The Day the River Ran Black This golden age of learning lasted from roughly the 700s to the 1300s. Then, in 1258, the Mongol army — fierce warriors from Central Asia — invaded Baghdad. They destroyed the House of Wisdom. According to legend, soldiers threw so many books into the Tigris River that the water turned black with ink. But here is what the Mongols could not destroy: the ideas had already traveled. Algebra was already being studied in distant classrooms. Medical knowledge was already saving lives in other cities. The books were gone, but the thinking lived on — in every equation you solve, in every map you read, in the zero sitting quietly between the numbers on your ruler.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the phrase 'golden age of learning' mean?

choices.text: a legend about books thrown into a river
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the legend about books being thrown into the river is something that happened during the Mongol invasion, not the meaning of 'golden age of learning.'

choices.text: a time when gold was discovered in Baghdad
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about finding gold — 'golden' here describes how special and valuable that time of learning was.

choices.text: a long period of great knowledge and study
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes hundreds of years of studying algebra, medicine, and other important ideas, which is exactly a long period of great knowledge and study.

choices.text: a short war between armies from Central Asia
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the golden age was a long stretch of learning and discovery, not a short war — the Mongol invasion actually ended the golden age.

prompt._: How were the scholars at the House of Wisdom different from people who simply copied old books?

choices.text: They used old ideas to create new knowledge of their own
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the scholars used old knowledge as a starting point and made their own discoveries, like algebra, spreading the concept of zero, and drawing better maps.

choices.text: They only studied Greek texts and ignored Indian writings
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says scholars studied manuscripts from Greek, Persian, AND Indian civilizations — not just Greek texts.

choices.text: They translated manuscripts but did not understand them
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the scholars clearly understood the manuscripts — they used those ideas to invent new things like algebra and better star-studying tools.

choices.text: They collected books but never shared them with others
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage explains that their ideas spread far beyond Baghdad to cities like Cairo and Cordoba, meaning they absolutely shared their knowledge.

prompt._: According to the text, why did the ideas from the House of Wisdom survive after the Mongols destroyed the library?

choices.text: Because the workers threw the ancient writings into the river to protect them from invaders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the Mongol soldiers who threw books into the Tigris River to destroy them, not workers trying to protect the writings.

choices.text: Because the Mongol army decided to save the medical encyclopedias and math textbooks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Mongols destroyed the House of Wisdom — the passage never says they chose to save any books.

choices.text: Because the caliph sent agents to distant lands to hide the most important manuscripts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the caliph sent agents to distant lands to find and bring back important books, not to hide them from enemies.

choices.text: Because scholars had already taken translated copies of the books to other countries.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that scholars had already spread the ideas to other cities and countries through translations, so the knowledge lived on even after the library was destroyed.

prompt._: How does the author organize the history of the House of Wisdom across the passage?

choices.text: By showing how Europeans wrote books, then how the caliph copied them, and finally how the Mongols saved them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Europeans didn't write the original books, the caliph collected manuscripts from many civilizations, and the Mongols destroyed the library rather than saving it.

choices.text: By showing how books were gathered, then how new discoveries were made, and finally how ideas spread.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage moves in this exact order: gathering manuscripts, making new discoveries, and then spreading those ideas to Europe and beyond.

choices.text: By showing how the library was destroyed, then how books were translated, and finally how the city was built.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't start with destruction — it starts with Baghdad being built and books being collected, and the destruction happens at the very end.

choices.text: By showing how new discoveries were made, then how books were gathered, and finally how ideas spread.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows that scholars first gathered old books and *then* used them as a starting point to make new discoveries — not the other way around.

prompt._: Which comparison best summarizes the main idea of the entire article?

choices.text: The ancient manuscripts were saved, but the new discoveries were lost.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's actually the opposite of what happened — the new discoveries made in Baghdad were NOT lost; they survived and spread to Europe and beyond.

choices.text: The physical library was destroyed, but its ideas changed the world.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the whole article shows how the library was physically destroyed by the Mongols, yet its ideas in math, medicine, and science spread across the world and still affect us today.

choices.text: The scholars translated old books, but they kept the new ideas hidden.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the scholars shared their new ideas widely — they definitely did NOT keep them hidden.

choices.text: The caliphs ruled a massive empire, but the Mongols had stronger armies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article is not mainly about comparing the caliphs' power to the Mongols' army — that's just one small part of the story.

syllabus_metadata.title: The House of Wisdom: Baghdad's Golden Age of Learning

assessments.title: Stars, Numbers, and Beautiful Tiles: Islamic Contributions

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Golden Age of Islamic Civilization

Numbers You Use Every Day

Look at a clock. You see the digits 1 through 12. Now try writing those same numbers the old European way: I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX, X, XI, XII. Those are Roman numerals, and for centuries, Europeans had nothing else. Imagine trying to multiply XIV by XXIII. It was a nightmare.

Then a better system arrived from the Islamic world. Muslim scholars had learned it from mathematicians in India and brought it west. This Hindu-Arabic number system — the ten digits from 0 to 9 — made math far easier. You might think Arab scholars invented these numbers. Actually, they discovered them in India and recognized how powerful they were. They carried them across continents to Europe. Today the whole world uses this system, thanks to that journey.
stimulus.content_text: The Golden Age of Islamic CivilizationNumbers You Use Every Day Look at a clock. You see the digits 1 through 12. Now try writing those same numbers the old European way: I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX, X, XI, XII. Those are Roman numerals, and for centuries, Europeans had nothing else. Imagine trying to multiply XIV by XXIII. It was a nightmare. Then a better system arrived from the Islamic world. Muslim scholars had learned it from mathematicians in India and brought it west. This Hindu-Arabic number system — the ten digits from 0 to 9 — made math far easier. You might think Arab scholars invented these numbers. Actually, they discovered them in India and recognized how powerful they were. They carried them across continents to Europe. Today the whole world uses this system, thanks to that journey.

prompt._: According to the text, which statement shows a contrast between Roman numerals and the Hindu-Arabic number system?

choices.text: Roman numerals came from India, but the newer system came from Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the origins — Roman numerals came from Europe, and the Hindu-Arabic system came from India, not the other way around.

choices.text: Roman numerals made math harder, but the newer system made it easier.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Roman numeral math 'was a nightmare' while the Hindu-Arabic system 'made math far easier,' showing a clear contrast.

choices.text: Roman numerals used ten digits, but the newer system used twelve digits.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Roman numerals having ten digits or the Hindu-Arabic system having twelve digits — those numbers are made up.

choices.text: Roman numerals made math easier, but the newer system made it harder.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says Roman numerals made math harder, not easier.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Scholar Named al-Khwarizmi

In the 800s, a brilliant scholar named al-Khwarizmi wrote a famous math book with the Arabic word "al-jabr" in its title. That word became algebra — a kind of math that uses letters and symbols to stand for unknown numbers, like solving a puzzle where some pieces are hidden. Even the word algorithm — a step-by-step set of instructions, like a recipe a computer follows — comes from his name.

You might think Islamic scholars only protected ancient knowledge while Europe forgot it. Actually, they pushed far beyond what earlier thinkers knew. They invented new fields of study and asked questions no one had asked before.
stimulus.content_text: A Scholar Named al-Khwarizmi In the 800s, a brilliant scholar named al-Khwarizmi wrote a famous math book with the Arabic word "al-jabr" in its title. That word became algebra — a kind of math that uses letters and symbols to stand for unknown numbers, like solving a puzzle where some pieces are hidden. Even the word algorithm — a step-by-step set of instructions, like a recipe a computer follows — comes from his name. You might think Islamic scholars only protected ancient knowledge while Europe forgot it. Actually, they pushed far beyond what earlier thinkers knew. They invented new fields of study and asked questions no one had asked before.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why do we have the word "algorithm" today?

choices.text: Because it comes from a famous scholar's name.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that the word 'algorithm' comes from al-Khwarizmi's name.

choices.text: Because it was the title of an ancient math book.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says 'al-jabr' was in the title of al-Khwarizmi's book, not 'algorithm.'

choices.text: Because it was an old word for a hidden puzzle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'hidden puzzle' is used in the passage to describe algebra, not algorithm.

choices.text: Because it was a recipe used by early computers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses a recipe as an example to explain what an algorithm means, but that's not where the word came from.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Hospitals and Healing

A scholar named Ibn Sina, also called Avicenna, wrote a medical textbook called The Canon of Medicine. It was so detailed and useful that European universities studied it for hundreds of years. Muslim doctors also wrote huge medical encyclopedias — collections of knowledge organized by topic, like a giant reference book.

Their hospitals were significant. Sick people received free care. Different illnesses were treated in separate rooms called wards. These were some of the most advanced hospitals anywhere in the medieval world.
stimulus.content_text: Hospitals and Healing A scholar named Ibn Sina, also called Avicenna, wrote a medical textbook called The Canon of Medicine. It was so detailed and useful that European universities studied it for hundreds of years. Muslim doctors also wrote huge medical encyclopedias — collections of knowledge organized by topic, like a giant reference book. Their hospitals were significant. Sick people received free care. Different illnesses were treated in separate rooms called wards. These were some of the most advanced hospitals anywhere in the medieval world.

prompt._: According to the text, why did European universities study Ibn Sina's book for hundreds of years?

choices.text: because it was so detailed and useful
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses those exact words: the book 'was so detailed and useful' that universities studied it for hundreds of years.

choices.text: because it was the oldest medical book ever written
choices.feedback: The passage never says Ibn Sina's book was the oldest medical book ever written — that detail simply isn't there.

choices.text: because it was organized like an encyclopedia
choices.feedback: The encyclopedias were the ones organized by topic, not Ibn Sina's book, so this mixes up two different things from the passage.

choices.text: because European doctors had no hospitals
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say European doctors had no hospitals — it actually talks about hospitals existing in the medieval world.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Mapping the Stars

Muslim astronomers built observatories — special buildings designed for watching the sky, like giant lookout towers pointed at the stars. They mapped the positions of stars and tracked how planets moved with great care. Many stars still carry their Arabic names today: Aldebaran, Betelgeuse, and Rigel. Muslim astronomers also improved the astrolabe, a handheld metal tool that helped people figure out where stars were in the sky and find their way on long journeys. Think of it as an ancient GPS made of brass.
stimulus.content_text: Mapping the Stars Muslim astronomers built observatories — special buildings designed for watching the sky, like giant lookout towers pointed at the stars. They mapped the positions of stars and tracked how planets moved with great care. Many stars still carry their Arabic names today: Aldebaran, Betelgeuse, and Rigel. Muslim astronomers also improved the astrolabe, a handheld metal tool that helped people figure out where stars were in the sky and find their way on long journeys. Think of it as an ancient GPS made of brass.

prompt._: Based on the author's comparison to an "ancient GPS," what is the meaning of the word "astrolabe" in the passage?

choices.text: A metal tool used to help travelers find their way
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the astrolabe was a handheld metal tool for finding stars and navigating, just like a GPS helps us find our way today.

choices.text: A special map used to track the movement of planets
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tracking planet movements is what astronomers did in general, not what the astrolabe tool was specifically used for.

choices.text: A brass tower used to point out the names of stars
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the astrolabe was made of brass, it was a small handheld tool — not a tower — and it helped with navigation, not naming stars.

choices.text: A tall building used to watch the night sky closely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a tall building for watching the sky describes an observatory, which is a different thing mentioned earlier in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Art Without Faces

Walk into a mosque from this era, and you won't see paintings of people. In religious spaces, showing living figures was often discouraged. So Islamic artists did something unexpected. They turned shapes into art.

They created intricate geometric patterns — designs so detailed and carefully made that they look like visual music. Circles, squares, and stars locked together and repeated endlessly across walls. Artists covered mosques, palaces, and everyday objects with colorful tiles, carved stone, and painted wood. Every surface told a story through shape and color.

Calligraphy — the art of beautiful handwriting — was treasured just as much. Artists wrote passages from the Quran in flowing, elegant scripts. The letters themselves became decoration. You could find calligraphy on buildings, books, pottery, and textiles.
stimulus.content_text: Art Without Faces Walk into a mosque from this era, and you won't see paintings of people. In religious spaces, showing living figures was often discouraged.
