Circles, squares, and stars locked together and repeated endlessly across walls. Artists covered mosques, palaces, and everyday objects with colorful tiles, carved stone, and painted wood. Every surface told a story through shape and color. Calligraphy — the art of beautiful handwriting — was treasured just as much. Artists wrote passages from the Quran in flowing, elegant scripts. The letters themselves became decoration. You could find calligraphy on buildings, books, pottery, and textiles.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is calligraphy?

choices.text: the art of drawing geometric shapes
choices.feedback: Drawing geometric shapes like circles, squares, and stars is another art form described in the passage, but it is separate from calligraphy.

choices.text: the art of painting colorful tiles
choices.feedback: Colorful tiles are mentioned in the passage as a way artists decorated buildings, but that is not the definition of calligraphy.

choices.text: the art of carving patterns into stone
choices.feedback: Carving patterns into stone is mentioned in the passage as one way artists decorated surfaces, but it is not what calligraphy means.

choices.text: the art of beautiful handwriting
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'Calligraphy — the art of beautiful handwriting' right in the text, giving us the exact definition.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Still With Us

Tonight, step outside and find a bright star. It may carry an Arabic name given over a thousand years ago. Tomorrow, when you write the number zero, you are using a gift that traveled from India through the Islamic world to your pencil. The algebra waiting for you in a few years? A scholar in Baghdad started it. Beauty and discovery traveled across borders then. They still do now.
stimulus.content_text: Still With Us Tonight, step outside and find a bright star. It may carry an Arabic name given over a thousand years ago. Tomorrow, when you write the number zero, you are using a gift that traveled from India through the Islamic world to your pencil. The algebra waiting for you in a few years? A scholar in Baghdad started it. Beauty and discovery traveled across borders then. They still do now.

choices.text: Scholars travel to Baghdad to learn about algebra.

choices.text: Travelers carry beauty and discovery across borders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is a general, poetic idea from the passage, not a specific detail that shows an Islamic world discovery is still in use today.

choices.text: Stargazers step outside to look at the night sky.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stepping outside to look at stars is something the reader does today, but it doesn't directly show that a discovery from the Islamic world is still being used.

choices.text: Students write the number zero with their pencils.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says writing the number zero is 'a gift' that came through the Islamic world, proving their discoveries are still used today.

prompt._: Based on the article about Islamic contributions, how was the origin of the number system different from the origin of algebra?

choices.text: Numbers were created by early Europeans, but algebra was created by Indian thinkers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Europeans used Roman numerals and received the Hindu-Arabic number system from the Islamic world, not the other way around.

choices.text: Numbers were invented by Islamic scholars, but algebra was borrowed from the Greeks.

choices.text: Numbers were discovered in India, but algebra was invented by a Muslim scholar.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the number system came from India while algebra was invented by the Muslim scholar al-Khwarizmi.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused Islamic scholars and artists to make lasting contributions in so many different fields?

choices.text: They built on earlier knowledge and created new ideas that spread widely.

prompt._: Based on the text, which sequence shows how mathematical and medical knowledge traveled over time?

choices.text: Thinkers in India and the Islamic world made discoveries, and Europe learned them.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows knowledge starting in India, being carried and expanded by Islamic scholars, and then reaching Europe.

choices.text: Roman scholars wrote detailed medical books, and Islamic hospitals used those books.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Islamic scholars like Ibn Sina wrote the important medical books, not Roman scholars.

assessments.title: Gold and Salt: The Kingdom of Ghana

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Ghana: The Kingdom Built on Gold and Salt

A Handful of Salt Worth a Handful of Gold

A trader kneels in the sand and sets down a pile of gold. He walks away. A second trader arrives, looks at the gold, and places a block of salt beside it. If the first trader thinks the trade is fair, he takes the salt. If not, he waits for more. The two traders never speak. They never even see each other's faces.

This was called "silent trading," and it happened over a thousand years ago in West Africa. Long before the first castles rose in England, powerful kingdoms already ruled in Africa. One of the greatest was the Kingdom of Ghana.
stimulus.content_text: Ghana: The Kingdom Built on Gold and SaltA Handful of Salt Worth a Handful of Gold A trader kneels in the sand and sets down a pile of gold. He walks away. A second trader arrives, looks at the gold, and places a block of salt beside it. If the first trader thinks the trade is fair, he takes the salt. If not, he waits for more. The two traders never speak. They never even see each other's faces. This was called "silent trading," and it happened over a thousand years ago in West Africa. Long before the first castles rose in England, powerful kingdoms already ruled in Africa. One of the greatest was the Kingdom of Ghana.

prompt._: According to the text, how was silent trading different from regular trading?

choices.text: The traders met face to face and argued over prices
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the traders never spoke to each other, so there was no arguing over prices.

choices.text: The traders never spoke to or saw each other
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage states the traders 'never speak' and 'never even see each other's faces,' which is exactly what made silent trading so unusual.

choices.text: The traders used coins instead of gold and salt
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the traders exchanged gold and salt, not coins.

choices.text: The traders wrote letters to agree on a fair price
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions letters — the whole point was that the traders did not communicate at all.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Not Where You Think

You might think the Kingdom of Ghana sat inside the borders of the country called Ghana today. Actually, the ancient kingdom was hundreds of miles farther north, in the area we now call Mauritania and Mali. The modern country of Ghana chose its name later to honor Africa's great past. Same name, different place.
stimulus.content_text: Not Where You Think You might think the Kingdom of Ghana sat inside the borders of the country called Ghana today. Actually, the ancient kingdom was hundreds of miles farther north, in the area we now call Mauritania and Mali. The modern country of Ghana chose its name later to honor Africa's great past. Same name, different place.

prompt._: The main idea of the text is that the ancient Kingdom of Ghana and the modern country are located in different places. Which detail explains why they still share the same name?

choices.text: The modern country was built on the ancient kingdom.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the ancient kingdom was hundreds of miles away from where the modern country is located.

choices.text: The modern country wanted to honor Africa's great past.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the modern country chose the name 'to honor Africa's great past.'

choices.text: The ancient kingdom moved inside the modern borders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because kingdoms don't move — the ancient kingdom was always in the area now called Mauritania and Mali.

choices.text: The ancient kingdom controlled the modern trade routes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions trade routes, so there is no evidence to support this idea.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Two Treasures That Made a Kingdom Rich

Ghana's power came from two resources — useful things found in nature. South of Ghana, miners dug gold from the earth. North of Ghana, in the burning Sahara Desert, workers cut huge blocks of salt from underground deposits — places where a material has built up over a long time.

Gold was easy to understand. People wanted it for jewelry and coins. But salt? Here's the surprising part: in ancient West Africa, salt was just as precious as gold. Imagine paying for a bag of salt with a bag of gold coins. That sounds wild today, because you can buy salt for less than a dollar. But far from the ocean and far from the desert salt mines, salt was rare. People needed it to keep food from going bad. They needed it to stay healthy in the heat. Without salt, people got sick. They would pay almost anything for it.
stimulus.content_text: Two Treasures That Made a Kingdom Rich Ghana's power came from two resources — useful things found in nature. South of Ghana, miners dug gold from the earth. North of Ghana, in the burning Sahara Desert, workers cut huge blocks of salt from underground deposits — places where a material has built up over a long time. Gold was easy to understand. People wanted it for jewelry and coins. But salt? Here's the surprising part: in ancient West Africa, salt was just as precious as gold. Imagine paying for a bag of salt with a bag of gold coins. That sounds wild today, because you can buy salt for less than a dollar. But far from the ocean and far from the desert salt mines, salt was rare. People needed it to keep food from going bad. They needed it to stay healthy in the heat. Without salt, people got sick. They would pay almost anything for it.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happened because salt was rare in places far from the ocean and desert mines?

choices.text: Workers moved the salt mines closer to the ocean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone moving the salt mines — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

choices.text: People stopped using salt to make jewelry and coins
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says gold was used for jewelry and coins, not salt — and nothing in the passage says people stopped using salt that way.

choices.text: People would pay almost anything to get salt
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says salt was rare far from the ocean and mines, and people needed it so badly that they 'would pay almost anything for it.'

choices.text: Miners began digging for gold instead of salt
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says miners switched from salt to gold — both resources were being collected at the same time.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The King Who Taxed Every Trade

The king of Ghana did not own the gold mines or the salt deposits. He did something smarter. He controlled the roads between them. Every merchant carrying gold north or salt south had to pay a tax — a payment to the king, like a toll you pay to cross a bridge. These taxes made Ghana's kings enormously rich.

The kings spent that wealth on a strong army. Their soldiers carried weapons tipped with iron, a metal that was harder and sharper than the wood and stone tools their neighbors used. The army kept the trade roads safe so merchants would keep coming — and keep paying taxes.
stimulus.content_text: The King Who Taxed Every Trade The king of Ghana did not own the gold mines or the salt deposits. He did something smarter. He controlled the roads between them. Every merchant carrying gold north or salt south had to pay a tax — a payment to the king, like a toll you pay to cross a bridge. These taxes made Ghana's kings enormously rich. The kings spent that wealth on a strong army. Their soldiers carried weapons tipped with iron, a metal that was harder and sharper than the wood and stone tools their neighbors used. The army kept the trade roads safe so merchants would keep coming — and keep paying taxes.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares a tax to a toll you pay to cross a bridge. What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word 'tax'?

choices.text: a gift given to the king for protection
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a gift is something you choose to give freely, but a tax is a required payment, not a choice.

choices.text: a payment required to use something
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares a tax to a toll, and both are payments you must make in order to use something — like a road or a bridge.

choices.text: a fine charged for breaking a rule
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the merchants weren't being punished for breaking a rule; they were simply paying to use the trade roads.

choices.text: a reward earned for carrying goods
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a reward is something you receive for doing something good, but a tax is money you have to pay, not something you earn.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Rise and Fall

Ghana flourished — grew strong and successful, like a garden in perfect sunlight — from about the year 500 to the 1200s. That is roughly seven hundred years of power.

But nothing lasts forever. Herds of animals ate too much grass, and fertile land — soil rich enough to grow crops — dried up and turned to dust. Enemy groups attacked Ghana's borders. New trade roads went around Ghana instead of through it. The kingdom weakened.

Then a new power rose: the Mali Empire. Mali conquered Ghana and grew even larger and wealthier. The story of great West African kingdoms did not end with Ghana. It grew bigger.
stimulus.content_text: The Rise and Fall Ghana flourished — grew strong and successful, like a garden in perfect sunlight — from about the year 500 to the 1200s. That is roughly seven hundred years of power. But nothing lasts forever. Herds of animals ate too much grass, and fertile land — soil rich enough to grow crops — dried up and turned to dust. Enemy groups attacked Ghana's borders. New trade roads went around Ghana instead of through it. The kingdom weakened. Then a new power rose: the Mali Empire. Mali conquered Ghana and grew even larger and wealthier. The story of great West African kingdoms did not end with Ghana. It grew bigger.

prompt._: In the article "Gold and Salt: The Kingdom of Ghana," what does the word "fertile" mean?

choices.text: soil that is crossed by trade roads
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because trade roads are about buying and selling, not about what makes soil good for growing food.

choices.text: soil that is guarded by enemies
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because enemies attacked Ghana's borders, but that has nothing to do with what the word 'fertile' means.

choices.text: soil that is dry and dusty
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'dry and dusty' is what the fertile land turned INTO after it was ruined — that's the opposite of fertile.

choices.text: soil that is rich for crops
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines 'fertile' as 'soil rich enough to grow crops,' which matches this answer perfectly.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Salt Still Matters

Tonight, you might shake salt onto your dinner without a thought. A thousand years ago in West Africa, that same salt could have bought you gold. Ghana's kings understood something simple and powerful: if you stand between what people have and what people need, you hold the power. That idea is as true in your world as it was in theirs.
stimulus.content_text: Why Salt Still Matters Tonight, you might shake salt onto your dinner without a thought. A thousand years ago in West Africa, that same salt could have bought you gold. Ghana's kings understood something simple and powerful: if you stand between what people have and what people need, you hold the power. That idea is as true in your world as it was in theirs.

prompt._: How does the author support the point that salt was extremely valuable a thousand years ago?

choices.text: By stating that people could trade it for gold.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says salt could be traded for gold a thousand years ago, which proves it was extremely valuable.

choices.text: By describing how people use it at dinner today.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because using salt at dinner today is used to show how common salt is now, not how valuable it was in the past.

choices.text: By explaining how kings shared it with the people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions kings sharing salt — it says kings controlled it to hold power.

choices.text: By listing the places where it was easily found.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never lists any places where salt was found or available.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did the trade roads change from the time Ghana was powerful to the time it weakened?

choices.text: They brought merchants safely through Ghana, but later they went around the kingdom.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says merchants once traveled safely through Ghana, but later 'new trade roads went around Ghana instead of through it.'

choices.text: They were covered in rich and green grass, but later they dried up into plain dust.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says fertile land dried up into dust, not the roads — those are two different things.

choices.text: They were controlled by the wealthy kings, but later they were owned by the miners.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says miners owned or took control of the trade roads.

choices.text: They were used to carry gold to the north, but later they carried salt to the south.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gold moving north and salt moving south describes how trade worked all along, not a change that happened over time.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did the location of Ghana cause its kings to become enormously rich?

choices.text: The kingdom was hidden inside the modern country, allowing the kings to avoid all enemy attacks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ancient Kingdom of Ghana is not inside the modern country of Ghana, and the article says enemy groups did attack and eventually weaken the kingdom.

choices.text: The kingdom sat between the gold and salt, allowing the kings to tax the trade roads.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Ghana sat between the gold mines and salt deposits, letting the kings charge taxes on every trader who used the roads between them.

choices.text: The kingdom was located next to the ocean, allowing the kings to trade easily with ships.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions an ocean or ships — Ghana was an inland kingdom far from the sea.

choices.text: The kingdom was built on top of salt deposits, allowing the kings to sell the salt.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the salt came from the Sahara Desert to the north of Ghana, not from underneath the kingdom itself.

prompt._: How does the author organize the history of the Kingdom of Ghana in the passage?

choices.text: By describing the problems the kingdom faced and showing how kings solved them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't set up a series of problems for the king to solve — it tells the kingdom's whole history from rise to fall.

choices.text: By listing the most valuable trade goods first and then the least valuable ones.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never ranks trade goods in any order of value — it actually says gold and salt were equally precious.

choices.text: By comparing the ancient kingdom's borders to the locations of modern countries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because comparing the ancient kingdom's location to modern countries is only mentioned briefly in one small section, not the main way the whole passage is organized.

choices.text: By explaining how the kingdom gained wealth, stayed strong, and eventually fell.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage follows Ghana's story from how it got rich (gold and salt taxes), to how it stayed powerful (army and iron weapons), to how it finally fell (dry land, enemies, and new trade roads).

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how the author compares gold and salt throughout the passage?

choices.text: Both were used to create beautiful jewelry and coins for the local people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only gold was used for jewelry and coins — salt was used to preserve food and keep people healthy.

choices.text: Both were traded in secret because they were too heavy to easily transport.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because silent trading was done to avoid face-to-face contact with strangers, not because the goods were too heavy to transport.

choices.text: Both were mined in the hot desert and given directly to the king's army.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only salt came from the desert, gold came from south of Ghana, and neither was given directly to the army.

choices.text: Both were highly valued resources that made the kingdom extremely wealthy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says salt was 'just as precious as gold' and both resources together made Ghana's kings enormously wealthy through taxes.

assessments.title: Mansa Musa: The Richest Man Who Ever Lived

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Mansa Musa: The Richest Man Who Broke Egypt's Economy

A Golden Parade Across the Desert

Eighty camels walked in a long line across the hot sand. Each one carried 300 pounds of gold on its back. Behind them marched 60,000 people — soldiers, servants, and travelers. The line stretched so far that you could not see the end of it. This enormous traveling group was a caravan — a large group of people journeying together, like a whole town getting up and walking across the desert. And leading it all was one man: Mansa Musa, emperor of Mali.
stimulus.content_text: Mansa Musa: The Richest Man Who Broke Egypt's EconomyA Golden Parade Across the Desert Eighty camels walked in a long line across the hot sand. Each one carried 300 pounds of gold on its back. Behind them marched 60,000 people — soldiers, servants, and travelers. The line stretched so far that you could not see the end of it. This enormous traveling group was a caravan — a large group of people journeying together, like a whole town getting up and walking across the desert. And leading it all was one man: Mansa Musa, emperor of Mali.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Mansa Musa's role different from the role of the 60,000 people who marched behind the camels?

choices.text: He carried 300 pounds of gold while they walked empty-handed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the camels carried the gold, not Mansa Musa himself.

choices.text: He led the entire caravan while they followed as part of it
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Mansa Musa was 'leading it all' while the 60,000 people marched behind as part of the caravan.

choices.text: He rode on a camel while they traveled the desert on foot
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never tells us whether Mansa Musa rode a camel or walked — that detail simply isn't there.

choices.text: He planned the trip alone while they joined at the last moment
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about how the trip was planned or when people decided to join.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
An Empire Built on Gold and Salt

Long before Europeans ever visited, African people built powerful empires with organized governments and busy trade cities. The Mali Empire was one of the greatest. It stretched across a wide part of West Africa from about 1235 to 1600.

Mali grew rich from trade. Merchants crossed the Sahara carrying gold and salt between southern and northern Africa. The Kingdom of Ghana had grown powerful from these same trade routes before Mali. But Ghana weakened over time, and Mali rose up even larger and stronger in its place.

Mansa Musa ruled Mali from about 1312 to 1337. His title, "Mansa," means "king of kings" or "emperor." Under his rule, Mali became one of the wealthiest empires on Earth.
stimulus.content_text: An Empire Built on Gold and Salt Long before Europeans ever visited, African people built powerful empires with organized governments and busy trade cities. The Mali Empire was one of the greatest. It stretched across a wide part of West Africa from about 1235 to 1600. Mali grew rich from trade. Merchants crossed the Sahara carrying gold and salt between southern and northern Africa. The Kingdom of Ghana had grown powerful from these same trade routes before Mali. But Ghana weakened over time, and Mali rose up even larger and stronger in its place. Mansa Musa ruled Mali from about 1312 to 1337. His title, "Mansa," means "king of kings" or "emperor." Under his rule, Mali became one of the wealthiest empires on Earth.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did the Mali Empire become so wealthy?

choices.text: Mansa Musa discovered gold mines in Africa
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions Mansa Musa finding gold mines — it only says he ruled during a time when Mali was already very wealthy from trade.

choices.text: It conquered the Kingdom of Ghana in battle
choices.feedback: The passage never says Mali fought or conquered Ghana — it only says Ghana weakened over time and Mali rose up in its place.

choices.text: Europeans brought riches to its trade cities
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the opposite — it tells us Europeans had not even visited Africa yet during Mali's rise.

choices.text: It profited from gold and salt trade
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says Mali grew rich from merchants trading gold and salt across the Sahara.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Journey That Stunned the World

Mansa Musa was a devoted Muslim. He wanted to make the Hajj — a holy trip, or pilgrimage, that Muslims make to the city of Mecca in Arabia. In 1324, he set off with his giant caravan.

When Musa passed through Egypt, he gave gold to everyone — poor people on the street, officials, and merchants. He spent lavishly — freely and in great amounts — in the markets of Cairo. He gave away so much gold that something strange happened. Gold became easy to find, so it lost its value. If everyone at your school had a thousand toys, each toy would feel less special. That drop in value is called inflation — when there is so much of something that prices get mixed up and money buys less. Egypt's economy struggled for years.
stimulus.content_text: The Journey That Stunned the World Mansa Musa was a devoted Muslim. He wanted to make the Hajj — a holy trip, or pilgrimage, that Muslims make to the city of Mecca in Arabia. In 1324, he set off with his giant caravan. When Musa passed through Egypt, he gave gold to everyone — poor people on the street, officials, and merchants. He spent lavishly — freely and in great amounts — in the markets of Cairo. He gave away so much gold that something strange happened. Gold became easy to find, so it lost its value. If everyone at your school had a thousand toys, each toy would feel less special. That drop in value is called inflation — when there is so much of something that prices get mixed up and money buys less. Egypt's economy struggled for years.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happened in Egypt after Mansa Musa gave away so much gold?

choices.text: Mansa Musa returned to Egypt to collect his gold
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Mansa Musa returned to Egypt; he gave the gold away freely as part of his pilgrimage.

choices.text: Gold became harder to find and prices went down
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says gold became *easier* to find, not harder, and inflation means money buys *less*, so prices go up, not down.

choices.text: Merchants stopped selling goods in Cairo's markets
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions merchants stopping their sales — it only talks about gold losing its value.

choices.text: Gold lost its value and Egypt's economy struggled
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that gold lost its value and that Egypt's economy struggled for years after Musa gave away so much gold.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A City of Books and Learning

Mansa Musa was not just showing off. He was building something lasting. On his way home, he brought back scholars, architects, and books. He hired the architects to design mosques — beautiful buildings where Muslims gather to pray, like churches or temples for the Islamic faith. He built schools where students studied math, science, law, and religion.

You might think Timbuktu is a made-up place. Actually, it is a real city that still exists today in the modern country of Mali. Under Mansa Musa, Timbuktu became one of the greatest centers of learning in the world.
stimulus.content_text: A City of Books and Learning Mansa Musa was not just showing off. He was building something lasting. On his way home, he brought back scholars, architects, and books. He hired the architects to design mosques — beautiful buildings where Muslims gather to pray, like churches or temples for the Islamic faith. He built schools where students studied math, science, law, and religion. You might think Timbuktu is a made-up place. Actually, it is a real city that still exists today in the modern country of Mali. Under Mansa Musa, Timbuktu became one of the greatest centers of learning in the world.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares mosques to 'churches or temples' in order to help the reader understand that mosques are what?

choices.text: schools where students learn about religion
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes schools as separate buildings where students study — mosques are for prayer, not for learning like a school.

choices.text: places where architects design new things
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because architects are the people who design buildings, but that is not what a mosque is — a mosque is a place where people go to pray.

choices.text: buildings where people gather to pray
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly tells us mosques are 'buildings where Muslims gather to pray,' and compares them to churches and temples, which are also places of prayer.

choices.text: libraries where scholars collect old books
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never describes mosques as libraries or places to collect books — those details are about Timbuktu's learning centers, not mosques.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A King on the Map

Mansa Musa's fame reached Europe. In 1375, he appeared on the famous Catalan Atlas — a detailed hand-drawn map of the known world made in Spain. The map showed Musa sitting on a golden throne, holding a gold nugget. For many Europeans, this drawing was their first glimpse of the wealth and power of African kingdoms.

Mansa Musa died around 1337. But the schools, mosques, and libraries he built kept teaching long after he was gone. Today, when people say "Timbuktu" to mean someplace impossibly far away, they are naming a real city — one that held some of the most valuable books and brightest minds on the planet, thanks to an emperor and eighty camels loaded with gold.

The map showed Musa sitting on a golden throne, holding a gold nugget. For many Europeans, this drawing was their first glimpse of the wealth and power of African kingdoms. Mansa Musa died around 1337. But the schools, mosques, and libraries he built kept teaching long after he was gone. Today, when people say "Timbuktu" to mean someplace impossibly far away, they are naming a real city — one that held some of the most valuable books and brightest minds on the planet, thanks to an emperor and eighty camels loaded with gold.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the Catalan Atlas?

choices.text: A hand-drawn map of the world made in Spain.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly describes the Catalan Atlas as 'a detailed hand-drawn map of the known world made in Spain.'

choices.text: A famous school that taught people about African kings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the Catalan Atlas as a map, not a school — the schools were things Mansa Musa built in his empire.

choices.text: A large golden throne where the African emperor sat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the golden throne is something pictured in a drawing on the map, not what the Catalan Atlas itself is.

prompt._: According to the passage, how were Mansa Musa's actions in Egypt different from his actions in Timbuktu?

choices.text: In Egypt he sold books to scholars, but in Timbuktu he bought a golden throne.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Mansa Musa never sold books in Egypt, and the golden throne shown on the Catalan Atlas was just a drawing made by mapmakers in Spain, not something he bought.

choices.text: In Egypt he gave away his gold, but in Timbuktu he built places to learn.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly says he gave away gold in Egypt and built schools and mosques in Timbuktu.

choices.text: In Egypt he hired famous architects, but in Timbuktu he gave gold to travelers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Mansa Musa hired architects on his way home to Timbuktu, not in Egypt, and he gave gold to people in Egypt, not to travelers.

choices.text: In Egypt he traded salt with merchants, but in Timbuktu he caused prices to drop.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Mansa Musa did not trade salt in Egypt — he gave away gold — and it was his gold-giving in Egypt that caused prices to drop, not anything he did in Timbuktu.

prompt._: What most likely caused Mansa Musa to become famous far beyond Mali?

choices.text: His soldiers helped Timbuktu become the capital of Spain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Musa's soldiers going to Spain, and Timbuktu is in Mali, not Spain.

choices.text: His maps made Europeans want to trade with Mali.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Musa didn't make maps — it was other people who drew him on the Catalan Atlas after hearing about his famous journey.

choices.text: His schools made Egypt's economy stronger after his visit.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Musa's gold-giving actually caused inflation and hurt Egypt's economy for years, not helped it.

choices.text: His gold-filled journey showed other lands his empire's wealth.
choices.feedback: This is correct because his massive caravan loaded with gold traveled through other lands, and word of his incredible wealth spread all the way to Europe, even landing him a spot on a famous map.

prompt._: How does the author organize the events of Mansa Musa's famous journey in the text?

choices.text: By describing his trip toward Mecca first, and then explaining his return trip to Mali.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage first describes Musa giving away gold in Egypt on his way to Mecca, and then describes him bringing back scholars and building schools on his return trip to Mali.

choices.text: By describing his eighty loaded camels first, and then explaining the African salt trade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the camels are mentioned at the very beginning as an introduction, and the salt trade is a separate detail about Mali's wealth — neither one organizes the journey itself.

choices.text: By describing his problems in Egypt first, and then explaining his solutions in Timbuktu.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author does not set up the journey as a problem-and-solution story; instead, the events are simply told in the order they happened during the trip.

choices.text: By describing his return trip to Mali first, and then explaining his trip toward Mecca.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author describes the trip toward Mecca (and Egypt) before describing the return journey home to Mali, not the other way around.

prompt._: How are the details about the giant caravan and the Catalan Atlas similar in supporting the main idea of the article?

choices.text: They both show that Mansa Musa shared his riches with the poor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Mansa Musa did give gold to poor people in Egypt, the Catalan Atlas is about his fame in Europe, not about sharing with the poor.

choices.text: They both explain how Mansa Musa created a great city of learning.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither the caravan nor the Catalan Atlas is about building schools or making Timbuktu a center of learning — that is a different part of the article.

choices.text: They both describe how Mansa Musa caused prices to rise in Egypt.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the caravan story is connected to gold causing inflation in Egypt, and the Catalan Atlas has nothing to do with prices rising.

choices.text: They both prove that Mansa Musa's wealth made him famous far away.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the giant caravan showed his amazing wealth to people across Africa and Egypt, and the Catalan Atlas shows his fame reached all the way to Europe — both prove his wealth made him famous far away.

assessments.title: Timbuktu: City of Books and Learning

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Timbuktu: The City Worth More Than Gold

A Real Place, Not a Fairy Tale

Picture a line of one thousand camels stretching across golden sand dunes, loaded with heavy white slabs of salt. The caravan — a long train of animals and traders traveling together — walked for weeks across the burning Sahara Desert. Their destination? A real city called Timbuktu.

You might think Timbuktu is a made-up place. People today use the name to mean "the most faraway spot on Earth." But Timbuktu is very real. It still exists in the West African country of Mali, near the Niger River. And for hundreds of years, it was one of the most important cities in the world.
stimulus.content_text: Timbuktu: The City Worth More Than GoldA Real Place, Not a Fairy Tale Picture a line of one thousand camels stretching across golden sand dunes, loaded with heavy white slabs of salt. The caravan — a long train of animals and traders traveling together — walked for weeks across the burning Sahara Desert. Their destination? A real city called Timbuktu. You might think Timbuktu is a made-up place. People today use the name to mean "the most faraway spot on Earth." But Timbuktu is very real. It still exists in the West African country of Mali, near the Niger River. And for hundreds of years, it was one of the most important cities in the world.

prompt._: According to the text, how does the real Timbuktu contrast with the way people use its name today?

choices.text: It was a small village with very few people in it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes Timbuktu as a major, important city, not a small village with few people.

choices.text: It was a fairy-tale kingdom found only in stories
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually starts by saying Timbuktu is 'a real place, not a fairy tale,' and confirms it still exists in Mali today.

choices.text: It was an important city, not just a faraway place
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly contrasts how people use the name today (meaning 'faraway') with the real city, which 'was one of the most important cities in the world.'

choices.text: It was a dangerous desert that traders tried to avoid
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says traders avoided Timbuktu — in fact, caravans of a thousand camels traveled there on purpose!

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Where the Desert Meets the River

Timbuktu sits right where the dry Sahara Desert meets the greener land to the south. You might think the Sahara blocked people from traveling between different parts of Africa. Actually, traders crossed it all the time — it just took courage and camels.

These trans-Saharan trade routes — paths that went across the Sahara — connected West Africa to North Africa and lands near the Mediterranean Sea. From the north, traders brought salt, cloth, and goods from the Middle East. From the south came gold, ivory (white material from elephant tusks), and enslaved people who were forced to work without pay or freedom. Timbuktu sat right in the middle, like a crossroads. Merchants from across Africa and the Islamic world — the many countries where people followed the religion of Islam — filled its busy markets. The city grew rich from all this buying and selling.
stimulus.content_text: Where the Desert Meets the River Timbuktu sits right where the dry Sahara Desert meets the greener land to the south. You might think the Sahara blocked people from traveling between different parts of Africa. Actually, traders crossed it all the time — it just took courage and camels. These trans-Saharan trade routes — paths that went across the Sahara — connected West Africa to North Africa and lands near the Mediterranean Sea. From the north, traders brought salt, cloth, and goods from the Middle East. From the south came gold, ivory (white material from elephant tusks), and enslaved people who were forced to work without pay or freedom. Timbuktu sat right in the middle, like a crossroads. Merchants from across Africa and the Islamic world — the many countries where people followed the religion of Islam — filled its busy markets. The city grew rich from all this buying and selling.

prompt._: According to the passage, what caused Timbuktu to become a rich city?

choices.text: The buying and selling of goods in the markets
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says 'The city grew rich from all this buying and selling' in its busy markets.

choices.text: The courage of travelers crossing the dry desert
choices.feedback: Courage helped traders cross the Sahara, but the passage never says courage is what made Timbuktu rich.

choices.text: The spread of the Islamic religion across Africa
choices.feedback: The Islamic world sent traders to Timbuktu, but the passage says the city's wealth came from trading, not from the spread of Islam.

choices.text: The discovery of gold and salt inside the desert
choices.feedback: Gold and salt came from different regions and were traded in Timbuktu, but they were not discovered inside the desert.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A King Who Put Timbuktu on the Map

Timbuktu belonged to the powerful Mali Empire. Its most famous ruler was Mansa Musa, which means "King Musa." He controlled enormous amounts of gold. In 1324, Mansa Musa traveled to the holy city of Mecca and gave away so much gold along the way that he stunned the entire world. He used his wealth to build up Timbuktu, including a grand mosque — a building where Muslims gather to pray — called the Djinguereber Mosque. It still stands today, built from mud brick and wood.
stimulus.content_text: A King Who Put Timbuktu on the Map Timbuktu belonged to the powerful Mali Empire. Its most famous ruler was Mansa Musa, which means "King Musa." He controlled enormous amounts of gold. In 1324, Mansa Musa traveled to the holy city of Mecca and gave away so much gold along the way that he stunned the entire world. He used his wealth to build up Timbuktu, including a grand mosque — a building where Muslims gather to pray — called the Djinguereber Mosque. It still stands today, built from mud brick and wood.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Mansa Musa do after he traveled to Mecca and gave away gold?

choices.text: He moved the Mali Empire to Mecca.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Mansa Musa moved the Mali Empire to Mecca — he only traveled there and then came back.

choices.text: He traveled to Timbuktu for the first time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Timbuktu already belonged to the Mali Empire that Mansa Musa ruled, so it was not his first time there.

choices.text: He used his wealth to build up Timbuktu.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states he 'used his wealth to build up Timbuktu,' including building the Djinguereber Mosque.

choices.text: He discovered gold mines near Timbuktu.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Mansa Musa discovering any gold mines near Timbuktu.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Books Worth More Than Gold

Here's the surprising part. Timbuktu became even more famous for knowledge than for gold. The University of Sankore attracted students and scholars from across Africa and beyond. At its busiest, about 25,000 students studied there.

Students learned theology (the study of religion), law, math, astronomy, medicine, and history. Scholars wrote new books, copied old ones by hand, and collected manuscripts — handwritten documents, like books created before printing presses existed. Timbuktu's libraries held hundreds of thousands of these manuscripts. Wealthy families treasured their private book collections and passed them down to their children and grandchildren. In Timbuktu, people said books were worth more than gold.

Many of those manuscripts survive today. Families in Mali hid them in basements, closets, and desert caves for centuries. Now, scholars are working to protect these old pages and digitize them — scan them into computers so people everywhere can read them.
stimulus.content_text: Books Worth More Than Gold Here's the surprising part. Timbuktu became even more famous for knowledge than for gold. The University of Sankore attracted students and scholars from across Africa and beyond. At its busiest, about 25,000 students studied there. Students learned theology (the study of religion), law, math, astronomy, medicine, and history. Scholars wrote new books, copied old ones by hand, and collected manuscripts — handwritten documents, like books created before printing presses existed. Timbuktu's libraries held hundreds of thousands of these manuscripts. Wealthy families treasured their private book collections and passed them down to their children and grandchildren. In Timbuktu, people said books were worth more than gold. Many of those manuscripts survive today. Families in Mali hid them in basements, closets, and desert caves for centuries. Now, scholars are working to protect these old pages and digitize them — scan them into computers so people everywhere can read them.

prompt._: In the passage, the phrase 'books were worth more than gold' compares books to gold in order to show that books were what?

choices.text: made with rare materials similar to gold
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions books being made from gold or any other rare materials.

choices.text: valued more for the knowledge they contained
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows people treasured books for the knowledge they held — students traveled far to learn, and families protected manuscripts for centuries.

choices.text: collected mainly by the wealthiest gold traders
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while wealthy families did collect books, the passage does not say these people were specifically gold traders.

choices.text: traded at markets for higher prices than gold
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about books being bought or sold at markets or having a higher price tag than gold.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Timbuktu Teaches Us

Three great mosques rose above Timbuktu's skyline. Thousands of students filled its classrooms. Hundreds of thousands of handwritten books lined its shelves. All of this happened while many cities in Europe had no universities at all.

Next time someone says "Timbuktu" to mean a place that barely exists, you will know the truth. It was a city where people crossed a giant desert just to read a book — because they believed knowledge was the most valuable thing on Earth.
stimulus.content_text: What Timbuktu Teaches Us Three great mosques rose above Timbuktu's skyline. Thousands of students filled its classrooms. Hundreds of thousands of handwritten books lined its shelves. All of this happened while many cities in Europe had no universities at all. Next time someone says "Timbuktu" to mean a place that barely exists, you will know the truth. It was a city where people crossed a giant desert just to read a book — because they believed knowledge was the most valuable thing on Earth.

prompt._: According to the passage, what do some people mean when they say the word "Timbuktu"?

choices.text: A city with no universities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says cities in Europe had no universities, not Timbuktu — Timbuktu actually had lots of students and learning.

choices.text: A town that is very crowded.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anyone uses the word 'Timbuktu' to describe a crowded town.

choices.text: A desert with many books.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Timbuktu was a great city, not a desert, even though people did cross a desert to get there.

choices.text: A place that barely exists.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses those exact words — 'a place that barely exists' — to describe what some people mean when they say 'Timbuktu.'

prompt._: Timbuktu first became rich from trade, but what did the city later become even more famous for instead?

choices.text: controlling the paths across the Sahara Desert
choices.feedback: Controlling the Sahara trade routes helped Timbuktu become rich first, but the question asks what it became famous for later instead of gold.

choices.text: giving away gold to people in other countries
choices.feedback: Giving away gold describes Mansa Musa's personal journey to Mecca in 1324, not what the entire city of Timbuktu became known for.

choices.text: collecting knowledge and handwritten books
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says Timbuktu became 'even more famous for knowledge than for gold,' pointing to its university and hundreds of thousands of handwritten manuscripts.

choices.text: building mosques out of mud brick and wood
choices.feedback: Building mosques, like the Djinguereber Mosque, was something Mansa Musa did with his wealth, but it wasn't what made Timbuktu most famous.

prompt._: Based on details from across the article, what is the main idea the author wants readers to understand about why Timbuktu became such an important city?

choices.text: Its libraries were built before any other libraries existed in the world
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never claims Timbuktu had the world's first libraries — it only says the libraries held hundreds of thousands of manuscripts.

choices.text: Its location in the desert made it impossible for outsiders to attack
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions the desert keeping Timbuktu safe from enemies — it actually says traders crossed the desert all the time.

choices.text: Its position as a crossroads led to wealth and then to great learning
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows that Timbuktu's crossroads location brought trade and gold first, and that wealth then helped build the university and libraries that made it famous for learning.

choices.text: Its king gave away gold, which made other countries want to visit
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Mansa Musa's gold journey made him famous, the article explains that Timbuktu's importance came from much more than just one king's generosity.

prompt._: Which event happened last in Timbuktu's story?

choices.text: Mansa Musa built the Djinguereber Mosque in the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Mansa Musa built the Djinguereber Mosque in 1324, which was about 700 years ago.

choices.text: Students came to Sankore to study math and history.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because students studying at the University of Sankore also happened hundreds of years ago, during Timbuktu's golden age.

choices.text: Traders crossed the Sahara to buy and sell goods there.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because traders crossing the Sahara happened hundreds of years ago, during Timbuktu's early days as a trading city.

choices.text: Scholars began scanning old manuscripts into computers.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because digitizing manuscripts is happening *now*, in the present day, making it the most recent event in the passage.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did Timbuktu compare to many cities in Europe during its most successful years?

choices.text: Timbuktu had a large university and many books, while European cities often lacked universities.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that Timbuktu had a huge university and hundreds of thousands of books 'while many cities in Europe had no universities at all.'

choices.text: Timbuktu hid its handwritten manuscripts in caves, while European cities kept theirs in large mosques.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hiding manuscripts in caves was something families did later to protect them, and mosques in Timbuktu were places of prayer, not libraries.

choices.text: Timbuktu focused mostly on trading salt and gold, while European cities focused on teaching students.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the comparison — Timbuktu was famous for both trade AND learning, and the article never says European cities focused on teaching students.

choices.text: Timbuktu was a faraway place that barely existed, while European cities were busy and wealthy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the whole point of the article is to prove that Timbuktu was NOT a faraway place that barely existed — it was actually one of the most important cities in the world.

assessments.title: The Songhai Empire: West Africa's Largest Kingdom

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Songhai Empire: West Africa's Biggest Kingdom

War Canoes on the Niger River

Hundreds of wooden canoes filled with soldiers sliced through the brown water of the Niger River. On the riverbanks, warriors on horseback charged forward. Together, they swept toward the city of Timbuktu. A commander named Sunni Ali was building an empire.

The Songhai people had lived along the Niger River for centuries. For a long time, they followed the rules of the Mali Empire. But the Songhai were a different people with their own language, customs, and leaders. You might think each West African empire was just the old one with a new name. Actually, Ghana, Mali, and Songhai were separate groups who rose to power in their own ways. As Mali grew weaker, the Songhai broke free and began to build something even bigger.
stimulus.content_text: The Songhai Empire: West Africa's Biggest KingdomWar Canoes on the Niger River Hundreds of wooden canoes filled with soldiers sliced through the brown water of the Niger River. On the riverbanks, warriors on horseback charged forward. Together, they swept toward the city of Timbuktu. A commander named Sunni Ali was building an empire. The Songhai people had lived along the Niger River for centuries. For a long time, they followed the rules of the Mali Empire. But the Songhai were a different people with their own language, customs, and leaders. You might think each West African empire was just the old one with a new name. Actually, Ghana, Mali, and Songhai were separate groups who rose to power in their own ways. As Mali grew weaker, the Songhai broke free and began to build something even bigger.

prompt._: According to the text, how were the Songhai different from the Mali Empire?

choices.text: They had their own language and leaders.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the Songhai were 'a different people with their own language, customs, and leaders.'

choices.text: They ruled the Niger River for centuries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because living along the Niger River for centuries describes where the Songhai lived, not how they were different from Mali.

choices.text: They became weaker than Mali.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Mali grew weaker, while the Songhai broke free and built something even bigger.

choices.text: They used the same language and leaders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Songhai had their own language and leaders, which is the opposite of sharing them with Mali.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Sunni Ali the Conqueror

Sunni Ali ruled from 1464 to 1492. He was a fearless fighter who captured city after city. His greatest prize was Timbuktu. His army also took Djenné, another busy trading center. Sunni Ali's soldiers rode horses across dry grasslands and paddled war canoes along the river. With both land and water forces, he controlled a huge stretch of West Africa — from the Atlantic coast to the borders of modern-day Nigeria.

At its largest, the Songhai Empire was the biggest empire in African history. This was one of the most organized and powerful states anywhere in the world during the 1400s and 1500s.
stimulus.content_text: Sunni Ali the Conqueror Sunni Ali ruled from 1464 to 1492. He was a fearless fighter who captured city after city. His greatest prize was Timbuktu. His army also took Djenné, another busy trading center. Sunni Ali's soldiers rode horses across dry grasslands and paddled war canoes along the river. With both land and water forces, he controlled a huge stretch of West Africa — from the Atlantic coast to the borders of modern-day Nigeria. At its largest, the Songhai Empire was the biggest empire in African history. This was one of the most organized and powerful states anywhere in the world during the 1400s and 1500s.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why was Sunni Ali able to control a huge stretch of West Africa?

choices.text: He ruled longer than other leaders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares how long Sunni Ali ruled to other leaders.

choices.text: He stayed close to the Atlantic coast.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Sunni Ali controlled a huge area stretching far inland, not just land close to the Atlantic coast.

choices.text: He traded more goods than nearby cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Sunni Ali conquered trading centers like Timbuktu and Djenné — he didn't win power by trading more goods.

choices.text: He used both land and water forces.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says his land and water forces together helped him control a huge stretch of West Africa.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Askia Muhammad the Organizer

After Sunni Ali died, a new leader named Askia Muhammad took power in 1493. He was not mainly a warrior. He was a builder of systems. Askia Muhammad divided the empire into provinces — smaller sections, like slicing a pizza into pieces so each piece can have its own leader. Each province had a governor in charge. He created a professional army, meaning soldiers who trained full-time, the way athletes practice every day. He also set up judges to settle disputes — arguments or disagreements — so people could solve problems fairly instead of fighting.

Askia Muhammad was also a devoted Muslim. Like Mansa Musa of Mali before him, he made a pilgrimage to Mecca — a long religious journey to Islam's holiest city. He invited scholars from across the Muslim world to come teach in Songhai's cities.
stimulus.content_text: Askia Muhammad the Organizer After Sunni Ali died, a new leader named Askia Muhammad took power in 1493. He was not mainly a warrior. He was a builder of systems. He created a professional army, meaning soldiers who trained full-time, the way athletes practice every day. He also set up judges to settle disputes — arguments or disagreements — so people could solve problems fairly instead of fighting. Askia Muhammad was also a devoted Muslim. He invited scholars from across the Muslim world to come teach in Songhai's cities.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened right before Askia Muhammad took power in 1493?

choices.text: Askia Muhammad fought.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says Askia Muhammad was NOT mainly a warrior — he was a builder of systems, not a fighter.

choices.text: Mansa Musa traveled.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Mansa Musa's travels happened in the Mali empire and are not connected to when Askia Muhammad took power in Songhai.

choices.text: Governors took charge.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because governors taking charge was something Askia Muhammad set up after he became leader, not something that happened before he took power.

choices.text: Sunni Ali died.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'After Sunni Ali died, a new leader named Askia Muhammad took power in 1493,' making Sunni Ali's death the event right before Askia Muhammad took over.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A City of Books

Under Askia Muhammad, Timbuktu became a world-class center of learning. Students studied there, and scholars wrote and collected books. The city's wealth helped make this possible. Merchants in Songhai's cities bought and sold gold and salt — two things people needed badly. Gold was used for money and jewelry. Salt kept food from spoiling. These trades made Songhai's cities rich and busy, supporting the schools and libraries that made Timbuktu famous.
stimulus.content_text: A City of Books Under Askia Muhammad, Timbuktu became a world-class center of learning. Students studied there, and scholars wrote and collected books. The city's wealth helped make this possible. Merchants in Songhai's cities bought and sold gold and salt — two things people needed badly. Gold was used for money and jewelry. Salt kept food from spoiling. These trades made Songhai's cities rich and busy, supporting the schools and libraries that made Timbuktu famous.

prompt._: How were gold and salt alike in Songhai?

choices.text: They were both used to store food.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because salt was used to keep food from spoiling, but gold was never mentioned as being used to store food.

choices.text: They were both traded in the cities.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly says merchants bought and sold both gold AND salt in Songhai's cities, making trading the one thing they shared.

choices.text: They were both used for jewelry.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says only gold was used for jewelry — salt was not used for jewelry.

choices.text: They were both used as money.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says only gold was used for money — it never says salt was used as money.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Guns Against Swords

For nearly a hundred years, the Songhai Empire was one of the most powerful states on Earth. Then, in 1591, an army from Morocco crossed the burning Sahara Desert to attack.

The Songhai soldiers were brave and skilled, but they fought with swords, spears, and arrows. The Moroccan army carried guns and cannons. These gunpowder weapons were newer technology that had spread through parts of North Africa and Europe but had not yet reached West Africa. It wasn't that the Songhai couldn't build great things — their cities and schools proved they could. Gunpowder simply hadn't traveled there yet. The Songhai could not match the blast of cannons. The empire fell fast.
stimulus.content_text: Guns Against Swords For nearly a hundred years, the Songhai Empire was one of the most powerful states on Earth. Then, in 1591, an army from Morocco crossed the burning Sahara Desert to attack. The Songhai soldiers were brave and skilled, but they fought with swords, spears, and arrows. The Moroccan army carried guns and cannons. These gunpowder weapons were newer technology that had spread through parts of North Africa and Europe but had not yet reached West Africa. It wasn't that the Songhai couldn't build great things — their cities and schools proved they could. Gunpowder simply hadn't traveled there yet. The Songhai could not match the blast of cannons. The empire fell fast.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the word "blast" mean in the sentence "The Songhai could not match the blast of cannons"?

choices.text: the careful building of city walls
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because building city walls has nothing to do with cannons or the battle described in this sentence.

choices.text: the sharp swing of metal swords
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because swords are actually the weapons the Songhai used, not the Moroccan cannons that the word 'blast' is describing.

choices.text: the quick movement of desert winds
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage is talking about weapons in a fight, not the weather or wind in the desert.

choices.text: the loud force of cannon fire
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage is all about cannons being powerful gunpowder weapons, and 'blast' describes the loud, explosive force they fire — something the Songhai's swords and spears couldn't compete with.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Gold, Salt, and Books

The fall of Songhai ended nearly a thousand years of great West African empires. Ghana, Mali, and Songhai each rose and fell in turn. But the books written in Timbuktu did not disappear. Every page is proof that long before outsiders arrived, West Africans were building great cities, training scholars, and writing their own history — in their own words.
stimulus.content_text: Gold, Salt, and Books The fall of Songhai ended nearly a thousand years of great West African empires. Ghana, Mali, and Songhai each rose and fell in turn. But the books written in Timbuktu did not disappear. Every page is proof that long before outsiders arrived, West Africans were building great cities, training scholars, and writing their own history — in their own words.

prompt._: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that West Africans recorded their own history?

choices.text: the kingdoms lasted nearly a thousand years
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because how long the kingdoms lasted tells us about their strength over time, but it doesn't show that West Africans were writing down their own history.

choices.text: the books written in Timbuktu remained
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly connects the surviving books from Timbuktu to proof that West Africans wrote their own history in their own words.

choices.text: the empires rose and fell in turn
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because empires rising and falling is about political change, not about West Africans writing or recording their own history.

choices.text: the Songhai Empire came to an end
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the end of the Songhai Empire is about a kingdom falling, not about anyone recording or writing history.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between Sunni Ali and Askia Muhammad?

choices.text: Sunni Ali built schools, while Askia Muhammad led river attacks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the leaders switched — the passage says Askia Muhammad supported learning and schools, while Sunni Ali was the one who led river attacks with war canoes.

choices.text: Sunni Ali won land by fighting, while Askia Muhammad strengthened the empire by organizing it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly tells us Sunni Ali was a fighter who captured cities, while Askia Muhammad focused on organizing the empire with provinces, governors, and judges.

choices.text: Sunni Ali traded gold and salt, while Askia Muhammad wrote books in Timbuktu.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage connects both the gold and salt trade and the books in Timbuktu to Askia Muhammad's time, so this isn't a real contrast between the two leaders.

choices.text: Sunni Ali ruled after Morocco attacked, while Askia Muhammad ruled before Timbuktu was captured.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it gets the timeline backwards — Sunni Ali ruled from 1464 to 1492, long before Morocco attacked in 1591, and Timbuktu was captured by Sunni Ali, not taken from him.

prompt._: What best explains why Timbuktu became a famous center of learning during the Songhai Empire?

choices.text: Sunni Ali captured the city, so soldiers stayed there to teach students.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Sunni Ali did capture Timbuktu, the passage never says his soldiers became teachers there.

choices.text: Morocco attacked the empire, so scholars hurried to hide their books there.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Morocco's attack came after Timbuktu was already famous, and it helped end the empire rather than build up its learning.

choices.text: The empire used guns and cannons, so people came to study new weapons there.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that gunpowder weapons had not yet reached West Africa, so they could not have been a reason people came to study in Timbuktu.

choices.text: Trade made the city wealthy, so schools and libraries could grow there.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that gold and salt trade made Songhai's cities rich, and that wealth supported the schools and libraries that made Timbuktu famous.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information about the Songhai Empire across the passage?

choices.text: By listing the events of the empire's history, from its early rise to its sudden fall.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage follows the Songhai Empire through time, from Sunni Ali building it, to Askia Muhammad organizing it, to the Moroccan army defeating it in 1591.

choices.text: By explaining the causes of the army's struggles, from the dry deserts to the lost trades.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't explain the army's struggles as its main focus — the army's defeat is just one part of the bigger history.

choices.text: By comparing the traits of the different empires, from their first leaders to their modern cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't compare Ghana, Mali, and Songhai in detail — it mostly focuses on the Songhai Empire's own story.

choices.text: By describing the solutions to the people's problems, from the fair judges to the new weapons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage isn't organized around solving people's problems — it's telling the full rise and fall of an empire over time.

prompt._: Based on the text, what is a main idea about how the Songhai Empire compared to the Ghana and Mali empires?

choices.text: They were all separate groups that built powerful states and eventually fell.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Ghana, Mali, and Songhai were separate groups that each built powerful states and each eventually fell.

choices.text: They were all ruled by leaders who only focused on fighting wars.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Askia Muhammad was described as 'a builder of systems,' not a warrior, and he focused on organizing provinces, courts, and schools.

choices.text: They were all the exact same group of people who changed their names.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage directly says 'you might think each empire was just the old one with a new name' — but then explains they were actually different groups of people.

choices.text: They were all defeated by armies from Morocco who carried new weapons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the Songhai Empire was defeated by Morocco's army with guns and cannons; the passage does not say this happened to Ghana or Mali.

assessments.title: The Golden Ages of China: Tang and Song Dynasties

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
China's Golden Ages: The Tang and Song Dynasties

A City of One Million People

Picture a city with streets as straight as graph paper lines, stretching farther than you can see. Merchants shout in a dozen languages. Camels carry silk and spices through the gates. A million people live here. This is Chang'an, the capital of Tang China, around the year 700 AD. At that same time, the biggest cities in Europe held only about 50,000 people. Chang'an was twenty times larger.

Chang'an belonged to the Tang Dynasty — a line of rulers from the same family, like a chain of grandparents, parents, and children all taking turns on the throne. You might think a dynasty means just one king or queen. Actually, the Tang Dynasty was a whole family of emperors who passed power down for nearly 300 years, from AD 618 to 907.
