At that same time, the biggest cities in Europe held only about 50,000 people. Chang'an was twenty times larger. Chang'an belonged to the Tang Dynasty — a line of rulers from the same family, like a chain of grandparents, parents, and children all taking turns on the throne. You might think a dynasty means just one king or queen. Actually, the Tang Dynasty was a whole family of emperors who passed power down for nearly 300 years, from AD 618 to 907.

prompt._: According to the text, what contrast does the author make between Chang'an and the biggest cities in Europe around AD 700?

choices.text: Chang'an was built later than the biggest cities in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about when European cities were built compared to Chang'an.

choices.text: Chang'an had more emperors than the biggest cities in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the number of emperors in Chang'an to anything about European cities.

choices.text: Chang'an had straighter streets than the biggest cities in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions straight streets to describe what Chang'an looked like, but it never compares Chang'an's streets to streets in European cities.

choices.text: Chang'an was much larger than the biggest cities in Europe.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Chang'an had one million people while Europe's biggest cities had only 50,000, making Chang'an twenty times larger.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Poetry and Power Under the Tang

The Tang emperors built one of the most powerful nations on Earth. Travelers from Persia, India, Japan, and Korea crowded into Chang'an's markets, trading jewels, exotic animals, and goods from distant lands. People of many cultures, languages, and religions lived side by side.

The Tang years were also a golden age for poetry. Li Bai wrote about moonlight, friendship, and the joy of being alive. Du Fu wrote about war, hunger, and the pain of ordinary people. Both men lived over a thousand years ago, yet Chinese students still memorize their poems today. Imagine reading a poem in class that a third grader memorized in the year 750.
stimulus.content_text: Poetry and Power Under the Tang The Tang emperors built one of the most powerful nations on Earth. Travelers from Persia, India, Japan, and Korea crowded into Chang'an's markets, trading jewels, exotic animals, and goods from distant lands. People of many cultures, languages, and religions lived side by side. The Tang years were also a golden age for poetry. Li Bai wrote about moonlight, friendship, and the joy of being alive. Du Fu wrote about war, hunger, and the pain of ordinary people. Both men lived over a thousand years ago, yet Chinese students still memorize their poems today. Imagine reading a poem in class that a third grader memorized in the year 750.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what caused Chang'an to become a city where people of many different cultures lived together?

choices.text: Students from around the world traveled there to study old poems.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about students traveling to Chang'an to study poems.

choices.text: Travelers from distant countries arrived to trade in the markets.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says travelers from many faraway countries came to trade in Chang'an's markets, which is what filled the city with people from different cultures.

choices.text: The Tang emperors forced people from other nations to move there.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Tang emperors forcing people from other nations to move to Chang'an.

choices.text: People wanted to escape the wars and hunger in their home lands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage mentions war and hunger in Du Fu's poetry, it never says people moved to Chang'an to escape those things.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Song Dynasty and Its Impressive Inventions

After the Tang Dynasty fell, China split apart for a time. Then the Song Dynasty united the country again and ruled from AD 960 to 1279. The Song military was not as strong as the Tang's. But Song China made up for it with a booming economy and astonishing advances.

Many things you use today were shaped by inventions from Tang and Song China. Chinese inventors discovered gunpowder during the Tang Dynasty, around the 800s AD. It is the explosive powder later used in fireworks and weapons. The compass, a tool with a magnetic needle that always points north, was also first developed before the Song period. Song inventors then improved these earlier creations and put them to wider use. During the Song Dynasty, a man named Bi Sheng developed movable type around the year 1040 — a way to print books by arranging small letter blocks, like building words with stamps. And Song merchants were the first people anywhere to use paper money — thin printed notes that stood for coins, just like the dollar bills in your pocket.
stimulus.content_text: The Song Dynasty and Its Impressive Inventions After the Tang Dynasty fell, China split apart for a time. Then the Song Dynasty united the country again and ruled from AD 960 to 1279. The Song military was not as strong as the Tang's. But Song China made up for it with a booming economy and astonishing advances. Many things you use today were shaped by inventions from Tang and Song China. Chinese inventors discovered gunpowder during the Tang Dynasty, around the 800s AD. It is the explosive powder later used in fireworks and weapons. The compass, a tool with a magnetic needle that always points north, was also first developed before the Song period. Song inventors then improved these earlier creations and put them to wider use. During the Song Dynasty, a man named Bi Sheng developed movable type around the year 1040 — a way to print books by arranging small letter blocks, like building words with stamps. And Song merchants were the first people anywhere to use paper money — thin printed notes that stood for coins, just like the dollar bills in your pocket.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after the Tang Dynasty fell that led to the Song Dynasty taking power?

choices.text: China lost its inventions and then had to start over again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because China did not lose its inventions — Song inventors actually built on and improved the inventions from before.

choices.text: China invented paper money and then formed a new government
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because paper money was invented during the Song Dynasty, not before it as a cause of the new government forming.

choices.text: China split apart for a time and then was united again
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that China split apart after the Tang Dynasty fell, and then the Song Dynasty united the country again.

choices.text: China built a stronger military and then chose new rulers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says the Song military was NOT as strong as the Tang's, so a stronger military didn't lead to new rulers.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A New Way to Choose Leaders

The Song government tried something new when it came to choosing leaders. Instead of giving important jobs to rich families, they held written tests. Anyone who studied hard could take the exam. The people who scored highest got the jobs. This was called a civil service system — a way of choosing government workers based on what they knew, not who their parents were. Think of it like earning a spot on a team by your skill, not just your last name.
stimulus.content_text: A New Way to Choose Leaders The Song government tried something new when it came to choosing leaders. Instead of giving important jobs to rich families, they held written tests. Anyone who studied hard could take the exam. The people who scored highest got the jobs. This was called a civil service system — a way of choosing government workers based on what they knew, not who their parents were. Think of it like earning a spot on a team by your skill, not just your last name.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how was the Song dynasty's civil service system different from the old way of choosing leaders?

choices.text: It gave jobs to people who were the oldest workers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions age or how long someone had been working as a reason for getting a job.

choices.text: It gave jobs to people who scored best on tests
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that the people who scored highest on the written tests were the ones who got the government jobs.

choices.text: It gave jobs to people who knew the most leaders
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because knowing other leaders was part of the old system where connections and family mattered — the new system was based on test scores, not who you knew.

choices.text: It gave jobs to people who had the richest families
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because giving jobs to rich families was the old way of doing things — the Song civil service system was created specifically to change that.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Everyday Life in Song China

Beyond its new system of government, Song China also thrived in everyday life. Farmers grew rice in flooded fields called paddies — shallow pools of water where rice plants stand in neat rows, like a garden that is also a pond. They grew enough food to feed millions. Song cities buzzed with activity as well, with restaurants, tea shops, and entertainment halls lining the streets.

Both the Tang and Song dynasties built grand temples, beautiful gardens, and majestic palaces. They traded with countries across Asia and beyond. These civilizations reached their greatest heights during times of peace and openness.

The next time you hold a printed book, check a compass, or pay with paper money, you are holding something that began in medieval China. Those inventions traveled across oceans and centuries — and landed right in your hands.

Those inventions traveled across oceans and centuries — and landed right in your hands.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information about paddies in the first paragraph?

choices.text: By explaining the steps farmers take to plant rice in them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not explain any steps for planting rice — it only describes what paddies look like.

choices.text: By comparing them to the dry fields used in other countries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions dry fields or compares paddies to fields in other countries.

choices.text: By defining what they are and describing what they look like.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author defines paddies as 'flooded fields' and then describes them as looking like 'a garden that is also a pond' with rice in neat rows.

choices.text: By detailing the problems that are caused by flooding the fields.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not discuss any problems or challenges caused by flooding the fields.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did the achievements of the Tang Dynasty contrast with those of the Song Dynasty?

choices.text: The Tang Dynasty gave important jobs to rich families, while the Song Dynasty gave important jobs to famous poets.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Song Dynasty gave jobs based on written tests open to anyone, not based on rich families or famous poets.

choices.text: The Tang Dynasty built grand temples and beautiful palaces, while the Song Dynasty focused only on growing rice in paddies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both dynasties built grand temples and palaces, and the Song Dynasty was known for much more than rice farming, including inventions and busy city life.

choices.text: The Tang Dynasty created the first paper money, while the Song Dynasty created the first magnetic compass.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says it was the other way around — gunpowder and the compass came from the Tang period, and paper money came from the Song Dynasty.

choices.text: The Tang Dynasty was famous for its poetry and military, while the Song Dynasty was famous for its inventions and economy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the Tang Dynasty had great military power and famous poets, while the Song Dynasty stood out for its inventions and strong economy.

prompt._: Based on the article, what allowed both the Tang and Song dynasties to reach their greatest heights and build successful civilizations?

choices.text: They experienced times of peace and openness.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article uses those exact words — 'times of peace and openness' — to explain how both dynasties reached their greatest heights.

choices.text: They required all citizens to pass written exams.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the written exams were only for people who wanted government jobs, not something all citizens were required to do.

choices.text: They built the strongest military in the world.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the Song military was not as strong as the Tang's, so having the strongest military was not true for both dynasties.

choices.text: They invented powerful new weapons using gunpowder.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while gunpowder was invented in China, the article never says it was the reason the dynasties built successful civilizations.

prompt._: According to the text about China's Golden Ages, which sequence correctly orders the major historical events from earliest to latest?

choices.text: The Tang Dynasty ruled, the country split apart, the Song Dynasty ruled, and movable type was invented.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the passage: Tang Dynasty (618–907), then the split, then the Song Dynasty (960–1279), then movable type around 1040.

choices.text: The Tang Dynasty ruled, movable type was invented, the country split apart, and the Song Dynasty ruled.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because movable type was invented during the Song Dynasty, which came after the country split apart — not before.

choices.text: The country split apart, the Tang Dynasty ruled, movable type was invented, and the Song Dynasty ruled.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the country split apart after the Tang Dynasty ended, not before it began.

choices.text: The country split apart, the Song Dynasty ruled, the Tang Dynasty ruled, and movable type was invented.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it places the Song Dynasty before the Tang Dynasty, but the Tang actually ruled first, hundreds of years earlier.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is one way the Tang and Song dynasties were similar in how they affected people's everyday lives?

choices.text: Both dynasties had the same size cities and populations.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article never compares the city sizes of the Tang and Song dynasties to each other.

choices.text: Both dynasties were known mainly for their strong armies.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article specifically says the Song military was not as strong as the Tang's, so strong armies were not a shared trait.

choices.text: Both dynasties created inventions and ideas still used today.
choices.feedback: This is correct because both dynasties produced inventions like gunpowder, the compass, movable type, and paper money that the article says people still use today.

choices.text: Both dynasties gave important jobs only to wealthy families.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Song Dynasty actually did the opposite — they created a civil service system so anyone who studied hard could earn a government job, not just wealthy families.

assessments.title: Printing, Gunpowder, and the Compass: Inventions That Changed the World

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Four Inventions from China That Reached the Whole World

A Page Carved in Wood

Picture a man in China around the year AD 700, bent over a block of wood. He carves each word backward into the surface, letter by letter. He spreads black ink across the carved wood, presses a sheet of paper down, and peels it away. Words appear on the page — perfectly printed. He can press that same block hundreds of times. This was woodblock printing, and it was born in China centuries before Europe had anything like it.

Before printing, every single book had to be copied by hand. That took months. Books were rare and expensive. Then, around AD 1040, a Chinese inventor named Bi Sheng had a brilliant idea. He made tiny pieces of clay, each stamped with one character. He could arrange them to spell out a page, print it, then rearrange them for the next page. This was movable type — individual letter pieces you could mix and reuse, like alphabet blocks in a tray.

You might think a German man named Johannes Gutenberg invented printing. Actually, Bi Sheng created movable type about four hundred years earlier.
stimulus.content_text: Four Inventions from China That Reached the Whole WorldA Page Carved in Wood Picture a man in China around the year AD 700, bent over a block of wood. He carves each word backward into the surface, letter by letter. He spreads black ink across the carved wood, presses a sheet of paper down, and peels it away. Words appear on the page — perfectly printed. He can press that same block hundreds of times. This was woodblock printing, and it was born in China centuries before Europe had anything like it. Before printing, every single book had to be copied by hand. That took months. Books were rare and expensive. Then, around AD 1040, a Chinese inventor named Bi Sheng had a brilliant idea. He made tiny pieces of clay, each stamped with one character. He could arrange them to spell out a page, print it, then rearrange them for the next page. This was movable type — individual letter pieces you could mix and reuse, like alphabet blocks in a tray. You might think a German man named Johannes Gutenberg invented printing. Actually, Bi Sheng created movable type about four hundred years earlier.

prompt._: According to the text, how is movable type different from woodblock printing?

choices.text: It uses individual pieces that can be reused.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Bi Sheng's clay pieces could be arranged, printed, and then rearranged and reused for the next page — that's the key difference from woodblock printing.

choices.text: It requires carving words backward into wood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carving words backward into wood describes woodblock printing, not movable type.

choices.text: It involves copying every single book by hand.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because copying books by hand is what people did before printing was invented — it's not a description of movable type.

choices.text: It prints a whole page from one block of wood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because printing a whole page from one block of wood describes woodblock printing, which is the older method that movable type improved upon.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Something to Print On

Printing needs paper, and paper came from China too. Around AD 105, a court official — a person who works for the emperor — named Cai Lun figured out how to make it. Before his paper, people wrote on strips of bamboo, sheets of silk, or dried animal skins. Paper was lighter, cheaper, and easier to carry. It spread along trade routes to the Middle East and then to Europe, changing how people kept records and shared ideas everywhere it traveled.
stimulus.content_text: Something to Print On Printing needs paper, and paper came from China too. Around AD 105, a court official — a person who works for the emperor — named Cai Lun figured out how to make it. Before his paper, people wrote on strips of bamboo, sheets of silk, or dried animal skins. Paper was lighter, cheaper, and easier to carry. It spread along trade routes to the Middle East and then to Europe, changing how people kept records and shared ideas everywhere it traveled.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did paper change how people kept records and shared ideas?

choices.text: because the emperor ordered everyone to use it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the emperor telling people to use paper — it spread because it was simply better, not because of an order.

choices.text: because it was lighter, cheaper, and easier to carry
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that paper was lighter, cheaper, and easier to carry, which are the exact reasons it was so useful for keeping records and sharing ideas.

choices.text: because it was made from bamboo and silk
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because bamboo and silk were the old writing materials that people used before paper existed, not ingredients in paper.

choices.text: because it was invented before other writing materials
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says people were already writing on bamboo, silk, and animal skins long before paper was invented around AD 105.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Looking for Magic, Finding an Explosion

Here's the surprising part about gunpowder: nobody meant to invent it. Chinese alchemists — early scientists who mixed chemicals hoping to create magic potions — were trying to make a drink that would let people live forever. Instead, their mixture caught fire. Then it exploded. They had created gunpowder by accident.

At first, the Chinese used gunpowder for fireworks and bright signal flares that soldiers could see from far away. Later, they built fire arrows, bombs, and early rockets. When gunpowder reached Europe and the Middle East, it changed warfare forever. Cannons could smash through castle walls. Guns made heavy suits of armor useless. A weapon born from a failed magic potion ended up making castles crumble.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after Chinese alchemists tried to make a drink that would let people live forever?

choices.text: Their mixture smashed through castle walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because smashing through castle walls also happened much later, when cannons were built in Europe and the Middle East.

choices.text: Their mixture turned into bright signal flares.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because signal flares came later, after the Chinese had already invented gunpowder and started using it.

choices.text: Their mixture made heavy armor useless.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because making armor useless happened even later, when gunpowder reached Europe and guns were invented.

choices.text: Their mixture caught fire and exploded.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says their mixture 'caught fire' and 'exploded' — that's exactly what happened right after they tried to make the potion.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Stone That Points the Way

Long ago, the Chinese noticed something strange about a rock called lodestone — a naturally magnetic stone that pulls metal toward it, the way a magnet sticks to your refrigerator. A piece of lodestone, when free to spin, always pointed in the same direction: north. The Chinese first used this discovery for feng shui, a practice of arranging buildings and spaces to feel balanced with nature. Later, someone realized this pointing stone could help travelers find their way.

The magnetic compass changed sailing completely. Before it, sailors depended on the stars. On cloudy nights, they were lost. With a compass, they could find direction in any weather, day or night. The compass spread to the Middle East and Europe and made long ocean voyages possible.

On cloudy nights, they were lost. With a compass, they could find direction in any weather, day or night. The compass spread to the Middle East and Europe and made long ocean voyages possible.

choices.text: It is a tool invented for ocean sailing
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because lodestone was first used for feng shui, and the compass came later — it wasn't invented specifically for ocean sailing.

choices.text: It is a stone that points toward the stars
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because lodestone points north, not toward the stars — using the stars for direction is what sailors did before the compass existed.

choices.text: It is a rock that pulls metal toward it
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says lodestone is 'a naturally magnetic stone that pulls metal toward it,' and the refrigerator magnet comparison helps explain exactly that.

choices.text: It is a stone used to build refrigerators
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only mentions a refrigerator to compare how magnets attract metal — it has nothing to do with building refrigerators.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Still Here, Still Everywhere

These four inventions — paper, printing, gunpowder, and the compass — are called the Four Great Inventions of China. While Europe was still in the Middle Ages, China already had huge cities, busy markets, and these powerful tools. The next time you hold a book, watch fireworks, or see a compass needle swing, remember: those ideas began with Chinese inventors more than a thousand years ago. They did not stay in one country. They circled the globe.

The next time you hold a book, watch fireworks, or see a compass needle swing, remember: those ideas began with Chinese inventors more than a thousand years ago. They did not stay in one country. They circled the globe.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because busy markets are mentioned in the passage as part of ancient China, but they have nothing to do with naming the four inventions.

choices.text: because European inventors copied the ideas and made them better
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions European inventors copying or improving on China's ideas.

choices.text: because they spread along trade routes and solved important problems
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article specifically says inventions like paper and the compass spread along trade routes and solved important problems like navigation and record-keeping.

assessments.title: The Silk Road: A Highway Between East and West

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Medieval China and the Silk Road

A Line of Camels in the Desert

A long line of camels walked slowly across burning sand. Each camel carried heavy bundles of silk, tea, and spices on its back. The air shimmered with heat. The nearest town was days away. These traders were crossing the Taklamakan Desert in western China, where temperatures could climb above 120 degrees. One wrong turn meant death. But the goods on those camels were worth a fortune.

This was life on the Silk Road, a network of trade paths that stretched over 4,000 miles from China all the way to the Mediterranean Sea. You might picture one long, paved road like a highway. Actually, the Silk Road was more like a spiderweb — hundreds of dirt trails, mountain passes, and desert tracks that split apart and came back together across an entire continent.
stimulus.content_text: Medieval China and the Silk RoadA Line of Camels in the Desert A long line of camels walked slowly across burning sand. Each camel carried heavy bundles of silk, tea, and spices on its back. The air shimmered with heat. The nearest town was days away. These traders were crossing the Taklamakan Desert in western China, where temperatures could climb above 120 degrees. One wrong turn meant death. But the goods on those camels were worth a fortune. This was life on the Silk Road, a network of trade paths that stretched over 4,000 miles from China all the way to the Mediterranean Sea. You might picture one long, paved road like a highway. Actually, the Silk Road was more like a spiderweb — hundreds of dirt trails, mountain passes, and desert tracks that split apart and came back together across an entire continent.

prompt._: According to the text, how was the Silk Road different from a highway?

choices.text: It was one long paved road.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says you might *picture* it that way, but the Silk Road was actually the opposite — many paths, not one paved road.

choices.text: It was a line of camel loads.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a line of camel loads describes how traders traveled, not what made the Silk Road different from a highway.

choices.text: It was a web of many paths.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares the Silk Road to a spiderweb made of hundreds of trails, mountain passes, and desert tracks.

choices.text: It was a route only in China.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the Silk Road stretched over 4,000 miles from China all the way to the Mediterranean Sea.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
China's Role in the Trade

Chinese cities like Chang'an were starting points for the Silk Road. Chang'an was the capital of the powerful Tang dynasty, one of the ruling families that governed China during medieval times. Tang rulers encouraged trade. They kept parts of the road safe and welcomed foreign merchants into their cities.

China sent some of the most desired goods in the world along these routes. Smooth, shining silk was so valuable that people sometimes used it as money. Fine porcelain — a type of smooth, painted pottery — was so famous that English speakers still call it "china" today. Tea and spices also traveled west in heavy bundles.

Heading east toward China came gold, glass, wool, and silver. But it's easy to think only China and the Roman Empire did the trading. That misses the bigger picture. Persian merchants sold jewels and perfumes in the city of Samarkand. Indian traders brought cotton and pepper to Central Asian markets. Turkish herders sold horses along mountain routes. Dozens of different peoples kept the Silk Road alive.
stimulus.content_text: China's Role in the Trade Chinese cities like Chang'an were starting points for the Silk Road. Chang'an was the capital of the powerful Tang dynasty, one of the ruling families that governed China during medieval times. Tang rulers encouraged trade. They kept parts of the road safe and welcomed foreign merchants into their cities. China sent some of the most desired goods in the world along these routes. Smooth, shining silk was so valuable that people sometimes used it as money. Fine porcelain — a type of smooth, painted pottery — was so famous that English speakers still call it "china" today. Tea and spices also traveled west in heavy bundles. Heading east toward China came gold, glass, wool, and silver. But it's easy to think only China and the Roman Empire did the trading. That misses the bigger picture. Persian merchants sold jewels and perfumes in the city of Samarkand. Indian traders brought cotton and pepper to Central Asian markets. Turkish herders sold horses along mountain routes. Dozens of different peoples kept the Silk Road alive.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the idea that China's rulers caused trade to grow on the Silk Road?

choices.text: They kept roads safe and welcomed traders.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Tang rulers kept roads safe and welcomed foreign merchants, which are specific actions that caused more trade to happen.

choices.text: They traded goods only with the Romans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says many peoples kept the Silk Road alive, not just China and Rome.

choices.text: They used valuable silk as a form of money.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because using silk as money is a fact about silk's value, not something rulers did to make trade grow.

choices.text: They traveled west carrying heavy bundles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because heavy bundles traveling west describes how goods moved, not an action taken by China's rulers.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Danger on Every Path

Traveling the Silk Road was dangerous. The Taklamakan Desert could kill with heat and thirst. Mountain passes in Central Asia were so narrow that one slip meant falling off a cliff. Bandits hid along lonely stretches, waiting to rob travelers.

So merchants moved in caravans — large groups of traders traveling together for safety. They stopped at oasis towns along the way, green spots in the desert where underground water reaches the surface. These towns had wells, food, and shelter.

Camels made the whole journey possible. They could carry heavy loads and survive for days without drinking water. A long caravan of camels loaded with silk became a common sight on desert trails.
stimulus.content_text: Danger on Every Path Traveling the Silk Road was dangerous. The Taklamakan Desert could kill with heat and thirst. Mountain passes in Central Asia were so narrow that one slip meant falling off a cliff. Bandits hid along lonely stretches, waiting to rob travelers. So merchants moved in caravans — large groups of traders traveling together for safety. They stopped at oasis towns along the way, green spots in the desert where underground water reaches the surface. These towns had wells, food, and shelter. Camels made the whole journey possible. They could carry heavy loads and survive for days without drinking water. A long caravan of camels loaded with silk became a common sight on desert trails.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did merchants do AFTER they realized the Silk Road was dangerous?

choices.text: They traveled together in large caravans for safety
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states merchants traveled in large caravans 'for safety' after facing the dangers of the Silk Road.

choices.text: They fought the bandits hiding along lonely stretches
choices.feedback: The passage mentions bandits as one of the dangers, but it never says merchants responded by fighting them.

choices.text: They built oasis towns with wells along the route
choices.feedback: The passage describes oasis towns as places merchants stopped at, but it never says merchants built those towns themselves.

choices.text: They bought camels to carry water across the desert
choices.feedback: Camels were used to carry heavy loads and go days without water, but the passage never says merchants bought camels specifically to carry water across the desert.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Goods

Here's the surprising part. The most important things that moved along the Silk Road were not things you could touch. Ideas traveled, too. Buddhism spread from India into China along these routes. Islam moved from Arabia into Central Asia. Christianity also followed the trails. When a Chinese merchant sat down to eat with a Persian trader, they didn't just swap goods. They swapped stories, recipes, farming tricks, and medical knowledge.

Indian monks met Turkish horsemen. Chinese artists saw new styles of painting from far-off lands. Languages blended. New ideas were born from these meetings between strangers.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Goods Here's the surprising part. The most important things that moved along the Silk Road were not things you could touch. Ideas traveled, too. Buddhism spread from India into China along these routes. Islam moved from Arabia into Central Asia. Christianity also followed the trails. When a Chinese merchant sat down to eat with a Persian trader, they didn't just swap goods. They swapped stories, recipes, farming tricks, and medical knowledge. Indian monks met Turkish horsemen. Chinese artists saw new styles of painting from far-off lands. Languages blended. New ideas were born from these meetings between strangers.

prompt._: How were Chinese merchants and Persian traders similar when they met on the Silk Road?

choices.text: They both spoke one single language.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says languages blended, meaning many different languages were spoken along the Silk Road.

choices.text: They both shared ideas as well as goods.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says merchants swapped both goods and ideas like stories, recipes, and medical knowledge.

choices.text: They both traded only things they could touch.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says the most important things on the Silk Road were ideas, not just goods you could touch.

choices.text: They both taught the same religion to others.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions several different religions traveling the Silk Road, not one shared religion that merchants taught together.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Legacy You Can Still See

The Silk Road lasted over 1,500 years, from roughly 130 BC to about AD 1453. It faded when ships began carrying goods by sea, which was easier and safer. But the connections it built between East and West never fully disappeared.

The next time you drink tea, touch a silk shirt, or eat a spicy meal, you are holding something that once rode on the back of a camel across a burning desert — carried by people brave enough to cross the world for it.

It faded when ships began carrying goods by sea, which was easier and safer. But the connections it built between East and West never fully disappeared. The next time you drink tea, touch a silk shirt, or eat a spicy meal, you are holding something that once rode on the back of a camel across a burning desert — carried by people brave enough to cross the world for it.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the word 'legacy' mean as it is used in the section heading?

choices.text: a story that people tell about the past
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the legacy mentioned in the passage is about real, physical things you can hold, not just stories from the past.

choices.text: something left behind that can still be seen
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that the Silk Road left behind things we can still see and use today, like tea, silk, and spices.

choices.text: a gift given by a king to his people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about a king or any gifts being given to people.

choices.text: a road that connects two distant places
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'legacy' describes what the Silk Road left behind, not the road itself.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the ideas that traveled the Silk Road different from goods like silk and tea?

choices.text: They were only shared inside the safe oasis towns.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article shows ideas like Buddhism and Islam spread across entire regions, not just inside oasis towns.

choices.text: They were only traded for heavy silver and gold.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ideas weren't traded for gold or silver — they were exchanged naturally when people from different places sat down and talked with each other.

choices.text: They could not be touched or packed onto a camel.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly says the most important things on the Silk Road 'were not things you could touch,' meaning ideas couldn't be packed or carried like physical goods.

choices.text: They could not survive the burning desert weather.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions ideas being stopped or damaged by desert weather — they traveled successfully all the way across harsh lands.

prompt._: Which cause-and-effect pattern best shows how the article is organized to explain why the Silk Road mattered for so long?

choices.text: The Tang rulers welcomed merchants, so the Silk Road ended when foreign traders left.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Silk Road faded due to sea travel becoming easier, not because foreign traders left after Tang rulers welcomed them.

choices.text: The road had many paths, so traders stopped using camels and began sailing ships.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ships replacing camels explains why the Silk Road ended, not why it mattered for so long.

choices.text: The desert was hot, so only Chinese merchants were able to trade valuable goods.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says many different peoples — not just Chinese merchants — kept the Silk Road alive.

choices.text: The road connected many peoples, so goods and ideas spread between East and West.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows that connecting many peoples caused goods and ideas to spread between East and West, which is the main reason the Silk Road was so important.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best summarizes the history of the Silk Road?

choices.text: Merchants shared goods and ideas for years, the routes faded when sea travel began, and then rulers in China encouraged trade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the events in the wrong order — rulers encouraged trade at the beginning, not at the end.

choices.text: Traders crossed the hot desert, caravans stopped at green oasis towns, and then camels carried heavy bundles to the next stop.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only describes one small part of a desert journey, not a summary of the Silk Road's whole history.

choices.text: Rulers in China built paved highways, merchants traveled safely without danger, and then the routes faded when sea travel began.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says the Silk Road was dirt trails and mountain passes, not paved highways, and traveling it was very dangerous.

choices.text: Rulers in China encouraged trade, merchants shared goods and ideas for years, and then the routes faded when sea travel began.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the passage's order: rulers encouraged trade, merchants shared goods and ideas for years, and then sea travel replaced the routes.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did camels and oasis towns both help merchants survive the Silk Road?

choices.text: Both helped the Tang rulers protect the starting points of trade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Tang rulers are described as keeping the road safe and welcoming merchants, which is a separate idea from what camels and oasis towns did.

choices.text: Both allowed merchants to travel safely across the steep mountain passes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions mountain passes were dangerous, but it never says camels or oasis towns helped travelers cross them safely.

choices.text: Both protected large groups of travelers from bandits on the trails.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says merchants traveled in caravans (large groups) to protect themselves from bandits, not camels or oasis towns.

choices.text: Both helped traders survive the extreme lack of water in deserts.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows both camels and oasis towns helped merchants survive the desert's extreme lack of water — camels could go days without drinking, and oasis towns provided wells and water.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Silk Road: A Highway Between East and West

assessments.title: Marco Polo's Amazing Journey to China

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Marco Polo and the Wonders of Medieval China

A Boy Leaves Home

A seventeen-year-old boy stood at the edge of Venice, Italy, in the year 1271. Behind him: canals, stone bridges, and everything he knew. Ahead: deserts, mountains, and a land most Europeans could not even imagine. His name was Marco Polo. He would not come home for twenty-four years.

Marco traveled with his father and uncle. They were merchants — people who made a living by buying and selling goods. The Polo family had already visited the court of a powerful emperor in China. Now they were returning, and young Marco was joining them. Their path followed the Silk Road, a network of ancient trading routes that stretched thousands of miles between Europe and Asia. It was called the Silk Road because silk cloth from China was one of the most prized goods carried along it. The journey took about three and a half years. They crossed burning deserts, climbed freezing mountains, and passed through kingdoms few Europeans had ever seen.
stimulus.content_text: Marco Polo and the Wonders of Medieval ChinaA Boy Leaves Home A seventeen-year-old boy stood at the edge of Venice, Italy, in the year 1271. Behind him: canals, stone bridges, and everything he knew. Ahead: deserts, mountains, and a land most Europeans could not even imagine. His name was Marco Polo. He would not come home for twenty-four years. Marco traveled with his father and uncle. They were merchants — people who made a living by buying and selling goods. The Polo family had already visited the court of a powerful emperor in China. Now they were returning, and young Marco was joining them. Their path followed the Silk Road, a network of ancient trading routes that stretched thousands of miles between Europe and Asia. It was called the Silk Road because silk cloth from China was one of the most prized goods carried along it. The journey took about three and a half years. They crossed burning deserts, climbed freezing mountains, and passed through kingdoms few Europeans had ever seen.

prompt._: According to the text, what was ahead of Marco Polo as he stood at the edge of Venice, in contrast to the familiar canals and bridges behind him?

choices.text: rivers, forests, and green valleys
choices.feedback: Rivers, forests, and green valleys are never mentioned in the passage at all — the text describes deserts and mountains, not green landscapes.

choices.text: busy markets and trading ships
choices.feedback: Busy markets and trading ships are not mentioned as being ahead of Marco — those sound more like things you'd find back in Venice behind him.

choices.text: silk cloth and valuable treasures
choices.feedback: Silk cloth and treasures were goods traded along the Silk Road, but the passage never says they were what Marco saw ahead of him as he left Venice.

choices.text: deserts, mountains, and unknown lands
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'Ahead: deserts, mountains, and a land most Europeans could not even imagine,' which is exactly what answer B says.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Thriving Empire

You might think Marco Polo "discovered" China. But China had been home to millions of people for thousands of years before he arrived. Its cities were already ancient. Its inventions were already brilliant. Marco Polo did not discover China. He simply told Europeans about it.

When the Polos arrived, China was ruled by Kublai Khan. He led the Yuan Dynasty — a period of rule over China, the way a president leads a term in office. Here is the surprising part: Kublai Khan was not Chinese. He was Mongol. The Mongols came from the grassy plains, called steppes, far north of China. His grandfather, the famous conqueror Genghis Khan — a leader who took land and power by force — had built a huge empire through war. Kublai Khan finished the job by taking control of all of China. Because the Mongols were outsiders ruling a foreign land, Kublai Khan was curious about other outsiders, too. That is one reason he welcomed Marco Polo so warmly.
stimulus.content_text: A Thriving Empire You might think Marco Polo "discovered" China. But China had been home to millions of people for thousands of years before he arrived. Its cities were already ancient. Its inventions were already brilliant. Marco Polo did not discover China. He simply told Europeans about it. When the Polos arrived, China was ruled by Kublai Khan. He led the Yuan Dynasty — a period of rule over China, the way a president leads a term in office. Here is the surprising part: Kublai Khan was not Chinese. He was Mongol. The Mongols came from the grassy plains, called steppes, far north of China. His grandfather, the famous conqueror Genghis Khan — a leader who took land and power by force — had built a huge empire through war. Kublai Khan finished the job by taking control of all of China. Because the Mongols were outsiders ruling a foreign land, Kublai Khan was curious about other outsiders, too. That is one reason he welcomed Marco Polo so warmly.

prompt._: A main idea of the text is that Kublai Khan warmly welcomed Marco Polo to China. How does the detail about the Mongols being outsiders support this main idea?

choices.text: It proves why China had millions of people living there.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Mongols being outsiders has nothing to do with how many people lived in China.

choices.text: It describes why Marco Polo told Europeans about the cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the detail about Mongols being outsiders doesn't explain why Marco Polo told Europeans about China's cities.

choices.text: It shows why Kublai Khan was curious about Marco Polo.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Kublai Khan was curious about outsiders because the Mongols were outsiders themselves, and that curiosity is why he welcomed Marco Polo.

choices.text: It explains why Genghis Khan built a huge Mongol empire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the detail about Mongols being outsiders doesn't explain why Genghis Khan built his empire — that's a separate idea in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A World Far Ahead

Marco Polo spent about seventeen years serving Kublai Khan. He traveled across China on missions for the emperor, visiting places no European had ever been. What he saw amazed him.

It is easy to assume that Europe and China were similar back then. They were not. China was far ahead. Its cities were enormous, with wide streets and grand buildings. People paid for things with paper money. Think about that. In Europe, people still lugged around heavy metal coins. In China, you could carry a light slip of paper worth the same amount. China also had systems for delivering mail across the empire, and people burned coal for heat — something most Europeans barely knew about.
stimulus.content_text: A World Far Ahead Marco Polo spent about seventeen years serving Kublai Khan. He traveled across China on missions for the emperor, visiting places no European had ever been. What he saw amazed him. It is easy to assume that Europe and China were similar back then. They were not. China was far ahead. Its cities were enormous, with wide streets and grand buildings. People paid for things with paper money. Think about that. In Europe, people still lugged around heavy metal coins. In China, you could carry a light slip of paper worth the same amount. China also had systems for delivering mail across the empire, and people burned coal for heat — something most Europeans barely knew about.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Marco Polo do while serving Kublai Khan for seventeen years?

choices.text: He made heavy metal coins for people.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because heavy metal coins were used in Europe, not China, and the passage never says Marco Polo made them.

choices.text: He delivered mail across the empire.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because delivering mail across the empire was a system China had, not something Marco Polo personally did.

choices.text: He traveled across China on missions.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states Marco Polo 'traveled across China on missions for the emperor' during his seventeen years serving Kublai Khan.

choices.text: He built grand buildings in the cities.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage mentions grand buildings as a feature of Chinese cities, but never says Marco Polo built them.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Story No One Believed

Marco Polo finally returned to Venice in 1295. He had been gone so long that his own family barely recognized him. He told wild stories about China's wealth and technology. Many people laughed. Paper money? Cities bigger than any in Europe? It sounded made up.

A few years later, Marco Polo was captured during a war between Venice and another Italian city. In prison, he shared his stories with a fellow prisoner who happened to be a writer. Together they created a book called The Travels of Marco Polo. Some historians believe Marco Polo exaggerated parts of his story — stretched the truth to make things sound even more impressive than they were. But even if some details were bigger than real life, the book opened European eyes to Asia.
stimulus.content_text: A Story No One Believed Marco Polo finally returned to Venice in 1295. He had been gone so long that his own family barely recognized him. He told wild stories about China's wealth and technology. Many people laughed. Paper money? Cities bigger than any in Europe? It sounded made up. A few years later, Marco Polo was captured during a war between Venice and another Italian city. In prison, he shared his stories with a fellow prisoner who happened to be a writer. Together they created a book called The Travels of Marco Polo. Some historians believe Marco Polo exaggerated parts of his story — stretched the truth to make things sound even more impressive than they were. But even if some details were bigger than real life, the book opened European eyes to Asia.

prompt._: According to some historians, how did the details in Marco Polo's book compare to real life?

choices.text: They were much less exciting than real life.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because exaggerating means making things sound MORE exciting and impressive, not less exciting than real life.

choices.text: They were completely different from real life.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says some details were 'bigger than real life,' not that the whole story was completely made up or totally different.

choices.text: They were more impressive than real life.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Marco Polo 'stretched the truth to make things sound even more impressive than they were,' which means his details were bigger than real life.

choices.text: They were exactly the same as real life.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says he exaggerated, which means he changed details to make them bigger, so they were not exactly the same as real life.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Words That Traveled Further Than He Did

Marco Polo's book spread across Europe. Readers were stunned. Centuries later, a sailor named Christopher Columbus carried a worn copy of it on his own famous voyage. Marco Polo walked to China and back. But his words traveled even further — across oceans, across generations, and into the hands of people who wanted to see the world for themselves.
stimulus.content_text: Words That Traveled Further Than He Did Marco Polo's book spread across Europe. Readers were stunned. Centuries later, a sailor named Christopher Columbus carried a worn copy of it on his own famous voyage. Marco Polo walked to China and back. But his words traveled even further — across oceans, across generations, and into the hands of people who wanted to see the world for themselves.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a "worn copy" of Marco Polo's book?

choices.text: a book that was short and simple
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the length or difficulty of the book.

choices.text: a book that was new and clean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'worn' means the opposite of new and clean — it describes something that has been used many times.

choices.text: a book that was old and used
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because 'worn' means something that has been used a lot over time, making it old and well-read — just like a book that has been passed around for centuries would be.

choices.text: a book that was lost and missing
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Columbus was carrying the book on his voyage, which means it was with him, not lost or missing.

prompt._: According to the article, how did Kublai Khan's reaction to Marco Polo contrast with his family's reaction?

choices.text: Kublai Khan welcomed him warmly, while his family barely recognized him.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says Kublai Khan welcomed Marco Polo warmly, while his family back in Venice barely recognized him after 24 years away.

choices.text: Kublai Khan laughed at his stories, while his family wrote them in a book.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was Marco Polo's fellow prisoner — not his family — who helped write the book, and Kublai Khan never laughed at his stories.

choices.text: Kublai Khan locked him in a prison, while his family helped him to escape.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was soldiers from a different Italian city who captured Marco Polo during a war, not Kublai Khan, and his family did not help him escape.

choices.text: Kublai Khan gave him paper money, while his family gave him metal coins.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says Kublai Khan gave Marco Polo paper money or that his family gave him coins — those were just examples of differences between China and Europe.

prompt._: Based on the article, why did Marco Polo's book inspire later explorers like Christopher Columbus to travel?

choices.text: It described the amazing wealth and advanced technology in China.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says the book described China's incredible wealth and advanced technology, which stunned readers and inspired explorers like Columbus.

choices.text: It explained how to survive the dangerous deserts and mountains.
choices.feedback: The book was full of descriptions of China's amazing cities and technology, not instructions for surviving dangerous travel routes.

choices.text: It proved that the Mongol emperors were outsiders from the north.
choices.feedback: The Mongols being outsiders explains why Kublai Khan liked Marco Polo, but it has nothing to do with why later explorers were inspired to travel.

choices.text: It showed how to build the large ships needed for ocean sailing.
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions anything about ships or sailing instructions being in Marco Polo's book.

prompt._: Which choice shows the events from Marco Polo's life in the order the article tells them?

choices.text: He served Kublai Khan, he traveled the Silk Road to China, he returned to Venice, and Columbus later carried his book.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says Marco Polo served Kublai Khan before traveling the Silk Road, but he had to travel the Silk Road first to even get to China.

choices.text: He traveled the Silk Road to China, he served Kublai Khan, he helped create a book in prison, and Columbus later carried it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it follows the exact order the article uses: Silk Road journey, serving Kublai Khan, writing the book in prison, and Columbus carrying it later.

choices.text: He traveled the Silk Road to China, he helped create a book in prison, he served Kublai Khan, and Columbus later carried it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it places the prison book before Marco Polo served Kublai Khan, but the book wasn't created until after he returned home from China.

choices.text: He returned to Venice, he traveled the Silk Road to China, he helped create a book in prison, and Columbus later carried it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Marco Polo traveled the Silk Road to China first — he didn't return to Venice until much later in the story.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did Marco Polo's hometown in Europe compare to the cities he saw in China?

choices.text: Venice featured canals and stone bridges, while Chinese cities had wide streets and grand buildings.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage specifically describes Venice as having canals and stone bridges, and Chinese cities as having wide streets and grand buildings.

choices.text: Venice was ruled by a powerful emperor, while Chinese cities were controlled by traveling merchants.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because China was ruled by the emperor Kublai Khan, not merchants, and there is no mention of Venice being ruled by an emperor.

choices.text: Venice was built on flat grassy plains, while Chinese cities were located in burning deserts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the grassy plains were home to the Mongols and the burning deserts were part of the travel route, not the locations of the cities being compared.

choices.text: Venice relied on light paper money, while Chinese cities used heavy metal coins for trading.
choices.feedback: B is backwards — the article says China used light paper money while Europe (including Venice) used heavy metal coins, not the other way around.
