units.title: Art from Ancient Rome & Medieval Period

assessments.title: Pictures Made of Tiny Pieces: Roman Mosaics

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Art from Ancient Rome and the Medieval Period

A Dog Made of Stones

On a doorstep in the ancient Roman city of Pompeii, a fierce black dog snarls at visitors. Its teeth are sharp. Its chain is pulled tight. But this dog is not real. It is made of thousands of tiny colored stones, pressed into the ground nearly 2,000 years ago. Beneath it, Latin words read "Cave Canem" — "Beware of the Dog."

This stone dog is a mosaic — a picture built by fitting together small colored pieces, the way you snap together a jigsaw puzzle. You might think the colors were painted on. They were not. Each piece is a different-colored stone, glass, or tile. The color goes all the way through, like a jellybean that is red on the outside and red on the inside too.
stimulus.content_text: Art from Ancient Rome and the Medieval PeriodA Dog Made of Stones On a doorstep in the ancient Roman city of Pompeii, a fierce black dog snarls at visitors. Its teeth are sharp. Its chain is pulled tight. But this dog is not real. It is made of thousands of tiny colored stones, pressed into the ground nearly 2,000 years ago. Beneath it, Latin words read "Cave Canem" — "Beware of the Dog." This stone dog is a mosaic — a picture built by fitting together small colored pieces, the way you snap together a jigsaw puzzle. You might think the colors were painted on. They were not. Each piece is a different-colored stone, glass, or tile. The color goes all the way through, like a jellybean that is red on the outside and red on the inside too.

prompt._: According to the text, how are the colors in a mosaic different from painted colors?

choices.text: The colors are added to the surface after the pieces are set
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the color is already part of each stone or glass piece before it is placed, not added on top afterward.

choices.text: The colors fade quickly when exposed to sunlight over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about colors fading in sunlight.

choices.text: Each piece is colored all the way through, not just on top
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the color goes all the way through each piece, like a jellybean that is the same color inside and outside — not just on the surface like paint.

choices.text: The colors are mixed together from several different shades
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about mixing colors together from different shades.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Romans Built Pictures from Tiny Cubes

The small pieces in a mosaic are called tesserae — tiny cubes of stone or glass, often no bigger than your fingernail. Think of them as nature's pixels. Artists cut each tessera to the right size and color, then pressed the pieces, one by one, into wet plaster. When the plaster dried, every piece was locked in place.

You might picture one artist working alone. Actually, large mosaics were team projects. A master artist drew the design. Then many helpers placed the tesserae, section by section. A big floor mosaic could hold hundreds of thousands of pieces and take months or even years to finish.

Romans decorated the floors and walls of their homes, public baths, and temples with mosaics. Wealthy families loved to show off scenes of lion hunts, leaping dolphins, or peacocks spreading their feathers — all made from stone.
stimulus.content_text: How Romans Built Pictures from Tiny Cubes The small pieces in a mosaic are called tesserae — tiny cubes of stone or glass, often no bigger than your fingernail. Think of them as nature's pixels. Artists cut each tessera to the right size and color, then pressed the pieces, one by one, into wet plaster. When the plaster dried, every piece was locked in place. You might picture one artist working alone. Actually, large mosaics were team projects. A master artist drew the design. Then many helpers placed the tesserae, section by section. A big floor mosaic could hold hundreds of thousands of pieces and take months or even years to finish. Romans decorated the floors and walls of their homes, public baths, and temples with mosaics. Wealthy families loved to show off scenes of lion hunts, leaping dolphins, or peacocks spreading their feathers — all made from stone.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what caused the tiny cubes to stay locked in place?

choices.text: The wet plaster dried around the pieces.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says the pieces were pressed into wet plaster, and when the plaster dried, every piece was locked in place.

choices.text: The master artist drew a very careful design.
choices.feedback: Drawing a careful design helped the artists know where to put the pieces, but it had nothing to do with keeping the pieces stuck in place.

choices.text: The artists cut them to the perfect size.
choices.feedback: Cutting the tesserae to the right size helped them fit together nicely, but the passage never says that's what made them stay put.

choices.text: The helpers worked together on the artwork.
choices.feedback: Helpers working together helped get the big job done faster, but teamwork isn't what caused the pieces to be locked in place.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What the Mosaics Showed

Roman mosaics covered many subjects. Mythological scenes — stories about gods and heroes from ancient legends — were popular. So were geometric patterns, designs built from repeating shapes like squares, triangles, and diamonds. Some mosaics showed daily life: farmers picking grapes or gladiators, trained fighters who battled in arenas for huge crowds.

One of the most famous mosaics is the Alexander Mosaic, found in the ruins of Pompeii. A volcano buried that city under ash in 79 AD, which kept the mosaic hidden and safe for centuries. This massive artwork shows Alexander the Great fighting the Persian king Darius. It contains about 1.5 million tesserae. You can see the fear on soldiers' faces and the shine on their armor — all made from tiny stones.
stimulus.content_text: What the Mosaics Showed Roman mosaics covered many subjects. Mythological scenes — stories about gods and heroes from ancient legends — were popular. So were geometric patterns, designs built from repeating shapes like squares, triangles, and diamonds. Some mosaics showed daily life: farmers picking grapes or gladiators, trained fighters who battled in arenas for huge crowds. One of the most famous mosaics is the Alexander Mosaic, found in the ruins of Pompeii. A volcano buried that city under ash in 79 AD, which kept the mosaic hidden and safe for centuries. This massive artwork shows Alexander the Great fighting the Persian king Darius. It contains about 1.5 million tesserae. You can see the fear on soldiers' faces and the shine on their armor — all made from tiny stones.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened to the Alexander Mosaic after a volcano buried the city of Pompeii?

choices.text: It was broken into tiny pieces by the ash.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the ash actually protected the mosaic and kept it safe, not broken.

choices.text: It was kept hidden and safe for centuries.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses almost the exact same words, saying the ash 'kept the mosaic hidden and safe for centuries.'

choices.text: It was rebuilt using repeating shape designs.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the mosaic being rebuilt; geometric repeating shapes are a different type of mosaic, not something done to the Alexander Mosaic.

choices.text: It was moved to a new arena for gladiators.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says the mosaic was moved anywhere; it was found in the ruins of Pompeii where it had always been.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Colors That Outlast Centuries

It is easy to assume that ancient art looks faded and dull. But stone and glass do not fade the way paint does. The bright reds, blues, and golds you see now are almost the same colors Romans saw. Archaeologists — scientists who study the past by digging up buried objects — keep finding mosaics underground across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.
stimulus.content_text: Colors That Outlast Centuries It is easy to assume that ancient art looks faded and dull. But stone and glass do not fade the way paint does. The bright reds, blues, and golds you see now are almost the same colors Romans saw. Archaeologists — scientists who study the past by digging up buried objects — keep finding mosaics underground across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.

prompt._: In the heading "Colors That Outlast Centuries," what does the word "outlast" mean when the author compares stone and glass with paint?

choices.text: to stay bright longer than paint
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says stone and glass don't fade like paint does, meaning they stay bright longer — which is exactly what 'outlast' means.

choices.text: to break faster than paint
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — stone and glass hold up better over time, not that they break faster than paint.

choices.text: to get buried deeper than paint
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about how deep stone, glass, or paint gets buried underground.

choices.text: to look older than paint
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'outlast' is about lasting longer and staying bright, not about looking old or aged.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
From Rome to Medieval Churches

When the Roman Empire fell, the art of mosaics did not disappear. Artists in the medieval period — the time in European history roughly from 500 AD to 1500 AD — carried the tradition forward. In Byzantine churches, workers covered huge domed ceilings with glass tesserae backed in gold. When candlelight hit the gold glass, the walls seemed to flicker and glow, as if the pictures were alive. Medieval mosaics traded Roman hunting scenes for religious stories, but the basic method stayed the same: tiny pieces, carefully placed, one by one.
stimulus.content_text: From Rome to Medieval Churches When the Roman Empire fell, the art of mosaics did not disappear. Artists in the medieval period — the time in European history roughly from 500 AD to 1500 AD — carried the tradition forward. In Byzantine churches, workers covered huge domed ceilings with glass tesserae backed in gold. When candlelight hit the gold glass, the walls seemed to flicker and glow, as if the pictures were alive. Medieval mosaics traded Roman hunting scenes for religious stories, but the basic method stayed the same: tiny pieces, carefully placed, one by one.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the term 'medieval period' mean?

choices.text: the time in European history from about 1500 AD to 1900 AD
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 1500 AD to 1900 AD is too late — that time range comes after the medieval period ended.

choices.text: the time in European history from about 500 AD to 1500 AD
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the medieval period was 'roughly from 500 AD to 1500 AD' in European history.

choices.text: the time in European history from about 100 AD to 500 AD
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 100 AD to 500 AD is the wrong time range — that's actually closer to when the Roman Empire was still around, not the medieval period.

choices.text: the time in European history from about 200 BC to 100 AD
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 200 BC to 100 AD is way too early and doesn't match the dates the passage gives for the medieval period at all.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Mosaics Still Matter

Next time you see a pixel on your screen, look closely. You are seeing the same idea Roman artists used — small pieces combining into something bigger. Art does not always need a brush. Sometimes it just needs a million tiny stones and a team patient enough to place every single one.
stimulus.content_text: Why Mosaics Still Matter Next time you see a pixel on your screen, look closely. You are seeing the same idea Roman artists used — small pieces combining into something bigger. Art does not always need a brush. Sometimes it just needs a million tiny stones and a team patient enough to place every single one.

prompt._: What evidence does the author use to support the point that mosaics are still important today?

choices.text: Roman artists worked together in large teams
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because working in large teams is a detail about how mosaics were created, not evidence that they are still important today.

choices.text: Stones had to be placed in a certain order
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions stones being placed in a certain order — that detail simply isn't in the text.

choices.text: Mosaics were made without using any brushes
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because not using brushes describes what makes mosaics different from painting, but it doesn't show why mosaics still matter now.

choices.text: Pixels on a screen use the same basic idea
choices.feedback: This is correct because the author directly compares pixels on modern screens to the same idea used in mosaics, showing the concept is still alive today.

prompt._: How does the author contrast the mosaics made by ancient Romans with the mosaics made by medieval artists?

choices.text: By explaining that Roman mosaics decorated the floors of temples, while medieval mosaics decorated the screens of computers.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because computers did not exist in ancient Roman or medieval times, and the passage never makes this comparison.

choices.text: By explaining that Roman mosaics required large teams of workers, while medieval mosaics required only one master artist.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage only mentions that Roman mosaics used large teams; it never says medieval mosaics were made by just one master artist.

choices.text: By explaining that Roman mosaics showed myths and daily life, while medieval mosaics showed scenes of religious stories.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states Roman mosaics showed myths and daily life, while medieval mosaics switched to showing religious stories.

choices.text: By explaining that Roman mosaics used tiny pieces of stone, while medieval mosaics used drops of wet paint.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because medieval artists still used tesserae — tiny pieces of stone and glass — just like the Romans did, not drops of wet paint.

prompt._: Why are archaeologists still able to find brightly colored mosaics buried underground after nearly 2,000 years?

choices.text: because artists used gold-backed glass that reflected light
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gold-backed glass was a technique used in medieval Byzantine churches to create a glowing effect, not a reason why buried mosaics kept their colors.

choices.text: because stone and glass do not fade the way paint does
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that stone and glass don't fade like paint does, which is why the colors are still bright after nearly 2,000 years.

choices.text: because the plaster kept the colors from being seen
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because plaster was used to lock the tesserae in place, not to protect or hide the colors.

choices.text: because the mosaics were covered with a layer of protective paint
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says mosaics were covered with a protective layer of paint — that detail doesn't exist in the text.

prompt._: Which choice best summarizes the article by putting its main ideas in the correct order?

choices.text: Medieval artists invented mosaics, Romans copied them, and churches hid them underground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the history backwards — Romans created mosaics first, and medieval artists came later and continued the tradition.

choices.text: Romans buried mosaics, artists added gold glass, and archaeologists made the first mosaics
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Romans did not bury their mosaics on purpose, and archaeologists study mosaics — they did not create the first ones.

choices.text: Romans made mosaics, medieval artists continued them, and the idea connects to pixels today
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it accurately captures the article's three main ideas in order: Romans made mosaics, medieval artists continued them, and the idea lives on in pixels today.

choices.text: Archaeologists designed mosaics, wealthy families bought them, and volcanoes spread them widely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because archaeologists discover and study mosaics, not design them, and volcanoes did not spread mosaics — a volcano actually buried and preserved one famous mosaic in Pompeii.

prompt._: Based on information from different parts of the article, what is one way the 'Cave Canem' dog mosaic and the Alexander Mosaic are similar?

choices.text: Both were found in the ancient Roman city of Pompeii.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article mentions both the 'Cave Canem' dog and the Alexander Mosaic were found in the ancient Roman city of Pompeii.

choices.text: Both were made by a single artist working alone over many years.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article explains that large mosaics were team projects with a master artist and many helpers, not one person working alone.

choices.text: Both were created using paint applied to wet plaster walls.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because mosaics are made from tiny colored stones and glass pieces pressed into wet plaster — not painted on.

choices.text: Both were discovered in medieval churches with gold ceilings.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both mosaics are from ancient Rome, not the medieval period, and neither was found in a church with gold ceilings.

assessments.title: Arches, Domes, and the Mighty Pantheon: Roman Architecture

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Art from Ancient Rome and the Medieval Period

A Beam of Light on an Ancient Floor

A bright circle of sunlight slides across a stone floor in Rome, Italy. It moves slowly, like the hand of a giant clock. The light pours through a hole in the ceiling — a hole that builders cut on purpose nearly 2,000 years ago. This building is called the Pantheon, and it is one of the greatest works of art the ancient Romans ever created.
stimulus.content_text: Art from Ancient Rome and the Medieval PeriodA Beam of Light on an Ancient Floor A bright circle of sunlight slides across a stone floor in Rome, Italy. It moves slowly, like the hand of a giant clock. The light pours through a hole in the ceiling — a hole that builders cut on purpose nearly 2,000 years ago. This building is called the Pantheon, and it is one of the greatest works of art the ancient Romans ever created.

prompt._: Unlike a hole that happens by accident, what does the text say about the hole in the Pantheon's ceiling?

choices.text: It was created carefully by a giant stone clock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions a giant clock only to describe how the light moves, not to explain how the hole was made.

choices.text: It was broken wide open by the heavy stone floor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the stone floor breaking or creating the hole in the ceiling.

choices.text: It was cut on purpose by the ancient builders.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the builders 'cut on purpose' the hole in the ceiling, meaning it was planned, not accidental.

choices.text: It was formed very slowly by the bright sunlight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says sunlight formed the hole — the sunlight simply passes through a hole that builders already made.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Romans Built to Last

You might think the Romans used steel beams or modern machines to build structures this strong. They didn't. They had no steel at all. Instead, they invented a special concrete made from volcanic ash, lime, and water. Volcanic ash is the powdery gray dust that shoots out of a volcano. Mixed into concrete, it made the material incredibly tough. Roman concrete even hardened underwater, which helped builders create harbors and bridges.

Romans also perfected the arch — a curved shape that holds up heavy weight over an open space. Before arches, builders used flat beams across doorways. Flat beams cracked if they stretched too wide. An arch spreads weight outward and downward through its curve, so builders could make much wider openings. Romans used arches in bridges, stadiums, and aqueducts — long raised channels that carried water from rivers to cities, like stone highways for water.

Instead, they invented a special concrete made from volcanic ash, lime, and water. Volcanic ash is the powdery gray dust that shoots out of a volcano. Mixed into concrete, it made the material incredibly tough. Roman concrete even hardened underwater, which helped builders create harbors and bridges. Romans also perfected the arch — a curved shape that holds up heavy weight over an open space. Before arches, builders used flat beams across doorways. Flat beams cracked if they stretched too wide. An arch spreads weight outward and downward through its curve, so builders could make much wider openings. Romans used arches in bridges, stadiums, and aqueducts — long raised channels that carried water from rivers to cities, like stone highways for water.

prompt._: Based on the section, which statement best summarizes why the Romans were able to build structures that lasted so long?

choices.text: They built aqueducts that carried water from cities to rivers across the land.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because aqueducts carried water FROM rivers TO cities, not the other way around, and aqueducts alone don't explain why all Roman structures lasted so long.

choices.text: They discovered that volcanic ash could harden steel beams and modern machines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Romans had NO steel at all, and volcanic ash was mixed into concrete — not used to harden steel.

choices.text: They used flat beams that could stretch across very wide openings without cracking.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says flat beams would CRACK if they stretched too wide — that's the problem the arch was invented to solve!

choices.text: They invented strong concrete and perfected the arch to support heavy weight.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Roman concrete was incredibly tough and that the arch helped support heavy weight, which are the two main reasons their buildings lasted.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Pantheon's Giant Dome

The Pantheon shows Roman building skill at its best. Emperor Hadrian had it built around the year AD 125. Its concrete dome — a rounded roof shaped like half of a hollow ball — stretches 142 feet across. That is wider than the distance between bases on a baseball field. No one has ever built a bigger concrete dome without using steel supports inside.

At the very top of the dome sits the oculus, which means "eye" in Latin. This round opening is about 27 feet wide and completely open to the sky. It's easy to think a hole in a roof is a mistake. Actually, the oculus is the building's only window. Sunlight streams through it and paints a glowing circle on the walls and floor. When rain falls in, a drain system carries the water away. The Pantheon was first a temple to all Roman gods. Today it is a Christian church, and visitors still feel the same awe that Romans felt nearly 2,000 years ago.

This round opening is about 27 feet wide and completely open to the sky. It's easy to think a hole in a roof is a mistake. Actually, the oculus is the building's only window. Sunlight streams through it and paints a glowing circle on the walls and floor. When rain falls in, a drain system carries the water away. The Pantheon was first a temple to all Roman gods. Today it is a Christian church, and visitors still feel the same awe that Romans felt nearly 2,000 years ago.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happened to the Pantheon after it was first used as a temple to all Roman gods?

choices.text: It was moved to a new location
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Pantheon being moved to a different location.

choices.text: It was torn down and rebuilt by Hadrian
choices.feedback: Hadrian is the one who had the Pantheon built around AD 125, but the passage never says it was torn down and rebuilt.

choices.text: It became a Christian church
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states the Pantheon was first a Roman temple and is now a Christian church.

choices.text: It was closed to all visitors
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says visitors still visit the Pantheon today, nearly 2,000 years later.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Roman Art You Could Walk On

Romans didn't just build big. They also filled their buildings with art. They painted bright frescoes — pictures made by brushing paint onto wet plaster walls, so the color soaked in and lasted. They also covered floors with mosaics, pictures made from hundreds of tiny colored tiles pressed into place. Roman mosaics showed animals, heroes, and patterns in brilliant reds, blues, and golds.
stimulus.content_text: Roman Art You Could Walk On Romans didn't just build big. They also filled their buildings with art. They painted bright frescoes — pictures made by brushing paint onto wet plaster walls, so the color soaked in and lasted. They also covered floors with mosaics, pictures made from hundreds of tiny colored tiles pressed into place. Roman mosaics showed animals, heroes, and patterns in brilliant reds, blues, and golds.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what is one major difference between a fresco and a mosaic?

choices.text: A fresco is made with tiny tiles, but a mosaic is made with wet plaster.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two art forms mixed up — the passage says mosaics are made with tiny tiles and frescoes are painted onto wet plaster, not the other way around.

choices.text: A fresco shows famous heroes, but a mosaic shows only simple shapes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says mosaics showed animals, heroes, and patterns — not just simple shapes.

choices.text: A fresco is found in small homes, but a mosaic is found in large temples.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions small homes or large temples, so there is no evidence for this answer.

choices.text: A fresco is painted on a wall, but a mosaic is created on a floor.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that frescoes were painted on walls and mosaics covered floors, which is one major difference between them.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A New Look in the Medieval Period

Centuries later, the Medieval Period began — roughly AD 500 to 1500 in Europe. You might think medieval art looks the same as Roman art. It doesn't. Roman art often celebrated powerful leaders and empire. Medieval art focused on religion.

Medieval artists painted glowing pictures in handmade books called illuminated manuscripts. They used gold leaf and vivid colors to illustrate Bible stories. Medieval builders also changed the Roman arch. They stretched it upward into a pointed shape, creating the Gothic arch. Gothic arches let builders raise cathedral walls much higher, filling them with enormous stained-glass windows that flooded churches with colored light.
stimulus.content_text: A New Look in the Medieval Period Centuries later, the Medieval Period began — roughly AD 500 to 1500 in Europe. You might think medieval art looks the same as Roman art. It doesn't. Roman art often celebrated powerful leaders and empire. Medieval art focused on religion. Medieval artists painted glowing pictures in handmade books called illuminated manuscripts. They used gold leaf and vivid colors to illustrate Bible stories. Medieval builders also changed the Roman arch. They stretched it upward into a pointed shape, creating the Gothic arch. Gothic arches let builders raise cathedral walls much higher, filling them with enormous stained-glass windows that flooded churches with colored light.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a Gothic arch?

choices.text: a pointed arch stretched upward
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that medieval builders stretched the Roman arch 'upward into a pointed shape, creating the Gothic arch.'

choices.text: a wall filled with stained-glass windows
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stained-glass windows were a result of using Gothic arches — they aren't the definition of the arch itself.

choices.text: a glowing picture in a handmade book
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because glowing pictures in handmade books describes illuminated manuscripts, which is a completely different medieval art form.

choices.text: a Roman arch used by powerful leaders
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Roman arches were connected to powerful leaders, but the Gothic arch was a new medieval invention that changed the Roman arch's shape.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Stones That Still Speak

Walk into almost any government building, library, or church today, and look up. You will likely spot a dome, an arch, or a row of columns. Those shapes are messages sent forward through time — from Roman builders mixing volcanic ash by hand, and medieval artists pressing gold onto the pages of books they believed would honor God forever.

prompt._: Which detail from the text provides evidence that Roman building styles are still used today?

choices.text: Government workers build libraries and churches today.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions government buildings and libraries as places where you can see Roman shapes, but it never says government workers are building them today.

choices.text: Medieval artists pressed gold onto the pages of books.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is about medieval artists making books, which is a different topic and has nothing to do with Roman building styles.

choices.text: Roman builders mixed volcanic ash by hand in the past.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only describes how Romans worked in the past, not that their building style is still used in buildings today.

choices.text: People can spot domes and arches in modern buildings.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says you can spot domes and arches in modern buildings today, which are shapes that came from Roman builders.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is one way Roman art contrasted with medieval art?

choices.text: Roman art focused on religious beliefs, while medieval art celebrated powerful leaders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two time periods switched — the article says Roman art celebrated powerful leaders and medieval art focused on religion, not the other way around.

choices.text: Roman art appeared only in temples, while medieval art appeared only in churches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Roman art appeared only in temples or that medieval art appeared only in churches — both types of art showed up in many places.

choices.text: Roman art used bright gold leaf, while medieval art used tiny colored tiles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the details — gold leaf belonged to medieval artists, and tiny colored tiles (mosaics) were a Roman art form.

choices.text: Roman art decorated walls and floors, while medieval art decorated handmade books.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Romans made art on walls and floors (frescoes and mosaics), while medieval artists made art in handmade books called illuminated manuscripts.

prompt._: According to the text, why were builders able to create wider openings and taller walls with enormous windows?

choices.text: They mixed volcanic ash to make concrete very strong.
choices.feedback: Volcanic ash made Roman concrete very tough, but the passage never says it helped builders create wider openings or taller walls with windows — that credit goes to the arches.

choices.text: They used curved and pointed arches to support weight.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says the curved Roman arch allowed much wider openings, and the pointed Gothic arch let builders raise walls higher and add enormous stained-glass windows.

choices.text: They painted bright frescoes to decorate the bare walls.
choices.feedback: Frescoes are a type of wall painting used for decoration, not a structural technique that helps support weight or create bigger openings.

choices.text: They left a large open hole to let in the bright light.
choices.feedback: The open hole, or oculus, is the Pantheon's skylight window, but the passage never says it helped builders make wider openings or taller walls — it was just a design choice for letting in light.

prompt._: How does the article organize the events and ideas it presents from beginning to end?

choices.text: It compares two Roman emperors and their building plans
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article only mentions one emperor, Hadrian, and never compares two emperors or their building plans.

choices.text: It describes modern buildings first and then ancient ones
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article starts with ancient Roman buildings like the Pantheon, not with modern buildings.

choices.text: It starts with medieval art and moves backward to Roman times
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article begins with ancient Roman buildings and moves forward to the Medieval Period, not the other way around.

choices.text: It moves from Roman building methods to medieval changes
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article first explains Roman building methods and art, then describes how medieval builders and artists made changes centuries later.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how the author compares Roman and Medieval achievements?

choices.text: Romans painted glowing pictures in handmade books, while Medieval creators pressed colored tiles into bright floor mosaics.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps the two groups — illuminated manuscripts were made by Medieval artists, and mosaics were created by Romans, not the other way around.

choices.text: Romans supported giant domes with steel beams, while Medieval creators mixed volcanic ash to make tough concrete.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the Romans who used volcanic ash concrete, and neither group used steel beams — the passage actually points out that Romans had no steel at all.

choices.text: Romans designed modern buildings for governments, while Medieval creators constructed long stone highways to carry river water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because aqueducts were a Roman invention described in the passage, and modern government buildings are mentioned as examples of Roman influence today, not something Romans actually designed.

choices.text: Romans used curved arches and celebrated leaders, while Medieval creators used pointed arches and focused on religion.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Roman art celebrated leaders while Medieval art focused on religion, and it describes Romans using curved arches while Medieval builders used pointed Gothic arches.

assessments.title: Books of Beauty: Medieval Illuminated Manuscripts

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Glowing Pages: Art from Ancient Rome to the Medieval Period

A Letter Carved in Stone, a Letter Painted in Gold

Walk through any old city in Europe, and you might spot Roman letters carved into stone buildings. The ancient Romans chiseled words into arches and temples with sharp, straight lines. They also painted bright murals and created mosaics — pictures made from tiny pieces of colored stone or glass, like a puzzle glued together.

But after the Roman Empire fell apart, around 1,500 years ago, something changed. The biggest, most vivid art in Europe moved off the walls — and onto the pages of books.
stimulus.content_text: Glowing Pages: Art from Ancient Rome to the Medieval PeriodA Letter Carved in Stone, a Letter Painted in Gold Walk through any old city in Europe, and you might spot Roman letters carved into stone buildings. The ancient Romans chiseled words into arches and temples with sharp, straight lines. They also painted bright murals and created mosaics — pictures made from tiny pieces of colored stone or glass, like a puzzle glued together. But after the Roman Empire fell apart, around 1,500 years ago, something changed. The biggest, most vivid art in Europe moved off the walls — and onto the pages of books.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the contrast between where vivid art was displayed before and after the Roman Empire fell apart?

choices.text: Before it fell, art was on glass, but after it fell, art was on stone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because glass and stone were both materials used together in Roman mosaics, not two separate time periods for displaying art.

choices.text: Before it fell, art was on walls, but after it fell, art was in books.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that vivid art was on walls during Roman times, and then moved onto the pages of books after the empire fell.

choices.text: Before it fell, art was in arches, but after it fell, art was in temples.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because arches and temples are both Roman-era locations for carved words, not a before-and-after contrast about vivid art.

choices.text: Before it fell, art was in books, but after it fell, art was on walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the timeline backwards — art was on walls first, then moved to books, not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Books That Glowed

Picture a single letter "B" the size of your whole hand, painted in red and blue and covered in real gold. That is what you would find inside an illuminated manuscript — a hand-written book decorated with bright paintings and gold. The word "illuminated" comes from a Latin word meaning "lit up." The gold and colors seemed to glow, as if a candle burned inside the page itself.

You might think these books were made on paper. Actually, paper was hard to find in Europe back then. Instead, artists wrote on parchment — sheets made from specially prepared animal skins, usually from sheep or calves. Parchment was smooth, tough, and perfect for holding ink and paint.

Here's the surprising part: most people in the medieval period never even saw one of these books. They were extremely rare and expensive. Only wealthy churches, kings, or rich families owned them.
stimulus.content_text: Books That Glowed Picture a single letter "B" the size of your whole hand, painted in red and blue and covered in real gold. That is what you would find inside an illuminated manuscript — a hand-written book decorated with bright paintings and gold. The word "illuminated" comes from a Latin word meaning "lit up." The gold and colors seemed to glow, as if a candle burned inside the page itself. You might think these books were made on paper. Actually, paper was hard to find in Europe back then. Instead, artists wrote on parchment — sheets made from specially prepared animal skins, usually from sheep or calves. Parchment was smooth, tough, and perfect for holding ink and paint. Here's the surprising part: most people in the medieval period never even saw one of these books. They were extremely rare and expensive. Only wealthy churches, kings, or rich families owned them.

prompt._: The main idea of the section is that illuminated manuscripts were extraordinary books. Which statement explains how the author supports this idea?

choices.text: By describing how real gold caused the pages to look like they glowed.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes how real gold made the pages seem to glow like a candle, which supports the idea that these books were extraordinary.

choices.text: By describing how large letters caused regular paper to be hard to find.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects large letters to paper being hard to find — those are two completely different details.

choices.text: By describing how smooth parchment caused the bright paint to fade away.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says parchment was great for holding ink and paint, not that it caused paint to fade away.

choices.text: By describing how wealthy families caused the books to be shared widely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says wealthy families owned these rare books for themselves, not that they shared them widely.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Inside the Monk's Workshop

Most illuminated manuscripts were made by monks — men who lived and worked in monasteries, which were religious communities, like small villages built around a church. Monks wrote in special rooms called scriptoriums — their writing workshops. They sat at wooden desks and used quill pens made from goose feathers dipped in ink.

A single book could take months or even years to finish. Every word was written by hand, letter by letter. One mistake? The monk had to scrape the ink off the parchment and start that part again.

You might think monks were the only people who could read or write back then. Not quite — some wealthy people and scholars could read too, and later, professional workshops outside monasteries also made manuscripts.
stimulus.content_text: Inside the Monk's Workshop Most illuminated manuscripts were made by monks — men who lived and worked in monasteries, which were religious communities, like small villages built around a church. Monks wrote in special rooms called scriptoriums — their writing workshops. They sat at wooden desks and used quill pens made from goose feathers dipped in ink. A single book could take months or even years to finish. Every word was written by hand, letter by letter. One mistake? The monk had to scrape the ink off the parchment and start that part again. You might think monks were the only people who could read or write back then. Not quite — some wealthy people and scholars could read too, and later, professional workshops outside monasteries also made manuscripts.

prompt._: According to the text, why did a monk have to scrape ink off the parchment and start that part again?

choices.text: because the quill pen broke during writing
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a quill pen breaking — it only says monks used quill pens to write.

choices.text: because the monastery required a new copy daily
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says monasteries required a new copy of a book every day — in fact, it says books took months or years to finish.

choices.text: because the monk made a mistake while writing
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that making a mistake meant the monk had to scrape the ink off and redo that part.

choices.text: because the ink dried too quickly on the page
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions ink drying too quickly as a problem for the monks.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Colors from Rocks and Bugs

The artists made their own paints from nature. They crushed a blue stone called lapis lazuli into powder for brilliant blue. They ground a green mineral called malachite for green. For red, they crushed tiny insects called kermes. Then they mixed these powders with egg yolks to make a paint called tempera — thick, bright, and long-lasting.

They pressed thin sheets of real gold, called gold leaf, onto the pages. Then they rubbed the gold smooth with a polishing tool until it gleamed like a mirror. Artists painted tiny detailed pictures called miniatures in the margins. They filled entire pages with a single decorated letter.
stimulus.content_text: Colors from Rocks and Bugs The artists made their own paints from nature. They crushed a blue stone called lapis lazuli into powder for brilliant blue. They ground a green mineral called malachite for green. For red, they crushed tiny insects called kermes. Then they mixed these powders with egg yolks to make a paint called tempera — thick, bright, and long-lasting. They pressed thin sheets of real gold, called gold leaf, onto the pages. Then they rubbed the gold smooth with a polishing tool until it gleamed like a mirror. Artists painted tiny detailed pictures called miniatures in the margins. They filled entire pages with a single decorated letter.

prompt._: In the section about medieval illuminated manuscripts, how were the materials for blue paint and green paint alike?

choices.text: Both were made by polishing stones until they gleamed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rubbing and polishing was done to make gold leaf shine like a mirror, not to create blue or green paint.

choices.text: Both were made by mixing gold leaf with egg yolks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gold leaf was pressed onto pages as decoration, and egg yolks were mixed with powders to make tempera paint — not used for blue or green specifically.

choices.text: Both were made by crushing minerals into powder.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says lapis lazuli (a stone) was crushed for blue and malachite (a mineral) was ground for green — both are minerals turned into powder.

choices.text: Both were made by crushing insects into powder.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only mentions crushing insects (kermes) to make red paint, not blue or green.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Book of Kells

One of the most famous illuminated manuscripts in the world is the Book of Kells. Celtic monks — monks from Ireland and Scotland — created it around the year 800. It contains four books from the Christian Bible called the Gospels. Its pages are packed with interlocking patterns, strange animals, and tiny human figures. Some details are so small you need a magnifying glass to see them. Today, the Book of Kells sits in Trinity College in Dublin, Ireland. These manuscripts tell us what medieval people believed, what they considered beautiful, and how deeply they valued knowledge.
stimulus.content_text: The Book of Kells One of the most famous illuminated manuscripts in the world is the Book of Kells. Celtic monks — monks from Ireland and Scotland — created it around the year 800. It contains four books from the Christian Bible called the Gospels. Its pages are packed with interlocking patterns, strange animals, and tiny human figures. Some details are so small you need a magnifying glass to see them. Today, the Book of Kells sits in Trinity College in Dublin, Ireland. These manuscripts tell us what medieval people believed, what they considered beautiful, and how deeply they valued knowledge.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'Celtic' mean?

choices.text: related to ancient Roman traditions
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions ancient Rome or Roman traditions at all.

choices.text: related to Ireland and Scotland
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly explains that Celtic monks are 'monks from Ireland and Scotland.'

choices.text: related to the Christian Bible
choices.feedback: The Gospels are the Bible books inside the Book of Kells, but that has nothing to do with the meaning of 'Celtic.'

choices.text: related to painting and drawing
choices.feedback: Celtic describes where the monks came from, not painting or drawing.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why These Pages Still Matter

Today you can print a book in seconds. But for hundreds of years, every book on Earth was made one page at a time, by hand. Those monks and artists turned animal skin, crushed rocks, and goose feathers into something people still travel across the world to see. A book was never just words on a page. It was art you could hold.

Those monks and artists turned animal skin, crushed rocks, and goose feathers into something people still travel across the world to see. A book was never just words on a page. It was art you could hold.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says people still travel across the world to see these books, which is strong proof they are valued today.

assessments.title: Heavy and Strong: Romanesque Architecture

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Art from Ancient Rome and the Medieval Period

Stones That Told Stories

Picture a farmer in the year 1100. He cannot read a single word. But he stands before a church doorway and looks up. Carved into the stone above him, he sees angels, demons, and the face of Jesus. He understands every part of the story. The building is speaking to him — not with words, but with art.

This church was built in a style called Romanesque, which means "Roman-like." The name tells you something important. Medieval builders admired the ancient Romans, who had created astonishing structures like the Colosseum and huge domed temples. But Romanesque buildings were not copies of Roman ones. You might think they are the same thing because the names sound so similar. Actually, Romanesque was a new style. Medieval builders borrowed one key idea from Rome — the rounded arch — and used it to create something all their own: massive stone churches.
stimulus.content_text: Art from Ancient Rome and the Medieval PeriodStones That Told Stories Picture a farmer in the year 1100. He cannot read a single word. But he stands before a church doorway and looks up. Carved into the stone above him, he sees angels, demons, and the face of Jesus. He understands every part of the story. The building is speaking to him — not with words, but with art. This church was built in a style called Romanesque, which means "Roman-like." The name tells you something important. Medieval builders admired the ancient Romans, who had created astonishing structures like the Colosseum and huge domed temples. But Romanesque buildings were not copies of Roman ones. You might think they are the same thing because the names sound so similar. Actually, Romanesque was a new style. Medieval builders borrowed one key idea from Rome — the rounded arch — and used it to create something all their own: massive stone churches.

prompt._: According to the text, how were Romanesque buildings different from Roman buildings?

choices.text: They were a new style, not copies of Roman ones
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says 'Romanesque was a new style' and that medieval builders were not simply copying Roman buildings.

choices.text: They were exact copies of Roman temples and arches
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage directly states that Romanesque buildings were NOT copies of Roman ones.

choices.text: They were built without using any Roman ideas at all
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the builders did borrow one Roman idea — the rounded arch — so they used at least some Roman influence.

choices.text: They were smaller and weaker than Roman buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the size or strength of Romanesque and Roman buildings.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Built Like Fortresses

Romanesque churches were popular across Europe from about AD 1000 to 1200. You can still find them in Spain, France, Italy, and Germany. They looked like fortresses — thick-walled castles built to protect soldiers. Their walls were enormous. Their pillars were as wide as tree trunks. Their towers rose heavy and square against the sky.

Why so thick? The roof was made of solid stone, and stone is incredibly heavy. The walls had to hold all that weight. Thick walls meant small windows. It's easy to think medieval builders just didn't know how to make big windows. They absolutely did. But if they cut large openings in those walls, the roof would collapse. So Romanesque churches stayed dim inside, with only thin beams of sunlight slipping through narrow openings.
stimulus.content_text: Built Like Fortresses Romanesque churches were popular across Europe from about AD 1000 to 1200. You can still find them in Spain, France, Italy, and Germany. They looked like fortresses — thick-walled castles built to protect soldiers. Their walls were enormous. Their pillars were as wide as tree trunks. Their towers rose heavy and square against the sky. Why so thick? The roof was made of solid stone, and stone is incredibly heavy. The walls had to hold all that weight. Thick walls meant small windows. It's easy to think medieval builders just didn't know how to make big windows. They absolutely did. But if they cut large openings in those walls, the roof would collapse. So Romanesque churches stayed dim inside, with only thin beams of sunlight slipping through narrow openings.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Romanesque churches have small windows?

choices.text: Because large openings would make the roof fall.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that cutting large openings in the walls would cause the heavy stone roof to collapse.

choices.text: Because the churches were used to hide soldiers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the churches *looked like* fortresses, but they were not actually used to hide soldiers.

choices.text: Because builders did not know how to make them big.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says builders 'absolutely did' know how to make big windows — they just couldn't use them safely.

choices.text: Because builders wanted to keep the inside dim.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the dim inside was a result of needing thick walls, not something builders planned or wanted.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Darkness with a Purpose

That darkness was not a mistake. It created a feeling. Imagine stepping inside. The heavy stone blocks out every sound from the street. The air is cool and still. Shadows fill the ceiling. Huge arches curve above your head like the ribs of a giant. You feel small. That was exactly the point. Builders wanted people to feel awe and reverence — a deep, quiet respect, like the hush that falls over a crowd during something important.
stimulus.content_text: Darkness with a Purpose That darkness was not a mistake. It created a feeling. Imagine stepping inside. The heavy stone blocks out every sound from the street. The air is cool and still. Shadows fill the ceiling. Huge arches curve above your head like the ribs of a giant. You feel small. That was exactly the point. Builders wanted people to feel awe and reverence — a deep, quiet respect, like the hush that falls over a crowd during something important.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens immediately after a person steps inside the Romanesque building?

choices.text: The heavy stone blocks out street sounds.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because right after 'stepping inside,' the very next sentence says the heavy stone blocks out street sounds — making it the first thing described.

choices.text: The dark shadows fill the entire ceiling.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while shadows do fill the ceiling, this is mentioned after the sound detail, so it doesn't happen 'immediately' first.

choices.text: The large crowd becomes completely silent.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the silent crowd is just an example used to explain what 'reverence' means — no actual crowd is mentioned inside the building.

choices.text: The person feels a deep and quiet respect.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because feeling deep, quiet respect is the overall purpose of the building's design, not the immediate first experience when you walk in.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Carvings and Painted Walls

The space above the main entrance of a Romanesque church is called the tympanum — like a half-moon of stone sitting over the doorway. Artists filled it with carved scenes from the Bible. The Last Judgment was a favorite subject. Since most people could not read, these carvings worked like picture books made of rock.

Inside, artists painted directly onto wet plaster walls. These paintings are called frescoes — imagine painting on a wall, except the colors soak into the plaster and become part of the wall itself. Frescoes were often bright with reds, blues, and golds. They brought Bible stories to life in glowing color against those dim stone walls.

Famous Romanesque buildings still stand today. The Church of Saint Sernin in Toulouse, France, stretches long and grand. The Cathedral of Pisa in Italy sits next to a bell tower that leans to one side — you probably know it as the Leaning Tower of Pisa.

Artists filled it with carved scenes from the Bible. The Last Judgment was a favorite subject. Since most people could not read, these carvings worked like picture books made of rock. Inside, artists painted directly onto wet plaster walls. These paintings are called frescoes — imagine painting on a wall, except the colors soak into the plaster and become part of the wall itself. Frescoes were often bright with reds, blues, and golds. They brought Bible stories to life in glowing color against those dim stone walls. Famous Romanesque buildings still stand today. The Church of Saint Sernin in Toulouse, France, stretches long and grand. The Cathedral of Pisa in Italy sits next to a bell tower that leans to one side — you probably know it as the Leaning Tower of Pisa.

prompt._: The text describes the stone carvings as 'picture books made of rock.' Based on this comparison, how are the carvings similar to picture books?

choices.text: Both are placed above the doorways of churches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the carvings are placed above doorways — picture books are not found there at all.

choices.text: Both are painted with bright reds and blues.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the bright reds, blues, and golds describe the frescoes painted on the walls inside the church, not the stone carvings.

choices.text: Both are used to teach people how to read.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the carvings helped people who already could not read, not that they were used to teach people how to read.

choices.text: Both use pictures to share stories with people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both picture books and the stone carvings use pictures to tell stories to people, which is exactly what the passage means by calling the carvings 'picture books made of rock.'

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Came Next

Over time, builders invented pointed arches and outside supports that carried the roof's weight. Now walls could be thinner and windows could be huge. This new style was called Gothic, and it filled churches with colored light. But the older Romanesque churches never lost their power.

Next time you see a rounded stone arch — over a doorway, on a bridge, in your own town — you are looking at an idea that traveled from ancient Rome, through the hands of medieval builders, and all the way to you.
stimulus.content_text: What Came Next Over time, builders invented pointed arches and outside supports that carried the roof's weight. Now walls could be thinner and windows could be huge. This new style was called Gothic, and it filled churches with colored light. But the older Romanesque churches never lost their power. Next time you see a rounded stone arch — over a doorway, on a bridge, in your own town — you are looking at an idea that traveled from ancient Rome, through the hands of medieval builders, and all the way to you.

choices.text: A style that was created only by ancient builders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gothic was created by medieval builders who invented pointed arches and outside supports, not by ancient builders.

choices.text: A style that filled churches with colored light.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Gothic style 'filled churches with colored light,' which matches this answer exactly.

choices.text: A style that traveled all the way from ancient Rome.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the rounded arch idea traveled from ancient Rome — not the Gothic style, which came later.

choices.text: A style that placed rounded arches on town bridges.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rounded arches on bridges are examples of the older Roman and Romanesque style, not the Gothic style.

prompt._: Based on the article, what was a major difference between the outside and the inside of a Romanesque church?

choices.text: The outside was covered in colorful frescoes, while the inside was made of plain stone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the colorful frescoes were painted on the inside walls, not the outside of the church.

choices.text: The outside looked like a heavy fortress, while the inside had brightly painted walls.

choices.text: The outside featured large glass windows, while the inside was kept completely dark.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Romanesque churches had small, narrow windows — not large glass ones — and the inside was dim, not completely dark.

prompt._: Based on the article, why were Gothic builders able to put huge windows in their churches when Romanesque builders could not?

choices.text: Gothic builders used a special type of wet plaster that made the stone walls much stronger.

choices.text: By listing the parts of a church building from the outside to the inside.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author doesn't follow an outside-to-inside order — the article is organized by when different building styles appeared in history.

choices.text: They borrowed Roman ideas but created a new style with its own purpose.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that builders borrowed the rounded arch from Rome but used it to create their own new style of massive stone churches with a specific purpose — making people feel small and full of reverence.

choices.text: They were smaller and weaker than the buildings of ancient Rome.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article describes Romanesque churches as enormous and powerful, with walls as thick as fortresses and towers rising heavy against the sky.

choices.text: They were exact copies that used the same building methods as Rome.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article specifically says 'Romanesque buildings were not copies of Roman ones' and calls it a new style.

choices.text: They replaced Roman buildings that had fallen down across Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions Romanesque churches replacing fallen Roman buildings — it only says medieval builders admired and borrowed ideas from ancient Rome.

syllabus_metadata.title: Heavy and Strong: Romanesque Architecture

assessments.title: Reaching for Heaven: Gothic Architecture and Notre Dame

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Reaching for the Sky: Gothic Cathedrals and the Art of Light

From Roman Arches to Dark Stone Churches

Picture a round stone arch. The ancient Romans built thousands of them — in bridges, aqueducts, and temples across their empire. Curving stones into a half-circle could hold enormous weight.

After Rome fell, builders in medieval Europe kept using those round arches. They constructed thick-walled churches in a style called Romanesque — meaning "Roman-like," because it borrowed Rome's heavy arches and solid stone walls. These churches were strong, but they were also dark. The walls had to be so thick to hold up the roof that there was little room for windows.

Then, around the year 1140, builders in France tried something new.
stimulus.content_text: Reaching for the Sky: Gothic Cathedrals and the Art of LightFrom Roman Arches to Dark Stone Churches Picture a round stone arch. The ancient Romans built thousands of them — in bridges, aqueducts, and temples across their empire. Curving stones into a half-circle could hold enormous weight. After Rome fell, builders in medieval Europe kept using those round arches. They constructed thick-walled churches in a style called Romanesque — meaning "Roman-like," because it borrowed Rome's heavy arches and solid stone walls. These churches were strong, but they were also dark. The walls had to be so thick to hold up the roof that there was little room for windows. Then, around the year 1140, builders in France tried something new.

prompt._: According to the text, Romanesque churches were strong, but they were also what?

choices.text: Very round
choices.feedback: While round arches are mentioned, the passage never describes the churches themselves as being 'very round' as a problem or feature.

choices.text: Very small
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say anything about the size of Romanesque churches, so 'very small' is not supported by the text.

choices.text: Very heavy
choices.feedback: The passage never says the churches were a problem because of their weight — heaviness isn't mentioned as something that made them different from strong.

choices.text: Very dark
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'these churches were strong, but they were also dark' because thick walls left little room for windows.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Tricks That Let Walls Disappear

They stretched the round arch into a point. A pointed arch — an arch shaped like two hands pressed together in prayer — could reach much higher than a round one. It also pushed weight downward more directly, like a tent pole sending rain straight to the ground. This new style of building became known as Gothic architecture.

Builders added another trick: ribbed vaults. These were thin stone ribs that crisscrossed the ceiling like the skeleton of an umbrella. Instead of the whole wall holding up the roof, the ribs carried the weight along specific paths.

But the biggest surprise stood outside the building. Flying buttresses are stone arms that reach out from the outer wall and press against it, the way you might brace a wobbly bookshelf by pushing it from the side. They carried the roof's weight outward and down to the ground. Now the walls no longer needed to be thick and heavy. They could be thin — thin enough for enormous windows.
stimulus.content_text: The Tricks That Let Walls Disappear They stretched the round arch into a point. A pointed arch — an arch shaped like two hands pressed together in prayer — could reach much higher than a round one. It also pushed weight downward more directly, like a tent pole sending rain straight to the ground. This new style of building became known as Gothic architecture. Builders added another trick: ribbed vaults. These were thin stone ribs that crisscrossed the ceiling like the skeleton of an umbrella. Instead of the whole wall holding up the roof, the ribs carried the weight along specific paths. But the biggest surprise stood outside the building. Flying buttresses are stone arms that reach out from the outer wall and press against it, the way you might brace a wobbly bookshelf by pushing it from the side. They carried the roof's weight outward and down to the ground. Now the walls no longer needed to be thick and heavy. They could be thin — thin enough for enormous windows.

prompt._: Based on the section about Gothic architecture, why could cathedral builders add very large windows to their buildings?

choices.text: Because flying buttresses carried the roof's weight outward and down
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that flying buttresses carried the roof's weight outward and down, so walls no longer needed to be thick — making large windows possible.

choices.text: Because ribbed vaults turned the windows into supports for the roof
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because ribbed vaults carried weight along specific paths in the ceiling, not through the windows.

choices.text: Because round arches pushed the roof's weight straight to the ground
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says round arches were the older style, and it was the pointed arch — not the round arch — that pushed weight downward more directly.

choices.text: Because pointed arches made the outside walls thicker and stronger
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because pointed arches helped direct weight downward, but it was flying buttresses that allowed walls to become thinner, not thicker.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Windows That Told Stories

And what windows they were! Gothic cathedrals held huge stained glass windows — colored glass pieces fitted together like a puzzle. Glassmakers added metals and minerals to melted glass to create rich colors. Cobalt made brilliant blue. Gold made red. Copper made green.

You might think these windows were just for decoration. Actually, they had an important job. Most people in medieval Europe could not read. Stained glass windows were their books. Artists arranged colored glass into pictures that told stories from the Bible. When sunlight poured through, the whole cathedral glowed with blue, red, green, and gold. For people who lived in dark wooden houses, stepping inside must have felt like stepping into heaven.

Glassmakers added metals and minerals to melted glass to create rich colors. Cobalt made brilliant blue. Gold made red. Copper made green. You might think these windows were just for decoration. Actually, they had an important job. Most people in medieval Europe could not read. Stained glass windows were their books. Artists arranged colored glass into pictures that told stories from the Bible. When sunlight poured through, the whole cathedral glowed with blue, red, green, and gold. For people who lived in dark wooden houses, stepping inside must have felt like stepping into heaven.

prompt._: According to the text, why did artists arrange colored glass into pictures that told stories from the Bible?

choices.text: Because sunlight made the glass glow with bright light
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sunlight making the glass glow describes how beautiful the windows looked, not the reason artists chose to tell Bible stories in them.

choices.text: Because the cathedral needed decoration on its walls
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says the windows were NOT just for decoration — they had an important job of telling stories to people who couldn't read.

choices.text: Because most people in medieval Europe could not read
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that most people couldn't read, so stained glass pictures were their way of learning Bible stories — like a book made of glass.

choices.text: Because glassmakers wanted to show off their colors
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions glassmakers wanting to show off their skills — that idea isn't in the text at all.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Hundreds of Workers, Hundreds of Years

It's easy to imagine one brilliant architect designing a whole cathedral alone. Here's the surprising part: these buildings took so long to finish that grandchildren completed what their grandparents had started. Notre Dame Cathedral in Paris began in 1163 and took nearly 200 years. Its twin towers rise 226 feet high. Its famous rose windows — giant circular stained glass windows shaped like blooming flowers — stretch over 40 feet across and hold thousands of glass pieces. A fire badly damaged Notre Dame in 2019, but people in France and around the world worked to repair it.

Stonemasons, glassmakers, carpenters, and laborers hauled stone up scaffolding by hand. Whole towns came together on a single cathedral. Chartres Cathedral in France still holds some of the finest stained glass in the world. Cologne Cathedral in Germany took over 600 years to finish.

choices.text: the pink color of the glass pieces

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says nothing about a smell inside the cathedral — windows let in light, not scent.
