choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions thorny or sharp edges on the windows at all.

choices.text: the flower-like shape of the windows
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says rose windows are 'shaped like blooming flowers,' which is exactly what a rose is.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Buildings Made of Light

The Romans gave Europe the arch. Medieval builders made it darker. Then Gothic builders did the opposite — they made stone walls almost disappear and filled the empty space with color and light. The next time you see sunlight streaming through a colored window, you are looking at an idea that is nearly nine hundred years old, still glowing.
stimulus.content_text: Buildings Made of Light The Romans gave Europe the arch. Medieval builders made it darker. Then Gothic builders did the opposite — they made stone walls almost disappear and filled the empty space with color and light. The next time you see sunlight streaming through a colored window, you are looking at an idea that is nearly nine hundred years old, still glowing.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by 'Gothic builders' making 'stone walls almost disappear'?

choices.text: They replaced wall space with colored windows
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Gothic builders 'filled the empty space with color and light,' meaning they used colored windows where solid stone walls used to be.

choices.text: They built walls that were thinner than Roman walls
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not compare wall thickness between Gothic and Roman buildings.

choices.text: They painted the stone walls a lighter color
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions painting the walls — it talks about light coming through colored windows.

choices.text: They tore down the old stone walls completely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the walls 'almost disappear,' not that they were completely torn down.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the older stone churches different from the newer cathedrals?

choices.text: The older churches were built very fast, but the newer ones took many long years.
choices.feedback: The article never says the older churches were built quickly, so we can't compare building speeds between the two styles.

choices.text: The older churches used plain clear glass, but the newer ones used colored stones.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the newer cathedrals used colored glass (not colored stones) for their windows, and the article never mentions the older churches having plain clear glass.

choices.text: The older churches had very thin walls, but the newer ones needed thick walls.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says the older Romanesque churches had thick, heavy walls, while the newer Gothic cathedrals had thin walls thanks to flying buttresses.

choices.text: The older churches were very dark inside, but the newer ones were full of light.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article says the older churches were dark because their thick walls left little room for windows, while the new Gothic cathedrals were filled with light through enormous stained glass windows.

prompt._: What best explains why Gothic cathedrals may have felt heavenly to many people in medieval Europe?

choices.text: Their round Roman arches made the buildings look ancient and strong
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Gothic cathedrals used pointed arches, not round Roman arches, which were part of the older style.

choices.text: Their huge colored windows filled dark spaces with glowing light
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says that for people used to dark wooden houses, the glowing colored light inside a cathedral must have felt like stepping into heaven.

choices.text: Their long building time made towns feel proud of their work
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says the long building time made the cathedrals feel heavenly to the people inside them.

choices.text: Their thick stone walls kept the rooms cool and quiet
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because thick stone walls belonged to the older Romanesque churches — Gothic builders actually made the walls thin to let in more light.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information to explain the history of cathedrals?

choices.text: By comparing the different types of colored glass used in ancient and medieval buildings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares glass from ancient Roman buildings to medieval ones — it only talks about stained glass in Gothic cathedrals.

choices.text: By describing the exact steps workers took to rebuild Notre Dame after a terrible fire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the rebuilding of Notre Dame is only mentioned briefly in one paragraph, and the article is mostly about the history of Gothic architecture, not that fire.

choices.text: By showing the order of changes from ancient Roman arches to bright Gothic cathedrals.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author moves in time order from Roman arches to Romanesque dark churches to Gothic bright cathedrals, showing each change that led to the next.

choices.text: By explaining how builders solved the problem of dark churches by inventing the round arch.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the round arch was invented by the Romans long before dark churches were a problem — Gothic builders actually replaced the round arch with the pointed arch to solve that problem.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was the job of the stone walls different in Romanesque churches compared to Gothic cathedrals?

choices.text: In Romanesque churches, the walls pushed weight straight down, while in Gothic cathedrals, they pushed weight to the side.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pushing weight straight down describes how the pointed arch works, not a difference in what the walls did in each type of church.

choices.text: In Romanesque churches, the walls supported outside stone arms, while in Gothic cathedrals, the walls supported thin stone ribs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because flying buttresses supported the Gothic walls from the outside, not the other way around, and ribbed vaults were on the ceiling, not supported by the walls.

choices.text: In Romanesque churches, the walls held up the roof, while in Gothic cathedrals, other stone pieces carried the weight.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states Romanesque walls had to be thick to hold up the roof, while Gothic cathedrals used ribbed vaults and flying buttresses to carry the weight instead.

choices.text: In Romanesque churches, the walls only held up the windows, while in Gothic cathedrals, the walls held up the entire heavy roof.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it gets the roles backwards — Romanesque walls held up the roof, not just the windows, and Gothic walls were freed from holding the roof up.

syllabus_metadata.title: Reaching for Heaven: Gothic Architecture and Notre Dame

assessments.title: A Rebirth of Learning

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Renaissance: Europe's Great Rebirth

A Dusty Discovery

In 1345, an Italian scholar named Petrarch found a stack of old letters stuffed away in a church library. They were written more than a thousand years earlier by Cicero, a famous Roman writer and speaker. Petrarch held the crumbling pages in his hands and felt a shock of excitement. These letters revealed how ancient Romans thought about friendship, duty, and government. To Petrarch, reading them was like hearing a brilliant voice that had been silent for centuries suddenly speak again.

That moment captures something important. Across Europe, ancient Greek and Roman writings, art, and ideas had been partly forgotten for hundreds of years — a long stretch of time called the Middle Ages. You might think that no one studied or created anything during those centuries, but that's not quite true. Monks living in monasteries — religious communities where men devoted their lives to prayer and work — carefully copied old books by hand and kept them safe on wooden shelves. Learning never vanished completely. Still, most people could not read, books were rare and expensive, and the brilliant ideas of ancient Greece and Rome reached very few.

Then, starting around 1350, curiosity caught fire. Scholars like Petrarch began hunting for forgotten texts. Artists studied ancient statues. Thinkers asked bold new questions. This explosion of rediscovery and creativity is called the Renaissance, a French word that means "rebirth." It was a rebirth of interest in the ancient world — and a birth of daring new ideas.
stimulus.content_text: The Renaissance: Europe's Great RebirthA Dusty Discovery In 1345, an Italian scholar named Petrarch found a stack of old letters stuffed away in a church library. They were written more than a thousand years earlier by Cicero, a famous Roman writer and speaker. Petrarch held the crumbling pages in his hands and felt a shock of excitement. These letters revealed how ancient Romans thought about friendship, duty, and government. To Petrarch, reading them was like hearing a brilliant voice that had been silent for centuries suddenly speak again. That moment captures something important. Learning never vanished completely. Still, most people could not read, books were rare and expensive, and the brilliant ideas of ancient Greece and Rome reached very few. Then, starting around 1350, curiosity caught fire. Scholars like Petrarch began hunting for forgotten texts. Artists studied ancient statues. Thinkers asked bold new questions. This explosion of rediscovery and creativity is called the Renaissance, a French word that means "rebirth." It was a rebirth of interest in the ancient world — and a birth of daring new ideas.

prompt._: According to the text, how were most people during the Middle Ages different from scholars like Petrarch?

choices.text: Most people lived in monasteries and copied books by hand
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says it was monks in monasteries who copied books, not most ordinary people.

choices.text: Most people studied ancient statues and asked bold questions
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because studying statues and asking bold questions describes what happened during the Renaissance, not what most people did during the Middle Ages.

choices.text: Most people could not read and rarely saw ancient ideas
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that most people could not read and that ancient ideas reached very few of them.

choices.text: Most people wrote letters about friendship and government
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because writing letters about friendship and government describes Cicero, the ancient Roman, not ordinary people during the Middle Ages.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Italy First?

The Renaissance began on the Italian peninsula — a piece of land surrounded by water on three sides, like a boot stepping into the Mediterranean Sea. It lasted from about 1350 to 1600. But why Italy? Several reasons came together.

First, Italian cities like Florence, Venice, and Rome had grown rich from trade with Asia and the Middle East. Wealthy merchants had money to spend, and many of them chose to spend it on paintings, buildings, and books. Second, Italy was the homeland of ancient Rome. Roman ruins, statues, and carvings stood everywhere — in fields, in town squares, even built into the walls of newer buildings. These remains were constant reminders of what humans had once achieved. They made people hungry to achieve great things again.
stimulus.content_text: Why Italy First? The Renaissance began on the Italian peninsula — a piece of land surrounded by water on three sides, like a boot stepping into the Mediterranean Sea. It lasted from about 1350 to 1600. But why Italy? Several reasons came together. First, Italian cities like Florence, Venice, and Rome had grown rich from trade with Asia and the Middle East. Wealthy merchants had money to spend, and many of them chose to spend it on paintings, buildings, and books. Second, Italy was the homeland of ancient Rome. Roman ruins, statues, and carvings stood everywhere — in fields, in town squares, even built into the walls of newer buildings. These remains were constant reminders of what humans had once achieved. They made people hungry to achieve great things again.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did ancient Roman ruins help start the Renaissance in Italy?

choices.text: They inspired people to want to achieve great things again.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the ruins 'made people hungry to achieve great things again,' which means they inspired people.

choices.text: They attracted traders from Asia and the Middle East.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because traders from Asia and the Middle East came to Italy for trade, not because of Roman ruins.

choices.text: They provided materials for merchants to sell for profit.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone selling Roman ruins or materials for profit.

choices.text: They taught people how to build newer and stronger buildings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says people studied the ruins to learn how to build stronger buildings.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A New Way of Thinking

At the heart of the Renaissance was an idea called humanism. Humanism was the belief that human beings have enormous potential and that studying, creating, and thinking can help people become their best selves. Think of it this way: if medieval thinkers often looked upward toward heaven for answers, humanists also looked around at the world and inward at their own abilities.

Humanists valued philosophy — the study of big questions about life, knowledge, and how to live well, like a never-ending conversation about what truly matters. They read the works of Greek thinkers like Plato and Aristotle and Roman writers like Cicero and Virgil. They believed that studying these ancient texts could make people wiser and more virtuous, meaning morally good and honest.

Humanist scholars traveled across Europe searching for ancient manuscripts — handwritten documents, often on animal skin or rough paper, created long before printing existed. Many of these manuscripts had sat unread in monastery libraries for centuries. Scholars translated them from Greek and Latin into Italian and other languages so more people could read them. They opened schools where students studied poetry, history, philosophy, and public speaking alongside religion. This kind of well-rounded education was new and exciting.

prompt._: According to the text, what did humanist scholars do after they found the ancient manuscripts?

choices.text: They hid them away inside dark monastery libraries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hiding the manuscripts is the opposite of what scholars did — the manuscripts were already sitting unread in monastery libraries, and scholars brought them out to share.

choices.text: They wrote them out on rough paper and animal skin.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because writing on rough paper and animal skin describes how the original manuscripts were created, not what scholars did after discovering them.

choices.text: They translated them into Italian and other languages.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that scholars translated the manuscripts from Greek and Latin into Italian and other languages so more people could read them.

choices.text: They printed them using a brand-new printing press.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a printing press — it actually says manuscripts were made long before printing existed.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Slow Changes, Big Results

The Renaissance did not happen overnight. It grew slowly over many decades, fed by trade, wealth, curiosity, and the courage to question old ways of thinking. Artists began painting the human body with lifelike accuracy. Scientists observed the natural world with fresh eyes. Architects designed buildings that blended ancient Roman styles with bold new ideas. Writers composed poems and stories in everyday languages instead of only in Latin.

Here's something easy to overlook, though: these changes mostly reached wealthy people living in cities. If you were a farmer working the land outside Florence, your daily life — planting, harvesting, surviving — probably looked much the same as your grandparents' lives had. The new art hung in rich families' homes. The new books sat in wealthy people's libraries. The Renaissance was real and powerful, but it did not touch everyone equally.

Scientists observed the natural world with fresh eyes. Architects designed buildings that blended ancient Roman styles with bold new ideas. Writers composed poems and stories in everyday languages instead of only in Latin. Here's something easy to overlook, though: these changes mostly reached wealthy people living in cities. If you were a farmer working the land outside Florence, your daily life — planting, harvesting, surviving — probably looked much the same as your grandparents' lives had. The new art hung in rich families' homes. The new books sat in wealthy people's libraries. The Renaissance was real and powerful, but it did not touch everyone equally.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how did life for a farmer compare to life for a wealthy city person during the Renaissance?

choices.text: Both groups enjoyed new art and books equally
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says the Renaissance 'did not touch everyone equally' and that art and books went to wealthy people, not farmers.

choices.text: Both groups continued living the same as before
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wealthy city people did experience many changes, like new art, books, and buildings — it was farmers who saw little difference.

choices.text: Farmers gained more from the Renaissance than the wealthy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — wealthy people benefited from the Renaissance, not farmers.

choices.text: Farmers saw little change while wealthy people benefited
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows farmers' lives barely changed while wealthy city people enjoyed new art, books, and ideas.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
An Idea That Still Lives

The Renaissance gave Europe something it had been missing: the fierce belief that ordinary human beings could observe, create, question, and improve the world around them. That belief did not stay locked inside Italian palaces forever. Centuries later, it shaped the idea that every person deserves the chance to learn, think, and make something meaningful.

That belief did not stay locked inside Italian palaces forever. Centuries later, it shaped the idea that every person deserves the chance to learn, think, and make something meaningful.

prompt._: Based on the article, which statement best explains the main idea of what caused the Renaissance?

choices.text: People forgot how to read during the Middle Ages until Petrarch found some old letters.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says learning never completely vanished during the Middle Ages — monks kept books and knowledge alive the whole time.

choices.text: Studying ancient writings inspired people to ask new questions and create new things.

choices.text: They both opened schools to teach about public speaking.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only humanist scholars are described as opening schools in the article — monks are not.

choices.text: They both painted lifelike pictures of the human body.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because painting lifelike human bodies was something Renaissance artists did, not monks or humanist scholars.

choices.text: They both traveled across Europe to find new artwork.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because monks stayed in their monasteries copying books, and humanist scholars traveled to find manuscripts — not artwork.

assessments.title: Florence and the Medici Family

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Florence and the Family That Funded the Renaissance

A Teenager Moves Into a Palace

Sometime around 1489, a fourteen-year-old boy named Michelangelo Buonarroti carried his belongings into one of the grandest homes in Florence, Italy. His father was not rich. But the most powerful man in the city, Lorenzo de' Medici, had spotted the boy carving stone in a sculpture garden — and invited him to live with the Medici family. Michelangelo ate dinner at their table. He studied ancient Greek and Roman statues in their collection. He learned alongside poets, philosophers, and other artists. That teenager would grow up to paint the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. But first, he needed a chance. The Medici gave him one.
stimulus.content_text: Florence and the Family That Funded the RenaissanceA Teenager Moves Into a Palace Sometime around 1489, a fourteen-year-old boy named Michelangelo Buonarroti carried his belongings into one of the grandest homes in Florence, Italy. His father was not rich. But the most powerful man in the city, Lorenzo de' Medici, had spotted the boy carving stone in a sculpture garden — and invited him to live with the Medici family. Michelangelo ate dinner at their table. He studied ancient Greek and Roman statues in their collection. He learned alongside poets, philosophers, and other artists. That teenager would grow up to paint the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. But first, he needed a chance. The Medici gave him one.

prompt._: The text contrasts Lorenzo de' Medici with Michelangelo's father. According to the text, what was true about the father?

choices.text: He carved stone statues.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Michelangelo himself who was spotted carving stone, not his father.

choices.text: He was a famous painter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the father being a painter or having any connection to painting.

choices.text: He was not a rich man.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses the exact words 'His father was not rich' to contrast the father with the wealthy Lorenzo de' Medici.

choices.text: He lived in a grand home.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the grand home belonged to the Medici family, not to Michelangelo's father.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A City Built on Wool and Gold

Florence did not become a center of art overnight. You might picture the Renaissance as a sudden explosion — one day medieval, the next day modern. Actually, the changes built slowly over many decades. Medieval traditions continued even as new ideas took root beside them, like new flowers pushing up through old stone.

By the 1300s and 1400s, Florence was one of the wealthiest cities in Europe. Its people made money through banking, wool trading, and weaving fine cloth. The city's gold coin, called the florin, was trusted in markets from London to Constantinople, the great capital of the eastern Roman Empire in what is now Turkey. All that wealth gave Florence something unusual: money to spend on things beyond survival.

And the wealth was not locked away with just a few powerful people. Skilled craftspeople filled the city's workshops alongside guild workers and ordinary merchants. Florence buzzed with people who made things, traded things, and argued about ideas.
stimulus.content_text: A City Built on Wool and Gold Florence did not become a center of art overnight. You might picture the Renaissance as a sudden explosion — one day medieval, the next day modern. Actually, the changes built slowly over many decades. Medieval traditions continued even as new ideas took root beside them, like new flowers pushing up through old stone. By the 1300s and 1400s, Florence was one of the wealthiest cities in Europe. Its people made money through banking, wool trading, and weaving fine cloth. The city's gold coin, called the florin, was trusted in markets from London to Constantinople, the great capital of the eastern Roman Empire in what is now Turkey. All that wealth gave Florence something unusual: money to spend on things beyond survival. And the wealth was not locked away with just a few powerful people. Skilled craftspeople filled the city's workshops alongside guild workers and ordinary merchants. Florence buzzed with people who made things, traded things, and argued about ideas.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why was Florence able to spend money on things beyond survival?

choices.text: Because its people stopped following medieval traditions
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says medieval traditions kept going even while new ideas were growing, so people didn't stop following them.

choices.text: Because its artists became famous across Europe
choices.feedback: The passage never says artists brought wealth to Florence — art came later, after the city was already rich.

choices.text: Because its gold coin was created in Constantinople
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the florin was Florence's own gold coin that was trusted in Constantinople, but it wasn't created there.

choices.text: Because its banking and cloth trades brought in wealth
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says Florence earned money through banking, wool trading, and weaving cloth, and that wealth let them spend on more than just survival.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Bankers Who Bet on Beauty

Still, no family shaped Florence's golden age more than the Medici. The Medici ran one of the largest banking businesses in Europe, with branches in Rome, Venice, London, and other major cities. But they did not simply stack up coins. They became patrons of the arts — people who use their money to support artists and thinkers, the way a sports sponsor today pays for a team's equipment and training.

Why bother? The Medici understood that paying for beautiful buildings and famous paintings brought them prestige — respect and admiration from others. A dramatic church or a sweeping sculpture told the world: this family matters.

The most famous Medici was Lorenzo, known as Lorenzo the Magnificent. He ruled Florence from 1469 until his death in 1492. He held no royal title. He was a private citizen who used his wealth and sharp political skills to guide the city. Lorenzo wrote poetry himself and surrounded Florence with art and ideas.
stimulus.content_text: Bankers Who Bet on Beauty Still, no family shaped Florence's golden age more than the Medici. The Medici ran one of the largest banking businesses in Europe, with branches in Rome, Venice, London, and other major cities. But they did not simply stack up coins. They became patrons of the arts — people who use their money to support artists and thinkers, the way a sports sponsor today pays for a team's equipment and training. Why bother? The Medici understood that paying for beautiful buildings and famous paintings brought them prestige — respect and admiration from others. A dramatic church or a sweeping sculpture told the world: this family matters. The most famous Medici was Lorenzo, known as Lorenzo the Magnificent. He ruled Florence from 1469 until his death in 1492. He held no royal title. He was a private citizen who used his wealth and sharp political skills to guide the city. Lorenzo wrote poetry himself and surrounded Florence with art and ideas.

prompt._: According to the text, which event happened in the year 1492?

choices.text: The Medici paid for new sports.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Medici paid for art and ideas, not sports equipment or teams.

choices.text: The Medici opened a large bank.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never gives a year for when the Medici opened their bank.

choices.text: Lorenzo began ruling the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Lorenzo began ruling in 1469, not 1492.

choices.text: Lorenzo the Magnificent died.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states Lorenzo ruled 'until his death in 1492,' so 1492 is the year he died.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Artists Did Not Work Alone

The Medici supported artists like Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo da Vinci early in their careers. It is easy to imagine these artists as lone geniuses, dreaming up masterpieces by themselves. Here's the surprising part: most Renaissance artists trained in busy workshops, painting alongside a master and several assistants. A young artist might spend years preparing materials before ever touching a brush to a real painting. Art was a team effort as much as an individual one.

The Medici also supported scholars and philosophers. They founded a school in Florence dedicated to studying the ideas of Plato, an ancient Greek thinker who asked big questions about truth, beauty, and how people should live. The family built libraries and collected ancient manuscripts — handwritten books and documents, often centuries old and fragile as dried leaves. Without that effort, some of those writings might have been lost forever.
stimulus.content_text: Artists Did Not Work Alone The Medici supported artists like Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo da Vinci early in their careers. It is easy to imagine these artists as lone geniuses, dreaming up masterpieces by themselves. Here's the surprising part: most Renaissance artists trained in busy workshops, painting alongside a master and several assistants. A young artist might spend years preparing materials before ever touching a brush to a real painting. Art was a team effort as much as an individual one. The Medici also supported scholars and philosophers. They founded a school in Florence dedicated to studying the ideas of Plato, an ancient Greek thinker who asked big questions about truth, beauty, and how people should live. The family built libraries and collected ancient manuscripts — handwritten books and documents, often centuries old and fragile as dried leaves. Without that effort, some of those writings might have been lost forever.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was a real Renaissance artist different from the idea of a "lone genius"?

choices.text: They wrote fragile documents instead of touching a real brush.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says young artists spent years preparing materials 'before ever touching a brush to a real painting,' meaning they did eventually paint — and fragile documents were old manuscripts, not something artists wrote.

choices.text: They built large libraries instead of working in art shops.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because building libraries was something the Medici family did, not what artists did in their workshops.

choices.text: They studied ancient thinkers instead of learning from a master.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because studying ancient thinkers like Plato was something scholars and philosophers did, not how artists learned their craft.

choices.text: They worked in busy teams instead of painting all alone.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says artists worked in busy workshops with a master and assistants, making art a team effort rather than a solo one.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Power and Its Price

The Medici were not gentle saints. They could be ruthless, using their fortune to silence enemies and control elections. Twice, the people of Florence drove the Medici out of the city. Twice, the family clawed their way back to power. Their story is a reminder that the same wealth that funded great art also funded sharp political games.
stimulus.content_text: Power and Its Price The Medici were not gentle saints. They could be ruthless, using their fortune to silence enemies and control elections. Twice, the people of Florence drove the Medici out of the city. Twice, the family clawed their way back to power. Their story is a reminder that the same wealth that funded great art also funded sharp political games.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by 'sharp political games'?

choices.text: quick decisions about art funding
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'sharp political games' is about fighting for political control, not making decisions about art funding.

choices.text: clever and tough moves for power
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes the Medici using their wealth to silence enemies and control elections, which are clever and tough moves to gain power.

choices.text: friendly debates about city rules
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Medici silenced their enemies rather than having friendly debates with them.

choices.text: fun contests between families
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Medici's actions were ruthless and dangerous, not fun or playful.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What They Left Behind

Walk through Florence today, and the Medici era is everywhere. The Uffizi Gallery, one of the most famous art museums on Earth, started as a government office building. Cosimo I de' Medici had it built in 1560 to house the city's magistrates — in fact, "uffizi" means "offices" in Italian. Over time, the Medici filled parts of the building with their growing art collection, and it eventually became the world-class museum visitors enjoy today. The enormous dome of the Florence Cathedral rises above the rooftops. Churches glow with frescoes — paintings made on wet plaster walls, their colors locked into the surface as the plaster dried.

None of it appeared because one genius had one brilliant idea. It took decades of slow change, thousands of skilled hands, and people willing to spend their money on something that would outlast them.

assessments.title: Leonardo da Vinci: The Ultimate Renaissance Man

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Renaissance

A Young Painter Stuns His Teacher

Sometime around 1472, a teenage boy picked up a brush in a busy Florence workshop and painted an angel so beautiful that his teacher, a famous artist named Andrea del Verrocchio, reportedly set down his own brush forever. That teenager was Leonardo da Vinci, and he was living in the middle of one of the most exciting periods in human history — the Renaissance.

The Renaissance was a time of renewed curiosity about art, science, and learning that stretched roughly from the 1300s to the 1600s. The word itself means "rebirth" in French. Before this period, during the Middle Ages, most Europeans focused on religious life and tradition. But during the Renaissance, people began asking new questions about the human body, the stars, mathematics, and nature. You might think the Renaissance was only about painting and sculpture. Actually, it touched everything — medicine, engineering, music, architecture, and more. It began in the cities of Italy, especially Florence, and eventually spread across Europe.

That teenager was Leonardo da Vinci, and he was living in the middle of one of the most exciting periods in human history — the Renaissance. The Renaissance was a time of renewed curiosity about art, science, and learning that stretched roughly from the 1300s to the 1600s. The word itself means "rebirth" in French. Before this period, during the Middle Ages, most Europeans focused on religious life and tradition. But during the Renaissance, people began asking new questions about the human body, the stars, mathematics, and nature. You might think the Renaissance was only about painting and sculpture. Actually, it touched everything — medicine, engineering, music, architecture, and more. It began in the cities of Italy, especially Florence, and eventually spread across Europe.

prompt._: According to the text, what did most Europeans focus on during the Middle Ages, in contrast to the Renaissance?

choices.text: Famous paintings and sculptures
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never says the Middle Ages were focused on famous paintings and sculptures; that's more connected to the Renaissance.

choices.text: The human body and the stars
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — asking new questions about the human body and the stars is what people did during the Renaissance, not the Middle Ages.

choices.text: Religious life and tradition
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'during the Middle Ages, most Europeans focused on religious life and tradition' — those are the exact words used.

choices.text: Medicine and new engineering
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — medicine and new engineering were also part of the Renaissance period, not what the Middle Ages were known for.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Florence: Where It All Started for Leonardo

Leonardo was born in 1452 in the small town of Vinci, near Florence, Italy. Florence was the beating heart of the Renaissance. Wealthy families there paid artists and thinkers to create new works and explore new ideas. As a teenager, Leonardo was apprenticed to Verrocchio — meaning he lived and trained in the older artist's workshop, the way a young soccer player might join an academy to learn from experienced coaches. Leonardo learned fast. He studied painting, sculpture, and mechanics all at once.

He studied the flight of birds, the flow of water, the movement of muscles, and the structure of the human skull. He designed machines that would not be built for centuries. His wide-ranging genius inspired the term "Renaissance man," meaning a person with many talents and deep knowledge in different fields.

Wealthy families there paid artists and thinkers to create new works and explore new ideas. As a teenager, Leonardo was apprenticed to Verrocchio — meaning he lived and trained in the older artist's workshop, the way a young soccer player might join an academy to learn from experienced coaches. Leonardo learned fast. He studied painting, sculpture, and mechanics all at once. He studied the flight of birds, the flow of water, the movement of muscles, and the structure of the human skull. He designed machines that would not be built for centuries. His wide-ranging genius inspired the term "Renaissance man," meaning a person with many talents and deep knowledge in different fields.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did artists and thinkers in Florence create new works?

choices.text: Because rich families paid them to explore new ideas.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that wealthy families paid artists and thinkers to create new works and explore new ideas.

choices.text: Because they studied the movement of muscles and water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because studying muscles and water describes what Leonardo observed to learn, not the reason artists and thinkers in Florence were creating new works.

choices.text: Because older artists trained them in special workshops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because training in workshops explains how artists learned, not why they created new works.

choices.text: Because they wanted to design machines for the future.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because designing machines for the future was something Leonardo did, and the passage never says that was the reason artists in Florence created new works.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Two Paintings the World Has Never Forgotten

Leonardo's two most famous paintings are among the most recognized works of art ever made. The first is the Mona Lisa, which now hangs in the Louvre Museum in Paris, France. It shows a woman with a mysterious smile that seems to shift depending on where you stand. Leonardo worked on this painting for years, applying thin layers of paint to create soft, lifelike shadows on her face. For more than five hundred years, people have tried to figure out who she was and what she was thinking.

The second masterpiece is The Last Supper, a mural — a large painting made directly on a wall, like a picture built into the building itself. This one covers an entire wall in a dining hall in Milan, Italy. It shows Jesus and his twelve apostles, his closest followers, at their final meal together. Leonardo captured the exact moment when Jesus announces that one of them will betray him. Each apostle reacts differently — shock, denial, grief. Leonardo used their hands, faces, and body positions to tell the story without a single word.

Leonardo worked on this painting for years, applying thin layers of paint to create soft, lifelike shadows on her face. For more than five hundred years, people have tried to figure out who she was and what she was thinking. The second masterpiece is The Last Supper, a mural — a large painting made directly on a wall, like a picture built into the building itself. This one covers an entire wall in a dining hall in Milan, Italy. It shows Jesus and his twelve apostles, his closest followers, at their final meal together. Leonardo captured the exact moment when Jesus announces that one of them will betray him. Each apostle reacts differently — shock, denial, grief. Leonardo used their hands, faces, and body positions to tell the story without a single word.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while they are at their final meal, it is the shocking news Jesus announces — not the meal itself — that causes their emotional reactions.

choices.text: because Jesus tells them one will betray him
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Leonardo captured the moment Jesus announces one apostle will betray him, which is what causes their reactions of shock, denial, and grief.

choices.text: because Leonardo painted them at different times
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about the apostles being painted at different times.

choices.text: because the mural is starting to fade on the wall
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the mural fading, and that has nothing to do with how the apostles are reacting in the painting.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Notebooks Full of the Future

Leonardo filled thousands of notebook pages with drawings and ideas. He sketched helicopters, parachutes, tanks, and scuba gear. They were designs on paper, dreams drawn in ink. It would take hundreds of years before engineers built anything like them. He wrote his notes in mirror writing, meaning you would need to hold the page up to a mirror to read it easily. Some scholars think he wanted to keep his ideas secret. Others believe it was simply more comfortable for him because he was left-handed.

Leonardo's curiosity reached into the human body itself. He dissected — carefully cut open and examined — human bodies to understand how muscles, bones, and organs worked. Think of it like taking apart a clock to see every gear inside. His anatomical drawings, meaning his detailed sketches of body parts and systems, were more accurate than any medical illustrations of his time. He also studied how water flows over rocks, how light creates shadows, and how plants grow toward the sun. He believed art and science were not separate subjects but two ways of seeing the same world.

choices.feedback: The passage says Leonardo studied muscles, bones, AND organs, so his drawings weren't focused on just bones and muscles.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Brilliant but Unfinished

For all his genius, Leonardo had a real weakness: he often started projects and never finished them. Some of his paintings remain incomplete to this day. New ideas constantly pulled his attention away, like a bee jumping from flower to flower before finishing with any single bloom.

Leonardo da Vinci died in France in 1519 at the age of sixty-seven. But the ideas he left behind — even the unfinished ones — have inspired artists, scientists, and inventors for centuries since. That restless curiosity did not belong only to him. It belongs to anyone willing to look closely and keep asking why.

assessments.title: Michelangelo: Sculptor and Painter of Genius

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Michelangelo: The Artist Who Freed Figures from Stone

A Block of Marble Nobody Wanted

A massive slab of marble sat in a yard in Florence, Italy, for nearly forty years. Two sculptors had already tried to carve it and failed. The stone was too tall, too narrow, and too damaged. Everyone agreed it was useless. Then, in 1501, a twenty-six-year-old artist named Michelangelo Buonarroti looked at that rejected block and saw something no one else could see — a hero waiting to step out of the stone.

Two sculptors had already tried to carve it and failed. The stone was too tall, too narrow, and too damaged. Everyone agreed it was useless. Then, in 1501, a twenty-six-year-old artist named Michelangelo Buonarroti looked at that rejected block and saw something no one else could see — a hero waiting to step out of the stone.

prompt._: According to the text, how did most people's view of the marble block contrast with Michelangelo's view of it?

choices.text: Most people thought it was useless, but Michelangelo saw a hero inside it
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 'everyone agreed it was useless,' while Michelangelo 'saw a hero waiting to step out of the stone,' which is a clear contrast.

choices.text: Most people thought it belonged to other sculptors, but Michelangelo claimed it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone fighting over ownership of the marble block.

choices.text: Most people thought it was too small, but Michelangelo thought it was the right size
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the block was 'too tall' and 'too narrow,' not too small, and Michelangelo never says anything about its size being right.

choices.text: Most people thought it needed more carving, but Michelangelo left it alone
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Michelangelo chose to carve the marble, not leave it alone — that's what makes his story so special.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Boy Who Chose Drawing over School

Michelangelo was born in 1475 near Florence. Even as a young boy, he cared more about drawing than schoolwork. His father disapproved. But Michelangelo's skill was impossible to ignore. At thirteen, he became an apprentice — a young person who learns a trade by working alongside an expert, the way a new cook learns by helping a head chef. He joined the workshop of a well-known painter named Domenico Ghirlandaio. Most artists learned in busy workshops filled with apprentices and assistants mixing paints, preparing panels, and practicing drawing side by side. This team approach was how nearly all Renaissance art got made.

Soon, a powerful man named Lorenzo de' Medici noticed Michelangelo's talent. The Medici family were wealthy bankers who used their money to support artists, scientists, and philosophers. Lorenzo invited the teenager to live in the Medici household, where Michelangelo studied ancient Greek and Roman sculptures and learned alongside thinkers exploring new ideas about science and medicine. The Renaissance was not just about painting and sculpture. It was a movement that stretched across art, science, and philosophy all at once.

The Renaissance was not just about painting and sculpture. It was a movement that stretched across art, science, and philosophy all at once.

choices.text: The Renaissance explored many new science ideas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Renaissance exploring new science ideas is background information about the time period, not a special opportunity that Michelangelo's talent earned him.

choices.text: His father disliked his focus on drawing.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because his father disapproving of his drawing was an obstacle, not a special opportunity caused by his talent.

choices.text: A busy workshop trained many young assistants.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes how workshops worked for many apprentices in general, not a special opportunity given to Michelangelo because of his talent.

choices.text: A powerful man noticed his great talent.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Lorenzo de' Medici noticing Michelangelo's talent and inviting him into his home is a clear example of talent leading directly to a special opportunity.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
David: A Giant Made from a Rejected Stone

Michelangelo's greatest love was sculpture. And the marble block nobody wanted became his greatest masterpiece. He spent over two years carving it into a towering statue of the biblical hero David — a figure from the Bible, the religious text of Christianity and Judaism. The finished statue stands over fourteen feet tall.

The statue shows David in the moment just before his battle with the giant Goliath. His muscles are tense. His eyes are locked on his enemy. Every vein and tendon — the tough cord that connects muscle to bone, like a rope pulling on a lever — looks real enough to touch. Michelangelo once said he saw his job as freeing the figure already trapped inside the stone. With David, you can believe it.
stimulus.content_text: David: A Giant Made from a Rejected Stone Michelangelo's greatest love was sculpture. And the marble block nobody wanted became his greatest masterpiece. He spent over two years carving it into a towering statue of the biblical hero David — a figure from the Bible, the religious text of Christianity and Judaism. The finished statue stands over fourteen feet tall. The statue shows David in the moment just before his battle with the giant Goliath. His muscles are tense. His eyes are locked on his enemy. Every vein and tendon — the tough cord that connects muscle to bone, like a rope pulling on a lever — looks real enough to touch. Michelangelo once said he saw his job as freeing the figure already trapped inside the stone. With David, you can believe it.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened before Michelangelo carved the statue of David?

choices.text: He found a marble block that nobody else wanted.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that the marble block nobody wanted was what Michelangelo used to create his masterpiece, so finding that block came first.

choices.text: He painted a picture of David fighting in battle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only talks about Michelangelo as a sculptor, and never mentions him painting a picture of David.

choices.text: He studied the biblical story of Goliath for many years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Michelangelo studying the story of Goliath for many years.

choices.text: He finished a fourteen-foot statue of a giant.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the fourteen-foot statue is the David statue he carved — it wasn't something that existed before he started working.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Painting a Ceiling While Standing Up

Michelangelo's most ambitious project was not a sculpture at all. In 1508, Pope Julius II ordered him to paint the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in Rome, one of the most important buildings in the Christian world. He insisted he was a sculptor, not a painter. The Pope would not take no for an answer.

The project took about four years, from 1508 to 1512. Michelangelo worked on scaffolding — a temporary wooden platform built high above the floor. You might think he lay flat on his back to paint. Here's the surprising part: he actually stood upright and reached his arms above his head. Paint dripped into his eyes. His neck and back ached from bending backward for hours. He even wrote a funny poem complaining about how miserable the whole experience was. Assistants helped him prepare fresh plaster and mix paints each day, but Michelangelo painted every figure himself.

The finished ceiling includes over three hundred figures and scenes from the Bible. The most famous scene is the Creation of Adam. In it, God reaches out his hand toward Adam, and their fingers nearly touch. That tiny gap between their fingertips has become one of the most recognized images in the world. It captures the connection between humans and the divine — a word meaning God or something sacred.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are God and Adam similar in the famous painting?

choices.text: They are both standing on a platform.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because standing on a platform describes how Michelangelo painted the ceiling, not what God and Adam are doing in the picture.

choices.text: They are both reaching out their hands.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage tells us that God reaches out his hand toward Adam, meaning both figures are reaching out their hands toward each other.

choices.text: They are both writing a funny poem.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Michelangelo wrote the funny poem about how uncomfortable it was to paint the ceiling — God and Adam are painted figures, not poets!

choices.text: They are both mixing the fresh plaster.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mixing fresh plaster was the job of Michelangelo's assistants, and has nothing to do with God or Adam in the painting.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Dome That Still Towers over Rome

Michelangelo kept creating well into old age. He also designed the dome of St. Peter's Basilica in Vatican City, one of the largest churches on Earth. The dome rises 448 feet above the ground — an engineering marvel, meaning a structure so impressive it amazes the people who study how things are built. Michelangelo did not live to see the dome finished. But builders followed his design, and it still stands today as one of the most famous structures in the world.

Michelangelo died in 1564 at the age of eighty-eight, still working on sculptures in his final weeks. He spent seventy-five years turning stone, paint, and empty space into things that made people stop and stare. Five centuries later, they still do. The next time you shape something with your hands — clay, paper, even a sandcastle — you are doing what Michelangelo did: looking at raw material and imagining what it could become.

Michelangelo did not live to see the dome finished. But builders followed his design, and it still stands today as one of the most famous structures in the world. prompt._: Read this sentence from the article: 'The dome rises 448 feet above the ground — an engineering marvel, meaning a structure so impressive it amazes the people who study how things are built.' Based on this sentence, what does the phrase 'engineering marvel' mean?

choices.text: a building that took a very long time to finish
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions how long the dome took to build as part of the definition of 'engineering marvel.'

choices.text: a structure so impressive it amazes builders
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines 'engineering marvel' as 'a structure so impressive it amazes the people who study how things are built,' which matches this answer exactly.

choices.text: a church that is one of the largest on Earth
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being one of the largest churches on Earth describes St. Peter's Basilica, not the meaning of 'engineering marvel.'

choices.text: a design that was created by a famous artist
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the definition in the passage is about how impressive the structure is, not about who designed it.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between how Michelangelo worked on David and how he worked on the Sistine Chapel ceiling?

choices.text: He finished David quickly, but he never finished the ceiling.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage tells us both projects were finished — David in over two years and the ceiling in about four years.

choices.text: He used old stone for David, but he used stone again for the ceiling.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Sistine Chapel ceiling was painted with plaster and paint, not carved from stone.

choices.text: He carved David with helpers, but he painted the ceiling alone.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says assistants helped with the ceiling but Michelangelo painted every figure himself, and it never says he had helpers carve David.

choices.text: He chose to carve David, but he was ordered to paint the ceiling.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows Michelangelo chose to carve David on his own, while Pope Julius II ordered him to paint the ceiling against his wishes.

prompt._: What most likely caused Michelangelo to create such lifelike human figures in both his sculpture and painting?

choices.text: His study of ancient statues and new ideas about the body
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Michelangelo studied ancient sculptures and new ideas about the human body, which directly helped him create lifelike details like veins and tendons in his work.

choices.text: His need to finish large jobs in famous buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because working on large, famous projects gave Michelangelo big opportunities, but the importance of a building doesn't explain why his figures look so real and detailed.

choices.text: His habit of choosing damaged stone for projects
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because choosing a damaged block of stone was a one-time decision about materials, not something that taught him how to make human figures look lifelike.

choices.text: His father's dislike of his interest in drawing
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because his father's disapproval shows that art mattered to Michelangelo, but it doesn't explain the skill and knowledge needed to make figures look so realistic.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence shows the correct order of Michelangelo's experiences as an artist?

choices.text: He painted a chapel ceiling, designed a church dome, and carved a marble statue.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it leaves out the marble statue entirely and puts the chapel ceiling first, but we know from the article that David was carved before Michelangelo ever painted the ceiling.

choices.text: He learned in a workshop, painted a chapel ceiling, and carved a marble statue.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says he painted the chapel ceiling before carving the marble statue, but the article tells us David was carved in 1501 and the ceiling was painted starting in 1508 — so the statue came first.

choices.text: He learned in a workshop, carved a marble statue, and painted a chapel ceiling.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Michelangelo first learned in a workshop at age thirteen, then carved the statue of David starting in 1501, and then painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling starting in 1508.

choices.text: He carved a marble statue, learned in a workshop, and designed a church dome.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts carving the statue before learning in the workshop, but the article clearly says Michelangelo joined the workshop at age thirteen, years before he ever carved David.

prompt._: Which statement best compares how Michelangelo worked on the statue of David and the Sistine Chapel ceiling?

choices.text: He created both projects by doing the main artistic work himself.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Michelangelo carved David himself and 'painted every figure himself' on the Sistine Chapel ceiling.

choices.text: He completed both projects with assistants painting or carving most of the figures.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because assistants only mixed paints and prepared materials — Michelangelo himself did the actual painting and carving on both projects.

choices.text: He made both projects mainly to test new building ideas for large structures.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both projects were created as religious art, not to test new building or engineering ideas.

choices.text: He began both projects only after other artists had already finished part of them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the other sculptors who tried the marble block failed and left nothing finished, and no one else worked on the Sistine Chapel ceiling before Michelangelo started.

syllabus_metadata.title: Michelangelo: Sculptor and Painter of Genius

assessments.title: Raphael and Brunelleschi: Beauty and Innovation

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Painter and the Engineer: Two Renaissance Minds

A Hole in the Sky

For more than a hundred years, the people of Florence, Italy, stared up at a problem. Every architect who studied it walked away shaking his head. No one alive knew how to cover it.

Then a goldsmith named Filippo Brunelleschi said he could do it. And about a century later, a young painter named Raphael Sanzio would cover the walls of the Vatican Palace with images so perfectly balanced they still take people's breath away. Both were shaped by the same significant period in history: the Renaissance, which unfolded over roughly three hundred years, from the 1300s into the 1600s. It wasn't only about art. Renaissance thinkers blended painting with mathematics, sculpture with engineering, and beauty with science — often inside the same person's mind.
stimulus.content_text: The Painter and the Engineer: Two Renaissance MindsA Hole in the Sky For more than a hundred years, the people of Florence, Italy, stared up at a problem. Every architect who studied it walked away shaking his head. No one alive knew how to cover it. Then a goldsmith named Filippo Brunelleschi said he could do it. And about a century later, a young painter named Raphael Sanzio would cover the walls of the Vatican Palace with images so perfectly balanced they still take people's breath away. Both were shaped by the same significant period in history: the Renaissance, which unfolded over roughly three hundred years, from the 1300s into the 1600s. It wasn't only about art. Renaissance thinkers blended painting with mathematics, sculpture with engineering, and beauty with science — often inside the same person's mind.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Brunelleschi different from the other architects who looked at the opening that no one knew how to cover?

choices.text: He said he could ignore it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Brunelleschi did the opposite of ignoring the problem — he boldly said he had a way to solve it.

choices.text: He said he could cover it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Brunelleschi 'said he could do it,' meaning cover the opening, while all the other architects gave up.

choices.text: He said he could study it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because studying the problem is what the other architects already did before walking away, so that would make Brunelleschi the same as them, not different.

choices.text: He said he could paint it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Brunelleschi offered to paint the opening — that was something Raphael did later on the walls of the Vatican Palace.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Brunelleschi's Impossible Dome

Filippo Brunelleschi was born in Florence in 1377. He wanted to solve the cathedral's dome problem, and he believed the answer lay in the past. Brunelleschi traveled to Rome to study ancient buildings, measuring crumbling walls and sketching old Roman domes. He built on knowledge that was already centuries old.

The main challenge was scaffolding — the temporary wooden framework builders stand on while they work, like a giant skeleton holding everything up until the structure can support itself. Traditional domes needed scaffolding underneath them. But the cathedral's opening was so wide that no wooden framework could stretch across it without collapsing.

Brunelleschi's solution was brilliant. He designed a double-shell dome — two domes, a thinner outer shell and a thicker inner shell, connected by stone ribs, like two bowls stacked one inside the other with supports between them. He arranged the bricks in a herringbone pattern, a zigzag design where bricks alternate direction like the bones of a fish. This pattern locked each brick against its neighbors so every new ring held itself up without scaffolding below.

When it was finished, the dome towered over Florence, visible from miles away. It remains the largest brick dome ever built.
stimulus.content_text: Brunelleschi's Impossible Dome Filippo Brunelleschi was born in Florence in 1377. He wanted to solve the cathedral's dome problem, and he believed the answer lay in the past. Brunelleschi traveled to Rome to study ancient buildings, measuring crumbling walls and sketching old Roman domes. He built on knowledge that was already centuries old. The main challenge was scaffolding — the temporary wooden framework builders stand on while they work, like a giant skeleton holding everything up until the structure can support itself. Traditional domes needed scaffolding underneath them. But the cathedral's opening was so wide that no wooden framework could stretch across it without collapsing. Brunelleschi's solution was brilliant. He designed a double-shell dome — two domes, a thinner outer shell and a thicker inner shell, connected by stone ribs, like two bowls stacked one inside the other with supports between them. He arranged the bricks in a herringbone pattern, a zigzag design where bricks alternate direction like the bones of a fish. This pattern locked each brick against its neighbors so every new ring held itself up without scaffolding below. When it was finished, the dome towered over Florence, visible from miles away. It remains the largest brick dome ever built.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Brunelleschi use a herringbone pattern for the bricks?

choices.text: To make the outer shell thicker than the inner shell
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the outer and inner shell thicknesses came from the double-shell dome design, which is a different idea from the herringbone brick pattern.

choices.text: To make the dome look like ancient Roman buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the herringbone pattern was used to copy Roman buildings — it was used to keep the bricks locked in place.

choices.text: To help each new ring of bricks hold itself up
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the herringbone pattern 'locked each brick against its neighbors so every new ring held itself up without scaffolding below.'

choices.text: To give workers more space on the scaffolding
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the herringbone pattern actually helped Brunelleschi avoid using scaffolding altogether, not make more room on it.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Seeing Depth on a Flat Wall

Brunelleschi gave Florence more than a dome. He also figured out the mathematical rules of linear perspective — the technique that lets artists create the illusion of depth on a flat surface, making a hallway in a painting seem to stretch back into the distance. He proved it with a clever experiment. He painted a small panel showing the Florence Baptistery, a church building where babies were baptized. He drilled a small hole in the back of the panel. A viewer would peek through the hole from behind the painting while holding a mirror out in front. The mirror reflected the painted image back to the viewer's eye. When the viewer lowered the mirror, they could see the real Baptistery standing right there. The reflection and the real building matched almost exactly. Flat paint had fooled the eye into seeing real depth.
stimulus.content_text: Seeing Depth on a Flat Wall Brunelleschi gave Florence more than a dome. He also figured out the mathematical rules of linear perspective — the technique that lets artists create the illusion of depth on a flat surface, making a hallway in a painting seem to stretch back into the distance. He proved it with a clever experiment. He painted a small panel showing the Florence Baptistery, a church building where babies were baptized. He drilled a small hole in the back of the panel. A viewer would peek through the hole from behind the painting while holding a mirror out in front. The mirror reflected the painted image back to the viewer's eye. When the viewer lowered the mirror, they could see the real Baptistery standing right there. The reflection and the real building matched almost exactly. Flat paint had fooled the eye into seeing real depth.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the viewer do right after peeking through the hole in the panel?

choices.text: walked over to the real building
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the viewer walked to the real building — they just lowered the mirror to compare the reflection to the real Baptistery from where they stood.

choices.text: painted a picture of the Baptistery
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the viewer was watching the experiment, not painting anything.

choices.text: drilled a small hole in the panel
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Brunelleschi drilled the hole when he made the panel, before any viewer was involved.

choices.text: held a mirror out in front of it
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the viewer peeked through the hole 'while holding a mirror out in front,' meaning holding the mirror was the action happening right along with peeking.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Raphael and the School of Athens

Raphael Sanzio was born in 1483 in the Italian city of Urbino, more than a century after Brunelleschi. You might imagine great artists as lonely geniuses who figure everything out alone. But Raphael learned his craft in a master's workshop, studying under an experienced painter named Perugino. He later studied the techniques of both Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, combining the best of each into his own style.

By his twenties, Raphael had moved to Rome. Pope Julius II hired him to paint the walls of several rooms in the Vatican Palace. His most famous work there is The School of Athens, a fresco — a painting made directly on wet plaster so the image becomes part of the wall itself, almost like a tattoo on the building's skin.

In the painting, the Greek philosophers Plato and Aristotle stand at the center of a grand arched hallway. Dozens of ancient thinkers, mathematicians, and scientists surround them. Raphael used Brunelleschi's rules of linear perspective to create that sense of depth. The work of one Renaissance mind flowed directly into another's.

Raphael arranged people in harmonious compositions — balanced arrangements where every figure seems to belong exactly where it stands. His colors are rich, his expressions gentle, every scene steady and balanced. Tragically, Raphael died at just thirty-seven years old.

He later studied the techniques of both Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, combining the best of each into his own style. By his twenties, Raphael had moved to Rome. Pope Julius II hired him to paint the walls of several rooms in the Vatican Palace. His most famous work there is The School of Athens, a fresco — a painting made directly on wet plaster so the image becomes part of the wall itself, almost like a tattoo on the building's skin. Raphael used Brunelleschi's rules of linear perspective to create that sense of depth. The work of one Renaissance mind flowed directly into another's. Raphael arranged people in harmonious compositions — balanced arrangements where every figure seems to belong exactly where it stands. His colors are rich, his expressions gentle, every scene steady and balanced. Tragically, Raphael died at just thirty-seven years old.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is a fresco similar to a tattoo?

choices.text: They both are painted by famous artists living in the Vatican Palace.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tattoos have nothing to do with famous artists or the Vatican Palace — the passage only uses the tattoo comparison to explain how a fresco sticks to a wall.

choices.text: They both become a permanent part of the surface they are placed on.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly compares a fresco to a tattoo by explaining that both become a permanent part of the surface they are on — the fresco bonds with the wall, just like a tattoo bonds with skin.

choices.text: They both follow the strict rules of linear perspective to show depth.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because linear perspective is about showing depth in a painting and has nothing to do with why the passage compares a fresco to a tattoo.

choices.text: They both use rich colors to show the faces of ancient philosophers.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says tattoos use rich colors or show ancient philosophers — that detail is about Raphael's painting style, not about the fresco-tattoo comparison.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Paint and Stone

A goldsmith who studied ancient Roman ruins built the largest brick dome on Earth and handed painters the math they needed to make flat walls look deep. The thread connecting them runs through the entire Renaissance: the idea that art and science are not separate worlds. They are two hands building the same thing.

Next time you see a photograph or a video game where a road seems to vanish into the distance, you're seeing Brunelleschi's math at work — rules a goldsmith figured out in Florence six hundred years ago, standing in front of a mirror.
