The thread connecting them runs through the entire Renaissance: the idea that art and science are not separate worlds. They are two hands building the same thing. Next time you see a photograph or a video game where a road seems to vanish into the distance, you're seeing Brunelleschi's math at work — rules a goldsmith figured out in Florence six hundred years ago, standing in front of a mirror.

prompt._: According to the section "More Than Paint and Stone," what does the author mean by the idea that art and science are "not separate worlds"?

choices.text: They are two ways of building the same thing.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says art and science are 'two hands building the same thing,' showing they work together toward one goal.

choices.text: They are two tools used to study old ruins.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because studying ruins is just something Brunelleschi did — it's not what the author means by art and science being connected.

choices.text: They are two pictures shown in games and videos.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because games and videos are only used as a modern-day example of Brunelleschi's math, not an explanation of the art-and-science idea.

choices.text: They are two subjects taught in different schools.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions schools or how subjects are taught.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Brunelleschi's way of learning different from Raphael's way of learning?

choices.text: Brunelleschi learned by studying math in school, but Raphael learned by studying science in church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions either Brunelleschi or Raphael studying math in school or science in church.

choices.text: Brunelleschi learned by painting flat walls, but Raphael learned by building massive stone domes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps their roles — Brunelleschi built the dome and Raphael painted flat walls, not the other way around.

choices.text: Brunelleschi learned by studying ancient buildings, but Raphael learned from master painters.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states Brunelleschi studied ancient Roman buildings on his own, while Raphael learned by training under master painters like Perugino.

choices.text: Brunelleschi learned by using small glass mirrors, but Raphael learned by mixing thick wet plaster.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mirrors and plaster were tools they used in their work, not the ways they learned their crafts.

prompt._: How does the author use a cause-and-effect structure to connect Brunelleschi's work to Raphael's art?

choices.text: By showing that Brunelleschi studied ancient Roman buildings, which caused Raphael to study under a master painter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though both men learned from others, the passage never says Brunelleschi studying Roman buildings caused Raphael to study under a master.

choices.text: By showing that Brunelleschi painted the Florence Baptistery, which caused Raphael to paint the Vatican Palace walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Brunelleschi's Baptistery painting was just an experiment to prove perspective worked, and the passage never connects it to Raphael choosing to paint the Vatican walls.

choices.text: By showing that Brunelleschi built a giant brick dome, which caused Raphael to paint on wet plaster.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the dome and Raphael painting on wet plaster are unrelated events — the passage never says one caused the other.

choices.text: By showing that Brunelleschi discovered rules of perspective, which allowed Raphael to paint with realistic depth.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Brunelleschi discovered the rules of linear perspective, and Raphael later used those same rules to make 'The School of Athens' look deep and realistic.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best summarizes the article's main idea about how knowledge grew during the Renaissance?

choices.text: Brunelleschi studied ancient ruins, he discovered rules for depth, and Raphael used those rules.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Brunelleschi studied ancient ruins, discovered linear perspective, and Raphael later used those exact rules in his painting.

choices.text: Raphael studied wet plaster, he discovered rules for painting, and Brunelleschi used those rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Raphael did not discover the rules for painting depth — he used rules that Brunelleschi had already worked out before Raphael was even born.

choices.text: Raphael studied ancient paintings, he discovered rules for depth, and Brunelleschi used those rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two people switched — it was Brunelleschi who discovered the rules for depth, not Raphael.

choices.text: Brunelleschi studied wooden scaffolds, he discovered rules for building, and Raphael used those rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Brunelleschi's most important discovery for other artists was linear perspective (depth on a flat surface), not scaffolding rules.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is a major similarity between Brunelleschi's dome and Raphael's The School of Athens?

choices.text: Both works needed wooden scaffolding to hold them up.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Brunelleschi's big breakthrough was designing the dome so it did NOT need wooden scaffolding to hold it up.

choices.text: Both works combined scientific ideas with beautiful art.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the dome and The School of Athens combined scientific ideas (like math and engineering) with beautiful art, which is exactly what the Renaissance was all about.

choices.text: Both works were painted directly onto wet plaster walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because painting on wet plaster (fresco) describes only Raphael's School of Athens, not Brunelleschi's dome.

choices.text: Both works were created by studying old Roman ruins.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only Brunelleschi traveled to Rome to study ancient ruins — the article never says Raphael did this.

syllabus_metadata.title: Raphael and Brunelleschi: Beauty and Innovation

assessments.title: Gutenberg and the Printing Revolution

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Machine That Set Ideas Free

A World Without Books

A monk sits hunched over a wooden desk in a cold stone room. His hand cramps around a quill pen. He has been copying the same book for four months — letter by letter, page by page. Outside, the year is 1440, and this is how every single book in Europe gets made.

Only churches, universities, and the richest families owned them. Actually, most people in the 1400s could not read at all. There was little reason to learn when you would never hold a book in your hands. Then one inventor in a German city built a machine that changed what was possible.
stimulus.content_text: The Machine That Set Ideas FreeA World Without Books A monk sits hunched over a wooden desk in a cold stone room. His hand cramps around a quill pen. He has been copying the same book for four months — letter by letter, page by page. Outside, the year is 1440, and this is how every single book in Europe gets made. Only churches, universities, and the richest families owned them. Actually, most people in the 1400s could not read at all. There was little reason to learn when you would never hold a book in your hands. Then one inventor in a German city built a machine that changed what was possible.

prompt._: According to the text, which contrast between book owners and most people in the 1400s is stated?

choices.text: Book owners lived in Germany, while most people lived in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says book owners lived in Germany; Germany is only mentioned as the place where the inventor built his printing machine.

choices.text: Book owners copied books by hand, while most people printed books with machines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was monks who copied books by hand, and printing machines didn't exist yet when the passage describes book ownership.

choices.text: Book owners had books, while most people could not read at all.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that only the wealthy owned books, while most people could not read at all — that's the exact contrast described.

choices.text: Book owners studied at universities, while most people worked in churches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while churches and universities owned books, the passage never says most people worked in churches.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Gutenberg's Big Idea

Johannes Gutenberg lived in Mainz, Germany. He worked as a goldsmith — a craftsman who shapes gold and other metals into fine objects. His skill with metal gave him an idea no one in Europe had tried before.

Gutenberg carved tiny metal blocks, each with a single raised letter on top. These blocks are called movable type — think of them like letter stamps you can rearrange again and again. A printer would line up the blocks to spell out words and sentences for one page. Then he rolled ink across the raised letters and pressed a sheet of paper down onto them. The result was a clean, sharp printed page. When that page was finished, the printer pulled the letters apart and rearranged them for the next page.

It's easy to imagine this working like a modern printer — push a button and pages fly out. Not even close. Setting up a single page of movable type took hours of careful work. Printing an entire book still required weeks or months of labor. But here is what made it so powerful: once you arranged one page, you could stamp out hundreds of identical copies. A scribe — a person whose job was to copy text by hand — could produce only one copy in that same time.

Before Gutenberg, some European printers used woodblock printing, a method where an entire page is carved into one block of wood, like a giant stamp. One mistake meant carving a whole new block. Movable type was faster and far more flexible.

Then he rolled ink across the raised letters and pressed a sheet of paper down onto them. The result was a clean, sharp printed page. When that page was finished, the printer pulled the letters apart and rearranged them for the next page. It's easy to imagine this working like a modern printer — push a button and pages fly out. But here is what made it so powerful: once you arranged one page, you could stamp out hundreds of identical copies. A scribe — a person whose job was to copy text by hand — could produce only one copy in that same time. Before Gutenberg, some European printers used woodblock printing, a method where an entire page is carved into one block of wood, like a giant stamp. One mistake meant carving a whole new block. Movable type was faster and far more flexible.

prompt._: A main idea of the text is that movable type was a powerful invention. What caused it to be more powerful than hand-copying by scribes?

choices.text: Pushing a simple button on the machine allowed pages to fly out in a few seconds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says movable type was 'not even close' to a modern printer — it actually took hours of careful work to set up just one page.

choices.text: Shaping gold and other fine metals created beautiful objects that lasted for years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because shaping gold and metals was Gutenberg's craft as a goldsmith, which gave him the idea for movable type, but it is not what made movable type powerful.

choices.text: Arranging one page of metal letters allowed printers to stamp hundreds of copies.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that arranging one page let printers stamp hundreds of copies, while a scribe could only make one copy in the same amount of time.

choices.text: Carving an entire page into a giant wooden block prevented any spelling mistakes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carving a whole page into wood describes woodblock printing, the older method, which was actually slower and less flexible than movable type.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Was Gutenberg Really First?

Hundreds of years earlier, printers in China and Korea had already developed both woodblock printing and their own forms of movable type. But Chinese and Korean writing uses thousands of different characters, which made movable type difficult to manage. Gutenberg independently built a system that worked especially well for European alphabets, which use far fewer letters.
stimulus.content_text: Was Gutenberg Really First? Hundreds of years earlier, printers in China and Korea had already developed both woodblock printing and their own forms of movable type. But Chinese and Korean writing uses thousands of different characters, which made movable type difficult to manage. Gutenberg independently built a system that worked especially well for European alphabets, which use far fewer letters.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened hundreds of years before Gutenberg built his printing system?

choices.text: European printers created thousands of new characters.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Chinese and Korean writing already used thousands of characters, not that European printers created new ones.

choices.text: Gutenberg invented the very first woodblock printing.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gutenberg did not invent woodblock printing — printers in China and Korea did that long before him.

choices.text: Chinese and Korean writers changed their old alphabet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Chinese or Korean writers changed their writing system — it only explains that their writing uses thousands of characters.

choices.text: Printers in China and Korea developed movable type.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that printers in China and Korea developed woodblock printing and movable type hundreds of years before Gutenberg.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Gutenberg Bible

Around 1455, Gutenberg printed his masterpiece: a complete Bible. About 180 copies rolled off his press. The text was crisp and even, and artists later decorated many copies by hand with colorful illustrations. Today, fewer than 50 of these Bibles survive. A single complete copy is worth tens of millions of dollars.
stimulus.content_text: The Gutenberg Bible Around 1455, Gutenberg printed his masterpiece: a complete Bible. About 180 copies rolled off his press. The text was crisp and even, and artists later decorated many copies by hand with colorful illustrations. Today, fewer than 50 of these Bibles survive. A single complete copy is worth tens of millions of dollars.

prompt._: In the sentence "The text was crisp and even," how are the meanings of the words "crisp" and "even" similar?

choices.text: Both words describe how rare and valuable the books became.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'crisp' and 'even' describe the quality of the printing, not how rare or valuable the Bibles are today.

choices.text: Both words describe how bright and colorful the pictures were.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sentence is about the printed text, not the colorful pictures that artists added to the Bibles later.

choices.text: Both words describe how neat and clear the letters looked.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both 'crisp' and 'even' describe how the printed letters looked — sharp, clear, and neatly lined up on the page.

choices.text: Both words describe how crunchy and flat the pages felt.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'crisp' and 'even' are about how the text looked visually, not about the physical feel or texture of the pages.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Ideas on the Move

Within fifty years of that first Bible, printing presses appeared in cities all across Europe. Millions of books poured into the world. For the first time, ordinary people could own a book. And as books became cheaper and easier to find, something else happened: more people learned to read. The printing press gave people a reason to become literate — able to read and write — because now there was actually something to read.

Students studied from printed textbooks instead of copying lectures by hand. Scientists shared discoveries with colleagues in distant countries. Farmers read almanacs — yearly books full of useful information about weather, planting seasons, and star positions. Merchants studied printed maps and trade routes. Citizens read about laws and their rights.

The press also spread religious ideas at immense speed. When Martin Luther challenged the Catholic Church decades later, his writings were printed and passed from town to town so fast that Church leaders could not stop them. This movement, called the Protestant Reformation — a period when many Christians broke away from the Catholic Church to form new churches — gained strength partly because printed pages carried Luther's words everywhere.

prompt._: According to the text, what is an almanac?

choices.text: a church writing about new ideas
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because church writings and religious ideas are about Martin Luther and the Reformation, which is a separate topic from almanacs.

choices.text: a school book for student lessons
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because printed textbooks were what students used for lessons, not almanacs.

choices.text: a printed map with trade routes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because printed maps and trade routes were used by merchants, not described as almanacs.

choices.text: a yearly book with useful information
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says almanacs are 'yearly books full of useful information about weather, planting seasons, and star positions' — that matches exactly.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Power on Paper

Gutenberg himself never grew rich from his invention. He fought with his financial backers — the people who lent him money to build his press — and lost control of his own workshop. He died in 1468, mostly forgotten.

But think about what his machine actually did. When books were rare, only a few powerful people controlled knowledge. When books became cheap, knowledge belonged to everyone. The printing press did not just put ink on paper. It put ideas into millions of hands that had never held a book before — and those hands built the modern world you live in now.

choices.text: The machine put ideas into millions of hands.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is a surviving Gutenberg Bible today similar to most books in the year 1440?

choices.text: They are copied by hand using a quill pen and ink.

choices.text: They are very rare and cost a large amount of money.

choices.text: They are owned by ordinary people who want to learn.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says ordinary people gaining access to books happened after the printing press spread — not in 1440, and surviving Gutenberg Bibles today are far too expensive for ordinary people to own.

choices.text: They are printed using large carved blocks of wood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Gutenberg used movable type made of small metal blocks, not large carved wooden blocks like woodblock printing.

assessments.title: Shakespeare: The Bard of England

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Shakespeare and the Renaissance: When Creativity Exploded Across Europe

A Theater with No Roof

Three thousand people packed into a round wooden building in London, England, with no roof over their heads. They were watching a play by William Shakespeare, and his words held them in place like a spell.

Shakespeare lived during a period called the Renaissance, a French word meaning "rebirth." The Renaissance was a time — roughly from the 1300s to the 1600s — when people across Europe threw themselves into new ideas with wild energy. You might think the Renaissance was only about painting and sculpture. Actually, it reached into almost everything: literature, science, music, architecture, and philosophy. In Italy, Leonardo da Vinci sketched flying machines and painted the Mona Lisa. Michelangelo carved statues so lifelike they seemed ready to breathe. A German inventor named Johannes Gutenberg built a printing press that could produce books faster than any hand could copy them. And in England, a glove maker's son from a small town was about to reshape the English language forever.
stimulus.content_text: Shakespeare and the Renaissance: When Creativity Exploded Across EuropeA Theater with No Roof Three thousand people packed into a round wooden building in London, England, with no roof over their heads. They were watching a play by William Shakespeare, and his words held them in place like a spell. Shakespeare lived during a period called the Renaissance, a French word meaning "rebirth." The Renaissance was a time — roughly from the 1300s to the 1600s — when people across Europe threw themselves into new ideas with wild energy. You might think the Renaissance was only about painting and sculpture. Actually, it reached into almost everything: literature, science, music, architecture, and philosophy. In Italy, Leonardo da Vinci sketched flying machines and painted the Mona Lisa. Michelangelo carved statues so lifelike they seemed ready to breathe. A German inventor named Johannes Gutenberg built a printing press that could produce books faster than any hand could copy them. And in England, a glove maker's son from a small town was about to reshape the English language forever.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was Gutenberg's printing press different from the way books were made before?

choices.text: It made books smaller and easier to carry around
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say anything about books getting smaller or easier to carry because of the printing press.

choices.text: It allowed people to write books in new languages
choices.feedback: The passage never says the printing press helped people write in new languages — that detail isn't mentioned anywhere.

choices.text: It printed books with colorful pictures and drawings
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions colorful pictures or drawings being something the printing press could make.

choices.text: It produced books faster than copying by hand
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the printing press 'could produce books faster than any hand could copy them,' which tells us it was much quicker than the old way of copying books by hand.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From Small Town to Big Stage

William Shakespeare was born in 1564 in Stratford-upon-Avon, a quiet market town along the Avon River, far from London's noise and energy. His father made gloves and served as a local official. Young William attended the town grammar school, where he studied Latin, literature, and rhetoric — the art of persuasive speaking, like a lawyer arguing a case or a leader rallying a crowd. Those skills would shape everything he later wrote.

As a young man, Shakespeare moved to London, where the theater scene was booming. He joined a company of actors and soon started writing his own plays. Over his career, he wrote at least 37 plays and 154 sonnets, which are short poems of exactly fourteen lines, like tiny songs built from careful rules. His plays included tragedies like Hamlet and Romeo and Juliet, comedies like A Midsummer Night's Dream, and histories like Henry V. Each one explored the biggest questions of human life: love, jealousy, ambition, loyalty, revenge, and forgiveness.

His father made gloves and served as a local official. Young William attended the town grammar school, where he studied Latin, literature, and rhetoric — the art of persuasive speaking, like a lawyer arguing a case or a leader rallying a crowd. Those skills would shape everything he later wrote. As a young man, Shakespeare moved to London, where the theater scene was booming. He joined a company of actors and soon started writing his own plays. Over his career, he wrote at least 37 plays and 154 sonnets, which are short poems of exactly fourteen lines, like tiny songs built from careful rules. His plays included tragedies like Hamlet and Romeo and Juliet, comedies like A Midsummer Night's Dream, and histories like Henry V. Each one explored the biggest questions of human life: love, jealousy, ambition, loyalty, revenge, and forgiveness.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that Shakespeare's early learning helped lead to his success as a writer?

choices.text: He lived in a quiet town beside the Avon River as a child.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because living near the Avon River is just a detail about where he grew up, not about how his learning helped him become a great writer.

choices.text: He moved to London after he was already a young man.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because moving to London happened after his school days, so it doesn't support the idea that his early learning led to his success.

choices.text: He wrote many plays and poems during his career.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because writing lots of plays and poems is a result of his success, not evidence that his early learning helped cause it.

choices.text: He studied how to speak in a strong, convincing way at school.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says that studying rhetoric at school shaped everything Shakespeare later wrote, linking his early learning to his writing success.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Globe Theatre

Shakespeare's plays were performed at the Globe Theatre, that round, open-air building that could squeeze in up to 3,000 spectators — people who came to watch, the way fans fill a stadium today. The cheapest ticket cost just one penny. Laborers and servants stood in the open yard, getting rained on or sunburned. Wealthier patrons — paying customers who could afford better seats — sat in covered galleries, which were raised balcony sections built along the walls, like the upper decks of a sports arena.

There were no microphones, no electric lights, and almost no props. The actors relied entirely on Shakespeare's words to paint pictures in the audience's imagination. And those words worked on everyone — from the poorest worker in the yard to Queen Elizabeth herself in the royal box.

The cheapest ticket cost just one penny. Laborers and servants stood in the open yard, getting rained on or sunburned. Wealthier patrons — paying customers who could afford better seats — sat in covered galleries, which were raised balcony sections built along the walls, like the upper decks of a sports arena. There were no microphones, no electric lights, and almost no props. The actors relied entirely on Shakespeare's words to paint pictures in the audience's imagination. And those words worked on everyone — from the poorest worker in the yard to Queen Elizabeth herself in the royal box.

prompt._: According to the text, what did wealthier patrons do after paying for better seats?

choices.text: They sat in the covered galleries.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says wealthier patrons 'sat in covered galleries' after paying for better seats.

choices.text: They watched from the royal box.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the royal box was mentioned as a place for Queen Elizabeth, not for regular wealthy patrons.

choices.text: They stood in the open yard.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because standing in the open yard — getting rained on or sunburned — was what laborers and servants did, not wealthier patrons.

choices.text: They performed in the Globe Theatre.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because patrons were paying customers who came to watch the plays, not actors who performed in them.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Man Who Gave English New Words

Shakespeare is credited with adding over 1,700 words to the English language, including "eyeball," "lonely," and "generous." But here is the surprising part: he did not pull these words out of thin air. When people say Shakespeare "invented" words, they usually mean he combined existing words in new ways — smashing "eye" and "ball" together, for instance — or borrowed from Latin and French and gave the words an English twist. Sometimes he was simply the first person to write down a word that people may have already been saying out loud. He was more like a chef mixing familiar ingredients into dishes nobody had tasted before.

He also created phrases you probably use without knowing where they came from. "Break the ice," "wild goose chase," "heart of gold," and "all that glitters is not gold" all flowed from Shakespeare's pen. It does sound old-fashioned, but it is still recognizably English — your English. You can read his sentences and understand most of them, especially once you get used to the rhythm.

But here is the surprising part: he did not pull these words out of thin air. When people say Shakespeare "invented" words, they usually mean he combined existing words in new ways — smashing "eye" and "ball" together, for instance — or borrowed from Latin and French and gave the words an English twist. Sometimes he was simply the first person to write down a word that people may have already been saying out loud. He was more like a chef mixing familiar ingredients into dishes nobody had tasted before. He also created phrases you probably use without knowing where they came from. "Break the ice," "wild goose chase," "heart of gold," and "all that glitters is not gold" all flowed from Shakespeare's pen. It does sound old-fashioned, but it is still recognizably English — your English. You can read his sentences and understand most of them, especially once you get used to the rhythm.

prompt._: In the sentence "He was more like a chef mixing familiar ingredients into dishes nobody had tasted before," what does the comparison help the reader understand about Shakespeare?

choices.text: He used known words in fresh ways.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the chef mixing familiar ingredients into new dishes is a perfect picture of Shakespeare combining words people already knew into brand new expressions.

choices.text: He wrote down words people already used.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because writing down words people already said is a different idea from the chef comparison, which is about creatively mixing and combining things.

choices.text: He made up every word by himself.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage directly says Shakespeare did NOT make up every word by himself — he used existing words and ideas.

choices.text: He borrowed words from other languages.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because borrowing from other languages is only one small part of what Shakespeare did, and the chef comparison is about the bigger idea of blending familiar things in new ways.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Shakespeare Still Matters

Shakespeare retired to Stratford-upon-Avon around 1613 and died there in 1616 at the age of 52. In the four centuries since, his reputation has only grown. Today, his plays are performed in every country on Earth and translated into every major language.

Shakespeare was part of the Renaissance spirit — that burst of curiosity and creativity that spread across Europe like a fire jumping from rooftop to rooftop. Artists painted, scientists experimented, inventors built, and Shakespeare wrote. He used the English language in ways no one had tried before, and he asked questions about love, power, justice, and what it means to be human. Those are the same questions you ask today, just in newer words — many of which he gave you.

Shakespeare was part of the Renaissance spirit — that burst of curiosity and creativity that spread across Europe like a fire jumping from rooftop to rooftop. Artists painted, scientists experimented, inventors built, and Shakespeare wrote. He used the English language in ways no one had tried before, and he asked questions about love, power, justice, and what it means to be human. Those are the same questions you ask today, just in newer words — many of which he gave you.

choices.text: a style of writing plays in the English language
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Renaissance was a broad movement across all of Europe involving many kinds of people, not just playwrights writing in English.

choices.text: a burst of curiosity and creativity in Europe
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly defines the Renaissance as 'a burst of curiosity and creativity that spread across Europe.'

choices.text: a period when only scientists made discoveries
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says artists, inventors, and writers were all part of the Renaissance, not just scientists.

choices.text: a time when people moved to new countries
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about people moving to new countries — that idea doesn't appear anywhere in the text.

prompt._: Based on the article, how does Shakespeare's early life contrast with his reputation today?

choices.text: He wrote short poems as a young student, but his plays are now completely forgotten.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article says Shakespeare's reputation has only grown over four centuries, so his plays are definitely not forgotten.

choices.text: He grew up in a quiet town, but his plays are now famous across the globe.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly states Shakespeare grew up in a quiet market town, but today his plays are performed in every country on Earth.

choices.text: He performed for poor workers as a youth, but his plays are now only for royalty.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Globe Theatre welcomed everyone from poor workers to royalty, and the article never says his plays are now only for royalty.

choices.text: He lived in a noisy and energetic city, but his plays are now read in quiet towns.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — Shakespeare left the quiet town and moved to the noisy, energetic city of London, and his plays are now performed worldwide, not just in quiet towns.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the main reason Shakespeare's writing is still important to people today?

choices.text: He traveled across Europe to spread new ideas during the Renaissance.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Shakespeare traveled across Europe — he moved from his small hometown to London, but he didn't journey around Europe spreading ideas.

choices.text: He built a large, open-air theater that could hold thousands of people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because building the Globe Theatre was something Shakespeare was connected to, but the theater isn't why his writing is still important — it's the words and ideas inside the plays that matter.

choices.text: He wrote about lasting human questions using words we still use.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that Shakespeare wrote about big human questions like love, jealousy, and justice that people still think about today, and that many words and phrases he created are still part of the English language.

choices.text: He invented the printing press so his plays could be copied quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Johannes Gutenberg, not Shakespeare, who invented the printing press.

prompt._: Which sequence best describes how the article organizes the key events of Shakespeare's life?

choices.text: He wrote plays in London, then learned rhetoric in school, then retired to Stratford.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts things in the wrong order — Shakespeare learned rhetoric as a young student in Stratford, which happened before he moved to London to write plays.

choices.text: He grew up in a small town, then moved to London to write plays, then retired back home.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows Shakespeare grew up in Stratford, then moved to London to write plays, and finally retired back to Stratford — in that exact order.

choices.text: He built the Globe Theatre, then invented new words, then became famous after he died.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Shakespeare did not build the Globe Theatre, and the article never says he invented words before becoming famous.

choices.text: He performed for Queen Elizabeth, then moved to Stratford, then started writing sonnets.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Shakespeare personally performed for Queen Elizabeth, and he moved back to Stratford at the end of his career, not in the middle.

prompt._: Which statement best tells the main idea of the article by comparing Shakespeare to other creative people of the Renaissance?

choices.text: Shakespeare was like other Renaissance workers because he copied old ideas instead of making anything new.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly explains that Shakespeare created new words, new phrases, and new kinds of plays — the opposite of just copying old ideas.

choices.text: Shakespeare was more important than other Renaissance thinkers because his plays reached the biggest crowds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never ranks Shakespeare as more important than other Renaissance figures — it treats them all as part of the same exciting creative movement.

choices.text: Shakespeare was like other Renaissance creators because he brought new ideas to his field and changed how people thought.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Shakespeare, like da Vinci, Michelangelo, and Gutenberg, brought bold new ideas to his field and changed the way people thought about language and human life.

choices.text: Shakespeare was different from other Renaissance artists because he worked only in London theaters and not across Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says Shakespeare's plays are performed in every country on Earth today, showing his work spread far beyond London theaters.

assessments.title: The Renaissance Spreads Across Europe

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Renaissance Spreads Across Europe

An Old Man Crosses the Mountains

The Renaissance — a period of new thinking about art, science, and what human beings could achieve — began in Italy in the 1300s. Actually, it was more like a slow sunrise. Ideas brightened in Italian cities first. Then, over more than a hundred years, that light crept across the rest of Europe. By the 1500s, it had reached France, Spain, England, Germany, and the Netherlands.

In 1516, a 64-year-old Leonardo da Vinci packed up his paintings, his notebooks, and his unfinished inventions. He loaded them onto mules and crossed the Alps into France. King Francis I had begged the famous artist to come live near his royal palace. He carried Italian ideas with him like seeds in a suitcase — and France would never be the same.
stimulus.content_text: The Renaissance Spreads Across EuropeAn Old Man Crosses the Mountains The Renaissance — a period of new thinking about art, science, and what human beings could achieve — began in Italy in the 1300s. Actually, it was more like a slow sunrise. Ideas brightened in Italian cities first. Then, over more than a hundred years, that light crept across the rest of Europe. By the 1500s, it had reached France, Spain, England, Germany, and the Netherlands. In 1516, a 64-year-old Leonardo da Vinci packed up his paintings, his notebooks, and his unfinished inventions. He loaded them onto mules and crossed the Alps into France. King Francis I had begged the famous artist to come live near his royal palace. He carried Italian ideas with him like seeds in a suitcase — and France would never be the same.

prompt._: According to the section "An Old Man Crosses the Mountains," which statement contrasts Italy with the rest of Europe during the Renaissance?

choices.text: Italy crossed the Alps first, while the rest of Europe stayed behind.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Leonardo da Vinci (one person) who crossed the Alps, not all of Italy crossing into Europe.

choices.text: Italy welcomed King Francis I first, while France followed much later.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because King Francis I was already in France and invited Leonardo to come there — France didn't follow Italy in welcoming the king.

choices.text: Italy began the new thinking first, while the rest of Europe received it later.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states ideas started in Italy in the 1300s and slowly spread to the rest of Europe over the next hundred-plus years.

choices.text: Italy changed in the 1500s first, while the rest of Europe changed in the 1300s.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Italy changed first in the 1300s, and the rest of Europe followed in the 1500s, not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Ideas Traveled Without the Internet

How did Renaissance thinking spread without phones, cars, or airplanes? It traveled the same way people did — slowly, and by several different roads.

First, kings and queens invited Italian artists and scholars to their royal courts. Diplomats — people sent by their governments to work with foreign leaders — visited Italian cities and brought new ideas home. Students from all over Europe traveled to Italian universities, then carried what they learned back to their own countries. Trade between cities also moved ideas along with goods.

But the biggest idea-spreader was a machine. Around 1440, a German inventor named Johannes Gutenberg built the printing press, a device that could produce copies of a book far faster than any person could write them by hand. Before Gutenberg, copying a single book by hand with a quill pen could take months. His machine could print hundreds of copies in weeks. A book printed in Florence, Italy, could be read in London, England, within a month. Ideas that once traveled at the speed of a walking person now traveled at the speed of a merchant ship.
stimulus.content_text: How Ideas Traveled Without the Internet How did Renaissance thinking spread without phones, cars, or airplanes? It traveled the same way people did — slowly, and by several different roads. First, kings and queens invited Italian artists and scholars to their royal courts. Diplomats — people sent by their governments to work with foreign leaders — visited Italian cities and brought new ideas home. Students from all over Europe traveled to Italian universities, then carried what they learned back to their own countries. Trade between cities also moved ideas along with goods. But the biggest idea-spreader was a machine. Around 1440, a German inventor named Johannes Gutenberg built the printing press, a device that could produce copies of a book far faster than any person could write them by hand. Before Gutenberg, copying a single book by hand with a quill pen could take months. His machine could print hundreds of copies in weeks. A book printed in Florence, Italy, could be read in London, England, within a month. Ideas that once traveled at the speed of a walking person now traveled at the speed of a merchant ship.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did the printing press help Renaissance ideas spread faster than before?

choices.text: It allowed diplomats to visit Italian cities more often than before.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the printing press changed how often diplomats traveled — diplomats are mentioned as a separate way ideas spread.

choices.text: It produced many copies of books much faster than hand copying.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the printing press could print hundreds of copies in weeks, while hand-copying a single book took months.

choices.text: It replaced merchant ships as the main way to move goods.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says merchant ships helped carry ideas, but the printing press made books, not ships, faster — it didn't replace ships at all.

choices.text: It helped students travel to Italian universities more quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the printing press made copying books faster, not student travel — students traveling to universities is a different idea-spreading method mentioned in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
New Flavors in the North

As Renaissance ideas moved north, they didn't just get copied. Each country shaped them in its own way. This period is called the Northern Renaissance because it describes what happened in countries north of Italy.

In Germany, an artist named Albrecht Dürer became a master printmaker — someone who carves images into wood or metal, then presses ink from those carved surfaces onto paper. His woodcuts (prints made from carved wood blocks) and engravings (prints made from carved metal plates) showed animals, plants, and people with striking detail. Because prints could be copied many times, Dürer's art spread across the whole continent.

In Flanders — a region in what is now Belgium — Jan van Eyck perfected a way of painting with oil-based paints. His colors glowed like jewels. He could paint the texture of velvet so real you wanted to touch it, and he captured the gleam of metal armor and the softness of human skin in ways that amazed everyone who saw his work.

The Northern Renaissance also produced bold thinkers. Erasmus of Rotterdam was a Dutch scholar who studied humanist ideas — a way of thinking that focused on human ability and the importance of education for everyone, not just monks and priests. He wrote clever, funny books that pointed out dishonesty and foolishness among powerful people. His most famous book, In Praise of Folly, used humor to criticize leaders in both the Church and secular society — meaning the non-religious world of kings, merchants, and politicians. Erasmus became so famous that he exchanged letters with kings and popes across Europe.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after Dürer carved images into wood or metal as part of printmaking?

choices.text: He copied ideas from Italy first
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Dürer needed to copy Italian ideas before making his prints — it just describes his printmaking process.

choices.text: He painted the prints with oil colors
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because painting with oil colors was Jan van Eyck's special skill, not something Dürer did with his prints.

choices.text: He wrote funny books about leaders
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because writing clever, funny books was what Erasmus did — Dürer was an artist, not a writer.

choices.text: He pressed ink onto paper from them
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that a printmaker 'presses ink from those carved surfaces onto paper,' which is exactly what happened after the carving.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Beyond Paint and Paper

The Renaissance reshaped more than just the visual arts. In England, it sparked a golden age of writing and theater. William Shakespeare filled stages with unforgettable plays. Other English writers, including Christopher Marlowe and Edmund Spenser, created powerful works of poetry and drama. Sir Thomas More wrote about what an ideal government might look like.

The Renaissance also changed how people studied nature. For centuries, European scholars had trusted ancient Greek and Roman writers without questioning them. Renaissance thinkers began to observe the world carefully and ask whether those ancient writers had always been right. This habit of careful observation and questioning built the foundation for the Scientific Revolution, which later produced breakthroughs in astronomy, physics, medicine, and biology.

The Renaissance grew slowly out of the medieval world that came before it, borrowing old knowledge and mixing it with new curiosity. By the early 1600s, when the Renaissance faded, Europe had changed deeply. People were no longer just looking backward at ancient wisdom. They were looking forward — ready to explore unknown continents, study the stars through telescopes, and ask questions nobody had dared to ask before.

prompt._: The author compares the Scientific Revolution to a building by stating that careful observation 'built the foundation.' Based on this comparison, what does the word 'foundation' mean in the passage?

choices.text: A final result of a very long process
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a foundation is the starting point of something, not the final result at the end of a long process.

choices.text: A strong starting point for new ideas
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses 'built the foundation' to show that careful observation created a strong base from which the Scientific Revolution could grow.

choices.text: An exact copy of an ancient Roman design
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Renaissance thinkers were moving away from simply copying ancient Roman and Greek ideas — they were questioning them.

choices.text: A hidden danger of asking many questions
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage presents questioning and observation as positive habits that led to great discoveries, not as something dangerous.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did the Renaissance in England contrast with the Renaissance in Flanders?

choices.text: England became known for ancient Greek ideas, while Flanders became known for printing books.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never connects England specifically to ancient Greek ideas or Flanders specifically to printing books.

choices.text: England became known for studying the stars, while Flanders became known for carving wood blocks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because studying the stars came later during the Scientific Revolution, and Flanders was known for oil painting, not carving wood blocks (that was Germany's Albrecht Dürer).

choices.text: England became known for foreign diplomats, while Flanders became known for university students.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because diplomats and university students were ways Renaissance ideas spread across all of Europe, not unique achievements tied to England or Flanders.

choices.text: England became known for writing and theater, while Flanders became known for oil painting.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states England became famous for writing and theater, while Flanders was where Jan van Eyck mastered oil painting — two different Renaissance strengths.

prompt._: Based on the article, what was one major effect of Renaissance ideas spreading to countries outside of Italy?

choices.text: Italian artists painted beautiful portraits of every single royal leader in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Italian artists painted every royal leader in Europe; that detail simply isn't in the passage.

choices.text: Different European countries developed their own highly unique styles of creative work.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states each country shaped Renaissance ideas in its own way, giving specific examples like Germany's printmaking, Flanders' oil painting, and England's theater and writing.

choices.text: German inventors abandoned their new machines to focus entirely on oil painting.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because German inventors didn't abandon their machines — Gutenberg's printing press was actually one of the biggest reasons Renaissance ideas spread so far and so fast.

choices.text: European scholars completely trusted the ancient Greek writers from earlier in history.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the opposite — Renaissance thinkers began to *question* ancient Greek and Roman writers instead of just trusting them.

prompt._: Which choice best summarizes the sequence of how the Renaissance spread and changed Europe?

choices.text: It began in France, it moved to Italy, and it inspired only painters and sculptors.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Renaissance started in Italy, not France, and it inspired much more than just painters and sculptors.

choices.text: It began in Italy, it moved to other countries, and it inspired new art, writing, and science.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the Renaissance began in Italy, spread across Europe, and changed art, writing, and science.

choices.text: It began in northern Europe, it moved south to Italy, and it replaced all ancient ideas at once.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Renaissance moved from Italy in the south northward, and it built on ancient ideas rather than replacing them all at once.

choices.text: It began with the printing press, it moved next to royal courts, and it ended before England changed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Renaissance began in Italy before the printing press existed, and England was greatly changed by the Renaissance.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were Johannes Gutenberg's printing press and Albrecht Dürer's printmaking similar?

choices.text: Both were used mainly to criticize foolish leaders in the government.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because criticizing foolish and dishonest leaders was what Erasmus did in his books, not the main purpose of the printing press or printmaking.

choices.text: Both were invented by Italian artists who traveled to northern Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both Gutenberg and Dürer were German, not Italian artists who traveled north.

choices.text: Both allowed creations to be copied many times to reach more people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says both Gutenberg's press and Dürer's prints could make many copies, which helped ideas and art spread far and wide across Europe.

choices.text: Both focused on using new oil paints to make images look highly realistic.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oil paints were the special technique of Jan van Eyck, not something connected to Gutenberg or Dürer.

assessments.title: Martin Luther and the 95 Theses

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Monk Who Shook the Church

A Piece of Paper on a Door

A hammer struck a nail into a wooden church door. The sound was ordinary. In the university town of Wittenberg, Germany, people posted notices on church doors all the time — the way you might pin a flyer to a school bulletin board. But on October 31, 1517, the paper that a monk named Martin Luther nailed to that door carried ideas so bold that they would split one of the most powerful organizations in the world: the Roman Catholic Church.

You might recognize the name Martin Luther and think of Martin Luther King Jr., the civil rights leader. Martin Luther King Jr. Lived hundreds of years later and was actually named after this earlier Martin Luther.
stimulus.content_text: The Monk Who Shook the ChurchA Piece of Paper on a Door A hammer struck a nail into a wooden church door. The sound was ordinary. In the university town of Wittenberg, Germany, people posted notices on church doors all the time — the way you might pin a flyer to a school bulletin board. But on October 31, 1517, the paper that a monk named Martin Luther nailed to that door carried ideas so bold that they would split one of the most powerful organizations in the world: the Roman Catholic Church. You might recognize the name Martin Luther and think of Martin Luther King Jr., the civil rights leader. Martin Luther King Jr. Lived hundreds of years later and was actually named after this earlier Martin Luther.

prompt._: According to the text, what was different about the paper Martin Luther nailed to the church door compared to ordinary notices?

choices.text: It shared very bold ideas.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the paper 'carried ideas so bold' that they changed the powerful Roman Catholic Church.

choices.text: It hung on a bulletin board.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the bulletin board is only used as a comparison to explain how church doors were used, and Martin Luther's paper was never on a bulletin board.

choices.text: It belonged to a university.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the paper belonged to Martin Luther, not a university — Wittenberg just happened to be a university town.

choices.text: It created a very loud sound.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'ordinary' sound described in the passage came from the hammer and nail, not from the paper.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Buying Forgiveness

Luther's document listed 95 arguments, called theses — formal statements meant to start a debate. At the heart of nearly all of them was one practice that made Luther furious: the sale of indulgences. An indulgence was a payment made to the Church that was supposed to reduce punishment for sins. Think of it like paying a fine to erase a bad mark on your record — except the Church claimed it worked even after you died.

The Church taught that sinners faced punishment in a place called purgatory, a kind of painful waiting room between death and heaven. By buying an indulgence, a person could supposedly shorten their time there. Some Church officials even promised that you could free a dead relative from purgatory by paying enough money. Priests traveled from town to town selling indulgences the way merchants sold cloth or spices. Much of that money flowed straight to Rome, where it paid for enormous building projects, including the construction of St. Peter's Basilica.

Luther, a professor of theology — the study of religion and God — believed this was deeply wrong. He argued that forgiveness was a gift from God, not something you could purchase like bread at a market. He believed the Bible taught that people were saved through faith alone, not through payments or deeds. And he pointed out that the Bible said nothing about indulgences at all.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what was the main reason Luther wrote his 95 theses against the Church?

choices.text: He wanted people to stop believing in purgatory and heaven.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Luther never argued against believing in purgatory or heaven; his anger was focused on charging money for forgiveness.

choices.text: He thought priests should not be allowed to travel between towns.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Luther never objected to priests traveling between towns — the passage mentions this only to show how widely indulgences were sold.

choices.text: He believed the Church was wrong to sell forgiveness for money.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Luther believed forgiveness came from God and could not be bought, which was the heart of his argument against indulgences.

choices.text: He wanted to stop the Church from building St. Peter's Basilica in Rome.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Luther's goal was to stop the selling of forgiveness, not to stop a building project — St. Peter's Basilica is only mentioned as where the money went.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Words That Traveled Like Fire

But what happened next stunned everyone, including Luther himself.

The printing press, invented by Johannes Gutenberg only about sixty years earlier, turned Luther's 95 Theses into something unstoppable. Before the printing press, copying a book meant writing every word by hand — slow, expensive work. Now, printers across Germany translated Luther's Latin words into German and stamped out hundreds of copies at a time. Within weeks, people in cities far from Wittenberg were reading Luther's arguments. For the first time in history, one person's ideas could reach millions in just months.
stimulus.content_text: Words That Traveled Like Fire But what happened next stunned everyone, including Luther himself. The printing press, invented by Johannes Gutenberg only about sixty years earlier, turned Luther's 95 Theses into something unstoppable. Before the printing press, copying a book meant writing every word by hand — slow, expensive work. Now, printers across Germany translated Luther's Latin words into German and stamped out hundreds of copies at a time. Within weeks, people in cities far from Wittenberg were reading Luther's arguments. For the first time in history, one person's ideas could reach millions in just months.

prompt._: According to the text, why were people in cities far from Wittenberg able to read Luther's ideas within weeks?

choices.text: Because Luther carried papers to each city
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never mentions Luther traveling to deliver his ideas to other cities.

choices.text: Because Gutenberg translated the words himself
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — Gutenberg invented the printing press about sixty years before these events and is not described as translating anything.

choices.text: Because monks copied each page by hand
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage explains that copying by hand was the slow, old method used before the printing press existed.

choices.text: Because printers made many copies at once
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says printers stamped out hundreds of copies at a time, which is exactly why Luther's ideas spread so fast and so far.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
"Here I Stand"

The Catholic Church demanded that Luther take back every word. The Pope ordered Luther to recant — to officially withdraw his criticisms, the way a student might be told to erase an answer and start over. Luther refused. So the Church excommunicated him, which meant cutting him off from the Church entirely. He could no longer receive any of its ceremonies or protections.

Then the Holy Roman Emperor, Charles V — the most powerful ruler in Europe — called Luther to a meeting known as the Diet of Worms. Despite its strange-sounding name, a "diet" here meant an official assembly, and "Worms" was simply the name of the German city where it took place. In 1521, Luther stood before emperors, princes, and bishops. They gave him one last chance to take everything back. Luther reportedly answered: "Here I stand. I can do no other."

The emperor declared Luther an outlaw — someone outside the law's protection, whom anyone could arrest or harm. But a sympathetic German prince named Frederick the Wise smuggled Luther to a hidden castle called the Wartburg. There, Luther spent months translating the Bible from Latin into German. For centuries, ordinary people could not read the Bible because it existed only in Latin, a language most of them had never learned.

prompt._: In the sentence, “The Pope ordered Luther to recant — to officially withdraw his criticisms, the way a student might be told to erase an answer and start over,” which meaning of recant best matches the comparison to erasing an answer?

choices.text: to take back his criticisms officially
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines recant as 'officially withdraw his criticisms,' and erasing an answer is the same idea — making it like it never existed.

choices.text: to speak his criticisms out loud again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because recanting means taking back what you said, not repeating or speaking it out loud again.

choices.text: to change his criticisms into kinder words
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Luther was asked to remove his criticisms completely, not rewrite them in a nicer way.

choices.text: to refuse to answer any more questions
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because refusing to answer future questions is a completely different action from taking back something you already said.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Protest That Kept Growing

Luther never planned to build a new church. He wanted to fix the one he already belonged to. But the Catholic Church refused to change, and Luther's protest grew far beyond what he imagined. This movement became known as the Protestant Reformation — "Protestant" coming from the word "protest." You might think it simply divided Christians into two groups: Catholic and Protestant. Actually, over time, Protestantism broke into many different churches and groups — Lutheran, Baptist, Methodist, and dozens more — each reading the Bible and reaching its own conclusions.

He proved that when ordinary people can read powerful ideas for themselves, no authority on earth can stop those ideas from spreading.

syllabus_metadata.title: Martin Luther and the 95 Theses

assessments.title: New Protestant Churches

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Reformation Splits Europe in Two

A Hymn That Shook the Walls

In Germany in the 1520s, a group of ordinary people sang together in German — not Latin — a song called "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God." The man who wrote it was a monk named Martin Luther. He had already nailed his famous 95 Theses to a church door, standing up against the Catholic Church. But that was just the start.

The Reformation was not one single event. It was more like a slow earthquake that spread across Europe for many years. It broke apart the unity of Western Christianity and created new churches that still exist today.

The word Protestant comes from a Latin word meaning "protest," or speak out against something. It first described a group of German princes and city leaders who stood up against the Holy Roman Emperor — the powerful ruler of much of central Europe. He had ordered everyone in his lands to follow Catholic worship. These leaders said no. Over time, "Protestant" came to mean all Christians who broke away from the Catholic Church in Rome.

It broke apart the unity of Western Christianity and created new churches that still exist today. The word Protestant comes from a Latin word meaning "protest," or speak out against something. He had ordered everyone in his lands to follow Catholic worship. These leaders said no. Over time, "Protestant" came to mean all Christians who broke away from the Catholic Church in Rome.

prompt._: According to the article, what does the author contrast with the idea that the Reformation was "one single event"?

choices.text: The idea that it was a fast earthquake shaking only Germany.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the earthquake was slow, not fast, and it spread across all of Europe, not just Germany.

choices.text: The idea that it was a quick protest changing the old rules.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never describes the Reformation as quick or uses the phrase 'changing the old rules.'

choices.text: The idea that it was a loud song shaking the church walls.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the song is mentioned in the opening paragraph as background, not as a contrast to the Reformation being one single event.

choices.text: The idea that it was a slow earthquake spreading over years.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says the Reformation was like 'a slow earthquake that spread across Europe for many years,' which is the contrast the author makes.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Luther and Calvin: Two Reformers, Two Branches of the Faith

Luther's followers started the Lutheran Church, one of the first Protestant denominations (groups within a larger religion). Lutheran churches spread quickly across northern Germany, Scandinavia (Norway, Sweden, and Denmark), and the Baltic region along the northeastern coast of Europe.

Luther taught that faith alone could save a person's soul. He believed the Bible was the highest authority. He also taught that every believer — not just priests — could have a direct connection to God. Luther loved music, too. That hymn his congregations — his groups of worshippers — sang together? People still sing it in churches around the world today.

Luther was not the only reformer changing Christianity. In Geneva, Switzerland, a man from France named John Calvin built his own branch of the faith. Calvin agreed with Luther that salvation — being saved by God — came through faith. But Calvin added a big idea called predestination. This meant that God had already decided, before time began, who would be saved and who would not. It was like a guest list written before the party was even planned. This belief made Calvin's followers very focused and disciplined. They believed that living a good, hardworking life showed they were on that list.

Calvin turned Geneva into a model city with very strict rules for how people should behave. Reformers came from all over Europe to learn his ideas, then brought them back home. Calvinist churches grew in France, where followers were called Huguenots. In Scotland, they became Presbyterians. Calvinism also spread through the Netherlands and parts of Germany.

choices.text: They believed good living showed they were chosen by God
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Calvin's followers believed living a good, hardworking life showed they were already on God's 'guest list' of saved people.

choices.text: They believed hard work would convince God to save them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Calvin's idea of predestination meant God already decided who was saved, so hard work couldn't change or convince God of anything.

choices.text: They believed working hard was the only way to read the Bible
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never links working hard to reading the Bible.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about following strict rules being connected to becoming a priest.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Bible You Could Actually Read

Here is something that might surprise you. Before the Reformation, most ordinary people had never read a single word of the Bible. This was not because they chose not to. They simply could not. Bibles were rare, expensive, and written in Latin — a language that most farmers, traders, and families never learned. The Catholic Mass — the main worship service — was also spoken in Latin.

Protestant churches changed this in a big way. They held services in the local language — German, English, French, or whatever people spoke. They encouraged everyone to read the Bible for themselves, instead of relying on priests to explain it. This push for personal Bible reading helped more people learn to read and write across Protestant parts of Europe.

This was not because they chose not to. They simply could not. Bibles were rare, expensive, and written in Latin — a language that most farmers, traders, and families never learned. The Catholic Mass — the main worship service — was also spoken in Latin. Protestant churches changed this in a big way. They held services in the local language — German, English, French, or whatever people spoke. They encouraged everyone to read the Bible for themselves, instead of relying on priests to explain it. This push for personal Bible reading helped more people learn to read and write across Protestant parts of Europe.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not mention priests translating Bibles — that detail simply isn't in the text.

choices.text: The Bible was read by most families at home.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — most families had never read the Bible at all before Protestant churches made changes.

choices.text: The Mass was spoken in Latin.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the Catholic Mass was spoken in Latin before Protestant churches began holding services in local languages.

choices.text: People learned German in church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about people learning German inside a church.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Plain Walls, Powerful Words, and a Continent Divided

The Reformation also changed how churches looked. Many Protestant churches removed stained glass windows, statues, and fancy decorations. Reformers felt these things got in the way of focusing on God's word. Protestant services were about preaching and reading the Bible, not ceremonies and rituals.

If you walked into a Protestant church in the 1500s, you would have seen plain white walls and a large pulpit where the preacher stood. This looked very different from the golden altars and painted ceilings of Catholic churches.

Within a few decades, Europe split along religious lines. Southern countries — Spain, Italy, and most of France — stayed Catholic. Northern regions — much of Germany, Scandinavia, England, and the Netherlands — became Protestant. These splits led to wars and conflicts that lasted for hundreds of years.

But something else happened, too. Millions of people gained the power to read, to worship in their own language, and to question authority for themselves. The Reformation did not just create new churches. It spread an idea that still matters today: that ordinary people have the right to think, read, and decide what they believe.

syllabus_metadata.title: New Protestant Churches

assessments.title: The Counter-Reformation

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Catholic Church Fights Back: The Counter-Reformation

A Church in Crisis

In 1545, dozens of Catholic bishops climbed the steep roads into Trent, a small city tucked between the mountains of northern Italy. Across Europe, millions of people were leaving the Catholic Church. Martin Luther, John Calvin, and other reformers had started new Protestant churches — not just one, but many different groups, including Lutherans, Calvinists, and others. The bishops gathering in Trent had a mission: save their Church.

You might think the Catholic Church simply tried to crush the Protestants and force everyone back. Actually, many Church leaders knew the reformers had a point. Some clergy — the priests, bishops, and other religious leaders — could barely read. Corruption had spread. Worst of all, the Church had been selling indulgences, which were special documents that promised to reduce a person's punishment for sins. The Church needed to fix real problems, not just fight enemies.

This effort to reform the Catholic Church from within and push back against Protestantism is called the Counter-Reformation. The word "counter" means "against" or "in response to." But this was more than a response. It was a complete makeover.

Martin Luther, John Calvin, and other reformers had started new Protestant churches — not just one, but many different groups, including Lutherans, Calvinists, and others. The bishops gathering in Trent had a mission: save their Church. You might think the Catholic Church simply tried to crush the Protestants and force everyone back. Actually, many Church leaders knew the reformers had a point. Some clergy — the priests, bishops, and other religious leaders — could barely read. Corruption had spread. Worst of all, the Church had been selling indulgences, which were special documents that promised to reduce a person's punishment for sins. The Church needed to fix real problems, not just fight enemies. This effort to reform the Catholic Church from within and push back against Protestantism is called the Counter-Reformation. The word "counter" means "against" or "in response to." But this was more than a response. It was a complete makeover.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the Catholic Church need to do instead of just fighting enemies?

choices.text: Sell more special documents
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because selling indulgences (special documents) was listed as one of the Church's problems, not something they should do more of.

choices.text: Fix its own real problems
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says exactly this: 'The Church needed to fix real problems, not just fight enemies.'

choices.text: Move to a new mountain city
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the Church needed to move to a new city — Trent was just where the bishops held their meeting.

choices.text: Start new Protestant groups
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Catholic Church was trying to stop people from joining Protestant groups, not create new ones themselves.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Council of Trent: Eighteen Years of Hard Decisions

The gathering in Trent became the most important event of the Counter-Reformation. The Council of Trent met in three separate sessions over eighteen years, from 1545 to 1563. Catholic bishops and theologians — scholars who study religion the way scientists study nature — debated every question the Protestants had raised.

Luther had taught that the Bible alone held all religious truth. The Council declared that both the Bible and Church tradition were sources of truth. The Council said salvation required both faith and good works — how you treated others mattered, too.

Then the Council turned inward. It ordered bishops to live in their own dioceses — the local areas they were responsible for, like a principal's school district — instead of living far away and ignoring their duties. Bishops had to watch over their priests more carefully. And the Council banned the sale of indulgences entirely. The very practice that had sparked Luther's protest was gone.

These decisions shaped the Catholic Church for centuries to come.
