Luther had taught that the Bible alone held all religious truth. The Council declared that both the Bible and Church tradition were sources of truth. The Council said salvation required both faith and good works — how you treated others mattered, too. Then the Council turned inward. It ordered bishops to live in their own dioceses — the local areas they were responsible for, like a principal's school district — instead of living far away and ignoring their duties. Bishops had to watch over their priests more carefully. And the Council banned the sale of indulgences entirely. The very practice that had sparked Luther's protest was gone. These decisions shaped the Catholic Church for centuries to come.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did the Council of Trent order bishops to live in their local areas?

choices.text: To keep them from staying far away and ignoring their duties.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states bishops were ordered to live in their dioceses instead of 'living far away and ignoring their duties.'

choices.text: To help them run local school districts like principals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the school district comparison was just used to explain what a diocese means, not to say bishops should act like principals.

choices.text: To teach them that the Bible alone held all religious truth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Council actually rejected the idea that the Bible alone held all religious truth — that was Luther's teaching, not theirs.

choices.text: To make sure they stopped the sale of indulgences entirely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because banning indulgences was a completely separate decision the Council made, unrelated to where bishops had to live.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Jesuits: Soldiers for a Different Kind of Battle

Around the same time, a wounded Spanish soldier lay in a hospital bed. His name was Ignatius of Loyola, and a cannonball had shattered his leg in battle. While recovering, he read about the lives of saints. That reading changed him completely. He experienced a religious conversion — a deep, personal change in belief, like suddenly seeing the world through new eyes.

In 1540, Ignatius founded a new religious order called the Society of Jesus. Its members became known as the Jesuits. They were disciplined, highly educated priests who pledged to serve the Pope. Think of them as the Catholic Church's most dedicated team — trained, organized, and sent wherever they were needed most.

Jesuits built schools and universities across Europe that became famous for their excellent teaching. But they did not stop at Europe's borders. Jesuit missionaries traveled to China, Japan, India, Africa, and Brazil. They built churches and converted millions of people to Catholicism. It's easy to assume the Catholic Church shrank after the Reformation. Here's the surprising part: through the Jesuits and other missionaries, the Church actually grew, spreading across Asia, Africa, and the Americas. It remained the largest Christian church in the world.

choices.feedback: This is incorrect because building famous schools across Europe was something the Jesuits did after the Society of Jesus was founded, not something Ignatius did while recovering.

choices.text: He read about the lives of saints.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that while Ignatius was recovering from his injury, he read about the lives of saints, and that happened before he founded the Society of Jesus.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Art That Takes Your Breath Away

The Counter-Reformation also changed what churches looked like. Catholic leaders wanted people to walk through church doors and feel the power of God immediately. So they filled their churches with a dramatic new style called Baroque art. Baroque means bold, emotional, and grand — the opposite of plain and simple.

Picture stepping inside a Baroque church. Golden altars gleam in candlelight. Swirling marble columns rise around you. Overhead, frescoes — paintings applied directly onto wet plaster ceilings — show angels and saints floating in bright blue skies. Composers wrote soaring music that echoed off the walls. Every detail was designed to fill you with awe.
stimulus.content_text: Art That Takes Your Breath Away The Counter-Reformation also changed what churches looked like. Catholic leaders wanted people to walk through church doors and feel the power of God immediately. So they filled their churches with a dramatic new style called Baroque art. Baroque means bold, emotional, and grand — the opposite of plain and simple. Picture stepping inside a Baroque church. Golden altars gleam in candlelight. Swirling marble columns rise around you. Overhead, frescoes — paintings applied directly onto wet plaster ceilings — show angels and saints floating in bright blue skies. Composers wrote soaring music that echoed off the walls. Every detail was designed to fill you with awe.

prompt._: How is Baroque art described in the passage compared with a plain style?

choices.text: It is older and weaker.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Baroque art being older or weaker — it actually describes it as powerful and awe-inspiring.

choices.text: It is bolder and grander.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 'Baroque means bold, emotional, and grand — the opposite of plain and simple.'

choices.text: It is smaller and simpler.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Baroque is described as the opposite of plain and simple, so it is bigger and grander, not smaller and simpler.

choices.text: It is darker and quieter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes Baroque art as dramatic and full of bright colors and golden altars, not dark and quiet.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Church Remade but a Faith Divided

The Counter-Reformation did not bring Protestants back. The split in Christianity was permanent. Catholics and Protestants would remain separate. But the Catholic Church came out of this period stronger, more organized, and clearer about what it believed. Its schools educated generations. Its missionaries circled the globe. Its churches still dazzle visitors today.

The next time you see a building with golden decoration, dramatic paintings, and soaring ceilings, you may be looking at the Counter-Reformation's lasting fingerprint — a reminder that sometimes the most powerful response to criticism is not argument, but change.
stimulus.content_text: A Church Remade but a Faith Divided The Counter-Reformation did not bring Protestants back. The split in Christianity was permanent. Catholics and Protestants would remain separate. But the Catholic Church came out of this period stronger, more organized, and clearer about what it believed. Its schools educated generations. Its missionaries circled the globe. Its churches still dazzle visitors today. The next time you see a building with golden decoration, dramatic paintings, and soaring ceilings, you may be looking at the Counter-Reformation's lasting fingerprint — a reminder that sometimes the most powerful response to criticism is not argument, but change.

prompt._: According to the section "A Church Remade but a Faith Divided," what is the meaning of the "split" in Christianity?

choices.text: Buildings featuring dramatic golden paintings
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because dramatic golden buildings were part of the Counter-Reformation's art style, not the meaning of the split in Christianity.

choices.text: Schools educating generations of new students
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because schools educating generations was a result of the Catholic Church getting stronger, not the meaning of the split.

choices.text: Catholics and Protestants remaining separate
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'Catholics and Protestants would remain separate,' which is exactly what a 'split' means.

choices.text: Missionaries traveling around the whole globe
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because missionaries traveling the globe was something the Catholic Church did after the split, not what the split means.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was the Catholic Church's situation at the end of the Counter-Reformation different from its situation in 1545?

choices.text: It had forced the Protestant groups to come back.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the split in Christianity was permanent and Protestants remained separate from Catholics.

choices.text: It had started selling special documents to its members.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Council of Trent banned the sale of indulgences — it did not start or continue selling them.

choices.text: It had grown larger by spreading to new continents.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Jesuit missionaries spread Catholicism to new continents like Asia, Africa, and the Americas, making the Church larger than before.

choices.text: It had stopped building large and decorated new churches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Counter-Reformation actually created MORE grand, decorated churches through the bold new Baroque art style.

prompt._: Based on the article, why did the Catholic Church end up growing larger even though millions of people were leaving it?

choices.text: The Council of Trent forced Protestants to rejoin the Catholic Church
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Council of Trent focused on reforming the Catholic Church from the inside, not on forcing Protestants to rejoin.

choices.text: Jesuit missionaries spread Catholicism to new places around the world
choices.feedback: This is correct because Jesuit missionaries traveled to places like China, Japan, Africa, and Brazil and converted millions of new people to Catholicism, making the Church grow overall.

choices.text: Baroque art convinced European Protestants to return to the Church
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Baroque art was designed to inspire Catholic worshippers, and the article clearly states that the split between Catholics and Protestants was permanent.

choices.text: The sale of indulgences brought in money to build more new churches
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Council of Trent actually banned the sale of indulgences entirely, so indulgences could not have helped the Church grow.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information in the first two sections of the article?

choices.text: By telling the story of Martin Luther's life in the exact order that events happened.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Martin Luther is only briefly mentioned — the article is not telling his life story or following the order of events in his life.

choices.text: By describing a problem the Church faced and explaining how leaders tried to fix it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the first section lays out the Church's big problems (corruption, indulgences, poorly trained clergy) and the second section shows how the Council of Trent worked to solve those exact problems.

choices.text: By comparing the beliefs of the Catholic Church to the new Protestant churches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Protestant churches are mentioned, the two sections don't go back and forth comparing Catholic and Protestant beliefs in detail.

choices.text: By explaining the reasons why the Catholic Church started selling special documents.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article mentions that indulgences were being sold as one of the Church's problems, but it never explains the reasons why the Church started selling them.

prompt._: The main idea of the article is that the Catholic Church made big changes to fix its problems. Which difference described in the text supports this main idea?

choices.text: Before the changes, the Church was losing members in Europe, but afterward it forced all the Protestants to return to the Church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the opposite — the split between Catholics and Protestants was permanent, and the Church never forced Protestants to return.

choices.text: Before the changes, the Church was losing members, but afterward it emerged stronger and more organized.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows the Church started out losing members and in crisis, but ended up stronger, more organized, and even growing worldwide — a clear before-and-after that supports the main idea.

choices.text: Before the changes, bishops lived far away from their local areas, but afterward they were sent to build schools in Asia and Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because bishops were ordered to move back to their own local areas (dioceses), not sent to Asia and Africa — traveling to those places was something the Jesuit missionaries did.

choices.text: Before the changes, the Church sold indulgences, but afterward it decided that only the Bible held religious truth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Martin Luther who taught that only the Bible held religious truth — the Catholic Church actually declared that both the Bible AND Church tradition were sources of truth.

assessments.title: Henry VIII and the Church of England

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The King Who Broke a Church to Break a Marriage

A King's Problem

In 1527, King Henry VIII of England sat in his palace with a problem no amount of power could solve. He needed a son. His wife, Catherine of Aragon, a Spanish princess he had married eighteen years earlier, had given him only one surviving child — a daughter named Mary. Without a male heir to carry on the Tudor dynasty — his royal family line — Henry feared England would fall into war after his death. He wanted to marry someone new. But the Catholic Church said no.
stimulus.content_text: The King Who Broke a Church to Break a MarriageA King's Problem In 1527, King Henry VIII of England sat in his palace with a problem no amount of power could solve. He needed a son. His wife, Catherine of Aragon, a Spanish princess he had married eighteen years earlier, had given him only one surviving child — a daughter named Mary. Without a male heir to carry on the Tudor dynasty — his royal family line — Henry feared England would fall into war after his death. He wanted to marry someone new. But the Catholic Church said no.

prompt._: According to the text, how was what Henry VIII wanted different from what the Catholic Church decided?

choices.text: Henry wanted to stay married, but the Church wanted him to remarry
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Henry wanted to remarry someone new, not stay married — it was the Church that blocked him from making a change.

choices.text: Henry wanted to marry someone new, but the Church said no
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states Henry 'wanted to marry someone new' and that 'the Catholic Church said no,' showing their disagreement clearly.

choices.text: Henry wanted a daughter, but the Church wanted him to have a son
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while Henry did want a son, the Church never said anything in the passage about sons or daughters.

choices.text: Henry wanted to leave England, but the Church told him to stay
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Henry wanting to leave England or the Church telling him to stay.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Not About Beliefs — About a Bride

You might think Henry broke away from the Catholic Church because he disagreed with its teachings. Actually, Henry had no problem with Catholic beliefs at all. In Germany, Martin Luther was protesting the sale of indulgences — payments people made to the Church to reduce punishment for their sins. In Switzerland, John Calvin was preaching about predestination — the idea that God had already chosen who would be saved. These reformers wanted to change what the Church taught about faith and theology, meaning the study of God and religious beliefs. Henry wanted none of that. He simply wanted a new wife.

The Reformation — the period when many Christians split from the Catholic Church — was not one single movement. It looked different in every country. Luther's protest in Germany, Calvin's teachings in Switzerland, and Henry's fight in England each had separate causes. England's break with Rome started not in a church, but in a royal bedroom.
stimulus.content_text: Not About Beliefs — About a Bride You might think Henry broke away from the Catholic Church because he disagreed with its teachings. Actually, Henry had no problem with Catholic beliefs at all. In Germany, Martin Luther was protesting the sale of indulgences — payments people made to the Church to reduce punishment for their sins. In Switzerland, John Calvin was preaching about predestination — the idea that God had already chosen who would be saved. These reformers wanted to change what the Church taught about faith and theology, meaning the study of God and religious beliefs. Henry wanted none of that. He simply wanted a new wife. The Reformation — the period when many Christians split from the Catholic Church — was not one single movement. It looked different in every country. Luther's protest in Germany, Calvin's teachings in Switzerland, and Henry's fight in England each had separate causes. England's break with Rome started not in a church, but in a royal bedroom.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Henry break away from the Catholic Church?

choices.text: Because he wanted to change church teachings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says Henry 'had no problem with Catholic beliefs at all' and did not want to change what the Church taught.

choices.text: Because he wanted a new wife
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states Henry 'simply wanted a new wife,' and that personal reason — not religion — is why he broke away from the Church.

choices.text: Because Luther and Calvin told him to protest
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that Luther, Calvin, and Henry each had 'separate causes,' meaning Henry was not following Luther or Calvin's lead.

choices.text: Because the Church wanted him to remarry
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the Church wanted Henry to remarry — in fact, the Church was refusing to let him end his marriage.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Pope Says No

Henry had fallen in love with a young woman at court named Anne Boleyn. To marry her, he needed an annulment — an official declaration that his first marriage had never been valid. Think of it like erasing a contract rather than tearing it up. Only the Pope, the head of the Catholic Church in Rome, had the power to grant one.

Henry asked. The Pope refused. One big reason was Catherine's family. She was the aunt of Charles V, the Holy Roman Emperor — the ruler of a vast empire stretching across much of Europe. Charles was one of the most powerful people alive. The Pope did not want to insult him by canceling his aunt's marriage. Henry was stuck.
stimulus.content_text: The Pope Says No Henry had fallen in love with a young woman at court named Anne Boleyn. To marry her, he needed an annulment — an official declaration that his first marriage had never been valid. Think of it like erasing a contract rather than tearing it up. Only the Pope, the head of the Catholic Church in Rome, had the power to grant one. Henry asked. The Pope refused. One big reason was Catherine's family. She was the aunt of Charles V, the Holy Roman Emperor — the ruler of a vast empire stretching across much of Europe. Charles was one of the most powerful people alive. The Pope did not want to insult him by canceling his aunt's marriage. Henry was stuck.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Henry do right before the Pope refused his request?

choices.text: He wrote a letter to Charles V
choices.feedback: Henry never contacted Charles V in this passage — Charles V is actually the reason the Pope refused, not someone Henry reached out to.

choices.text: He broke away from the Catholic Church
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions Henry breaking away from the Catholic Church — that detail isn't in this part of the story.

choices.text: He asked the Pope for an annulment
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'Henry asked. The Pope refused,' which shows Henry asked for the annulment right before the Pope said no.

choices.text: He married Anne Boleyn at court
choices.feedback: Henry wanted to marry Anne Boleyn but the passage says he needed an annulment first — he hadn't married her yet.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Henry Takes Control

Henry was furious — and he was not the type of king who accepted "no." In 1534, the English Parliament passed the Act of Supremacy, a law that made the king, not the Pope, the supreme head of the Church in England. With one law, Henry cut the ties between England and Rome. He quickly annulled his own marriage to Catherine and married Anne Boleyn.

The new Church of England, also called the Anglican Church, might surprise you. It's easy to assume it became fully Protestant overnight. But walk into an English church the week after the split, and you would barely notice a difference. The stained glass windows still glowed. The altars still stood. The prayers still sounded like Catholic Mass. The biggest change was invisible — it was about who held authority. The king of England, not the Pope in Rome, now controlled the Church.

Henry also shut down hundreds of monasteries — religious communities where monks lived, prayed, and worked. He seized their land and wealth, then sold the property to his supporters. This created a new class of wealthy landowners who owed their fortunes to the king. They had every reason to stay loyal to him.

The new Church of England, also called the Anglican Church, might surprise you. It's easy to assume it became fully Protestant overnight. But walk into an English church the week after the split, and you would barely notice a difference. The stained glass windows still glowed. The altars still stood. The prayers still sounded like Catholic Mass. The biggest change was invisible — it was about who held authority. The king of England, not the Pope in Rome, now controlled the Church. prompt._: Based on the passage, how was the new Church of England similar to the Catholic Church?

choices.text: Both churches followed the Pope as their true leader.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Act of Supremacy specifically took power away from the Pope and gave it to the king of England.

choices.text: Both churches looked and sounded mostly the same.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the stained glass, altars, and prayers stayed the same, and you would 'barely notice a difference' between the two churches.

choices.text: Both churches gave their land to wealthy supporters.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only Henry seized land from monasteries and gave it to supporters — this was not something both churches did.

choices.text: Both churches became completely Protestant overnight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage directly says 'it's easy to assume it became fully Protestant overnight,' but then explains that it actually did not.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Six Wives and a Son

Henry's personal life grew only more dramatic. Henry VIII married six wives over his lifetime. When Anne Boleyn also failed to give him a son, he had her executed on charges of treason — the crime of betraying your king or country. His third wife, Jane Seymour, finally gave birth to a boy named Edward. But Jane died shortly after. Henry married three more times before his own death in 1547.
stimulus.content_text: Six Wives and a Son Henry's personal life grew only more dramatic. Henry VIII married six wives over his lifetime. When Anne Boleyn also failed to give him a son, he had her executed on charges of treason — the crime of betraying your king or country. His third wife, Jane Seymour, finally gave birth to a boy named Edward. But Jane died shortly after. Henry married three more times before his own death in 1547.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'treason' mean?

choices.text: the punishment given to a queen by a king
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a punishment is what happens after a crime — it is not the definition of treason itself.

choices.text: the crime of betraying your king or country
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that treason is 'the crime of betraying your king or country,' using a dash to give us the definition.

choices.text: the act of ruling over a country
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ruling over a country is what a king does — it has nothing to do with the definition of treason given in the passage.

choices.text: the process of marrying into a royal family
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because marrying into a royal family is something Henry's wives did, but the passage never connects that idea to the word treason.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Nation Pulled Back and Forth

Henry's break with Rome sent England spinning through decades of religious conflict. His son Edward pushed the country toward Protestantism. His daughter Mary — Catherine of Aragon's child — tried to drag it back to Catholicism. His second daughter Elizabeth — Anne Boleyn's child — finally settled the fight. She established a moderate form of Protestantism that became the lasting foundation of the Church of England.

And all of it began because one king wanted a son badly enough to split a nation from its Church. Henry VIII never set out to start a religious movement. But power and personal desire, it turns out, can reshape a country's identity just as deeply as any argument about God.

His daughter Mary — Catherine of Aragon's child — tried to drag it back to Catholicism. His second daughter Elizabeth — Anne Boleyn's child — finally settled the fight. She established a moderate form of Protestantism that became the lasting foundation of the Church of England. And all of it began because one king wanted a son badly enough to split a nation from its Church. Henry VIII never set out to start a religious movement. But power and personal desire, it turns out, can reshape a country's identity just as deeply as any argument about God.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that Elizabeth ended the long religious struggle in England?

choices.text: She finally settled the fight.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'finally settled the fight' to describe what Elizabeth did, showing she ended the religious struggle.

choices.text: She followed her brother Edward.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage does not say Elizabeth followed Edward; it shows each ruler made their own separate religious changes.

choices.text: She began the conflict with Rome.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Elizabeth did not begin the conflict — the passage tells us it was her father, Henry VIII, who started everything by breaking with Rome.

choices.text: She moved England back to Catholicism.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says Elizabeth set up a form of Protestantism, not Catholicism — it was her sister Mary who tried to bring Catholicism back.

choices.text: Luther and Calvin wanted to close down monasteries, while Henry wanted to build more of them.

choices.text: Luther and Calvin wanted to sell church indulgences, while Henry wanted to stop people from buying them.

choices.text: Luther and Calvin wanted to start a royal family, while Henry wanted to change the church prayers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither Luther nor Calvin tried to start a royal family, and Henry never tried to change church prayers.

prompt._: According to the article, what caused King Henry VIII to pass the Act of Supremacy and take control of the church?

choices.text: He wanted to change Catholic teachings, but the church refused to listen to him.

choices.text: He wanted to gain more wealth, but the local monasteries refused to pay him.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Henry took the monasteries' wealth after he already passed the Act of Supremacy — it was not the reason he created the law.

choices.text: He needed a son to rule, but the Pope refused to cancel his marriage.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article explains Henry needed a male heir and passed the Act of Supremacy after the Pope refused to cancel his marriage to Catherine.

choices.text: He needed to stop a future war, but his three children refused to agree.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Henry only had one child (Mary) when this happened, and the article never mentions his children refusing to agree on anything.

prompt._: Which choice best describes the order of the main events in the article from beginning to end?

choices.text: Henry asked the Pope for help, then he closed the monasteries, then he executed Anne Boleyn.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it skips the most important first event — Henry needing a son — which is what started everything in the story.

choices.text: Henry started a new church, then he asked the Pope for help, then his children ruled.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Henry asked the Pope for help first, and only started a new church after the Pope said no — the order is backwards.

choices.text: Henry needed a son, then he broke from the Catholic Church, then his children ruled.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's order: Henry needed a son, broke from the Catholic Church to get a new wife, and then his children took turns ruling England.

choices.text: Henry married six wives, then he needed a son, then he broke from the Catholic Church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Henry didn't marry six wives before needing a son; wanting a son was actually the reason he began marrying multiple women.

prompt._: Which sentence best describes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: He wanted to change what the church taught, but he only changed who owned the land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Henry did change who owned the land — he seized monastery lands and gave them to his supporters — and he never tried to change church teachings at all.

choices.text: He wanted to follow the Pope's rules, but he let his friends take over the new church.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Henry did the opposite of following the Pope — he passed a law making himself the head of the church instead of the Pope.

choices.text: He wanted to solve a personal problem, but he changed the religion of a whole nation.
choices.feedback: This is correct because Henry only wanted to solve his personal problem of getting a son, but his actions ended up changing the religion of the entire nation of England.

choices.text: He wanted to copy other religious leaders, but he kept the churches looking the same.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Henry was not trying to copy other religious leaders like Luther or Calvin; the article explains that each country's break from the Catholic Church had completely separate causes.

assessments.title: Why Europeans Set Sail

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Worth Its Weight in Silver: Why Europeans Sailed Into the Unknown

A Handful of Pepper

In a market in London around the year 1400, a small bag of pepper cost as much as a bag of silver coins the same size. The same spice sitting in your kitchen right now. Back then, it had traveled thousands of miles on the backs of camels, passed through dozens of hands, and crossed burning deserts and frozen mountain passes before reaching Europe. Each merchant along the way raised the price. By the end of that journey, ordinary spices had become treasures only the wealthy could afford.

Europeans were desperate to find a cheaper, faster way to get spices, silk, and gold from Asia. That desperation would launch one of the most dramatic eras in history: the Age of Exploration.
stimulus.content_text: Worth Its Weight in Silver: Why Europeans Sailed Into the UnknownA Handful of Pepper In a market in London around the year 1400, a small bag of pepper cost as much as a bag of silver coins the same size. The same spice sitting in your kitchen right now. Back then, it had traveled thousands of miles on the backs of camels, passed through dozens of hands, and crossed burning deserts and frozen mountain passes before reaching Europe. Each merchant along the way raised the price. By the end of that journey, ordinary spices had become treasures only the wealthy could afford. Europeans were desperate to find a cheaper, faster way to get spices, silk, and gold from Asia. That desperation would launch one of the most dramatic eras in history: the Age of Exploration.

prompt._: According to the passage, how is pepper different now compared to how it was around the year 1400?

choices.text: Pepper now is a common kitchen spice, but back then it was a costly treasure.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says pepper is in your kitchen today but back in 1400 it was so expensive only wealthy people could afford it.

choices.text: Pepper now is mixed with silver coins, but back then it was sold in small bags.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage compares the cost of pepper to silver coins to show how expensive it was, but never says they were mixed together.

choices.text: Pepper now is shipped from nearby countries, but back then it came from Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says spices came from Asia, and it never mentions nearby countries or Africa.

choices.text: Pepper now is mainly used by wealthy people, but back then everyone could get it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has things backwards — the passage says only wealthy people could afford pepper back in 1400, not everyone.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Flavor

In a world without refrigerators, meat spoiled quickly. Spices like pepper, nutmeg, cloves, and cinnamon helped preserve food and covered the taste of meat that had gone bad. The demand was enormous, and whoever controlled the spice trade controlled a fortune.

For centuries, these goods traveled overland through trade routes — paths used by merchants to move goods between distant lands, like highways connecting continents. Caravans, which were long lines of camels and traders traveling together for safety, carried the spices westward. But European merchants never made the full journey themselves. They bought from middlemen, and every middleman added cost.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Flavor In a world without refrigerators, meat spoiled quickly. Spices like pepper, nutmeg, cloves, and cinnamon helped preserve food and covered the taste of meat that had gone bad. The demand was enormous, and whoever controlled the spice trade controlled a fortune. For centuries, these goods traveled overland through trade routes — paths used by merchants to move goods between distant lands, like highways connecting continents. Caravans, which were long lines of camels and traders traveling together for safety, carried the spices westward. But European merchants never made the full journey themselves. They bought from middlemen, and every middleman added cost.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did spices become so valuable in Europe?

choices.text: They made the overland journey shorter and safer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects spices to making the overland journey shorter or safer — that is not a role spices played at all.

choices.text: They were grown by European merchants in large amounts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says European merchants bought spices from middlemen, which means they did not grow the spices themselves.

choices.text: They helped keep food longer and hide bad tastes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that spices helped preserve food and covered the taste of meat that had gone bad, making them extremely valuable.

choices.text: They were used to guide caravans across trade routes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says spices guided caravans — caravans were groups of camels and traders traveling together for safety, not something directed by spices.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A City Falls, and Europe Panics

In 1453, something happened that made the problem worse. The Ottoman Turks — a powerful Muslim empire based in modern-day Turkey — captured the great city of Constantinople. Constantinople sat right where Europe meets Asia, like a bridge between two worlds. For centuries, it had been the main gateway for East-West trade.

But they charged higher taxes and made the routes more dangerous for Christian merchants. European rulers realized they could no longer depend on overland paths. They needed to find a sea route to Asia instead.
stimulus.content_text: A City Falls, and Europe Panics In 1453, something happened that made the problem worse. The Ottoman Turks — a powerful Muslim empire based in modern-day Turkey — captured the great city of Constantinople. Constantinople sat right where Europe meets Asia, like a bridge between two worlds. For centuries, it had been the main gateway for East-West trade. But they charged higher taxes and made the routes more dangerous for Christian merchants. European rulers realized they could no longer depend on overland paths. They needed to find a sea route to Asia instead.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the Ottoman Turks do after they captured the city of Constantinople?

choices.text: They built a large bridge between Europe and Asia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses 'bridge' as a comparison to describe Constantinople's location, not to say the Ottomans actually built one.

choices.text: They charged higher taxes to Christian merchants.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the Ottoman Turks 'charged higher taxes and made the routes more dangerous for Christian merchants.'

choices.text: They searched for a new sea route to reach Asia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the European rulers, not the Ottoman Turks, who went looking for a new sea route to Asia.

choices.text: They depended on overland paths for many years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says European rulers realized they could *no longer* depend on overland paths, meaning those paths were no longer a good option.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
New Tools for Unknown Waters

European sailors needed better tools, and by the 1400s, they finally had them.

The magnetic compass, originally invented in China, let sailors figure out which direction they were heading even when thick clouds hid the sun and stars. The astrolabe — a metal disk covered in markings that measured the height of stars above the horizon — helped sailors calculate their latitude, meaning how far north or south they were. Think of it like a GPS that used starlight instead of satellites.

New ships helped too. The caravel was a small, sturdy vessel with triangular sails. Unlike older European ships, it could sail against the wind and handle rough ocean waves. Portugal built many of these ships, and Portuguese sailors became some of the first Europeans to explore the west coast of Africa by sea.

Think of it like a GPS that used starlight instead of satellites. New ships helped too. The caravel was a small, sturdy vessel with triangular sails. Unlike older European ships, it could sail against the wind and handle rough ocean waves. Portugal built many of these ships, and Portuguese sailors became some of the first Europeans to explore the west coast of Africa by sea.

prompt._: How is an astrolabe like a GPS, based on the passage?

choices.text: Both are tools that show direction when clouds hide the sky.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because helping travelers when clouds hide the sky describes the compass, not the astrolabe or GPS.

choices.text: Both are tools that let ships sail against the wind.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sailing against the wind is a feature of the caravel ship, not the astrolabe or GPS.

choices.text: Both are tools that measure the height of stars.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because measuring the height of stars is only what the astrolabe does — a GPS uses satellites, not stars.

choices.text: Both are tools that help travelers figure out location.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly compares the astrolabe to a GPS by saying both help you figure out your location, just using different technology.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Gold, God, and Glory

Technology alone did not send ships across the ocean. Money and ambition did. European monarchs — kings and queens who ruled entire nations — funded these voyages for three big reasons. First, wealth: if a nation found a direct sea route to Asia, it could control the spice trade and grow rich. Second, power: any new lands an explorer found could be claimed for the crown. Third, religion: the Catholic Church encouraged exploration as a way to spread Christianity to people in distant lands, gaining new converts — people who adopt a new faith.

Their rulers poured money into expeditions and competed fiercely with each other. Sailors were willing to risk their lives on tiny wooden ships, spending months on open water, because the rewards seemed worth the danger. Some truly believed they were doing holy work.

Third, religion: the Catholic Church encouraged exploration as a way to spread Christianity to people in distant lands, gaining new converts — people who adopt a new faith. Their rulers poured money into expeditions and competed fiercely with each other. Sailors were willing to risk their lives on tiny wooden ships, spending months on open water, because the rewards seemed worth the danger. Some truly believed they were doing holy work.

prompt._: According to the text, what are converts?

choices.text: people who adopt a new faith
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines converts right after a dash, saying they are 'people who adopt a new faith.'

choices.text: explorers who claim new lands
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because explorers who claim new lands are mentioned earlier in the passage as a different group entirely.

choices.text: kings and queens who ruled nations
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses the word 'monarchs' to describe kings and queens who ruled nations, not converts.

choices.text: sailors who risk their lives at sea
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sailors who risk their lives are a separate group described in the second paragraph of the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Not the First

You might think European explorers of the 1400s were the first to cross the Atlantic Ocean. Nearly five hundred years earlier, Norse sailors led by Leif Erikson had reached North America. But those early voyages didn't lead to lasting contact between Europe and the Americas. When Spanish and Portuguese ships arrived centuries later, the encounters that followed would be far more permanent — and far more destructive.

European exploration brought Europeans face to face with the Aztec Empire in Mexico, the Inca Empire in South America, and hundreds of other Indigenous peoples across three continents. It triggered an enormous exchange of foods, animals, diseases, and ideas between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres. Potatoes and tomatoes traveled to Europe. Horses and wheat traveled to the Americas. But deadly diseases like smallpox traveled too, killing millions of Indigenous people who had no immunity.

The next time you shake pepper onto your dinner, remember: that simple spice once had the power to send ships across oceans, topple empires, and connect — and shatter — worlds that had never known each other existed.

choices.text: Explorers crossed the wide Atlantic Ocean.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was the older way of getting spices different from the new way European rulers wanted to use?

choices.text: The older way provided cheap spices, but the new way made them costly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us the old land routes made spices very expensive, not cheap — that's actually why Europeans wanted a new way!

choices.text: The Ottoman Turks captured the city of Constantinople.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how the text compares land and sea routes to support its main idea?

choices.text: Land routes became too costly, while sea routes offered a direct path to vast wealth.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly shows land routes became too expensive due to middlemen and Ottoman taxes, while sea routes promised direct access to Asia's wealth.

choices.text: Land routes seemed safe but slow, while sea routes were fast but attacked by an enemy.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says sea routes were attacked by an enemy, and land routes were described as costly and dangerous, not safe.

choices.text: Land routes carried mostly gold, while sea routes focused on spreading new religions.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never separates gold from land routes or says sea routes were mainly for spreading religion — both goals existed together.

choices.text: Land routes reached the Americas, while sea routes linked Europe directly with Asia.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says land routes connected Europe to Asia, and explorers wanted sea routes to reach Asia — the Americas were an unexpected discovery.

syllabus_metadata.title: Why Europeans Set Sail

assessments.title: Portugal Leads the Way

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Tiny Kingdom That Mapped the World

A Prince Who Never Sailed

In the 1420s, on a windy cliff at the southwestern tip of Europe, a Portuguese prince stared out at the Atlantic Ocean. Prince Henry never climbed aboard an exploration ship himself. He never gripped a rope in a storm or watched an unfamiliar coastline rise from the horizon. Yet from his court in the small town of Sagres, he set in motion a chain of voyages that would reshape the map of the world.

You might picture Portugal as a major power to pull this off. Actually, it was one of Europe's smallest kingdoms. France and England, the continent's giants, were busy fighting each other in a long, expensive war. Portugal sat on the sidelines — small, relatively poor, but positioned right on the edge of the Atlantic with a long coastline and generations of fishing families who knew the sea.

So why did Portugal pour its money into ocean voyages? Spices like pepper and cinnamon, along with silk and gold, traveled to Europe over long, dangerous land routes through Asia and the Middle East. By the time a bag of pepper reached a European market, it could be worth more than silver. Portugal wanted to find a sea route around Africa to reach those riches directly — and keep the profits.

Prince Henry never climbed aboard an exploration ship himself. He never gripped a rope in a storm or watched an unfamiliar coastline rise from the horizon. Yet from his court in the small town of Sagres, he set in motion a chain of voyages that would reshape the map of the world. You might picture Portugal as a major power to pull this off. Actually, it was one of Europe's smallest kingdoms. France and England, the continent's giants, were busy fighting each other in a long, expensive war. Portugal sat on the sidelines — small, relatively poor, but positioned right on the edge of the Atlantic with a long coastline and generations of fishing families who knew the sea. So why did Portugal pour its money into ocean voyages? Spices like pepper and cinnamon, along with silk and gold, traveled to Europe over long, dangerous land routes through Asia and the Middle East. By the time a bag of pepper reached a European market, it could be worth more than silver. Portugal wanted to find a sea route around Africa to reach those riches directly — and keep the profits.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Portugal different from the giant countries of France and England?

choices.text: It was fighting a long and expensive war.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says France and England were the ones 'fighting each other in a long, expensive war,' not Portugal.

choices.text: It was trading spices over long land routes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Portugal wanted to find a sea route to avoid the long land trade routes, not use them.

choices.text: It was a major power with a large military.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Portugal was actually small and poor, not a major power with a large military.

choices.text: It was a small and relatively poor kingdom.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly describes Portugal as 'one of Europe's smallest kingdoms' and 'small, relatively poor,' which is the opposite of the giants France and England.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Workshop at Sagres

Prince Henry gathered the best minds he could find. Mapmakers, shipbuilders, astronomers — scientists who study the stars and planets to track position and time — and experienced sailors all came to his court. Think of Sagres as a workshop where experts from different fields shared what they knew. Historians still debate exactly how this workshop was organized, but the results are clear.

One major result was a new kind of ship: the caravel. Older European ships used square sails that caught the wind well when it blew from behind, but struggled to sail in other directions. The caravel used triangular lateen sails — sails shaped like a slanted wing — that let sailors angle into the wind and change direction quickly. The caravel was also smaller and lighter than most trading ships, so it could slip into shallow harbors and hug unfamiliar coastlines. It became Portugal's secret weapon on the open ocean.

Here's something easy to forget: African and Arab sailors had been traveling these waters and trading along the African coast for centuries before any Portuguese ship arrived. These were not empty, unknown seas. But they were unknown to Europeans, and Portugal's seafaring — the practice of traveling by sea, the way a truck driver's skill is traveling by road — gave it a head start over other European nations.

These were not empty, unknown seas. But they were unknown to Europeans, and Portugal's seafaring — the practice of traveling by sea, the way a truck driver's skill is traveling by road — gave it a head start over other European nations.

prompt._: Based on the section, what was the main reason Prince Henry's workshop at Sagres was important to Portugal?

choices.text: It taught European sailors how to use square sails on the open ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the workshop helped create triangular lateen sails, which were better than the old square sails, not a way to teach sailors to use square sails.

choices.text: It proved that African and Arab sailors had already explored the seas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions African and Arab sailors as background information, not as something Prince Henry's workshop proved or focused on.

choices.text: It helped Portugal build the largest trading ships in all of Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the caravel was described as smaller and lighter than most trading ships, not the largest.

choices.text: It brought experts together, leading to advances that gave Portugal an advantage.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that bringing experts together led to the invention of the caravel, which gave Portugal an advantage over other European nations.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Trading Posts and Terrible Costs

Under Prince Henry's sponsorship — his financial support, like a company paying for a team's equipment — Portuguese captains pushed farther south along Africa's western coast with each voyage. They mapped the coastline, charted currents and winds, and found harbors where ships could stop for fresh water and supplies.

Portugal built trading posts at these stops, creating a chain of stations stretching down the coast. At these posts, Portuguese merchants traded for gold and ivory, the hard white material from elephant tusks that Europeans prized for carvings and luxury goods. But they also bought and sold enslaved African people. This trade in human beings, which began during these decades, would grow into one of the greatest tragedies in human history — the transatlantic slave trade that would eventually force millions of Africans into bondage.

Prince Henry died in 1460, never seeing how far his program would reach.
