Portugal built trading posts at these stops, creating a chain of stations stretching down the coast. At these posts, Portuguese merchants traded for gold and ivory, the hard white material from elephant tusks that Europeans prized for carvings and luxury goods. But they also bought and sold enslaved African people. This trade in human beings, which began during these decades, would grow into one of the greatest tragedies in human history — the transatlantic slave trade that would eventually force millions of Africans into bondage. Prince Henry died in 1460, never seeing how far his program would reach.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Portuguese captains do before Portugal built trading posts along the coast?

choices.text: They sailed across the Atlantic Ocean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Portuguese captains sailing across the Atlantic Ocean — they were exploring Africa's western coast.

choices.text: They traded for gold and ivory at the posts
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because trading for gold and ivory happened at the trading posts, which were built after the exploring and mapping.

choices.text: They mapped the coastline and found harbors
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says captains mapped the coastline and found harbors during their voyages, which came before Portugal built the trading posts at those locations.

choices.text: They bought and sold enslaved African people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because buying and selling enslaved people happened at the trading posts, not before they were built.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Rounding the Cape

In 1488, a captain named Bartolomeu Dias sailed into ferocious storms near the bottom of Africa. When the storms finally broke, Dias looked at his charts and realized something astonishing: the coastline now ran northward. He had sailed around the southern tip of the entire continent — a point later named the Cape of Good Hope.

Dias wanted to keep going, all the way to India. But Dias carried home proof that a sea route to Asia's spice markets actually existed. Within a decade, another Portuguese captain, Vasco da Gama, would complete that journey.
stimulus.content_text: Rounding the Cape In 1488, a captain named Bartolomeu Dias sailed into ferocious storms near the bottom of Africa. When the storms finally broke, Dias looked at his charts and realized something astonishing: the coastline now ran northward. He had sailed around the southern tip of the entire continent — a point later named the Cape of Good Hope. Dias wanted to keep going, all the way to India. But Dias carried home proof that a sea route to Asia's spice markets actually existed. Within a decade, another Portuguese captain, Vasco da Gama, would complete that journey.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what is one way Bartolomeu Dias and Vasco da Gama were alike?

choices.text: Both men named the tip of Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the tip of Africa was named the Cape of Good Hope, but doesn't say either Dias or da Gama gave it that name.

choices.text: Both men reached the spice markets.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Dias turned back before reaching the spice markets — only da Gama completed that journey.

choices.text: Both men were captains from Portugal.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes both Dias and da Gama as 'Portuguese captains,' so they shared the same job and nationality.

choices.text: Both men sailed during the same year.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Dias sailed in 1488 and da Gama sailed within a decade after that, meaning they sailed in different years.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Lines on a Map That Still Matter

Portugal's ships eventually reached India, Southeast Asia, China, Japan, and Brazil, building a trading empire across three continents. All of it grew from decades of patient, expensive work — better ships, trained sailors, and a growing chain of knowledge passed from one voyage to the next.

Today, when you look at a world map showing sea routes connecting continents, many of those lines trace back to Portuguese captains who sailed small caravels along the African coast six hundred years ago.
stimulus.content_text: Lines on a Map That Still Matter Portugal's ships eventually reached India, Southeast Asia, China, Japan, and Brazil, building a trading empire across three continents. All of it grew from decades of patient, expensive work — better ships, trained sailors, and a growing chain of knowledge passed from one voyage to the next. Today, when you look at a world map showing sea routes connecting continents, many of those lines trace back to Portuguese captains who sailed small caravels along the African coast six hundred years ago.

prompt._: According to the passage, what was a "chain of knowledge"?

choices.text: information shared from one trip to the next
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says the 'chain of knowledge' was 'passed from one voyage to the next,' which means information was shared from one trip to the next.

choices.text: a line of sea routes drawn on maps
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because sea routes drawn on maps are mentioned separately in the passage — the 'chain of knowledge' is about sailors sharing what they learned, not about drawing lines on a map.

choices.text: a set of trade goods carried to Brazil
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage doesn't describe the chain of knowledge as trade goods; it's about what sailors learned and passed on, not what they carried.

choices.text: a group of ships tied together at sea
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions ships being tied together — a 'chain of knowledge' is about sharing information, not connecting ships.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Prince Henry's role different from the roles of the Portuguese captains?

choices.text: Prince Henry traded for the spices, while the captains traded for the gold.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Henry traded for spices — his role was providing financial support (sponsorship), not doing the trading himself.

choices.text: Prince Henry stayed safely on land, while the captains traveled the ocean.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states Prince Henry never sailed on an exploration ship and ran things from his court on land, while captains like Dias and da Gama were the ones who actually traveled the ocean.

choices.text: Prince Henry built the new ships, while the captains created the sea maps.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says shipbuilders created the caravel and mapmakers made the charts — these were separate experts at Henry's workshop, not jobs split between Henry and the captains.

choices.text: Prince Henry sailed toward Asia, while the captains sailed toward Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the roles backwards — the captains sailed along Africa and eventually toward Asia, and Henry never sailed anywhere at all.

prompt._: What most helped Portugal become able to reach faraway places like India and Brazil?

choices.text: improving ships and sharing sailing knowledge over time
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Portugal's success came from building better ships like the caravel, training skilled sailors, and sharing knowledge from each voyage to help the next one go even farther.

choices.text: building trading posts before sailors explored the coast
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage shows that sailors explored and mapped the coastline first, and trading posts were built at the stops they discovered along the way.

choices.text: waiting for France and England to finish their war
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Portugal didn't wait — it actually moved ahead while France and England were distracted by their war.

choices.text: sending Prince Henry to lead voyages across the ocean
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage tells us Prince Henry never sailed on any exploration ship himself; he supported the voyages from his court on land.

prompt._: Based on the article, which event happened first in Portugal's search for a sea route to Asia?

choices.text: Experts designed a new ship called the caravel.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the caravel was created at Prince Henry's workshop in the 1420s, making it the earliest event in Portugal's search for a sea route to Asia.

choices.text: Bartolomeu Dias sailed around the tip of Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Dias rounded the tip of Africa in 1488, which was decades after the caravel was designed and trading posts were already being built.

choices.text: Captains built trading posts on the African coast.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the trading posts were built after the caravel was already being used to sail down the African coast.

choices.text: Vasco da Gama finished the sea route to India.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because da Gama's completed voyage to India happened last of all, after Dias had already proven a sea route was possible.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how Portugal's situation at the end of the passage compares to its situation at the beginning?

choices.text: It began as a small, poor kingdom but grew into a massive global trading empire.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Portugal going from 'small, relatively poor' at the beginning to building 'a trading empire across three continents' by the end.

choices.text: It began by fighting expensive wars but ended up peacefully sharing the seas with others.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was France and England that were fighting expensive wars, not Portugal, and the passage never mentions Portugal peacefully sharing the seas.

choices.text: It began by trading with Asian markets but later decided to explore the African coast.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Portugal started by exploring Africa in order to find a sea route to Asia, not the other way around.

choices.text: It began with many large ships but eventually lost them all in terrible ocean storms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Portugal lost its ships — Dias survived the storms and sailed home safely.

syllabus_metadata.title: Portugal Leads the Way

assessments.title: Vasco da Gama Reaches India

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Vasco da Gama's Voyage to India

Sixty Times the Price

These dried plants, small enough to fit in your backpack, were worth more than gold in 1490s Europe. The king of Portugal wanted them badly — and he was willing to send ships halfway around the world to get them.

In July 1497, a nobleman — a person born into a powerful, high-ranking family — named Vasco da Gama sailed out of Lisbon, Portugal, with four ships and about 170 men. Da Gama was an experienced sailor, and the king had chosen him for one reason: to find a sea route all the way to India, where spices grew. European merchants had been buying spices for centuries, but they had never sailed to India themselves. Da Gama intended to be the first.
stimulus.content_text: Vasco da Gama's Voyage to IndiaSixty Times the Price These dried plants, small enough to fit in your backpack, were worth more than gold in 1490s Europe. The king of Portugal wanted them badly — and he was willing to send ships halfway around the world to get them. In July 1497, a nobleman — a person born into a powerful, high-ranking family — named Vasco da Gama sailed out of Lisbon, Portugal, with four ships and about 170 men. Da Gama was an experienced sailor, and the king had chosen him for one reason: to find a sea route all the way to India, where spices grew. European merchants had been buying spices for centuries, but they had never sailed to India themselves. Da Gama intended to be the first.

prompt._: According to the passage, how were European merchants different from Vasco da Gama when it came to reaching India by sea?

choices.text: They had sailed to India many times before him
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says merchants had never sailed to India themselves, not that they had done it many times.

choices.text: They had already found a sea route to India
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the whole reason the king sent da Gama was to find a sea route to India, which means no one had found one yet.

choices.text: They had never sailed to India themselves
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that European merchants 'had never sailed to India themselves,' which is exactly what made da Gama's mission special.

choices.text: They had stopped buying spices from India
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says merchants had been buying spices for centuries, meaning they were still purchasing them.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Ten Months on the Open Sea

Da Gama's fleet — a group of ships traveling together, like a team on the same mission — sailed south along the African coast. They rounded the Cape of Good Hope at Africa's southern tip and turned north along the eastern shore. The journey was brutal. Storms battered the wooden ships. Fresh food ran out. Sailors began falling sick with scurvy, a painful disease caused by not getting enough vitamin C. Their gums bled. Their teeth loosened. Nearly half the crew would die before the expedition returned home.

Along the East African coast, da Gama's ships stopped at ports in Mozambique and Kenya. Here, the Portuguese encountered Arab traders who had been sailing these waters for hundreds of years. You might think da Gama was entering unknown territory, but actually, the Indian Ocean was already one of the busiest trade highways in the world. Arab, Indian, and Chinese merchants had been crossing it long before any European ship appeared. With the help of an experienced Arab guide, da Gama's fleet crossed the Indian Ocean and reached the port of Calicut, on India's southwestern coast, in May 1498. The voyage had taken ten months and covered more than 14,000 miles.
stimulus.content_text: Ten Months on the Open Sea Da Gama's fleet — a group of ships traveling together, like a team on the same mission — sailed south along the African coast. They rounded the Cape of Good Hope at Africa's southern tip and turned north along the eastern shore. The journey was brutal. Storms battered the wooden ships. Fresh food ran out. Sailors began falling sick with scurvy, a painful disease caused by not getting enough vitamin C. Their gums bled. Their teeth loosened. Nearly half the crew would die before the expedition returned home. Along the East African coast, da Gama's ships stopped at ports in Mozambique and Kenya. Here, the Portuguese encountered Arab traders who had been sailing these waters for hundreds of years. You might think da Gama was entering unknown territory, but actually, the Indian Ocean was already one of the busiest trade highways in the world. Arab, Indian, and Chinese merchants had been crossing it long before any European ship appeared. With the help of an experienced Arab guide, da Gama's fleet crossed the Indian Ocean and reached the port of Calicut, on India's southwestern coast, in May 1498. The voyage had taken ten months and covered more than 14,000 miles.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did many of da Gama's sailors get sick with scurvy?

choices.text: They drank too much salty ocean water during the trip
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions sailors drinking salty ocean water — that detail simply isn't in the story.

choices.text: They were injured by the storms that hit their ships
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while storms did batter the ships, the passage says scurvy was caused by not getting enough vitamin C, not by storm injuries.

choices.text: They ran out of fresh food on the long voyage
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly links running out of fresh food to sailors getting scurvy, a disease caused by not getting enough vitamin C.

choices.text: They caught the disease from Arab traders at ports
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains scurvy comes from a lack of vitamin C, not from contact with Arab traders at the ports.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Not Exactly Impressed

Their city was a busy center of trade, filled with silks, jewels, and spices from across Asia. Merchants from Arabia, China, and East Africa visited regularly. When da Gama presented his European trade goods — cloth, coral, honey — the rulers of Calicut thought they looked cheap. Compared to what Arab and Chinese merchants offered, the Portuguese gifts were not impressive at all.

Still, da Gama managed to collect a small cargo of spices, including pepper and cinnamon, and he turned his ships toward home.
stimulus.content_text: Not Exactly Impressed Their city was a busy center of trade, filled with silks, jewels, and spices from across Asia. Merchants from Arabia, China, and East Africa visited regularly. When da Gama presented his European trade goods — cloth, coral, honey — the rulers of Calicut thought they looked cheap. Compared to what Arab and Chinese merchants offered, the Portuguese gifts were not impressive at all. Still, da Gama managed to collect a small cargo of spices, including pepper and cinnamon, and he turned his ships toward home.

prompt._: According to the text, what did da Gama do after the rulers of Calicut thought his trade goods looked cheap?

choices.text: He collected a small cargo of spices.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that after the rulers were unimpressed, da Gama collected a small cargo of spices like pepper and cinnamon.

choices.text: He filled the city with silks, jewels, and spices.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the city was already a busy trade center filled with silks, jewels, and spices — da Gama had nothing to do with that.

choices.text: He presented his European trade goods.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because da Gama presented his trade goods before the rulers decided they looked cheap, not after.

choices.text: He visited merchants from Arabia, China, and East Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Arab, Chinese, and East African merchants who visited Calicut — da Gama didn't go visit them.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Handful of Spice, a Fortune in Gold

That small cargo of spices was worth sixty times the cost of the entire voyage. When the king and merchants of Lisbon added up the profits, the numbers were staggering. One successful trip to India could pay for dozens more expeditions. Spices were lightweight, easy to carry, and wildly expensive in Europe. The sea route to Asia was not just a geographic success.

For centuries, the spice trade had passed through intermediaries — middlemen, like links in a chain, who each bought goods and sold them at a higher price. Arab merchants carried spices across the Indian Ocean. Other traders moved them through the Middle East. Venetian merchants in Italy sold them across Europe. Every middleman added cost. By sailing directly to India, Portugal could buy spices at their source and skip every link in that chain.
stimulus.content_text: A Handful of Spice, a Fortune in Gold That small cargo of spices was worth sixty times the cost of the entire voyage. When the king and merchants of Lisbon added up the profits, the numbers were staggering. One successful trip to India could pay for dozens more expeditions. Spices were lightweight, easy to carry, and wildly expensive in Europe. The sea route to Asia was not just a geographic success. For centuries, the spice trade had passed through intermediaries — middlemen, like links in a chain, who each bought goods and sold them at a higher price. Arab merchants carried spices across the Indian Ocean. Other traders moved them through the Middle East. Venetian merchants in Italy sold them across Europe. Every middleman added cost. By sailing directly to India, Portugal could buy spices at their source and skip every link in that chain.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares intermediaries to 'links in a chain.' How are intermediaries and links in a chain similar?

choices.text: They are both found along ocean trading routes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison is about how intermediaries and chain links are both connected parts that pass things along, not about where they are located.

choices.text: They are both strong and difficult to break apart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about intermediaries or chain links being strong or difficult to break — that's not the comparison the author is making.

choices.text: They are both lightweight and easy to carry around
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'lightweight and easy to carry' is used in the passage to describe spices, not intermediaries or chain links.

choices.text: They are both connected parts that pass something along
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because just like links in a chain connect and pass along to the next link, each intermediary received the spices and passed them to the next trader down the line.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Portugal Builds a Trading Empire

Portugal moved fast. Portuguese fleets returned to the Indian Ocean carrying cannons and soldiers alongside their cargo. They built fortified trading posts — buildings protected by thick walls and weapons, like small castles on foreign shores — along the coasts of East Africa, India, and Southeast Asia. They used military force to control trade routes and push aside competitors. Within a few decades, tiny Portugal controlled outposts in Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and Brazil. Portuguese ships carried spices, silk, and porcelain — fine, smooth pottery prized across the world — back to Europe.

Portuguese soldiers seized ports that local people had built and controlled. European weapons overpowered communities along the African and Asian coasts. European diseases spread to populations that had no protection against them. Portugal grew rich, but the people already living in these places paid the price.
stimulus.content_text: Portugal Builds a Trading Empire Portugal moved fast. Portuguese fleets returned to the Indian Ocean carrying cannons and soldiers alongside their cargo. They built fortified trading posts — buildings protected by thick walls and weapons, like small castles on foreign shores — along the coasts of East Africa, India, and Southeast Asia. They used military force to control trade routes and push aside competitors. Within a few decades, tiny Portugal controlled outposts in Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and Brazil. Portuguese ships carried spices, silk, and porcelain — fine, smooth pottery prized across the world — back to Europe. Portuguese soldiers seized ports that local people had built and controlled. European weapons overpowered communities along the African and Asian coasts. European diseases spread to populations that had no protection against them. Portugal grew rich, but the people already living in these places paid the price.

prompt._: In the section "Portugal Builds a Trading Empire," what does the word "porcelain" mean?

choices.text: large wooden ships
choices.feedback: Large wooden ships are never defined in the passage, and nothing connects this idea to the word porcelain.

choices.text: thick walls and weapons
choices.feedback: This is the definition of 'fortified' from the passage, not porcelain — fortified means protected by thick walls and weapons.

choices.text: important ocean paths
choices.feedback: This describes 'trade routes,' which are the important ocean paths mentioned earlier in the passage, not porcelain.

choices.text: fine, smooth pottery
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage defines porcelain right after the word as 'fine, smooth pottery prized across the world.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Pepper in Your Kitchen

You will probably find a jar of black pepper. In da Gama's time, that same pepper was so rare and expensive in Europe that people locked it in safes. Da Gama's voyage connected two sides of the world by sea, and over the centuries, goods that were once impossibly rare became ordinary. The pepper on your table is ordinary now precisely because, more than five hundred years ago, a fleet of wooden ships crossed an ocean to find it.
stimulus.content_text: The Pepper in Your Kitchen You will probably find a jar of black pepper. In da Gama's time, that same pepper was so rare and expensive in Europe that people locked it in safes. Da Gama's voyage connected two sides of the world by sea, and over the centuries, goods that were once impossibly rare became ordinary. The pepper on your table is ordinary now precisely because, more than five hundred years ago, a fleet of wooden ships crossed an ocean to find it.

prompt._: Which detail does the author use as evidence to show that pepper was very expensive in the past?

choices.text: People connected two sides of the world.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because connecting two sides of the world describes the result of da Gama's voyage, not evidence about pepper's price.

choices.text: People kept the pepper in kitchen jars.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because storing pepper in kitchen jars is what people do now, which actually shows pepper has become cheap and ordinary.

choices.text: People sailed wooden ships to find it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sailing wooden ships describes how explorers traveled, not how much pepper cost.

choices.text: People locked the pepper inside safes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says people locked pepper in safes, which shows it was treated like something very valuable and expensive.

prompt._: How was the Indian Ocean trade different from what the Portuguese expected when da Gama arrived?

choices.text: The Indian Ocean only had Chinese ships sailing it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it wasn't only Chinese ships — Arab and Indian merchants were also sailing the Indian Ocean for hundreds of years.

choices.text: The Indian Ocean had no traders or merchants at all
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says the Indian Ocean was one of the busiest trade highways in the world, full of Arab, Indian, and Chinese merchants.

choices.text: The Indian Ocean trade had just recently started
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Arab, Indian, and Chinese merchants had been crossing the Indian Ocean long before any European ship appeared, meaning the trade was very old, not new.

choices.text: The Indian Ocean was already a busy trade highway
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the Indian Ocean was 'already one of the busiest trade highways in the world,' with many merchants sailing it long before the Portuguese arrived.

prompt._: Which statement best explains how the author uses cause-and-effect structure across the article?

choices.text: The author explains that a long sea voyage caused sailors to get sick, which caused the entire expedition to fail.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the voyage did not fail — despite sailors getting sick, the ships returned home with spices that earned huge profits.

choices.text: The author explains that finding a sea route to India caused huge profits, which led Portugal to build an empire.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows that finding the sea route caused massive profits, which then caused Portugal to build a trading empire with forts and soldiers across multiple continents.

choices.text: The author explains that storing pepper in safes caused Europeans to begin sailing around the coast of Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because locking pepper in safes was a result of it being rare and expensive, not a cause that led Europeans to sail around Africa.

choices.text: The author explains that cheap gifts in Calicut caused the rulers there to completely stop trade with Portugal.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the rulers of Calicut were unimpressed by Portugal's gifts, the article never says they completely stopped trading with Portugal.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best summarizes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Explorers sailed to East Africa, bought cheap goods from Arab guides, and returned home without spices.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because da Gama did return home with spices, and the Arab guide helped him sail to India — he didn't buy cheap goods from the guide.

choices.text: Sailors traveled along the African coast, fought terrible storms, and suffered from a painful sickness.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only describes the difficult parts of the journey, which is just a small detail, not the main idea of the whole article.

choices.text: Portugal built a powerful trading empire, sent explorers to find spices, and reached India by sea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the events in the wrong order — Portugal built its empire as a result of the voyage, not before it happened.

choices.text: Portugal sent explorers to find spices, reached India by sea, and built a powerful trading empire.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it follows the right order of events and captures the main idea: Portugal wanted spices, sailed to India, and then built a trading empire.

prompt._: How does the article show that the way people in Calicut viewed spices was different from the way people in Europe viewed spices?

choices.text: People in Calicut used spices as medicine, while Europeans used them only for cooking.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions anyone using spices as medicine — it only talks about spices as trade goods.

choices.text: Spices grew wild everywhere in Calicut, while Europeans had never seen spices before da Gama's voyage.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article actually says European merchants had been buying spices for centuries, so Europeans had seen spices before — they just couldn't get them easily or cheaply.

choices.text: Spices were common trade goods in Calicut, while they were rare and extremely valuable in Europe.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows spices were common in Calicut's busy markets, while in Europe they were so rare and expensive that people locked pepper in safes.

choices.text: People in Calicut did not want spices at all, while Europeans wanted them more than anything.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because people in Calicut did trade spices — they just weren't impressed by the Portuguese because they already had plenty of spices and better trade partners.

syllabus_metadata.title: Vasco da Gama Reaches India

assessments.title: Columbus Sails West

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Columbus's Great Mistake

A Sailor with a Wrong Map

Three small wooden ships rocked in the harbor of Palos, Spain, on the morning of August 3, 1492. Standing on the deck of the largest ship, an Italian captain named Christopher Columbus stared west across the Atlantic Ocean. He believed Asia waited on the other side — just a few weeks away.

Columbus was born around 1451 in Genoa, a busy port city in Italy. He went to sea as a teenager and spent years sailing the Mediterranean and the Atlantic coasts. He studied maps and read the writings of ancient geographers who had measured the size of the Earth. Here's the surprising part: educated Europeans in Columbus's time already knew the Earth was round. Scholars had understood the Earth's shape for centuries. How far west would a ship have to sail to reach Asia?

Columbus did the math — and got it badly wrong. He believed the Atlantic Ocean was narrow enough to cross in a few weeks. They calculated that the true distance was far too great for any ship to carry enough food and water. Columbus had no idea that two entire continents — North and South America — sat between Europe and Asia.
stimulus.content_text: Columbus's Great MistakeA Sailor with a Wrong Map Three small wooden ships rocked in the harbor of Palos, Spain, on the morning of August 3, 1492. Standing on the deck of the largest ship, an Italian captain named Christopher Columbus stared west across the Atlantic Ocean. He believed Asia waited on the other side — just a few weeks away. Columbus was born around 1451 in Genoa, a busy port city in Italy. He went to sea as a teenager and spent years sailing the Mediterranean and the Atlantic coasts. He studied maps and read the writings of ancient geographers who had measured the size of the Earth. Here's the surprising part: educated Europeans in Columbus's time already knew the Earth was round. Scholars had understood the Earth's shape for centuries. How far west would a ship have to sail to reach Asia? Columbus did the math — and got it badly wrong. He believed the Atlantic Ocean was narrow enough to cross in a few weeks. They calculated that the true distance was far too great for any ship to carry enough food and water. Columbus had no idea that two entire continents — North and South America — sat between Europe and Asia.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Columbus believe about the Atlantic Ocean, in contrast to what others calculated?

choices.text: He believed it was too wide for a ship to carry enough food.
choices.feedback: This is actually what other people calculated, not what Columbus believed — Columbus thought the opposite, that the ocean was easy to cross.

choices.text: He believed it was large enough to take many years to cross.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what Columbus believed — he thought the Atlantic was narrow and quick to cross, not that it would take many years.

choices.text: He believed it was small enough to cross in just a few days.
choices.feedback: This is close but tricky — the passage says Columbus thought it would take a few *weeks*, not a few *days*, so this answer changes an important detail.

choices.text: He believed it was narrow enough to cross in a few weeks.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses almost these exact words: Columbus 'believed the Atlantic Ocean was narrow enough to cross in a few weeks.'

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Finding a Sponsor

Columbus needed money, ships, and crew. He could not pay for a voyage like this on his own, so he asked a monarch — a king or queen who rules a country — to sponsor his trip. The king of Portugal said no. His advisors had done the math correctly and knew the voyage seemed impossible.

Queen Isabella and King Ferdinand listened carefully. Isabella was excited by the idea of reaching Asia and spreading Christianity to new lands. After years of discussion, she agreed to give Columbus three ships and supplies. Those ships were the Niña, the Pinta, and the Santa María.
stimulus.content_text: Finding a Sponsor Columbus needed money, ships, and crew. He could not pay for a voyage like this on his own, so he asked a monarch — a king or queen who rules a country — to sponsor his trip. The king of Portugal said no. His advisors had done the math correctly and knew the voyage seemed impossible. Queen Isabella and King Ferdinand listened carefully. Isabella was excited by the idea of reaching Asia and spreading Christianity to new lands. After years of discussion, she agreed to give Columbus three ships and supplies. Those ships were the Niña, the Pinta, and the Santa María.

prompt._: Based on the text, why did the king of Portugal refuse to sponsor Columbus's voyage?

choices.text: Because he did not have enough money for the crew.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never mentions the king of Portugal having a money problem; that detail simply isn't in the text.

choices.text: Because his advisors thought the trip was impossible.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly states that Portugal's advisors did the math and believed the voyage seemed impossible, which is why the king said no.

choices.text: Because he wanted to spread his religion to Asia.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — spreading religion to Asia was Queen Isabella's motivation for agreeing to sponsor Columbus, not something connected to Portugal's refusal.

choices.text: Because his advisors needed years to discuss the plan.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — it was Queen Isabella and King Ferdinand who spent years discussing Columbus's plan before agreeing, not the king of Portugal.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Weeks on Open Water

Day after day, the crew saw nothing but water and sky. Columbus urged them to keep going, promising land was close.

On October 12, 1492, a lookout on the Pinta finally spotted a dark line on the horizon — land. Columbus and his men rowed ashore on a small island in what we now call the Bahamas. The Taíno people, who had lived on these islands for generations, came to meet the strangers. Columbus called them "Indians" because he believed he had landed in the East Indies, a chain of islands near Southeast Asia.

The Taíno were among the indigenous peoples of the Americas — the original inhabitants of a place, the way your family might be the first to build a house on an empty lot. Indigenous peoples had been living across the Americas for at least 15,000 years. Columbus was not even the first European to reach these shores. Nearly 500 years earlier, Norse explorer Leif Erikson had sailed to the coast of present-day Canada.

Columbus called them "Indians" because he believed he had landed in the East Indies, a chain of islands near Southeast Asia. The Taíno were among the indigenous peoples of the Americas — the original inhabitants of a place, the way your family might be the first to build a house on an empty lot. Indigenous peoples had been living across the Americas for at least 15,000 years. Columbus was not even the first European to reach these shores. Nearly 500 years earlier, Norse explorer Leif Erikson had sailed to the coast of present-day Canada.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened next after a lookout on the Pinta spotted land?

choices.text: Columbus named the people Indians.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Columbus calling the people 'Indians' happened even later in the story, not right after land was spotted.

choices.text: Columbus and his men rowed ashore.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that after land was spotted, Columbus and his men rowed ashore.

choices.text: The Taíno people came to meet them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Taíno coming to meet them happened after Columbus and his men had already rowed ashore.

choices.text: The crew saw only water and sky.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because seeing only water and sky happened before the land was spotted, not after.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Four Voyages, One Stubborn Belief

Columbus made four voyages across the Atlantic between 1492 and 1504. He explored Cuba, Hispaniola (the island now shared by Haiti and the Dominican Republic), Jamaica, Puerto Rico, and the coast of Central and South America. Yet through all four voyages, Columbus insisted he had reached the edges of Asia. He died in 1506 still believing it.
stimulus.content_text: Four Voyages, One Stubborn Belief Columbus made four voyages across the Atlantic between 1492 and 1504. He explored Cuba, Hispaniola (the island now shared by Haiti and the Dominican Republic), Jamaica, Puerto Rico, and the coast of Central and South America. Yet through all four voyages, Columbus insisted he had reached the edges of Asia. He died in 1506 still believing it.

prompt._: In the passage, the word 'insisted' is used to describe Columbus's belief about reaching Asia. How is the meaning of 'insisted' similar to the meaning of 'stubborn' as used in the heading 'Four Voyages, One Stubborn Belief'?

choices.text: Both words suggest Columbus refused to change his mind
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both words describe how Columbus refused to change his mind, even after four voyages showed he had not reached Asia.

choices.text: Both words suggest Columbus made an important discovery
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither 'insisted' nor 'stubborn' has anything to do with making a discovery — they are both about stubbornly sticking to a belief.

choices.text: Both words suggest Columbus traveled to many places
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because traveling to many places describes Columbus's actions, not the meaning of 'insisted' or 'stubborn.'

choices.text: Both words suggest Columbus felt unsure about his ideas
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'insisted' and 'stubborn' both mean the opposite of being unsure — they mean being very fixed and certain in your belief.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Came After

Columbus's voyages had enormous consequences — some exciting for Europeans and many devastating for the people already living in the Americas. For European kingdoms, the voyages opened a world of territory and resources. For the Taíno and countless other indigenous peoples, they marked the beginning of a catastrophe — a terrible disaster, like a storm that destroys an entire town. European explorers and settlers who followed Columbus brought deadly diseases, violence, and slavery that wiped out huge numbers of native people across the Western Hemisphere.

Columbus himself ruled the colony on Hispaniola with cruelty. He enslaved Taíno people and punished them harshly for small offenses. Even people in his own time thought he went too far. Spanish officials arrested him and sent him back to Spain in chains, though he was later released.
stimulus.content_text: What Came After Columbus's voyages had enormous consequences — some exciting for Europeans and many devastating for the people already living in the Americas. For European kingdoms, the voyages opened a world of territory and resources. For the Taíno and countless other indigenous peoples, they marked the beginning of a catastrophe — a terrible disaster, like a storm that destroys an entire town. European explorers and settlers who followed Columbus brought deadly diseases, violence, and slavery that wiped out huge numbers of native people across the Western Hemisphere. Columbus himself ruled the colony on Hispaniola with cruelty. He enslaved Taíno people and punished them harshly for small offenses. Even people in his own time thought he went too far. Spanish officials arrested him and sent him back to Spain in chains, though he was later released.

prompt._: In the section 'What Came After,' what does the word catastrophe mean?

choices.text: a new colony started by settlers
choices.feedback: A new colony started by settlers is something mentioned in the passage, but it has nothing to do with the meaning of 'catastrophe.'

choices.text: a terrible disaster that destroys things
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly explains that a catastrophe is 'a terrible disaster, like a storm that destroys an entire town.'

choices.text: a long journey across the ocean
choices.feedback: A long journey across the ocean describes Columbus's voyages, not what the word 'catastrophe' means.

choices.text: a harsh punishment given by a ruler
choices.feedback: A harsh punishment given by a ruler describes how Columbus treated the Taíno people, but that is not what the word 'catastrophe' means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Legacy That Still Stirs Debate

A person's legacy is what they leave behind — like footprints in wet concrete that harden and last long after the person walks away. Columbus's legacy is deeply controversial, meaning people strongly disagree about how to judge it. He was a daring sailor who crossed an ocean no European had crossed before. But his arrival also triggered waves of colonization and suffering that lasted for centuries. Today, some cities celebrate Columbus Day while others have renamed it Indigenous Peoples' Day. That single change tells you something: history does not sit still. Every generation looks back at the same facts and asks new questions about what they mean.

But his arrival also triggered waves of colonization and suffering that lasted for centuries. Today, some cities celebrate Columbus Day while others have renamed it Indigenous Peoples' Day. That single change tells you something: history does not sit still. Every generation looks back at the same facts and asks new questions about what they mean.

prompt._: Which detail does the author use as evidence to show that Columbus's legacy is controversial?

choices.text: Some cities have renamed the holiday that celebrates him.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the author specifically points to cities renaming Columbus Day as evidence that people strongly disagree about how to judge Columbus's legacy.

choices.text: Every generation looks back at the exact same historical facts.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says generations ask *new* questions about history, which actually supports the idea of controversy rather than serving as evidence of it.

choices.text: A person's legacy is like footprints left in wet concrete.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the wet concrete comparison is a metaphor to help explain what a legacy is, not evidence of debate about Columbus.

choices.text: He crossed an ocean that no European had crossed before.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because crossing an uncharted ocean shows Columbus was a daring explorer, not that his legacy is controversial.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between what Columbus believed and what the article explains was true?

choices.text: Columbus thought Portugal would help him, but Spain gave him only one ship.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Spain gave Columbus three ships, not one, and the passage doesn't say Columbus expected Portugal to fund him.

choices.text: Columbus thought the Taíno were the first people there, but Leif Erikson arrived later.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Columbus called the Taíno 'Indians' thinking he was near Asia, not because he thought they were first, and Leif Erikson came before Columbus, not after.

choices.text: Columbus thought he had reached Asia, but he had landed on islands in the Americas.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly contrasts Columbus believing he reached Asia with the truth that he had landed on islands in the Americas.

choices.text: Columbus thought educated Europeans believed the Earth was flat, but they knew it was round.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says educated Europeans already knew the Earth was round — Columbus didn't think they believed it was flat.

prompt._: Why did Columbus mistakenly call the Taíno people "Indians" when he arrived in the Americas?

choices.text: Because he calculated the ocean's size incorrectly and thought he was in Asia.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Columbus thought he had reached the East Indies near Asia due to his math mistake about the ocean's size, so he called the people he met 'Indians.'

choices.text: Because he knew Norse explorers had already visited those specific island shores.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Columbus had no knowledge of Leif Erikson's earlier voyages, and that information played no role in what Columbus called the Taíno people.

choices.text: Because he needed to prove to the Spanish queen that the Earth was round.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that educated Europeans already understood the Earth was round long before Columbus sailed, so proving that was never his goal.

choices.text: Because he wanted to spread his religion to the new people living there.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because spreading Christianity was Queen Isabella's motivation for funding the trip, not the reason Columbus named the Taíno people 'Indians.'

prompt._: According to the article, what is the correct order of groups who lived in or traveled to the Americas, from earliest to latest?

choices.text: The Taíno people, Christopher Columbus, Leif Erikson, other European explorers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it places Columbus before Leif Erikson, but the article clearly states Erikson sailed to the Americas nearly 500 years before Columbus made his voyage in 1492.

choices.text: The Taíno people, Leif Erikson, Christopher Columbus, other European explorers
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows the Taíno were there first (15,000+ years), then Leif Erikson (about 500 years before Columbus), then Columbus in 1492, and then other European explorers followed after his voyages.

choices.text: Christopher Columbus, the Taíno people, Leif Erikson, other European explorers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts Columbus first, but both the Taíno people and Leif Erikson were in the Americas long before Columbus ever set sail.

choices.text: Leif Erikson, the Taíno people, Christopher Columbus, other European explorers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts Leif Erikson before the Taíno people, but the article says indigenous peoples like the Taíno had been living in the Americas for at least 15,000 years — way before Erikson arrived.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Columbus's voyages helped explorers map the entire world but stopped indigenous peoples from sailing oceans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Columbus mapped the whole world, and it never says indigenous peoples were prevented from sailing oceans.

choices.text: Columbus's voyages allowed the king of Portugal to gain riches but forced Queen Isabella to lose her ships.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the king of Portugal actually said no to Columbus, and Queen Isabella willingly gave him ships — she didn't lose them.

choices.text: Columbus's voyages brought wealth to European kingdoms but caused terrible disasters for native peoples.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it captures both major points the article makes — the benefits for Europeans and the devastating harm to native peoples.

choices.text: Columbus's voyages proved that the Earth was round but failed to provide enough food and water for the crew.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that educated Europeans already knew the Earth was round long before Columbus, so his voyages didn't prove that.

syllabus_metadata.title: Columbus Sails West

assessments.title: Magellan's Voyage Around the World

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Magellan's Voyage: Around the World the Hard Way

Why Spices Were Worth Dying For and How Magellan Got His Chance

In the early 1500s, spices like cloves, pepper, and cinnamon were wildly expensive in Europe. Spices preserved food, flavored bland meals, and masked the taste of meat going bad. European merchants and kings wanted a faster, cheaper route to get them from Southeast Asia's Spice Islands.

By this time, Europeans knew that Columbus had not reached Asia. A huge landmass — the Americas — blocked the western route. But was there a way through? A Portuguese sailor named Ferdinand Magellan believed the answer was yes.

Magellan asked the king of Portugal to pay for his expedition. The king refused. So Magellan went to Spain instead. King Charles I of Spain agreed to sponsor the voyage, hoping Spain could claim a new trade route to the Spice Islands. Circumnavigation only happened because the crew had no other way home.

In 1519, Magellan left Spain with five ships and about 270 men. The fleet crossed the Atlantic Ocean to South America, then turned south along the coast. Magellan checked every inlet and river opening, searching for a passage through the continent.
stimulus.content_text: Magellan's Voyage: Around the World the Hard WayWhy Spices Were Worth Dying For and How Magellan Got His Chance In the early 1500s, spices like cloves, pepper, and cinnamon were wildly expensive in Europe. Spices preserved food, flavored bland meals, and masked the taste of meat going bad. European merchants and kings wanted a faster, cheaper route to get them from Southeast Asia's Spice Islands. By this time, Europeans knew that Columbus had not reached Asia. A huge landmass — the Americas — blocked the western route. But was there a way through? A Portuguese sailor named Ferdinand Magellan believed the answer was yes. Magellan asked the king of Portugal to pay for his expedition. The king refused. So Magellan went to Spain instead. King Charles I of Spain agreed to sponsor the voyage, hoping Spain could claim a new trade route to the Spice Islands. Circumnavigation only happened because the crew had no other way home. In 1519, Magellan left Spain with five ships and about 270 men. The fleet crossed the Atlantic Ocean to South America, then turned south along the coast. Magellan checked every inlet and river opening, searching for a passage through the continent.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the king of Spain do differently than the king of Portugal when Magellan asked for help?

choices.text: He agreed to sponsor the trip.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that King Charles I of Spain 'agreed to sponsor the voyage,' unlike the king of Portugal who refused.

choices.text: He sailed to the Spice Islands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the king of Spain never sailed to the Spice Islands — he stayed home and just paid for the trip.

choices.text: He crossed the Atlantic Ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Magellan and his crew who crossed the Atlantic Ocean, not the king of Spain.

choices.text: He searched for a new passage.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Magellan was the one searching for a passage through South America, not the king of Spain.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Mutiny, Ice, and a Winding Channel

The journey south was brutal. Temperatures dropped. Food supplies shrank. One ship smashed apart in a storm. Some crew members mutinied — they rebelled against Magellan's orders, with swords drawn. Magellan crushed the rebellion by force and kept going.

Finally, in October 1520, he found it: a narrow, twisting water channel at the southern tip of South America. This passage is called a strait — a thin strip of sea between two pieces of land, like a hallway connecting two rooms. Today we call it the Strait of Magellan. The passage was treacherous, meaning extremely dangerous. Sharp rocks hid beneath the surface. Currents pulled ships in unexpected directions. Fierce winds screamed through the channel. It took 38 days to get through. During the crossing, one ship turned around and fled back to Spain.

Three ships made it out the other side. They entered a vast, calm ocean. After the violent strait, the water seemed so peaceful that Magellan named it the Pacific Ocean. "Pacific" means "peaceful." But this peaceful ocean nearly killed them all.
stimulus.content_text: Mutiny, Ice, and a Winding Channel The journey south was brutal. Temperatures dropped. Food supplies shrank. One ship smashed apart in a storm. Some crew members mutinied — they rebelled against Magellan's orders, with swords drawn. Magellan crushed the rebellion by force and kept going. Finally, in October 1520, he found it: a narrow, twisting water channel at the southern tip of South America. This passage is called a strait — a thin strip of sea between two pieces of land, like a hallway connecting two rooms. Today we call it the Strait of Magellan. The passage was treacherous, meaning extremely dangerous. Sharp rocks hid beneath the surface. Currents pulled ships in unexpected directions. Fierce winds screamed through the channel. It took 38 days to get through. During the crossing, one ship turned around and fled back to Spain. Three ships made it out the other side. They entered a vast, calm ocean. After the violent strait, the water seemed so peaceful that Magellan named it the Pacific Ocean. "Pacific" means "peaceful." But this peaceful ocean nearly killed them all.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Magellan name the ocean he entered the 'Pacific Ocean'?

choices.text: because the crew found peaceful islands with fresh food
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions the crew finding peaceful islands with fresh food when they named the ocean — in fact, it says the peaceful ocean 'nearly killed them all.'

choices.text: because the ocean was the largest one his crew had ever seen
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about the ocean being the largest Magellan had ever seen — that detail simply isn't there.

choices.text: because the water seemed so calm after the dangerous strait
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says the water seemed peaceful after the violent strait, and that 'Pacific' means 'peaceful,' which is exactly why Magellan chose that name.

choices.text: because the ocean helped the crew feel safe from mutiny
choices.feedback: The mutiny happened before Magellan even reached the ocean, and the passage never connects the ocean's name to the mutiny at all.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Rats, Sawdust, and Leather for Dinner

The sailors chewed on strips of leather peeled from the ship's ropes. They caught rats scurrying below deck and ate them. They mixed sawdust into water and swallowed it down. For more than three months, the crew drifted across an ocean so enormous that no European had ever imagined it. Men died of scurvy — a painful disease caused by not eating fresh fruits or vegetables. Others simply starved. And the terrible thing? Reaching this ocean had been the goal all along.
stimulus.content_text: Rats, Sawdust, and Leather for Dinner The sailors chewed on strips of leather peeled from the ship's ropes. They caught rats scurrying below deck and ate them. They mixed sawdust into water and swallowed it down. For more than three months, the crew drifted across an ocean so enormous that no European had ever imagined it. Men died of scurvy — a painful disease caused by not eating fresh fruits or vegetables. Others simply starved. And the terrible thing? Reaching this ocean had been the goal all along.

prompt._: According to the text, what sequence of steps did the sailors follow to eat the sawdust?

choices.text: They cooked the sawdust with fresh fruit and then ate it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sailors had no fresh fruit — the whole passage explains they were starving and getting sick from not having food like fruit.

choices.text: They peeled the sawdust from the ropes and then chewed it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because peeling from ropes describes what they did with leather, not sawdust.

choices.text: They swallowed the dry sawdust and then drank some water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sailors mixed the sawdust into water first before swallowing it — they didn't swallow it dry.

choices.text: They mixed the sawdust into water and then swallowed it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says exactly this: 'They mixed sawdust into water and swallowed it down.'

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Death in the Philippines and the End of Magellan's Journey

That enormous Pacific crossing — more than three months with almost no land in sight — left the fleet shattered. When the surviving sailors finally reached the Philippines in March 1521, they were overjoyed. Magellan traded with the local people and tried to convince them to become Christians. This effort led to conflict. On April 27, 1521, Magellan was killed in a battle against a local chief named Lapu-Lapu. The man who had led the expedition across two oceans would never finish the voyage himself.
stimulus.content_text: Death in the Philippines and the End of Magellan's Journey That enormous Pacific crossing — more than three months with almost no land in sight — left the fleet shattered. When the surviving sailors finally reached the Philippines in March 1521, they were overjoyed. Magellan traded with the local people and tried to convince them to become Christians. This effort led to conflict. On April 27, 1521, Magellan was killed in a battle against a local chief named Lapu-Lapu. The man who had led the expedition across two oceans would never finish the voyage himself.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how were Magellan's efforts to trade and his efforts to spread Christianity similar?

choices.text: Both efforts prevented a dangerous battle with the chief.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says these efforts actually led to conflict and a battle, not the prevention of one.

choices.text: Both efforts involved interacting with the local people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that both trading and spreading Christianity involved Magellan interacting directly with the local people in the Philippines.

choices.text: Both efforts helped the shattered fleet cross the ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the fleet crossed the ocean before arriving in the Philippines, so trading and spreading Christianity had nothing to do with that crossing.

choices.text: Both efforts caused the surviving sailors to be overjoyed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sailors were overjoyed about finally reaching land after the long ocean crossing, not because of trading or spreading Christianity.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Eighteen Survivors and the Price of Knowledge

After Magellan's death, the crew had already lost many men to starvation, disease, and battle. Only a small fraction of the original 270 remained. They pressed on to the Spice Islands, filled their ships with valuable cloves, and headed home. Of the original five ships, only one — the Victoria — completed the journey. It arrived back in Spain on September 6, 1522, about three years after leaving. Just 18 of the original 270 crew members walked off the ship.

Those 18 men had completed a circumnavigation — a full trip around the entire Earth, the way your finger can trace a complete circle around a globe. Educated Europeans did believe the Earth was round before Columbus, but nobody had demonstrated it by traveling all the way around. Magellan's crew were the first. Their voyage proved that every ocean on Earth connects and showed Europeans just how gigantic the Pacific is.

Open your kitchen cabinet. You'll probably find pepper, cinnamon, maybe some cloves. They cost a few dollars. Five hundred years ago, those same spices were so valuable that 270 men sailed into unknown waters to find them. Most never came back. Magellan himself died far from home in the Philippines, and no known grave marks his resting place — yet the ocean he named still carries the word he gave it: Pacific.

Just 18 of the original 270 crew members walked off the ship. prompt._: According to the text, how does the author help the reader understand the definition of the word "circumnavigation"?

choices.text: By listing the names of all five original ships
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only names one ship, the Victoria, and never lists all five ships.

choices.text: By comparing it to tracing a circle on a globe
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author directly compares circumnavigation to tracing a circle around a globe, which makes the meaning of the word easy to understand.

choices.text: By explaining the high cost of valuable spices
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because explaining how expensive spices were is about why the voyage happened, not about what circumnavigation means.

choices.text: By describing how the Pacific Ocean was named
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Pacific Ocean's name is mentioned as a fun fact at the end of the passage, not to explain the word circumnavigation.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the main difference between spices today and spices in the early 1500s?

choices.text: Today spices cost just a few dollars, but in the 1500s they were wildly expensive.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states spices were 'wildly expensive' in the 1500s, and then tells us those same spices 'cost a few dollars' today.

choices.text: Today spices are used to cure scurvy, but in the 1500s they were used to preserve meat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says scurvy is caused by not eating fresh fruits or vegetables, and spices were never described as a cure for it.

choices.text: Today spices come from South America, but in the 1500s they came from the Spice Islands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says spices came from Southeast Asia's Spice Islands in the 1500s, and never mentions South America as a source of spices today.

choices.text: Today spices are wildly expensive to buy, but in the 1500s they cost just a few dollars.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the facts flipped — spices were wildly expensive in the 1500s, not today, when they only cost a few dollars.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused only 18 of the original 270 crew members to complete the voyage around the world?

choices.text: Magellan sent most of the crew back to Spain on the other ships
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Magellan never sent most of the crew back to Spain — only one ship turned around on its own during the dangerous strait crossing.

choices.text: Dangers like starvation, disease, and battles killed most of the crew
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that men died from starvation, disease, and battle throughout the voyage, leaving only 18 survivors out of 270.

choices.text: Most sailors chose to stay behind and live in the Spice Islands
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says any sailors decided to stay and live in the Spice Islands.

choices.text: The crew split up to explore different routes across the Pacific Ocean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions the crew splitting up to explore different routes across the Pacific.

prompt._: How does the author organize the overall information about Magellan's expedition?

choices.text: By comparing Magellan's long journey to Columbus's earlier journey.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Columbus is only mentioned once to explain that Europeans knew the Americas blocked the western route — the article never really compares his journey to Magellan's.

choices.text: By explaining the problems the crew faced and how they solved them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though the crew faced many problems like storms, starvation, and mutiny, the author doesn't organize the article around how those problems were solved.

choices.text: By describing the voyage's events in the order they happened.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author follows the voyage step by step in time order, using dates like 1519, October 1520, March 1521, and September 1522 to show what happened first, next, and last.

choices.text: By listing the causes of the sailors' deaths from worst to least.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never ranks the causes of sailors' deaths in any kind of order from worst to least.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the beginning and ending of the article to show its main idea?

choices.text: Both parts show that Magellan's trip was mostly a peaceful success for everyone involved.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the voyage was extremely dangerous and deadly — most of the 270 men never made it home.

choices.text: The beginning shows that Europe already knew all oceans connected, and the ending repeats that same idea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Europeans did NOT already know all oceans connected — Magellan's crew were the first to prove it.

choices.text: The beginning shows that Magellan wanted to prove the Earth was round, and the ending shows that he proved it himself.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Magellan's goal was to find a spice trade route, not to prove the Earth was round, and he died before the journey was completed.

choices.text: The beginning shows why spices were worth chasing, and the ending shows the huge human cost of getting them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article opens with how valuable spices were and closes by showing the terrible human price paid to get them, which is the article's main idea.

assessments.title: Conquistadors: Cortes and the Aztecs

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Fall of Tenochtitlan: How the Aztec Empire Ended

A City on a Lake

Picture a city built on an island in the middle of a lake. Stone bridges called causeways stretch across the water, connecting the island to the shore. Fresh water flows through stone channels called aqueducts into homes and fountains. Huge pyramids rise above busy markets where tens of thousands of people trade chocolate, gold, and jaguar skins every day. This was Tenochtitlan, the capital of the Aztec Empire, and in 1519, it was larger than any city in Spain.

Tenochtitlan was one of the most impressive cities on Earth. Their astronomers tracked the stars and built a calendar as accurate as any in Europe.

But in 1519, a Spanish soldier named Hernán Cortés landed on the coast of Mexico with about 600 men, a few cannons, and sixteen horses. Within two years, the great city would be destroyed.

Stone bridges called causeways stretch across the water, connecting the island to the shore. Fresh water flows through stone channels called aqueducts into homes and fountains. Huge pyramids rise above busy markets where tens of thousands of people trade chocolate, gold, and jaguar skins every day. This was Tenochtitlan, the capital of the Aztec Empire, and in 1519, it was larger than any city in Spain. Tenochtitlan was one of the most impressive cities on Earth. Their astronomers tracked the stars and built a calendar as accurate as any in Europe. But in 1519, a Spanish soldier named Hernán Cortés landed on the coast of Mexico with about 600 men, a few cannons, and sixteen horses. Within two years, the great city would be destroyed.

prompt._: According to the text, which detail shows a contrast between Tenochtitlan and cities in Spain in 1519?

choices.text: Tenochtitlan had stone bridges
choices.feedback: Stone bridges are mentioned as a feature of Tenochtitlan, but the passage never compares them to anything in Spain.

choices.text: Tenochtitlan had fresh water
choices.feedback: Fresh water flowing through aqueducts is a cool detail, but the passage doesn't use it to compare Tenochtitlan to Spanish cities.

choices.text: Tenochtitlan was larger
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Tenochtitlan 'was larger than any city in Spain,' which is a direct contrast between the two.

choices.text: Tenochtitlan had busy markets
choices.feedback: Having busy markets is mentioned in the passage, but the text never says anything about comparing Tenochtitlan's markets to markets in Spain.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Enemies of the Aztecs

Cortés was outnumbered thousands to one. His tiny army could never defeat the Aztec Empire alone. Here's the surprising part: he didn't have to. The Aztecs had conquered many neighboring peoples and forced them to pay tribute — regular payments of food, gold, or goods, like a bully demanding lunch money from every kid in school. The Aztecs also demanded human sacrifices from these conquered groups.

These indigenous peoples — the native groups who had lived in Mexico long before any Europeans arrived — hated Aztec rule. When Cortés promised to help overthrow the Aztecs, thousands of indigenous warriors joined him. By the final battle, indigenous allies far outnumbered Spanish soldiers in Cortés's forces. The fall of the Aztec Empire was not simply a Spanish victory. It was a rebellion by many peoples, with the Spanish leading the charge.
stimulus.content_text: Enemies of the Aztecs Cortés was outnumbered thousands to one. His tiny army could never defeat the Aztec Empire alone. Here's the surprising part: he didn't have to. The Aztecs had conquered many neighboring peoples and forced them to pay tribute — regular payments of food, gold, or goods, like a bully demanding lunch money from every kid in school. The Aztecs also demanded human sacrifices from these conquered groups. These indigenous peoples — the native groups who had lived in Mexico long before any Europeans arrived — hated Aztec rule. When Cortés promised to help overthrow the Aztecs, thousands of indigenous warriors joined him. By the final battle, indigenous allies far outnumbered Spanish soldiers in Cortés's forces. The fall of the Aztec Empire was not simply a Spanish victory. It was a rebellion by many peoples, with the Spanish leading the charge.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that the Aztecs' cruel actions caused neighboring groups to rebel against them?

choices.text: Cortés's tiny army could never defeat the powerful Aztec Empire alone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it talks about how small Cortés's army was, which doesn't explain why neighboring peoples were angry at the Aztecs.

choices.text: The Spanish soldiers led the charge against the Aztec Empire's forces.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes who led the attack, not what caused the neighboring peoples to turn against the Aztecs.

choices.text: These native groups had lived in Mexico long before Europeans arrived.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only tells us that native groups lived in Mexico before Europeans, which has nothing to do with the Aztecs' cruel treatment of them.

choices.text: The Aztecs also demanded human sacrifices from these conquered groups.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because demanding human sacrifices is a specific cruel action by the Aztecs that directly supports why conquered peoples wanted to rebel against them.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Steel, Horses, and Gunpowder

Cortés also had weapons the Aztecs had never seen. Spanish soldiers carried steel swords and wore metal armor. Aztec warriors fought bravely with wooden clubs edged in obsidian — a volcanic glass sharper than a surgeon's knife but far more brittle than steel. One hard blow against a steel breastplate could shatter an obsidian blade.

Horses terrified indigenous soldiers who had never seen such animals. And the blast of cannons and muskets created panic on the battlefield. These weapons did not make the Spanish unbeatable, but they gave Cortés an edge in close combat.
stimulus.content_text: Steel, Horses, and Gunpowder Cortés also had weapons the Aztecs had never seen. Spanish soldiers carried steel swords and wore metal armor. Aztec warriors fought bravely with wooden clubs edged in obsidian — a volcanic glass sharper than a surgeon's knife but far more brittle than steel. One hard blow against a steel breastplate could shatter an obsidian blade. Horses terrified indigenous soldiers who had never seen such animals. And the blast of cannons and muskets created panic on the battlefield. These weapons did not make the Spanish unbeatable, but they gave Cortés an edge in close combat.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happened when an obsidian blade hit a steel breastplate?

choices.text: The obsidian blade shattered
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that one hard blow against a steel breastplate could shatter an obsidian blade, since obsidian is brittle.

choices.text: The obsidian blade cut through it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because obsidian could not cut through steel — the passage explains that obsidian was far more brittle than steel, meaning it would break before cutting through.

choices.text: The steel breastplate bent inward
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the breastplate bent inward — steel armor was strong enough to withstand the blow without bending.

choices.text: The steel breastplate cracked apart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the obsidian blade shattered, not the steel breastplate — steel was stronger and stayed intact.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Montezuma's Choice

The Aztec emperor, Montezuma, ruled over millions of people. When he heard that strangers had arrived from the east, he faced a difficult decision. An old Aztec prophecy said the feathered serpent god Quetzalcoatl would one day return from the direction of the rising sun — exactly where Cortés had appeared.

It's easy to assume Montezuma was foolish for welcoming the Spanish into his capital. But think about it from his side. He was responsible for millions of lives. He didn't know if these strangers were gods, traders, or enemies. He chose diplomacy — using careful conversation and negotiation instead of weapons, the way a principal might talk to a stranger at the school door before deciding whether to call the police. Montezuma invited the Spanish in as honored guests while he figured out what they wanted.

Cortés, however, did not come in peace. He took Montezuma prisoner and tried to control the empire through him. When the Aztecs finally fought back, a bloody battle erupted, and Cortés and his men barely escaped the city in a desperate nighttime retreat.
