An old Aztec prophecy said the feathered serpent god Quetzalcoatl would one day return from the direction of the rising sun — exactly where Cortés had appeared. It's easy to assume Montezuma was foolish for welcoming the Spanish into his capital. But think about it from his side. He was responsible for millions of lives. He didn't know if these strangers were gods, traders, or enemies. He chose diplomacy — using careful conversation and negotiation instead of weapons, the way a principal might talk to a stranger at the school door before deciding whether to call the police. Montezuma invited the Spanish in as honored guests while he figured out what they wanted. Cortés, however, did not come in peace. He took Montezuma prisoner and tried to control the empire through him. When the Aztecs finally fought back, a bloody battle erupted, and Cortés and his men barely escaped the city in a desperate nighttime retreat.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is Montezuma's way of handling the strangers compared to a principal's way of handling a stranger at school?

choices.text: Both used weapons to protect the people they were responsible for.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says Montezuma chose conversation and negotiation *instead of* weapons, and the principal talks to the stranger rather than using force.

choices.text: Both invited the strangers to stay forever as honored guests.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the principal does not invite strangers to stay forever, and Montezuma only welcomed the Spanish temporarily while he figured out who they were.

choices.text: Both already knew the strangers were dangerous enemies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither Montezuma nor the principal knew yet whether the strangers were dangerous — that uncertainty is the whole reason they chose to talk first.

choices.text: Both tried to talk and learn more before taking action.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly compares Montezuma's diplomacy to a principal talking to a stranger at the door — both are trying to learn more before deciding what to do.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Invisible Killer

But the deadliest weapon was one nobody could see. European diseases like smallpox had been common in Europe for centuries. The Aztecs' immune systems — the body's built-in defenses against sickness, like an invisible army inside your blood — had never encountered these germs. Smallpox swept through Tenochtitlan, killing emperors' advisors, soldiers, and ordinary families by the thousands. Some historians believe disease killed more Aztecs than any sword or cannon.

With the city weakened, Cortés regrouped, gathered even more indigenous allies, and surrounded Tenochtitlan in a siege — cutting it off from food and fresh water the way you might trap an ant colony by blocking every tunnel. The siege lasted months. In August 1521, Tenochtitlan fell.
stimulus.content_text: The Invisible Killer But the deadliest weapon was one nobody could see. European diseases like smallpox had been common in Europe for centuries. The Aztecs' immune systems — the body's built-in defenses against sickness, like an invisible army inside your blood — had never encountered these germs. Smallpox swept through Tenochtitlan, killing emperors' advisors, soldiers, and ordinary families by the thousands. Some historians believe disease killed more Aztecs than any sword or cannon. With the city weakened, Cortés regrouped, gathered even more indigenous allies, and surrounded Tenochtitlan in a siege — cutting it off from food and fresh water the way you might trap an ant colony by blocking every tunnel. The siege lasted months. In August 1521, Tenochtitlan fell.

prompt._: According to the text, what is an immune system?

choices.text: a group of soldiers who protect a city
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because soldiers protecting a city relates to the siege of Tenochtitlan, not to what an immune system does.

choices.text: an invisible army that fights in battles
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'invisible army' is just a comparison the author uses to help explain the immune system — it's not the actual definition.

choices.text: the body's built-in defenses against sickness
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines immune system as 'the body's built-in defenses against sickness.'

choices.text: a type of European disease that spread quickly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because smallpox is a European disease mentioned in the passage, not what an immune system is.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What Rose from the Ruins

After the conquest, Cortés's men melted Aztec gold, smashed temples, and destroyed artworks that had taken generations to create. The Spanish built a new colonial capital on top of the ruins and forced their language, religion, and government onto the conquered people.

That colonial capital grew into the city you know as Mexico City, the capital of modern Mexico. Beneath its streets and buildings lie the buried stones of Tenochtitlan — a city of floating gardens and stone aqueducts that once amazed even the soldiers who destroyed it.
stimulus.content_text: What Rose from the Ruins After the conquest, Cortés's men melted Aztec gold, smashed temples, and destroyed artworks that had taken generations to create. The Spanish built a new colonial capital on top of the ruins and forced their language, religion, and government onto the conquered people. That colonial capital grew into the city you know as Mexico City, the capital of modern Mexico. Beneath its streets and buildings lie the buried stones of Tenochtitlan — a city of floating gardens and stone aqueducts that once amazed even the soldiers who destroyed it.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that Tenochtitlan was an impressive city before it was destroyed?

choices.text: The Spanish forced their language, religion, and government onto the people.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it describes how the Spanish treated the conquered people, which has nothing to do with how impressive Tenochtitlan was.

choices.text: Mexico City became the capital of modern Mexico.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it tells us what happened after the conquest, not anything about Tenochtitlan's greatness before it was destroyed.

choices.text: Cortés's men melted Aztec gold and smashed temples.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it describes the destruction of the city, not what made the city impressive in the first place.

choices.text: It was a city of floating gardens and stone aqueducts.
choices.feedback: This is correct because floating gardens and stone aqueducts are amazing features, and the passage even says they amazed the soldiers who saw them.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did Montezuma's actions toward the Spanish contrast with the actions of Cortés's men after the conquest?

choices.text: Montezuma attacked the strangers immediately, while the Spanish tried to negotiate a peaceful trade.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Montezuma actually welcomed the Spanish as guests instead of attacking them, and it was Cortés who came with weapons, not peaceful trade.

choices.text: Montezuma hid his army inside the pyramids, while the Spanish built a new capital nearby.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions Montezuma hiding an army inside pyramids — that detail does not appear anywhere in the passage.

choices.text: Montezuma used invisible diseases to fight, while the Spanish relied only on steel weapons.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because diseases were brought by the Europeans, not used by Montezuma, and the Spanish used many types of weapons including horses, cannons, and steel swords.

choices.text: Montezuma welcomed the strangers as guests, while the Spanish destroyed the city and artworks.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly shows Montezuma used diplomacy and welcomed the Spanish, while Cortés's men later destroyed temples, melted gold, and tore down the city.

prompt._: Based on the article, what were the main causes that allowed Cortés to defeat the massive Aztec Empire?

choices.text: Native groups joined his army, and a deadly sickness weakened the city.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that thousands of indigenous allies joined Cortés and that smallpox killed enormous numbers of Aztecs, both of which were key reasons the empire fell.

choices.text: The Spanish built a new capital city, and they smashed the Aztec artworks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because melting gold and smashing temples happened after the Aztecs were already defeated, so these were results of the conquest, not causes.

choices.text: The emperor gave away the city's gold, and the Aztec warriors refused to fight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions Montezuma giving away gold, and Aztec warriors did fight back fiercely against the Spanish invaders.

choices.text: He brought thousands of soldiers from Spain, and he blocked the city's tunnels.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cortés only arrived with about 600 soldiers, not thousands, and the siege blocked food and water — not tunnels.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence correctly describes the order of events that led to the fall of Tenochtitlan?

choices.text: Disease strikes the city, Montezuma welcomes the Spanish, Cortés begins a siege, and the Spanish retreat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because disease struck the city after Montezuma welcomed the Spanish and after the Spanish retreat — not at the very beginning.

choices.text: Montezuma welcomes the Spanish, the Spanish retreat, disease strikes the city, and Cortés begins a siege.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows this exact order: Montezuma welcomes the Spanish, the Spanish retreat after a battle, disease weakens the city, and then Cortés launches his siege.

choices.text: Montezuma welcomes the Spanish, the Spanish build a city, disease strikes, and Cortés begins a siege.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Spanish building a new city happened after the conquest was completely over, not in the middle of the events leading to the fall.

choices.text: Cortés begins a siege, Montezuma welcomes the Spanish, the Spanish retreat, and disease strikes the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the siege was one of the last things that happened, not the first step in the sequence of events.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the beginning and the ending of the article to show its main idea?

choices.text: A city that welcomed strangers became a city that defeated the Spanish in the end.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Aztecs did not defeat the Spanish; Tenochtitlan was conquered and destroyed in 1521.

choices.text: A city that was famous mainly for trade became a place remembered only for Spanish weapons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article focuses on the rise and fall of the whole empire, not just trade, and the Spanish weapons were only one of several reasons the city fell.

choices.text: A city that depended on prophecy became a city that grew larger than any city in Spain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the prophecy was just one detail about Montezuma's decision, and the comparison to Spanish cities was only a small fact at the very beginning.

choices.text: A city that was powerful and impressive became a ruined capital after conquest and disease.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article begins by showing how powerful and impressive Tenochtitlan was, then ends with it destroyed and buried — that contrast is the main idea.

syllabus_metadata.title: Conquistadors: Cortes and the Aztecs

assessments.title: Conquistadors: Pizarro and the Inca

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Pizarro and the Fall of the Inca Empire

A Room Filled with Gold

Picture a room about seventeen feet wide and twenty-two feet long — roughly the size of your classroom. Now imagine filling it from floor to ceiling with gold. That was the price an emperor named Atahualpa offered for his freedom. He sat in a Spanish prison cell in 1532, and he promised his captors more treasure than they had ever seen. The freedom did not.

The man who held Atahualpa prisoner was Francisco Pizarro, a conquistador — a Spanish soldier-explorer who seized land and treasure in the Americas. Pizarro came from a poor family in Spain. He could not read or write. But he had heard stories of a wealthy empire high in the Andes Mountains of South America. That empire belonged to the Inca, and Pizarro wanted its riches.
stimulus.content_text: Pizarro and the Fall of the Inca EmpireA Room Filled with Gold Picture a room about seventeen feet wide and twenty-two feet long — roughly the size of your classroom. Now imagine filling it from floor to ceiling with gold. That was the price an emperor named Atahualpa offered for his freedom. He sat in a Spanish prison cell in 1532, and he promised his captors more treasure than they had ever seen. The freedom did not. The man who held Atahualpa prisoner was Francisco Pizarro, a conquistador — a Spanish soldier-explorer who seized land and treasure in the Americas. Pizarro came from a poor family in Spain. He could not read or write. But he had heard stories of a wealthy empire high in the Andes Mountains of South America. That empire belonged to the Inca, and Pizarro wanted its riches.

prompt._: According to the text, which detail shows a contrast between Pizarro's background and his goal?

choices.text: He was a poor man who offered a room full of gold.
choices.feedback: A is tricky, but it describes Atahualpa offering gold, not Pizarro — so it mixes up who did what.

choices.text: He was a Spanish explorer who ruled the Inca Empire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Pizarro never ruled the Inca Empire — he wanted its riches, but the passage doesn't say he took over as ruler.

choices.text: He was a poor man who wanted the Inca's riches.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it uses details straight from the passage — Pizarro's poor background versus his goal of grabbing the Inca's great riches — to show a real contrast.

choices.text: He was an emperor who sat in a Spanish prison cell.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes Atahualpa, the emperor sitting in a Spanish prison cell, not Pizarro.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
An Empire Built Through Mountains

Actually, they ruled one of the largest empires on Earth. About twelve million people lived under Inca rule. Their territory stretched nearly 2,500 miles along the western coast of South America, from present-day Colombia to Chile.

The Inca built things that still amaze engineers today. They carved terraced farms — flat steps cut into steep mountainsides, like a giant staircase of cropland — so they could grow food where flat ground did not exist. Their road system ran for about 25,000 miles, longer than the famous roads of ancient Rome. They built suspension bridges over deep mountain canyons and constructed enormous stone cities without using wheels or iron tools.

The Inca had no written alphabet, but they kept detailed records using quipu, a system of knotted and colored strings. Each knot's position and color stored specific information — think of it like a computer made of thread. Their capital city, Cusco, gleamed with temples covered in sheets of gold.
stimulus.content_text: An Empire Built Through Mountains Actually, they ruled one of the largest empires on Earth. About twelve million people lived under Inca rule. Their territory stretched nearly 2,500 miles along the western coast of South America, from present-day Colombia to Chile. The Inca built things that still amaze engineers today. They carved terraced farms — flat steps cut into steep mountainsides, like a giant staircase of cropland — so they could grow food where flat ground did not exist. Their road system ran for about 25,000 miles, longer than the famous roads of ancient Rome. They built suspension bridges over deep mountain canyons and constructed enormous stone cities without using wheels or iron tools. The Inca had no written alphabet, but they kept detailed records using quipu, a system of knotted and colored strings. Each knot's position and color stored specific information — think of it like a computer made of thread. Their capital city, Cusco, gleamed with temples covered in sheets of gold.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the idea that the Inca solved problems caused by their mountain landscape?

choices.text: They carved flat steps to farm without flat ground.
choices.feedback: This is correct because carving flat steps into steep mountainsides was a direct solution to the problem of having no flat ground to farm in the mountains.

choices.text: They covered their capital city temples in golden sheets.
choices.feedback: Covering temples in gold shows the Inca were wealthy and valued their religion, but it does not connect to solving a problem caused by the mountain landscape.

choices.text: They ruled an empire of about twelve million people.
choices.feedback: This tells us how many people lived under Inca rule, which is about the size of their empire — not about solving any mountain landscape problem.

choices.text: They kept detailed records using colored and knotted strings.
choices.feedback: Using knotted and colored strings to keep records was a clever idea, but it had nothing to do with the challenges of living in the mountains.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Trap at Cajamarca

Pizarro arrived in Peru in 1532 with fewer than 200 soldiers. How could such a tiny force threaten an empire of millions? The answer was not just Spanish weapons. It was a combination of disease, treachery — dishonest betrayal of someone's trust — and terrible timing for the Inca.

Before Pizarro even landed, a disease called smallpox had already swept south from Central America. The Inca had no immunity — no natural defense against the sickness, the way your body fights off a cold you've had before. Smallpox was completely new to them. It killed the emperor Huayna Capac and millions of his people. His two sons, Atahualpa and Huascar, then fought a bloody civil war over who would rule. Atahualpa won, but the empire was shattered and divided.

Pizarro used this moment. He invited Atahualpa to a peaceful meeting in the city of Cajamarca. When Atahualpa arrived with thousands of unarmed attendants, the Spanish attacked. Cannons fired. Cavalry — soldiers on horseback — charged into the crowd. The Inca had never seen horses before. In the chaos, Pizarro grabbed Atahualpa himself.
stimulus.content_text: A Trap at Cajamarca Pizarro arrived in Peru in 1532 with fewer than 200 soldiers. How could such a tiny force threaten an empire of millions? The answer was not just Spanish weapons. It was a combination of disease, treachery — dishonest betrayal of someone's trust — and terrible timing for the Inca. Before Pizarro even landed, a disease called smallpox had already swept south from Central America. The Inca had no immunity — no natural defense against the sickness, the way your body fights off a cold you've had before. Smallpox was completely new to them. It killed the emperor Huayna Capac and millions of his people. His two sons, Atahualpa and Huascar, then fought a bloody civil war over who would rule. Atahualpa won, but the empire was shattered and divided. Pizarro used this moment. He invited Atahualpa to a peaceful meeting in the city of Cajamarca. When Atahualpa arrived with thousands of unarmed attendants, the Spanish attacked. Cannons fired. Cavalry — soldiers on horseback — charged into the crowd. The Inca had never seen horses before. In the chaos, Pizarro grabbed Atahualpa himself.

prompt._: According to the text, what sequence of events caused the Inca empire to become shattered and divided?

choices.text: Atahualpa brought his attendants, and then the Spanish soldiers attacked the crowd.
choices.feedback: Atahualpa bringing his attendants and the Spanish attacking is what happened at Cajamarca, which led to Atahualpa's capture, not to the empire being shattered and divided.

choices.text: Pizarro arrived in Peru, and then he asked for a peaceful meeting.
choices.feedback: Pizarro arriving and asking for a meeting happened later in the story, and it led to Atahualpa being captured — not to the empire becoming shattered and divided.

choices.text: Smallpox killed the emperor, and then his sons fought a civil war.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that smallpox killed the emperor, his sons then fought a civil war, and that war left the empire shattered and divided.

choices.text: The Spanish fired their cannons, and then the soldiers charged on horseback.
choices.feedback: The cannons and cavalry charging happened at the meeting in Cajamarca, which led to Atahualpa being grabbed — not to the empire becoming shattered and divided.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Broken Promise

Atahualpa offered his famous ransom — a payment demanded in exchange for a prisoner's release. He promised to fill one large room with gold and two more with silver. Inca people from across the empire carried golden cups, statues, and jewelry to Cajamarca. The rooms filled up. The treasure was worth millions of dollars in today's money.

Pizarro took every piece. Then he broke his word. He put Atahualpa on trial and executed him.

With the emperor dead and the empire already weakened by war and disease, the Spanish captured Cusco. They placed a puppet emperor — a ruler who only pretends to lead while someone else gives the real orders, like a puppet moved by strings — on the Inca throne.

But the Inca did not simply give up. They established a hidden state at Vilcabamba in the mountains and fought the Spanish for nearly forty more years. The Inca people were not helpless. They were outmatched by disease more than by swords. Historians estimate that European sicknesses killed far more Inca than any battle did — possibly nine out of every ten people.
stimulus.content_text: A Broken Promise Atahualpa offered his famous ransom — a payment demanded in exchange for a prisoner's release. He promised to fill one large room with gold and two more with silver. Inca people from across the empire carried golden cups, statues, and jewelry to Cajamarca. The rooms filled up. The treasure was worth millions of dollars in today's money. Pizarro took every piece. Then he broke his word. He put Atahualpa on trial and executed him. With the emperor dead and the empire already weakened by war and disease, the Spanish captured Cusco. They placed a puppet emperor — a ruler who only pretends to lead while someone else gives the real orders, like a puppet moved by strings — on the Inca throne. But the Inca did not simply give up. They established a hidden state at Vilcabamba in the mountains and fought the Spanish for nearly forty more years. The Inca people were not helpless. They were outmatched by disease more than by swords. Historians estimate that European sicknesses killed far more Inca than any battle did — possibly nine out of every ten people.

prompt._: The article compares a puppet emperor to a puppet moved by strings. Based on this comparison, what does the phrase 'puppet emperor' most likely mean?

choices.text: A ruler who entertains people like a puppet show performer
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses 'puppet' to describe someone being controlled, not someone putting on a show to entertain people.

choices.text: A ruler who uses strings and ropes to send secret messages
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'strings' in the comparison are a metaphor for control, not a real method of sending secret messages.

choices.text: A ruler who was chosen fairly by the people of the empire
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because this ruler was placed on the throne by the Spanish conquerors, not chosen fairly by the Inca people.

choices.text: A ruler who is controlled by someone else with real power
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly explains that a puppet emperor only pretends to lead while someone else — in this case, the Spanish — holds the real power.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Was Lost

The Spanish melted down golden artwork and shipped the metal to Spain. They destroyed temples and cities. They abandoned the quipu system, and centuries of Inca knowledge — their history, their population records, their stories — vanished with those knotted strings.

Spain built a colonial empire on Inca land and forced the surviving people to mine silver and gold for generations. Today, in countries like Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador, millions of people still speak Quechua, the language of the Inca. Inca terraced farms still cling to Andean mountainsides. The empire fell, but the people and their knowledge did not disappear entirely. Every terrace carved into those mountains is a reminder: someone brilliant was here first.
stimulus.content_text: What Was Lost The Spanish melted down golden artwork and shipped the metal to Spain. They destroyed temples and cities. They abandoned the quipu system, and centuries of Inca knowledge — their history, their population records, their stories — vanished with those knotted strings. Spain built a colonial empire on Inca land and forced the surviving people to mine silver and gold for generations. Today, in countries like Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador, millions of people still speak Quechua, the language of the Inca. Inca terraced farms still cling to Andean mountainsides. The empire fell, but the people and their knowledge did not disappear entirely. Every terrace carved into those mountains is a reminder: someone brilliant was here first.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the quipu system?

choices.text: a language spoken by millions of people today
choices.feedback: Quechua is the language still spoken today, not the quipu — the passage names them as two different things.

choices.text: a type of golden artwork melted down by Spain
choices.feedback: The golden artwork that Spain melted down is mentioned separately from the quipu system, so they are not the same thing.

choices.text: a way of recording information using knotted strings
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the quipu system was abandoned and that knowledge was lost in 'those knotted strings,' showing it was a way of recording information with knots.

choices.text: a method of farming on steep mountainsides
choices.feedback: Terraced farming on steep mountainsides is a different Inca practice — the passage mentions it separately from the quipu system.

prompt._: According to the article, what was a major difference between how the Inca and the Spanish treated golden objects?

choices.text: The Inca used gold for everyday tools, but the Spanish saved it for their new kings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the Inca used gold for temples and artwork, not for everyday tools.

choices.text: The Inca created golden artwork, but the Spanish melted it down into plain metal.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states the Inca created golden cups, statues, and jewelry, while the Spanish melted all of it down into plain metal and sent it to Spain.

choices.text: The Inca hid their golden statues, but the Spanish found them in the high mountains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions the Inca hiding golden statues in the mountains — in fact, Inca people brought their gold openly to Cajamarca as ransom.

choices.text: The Inca mined gold for their rulers, but the Spanish used it to build large cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the Spanish forced the Inca to mine silver and gold for generations, and there is no mention of using gold to build cities.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused centuries of Inca history and population records to vanish?

choices.text: The Spanish abandoned the knotted string system the Inca used to store their information.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that when the Spanish abandoned the quipu knotted string system, centuries of Inca history, population records, and stories disappeared along with it.

choices.text: The Spanish melted down the golden temples that held the empire's written alphabet system.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Inca never had a written alphabet — they stored information using the quipu string system, not in golden temples.

choices.text: The Inca people forgot how to speak their native language after the Spanish attacked.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article tells us that millions of people still speak Quechua, the Inca language, in countries like Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador today.

choices.text: The Inca hid their historical documents in a secret mountain state located at Vilcabamba.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Vilcabamba was a hidden place where the Inca continued to fight the Spanish for forty more years, not a place where they stored historical documents.

prompt._: According to the article, what happened after Atahualpa won the civil war?

choices.text: He was placed on the throne as a fake leader with no real power.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a puppet emperor came much later, after Atahualpa was executed and the Spanish took over Cusco.

choices.text: He caught a new disease that swept down from Central America.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because smallpox arrived before the civil war even started — it killed the previous emperor, which is what caused the war in the first place.

choices.text: He established a hidden state and fought the Spanish for years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the hidden state at Vilcabamba was set up by the remaining Inca people after Atahualpa's death, not by Atahualpa himself after the civil war.

choices.text: He was captured at a meeting and offered gold for his freedom.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because right after winning the civil war, Atahualpa was captured by Pizarro at Cajamarca and offered a huge ransom of gold and silver to try to get his freedom.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how the author contrasts the Inca Empire before Pizarro arrived with the empire when he attacked?

choices.text: It was once a poor and struggling society, but it became a rich and powerful empire by mining silver for Spain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Inca were already wealthy and powerful before Pizarro — it was the Spanish who later forced the Inca to mine silver for Spain.

choices.text: It was once a powerful and advanced empire, but it became a helpless and forgotten society by giving up the fight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the Inca did not give up, fighting the Spanish for nearly forty more years from their hidden state at Vilcabamba.

choices.text: It was once a small and hidden society, but it became a large and famous empire by building a secret state.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Inca Empire was never small or hidden — it stretched 2,500 miles and had about 12 million people before Pizarro arrived.

choices.text: It was once a strong and united empire, but it became a weak and divided society from sickness and civil war.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows the Inca went from a strong, united empire with millions of people to a weak, divided society after smallpox killed millions and a civil war split the empire in two.

syllabus_metadata.title: Conquistadors: Pizarro and the Inca

assessments.title: The Columbian Exchange

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Columbian Exchange: When Two Worlds Traded Everything

A Tomato in Italy

Picture a plate of spaghetti with tomato sauce in Italy. Now picture it vanishing. Before 1492, that plate could not exist. Tomatoes grew only in the Americas. No Italian chef had ever seen one. No Irish farmer had ever planted a potato. No one in Africa had ever tasted corn. The foods you think of as belonging to certain countries actually came from the other side of the world — and they arrived because of a massive swap that started when Columbus crossed the Atlantic Ocean.

You might think Columbus arrived in an empty land, but the Americas were already home to millions of people. Diverse civilizations — the Aztec, the Inca, the Maya, and hundreds of other nations — had been building cities, farming crops, and trading goods for thousands of years. Historians call what happened next the Columbian Exchange — the enormous transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and ideas between the Americas and Europe, Africa, and Asia after 1492.

Before 1492, that plate could not exist. Tomatoes grew only in the Americas. No Italian chef had ever seen one. No Irish farmer had ever planted a potato. No one in Africa had ever tasted corn. The foods you think of as belonging to certain countries actually came from the other side of the world — and they arrived because of a massive swap that started when Columbus crossed the Atlantic Ocean. You might think Columbus arrived in an empty land, but the Americas were already home to millions of people. Diverse civilizations — the Aztec, the Inca, the Maya, and hundreds of other nations — had been building cities, farming crops, and trading goods for thousands of years. Historians call what happened next the Columbian Exchange — the enormous transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and ideas between the Americas and Europe, Africa, and Asia after 1492.

prompt._: According to the text, how was life in Italy different before 1492 compared to after?

choices.text: No Italian chef had ever seen a tomato before
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'No Italian chef had ever seen one,' showing that tomatoes were completely unknown in Italy before 1492.

choices.text: Italian farmers grew tomatoes for other countries
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Italian farmers weren't growing tomatoes at all — the passage tells us tomatoes only existed in the Americas before 1492.

choices.text: Italy traded tomatoes with the Aztec and Inca
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Italy had no contact with the Aztec or Inca before Columbus crossed the Atlantic in 1492, which is exactly what the passage is explaining.

choices.text: Italian chefs had many types of tomatoes to cook with
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Italian chefs had never even seen a tomato before 1492, so they definitely didn't have many types to cook with.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
New Foods Cross the Ocean

The Eastern and Western Hemispheres — the two great halves of the globe, split by the Atlantic Ocean — had grown different crops for centuries. Each side had its own plants and animals. When they connected, foods traveled in both directions.

From the Americas to Europe came potatoes, tomatoes, corn, and cacao beans for making chocolate. Indigenous peoples — the original inhabitants who had lived in the Americas long before Europeans arrived — had grown these crops for thousands of years. These foods became staples, meaning everyday basics that people depended on. The potato, first grown high in the Andes Mountains of South America, became so important in Ireland that when a potato disease struck in the 1840s, it caused a terrible famine — a time when huge numbers of people starved because food ran out. Corn spread across Africa and Asia. Tomatoes changed Italian cooking forever. Chocolate became one of Europe's favorite treats.

It's easy to assume the exchange only went one direction, from Europe outward. Actually, the flow ran both ways. Europeans brought wheat, barley, rice, sugarcane, horses, cattle, pigs, sheep, and chickens to the Americas. Horses had once roamed the Americas thousands of years earlier but had gone extinct. When Spanish explorers brought them back, indigenous peoples of the Great Plains became expert riders and hunters. Coffee, originally from Africa, was planted in Central and South America, which today grow much of the world's supply. Bananas traveled from Southeast Asia to the Americas and became a major crop.
stimulus.content_text: New Foods Cross the Ocean The Eastern and Western Hemispheres — the two great halves of the globe, split by the Atlantic Ocean — had grown different crops for centuries. Each side had its own plants and animals. When they connected, foods traveled in both directions. From the Americas to Europe came potatoes, tomatoes, corn, and cacao beans for making chocolate. Indigenous peoples — the original inhabitants who had lived in the Americas long before Europeans arrived — had grown these crops for thousands of years. These foods became staples, meaning everyday basics that people depended on. The potato, first grown high in the Andes Mountains of South America, became so important in Ireland that when a potato disease struck in the 1840s, it caused a terrible famine — a time when huge numbers of people starved because food ran out. Corn spread across Africa and Asia. Tomatoes changed Italian cooking forever. Chocolate became one of Europe's favorite treats. It's easy to assume the exchange only went one direction, from Europe outward. Actually, the flow ran both ways. Europeans brought wheat, barley, rice, sugarcane, horses, cattle, pigs, sheep, and chickens to the Americas. Horses had once roamed the Americas thousands of years earlier but had gone extinct. When Spanish explorers brought them back, indigenous peoples of the Great Plains became expert riders and hunters. Coffee, originally from Africa, was planted in Central and South America, which today grow much of the world's supply. Bananas traveled from Southeast Asia to the Americas and became a major crop.

prompt._: Based on the main idea of the text, what caused everyday foods to change in both the Eastern and Western Hemispheres?

choices.text: A terrible disease caused a famine that ruined the older crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the famine was just one sad result of depending on the potato in Ireland, not the main cause of food changes across both hemispheres.

choices.text: The Spanish explorers brought many new animals across the Atlantic Ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Spanish explorers did bring animals, the big change came from both sides sharing many different foods and animals — not just one group bringing animals.

choices.text: The native peoples traveled across the ocean to find new crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says it was European explorers who crossed the ocean, not indigenous peoples traveling to find new crops.

choices.text: The two halves of the globe connected and shared their goods.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that when the two hemispheres connected, foods traveled in both directions, which is exactly what changed everyday eating on both sides.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Sugar and Suffering

Sugarcane tells one of the darker stories of the Columbian Exchange. Europeans planted enormous sugar fields across the Caribbean and Brazil. These plantations made fortunes for their owners — but the work was brutal. The demand for workers to cut sugarcane drove the transatlantic slave trade. Millions of enslaved Africans were forced across the ocean to labor on plantations and in mines.
stimulus.content_text: Sugar and Suffering Sugarcane tells one of the darker stories of the Columbian Exchange. Europeans planted enormous sugar fields across the Caribbean and Brazil. These plantations made fortunes for their owners — but the work was brutal. The demand for workers to cut sugarcane drove the transatlantic slave trade. Millions of enslaved Africans were forced across the ocean to labor on plantations and in mines.

prompt._: According to the text, what were enslaved Africans forced to do after being taken across the ocean?

choices.text: They started the transatlantic slave trade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the demand for sugarcane workers drove the slave trade — enslaved Africans were the victims of it, not the ones who started it.

choices.text: They planted sugar fields across Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sugar fields were planted in the Caribbean and Brazil, not across Europe.

choices.text: They labored on plantations and in mines.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses almost these exact words: enslaved Africans were 'forced across the ocean to labor on plantations and in mines.'

choices.text: They kept the fortunes from the crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the text says the plantation owners made the fortunes, not the enslaved people who were forced to work.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Invisible Killer

The deadliest part of the Columbian Exchange was something no one could see: germs. European diseases — smallpox, measles, typhus, and influenza — swept through indigenous communities like wildfire. You might think warfare between Europeans and indigenous peoples caused most of the deaths. Warfare and forced labor did kill many people. But disease was by far the biggest killer.

The peoples of the Americas had lived separated from Europe, Asia, and Africa for thousands of years. They had no immunity — the body's built-in defense against a disease, like armor your immune system builds after fighting off a sickness once before. Their bodies had never encountered smallpox or measles, so they had no armor against them. The results were catastrophic, meaning they caused destruction so enormous it is hard to picture. Entire villages disappeared. Powerful empires crumbled. Some historians estimate that the population of the Americas fell from about 60 million to fewer than 6 million within a hundred years — roughly 90 percent of some populations.

This massive loss of life made it far easier for European colonists to seize control of the Americas. It also deepened the demand for enslaved African workers to replace the labor force that disease had destroyed.

European diseases — smallpox, measles, typhus, and influenza — swept through indigenous communities like wildfire. You might think warfare between Europeans and indigenous peoples caused most of the deaths. Warfare and forced labor did kill many people. But disease was by far the biggest killer. The peoples of the Americas had lived separated from Europe, Asia, and Africa for thousands of years. They had no immunity — the body's built-in defense against a disease, like armor your immune system builds after fighting off a sickness once before. Their bodies had never encountered smallpox or measles, so they had no armor against them. The results were catastrophic, meaning they caused destruction so enormous it is hard to picture. Entire villages disappeared. Powerful empires crumbled. Some historians estimate that the population of the Americas fell from about 60 million to fewer than 6 million within a hundred years — roughly 90 percent of some populations. This massive loss of life made it far easier for European colonists to seize control of the Americas. It also deepened the demand for enslaved African workers to replace the labor force that disease had destroyed.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares immunity to 'armor your immune system builds.' Based on this comparison, what does the word 'immunity' most likely mean?

choices.text: a weakness the body has when it meets new germs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because immunity is actually a strength that protects you — having no immunity was the weakness that made indigenous peoples vulnerable.

choices.text: a protection the body develops against disease
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares immunity to 'armor,' which protects you, meaning immunity is the protection your body builds against disease.

choices.text: a weapon the body uses to attack germs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because armor protects you from being hurt, it doesn't attack — so immunity is about defense, not attacking germs.

choices.text: a sickness the body catches from other people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because immunity is what prevents you from catching a sickness, not the sickness itself.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Still on Your Plate

Nobody in 1492 planned the Columbian Exchange. Nobody predicted it. Some of its effects fed millions of people with nutritious new crops. Others caused suffering almost too large to measure. But the next time you bite into a french fry, pour ketchup on your burger, or stir sugar into hot chocolate, you are eating the Columbian Exchange. It is not ancient history sitting behind glass in a museum. It is sitting on your dinner plate tonight.
stimulus.content_text: Still on Your Plate Nobody in 1492 planned the Columbian Exchange. Nobody predicted it. Some of its effects fed millions of people with nutritious new crops. Others caused suffering almost too large to measure. But the next time you bite into a french fry, pour ketchup on your burger, or stir sugar into hot chocolate, you are eating the Columbian Exchange. It is not ancient history sitting behind glass in a museum. It is sitting on your dinner plate tonight.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by saying the Columbian Exchange is 'not ancient history sitting behind glass in a museum'?

choices.text: It is something that only changed what people ate
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage also mentions that the Columbian Exchange caused suffering and fed millions of people, so it changed much more than just food.

choices.text: It is something that only happened a long time ago
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author is saying the opposite — the Columbian Exchange is NOT just something from long ago, it still matters today.

choices.text: It is something that still affects our lives today
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author uses examples like french fries and ketchup to show that the Columbian Exchange still affects what we eat every single day.

choices.text: It is something that belongs in a museum display
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author is actually saying it does NOT belong in a museum — it's part of real life, not a dusty old display.

prompt._: How does the article contrast the positive effects of the Columbian Exchange with its negative effects?

choices.text: New crops helped people eat better, but Europeans lost control of their own farmland.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Europeans lost control of their own farmland — that simply doesn't happen in the passage.

choices.text: Horses helped indigenous peoples hunt, but tomatoes caused famines across Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while horses did help indigenous peoples hunt, tomatoes never caused famines in Europe — it was actually a potato disease that caused a terrible famine in Ireland.

choices.text: Sugar became a popular treat, but chocolate disappeared from the Americas forever.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says chocolate disappeared from the Americas — in fact, chocolate became one of Europe's favorite treats, which is a positive effect.

choices.text: New crops fed millions of people, but diseases and slavery caused enormous suffering.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly shows that new crops fed millions of people around the world, while diseases devastated indigenous populations and the slave trade caused enormous suffering.

prompt._: Which event helped cause horses to become important to some Native peoples of the Great Plains?

choices.text: People in Europe began eating potatoes from the Andes
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because people in Europe eating potatoes is about food traveling from the Americas to Europe, which has nothing to do with horses on the Great Plains.

choices.text: European farmers planted wheat in the Americas
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because European farmers planting wheat in the Americas is about a crop, not about horses, and the passage never connects wheat to the Great Plains peoples.

choices.text: Diseases spread quickly through Native communities
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because diseases spreading through Native communities was a terrible tragedy, but the passage never connects it to horses becoming important to Great Plains peoples.

choices.text: Spanish explorers brought horses back to the Americas
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly says that when Spanish explorers brought horses back, Native peoples of the Great Plains became expert riders and hunters.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence of events explains why the transatlantic slave trade grew so large?

choices.text: Europeans planted sugar fields, diseases killed the local workers, and enslaved Africans were forced to labor.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it follows the exact sequence the article gives: sugar fields first, then disease killing local workers, then enslaved Africans being forced to labor.

choices.text: Diseases killed the local workers, enslaved Africans were forced to labor, and Europeans abandoned sugar fields.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Europeans did not abandon the sugar fields — they kept running them using enslaved African workers.

choices.text: Enslaved Africans were forced to labor, Europeans planted sugar fields, and diseases killed the local workers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because enslaved Africans were brought over as a result of the sugar fields and disease — they didn't come first.

choices.text: Europeans planted sugar fields, indigenous peoples became expert riders, and enslaved Africans were forced to labor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because indigenous peoples becoming expert horse riders is a separate event that had nothing to do with why the slave trade grew.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did the introduction of the potato in Ireland compare to the introduction of sugarcane in the Americas?

choices.text: The potato made farmers incredibly wealthy, while sugarcane caused a terrible famine to spread.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the facts — it was the potato crop that caused the famine in Ireland, and the article never says potatoes made farmers wealthy.

choices.text: The potato became an everyday basic food, while sugarcane drove the brutal slave trade.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states the potato became a staple food in Ireland while sugarcane plantations drove the brutal transatlantic slave trade.

choices.text: The potato was brought by Spanish explorers, while sugarcane was brought by indigenous peoples.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Spanish explorers brought horses (not potatoes) to the Americas, and sugarcane was brought by Europeans, not indigenous peoples.

choices.text: The potato grew well in tropical climates, while sugarcane grew well in the high mountain areas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the potato originally grew high in the Andes Mountains, and sugarcane was grown in the Caribbean and Brazil, not in high mountain areas.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Columbian Exchange

assessments.title: Japan's Island Geography

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Islands of Warriors: The Geography That Built Feudal Japan

A Kingdom on the Water

In the year 1180, a warrior on horseback raised his sword on a narrow strip of flat land between the mountains and the sea. Behind him stretched rice fields. Ahead of him stood an enemy army. There was nowhere to retreat — the ocean blocked one side, and steep forested peaks blocked the other. This was Japan, a place where the shape of the land decided how people fought, farmed, and lived for centuries.

You might picture Japan as one big island, but it is actually an archipelago — a chain or group of islands, like stepping stones scattered across the water. Japan has four main islands: Honshu, Hokkaido, Kyushu, and Shikoku. Honshu is by far the largest, and it holds the cities of Tokyo, Osaka, and Kyoto. Hokkaido sits farthest north, with harsh, cold winters. Kyushu and Shikoku are smaller islands to the south and west.
stimulus.content_text: Islands of Warriors: The Geography That Built Feudal JapanA Kingdom on the Water In the year 1180, a warrior on horseback raised his sword on a narrow strip of flat land between the mountains and the sea. Behind him stretched rice fields. Ahead of him stood an enemy army. There was nowhere to retreat — the ocean blocked one side, and steep forested peaks blocked the other. This was Japan, a place where the shape of the land decided how people fought, farmed, and lived for centuries. You might picture Japan as one big island, but it is actually an archipelago — a chain or group of islands, like stepping stones scattered across the water. Japan has four main islands: Honshu, Hokkaido, Kyushu, and Shikoku. Honshu is by far the largest, and it holds the cities of Tokyo, Osaka, and Kyoto. Hokkaido sits farthest north, with harsh, cold winters. Kyushu and Shikoku are smaller islands to the south and west.

prompt._: According to the text, how is Hokkaido different from Kyushu and Shikoku?

choices.text: It holds the cities of Tokyo and Osaka
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Tokyo and Osaka are on Honshu, not on Hokkaido.

choices.text: It is the largest of Japan's four main islands
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Honshu is 'by far the largest' island, not Hokkaido.

choices.text: It sits farthest north with harsh, cold winters
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 'Hokkaido sits farthest north, with harsh, cold winters,' which is what makes it different from Kyushu and Shikoku.

choices.text: It is a smaller island to the south and west
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses 'smaller islands to the south and west' to describe Kyushu and Shikoku, not Hokkaido.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Mountains, Rice, and Very Little Room

About 73 percent of Japan is covered in mountains. Forests of bamboo, cedar, and cherry trees climb their slopes. The mountains are beautiful — but they squeeze people into narrow coastal plains and river valleys. Only a thin ribbon of flat land is left for farming and building towns.

That limited space forced the Japanese to become brilliant farmers. They grew rice in flooded fields called paddies — flat, shallow pools of water carved into the earth like giant steps on a hillside. These paddies produced huge amounts of food from small patches of ground. Without this clever method, Japan could never have fed its large population.

The mountains also made travel between regions slow and difficult, so local leaders controlled their own valleys and plains. This mattered deeply when Japan entered its feudal period — a time when powerful lords ruled their own lands and commanded their own armies, similar to how kings and nobles divided up territory in medieval Europe. Japan's feudal era began around the 1100s and lasted for several hundred years, but it did not exist throughout all of Japanese history. Before it, Japan had a central emperor who held real power. During the feudal age, that power shifted to warlords and their samurai soldiers.

The mountains are beautiful — but they squeeze people into narrow coastal plains and river valleys. Only a thin ribbon of flat land is left for farming and building towns. That limited space forced the Japanese to become brilliant farmers. They grew rice in flooded fields called paddies — flat, shallow pools of water carved into the earth like giant steps on a hillside. These paddies produced huge amounts of food from small patches of ground. Without this clever method, Japan could never have fed its large population. The mountains also made travel between regions slow and difficult, so local leaders controlled their own valleys and plains. This mattered deeply when Japan entered its feudal period — a time when powerful lords ruled their own lands and commanded their own armies, similar to how kings and nobles divided up territory in medieval Europe. Japan's feudal era began around the 1100s and lasted for several hundred years, but it did not exist throughout all of Japanese history. Before it, Japan had a central emperor who held real power. During the feudal age, that power shifted to warlords and their samurai soldiers.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that Japan's lack of flat land caused people to change how they grew food?

choices.text: They allowed powerful local lords to command their own samurai armies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because local lords commanding samurai armies is about how power and government worked, not about how people grew food.

choices.text: They planted large forests of bamboo and cherry trees on the slopes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says bamboo and cherry trees grew on the mountain slopes naturally — it wasn't something people did to solve a food-growing problem.

choices.text: They forced the central emperor to give up his power to warlords.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the emperor losing power to warlords is about politics and leadership, not about farming or food.

choices.text: They carved steps into the hillsides to make flooded rice paddies.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says people carved flooded rice paddies into hillsides to grow lots of food in a small space, which is exactly how they changed their farming because of limited flat land.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
An Island Shield — Open and Closed

The Sea of Japan acted like a giant moat around a castle. No enemy army could simply march across a border to invade. The water gave Japan a choice that countries on the Asian mainland did not have: it could decide when to open its doors and when to shut them.

It's easy to assume that Japan was always cut off from the rest of the world, but actually Japan swung back and forth. During some centuries, it eagerly borrowed ideas from China and Korea. By the 500s and 600s, the Japanese had adopted Chinese writing, the Buddhist religion, and Chinese styles of government. During other periods, Japan closed itself off almost completely, spending those quieter years developing its own arts, customs, and social classes. Japan was not permanently isolated — it was selective.
stimulus.content_text: An Island Shield — Open and Closed The Sea of Japan acted like a giant moat around a castle. No enemy army could simply march across a border to invade. The water gave Japan a choice that countries on the Asian mainland did not have: it could decide when to open its doors and when to shut them. It's easy to assume that Japan was always cut off from the rest of the world, but actually Japan swung back and forth. During some centuries, it eagerly borrowed ideas from China and Korea. By the 500s and 600s, the Japanese had adopted Chinese writing, the Buddhist religion, and Chinese styles of government. During other periods, Japan closed itself off almost completely, spending those quieter years developing its own arts, customs, and social classes. Japan was not permanently isolated — it was selective.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did Japan do first before it later closed itself off to develop its own arts and customs?

choices.text: stopped all trade with other countries
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Japan stopped all trade; it says Japan chose when to open and close itself, which is different from stopping everything permanently.

choices.text: borrowed ideas from China and Korea
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Japan borrowed ideas from China and Korea first, adopting their writing, religion, and government styles before later closing its doors.

choices.text: developed its own social classes and traditions
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because developing its own social classes and arts is what Japan did AFTER it closed itself off, not before.

choices.text: built a giant moat around the islands
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Japan didn't build a moat — the Sea of Japan is a natural body of water, not something Japan constructed.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Living on the Ring of Fire

Japan sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire, a zone of intense geological activity — meaning activity deep within the earth, where rock and heat are always shifting. The Ring of Fire circles the edges of the Pacific Ocean. Beneath Japan, massive slabs of rock called tectonic plates press against each other like two fists pushing together. When they slip, the ground shakes. Earthquakes strike Japan often. Some earthquakes trigger tsunamis, enormous ocean waves that slam into the coast and flood entire towns. Japan also has many active volcanoes, including the famous Mount Fuji, which rises 12,388 feet — one of the most recognized mountains on Earth.

These dangers shaped how the Japanese built and how they thought. Traditional Japanese houses used flexible wooden frames that could sway during a quake without crashing down, the way a willow tree bends in a storm instead of snapping. Today, Japan's skyscrapers use advanced engineering to survive powerful earthquakes. Living with constant natural danger also shaped Japanese philosophy. Many traditions celebrate impermanence — the idea that nothing lasts forever. A cherry blossom is beautiful precisely because it falls. A moment matters because it will not come again.
