assessments.title: Defeating the Spanish Armada

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Fire on the Water: How England Defeated the Spanish Armada

Old Ships Stuffed with Gunpowder

Just before midnight on a summer night in 1588, eight wooden ships drifted silently toward the Spanish fleet anchored off the French coast. The ships carried no sailors. They carried no cargo. They carried gunpowder, tar, and fire. English crews had lit them ablaze and aimed them straight at the enemy. Within minutes, the darkness exploded with orange light, and terrified Spanish captains scrambled to cut their anchor ropes and flee. That night of fire helped decide the fate of England.
stimulus.content_text: Fire on the Water: How England Defeated the Spanish ArmadaOld Ships Stuffed with Gunpowder Just before midnight on a summer night in 1588, eight wooden ships drifted silently toward the Spanish fleet anchored off the French coast. The ships carried no sailors. They carried no cargo. They carried gunpowder, tar, and fire. English crews had lit them ablaze and aimed them straight at the enemy. Within minutes, the darkness exploded with orange light, and terrified Spanish captains scrambled to cut their anchor ropes and flee. That night of fire helped decide the fate of England.

prompt._: According to the text, how were the English ships different from normal ships that carry people or goods?

choices.text: They carried captains and crews.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the captains who scrambled to cut ropes were Spanish, and the English ships carried no people at all.

choices.text: They carried sailors and cargo.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states the ships carried NO sailors and NO cargo.

choices.text: They carried anchors and ropes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because anchors and ropes are mentioned in connection with the Spanish fleet, not the English fire ships.

choices.text: They carried fire and gunpowder.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the ships carried gunpowder, tar, and fire instead of people or goods.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Spain Wanted to Invade

King Philip II of Spain had plenty of reasons to attack England. He was a devout Catholic — deeply and seriously religious — and he wanted to force England back to the Catholic faith. English privateers had been robbing him for years. Privateers were sailors who had their queen's permission to attack enemy ships, like pirates with a royal license. Men like Sir Francis Drake raided Spanish treasure ships and stole enormous amounts of gold. On top of that, Queen Elizabeth had been sending money and soldiers to help Protestant rebels in the Netherlands, a territory Spain controlled. Philip had had enough. He believed God supported his cause, and he built the most powerful navy the world had ever seen to crush England once and for all.
stimulus.content_text: Why Spain Wanted to Invade King Philip II of Spain had plenty of reasons to attack England. He was a devout Catholic — deeply and seriously religious — and he wanted to force England back to the Catholic faith. English privateers had been robbing him for years. Privateers were sailors who had their queen's permission to attack enemy ships, like pirates with a royal license. Men like Sir Francis Drake raided Spanish treasure ships and stole enormous amounts of gold. On top of that, Queen Elizabeth had been sending money and soldiers to help Protestant rebels in the Netherlands, a territory Spain controlled. Philip had had enough. He believed God supported his cause, and he built the most powerful navy the world had ever seen to crush England once and for all.

prompt._: Based on the section "Why Spain Wanted to Invade," what caused King Philip II to decide to attack England?

choices.text: England had angered Spain in several important ways
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage lists multiple reasons Philip was angry at England, including stolen gold, religious differences, and England helping rebels in the Netherlands.

choices.text: England had given Spain control of the Netherlands
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says Spain already controlled the Netherlands, and England was actually causing trouble there by helping rebels.

choices.text: Spain wanted privateers to bring home more gold
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — privateers were stealing gold FROM Spain, which made Philip angry, not happy.

choices.text: Spain wanted to help Protestant rebels in the Netherlands
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — it was Queen Elizabeth of England who was helping the Protestant rebels, not Spain.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Armada Sets Sail

Philip's fleet was called the Spanish Armada, and it was enormous — about 130 ships. It included huge warships called galleons, which were heavy, tall sailing vessels built like floating fortresses. Supply ships and troop transports carried thousands of soldiers. The plan seemed simple: sail up the English Channel, collect a Spanish army waiting in the Netherlands, then cross over and invade England. If it worked, Elizabeth would lose her throne, and England would become Catholic again.
stimulus.content_text: The Armada Sets Sail Philip's fleet was called the Spanish Armada, and it was enormous — about 130 ships. It included huge warships called galleons, which were heavy, tall sailing vessels built like floating fortresses. Supply ships and troop transports carried thousands of soldiers. The plan seemed simple: sail up the English Channel, collect a Spanish army waiting in the Netherlands, then cross over and invade England. If it worked, Elizabeth would lose her throne, and England would become Catholic again.

prompt._: According to the text, what was the Spanish Armada supposed to do right after sailing up the English Channel?

choices.text: Build heavy and tall floating fortresses
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'floating fortresses' describes what the galleons looked like, not a step in the Armada's plan.

choices.text: Collect a Spanish army in the Netherlands
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the plan was to sail up the English Channel and then collect a Spanish army waiting in the Netherlands — that was the very next step.

choices.text: Cross over the water and then invade England
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because crossing over to invade England came after collecting the army in the Netherlands, so it was a later step, not the one right after sailing up the Channel.

choices.text: Take the royal throne away from Elizabeth
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because removing Elizabeth from her throne was the overall goal at the end of the plan, not what the Armada was supposed to do right after sailing up the Channel.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Nine Days of Fighting

You might picture one giant battle between the two fleets, cannons blazing on a single afternoon. Actually, the fighting stretched across roughly nine days of chasing, shooting, and retreating along the English Channel. The English navy used smaller, faster ships that were more maneuverable — able to turn quickly and change direction easily. English captains could sail closer to the wind, spin their ships around faster, and reload their cannons more rapidly.

Sir Francis Drake was one of England's best commanders. When messengers told him the Armada had been spotted, he reportedly kept playing a game of bowls, saying there was plenty of time to finish and still beat the Spanish.

Day after day, the English fleet followed behind the Armada, firing at Spanish ships and darting away before the slow galleons could strike back. The Spanish could not land a solid blow. Then came the night attack at Calais — those burning fire ships. The panicked Spanish fleet scattered in every direction, and the Armada's tight formation broke apart.
stimulus.content_text: Nine Days of Fighting You might picture one giant battle between the two fleets, cannons blazing on a single afternoon. Actually, the fighting stretched across roughly nine days of chasing, shooting, and retreating along the English Channel. The English navy used smaller, faster ships that were more maneuverable — able to turn quickly and change direction easily. English captains could sail closer to the wind, spin their ships around faster, and reload their cannons more rapidly. Sir Francis Drake was one of England's best commanders. When messengers told him the Armada had been spotted, he reportedly kept playing a game of bowls, saying there was plenty of time to finish and still beat the Spanish. Day after day, the English fleet followed behind the Armada, firing at Spanish ships and darting away before the slow galleons could strike back. The Spanish could not land a solid blow. Then came the night attack at Calais — those burning fire ships. The panicked Spanish fleet scattered in every direction, and the Armada's tight formation broke apart.

prompt._: What does the phrase "darting away" show about how the English ships compared to the Spanish galleons?

choices.text: The English ships carried more cannons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions how many cannons each side had — it only talks about how quickly the English could reload theirs.

choices.text: The English ships burned much brighter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'darting away' is about moving fast, not about light or fire — the fire ships are a separate part of the story.

choices.text: The English ships moved much faster.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the English ships were 'smaller, faster,' and 'darting away' shows they could speed off before the slow Spanish galleons could hit them.

choices.text: The English ships held braver captains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'darting away' describes how the ships moved, not how brave the captains were.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Gravelines and the Protestant Wind

The next morning brought the Battle of Gravelines. English ships pounded the disorganized Spanish fleet with cannon fire. The Spanish sailors fought bravely, but they could not pull their scattered ships back together. Their invasion plan was ruined. The surviving ships had to retreat.

Here's the surprising part: English cannons alone did not destroy the Armada. The wind did. Strong winds blocked the Spanish from sailing back down the English Channel, so they were forced to take a long, dangerous route around the north of Scotland and down the west coast of Ireland. Violent storms smashed ship after ship against rocky coasts. Thousands of Spanish sailors drowned or died from exposure — suffering caused by cold, wind, and rain with no shelter — along with disease and starvation. Fewer than half the original ships made it home. The English called the storms a "Protestant Wind," giving credit to nature as much as to their own navy.

The Spanish sailors fought bravely, but they could not pull their scattered ships back together. Their invasion plan was ruined. The surviving ships had to retreat. Here's the surprising part: English cannons alone did not destroy the Armada. The wind did. Strong winds blocked the Spanish from sailing back down the English Channel, so they were forced to take a long, dangerous route around the north of Scotland and down the west coast of Ireland. Violent storms smashed ship after ship against rocky coasts. Thousands of Spanish sailors drowned or died from exposure — suffering caused by cold, wind, and rain with no shelter — along with disease and starvation. Fewer than half the original ships made it home. The English called the storms a "Protestant Wind," giving credit to nature as much as to their own navy.

prompt._: In the article section "Gravelines and the Protestant Wind," what does the word "exposure" mean in this sentence: "Thousands of Spanish sailors drowned or died from exposure"?

choices.text: hunger caused by lack of food
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because starvation (hunger) is listed as a separate cause of death in the passage, not part of what 'exposure' means.

choices.text: fear caused by enemy attacks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because exposure has nothing to do with fear — the passage's own definition talks about cold and weather, not enemy attacks.

choices.text: suffering caused by cold weather
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines 'exposure' right in the sentence as 'suffering caused by cold, wind, and rain with no shelter,' which is exactly what option B says.

choices.text: damage caused by cannon fire
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cannon fire was a danger in the battle, but the passage lists exposure separately from fighting, and the definition given is about cold weather, not cannons.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What the Armada's Defeat Really Meant

It's easy to assume that Spain collapsed after losing the Armada. In truth, Spain rebuilt much of its navy and stayed powerful in Europe for decades. But the defeat was a turning point. England's confidence soared. The victory proved that a smaller, faster fleet with clever tactics could beat a much larger force. It strengthened Elizabeth's reputation as a ruler tough enough to defend her country against the mightiest military in Europe.

England's rise as the world's leading naval power began here. That night of burning ships off the coast of France did not just save Elizabeth's throne — it showed the world that the sea belonged to whoever was bold enough, and quick enough, to claim it.

In truth, Spain rebuilt much of its navy and stayed powerful in Europe for decades. But the defeat was a turning point. England's confidence soared. The victory proved that a smaller, faster fleet with clever tactics could beat a much larger force. It strengthened Elizabeth's reputation as a ruler tough enough to defend her country against the mightiest military in Europe. England's rise as the world's leading naval power began here. That night of burning ships off the coast of France did not just save Elizabeth's throne — it showed the world that the sea belonged to whoever was bold enough, and quick enough, to claim it.

prompt._: The author states that Spain did not completely collapse after losing the Armada. Which detail provides evidence to support this point?

choices.text: England became the leading naval power in the world.
choices.feedback: This is about England getting stronger, not about Spain surviving, so it doesn't support the point about Spain.

choices.text: The burning ships saved Queen Elizabeth's throne.
choices.feedback: This is about protecting Queen Elizabeth's throne, which is about England's safety, not about Spain staying powerful.

choices.text: Spain rebuilt its navy and stayed powerful.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses these exact facts — rebuilding the navy and staying powerful — as proof that Spain didn't completely collapse.

choices.text: A smaller fleet used clever tactics to win the fight.
choices.feedback: This detail explains how England won the battle using smart tactics, but it says nothing about what happened to Spain afterward.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the Spanish ships different from the English ships during the battles?

choices.text: The Spanish ships were built heavy and tall, while the English ships were made small and fast.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly describes Spanish galleons as 'heavy, tall' floating fortresses, while English ships were 'smaller, faster,' and easier to steer.

choices.text: The Spanish ships were sunk by English cannons, while the English ships were wrecked by violent storms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses what happened — the Spanish ships were wrecked by storms, and English cannons fired at the Spanish, not at their own ships.

choices.text: The Spanish ships were sailed by royal pirates, while the English ships were led by Catholic kings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the English privateers were the ones who acted like pirates with royal permission, and King Philip II of Spain was the Catholic king — not an English leader.

choices.text: The Spanish ships were loaded with burning fire, while the English ships were packed with heavy cargo.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the burning fire ships were sent by the English toward the Spanish fleet, not the other way around.

prompt._: Based on the article, why did so many Spanish ships fail to return home after the Armada was defeated?

choices.text: The fire ships burned the entire Spanish fleet while it was anchored
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the fire ships only panicked the Spanish and scattered their formation, but did not burn the entire fleet.

choices.text: English cannons sank most of the ships during the Battle of Gravelines
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article specifically says 'English cannons alone did not destroy the Armada' — it was the storms that caused most of the destruction.

choices.text: Storms destroyed scattered ships that could not sail back the safe way
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article explains that storms forced the Spanish on a dangerous route around Scotland and Ireland, where violent weather destroyed ship after ship.

choices.text: Spanish captains chose to surrender their ships to the English navy
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions any Spanish captains surrendering their ships to the English navy.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information across the sections "The Armada Sets Sail," "Nine Days of Fighting," and "Gravelines and the Protestant Wind"?

choices.text: By comparing the sizes of the two different navies
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage does mention that the English ships were smaller, the sections are not organized around comparing the two navies' sizes.

choices.text: By listing the problems the Spanish faced at sea
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because these sections tell the full story of the battle in order, not just a list of problems the Spanish faced.

choices.text: By explaining the reasons why the war first began
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the reasons the war began are explained in the earlier section 'Why Spain Wanted to Invade,' not in these three sections.

choices.text: By telling the events in the order they happened
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the three sections follow the events in time order — the Armada sailing, the days of fighting, and finally the storms that ended the battle.

prompt._: Compared to Spain's plan of using size and strength to win, what does the article show was more important for England's success?

choices.text: Using speed, clever tactics, and help from nature
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because England used faster ships, clever tactics like the fire ships, and benefited from powerful storms that wrecked the Spanish fleet.

choices.text: Building stronger ships that could survive storms
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says English ships were built to survive storms — the storms actually hurt the Spanish, not the English.

choices.text: Signing agreements with other countries for support
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article does not mention England getting help from other countries through any kind of agreement.

choices.text: Having more soldiers and bigger cannons than Spain
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says England had smaller, faster ships — not more soldiers or bigger cannons than Spain.

assessments.title: The English Civil War

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The War That Put a King on Trial

A King Loses His Head

On a freezing January morning in 1649, King Charles I stepped through a window of the Banqueting House in London and onto a wooden platform. A huge crowd stood below, packed together in the cold. Minutes later, the king was dead — beheaded by order of his own government. Nothing like this had ever happened before in England.
stimulus.content_text: The War That Put a King on TrialA King Loses His Head On a freezing January morning in 1649, King Charles I stepped through a window of the Banqueting House in London and onto a wooden platform. A huge crowd stood below, packed together in the cold. Minutes later, the king was dead — beheaded by order of his own government. Nothing like this had ever happened before in England.

prompt._: According to the text, how was the crowd's location different from the king's location?

choices.text: The crowd watched from high above him.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is the opposite of what the passage says — the crowd was below the king, not high above him.

choices.text: The crowd stood below the wooden platform.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states 'A huge crowd stood below,' meaning they were at a lower level than the king on his wooden platform.

choices.text: The crowd hid behind the open window.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone hiding behind the window — that detail is made up.

choices.text: The crowd waited inside the Banqueting House.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the crowd was outside standing below the platform, not inside the Banqueting House.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Sides of a Bitter Argument

Charles I believed in something called the divine right of kings — the idea that God personally chose the king to rule, the way a principal is chosen to lead a school. Under this belief, no one could question the king's decisions. Charles collected taxes without asking permission. He forced churches to worship his way. He even tried to govern for eleven years without calling Parliament at all.

Parliament was England's lawmaking body, a group that met to approve taxes and pass laws. You might picture Parliament as a group speaking for all English people, but actually, its members were wealthy landowners and rich merchants. Ordinary farmers and workers had no voice in it. Still, Parliament believed it had the right to limit the king's power and control how taxes were spent. Many members of Parliament were Puritans — strict Protestants who thought the Church of England still looked and felt too much like the Catholic Church. They clashed with Charles over religion just as fiercely as they clashed over money and power.

It is easy to think of Charles as simply a villain. But many ordinary English people genuinely supported him. They believed God had placed the king above everyone else, and that disobeying him was a sin. This was not a simple fight between good and evil. It was a real argument about how a country should be run.
stimulus.content_text: Two Sides of a Bitter Argument Charles I believed in something called the divine right of kings — the idea that God personally chose the king to rule, the way a principal is chosen to lead a school. Under this belief, no one could question the king's decisions. Charles collected taxes without asking permission. He forced churches to worship his way. He even tried to govern for eleven years without calling Parliament at all. Parliament was England's lawmaking body, a group that met to approve taxes and pass laws. You might picture Parliament as a group speaking for all English people, but actually, its members were wealthy landowners and rich merchants. Ordinary farmers and workers had no voice in it. Still, Parliament believed it had the right to limit the king's power and control how taxes were spent. Many members of Parliament were Puritans — strict Protestants who thought the Church of England still looked and felt too much like the Catholic Church. They clashed with Charles over religion just as fiercely as they clashed over money and power. It is easy to think of Charles as simply a villain. But many ordinary English people genuinely supported him. They believed God had placed the king above everyone else, and that disobeying him was a sin. This was not a simple fight between good and evil. It was a real argument about how a country should be run.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did the Puritans clash with King Charles over religion?

choices.text: They believed that God had personally chosen the king to rule the country.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because believing God chose the king to rule was actually King Charles's own belief — the divine right of kings — not something the Puritans believed or fought for.

choices.text: They wanted ordinary farmers and workers to have a bigger voice in the laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wanting farmers and workers to have a bigger voice was not mentioned as a Puritan goal — the passage says ordinary people had no voice in Parliament, but that's a separate detail, not the reason for the religious clash.

choices.text: They felt the Church of England was too much like the Catholic Church.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Puritans felt the Church of England 'still looked and felt too much like the Catholic Church,' which is exactly why they clashed with Charles over religion.

choices.text: They needed to collect more taxes from the wealthy landowners in England.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because collecting taxes was a separate argument about money and power, not the reason the Puritans clashed with Charles over religion.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
England at War with Itself

In 1642, Charles tried to arrest five members of Parliament by force. That act lit the fuse. Charles raised his royal standard — a large flag that served as a king's official battle banner — in the city of Nottingham, calling loyal subjects to fight. Parliament gathered its own army in London. England was at war with itself.

The king's supporters were called Royalists, or Cavaliers. Parliament's supporters were called Parliamentarians, or Roundheads, a nickname that came from their short haircuts. The Royalist army depended on aristocratic officers — men born into wealthy, noble families.

Parliament's greatest commander was Oliver Cromwell, a country gentleman and devoted Puritan. Cromwell turned out to be a brilliant military leader. He built a professional fighting force called the New Model Army. His soldiers were disciplined, well trained, and deeply motivated by their religious beliefs. Cromwell did something different from the Royalists: he promoted soldiers based on skill, not on family name.

Cromwell's forces crushed the Royalists at the Battle of Marston Moor in 1644 and again at the Battle of Naseby in 1645. Charles fled and eventually surrendered to a Scottish army, which handed him over to Parliament. But Charles refused to accept any limits on his power. He secretly plotted with the Scots to start a second war. That rebellion flared up in 1648 and was quickly defeated.
stimulus.content_text: England at War with Itself In 1642, Charles tried to arrest five members of Parliament by force. That act lit the fuse. Charles raised his royal standard — a large flag that served as a king's official battle banner — in the city of Nottingham, calling loyal subjects to fight. Parliament gathered its own army in London. England was at war with itself. The king's supporters were called Royalists, or Cavaliers. Parliament's supporters were called Parliamentarians, or Roundheads, a nickname that came from their short haircuts. The Royalist army depended on aristocratic officers — men born into wealthy, noble families. Parliament's greatest commander was Oliver Cromwell, a country gentleman and devoted Puritan. Cromwell turned out to be a brilliant military leader. He built a professional fighting force called the New Model Army. His soldiers were disciplined, well trained, and deeply motivated by their religious beliefs. Cromwell did something different from the Royalists: he promoted soldiers based on skill, not on family name. Cromwell's forces crushed the Royalists at the Battle of Marston Moor in 1644 and again at the Battle of Naseby in 1645. Charles fled and eventually surrendered to a Scottish army, which handed him over to Parliament. But Charles refused to accept any limits on his power. He secretly plotted with the Scots to start a second war. That rebellion flared up in 1648 and was quickly defeated.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after Charles surrendered to a Scottish army?

choices.text: The king accepted new limits on his royal power.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — Charles 'refused to accept any limits on his power' even after surrendering.

choices.text: The Scottish army defeated the Parliamentarians.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was Cromwell's forces that defeated the Royalists, and the Scottish army did not defeat the Parliamentarians.

choices.text: The king raised his royal standard in Nottingham.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Charles raised his royal standard in Nottingham at the very beginning of the war, not after he surrendered.

choices.text: The Scottish army handed him over to Parliament.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states the Scottish army 'handed him over to Parliament' after Charles surrendered.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A King on Trial

Parliament had run out of patience. Charles had broken promises and tried to restart the fighting. Leaders decided he could never be trusted. In a decision that shocked every country in Europe, Parliament charged the king with treason — the crime of betraying your own country, like a ship captain deliberately steering into rocks. Charles I became the first English king ever tried and executed by his own people.

Not everyone agreed with what Parliament had done. Some called Charles a tyrant who got what he deserved. Others believed killing a king God had chosen was a terrible crime.
stimulus.content_text: A King on Trial Parliament had run out of patience. Charles had broken promises and tried to restart the fighting. Leaders decided he could never be trusted. In a decision that shocked every country in Europe, Parliament charged the king with treason — the crime of betraying your own country, like a ship captain deliberately steering into rocks. Charles I became the first English king ever tried and executed by his own people. Not everyone agreed with what Parliament had done. Some called Charles a tyrant who got what he deserved. Others believed killing a king God had chosen was a terrible crime.

prompt._: The passage compares treason to 'a ship captain deliberately steering into rocks.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about the meaning of treason?

choices.text: It is a crime that only ship captains can be charged with
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ship captain is just used as an example to explain treason — the crime is not limited to ship captains at all.

choices.text: It is a crime that happens by accident during wartime
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison uses the word 'deliberately,' which means on purpose — the opposite of an accident.

choices.text: It is a crime of purposely harming what you are responsible for
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the ship captain comparison shows someone purposely harming the very thing they are responsible for protecting, which is exactly what treason means.

choices.text: It is a crime of breaking a promise made to Parliament
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because breaking a promise to Parliament is mentioned separately in the passage as something Charles did, but it is not what the ship captain comparison is explaining.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Came After

Here is the surprising part: England did not become a democracy after the king died. Cromwell took charge as Lord Protector, ruling with almost as much power as any king ever had. After Cromwell died in 1658, England actually brought back the monarchy and crowned Charles's son, Charles II.

Yet the trial and execution of Charles I had planted a powerful idea — that even a king must answer to the law. Parliament was not yet a voice for all people, but the principle that one person cannot hold absolute power had taken root. More than a century later, American colonists fighting King George III would reach back to that same idea: rulers govern only because the people allow it.

A single execution on a cold London morning did not create democracy. But it cracked open a door that no king could ever fully shut again.

syllabus_metadata.title: The English Civil War

assessments.title: Oliver Cromwell and the Commonwealth

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Cromwell's England: The Republic That Banned Christmas

A Country Without a Crown

In 1642, Parliament ordered the closure of London's theaters. Soldiers nailed shut their doors and made sure no plays were performed. A few years later, they marched through towns ripping down Christmas decorations. Singing, dancing, and even wearing colorful clothes could get you in trouble. England had cut off its king's head — and now it was cutting out its fun.

After the execution of King Charles I in 1649, England became a republic called the Commonwealth. A republic is a country with no king or queen, where chosen representatives govern instead. You might think this meant ordinary people finally got a voice. Actually, most men and all women still could not vote. The army, not the voters, held the real power.

Parliament tried to run the country alone, but it stumbled. Different factions — groups that disagree with each other, like rival teams pulling in opposite directions — fought over religion, the army, and what England should become. Arguments stalled decisions. The army grew impatient. Its commander, Oliver Cromwell, grew impatient too. By 1653, Cromwell marched soldiers into Parliament and shut it down himself. England's experiment in shared government had collapsed in just four years.
stimulus.content_text: Cromwell's England: The Republic That Banned ChristmasA Country Without a Crown In 1642, Parliament ordered the closure of London's theaters. Soldiers nailed shut their doors and made sure no plays were performed. A few years later, they marched through towns ripping down Christmas decorations. Singing, dancing, and even wearing colorful clothes could get you in trouble. England had cut off its king's head — and now it was cutting out its fun. You might think this meant ordinary people finally got a voice. Actually, most men and all women still could not vote. The army, not the voters, held the real power. Parliament tried to run the country alone, but it stumbled. Different factions — groups that disagree with each other, like rival teams pulling in opposite directions — fought over religion, the army, and what England should become. Arguments stalled decisions. The army grew impatient. Its commander, Oliver Cromwell, grew impatient too. By 1653, Cromwell marched soldiers into Parliament and shut it down himself. England's experiment in shared government had collapsed in just four years.

prompt._: According to the text, how was the Commonwealth different from what people might have expected?

choices.text: Parliament gave equal rights to men and women
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says women could not vote, so men and women did not have equal rights.

choices.text: Ordinary citizens were finally allowed to vote freely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ordinary citizens were NOT allowed to vote freely, which is the opposite of what this answer claims.

choices.text: Most people still had no voice in the government
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says most men and all women still could not vote, meaning ordinary people still had no voice — the opposite of what you might expect from a republic.

choices.text: The army lost its power after the king was removed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that the army held the real power after the king was removed.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Lord Protector

Cromwell took the title "Lord Protector" in 1653. The name sounds gentle, like someone guarding the people. Here's the surprising part: Cromwell held more unchecked power than most English kings ever had. He dissolved Parliament whenever it disagreed with him. He divided England into military districts, each controlled by a major general who enforced strict rules. Cromwell ruled as a dictator backed by soldiers.

Cromwell was a devout Puritan — deeply and seriously religious. Puritans were Protestants who believed Christian life should be simple, strict, and focused on prayer. Cromwell forced his beliefs onto the entire country. He shut down theaters and alehouses. He banned most sports. Swearing in public earned you a fine. Dancing was discouraged.

Most famously, Cromwell banned Christmas celebrations. Puritans believed the holiday involved too much feasting and merriment — joyful, noisy fun — and not enough prayer. Imagine soldiers knocking on your door on Christmas morning and ordering your family to stop singing carols. That was life under Cromwell. England was orderly, but for many people, it felt like living under a gray blanket.

Cromwell forced his beliefs onto the entire country. He shut down theaters and alehouses. He banned most sports. Swearing in public earned you a fine. Dancing was discouraged. Most famously, Cromwell banned Christmas celebrations. Puritans believed the holiday involved too much feasting and merriment — joyful, noisy fun — and not enough prayer. Imagine soldiers knocking on your door on Christmas morning and ordering your family to stop singing carols. That was life under Cromwell. England was orderly, but for many people, it felt like living under a gray blanket.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Cromwell ban Christmas celebrations?

choices.text: because Christmas celebrations cost too much money for families
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about Christmas costing too much money for families.

choices.text: because Puritans thought the holiday had too much fun and not enough prayer
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that Puritans believed Christmas had too much feasting and merriment and not enough prayer, which is exactly why Cromwell banned it.

choices.text: because he wanted to punish people who disagreed with him
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Cromwell banned Christmas due to his Puritan religious beliefs, not to punish people who disagreed with him.

choices.text: because the king had celebrated Christmas before Cromwell took power
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about a king celebrating Christmas or that being a reason for the ban.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Conquest and Cruelty in Ireland

Cromwell also turned his army toward Ireland and Scotland. His conquest of Ireland was brutal. At the towns of Drogheda and Wexford, Cromwell's soldiers killed thousands of Irish soldiers and civilians. After the fighting, Cromwell seized vast amounts of Irish land and handed it to English soldiers and settlers. The Catholic population was pushed to the poorest lands in the west of Ireland.

The resentment — a deep, long-lasting anger, like a burn that never fully heals — created by these actions lasted for centuries. In Ireland, Cromwell's name still carries pain and bitterness hundreds of years later.
stimulus.content_text: Conquest and Cruelty in Ireland Cromwell also turned his army toward Ireland and Scotland. His conquest of Ireland was brutal. At the towns of Drogheda and Wexford, Cromwell's soldiers killed thousands of Irish soldiers and civilians. After the fighting, Cromwell seized vast amounts of Irish land and handed it to English soldiers and settlers. The Catholic population was pushed to the poorest lands in the west of Ireland. The resentment — a deep, long-lasting anger, like a burn that never fully heals — created by these actions lasted for centuries. In Ireland, Cromwell's name still carries pain and bitterness hundreds of years later.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did Cromwell do after the fighting in Ireland?

choices.text: He pushed English settlers to poor lands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the Irish Catholic population, not English settlers, who were pushed to the poorest lands in the west.

choices.text: He sent his army to Scottish towns.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions Scotland separately and does not say Cromwell attacked Scottish towns after the fighting in Ireland.

choices.text: He returned the land to Irish people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cromwell did the opposite — he took land away from the Irish people instead of returning it to them.

choices.text: He gave Irish land to English soldiers.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Cromwell 'seized vast amounts of Irish land and handed it to English soldiers and settlers' after the fighting.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Not All Darkness

Despite his harsh methods, Cromwell made some real achievements. He allowed some Protestant groups — such as Baptists and certain other congregations — to worship more freely than before, though groups like the Quakers still faced persecution. This kind of tolerance, or willingness to accept people whose beliefs differ from your own, was unusual for the time, even if it had limits. Cromwell even allowed Jewish people to live and worship in England again. They had been expelled from the country all the way back in 1290, over 350 years earlier. Catholics, however, were still excluded.

Cromwell also strengthened England's navy, winning important battles against the Dutch and the Spanish. English trade grew, and other nations took England more seriously.

He allowed some Protestant groups — such as Baptists and certain other congregations — to worship more freely than before, though groups like the Quakers still faced persecution. Cromwell even allowed Jewish people to live and worship in England again. They had been expelled from the country all the way back in 1290, over 350 years earlier. Catholics, however, were still excluded. Cromwell also strengthened England's navy, winning important battles against the Dutch and the Spanish. English trade grew, and other nations took England more seriously.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how was Cromwell's treatment of Baptists different from his treatment of Quakers?

choices.text: Baptists were treated very harshly, but Quakers were given freedom.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has things backwards — the passage says Quakers faced persecution, not Baptists, who were given more freedom to worship.

choices.text: Baptists were forced to leave, but Quakers were welcomed back.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because no group was forced to leave in this passage — it was actually Jewish people who were welcomed back after being expelled 350 years earlier.

choices.text: Baptists were allowed to worship, but Quakers were still punished.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Baptists could worship more freely while Quakers still faced persecution, showing they were treated differently.

choices.text: Baptists were excluded from England, but Quakers were allowed inside.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the groups switched around — Baptists were allowed to worship, not excluded, and Quakers faced punishment, not freedom.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Republic Falls Apart

Still, many English people were miserable. They missed their festivals, their music, and their theaters. They resented army officers telling them how to live. Some began to wonder: was life under a Lord Protector really better than life under a king?

When Cromwell died in 1658, his son Richard briefly took over. But Richard had never led soldiers or commanded respect from the army. Without military support, his government crumbled within months.

Faced with disorder, England's leaders made a choice that would have seemed unthinkable just years before. They invited the dead king's son to come home and take the throne. The republic was over. England wanted its king back.
stimulus.content_text: The Republic Falls Apart Still, many English people were miserable. They missed their festivals, their music, and their theaters. They resented army officers telling them how to live. Some began to wonder: was life under a Lord Protector really better than life under a king? When Cromwell died in 1658, his son Richard briefly took over. But Richard had never led soldiers or commanded respect from the army. Without military support, his government crumbled within months. Faced with disorder, England's leaders made a choice that would have seemed unthinkable just years before. They invited the dead king's son to come home and take the throne. The republic was over. England wanted its king back.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'republic' mean?

choices.text: a country led by a Lord Protector
choices.feedback: Lord Protector was Cromwell's special title, but the passage makes clear the republic existed as a system without a king, which is bigger than just one leader's title.

choices.text: a country ruled by army officers
choices.feedback: Army officers did have power during this time, but the passage uses 'republic' to describe the whole system without a king, not just rule by army officers.

choices.text: a country that has no government
choices.feedback: England had a government during the republic, first under Cromwell and then his son Richard, so a republic is not a country with no government.

choices.text: a country without a king or queen
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage shows the republic ended when England brought back a king, meaning a republic is a country without a king or queen.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Legacy Split in Two

Some see him as a champion of Parliament who proved that ordinary representatives could challenge a king. Others, especially in Ireland, see a military dictator whose armies caused enormous suffering. Cromwell's England proved something that still matters: removing a tyrant is only the first step. Building something fair and lasting in the tyrant's place — that is the harder work.

Others, especially in Ireland, see a military dictator whose armies caused enormous suffering. Cromwell's England proved something that still matters: removing a tyrant is only the first step. Building something fair and lasting in the tyrant's place — that is the harder work.

choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never says Cromwell actually built a fair government — in fact, it suggests that was the hard part that wasn't fully achieved.

choices.feedback: This is wrong because it's a general lesson from history, not a detail showing that Cromwell himself was cruel.

choices.text: He proved that representatives could challenge a king.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because it describes Cromwell in a positive way — showing he could stand up to a king — not as a cruel leader.

choices.text: He led armies that caused great suffering in Ireland.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says his armies caused 'enormous suffering,' which is evidence that some people see him as cruel.

choices.feedback: The article doesn't really compare Cromwell's rules to the kings before him — it focuses on what happened after the king was gone.

syllabus_metadata.title: Oliver Cromwell and the Commonwealth

assessments.title: The Restoration: A King Returns

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Merry Monarch: Charles II and the Restoration of England

A King's Son Comes Home

On May 29, 1660, a tall, dark-haired man rode into London on horseback while thousands of people packed the streets, tossing flowers and screaming with joy. His name was Charles Stuart. Eleven years earlier, England had cut off his father's head.

Now England wanted him back. Charles II had been living in exile — forced to leave his own country and survive in foreign lands. Since his father's execution in 1649, Charles had wandered across Europe, depending on the kindness of foreign rulers and waiting for a chance to reclaim his family's crown.

You might think England invited Charles back because everyone suddenly loved the monarchy again. Actually, many people who had fought against his father also supported his return. Oliver Cromwell's military rule had felt like a dictatorship. After Cromwell died, the government fell into confusion. Even people who disliked monarchs decided a king was better than chaos.

Parliament's invitation to Charles II is called the Restoration — the act of bringing something back to the way it was before. But the Restoration didn't actually put things back exactly the way they were. Charles II understood something his father never did. His father had clashed with Parliament and lost his throne — and his life. Charles II would not make that mistake. He shared power with Parliament in ways earlier kings had refused to do. The king was back, but his power had limits it never had before.
stimulus.content_text: The Merry Monarch: Charles II and the Restoration of EnglandA King's Son Comes Home On May 29, 1660, a tall, dark-haired man rode into London on horseback while thousands of people packed the streets, tossing flowers and screaming with joy. His name was Charles Stuart. Eleven years earlier, England had cut off his father's head. Now England wanted him back. Charles II had been living in exile — forced to leave his own country and survive in foreign lands. Since his father's execution in 1649, Charles had wandered across Europe, depending on the kindness of foreign rulers and waiting for a chance to reclaim his family's crown. You might think England invited Charles back because everyone suddenly loved the monarchy again. Actually, many people who had fought against his father also supported his return. Oliver Cromwell's military rule had felt like a dictatorship. After Cromwell died, the government fell into confusion. Even people who disliked monarchs decided a king was better than chaos. Parliament's invitation to Charles II is called the Restoration — the act of bringing something back to the way it was before. But the Restoration didn't actually put things back exactly the way they were. Charles II understood something his father never did. His father had clashed with Parliament and lost his throne — and his life. Charles II would not make that mistake. He shared power with Parliament in ways earlier kings had refused to do. The king was back, but his power had limits it never had before.

choices.text: He shared power with Parliament in new ways
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Charles II shared power with Parliament in ways earlier kings, including his father, had refused to do.

choices.text: He left the country to avoid working with Parliament
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Charles II returned to England and ruled there — it was before the Restoration that he had been living in exile in foreign lands.

choices.text: He refused to share any power with Parliament
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Charles II shared power with Parliament, which is the opposite of refusing to share power.

choices.text: He ended Parliament so he could rule alone
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us Charles II worked with Parliament and shared power with them rather than trying to end or remove Parliament.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Merry Monarch

Charles II was the opposite of Oliver Cromwell in almost every way. Cromwell had been stern and deeply religious. He had banned theaters, shut down festivals, and made Christmas celebrations illegal. Charles was charming, funny, and loved a good time. People called him the "Merry Monarch."

Women performed on stage for the first time in English history. The gloomy years of Puritan restriction melted away.

Charles was also shrewd — clever and careful in a quiet way, like a chess player thinking five moves ahead. He knew how to keep Parliament happy while protecting his own authority. But tensions still simmered — especially over religion. Charles secretly sympathized with Catholicism, and his brother James was openly Catholic. This terrified many Protestants. England had broken away from the Catholic Church under Henry VIII more than a hundred years earlier, and most English people saw Catholicism as a threat to their country and their freedom.

choices.text: Because they supported the Catholic religion.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly says Charles secretly supported Catholicism and James was openly Catholic, which terrified Protestants who saw that religion as a threat.

choices.text: Because they wanted to ban all theaters and festivals.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because banning theaters and festivals was something Cromwell did, not Charles or James — Charles actually brought fun and entertainment back!

choices.text: Because they broke away from the Catholic Church.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because breaking away from the Catholic Church was something Henry VIII did over a hundred years earlier, not Charles or James.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Plague, Fire, and a City Reborn

Charles's reign was not all parties and plays. In 1665, the Great Plague swept through London, killing about 100,000 people — roughly one out of every four Londoners. The poor had no choice but to stay.

The very next year, a fire broke out in a bakery on Pudding Lane. London's streets were narrow, and most buildings were made of wood. The Great Fire of 1666 burned for four straight days, destroying over 13,000 houses and 87 churches, including the old St. Paul's Cathedral. Amazingly, fewer than ten people are known to have died in the fire itself.

It's easy to assume the Great Fire was nothing but a disaster. Yet out of the ashes came a better city. London was rebuilt with wider streets and buildings of stone and brick. The great architect Sir Christopher Wren designed a magnificent new St. Paul's Cathedral that still stands today.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A New Age of Science

The Restoration also sparked an explosion of scientific thinking. In 1660, a group of thinkers founded the Royal Society, an organization dedicated to advancing knowledge through observation and experiment. Instead of just accepting ancient ideas, its members tested things for themselves.

Isaac Newton explained how gravity works. Robert Hooke peered through microscopes and studied tiny structures. When he looked at thin slices of cork, he saw rows of small, empty boxes that reminded him of rooms in a monastery, so he gave them the name "cells." Later scientists built on his work and discovered that all living things are made of cells. Robert Boyle studied how gases behave under pressure. The Royal Society encouraged this kind of inquiry — asking questions and searching for answers through careful testing — and pushed forward the Scientific Revolution.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Shadow on the Horizon

When Charles II died in 1685, his openly Catholic brother took the throne as King James II. The question of whether a Catholic could rule a Protestant nation would soon drag England into another crisis.

But Charles himself had done something pivotal. He brought stability and joy back to a country ripped apart by war and harsh rule. And the England he left behind was not the same England his father had ruled. London was literally rebuilt from the ground up. The Restoration proved that a nation can survive its worst moments — not by going backward, but by building something new from what remains.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Restoration: A King Returns

assessments.title: The Glorious Revolution and the Bill of Rights

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Revolution Without a Battle

A Catholic King in a Protestant Country

On a November morning in 1688, a fleet of Dutch ships appeared off the coast of southwestern England. On board stood a large invasion force — and yet this army would barely need to draw a single sword. England was about to have a revolution without a real fight.

It started three years earlier. When Charles II died in 1685, his brother took the throne as King James II. James was openly Catholic, and that made most English people nervous. England was an overwhelmingly Protestant country. Still, many people were willing to wait. James's two daughters, Mary and Anne, were both Protestant. People figured Catholic rule would not last long.

Then James pushed too hard. He appointed Catholics to powerful positions in the government, the military, and the universities. He issued official orders that suspended — meaning temporarily canceled — laws against Catholics and dissenters, people who disagreed with the official Church of England over matters of religious belief. Parliament watched all of this with growing alarm.
stimulus.content_text: The Revolution Without a BattleA Catholic King in a Protestant Country On a November morning in 1688, a fleet of Dutch ships appeared off the coast of southwestern England. When Charles II died in 1685, his brother took the throne as King James II. James was openly Catholic, and that made most English people nervous. England was an overwhelmingly Protestant country. Still, many people were willing to wait. James's two daughters, Mary and Anne, were both Protestant. People figured Catholic rule would not last long. Then James pushed too hard. He appointed Catholics to powerful positions in the government, the military, and the universities. He issued official orders that suspended — meaning temporarily canceled — laws against Catholics and dissenters, people who disagreed with the official Church of England over matters of religious belief. Parliament watched all of this with growing alarm.

prompt._: According to the text, how was King James II different from most people in England?

choices.text: He was Protestant while most people were Catholic
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it has it backwards — James was Catholic and most people in England were Protestant.

choices.text: He wanted his daughters to rule instead of him
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says James wanted his daughters to take his place on the throne.

choices.text: He wanted to keep the old laws in place
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because James actually canceled laws, not kept them in place.

choices.text: He was Catholic while most people were Protestant
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states James was Catholic while England was an overwhelmingly Protestant country.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Baby Changes Everything

In June 1688, James's wife gave birth to a baby boy. Before that moment, Protestants could tell themselves that Catholic rule was temporary. But a Catholic son meant a Catholic heir. The Catholic royal line could now continue indefinitely — without end, stretching on like a road with no visible finish. Protestant leaders in Parliament decided they could not wait any longer.

Seven powerful English noblemen wrote a secret letter. They sent it across the sea to William of Orange, a Dutch prince married to James's own Protestant daughter Mary. The letter was an invitation: come to England and take the crown.

You might think William conquered England the way an invading army captures enemy territory. Actually, this was more like being asked to step in. English leaders wanted William there. They planned this together. When William landed in southwestern England that November, English soldiers and officials began defecting — abandoning James's side and switching over to William, the way players might walk off one team's bench and sit down on the other's. Day after day, more of James's own supporters left him.

Protestant leaders in Parliament decided they could not wait any longer. Seven powerful English noblemen wrote a secret letter. They sent it across the sea to William of Orange, a Dutch prince married to James's own Protestant daughter Mary. The letter was an invitation: come to England and take the crown. You might think William conquered England the way an invading army captures enemy territory. Actually, this was more like being asked to step in. English leaders wanted William there. They planned this together. When William landed in southwestern England that November, English soldiers and officials began defecting — abandoning James's side and switching over to William, the way players might walk off one team's bench and sit down on the other's. Day after day, more of James's own supporters left him.

prompt._: Based on the text, why did Protestant leaders send a secret letter to William of Orange?

choices.text: They planned to use an invading army to capture enemy territory.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage specifically says this was NOT like an invading army capturing territory; William was actually invited to come.

choices.text: They wanted to stop a Catholic royal family from ruling forever.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says a Catholic son meant the Catholic royal line could continue 'indefinitely,' and that's why Protestant leaders decided they could not wait any longer and sent the letter.

choices.text: They wanted to help James's new baby become the next king.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the Protestant leaders were worried about James's Catholic baby becoming king, so they wanted to prevent that, not help it happen.

choices.text: They hoped to stop English soldiers from leaving the king's army.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — soldiers switching sides happened after William landed in England, so it had nothing to do with why the letter was sent.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A King Runs Away

James found himself nearly alone. Fearing for his life, he made a choice: he fled to France. No one dragged him from the throne. No executioner sharpened an axe. James simply ran.

This is why people called it the "Glorious Revolution." The word "revolution" might make you picture cannons firing and cities burning. But this revolution had no great battles, no sieges, and no executions. Power passed from one ruler to another through political planning, popular support, and one king's decision to flee rather than fight. That was what made it glorious — not glory in war, but glory in avoiding war.
stimulus.content_text: A King Runs Away James found himself nearly alone. Fearing for his life, he made a choice: he fled to France. No one dragged him from the throne. No executioner sharpened an axe. James simply ran. This is why people called it the "Glorious Revolution." The word "revolution" might make you picture cannons firing and cities burning. But this revolution had no great battles, no sieges, and no executions. Power passed from one ruler to another through political planning, popular support, and one king's decision to flee rather than fight. That was what made it glorious — not glory in war, but glory in avoiding war.

prompt._: According to the section, what happened after King James II feared for his life?

choices.text: He was executed with an axe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says 'No executioner sharpened an axe,' meaning James was never executed.

choices.text: He fled to France.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that James feared for his life and 'fled to France' by his own choice.

choices.text: He was dragged from the throne.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says 'No one dragged him from the throne' — James left on his own.

choices.text: He fought in a great battle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says there were 'no great battles,' so James did not fight.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Rules for the New Rulers

Parliament offered the crown to William and Mary — but with a catch. Before they could be crowned, they had to agree to a written set of rules limiting royal power. These rules became the English Bill of Rights of 1689.

The Bill of Rights declared that the king could not suspend laws without Parliament's consent. He could not levy taxes — collect money from the people — without Parliament's approval. He could not keep a standing army, a permanent military force ready at all times, unless Parliament agreed. Parliament must be called frequently. The document guaranteed free elections and freedom of speech inside Parliament. It banned cruel and unusual punishment. It banned excessive bail — unreasonably high payments demanded to release someone from jail, like charging a hundred dollars for a pencil. It also guaranteed Protestant subjects the right to bear arms for self-defense.

Only a small fraction of the population could vote. Women could not vote. Poor men could not vote. Religious minorities were shut out. The Bill of Rights protected some people's freedoms, not everyone's — not yet.

But it planted a powerful idea: even the most powerful ruler must follow the law.

He could not keep a standing army, a permanent military force ready at all times, unless Parliament agreed. Parliament must be called frequently. The document guaranteed free elections and freedom of speech inside Parliament. It banned cruel and unusual punishment. It banned excessive bail — unreasonably high payments demanded to release someone from jail, like charging a hundred dollars for a pencil. It also guaranteed Protestant subjects the right to bear arms for self-defense. Only a small fraction of the population could vote. Women could not vote. Poor men could not vote. Religious minorities were shut out. The Bill of Rights protected some people's freedoms, not everyone's — not yet. But it planted a powerful idea: even the most powerful ruler must follow the law.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the whole point of both examples is that the price is NOT fair — it is way too high.

choices.text: Both involve paying an unreasonably high amount
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines excessive bail as an unreasonably high payment, just like charging a hundred dollars for a pencil is an unreasonably high price.

choices.text: Both involve paying a tax to the government
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither excessive bail nor the pencil comparison involves paying a tax to the government.

choices.text: Both involve paying money to get out of jail
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the pencil example is just used to show what 'unreasonably high' means — it has nothing to do with jail.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
An Idea That Crossed an Ocean

That idea did not stay in England. About a hundred years later, American colonists broke away from Britain and built their own government. When they wrote their own Bill of Rights, they borrowed heavily from the English version of 1689. The ban on cruel and unusual punishment? Straight from the English document. The right to bear arms? Right there in the original.

A baby born in London in 1688 triggered a chain of events that put words into the United States Constitution. A king's decision to run instead of fight led to a document that still protects your rights today. History does not stay in the past. It lives in the laws that surround you right now.

When they wrote their own Bill of Rights, they borrowed heavily from the English version of 1689. The ban on cruel and unusual punishment? Straight from the English document. The right to bear arms? Right there in the original. A baby born in London in 1688 triggered a chain of events that put words into the United States Constitution. A king's decision to run instead of fight led to a document that still protects your rights today. History does not stay in the past. It lives in the laws that surround you right now.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'borrowed heavily' is about using ideas, not physically picking up and taking away a piece of paper.

choices.text: asked England for permission to copy the document
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the colonists asking England for permission to use the ideas.

choices.text: used many of the same ideas from the English document
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage gives specific examples — like the ban on cruel and unusual punishment — showing the colonists used many of the same ideas from the English document.

prompt._: Which statement best explains how the Glorious Revolution helped shape rights in the United States?

prompt._: Which sequence shows the correct order of events during the Glorious Revolution?

choices.text: A Catholic baby was born, King James ran away, and Parliament made new rules.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Parliament didn't make the rules first — the rules came at the very end, after James had already run away.

choices.text: American colonists fought, King James ran away, and Parliament made new rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the American colonists lived about 100 years after the Glorious Revolution, so they couldn't have been part of these events.

prompt._: Which statement best states the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Parliament and the army both worked mainly to keep James II on the throne.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Parliament and English soldiers were actually switching sides *away* from James II, working to remove him from power.

choices.text: A new baby and a Dutch fleet both showed that England wanted a stronger king.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the baby and the Dutch fleet actually led people to want James II *gone* and replaced with a ruler who had less power, not more.

choices.text: England and America both ended unfair voting rules as soon as they wrote bills of rights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article specifically says that even after both bills of rights were written, unfair voting rules still kept women, poor men, and religious minorities from voting.

choices.text: A peaceful change in rulers and a written list of rules both helped limit royal power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article is mainly about how James II was replaced peacefully AND how the Bill of Rights then limited royal power — both ideas together make up the main point.

assessments.title: Thomas Jefferson and the Declaration

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Thomas Jefferson and the Declaration of Independence

A Young Writer Takes On a Giant Task

A thirty-three-year-old man sat alone in a rented room in Philadelphia, dipping his quill pen into ink. Outside, church bells rang and horse-drawn carriages clattered over cobblestones. Inside, Thomas Jefferson bent over a wooden desk, crossing out words, rewriting sentences, and trying to explain to the entire world why thirteen small colonies dared to break away from the most powerful empire on Earth.

It was the summer of 1776, and war between the American colonies and Great Britain had already begun. The Continental Congress — the group of leaders representing the colonies — needed a written document to announce their independence. But they also needed it to do something harder: convince people everywhere that the colonists' cause was fair and right.

He rarely spoke up during the loud debates in Congress. But when he picked up a pen, his ideas came alive on the page. John Adams, who worked with Jefferson on the project, later explained that Jefferson had a "peculiar felicity of expression." That old-fashioned phrase simply meant Jefferson had a pivotal gift for turning big ideas into beautiful, powerful words — the way a musician can turn simple notes into a song that sticks in your head.
stimulus.content_text: Thomas Jefferson and the Declaration of IndependenceA Young Writer Takes On a Giant Task A thirty-three-year-old man sat alone in a rented room in Philadelphia, dipping his quill pen into ink. Outside, church bells rang and horse-drawn carriages clattered over cobblestones. Inside, Thomas Jefferson bent over a wooden desk, crossing out words, rewriting sentences, and trying to explain to the entire world why thirteen small colonies dared to break away from the most powerful empire on Earth. It was the summer of 1776, and war between the American colonies and Great Britain had already begun. The Continental Congress — the group of leaders representing the colonies — needed a written document to announce their independence. But they also needed it to do something harder: convince people everywhere that the colonists' cause was fair and right. He rarely spoke up during the loud debates in Congress. But when he picked up a pen, his ideas came alive on the page. John Adams, who worked with Jefferson on the project, later explained that Jefferson had a "peculiar felicity of expression." That old-fashioned phrase simply meant Jefferson had a pivotal gift for turning big ideas into beautiful, powerful words — the way a musician can turn simple notes into a song that sticks in your head.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Jefferson different when he was writing compared to when he was in Congress debates?

choices.text: He led the debates in Congress and also wrote many letters
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jefferson did not lead the debates; the passage says he rarely spoke up during them.

choices.text: He rarely spoke in debates but expressed ideas powerfully in writing
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states he rarely spoke in debates but had a special gift for expressing powerful ideas through writing.

choices.text: He spoke loudly during debates and wrote quietly at his desk
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Jefferson rarely spoke up in debates — he was NOT loud during them.

choices.text: He argued often in debates but refused to write any documents
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jefferson did write documents — the whole passage is about him writing the Declaration of Independence.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Big Ideas from Bold Thinkers

Jefferson spent about two weeks writing and rewriting. He didn't pull his ideas from thin air. He was deeply inspired by the Enlightenment, a period in history when thinkers across Europe began using reason and science to question old ideas about power. Think of the Enlightenment as a time when people started asking, "Why should kings get to make all the rules?"

One Enlightenment thinker especially shaped Jefferson's writing: an English philosopher named John Locke. Decades earlier, Locke had argued that governments get their power from the consent of the governed — meaning the agreement of the people they rule. Imagine your class choosing its own rules together. That's consent of the governed. Locke also wrote that if a government stops protecting its people's rights, the people can replace it.

Jefferson took Locke's ideas and expressed them in words that still echo today. The Declaration stated that all people are created equal and have unalienable rights — rights that can never be taken away, like something built into you from birth. These rights included life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. Jefferson was saying that your rights don't come from a king's generosity. They belong to you simply because you are a human being.

He was deeply inspired by the Enlightenment, a period in history when thinkers across Europe began using reason and science to question old ideas about power.
