Decades earlier, Locke had argued that governments get their power from the consent of the governed — meaning the agreement of the people they rule. Imagine your class choosing its own rules together. That's consent of the governed. Locke also wrote that if a government stops protecting its people's rights, the people can replace it. Jefferson took Locke's ideas and expressed them in words that still echo today. The Declaration stated that all people are created equal and have unalienable rights — rights that can never be taken away, like something built into you from birth. These rights included life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. Jefferson was saying that your rights don't come from a king's generosity. They belong to you simply because you are a human being.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Jefferson believe that people have "unalienable rights"?

choices.text: Because people are simply born as human beings.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says unalienable rights are 'built into you from birth' and belong to you 'simply because you are a human being.'

choices.text: Because a good government agrees to protect them.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because unalienable rights can never be taken away, so they exist whether or not a government chooses to protect them.

choices.text: Because Enlightenment thinkers invented them first.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Enlightenment thinkers inspired Jefferson's writing, but the passage says these rights belong to people because they are human beings, not because thinkers invented them.

choices.text: Because a generous king gives them to the people.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage directly says your rights don't come from a king's generosity — that's the opposite of what Jefferson believed.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A List of Complaints Against the King

The Declaration didn't just share big ideas. It also listed specific grievances — complaints, like a detailed letter explaining exactly what someone did wrong. Jefferson accused King George III of imposing taxes without the colonists' agreement. He accused the king of shutting down colonial legislatures, the elected groups that made local laws — imagine someone canceling your student council and making all the decisions themselves. The Declaration also charged the king with keeping standing armies — permanent groups of soldiers — stationed among colonists during peacetime, and cutting off their trade with other countries.

By stacking up all these grievances, Jefferson built a case.
stimulus.content_text: A List of Complaints Against the King The Declaration didn't just share big ideas. It also listed specific grievances — complaints, like a detailed letter explaining exactly what someone did wrong. Jefferson accused King George III of imposing taxes without the colonists' agreement. He accused the king of shutting down colonial legislatures, the elected groups that made local laws — imagine someone canceling your student council and making all the decisions themselves. The Declaration also charged the king with keeping standing armies — permanent groups of soldiers — stationed among colonists during peacetime, and cutting off their trade with other countries. By stacking up all these grievances, Jefferson built a case.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Jefferson do after listing all the grievances against King George III?

choices.text: He asked the king to change the laws
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions Jefferson asking the king to change any laws — that idea isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: He created a new student council
choices.feedback: The student council was just an example the author used to explain what a legislature is — Jefferson didn't create a new one.

choices.text: He built a case against the king
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'By stacking up all these grievances, Jefferson built a case,' meaning all those complaints worked together to make a strong argument against the king.

choices.text: He started trading with other countries
choices.feedback: Cutting off trade was something the king did to the colonists, and it was one of Jefferson's complaints, not something Jefferson did himself.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Not Jefferson's Words Alone

You might think Jefferson wrote the Declaration entirely by himself. Actually, Congress debated his draft and made many changes — some that Jefferson did not like at all. One of the biggest changes was removing a passage that attacked the slave trade. Several southern delegates, whose wealth depended on enslaved labor, demanded it be cut.

This revealed a painful contradiction at the heart of the Declaration. It proclaimed that all people are created equal, yet many of the men who signed it enslaved other human beings. Women, too, were denied equal rights in practice. The Declaration's promise was enormous, but in 1776, it did not yet apply to everyone.
stimulus.content_text: Not Jefferson's Words Alone You might think Jefferson wrote the Declaration entirely by himself. Actually, Congress debated his draft and made many changes — some that Jefferson did not like at all. One of the biggest changes was removing a passage that attacked the slave trade. Several southern delegates, whose wealth depended on enslaved labor, demanded it be cut. This revealed a painful contradiction at the heart of the Declaration. It proclaimed that all people are created equal, yet many of the men who signed it enslaved other human beings. Women, too, were denied equal rights in practice. The Declaration's promise was enormous, but in 1776, it did not yet apply to everyone.

prompt._: How were enslaved people and women similar in 1776, based on the section?

choices.text: Both helped Congress revise Jefferson's draft.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only mentions southern delegates making changes to Jefferson's draft — not enslaved people or women.

choices.text: Both were fully included in the Declaration's promise.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the Declaration's promise 'did not yet apply to everyone,' which means neither enslaved people nor women were fully included.

choices.text: Both were denied equal rights in practice.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that women 'were denied equal rights in practice,' and enslaved people were not free, showing both groups were treated unequally.

choices.text: Both gained freedom when the passage was removed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because removing the passage about the slave trade actually helped protect slavery, and it did not give freedom to anyone.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Declaration, Not a Victory

Here's something that surprises many people: the Declaration of Independence did not actually free the colonies. It announced their intention to be free. The war with Britain raged on for seven more years, until 1783.

Jefferson later became the third President of the United States, serving from 1801 to 1809. He doubled the country's size through the Louisiana Purchase and sent Lewis and Clark to explore western lands. Yet of everything he accomplished, Jefferson was most proud of the Declaration. He asked for it to be carved on his tombstone.

And the Declaration's reach stretched far beyond America. Its words inspired the French Revolution. More than a century later, leaders of the civil rights movement in the 1960s pointed to Jefferson's own sentences and demanded that the nation finally live up to them. The Declaration did not just announce the birth of a country.

It announced their intention to be free. The war with Britain raged on for seven more years, until 1783. Jefferson later became the third President of the United States, serving from 1801 to 1809. He doubled the country's size through the Louisiana Purchase and sent Lewis and Clark to explore western lands. Yet of everything he accomplished, Jefferson was most proud of the Declaration. He asked for it to be carved on his tombstone. And the Declaration's reach stretched far beyond America. Its words inspired the French Revolution. More than a century later, leaders of the civil rights movement in the 1960s pointed to Jefferson's own sentences and demanded that the nation finally live up to them. The Declaration did not just announce the birth of a country.

prompt._: According to the text, which phrase best describes the Declaration of Independence?

choices.text: An official agreement that ended the war with Britain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Declaration did not end the war — the passage tells us the war continued for seven more years until 1783.

choices.text: A legal document that immediately freed the American colonies.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the Declaration did NOT actually free the colonies — it only announced their intention to be free.

choices.text: An announcement of the colonies' intention to be free.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses almost these exact words, saying the Declaration 'announced their intention to be free.'

choices.text: A powerful message that started the French Revolution overseas.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because inspiring the French Revolution was just one effect the Declaration had, not a description of what the document itself was.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Thomas Jefferson's first draft of the Declaration different from the final version?

choices.text: He wrote the first draft in two weeks, but the final version took seven years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the seven years mentioned in the article refers to how long the war with Britain lasted, not how long it took to write the Declaration.

choices.text: He wrote the first draft with John Adams, but the final version used Locke's words.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jefferson used John Locke's ideas for inspiration, but he wrote his own words — he didn't copy Locke's sentences.

choices.text: He wrote the first draft by himself, but the final version included changes by Congress.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Jefferson wrote the first draft alone, but Congress debated it and made many changes before it became the final version.

choices.text: He wrote the first draft to start the war, but the final version ended it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the Declaration announced the colonies' intention to be free, and the war went on for seven more years after it was written.

prompt._: The author uses cause and effect to explain the power of the Declaration of Independence. Which statement best describes a cause-and-effect connection made in the article?

choices.text: Because Jefferson rarely spoke during debates, the king decided to keep standing armies in the colonies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never links Jefferson being quiet during debates to the king's decision to keep standing armies in the colonies.

choices.text: Because Jefferson wrote that all people are equal, civil rights leaders used his words to demand rights.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that civil rights leaders in the 1960s used Jefferson's words about equality to demand rights, showing a clear cause-and-effect link.

choices.text: Because Congress changed parts of Jefferson's draft, the war with Great Britain lasted seven more years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never connects Congress changing Jefferson's draft to how long the war with Great Britain lasted.

choices.text: Because Jefferson became the third U.S. president, leaders in France decided to start a new revolution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the Declaration's words inspired the French Revolution, not that Jefferson becoming president caused it.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best summarizes the history of the Declaration of Independence as described in the article?

choices.text: Jefferson wrote the final document completely alone, the king read the complaints, and the colonies became free.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says Congress made many changes Jefferson didn't like, and signing the Declaration did not immediately free the colonies since the war continued for seven more years.

choices.text: Jefferson wrote a draft using older ideas, Congress changed it, and the document inspired future groups.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Jefferson borrowed ideas from Locke, Congress edited his draft, and the Declaration later inspired the French Revolution and the civil rights movement.

choices.text: Congress changed the written draft, Jefferson used older ideas to fix it, and the war immediately ended.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — Jefferson used older ideas first when writing his draft, and then Congress made changes afterward.

choices.text: The king created unfair rules for the colonies, Jefferson fought in the war, and America won freedom.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jefferson wrote the Declaration rather than fighting in the war, and the article never says the king's rules directly led to Jefferson going to battle.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the American colonists in 1776 and the civil rights leaders in the 1960s similar?

choices.text: They both used the Declaration to demand their own rights.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says the colonists used the Declaration to claim independence, and that civil rights leaders in the 1960s used Jefferson's words to demand equal rights.

choices.text: They both explored unknown western lands to expand the country.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because exploring western lands refers to Lewis and Clark, which was something Jefferson did as president — not something the colonists or civil rights leaders did.

choices.text: They both wrote new legal documents to change the local laws.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because neither group is described in the passage as writing new legal documents to change local laws.

choices.text: They both fought a long seven-year war against Great Britain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the colonists fought a seven-year war against Great Britain — the civil rights leaders in the 1960s were not fighting Britain at all.

assessments.title: Benjamin Franklin: Diplomat and Inventor

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Benjamin Franklin: The Man Who Won France for America

A Fur Cap in a Room Full of Wigs

Picture a seventy-year-old man walking into a glittering French palace. Every person around him wears a powdered white wig, silk stockings, and a coat trimmed with gold thread. But this man wears a simple brown suit and a fur cap made from marten skin. He looks like he wandered in from the American woods. The French crowd goes wild. His name is Benjamin Franklin, and he is about to pull off the most important deal in American history.
stimulus.content_text: Benjamin Franklin: The Man Who Won France for AmericaA Fur Cap in a Room Full of Wigs Picture a seventy-year-old man walking into a glittering French palace. Every person around him wears a powdered white wig, silk stockings, and a coat trimmed with gold thread. But this man wears a simple brown suit and a fur cap made from marten skin. He looks like he wandered in from the American woods. The French crowd goes wild. His name is Benjamin Franklin, and he is about to pull off the most important deal in American history.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Benjamin Franklin's clothing different from what the French people wore?

choices.text: He wore a powdered wig instead of a fur cap
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it has it backwards — Franklin was the one wearing the fur cap, and the French crowd wore the powdered wigs.

choices.text: He wore a fur cap instead of a powdered wig
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states the French wore powdered white wigs while Franklin wore a fur cap made from marten skin.

choices.text: He wore silk stockings instead of a brown suit
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because silk stockings were worn by the French people, not by Franklin, who wore a simple brown suit.

choices.text: He wore a gold coat instead of a silk one
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Franklin wore a simple brown suit, and it was the French people whose coats were trimmed with gold thread.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why America Needed Help

By 1776, the American colonies were at war with Britain, one of the most powerful nations on Earth. The Continental Army was short on soldiers, ships, money, and weapons. American leaders knew a hard truth: they probably could not win this war alone. They needed a powerful ally — a nation willing to fight alongside them. So they sent Franklin across the Atlantic Ocean to ask France for help.

Franklin was already one of the most famous people in the world. He had been a printer, a writer known for his witty sayings that Americans still quote today, and a brilliant scientist. He invented bifocal glasses, the lightning rod for protecting buildings, and a better heating stove called the Franklin stove. The French admired his scientific mind and saw him as a symbol of America's bold, independent spirit.
stimulus.content_text: Why America Needed Help By 1776, the American colonies were at war with Britain, one of the most powerful nations on Earth. The Continental Army was short on soldiers, ships, money, and weapons. American leaders knew a hard truth: they probably could not win this war alone. They needed a powerful ally — a nation willing to fight alongside them. So they sent Franklin across the Atlantic Ocean to ask France for help. Franklin was already one of the most famous people in the world. He had been a printer, a writer known for his witty sayings that Americans still quote today, and a brilliant scientist. He invented bifocal glasses, the lightning rod for protecting buildings, and a better heating stove called the Franklin stove. The French admired his scientific mind and saw him as a symbol of America's bold, independent spirit.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did American leaders choose Franklin to go to France for help?

choices.text: because he was the leader of the Continental Army
choices.feedback: Franklin was not the army's leader — the passage describes him as a printer, writer, and scientist.

choices.text: because the French already admired and respected him
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says the French admired Franklin's scientific mind and saw him as a symbol of America's spirit.

choices.text: because he had invented ships and weapons for war
choices.feedback: Franklin's inventions listed in the passage are bifocal glasses, a lightning rod, and a heating stove — nothing related to ships or weapons.

choices.text: because he had traveled to France many times before
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions Franklin traveling to France before, so we have no evidence for this.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Charm as a Weapon

Franklin was a diplomat — a person sent by one country to work with the government of another, the way a translator works between two languages. His job was to convince France to support America with troops, ships, and money.

Franklin used his fame like a tool. That fur cap? It was a deliberate choice. European aristocrats — wealthy people born into powerful, high-ranking families — expected everyone to dress in expensive clothing. Franklin dressed plainly on purpose. The French public saw him as a man of the people. He attended parties and visited salons, which were gatherings in private homes where important people discussed art, science, and politics. Everywhere he went, Franklin charmed the room. Behind his friendly smile, though, he was working every conversation toward one goal: French support for America.
stimulus.content_text: Charm as a Weapon Franklin was a diplomat — a person sent by one country to work with the government of another, the way a translator works between two languages. His job was to convince France to support America with troops, ships, and money. Franklin used his fame like a tool. That fur cap? It was a deliberate choice. European aristocrats — wealthy people born into powerful, high-ranking families — expected everyone to dress in expensive clothing. Franklin dressed plainly on purpose. The French public saw him as a man of the people. He attended parties and visited salons, which were gatherings in private homes where important people discussed art, science, and politics. Everywhere he went, Franklin charmed the room. Behind his friendly smile, though, he was working every conversation toward one goal: French support for America.

prompt._: According to the text, what does a diplomat do after being sent by one country?

choices.text: Wear very expensive and fancy clothing.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Franklin deliberately dressed plainly, and wearing expensive clothing is what European aristocrats expected, not what diplomats do.

choices.text: Send troops and ships to help America.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because sending troops and ships was what Franklin wanted France to do for America, not the job description of a diplomat.

choices.text: Discuss art and science in private homes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because visiting salons to discuss art and science was something Franklin did as a strategy, not the definition of what a diplomat does.

choices.text: Work with the government of another.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines a diplomat as 'a person sent by one country to work with the government of another.'

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
France Had Its Own Reasons

You might think France wanted to help America because the French believed in liberty. But France and Britain had been enemies for centuries. French leaders saw a golden opportunity: if America broke free, Britain would lose valuable colonies and grow weaker. So France's decision was not just generous — it was strategic.

Still, French leaders were cautious. If they backed America and America lost, Britain might punish France with retaliation — a strike back in revenge, like a bigger kid hitting you after you helped their opponent in a game. France wanted proof that Americans could actually win.

That proof came in 1777 at the Battle of Saratoga in New York, where American forces surrounded a large British army and forced nearly 6,000 soldiers to surrender. When news reached Paris, the French government finally agreed to an alliance.
stimulus.content_text: France Had Its Own Reasons You might think France wanted to help America because the French believed in liberty. But France and Britain had been enemies for centuries. French leaders saw a golden opportunity: if America broke free, Britain would lose valuable colonies and grow weaker. So France's decision was not just generous — it was strategic. Still, French leaders were cautious. If they backed America and America lost, Britain might punish France with retaliation — a strike back in revenge, like a bigger kid hitting you after you helped their opponent in a game. France wanted proof that Americans could actually win. That proof came in 1777 at the Battle of Saratoga in New York, where American forces surrounded a large British army and forced nearly 6,000 soldiers to surrender. When news reached Paris, the French government finally agreed to an alliance.

prompt._: To help explain the word retaliation, the author compares Britain to 'a bigger kid.' Based on the text, what do Britain and the bigger kid have in common?

choices.text: They both strike back at someone who helped an opponent.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both Britain and the bigger kid strike back at someone who helped their opponent — that's exactly what the word retaliation means in the passage.

choices.text: They both decide to be generous to a struggling neighbor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being generous is the opposite of retaliation — the bigger kid and Britain are threatening to hurt someone, not help them.

choices.text: They both lose a game to a much smaller and weaker team.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither Britain nor the bigger kid loses a game in the comparison — the author is talking about hitting back, not losing.

choices.text: They both surrender when they are surrounded by enemies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because surrendering when surrounded describes what happened to the British army at Saratoga, not what the bigger kid does in the comparison.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Alliance That Won the War

France's alliance with America in 1778 changed everything. France sent thousands of soldiers, including an army commanded by the Comte de Rochambeau. French warships challenged British control of the seas. French money paid for supplies, weapons, and uniforms that the struggling American army desperately needed. Without French support, the American Revolution might well have failed.

Franklin remained in France for nearly nine years, serving as America's ambassador. He negotiated loans, arranged shipments of military supplies, and kept France committed to the cause. When the war ended, Franklin helped negotiate the Treaty of Paris in 1783. Britain officially recognized American independence and gave up its claims to the territory stretching from the Appalachian Mountains to the Mississippi River — land that would eventually become states like Ohio, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

France sent thousands of soldiers, including an army commanded by the Comte de Rochambeau. French warships challenged British control of the seas. French money paid for supplies, weapons, and uniforms that the struggling American army desperately needed. Without French support, the American Revolution might well have failed. Franklin remained in France for nearly nine years, serving as America's ambassador. He negotiated loans, arranged shipments of military supplies, and kept France committed to the cause. When the war ended, Franklin helped negotiate the Treaty of Paris in 1783. Britain officially recognized American independence and gave up its claims to the territory stretching from the Appalachian Mountains to the Mississippi River — land that would eventually become states like Ohio, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

prompt._: According to the text, what was the "territory" that Britain gave up?

choices.text: Land that the French army claimed
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the French army never claimed this territory — the land was given up by Britain to America.

choices.text: Land that belonged to Rochambeau
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Rochambeau was a French military commander, and the passage never says he owned or claimed any land.

choices.text: Land that became states like Ohio
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states the territory would 'eventually become states like Ohio, Kentucky, and Tennessee.'

choices.text: Land that French warships guarded
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage mentions French warships challenging British sea control, not guarding any territory on land.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Founder Who Was Never President

Franklin returned home as a hero. Despite his age, he served as a delegate — a person chosen to represent others and speak on their behalf — at the Constitutional Convention in 1787. His calm wisdom helped settle arguments between younger, more hot-tempered leaders. He died in 1790 at eighty-four, mourned by people in both America and France.

After all, his face is on the hundred-dollar bill. But Franklin never served as president. His greatest roles were as a scientist, inventor, printer, writer, and the diplomat who convinced a foreign king to bet on a brand-new nation. The next time you see that face on a hundred-dollar bill, remember: he earned his place there not from the president's chair, but from a wooden seat in a French palace, wearing a fur cap and winning a war with words.

Despite his age, he served as a delegate — a person chosen to represent others and speak on their behalf — at the Constitutional Convention in 1787. His calm wisdom helped settle arguments between younger, more hot-tempered leaders. He died in 1790 at eighty-four, mourned by people in both America and France. After all, his face is on the hundred-dollar bill. But Franklin never served as president. His greatest roles were as a scientist, inventor, printer, writer, and the diplomat who convinced a foreign king to bet on a brand-new nation. The next time you see that face on a hundred-dollar bill, remember: he earned his place there not from the president's chair, but from a wooden seat in a French palace, wearing a fur cap and winning a war with words.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the idea that Benjamin Franklin helped people solve disagreements?

choices.text: His calm wisdom helped settle arguments.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Franklin's calm wisdom helped settle arguments, which is exactly the same as helping people solve disagreements.

choices.text: His face is on the hundred-dollar bill.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being on the hundred-dollar bill is about how he is remembered and honored, not about helping people work out their differences.

choices.text: He returned home as a hero.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being called a hero tells us people admired Franklin, but it doesn't say anything about him helping people solve disagreements.

choices.text: He died at eighty-four in 1790.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because when and how old Franklin was when he died has nothing to do with solving disagreements.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did the situation for the Americans contrast before and after the French alliance?

choices.text: They relied on Benjamin Franklin to fight at first, but later they relied on French army commanders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Franklin never fought in battles — his job was to be a diplomat who used conversation and charm to win French support.

choices.text: They struggled to win any battles at first, but later they won the Battle of Saratoga in New York.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Battle of Saratoga actually happened before the French alliance, not after — it was the victory that convinced France to join America's side.

choices.text: They lacked money and weapons at first, but later they received French funds for military supplies.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states Americans lacked money and weapons before the alliance, and that French money later paid for the military supplies they needed.

choices.text: They fought only on the land at first, but later they built American warships to control the seas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says French warships challenged British sea power, not American-built warships.

prompt._: According to the text, why were the American colonies able to win the war against Britain?

choices.text: The American army surrounded the British in New York, which forced the French government to surrender its land.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the British army — not the French — that was surrounded and surrendered at Saratoga, and that victory convinced France to support America, not surrender land.

choices.text: Benjamin Franklin convinced France to form an alliance, which gave the Americans needed soldiers and supplies.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that France's soldiers, warships, and money changed everything for the American army, and Franklin was the one who made that alliance happen.

choices.text: The French aristocrats wore expensive clothing to parties, which showed the British that France was very powerful.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the detail about aristocrats wearing expensive clothing is only used to explain why Franklin's simple fur cap stood out, and has nothing to do with why America won the war.

choices.text: Benjamin Franklin invented the lightning rod and stove, which protected the American troops from harsh weather.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Franklin's inventions like the lightning rod and stove made him famous in France, but the passage never says they helped protect or benefit American troops in battle.

prompt._: How does the author organize the events of Benjamin Franklin's service to America in the article?

choices.text: By describing his journey to France, his success in forming an alliance, and his later work at home.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article really does go in this order: Franklin's journey to France, then the alliance being formed, then his later work at home at the Constitutional Convention.

choices.text: By describing his later work at home, his journey to France, and his success in forming an alliance.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article does NOT start with his work at home — it starts with Franklin arriving in France, not with what he did later in America.

choices.text: By describing his victory in a battle, his success in forming an alliance, and his later work at home.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Franklin did not win a battle — it was American soldiers who won at Saratoga, and Franklin wasn't there fighting.

choices.text: By describing his journey to France, his later work at home, and his success in forming an alliance.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts 'his later work at home' in the middle, but the article actually puts that part at the end, after the alliance is formed.

prompt._: Which comparison best describes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: America's fight for freedom was much harder than France's war against Britain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never compares America's fight for freedom to France's wars — it stays focused on Franklin's diplomatic mission.

choices.text: France's wealthy leaders were more helpful to the Americans than its soldiers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly shows that French soldiers, warships, and money were all very important to helping America win the Revolution.

choices.text: Franklin's scientific inventions were more important than his political deals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Franklin's inventions helped make him famous, the article's main focus is on his work as a diplomat in France, not his scientific discoveries.

choices.text: Franklin's simple style and clever words were as powerful as a fighting army.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Franklin's charm, plain clothing, and clever words were the key tools that convinced France to join America's side and help win the war.

assessments.title: The Battle of Yorktown

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Trap at Yorktown: How the Revolution Was Won

A Trap Closes Shut

On a warm October morning in 1781, British General Charles Cornwallis stood inside the battered town of Yorktown, Virginia, and realized he had nowhere to go. Cannonballs had smashed the buildings around him into piles of broken brick. Six long years of war were about to end — not with another retreat, but with a surrender.

The answer involves a secret march, a massive fleet of French warships, and one of the boldest tricks of the entire American Revolution.
stimulus.content_text: The Trap at Yorktown: How the Revolution Was WonA Trap Closes Shut On a warm October morning in 1781, British General Charles Cornwallis stood inside the battered town of Yorktown, Virginia, and realized he had nowhere to go. Cannonballs had smashed the buildings around him into piles of broken brick. Six long years of war were about to end — not with another retreat, but with a surrender. The answer involves a secret march, a massive fleet of French warships, and one of the boldest tricks of the entire American Revolution.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was the end of the war at Yorktown different from what had happened during the six years before it?

choices.text: It ended with a surrender instead of a retreat
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the war ended 'not with another retreat, but with a surrender,' showing that surrendering was different from what had happened before.

choices.text: It ended with a march instead of a sea fight
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions a march and a French fleet, but it never compares them to each other as the way the war ended.

choices.text: It ended with a bold trick instead of a battle
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions a bold trick as part of the story, but it never says the war ended with a trick instead of a battle.

choices.text: It ended with broken buildings instead of a win
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the broken buildings describe what Yorktown looked like, not how the ending of the war was different from before.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Cornwallis Walks Into a Trap

Earlier that year, Cornwallis had marched his British army into Virginia. He set up camp at Yorktown, a small port town on the Chesapeake Bay. From there, he expected reinforcements — fresh troops and supplies sent to help an army already fighting — to arrive by ship. Britain controlled the world's most powerful navy. Why would he worry about the sea?

But General George Washington saw an opening. Washington and his French ally, the Comte de Rochambeau, had been planning to attack the British base in New York City. When they learned Cornwallis was sitting exposed at Yorktown, they changed their plans fast. Washington marched his army over 400 miles south, from New York to Virginia, in just a few weeks. He kept the movement secret from the British for as long as possible.

You might think the Americans won the Revolution on their own. Actually, they could not have won this battle — or likely the whole war — without France. France had joined the American side partly to weaken its old rival, Britain. French soldiers marched alongside Americans, French money paid for supplies, and now the French navy was about to slam the door on Cornwallis.

From there, he expected reinforcements — fresh troops and supplies sent to help an army already fighting — to arrive by ship. Britain controlled the world's most powerful navy. Why would he worry about the sea? But General George Washington saw an opening. Washington and his French ally, the Comte de Rochambeau, had been planning to attack the British base in New York City. When they learned Cornwallis was sitting exposed at Yorktown, they changed their plans fast. Washington marched his army over 400 miles south, from New York to Virginia, in just a few weeks. He kept the movement secret from the British for as long as possible. You might think the Americans won the Revolution on their own. Actually, they could not have won this battle — or likely the whole war — without France. France had joined the American side partly to weaken its old rival, Britain. French soldiers marched alongside Americans, French money paid for supplies, and now the French navy was about to slam the door on Cornwallis.

prompt._: According to the text, why did General Washington and his French ally change their plans?

choices.text: They decided to attack the main British army base in New York City.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because attacking the British base in New York City was the plan Washington *gave up* when he heard about Cornwallis at Yorktown.

choices.text: They wanted to weaken their old rival by starting a brand new war.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because France had already joined the war on America's side — the passage says France wanted to weaken Britain, but that was their reason for joining, not for changing battle plans.

choices.text: They needed a safe port to receive fresh troops and new supplies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because waiting for reinforcements by ship was Cornwallis's plan, not Washington's.

choices.text: They learned that Cornwallis was left unprotected at Yorktown.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says they changed their plans when they learned Cornwallis was 'sitting exposed' — meaning left unprotected — at Yorktown.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The French Fleet Blocks the Escape

Admiral de Grasse sailed a powerful French fleet up from the Caribbean Sea. His warships took position at the mouth of the Chesapeake Bay, like a cork jammed into a bottle. When a British fleet arrived to rescue Cornwallis, de Grasse's ships drove them away in a fierce fight called the Battle of the Chesapeake. The British ships limped back to New York. Without those French ships, the entire trap would have failed.
stimulus.content_text: The French Fleet Blocks the Escape Admiral de Grasse sailed a powerful French fleet up from the Caribbean Sea. His warships took position at the mouth of the Chesapeake Bay, like a cork jammed into a bottle. When a British fleet arrived to rescue Cornwallis, de Grasse's ships drove them away in a fierce fight called the Battle of the Chesapeake. The British ships limped back to New York. Without those French ships, the entire trap would have failed.

prompt._: According to the text, what did the French warships do before the British fleet arrived?

choices.text: They rescued Cornwallis from the trap.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the French ships actually trapped Cornwallis by blocking the bay, which is the opposite of rescuing him.

choices.text: They took position at the Chesapeake Bay.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the French warships 'took position at the mouth of the Chesapeake Bay' before the British fleet arrived.

choices.text: They sailed back to the Caribbean Sea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the French fleet sailed up *from* the Caribbean Sea — they didn't go back there.

choices.text: They limped away and returned to New York.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was the British ships that limped back to New York after losing the battle, not the French ships.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Siege of Yorktown

With the French fleet controlling the water and the combined American-French army surrounding him on land, Cornwallis was stuck. The allied forces began a siege — a military strategy where you surround an enemy and slowly squeeze them until they give up. Soldiers dug trenches that crept closer and closer to the British walls. American and French cannons pounded British positions day and night.

On the night of October 14, American and French soldiers attacked two redoubts — small, heavily defended forts built to protect a larger position, like guard towers at the corners of a castle. Alexander Hamilton, a young officer who would later become the first Secretary of the Treasury, led a bayonet charge — an attack where soldiers rush forward with blades fixed to the ends of their muskets. His men captured their target in just ten minutes. Now allied cannons could fire directly into the heart of the British camp.

Cornwallis tried one last desperate move. He ordered his troops to cross the York River in small boats under cover of darkness. But a sudden storm scattered the boats and wrecked the plan. With no escape by land or sea, his army starving, sick, and battered by constant bombardment — nonstop heavy cannon fire — Cornwallis had no choice left.

The allied forces began a siege — a military strategy where you surround an enemy and slowly squeeze them until they give up. Soldiers dug trenches that crept closer and closer to the British walls. American and French cannons pounded British positions day and night. On the night of October 14, American and French soldiers attacked two redoubts — small, heavily defended forts built to protect a larger position, like guard towers at the corners of a castle. Alexander Hamilton, a young officer who would later become the first Secretary of the Treasury, led a bayonet charge — an attack where soldiers rush forward with blades fixed to the ends of their muskets. His men captured their target in just ten minutes. Now allied cannons could fire directly into the heart of the British camp. Cornwallis tried one last desperate move. He ordered his troops to cross the York River in small boats under cover of darkness. But a sudden storm scattered the boats and wrecked the plan. With no escape by land or sea, his army starving, sick, and battered by constant bombardment — nonstop heavy cannon fire — Cornwallis had no choice left.

prompt._: The passage compares redoubts to 'guard towers at the corners of a castle.' How does this comparison help the reader understand what redoubts are?

choices.text: It shows that redoubts were very tall stone buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison to castle guard towers is about their protective purpose, not about how tall or what material they were made of.

choices.text: It shows that redoubts were built along river banks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects redoubts to river banks; that detail is about Cornwallis's escape attempt, not the redoubts.

choices.text: It shows that redoubts were small protective forts
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says redoubts were small forts that protected a larger position, just like guard towers protect a castle.

choices.text: It shows that redoubts were used to store weapons
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions redoubts being used to store weapons — they were used for defense.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The World Turned Upside Down

On October 19, 1781, Cornwallis surrendered. According to legend, as British soldiers marched out to lay down their weapons, their band played a song called "The World Turned Upside Down." Whether or not that story is true, the title fit perfectly. A ragged colonial army, fighting alongside its French allies, had defeated the most powerful military force on the planet.

It's easy to assume the war ended right there. It didn't. Small skirmishes — brief, scattered fights between small groups — continued for another year. Diplomats, people who represent their country in talks with other nations, spent months working out peace terms. The official agreement, called the Treaty of Paris, was not signed until 1783. It recognized the United States as a free and independent nation.

But everyone understood that Yorktown was the moment the outcome became clear. When the news reached London, the British prime minister reportedly said, "Oh God, it is all over." A country that did not even exist ten years earlier had just forced the mightiest empire on Earth to let it go — because of a trap at a small Virginia port town, a secret march, a French fleet, and an army that refused to quit.

According to legend, as British soldiers marched out to lay down their weapons, their band played a song called "The World Turned Upside Down." Whether or not that story is true, the title fit perfectly. A ragged colonial army, fighting alongside its French allies, had defeated the most powerful military force on the planet. It's easy to assume the war ended right there. It didn't. Small skirmishes — brief, scattered fights between small groups — continued for another year. Diplomats, people who represent their country in talks with other nations, spent months working out peace terms. The official agreement, called the Treaty of Paris, was not signed until 1783. It recognized the United States as a free and independent nation. But everyone understood that Yorktown was the moment the outcome became clear. When the news reached London, the British prime minister reportedly said, "Oh God, it is all over." A country that did not even exist ten years earlier had just forced the mightiest empire on Earth to let it go — because of a trap at a small Virginia port town, a secret march, a French fleet, and an army that refused to quit.

prompt._: According to the text, what are diplomats?

choices.text: People who fight in brief, scattered battles between small groups.
choices.feedback: This describes skirmishes — brief, scattered fights between small groups — not diplomats.

choices.text: People who represent their country in talks with other nations.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly defines diplomats as 'people who represent their country in talks with other nations.'

choices.text: People who play instruments while defeated soldiers march away.
choices.feedback: This describes what a band does as soldiers march away, which has nothing to do with diplomats.

choices.text: People who command the most powerful military forces on the planet.
choices.feedback: This describes powerful military commanders, not diplomats, who work through talking and negotiating, not fighting.

prompt._: Which statement best shows the contrast between what Cornwallis expected at Yorktown and what actually happened?

choices.text: He expected the war to end at once, but small fights continued for another year.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes events after the surrender and has nothing to do with what Cornwallis expected when he set up camp at Yorktown.

choices.text: He expected French soldiers to leave, but they joined Hamilton in the attack.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Hamilton led American soldiers in the bayonet charge, not French soldiers who had been expected to leave.

choices.text: He expected help to arrive by sea, but he was cut off by the French fleet.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Cornwallis expected reinforcements by ship, but the French fleet blocked the bay and drove away the British rescue ships, trapping him.

choices.text: He expected a quick attack on New York, but he faced a long march to Virginia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was General Washington who marched from New York to Virginia, not Cornwallis — Cornwallis was already camped at Yorktown.

prompt._: Based on the article, why was France's help a major cause of the American victory at Yorktown?

choices.text: France blocked the British escape by sea and provided soldiers who fought on land.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the French fleet blocked the British from escaping or getting help by sea, and French soldiers fought on land alongside the Americans during the siege.

choices.text: France convinced Cornwallis to surrender by sending diplomats to negotiate peace terms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because France did not send diplomats to convince Cornwallis to surrender — it was the military trap of cannons, trenches, and blocked escape routes that forced him to give up.

choices.text: France paid Britain to withdraw its army from Yorktown and end the war early.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says France paid Britain any money to leave — Britain was defeated militarily, not bought off.

choices.text: France helped Washington keep his march secret so the British never reached Yorktown.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Washington did keep his march secret, the article does not say France helped with that secrecy, and British forces did eventually reach Yorktown — that's where the battle happened.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best describes how the article organizes the steps that led to the British surrender at Yorktown?

choices.text: Cornwallis surrenders, the Treaty of Paris is signed, and the siege begins
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it starts with the surrender, which was the final outcome, and then jumps around in time instead of showing the steps that led up to it.

choices.text: The siege begins, the French fleet arrives, and Washington marches south
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the siege first, but the siege could only happen after Washington marched south and the French fleet blocked the bay.

choices.text: The French fleet blocks the bay, Cornwallis crosses the river, and France joins the war
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the timeline — France joining the war happened long before Yorktown, and Cornwallis's river crossing was a desperate escape attempt during the siege, not a step that set up the trap.

choices.text: Washington marches south, the French fleet blocks the bay, and the siege begins
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the article: Washington's secret march south, then the French fleet blocking the bay, then the siege closing the trap around Cornwallis.

prompt._: Compared to the British, who had the world's most powerful military, what main idea does the article develop about how the Americans were able to win at Yorktown?

choices.text: The Americans won because they had more soldiers and better weapons than the British.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article describes the Americans as a 'ragged colonial army' facing the world's most powerful military, so they did NOT have more soldiers or better weapons.

choices.text: The Americans won by combining clever planning with help from French allies.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article shows that Washington's secret march (clever planning) combined with the French fleet and French soldiers (allied help) together made the trap at Yorktown work.

choices.text: The Americans won because the British chose to surrender without a real fight.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Cornwallis fought hard and even tried a desperate nighttime river crossing to escape before he was finally forced to surrender.

choices.text: The Americans won because storms and bad weather destroyed the British army.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while a storm did ruin one escape attempt, the main reasons Cornwallis was trapped were the French fleet blocking the sea and the American-French army surrounding him on land.

assessments.title: Peace and a New Beginning

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
After the Last Battle: Building a Nation from Scratch

Peace on Paper

On September 3, 1783, three Americans sat at a table in Paris and signed their names on a document that changed everything. You might think the United States became a real country back in 1776, when the Declaration of Independence was signed. But declaring yourself free and actually winning that freedom are very different things. It took years of bloody fighting and then months of tough talking in a French city to make independence real.

Those three Americans were diplomats — people sent to represent their country in dealings with foreign governments, like messengers trusted to speak for an entire nation. John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, and John Jay worked together to shape the Treaty of Paris. Britain officially recognized the United States as an independent nation. Even better, Britain gave up its claim to a huge stretch of land — from the Atlantic coast all the way to the Mississippi River, and from the Great Lakes down to Florida. Overnight, the new country became enormous, covering wilderness that most Americans had never seen.

John Adams, a brilliant lawyer from Massachusetts, fought hard for every detail. He insisted that American fishermen keep their rights to fish off the coast of Newfoundland. He demanded that British soldiers leave every fort on American soil. Adams would later become the second President of the United States.

You might think the United States became a real country back in 1776, when the Declaration of Independence was signed. But declaring yourself free and actually winning that freedom are very different things. It took years of bloody fighting and then months of tough talking in a French city to make independence real. Those three Americans were diplomats — people sent to represent their country in dealings with foreign governments, like messengers trusted to speak for an entire nation. John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, and John Jay worked together to shape the Treaty of Paris. Britain officially recognized the United States as an independent nation. Even better, Britain gave up its claim to a huge stretch of land — from the Atlantic coast all the way to the Mississippi River, and from the Great Lakes down to Florida. Overnight, the new country became enormous, covering wilderness that most Americans had never seen. John Adams, a brilliant lawyer from Massachusetts, fought hard for every detail. He insisted that American fishermen keep their rights to fish off the coast of Newfoundland. He demanded that British soldiers leave every fort on American soil. Adams would later become the second President of the United States.

prompt._: According to the text, what contrast does the author make about 1776 and 1783?

choices.text: 1776 was when diplomats met, but 1783 was when soldiers fought.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says diplomats met in 1783, not 1776, and it doesn't say soldiers fought in 1776 as part of this contrast.

choices.text: 1776 was when Britain ruled, but 1783 was when France ruled.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions France ruling the United States — France was just the city where the treaty was signed.

choices.text: 1776 was when the war ended, but 1783 was when it began.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 1776 was when independence was declared, not when the war ended, and the war ended closer to 1783.

choices.text: 1776 was when freedom was declared, but 1783 was when it was won.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly contrasts declaring freedom in 1776 with actually winning and securing that freedom through the 1783 Treaty of Paris.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Government Too Weak to Stand

Here's the surprising part. After winning the war, Americans did not set up the government you know today. Not even close. Their first attempt was a document called the Articles of Confederation — the nation's first constitution. It created a central government so weak it could barely do anything. Congress could not collect taxes. It could not control trade between states. It could not even enforce its own laws. Imagine a school principal who could make rules but had no way to make anyone follow them. That was the new American government.

It's easy to assume that Americans celebrated together after the war, united and happy. The truth was messier. The Continental Congress had borrowed mountains of money to pay for the war, and now the debt — the total amount owed — was staggering. States argued over borders and trade. Soldiers who had risked their lives came home to find the government had no money to pay them.
stimulus.content_text: A Government Too Weak to Stand Here's the surprising part. After winning the war, Americans did not set up the government you know today. Not even close. Their first attempt was a document called the Articles of Confederation — the nation's first constitution. It created a central government so weak it could barely do anything. Congress could not collect taxes. It could not control trade between states. It could not even enforce its own laws. Imagine a school principal who could make rules but had no way to make anyone follow them. That was the new American government. It's easy to assume that Americans celebrated together after the war, united and happy. The truth was messier. The Continental Congress had borrowed mountains of money to pay for the war, and now the debt — the total amount owed — was staggering. States argued over borders and trade. Soldiers who had risked their lives came home to find the government had no money to pay them.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did soldiers come home without being paid?

choices.text: The states refused to let Congress make any laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Congress couldn't enforce its own laws, not that states stopped Congress from making laws.

choices.text: The government spent all its money on border fights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage does mention states arguing over borders, it never says the government spent its money on those arguments.

choices.text: The soldiers chose to return before the war was over.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions soldiers leaving early or returning before the war was finished.

choices.text: The government did not have enough money to pay them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the government had no money to pay the soldiers after borrowing so much to fund the war.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Hamilton Builds a Financial System

One person who stepped up to face this crisis was Alexander Hamilton. Born in the Caribbean, Hamilton had come to New York as a teenager with little money and big ambitions. During the Revolution, he served as George Washington's chief aide, managing letters and organizing military supplies. At Yorktown, he personally led a daring nighttime charge against a British redoubt — a small, heavily defended fort — helping seal the American victory.

After the war, President Washington chose Hamilton as the first Secretary of the Treasury. Hamilton created a national bank, designed a system for collecting taxes, and made a plan to pay off the country's massive war debts. His ideas sparked fierce arguments. But they gave the young nation economic stability and made other countries trust that America could manage its own money. Without Hamilton's financial system, the new republic — a government where citizens elect leaders instead of being ruled by a king — might have collapsed under the weight of its debts.

At Yorktown, he personally led a daring nighttime charge against a British redoubt — a small, heavily defended fort — helping seal the American victory. After the war, President Washington chose Hamilton as the first Secretary of the Treasury. Hamilton created a national bank, designed a system for collecting taxes, and made a plan to pay off the country's massive war debts. His ideas sparked fierce arguments. But they gave the young nation economic stability and made other countries trust that America could manage its own money. Without Hamilton's financial system, the new republic — a government where citizens elect leaders instead of being ruled by a king — might have collapsed under the weight of its debts.

prompt._: According to the text, which event happened to Alexander Hamilton after the war?

choices.text: He led a charge against a fort.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because leading the charge against the fort happened at Yorktown, which was a battle during the war, not something that happened after it ended.

choices.text: He served as Washington's chief aide.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Hamilton served as Washington's chief aide during the Revolution, which means it happened while the war was still going on, not after.

choices.text: He was born in the Caribbean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Hamilton was born in the Caribbean long before the war even started — it was one of the first things mentioned about his early life.

choices.text: He became Secretary of the Treasury.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that 'after the war,' Washington chose Hamilton to be the first Secretary of the Treasury.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Shays' Rebellion Sounds the Alarm

By 1786, the weakness of the Articles of Confederation was causing real danger. Farmers across Massachusetts were drowning in taxes imposed by their state government. A former soldier named Daniel Shays had seen enough. He gathered hundreds of desperate farmers, and they attacked courthouses to stop judges from taking their property.

But it sent a shock through the country. If the national government could not keep order or protect its own citizens, the whole experiment in self-government could fall apart.
stimulus.content_text: Shays' Rebellion Sounds the Alarm By 1786, the weakness of the Articles of Confederation was causing real danger. Farmers across Massachusetts were drowning in taxes imposed by their state government. A former soldier named Daniel Shays had seen enough. He gathered hundreds of desperate farmers, and they attacked courthouses to stop judges from taking their property. But it sent a shock through the country. If the national government could not keep order or protect its own citizens, the whole experiment in self-government could fall apart.

prompt._: The text describes the nation as an "experiment in self-government." Based on the passage, how are the new government and an experiment similar?

choices.text: Both are systems that are controlled by local judges.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the government or an experiment is controlled by local judges — judges actually appear as people the farmers were fighting against.

choices.text: Both are laws that force citizens to pay taxes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because taxes are mentioned in the passage, but the word 'experiment' is used to describe whether the government will survive, not to describe a law or tax.

choices.text: Both are events that cause danger for poor farmers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage does mention danger for farmers, that's not what makes the government similar to an experiment — experiments are about testing something to see if it works.

choices.text: Both are tests that could fail and fall apart.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage warns that the self-government 'could fall apart,' showing it was being tested just like an experiment that might succeed or fail.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A New Plan in Philadelphia

These crises pushed American leaders to act. In the summer of 1787, delegates from twelve states gathered in Philadelphia. Their job was bold: replace the Articles of Confederation with something stronger. The document they wrote — the Constitution — created a government with real power. It could levy taxes, meaning it could require citizens to pay money to fund the government, the way your school collects supplies for a class project. It could regulate trade, raise armies, and enforce laws. At the same time, it balanced power between the national government and the states, and it protected individual rights.

The American Revolution was not just a war won with muskets and cannons. The soldiers at Yorktown answered part of that question. They answered the hardest part of all: what comes after you win.
