The Founders split power among three branches of government on purpose. They knew that giving too much power to one person or group was dangerous. Even well-meaning leaders can become tyrants. So they built in protection. Article I is the longest. It creates Congress, the legislative branch — the part of government that writes and passes laws. Congress has the power to collect taxes, control trade, declare war, and make new laws. Article I also explains how citizens choose their representatives. Article II creates the executive branch, led by the President. Think of the executive branch as the part of government that carries out the laws Congress writes. This article describes how the President is chosen through the Electoral College, a special voting system where each state gets a number of votes equal to its total members in Congress — that means its representatives in the House plus its two senators. It also explains impeachment — the process for removing a president from office if that president breaks the law or abuses power. Article III creates the judicial branch, led by the Supreme Court. Federal judges interpret the Constitution and settle disagreements about what the law means. Imagine a referee making a call during a game — the judicial branch decides whether actions follow the rules of the Constitution. The remaining articles handle other important details. Article IV requires states to respect each other's laws and court decisions. Article V describes how to change the Constitution. Article VI declares that the Constitution is the supreme law of the land, meaning no state law can contradict it. Article VII describes how the Constitution was officially approved, or ratified.

prompt._: Based on the text, why did the Founders split the government's power into three branches?

choices.text: To give the Supreme Court the power to write and pass new laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says writing and passing laws is the job of Congress (the legislative branch), not the Supreme Court.

choices.text: To build a special voting system called the Electoral College.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Electoral College is a system for electing the President described in Article II, not the reason the Founders created three branches of government.

choices.text: To stop one person or group from getting too much control.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the Founders split power on purpose to prevent any one person or group from having too much control, since that kind of power is dangerous.

choices.text: To require all the states to respect each other's court decisions.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because respecting other states' laws and court decisions is the job of Article IV, and has nothing to do with why power was split into three branches.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Document Built to Change

Here's the surprising part: even though the Constitution is the highest law in the country, the Founders designed it so Americans could change it. You might think "supreme law" means the rules are locked forever. But the Founders knew the world would change in ways they could not predict. So Article V includes an amendment process — a way to add changes, like updating the rules of a game when players discover something unfair.

An amendment needs approval from two-thirds of Congress and three-fourths of the state legislatures. The Founders did not want anyone changing the nation's basic rules on a whim.

The Constitution has been amended twenty-seven times since 1788. The first ten amendments are called the Bill of Rights. Many people assume these were part of the original Constitution, written at the same time. They were not. The original document was ratified in 1788 without them. Many Americans worried that the Constitution did not do enough to protect individual freedoms, like the right to speak freely or practice their religion. So in 1791, the Bill of Rights was added.

Later amendments abolished slavery, gave African Americans the right to vote, and extended voting rights to women. Each amendment marks a moment when Americans decided their government needed to grow fairer.

You might think "supreme law" means the rules are locked forever. But the Founders knew the world would change in ways they could not predict. So Article V includes an amendment process — a way to add changes, like updating the rules of a game when players discover something unfair. prompt._: According to the text, which event happened before the Bill of Rights was added in 1791?

choices.text: Women were given the right to vote.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because women's voting rights came much later, after 1791, not before the Bill of Rights.

choices.text: Congress updated the rules of a game.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because updating a game's rules is just an example the author uses to explain what an amendment is — it's not something that really happened.

choices.text: The original Constitution was ratified.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the Constitution was ratified in 1788, which is before the Bill of Rights was added in 1791.

choices.text: The Founders predicted future changes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Founders knew they could NOT predict future changes, so they didn't predict them.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Four Pages That Still Matter

The Founders divided power among three branches, created checks and balances so no branch could overpower the others, and reserved important rights for the states and the people. They built a government strong enough to hold a nation together but flexible enough to correct its own mistakes. Every time you hear about a new law being debated, a president being held accountable, or a court protecting someone's rights, you are watching those four handwritten pages still at work.
stimulus.content_text: Four Pages That Still Matter The Founders divided power among three branches, created checks and balances so no branch could overpower the others, and reserved important rights for the states and the people. They built a government strong enough to hold a nation together but flexible enough to correct its own mistakes. Every time you hear about a new law being debated, a president being held accountable, or a court protecting someone's rights, you are watching those four handwritten pages still at work.

prompt._: Based on the text, how does the power of one branch compare to the power of the others?

choices.text: No branch is given more power than the others.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says 'no branch could overpower the others,' meaning all branches share equal power.

choices.text: The states are given more power than the branches.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions states having reserved rights, but never says states have more power than the three branches.

choices.text: One branch is given more power than the others.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that checks and balances were created so that NO branch could overpower the others.

choices.text: The courts are given more power than the president.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the power of courts and the president — it treats all branches as equal.

prompt._: How is the Constitution today different from when it was first written in 1787?

choices.text: It forces the states to write all their own rules.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says Article VI declares the Constitution is the supreme law of the land, meaning state laws cannot contradict it.

choices.text: It protects the rights of more groups of people.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage explains that amendments like the Bill of Rights and later changes gave rights to more groups of people who were left out when the Constitution was first written.

choices.text: It stops the government from passing any new laws.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says Congress still has the power to make new laws — that has not changed.

choices.text: It gives all the power to one single strong leader.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the Founders split power among three branches on purpose so that no one person or group would have too much power.

prompt._: According to the article, why did the Founders make it possible to add amendments to the Constitution?

choices.text: because they knew the world would change in unpredictable ways
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly states the Founders knew the world would change in unpredictable ways, so they built in the amendment process to allow updates.

choices.text: because they wanted to make changing the rules quick and easy
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Founders actually made the amendment process difficult on purpose, requiring approval from two-thirds of Congress and three-fourths of states.

choices.text: because they wanted each state to write its own laws
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says the Constitution is the supreme law of the land, meaning state laws cannot contradict it — so states don't just write their own rules.

choices.text: because the Bill of Rights was already part of the original plan
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article specifically tells us the Bill of Rights was NOT part of the original Constitution — it was added separately in 1791.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information about the parts of the Constitution across the passage?

choices.text: By explaining the introduction, then the main sections, and finally the later changes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage moves from the Preamble (introduction), to the seven articles (main sections), to the amendments like the Bill of Rights (later changes) — exactly in that order.

choices.text: By listing the newest amendments, then the main sections, and finally the introduction.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage starts with the introduction (the Preamble), not the newest amendments — that's the opposite of what the passage actually does.

choices.text: By describing the three branches, then the state powers, and finally the voting rights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the three branches are discussed, the passage covers much more, and 'state powers' and 'voting rights' are not the main organizing ideas.

choices.text: By showing the nation's problems, then the new solutions, and finally the future plans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't organize information around problems, solutions, and future plans — it follows the actual structure of the Constitution itself.

prompt._: How is the way the Founders divided power among three branches similar to the way they created the amendment process?

choices.text: Both were designed to give the President more control over the government.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both systems were designed to limit power, not give the President more of it.

choices.text: Both were designed to make sure Congress could pass laws quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the amendment process requires approval from many groups, making changes slow and difficult, not quick.

choices.text: Both were designed to let the Supreme Court change the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Supreme Court's job is to interpret the Constitution, not change it — only amendments can change it.

choices.text: Both were designed to keep any one group from having too much power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the three branches and the amendment process were carefully designed to prevent any one person or group from having too much control.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Structure of the Constitution

assessments.title: Three Branches: Separation of Powers

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Making a Constitutional Government

One Person with All the Power

In 1787, fifty-five men gathered in a stuffy room in Philadelphia with the windows nailed shut for secrecy. They had a problem. The young United States needed a stronger government, but every person in that room remembered life under British rule. The colonists had felt the king had too much power over their lives — taxing them, punishing them, and making decisions for them without their agreement. The men in that room — the Founders — refused to build that kind of government again.

Their solution? Split the power three ways so that no single person or group could ever hold it all. This idea is called separation of powers, and it became the skeleton of the United States Constitution. It is one of the most influential ideas in the history of democracy.
stimulus.content_text: Making a Constitutional GovernmentOne Person with All the Power In 1787, fifty-five men gathered in a stuffy room in Philadelphia with the windows nailed shut for secrecy. They had a problem. The young United States needed a stronger government, but every person in that room remembered life under British rule. The colonists had felt the king had too much power over their lives — taxing them, punishing them, and making decisions for them without their agreement. The men in that room — the Founders — refused to build that kind of government again. Their solution? Split the power three ways so that no single person or group could ever hold it all. This idea is called separation of powers, and it became the skeleton of the United States Constitution. It is one of the most influential ideas in the history of democracy.

prompt._: According to the text, how was the Founders' new government different from British rule under the king?

choices.text: It split the power into three parts
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the Founders split the power three ways so no single person could hold it all, which is the opposite of British rule.

choices.text: It kept the same system of government
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Founders created something completely different from British rule, not the same system.

choices.text: It let the king keep making all decisions
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Founders were done with king-style rule — they built a whole new system to make sure no one person made all the decisions.

choices.text: It gave all the power to one strong leader
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Founders specifically did NOT want one strong leader — that's what they had under the king, and they hated it.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Thinker from France

The Founders did not invent this idea on their own. They borrowed it from a French philosopher — a person who studies big questions about how people should live and be governed. His name was Montesquieu. He studied the governments of ancient Rome and modern England and noticed a pattern. Whenever one ruler controlled everything, the people lost their liberty — their freedom to live without unfair control, the way you feel free to speak your mind without being punished for it. Montesquieu argued that the only way to protect liberty was to divide government power among different groups. The Founders read his work carefully and built his idea right into the Constitution.
stimulus.content_text: A Thinker from France The Founders did not invent this idea on their own. They borrowed it from a French philosopher — a person who studies big questions about how people should live and be governed. His name was Montesquieu. He studied the governments of ancient Rome and modern England and noticed a pattern. Whenever one ruler controlled everything, the people lost their liberty — their freedom to live without unfair control, the way you feel free to speak your mind without being punished for it. Montesquieu argued that the only way to protect liberty was to divide government power among different groups. The Founders read his work carefully and built his idea right into the Constitution.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Montesquieu argue that government power should be divided among different groups?

choices.text: to help one strong ruler stay in control
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what Montesquieu believed — he saw one ruler controlling everything as the problem, not the solution.

choices.text: to give philosophers more power in government
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions giving philosophers more power — Montesquieu wanted to divide power among different parts of government, not hand it to thinkers like himself.

choices.text: to protect people's liberty from unfair rule
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says Montesquieu argued that dividing power was the only way to protect people's liberty from unfair rule.

choices.text: to make governments work like ancient Rome
choices.feedback: Montesquieu studied ancient Rome to learn from its history, but the passage does not say he wanted governments to work like Rome.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Three Branches, Three Jobs

The Constitution splits the federal government into three branches, each with its own specific job.

The legislative branch — Congress — makes the laws. Article I of the Constitution describes it. Congress has two chambers: the Senate and the House of Representatives. Before any new law can take effect, both chambers must approve it. That means hundreds of elected officials discuss, debate, and vote on every law. No small group can push through rules that most people oppose.

The executive branch is led by the President and described in Article II. You might think the President makes the laws, since the President is the most visible leader in the country. Actually, that is Congress's job. The President's role is to enforce the laws — to make sure they are carried out. Think of it this way: Congress writes the rulebook, and the President makes sure everyone follows it. The President also leads the armed forces, handles relationships with other countries, and appoints officials to run government agencies.

The judicial branch, described in Article III, is led by the Supreme Court. It interprets the laws — which means it explains what the laws mean and decides whether they are fair, the way a referee interprets the rules of a game during a close play. When people disagree about a law's meaning, the courts settle the dispute. The Supreme Court is the highest court in the land, and its decisions are final. Supreme Court justices are appointed for life so they stay independent. Their only job is to judge whether laws follow the Constitution.

The legislative branch — Congress — makes the laws. Article I of the Constitution describes it. Congress has two chambers: the Senate and the House of Representatives. Before any new law can take effect, both chambers must approve it. That means hundreds of elected officials discuss, debate, and vote on every law. No small group can push through rules that most people oppose. The executive branch is led by the President and described in Article II. You might think the President makes the laws, since the President is the most visible leader in the country. Actually, that is Congress's job. The President's role is to enforce the laws — to make sure they are carried out. Think of it this way: Congress writes the rulebook, and the President makes sure everyone follows it. The President also leads the armed forces, handles relationships with other countries, and appoints officials to run government agencies. The judicial branch, described in Article III, is led by the Supreme Court. It interprets the laws — which means it explains what the laws mean and decides whether they are fair, the way a referee interprets the rules of a game during a close play. When people disagree about a law's meaning, the courts settle the dispute. The Supreme Court is the highest court in the land, and its decisions are final. Supreme Court justices are appointed for life so they stay independent. Their only job is to judge whether laws follow the Constitution.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the correct order of steps that happen when Congress creates a new law?

choices.text: Officials discuss and debate the law, then both chambers vote to approve it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says officials discuss, debate, and vote on every law, meaning discussion and debate come before both chambers vote to approve it.

choices.text: The President writes the law, then Congress votes to approve it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the President does not make laws — that is Congress's job.

choices.text: Officials vote on the law, then they discuss and debate it in both chambers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — officials discuss and debate the law first, and then they vote on it.

choices.text: The Supreme Court reviews the law, then both chambers debate it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Supreme Court interprets laws after they are made, not before Congress debates them.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Each Branch Watches the Others

Separation of powers means each branch has its own job. But the Founders went further. They gave each branch specific tools to limit the others. For example, the President can veto a bill — reject it and send it back to Congress, like a coach waving off a bad play. Congress may also pass a law that seems unfair or goes against the Constitution. The Supreme Court can declare that law unconstitutional — meaning it breaks the rules the Constitution set down. Each branch needs the others, and each branch watches the others.
stimulus.content_text: Each Branch Watches the Others Separation of powers means each branch has its own job. But the Founders went further. They gave each branch specific tools to limit the others. For example, the President can veto a bill — reject it and send it back to Congress, like a coach waving off a bad play. Congress may also pass a law that seems unfair or goes against the Constitution. The Supreme Court can declare that law unconstitutional — meaning it breaks the rules the Constitution set down. Each branch needs the others, and each branch watches the others.

prompt._: In the passage, the President's veto is compared to a coach 'waving off a bad play.' How does this comparison help the reader understand the meaning of the word 'veto'?

choices.text: It shows that a veto means letting Congress decide on its own
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a veto is the President using their own power to block something, not stepping aside for Congress to handle it alone.

choices.text: It shows that a veto means rejecting something that is not good enough
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the President's veto and a coach waving off a bad play mean saying 'no' to something that isn't good enough, which matches the passage's definition of veto as rejecting a bill.

choices.text: It shows that a veto means asking players to try the same play again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because waving off a play means stopping it completely, not asking players to run the same play again.

choices.text: It shows that a veto means creating a new rule for everyone to follow
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a veto rejects something that already exists — it doesn't create a new rule, and neither does a coach waving off a play.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Slow on Purpose

This system was not designed to be fast. It was designed to be slow and careful. The Founders believed that quick, unchecked action by a government was dangerous. They wanted important decisions to require agreement among different groups of people with different views. When the President, Congress, and the Supreme Court all agree something should be done, it is much more likely to be the right thing. When they clash, that tension is not a sign of failure. It is the system doing exactly what it was built to do — forcing debate before action.

The next time you hear that Congress and the President disagree, or that the Supreme Court struck down a law, remember those fifty-five men in that sealed room. They designed a government where arguing is part of the job — because a government that never argues with itself is a government where someone has too much power.

The Founders believed that quick, unchecked action by a government was dangerous. They wanted important decisions to require agreement among different groups of people with different views. When the President, Congress, and the Supreme Court all agree something should be done, it is much more likely to be the right thing. When they clash, that tension is not a sign of failure. It is the system doing exactly what it was built to do — forcing debate before action. The next time you hear that Congress and the President disagree, or that the Supreme Court struck down a law, remember those fifty-five men in that sealed room. They designed a government where arguing is part of the job — because a government that never argues with itself is a government where someone has too much power.

prompt._: According to the section "Slow on Purpose," what does the author mean by a government that is "slow and careful"?

choices.text: a government that requires agreement before action
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says important decisions require agreement among different groups, which is exactly what makes the government slow and careful.

choices.text: a government that avoids making big choices
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the government does make important decisions — it just requires agreement and debate before doing so.

choices.text: a government that changes laws without debate
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says debate must happen before action, meaning laws cannot be changed without discussion.

choices.text: a government that lets one branch decide quickly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says quick action by one group is dangerous, so the system was built to prevent that.

prompt._: How does the government created by the Founders differ from the British rule they experienced?

choices.text: The new government needs many leaders to agree, while the British king decided alone.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage shows the British king held all the power alone, while the new government requires many leaders across three branches to agree before anything happens.

choices.text: The new government makes choices very quickly, while the British king acted slowly.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage actually says the new government was designed to be slow and careful on purpose, not fast.

choices.text: The new government gives power to the President, while the British king shared power.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the new government does not give all the power to the President; instead, it splits power among three branches so no one person is in charge.

choices.text: The new government lets the courts write the laws, while the British king held votes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage clearly states that Congress (the legislative branch) writes the laws, not the courts; the courts interpret laws to decide if they are fair.

prompt._: Why did the Founders design the government so that each branch can limit the power of the other branches?

choices.text: because the Supreme Court needed to be more powerful than Congress
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Constitution was designed so that no branch is more powerful than the others — each branch watches and limits the others equally.

choices.text: because they wanted the government to make decisions as quickly as possible
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the system was designed to be slow and careful, not fast, because quick unchecked action was seen as dangerous.

choices.text: because Montesquieu told them that ancient Rome had three branches of government
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Montesquieu did study ancient Rome, the passage never says Rome had three branches of government — that idea came from the Founders themselves.

choices.text: because they learned that liberty is lost when one ruler controls everything
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly explains that Montesquieu showed that liberty is lost when one ruler controls everything, and the Founders built a system of checks to prevent that from happening.

prompt._: Which choice shows the order the article uses to explain how the Constitution guards against too much power?

choices.text: It describes how courts judge laws, then tells how Montesquieu studied governments.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article doesn't begin with how courts judge laws — that topic comes in the middle of the article.

choices.text: It names the three branches, then explains why the Founders feared one ruler.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains the danger of one ruler first, and then names the three branches — not the other way around.

choices.text: It shows how the President enforces laws, then explains how Congress writes the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says Congress writes the Constitution, and this doesn't match the overall order of the article's ideas.

choices.text: It explains the danger of one ruler, then shows how three branches share and check power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article begins with the danger of one ruler having too much power and then explains how the three branches share and check that power.

prompt._: Compared to a government where one ruler holds all the power, what is the main idea the article develops about the U.S. government created by the Constitution?

choices.text: It copies the British system of government but adds a Supreme Court to judge laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Founders were trying to avoid the British system of powerful rule, not copy it.

choices.text: It protects people's freedom by requiring different groups to share and limit power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that splitting power among three branches protects people's freedom by making sure no single person or group can control everything.

choices.text: It works faster because three branches share the work equally among themselves.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the system was designed to be slow and careful on purpose, not fast.

choices.text: It gives the President the most power because the President enforces all the laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that Congress makes the laws, and the President's job is only to enforce them.

syllabus_metadata.title: Three Branches: Separation of Powers

assessments.title: Checks and Balances: No Branch Too Powerful

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Making a Constitutional Government: Checks and Balances

A President Says No

On April 5, 1792, George Washington did something no president had ever done. He picked up his pen — not to sign a bill, but to reject one. Congress had sent him the Apportionment Bill, which would have decided how to divide seats in the House of Representatives among the states. Washington believed the bill was unfair and did not follow the rules laid out in the Constitution. That rejection had a name: a veto, which means the President refuses to sign a bill into law. With that single act, Washington proved that the President could push back against Congress — and that Congress would have to listen.

The Founders designed this power on purpose. They had already split the government into three branches — legislative, executive, and judicial. But splitting power was not enough. They worried that one branch might try to grab more authority than it deserved. So they built a system of checks and balances — tools that let each branch limit the power of the other two. No single branch can win every round.
stimulus.content_text: Making a Constitutional Government: Checks and BalancesA President Says No On April 5, 1792, George Washington did something no president had ever done. He picked up his pen — not to sign a bill, but to reject one. Congress had sent him the Apportionment Bill, which would have decided how to divide seats in the House of Representatives among the states. Washington believed the bill was unfair and did not follow the rules laid out in the Constitution. That rejection had a name: a veto, which means the President refuses to sign a bill into law. With that single act, Washington proved that the President could push back against Congress — and that Congress would have to listen. The Founders designed this power on purpose. They had already split the government into three branches — legislative, executive, and judicial. But splitting power was not enough. They worried that one branch might try to grab more authority than it deserved. So they built a system of checks and balances — tools that let each branch limit the power of the other two. No single branch can win every round.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the contrast between a veto and signing a bill?

choices.text: A veto rejects a bill, but signing a bill changes it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because signing a bill makes it become a law, not changes it — the passage never says signing changes a bill.

choices.text: A veto rejects a bill, but signing a bill makes it law.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a veto means the President refuses to sign a bill into law, so signing does the opposite — it makes the bill a law.

choices.text: A veto creates a bill, but signing a bill rejects it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the definitions completely backwards — a veto rejects a bill, and signing a bill makes it law, not the other way around.

choices.text: A veto sends a bill to Congress, but signing a bill ends it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says a veto sends a bill to Congress or that signing a bill ends it — those descriptions don't match anything in the text.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The President's Check on Congress

When the President vetoes a bill, it goes back to Congress with a written explanation of why.

But Congress is not helpless against a veto. Congress can override it — meaning Congress can cancel the President's rejection — if two-thirds of both the House and the Senate vote yes. Getting two-thirds of both chambers to agree is like getting almost every kid in your class to choose the same flavor of ice cream. It rarely happens, but the power exists so that no President can block the will of Congress forever.
stimulus.content_text: The President's Check on Congress When the President vetoes a bill, it goes back to Congress with a written explanation of why. But Congress is not helpless against a veto. Congress can override it — meaning Congress can cancel the President's rejection — if two-thirds of both the House and the Senate vote yes. Getting two-thirds of both chambers to agree is like getting almost every kid in your class to choose the same flavor of ice cream. It rarely happens, but the power exists so that no President can block the will of Congress forever.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is it so hard for Congress to override a President's veto?

choices.text: Because two-thirds of both chambers must vote yes
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that two-thirds of both the House and the Senate must vote yes, which is very hard to achieve.

choices.text: Because only the Senate is allowed to vote on it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says both the House AND the Senate must vote, not just the Senate alone.

choices.text: Because the President can veto the override too
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the President gets to veto the override — once Congress votes, the President's veto is cancelled.

choices.text: Because Congress must wait a full year to try again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions any waiting period — the only challenge is getting enough votes.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Supreme Court's Special Power

The judicial branch holds a different kind of check. The Supreme Court can declare a law unconstitutional, meaning the law breaks the rules written in the Constitution. This power is called judicial review — the court's ability to examine a law and measure it against the Constitution, the way a teacher checks your work against an answer key.

The Supreme Court can only strike down a law if it violates the Constitution's actual words or principles.

Judicial review is not written directly in the Constitution. It was established in 1803 when the Supreme Court decided a famous case called Marbury v. Madison. In that case, the Court declared that it had the authority to decide whether laws followed the Constitution. That decision gave the judicial branch a powerful check on both Congress and the President.
stimulus.content_text: The Supreme Court's Special Power The judicial branch holds a different kind of check. The Supreme Court can declare a law unconstitutional, meaning the law breaks the rules written in the Constitution. This power is called judicial review — the court's ability to examine a law and measure it against the Constitution, the way a teacher checks your work against an answer key. The Supreme Court can only strike down a law if it violates the Constitution's actual words or principles. Judicial review is not written directly in the Constitution. It was established in 1803 when the Supreme Court decided a famous case called Marbury v. Madison. In that case, the Court declared that it had the authority to decide whether laws followed the Constitution. That decision gave the judicial branch a powerful check on both Congress and the President.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after the Supreme Court decided the Marbury v. Madison case?

choices.text: The judicial branch gained a powerful check on Congress and the President.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states clearly that the Marbury v. Madison decision 'gave the judicial branch a powerful check on both Congress and the President.'

choices.text: The writers added the rule of judicial review directly into the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — it tells us that judicial review is NOT written directly in the Constitution.

choices.text: The Supreme Court completely lost its ability to examine and measure laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Marbury v. Madison decision actually gave the Supreme Court MORE power to examine laws, not less.

choices.text: The President received the special power to strike down any unconstitutional laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is the Supreme Court, not the President, that has the power to strike down unconstitutional laws.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Checking the Courts Right Back

The other branches have tools against the judiciary, too. The President chooses who serves on the Supreme Court by picking a nominee — a person the President wants to appoint to the job. But the President cannot simply place someone on the Court. The Senate must approve the choice. Senators hold hearings, ask the nominee tough questions, and then vote. This gives Congress a say in who sits on the most powerful court in the country.
stimulus.content_text: Checking the Courts Right Back The other branches have tools against the judiciary, too. The President chooses who serves on the Supreme Court by picking a nominee — a person the President wants to appoint to the job. But the President cannot simply place someone on the Court. The Senate must approve the choice. Senators hold hearings, ask the nominee tough questions, and then vote. This gives Congress a say in who sits on the most powerful court in the country.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the word 'nominee' similar to the word 'choice' as used in the sentence 'The Senate must approve the choice'?

choices.text: Both words refer to a question asked at a trial
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither word is about a question asked at a trial — the passage is about picking a judge, not about a court trial.

choices.text: Both words refer to a hearing held by senators
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither 'nominee' nor 'choice' means a hearing — hearings are something senators hold to ask questions.

choices.text: Both words refer to a person picked for a position
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines 'nominee' as a person the President wants to appoint, and 'the choice' in the next sentence refers to that same picked person.

choices.text: Both words refer to a group that votes on judges
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'nominee' and 'choice' both refer to a person, not a group that votes — the Senate is the group that votes.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Power to Impeach

Congress holds one more dramatic check. It can impeach the President or federal judges for serious misconduct — behavior that abuses the power of their office. You might think impeachment means a president is kicked out of office. It does not. Impeachment is only the first step, like being charged with a crime. The House of Representatives votes on whether to impeach. If the House votes yes, the Senate holds a trial and decides whether to convict — to find the official guilty — and remove that person from office. Only a handful of presidents have been impeached. None has ever been removed by the Senate. Still, the threat alone reminds every leader that no one is above the law.
stimulus.content_text: The Power to Impeach Congress holds one more dramatic check. It can impeach the President or federal judges for serious misconduct — behavior that abuses the power of their office. You might think impeachment means a president is kicked out of office. It does not. Impeachment is only the first step, like being charged with a crime. The House of Representatives votes on whether to impeach. If the House votes yes, the Senate holds a trial and decides whether to convict — to find the official guilty — and remove that person from office. Only a handful of presidents have been impeached. None has ever been removed by the Senate. Still, the threat alone reminds every leader that no one is above the law.

prompt._: In the article "Checks and Balances: No Branch Too Powerful," what does the word "convict" mean?

choices.text: to vote to impeach someone
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because voting to impeach is the House's job and happens before the Senate trial where a conviction takes place.

choices.text: to decide someone is guilty
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines 'convict' as 'to find the official guilty,' which means deciding someone is guilty.

choices.text: to remove someone from office
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because removal from office is what happens as a result of a conviction, but it is not the meaning of the word 'convict' itself.

choices.text: to ask if someone did wrong
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'asking if someone did wrong' describes an investigation, not what 'convict' means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Slow Can Be Strong

Congress also controls the government's money. Even if the President wants to spend funds on something, Congress can refuse to pay for it. This power of the purse is yet another check — one that keeps the President from acting alone.

All these checks sometimes cause gridlock — a situation where the branches disagree so deeply that the government stalls, like cars stuck in traffic going nowhere. The Founders accepted that trade-off. They had lived under a king who held all the power. They called that tyranny — one person or group holding so much power that ordinary people lose their freedom. A slower government frustrated people in 1789, and it still frustrates people today. But every time a President vetoes a bill, every time the Senate questions a nominee, every time the Court measures a law against the Constitution, the same invisible machinery clicks into motion. Three branches push against each other so that none of them can push you around.
stimulus.content_text: Why Slow Can Be Strong Congress also controls the government's money. Even if the President wants to spend funds on something, Congress can refuse to pay for it. This power of the purse is yet another check — one that keeps the President from acting alone. All these checks sometimes cause gridlock — a situation where the branches disagree so deeply that the government stalls, like cars stuck in traffic going nowhere. The Founders accepted that trade-off. They had lived under a king who held all the power. They called that tyranny — one person or group holding so much power that ordinary people lose their freedom. A slower government frustrated people in 1789, and it still frustrates people today. But every time a President vetoes a bill, every time the Senate questions a nominee, every time the Court measures a law against the Constitution, the same invisible machinery clicks into motion. Three branches push against each other so that none of them can push you around.

prompt._: Which detail from the text gives evidence that Congress can keep the President from acting alone?

choices.text: The branches can disagree so deeply that the government stalls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gridlock is about all the branches getting stuck, not specifically about Congress stopping the President from acting alone.

choices.text: A king can hold all the power over ordinary people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes a king holding power, which is an example of tyranny — not an example of Congress checking the President.

choices.text: The Court can measure a law against the written Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because measuring a law against the Constitution is something the Court does, not Congress.

choices.text: Congress can refuse to pay for what the President wants.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Congress can refuse to pay for what the President wants, which is direct evidence that Congress can keep the President from acting alone.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between how the President and the Supreme Court can check Congress?

choices.text: The President pays for government plans, but the Supreme Court writes new laws for Congress.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Congress controls the government's money (power of the purse), and the Supreme Court reviews laws rather than writing new ones.

choices.text: The President picks judges, but the Supreme Court approves the President's choices.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the President nominates judges and the Senate approves them, not the Supreme Court.

choices.text: The President rejects a bill, but the Supreme Court checks whether a law follows the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the President vetoes bills while the Supreme Court uses judicial review to check if laws follow the Constitution — two different checks on Congress.

choices.text: The President removes judges, but the Supreme Court sends bills back to Congress.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither of these things happens in the passage — the President doesn't remove judges, and it's the President (not the Court) who sends bills back to Congress with a veto.

prompt._: Based on the article, why did the Founders create a government where the branches limit each other's power?

choices.text: They wanted to force the government to stop working entirely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the Founders knew checks and balances might cause gridlock sometimes, their goal was to protect freedom, not to make the government stop working.

choices.text: They wanted to give the Supreme Court the final decision.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the system gives all three branches — not just the Supreme Court — tools to limit each other's power.

choices.text: They wanted to ensure the President could cancel bad laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the veto is just one small part of the checks and balances system, not the main reason the Founders created it.

choices.text: They wanted to stop one group from gaining total control.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the Founders feared one branch grabbing too much power, and they had already seen tyranny under a king, so they built checks and balances to prevent any one group from taking total control.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information about how a person is chosen for the Supreme Court?

choices.text: By describing the steps of the process in the order they happen.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes the nomination process in order: the President picks someone, the Senate holds hearings, and then the Senate votes.

choices.text: By comparing the President's power to the Supreme Court's power.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the section is about how a justice is chosen, not about comparing the President's power to the Supreme Court's power.

choices.text: By explaining the problem of picking a judge and how to solve it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't present picking a judge as a problem that needs solving — it just explains the steps.

choices.text: By showing the causes that make the President pick a certain person.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never tells us why the President chooses a particular person, only what happens after the choice is made.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are the powers of the President, Congress, and the Supreme Court similar?

choices.text: They all have the power to choose who serves on the Supreme Court.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the President nominates someone for the Supreme Court, and only the Senate approves that choice — the Supreme Court itself has no say in picking its own members.

choices.text: They all have the right to cancel the Constitution if they disagree with it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because no branch has the power to cancel the Constitution — in fact, the Supreme Court's whole job is to make sure laws follow the Constitution, not throw it out.

choices.text: They all have the ability to write new laws for the country.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only Congress has the power to write and pass new laws — the President and Supreme Court do not write laws.

choices.text: They all have ways to stop the other branches from getting too much power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that every branch — the President, Congress, and the Supreme Court — has specific powers to limit what the other branches can do, which is the whole point of checks and balances.

assessments.title: The Bill of Rights

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Fight for the Bill of Rights

A Missing Piece

On September 17, 1787, thirty-nine men signed their names at the bottom of a brand-new Constitution. But one delegate, George Mason of Virginia, put down his pen and refused. He had helped write the document all summer long. His reason was simple and sharp: the Constitution had no list of rights protecting ordinary people.

The Constitution created a powerful national government with the authority to collect taxes, raise armies, and make laws.
stimulus.content_text: The Fight for the Bill of RightsA Missing Piece On September 17, 1787, thirty-nine men signed their names at the bottom of a brand-new Constitution. But one delegate, George Mason of Virginia, put down his pen and refused. He had helped write the document all summer long. His reason was simple and sharp: the Constitution had no list of rights protecting ordinary people. The Constitution created a powerful national government with the authority to collect taxes, raise armies, and make laws.

prompt._: According to the text, what contrast is shown between George Mason and the other delegates?

choices.text: George Mason signed the Constitution, but the other delegates refused.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says the thirty-nine delegates signed, and George Mason was the one who refused.

choices.text: George Mason wrote the Constitution alone, but the other delegates watched.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Mason helped write the Constitution all summer, meaning others were involved too, not just watching.

choices.text: George Mason refused to sign the Constitution, but the other delegates signed it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage tells us thirty-nine men signed the Constitution while George Mason 'put down his pen and refused' — that is exactly the contrast.

choices.text: George Mason wanted a stronger army, but the other delegates wanted lower taxes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about Mason wanting a stronger army or the other delegates wanting lower taxes — those details are not in the text.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Federalists Against Anti-Federalists

Before the Constitution could become law, the states had to ratify it — officially approve it, the way a principal signs off on a new school rule. A fierce argument broke out across the country. Supporters of the Constitution called themselves Federalists. Their opponents became known as Anti-Federalists.

Federalists like Alexander Hamilton and James Madison believed the Constitution was safe enough on its own. Anti-Federalists disagreed. They wanted specific, written protections — promises in black ink that the government could never ignore.

You might think the Bill of Rights was always part of the Constitution, but it wasn't. The original document said nothing about your right to speak freely or worship as you choose. Many states flat-out refused to ratify the Constitution until the Federalists made a deal: approve the Constitution now, and a bill of rights will be added right away. That promise convinced key states like Virginia, New York, and Massachusetts. The Constitution was ratified in 1788.
stimulus.content_text: Federalists Against Anti-Federalists Before the Constitution could become law, the states had to ratify it — officially approve it, the way a principal signs off on a new school rule. A fierce argument broke out across the country. Supporters of the Constitution called themselves Federalists. Their opponents became known as Anti-Federalists. Federalists like Alexander Hamilton and James Madison believed the Constitution was safe enough on its own. Anti-Federalists disagreed. They wanted specific, written protections — promises in black ink that the government could never ignore. You might think the Bill of Rights was always part of the Constitution, but it wasn't. The original document said nothing about your right to speak freely or worship as you choose. Many states flat-out refused to ratify the Constitution until the Federalists made a deal: approve the Constitution now, and a bill of rights will be added right away. That promise convinced key states like Virginia, New York, and Massachusetts. The Constitution was ratified in 1788.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did some key states decide to ratify the Constitution?

choices.text: because they wanted to end the argument between groups
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the states were convinced by a specific promise, not just a desire to stop arguing.

choices.text: because they thought the Constitution already protected speech
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the original Constitution said nothing about rights like free speech.

choices.text: because they wanted the president to have more power
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about giving the president more power.

choices.text: because they were promised a bill of rights soon
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says states agreed after the Federalists promised to add a bill of rights right away.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
James Madison Gets to Work

James Madison kept that promise. He sat down and drafted a set of amendments — official changes or additions to the Constitution, like updating the rules of a game after players find a problem. Madison did not start from scratch. He used George Mason's Virginia Declaration of Rights from 1776 as a model. He also gathered suggestions from the ratification debates in different states.

Madison originally proposed twelve amendments. Congress approved all twelve in 1789 and sent them to the states. Ten of the twelve were ratified by enough states to become law. Those ten amendments became the Bill of Rights in 1791.
stimulus.content_text: James Madison Gets to Work James Madison kept that promise. He sat down and drafted a set of amendments — official changes or additions to the Constitution, like updating the rules of a game after players find a problem. Madison did not start from scratch. He used George Mason's Virginia Declaration of Rights from 1776 as a model. He also gathered suggestions from the ratification debates in different states. Madison originally proposed twelve amendments. Congress approved all twelve in 1789 and sent them to the states. Ten of the twelve were ratified by enough states to become law. Those ten amendments became the Bill of Rights in 1791.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Madison do right after he drafted the amendments?

choices.text: He asked George Mason to review them first.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because George Mason's work was used as a model while Madison was writing the amendments, not as a review step afterward.

choices.text: He sent them directly to the states for approval.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Madison gave the amendments to Congress first, and it was Congress that sent them to the states — not Madison directly.

choices.text: He presented them to Congress for approval.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage tells us Congress approved the twelve amendments in 1789, meaning Madison presented them to Congress before anything else happened.

choices.text: He added two more amendments to the list.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Madison proposed twelve amendments from the beginning — the passage never says he added more to the list.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What the Bill of Rights Protects

The First Amendment protects five freedoms all by itself: speech, religion, the press, assembly, and the right to petition the government — to formally ask leaders to fix a problem, the way you might write a letter to your mayor about a broken playground. Americans can speak their minds, worship as they choose, publish books and newspapers, gather in groups to protest, and demand change from their leaders. Even so, the First Amendment has boundaries. Freedom of speech does not protect someone who shouts "Fire!" in a packed theater just to cause panic.

The Second Amendment protects the right to bear arms. The Third Amendment stops the government from forcing you to let soldiers live in your home. The Fourth Amendment guards against unreasonable searches and seizures — seizure means the government taking your belongings, like a teacher confiscating a phone, except here the police need a warrant, a written permission slip from a judge, before they can search your house.

The Fifth through Eighth Amendments protect people accused of crimes. They guarantee the right to a speedy trial, the right to a lawyer, the right to a jury, and protection against cruel and unusual punishment. No one can be forced to testify against themselves — to give evidence in court that proves their own guilt. And the government cannot take private property without fair compensation, meaning payment that matches what the property is worth.

The Ninth Amendment says the rights listed in the Constitution are not the only rights you have. Just because a right isn't written down does not mean it doesn't exist. The Tenth Amendment reserves all powers not given to the national government to the states or to the people. The national government can only do what the Constitution says it can do.

Americans can speak their minds, worship as they choose, publish books and newspapers, gather in groups to protest, and demand change from their leaders. Even so, the First Amendment has boundaries. Freedom of speech does not protect someone who shouts "Fire!" in a packed theater just to cause panic. The Second Amendment protects the right to bear arms. The Third Amendment stops the government from forcing you to let soldiers live in your home. The Fourth Amendment guards against unreasonable searches and seizures — seizure means the government taking your belongings, like a teacher confiscating a phone, except here the police need a warrant, a written permission slip from a judge, before they can search your house.
