They guarantee the right to a speedy trial, the right to a lawyer, the right to a jury, and protection against cruel and unusual punishment. No one can be forced to testify against themselves — to give evidence in court that proves their own guilt. And the government cannot take private property without fair compensation, meaning payment that matches what the property is worth. The Ninth Amendment says the rights listed in the Constitution are not the only rights you have. Just because a right isn't written down does not mean it doesn't exist. The Tenth Amendment reserves all powers not given to the national government to the states or to the people. The national government can only do what the Constitution says it can do.

prompt._: How is the word 'warrant' in the passage similar to a 'permission slip' based on the comparison the author makes?

choices.text: Both are spoken promises made between two people in court
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage makes clear that a warrant is written, not a spoken promise, and permission slips are written documents too.

choices.text: Both are punishments given by a judge to someone who breaks the law
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a warrant is not a punishment — it is permission for police to search, and a permission slip is not a punishment either.

choices.text: Both are written approvals that allow someone to do something
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly calls a warrant 'a written permission slip from a judge,' meaning both are written approvals that allow someone to do something.

choices.text: Both are lists of rights that belong to every American citizen
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither a warrant nor a permission slip is a list of rights — that describes something like the Bill of Rights itself.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Shield That Still Works

George Mason refused to sign the Constitution because that shield was missing. At first, the Bill of Rights only limited the national government. Over time, courts decided that these protections also apply to state and local governments. Today, no government in the United States — federal, state, or local — can take away your basic rights.

Every time a court hears a case about free speech, religious liberty, or the rights of someone accused of a crime, judges return to the words James Madison wrote more than two hundred years ago. The Bill of Rights turned the Constitution into more than a plan for running a government. It became a shield — a written promise that certain freedoms belong to you, not because the government gave them to you, but because you were born with them.
stimulus.content_text: A Shield That Still Works George Mason refused to sign the Constitution because that shield was missing. At first, the Bill of Rights only limited the national government. Over time, courts decided that these protections also apply to state and local governments. Today, no government in the United States — federal, state, or local — can take away your basic rights. Every time a court hears a case about free speech, religious liberty, or the rights of someone accused of a crime, judges return to the words James Madison wrote more than two hundred years ago. The Bill of Rights turned the Constitution into more than a plan for running a government. It became a shield — a written promise that certain freedoms belong to you, not because the government gave them to you, but because you were born with them.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by calling the Bill of Rights a 'shield'?

choices.text: a written promise that protects people's freedoms
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'a written promise that certain freedoms belong to you' to explain what the shield means.

choices.text: a document that gives the government new powers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the Bill of Rights limits what the government can do — it does not give the government new powers.

choices.text: a set of rules for electing government leaders
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never talks about rules for electing government leaders.

choices.text: a list of punishments for people who break laws
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Bill of Rights is about protecting people's freedoms, not listing punishments for breaking laws.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the original Constitution different from the Bill of Rights?

choices.text: The Constitution gave the government power, while the Bill of Rights limited it.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article clearly shows the Constitution gave the government power, while the Bill of Rights was added to limit that power and protect people's freedoms.

choices.text: The Constitution was a permission slip, while the Bill of Rights was a court trial.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because neither document is described as a permission slip or a court trial — these descriptions are completely made up and not supported by the article.

choices.text: The Constitution protected free speech, while the Bill of Rights raised an army.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the Constitution dealt with raising armies, and it was the Bill of Rights (specifically the First Amendment) that protected free speech, not the other way around.

choices.text: The Constitution applied to the states, while the Bill of Rights applied to cities.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article says the Bill of Rights eventually applied to all levels of government — federal, state, AND local — not just cities.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused the Anti-Federalists to demand a bill of rights?

choices.text: They needed a special way to force George Mason to sign the document.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because George Mason refused to sign on his own because of missing rights — the Bill of Rights was not created to get his signature.

choices.text: They worried the new national government was too weak to make laws.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Anti-Federalists were actually worried the government had too much power, not too little.

choices.text: They believed the individual states should have the power to raise armies.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article never mentions Anti-Federalists arguing about which government should have the power to raise armies.

choices.text: They wanted written rules to protect citizens from the government.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly states Anti-Federalists wanted specific written protections to make sure the government could never ignore people's rights.

prompt._: Which choice shows the events from the article in the correct order?

choices.text: Ten amendments became the Bill of Rights, the Constitution was ratified in 1788, and George Mason refused to sign the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the Bill of Rights becoming law before the Constitution was ratified, but the Constitution had to be ratified in 1788 before any amendments could even be added.

choices.text: Madison wrote twelve amendments, George Mason refused to sign the Constitution, and the Constitution was ratified in 1788.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says Madison wrote the amendments before Mason refused to sign, but the article tells us Mason refused to sign first, back in 1787.

choices.text: George Mason refused to sign the Constitution, Madison wrote twelve amendments, and ten amendments became the Bill of Rights.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the article: Mason refused to sign in 1787, Madison wrote twelve amendments in 1789, and ten became the Bill of Rights in 1791.

choices.text: The states ratified ten amendments, Madison wrote twelve amendments, and George Mason refused to sign the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the ratification of the ten amendments first, but that was actually the last event — Mason refusing to sign happened first in 1787.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the main idea of the article by comparing the Constitution before and after the Bill of Rights was added?

choices.text: Before, the states refused to pay their required taxes; after, the states agreed to raise a national army.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions anything about states refusing to pay taxes — that idea is not in the passage at all.

choices.text: Before, only the national leaders followed the rules; after, only the state governments followed the laws.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says that over time the Bill of Rights came to apply to all governments in the United States, not just national or just state governments.

choices.text: Before, citizens lacked written protections; after, their basic freedoms were permanently shielded.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows that before the Bill of Rights, citizens had no written protections, and after it was added, their basic freedoms were permanently shielded by law.

choices.text: Before, the national government lacked any real power; after, the government became strong enough to rule.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage tells us the original Constitution already gave the national government plenty of power — the problem was that it didn't protect ordinary people's rights.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Bill of Rights

assessments.title: Federalism: Power Shared

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Making a Constitutional Government: How Federalism Splits the Power

Two Sets of Rules

In Texas, you can get a driver's license at fifteen. In New Jersey, you have to wait until seventeen. School starts in early August in Georgia but not until after Labor Day in some parts of Minnesota. If one big government in Washington, D.C., made every rule for every American, these differences wouldn't exist. But they do — and that is on purpose.

When the Founders designed the Constitution in 1787, they built a system called federalism — the division of power between a central national government and individual state governments. Here's the surprising part: even though "federalism" sounds like it means the federal government runs everything, it actually means the opposite. Federalism is about splitting power so that no single government controls it all. Think of it like a two-story house. The national government lives on one floor. The state governments live on the other. They share the staircase, but each floor has its own rooms and its own rules.
stimulus.content_text: Making a Constitutional Government: How Federalism Splits the PowerTwo Sets of Rules In Texas, you can get a driver's license at fifteen. In New Jersey, you have to wait until seventeen. School starts in early August in Georgia but not until after Labor Day in some parts of Minnesota. If one big government in Washington, D.C., made every rule for every American, these differences wouldn't exist. But they do — and that is on purpose. When the Founders designed the Constitution in 1787, they built a system called federalism — the division of power between a central national government and individual state governments. Here's the surprising part: even though "federalism" sounds like it means the federal government runs everything, it actually means the opposite. Federalism is about splitting power so that no single government controls it all. Think of it like a two-story house. The national government lives on one floor. The state governments live on the other. They share the staircase, but each floor has its own rooms and its own rules.

prompt._: According to the passage, how is federalism different from having one big government in Washington, D.C., make every rule?

choices.text: It splits power between a national government and state governments.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines federalism as 'the division of power between a central national government and individual state governments,' which means splitting power between two levels.

choices.text: It gives state governments all the power over national laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because state governments don't control national laws — federalism means both levels share power, not that states are in charge of everything.

choices.text: It lets the national government control all the power by itself.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says federalism means the opposite of one government controlling everything — power is split, not handed to the national government alone.

choices.text: It removes the need for any rules at the state level.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage gives examples like driving ages and school start dates that show states very much still have their own rules.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What the National Government Does

Some jobs only make sense when one government handles them for the whole country. The national government maintains the armed forces, prints money, and runs the postal service. It negotiates treaties — official agreements — with foreign countries. It also regulates interstate commerce, which means trade and business that crosses state lines, like a truck carrying oranges from Florida to Ohio.

Imagine if each state printed its own dollar bills. A five-dollar bill from Virginia might be worthless in Pennsylvania. Or picture fifty states each trying to make separate deals with other countries. The confusion would be enormous. That is why the Founders gave these big, nationwide responsibilities to one central government.
stimulus.content_text: What the National Government Does Some jobs only make sense when one government handles them for the whole country. The national government maintains the armed forces, prints money, and runs the postal service. It negotiates treaties — official agreements — with foreign countries. It also regulates interstate commerce, which means trade and business that crosses state lines, like a truck carrying oranges from Florida to Ohio. Imagine if each state printed its own dollar bills. A five-dollar bill from Virginia might be worthless in Pennsylvania. Or picture fifty states each trying to make separate deals with other countries. The confusion would be enormous. That is why the Founders gave these big, nationwide responsibilities to one central government.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did the Founders give responsibilities like printing money to one central government?

choices.text: because the states did not have enough people to do these jobs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions states not having enough people to handle these jobs.

choices.text: because having fifty different versions would cause confusion
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says having fifty states each do these jobs separately would cause 'enormous' confusion, like worthless dollar bills or messy deals with other countries.

choices.text: because each state wanted to use the same coins
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about states wanting to use the same coins — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

choices.text: because foreign countries refused to work with individual states
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage mentions making deals with foreign countries, it never says those countries refused to work with individual states.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What the State Governments Do

Actually, states hold a great deal of power on their own. Each state runs its own public school system, sets its own speed limits, manages its own parks, and writes its own criminal laws. Your state decides how old you must be to drive, how many days you attend school each year, and what subjects you study.

Because people in Alaska face different conditions than people in Arizona. A state government is closer to its citizens — like a local coach who knows each player, compared to a league official who has never visited your field. State leaders understand what their communities need.
stimulus.content_text: What the State Governments Do Actually, states hold a great deal of power on their own. Each state runs its own public school system, sets its own speed limits, manages its own parks, and writes its own criminal laws. Your state decides how old you must be to drive, how many days you attend school each year, and what subjects you study. Because people in Alaska face different conditions than people in Arizona. A state government is closer to its citizens — like a local coach who knows each player, compared to a league official who has never visited your field. State leaders understand what their communities need.

prompt._: According to the passage, which of the following lists state government powers in the same order the author presents them?

choices.text: writes criminal laws, manages parks, sets speed limits, runs public schools
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it starts with 'writes criminal laws,' which is actually the last power mentioned in the passage.

choices.text: manages parks, writes criminal laws, runs public schools, sets speed limits
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it starts with 'manages parks,' which is actually the third item listed in the passage, not the first.

choices.text: runs public schools, sets speed limits, manages parks, writes criminal laws
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it lists all four state powers in the exact same order they appear in the passage.

choices.text: sets speed limits, runs public schools, writes criminal laws, manages parks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it starts with 'sets speed limits,' but the passage lists 'runs public schools' first.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Shared Powers and Disagreements

Some powers belong to both levels of government. Both can levy taxes — that means collect money from citizens to pay for services. Both can build roads, set up courts, and pass laws. These overlapping powers sometimes cause tension. When the national government and a state government disagree about who has authority, courts step in. Judges look carefully at the Constitution to decide which level of government should be in charge.

The Tenth Amendment, part of the Bill of Rights (the first ten additions to the Constitution), draws a clear line. It says that any power not specifically given to the national government belongs to the states or to the people. The Founders included this amendment because many Americans worried the new national government would swallow up state authority. The Tenth Amendment was their promise: if the Constitution doesn't say the national government can do it, then states and citizens keep that power.
stimulus.content_text: Shared Powers and Disagreements Some powers belong to both levels of government. Both can levy taxes — that means collect money from citizens to pay for services. Both can build roads, set up courts, and pass laws. These overlapping powers sometimes cause tension. When the national government and a state government disagree about who has authority, courts step in. Judges look carefully at the Constitution to decide which level of government should be in charge. The Tenth Amendment, part of the Bill of Rights (the first ten additions to the Constitution), draws a clear line. It says that any power not specifically given to the national government belongs to the states or to the people. The Founders included this amendment because many Americans worried the new national government would swallow up state authority. The Tenth Amendment was their promise: if the Constitution doesn't say the national government can do it, then states and citizens keep that power.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are the national government and state governments alike?

choices.text: They both decide what the Constitution means.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says judges and courts look at the Constitution to decide who is in charge — not both levels of government together.

choices.text: They both collect money to pay for services.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says both the national and state governments can levy taxes, which means collecting money from citizens to pay for services.

choices.text: They both give extra power to the citizens.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Tenth Amendment protects powers that already belong to states and citizens, rather than governments giving citizens extra power.

choices.text: They both write rules for the Bill of Rights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that the Founders added the Bill of Rights to the Constitution — it is not something both governments write rules for.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Balance That Keeps Changing

Federalism has not stayed frozen since 1787. In the early years of the republic — a government where citizens elect their leaders — the states held more power, and the national government stayed small. It didn't fund schools or protect the environment. Over time, especially after the Civil War and throughout the twentieth century, the national government grew. Today it is involved in healthcare, environmental protection, and civil rights. In the 1800s, the national government sent almost no money to local schools. Now it helps pay for school lunches, special education, and more.
stimulus.content_text: A Balance That Keeps Changing Federalism has not stayed frozen since 1787. In the early years of the republic — a government where citizens elect their leaders — the states held more power, and the national government stayed small. It didn't fund schools or protect the environment. Over time, especially after the Civil War and throughout the twentieth century, the national government grew. Today it is involved in healthcare, environmental protection, and civil rights. In the 1800s, the national government sent almost no money to local schools. Now it helps pay for school lunches, special education, and more.

prompt._: In the section 'A Balance That Keeps Changing,' what does the word 'republic' mean?

choices.text: a group of states that share the same laws
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a republic is about how leaders are elected, not about states sharing the same laws.

choices.text: a government where citizens elect their leaders
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines 'republic' as 'a government where citizens elect their leaders' right after the word.

choices.text: a system where one ruler makes all decisions
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a republic is the opposite of one ruler deciding everything — it's the citizens who choose who leads them.

choices.text: a country that has a very large army
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about army size when explaining what a republic is.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Strengths and Struggles

Federalism is not perfect. When national and state laws clash, confusion follows. Some states provide better schools or healthcare than others, creating real inequalities. Americans still argue about exactly where national authority ends and state authority begins.

But federalism also gives states the freedom to experiment. One state can try a new idea — like a different way of running elections — without forcing it on the whole country. If the idea works, other states can copy it. If it fails, only one state is affected. Federalism also builds extra protection for your rights. If the national government threatens a freedom, your state may still defend it.

That difference is not a mistake. It is the Founders' design — a government built not as one giant machine, but as fifty workshops and one shared engine, each with a job to do.

Some states provide better schools or healthcare than others, creating real inequalities. Americans still argue about exactly where national authority ends and state authority begins. But federalism also gives states the freedom to experiment. One state can try a new idea — like a different way of running elections — without forcing it on the whole country. If the idea works, other states can copy it. If it fails, only one state is affected. Federalism also builds extra protection for your rights. If the national government threatens a freedom, your state may still defend it. That difference is not a mistake. It is the Founders' design — a government built not as one giant machine, but as fifty workshops and one shared engine, each with a job to do.

prompt._: Which detail from the text supports the author's point that federalism is not perfect?

choices.text: A state can defend freedoms against the government.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a state defending your freedoms is a benefit of federalism, not one of its weaknesses.

choices.text: Clashing laws can cause confusion for the people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says clashing laws cause confusion, which is one of the struggles the author mentions to show federalism isn't perfect.

choices.text: The Founders built the government as fifty workshops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'fifty workshops' detail describes how the government was designed, not what makes federalism imperfect.

choices.text: States can try out new ways to run elections.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because states trying new election ideas is listed as a strength of federalism, not a problem with it.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the national government's role different from a state government's role?

choices.text: The national government sets the speed limits on roads, but a state government controls the military.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says states set their own speed limits and the national government maintains the armed forces, so this answer has the two roles switched around.

choices.text: The national government manages trade between states, but a state government runs the local schools.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the national government handles interstate commerce (trade between states) and state governments run their own public schools — two separate jobs for two separate levels of government.

choices.text: The national government collects taxes from citizens, but a state government prints the dollar bills.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says both the national and state governments can collect taxes, and it's the national government — not states — that prints money.

choices.text: The national government makes all rules for the country, but a state government only collects taxes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because federalism means the national government does NOT make all the rules — power is divided between the national government and state governments on purpose.

prompt._: Why did the Founders add the Tenth Amendment to the Constitution?

choices.text: They hoped all fifty states would eventually follow the same laws.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because federalism is designed to let states make their own different laws, like different driving ages, not to make every state follow the same rules.

choices.text: They worried the national government would take over state power.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Americans worried the national government would 'swallow up' state authority, so the Tenth Amendment promised to keep powers not listed in the Constitution with the states and the people.

choices.text: They needed courts to settle arguments between the two levels.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because courts stepping in to settle disagreements is a different part of federalism, not the reason the Tenth Amendment was added.

choices.text: They wanted the national government to grow stronger over time.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Tenth Amendment was meant to protect state power and limit the national government, not help it grow stronger.

prompt._: Which choice best shows the order the author uses to explain federalism across the article?

choices.text: First the author explains shared powers, next gives examples of state rules, and last tells why the government prints money.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it skips the definition of federalism entirely and puts the topics in the wrong order.

choices.text: First the author defines federalism, next lists problems with federalism, and last explains government powers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains government powers before listing problems with federalism, not after.

choices.text: First the author tells how federalism changed, next lists government powers, and last defines federalism.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article defines federalism at the beginning, not at the end, and talks about how it changed near the end, not near the beginning.

choices.text: First the author defines federalism, next explains government powers, and last describes how federalism changed.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article first defines federalism, then explains what national and state governments can do, and finally describes how federalism has changed since 1787.

prompt._: Based on the whole article, what is the main idea the author develops by comparing the national government and state governments?

choices.text: State governments are stronger because they are closer to their citizens
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because being close to citizens is just one reason states have power, not the main idea the author develops throughout the whole article.

choices.text: The national government has slowly taken over most state powers since 1787
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while the article does say the national government grew over time, it never says it took over most state powers — states still control schools, speed limits, and more.

choices.text: Both levels of government do the same work in different locations
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article clearly shows the two levels of government do *different* jobs, not the same work in different places.

choices.text: Both levels of government share some powers but handle different jobs
choices.feedback: This is correct because the entire article explains how the national government handles big nationwide jobs, states handle local jobs, and both share some powers like taxing and building roads.

syllabus_metadata.title: Federalism: Power Shared

assessments.title: President Washington Sets the Example

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The President Without a Rulebook

A Job No One Had Ever Done

On April 30, 1789, George Washington stood on the balcony of Federal Hall in New York City, placed his hand on a Bible, and spoke the words that made him the first President of the United States. Because no one, including Washington himself, knew exactly what a president was supposed to do next.

You might think Washington was the first person to lead the country, but that's not quite right. Other men had led Congress under an earlier set of rules called the Articles of Confederation. Washington was the first president under the brand-new Constitution. And that Constitution, while it described the president's powers in broad terms, left enormous questions unanswered. Every choice Washington made would set a precedent — an example that future presidents would feel expected to follow, the way the first footprints in fresh snow create a path for everyone walking behind.
stimulus.content_text: The President Without a RulebookA Job No One Had Ever Done On April 30, 1789, George Washington stood on the balcony of Federal Hall in New York City, placed his hand on a Bible, and spoke the words that made him the first President of the United States. Because no one, including Washington himself, knew exactly what a president was supposed to do next. You might think Washington was the first person to lead the country, but that's not quite right. Other men had led Congress under an earlier set of rules called the Articles of Confederation. Washington was the first president under the brand-new Constitution. And that Constitution, while it described the president's powers in broad terms, left enormous questions unanswered. Every choice Washington made would set a precedent — an example that future presidents would feel expected to follow, the way the first footprints in fresh snow create a path for everyone walking behind.

prompt._: According to the article, how was George Washington different from the earlier men who led Congress?

choices.text: He led the country without any rules to guide him.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington did have the Constitution to guide him, even though it didn't answer every question.

choices.text: He led the country under the brand-new Constitution.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states Washington was the first president under the brand-new Constitution, setting him apart from earlier leaders.

choices.text: He led the country from a balcony in New York City.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the balcony in New York City is just where Washington took his oath — it has nothing to do with how he was different from earlier leaders.

choices.text: He led the country under the Articles of Confederation.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Articles of Confederation is what the earlier leaders used, not Washington.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Leader Who Didn't Want the Job

Washington was elected unanimously, meaning every single voter in the Electoral College — the group of representatives who officially pick the president on behalf of their states — chose him. No president since has received every single vote. Yet Washington had not campaigned for the job. After leading the Continental Army through eight brutal years of war, he had retired to his plantation at Mount Vernon, Virginia, hoping to live as a farmer. But the young nation needed someone everyone trusted. Washington answered the call.
stimulus.content_text: The Leader Who Didn't Want the Job Washington was elected unanimously, meaning every single voter in the Electoral College — the group of representatives who officially pick the president on behalf of their states — chose him. No president since has received every single vote. Yet Washington had not campaigned for the job. After leading the Continental Army through eight brutal years of war, he had retired to his plantation at Mount Vernon, Virginia, hoping to live as a farmer. But the young nation needed someone everyone trusted. Washington answered the call.

prompt._: Based on this section, what is the main reason Washington became president even though he wanted to be a farmer?

choices.text: He campaigned hard and won every single vote in the election.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states Washington had NOT campaigned for the job, even though he did end up winning every vote.

choices.text: He wanted to leave Mount Vernon and find a more important job.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington actually wanted to stay at Mount Vernon as a farmer — he did not want to leave for a more important job.

choices.text: The young nation needed a leader that everyone trusted.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says 'the young nation needed someone everyone trusted,' which is why Washington gave up his farming life to become president.

choices.text: The Electoral College forced him to take the job against his will.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Washington 'answered the call,' meaning he made his own choice to serve, not that anyone forced him.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Inventing the Presidency

One of Washington's first big moves was creating the Cabinet, a small group of expert advisors — like assembling a team of coaches, each one responsible for a different part of the game. The Constitution never mentioned a Cabinet. Washington invented it because he knew one person could not run an entire government alone. He appointed Thomas Jefferson as Secretary of State to handle relationships with other countries, Alexander Hamilton as Secretary of the Treasury to manage the nation's money, Henry Knox as Secretary of War, and Edmund Randolph as Attorney General. Every president since has kept a Cabinet.

Washington also shaped how the president should act. Some people wanted to call him "His Highness" or "His Excellency," titles that sounded like names for a king. Washington refused. He insisted on the simple title "Mr. President." He held formal gatherings but kept them open to ordinary citizens. He traveled through the states so people could see their leader face to face. In a country that had just fought a war to escape a monarchy — a government ruled by a king or queen with unlimited power — Washington wanted to prove that the president served the people, not the other way around.

The Constitution never mentioned a Cabinet. Washington invented it because he knew one person could not run an entire government alone. He appointed Thomas Jefferson as Secretary of State to handle relationships with other countries, Alexander Hamilton as Secretary of the Treasury to manage the nation's money, Henry Knox as Secretary of War, and Edmund Randolph as Attorney General. Every president since has kept a Cabinet. Washington also shaped how the president should act. Some people wanted to call him "His Highness" or "His Excellency," titles that sounded like names for a king. Washington refused. He insisted on the simple title "Mr. President." He held formal gatherings but kept them open to ordinary citizens. He traveled through the states so people could see their leader face to face. In a country that had just fought a war to escape a monarchy — a government ruled by a king or queen with unlimited power — Washington wanted to prove that the president served the people, not the other way around.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did Washington do after he created the Cabinet?

choices.text: He fought a war to escape a monarchy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the war to escape a monarchy happened before Washington became president — it's mentioned as background history, not something he did after creating the Cabinet.

choices.text: He appointed himself Secretary of State
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington appointed Thomas Jefferson as Secretary of State, not himself.

choices.text: He wrote the Cabinet into the Constitution
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the Constitution never mentioned a Cabinet — Washington invented it on his own.

choices.text: He shaped how the president should act
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage's second paragraph begins with 'Washington also shaped how the president should act,' showing this came after he created the Cabinet.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Troubles at Home and Abroad

Jefferson wanted power to stay with the states and with farmers. Washington tried to keep both men working together, but their arguments grew hotter every year.

Meanwhile, when the federal government placed a new tax on whiskey, farmers in western Pennsylvania exploded with anger. They refused to pay. Some attacked the tax collectors. This uprising became known as the Whiskey Rebellion. Washington sent thousands of troops to restore order. The rebellion ended without a major battle, but Washington had made his point: the new government had the authority to enforce its laws.

Across the ocean, war erupted between Britain and France. Both nations pressured the United States to pick a side. Washington kept the country neutral, believing the young nation was too fragile to survive another war.
stimulus.content_text: Troubles at Home and Abroad Jefferson wanted power to stay with the states and with farmers. Washington tried to keep both men working together, but their arguments grew hotter every year. Meanwhile, when the federal government placed a new tax on whiskey, farmers in western Pennsylvania exploded with anger. They refused to pay. Some attacked the tax collectors. This uprising became known as the Whiskey Rebellion. Washington sent thousands of troops to restore order. The rebellion ended without a major battle, but Washington had made his point: the new government had the authority to enforce its laws. Across the ocean, war erupted between Britain and France. Both nations pressured the United States to pick a side. Washington kept the country neutral, believing the young nation was too fragile to survive another war.

prompt._: How was Washington's response to the Whiskey Rebellion similar to his decision to stay neutral in the war between Britain and France?

choices.text: Both actions showed he agreed with Jefferson about states' rights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually tells us Washington and Jefferson disagreed, and neither decision was about supporting states' rights.

choices.text: Both actions showed he wanted the United States to build a strong army
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington chose to stay OUT of the war between Britain and France, which is the opposite of trying to build a strong army through fighting.

choices.text: Both actions showed he wanted to protect the young nation from danger
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows Washington sent troops to protect the government's stability and stayed neutral to protect a nation too fragile for war — both choices were about keeping the young country safe.

choices.text: Both actions showed he wanted to punish people who broke the law
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because staying neutral in the war between Britain and France had nothing to do with punishing lawbreakers — Washington just wanted to keep the country out of danger.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Power of Walking Away

Perhaps Washington's greatest gift to the country was something he chose not to do. After serving two terms, he stepped down voluntarily. You might assume that a two-term limit was always the law, but it wasn't. Washington simply decided that eight years was enough. His choice became a tradition that presidents honored for over 150 years, until the 22nd Amendment finally made it official law in 1951.

The world was stunned. Kings did not surrender their thrones. But Washington believed that no single person should hold power indefinitely — meaning forever, with no end in sight. He believed the peaceful handoff of authority mattered more than any one leader.

In his Farewell Address, published in newspapers across the country, Washington left two warnings. He cautioned against political parties, fearing they would split citizens into angry groups that cared more about winning than governing. He also warned against binding agreements with foreign nations that could drag the country into distant wars.

Washington returned to Mount Vernon in 1797 and died just two years later, in December 1799. Every peaceful election since — every time one president hands the office to the next — traces back to that single, quiet decision.

His choice became a tradition that presidents honored for over 150 years, until the 22nd Amendment finally made it official law in 1951. The world was stunned. Kings did not surrender their thrones. But Washington believed that no single person should hold power indefinitely — meaning forever, with no end in sight. He believed the peaceful handoff of authority mattered more than any one leader.
