He cautioned against political parties, fearing they would split citizens into angry groups that cared more about winning than governing. He also warned against binding agreements with foreign nations that could drag the country into distant wars. Washington returned to Mount Vernon in 1797 and died just two years later, in December 1799. Every peaceful election since — every time one president hands the office to the next — traces back to that single, quiet decision.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the "22nd Amendment"?

choices.text: A speech that warns the country about distant wars
choices.feedback: Warning about distant wars was also part of Washington's Farewell Address, not the 22nd Amendment.

choices.text: A rule that stops a king from endless power
choices.feedback: This is tricky, but the passage talks about kings not giving up power when describing Washington's decision — that idea is not what the 22nd Amendment is about.

choices.text: A law that limits a president to two terms
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the 22nd Amendment made the two-term limit 'official law in 1951,' which means it's the law that stops a president from serving more than two terms.

choices.text: A party that splits the citizens into angry groups
choices.feedback: Political parties splitting citizens into angry groups is something Washington warned about in his Farewell Address, not what the 22nd Amendment means.

prompt._: In a monarchy, rulers often keep power for life. Which choice best shows how Washington set a different example for American presidents?

choices.text: By serving two terms and then giving up power on purpose
choices.feedback: This is correct because Washington chose to step down after two terms on purpose, proving that unlike kings, American presidents give up power peacefully instead of holding it forever.

choices.text: By creating a Cabinet and asking advisors to help lead the country
choices.feedback: Creating a Cabinet was about getting expert help to run the country, not about showing that power should have a time limit like a monarchy does not.

choices.text: By sending troops to stop the Whiskey Rebellion quickly
choices.feedback: Sending troops to stop the Whiskey Rebellion showed the government could enforce its laws, but it did not set an example about giving up power the way a monarchy never would.

choices.text: By traveling through the states so citizens could see him
choices.feedback: Traveling through the states showed citizens their leader, but it had nothing to do with limiting how long a president stays in power.

prompt._: What is the main reason George Washington had to invent so many parts of the presidency?

choices.text: The young nation was too weak to survive another war.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the nation being too weak for war was the reason Washington stayed neutral between Britain and France, not the reason he had to invent parts of the presidency.

choices.text: The new Constitution did not give exact rules for the job.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the Constitution left 'enormous questions unanswered,' so Washington had to make up the rules as he went.

choices.text: He was chosen by all voters to make everyone agree on politics.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being chosen by all voters showed people trusted him, but it didn't explain why he had to create new rules for the job.

choices.text: He needed to prove the government had the power to stop rebellions.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stopping the Whiskey Rebellion was one thing Washington did as president, not the reason he had to invent the presidency.

prompt._: Which choice shows the correct order of events in how Washington helped shape the presidency?

choices.text: He became president, then he created the Cabinet, and later he stepped down after two terms.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Washington became president first, then created the Cabinet as one of his first moves, and finally chose to step down after two terms.

choices.text: He warned against political parties, then he sent troops to stop the Whiskey Rebellion, and later he became president.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because warning against political parties and sending troops to stop the Whiskey Rebellion both happened while Washington was already president, so they could not have come before he took office.

choices.text: He created the Cabinet, then he became president, and later he warned against political parties.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington created the Cabinet after he became president, not before.

choices.text: He stepped down after two terms, then he created the Cabinet, and then he became president.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington became president first, then created the Cabinet, and stepping down after two terms was the last thing that happened — not the first.

prompt._: How does the author use Washington's choice of the title 'Mr. President' and his decision to step down after two terms to support the same point?

choices.text: Both examples show that Washington wanted to be remembered as the greatest leader in history
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Washington wanted to be remembered as the greatest leader — he actually tried to avoid acting like he was more important than the people he served.

choices.text: Both examples show that Washington wanted the president to be a servant of the people, not a king
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both examples — refusing a kingly title and stepping down from power — show Washington wanted the president to serve the people, not act like a king with unlimited power.

choices.text: Both examples show that Washington followed rules that were already written in the Constitution
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually tells us the Constitution did NOT mention the Cabinet, and the two-term limit wasn't a law until 1951, so Washington was making his own choices, not following written rules.

choices.text: Both examples show that Washington was copying what earlier leaders had done under the Articles of Confederation
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington was setting brand-new examples called precedents, not copying what earlier leaders had done under the Articles of Confederation.

syllabus_metadata.title: President Washington Sets the Example

assessments.title: Hamilton vs. Jefferson: A Battle of Ideas

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Hamilton vs. Jefferson: The Argument That Built American Politics

Two Men, One Room, Zero Agreement

Picture a long wooden table in Philadelphia, around 1790. President George Washington sits at the head. To his left, Alexander Hamilton leans forward, talking fast about plans for banks and factories. To his right, Thomas Jefferson sits tall and still, shaking his head. These two men served in Washington's Cabinet — his team of top advisors — but they disagreed about almost everything. Big things, like what kind of country America should become.

You might think the Founding Fathers all shared one vision for the new nation. Actually, they argued fiercely. And the biggest argument of all — between Hamilton and Jefferson — created something that still affects your life today: political parties.
stimulus.content_text: Hamilton vs. Jefferson: The Argument That Built American PoliticsTwo Men, One Room, Zero Agreement Picture a long wooden table in Philadelphia, around 1790. President George Washington sits at the head. To his left, Alexander Hamilton leans forward, talking fast about plans for banks and factories. To his right, Thomas Jefferson sits tall and still, shaking his head. These two men served in Washington's Cabinet — his team of top advisors — but they disagreed about almost everything. Big things, like what kind of country America should become. You might think the Founding Fathers all shared one vision for the new nation. Actually, they argued fiercely. And the biggest argument of all — between Hamilton and Jefferson — created something that still affects your life today: political parties.

prompt._: According to the text, how did Hamilton and Jefferson differ when they were in Washington's Cabinet?

choices.text: They disagreed about what kind of country America should become.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states they disagreed about 'what kind of country America should become,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: They argued about who should be the next president.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about Hamilton and Jefferson arguing over who should be the next president.

choices.text: They wanted to create different political parties.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Hamilton and Jefferson weren't trying to create political parties — the passage says parties were something that came out of their disagreements, not a goal they had.

choices.text: They both shared the same vision for the new nation.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says the opposite of what the passage tells us — the text clearly says they disagreed about almost everything.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Hamilton's Vision: Banks, Factories, and a Powerful Government

Alexander Hamilton was the Secretary of the Treasury, the person in charge of the nation's money. He wanted a strong national government that could make bold decisions for the whole country. He believed the United States needed to become a modern, commercial nation like Britain.

Hamilton proposed creating a national bank — a central place to manage the government's money, issue a stable currency (the official coins and paper money people use, like the dollars in your pocket), and make loans to businesses. He also wanted the federal government to take on the debts that individual states had built up during the War, binding the states more tightly to the national government.

Industry, he believed, would make America wealthy and strong.
stimulus.content_text: Hamilton's Vision: Banks, Factories, and a Powerful Government Alexander Hamilton was the Secretary of the Treasury, the person in charge of the nation's money. He wanted a strong national government that could make bold decisions for the whole country. He believed the United States needed to become a modern, commercial nation like Britain. Hamilton proposed creating a national bank — a central place to manage the government's money, issue a stable currency (the official coins and paper money people use, like the dollars in your pocket), and make loans to businesses. He also wanted the federal government to take on the debts that individual states had built up during the War, binding the states more tightly to the national government. Industry, he believed, would make America wealthy and strong.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Hamilton want the federal government to take on the debts of individual states?

choices.text: to tie the states more closely to the national government
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says taking on state debts would bind, or tie, the states more tightly to the national government.

choices.text: to help states build more factories and banks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't connect taking on state debts to building factories or banks — those were separate ideas Hamilton had.

choices.text: to replace the currency that states were already using
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because replacing state currencies isn't mentioned as a reason for taking on debts — that idea is related to the national bank, not the debts.

choices.text: to punish states that had spent too much money during the War
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Hamilton wanted to punish states — he wanted to unite them, not get back at them.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Jefferson's Vision: Farms, Freedom, and Local Power

Thomas Jefferson, the Secretary of State, saw a completely different future. He pictured a nation of independent farmers who owned their own land and governed themselves through local and state governments. He worried that wealthy bankers would hold power over ordinary people, just as they did in Europe.

Jefferson feared that a powerful national government, combined with a wealthy elite (a small group of rich, powerful people at the top), would crush the freedom of everyday citizens. He wanted government kept small and close to home, where regular people could watch it and control it. While Hamilton admired Britain's financial system, Jefferson looked to France, whose revolution had been inspired partly by America's own fight for liberty.
stimulus.content_text: Jefferson's Vision: Farms, Freedom, and Local Power Thomas Jefferson, the Secretary of State, saw a completely different future. He pictured a nation of independent farmers who owned their own land and governed themselves through local and state governments. He worried that wealthy bankers would hold power over ordinary people, just as they did in Europe. Jefferson feared that a powerful national government, combined with a wealthy elite (a small group of rich, powerful people at the top), would crush the freedom of everyday citizens. He wanted government kept small and close to home, where regular people could watch it and control it. While Hamilton admired Britain's financial system, Jefferson looked to France, whose revolution had been inspired partly by America's own fight for liberty.

prompt._: According to the text, which event happened first in the sequence described by the author?

choices.text: Jefferson became the Secretary of State
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Jefferson being Secretary of State is just his role in the story — the passage doesn't say this happened before America's fight for liberty.

choices.text: America fought for its own liberty
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says France's revolution was inspired by America's fight for liberty, so America's fight had to come first in the sequence.

choices.text: France had a revolution inspired by America
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because France's revolution was inspired by America's fight for liberty, which means America's fight happened first.

choices.text: Hamilton set up Britain's financial system
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Hamilton only admired Britain's financial system — he didn't create it, so this event isn't even described in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Bank Battle and the Fight Over Friends

Their biggest clash came over Hamilton's national bank. Jefferson argued that the Constitution did not give Congress the power to create one. If the Constitution didn't say you could do it, you couldn't — that was Jefferson's rule. Hamilton disagreed. He pointed out that the Constitution gave Congress the power to do whatever was "necessary and proper" to carry out its duties. A bank was necessary, Hamilton said, to manage the nation's money.

Washington listened to both sides. He agreed with Hamilton, and in 1791, the Bank of the United States opened its doors.

Then came foreign policy. When war erupted between Britain and France in 1793, Hamilton wanted closer ties with Britain, America's biggest trading partner. Jefferson supported France. Washington chose neutrality — refusing to pick a side. But the debate made the split between Hamilton and Jefferson even deeper.
stimulus.content_text: The Bank Battle and the Fight Over Friends Their biggest clash came over Hamilton's national bank. Jefferson argued that the Constitution did not give Congress the power to create one. If the Constitution didn't say you could do it, you couldn't — that was Jefferson's rule. Hamilton disagreed. He pointed out that the Constitution gave Congress the power to do whatever was "necessary and proper" to carry out its duties. A bank was necessary, Hamilton said, to manage the nation's money. Washington listened to both sides. He agreed with Hamilton, and in 1791, the Bank of the United States opened its doors. Then came foreign policy. When war erupted between Britain and France in 1793, Hamilton wanted closer ties with Britain, America's biggest trading partner. Jefferson supported France. Washington chose neutrality — refusing to pick a side. But the debate made the split between Hamilton and Jefferson even deeper.

prompt._: Based on the text, how did Hamilton's view of the Constitution compare to Jefferson's view?

choices.text: Hamilton thought it gave power to the president, while Jefferson thought it empowered Congress.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Hamilton thought the Constitution gave extra power to the president — the debate was about what Congress could do.

choices.text: Hamilton thought it should be completely changed, while Jefferson thought it should stay as is.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither Hamilton nor Jefferson wanted to completely change the Constitution; they just disagreed on how to interpret what it already said.

choices.text: Hamilton thought it allowed necessary actions, while Jefferson thought it needed exact words.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Hamilton believed the Constitution allowed 'necessary and proper' actions, while Jefferson believed it had to use exact words to allow something.

choices.text: Hamilton thought it supported British trade, while Jefferson thought it supported French trade.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because preferring British or French trade was part of the foreign policy argument, not the debate about how to read the Constitution.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Birth of Political Parties

Here's the surprising part: when the United States began, there were no political parties at all. The Constitution never mentions them. But Hamilton and Jefferson's disagreements grew so intense that their supporters organized into rival groups competing for votes.

Hamilton founded the Federalist Party, which backed a strong national government, close ties with Britain, and support for business and industry. Jefferson and James Madison founded the Democratic-Republican Party, which favored states' rights, friendship with France, and a farming economy.

Washington never joined either party. When he left office, he warned Americans that political parties could tear the nation apart by making people more loyal to their party than to their country. But the split had already happened. The two-party system was here to stay.
stimulus.content_text: The Birth of Political Parties Here's the surprising part: when the United States began, there were no political parties at all. The Constitution never mentions them. But Hamilton and Jefferson's disagreements grew so intense that their supporters organized into rival groups competing for votes. Hamilton founded the Federalist Party, which backed a strong national government, close ties with Britain, and support for business and industry. Jefferson and James Madison founded the Democratic-Republican Party, which favored states' rights, friendship with France, and a farming economy. Washington never joined either party. When he left office, he warned Americans that political parties could tear the nation apart by making people more loyal to their party than to their country. But the split had already happened. The two-party system was here to stay.

prompt._: According to the text, what was the Federalist Party?

choices.text: A group that favored states' rights and a farming economy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because favoring states' rights and a farming economy describes Jefferson's Democratic-Republican Party, not the Federalist Party.

choices.text: A group that backed a strong national government.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the Federalist Party 'backed a strong national government,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: A group that George Washington joined after leaving office.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Washington never joined either party, even after leaving office.

choices.text: A group that was officially created by the Constitution.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically tells us the Constitution never mentions political parties at all.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Both Right, Still Arguing

The striking thing is that both men got something right. Hamilton was correct that the nation needed a strong financial system and a thriving economy. Jefferson was correct that individual rights and suspicion of concentrated power protect a free society. America has spent more than two hundred years trying to balance these two ideas — like a seesaw that never quite levels out.

Every time you hear adults argue about whether the government should do more or do less, they are carrying on the same debate that Hamilton and Jefferson started at that long table in Philadelphia. Two men who could barely stand each other gave the country something it never planned for — and never got rid of.
stimulus.content_text: Both Right, Still Arguing The striking thing is that both men got something right. Hamilton was correct that the nation needed a strong financial system and a thriving economy. Jefferson was correct that individual rights and suspicion of concentrated power protect a free society. America has spent more than two hundred years trying to balance these two ideas — like a seesaw that never quite levels out. Every time you hear adults argue about whether the government should do more or do less, they are carrying on the same debate that Hamilton and Jefferson started at that long table in Philadelphia. Two men who could barely stand each other gave the country something it never planned for — and never got rid of.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that Hamilton and Jefferson's debate still affects America today?

choices.text: Adults still argue about government doing more or less.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says adults today still argue about the same things Hamilton and Jefferson argued about, showing the debate is still alive.

choices.text: The nation needed a strong financial system.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes Hamilton's idea about the economy, not evidence that the debate is still affecting America today.

choices.text: America solved the debate more than two hundred years ago.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — America has spent over two hundred years trying to balance these ideas and never resolved the debate.

choices.text: The two men met at a long table in Philadelphia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because where the men met is just a background detail and has nothing to do with the debate continuing into the present.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Thomas Jefferson's vision for America different from Alexander Hamilton's vision?

choices.text: Jefferson focused on paying off debts, while Hamilton focused on ignoring the debts.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article says it was Hamilton who wanted the federal government to take on states' debts, not Jefferson.

choices.text: Jefferson wanted to eliminate the government, while Hamilton wanted to create a monarchy.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Jefferson wanted small government, not no government at all, and Hamilton never proposed making America a monarchy.

choices.text: Jefferson favored a nation of farmers, while Hamilton favored a nation of industry.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly states Jefferson wanted a nation of farmers with local power, while Hamilton wanted industry, banks, and a strong national government.

choices.text: Jefferson supported an alliance with Britain, while Hamilton supported an alliance with France.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says Hamilton wanted closer ties with Britain and Jefferson supported France, not the other way around.

prompt._: According to the article, what caused the creation of the first political parties in the United States?

choices.text: The decision by President Washington to stay neutral during the war in Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington's neutrality decision was just one of many disagreements between Hamilton and Jefferson, not the reason political parties were created.

choices.text: The warning from George Washington about the dangers of dividing the new nation.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington gave his warning when he left office, which was after the political parties had already formed, so it could not have caused them.

choices.text: The intense arguments between Hamilton and Jefferson over the nation's future.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that Hamilton and Jefferson's intense disagreements caused their supporters to organize into rival groups, which became the first political parties.

choices.text: The rule in the Constitution that required leaders to form rival voting groups.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article specifically tells us the Constitution never mentions political parties — there was no rule requiring them.

prompt._: According to the article, which sequence best describes how Hamilton and Jefferson's disagreements led to lasting changes in American politics?

choices.text: Hamilton and Jefferson argued in Washington's Cabinet, then their supporters formed rival parties.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Hamilton and Jefferson argued in Washington's Cabinet first, and then their supporters formed the Federalist Party and the Democratic-Republican Party as a result.

choices.text: Political parties formed first, then Hamilton and Jefferson began to disagree about banks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the parties didn't form first — Hamilton and Jefferson's arguments in the Cabinet came first, and the parties grew out of those disagreements.

choices.text: Jefferson created a national bank first, then Hamilton started the Democratic-Republican Party.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Hamilton proposed the national bank (not Jefferson), and it was Jefferson and Madison who founded the Democratic-Republican Party (not Hamilton).

choices.text: Washington warned about parties first, then Hamilton and Jefferson started to argue.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Washington gave his warning about political parties when he left office, which happened after the parties had already been created.

prompt._: What main idea does the author support by comparing Hamilton's and Jefferson's contributions to America?

choices.text: Jefferson's ideas turned out to be more important over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author gives equal credit to both men's ideas, not more credit to Jefferson.

choices.text: The Founding Fathers always agreed on important decisions
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Hamilton and Jefferson 'disagreed about almost everything' and even argued fiercely.

choices.text: Both men's ideas were needed to shape a balanced nation
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author says both men got something right and compares their ideas to a seesaw that America has been trying to balance for over two hundred years.

choices.text: Political parties were planned from the very beginning
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states there were no political parties when America began and the Constitution never mentions them.

syllabus_metadata.title: Hamilton vs. Jefferson: A Battle of Ideas

assessments.title: The Rise of Political Parties

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Parties Nobody Wanted

A Dinner That Split a Nation

In 1790, Thomas Jefferson arrived at a dinner party in New York City. Across the table sat Alexander Hamilton. The two men smiled politely, ate their meal, and struck a deal about where to build the nation's capital. But behind those polite smiles, a fierce disagreement was growing — one that would split Americans into two rival groups and shape the country's politics for over two hundred years.

The Constitution says nothing about political parties. For the first few years under the new government, no official parties existed at all. Most of the Founders, including George Washington, hoped parties would never form. They believed Americans should work together as one people, not fight each other in organized teams. But disagreements about how to run the country proved too strong to ignore.
stimulus.content_text: The Parties Nobody WantedA Dinner That Split a Nation In 1790, Thomas Jefferson arrived at a dinner party in New York City. Across the table sat Alexander Hamilton. The two men smiled politely, ate their meal, and struck a deal about where to build the nation's capital. But behind those polite smiles, a fierce disagreement was growing — one that would split Americans into two rival groups and shape the country's politics for over two hundred years. The Constitution says nothing about political parties. For the first few years under the new government, no official parties existed at all. Most of the Founders, including George Washington, hoped parties would never form. They believed Americans should work together as one people, not fight each other in organized teams. But disagreements about how to run the country proved too strong to ignore.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the contrast between what the Founders hoped for and what actually happened with political parties?

choices.text: The Founders hoped parties would never form, but disagreements proved too strong to ignore.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the Founders hoped parties would never form, but disagreements proved too strong, so parties formed anyway.

choices.text: The Founders wanted two parties, but Americans formed many smaller groups instead.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Founders didn't want two parties — they hoped no parties would ever form at all.

choices.text: The Founders created political parties, but Americans chose not to join any of them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Founders didn't create political parties — the passage says no official parties existed in the early years of the government.

choices.text: The Founders wrote about parties in the Constitution, but no one followed those rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the Constitution says nothing about political parties.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Sides Take Shape

The Federalists — people who wanted a strong national government — gathered around Alexander Hamilton and John Adams. Federalists believed the country needed an energetic central government to keep order, manage money, and deal with other nations. Most Federalists were merchants, bankers, and professionals living in cities along the eastern coast. They admired Britain's stable government and wanted the United States to build similar strong institutions.

On the other side stood the Democratic-Republicans, led by Thomas Jefferson and James Madison. They favored states' rights — the idea that state governments, not the national government, should hold most of the power. Democratic-Republicans believed the best government was the one closest to the people. Their supporters tended to be farmers, craftsmen, and settlers in rural areas and along the frontier. They distrusted putting too much wealth and power in the hands of a few.

You might think these old parties match up neatly with today's Democrats and Republicans. Actually, they don't. Over the next two centuries, party names changed, parties disappeared, and brand-new ones formed. The beliefs of each party shifted dramatically. The Federalists of the 1790s are not the same as any party that exists today.
stimulus.content_text: Two Sides Take Shape The Federalists — people who wanted a strong national government — gathered around Alexander Hamilton and John Adams. Federalists believed the country needed an energetic central government to keep order, manage money, and deal with other nations. Most Federalists were merchants, bankers, and professionals living in cities along the eastern coast. They admired Britain's stable government and wanted the United States to build similar strong institutions. On the other side stood the Democratic-Republicans, led by Thomas Jefferson and James Madison. They favored states' rights — the idea that state governments, not the national government, should hold most of the power. Democratic-Republicans believed the best government was the one closest to the people. Their supporters tended to be farmers, craftsmen, and settlers in rural areas and along the frontier. They distrusted putting too much wealth and power in the hands of a few. You might think these old parties match up neatly with today's Democrats and Republicans. Actually, they don't. Over the next two centuries, party names changed, parties disappeared, and brand-new ones formed. The beliefs of each party shifted dramatically. The Federalists of the 1790s are not the same as any party that exists today.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did the Federalists want an energetic central government?

choices.text: To match today's political parties.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says today's parties do NOT match the old ones, and matching modern parties was never a reason the Federalists wanted strong government.

choices.text: To keep power close to the people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because believing the best government is closest to the people was the Democratic-Republicans' idea, not the Federalists'.

choices.text: To give more power to the states.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because giving more power to the states was the goal of the Democratic-Republicans, who were actually the opposite of the Federalists.

choices.text: To keep order and manage money.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Federalists wanted a strong central government to keep order and manage money — those are the exact words used in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Washington's Warning

George Washington refused to join either side. When he left office in 1796, he gave a famous Farewell Address — a final public letter to the American people. In it, he warned that the "spirit of party" was the worst enemy of democratic government. He feared that loyalty to a party would replace love of country. He worried that partisan divisions — fights driven by team loyalty rather than good ideas — would tear the nation apart.

But Americans ignored his advice. By the time he retired to Mount Vernon, the two parties were already battling fiercely for control.
stimulus.content_text: Washington's Warning George Washington refused to join either side. When he left office in 1796, he gave a famous Farewell Address — a final public letter to the American people. In it, he warned that the "spirit of party" was the worst enemy of democratic government. He feared that loyalty to a party would replace love of country. He worried that partisan divisions — fights driven by team loyalty rather than good ideas — would tear the nation apart. But Americans ignored his advice. By the time he retired to Mount Vernon, the two parties were already battling fiercely for control.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happened after Washington gave his Farewell Address?

choices.text: The two parties agreed to work together peacefully
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the parties battling fiercely, which is the opposite of working together peacefully.

choices.text: Washington decided to join one of the two parties
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the very first sentence of the passage tells us Washington refused to join either side.

choices.text: Americans followed his advice and ended the party fights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that Americans ignored Washington's advice, so the party fights did not end.

choices.text: The two parties kept battling fiercely for control
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says 'the two parties were already battling fiercely for control' after Washington gave his warning.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Nastiest Election

The rivalry exploded in the presidential election of 1800. John Adams, the Federalist president running for a second term, faced Thomas Jefferson, the Democratic-Republican challenger. The campaign was vicious. Federalists warned that Jefferson would destroy religion and bring violent chaos to America. Democratic-Republicans accused Adams of wanting to become a king. They attacked him for signing the Alien and Sedition Acts — laws that made it a crime to criticize the government, which many people saw as an attack on free speech.

And then something momentous happened — something that had almost never happened anywhere in the world before.
stimulus.content_text: The Nastiest Election The rivalry exploded in the presidential election of 1800. John Adams, the Federalist president running for a second term, faced Thomas Jefferson, the Democratic-Republican challenger. The campaign was vicious. Federalists warned that Jefferson would destroy religion and bring violent chaos to America. Democratic-Republicans accused Adams of wanting to become a king. They attacked him for signing the Alien and Sedition Acts — laws that made it a crime to criticize the government, which many people saw as an attack on free speech. And then something momentous happened — something that had almost never happened anywhere in the world before.

prompt._: How were the two sides alike in the election of 1800?

choices.text: Both sides praised their own leader as calm and wise.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both sides were attacking each other harshly, not praising anyone as calm or wise.

choices.text: Both sides said the other leader would harm the country.
choices.feedback: This is correct because Federalists said Jefferson would destroy religion and cause chaos, while Democratic-Republicans said Adams wanted to be a king — both sides were warning that the other leader was dangerous.

choices.text: Both sides agreed religion should stay out of government.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions either side agreeing about religion and government — in fact, religion was used as an attack against Jefferson.

choices.text: Both sides wanted laws against speaking badly about leaders.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the Federalists (Adams's side) supported the Alien and Sedition Acts that limited criticism of the government.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Power of Walking Away

Adams lost the election, and he simply left. No one was forced to flee the country. Adams packed his bags and went home to Massachusetts. Jefferson moved into the president's house and got to work.

But think about what usually happened in other countries when rulers lost power. In France, just a few years earlier, the king had been executed and thousands of people died in a bloody revolution. Throughout history, losing leaders were often killed, imprisoned, or forced into hiding. A peaceful handoff between rival groups was almost unheard of.

Jefferson called it the "Revolution of 1800." He believed it proved that American democracy actually worked. Power could pass from one group to another through votes, not through violence.

Jefferson moved into the president's house and got to work. But think about what usually happened in other countries when rulers lost power. In France, just a few years earlier, the king had been executed and thousands of people died in a bloody revolution. Throughout history, losing leaders were often killed, imprisoned, or forced into hiding. A peaceful handoff between rival groups was almost unheard of. Jefferson called it the "Revolution of 1800." He believed it proved that American democracy actually worked. Power could pass from one group to another through votes, not through violence.

prompt._: According to the text, what does Jefferson mean by the 'Revolution of 1800'?

choices.text: a new law that changed how elections worked
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions any new law or change to how elections worked.

choices.text: a violent overthrow of the government by citizens
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says power passed through votes, not violence — the whole point is that it was peaceful, unlike the bloody revolutions in other countries.

choices.text: a peaceful transfer of power through voting
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Jefferson himself said power could pass 'through votes, not through violence,' which is exactly what a peaceful transfer of power means.

choices.text: a period when Adams refused to leave office
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states Adams packed his bags and left, so he did not refuse to leave office.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Parties That Wouldn't Go Away

The Federalist Party slowly faded after 1800 and eventually vanished. The Democratic-Republican Party survived longer but eventually split into new groups. The modern Democratic Party traces its roots back to Jefferson's party. Other groups, like the Whig Party and later the Republican Party, rose up to represent different viewpoints. The two-party system that began with Hamilton and Jefferson's arguments at that dinner table still drives American politics today.

The Founders never wanted political parties. But those two stubborn teams gave voters something powerful: a clear choice. Every time you watch an election — every time one leader steps aside so another can take over — you are watching an idea that started in 1800, when a president who lost simply walked out the door.

assessments.title: John Adams: The Second President

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
John Adams: The President Who Chose Peace

A Stubborn Man Takes Office

On March 4, 1797, a short, round, balding man stood before the nation and took the presidential oath. George Washington, tall and beloved, watched from nearby. John Adams later admitted he could almost hear people thinking: "I wish his successor had brains of equal size." Adams knew he would never be as popular as Washington. But he did not come to be popular. He came to do what was right — even when it made people furious.

Adams was brilliant, honest, and deeply loyal to his country. He turned possible friends into enemies with sharp words. These qualities — the good ones and the difficult ones — would define his four years as president.
stimulus.content_text: John Adams: The President Who Chose PeaceA Stubborn Man Takes Office On March 4, 1797, a short, round, balding man stood before the nation and took the presidential oath. George Washington, tall and beloved, watched from nearby. John Adams later admitted he could almost hear people thinking: "I wish his successor had brains of equal size." Adams knew he would never be as popular as Washington. But he did not come to be popular. He came to do what was right — even when it made people furious. Adams was brilliant, honest, and deeply loyal to his country. He turned possible friends into enemies with sharp words. These qualities — the good ones and the difficult ones — would define his four years as president.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Adams different from Washington in terms of popularity?

choices.text: Adams was equally popular but less respected
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Adams was equally popular — in fact, it says the opposite, that he would never match Washington's popularity.

choices.text: Adams knew he would never be as popular
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'Adams knew he would never be as popular as Washington,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: Adams became more popular over his four years
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage gives no evidence that Adams became more popular during his four years; it focuses on how he did what was right even when it made people furious.

choices.text: Adams was more popular with sharp words
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sharp words actually turned people away from Adams, not toward him — they made him less popular, not more.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Trouble with France

Adams inherited a dangerous situation. France was at war with most of Europe, including Britain. The French expected the United States to take its side. After all, France had helped America win independence. But Washington had declared neutrality — a refusal to support either side in a conflict, like staying out of an argument between two friends. The French were furious.

By the time Adams took office, French warships were attacking American merchant vessels — ships carrying goods to sell in foreign ports. Sailors lost their cargo. Some lost their lives. The two countries seemed headed for war.

Adams sent diplomats — official representatives who negotiate with foreign governments — to Paris to work out a deal. But French agents demanded enormous bribes before any talks could begin. When Americans learned about this, they were outraged. The incident became known as the XYZ Affair because the French agents' names were replaced with the letters X, Y, and Z to keep their identities secret. The slogan "Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute" swept through the country. Tribute means a payment one nation forces from another.

Congress authorized new warships, and an undeclared naval war with France erupted in the Caribbean Sea.
stimulus.content_text: Trouble with France Adams inherited a dangerous situation. France was at war with most of Europe, including Britain. The French expected the United States to take its side. After all, France had helped America win independence. But Washington had declared neutrality — a refusal to support either side in a conflict, like staying out of an argument between two friends. The French were furious. By the time Adams took office, French warships were attacking American merchant vessels — ships carrying goods to sell in foreign ports. Sailors lost their cargo. Some lost their lives. The two countries seemed headed for war. Adams sent diplomats — official representatives who negotiate with foreign governments — to Paris to work out a deal. But French agents demanded enormous bribes before any talks could begin. When Americans learned about this, they were outraged. The incident became known as the XYZ Affair because the French agents' names were replaced with the letters X, Y, and Z to keep their identities secret. The slogan "Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute" swept through the country. Tribute means a payment one nation forces from another. Congress authorized new warships, and an undeclared naval war with France erupted in the Caribbean Sea.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why were Americans outraged when they learned about the XYZ Affair?

choices.text: because American diplomats secretly paid tribute to France
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage shows Americans refused to pay, as seen in the famous slogan 'not one cent for tribute.'

choices.text: because Congress had already declared war against France
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the naval conflict was 'undeclared,' meaning Congress never officially declared war against France.

choices.text: because French agents demanded bribes before they would negotiate
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that French agents demanded enormous bribes before any talks could begin, and when Americans heard this, they were outraged.

choices.text: because France had refused to help America win independence
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — France had helped America win independence, which is why France expected America's support.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Adams Chooses Peace

Many members of Adams's own Federalist Party wanted a full-scale war with France. The Federalists pressured Adams to push for it.

Adams refused. He sent new negotiators to France instead. They eventually reached a peaceful agreement. Adams believed he had saved the country from a destructive conflict, and he called this decision his greatest achievement. Many Federalists were angry, and Adams's choice cost him their support. But history has generally proved him right.
stimulus.content_text: Adams Chooses Peace Many members of Adams's own Federalist Party wanted a full-scale war with France. The Federalists pressured Adams to push for it. Adams refused. He sent new negotiators to France instead. They eventually reached a peaceful agreement. Adams believed he had saved the country from a destructive conflict, and he called this decision his greatest achievement. Many Federalists were angry, and Adams's choice cost him their support. But history has generally proved him right.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Adams do after he refused to start a full-scale war with France?

choices.text: He sent new negotiators to France.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states 'He sent new negotiators to France instead' right after he refused to start a war.

choices.text: He lost the next election right away.
choices.feedback: The passage mentions that Adams lost Federalist support, but it never says he lost an election right after refusing war.

choices.text: He signed a peace deal before refusing.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the order is backwards — Adams first refused war, then sent negotiators, and then they reached a peaceful agreement.

choices.text: He asked the Federalists to lead the war.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Adams refused to push for war at all — he did not ask the Federalists to lead anything.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Laws That Silenced Critics

Adams also signed one of the most controversial sets of laws in early American history: the Alien and Sedition Acts. Sedition means stirring up resistance against the government — writing or speaking to turn people against their leaders.

The Alien Acts extended the waiting period for immigrants to become citizens from five years to fourteen. They also gave the president power to deport — forcibly send away — foreigners he considered dangerous. The Sedition Act made it a crime to publish "false, scandalous, and malicious" statements about the government or the president.

It's easy to assume that Americans always agreed on what free speech meant. Here's the surprising part: even the people who wrote the First Amendment disagreed about its limits. Democratic-Republican newspaper editors who criticized Adams were arrested, fined, and jailed. Thomas Jefferson and James Madison argued that the acts were unconstitutional. They believed states had the right to nullify — refuse to enforce — federal laws they considered unjust.

Adams and Jefferson, once close friends during the Revolution, now led opposing parties with very different visions for the country. Their disagreement was not a sign of failure. It was democracy in action — messy, loud, and real.

Sedition means stirring up resistance against the government — writing or speaking to turn people against their leaders. The Alien Acts extended the waiting period for immigrants to become citizens from five years to fourteen. They also gave the president power to deport — forcibly send away — foreigners he considered dangerous. The Sedition Act made it a crime to publish "false, scandalous, and malicious" statements about the government or the president. It's easy to assume that Americans always agreed on what free speech meant. Here's the surprising part: even the people who wrote the First Amendment disagreed about its limits. Democratic-Republican newspaper editors who criticized Adams were arrested, fined, and jailed. Thomas Jefferson and James Madison argued that the acts were unconstitutional. They believed states had the right to nullify — refuse to enforce — federal laws they considered unjust. Adams and Jefferson, once close friends during the Revolution, now led opposing parties with very different visions for the country. Their disagreement was not a sign of failure. It was democracy in action — messy, loud, and real.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how were the Alien Acts and the Sedition Act similar?

choices.text: Both were written by Thomas Jefferson and James Madison together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jefferson and Madison opposed these laws and called them unconstitutional — they did not write them.

choices.text: Both gave more power to newspaper editors who criticized the president.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says newspaper editors who criticized Adams were arrested and jailed, so the laws hurt editors, not helped them.

choices.text: Both changed how long immigrants had to wait to become citizens.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the Alien Acts changed the waiting period for immigrants to become citizens; the Sedition Act was about speech, not citizenship.

choices.text: Both were used to stop people from going against the government.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the Alien Acts and the Sedition Act were tools to stop people — whether foreigners or critics — from going against the government.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Two Old Friends, One Last Day

Adams lost the presidential election of 1800 to Jefferson. He left Washington, D.C., early on the morning of Jefferson's inauguration, reportedly bitter. The two men did not speak for years.

But time softened their anger. In old age, Adams and Jefferson began exchanging letters — sharing memories and debating ideas with the same brilliance that had once made them partners in building a nation.

Then came July 4, 1826 — exactly fifty years after the Declaration of Independence was signed. On that day, both men died. Adams's last words were reportedly, "Thomas Jefferson survives." He did not know that Jefferson had died just hours earlier.

Adams was never the most popular president. He didn't try to be. He chose peace when his own party wanted war. He signed laws that tested the meaning of free speech in ways Americans still argue about today. Every time you hear a debate about what the government can or cannot do — about how far free speech reaches, about when to fight and when to talk — you are hearing an argument John Adams helped start.

Every time you hear a debate about what the government can or cannot do — about how far free speech reaches, about when to fight and when to talk — you are hearing an argument John Adams helped start.

choices.text: They argued about laws and free speech.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because debating laws and free speech describes Adams's time as president, not what he and Jefferson wrote about in their letters.

choices.text: They fought over the presidential election.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the election fight happened long before the letters — the letters only started after both men had calmed down.

choices.text: He let his political party fight a war with France, but he made a peaceful agreement with his critics.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Adams actually went against his own Federalist Party to avoid war with France, and there is no mention of him making a peaceful deal with his American critics.

assessments.title: The War of 1812

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The War of 1812: A Young Nation Fights Again

Flames Over the Capital

On a hot August night in 1814, the sky above Washington, D.C., glowed orange. British soldiers marched through the streets of the American capital and set fire to the White House and the Capitol building. President James Madison and his wife, Dolley, had fled the city just hours before. Dolley famously rescued a large portrait of George Washington from the White House walls before escaping. The young United States, barely thirty years old, was in serious trouble.
stimulus.content_text: The War of 1812: A Young Nation Fights AgainFlames Over the Capital On a hot August night in 1814, the sky above Washington, D.C., glowed orange. British soldiers marched through the streets of the American capital and set fire to the White House and the Capitol building. President James Madison and his wife, Dolley, had fled the city just hours before. Dolley famously rescued a large portrait of George Washington from the White House walls before escaping. The young United States, barely thirty years old, was in serious trouble.

prompt._: According to the text, how did Dolley Madison's action at the White House contrast with the British soldiers' actions?

choices.text: She led soldiers into the capital while they fled the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the details backwards — it was President Madison and Dolley who fled the city, not the British soldiers.

choices.text: She rescued a portrait while they set the building on fire.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Dolley rescued George Washington's portrait while the British soldiers were setting the White House on fire — showing opposite actions.

choices.text: She hid in the White House while they marched through the streets.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Dolley escaped from the city before the British arrived — she did not hide inside the White House.

choices.text: She painted George Washington's portrait while they burned the Capitol.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Dolley did not paint the portrait; she rescued a portrait that was already hanging on the White House walls.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Were the Americans and British Fighting Again?

The War of 1812 grew from new problems between two independent nations.

Britain was locked in a huge war against Napoleon's France and desperately needed sailors. British warships began stopping American merchant vessels — trading ships that carried goods between ports — on the open ocean. British officers practiced impressment, which meant seizing American sailors and forcing them to serve in the Royal Navy. Thousands of Americans were grabbed from their ships and made to work for a foreign navy. Britain also blocked American trade with France, which hurt American merchants.

Along the western frontier, many Americans believed the British were encouraging Native American nations to attack settlers. Settlers were pushing into lands where Native peoples had lived for generations. Native American leaders like Tecumseh had their own reasons for resisting American expansion. Some Native nations allied with the British because they saw the United States as the greater threat to their homelands.

In June 1812, President Madison asked Congress to declare war. A group of young congressmen called the War Hawks eagerly supported the fight. They dreamed of conquering Canada and ending British support for Native American resistance. But merchants in New England who depended on trade with Britain opposed the war strongly.
stimulus.content_text: Why Were the Americans and British Fighting Again? The War of 1812 grew from new problems between two independent nations. Britain was locked in a huge war against Napoleon's France and desperately needed sailors. British warships began stopping American merchant vessels — trading ships that carried goods between ports — on the open ocean. British officers practiced impressment, which meant seizing American sailors and forcing them to serve in the Royal Navy. Thousands of Americans were grabbed from their ships and made to work for a foreign navy. Britain also blocked American trade with France, which hurt American merchants. Along the western frontier, many Americans believed the British were encouraging Native American nations to attack settlers. Settlers were pushing into lands where Native peoples had lived for generations. Native American leaders like Tecumseh had their own reasons for resisting American expansion. Some Native nations allied with the British because they saw the United States as the greater threat to their homelands. In June 1812, President Madison asked Congress to declare war. A group of young congressmen called the War Hawks eagerly supported the fight. They dreamed of conquering Canada and ending British support for Native American resistance. But merchants in New England who depended on trade with Britain opposed the war strongly.

prompt._: Based on the passage, which statement best describes the main reason the United States went to war with Britain in 1812?

choices.text: Native American nations declared war on Britain and asked America to help
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says some Native American nations sided with Britain against the United States, not the other way around.

choices.text: Britain's actions on the seas and frontier threatened American freedom
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Britain was threatening Americans both on the seas (by grabbing sailors) and on the frontier (by encouraging attacks on settlers), which pushed the U.S. into war.

choices.text: American merchants wanted to stop all trade with European countries
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says American merchants in New England wanted to keep trading with Britain and even opposed the war.

choices.text: Britain wanted to take back control of the United States as a colony
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Britain wanted to make America a colony again — the war was about impressment and frontier troubles, not Britain trying to take back control.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Early Defeats and Surprising Victories

The war started badly for the Americans. An invasion of Canada failed completely. British forces, aided by Native American allies, pushed them back across the border. That devastating moment in August 1814, when the British burned Washington, stunned the entire nation. At sea, though, the small American navy scored several shocking victories against individual British warships. These wins lifted American morale — the confidence and fighting spirit of a group, like the energy a team feels after winning a close game.
stimulus.content_text: Early Defeats and Surprising Victories The war started badly for the Americans. An invasion of Canada failed completely. British forces, aided by Native American allies, pushed them back across the border. That devastating moment in August 1814, when the British burned Washington, stunned the entire nation. At sea, though, the small American navy scored several shocking victories against individual British warships. These wins lifted American morale — the confidence and fighting spirit of a group, like the energy a team feels after winning a close game.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after the invasion of Canada failed?

choices.text: The Americans invaded Canada again right away.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Americans trying to invade Canada a second time.

choices.text: British forces pushed Americans back across the border.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that after the invasion failed, British forces and their allies pushed the Americans back across the border.

choices.text: The British burned Washington before the invasion.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the burning of Washington happened in August 1814 and is described as a separate event — it did not happen before the invasion of Canada.

choices.text: The small American navy won battles at sea.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the navy's victories at sea are a separate event mentioned later in the passage, not what happened right after the invasion of Canada failed.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Flag Still Flying at Dawn

After burning Washington, the British aimed at Baltimore, a major port city defended by Fort McHenry. On the night of September 13, 1814, the British fleet bombarded the fort — blasting it with cannon fire, like a thunderstorm made of iron and gunpowder. The attack lasted all night long.

A young American lawyer named Francis Scott Key watched from a ship in the harbor. Smoke and darkness hid the fort from his view. Key spent the entire night watching and waiting. Only when dawn broke did he see the enormous American flag still waving above the fort. Overcome with relief, Key wrote the poem that later became "The Star-Spangled Banner," America's national anthem.
stimulus.content_text: A Flag Still Flying at Dawn After burning Washington, the British aimed at Baltimore, a major port city defended by Fort McHenry. On the night of September 13, 1814, the British fleet bombarded the fort — blasting it with cannon fire, like a thunderstorm made of iron and gunpowder. The attack lasted all night long. A young American lawyer named Francis Scott Key watched from a ship in the harbor. Smoke and darkness hid the fort from his view. Key spent the entire night watching and waiting. Only when dawn broke did he see the enormous American flag still waving above the fort. Overcome with relief, Key wrote the poem that later became "The Star-Spangled Banner," America's national anthem.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares the bombardment of Fort McHenry to 'a thunderstorm made of iron and gunpowder.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word 'bombarded'?

choices.text: It means the fort was damaged by heavy rain and wind
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses 'thunderstorm' to describe the force of the cannon attack, not actual rain or wind damaging the fort.

choices.text: It means the fort was attacked with loud, powerful blasts
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the thunderstorm comparison shows the bombardment was loud and powerful, just like cannon fire blasting the fort all night.

choices.text: It means the fort was surrounded by water during a storm
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison to a thunderstorm is describing the loud, powerful cannon blasts — not water surrounding the fort.

choices.text: It means the fort was hidden by thick clouds of smoke
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because smoke hiding the fort is mentioned separately in the passage — it has nothing to do with what 'bombarded' means.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Dramatic Victory That Came Too Late

The war's most dramatic battle happened in January 1815. Andrew Jackson, a tough frontier general, defended New Orleans against a large British army. Jackson assembled a mixed force of soldiers, militia — ordinary citizens who volunteer to fight in emergencies — along with free Black men and even pirates. On January 8, Jackson's defenders devastated the British, killing or wounding over 2,000 soldiers. Jackson's side suffered fewer than 70 casualties, meaning soldiers killed or wounded.

However, the Treaty of Ghent, which officially ended the war, had already been signed in Belgium on December 24, 1814 — two weeks earlier. But news traveled by ship in those days. Neither army in New Orleans knew the war was already over.
stimulus.content_text: A Dramatic Victory That Came Too Late The war's most dramatic battle happened in January 1815. Andrew Jackson, a tough frontier general, defended New Orleans against a large British army. Jackson assembled a mixed force of soldiers, militia — ordinary citizens who volunteer to fight in emergencies — along with free Black men and even pirates. On January 8, Jackson's defenders devastated the British, killing or wounding over 2,000 soldiers. Jackson's side suffered fewer than 70 casualties, meaning soldiers killed or wounded. However, the Treaty of Ghent, which officially ended the war, had already been signed in Belgium on December 24, 1814 — two weeks earlier. But news traveled by ship in those days. Neither army in New Orleans knew the war was already over.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a "militia"?

choices.text: Free Black men and pirates who join the military forces
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — free Black men and pirates were separate groups listed alongside the militia, not the definition of militia.

choices.text: Soldiers who are killed or wounded during a major battle
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage uses the word 'casualties' to describe soldiers who are killed or wounded, not militia.

choices.text: A large British army that attacks a new frontier city
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the large British army was the enemy force that Jackson was defending New Orleans against.

choices.text: Ordinary citizens who volunteer to fight in emergencies
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly defines militia as 'ordinary citizens who volunteer to fight in emergencies' right after the word appears.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What the War Really Settled

With victories like New Orleans, you might think the United States won the War of 1812 clearly. In fact, the war ended in something close to a draw, and many of the original disputes were never fully resolved in the treaty. Even so, the war had important consequences. It ended British support for Native American resistance east of the Mississippi River, which opened the door for American expansion westward — with painful consequences for Native peoples. It made Andrew Jackson a national hero. And it proved that the young republic could survive a fight with one of the world's strongest empires.

The war also gave Americans something they still carry with them. Every time you stand for "The Star-Spangled Banner" at a ball game or school assembly, you are connected to a young lawyer on a ship in Baltimore harbor, squinting through smoke, waiting for the sunrise to show him whether his country's flag still flew.
stimulus.content_text: What the War Really Settled With victories like New Orleans, you might think the United States won the War of 1812 clearly. In fact, the war ended in something close to a draw, and many of the original disputes were never fully resolved in the treaty. Even so, the war had important consequences. It ended British support for Native American resistance east of the Mississippi River, which opened the door for American expansion westward — with painful consequences for Native peoples. It made Andrew Jackson a national hero. And it proved that the young republic could survive a fight with one of the world's strongest empires. The war also gave Americans something they still carry with them. Every time you stand for "The Star-Spangled Banner" at a ball game or school assembly, you are connected to a young lawyer on a ship in Baltimore harbor, squinting through smoke, waiting for the sunrise to show him whether his country's flag still flew.

prompt._: Which detail from the passage gives evidence that the United States did not clearly win the War of 1812?

choices.text: The battle of New Orleans was a major victory.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because New Orleans was a big victory for the U.S., which would make you think they won — it's the opposite of evidence for a draw.

choices.text: The fighting made Andrew Jackson a national hero.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jackson becoming a hero is a result of the war, not evidence about whether the U.S. won or lost.

choices.text: The young nation survived a fight with an empire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because surviving a tough fight shows the country was strong, but it doesn't prove the U.S. won the war clearly.

choices.text: The treaty failed to solve the original disagreements.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the original disputes were never fully resolved in the treaty, which is strong evidence the U.S. did not clearly win.

prompt._: What is one important difference between what the War Hawks wanted and what really happened by the end of the war?

choices.text: They wanted to protect trade, but the war ended with Britain taking New Orleans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the War Hawks wanted to conquer Canada, not protect trade, and Britain never took New Orleans — Jackson's forces won that battle.

choices.text: They wanted to stop all fighting, but the war ended before any major battles were fought.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the War Hawks eagerly supported fighting, and major battles like the Battle of New Orleans and the attack on Fort McHenry definitely took place.

choices.text: They wanted to save Washington, but the war ended after Fort McHenry was destroyed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Fort McHenry was NOT destroyed — the flag was still flying after the attack — and the War Hawks never mentioned saving Washington as their goal.

choices.text: They wanted to conquer Canada, but the war ended without a clear American win.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the War Hawks dreamed of conquering Canada, but the invasion failed and the war ended in a draw, not an American victory.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the main effect the War of 1812 had on the United States?

choices.text: It caused the nation to solve all its arguments with Britain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says 'many of the original disputes were never fully resolved in the treaty,' so arguments were not all solved.

choices.text: It caused the nation to prove its strength and build pride.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the war proved the young nation could survive against a powerful empire, and it gave Americans lasting pride through things like the national anthem.

choices.text: It caused the nation to conquer Canada and end British trade.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states the American invasion of Canada 'failed completely,' and British trade was never ended.

choices.text: It caused the nation to lose its capital and surrender completely.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because even though the British burned Washington, the United States never surrendered — it kept fighting and the war ended in a draw.

prompt._: Which of the following lists three major events of the War of 1812 in the correct order they happened?

choices.text: Fort McHenry was bombarded, the British burned Washington, and the Treaty of Ghent was signed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts Fort McHenry before Washington, but the passage says Washington was burned in August 1814 and Fort McHenry was bombarded a month later in September 1814.

choices.text: The British burned Washington, Fort McHenry was bombarded, and the Battle of New Orleans was fought.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage gives us clear dates showing Washington was burned in August 1814, Fort McHenry was bombarded in September 1814, and the Battle of New Orleans was fought in January 1815 — in exactly that order.

choices.text: The Battle of New Orleans was fought, Fort McHenry was bombarded, and the British burned Washington.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it lists the Battle of New Orleans first, but that battle actually happened last — in January 1815 — after both Washington and Fort McHenry.

choices.text: The British burned Washington, the Battle of New Orleans was fought, and the Treaty of Ghent was signed.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says the Treaty of Ghent was signed after the Battle of New Orleans, but the passage tells us the treaty was signed on December 24, 1814 — two weeks before the battle in January 1815.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what did the American navy's battles at sea have in common with the Battle of New Orleans?

choices.text: They both happened after the peace treaty was officially signed.
choices.feedback: Only the Battle of New Orleans took place after the Treaty of Ghent was signed — the naval battles happened earlier during the war.

choices.text: They both resulted in surprising victories for the Americans.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage calls the navy's wins 'shocking victories' and New Orleans was a dramatic, lopsided American win, so both were surprising victories.

choices.text: They both relied on ordinary citizens volunteering to fight.
choices.feedback: Militia (ordinary citizen volunteers) are only mentioned at the Battle of New Orleans, not in the naval battles at sea.

choices.text: They both failed completely against the strong British military.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what happened — both the naval battles and New Orleans were wins for the Americans, not failures.
