assessments.title: Frederick Douglass: Voice Against Slavery

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Frederick Douglass: The Voice That Fought Slavery

A Boy Without a Birthday

Somewhere in Maryland, around the year 1818, a baby was born into slavery. The boy would grow up never knowing his own birthday. His name was Frederick Douglass, and one day his words would shake an entire nation awake.

Like many enslaved children, Frederick was torn from his mother when he was still very young. Frederick was sent to work for different masters. Some were less violent than others, but every single one of them claimed to own him — the way you might own a chair or a horse.
stimulus.content_text: Frederick Douglass: The Voice That Fought SlaveryA Boy Without a Birthday Somewhere in Maryland, around the year 1818, a baby was born into slavery. The boy would grow up never knowing his own birthday. His name was Frederick Douglass, and one day his words would shake an entire nation awake. Like many enslaved children, Frederick was torn from his mother when he was still very young. Frederick was sent to work for different masters. Some were less violent than others, but every single one of them claimed to own him — the way you might own a chair or a horse.

prompt._: According to the text, even though some of Frederick's masters were less violent than others, what did every single one of them do?

choices.text: They tore him from his mother.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Frederick was only torn from his mother once when he was young — this was not something every master did.

choices.text: They shook the nation awake.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Frederick's own words shook the nation awake, not anything his masters did.

choices.text: They claimed to own him.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that every single one of his masters 'claimed to own him,' just like owning a chair or a horse.

choices.text: They gave him a new horse.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone giving Frederick a horse — horses are only used as a comparison to show how masters treated enslaved people like property.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Secret Power of Reading

One day, the wife of a slaveholder started teaching Frederick the alphabet. Her husband flew into a rage. He ordered her to stop immediately. Reading, he said, would ruin an enslaved person — make them want freedom. You might think enslaved people couldn't read because they lacked ability. Actually, the opposite was true. Slaveholders forbade reading — made it illegal, even — because they knew exactly how powerful it was. Literacy was a key, and slaveholders wanted every door locked.

That angry reaction taught Frederick something he never forgot: if reading scared slaveholders this much, then reading was worth fighting for. He taught himself in secret, trading pieces of bread to white children in exchange for lessons. He practiced forming letters whenever no one was watching. Over time, he read newspapers, books, and speeches about freedom. Each page sharpened his understanding of how deeply wrong slavery was.
stimulus.content_text: The Secret Power of Reading One day, the wife of a slaveholder started teaching Frederick the alphabet. Her husband flew into a rage. He ordered her to stop immediately. Reading, he said, would ruin an enslaved person — make them want freedom. You might think enslaved people couldn't read because they lacked ability. Actually, the opposite was true. Slaveholders forbade reading — made it illegal, even — because they knew exactly how powerful it was. Literacy was a key, and slaveholders wanted every door locked. That angry reaction taught Frederick something he never forgot: if reading scared slaveholders this much, then reading was worth fighting for. He taught himself in secret, trading pieces of bread to white children in exchange for lessons. He practiced forming letters whenever no one was watching. Over time, he read newspapers, books, and speeches about freedom. Each page sharpened his understanding of how deeply wrong slavery was.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Frederick decide that reading was worth fighting for?

choices.text: Because white children told him that books were fun to read
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the white children in the passage traded lessons for bread, but they never told Frederick that books were fun to read.

choices.text: Because the slaveholder's anger showed that reading could lead to freedom
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the slaveholder's angry reaction taught Frederick that reading must be powerful and worth fighting for.

choices.text: Because his teacher wanted him to become a great speaker
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions any teacher or anyone wanting Frederick to become a great speaker.

choices.text: Because newspapers explained how to escape from slavery
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Frederick read newspapers to sharpen his understanding of how wrong slavery was, not to learn how to escape.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Escape to Freedom

In 1838, Frederick Douglass escaped. He disguised himself as a free Black sailor, borrowing official-looking papers. Then he boarded a train in Baltimore and traveled by rail and boat all the way to New York City. He freed himself — no president or law did it for him.

Douglass settled in New Bedford, Massachusetts, where he married and joined the abolitionist movement — an organized effort to abolish, or completely end, slavery in America.
stimulus.content_text: Escape to Freedom In 1838, Frederick Douglass escaped. He disguised himself as a free Black sailor, borrowing official-looking papers. Then he boarded a train in Baltimore and traveled by rail and boat all the way to New York City. He freed himself — no president or law did it for him. Douglass settled in New Bedford, Massachusetts, where he married and joined the abolitionist movement — an organized effort to abolish, or completely end, slavery in America.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Frederick Douglass do after he boarded a train in Baltimore?

choices.text: He joined the abolitionist movement
choices.feedback: Joining the abolitionist movement came later, after he had already settled in New Bedford, Massachusetts.

choices.text: He traveled to New York City
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states he traveled by rail and boat all the way to New York City after boarding the train.

choices.text: He borrowed official-looking papers
choices.feedback: Borrowing the official-looking papers happened before he boarded the train — it was part of his disguise to escape.

choices.text: He settled in New Bedford
choices.feedback: Settling in New Bedford happened after Douglass reached New York City, not right after he boarded the train in Baltimore.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Voice That Could Fill a Room

In 1841, Douglass stood up at an abolitionist meeting and gave his first public speech. He described the beatings, the hunger, the shame, and the constant fear of life under slavery. White listeners who had never set foot on a plantation heard the truth directly from someone who had survived it. Douglass spoke with sharp intelligence and deep emotion. He shattered the racist lie that Black people could not think as well as white people.

In 1845, Douglass published his autobiography, Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass. The book became a bestseller in both the United States and Europe. It turned thousands of people against slavery. But the book also put Douglass in danger. He was still a fugitive — a person who has escaped and can legally be captured and dragged back. Friends helped him travel to Britain, where he spent two years giving speeches to huge crowds. British supporters eventually raised enough money to purchase his legal freedom.
stimulus.content_text: A Voice That Could Fill a Room In 1841, Douglass stood up at an abolitionist meeting and gave his first public speech. He described the beatings, the hunger, the shame, and the constant fear of life under slavery. White listeners who had never set foot on a plantation heard the truth directly from someone who had survived it. Douglass spoke with sharp intelligence and deep emotion. He shattered the racist lie that Black people could not think as well as white people. In 1845, Douglass published his autobiography, Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass. The book became a bestseller in both the United States and Europe. It turned thousands of people against slavery. But the book also put Douglass in danger. He was still a fugitive — a person who has escaped and can legally be captured and dragged back. Friends helped him travel to Britain, where he spent two years giving speeches to huge crowds. British supporters eventually raised enough money to purchase his legal freedom.

prompt._: In the passage, the author says Douglass 'shattered the racist lie.' This phrase compares proving a false idea wrong to which physical action?

choices.text: Shouting a loud message to a massive crowd
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because shouting to a crowd describes volume or size of an audience, not the physical action of breaking something that the word 'shattered' suggests.

choices.text: Breaking a solid object into many small pieces
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because 'shattered' literally means breaking a solid object into pieces, and the author uses it as a comparison to show how completely Douglass destroyed the false racist idea.

choices.text: Throwing a heavy stone across a very wide field
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because throwing a stone across a field doesn't match the meaning of 'shattered,' which is about breaking something apart, not throwing something far away.

choices.text: Hiding a secret paper inside a quiet room
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hiding something in a quiet room is the opposite of what Douglass did — he spoke openly and loudly to expose the truth.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Newspapers, Presidents, and Equal Rights

Douglass returned to America as a legally free man. He started his own newspaper called The North Star, named after the star that guided escaped enslaved people northward at night. The paper published articles demanding the end of slavery and became one of the most widely read abolitionist newspapers in the country.

During the Civil War, Douglass advised President Abraham Lincoln directly. He pushed hard for the enlistment — the official signing up — of Black soldiers into the Union Army. He believed Black men deserved the right to fight for their own freedom.

Douglass also fought for women's rights. In 1848, he attended the Seneca Falls Convention, a historic meeting in New York where women and their supporters demanded equal rights. He spoke out strongly for women's suffrage — the right to vote. It's easy to assume that all abolitionists agreed on this. They didn't. Many refused to support voting rights for women. But Douglass held firm. If Black people deserved freedom, he said, then women deserved equality too.
stimulus.content_text: Newspapers, Presidents, and Equal Rights Douglass returned to America as a legally free man. He started his own newspaper called The North Star, named after the star that guided escaped enslaved people northward at night. The paper published articles demanding the end of slavery and became one of the most widely read abolitionist newspapers in the country. During the Civil War, Douglass advised President Abraham Lincoln directly. He pushed hard for the enlistment — the official signing up — of Black soldiers into the Union Army. He believed Black men deserved the right to fight for their own freedom. Douglass also fought for women's rights. In 1848, he attended the Seneca Falls Convention, a historic meeting in New York where women and their supporters demanded equal rights. He spoke out strongly for women's suffrage — the right to vote. It's easy to assume that all abolitionists agreed on this. They didn't. Many refused to support voting rights for women. But Douglass held firm. If Black people deserved freedom, he said, then women deserved equality too.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'suffrage' mean?

choices.text: the right to own property
choices.feedback: The passage never connects the word 'suffrage' to owning property — that's not the definition given.

choices.text: the right to attend meetings
choices.feedback: The passage mentions a meeting called the Seneca Falls Convention, but 'suffrage' is not defined as the right to attend meetings.

choices.text: the right to give speeches
choices.feedback: Douglass did give speeches, but the passage does not say that 'suffrage' means the right to give speeches.

choices.text: the right to vote
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'suffrage — the right to vote,' giving us the meaning right in the sentence.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What One Voice Can Do

Frederick Douglass died in 1895. By then, slavery had been abolished. The Constitution had been amended — officially changed — to grant citizenship and voting rights to formerly enslaved people. Douglass had lived long enough to see those victories, even as the longer struggle for true equality continued.

He was born with no recorded birthday, no last name, and no legal right to read a single word. He died one of the most powerful voices in American history. Every time someone picks up a book, speaks against injustice, or demands that rights belong to everyone — not just some people — they are carrying forward exactly what Frederick Douglass spent his life fighting for.
stimulus.content_text: What One Voice Can Do Frederick Douglass died in 1895. By then, slavery had been abolished. The Constitution had been amended — officially changed — to grant citizenship and voting rights to formerly enslaved people. Douglass had lived long enough to see those victories, even as the longer struggle for true equality continued. He was born with no recorded birthday, no last name, and no legal right to read a single word. He died one of the most powerful voices in American history. Every time someone picks up a book, speaks against injustice, or demands that rights belong to everyone — not just some people — they are carrying forward exactly what Frederick Douglass spent his life fighting for.

prompt._: How does the author of the text support the idea that Frederick Douglass's work is being carried forward by others?

choices.text: By explaining that the Constitution was officially changed
choices.feedback: This tells us about something that happened while Douglass was alive, not about how other people are continuing his work today.

choices.text: By stating that he died as a very powerful voice in history
choices.feedback: This describes how important Douglass was in history, but it doesn't show how other people are keeping his work going.

choices.text: By mentioning that he was born without the legal right to read
choices.feedback: This detail is about the tough conditions Douglass was born into, not about how others are continuing his fight for justice.

choices.text: By describing how people continue to speak against injustice
choices.feedback: This is correct because the author specifically says that when people speak against injustice and fight for everyone's rights, they are carrying forward exactly what Douglass worked for.

prompt._: How was Frederick Douglass's life at the beginning different from his life at the end?

choices.text: He started with no legal rights but ended as a powerful voice
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Douglass started life with no legal rights as an enslaved person and ended as one of the most powerful voices in American history.

choices.text: He started as a free person but ended as someone owned by others
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is also backwards — Douglass started out enslaved and worked hard to become a free man, not the other way around.

choices.text: He started by giving speeches but ended by learning to read
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the order completely backwards — Douglass learned to read first, and then later gave speeches.

choices.text: He started with many supporters but ended with very few allies
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows Douglass actually gained more and more supporters over time, not fewer.

prompt._: According to the passage, why was Frederick Douglass able to write a bestselling book and publish his own newspaper?

choices.text: He advised President Abraham Lincoln during the American Civil War.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because advising President Lincoln happened after Douglass had already written his book and started his newspaper, so it couldn't explain those achievements.

choices.text: He borrowed official papers from a free sailor to escape northward.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because borrowing papers helped Frederick escape slavery, but it has nothing to do with his ability to read, write, or publish.

choices.text: He received money from British supporters to buy his legal freedom.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because British supporters buying his legal freedom made him a free man, but that didn't give him the skills needed to write a book or run a newspaper.

choices.text: He secretly taught himself to read and write while he was enslaved.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly connects Frederick's secret self-education in reading and writing to his later achievements as an author and newspaper publisher.

prompt._: According to the article, what happened after Frederick Douglass published his autobiography but before he started his own newspaper?

choices.text: He hid on a boat named the North Star at night.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because The North Star was the name of Douglass's newspaper, not a boat — and the article never says he hid on any boat by that name.

choices.text: He disguised himself as a sailor and escaped on a train.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because disguising himself as a sailor and escaping on a train happened in 1838, which was years before he even wrote his autobiography.

choices.text: He changed the Constitution to grant voting rights to all people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Douglass did not personally change the Constitution — the article says the Constitution was amended, but that was a government action, not something Douglass did himself.

choices.text: He traveled to Britain where his supporters bought his legal freedom.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows the order: autobiography published in 1845, then trip to Britain where supporters bought his freedom, then he returned and started The North Star newspaper.

prompt._: The author includes details about Douglass fighting to end slavery and fighting for women's rights. How do these details support the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: They show that he believed ending slavery was harder than getting the right to vote.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about comparing how difficult ending slavery was versus winning the right to vote.

choices.text: They show that he spent his life fighting to make sure rights belong to everyone.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage ends by saying Douglass fought for rights belonging to 'everyone — not just some people,' and his work against slavery AND for women's voting rights both prove that main idea.

choices.text: They show that he thought ending slavery should happen before helping women.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows Douglass supported both causes at the same time — he attended the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848 while still actively fighting against slavery.

choices.text: They show that he wanted women to help enslaved people escape.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Douglass supported women's suffrage, meaning the right to vote, which has nothing to do with helping enslaved people escape.

assessments.title: Harriet Tubman and the Underground Railroad

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Harriet Tubman: The Moses of Her People

Ninety Miles to Freedom: Harriet Tubman's Early Life and Escape

On a dark night in 1849, a young enslaved woman slipped off a plantation in Maryland and started walking north. She had no companion. She followed the North Star through forests and swamps, hiding by day and moving by night. Strangers along the way risked their own safety to shelter her. After nearly 90 miles of terrifying travel, she crossed into Pennsylvania — and into freedom. Her name was Harriet Tubman.

But her escape was only the beginning.

Tubman was born into slavery in Maryland around 1820. As a child, she was forced to work long hours in fields and houses. Enslaved people could be beaten, separated from their families, or sold to faraway owners without warning. As a teenager, Tubman was struck in the head by a heavy metal weight thrown by an overseer, causing painful headaches and blackouts for the rest of her life. Despite this, she grew into a strong, determined woman who refused to accept that anyone deserved to be owned.
stimulus.content_text: Harriet Tubman: The Moses of Her PeopleNinety Miles to Freedom: Harriet Tubman's Early Life and Escape On a dark night in 1849, a young enslaved woman slipped off a plantation in Maryland and started walking north. She had no companion. She followed the North Star through forests and swamps, hiding by day and moving by night. Strangers along the way risked their own safety to shelter her. After nearly 90 miles of terrifying travel, she crossed into Pennsylvania — and into freedom. Her name was Harriet Tubman. But her escape was only the beginning. Tubman was born into slavery in Maryland around 1820. As a child, she was forced to work long hours in fields and houses. Enslaved people could be beaten, separated from their families, or sold to faraway owners without warning. As a teenager, Tubman was struck in the head by a heavy metal weight thrown by an overseer, causing painful headaches and blackouts for the rest of her life. Despite this, she grew into a strong, determined woman who refused to accept that anyone deserved to be owned.

prompt._: According to the text, how did Harriet Tubman travel differently during the day compared to at night?

choices.text: She traveled with companions by day and alone at night
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states she had no companion on her journey.

choices.text: She walked through towns during the day and rested at night
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Harriet was hiding to stay safe, not walking through towns during the day.

choices.text: She rode horses during the day and walked at night
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions horses — Harriet traveled the nearly 90 miles on foot.

choices.text: She hid during the day and moved at night
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says she was 'hiding by day and moving by night' during her escape to freedom.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Going Back: Rescuing Others from Bondage

Once free, Tubman could have started a quiet new life in the North. But she could not rest while her family and friends remained in bondage — a word that means being held as a slave, trapped the way an animal is trapped in a cage it never chose. She made a decision that required historic courage: she would go back into slave territory to rescue them.

Over the next several years, Tubman made about 13 trips back to the South. She personally led roughly 70 enslaved people to freedom. If Tubman were caught, she would be returned to slavery — or killed.

You might think that reaching a Northern state meant safety. Actually, it did not. In 1850, Congress passed the Fugitive Slave Act, a law that forced people in Northern states to help capture and return escaped enslaved people. Even in Pennsylvania or New York, a person who had escaped slavery could be dragged back South. That is why many runaways had to travel all the way to Canada, where American laws could not reach them.
stimulus.content_text: Going Back: Rescuing Others from Bondage Once free, Tubman could have started a quiet new life in the North. But she could not rest while her family and friends remained in bondage — a word that means being held as a slave, trapped the way an animal is trapped in a cage it never chose. She made a decision that required historic courage: she would go back into slave territory to rescue them. Over the next several years, Tubman made about 13 trips back to the South. She personally led roughly 70 enslaved people to freedom. If Tubman were caught, she would be returned to slavery — or killed. You might think that reaching a Northern state meant safety. Actually, it did not. In 1850, Congress passed the Fugitive Slave Act, a law that forced people in Northern states to help capture and return escaped enslaved people. Even in Pennsylvania or New York, a person who had escaped slavery could be dragged back South. That is why many runaways had to travel all the way to Canada, where American laws could not reach them.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that Harriet Tubman's feelings for her loved ones caused her to return to the South?

choices.text: She learned that Congress had passed the Fugitive Slave Act.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Fugitive Slave Act is a law about capturing escaped enslaved people, and it has nothing to do with Tubman's personal feelings for her loved ones.

choices.text: She led roughly seventy enslaved people to freedom over time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it tells us how many people she helped, which is a result of her actions, not a detail about why her feelings made her go back.

choices.text: She knew she could start a quiet new life in the North.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the idea of starting a quiet new life is a reason she could have stayed in the North, not a reason her feelings pushed her to go back.

choices.text: She could not rest while her friends were trapped as slaves.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Tubman could not rest while her friends and family were trapped, showing her deep feelings for them caused her to return.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Underground Railroad and the Legend of Moses

Tubman moved her passengers along a secret network called the Underground Railroad. It was not an actual railroad, and it was not underground. The name was a code. Hiding places were called "stations." People who guided runaways were called "conductors." Tubman became the most famous conductor of all — but she was not the only one. Thousands of brave people, both Black and white, hid families in attics, fed them, and pointed them toward the next safe house.

People called Tubman "Moses" because, like the biblical figure who led the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt, she led her people to freedom. She never lost a single passenger. She carried a pistol on her journeys — partly for protection against slave catchers, and partly to stop anyone in her group from turning back. A returning runaway could reveal the route and endanger everyone else.
stimulus.content_text: The Underground Railroad and the Legend of Moses Tubman moved her passengers along a secret network called the Underground Railroad. It was not an actual railroad, and it was not underground. The name was a code. Hiding places were called "stations." People who guided runaways were called "conductors." Tubman became the most famous conductor of all — but she was not the only one. Thousands of brave people, both Black and white, hid families in attics, fed them, and pointed them toward the next safe house. People called Tubman "Moses" because, like the biblical figure who led the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt, she led her people to freedom. She never lost a single passenger. She carried a pistol on her journeys — partly for protection against slave catchers, and partly to stop anyone in her group from turning back. A returning runaway could reveal the route and endanger everyone else.

prompt._: According to the text, what did brave people do after they hid families in attics and fed them?

choices.text: Built a real railroad under the ground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the Underground Railroad was NOT an actual railroad and was NOT underground — it was just a code name.

choices.text: Carried a pistol to protect the runaways
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Harriet Tubman who carried a pistol, not the people who hid families in their attics.

choices.text: Guided them out of slavery in Egypt
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because leading people out of Egypt is part of the Bible story about Moses, which the passage uses only as a comparison to Tubman.

choices.text: Pointed them toward the next safe house
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says brave people 'hid families in attics, fed them, and pointed them toward the next safe house' — those are the exact words from the text!

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Spy, Scout, Soldier: Tubman's Role in the Civil War

Tubman's bravery stretched far beyond rescue missions. During the Civil War, the fight between Northern and Southern states over slavery, Tubman served the Union Army as a spy, scout, and nurse. She used her skill at moving unseen to gather intelligence — secret information about the enemy. She slipped behind Confederate lines, the military forces of the Southern states that had broken away.

In June 1863, Tubman became the first woman to lead a military raid in American history. She guided Union gunboats up the Combahee River in South Carolina. In a single night, that raid freed more than 700 enslaved people.
stimulus.content_text: Spy, Scout, Soldier: Tubman's Role in the Civil War Tubman's bravery stretched far beyond rescue missions. During the Civil War, the fight between Northern and Southern states over slavery, Tubman served the Union Army as a spy, scout, and nurse. She used her skill at moving unseen to gather intelligence — secret information about the enemy. She slipped behind Confederate lines, the military forces of the Southern states that had broken away. In June 1863, Tubman became the first woman to lead a military raid in American history. She guided Union gunboats up the Combahee River in South Carolina. In a single night, that raid freed more than 700 enslaved people.

prompt._: How were Tubman's rescue missions and her Civil War work alike?

choices.text: Both jobs used her ability to move without being seen.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Tubman used her 'skill at moving unseen' as a spy, and that same sneaky skill helped her lead people to freedom on her rescue missions too.

choices.text: Both jobs helped her care for sick soldiers.
choices.feedback: Caring for sick soldiers was something Tubman did during the Civil War only — the passage never mentions her being a nurse during her rescue missions.

choices.text: Both jobs made her the first woman to lead a raid.
choices.feedback: Tubman became the first woman to lead a military raid during the Civil War, but her rescue missions before the war were not military raids.

choices.text: Both jobs took place on Union gunboats at night.
choices.feedback: The gunboats on the Combahee River were only part of the 1863 raid — her earlier rescue missions did not take place on gunboats.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Life of Giving and a Lasting Legacy

After the war, Tubman settled in Auburn, New York. She opened her home to elderly and disabled African Americans who had nowhere else to go. She also supported women's suffrage — the fight for women's right to vote. She worked alongside leaders like Susan B. Anthony because she believed freedom meant nothing if it did not belong to everyone.

Tubman died in 1913 at about 93 years old. She had lived to see slavery abolished, though the full equality she fought for still lay ahead.

Harriet Tubman was born with no rights, no freedom, and no power — at least not the kind the world usually recognizes. Yet she walked back into danger thirteen times. She outsmarted bounty hunters, led a military raid, and spent her final years caring for people society had forgotten. The next time you feel too small to make a difference, remember: Tubman stood barely five feet tall, and she freed hundreds.
stimulus.content_text: A Life of Giving and a Lasting Legacy After the war, Tubman settled in Auburn, New York. She opened her home to elderly and disabled African Americans who had nowhere else to go. She also supported women's suffrage — the fight for women's right to vote. She worked alongside leaders like Susan B. Anthony because she believed freedom meant nothing if it did not belong to everyone. Tubman died in 1913 at about 93 years old. She had lived to see slavery abolished, though the full equality she fought for still lay ahead. Harriet Tubman was born with no rights, no freedom, and no power — at least not the kind the world usually recognizes. Yet she walked back into danger thirteen times. She outsmarted bounty hunters, led a military raid, and spent her final years caring for people society had forgotten. The next time you feel too small to make a difference, remember: Tubman stood barely five feet tall, and she freed hundreds.

prompt._: According to the text, how did Harriet Tubman define the idea of freedom?

choices.text: It meant outsmarting bounty hunters to escape from danger.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because outsmarting bounty hunters was a way she helped people escape slavery, not her definition of freedom.

choices.text: It meant living safely in Auburn after the war ended.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because settling in Auburn was just where she lived after the war, not what she believed freedom meant.

choices.text: It meant having the kind of power the world recognizes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Tubman had no power 'of the kind the world usually recognizes,' so that was never her definition of freedom.

choices.text: It meant nothing if it did not belong to everyone.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses almost these exact words: Tubman 'believed freedom meant nothing if it did not belong to everyone.'

prompt._: How was Harriet Tubman's first escape to the North different from her later journeys?

choices.text: On her first escape she rode a real train, but later she walked through the swamps.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Tubman walked through forests and swamps on her first escape — she did not ride a real train, and the Underground Railroad was not an actual railroad at all.

choices.text: On her first escape she went to Canada, but later she stayed in Pennsylvania.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Tubman's first escape ended in Pennsylvania, not Canada — it was escaped enslaved people who needed to reach Canada to be truly safe from the Fugitive Slave Act.

choices.text: On her first escape she traveled by day, but later she traveled only at night.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says that on her very first escape she was already 'moving by night,' so nighttime travel was not something she switched to later.

choices.text: On her first escape she traveled alone, but later she guided groups of people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Tubman had no companion on her first escape, but later she led groups of about 70 people to freedom over 13 trips.

prompt._: According to the text, why did Tubman have to lead her passengers to Canada, even though she originally found freedom in Pennsylvania?

choices.text: The secret hiding places were located only in Canada.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says hiding places or 'stations' on the Underground Railroad were located only in Canada.

choices.text: The North Star was much easier to follow from Canada.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about the North Star being easier to follow from Canada — that idea is not supported by the text at all.

choices.text: A new law made Northern states unsafe for runaways.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 forced Northern states to capture and return runaways, making Canada — where American laws had no power — the only truly safe destination.

choices.text: She wanted to join the Union Army forces in the North.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Tubman's work with the Union Army happened during the Civil War, which was a separate part of her life and had nothing to do with leading runaways to Canada.

prompt._: Which choice shows the events in the same time order as the article?

choices.text: Tubman escaped to Pennsylvania, opened her home in New York, then served in the Civil War.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the Civil War before opening her home in New York, but the article clearly says she settled in Auburn and opened her home after the war ended.

choices.text: Tubman escaped to Pennsylvania, served in the Civil War, then opened her home in New York.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the article: escape to Pennsylvania (1849), Civil War service (1860s), then opening her home in New York after the war.

choices.text: Tubman opened her home in New York, escaped to Pennsylvania, then served in the Civil War.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts opening her home in New York first, but the article tells us that happened at the end of her life, after the Civil War.

choices.text: Tubman served in the Civil War, escaped to Pennsylvania, then opened her home in New York.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says Tubman served in the Civil War first, but her escape to Pennsylvania in 1849 happened years before the Civil War began.

prompt._: Which statement best explains how the author supports the main idea that Harriet Tubman dedicated her life to helping others?

choices.text: The author shows that before the war she worked secretly, and after the war she stopped helping people altogether.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Tubman never stopped helping people — after the war she cared for elderly and disabled African Americans and supported women's suffrage.

choices.text: The author explains that before the war she helped small groups, and after the war she helped only her own family members.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Tubman led roughly 70 people to freedom (not small groups), and after the war she helped elderly and disabled strangers, not just her own family.

choices.text: The author describes how before the war she led people to freedom, and after the war she cared for people who had been forgotten.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Tubman leading enslaved people to freedom before the war and caring for forgotten people in her home after the war, proving she dedicated her whole life to helping others.

choices.text: The author states that before the war she fought for voting rights, and after the war she guided people on the Underground Railroad.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the timeline backwards — Tubman guided people on the Underground Railroad before the war, and supported women's voting rights after the war.
