assessments.title: Sojourner Truth: A Voice for Freedom and Equality

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Sojourner Truth: The Voice That Could Not Be Silenced

A Woman Who Could Not Write Her Name — But Changed Millions of Minds

She stood nearly six feet tall, with a deep, powerful voice that could silence a crowded room. She could not read a single word on a page. Yet when Sojourner Truth spoke, audiences wept, argued, and rethought everything they believed about race and gender.

Her birth name was Isabella Baumfree, and she was born into slavery in New York around 1797. You might think slavery only existed in Southern states like Georgia or Virginia. Actually, Northern states like New York and New Jersey kept people in slavery for decades. Different states abolished slavery — officially ended it by law — at different times. New York did not free enslaved people until 1827.
stimulus.content_text: Sojourner Truth: The Voice That Could Not Be SilencedA Woman Who Could Not Write Her Name — But Changed Millions of Minds She stood nearly six feet tall, with a deep, powerful voice that could silence a crowded room. She could not read a single word on a page. Yet when Sojourner Truth spoke, audiences wept, argued, and rethought everything they believed about race and gender. Her birth name was Isabella Baumfree, and she was born into slavery in New York around 1797. You might think slavery only existed in Southern states like Georgia or Virginia. Actually, Northern states like New York and New Jersey kept people in slavery for decades. Different states abolished slavery — officially ended it by law — at different times. New York did not free enslaved people until 1827.

prompt._: According to the text, how does Sojourner Truth's ability to read contrast with her ability to speak?

choices.text: She could both read and speak but chose not to do either one
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows she absolutely did speak — and did it very powerfully.

choices.text: She could read well but had trouble speaking to large crowds
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says she could NOT read, and her speaking was powerful — not the other way around.

choices.text: She could not read a single word but could powerfully move audiences
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states she could not read a single word, yet her speaking moved entire audiences to weep and rethink their beliefs.

choices.text: She could not read or speak until she moved to New York State
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Sojourner Truth was already born in New York, and the passage never says she couldn't speak.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Childhood in Chains

Isabella spent her childhood as the property of several different owners. She endured harsh treatment and the deep pain of being torn from her family. Her first language was Dutch, because her earliest owners were Dutch-speaking New Yorkers. She was treated not as a child but as property.

In 1826, one year before New York's law freeing enslaved people took effect, Isabella escaped. She carried her infant daughter in her arms and found shelter with a sympathetic family nearby. Her young son had been illegally sold to a slaveholder in Alabama. She took a white man to court to get her son back. She became one of the first Black women in American history to win a court case against a white man.
stimulus.content_text: Childhood in Chains Isabella spent her childhood as the property of several different owners. She endured harsh treatment and the deep pain of being torn from her family. Her first language was Dutch, because her earliest owners were Dutch-speaking New Yorkers. She was treated not as a child but as property. In 1826, one year before New York's law freeing enslaved people took effect, Isabella escaped. She carried her infant daughter in her arms and found shelter with a sympathetic family nearby. Her young son had been illegally sold to a slaveholder in Alabama. She took a white man to court to get her son back. She became one of the first Black women in American history to win a court case against a white man.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Isabella take a white man to court?

choices.text: She hoped to free every enslaved child in the South.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only mentions Isabella fighting for her own son, not for all enslaved children in the South.

choices.text: She needed to rescue her child from Alabama.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Isabella's son was illegally sold to Alabama, and she took a white man to court to bring her son home.

choices.text: She planned to escape to a sympathetic new family.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Isabella had already escaped to a sympathetic family before she ever went to court.

choices.text: She wanted to change the unfair laws in New York.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Isabella went to court to get her son back, not to change any laws in New York.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A New Name, a New Mission

In 1843, Isabella Baumfree chose a completely new name: Sojourner Truth. A sojourner is a traveler — someone who moves from place to place. She picked this name because she felt called by God to travel the country and speak the truth about injustice. Her deep religious faith was not just a part of her life. It was the engine that drove her work. She believed God had given her a mission, and she followed it for the rest of her days.

Truth traveled to towns and cities across the country, speaking out against slavery and in favor of equal rights for women. She was one of many reformers — people who work to fix unfair laws and change society for the better. It's easy to assume that the fight against slavery and the fight for women's rights were two completely separate movements. Here's the surprising part: many reformers, including Truth, fought for both causes at the same time. They saw a single idea connecting the two struggles — that every human being deserves dignity and equal treatment.
stimulus.content_text: A New Name, a New Mission In 1843, Isabella Baumfree chose a completely new name: Sojourner Truth. A sojourner is a traveler — someone who moves from place to place. She picked this name because she felt called by God to travel the country and speak the truth about injustice. Her deep religious faith was not just a part of her life. It was the engine that drove her work. She believed God had given her a mission, and she followed it for the rest of her days. Truth traveled to towns and cities across the country, speaking out against slavery and in favor of equal rights for women. She was one of many reformers — people who work to fix unfair laws and change society for the better. It's easy to assume that the fight against slavery and the fight for women's rights were two completely separate movements. Here's the surprising part: many reformers, including Truth, fought for both causes at the same time. They saw a single idea connecting the two struggles — that every human being deserves dignity and equal treatment.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Isabella Baumfree do after she chose the name Sojourner Truth in 1843?

choices.text: She moved to a new town and started a church
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Truth starting a church — it says she traveled and gave speeches.

choices.text: She stayed in one city and wrote books about injustice
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Truth traveled to many towns and cities — she didn't stay in one place or write books.

choices.text: She changed her name again to become a reformer
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says she changed her name a second time — she kept the name Sojourner Truth.

choices.text: She traveled the country speaking out against slavery
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says Truth 'traveled to towns and cities across the country, speaking out against slavery.'

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
"Ain't I a Woman?"

Truth's most famous moment came at a women's rights convention — a large organized meeting about an important cause — in Akron, Ohio, in 1851. Several men at the convention had argued that women were too weak and delicate to deserve equal rights.

She had watched her own children sold away from her. She had worked as hard as any man and suffered more than most.

Truth spoke from the heart in plain language that everyone could understand. She combined personal experience, knowledge of the Bible, sharp wit, and raw emotion. Her words cut through the arguments of people who wanted to keep both Black people and women powerless.
stimulus.content_text: "Ain't I a Woman?" Truth's most famous moment came at a women's rights convention — a large organized meeting about an important cause — in Akron, Ohio, in 1851. Several men at the convention had argued that women were too weak and delicate to deserve equal rights. She had watched her own children sold away from her. She had worked as hard as any man and suffered more than most. Truth spoke from the heart in plain language that everyone could understand. She combined personal experience, knowledge of the Bible, sharp wit, and raw emotion. Her words cut through the arguments of people who wanted to keep both Black people and women powerless.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how does the text show that the men at the convention were wrong about women being too weak?

choices.text: By comparing the Akron meeting to other events.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the Akron convention to any other meetings or events.

choices.text: By comparing the men's arguments to unfair laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not mention any unfair laws or compare them to the men's arguments.

choices.text: By comparing Truth's plain language to the Bible.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage lists plain language and the Bible as tools Truth used in her speech, not as things being compared to each other.

choices.text: By comparing Truth's hard work to a man's work.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Truth 'had worked as hard as any man,' directly comparing her hard work to a man's to show women are not too weak.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Fighting Through the Civil War and Beyond

Truth kept advocating — speaking up and taking action to support a cause, the way a lawyer argues for a client — throughout the Civil War. She met President Abraham Lincoln at the White House in 1864 and helped recruit Black troops for the Union Army. At the time, many people doubted that Black men should serve as soldiers. Truth pushed back against that doubt.

After the war ended and enslaved people were legally freed, Truth's work was far from over. Freedom on paper did not mean freedom in daily life. Formerly enslaved people needed jobs, homes, and fair treatment. Truth worked to help them find all three. She also pushed for women's suffrage — the legal right to vote. She argued that Black women deserved the vote just as much as anyone else.
stimulus.content_text: Fighting Through the Civil War and Beyond Truth kept advocating — speaking up and taking action to support a cause, the way a lawyer argues for a client — throughout the Civil War. She met President Abraham Lincoln at the White House in 1864 and helped recruit Black troops for the Union Army. At the time, many people doubted that Black men should serve as soldiers. Truth pushed back against that doubt. After the war ended and enslaved people were legally freed, Truth's work was far from over. Freedom on paper did not mean freedom in daily life. Formerly enslaved people needed jobs, homes, and fair treatment. Truth worked to help them find all three. She also pushed for women's suffrage — the legal right to vote. She argued that Black women deserved the vote just as much as anyone else.

prompt._: According to the text, what does suffrage mean?

choices.text: the legal right to vote
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that suffrage means 'the legal right to vote.'

choices.text: the legal right to speak
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the right to speak is not what the passage says suffrage means, even though Truth did speak up for causes.

choices.text: the legal right to serve
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the right to serve is not part of the definition of suffrage given in the passage.

choices.text: the legal right to work
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage defines suffrage as the right to vote, not the right to work.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Voice That Still Carries

Sojourner Truth died in 1883 in Battle Creek, Michigan. She had spent decades fighting for people that society had cast aside. She could not even write her own name.

But think about what she did have: a voice sharpened by suffering, a faith that would not bend, and the stubborn courage to keep speaking when powerful people wanted her silent. Today, when you hear someone stand up for another person's rights — even when it is hard, even when no one is clapping — you are hearing an echo of Sojourner Truth.
stimulus.content_text: A Voice That Still Carries Sojourner Truth died in 1883 in Battle Creek, Michigan. She had spent decades fighting for people that society had cast aside. She could not even write her own name. But think about what she did have: a voice sharpened by suffering, a faith that would not bend, and the stubborn courage to keep speaking when powerful people wanted her silent. Today, when you hear someone stand up for another person's rights — even when it is hard, even when no one is clapping — you are hearing an echo of Sojourner Truth.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that Sojourner Truth had to be brave to share her message?

choices.text: Powerful people wanted her to stay silent.
choices.feedback: This is correct because powerful people trying to silence her means she had to be very brave to keep speaking up anyway.

choices.text: People today still stand up for others' rights.
choices.feedback: This is about what happens today, not about the dangers or challenges Sojourner Truth faced when she was alive.

choices.text: She lived for many decades in Michigan.
choices.feedback: This just tells us she lived in Michigan for many years — it doesn't show anything about needing courage to speak up.

choices.text: She was unable to write her own name.
choices.feedback: Not being able to write her own name shows a hardship she faced, but it doesn't directly show that she had to be brave to share her message.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between Sojourner Truth's early life and her later life?

choices.text: She lived in New York when she was young, but later she lived only in Ohio as a speaker.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows Truth traveled to many towns and cities across the whole country, not just Ohio.

choices.text: She was moved from owner to owner as a child, but later she traveled from town to town by choice.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that being moved as a child was forced on her, while her later travels as Sojourner Truth were her own free choice — that's a strong contrast.

choices.text: She spoke Dutch as a child, but later she spoke only to Dutch-speaking crowds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only mentions Dutch as her first language from childhood; it never says she later spoke only to Dutch-speaking audiences.

choices.text: She escaped with one daughter as a young mother, but later she stopped helping families in need.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Truth continued helping formerly enslaved people find jobs and homes even after the Civil War ended.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused Sojourner Truth to be such a powerful and convincing speaker?

choices.text: She used her deep faith and her own painful memories.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says Truth used personal experience, her Bible knowledge, and raw emotion — her faith was 'the engine that drove her work,' and her own suffering made her speaking deeply convincing.

choices.text: She learned to read and write at a women's convention.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states Truth could not read a single word, and there is no mention of her learning to read or write at a convention.

choices.text: She wanted to get revenge on the men who enslaved her.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article shows Truth was driven by her faith and the belief that all people deserve equal treatment, not by wanting revenge on anyone.

choices.text: She was hired by the president to speak to the army.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Truth chose to help recruit Black soldiers as part of her own mission — the president met with her, but did not hire her as a speaker.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence best describes the order in which the author presents the main events of Sojourner Truth's life?

choices.text: She chose a new name, then escaped slavery, then fought for suffrage.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says she escaped slavery in 1826 and didn't choose her new name until 1843, so escaping came first, not second.

choices.text: She met President Lincoln, then escaped slavery, then gave speeches.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because meeting President Lincoln happened near the end of her story in 1864, not at the beginning before she escaped slavery.

choices.text: She escaped slavery, then chose a new name, then met President Lincoln.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly shows she escaped slavery in 1826, chose her new name in 1843, and then met President Lincoln in 1864 — in that exact order.

choices.text: She gave her famous speech, then escaped slavery, then chose a new name.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article tells us she escaped slavery in 1826, which happened long before her famous 1851 speech — not after it.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Sojourner Truth's work before the Civil War similar to her work after the Civil War?

choices.text: She demanded fair treatment for Black Americans and equal legal rights for women.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Truth fighting for Black Americans' freedom and women's equal rights both before and after the Civil War.

choices.text: She organized large conventions in Ohio to change unfair voting laws for citizens.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Truth spoke at one convention in Ohio, but the article never says she organized conventions to change voting laws.

choices.text: She helped enslaved people escape to Northern states and find safe hiding places.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions Truth helping enslaved people escape to hiding places — that is not something she is described as doing.

choices.text: She traveled across the country to recruit Black soldiers and win court cases.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because recruiting Black soldiers happened only during the Civil War, and winning a court case was a single early event, not work she did throughout her life.

syllabus_metadata.title: Sojourner Truth: A Voice for Freedom and Equality

assessments.title: Seneca Falls: The Fight for Women's Rights Begins

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The 300 People in a Small Church: The Seneca Falls Convention

Behind a Curtain in London

In 1840, Elizabeth Cady Stanton traveled all the way to London, England, for a conference against slavery. She was ready to speak, ready to listen, ready to participate. Instead, the men running the conference forced her and the other women to sit behind a curtain. They could hear the debates, but they could not be seen. They were hidden like furniture pushed into a back room.

So was another woman behind that curtain — Lucretia Mott. The two became friends that day, bonded by anger and a shared idea. If women could be silenced this easily, something had to change. That idea stayed with them for eight years. Then, in the summer of 1848, they did something about it.
stimulus.content_text: The 300 People in a Small Church: The Seneca Falls ConventionBehind a Curtain in London In 1840, Elizabeth Cady Stanton traveled all the way to London, England, for a conference against slavery. She was ready to speak, ready to listen, ready to participate. Instead, the men running the conference forced her and the other women to sit behind a curtain. They could hear the debates, but they could not be seen. They were hidden like furniture pushed into a back room. So was another woman behind that curtain — Lucretia Mott. The two became friends that day, bonded by anger and a shared idea. If women could be silenced this easily, something had to change. That idea stayed with them for eight years. Then, in the summer of 1848, they did something about it.

prompt._: According to the text, Elizabeth Cady Stanton was ready to speak and participate, but what did the men force her to do instead?

choices.text: Leave the event early.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Elizabeth leaving early — she stayed and listened from behind the curtain.

choices.text: Sit behind a curtain.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the men 'forced her and the other women to sit behind a curtain' instead of letting them participate.

choices.text: Run the whole meeting.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the men kept control of the meeting themselves and actually stopped the women from participating.

choices.text: Wait for eight years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the eight years refers to how long Elizabeth waited before taking action, not what the men forced her to do.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Three Hundred People in Seneca Falls

Stanton and Mott organized a meeting in a small church in Seneca Falls, New York. About 300 people came. Most were women, but not all. They gathered to discuss something that many Americans in 1848 considered radical — meaning extreme or shocking, like suggesting that students should run the school. The radical idea? That women deserved the same rights as men.

This was the first women's rights convention ever held in the United States. Before Seneca Falls, no group of Americans had publicly and formally demanded equal rights for women. Stanton had prepared something special for the occasion — a document she called the Declaration of Sentiments.
stimulus.content_text: Three Hundred People in Seneca Falls Stanton and Mott organized a meeting in a small church in Seneca Falls, New York. About 300 people came. Most were women, but not all. They gathered to discuss something that many Americans in 1848 considered radical — meaning extreme or shocking, like suggesting that students should run the school. The radical idea? That women deserved the same rights as men. This was the first women's rights convention ever held in the United States. Before Seneca Falls, no group of Americans had publicly and formally demanded equal rights for women. Stanton had prepared something special for the occasion — a document she called the Declaration of Sentiments.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what caused many Americans in 1848 to view the meeting as extreme?

choices.text: The group asked for equal rights for women.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the radical idea — the thing people found extreme — was that women deserved the same rights as men.

choices.text: The organizers held the event in a small church.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions the small church as the location, but never says the size or type of building made people think the meeting was extreme.

choices.text: The convention was the first one in the country.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being the first convention in the country is an important fact, but the passage never says that's the reason people found it extreme.

choices.text: The leaders allowed some men to join the women.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions that some men attended, but it never says that's what made people think the meeting was extreme.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Declaration Modeled on a Declaration

You may already know about the Declaration of Independence, the document that announced America's freedom from British rule in 1776. In it, Thomas Jefferson wrote that "all men are created equal" and listed the colonists' grievances — their complaints and objections, like a list of reasons you might give your principal for why a school rule is unfair. Jefferson's grievances explained why the colonies wanted independence from King George III.

Stanton borrowed Jefferson's format on purpose. Her Declaration of Sentiments stated that "all men and women are created equal." Then she listed grievances of her own. Women could not vote. Married women could not own property. Most colleges refused to admit women. Most professions barred women from entering. Women had almost no legal standing in courts. Stanton demanded that every one of these injustices be fixed.

You might think the Declaration of Sentiments was a new law that gave women these rights. It was not. It had no legal power at all. It was a statement of beliefs and demands — a rallying cry, like a flag raised to show which direction a movement would march. The real fight was just beginning.
stimulus.content_text: A Declaration Modeled on a Declaration You may already know about the Declaration of Independence, the document that announced America's freedom from British rule in 1776. In it, Thomas Jefferson wrote that "all men are created equal" and listed the colonists' grievances — their complaints and objections, like a list of reasons you might give your principal for why a school rule is unfair. Jefferson's grievances explained why the colonies wanted independence from King George III. Stanton borrowed Jefferson's format on purpose. Her Declaration of Sentiments stated that "all men and women are created equal." Then she listed grievances of her own. Women could not vote. Married women could not own property. Most colleges refused to admit women. Most professions barred women from entering. Women had almost no legal standing in courts. Stanton demanded that every one of these injustices be fixed. You might think the Declaration of Sentiments was a new law that gave women these rights. It was not. It had no legal power at all. It was a statement of beliefs and demands — a rallying cry, like a flag raised to show which direction a movement would march. The real fight was just beginning.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Stanton do right after she stated that 'all men and women are created equal'?

choices.text: She wrote the Declaration of Independence
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Thomas Jefferson wrote the Declaration of Independence — Stanton only borrowed his format to write her own document.

choices.text: She created a new law giving women rights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the Declaration of Sentiments was not a new law and had no legal power at all.

choices.text: She asked colleges to admit more women
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because colleges refusing to admit women was one of the grievances Stanton listed, not a separate action she took.

choices.text: She listed grievances about women's rights
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says 'Then she listed grievances of her own' right after mentioning her 'all men and women are created equal' statement.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Frederick Douglass Stands Up

Among the 300 people at Seneca Falls sat Frederick Douglass, already famous as an abolitionist — a person who fought to end slavery. Douglass understood something important: the fight for equality did not stop at race. He spoke powerfully in favor of women's suffrage, which means the right to vote.

His support mattered enormously. Here is the surprising part: even some women at the convention were unsure about demanding the vote. It seemed too extreme, too far ahead of its time. But Douglass argued that voting was the foundation of every other right. Without it, women would always depend on men who made laws without asking them. His speech helped convince enough people. The resolution — a formal proposal, like a motion made at a class meeting — calling for women's suffrage passed by a narrow margin.

But 32 men signed the Declaration of Sentiments alongside 68 women. About 100 people total, out of 300, put their names on the document. Signing took real courage. Newspapers mocked the convention and called its members foolish. Some signers later removed their names after pressure from family and friends.
stimulus.content_text: Frederick Douglass Stands Up Among the 300 people at Seneca Falls sat Frederick Douglass, already famous as an abolitionist — a person who fought to end slavery. Douglass understood something important: the fight for equality did not stop at race. He spoke powerfully in favor of women's suffrage, which means the right to vote. His support mattered enormously. Here is the surprising part: even some women at the convention were unsure about demanding the vote. It seemed too extreme, too far ahead of its time. But Douglass argued that voting was the foundation of every other right. Without it, women would always depend on men who made laws without asking them. His speech helped convince enough people. The resolution — a formal proposal, like a motion made at a class meeting — calling for women's suffrage passed by a narrow margin. But 32 men signed the Declaration of Sentiments alongside 68 women. About 100 people total, out of 300, put their names on the document. Signing took real courage. Newspapers mocked the convention and called its members foolish. Some signers later removed their names after pressure from family and friends.

prompt._: The passage compares a resolution to 'a motion made at a class meeting.' How does this comparison help the reader understand the word resolution?

choices.text: It shows that a resolution is a strong disagreement between two sides
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a resolution is not a disagreement — it is a formal proposal meant to help a group make a decision together.

choices.text: It shows that a resolution is a type of speech given to persuade people
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a resolution is not a speech — Douglass gave a persuasive speech, but the resolution was the formal written proposal that the group then voted on.

choices.text: It shows that a resolution is a formal proposal that a group votes on
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares a resolution to a class meeting motion, and motions are formal proposals that a group votes on — just like the resolution that 'passed by a narrow margin' at Seneca Falls.

choices.text: It shows that a resolution is a document that people sign their names on
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the document people signed their names on was the Declaration of Sentiments, not the resolution.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Seventy-Two Years

The Seneca Falls Convention launched an organized movement for women's suffrage across the United States. But winning the vote did not come quickly. It took 72 years. Think about that number. If you are nine years old, that is eight of your lifetimes stacked end to end.

The 19th Amendment, which finally guaranteed women the right to vote, was ratified — officially approved by enough states to become law — in 1920. Elizabeth Cady Stanton never saw that day. She died in 1902, eighteen years too soon. She spent her entire adult life fighting for a right she never got to use.

Stanton, Mott, Douglass, and the other signers at Seneca Falls knew they might not live to see their goal reached. They fought anyway. The women who finally voted in 1920 carried ballots that 300 people in a small church had made possible — not with a law, but with the stubborn belief that equal meant everyone.
stimulus.content_text: Seventy-Two Years The Seneca Falls Convention launched an organized movement for women's suffrage across the United States. But winning the vote did not come quickly. It took 72 years. Think about that number. If you are nine years old, that is eight of your lifetimes stacked end to end. The 19th Amendment, which finally guaranteed women the right to vote, was ratified — officially approved by enough states to become law — in 1920. Elizabeth Cady Stanton never saw that day. She died in 1902, eighteen years too soon. She spent her entire adult life fighting for a right she never got to use. Stanton, Mott, Douglass, and the other signers at Seneca Falls knew they might not live to see their goal reached. They fought anyway. The women who finally voted in 1920 carried ballots that 300 people in a small church had made possible — not with a law, but with the stubborn belief that equal meant everyone.

prompt._: According to the section "Seventy-Two Years," what does the word "ratified" mean?

choices.text: publicly announced by one speaker
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ratified means it was approved and became law, not simply announced out loud by one speaker.

choices.text: officially changed by one leader
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ratified means approved by enough states — not changed by a single leader.

choices.text: officially approved by enough states
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines ratified as 'officially approved by enough states to become law,' right after the dash.

choices.text: carefully written by many voters
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ratified is about states officially approving something, not voters carefully writing it.

prompt._: How is the Declaration of Sentiments different from the Declaration of Independence in what it claimed about equality?

choices.text: It was a law that immediately gave women the right to vote
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the Declaration of Sentiments had no legal power and was not a law — it was a statement of beliefs and demands.

choices.text: It was written by Thomas Jefferson instead of Elizabeth Cady Stanton
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us Elizabeth Cady Stanton wrote the Declaration of Sentiments, while Thomas Jefferson wrote the Declaration of Independence.

choices.text: It stated that all men and women are created equal, not just men
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows Stanton changed 'all men are created equal' to 'all men and women are created equal,' which is the key difference between the two documents.

choices.text: It listed grievances against King George III instead of against American laws
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because listing grievances against King George III was what Thomas Jefferson did in the Declaration of Independence, not what Stanton did in the Declaration of Sentiments.

prompt._: What event most clearly caused Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott to begin planning for the Seneca Falls Convention?

choices.text: being mocked by newspapers after the meeting
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because newspapers mocked the convention after it happened, so that couldn't have caused Stanton and Mott to plan it in the first place.

choices.text: hearing Frederick Douglass support voting rights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Frederick Douglass spoke at the Seneca Falls Convention itself, which means his support came after the planning had already been done.

choices.text: seeing the 19th Amendment become law
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 19th Amendment was ratified in 1920, which was decades after the Seneca Falls Convention took place in 1848.

choices.text: being hidden behind a curtain in London
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because being hidden behind a curtain in London in 1840 made Stanton and Mott angry and gave them the shared idea that led directly to organizing the Seneca Falls Convention eight years later.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information about the women's rights movement in the article?

choices.text: By explaining the causes of unfair laws and showing how they were fixed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that the Declaration of Sentiments had no legal power and the unfair laws were not quickly fixed — the fight lasted 72 years.

choices.text: By describing the events in the order that they happened over many years
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author moves through events in time order — from 1840 in London, to 1848 in Seneca Falls, to 1920 when women finally won the right to vote.

choices.text: By comparing the Seneca Falls meeting to other famous meetings in American history
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article compares two documents (the Declaration of Sentiments and the Declaration of Independence), not two meetings.

choices.text: By listing the problems women faced and providing a quick solution for them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article shows there was no quick solution — women had to fight for 72 years before winning the right to vote.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the actions of Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Frederick Douglass similar?

choices.text: They both fought to win equal rights for more than one group of people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both Stanton and Douglass worked for equal rights for more than one group — Stanton for women, and Douglass for both enslaved people and women.

choices.text: They both lived long enough to see women finally win voting rights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Stanton died in 1902, and women didn't win the right to vote until 1920, so she never saw that day.

choices.text: They both traveled to London to speak at a women's rights meeting.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Douglass was not at the London conference — only Stanton and Mott were there, and it was about slavery, not women's rights.

choices.text: They both wrote famous documents that quickly became official American laws.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Declaration of Sentiments never became a law — the passage clearly says it had no legal power at all.

syllabus_metadata.title: Seneca Falls: The Fight for Women's Rights Begins

assessments.title: Susan B. Anthony: A Lifetime of Struggle

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Susan B. Anthony: The Woman Who Voted When It Was a Crime

A Knock at the Door

On a cold November morning in 1872, Susan B. Anthony walked into a polling station in Rochester, New York, and did something that could land her in prison. Two weeks later, a federal marshal knocked on her door and arrested her. Being a woman who cast a ballot in a presidential election.

That arrest was just one moment in a fight that lasted more than fifty years. Anthony gave speeches, organized campaigns, published newspapers, and traveled tens of thousands of miles. She was ridiculed, put on trial, and fined. She never gave up.
stimulus.content_text: Susan B. Anthony: The Woman Who Voted When It Was a CrimeA Knock at the Door On a cold November morning in 1872, Susan B. Anthony walked into a polling station in Rochester, New York, and did something that could land her in prison. Two weeks later, a federal marshal knocked on her door and arrested her. Being a woman who cast a ballot in a presidential election. That arrest was just one moment in a fight that lasted more than fifty years. Anthony gave speeches, organized campaigns, published newspapers, and traveled tens of thousands of miles. She was ridiculed, put on trial, and fined. She never gave up.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Susan B. Anthony do even though she was ridiculed, put on trial, and fined?

choices.text: She stayed in prison.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says she was fined, not that she stayed in prison.

choices.text: She never gave up.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says 'She was ridiculed, put on trial, and fined. She never gave up.' — those two sentences go right together!

choices.text: She paid the marshal.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about Anthony paying the marshal.

choices.text: She stopped her work.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says she kept giving speeches and organizing campaigns for more than fifty years — she definitely did not stop her work.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Growing Up and Finding the Fight

Anthony was born in 1820 in Massachusetts. Her family belonged to a religious group called the Quakers, who believed that men and women were equal in the eyes of God. At a time when many girls received little schooling, Anthony's parents made sure she got a full education.

She became a teacher — and quickly discovered something infuriating. Male teachers earned far more money than she did for doing the exact same work. That unfairness stuck with her like a splinter she could not pull out.

Anthony first joined the temperance movement, a campaign to ban alcohol. But when she tried to speak at a temperance meeting, the men in charge told her that women should listen, not talk. That insult pushed her straight toward the fight for women's rights.
stimulus.content_text: Growing Up and Finding the Fight Anthony was born in 1820 in Massachusetts. Her family belonged to a religious group called the Quakers, who believed that men and women were equal in the eyes of God. At a time when many girls received little schooling, Anthony's parents made sure she got a full education. She became a teacher — and quickly discovered something infuriating. Male teachers earned far more money than she did for doing the exact same work. That unfairness stuck with her like a splinter she could not pull out. Anthony first joined the temperance movement, a campaign to ban alcohol. But when she tried to speak at a temperance meeting, the men in charge told her that women should listen, not talk. That insult pushed her straight toward the fight for women's rights.

prompt._: Based on the article, what main idea is supported by both Anthony's experience as a teacher and her experience at the temperance meeting?

choices.text: Anthony's parents caused her to join the temperance movement
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Anthony's own experiences with unfairness pushed her toward women's rights, not anything her parents did.

choices.text: Anthony wanted to earn more money than other teachers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anthony was angry that she earned less than male teachers doing the same job, not that she wanted to out-earn other teachers.

choices.text: Unfair treatment pushed Anthony to fight for women's rights
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both experiences — unequal pay as a teacher and being silenced at the temperance meeting — show unfair treatment that motivated Anthony to fight for women's rights.

choices.text: Women were not allowed to get an education at that time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us Anthony did receive a full education, even though many other girls at that time did not.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Powerful Partnership and a Growing Movement

She had a partner who was just as important: Elizabeth Cady Stanton. The two women became lifelong friends and allies. Stanton was the brilliant writer who crafted speeches and arguments. Anthony was the tireless organizer who turned those ideas into action. They needed each other.

In 1869, Anthony and Stanton founded the National Woman Suffrage Association. Suffrage means the right to vote — think of it as the permission slip that lets you have a say in how your country is run. Together, they published a newspaper called The Revolution and organized conventions across the country. They lobbied Congress, which means they met with lawmakers again and again, pressing them to support change. They also gathered petitions, documents signed by hundreds or thousands of people demanding action, like a giant letter that says, "We all agree. Listen to us."

Sojourner Truth, Lucy Stone, Ida B. Wells, and thousands of ordinary women and men joined this movement. No single person could win this battle alone.
stimulus.content_text: A Powerful Partnership and a Growing Movement She had a partner who was just as important: Elizabeth Cady Stanton. The two women became lifelong friends and allies. Stanton was the brilliant writer who crafted speeches and arguments. Anthony was the tireless organizer who turned those ideas into action. They needed each other. In 1869, Anthony and Stanton founded the National Woman Suffrage Association. Suffrage means the right to vote — think of it as the permission slip that lets you have a say in how your country is run. Together, they published a newspaper called The Revolution and organized conventions across the country. They lobbied Congress, which means they met with lawmakers again and again, pressing them to support change. They also gathered petitions, documents signed by hundreds or thousands of people demanding action, like a giant letter that says, "We all agree. Listen to us." Sojourner Truth, Lucy Stone, Ida B. Wells, and thousands of ordinary women and men joined this movement. No single person could win this battle alone.

prompt._: According to the passage, what did Anthony and Stanton do after they founded the National Woman Suffrage Association?

choices.text: They published a newspaper and organized conventions
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states they 'published a newspaper called The Revolution and organized conventions across the country' after founding the association.

choices.text: They wrote the Constitution and changed the laws
choices.feedback: The passage never says they wrote the Constitution — the Constitution was written decades before Anthony and Stanton were even born.

choices.text: They founded a school and trained young organizers
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions Anthony and Stanton starting a school or training young organizers, so this answer has no support from the text.

choices.text: They moved to Washington and ran for Congress
choices.feedback: The passage never says Anthony and Stanton moved to Washington or ran for Congress — they lobbied Congress, which means they visited lawmakers, but that's different from running for office.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Arrested for Voting and Decades of Determination

Anthony believed the best way to fight for a right was to use it. That is why she walked into that polling station in 1872. The judge refused to let her speak in her own defense. He ordered the jury to find her guilty.

Anthony's arrest made people across the country talk about one uncomfortable question: Why couldn't women vote?

Year after year, Anthony kept working. She spoke in living rooms, churches, and lecture halls. She petitioned Congress every single year for a constitutional amendment granting women the vote. She organized campaigns state by state, winning some and losing many. Some places did not wait for a national law. Wyoming Territory granted women the right to vote as early as 1869. Colorado, Utah, and Idaho followed before 1900. But most states still refused.

Anthony died in 1906 at age eighty-six. Her last public words on the subject were simple and fierce: "Failure is impossible."
stimulus.content_text: Arrested for Voting and Decades of Determination Anthony believed the best way to fight for a right was to use it. That is why she walked into that polling station in 1872. The judge refused to let her speak in her own defense. He ordered the jury to find her guilty. Anthony's arrest made people across the country talk about one uncomfortable question: Why couldn't women vote? Year after year, Anthony kept working. She spoke in living rooms, churches, and lecture halls. She petitioned Congress every single year for a constitutional amendment granting women the vote. She organized campaigns state by state, winning some and losing many. Some places did not wait for a national law. Wyoming Territory granted women the right to vote as early as 1869. Colorado, Utah, and Idaho followed before 1900. But most states still refused. Anthony died in 1906 at age eighty-six. Her last public words on the subject were simple and fierce: "Failure is impossible."

prompt._: In the sentence "Wyoming Territory granted women the right to vote as early as 1869. Colorado, Utah, and Idaho followed before 1900," what does the word "followed" show about those states compared with Wyoming?

choices.text: They gave women voting rights for a short time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says nothing about the voting rights being taken away or only lasting a short time.

choices.text: They gave women voting rights after Wyoming did.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because 'followed' means they came after Wyoming, which gave women voting rights first in 1869.

choices.text: They gave women voting rights only in Wyoming.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sentence is talking about Colorado, Utah, and Idaho — not just Wyoming.

choices.text: They gave women voting rights before Wyoming did.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Wyoming acted first in 1869, so the other states could not have gone before Wyoming.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Victory, Legacy, and Why It Still Matters

Anthony did not live to see her dream come true. Fourteen years passed after her death before the Nineteenth Amendment was ratified, or officially approved, in 1920. Ratifying an amendment is like getting a giant group project signed off — three-fourths of all states had to agree. When Tennessee became the thirty-sixth state to say yes, millions of American women finally gained the right to vote. The amendment is sometimes called the Susan B. Anthony Amendment.

Anthony spent more than half her life fighting for something she never got to enjoy. But here is what makes her story matter right now, to you: every time you see your mom, your grandmother, your aunt, or your older sister walk into a voting booth, they are finishing the walk that Susan B. Anthony started in that Rochester polling station in 1872. The door she pried open, they step through.
stimulus.content_text: Victory, Legacy, and Why It Still Matters Anthony did not live to see her dream come true. Fourteen years passed after her death before the Nineteenth Amendment was ratified, or officially approved, in 1920. Ratifying an amendment is like getting a giant group project signed off — three-fourths of all states had to agree. When Tennessee became the thirty-sixth state to say yes, millions of American women finally gained the right to vote. The amendment is sometimes called the Susan B. Anthony Amendment. Anthony spent more than half her life fighting for something she never got to enjoy. But here is what makes her story matter right now, to you: every time you see your mom, your grandmother, your aunt, or your older sister walk into a voting booth, they are finishing the walk that Susan B. Anthony started in that Rochester polling station in 1872. The door she pried open, they step through.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'ratified' mean?

choices.text: written down for the first time
choices.feedback: Answer C is wrong because writing something down for the first time is drafting or creating it, not ratifying it — ratifying happens after it's already been written.

choices.text: sent to every state for discussion
choices.feedback: Answer A is wrong because the passage doesn't say ratifying means sending the amendment out for discussion — it means the states have already made their final decision and approved it.

choices.text: officially approved by enough states
choices.feedback: Answer B is correct because the passage says 'ratified, or officially approved,' and explains that enough states (three-fourths) had to agree for it to count.

choices.text: voted against by most lawmakers
choices.feedback: Answer D is wrong because ratifying is the opposite of voting against something — it means enough people voted FOR it to make it official.

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Susan B. Anthony's experience at the polling station in 1872 different from a woman's experience voting today?

choices.text: She was voting to ban the sale of alcohol, while women today vote for leaders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anthony voted in a presidential election in 1872 — her work against alcohol happened separately, earlier in her life.

choices.text: She was praised for casting her ballot, while women today are mostly ignored.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says Anthony was arrested and fined for voting, not praised.

choices.text: She was arrested for casting her ballot, while women today use a legal right.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows Anthony was arrested for voting in 1872, while the Nineteenth Amendment in 1920 made voting a legal right for women today.

choices.text: She was voting in a small local election, while women today vote nationally.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article specifically says Anthony voted in a presidential election, which is a national election, not a small local one.

prompt._: According to the article, what event caused people across the country to start asking why women could not vote?

choices.text: A federal marshal arrested Susan B. Anthony for voting in a presidential election.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article clearly states that Anthony's arrest for voting made people across the country ask why women couldn't vote.

choices.text: Elizabeth Cady Stanton helped Susan B. Anthony publish a newspaper about women's rights.
choices.feedback: Publishing the newspaper helped spread their message, but the article does not connect it to people nationwide questioning women's voting rights.

choices.text: A school paid Susan B. Anthony less money than male teachers for the exact same work.
choices.feedback: The unfair pay motivated Anthony personally, but the article does not say it caused a nationwide conversation about women's voting rights.

choices.text: Male leaders told Susan B. Anthony to listen instead of speak at a temperance meeting.
choices.feedback: This event pushed Anthony to fight for women's rights, but the article never says it made people across the country question why women couldn't vote.

prompt._: Which answer shows the events from earliest to latest in Susan B. Anthony's life?

choices.text: She taught school, the amendment was approved, and she was arrested for voting.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the amendment before the arrest, but Anthony was arrested in 1872 and the amendment wasn't approved until 1920 — much later.

choices.text: She taught school, she was arrested for voting, and the amendment was approved.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows she taught school first, was arrested for voting in 1872 second, and the amendment was approved in 1920 last.

choices.text: The amendment was approved, she taught school, and she was arrested for voting.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the amendment first, but the amendment was approved in 1920, which was actually the last of the three events to happen.

choices.text: She was arrested for voting, she taught school, and the amendment was approved.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says she was arrested before she taught school, but the passage tells us she became a teacher early in her life, well before her arrest in 1872.

prompt._: Which statement best compares Susan B. Anthony's work with the work of other people in the voting-rights movement?

choices.text: Both Anthony and others worked in many ways to help women win the right to vote.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows Anthony, Stanton, Truth, Wells, and many others all worked together in different ways — speeches, writing, organizing, and petitioning — to win women the right to vote.

choices.text: Both Anthony and others waited until 1920 before taking action for voting rights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anthony and others started fighting for voting rights decades before 1920, with Anthony voting as early as 1872 and founding the suffrage association in 1869.

choices.text: Both Anthony and others believed voting should be allowed only in some states.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anthony and others fought for women to be able to vote everywhere in the country, not just in certain states.

choices.text: Both Anthony and others focused more on banning alcohol than on voting rights.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Anthony only briefly joined the temperance movement before turning her full attention to fighting for women's voting rights, which became her main cause.

syllabus_metadata.title: Susan B. Anthony: A Lifetime of Struggle

assessments.title: Africa: A Continent of Contrasts

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Africa: One Continent, a Thousand Places

Africa: A Continent Full of Surprises

A climber stands at 19,341 feet, breath coming in thin gasps, ice crunching under her boots. She is on the summit of Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania, the tallest mountain in Africa. Below her, tropical plains shimmer in the heat. Up here, snow and glaciers cover the rock. Most people never picture snow when they think of Africa. But Africa holds surprises like this around every corner.

You might think of Africa as one big stretch of golden grassland filled with elephants and lions. That picture is real — but it is only a tiny slice of the truth. Africa is not a single country. It is a continent made up of 54 different countries, each with its own languages, cultures, and governments. And it contains some of the most different places on Earth, packed onto one enormous block of land.

Africa is the second largest continent, covering about 11.7 million square miles. That is more than three times the size of the United States. If you cut out the shapes of the United States, China, India, and most of Europe, you could fit them all inside Africa's borders — with room left over. The continent stretches from the Mediterranean Sea in the north to the Cape of Good Hope in the south, and from the Atlantic Ocean in the west to the Indian Ocean in the east.

She is on the summit of Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania, the tallest mountain in Africa. Below her, tropical plains shimmer in the heat. Up here, snow and glaciers cover the rock. Most people never picture snow when they think of Africa. But Africa holds surprises like this around every corner. You might think of Africa as one big stretch of golden grassland filled with elephants and lions. That picture is real — but it is only a tiny slice of the truth. Africa is not a single country. It is a continent made up of 54 different countries, each with its own languages, cultures, and governments. And it contains some of the most different places on Earth, packed onto one enormous block of land. Africa is the second largest continent, covering about 11.7 million square miles. That is more than three times the size of the United States. If you cut out the shapes of the United States, China, India, and most of Europe, you could fit them all inside Africa's borders — with room left over. The continent stretches from the Mediterranean Sea in the north to the Cape of Good Hope in the south, and from the Atlantic Ocean in the west to the Indian Ocean in the east.

prompt._: According to the text, what contrast does the author describe between the summit of Mount Kilimanjaro and the plains below it?

choices.text: The summit has snow and glaciers while the plains shimmer in the heat
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the summit has 'snow and glaciers' while the plains below 'shimmer in the heat,' showing a clear contrast between cold and hot.

choices.text: The summit has hot tropical air while the plains are covered in snow
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the details flipped — the passage says the summit is cold with snow and ice, not hot, and the plains are warm, not snowy.

choices.text: The summit has thin air and ice while the plains are filled with elephants
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while thin air and ice at the summit are mentioned, the passage does not say the plains below are 'filled with elephants' in this section.

choices.text: The summit has warm sunshine while the plains are cool and breezy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the summit as cold and icy, not warm and sunny, and says nothing about the plains being cool and breezy.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Deserts, Forests, Grasslands, and Mountains

Start in the north, and you hit the Sahara Desert. It sprawls across the top of the continent like a lid on a pot. Only about one quarter of it is actually sandy. The rest is rocky plateau and flat gravel plains that stretch for miles. Movies love to show rolling dunes, but most of the Sahara looks more like a stony parking lot baking in the sun.

Move south into central Africa, and the world changes completely. The Congo Rainforest is one of the densest forests on the planet. Its trees grow so tall and so close together that they form a canopy — a ceiling of leaves and branches high above the ground, like a green roof blocking out the sky. So little sunlight reaches the forest floor that the ground stays dim even at noon.

Across East and Southern Africa, wide savannas — flat grasslands dotted with scattered trees — roll to the horizon. Lions, zebras, giraffes, and enormous herds of wildebeest live here. Along the coasts, sandy beaches meet coral reefs bursting with colorful fish. Desert, rainforest, grassland, mountain, beach — all of it sits on one continent.

Kilimanjaro is a dormant volcano — a volcano that is not erupting now but could wake up again someday, like a sleeping dog that might still bark. It does not belong to a mountain chain. It rises alone from the flat plains, which makes it look even more dramatic. Climbers who trek to its summit pass through five different climate zones on the way up. They start in tropical forest at the base, climb through moorland and rocky desert, and finish in arctic ice at the peak. It is like walking from the equator to the North Pole in a few days.

Only about one quarter of it is actually sandy. The rest is rocky plateau and flat gravel plains that stretch for miles. Movies love to show rolling dunes, but most of the Sahara looks more like a stony parking lot baking in the sun. Move south into central Africa, and the world changes completely. The Congo Rainforest is one of the densest forests on the planet. Its trees grow so tall and so close together that they form a canopy — a ceiling of leaves and branches high above the ground, like a green roof blocking out the sky. So little sunlight reaches the forest floor that the ground stays dim even at noon. Across East and Southern Africa, wide savannas — flat grasslands dotted with scattered trees — roll to the horizon. Lions, zebras, giraffes, and enormous herds of wildebeest live here. Along the coasts, sandy beaches meet coral reefs bursting with colorful fish. Desert, rainforest, grassland, mountain, beach — all of it sits on one continent. Kilimanjaro is a dormant volcano — a volcano that is not erupting now but could wake up again someday, like a sleeping dog that might still bark. It does not belong to a mountain chain. It rises alone from the flat plains, which makes it look even more dramatic. Climbers who trek to its summit pass through five different climate zones on the way up. They start in tropical forest at the base, climb through moorland and rocky desert, and finish in arctic ice at the peak. It is like walking from the equator to the North Pole in a few days.

prompt._: A main idea of the text is that Africa's land features are unique and dramatic. Which detail explains what causes Mount Kilimanjaro to look so dramatic?

choices.text: It features five unique climate zones.
choices.feedback: The five climate zones are a cool detail about climbing Kilimanjaro, but the passage doesn't say they are what makes the mountain look dramatic.

choices.text: It finishes in arctic ice at the peak.
choices.feedback: The arctic ice at the peak is a fascinating fact, but the passage doesn't connect the icy summit to why the mountain looks dramatic.

choices.text: It is a volcano that might wake up.
choices.feedback: Being a dormant volcano is an interesting fact, but the passage never says that's what makes Kilimanjaro look dramatic.

choices.text: It rises alone from the flat plains.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the word 'dramatic' right after explaining that Kilimanjaro rises alone from the flat plains, directly connecting the two ideas.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Great Rift Valley, Wildlife, and the Human Story

Stretching over 4,000 miles from Mozambique in southeastern Africa all the way north to Syria in the Middle East, the Great Rift Valley is a giant crack in the Earth's surface. It formed because of tectonic plates — massive slabs of rock that make up the Earth's outer shell, fitting together like pieces of a giant jigsaw puzzle. In this region, two plates are slowly pulling apart, splitting the ground and creating deep valleys and lakes. Lake Tanganyika, sitting inside the Rift, is the second deepest lake on Earth.

The Rift Valley also holds some of the oldest human fossils ever found. Scientists believe the earliest ancestors of all humans lived in this region millions of years ago. That is why East Africa is sometimes called the "Cradle of Humankind."

Africa is home to the largest land animal (the African elephant), the tallest animal (the giraffe), and the fastest land animal (the cheetah). Every year, vast herds of wildebeest and zebra cross the East African savannas, following the rains to find fresh grass. Hippos and crocodiles share the rivers. Gorillas and chimpanzees move through the forest branches overhead.

Every person alive today can trace their family tree back to East Africa if they go far enough. The ground where the Great Rift Valley splits open is not just a geography lesson. It is the place where your story — and every human story — began.

It formed because of tectonic plates — massive slabs of rock that make up the Earth's outer shell, fitting together like pieces of a giant jigsaw puzzle. In this region, two plates are slowly pulling apart, splitting the ground and creating deep valleys and lakes. Lake Tanganyika, sitting inside the Rift, is the second deepest lake on Earth. The Rift Valley also holds some of the oldest human fossils ever found. Scientists believe the earliest ancestors of all humans lived in this region millions of years ago. That is why East Africa is sometimes called the "Cradle of Humankind." Africa is home to the largest land animal (the African elephant), the tallest animal (the giraffe), and the fastest land animal (the cheetah). Every year, vast herds of wildebeest and zebra cross the East African savannas, following the rains to find fresh grass. Hippos and crocodiles share the rivers. Gorillas and chimpanzees move through the forest branches overhead. Every person alive today can trace their family tree back to East Africa if they go far enough. The ground where the Great Rift Valley splits open is not just a geography lesson. It is the place where your story — and every human story — began.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened because tectonic plates pulled apart in the Great Rift Valley region?

choices.text: Deep valleys and lakes were created in the area
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that the two plates pulling apart split the ground and created deep valleys and lakes in the region.

choices.text: The oldest human fossils were buried underground
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never connects tectonic plates pulling apart to fossils being buried underground — those are two separate ideas in the text.

choices.text: Scientists started calling East Africa the Cradle of Humankind
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because East Africa is called the 'Cradle of Humankind' because of the ancient human fossils found there, not as a result of tectonic plates pulling apart.

choices.text: Wildebeest and zebra began crossing the savannas
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because wildebeest and zebra cross the savannas to follow the rains and find fresh grass, which has nothing to do with tectonic plates.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast the author uses to prove that Africa has many very different places?

choices.text: The Sahara is dry and stony, while the Congo Rainforest is dark and full of thick trees.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author directly contrasts the hot, dry, stony Sahara with the dark, dense, wet Congo Rainforest to show how wildly different Africa's environments can be.

choices.text: Africa has 54 countries, while East Africa is called the Cradle of Humankind.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because having 54 countries and being called the Cradle of Humankind are facts about people and history, not a contrast showing Africa's different landscapes or environments.

choices.text: Kilimanjaro has five climate zones, while the Rift Valley has some of the oldest human fossils.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Kilimanjaro's climate zones and the Rift Valley's fossils are two separate interesting facts, not a contrast between two very different places.

choices.text: The Sahara is mostly rocky land, while the savannas are mostly open grassland.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both the Sahara and the savannas are open, dry-ish landscapes, so they don't show a dramatic contrast the way the author intends.

prompt._: A main idea of the article is that Africa contains many completely different environments. Which statement provides key details explaining what causes two of these unique environments?

choices.text: Dormant volcanoes erupting suddenly create flat rocky plateaus, and large animals crossing the land create long rivers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Kilimanjaro is dormant and not erupting, and nowhere does it say animals walking across land create rivers.

choices.text: Melting snows flowing down mountains create wide flat grasslands, and strong winds blowing across plains create deserts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says melting snow creates grasslands or that wind blowing across plains creates deserts — those causes are made up.

choices.text: Hot sunbeams baking the rocky plains create rolling sandy dunes, and heavy rains falling on savannas create coral reefs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says most of the Sahara is rocky and stony — not rolling sandy dunes — and coral reefs are not caused by rain on savannas.

choices.text: Tall trees growing closely together create dark forest floors, and tectonic plates pulling apart create deep valleys.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly explains that closely packed tall trees cause the dark Congo Rainforest floor, and tectonic plates pulling apart cause the deep valleys of the Great Rift Valley.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence best describes the order of land features a traveler would encounter moving from northern Africa to central Africa and then to East Africa?

choices.text: The Congo Rainforest, then the Sahara Desert, then the Great Rift Valley
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order, placing the Congo Rainforest before the Sahara Desert, when the article tells us the Sahara is in the north and the rainforest is further south.

choices.text: Savannas with scattered trees, then dense rainforest, then rocky desert
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it starts with savannas, but the article places savannas in East and Southern Africa — the last stop in the journey, not the first.

choices.text: Coastal beaches, then the Sahara Desert, then dense rainforest
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article does not describe coastal beaches as the starting point when traveling from northern to central Africa.

choices.text: The Sahara Desert, then the Congo Rainforest, then wide savannas
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly describes moving from the Sahara Desert in the north, to the Congo Rainforest in central Africa, to the wide savannas of East Africa — in that exact order.

prompt._: How does the author compare Africa's size to other large places to help the reader understand the continent?

choices.text: By explaining that Africa has 54 countries while most continents have fewer than ten
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the author mentions 54 countries to show that Africa has many different cultures and governments, not to compare Africa's size to other places.

choices.text: By stating that Africa stretches across every climate zone found on Earth
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the five climate zones are only mentioned when describing the climb up Mount Kilimanjaro, not to explain the size of the whole continent.

choices.text: By showing that several large countries could fit inside Africa with room to spare
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says the United States, China, India, and most of Europe could all fit inside Africa with room to spare, which helps readers understand how enormous Africa is.

choices.text: By describing how Africa is surrounded by two oceans and one large sea
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because describing the oceans and sea around Africa tells us where Africa is located, not how large it is compared to other places.

syllabus_metadata.title: Africa: A Continent of Contrasts

assessments.title: The Sahara and Africa's Climate Zones

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Africa's Mirror: A Continent of Climate Zones

A View from Above: Africa's Symmetrical Climate Pattern

If you flew over Africa from north to south, this is exactly what you would see below you — a continent that changes color in neat, orderly stripes. That pattern holds a secret about how Africa works.

Actually, Africa contains some of the driest places on Earth and some of the wettest, freezing mountain peaks and scorching desert floors. The reason for all this variety comes down to one invisible line: the equator.

Africa's climate zones are symmetrical — meaning they form a mirror image, like a butterfly's wings. The equator runs through Africa's middle like a fold line. Near the equator, the Sun beats down almost directly overhead. That intense heat pulls huge amounts of water into the air through evaporation, the process where liquid water heats up and turns into invisible gas. Think of steam rising from a hot sidewalk after a summer rain. All that moisture falls back down as heavy rain, feeding thick tropical forests.

Move north or south from the equator, and the climate dries out step by step. And the pattern on one side of the equator nearly mirrors the other.
