The reason for all this variety comes down to one invisible line: the equator. Africa's climate zones are symmetrical — meaning they form a mirror image, like a butterfly's wings. The equator runs through Africa's middle like a fold line. Near the equator, the Sun beats down almost directly overhead. That intense heat pulls huge amounts of water into the air through evaporation, the process where liquid water heats up and turns into invisible gas. Think of steam rising from a hot sidewalk after a summer rain. All that moisture falls back down as heavy rain, feeding thick tropical forests. Move north or south from the equator, and the climate dries out step by step. And the pattern on one side of the equator nearly mirrors the other.

prompt._: According to the text, which contrast does the author use to show Africa's climate variety?

choices.text: some of the driest places and some of the wettest places
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the exact phrase 'some of the driest places on Earth and some of the wettest' to show Africa's climate variety.

choices.text: some of the highest mountains and some of the lowest valleys
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while the passage mentions mountain peaks, it never talks about lowest valleys or uses that as a contrast.

choices.text: some of the cloudiest coasts and some of the sunniest plains
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions cloudy coasts or sunny plains at all.

choices.text: some of the hottest forests and some of the coldest grasslands
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never compares hottest forests to coldest grasslands — that combination doesn't appear anywhere in the text.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Sahara and the Sahel: Desert and Its Shoreline

The Sahara Desert covers about 3.6 million square miles of northern Africa. That is nearly the size of the entire United States. It stretches across eleven countries, from the Atlantic Ocean in the west to the Red Sea in the east. It is the largest hot desert on the planet.

Here's the surprising part: the Sahara is not an endless ocean of sand dunes. Only about one-quarter of it is sand. The rest is rocky plateaus — flat, raised areas of rock, like giant stone tables — along with gravel plains, dry riverbeds, and even mountains. The Ahaggar Mountains in Algeria rise over 9,000 feet. And deserts are not always hot. Sahara temperatures can climb above 130°F during the day, then plunge below freezing at night. The same ground that burns your feet at noon could frost over by midnight.

Just south of the Sahara sits a band of land called the Sahel, a semi-arid region — a place that gets some rain, but not much. The word Sahel comes from Arabic and means "shore," as if the Sahara were a sea of sand and the Sahel were its coastline. Some years, enough rain falls for grasses and shrubs to grow green. Other years, drought turns everything brown and dusty. Millions of people in the Sahel farm crops and herd animals. They watch the sky carefully, shifting what they plant and where their herds graze depending on the rain. Their survival depends on reading the weather the way you might read a book.
stimulus.content_text: The Sahara and the Sahel: Desert and Its Shoreline The Sahara Desert covers about 3.6 million square miles of northern Africa. That is nearly the size of the entire United States. It stretches across eleven countries, from the Atlantic Ocean in the west to the Red Sea in the east. It is the largest hot desert on the planet. Here's the surprising part: the Sahara is not an endless ocean of sand dunes. Only about one-quarter of it is sand. The rest is rocky plateaus — flat, raised areas of rock, like giant stone tables — along with gravel plains, dry riverbeds, and even mountains. The Ahaggar Mountains in Algeria rise over 9,000 feet. And deserts are not always hot. Sahara temperatures can climb above 130°F during the day, then plunge below freezing at night. The same ground that burns your feet at noon could frost over by midnight. Just south of the Sahara sits a band of land called the Sahel, a semi-arid region — a place that gets some rain, but not much. The word Sahel comes from Arabic and means "shore," as if the Sahara were a sea of sand and the Sahel were its coastline. Some years, enough rain falls for grasses and shrubs to grow green. Other years, drought turns everything brown and dusty. Millions of people in the Sahel farm crops and herd animals. They watch the sky carefully, shifting what they plant and where their herds graze depending on the rain. Their survival depends on reading the weather the way you might read a book.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that rainfall causes life in the Sahel to change?

choices.text: The Sahel region sits just south of the large desert.
choices.feedback: This only tells us where the Sahel is located, not how rainfall affects the people and animals living there.

choices.text: The desert temperature falls below freezing at night.
choices.feedback: This is about the Sahara's nighttime temperatures, not about how rainfall changes life in the Sahel.

choices.text: The amount of rainfall changes where the herds graze.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says herds move to different places depending on how much rain falls, showing that rainfall changes life in the Sahel.

choices.text: The desert ground is made of flat and rocky plateaus.
choices.feedback: Rocky plateaus describe what the Sahara looks like, and have nothing to do with rainfall or life in the Sahel.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Savannas: Grasslands and the Great Migrations

South of the Sahel, the land grows wetter. Broad savannas — wide grasslands dotted with scattered trees — spread across much of East and Southern Africa. These savannas have two seasons: wet and dry. When the rains arrive, grasses shoot up thick and green, feeding millions of zebras, wildebeest, and antelope. When the dry season hits, the herds move on, searching for water and fresh pasture. This yearly movement is one of the greatest animal migrations on Earth.
stimulus.content_text: Savannas: Grasslands and the Great Migrations South of the Sahel, the land grows wetter. Broad savannas — wide grasslands dotted with scattered trees — spread across much of East and Southern Africa. These savannas have two seasons: wet and dry. When the rains arrive, grasses shoot up thick and green, feeding millions of zebras, wildebeest, and antelope. When the dry season hits, the herds move on, searching for water and fresh pasture. This yearly movement is one of the greatest animal migrations on Earth.

prompt._: According to the passage, why do the herds of zebras, wildebeest, and antelope move on when the dry season hits?

choices.text: They are searching for water and fresh pasture.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses almost the exact same words: the herds move on 'searching for water and fresh pasture.'

choices.text: They are trying to return to the Sahel region.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the animals traveling back to the Sahel region.

choices.text: They are looking for shade from the hot sun.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about the animals looking for shade from the sun.

choices.text: They are following scattered trees across the land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the animals follow water and fresh pasture, not scattered trees.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Congo Rainforest: A Green Engine at the Equator

Near the equator, at the center of the continent, lies the Congo Rainforest. It is the second largest tropical rainforest in the world, smaller only than the Amazon in South America. It covers parts of six countries. Forest elephants, okapis — shy, striped relatives of the giraffe — and bonobo apes all live beneath its trees. The forest canopy, the thick ceiling of treetops high above the ground, blocks so much sunlight that the floor below stays dim and damp. That canopy also traps moisture. Water evaporates from the leaves, rises, forms clouds, and falls right back down as rain — over and over, like a giant recycling machine that waters itself.
stimulus.content_text: The Congo Rainforest: A Green Engine at the Equator Near the equator, at the center of the continent, lies the Congo Rainforest. It is the second largest tropical rainforest in the world, smaller only than the Amazon in South America. It covers parts of six countries. Forest elephants, okapis — shy, striped relatives of the giraffe — and bonobo apes all live beneath its trees. The forest canopy, the thick ceiling of treetops high above the ground, blocks so much sunlight that the floor below stays dim and damp. That canopy also traps moisture. Water evaporates from the leaves, rises, forms clouds, and falls right back down as rain — over and over, like a giant recycling machine that waters itself.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the rainforest's water cycle similar to a recycling machine?

choices.text: It uses the exact same water over and over again.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that water evaporates, rises, forms clouds, and falls as rain repeatedly — the same water is used over and over, just like a recycling machine.

choices.text: It provides a safe home for many different wild animals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because providing a home for animals is a separate idea in the passage and has nothing to do with the water cycle comparison.

choices.text: It spreads across six different countries in the region.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because covering six countries describes the rainforest's size and location, not anything about how its water cycle works.

choices.text: It blocks the bright sunlight from reaching the ground.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blocking sunlight describes what the canopy does to the forest floor, not how water is recycled through the forest.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Southern Deserts: The Mirror Completes Itself

Savannas give way to drier land, and at Africa's southwestern edge, two more deserts appear: the Namib and the Kalahari. They are much smaller than the Sahara, but they complete the mirror. The Namib hugs the Atlantic coast, where cold ocean currents keep rain from reaching shore. The Kalahari, further inland, receives slightly more rain and supports tough grasses and thorny bushes that the Sahara cannot.

See stripes of sand, grass, and forest folding around the equator like wings. Each stripe shapes what grows there, what animals live there, and how people build their lives. It is a whole collection of them, arranged in a pattern so neat it almost looks planned.
stimulus.content_text: The Southern Deserts: The Mirror Completes Itself Savannas give way to drier land, and at Africa's southwestern edge, two more deserts appear: the Namib and the Kalahari. They are much smaller than the Sahara, but they complete the mirror. The Namib hugs the Atlantic coast, where cold ocean currents keep rain from reaching shore. The Kalahari, further inland, receives slightly more rain and supports tough grasses and thorny bushes that the Sahara cannot. See stripes of sand, grass, and forest folding around the equator like wings. Each stripe shapes what grows there, what animals live there, and how people build their lives. It is a whole collection of them, arranged in a pattern so neat it almost looks planned.

prompt._: In the sentence 'They are much smaller than the Sahara, but they complete the mirror,' what does the word 'mirror' mean?

choices.text: a flat surface found in the desert sand
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a flat surface made of sand — that idea is not connected to how 'mirror' is used here.

choices.text: a matching pattern on the opposite side
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the Namib and Kalahari deserts in the south match the desert pattern of the Sahara in the north, completing a mirror-like, symmetrical pattern across Africa.

choices.text: a border between two different climates
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'mirror' is about patterns that repeat and match, not about a dividing line or border between two climates.

choices.text: a piece of glass that shows a reflection
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'mirror' here is not a real glass object — it's being used as a describing word for how the land patterns match on both sides of Africa.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the Sahara Desert different from the Congo Rainforest?

choices.text: The Sahara is located exactly on the equator, while the Congo is found in northern Africa.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the Congo Rainforest is near the equator, while the Sahara is in northern Africa.

choices.text: The Sahara is a giant water recycling machine, while the Congo is mostly an ocean of sand.
choices.feedback: B is backwards — the article describes the Congo Rainforest as the giant water recycling machine, and the Sahara is the one described as having sand.

choices.text: The Sahara is mostly made of rocky plateaus, while the Congo is covered by a thick canopy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article specifically says the Sahara is mostly rocky plateaus and gravel, while the Congo Rainforest is covered by a thick canopy of treetops.

choices.text: The Sahara is home to huge herds of zebras, while the Congo is filled with thorny bushes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article places zebras in the savannas and thorny bushes in the Kalahari, not in the Sahara or the Congo.

prompt._: Based on the article, what causes the Congo Rainforest to receive so much heavy rain?

choices.text: The wide savannas of the south and the tall grasses that collect water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the savannas and their grasses are a separate region described south of the Sahel, and the article never says they send water to the rainforest.

choices.text: The high mountains of the desert and the rocky plains that block sunlight
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because high mountains and rocky plains are features of the Sahara Desert, which is extremely dry — the opposite of a rainforest.

choices.text: The cold currents of the ocean and the strong winds that push clouds
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cold ocean currents and winds are mentioned in connection with the Namib Desert on Africa's southwestern coast, not the Congo Rainforest.

choices.text: The intense heat of the equator and the thick canopy that traps moisture
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that the equator's intense heat drives evaporation and heavy rainfall, and that the forest canopy traps moisture and recycles it as rain over and over.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the sequence of Africa's climate zones moving outward from the equator?

choices.text: The zones begin with dry deserts, change to wet rainforests, and end with grassy savannas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — the wet rainforests are closest to the equator, and the dry deserts are farthest away, not the other way around.

choices.text: The zones begin with rocky plateaus, change to sandy dunes, and end with freezing mountains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rocky plateaus, sand dunes, and freezing mountains are all features found within the Sahara Desert, not a description of Africa's climate zones from the equator outward.

choices.text: The zones begin with thick forests, change to sandy beaches, and end with ocean coastlines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because thick forests, sandy beaches, and ocean coastlines are not the climate zones the passage describes — the article focuses on rainforests, savannas, and deserts.

choices.text: The zones begin with wet rainforests, change to grassy savannas, and end with dry deserts.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows the zones moving outward from the equator go from wet rainforests, to grassy savannas, to dry deserts — just like a butterfly's wings on both sides.

prompt._: Which comparison best shows how the author supports the idea that Africa's climate zones form a mirror pattern around the equator?

choices.text: The Sahara and the Namib are both dry desert regions on opposite sides of the continent.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the Sahara in the north and the Namib and Kalahari in the south are both dry deserts on opposite sides of the equator, showing the mirror pattern the author describes.

choices.text: The Sahel and the Congo Rainforest are both near the equator and get heavy rain all year.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Sahel is a semi-arid region that gets very little rain, not heavy rain like the Congo Rainforest.

choices.text: The savannas and the Kalahari are both the wettest parts of Africa and stay green year-round.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the savannas and Kalahari are not the wettest parts of Africa — they both have dry seasons and are not green all year.

choices.text: The Congo Rainforest and the Sahara are both extreme climates because they both freeze at night.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Congo Rainforest is hot and rainy, not freezing, so it does not share that extreme with the Sahara.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Sahara and Africa's Climate Zones

assessments.title: The Nile and Other Great Rivers

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Africa's Great Rivers: Water That Built Empires

A River Flowing the "Wrong" Way

Picture a river stretching 4,130 miles — long enough to cross the entire United States from New York to Los Angeles, and then halfway back again. Now picture it flowing north. That is the Nile, Africa's longest river and one of the longest on Earth. It cuts through eleven different countries before spilling into the Mediterranean Sea in Egypt.

You might think rivers always flow south, because south looks like "down" on a map. But rivers don't read maps. They flow downhill, following gravity wherever the ground slopes. The Nile begins high in the mountains of East Africa and runs north because the land drops toward the sea in that direction. Direction on a map has nothing to do with it.

And if you imagine Africa as mostly desert, you are picturing only a small part of a very big story. Africa is a continent of more than fifty nations, with scorching deserts, steamy rainforests, wide-open grasslands, and snow-capped mountains.
stimulus.content_text: Africa's Great Rivers: Water That Built EmpiresA River Flowing the "Wrong" Way Picture a river stretching 4,130 miles — long enough to cross the entire United States from New York to Los Angeles, and then halfway back again. Now picture it flowing north. That is the Nile, Africa's longest river and one of the longest on Earth. It cuts through eleven different countries before spilling into the Mediterranean Sea in Egypt. You might think rivers always flow south, because south looks like "down" on a map. But rivers don't read maps. They flow downhill, following gravity wherever the ground slopes. The Nile begins high in the mountains of East Africa and runs north because the land drops toward the sea in that direction. Direction on a map has nothing to do with it. And if you imagine Africa as mostly desert, you are picturing only a small part of a very big story. Africa is a continent of more than fifty nations, with scorching deserts, steamy rainforests, wide-open grasslands, and snow-capped mountains.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the real Africa different from the idea that Africa is mostly desert?

choices.text: It has mostly flat land across the continent.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions mountains and varied landscapes, which means Africa is far from mostly flat.

choices.text: It has many kinds of land and climate.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Africa has deserts, rainforests, grasslands, and mountains — many different kinds of land and climate.

choices.text: It has only hot deserts in many places.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage lists rainforests, grasslands, and mountains alongside deserts, showing Africa has much more than just hot, dry land.

choices.text: It has fewer than fifty nations altogether.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Africa has 'more than fifty nations,' not fewer than fifty.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Nile: Mud That Fed a Civilization

For thousands of years, the Nile gave Egyptian farmers a gift every summer. The river swelled and flooded, spreading thick, dark mud across the flat land beside its banks. That flat land is called a floodplain — the area a river covers when it overflows, like the wet ring a bathtub leaves on the floor. The mud was packed with nutrients, turning dry, sandy ground into some of the richest farmland in the ancient world.

Without that yearly flood, ancient Egypt could not have grown enough food to feed its cities, build its pyramids, or support its scribes and artists. The entire civilization depended on a single river's schedule. Today, the Aswan High Dam controls the flooding and generates electricity for millions of people. But the Nile still provides drinking water, irrigation — water channeled to crops through ditches and canals — and transportation for the communities along its banks.
stimulus.content_text: The Nile: Mud That Fed a Civilization For thousands of years, the Nile gave Egyptian farmers a gift every summer. The river swelled and flooded, spreading thick, dark mud across the flat land beside its banks. That flat land is called a floodplain — the area a river covers when it overflows, like the wet ring a bathtub leaves on the floor. The mud was packed with nutrients, turning dry, sandy ground into some of the richest farmland in the ancient world. Without that yearly flood, ancient Egypt could not have grown enough food to feed its cities, build its pyramids, or support its scribes and artists. The entire civilization depended on a single river's schedule. Today, the Aswan High Dam controls the flooding and generates electricity for millions of people. But the Nile still provides drinking water, irrigation — water channeled to crops through ditches and canals — and transportation for the communities along its banks.

prompt._: In the section "The Nile: Mud That Fed a Civilization," which detail best supports the main idea that the Nile River caused ancient Egypt to succeed?

choices.text: The yearly floods provided the rich mud needed to grow enough food.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the flood's rich mud created farmland that fed Egypt's cities and made its whole civilization possible.

choices.text: The river canals supplied the drinking water needed to build the pyramids.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects river canals to drinking water for building the pyramids.

choices.text: The flat floodplain created a wet ring needed to wash the ground.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'wet ring' is just a comparison to help you picture a floodplain — it is not a reason Egypt succeeded.

choices.text: The Aswan High Dam generated the electricity needed to power the cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Aswan High Dam was built in modern times and had nothing to do with ancient Egypt's success.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Congo: A Deep Giant in the Rainforest

Travel south from the Sahara Desert into central Africa, and the world turns green. Thick tropical rainforest stretches in every direction. Here, the Congo River carries more water than any other river in Africa. In some spots, it plunges more than 700 feet deep, making it the second deepest river on the planet.

The Congo drains a massive basin — the wide, bowl-shaped area of land that collects rainwater and funnels it into a single river, the way a sink catches water that hits it. The Congo's basin stretches across parts of six countries. Hundreds of smaller rivers called tributaries feed into it, like branches connecting to a tree trunk. Those tributaries gather rainfall from the wettest region on the continent and pour it into the Congo's powerful current.

People have lived along the Congo for thousands of years. Fishing communities depend on its waters, and traders use it as a highway through the forest, where building roads through tangled roots and mud is extremely difficult. The river's strong flow also holds enormous potential for hydroelectric power — electricity generated by the force of moving water, the way a waterwheel spins when a stream pushes it.
stimulus.content_text: The Congo: A Deep Giant in the Rainforest Travel south from the Sahara Desert into central Africa, and the world turns green. Thick tropical rainforest stretches in every direction. Here, the Congo River carries more water than any other river in Africa. In some spots, it plunges more than 700 feet deep, making it the second deepest river on the planet. The Congo drains a massive basin — the wide, bowl-shaped area of land that collects rainwater and funnels it into a single river, the way a sink catches water that hits it. The Congo's basin stretches across parts of six countries. Hundreds of smaller rivers called tributaries feed into it, like branches connecting to a tree trunk. Those tributaries gather rainfall from the wettest region on the continent and pour it into the Congo's powerful current. People have lived along the Congo for thousands of years. Fishing communities depend on its waters, and traders use it as a highway through the forest, where building roads through tangled roots and mud is extremely difficult. The river's strong flow also holds enormous potential for hydroelectric power — electricity generated by the force of moving water, the way a waterwheel spins when a stream pushes it.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after rain falls in the Congo's basin?

choices.text: The rainwater turns into electricity for nearby communities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hydroelectric power comes from the force of the river's moving water, not from rainfall going directly into electricity.

choices.text: The rainwater flows into smaller rivers that feed the Congo.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says tributaries gather rainfall and pour it into the Congo, meaning rain flows into smaller rivers that feed the big river.

choices.text: The rainwater spreads into the Sahara Desert to the north.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says water flows into the Congo River, not northward toward the Sahara Desert.

choices.text: The rainwater stays in the basin and does not reach the river.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that the basin works like a sink, funneling water into the river rather than keeping it trapped.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Niger: A River of Gold and Knowledge

In West Africa, the Niger River bends in a great arc through Guinea, Mali, Niger, and Nigeria before reaching the Atlantic Ocean. Along its banks, powerful trading empires rose during the medieval period — the centuries between roughly 500 and 1500 CE, long after ancient Rome fell but before the modern age began.

The empires of Mali and Songhai grew wealthy by controlling trade routes that followed the Niger. Near the river's northern bend sat the city of Timbuktu. Caravans loaded with gold and salt arrived there from across the Sahara. Scholars traveled from distant lands to study at Timbuktu's universities. It became one of the greatest centers of learning and trade in its era — proof that Africa's rivers didn't just carry water. They carried ideas.
stimulus.content_text: The Niger: A River of Gold and Knowledge In West Africa, the Niger River bends in a great arc through Guinea, Mali, Niger, and Nigeria before reaching the Atlantic Ocean. Along its banks, powerful trading empires rose during the medieval period — the centuries between roughly 500 and 1500 CE, long after ancient Rome fell but before the modern age began. The empires of Mali and Songhai grew wealthy by controlling trade routes that followed the Niger. Near the river's northern bend sat the city of Timbuktu. Caravans loaded with gold and salt arrived there from across the Sahara. Scholars traveled from distant lands to study at Timbuktu's universities. It became one of the greatest centers of learning and trade in its era — proof that Africa's rivers didn't just carry water. They carried ideas.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how were the empires of Mali and Songhai similar?

choices.text: They both carried salt to the Atlantic Ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says caravans brought salt to Timbuktu, and nothing in the passage says either empire carried salt all the way to the Atlantic Ocean.

choices.text: They both built universities in the Sahara.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the universities were located in Timbuktu, a city near the Niger River, not in the Sahara desert.

choices.text: They both fell apart before ancient Rome.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says these empires existed during the medieval period, which came after ancient Rome fell — not before it.

choices.text: They both grew wealthy from trade routes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that both Mali and Songhai 'grew wealthy by controlling trade routes that followed the Niger.'

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Rivers Under Pressure

Africa's rivers still support millions of people and rich ecosystems where hippos, crocodiles, and hundreds of fish species live. But these rivers face real dangers. Pollution from cities and factories dirties the water. Dams block fish from migrating. Climate change is shifting rainfall patterns, shrinking some rivers during dry seasons. When a river weakens, every community and creature that depends on it feels the loss.

The Nile gave ancient Egypt its food. The Niger gave West Africa its libraries. The Congo gives central Africa its roads. These rivers are not just water — they are the systems that people built entire ways of life around, and they still are today.
stimulus.content_text: Rivers Under Pressure Africa's rivers still support millions of people and rich ecosystems where hippos, crocodiles, and hundreds of fish species live. But these rivers face real dangers. Pollution from cities and factories dirties the water. Dams block fish from migrating. Climate change is shifting rainfall patterns, shrinking some rivers during dry seasons. When a river weakens, every community and creature that depends on it feels the loss. The Nile gave ancient Egypt its food. The Niger gave West Africa its libraries. The Congo gives central Africa its roads. These rivers are not just water — they are the systems that people built entire ways of life around, and they still are today.

prompt._: According to the text, what are Africa's great rivers like the Nile and the Congo?

choices.text: Dangers that threaten the communities and creatures living nearby.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because dangers like pollution and dams are things that threaten the rivers, not what the rivers themselves are.

choices.text: Roads that connect all of the cities and factories together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Congo 'gives central Africa its roads' as a way of showing its importance, not that it literally connects cities and factories.

choices.text: Ecosystems where only hippos and crocodiles are able to survive.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage also mentions hundreds of fish species living in the rivers, so it's not only hippos and crocodiles.

choices.text: Systems that people built entire ways of life around.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that these rivers 'are the systems that people built entire ways of life around.'

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the land around the Congo River different from the land around the Nile River?

choices.text: The Congo flows through wide-open grasslands, while the Nile flows through steep, snow-capped mountains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never describes the Congo as flowing through grasslands or the Nile as flowing through snow-capped mountains.

choices.text: The Congo flows through dry, sandy ground, while the Nile flows through a wet rainforest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the details completely backwards, giving the dry sandy ground to the Congo and the rainforest to the Nile, which is the opposite of what the article says.

choices.text: The Congo flows through flat floodplains, while the Nile flows through a bowl-shaped basin.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the two rivers — the Nile's land has the flat floodplains, and the Congo's basin is the bowl-shaped area that collects rainwater.

choices.text: The Congo flows through a wet rainforest, while the Nile flows through dry, sandy ground.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly says the Congo flows through 'thick tropical rainforest' while the Nile flows through dry, sandy ground that needs flood mud to become farmable.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the main reason why powerful empires and communities were able to develop along Africa's rivers?

choices.text: Because the rivers prevented people from building roads through the thick forests.
choices.feedback: The article does say roads are hard to build through the Congo's forest, but that is a reason people used the river, not the main reason empires grew — and it only applies to one river, not all three.

choices.text: Because the rivers provided the food and travel routes that helped societies grow.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article shows that rivers provided food through farming and travel routes for trade, which is exactly what allowed powerful empires and communities to develop along all three rivers.

choices.text: Because the rivers were blocked by large dams that generated power for the cities.
choices.feedback: The Aswan High Dam is mentioned in the article, but it was built in modern times — it did not help ancient empires develop, and dams can actually hurt rivers by blocking fish.

choices.text: Because the rivers flowed downhill from high mountains to reach the distant oceans.
choices.feedback: This describes how rivers flow downhill due to gravity, which is a science fact from the article, but it has nothing to do with why empires and communities were able to grow.

prompt._: Which choice best shows the order the author uses to explain how Africa's rivers matter to people?

choices.text: First the author tells where each river ends, then explains how dams changed every river.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author does not focus on where rivers end, and dams are only briefly mentioned for the Nile, not for every river.

choices.text: First the author explains problems facing rivers, then tells how rivers helped people in the past.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the problems facing rivers are discussed at the end of the article, not at the beginning.

choices.text: First the author tells how people use each river today, then explains the river's natural features.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author actually describes each river's natural features first, before explaining how people use it today.

choices.text: First the author describes each river's land and water, then tells how people depended on it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because for each river, the author first describes its land and water features, then explains how people have depended on it.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the Nile section with the main idea of the Niger section?

choices.text: Both sections show that rivers helped great societies grow, but in different ways.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the Nile section shows how floods helped Egypt grow food and build a civilization, while the Niger section shows how trade and learning helped West African empires grow powerful.

choices.text: Both sections explain that rivers were most important because they created electric power for people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because electric power is only briefly mentioned for the Nile as a modern detail, and it is not the main idea of either section.

choices.text: Both sections describe rivers mainly as natural borders that kept groups apart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither section describes rivers as borders keeping groups apart — instead, both show rivers bringing people and ideas together.

choices.text: Both sections prove that rivers mattered only in the past and are no longer useful today.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that both rivers still provide water, transportation, and support for millions of people today.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Nile and Other Great Rivers

assessments.title: Africa's Regions and Nations

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Africa: One Continent, Fifty-Four Countries, Two Thousand Languages

A Child's Morning in Lagos

A ten-year-old girl in Lagos, Nigeria, wakes up in a high-rise apartment building. Outside her window, eight lanes of traffic already hum below. She rides an elevator down to a city of over fifteen million people — more than the entire population of some countries. Thousands of miles south, a boy in Botswana watches elephants cross a dusty plain near his village. Same continent. Completely different world.

You might think of Africa as a single country. Many people do. But Africa is a continent — a massive landmass — made up of 54 separate countries. That is more countries than any other continent on Earth. These nations range from Algeria, the largest in Africa by area, to tiny island nations like Seychelles, floating in the Indian Ocean. Some are wealthy from oil. Some have fast-growing businesses and technology. Others are among the poorest places on Earth. The differences between them are enormous.

prompt._: According to the text, how is Algeria different from the nation of Seychelles?

choices.text: Algeria is a wealthy nation, while Seychelles is a very poor place.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects Algeria or Seychelles to being wealthy or poor.

choices.text: Algeria is a massive continent, while Seychelles is a single country.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both Algeria and Seychelles are countries — Africa is the continent, not Algeria.

choices.text: Algeria is the largest country, while Seychelles is a tiny island.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states Algeria is the largest country in Africa and Seychelles is a tiny island nation.

choices.text: Algeria is a crowded city, while Seychelles is a quiet dusty plain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Algeria and Seychelles are countries, not a city or a dusty plain.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Five Regions, Five Different Worlds

Geographers split Africa into several major regions. North Africa stretches along the Mediterranean coast and includes Egypt, Libya, Tunisia, Algeria, and Morocco. Arab culture and the Islamic faith run deep here. The Sahara Desert covers much of the land, so most people live along the coast or near the Nile River, where they can find water.

Everything south of the Sahara is called Sub-Saharan Africa — the part of the continent below that giant desert. About one billion people live there. This region breaks into four smaller pieces. West Africa, including Nigeria, Ghana, and Senegal, is densely populated, meaning people live packed closely together, like students crowded into a small cafeteria. East Africa — Kenya, Tanzania, and Ethiopia — is known for cool highlands, enormous lakes, and astonishing wildlife. Central Africa holds the Congo Rainforest, one of the largest tropical forests on Earth, and includes the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Cameroon, and Gabon. Southern Africa, home to South Africa, Botswana, and Zimbabwe, sits on rich deposits of gold and diamonds buried deep underground.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that North Africa's dry environment causes people to live in specific areas?

choices.text: The rich deposits of gold and diamonds buried deep underground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gold and diamonds are found in Southern Africa, and they have nothing to do with North Africa or its dry desert environment.

choices.text: The four smaller pieces that make up the Sub-Saharan region
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the four smaller pieces of Sub-Saharan Africa are about a completely different region, not North Africa's dry environment.

choices.text: The large desert forcing people to live near the water
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that the Sahara Desert covers most of the land, which forces people to live near the coast or the Nile River where water is available.

choices.text: The deep roots of Arab culture and the Islamic faith
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Arab culture and the Islamic faith describe the beliefs of people in North Africa, not why the dry environment makes them live in certain places.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Languages Than You Can Count

Here is a fact that might surprise you. There is no single language called "African." Africa is home to over 2,000 different languages. Nigeria alone has more than 500. Swahili, Arabic, Hausa, and Yoruba are among the most widely spoken, but hundreds of other languages fill homes, markets, and schoolyards across the continent.

This striking linguistic diversity — the huge variety of languages people speak — comes from the many different ethnic groups living on the continent. An ethnic group is a community of people who share a common history, traditions, and often a language, like branches of a very old family tree. In some African countries, a child speaks one language at home with grandparents, a national language at school, and a European language like English or French for official business. By age ten, that child may already speak three languages fluently.

In some African countries, a child speaks one language at home with grandparents, a national language at school, and a European language like English or French for official business. By age ten, that child may already speak three languages fluently.

choices.text: one language at school, a national language for business, then a European language at home
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it scrambles the order — the passage never says a European language is used at home or that a national language is used for business.

choices.text: one language at home, a national language at school, then a European language for business
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches exactly what the passage says: one language at home, a national language at school, and a European language for official business.

choices.text: a European language at home, a national language at school, then a local language for business
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a European language is used for official business, not at home.

choices.text: a national language at home, a European language at school, then a local language for business
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a local language is spoken at home with grandparents, not a national language.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Borders Drawn by Strangers

Why does Africa have so many countries with so many different groups inside them? The answer reaches back to the late 1800s, when European colonial powers — nations that took control of other people's lands — carved up Africa for themselves. At the Berlin Conference of 1884 to 1885, European leaders spread a map across a table and drew borders with rulers and pens. They paid almost no attention to the people already living there.

Ethnic groups that had been together for centuries were suddenly split between two or three different colonies. Rival groups that had never shared land were forced inside the same borders. These artificial lines created tensions that many African nations still face today.

Ethnic groups that had been together for centuries were suddenly split between two or three different colonies. Rival groups that had never shared land were forced inside the same borders. These artificial lines created tensions that many African nations still face today.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how did the way European leaders drew borders compare to the way ethnic groups had been living before?

choices.text: The Europeans carefully studied each group before drawing the borders on the map.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Europeans paid 'almost no attention' to the people living there, which is the opposite of carefully studying them.

choices.text: Both the Europeans and the ethnic groups wanted to create new nations together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says European leaders drew the borders on their own, without working together with the African ethnic groups.

choices.text: The ethnic groups had no set homelands, so the European borders did not change anything.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ethnic groups had been together for centuries, which means they did have their own homelands before the borders were drawn.

choices.text: The Europeans ignored existing groups, while those groups had their own lands and connections.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows the Europeans ignored the people already living there, while those groups had lived on their own lands together for centuries.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Continent of Energy and Youth

It is easy to picture Africa as only desert and savanna. But some of Africa's cities are among the fastest growing on the planet. Lagos, Nigeria, and Kinshasa in the Democratic Republic of the Congo are megacities — cities with more than ten million people, so large they could swallow several smaller cities whole. In Lagos, tech startups fill modern office buildings. In Nairobi, Kenya, people pay for groceries with their phones.

Africa also has the youngest population of any continent. Its median age — the age right in the middle, where half the people are younger and half are older — is only about 19. African musicians, artists, writers, and filmmakers are already creating work celebrated around the globe.

Fifty-four countries. Over 2,000 languages. Deserts, rainforests, highlands, and megacities buzzing with invention. The next time someone says "Africa" as if it were one place, you will know better — because you now carry a map in your mind that is 54 countries wide.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says ethnic groups speak only one language, and speaking three languages is about individual children, not about how borders and ethnic groups are different.

syllabus_metadata.title: Africa's Regions and Nations

assessments.title: Europe: A Peninsula of Peninsulas

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Europe: The Continent of Jagged Edges

A Coastline That Refuses to Be Straight

Europe's coastline stretches nearly as far as Africa's — even though Africa is more than three times larger. How? Trace your finger along a map of Europe and you will feel it immediately. The edge of this continent does not glide in smooth curves. It zigzags. It jabs outward. Fingers of land reach into the ocean, then pull back, then reach out again. Water rushes in to fill every gap. The result is a wild, crinkled border packed with bays, inlets — narrow strips of water that push inland like rivers but are actually part of the sea — and dozens of peninsulas.

An island has water on all sides, but a peninsula always has one side attached to the bigger landmass. Europe has so many of these thumbs poking into the sea that geographers call it "a peninsula of peninsulas." The whole continent is itself a giant peninsula — not a separate landmass surrounded by water, like Australia. Europe is actually the western end of a much larger body of land called Eurasia, which includes Asia too.

The result is a wild, crinkled border packed with bays, inlets — narrow strips of water that push inland like rivers but are actually part of the sea — and dozens of peninsulas. An island has water on all sides, but a peninsula always has one side attached to the bigger landmass. Europe has so many of these thumbs poking into the sea that geographers call it "a peninsula of peninsulas." The whole continent is itself a giant peninsula — not a separate landmass surrounded by water, like Australia. Europe is actually the western end of a much larger body of land called Eurasia, which includes Asia too.

prompt._: According to the passage, how is a peninsula different from an island?

choices.text: A peninsula has one side attached to land.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that a peninsula always has one side attached to the bigger landmass, unlike an island.

choices.text: A peninsula is surrounded by water on all sides.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being surrounded by water on all sides describes an island, which is the opposite of a peninsula.

choices.text: A peninsula is smaller than an island.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the sizes of peninsulas and islands at all.

choices.text: A peninsula is always found inside a continent.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because peninsulas actually jut outward into the sea, not inward into a continent.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Small Continent, Big Personality

Europe is the second smallest continent by area. You might assume that a place with over 40 countries must be enormous. Actually, all of Europe fits inside a space smaller than the single country of Canada. Those 40-plus countries are packed tightly together, and the sea is never far away. That closeness to the ocean encouraged fishing, trade, exploration, and naval power — military strength at sea — for thousands of years.
stimulus.content_text: Small Continent, Big Personality Europe is the second smallest continent by area. You might assume that a place with over 40 countries must be enormous. Actually, all of Europe fits inside a space smaller than the single country of Canada. Those 40-plus countries are packed tightly together, and the sea is never far away. That closeness to the ocean encouraged fishing, trade, exploration, and naval power — military strength at sea — for thousands of years.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Europe develop strong naval power?

choices.text: Because the sea was always close by
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says the sea is never far away, and that closeness to the ocean is what encouraged naval power.

choices.text: Because Europe had more than 40 countries
choices.feedback: Having more than 40 countries tells us Europe is crowded, but the passage never says that caused naval power.

choices.text: Because Canada was larger than Europe
choices.feedback: Canada being larger than Europe is just a size comparison in the passage, and has nothing to do with why Europe built naval strength.

choices.text: Because fishing boats made Europe smaller
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because fishing boats didn't make Europe smaller — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Four Famous Peninsulas

Four major peninsulas give Europe much of its shape. In the southwest, the Iberian Peninsula holds Spain and Portugal and juts into the Atlantic Ocean. Explorers like Columbus and Vasco da Gama sailed from Iberian ports to find new trade routes across the globe.

Below that jagged coastline, the Italian Peninsula drops into the Mediterranean Sea shaped exactly like a tall boot about to kick a football. Italy's position at the center of the Mediterranean made it a crossroads for trade and culture. Ancient Romans built their empire from here, and centuries later, Italian cities gave birth to the Renaissance — a period of unusual art, science, and learning that spread across Europe.

Far to the north, the Scandinavian Peninsula stretches toward the Arctic. Norway and Sweden share this land, where ancient glaciers carved deep, narrow channels of sea into the rocky coast. These channels are called fjords — imagine a river valley so deep that the ocean flooded it, with walls of rock rising on either side.

In the southeast, the Balkan Peninsula is a rugged, mountainous region. Greece sits at its southern tip, surrounded by hundreds of islands. The Balkans' steep terrain created natural walls between communities. Many different cultures and nations grew side by side in this small, rough space.

The Balkans' steep terrain created natural walls between communities. Many different cultures and nations grew side by side in this small, rough space.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened on the Italian Peninsula centuries after the ancient Romans built their empire?

choices.text: Italian cities gave birth to the Renaissance.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 'centuries later, Italian cities gave birth to the Renaissance,' which happened after the ancient Romans built their empire.

choices.text: The peninsula dropped into the Mediterranean Sea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'dropped into the Mediterranean Sea' describes the shape of the Italian Peninsula like a boot, not a historical event that happened after the Roman Empire.

choices.text: Explorers sailed to find new trade routes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because explorers sailing to find new trade routes is mentioned in the section about the Iberian Peninsula, not the Italian Peninsula.

choices.text: Glaciers carved deep channels into the coast.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because glaciers carving deep channels into the coast is something that happened on the Scandinavian Peninsula, not the Italian Peninsula.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Islands and a Famous Channel

The British Isles include Great Britain and Ireland, separated from each other by the Irish Sea. Great Britain sits close to mainland Europe, with only the English Channel and the North Sea in between. At its narrowest point, the Channel is only about 21 miles wide. That thin ribbon of water protected England from invasion and helped Britain grow into a separate trading and naval power. Today, the Channel Tunnel — an underwater rail link opened in 1994 — carries trains between England and France beneath the seabed.
stimulus.content_text: Islands and a Famous Channel The British Isles include Great Britain and Ireland, separated from each other by the Irish Sea. Great Britain sits close to mainland Europe, with only the English Channel and the North Sea in between. At its narrowest point, the Channel is only about 21 miles wide. That thin ribbon of water protected England from invasion and helped Britain grow into a separate trading and naval power. Today, the Channel Tunnel — an underwater rail link opened in 1994 — carries trains between England and France beneath the seabed.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is travel between England and France different today compared to the past?

choices.text: Navies can sail across the new channel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because navies were already sailing across the Channel in the past, so that is not a new or different way to travel today.

choices.text: People can walk across a narrow island.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because there is no island in the English Channel to walk across — the passage describes it as a stretch of water about 21 miles wide.

choices.text: Trains can travel underneath the water.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the Channel Tunnel is an underwater rail link that carries trains beneath the seabed, which is a new way to travel that did not exist before 1994.

choices.text: Cars can drive across a floating bridge.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a floating bridge or any way for cars to drive across the Channel.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Plains, Rivers, and Mountains

Move inland and Europe changes. The North European Plain stretches from France all the way to Russia. This flat, fertile lowland holds some of the richest soil on Earth, deposited over ages by rivers and ancient glaciers. Farmers grow wheat, corn, and other crops here. Major cities like Paris, Berlin, and Warsaw sit on this plain. But flat land with no natural barriers works like an open highway. Invading armies marched across it again and again, which is one reason Europe has seen so many wars.

Great rivers cut through the continent. The Rhine carries cargo ships between Switzerland and the North Sea. The Danube winds east through ten countries — more than any other river in Europe. For centuries, rivers served as roads for moving goods and ideas.

Mountain ranges act as walls. The Alps divide southern Europe from the north with snow-capped peaks. The Pyrenees separate Spain from France. The Carpathian Mountains curve through Central and Eastern Europe. And far to the east, the Ural Mountains mark the traditional boundary between Europe and Asia.

Invading armies marched across it again and again, which is one reason Europe has seen so many wars. Great rivers cut through the continent.
