The Pyrenees separate Spain from France. The Carpathian Mountains curve through Central and Eastern Europe. And far to the east, the Ural Mountains mark the traditional boundary between Europe and Asia.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by saying flat land with no natural barriers 'works like an open highway'?

choices.text: It is easy for cities to be built across it
choices.feedback: The passage does mention that cities like Paris and Berlin sit on the plain, but the author uses the 'open highway' idea to explain invasions, not city building.

choices.text: It is easy for rivers to flow across it
choices.feedback: The passage talks about rivers cutting through the continent, but the 'open highway' phrase is about armies moving across the land, not about how rivers flow.

choices.text: It is easy for armies to march across it
choices.feedback: This is correct because the very next sentence says 'Invading armies marched across it again and again,' which is exactly what the author means by comparing flat land to an open highway.

choices.text: It is easy for farmers to plant crops across it
choices.feedback: While the passage does say farmers grow crops on the plain, the author uses the 'open highway' phrase to explain why armies could invade easily, not why farming is easy.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Geography Built the Differences

Over centuries, these physical features helped create dozens of separate languages, foods, traditions, and nations — all squeezed onto a continent smaller than Canada. Europe's geography did not just shape the land. It shaped every culture that grew from it, and those cultures went on to shape the world.
stimulus.content_text: Geography Built the Differences Over centuries, these physical features helped create dozens of separate languages, foods, traditions, and nations — all squeezed onto a continent smaller than Canada. Europe's geography did not just shape the land. It shaped every culture that grew from it, and those cultures went on to shape the world.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that Europe has many different cultures?

choices.text: The comparison of its size to Canada
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because comparing Europe's size to Canada only tells us Europe is small — it doesn't give us details about its many different cultures.

choices.text: The detail about shaping the physical land
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because shaping the physical land is about geography and landforms, not about the different cultures that people developed.

choices.text: The creation of many separate languages
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Europe created 'dozens of separate languages, foods, traditions, and nations,' which directly shows that many different cultures exist there.

choices.text: The statement about shaping the whole world
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because saying Europe shaped the whole world tells us Europe had a big impact on other places, but it doesn't show that Europe itself has many different cultures.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between the North European Plain and Europe's mountain ranges?

choices.text: The plain had deep fjords, while the mountains had rich farm soil.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps the details — fjords are carved into Norway's coastal mountains, and the rich, fertile soil belongs to the North European Plain.

choices.text: The plain was surrounded by sea, while the mountains were full of ports.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the plain is surrounded by sea or that the mountains had ports — those details simply aren't in the text.

choices.text: The plain was easy to cross, while the mountains acted like walls.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the plain worked 'like an open highway' while mountain ranges 'act as walls,' showing a clear contrast between easy crossing and blocked passage.

choices.text: The plain helped keep countries apart, while the mountains made travel easier.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the contrast backwards — the plain made travel and invasion easier, while the mountains acted as barriers, not the other way around.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused European countries to focus so heavily on sea trade and exploration?

choices.text: The massive size of the continent when compared to Africa and Canada.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article actually says Europe is the second smallest continent and fits inside a space smaller than Canada.

choices.text: The continent's jagged shape and its closeness to the ocean waters.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that Europe's closeness to the ocean encouraged fishing, trade, exploration, and naval power for thousands of years.

choices.text: The invention of the underwater tunnel connecting England and France.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Channel Tunnel was opened in 1994, which is way too recent to explain thousands of years of sea trade and exploration.

choices.text: The rich soil left behind by ancient glaciers on the northern plains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the rich soil on the North European Plain helped people farm, not explore the seas.

prompt._: Which choice best shows the order the article uses to explain Europe?

choices.text: From the coastline and peninsulas to inland landforms
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article begins with the coastline and peninsulas, then moves inland to cover plains, rivers, and mountains.

choices.text: From mountain borders to the size of the continent
choices.feedback: The article mentions the size of the continent early, but it doesn't start with mountain borders — mountains are discussed near the end.

choices.text: From modern train travel to ancient Roman history
choices.feedback: The article doesn't start with modern train travel — the Channel Tunnel is just a small detail, and the article moves forward through history, not backward from modern times.

choices.text: From rivers and plains to peninsulas and islands
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article covers peninsulas and the coastline first, and rivers and plains come later toward the end.

prompt._: Which contrast best summarizes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Europe is a rugged continent, but its ancient glaciers made the land completely flat and open.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Europe is described as rugged and mountainous, and while glaciers did help create flat, fertile plains, the continent is far from completely flat and open.

choices.text: Europe is a small continent, but its physical features created many separate cultures and nations.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows that despite being a small continent, Europe's many physical features — coastlines, peninsulas, mountains, and plains — helped create dozens of separate cultures and nations.

choices.text: Europe is a crowded continent, but its long coastline stopped people from exploring oceans and lands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that Europe's long, jagged coastline encouraged fishing, trade, exploration, and naval power.

choices.text: Europe is a flat continent, but its deep rivers prevented people from trading goods and ideas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says rivers were like roads that helped people move goods and ideas, so they encouraged trade rather than preventing it.

syllabus_metadata.title: Europe: A Peninsula of Peninsulas

assessments.title: Europe's Great Rivers

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Europe's Rivers: The Roads That Shaped a Continent

Water Highways

A merchant loads wooden barrels onto a flat boat in Germany. He floats downstream for days, passing castles, farms, and cities in country after country. He never sets foot on a road. The year is 1450, and the river beneath him is the only highway he needs.

Long before trucks and trains, rivers were Europe's greatest roads. They carried food to markets, powered machines, watered crops, and connected inland cities to the sea. Europe is a continent of more than forty nations, and rivers flow across many of them, linking people who speak different languages and follow different customs.
stimulus.content_text: Europe's Rivers: The Roads That Shaped a ContinentWater Highways A merchant loads wooden barrels onto a flat boat in Germany. He floats downstream for days, passing castles, farms, and cities in country after country. He never sets foot on a road. The year is 1450, and the river beneath him is the only highway he needs. Long before trucks and trains, rivers were Europe's greatest roads. They carried food to markets, powered machines, watered crops, and connected inland cities to the sea. Europe is a continent of more than forty nations, and rivers flow across many of them, linking people who speak different languages and follow different customs.

prompt._: According to the section "Water Highways," which forms of transportation are contrasted with rivers?

choices.text: Ships and ferries
choices.feedback: Ships and ferries are not mentioned in the passage, even though they also travel on water.

choices.text: Cars and airplanes
choices.feedback: Cars and airplanes are never mentioned anywhere in the passage, so they can't be the right answer.

choices.text: Trucks and trains
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'Long before trucks and trains, rivers were Europe's greatest roads,' showing that rivers are being compared to trucks and trains.

choices.text: Carts and wagons
choices.feedback: Carts and wagons are not mentioned in the passage, so there is no evidence to support this choice.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Danube: One River, Ten Countries

The Danube River touches more countries than any other river on Earth — ten in all. It begins as a small stream in the Black Forest of Germany and winds southeast for nearly 1,800 miles. Rivers follow gravity and flow downhill, no matter which direction that takes them. The Danube flows mostly southeast because the land slopes that way from Germany toward the sea. Along the way, it passes through or along the borders of Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria, Moldova, and Ukraine. It finally empties into the Black Sea.

Great cities grew up along the Danube's banks. Vienna, Budapest, and Belgrade all used the river for trade and travel. The Danube also carried ideas, music, and traditions from one nation to the next. The famous waltz "The Blue Danube" by composer Johann Strauss celebrates the river's beauty.
stimulus.content_text: The Danube: One River, Ten Countries The Danube River touches more countries than any other river on Earth — ten in all. It begins as a small stream in the Black Forest of Germany and winds southeast for nearly 1,800 miles. Rivers follow gravity and flow downhill, no matter which direction that takes them. The Danube flows mostly southeast because the land slopes that way from Germany toward the sea. Along the way, it passes through or along the borders of Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria, Moldova, and Ukraine. It finally empties into the Black Sea. Great cities grew up along the Danube's banks. Vienna, Budapest, and Belgrade all used the river for trade and travel. The Danube also carried ideas, music, and traditions from one nation to the next. The famous waltz "The Blue Danube" by composer Johann Strauss celebrates the river's beauty.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does the Danube flow mostly southeast?

choices.text: Because it begins as a small stream in Germany
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because starting as a small stream in Germany tells us where the Danube begins, but not why it travels in a southeast direction.

choices.text: Because many famous cities were built along its banks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because famous cities were built near the river because of the river, not the other way around — cities don't control which direction a river flows.

choices.text: Because the land slopes that way toward the sea
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the Danube flows southeast 'because the land slopes that way from Germany toward the sea,' and rivers always follow gravity downhill.

choices.text: Because it carries music and traditions between nations
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carrying music and traditions describes how the river helped people share culture, which has nothing to do with the direction the river flows.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Rhine: A River of Castles and Cargo

The Rhine River has served as one of Europe's busiest trade routes for over 2,000 years. It flows about 760 miles from Switzerland to the North Sea, passing through some of the most industrialized parts of Europe — areas packed with factories and businesses. Roman soldiers once guarded its banks as the edge of their empire. Centuries later, medieval lords built castles on rocky cliffs above the water and charged fees to every boat that passed below.

Today, barges — long, flat-bottomed boats built for hauling heavy loads — travel up and down the Rhine every day. They carry coal, steel, grain, and factory-made goods. The Rhine remains one of the busiest commercial waterways in the world.
stimulus.content_text: The Rhine: A River of Castles and Cargo The Rhine River has served as one of Europe's busiest trade routes for over 2,000 years. It flows about 760 miles from Switzerland to the North Sea, passing through some of the most industrialized parts of Europe — areas packed with factories and businesses. Roman soldiers once guarded its banks as the edge of their empire. Centuries later, medieval lords built castles on rocky cliffs above the water and charged fees to every boat that passed below. Today, barges — long, flat-bottomed boats built for hauling heavy loads — travel up and down the Rhine every day. They carry coal, steel, grain, and factory-made goods. The Rhine remains one of the busiest commercial waterways in the world.

prompt._: According to the text, what sequence of events explains how medieval lords used the Rhine River?

choices.text: They guarded the river banks and then expanded the edge of the empire.
choices.feedback: This describes what Roman soldiers did — guarding the river banks — not what medieval lords did, so it's mixing up two different groups of people.

choices.text: They traveled from Switzerland and then built flat-bottomed cargo barges.
choices.feedback: No one in the passage traveled from Switzerland to build barges — that answer mixes up unrelated details about where the river starts and what modern barges look like.

choices.text: They built castles on cliffs and then charged fees to passing boats.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says medieval lords built castles on cliffs and then charged fees to passing boats, in that order.

choices.text: They built large factories and then hauled heavy loads of coal and steel.
choices.feedback: Building factories and hauling coal and steel describes what happens on the Rhine TODAY with modern barges, not what medieval lords did hundreds of years ago.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Volga: Mother of Russia

Farther east, the Volga River stretches about 2,300 miles through Russia, making it the longest river in Europe. It begins in the Valdai Hills northwest of Moscow and flows south to the Caspian Sea — which is actually the world's largest lake, with no connection to the ocean. So not all rivers reach the sea.

Russians call the Volga "Mother Volga" because it means so much to their history and daily life. The river and its tributaries — smaller rivers that feed into a larger one, the way small branches connect to a tree trunk — drain a huge area. About one-third of Russia's population lives in this region. The river also irrigates farmland, meaning its water is channeled through ditches and pipes to water crops, like a giant sprinkler system. Much of Russia's grain grows on land the Volga helps keep green. Hydroelectric dams along the Volga use that same rushing water to produce electricity for the people who live there.
stimulus.content_text: The Volga: Mother of Russia Farther east, the Volga River stretches about 2,300 miles through Russia, making it the longest river in Europe. It begins in the Valdai Hills northwest of Moscow and flows south to the Caspian Sea — which is actually the world's largest lake, with no connection to the ocean. So not all rivers reach the sea. Russians call the Volga "Mother Volga" because it means so much to their history and daily life. The river and its tributaries — smaller rivers that feed into a larger one, the way small branches connect to a tree trunk — drain a huge area. About one-third of Russia's population lives in this region. The river also irrigates farmland, meaning its water is channeled through ditches and pipes to water crops, like a giant sprinkler system. Much of Russia's grain grows on land the Volga helps keep green. Hydroelectric dams along the Volga use that same rushing water to produce electricity for the people who live there.

prompt._: The passage compares tributaries to branches on a tree. Based on this comparison, what does the word 'tributaries' mean?

choices.text: dams that block the flow of a large river
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because dams block river water, and the passage describes those separately as hydroelectric dams — not tributaries.

choices.text: lakes that form at the end of a large river
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because lakes collect standing water at the end of a river, while tributaries are moving rivers that join into a larger river.

choices.text: ditches that carry water to nearby farmland
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ditches that carry water to farmland describe irrigation, which the passage explains as a separate idea from tributaries.

choices.text: smaller rivers that flow into a larger river
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines tributaries as 'smaller rivers that feed into a larger one,' like branches connecting to a tree trunk.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Rivers and the Rise of Factories

When the Industrial Revolution began in the 1700s and 1800s, rivers became even more valuable. Factory owners built along riverbanks because rushing water turned waterwheels that powered their machines, and boats brought in raw materials like cotton and iron and carried finished products away to be sold. Cities like Manchester in England and the Ruhr Valley cities in Germany grew fast because of their river connections.

But growth came at a cost. Factories dumped waste into the water. Sewage — dirty water carrying human and household waste — flowed in too. Many rivers turned foul and gray. In recent decades, European countries have worked hard to clean up their waterways, and many rivers are now cleaner than they have been in centuries.
stimulus.content_text: Rivers and the Rise of Factories When the Industrial Revolution began in the 1700s and 1800s, rivers became even more valuable. Factory owners built along riverbanks because rushing water turned waterwheels that powered their machines, and boats brought in raw materials like cotton and iron and carried finished products away to be sold. Cities like Manchester in England and the Ruhr Valley cities in Germany grew fast because of their river connections. But growth came at a cost. Factories dumped waste into the water. Sewage — dirty water carrying human and household waste — flowed in too. Many rivers turned foul and gray. In recent decades, European countries have worked hard to clean up their waterways, and many rivers are now cleaner than they have been in centuries.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the meaning of the word sewage?

choices.text: Factory waste that is dumped directly into rivers.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage lists factory waste and sewage separately, meaning they are two different things, not the same thing.

choices.text: Dirty water that carries human and household waste.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage defines sewage right in the sentence as 'dirty water carrying human and household waste.'

choices.text: Rushing water that turns large wheels for machines.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because rushing water that turns wheels describes how waterwheels work, not what sewage is.

choices.text: Clean water that flows through cities like Manchester.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because sewage is described as dirty, foul water — the opposite of clean water.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Small Rivers, Giant Cities

Not every important river is long. The Thames in London, the Seine in Paris, and the Tiber in Rome are short compared to the Danube or Volga. But the cities built along them became capitals of empires and centers of art, science, and government. These rivers gave their cities drinking water, natural barriers against enemies, and routes for moving people and goods.

Next time you look at a map of Europe, trace the blue lines with your finger. Those thin, winding marks are rivers — and they carried far more than water. They carried grain and steel, music and ideas, and the everyday lives of millions of people across dozens of countries. The highways came later. The rivers came first.
stimulus.content_text: Small Rivers, Giant Cities Not every important river is long. The Thames in London, the Seine in Paris, and the Tiber in Rome are short compared to the Danube or Volga. But the cities built along them became capitals of empires and centers of art, science, and government. These rivers gave their cities drinking water, natural barriers against enemies, and routes for moving people and goods. Next time you look at a map of Europe, trace the blue lines with your finger. Those thin, winding marks are rivers — and they carried far more than water. They carried grain and steel, music and ideas, and the everyday lives of millions of people across dozens of countries. The highways came later. The rivers came first.

prompt._: Which detail from the passage best supports the author's point that short rivers are still very important?

choices.text: They were created by empires to move heavy grain and steel.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because rivers are natural — the passage never says empires created them, and grain and steel were things *carried* by the rivers, not a reason they were built.

choices.text: They provided cities with water, protection, and useful travel routes.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states these rivers provided 'drinking water, natural barriers against enemies, and routes for moving people and goods,' which shows why short rivers are still very important.

choices.text: They are much longer than the famous Danube and Volga rivers.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — the Thames, Seine, and Tiber are described as *shorter* than the Danube and Volga, not longer.

choices.text: They are the only source of drinking water in all of Europe.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never claims these rivers are the only source of drinking water in Europe — that would be an exaggeration the author never makes.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the Volga River different from the Danube and the Rhine rivers?

choices.text: It powers many factories, but the others only water crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Volga both irrigates crops AND powers hydroelectric dams, and the Rhine also supports heavy industry and factories.

choices.text: It flows through ten nations, but the others stay in one.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's the Danube River, not the Volga, that flows through ten countries.

choices.text: It empties into a lake, but the others flow to the sea.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the Volga empties into the Caspian Sea, which it explains is actually a lake with no connection to the ocean, while the Danube flows to the Black Sea and the Rhine flows to the North Sea.

choices.text: It is a very short river, but the others are extremely long.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Volga is actually the longest river in Europe at around 2,300 miles, making it longer than both the Danube and the Rhine.

prompt._: According to the article, what caused cities like Budapest, Manchester, and Paris to grow and become powerful along riverbanks?

choices.text: The rivers flowed downhill toward the sea and linked people who spoke different languages.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because flowing downhill and linking different people describes how rivers work, not why cities became important centers of power.

choices.text: The rivers were the only places where citizens could find clean drinking water and fresh food.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says rivers were the only source of clean water and food — it mentions many other benefits rivers provided.

choices.text: The rivers supplied important benefits like travel routes, drinking water, and machine power.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that rivers helped cities grow by providing trade routes, drinking water, and power for machines — all working together.

choices.text: The rivers allowed medieval lords to build tall castles and collect fees from passing boats.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because building castles and collecting fees is only a detail about the Rhine River, not a reason why major cities grew powerful.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence correctly shows how people used Europe's rivers over the centuries?

choices.text: Romans guarded the riverbanks, medieval lords charged boat fees, and factory owners powered machines.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the time order in the article: Romans (2,000+ years ago), then medieval lords (centuries later), then factory owners (1700s–1800s).

choices.text: Romans guarded the riverbanks, factory owners powered machines, and medieval lords charged boat fees.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it leaves out the correct order of medieval lords — they came after the Romans but before the factory owners, not after them.

choices.text: Medieval lords charged boat fees, Romans guarded the riverbanks, and factory owners powered machines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts medieval lords before the Romans, but the article clearly shows Romans came first, over 2,000 years ago.

choices.text: Factory owners powered machines, medieval lords charged boat fees, and Romans guarded the riverbanks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because factory owners came last in history, not first — the article places them in the 1700s and 1800s, long after the Romans and medieval lords.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is one way the history of the Rhine River is similar to the history of the Thames, Seine, and Tiber rivers?

choices.text: They supplied rushing water and machine power for industrial factories.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because powering factory machines with rushing water is mentioned during the Industrial Revolution section, but it is not listed as something the Thames, Seine, and Tiber specifically did.

choices.text: They provided natural barriers and protection against invading enemies.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the Thames, Seine, and Tiber gave cities 'natural barriers against enemies,' and the Rhine was guarded by Roman soldiers as the edge of their empire — both were used for protection.

choices.text: They carried new ideas and musical traditions from one nation to another.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carrying ideas and musical traditions is only mentioned about the Danube River, not the Rhine or the shorter rivers like the Thames, Seine, and Tiber.

choices.text: They irrigated large areas of farmland for the farmers growing the grain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because irrigating farmland is only described as something the Volga River in Russia does, not the Rhine or the other shorter rivers.

syllabus_metadata.title: Europe's Great Rivers

assessments.title: Climates and Countries of Europe

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Europe's Climate Zones and Countries

One Continent, Many Climates

In January, a child in Athens, Greece, picks oranges from a tree in her backyard. That same morning, a boy in northern Norway walks to school in total darkness, his boots crunching through snow at twenty degrees below zero. Both of them call the same continent home. Yet their daily lives could hardly be more different — and the reason comes down to geography.

You might think of Europe as its own separate chunk of land, completely divided from Asia. Actually, Europe and Asia sit on one connected landmass called Eurasia. Geographers draw Europe's eastern border along the Ural Mountains — a line people agreed on, not one carved by water. Still, Europe is treated as its own continent because its climates, cultures, and histories set it apart.
stimulus.content_text: Europe's Climate Zones and CountriesOne Continent, Many Climates In January, a child in Athens, Greece, picks oranges from a tree in her backyard. That same morning, a boy in northern Norway walks to school in total darkness, his boots crunching through snow at twenty degrees below zero. Both of them call the same continent home. Yet their daily lives could hardly be more different — and the reason comes down to geography. You might think of Europe as its own separate chunk of land, completely divided from Asia. Actually, Europe and Asia sit on one connected landmass called Eurasia. Geographers draw Europe's eastern border along the Ural Mountains — a line people agreed on, not one carved by water. Still, Europe is treated as its own continent because its climates, cultures, and histories set it apart.

prompt._: According to the text, how is Europe's eastern border different from a border carved by water?

choices.text: The eastern border was agreed on by people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the eastern border is 'a line people agreed on,' meaning humans chose it rather than water forming it naturally.

choices.text: The eastern border was split by a landmass.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually explains that Europe and Asia share one connected landmass called Eurasia, so no landmass divided them.

choices.text: The eastern border was created by climates.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while climates help set Europe apart as a continent, the passage does not say climates created the eastern border.

choices.text: The eastern border was formed by deep snow.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions snow having anything to do with creating Europe's eastern border.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Frozen North: Tundra, Taiga, and the Midnight Sun

Start at the top of the map. In far northern Scandinavia — Norway, Sweden, and Finland — winters are long, dark, and brutally cold. Temperatures plunge well below zero. The ground in some areas stays frozen nearly all year. This type of flat, treeless land is called tundra, a word meaning ground too cold for trees to grow. Picture a white, windswept plain stretching to the horizon with no forests in sight.

A little farther south, thick evergreen forests take over. Geographers call this forest belt the taiga — a sea of pine and spruce trees so vast you could walk for days without finding its edge. Then summer arrives, and something astonishing happens. The sun barely dips below the horizon. For weeks, northern Scandinavia glows with daylight even at midnight. People call this the "midnight sun."
stimulus.content_text: The Frozen North: Tundra, Taiga, and the Midnight Sun Start at the top of the map. In far northern Scandinavia — Norway, Sweden, and Finland — winters are long, dark, and brutally cold. Temperatures plunge well below zero. The ground in some areas stays frozen nearly all year. This type of flat, treeless land is called tundra, a word meaning ground too cold for trees to grow. Picture a white, windswept plain stretching to the horizon with no forests in sight. A little farther south, thick evergreen forests take over. Geographers call this forest belt the taiga — a sea of pine and spruce trees so vast you could walk for days without finding its edge. Then summer arrives, and something astonishing happens. The sun barely dips below the horizon. For weeks, northern Scandinavia glows with daylight even at midnight. People call this the "midnight sun."

prompt._: Based on the passage, why are there no trees growing in the tundra?

choices.text: The thick forests take up the space.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the taiga forests are located farther south than the tundra, so they aren't taking up the tundra's space.

choices.text: The frozen ground is much too cold.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines tundra as 'ground too cold for trees to grow,' and explains the ground stays frozen nearly all year.

choices.text: The strong winds blow the trees away.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though the passage mentions a 'windswept plain,' it never says wind is the reason trees can't grow there.

choices.text: The winter season is much too dark.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says darkness prevents trees from growing — it only says winters are long and dark.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Western Europe's Maritime Climate and the Gulf Stream

Move south and west into the United Kingdom, France, the Netherlands, and Germany. Here, the weather feels gentler. Summers stay cool. Winters stay mild. Rain falls throughout the year, not just in one season. This pattern is called a maritime climate — "maritime" means "related to the sea," and that name is a clue. The nearby Atlantic Ocean acts like a giant temperature regulator, keeping the air from getting too hot or too cold.

One ocean current deserves special credit. The Gulf Stream is a river of warm water that flows from near the Americas across the Atlantic toward Europe. It warms the air above it, and winds carry that warmth over Western Europe. London sits as far north as parts of Canada where winters are fierce. Without the Gulf Stream, Londoners would need much heavier coats. Latitude — your distance north or south of the equator — is not the only thing that controls temperature. Ocean currents matter enormously.
stimulus.content_text: Western Europe's Maritime Climate and the Gulf Stream Move south and west into the United Kingdom, France, the Netherlands, and Germany. Here, the weather feels gentler. Summers stay cool. Winters stay mild. Rain falls throughout the year, not just in one season. This pattern is called a maritime climate — "maritime" means "related to the sea," and that name is a clue. The nearby Atlantic Ocean acts like a giant temperature regulator, keeping the air from getting too hot or too cold. One ocean current deserves special credit. The Gulf Stream is a river of warm water that flows from near the Americas across the Atlantic toward Europe. It warms the air above it, and winds carry that warmth over Western Europe. London sits as far north as parts of Canada where winters are fierce. Without the Gulf Stream, Londoners would need much heavier coats. Latitude — your distance north or south of the equator — is not the only thing that controls temperature. Ocean currents matter enormously.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after the Gulf Stream warms the air above it?

choices.text: The Atlantic Ocean keeps the air from changing much.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses that detail to describe the Atlantic Ocean acting as a temperature regulator in general, not as the next step after the Gulf Stream warms the air.

choices.text: London has winters fiercer than parts of Canada.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says London would have fierce winters WITHOUT the Gulf Stream, meaning the Gulf Stream actually prevents those harsh winters.

choices.text: Winds carry that warmth over Western Europe.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that after the Gulf Stream warms the air above it, 'winds carry that warmth over Western Europe.'

choices.text: The Gulf Stream flows back toward the Americas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the Gulf Stream flows back toward the Americas — it only describes it flowing from near the Americas toward Europe.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Continental and Mediterranean Climates: Extremes East and South

Central and Eastern Europe tell a different story. Cities like Warsaw, Prague, and Moscow sit far from the ocean's calming influence. They experience a continental climate, meaning the weather swings between extremes — think of a pendulum that swings wide instead of gently rocking. Summers can be hot, with booming thunderstorms that drench fertile farmland. Winters bring bitter cold and heavy blankets of snow.

Along Europe's southern edge, the Mediterranean Sea shapes everything. Spain, Italy, Greece, and southern France enjoy a Mediterranean climate: hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters. The sea works like a giant heat reservoir — it stores warmth the way a brick wall holds heat from a fireplace, then slowly releases it in cooler months. This climate is perfect for growing olives, grapes, and citrus fruits. Those crops have fed Mediterranean communities for thousands of years. Millions of tourists also flock to the sunny coasts each summer. Yet it's easy to assume that everything in southern Europe stays warm. Mountain peaks in Spain and Italy can be bitterly cold and snow-covered, even though they sit far south.
stimulus.content_text: Continental and Mediterranean Climates: Extremes East and South Central and Eastern Europe tell a different story. Cities like Warsaw, Prague, and Moscow sit far from the ocean's calming influence. They experience a continental climate, meaning the weather swings between extremes — think of a pendulum that swings wide instead of gently rocking. Summers can be hot, with booming thunderstorms that drench fertile farmland. Winters bring bitter cold and heavy blankets of snow. Along Europe's southern edge, the Mediterranean Sea shapes everything. Spain, Italy, Greece, and southern France enjoy a Mediterranean climate: hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters. The sea works like a giant heat reservoir — it stores warmth the way a brick wall holds heat from a fireplace, then slowly releases it in cooler months. This climate is perfect for growing olives, grapes, and citrus fruits. Those crops have fed Mediterranean communities for thousands of years. Millions of tourists also flock to the sunny coasts each summer. Yet it's easy to assume that everything in southern Europe stays warm. Mountain peaks in Spain and Italy can be bitterly cold and snow-covered, even though they sit far south.

prompt._: The author compares the Mediterranean Sea to a brick wall to explain the phrase "heat reservoir." Based on this comparison, what does a "heat reservoir" do?

choices.text: It grows crops for many long years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because growing crops is a benefit of the Mediterranean climate, but it has nothing to do with what a heat reservoir does.

choices.text: It blocks the freezing winter winds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the sea blocking cold winds — that's not part of the brick wall comparison at all.

choices.text: It creates heavy summer thunderstorms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because heavy thunderstorms are described in the continental climate of Central and Eastern Europe, not the Mediterranean.

choices.text: It saves up and slowly shares warmth.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly compares the sea to a brick wall that holds heat and then slowly releases it, which means it saves up and slowly shares warmth.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Fifty Countries, One Connected Continent

Europe holds about 50 countries and roughly 750 million people. Russia is the largest country in Europe — and in the entire world — but only its western portion, the part west of the Ural Mountains, counts as European. At the opposite extreme, Vatican City covers just 109 acres in the heart of Rome.

Between those extremes, you find Germany with Europe's largest economy, France with its celebrated food and culture, and the United Kingdom, whose language and legal traditions spread across the globe. The Scandinavian countries rank among the highest in quality of life. The small nations of the Balkans, the Baltic states, and Central Europe each carry their own languages, traditions, and histories. That constant movement across four very different climate zones did not just create variety — it wove fifty countries into neighbors who have been borrowing from each other for centuries.

Russia is the largest country in Europe — and in the entire world — but only its western portion, the part west of the Ural Mountains, counts as European. At the opposite extreme, Vatican City covers just 109 acres in the heart of Rome. Between those extremes, you find Germany with Europe's largest economy, France with its celebrated food and culture, and the United Kingdom, whose language and legal traditions spread across the globe. The Scandinavian countries rank among the highest in quality of life. The small nations of the Balkans, the Baltic states, and Central Europe each carry their own languages, traditions, and histories. That constant movement across four very different climate zones did not just create variety — it wove fifty countries into neighbors who have been borrowing from each other for centuries.

prompt._: According to the text, which of the following defines the European part of Russia?

choices.text: The part east of the Ural Mountains
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says the European part is WEST of the Ural Mountains, not east, so this is the opposite of the right answer.

choices.text: The part covering the heart of Rome
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — 'the heart of Rome' is where Vatican City is located, not a description of Russia.

choices.text: The part spreading across the globe
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — 'spreading across the globe' describes the United Kingdom's language and legal traditions, not Russia's borders.

choices.text: The part west of the Ural Mountains
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says exactly that the European part of Russia is 'the part west of the Ural Mountains.'

prompt._: According to the article, how is the continental climate of Eastern Europe different from the maritime climate of Western Europe?

choices.text: It has milder winters because of warm ocean currents nearby
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mild winters from warm ocean currents describe Western Europe's maritime climate, not the continental climate of Eastern Europe.

choices.text: It gets steady rain throughout every season of the year
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because steady rain throughout every season is a feature of the maritime climate in Western Europe, not the continental climate.

choices.text: It swings between hot summers and very cold winters
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly says the continental climate 'swings between extremes' — hot summers and bitterly cold winters.

choices.text: It stays warm and dry because of the Mediterranean Sea
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because staying warm and dry due to the Mediterranean Sea describes the Mediterranean climate of southern Europe, like Spain, Italy, and Greece.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the main reason Europe is considered its own continent despite being connected to Asia?

choices.text: Its massive overall size makes it too large to be managed as one connected landmass.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions Europe's size being a reason it is considered a separate continent.

choices.text: Its freezing northern temperatures prevent people from easily traveling across the agreed upon eastern borders.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not say that cold temperatures prevent people from traveling across Europe's eastern border.

choices.text: Its unique mix of different climates and cultures sets it apart from the connected landmass.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states Europe is treated as its own continent due to its unique climates, cultures, and histories setting it apart from the rest of Eurasia.

choices.text: Its deep ocean waters carve natural borders that physically divide the land into separate pieces.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states the eastern border is a line people agreed on, not one carved by water or ocean.

prompt._: Based on the article, in what order would a traveler experience these climates if they traveled from the far north of Europe down to the southern edge?

choices.text: The Mediterranean climate, then the taiga, then the tundra
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Mediterranean climate is at the southern edge of Europe, so a traveler starting in the far north would reach it last, not first.

choices.text: The tundra, then the taiga, then the Mediterranean climate
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly places tundra in the far north, taiga a little farther south, and the Mediterranean climate at Europe's southern edge — exactly the order a traveler going south would experience.

choices.text: The tundra, then the Mediterranean climate, then the taiga
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the taiga comes right after the tundra as you move south, not at the very end of the journey.

choices.text: The taiga, then the tundra, then the Mediterranean climate
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says the tundra comes before the taiga as you travel north to south, not the other way around.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are the tundras of Scandinavia similar to the mountain peaks of Spain and Italy?

choices.text: They both experience bitterly cold weather.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article describes both the Scandinavian tundra and the mountain peaks of Spain and Italy as bitterly cold and snowy.

choices.text: They both receive warmth from ocean waters.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the tundra in Scandinavia is far from warm ocean waters — it's the western European countries like the UK that get warmth from the Gulf Stream and the Atlantic Ocean.

choices.text: They both grow crops like olives and grapes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because olives and grapes grow in the warm Mediterranean climate of southern Europe, not in the freezing cold tundra of Scandinavia.

choices.text: They both have long periods of total night.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because long periods of darkness (the midnight sun's opposite) happen in northern Scandinavia, not on mountain peaks in Spain and Italy.

syllabus_metadata.title: Climates and Countries of Europe

assessments.title: The European Union: Working Together

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Geography of Europe: How 27 Countries Chose to Work Together

From Ruins to a Radical Idea

Picture a map of Europe in 1945. Bridges are broken. Entire city blocks are flattened rubble. Millions of people have no homes. Two world wars in just thirty years killed tens of millions across this small continent. Then six neighboring countries — France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg — tried something no one expected. They decided to share.

Look at where those six countries sit on a map. They cluster together in western Europe, their borders touching like puzzle pieces. Leaders in those nations believed something simple but powerful: countries that depend on each other for business and prosperity — the wealth and success that come from a strong economy — would have too much to lose by fighting. In 1951, the six agreed to share control of their coal and steel industries. Coal and steel were the raw materials needed to build weapons. Sharing them was like handing your neighbor a key to your house. It built trust.
stimulus.content_text: Geography of Europe: How 27 Countries Chose to Work TogetherFrom Ruins to a Radical Idea Picture a map of Europe in 1945. Bridges are broken. Entire city blocks are flattened rubble. Millions of people have no homes. Two world wars in just thirty years killed tens of millions across this small continent. Then six neighboring countries — France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg — tried something no one expected. They decided to share. Look at where those six countries sit on a map. They cluster together in western Europe, their borders touching like puzzle pieces. Leaders in those nations believed something simple but powerful: countries that depend on each other for business and prosperity — the wealth and success that come from a strong economy — would have too much to lose by fighting. In 1951, the six agreed to share control of their coal and steel industries. Coal and steel were the raw materials needed to build weapons. Sharing them was like handing your neighbor a key to your house. It built trust.

prompt._: According to the text, how was the six countries' decision in 1951 different from what people expected them to do after the wars?

choices.text: They chose to rebuild their cities before helping neighbors
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about countries choosing to rebuild their own cities before helping their neighbors.

choices.text: They agreed to stop making coal and steel completely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the countries agreed to share control of coal and steel production, not stop making those materials completely.

choices.text: They moved their borders so they would no longer touch
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually describes the countries' borders as already touching like puzzle pieces, and there is no mention of moving borders.

choices.text: They decided to share resources instead of continuing to fight
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the six countries did 'something no one expected' by deciding to share their coal and steel instead of continuing to fight after two world wars.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Six Countries Become Twenty-Seven

That small partnership grew. Over the decades, more countries joined, and the group expanded beyond coal and steel into trade, farming, science, and education. Today it is called the European Union, or EU — a political and economic partnership of 27 member countries spread across the continent. The EU has helped keep peace in Western Europe for more than 75 years.

Norway and Switzerland, for example, chose to stay out. Being in the EU is a decision, not something that happens automatically just because a country is in Europe.

Here is another thing that surprises many people: the EU is not a single country. It does not replace the governments of its members. France still has its own president. Germany still has its own parliament — a group of elected leaders who make laws, like Congress in the United States. Italy still speaks Italian. Poland still speaks Polish. Each country keeps its own identity, its own flag, and its own leaders. But they all agree to follow a shared set of rules. The EU also has its own parliament and its own court of justice to settle disagreements between members.
stimulus.content_text: Six Countries Become Twenty-Seven That small partnership grew. Over the decades, more countries joined, and the group expanded beyond coal and steel into trade, farming, science, and education. Today it is called the European Union, or EU — a political and economic partnership of 27 member countries spread across the continent. The EU has helped keep peace in Western Europe for more than 75 years. Norway and Switzerland, for example, chose to stay out. Being in the EU is a decision, not something that happens automatically just because a country is in Europe. Here is another thing that surprises many people: the EU is not a single country. It does not replace the governments of its members. France still has its own president. Germany still has its own parliament — a group of elected leaders who make laws, like Congress in the United States. Italy still speaks Italian. Poland still speaks Polish. Each country keeps its own identity, its own flag, and its own leaders. But they all agree to follow a shared set of rules. The EU also has its own parliament and its own court of justice to settle disagreements between members.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is the EU not the same as one single country?

choices.text: Because all European countries are required to join it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — joining the EU is a choice, not automatic, and countries like Norway and Switzerland chose to stay out.

choices.text: Because member countries no longer have their own flags
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says each country KEEPS its own flag, so member countries still have their own flags.

choices.text: Because the EU replaces each country's laws and language
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the EU does NOT replace each country's laws and language — Italy still speaks Italian and Poland still speaks Polish.

choices.text: Because member countries keep their own governments and leaders
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the EU does not replace member governments, and each country still has its own president, parliament, and leaders.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
One Currency, Many Countries

One of the easiest ways to spot the EU's influence is in people's wallets. Twenty of the 27 member countries use the same money: the euro. A currency is the type of money a country uses — dollars in the United States, pesos in Mexico. Before the euro arrived in 1999, a traveler going from France to Germany to Italy had to exchange money three separate times, paying a fee each time. Now the same coins and bills work in all three countries.

Not every EU country uses the euro, though. Using the euro is a choice within the EU, just like joining the EU itself is a choice within Europe.
stimulus.content_text: One Currency, Many Countries One of the easiest ways to spot the EU's influence is in people's wallets. Twenty of the 27 member countries use the same money: the euro. A currency is the type of money a country uses — dollars in the United States, pesos in Mexico. Before the euro arrived in 1999, a traveler going from France to Germany to Italy had to exchange money three separate times, paying a fee each time. Now the same coins and bills work in all three countries. Not every EU country uses the euro, though. Using the euro is a choice within the EU, just like joining the EU itself is a choice within Europe.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after the euro arrived in 1999?

choices.text: People exchanged their money three separate times.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because exchanging money three separate times is what travelers had to do *before* the euro arrived in 1999, not after.

choices.text: People started paying for things using dollars and pesos.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because dollars are used in the United States and pesos are used in Mexico — the passage uses these as examples of other currencies, not ones used in EU countries.

choices.text: People used the euro in all twenty-seven member countries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says only 20 of the 27 EU member countries use the euro, not all 27.

choices.text: People used the same coins and bills in three countries.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that after the euro arrived, the same coins and bills work in France, Germany, and Italy.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Borders You Can Walk Across

The EU also changed what it feels like to cross a border. Many member countries belong to the Schengen Area — an agreement that lets people travel between certain countries without showing a passport. It works like moving between states in the U.S. Without stopping at a checkpoint.

This freedom of movement — the right to live, work, or study in another member country — would have seemed impossible a hundred years ago. Back then, crossing a European border often meant passing through guarded gates. Today, a student from Poland can attend a university in Spain. A worker from Italy can take a job in Ireland. People and goods flow across the continent in ways earlier generations never imagined.
stimulus.content_text: Borders You Can Walk Across The EU also changed what it feels like to cross a border. Many member countries belong to the Schengen Area — an agreement that lets people travel between certain countries without showing a passport. It works like moving between states in the U.S. Without stopping at a checkpoint. This freedom of movement — the right to live, work, or study in another member country — would have seemed impossible a hundred years ago. Back then, crossing a European border often meant passing through guarded gates. Today, a student from Poland can attend a university in Spain. A worker from Italy can take a job in Ireland. People and goods flow across the continent in ways earlier generations never imagined.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is crossing a border in the Schengen Area today similar to something in the United States?

choices.text: It means paying a fee before entering a new area
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never mentions any fees or costs for crossing borders in the Schengen Area.

choices.text: It involves getting special permission from leaders
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Schengen Area is all about traveling freely, not getting special permission from anyone.

choices.text: It is like moving between states without stopping
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly compares the Schengen Area to moving between U.S. states without stopping at a checkpoint.

choices.text: It requires showing a passport at a guarded gate
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the Schengen Area lets people travel WITHOUT showing a passport or stopping at guarded gates.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Not Everyone Agrees

Some people believe the EU holds too much power over individual countries. They argue that important decisions should be made at home, not by a distant group of officials. This debate led to one of the biggest events in recent European history. In 2016, citizens of the United Kingdom voted to leave the EU. The process, called Brexit, took years. Britain had to untangle its laws, trade agreements, and regulations — official rules set by a government — from the EU's. The UK officially left in 2020. Brexit proved that the question of how closely European nations should cooperate is still deeply personal and hotly debated.
stimulus.content_text: Not Everyone Agrees Some people believe the EU holds too much power over individual countries. They argue that important decisions should be made at home, not by a distant group of officials. This debate led to one of the biggest events in recent European history. In 2016, citizens of the United Kingdom voted to leave the EU. The process, called Brexit, took years. Britain had to untangle its laws, trade agreements, and regulations — official rules set by a government — from the EU's. The UK officially left in 2020. Brexit proved that the question of how closely European nations should cooperate is still deeply personal and hotly debated.

prompt._: According to the text, what are regulations?

choices.text: debates about the EU
choices.feedback: Debates about the EU are part of the passage's topic, but that is not what the word regulations means.

choices.text: official rules set by a government
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines regulations as 'official rules set by a government' right after the word appears.

choices.text: agreements between countries
choices.feedback: Trade agreements are mentioned in the passage, but the passage does not define regulations as agreements between countries.

choices.text: votes made by citizens
choices.feedback: Votes made by citizens describes how Brexit started, but the passage never calls votes 'regulations.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why a Partnership Matters on a Map

Pull up a map of Europe and trace the borders of the 27 EU countries. You will see a patchwork of nations stretching from Portugal on the Atlantic coast to Finland near the Arctic. That idea continues to inspire people around the world. You are seeing a continent still deciding, country by country, how close its neighbors should be.

You will see a patchwork of nations stretching from Portugal on the Atlantic coast to Finland near the Arctic. That idea continues to inspire people around the world. You are seeing a continent still deciding, country by country, how close its neighbors should be.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that the EU countries cover many different parts of Europe?

choices.text: The idea inspires people around the world.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the idea inspiring people around the world tells us about the EU's influence, not about which parts of Europe its countries cover.

choices.text: The countries stretch from Portugal to Finland.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because naming Portugal in the west and Finland near the Arctic shows that EU countries spread across very different and distant parts of Europe.

choices.text: The countries are still deciding how close neighbors should be.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because countries deciding how close neighbors should be is about relationships between countries, not about where they are located on the map.

choices.text: The map shows the borders of 27 EU countries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because knowing there are 27 countries tells us how many there are, but it doesn't tell us where those countries are spread across Europe.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is traveling between many European countries today different from traveling in the past?

choices.text: They must learn new languages and follow strict national rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions travelers needing to learn new languages or follow stricter national rules when crossing borders.

choices.text: They can use the same money and cross without being stopped.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that the shared euro removes money exchange fees and the Schengen Area lets people cross borders without stopping.

choices.text: They must show passports and pay extra fees to cross borders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because showing passports and paying fees is how travel used to work before the euro and the Schengen Area made things easier.

choices.text: They can only visit the six original countries that joined.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the EU grew from 6 countries to 27, and travelers can move freely among many of those member countries, not just the original six.

prompt._: How does the author use a cause-and-effect structure to explain the history of the European Union?

choices.text: By showing how the destruction of war caused six nations to share resources, which later led to a massive partnership.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly shows that war's destruction caused six nations to share resources, which then grew into the 27-country EU — a clear cause-and-effect chain.

choices.text: By explaining how the creation of the euro caused travelers to save money, which led to the invention of the Schengen Area.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the euro caused the Schengen Area to be created — they were two separate agreements.

choices.text: By detailing how the need for coal and steel caused countries to work together, which led to building new bridges.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while coal and steel sharing did lead to cooperation, the passage never says it caused countries to build new bridges.

choices.text: By describing how the United Kingdom leaving the EU caused other countries to worry, which led to them changing their borders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions other countries changing their borders as a result of Brexit.

prompt._: According to the article, what happened next after six countries agreed to share coal and steel in 1951?

choices.text: Other countries joined to form the European Union.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says the small partnership of six countries grew over time as more countries joined, eventually becoming the 27-member European Union.

choices.text: The six countries combined into one single nation.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article specifically says the EU is not a single country and that each member country keeps its own government, language, and identity.

choices.text: The United Kingdom voted to leave the partnership.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the UK's vote to leave happened in 2016, which was decades after the six countries first agreed to share coal and steel in 1951.

choices.text: All of the European countries began using the euro.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the euro didn't exist until 1999, and even then, only 20 of the 27 EU countries chose to use it.

prompt._: How is the choice to use the euro similar to the choice to join the EU, based on evidence from across the article?

choices.text: Both choices are decisions that each country can make for itself.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that joining the EU is 'a decision' and that using the euro is also 'a choice within the EU,' showing both are up to each country.

choices.text: Both choices happened at the same time in 1951 when the partnership began.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the partnership began in 1951 but the euro didn't come until 1999, so these two things happened almost 50 years apart.

choices.text: Both choices require countries to give up their own governments.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly says EU countries keep their own governments, presidents, and parliaments — they don't give those up.

choices.text: Both choices were forced on countries by EU leaders in order to keep peace.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article shows that joining the EU and using the euro are both voluntary choices — no one is forced, as proven by countries like Norway that chose to stay out.

syllabus_metadata.title: The European Union: Working Together

assessments.title: The Heart: Your Body's Pump

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Circulatory System: Your Body's Nonstop Delivery Network

A Pump That Never Stops

Place your hand in the center of your chest, then slide it just slightly to the left. Right there, behind your ribs, you can feel a steady thump. That is your heart beating — and it has not stopped once since before you were born.

It is about the size of your closed fist, and it is made of strong muscle tissue — layers of tightly packed muscle cells that can squeeze and relax over and over without wearing out.

Here is something that surprises many students: the heart does not make blood. It only pumps it. Blood is produced inside your bones, in a soft material called bone marrow. The heart's job is to keep that blood moving so every cell in your body gets what it needs.
stimulus.content_text: The Circulatory System: Your Body's Nonstop Delivery NetworkA Pump That Never Stops Place your hand in the center of your chest, then slide it just slightly to the left. Right there, behind your ribs, you can feel a steady thump. That is your heart beating — and it has not stopped once since before you were born. It is about the size of your closed fist, and it is made of strong muscle tissue — layers of tightly packed muscle cells that can squeeze and relax over and over without wearing out. Here is something that surprises many students: the heart does not make blood. It only pumps it. Blood is produced inside your bones, in a soft material called bone marrow. The heart's job is to keep that blood moving so every cell in your body gets what it needs.

prompt._: According to the text, which statement shows the contrast between what the heart does and what bone marrow does?

choices.text: The heart pumps blood, but bone marrow makes it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the heart 'only pumps' blood and that blood is made inside bones in the bone marrow, showing they have two different jobs.

choices.text: The heart makes blood, but bone marrow pumps it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the jobs switched around — the passage tells us it's the heart that pumps blood and bone marrow that makes it, not the other way around.

choices.text: The heart and bone marrow both make blood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the heart does not make blood — the passage specifically says 'the heart does not make blood.'

choices.text: The heart and bone marrow both pump blood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because bone marrow does not pump blood — that is only the heart's job.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Four Rooms, Two Jobs

If you could slice a heart open and look inside, you would find four hollow spaces called chambers — like four small rooms in a house, each with its own job. The two upper chambers are the atria. Think of them as waiting rooms. Blood flows into the atria and pauses there for just a moment. The two lower chambers are the ventricles. These are the powerhouses. They squeeze hard to launch blood out of the heart.

The right side and the left side of the heart handle different tasks. The right atrium collects blood that has already traveled through your body and used up most of its oxygen. That tired blood drops down into the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs to pick up a fresh supply of oxygen. Meanwhile, the left atrium receives bright, oxygen-rich blood returning from the lungs. It sends that refreshed blood down to the left ventricle, the strongest chamber of all. The left ventricle then pumps oxygen-rich blood out to your entire body, from your brain down to your toes.

You may have heard that blood without oxygen is blue, but that is actually a myth. It is always red — just a darker shade of red when it carries less oxygen. The blue color you see in the veins beneath your skin is a trick of light passing through your skin.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does the right ventricle pump blood to the lungs?

choices.text: because the blood turns blue and the lungs change it back to red
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that blood is never actually blue, so there is no blue color for the lungs to fix.

choices.text: because the left ventricle is too strong to send blood to the lungs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the left ventricle is too strong to pump to the lungs — the two sides simply have different jobs.

choices.text: because the lungs need to remove oxygen from the blood
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the lungs give blood a fresh supply of oxygen, not take oxygen away from it.

choices.text: because the blood has used up its oxygen and needs a fresh supply
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the blood has used up its oxygen and the right ventricle sends it to the lungs to pick up a fresh supply.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
One-Way Doors and the Lub-Dub Sound

Between the chambers sit small flaps called valves. Valves work like one-way doors in a hallway. They swing open to let blood pass through, then snap shut so blood cannot flow backward. When a doctor places a stethoscope against your chest, the familiar "lub-dub" sound comes from those valves closing. The "lub" happens when the valves between the atria and ventricles shut. The "dub" happens when the valves at the exits of the ventricles close right after.
stimulus.content_text: One-Way Doors and the Lub-Dub Sound Between the chambers sit small flaps called valves. Valves work like one-way doors in a hallway. They swing open to let blood pass through, then snap shut so blood cannot flow backward. When a doctor places a stethoscope against your chest, the familiar "lub-dub" sound comes from those valves closing. The "lub" happens when the valves between the atria and ventricles shut. The "dub" happens when the valves at the exits of the ventricles close right after.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens right after the valves between the atria and ventricles shut?

choices.text: The small flaps swing open to let blood pass.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the valves swing open before they shut to let blood pass through, not after they close.

choices.text: The valves at the exits of the ventricles close.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the 'dub' sound — caused by the valves at the exits of the ventricles closing — happens right after the 'lub,' which is the valves between the atria and ventricles shutting.

choices.text: The blood flows backward through the hallway doors.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the valves are designed to prevent blood from flowing backward — that is the opposite of what happens.

choices.text: A doctor places a stethoscope against your chest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the doctor placing the stethoscope is how the sounds are heard, not something that happens right after the valves shut.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Highway of Blood Vessels

Blood leaves the heart through thick, muscular tubes called arteries. Arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart the way highways carry traffic out of a city. As arteries travel farther from the heart, they branch into smaller and smaller paths. The tiniest branches are called capillaries — tubes so narrow that blood cells pass through them in a single-file line. Capillaries deliver oxygen and nutrients — useful substances from the food you eat, like vitamins and sugars — directly to your cells. They also pick up waste products your cells no longer need. Then the blood flows into veins, which carry it back toward the heart like return roads heading into the city. Some of that waste, like carbon dioxide, leaves your body when you breathe out. Other waste gets filtered by your kidneys.

If you stretched all the blood vessels in your body end to end, they would circle Earth about two and a half times. Your heart could fill them in just a few days.
