Arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart the way highways carry traffic out of a city. As arteries travel farther from the heart, they branch into smaller and smaller paths. The tiniest branches are called capillaries — tubes so narrow that blood cells pass through them in a single-file line. Capillaries deliver oxygen and nutrients — useful substances from the food you eat, like vitamins and sugars — directly to your cells. They also pick up waste products your cells no longer need. Then the blood flows into veins, which carry it back toward the heart like return roads heading into the city. Some of that waste, like carbon dioxide, leaves your body when you breathe out. Other waste gets filtered by your kidneys. If you stretched all the blood vessels in your body end to end, they would circle Earth about two and a half times. Your heart could fill them in just a few days.

prompt._: To help explain the word veins, the text compares them to return roads. How are veins similar to return roads?

choices.text: Both stretch around the Earth several times.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stretching around the Earth is a fact about all blood vessels combined, not a similarity between veins and return roads.

choices.text: Both drop off useful nutrients to the cells.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because dropping off nutrients is what capillaries do, not veins or return roads.

choices.text: Both branch into very narrow, tiny pathways.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because branching into narrow pathways describes arteries becoming capillaries, not veins or return roads.

choices.text: Both carry things back toward a main center.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly compares veins to return roads, and both carry things back toward a main center — blood back to the heart, and traffic back into the city.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Beat Goes On

Your heart keeps its rhythm without any help from your brain. A tiny cluster of cells inside the heart sends out electrical signals — small bursts of energy, like a built-in alarm clock — that tell the heart muscle when to squeeze and when to relax. Doctors call this cluster the heart's natural pacemaker, a word that means "something that sets the pace." It keeps the beat steady whether you are sleeping, reading, or sprinting across a playground.

You can help your heart stay strong through healthy habits. Exercise makes the heart muscle stronger, just as shooting baskets makes your arms stronger. A stronger heart pumps more blood with each beat, so it does not have to beat as often. That is why athletes often have slower resting heart rates than people who do not exercise. Eating fruits, vegetables, and whole grains gives the heart the fuel it needs to keep working well.

Doctors call this cluster the heart's natural pacemaker, a word that means "something that sets the pace." It keeps the beat steady whether you are sleeping, reading, or sprinting across a playground. You can help your heart stay strong through healthy habits. Exercise makes the heart muscle stronger, just as shooting baskets makes your arms stronger. A stronger heart pumps more blood with each beat, so it does not have to beat as often. That is why athletes often have slower resting heart rates than people who do not exercise. Eating fruits, vegetables, and whole grains gives the heart the fuel it needs to keep working well.

prompt._: According to the section "The Beat Goes On," what is the meaning of the word pacemaker?

choices.text: Something that wakes the brain
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the heart works without help from the brain, so pacemaker has nothing to do with waking the brain.

choices.text: Something that sets the pace
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that pacemaker means 'something that sets the pace.'

choices.text: Something that sends the energy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sending electrical signals is what the pacemaker does, but it is not the definition of the word pacemaker itself.

choices.text: Something that pumps the blood
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pumping blood is what the whole heart does, not the specific meaning of pacemaker given in the passage.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the difference between the atria and the valves?

choices.text: The atria make the heartbeat sound, but the valves send out small bursts of energy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'lub-dub' heartbeat sounds come from the valves closing, and the electrical signals come from the pacemaker cells, not the valves.

choices.text: The atria pump blood to the lungs, but the valves pump blood to the rest of the body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ventricles — not the atria — are the chambers that pump blood to the lungs and to the rest of the body.

choices.text: The atria carry blood away from the heart, but the valves carry blood back to the heart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because arteries carry blood away from the heart and veins carry blood back — that is the job of blood vessels, not the atria or valves.

choices.text: The atria hold blood for a short time, but the valves stop blood from flowing backward.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly says atria are like waiting rooms where blood pauses, and valves are one-way doors that stop blood from flowing backward.

prompt._: According to the article, why does a stronger heart not have to beat as often?

choices.text: because a stronger heart produces more blood in the bone marrow
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article explains that blood is made in bone marrow inside your bones, not by the heart.

choices.text: because a stronger heart pumps more blood with each beat
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article states directly that a stronger heart pumps more blood with each beat, so it doesn't need to beat as frequently.

choices.text: because a stronger heart sends electrical signals less frequently
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never connects heart strength to how often electrical signals are sent — those signals just keep a steady rhythm.

choices.text: because a stronger heart has larger valves that open more widely
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says anything about stronger hearts having larger valves — valves are only described as one-way doors that prevent blood from flowing backward.

prompt._: Based on the article, which sequence correctly describes what happens to blood after the left ventricle pumps it out to the body?

choices.text: It travels through capillaries, then moves into arteries, then returns through veins.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because capillaries are the tiny branches that come after arteries, not before them.

choices.text: It travels through veins, then moves into capillaries, then returns through arteries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blood travels through arteries first after leaving the heart, not veins — veins are the return path at the end of the journey.

choices.text: It travels through arteries, then moves into veins, then returns through capillaries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blood does not go from arteries straight into veins — it must pass through the tiny capillaries in between to deliver oxygen and nutrients to your cells.

choices.text: It travels through arteries, then moves into capillaries, then returns through veins.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states blood leaves through arteries, branches into capillaries for delivery, and then flows into veins to return to the heart.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are the left ventricle and the arteries similar?

choices.text: Both filter waste products out of the moving blood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because filtering waste products is the job of the kidneys, not the left ventricle or the arteries.

choices.text: Both move oxygen-rich blood away from the heart.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that the left ventricle pumps oxygen-rich blood out to the body, and arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart — both are moving fresh blood in the same direction.

choices.text: Both act as waiting rooms where the blood pauses.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because acting as a waiting room where blood pauses is the job of the atria, not the left ventricle or the arteries.

choices.text: Both collect tired blood returning from the body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because collecting tired blood returning from the body is the job of the right atrium, not the left ventricle or the arteries.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Heart: Your Body's Pump

assessments.title: Highways of Blood: Arteries, Veins, and Capillaries

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Three Roads Your Blood Travels

Sixty Thousand Miles Inside You

Press two fingers gently against the inside of your wrist. Wait. Feel that steady throbbing? That is blood surging through a tube no wider than a pencil, pushed by the force of your heartbeat. Right now, at this very second, your blood is racing through a network of tubes so long that if you stretched them all out in a line, they would reach about sixty thousand miles. That is enough to wrap around the Earth more than twice.

These tubes are called blood vessels — hollow passages that carry blood to every corner of your body. There are three types, and each one has a different job. Together, they form a continuous one-way loop. Blood does not slosh back and forth like water in a bathtub. Instead, it flows in a circle, always moving forward, always completing the same route again and again.
stimulus.content_text: The Three Roads Your Blood TravelsSixty Thousand Miles Inside You Press two fingers gently against the inside of your wrist. Wait. Feel that steady throbbing? That is blood surging through a tube no wider than a pencil, pushed by the force of your heartbeat. Right now, at this very second, your blood is racing through a network of tubes so long that if you stretched them all out in a line, they would reach about sixty thousand miles. That is enough to wrap around the Earth more than twice. These tubes are called blood vessels — hollow passages that carry blood to every corner of your body. There are three types, and each one has a different job. Together, they form a continuous one-way loop. Blood does not slosh back and forth like water in a bathtub. Instead, it flows in a circle, always moving forward, always completing the same route again and again.

prompt._: According to the article, how is the movement of blood inside blood vessels different from the movement of water in a bathtub?

choices.text: Blood stretches out in a long, straight line.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stretching sixty thousand miles in a line describes how long the blood vessels are, not how blood moves through them.

choices.text: Blood always moves forward in a circle.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that blood 'flows in a circle, always moving forward,' which is the opposite of water sloshing back and forth in a bathtub.

choices.text: Blood sloshes back and forth inside the tubes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says blood does NOT slosh back and forth — that's the bathtub comparison used to show what blood does NOT do.

choices.text: Blood throbs gently against the inside of a wrist.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because feeling a throb at your wrist describes your heartbeat, not the difference between how blood and bathtub water move.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Arteries: The Outbound Highway

The first type of blood vessel is the artery — a thick-walled tube that carries oxygen-rich blood away from the heart. Think of arteries as the outbound lanes of a highway, rushing supplies from a central warehouse to towns across the country. The heart pumps blood into arteries with serious force, so artery walls are thick and muscular to handle the pressure. That throbbing in your wrist? You are feeling blood push through an artery each time your heart beats.

The largest artery in your body is called the aorta, the first big tube blood enters after leaving the heart. The aorta is about as wide as a garden hose. It connects to a chamber called the left ventricle — the lower-left room of the heart that squeezes blood outward with the strongest push. From the aorta, blood branches into smaller and smaller arteries, the way a tree trunk splits into branches and then into twigs.
stimulus.content_text: Arteries: The Outbound Highway The first type of blood vessel is the artery — a thick-walled tube that carries oxygen-rich blood away from the heart. Think of arteries as the outbound lanes of a highway, rushing supplies from a central warehouse to towns across the country. The heart pumps blood into arteries with serious force, so artery walls are thick and muscular to handle the pressure. That throbbing in your wrist? You are feeling blood push through an artery each time your heart beats. The largest artery in your body is called the aorta, the first big tube blood enters after leaving the heart. The aorta is about as wide as a garden hose. It connects to a chamber called the left ventricle — the lower-left room of the heart that squeezes blood outward with the strongest push. From the aorta, blood branches into smaller and smaller arteries, the way a tree trunk splits into branches and then into twigs.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why are artery walls thick and muscular?

choices.text: to handle the strong pressure
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that artery walls are thick and muscular to handle the strong pressure created by the heart's pumping.

choices.text: to slow blood down near the heart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the heart pumps blood with serious force, meaning blood moves fast through arteries — it is not slowed down.

choices.text: to make blood rich in oxygen
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says arteries *carry* oxygen-rich blood, but the artery walls themselves do not make blood rich in oxygen.

choices.text: to help the left ventricle rest
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about artery walls helping the left ventricle rest — that idea is not in the passage at all.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Capillaries: Where the Real Work Happens

Those tiny twig-like branches lead to the second type of blood vessel: the capillary. A capillary is the smallest blood vessel in your body — so narrow that blood cells must squeeze through in single file, one cell at a time.

Despite their tiny size, capillaries do the most important work. Their walls are only one cell thick, thin enough for oxygen and nutrients — the useful substances from food that your cells need for energy and growth — to slip right through. Oxygen and nutrients pass out of the blood and into the body's cells. At the same time, waste products like carbon dioxide, a gas your cells produce when they use energy, pass from the cells back into the blood. This swap happens in seconds. Every tissue in your body depends on it to stay alive.
stimulus.content_text: Capillaries: Where the Real Work Happens Those tiny twig-like branches lead to the second type of blood vessel: the capillary. A capillary is the smallest blood vessel in your body — so narrow that blood cells must squeeze through in single file, one cell at a time. Despite their tiny size, capillaries do the most important work. Their walls are only one cell thick, thin enough for oxygen and nutrients — the useful substances from food that your cells need for energy and growth — to slip right through. Oxygen and nutrients pass out of the blood and into the body's cells. At the same time, waste products like carbon dioxide, a gas your cells produce when they use energy, pass from the cells back into the blood. This swap happens in seconds. Every tissue in your body depends on it to stay alive.

prompt._: According to the text, why are capillary walls only one cell thick?

choices.text: so waste products can be stored along the walls
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says waste products move through the capillary walls and into the blood to be carried away, not stored along the walls.

choices.text: so oxygen and nutrients can pass through them
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the walls are thin enough for oxygen and nutrients to 'slip right through' into the body's cells.

choices.text: so carbon dioxide can stay trapped inside them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says carbon dioxide passes out through the walls and into the blood — it is not trapped inside.

choices.text: so blood cells can split into smaller pieces
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says blood cells squeeze through capillaries one at a time, but never mentions them splitting into smaller pieces.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Veins: The Return Trip

After blood drops off oxygen and picks up waste in the capillaries, it needs a ride home. That is the job of the third type of blood vessel: the vein. Veins carry oxygen-poor blood back toward the heart. Their walls are thinner than artery walls because blood inside veins flows at much lower pressure — more like a gentle stream than a fire hose.

But the blood inside your veins is actually dark red — veins only look bluish because of the way light passes through your skin.

Veins have a clever feature that arteries do not need. Tiny valves — small flaps that work like one-way gates — sit inside your veins. These valves open to let blood flow toward the heart, then snap shut to stop it from sliding backward. This matters most in your legs, where blood must climb upward against the pull of gravity.
stimulus.content_text: Veins: The Return Trip After blood drops off oxygen and picks up waste in the capillaries, it needs a ride home. That is the job of the third type of blood vessel: the vein. Veins carry oxygen-poor blood back toward the heart. Their walls are thinner than artery walls because blood inside veins flows at much lower pressure — more like a gentle stream than a fire hose. But the blood inside your veins is actually dark red — veins only look bluish because of the way light passes through your skin. Veins have a clever feature that arteries do not need. Tiny valves — small flaps that work like one-way gates — sit inside your veins. These valves open to let blood flow toward the heart, then snap shut to stop it from sliding backward. This matters most in your legs, where blood must climb upward against the pull of gravity.

prompt._: Read the sentence from the article: 'Their walls are thinner than artery walls because blood inside veins flows at much lower pressure — more like a gentle stream than a fire hose.' What does the comparison 'more like a gentle stream than a fire hose' help the reader understand about blood in veins?

choices.text: that blood in veins is cooler than blood in arteries
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because neither a stream nor a fire hose has anything to do with temperature, and the passage never mentions blood being cooler in veins.

choices.text: that blood in veins carries more water than other blood
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because streams and fire hoses are being used to describe how forceful the flow is, not what the water (or blood) is made of.

choices.text: that blood in veins flows in only one direction at a time
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because flowing in one direction is about valves, which the passage talks about separately — the stream vs. fire hose comparison is only about pressure and speed.

choices.text: that blood in veins moves slowly and with less force
choices.feedback: This is correct because a 'gentle stream' means slow and low-force movement, and the passage says veins have 'much lower pressure' — both clues point to blood moving calmly and gently.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Completing the Loop

Once veins deliver oxygen-poor blood back to the heart, the journey is not over. The heart sends that used blood to the lungs through a special vessel called the pulmonary artery. Here is the surprising part: even though it is called an artery, the pulmonary artery carries oxygen-poor blood, not oxygen-rich blood. In the lungs, blood drops off its carbon dioxide waste — which you breathe out — and picks up a fresh load of oxygen from the air you breathe in. Then oxygen-rich blood returns to the heart through the pulmonary vein, another exception to the usual rule. The heart pumps that refreshed blood out through the aorta, and the whole loop starts again.

Arteries rush blood outward. Capillaries make the exchange. Veins return the used blood. The lungs reload the supply. Every organ, every muscle, every single cell gets what it needs because these three types of vessels keep the loop running.

You are feeling the first push of a sixty-thousand-mile journey that your blood will complete in about one minute — and then start all over again.

Then oxygen-rich blood returns to the heart through the pulmonary vein, another exception to the usual rule. The lungs reload the supply. Every organ, every muscle, every single cell gets what it needs because these three types of vessels keep the loop running. You are feeling the first push of a sixty-thousand-mile journey that your blood will complete in about one minute — and then start all over again.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the pulmonary artery?

choices.text: a vessel that carries oxygen-poor blood to the lungs
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the pulmonary artery carries oxygen-poor blood from the heart to the lungs.

choices.text: a vessel that moves oxygen-rich blood out through the body
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because moving oxygen-rich blood out through the body is the job of the aorta, not the pulmonary artery.

choices.text: a vessel that lets blood trade gases with body cells
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because exchanging gases with body cells happens in the capillaries, not in the pulmonary artery.

choices.text: a vessel that brings oxygen-rich blood back to the heart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the pulmonary artery carries oxygen-poor blood away from the heart, not oxygen-rich blood back to it — that's the job of the pulmonary vein.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the blood inside the aorta different from the blood inside the pulmonary artery?

choices.text: The aorta carries blood toward the heart, but the pulmonary artery carries it away.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the aorta carries blood away from the heart, not toward it — that return trip is the job of veins.

choices.text: The aorta moves blood very slowly, but the pulmonary artery moves it with force.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the heart pumps blood into arteries with serious force, so the aorta moves blood powerfully, not slowly.

choices.text: The aorta contains carbon dioxide waste, but the pulmonary artery contains fresh nutrients.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the aorta carries oxygen-rich blood, not carbon dioxide waste, and nutrients are delivered through capillaries, not the aorta or pulmonary artery.

choices.text: The aorta carries oxygen-rich blood, but the pulmonary artery carries oxygen-poor blood.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states the aorta carries oxygen-rich blood out from the heart, while the pulmonary artery — surprisingly — carries oxygen-poor blood to the lungs.

prompt._: Based on information from different parts of the article, why must blood travel through the lungs before the heart can pump it back out through the arteries?

choices.text: because the heart needs to rest before it can pump blood a second time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article describes the heart as constantly pumping in a continuous loop, completing the journey in about one minute, with no mention of it needing to rest.

choices.text: because veins cannot push blood hard enough to reach the body's cells
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that veins successfully carry blood back to the heart — the issue is that the blood is oxygen-poor, not that the veins lack pushing power.

choices.text: because the lungs add nutrients from food that body cells need for energy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the lungs add oxygen from the air you breathe, not nutrients from food — nutrients come from what you eat and are delivered through the blood to body cells.

choices.text: because capillaries used up the blood's oxygen and it must be reloaded
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the capillaries use up the blood's oxygen delivering it to body cells, so blood must stop at the lungs to reload a fresh supply of oxygen before the heart pumps it out again.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information in the sections about arteries, capillaries, and veins?

choices.text: By describing the problems each vessel faces and how the body solves them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author describes what each vessel does, not problems they face and how the body fixes them.

choices.text: By explaining what causes the heart to beat and how it moves blood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sections focus on the three types of blood vessels and their roles, not on what causes the heart to beat.

choices.text: By comparing the sizes of the vessels from the largest to the smallest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while size is mentioned (like capillaries being the smallest), the sections are organized by the order blood travels, not by vessel size.

choices.text: By following the path that blood takes as it travels through the body.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because each section follows blood in order — out through arteries, through capillaries for the exchange, and back through veins — matching the actual path blood takes.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the jobs of arteries, capillaries, and veins to show the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Arteries, capillaries, and veins each do a different job to keep blood moving in one complete loop.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that each vessel does a different job — carrying blood out, making the exchange, and returning blood — to keep one complete loop going.

choices.text: Arteries, capillaries, and veins are mostly alike because they all send oxygen-rich blood away from the heart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart; veins carry oxygen-poor blood in the opposite direction.

choices.text: Arteries, capillaries, and veins are different sizes because only the smallest vessels matter to the body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows that all three types of vessels are important to the body, not just the smallest ones.

choices.text: Arteries, capillaries, and veins all carry the same blood for the same purpose in every part of the loop.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly shows that arteries, capillaries, and veins each have different jobs, not the same job.

assessments.title: What Is Blood Made Of?

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Circulatory System

A Whole World in One Drop

A single drop of blood sits on your scraped knee — tiny, red, and ordinary-looking. But squeeze that drop under a powerful microscope, and you would find millions of living cells floating in a golden river of liquid. It is a mixture of four different parts, each with its own job, all traveling through a network of tubes that loops around your entire body and back again.
stimulus.content_text: The Circulatory SystemA Whole World in One Drop A single drop of blood sits on your scraped knee — tiny, red, and ordinary-looking. But squeeze that drop under a powerful microscope, and you would find millions of living cells floating in a golden river of liquid. It is a mixture of four different parts, each with its own job, all traveling through a network of tubes that loops around your entire body and back again.

prompt._: According to the passage, how does a drop of blood look different from what is actually inside it?

choices.text: It appears mixed and colorful but contains just a simple liquid
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes blood as having many complex parts inside, including living cells and four different components, not just a simple liquid.

choices.text: It appears golden and flowing but contains red and solid pieces
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the golden liquid is found inside the blood under a microscope, not what blood looks like from the outside.

choices.text: It appears tiny and ordinary but contains many parts with different jobs
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says blood looks tiny and ordinary on the outside, but inside it has millions of cells and four different parts with different jobs.

choices.text: It appears red and ordinary but contains only one kind of cell
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states blood has four different parts inside it, not just one kind of cell.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Loop That Never Stops

Your circulatory system — the body's transportation network — works like a giant loop of highways. At the center sits your heart, a muscular pump about the size of your fist. Every time it beats, it pushes blood outward through tubes called arteries, which are like wide highways leading away from the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller paths until they become capillaries — blood vessels so thin that only one tiny cell can squeeze through at a time. In those capillaries, blood drops off oxygen and picks up waste. Then it flows into veins, the return highways that carry blood back to the heart. The heart pumps that returning blood to the lungs, where it picks up fresh oxygen, and the loop begins again.
stimulus.content_text: The Loop That Never Stops Your circulatory system — the body's transportation network — works like a giant loop of highways. At the center sits your heart, a muscular pump about the size of your fist. Every time it beats, it pushes blood outward through tubes called arteries, which are like wide highways leading away from the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller paths until they become capillaries — blood vessels so thin that only one tiny cell can squeeze through at a time. In those capillaries, blood drops off oxygen and picks up waste. Then it flows into veins, the return highways that carry blood back to the heart. The heart pumps that returning blood to the lungs, where it picks up fresh oxygen, and the loop begins again.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what causes blood to move outward through the arteries?

choices.text: The beating of the heart
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that every time the heart beats, it pushes blood outward through the arteries.

choices.text: The branching of the paths
choices.feedback: The branching of paths describes how arteries get smaller over time, but branching doesn't cause the blood to move — the heartbeat does.

choices.text: The dropping off of waste
choices.feedback: Dropping off waste happens later in the capillaries, not in the arteries, and it doesn't cause blood to move outward.

choices.text: The squeezing of the cells
choices.feedback: Cells squeezing through capillaries describes what happens in the tiniest blood vessels, not what pushes blood through the arteries.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Red Blood Cells and Plasma: Delivering Oxygen and Carrying the Load

The most common cells in your blood are red blood cells. Millions of them fill a single drop. Each one is shaped like a tiny disc with a dent in the middle — picture a doughnut that never got its hole punched out. Their job is to carry oxygen from your lungs to every cell in your body. They do this using a protein called hemoglobin, a molecule that works like a tiny suitcase built to hold oxygen. Hemoglobin grabs oxygen molecules inside the lungs, holds them tight during the ride through arteries and capillaries, and then releases them wherever cells need fuel.

Your heart pumps blood but does not add oxygen — the lungs are the refueling station. Hemoglobin contains iron, and that iron is what makes blood red. When hemoglobin is loaded with oxygen, blood turns bright cherry red. When it has delivered its oxygen, blood becomes a darker shade. You might think blood in your veins is blue, since veins look bluish through your skin. Actually, blood is never blue — veins appear that color because of the way light passes through skin. Eating iron-rich foods like spinach and beans helps your body build strong hemoglobin.

More than half of your blood is plasma, a yellowish liquid made mostly of water. Plasma is the river that carries everything else along. Nutrients from digested food, hormones — chemical messages sent between organs, like tiny text messages telling your body what to do — and waste products all travel dissolved in plasma. It also carries the clotting proteins that help platelets seal wounds and the antibodies that fight disease.

Eating iron-rich foods like spinach and beans helps your body build strong hemoglobin. More than half of your blood is plasma, a yellowish liquid made mostly of water. Plasma is the river that carries everything else along. Nutrients from digested food, hormones — chemical messages sent between organs, like tiny text messages telling your body what to do — and waste products all travel dissolved in plasma. It also carries the clotting proteins that help platelets seal wounds and the antibodies that fight disease.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after hemoglobin grabs oxygen in the lungs?

choices.text: It carries oxygen through the heart and releases it in the lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hemoglobin releases oxygen to cells throughout the body, not back in the lungs where it started.

choices.text: It carries oxygen through the veins and turns the blood blue.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that blood is never blue — veins just look bluish because of the way light passes through your skin.

choices.text: It carries oxygen through the plasma and makes the heart add oxygen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the heart pumps blood but does not add oxygen — only the lungs refuel the blood with oxygen.

choices.text: It carries oxygen through the blood and releases it to cells.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says hemoglobin grabs oxygen in the lungs, travels through arteries and capillaries, and releases oxygen wherever cells need fuel.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
White Blood Cells and Platelets: Defenders and Repair Crew

White blood cells are far less common than red ones, but they keep you alive in a different way. They are your body's defenders. When bacteria, viruses, or other harmful invaders sneak inside you, white blood cells rush to meet them. Some surround a germ and swallow it whole, digesting it the way your stomach breaks down food. Others produce proteins called antibodies — sticky markers that latch onto invaders and flag them for destruction, like putting a bright sticker on a target. When you catch a cold and your body fights it off, white blood cells are doing that hard work.

Platelets are not full cells. They are tiny cell fragments, much smaller than red or white blood cells. Think of them as an emergency repair crew always on standby. When you get a cut, platelets race to the wound and stick together, forming a plug over the opening. Then they release chemicals that start a chain reaction. Proteins in the blood weave a net of tough fibers over the plug, building a clot — a hardened patch that becomes the scab protecting your wound while new skin grows underneath. Without platelets, even a small scrape could cause dangerous blood loss.

They are your body's defenders. When bacteria, viruses, or other harmful invaders sneak inside you, white blood cells rush to meet them. Some surround a germ and swallow it whole, digesting it the way your stomach breaks down food. Others produce proteins called antibodies — sticky markers that latch onto invaders and flag them for destruction, like putting a bright sticker on a target. When you catch a cold and your body fights it off, white blood cells are doing that hard work. Platelets are not full cells. They are tiny cell fragments, much smaller than red or white blood cells. Think of them as an emergency repair crew always on standby. When you get a cut, platelets race to the wound and stick together, forming a plug over the opening. Then they release chemicals that start a chain reaction. Proteins in the blood weave a net of tough fibers over the plug, building a clot — a hardened patch that becomes the scab protecting your wound while new skin grows underneath. Without platelets, even a small scrape could cause dangerous blood loss.

prompt._: To explain the meaning of the word "antibodies," the author compares them to "bright stickers." Based on the text, how are they similar?

choices.text: They both mark a target for destruction.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says antibodies 'flag' invaders for destruction, just like a bright sticker marks a target.

choices.text: They both swallow up harmful invaders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because swallowing invaders whole is what certain white blood cells do, not what antibodies do.

choices.text: They both cover up germs to hide them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because antibodies mark invaders to be found and destroyed — they don't hide or cover them up.

choices.text: They both fix cuts before new skin can grow.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because covering cuts and helping new skin grow is the job of platelets, which is a completely different part of the blood.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
One Team, One Mission

Red blood cells ride it to deliver oxygen and haul away carbon dioxide waste. Platelets stand ready to patch any leak. Your heart pumps this mixture through about 60,000 miles of blood vessels — enough to wrap around Earth more than twice. All of it happens without you thinking a single thought about it. Right now, as your eyes move across these words, your blood is completing that entire loop in less than a minute. You are not just reading about the circulatory system. You are running one.
stimulus.content_text: One Team, One Mission Red blood cells ride it to deliver oxygen and haul away carbon dioxide waste. Platelets stand ready to patch any leak. Your heart pumps this mixture through about 60,000 miles of blood vessels — enough to wrap around Earth more than twice. All of it happens without you thinking a single thought about it. Right now, as your eyes move across these words, your blood is completing that entire loop in less than a minute. You are not just reading about the circulatory system. You are running one.

prompt._: According to the text, what are platelets?

choices.text: cells that carry oxygen
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says red blood cells carry oxygen, not platelets.

choices.text: tubes that move blood
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes blood vessels as the tubes that blood travels through, not platelets.

choices.text: muscles that pump blood
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the heart is what pumps blood through the body.

choices.text: parts that patch leaks
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 'Platelets stand ready to patch any leak.'

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between red blood cells and platelets?

choices.text: Red blood cells return blood to the heart, but platelets send blood to the lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither red blood cells nor platelets return blood to the heart or send it to the lungs — veins and the heart do that.

choices.text: Red blood cells fight germs, but platelets carry oxygen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because red blood cells carry oxygen, not fight germs — that's the job of white blood cells.

choices.text: Red blood cells carry hormones, but platelets make antibodies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because red blood cells carry oxygen (not hormones), and antibodies are made by white blood cells, not platelets.

choices.text: Red blood cells deliver oxygen, but platelets help seal cuts.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says red blood cells deliver oxygen and platelets seal cuts, showing a clear contrast between their jobs.

prompt._: Based on the article, what causes a protective scab to form when you get a cut?

choices.text: Platelets form a plug and plasma proteins weave a tough net.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says platelets form a plug and then blood proteins weave a tough fiber net over it, creating the clot that becomes your scab.

choices.text: The heart pumps faster and wide arteries wash away the germs.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never says the heart pumping faster or arteries washing away germs plays any role in forming a scab.

choices.text: White blood cells swallow germs and antibodies build a patch.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — white blood cells and antibodies are your body's germ fighters, not the team responsible for sealing cuts and forming scabs.

choices.text: Red blood cells drop off oxygen and hemoglobin grows new skin.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — red blood cells and hemoglobin carry oxygen around your body; the passage never says they grow new skin.

prompt._: According to the article, what happens right after blood drops off oxygen in the capillaries?

choices.text: It moves into arteries heading away from the heart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because arteries carry blood away from the heart, but at this point in the loop, blood is heading back toward the heart, not away from it.

choices.text: It flows into veins and travels back to the heart.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that after blood drops off oxygen in the capillaries, 'it flows into veins, the return highways that carry blood back to the heart.'

choices.text: It picks up oxygen again inside the lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because picking up fresh oxygen in the lungs happens after the blood has already returned to the heart, not right after leaving the capillaries.

choices.text: It forms a clot to protect the body from harm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because clot formation is something platelets do when you get a cut or injury, and has nothing to do with the normal step-by-step journey of blood through the circulatory system.

prompt._: Which comparison best summarizes the main idea of the article?

choices.text: The circulatory system is like an emergency repair crew that focuses only on fixing cuts and skin scrapes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sealing cuts is only the job of platelets, and the article is about the entire circulatory system, not just one part of it.

choices.text: The circulatory system is like a golden river that uses white blood cells to wash away all harmful germs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while plasma is the yellowish liquid that carries things, it is white blood cells — not plasma — that fight germs, and they destroy invaders rather than 'wash them away.'

choices.text: The circulatory system is like a tiny suitcase that changes the color of blood from blue to bright red.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'tiny suitcase' is a detail about hemoglobin inside red blood cells, and the article clearly explains that blood is never actually blue.

choices.text: The circulatory system is like a highway network where different blood parts travel to do separate jobs.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares the circulatory system to a highway network and explains how all four parts of blood travel through it doing different jobs, which covers the whole article's main idea.

syllabus_metadata.title: What Is Blood Made Of?

assessments.title: Feel the Beat: Pulse and Heart Health

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Circulatory System

A Fist-Sized Powerhouse

Right now, a muscle the size of your fist is squeezing inside your chest — and it has not stopped once since before you were born. That muscle is your heart. It beats about 100,000 times every single day, pushing blood through a network of tubes that would stretch over 60,000 miles if you laid them end to end. That network is your circulatory system — the body's delivery route, carrying supplies to every cell and hauling waste away, the way a mail truck picks up and drops off packages on a loop through your neighborhood.
stimulus.content_text: The Circulatory SystemA Fist-Sized Powerhouse Right now, a muscle the size of your fist is squeezing inside your chest — and it has not stopped once since before you were born. That muscle is your heart. It beats about 100,000 times every single day, pushing blood through a network of tubes that would stretch over 60,000 miles if you laid them end to end. That network is your circulatory system — the body's delivery route, carrying supplies to every cell and hauling waste away, the way a mail truck picks up and drops off packages on a loop through your neighborhood.

prompt._: According to the text, which contrast is true about the heart and the circulatory system?

choices.text: The heart is a network of tubes, but the circulatory system is a fist-sized muscle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the definitions switched — the passage says the heart is the fist-sized muscle and the circulatory system is the network of tubes, not the other way around.

choices.text: The heart carries supplies to every cell, but the circulatory system beats 100,000 times a day.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the jobs — the passage says the circulatory system carries supplies to cells, and the heart is what beats 100,000 times a day.

choices.text: The heart stretches over 60,000 miles, but the circulatory system squeezes inside your chest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it swaps the facts — the passage says the tubes stretch over 60,000 miles and the heart is what squeezes inside your chest.

choices.text: The heart is a fist-sized muscle, but the circulatory system is a network of tubes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the heart is 'a muscle the size of your fist' and the circulatory system is 'a network of tubes,' which matches this answer perfectly.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Loop Your Blood Travels

Your blood follows a giant loop. It starts in the heart, flows out to the body, and circles back again. The heart squeezes and pushes oxygen-rich blood into thick, stretchy tubes called arteries — blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller paths until they become capillaries, tiny vessels so thin that oxygen and nutrients slip right through their walls and into your cells.

Once your cells grab the oxygen they need, they hand off carbon dioxide and other waste. The blood, now low on oxygen, flows into veins — blood vessels that carry blood back toward the heart.

You might think blood in veins is blue because the veins you see through your skin look bluish. Actually, blood is always red. It is bright red when full of oxygen and darker red when it carries less. The bluish color you see is just a trick of light passing through your skin.
stimulus.content_text: The Loop Your Blood Travels Your blood follows a giant loop. It starts in the heart, flows out to the body, and circles back again. The heart squeezes and pushes oxygen-rich blood into thick, stretchy tubes called arteries — blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller paths until they become capillaries, tiny vessels so thin that oxygen and nutrients slip right through their walls and into your cells. Once your cells grab the oxygen they need, they hand off carbon dioxide and other waste. The blood, now low on oxygen, flows into veins — blood vessels that carry blood back toward the heart. You might think blood in veins is blue because the veins you see through your skin look bluish. Actually, blood is always red. It is bright red when full of oxygen and darker red when it carries less. The bluish color you see is just a trick of light passing through your skin.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why are capillaries so thin?

choices.text: so they can carry blood back toward the heart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carrying blood back toward the heart is the job of veins, not capillaries.

choices.text: so they can change blood from red to blue
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that blood is always red and never actually changes to blue.

choices.text: so oxygen and nutrients can pass through their walls
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says capillaries are so thin that oxygen and nutrients can slip right through their walls into your cells.

choices.text: so they can push blood with a strong squeeze
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because squeezing and pushing blood is the heart's job, not something capillaries do.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Muscle That Never Quits

Your heart is made of a special kind of muscle called cardiac muscle — muscle tissue found only in the heart. After a long run, your leg muscles burn and beg you to stop. Cardiac muscle never does that. It contracts and relaxes every second of your life without getting tired. No other muscle in your body can do that. If you place your hand on your chest, you can feel your heart beating slightly to the left of center. It feels stronger on the left because that side of the heart is a bit larger and pumps with more force.

After a long run, your leg muscles burn and beg you to stop. Cardiac muscle never does that. It contracts and relaxes every second of your life without getting tired. No other muscle in your body can do that. If you place your hand on your chest, you can feel your heart beating slightly to the left of center. It feels stronger on the left because that side of the heart is a bit larger and pumps with more force.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens to cardiac muscle after it contracts?

choices.text: It relaxes and contracts again
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says cardiac muscle 'contracts and relaxes every second of your life,' meaning it relaxes after contracting and then contracts again.

choices.text: It gets tired and stops working
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states that cardiac muscle never gets tired — it keeps working every second of your life.

choices.text: It burns like leg muscles do
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says leg muscles burn after a run, but cardiac muscle never does that.

choices.text: It pumps only on the left side
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the left side detail is about where you feel your heartbeat on your chest, not about what happens after cardiac muscle contracts.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Feeling the Beat

Every time your heart squeezes, it sends a wave of blood rushing through your arteries. You can feel that wave as your pulse — a small, rhythmic throb at certain spots on your body. The easiest place to find it is on the inside of your wrist, just below the base of your thumb. Press your index and middle fingers gently on that spot. You should feel a soft, steady beat. You can also find your pulse on the side of your neck, just below the jawbone.

To measure your heart rate — the number of times your heart beats in one minute — count the beats you feel in fifteen seconds. Then multiply by four. A typical resting heart rate for kids ages six to fifteen falls between seventy and one hundred beats per minute. Adults usually land between sixty and one hundred.

You can feel that wave as your pulse — a small, rhythmic throb at certain spots on your body. The easiest place to find it is on the inside of your wrist, just below the base of your thumb. Press your index and middle fingers gently on that spot. You should feel a soft, steady beat. You can also find your pulse on the side of your neck, just below the jawbone. To measure your heart rate — the number of times your heart beats in one minute — count the beats you feel in fifteen seconds. Then multiply by four. A typical resting heart rate for kids ages six to fifteen falls between seventy and one hundred beats per minute. Adults usually land between sixty and one hundred.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how does a typical resting heart rate for kids compare to a typical resting heart rate for adults?

choices.text: Adults have a higher maximum resting heart rate than kids do.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both kids and adults share the same maximum of 100 beats per minute, so adults do not have a higher maximum.

choices.text: Kids have a higher minimum resting heart rate than adults do.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows kids' lowest heart rate is 70, which is higher than adults' lowest heart rate of 60.

choices.text: Kids and adults have the exact same resting heart rate range.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ranges are not exactly the same — kids start at 70 beats per minute while adults start at 60.

choices.text: Kids have a lower minimum resting heart rate than adults do.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says kids have a LOWER minimum than adults, but it's actually the opposite — kids' minimum of 70 is higher than adults' minimum of 60.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Your Heart Speeds Up

When you sit quietly reading, your heart keeps a calm, steady pace. But the moment you sprint across the playground, your muscles demand more oxygen. Your heart responds by beating faster and harder, pumping extra blood to those working muscles. That is why you feel your heart pounding after a race. Once you stop and rest, it gradually slows back down.

Regular exercise makes your heart stronger, the same way practice makes your arms stronger for monkey bars. A stronger heart pumps more blood with each beat, so it does not need to beat as often. People who exercise regularly tend to have lower resting heart rates. A well-trained athlete might rest at just forty or fifty beats per minute — delivering the same amount of blood with fewer squeezes.

Your heart responds by beating faster and harder, pumping extra blood to those working muscles. That is why you feel your heart pounding after a race. Once you stop and rest, it gradually slows back down. Regular exercise makes your heart stronger, the same way practice makes your arms stronger for monkey bars. A stronger heart pumps more blood with each beat, so it does not need to beat as often. People who exercise regularly tend to have lower resting heart rates. A well-trained athlete might rest at just forty or fifty beats per minute — delivering the same amount of blood with fewer squeezes.

prompt._: According to the text, what does it mean when an athlete's heart rests at forty or fifty beats per minute?

choices.text: It pumps an extra amount of blood to working muscles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pumping extra blood to working muscles describes what your heart does when you are running or exercising, not when it is resting.

choices.text: It maintains a calm and steady pace while sitting quietly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sitting quietly and having a calm heart applies to everyone at rest, and it does not explain what is special about an athlete's low resting heart rate.

choices.text: It demands a much larger amount of oxygen for muscles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because demanding more oxygen is something muscles do during exercise, not something a resting heart rate tells us about.

choices.text: It delivers the same amount of blood with fewer squeezes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that a well-trained athlete delivers 'the same amount of blood with fewer squeezes,' which is exactly what a low resting heart rate means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Keeping the System Running

Besides exercise, other habits help your circulatory system stay healthy. Eating fruits, vegetables, and whole grains gives your heart and blood vessels the nutrients they need. Getting enough sleep lets the heart rest and repair. Avoiding junk food loaded with salt and unhealthy fats helps keep blood vessels clear and flexible.

You can see your circulatory system respond to these habits in real time. Try measuring your pulse while sitting still. Then do jumping jacks for one minute and measure again. Sit down, rest for a few minutes, and measure a third time. That is your circulatory system adjusting in real time — a network of heart, blood, and vessels working together every second to keep you alive, whether you are sleeping, reading, or racing your fastest friend to the fence.

That is your circulatory system adjusting in real time — a network of heart, blood, and vessels working together every second to keep you alive, whether you are sleeping, reading, or racing your fastest friend to the fence.

prompt._: How does the author support the point that habits besides exercise keep the circulatory system healthy?

choices.text: By mentioning how you can race your fastest friend to a fence
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because racing a friend is just a fun example used to describe everyday activities, not a healthy habit the author is recommending.

choices.text: By showing how doing jumping jacks changes your heart rate
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because jumping jacks are exercise, and the question asks about habits *besides* exercise.

choices.text: By describing how to measure your pulse while sitting still
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because measuring your pulse is a way to observe your heart rate, not a habit that keeps your circulatory system healthy.

choices.text: By explaining that sleep lets the heart rest and repair
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that sleep helps the heart rest and repair, which is a non-exercise habit that supports circulatory health.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the blood flow in your arteries different from the blood flow in your veins?

choices.text: Blood in arteries flows back to the heart in a steady stream, while blood in veins flows away from the heart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the directions switched — arteries carry blood away from the heart, and veins carry blood back to the heart, not the other way around.

choices.text: Blood in arteries flows away from the heart in pulsing waves, while blood in veins flows back to the heart.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says arteries carry blood away from the heart in waves you feel as your pulse, while veins carry blood back to the heart.

choices.text: Blood in arteries carries waste away from the body's cells, while blood in veins delivers fresh oxygen to the cells.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the jobs — arteries deliver oxygen-rich blood to cells, while veins carry the waste (like carbon dioxide) back toward the heart.

choices.text: Blood in arteries flows smoothly without a noticeable beat, while blood in veins flows in strong waves you can feel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the pulsing waves happen in the arteries, which is exactly what you feel when you check your pulse on your wrist or neck.

prompt._: Based on the article, what causes your pulse to beat faster and harder after you run?

choices.text: Your muscles need more oxygen, so your heart pumps extra blood into your arteries.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that sprinting muscles demand more oxygen, so the heart beats faster and harder to pump extra blood to them.

choices.text: Your blood vessels become blocked with salt, so your heart has to squeeze much harder.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blocked blood vessels from salt and unhealthy fats is a long-term health problem, not the reason your heart speeds up when you run.

choices.text: Your blood loses its oxygen, so your veins carry it back to the heart faster.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says veins speed up to cause your pulse to race — that's not how the circulatory system responds to exercise.

choices.text: Your heart gets tired from beating, so your cardiac muscle needs to rest and repair.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly explains that cardiac muscle never gets tired, which is what makes it different from all other muscles in your body.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information in 'Why Your Heart Speeds Up' and 'Keeping the System Running' to show how the circulatory system reacts to activity?

choices.text: By detailing the sequence of events from resting to exercising and back to resting
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author follows a clear sequence: resting heart → heart speeds up during exercise → heart slows back down, and even asks you to repeat that sequence yourself with the jumping jacks activity.

choices.text: By describing the cause of a strong heart and its effect on the body's blood vessels
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sections are about how activity causes your heart rate to change, not about how a strong heart affects your blood vessels specifically.

choices.text: By stating that it travels in a giant loop like a mail truck and stops working when the body rests.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the mail truck comparison describes how blood travels, not the heart's power, and the passage clearly states the heart never stops working.

syllabus_metadata.title: Feel the Beat: Pulse and Heart Health

assessments.title: Breathing In: The Air We Need

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Respiratory System

How Breathing Starts: The Diaphragm and Your Nose

Put your hand flat against your ribcage, right below your chest. Now take a deep breath. Feel that? Your ribs pushed outward, and your belly expanded. A dome-shaped muscle called the diaphragm just pulled downward inside you, stretching your chest cavity open like someone pulling the bottom of a balloon. That motion created extra space in your chest, and air rushed in to fill it. Your lungs did not suck the air in on their own. They can't — lungs have no muscles. The diaphragm does all the heavy lifting, contracting and flattening about twelve to twenty times every minute, every hour, every day of your life.

Air's journey begins at your nose. When you inhale through your nostrils, the air passes over tiny hairs called cilia — picture rows of miniature brooms sweeping the hallway before a guest arrives. These hairs trap dust, pollen, and other floating particles so they never reach your lungs. A layer of sticky mucus, a thick fluid like the slime on a snail's trail, coats the inside of your nose and catches even smaller bits that the cilia miss.

Your nose also warms and moistens the air. Cold, dry air would sting the soft tissues deeper inside your body, so the nose heats each breath like a tiny radiator before sending it along. You can breathe through your mouth, too, but your mouth doesn't filter or warm the air nearly as well.

prompt._: According to the text, how is breathing through the nose different from breathing through the mouth?

choices.text: The mouth traps dust with cilia but the nose uses only mucus
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has things backwards — the passage says the nose has cilia and mucus, not the mouth.

choices.text: The mouth warms the air more quickly but the nose cools it down
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the nose warms the air to protect your body, and never describes the mouth as a better or faster warmer.

choices.text: The nose sends air to the diaphragm but the mouth does not
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the mouth bypasses the diaphragm — both nose and mouth breathing still involves the diaphragm doing the work.

choices.text: The nose filters and warms the air better than the mouth does
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the nose uses cilia and mucus to filter air and acts like a tiny radiator to warm it, and that the mouth doesn't do these things nearly as well.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Path Air Travels: From the Windpipe to the Lungs

After leaving the nose, air enters the throat and passes through the larynx, or voice box — the part of your throat that vibrates to let you talk, sing, and shout. From there, air moves into a tube called the trachea, also known as the windpipe. The trachea is about four to five inches long and sits right in front of the esophagus, the separate tube that carries food to your stomach.

Run your fingers slowly down the front of your neck. Feel those small bumps? Those are rings of cartilage, a tough but bendable tissue — the same springy material that shapes your ears. These rings wrap around the trachea like the plastic rings inside a vacuum hose, keeping the tube open so air flows freely. Without them, the trachea could collapse shut.

At the bottom of the trachea, the tube splits into two branches called bronchi. One bronchus leads to the right lung, and the other leads to the left. Inside the lungs, each bronchus divides again and again into smaller and smaller tubes called bronchioles. Picture an upside-down tree: the trachea is the trunk, the bronchi are the main branches, and the bronchioles are the tiniest twigs. This branching pattern spreads air into every corner of your lungs.

prompt._: Based on the text, why does the trachea have rings of cartilage around it?

choices.text: To carry food safely into the stomach
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carrying food to the stomach is the job of the esophagus, which is a separate tube from the trachea.

choices.text: To keep the windpipe open for airflow
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the cartilage rings keep the trachea open so air flows freely, and without them the trachea could collapse shut.

choices.text: To vibrate so a person can speak loudly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because vibrating to help you speak is the job of the larynx (voice box), not the cartilage rings around the trachea.

choices.text: To shape the outside of a person's ears
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage mentions that cartilage shapes your ears, it uses that only as a comparison — the rings around the trachea are there to keep the airway open, not to shape ears.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Where the Swap Happens: Alveoli and Gas Exchange

At the tips of the smallest bronchioles sit clusters of microscopic — too small to see — air sacs called alveoli. They look like bunches of tiny grapes, and your lungs hold about 300 million of them. This is where the real work of breathing pays off.

Oxygen from the air you inhaled passes through the paper-thin walls of the alveoli and slips into your blood. At the same time, carbon dioxide, a waste gas your cells produce, moves out of the blood and into the alveoli so you can breathe it out. Your cells need that oxygen to release energy from the food you eat — the way a campfire needs air to keep burning.

The air you exhale still carries enough oxygen to help someone else — which is exactly why mouth-to-mouth rescue breathing can save a life.

People often use the words "breathing" and "respiration" as if they mean the same thing. Actually, they describe two different steps. Breathing is the physical act — your diaphragm contracts, your chest expands, and air moves in and out. Respiration is the gas exchange that happens deep inside, at the alveoli and in your cells, where oxygen and carbon dioxide trade places. Breathing is what you can feel. Respiration is the invisible chemistry that keeps every cell in your body alive.

From the cilia in your nose to the 300 million alveoli in your lungs, each part of the respiratory system has a specific job — filtering, warming, channeling, delivering, exchanging. And beneath it all, the diaphragm keeps pulling, keeps pushing, breath after breath — even while you sleep. Your next breath has already started by the time you finish reading this sentence. That quiet, automatic rhythm is the engine running underneath everything else your body does.

choices.feedback: Oxygen doesn't move into the bronchioles — it has already traveled past the bronchioles to reach the alveoli, so it moves forward into the blood, not backward.

choices.feedback: Oxygen doesn't go back to the nose — once it passes through the alveoli walls, it enters the bloodstream to be carried to your cells.

choices.feedback: Oxygen doesn't change into carbon dioxide — carbon dioxide is a separate waste gas that moves out of the blood at the same time oxygen moves in.

choices.text: Breathing moves air in and out, but respiration swaps gases in the lungs and cells.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that breathing moves air in and out through the diaphragm, while respiration is the gas exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide happening at the alveoli and in the cells.

syllabus_metadata.title: Breathing In: The Air We Need

assessments.title: Gas Exchange: The Swap of Life

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Tiny Air Sacs Keeping You Alive

A Trade You Never Notice

In that time, your body pulled in fresh oxygen and dumped out a waste gas — and you didn't feel a thing.

Most people think breathing is simply about filling your lungs with air. But filling your lungs is just the delivery step. The real action happens when oxygen passes into your blood and a waste gas called carbon dioxide passes out. This swap is called gas exchange, and it takes place in some of the smallest structures in your entire body.
stimulus.content_text: The Tiny Air Sacs Keeping You AliveA Trade You Never Notice In that time, your body pulled in fresh oxygen and dumped out a waste gas — and you didn't feel a thing. Most people think breathing is simply about filling your lungs with air. But filling your lungs is just the delivery step. The real action happens when oxygen passes into your blood and a waste gas called carbon dioxide passes out. This swap is called gas exchange, and it takes place in some of the smallest structures in your entire body.

prompt._: The text contrasts what most people think about breathing with the "real action." According to the text, what is the "real action"?

choices.text: The lungs deliver air to the body and keep the carbon dioxide.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the lungs don't keep the carbon dioxide — the whole point is that carbon dioxide is released out of the body during gas exchange.

choices.text: Oxygen passes into the blood and carbon dioxide passes out.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses almost these exact words to describe the 'real action' — oxygen passes into the blood and carbon dioxide passes out.

choices.text: The lungs pull in waste gas and dump out the fresh oxygen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the gases backwards — oxygen passes into the blood and carbon dioxide passes out, not the other way around.

choices.text: The body feels the fresh air moving into small structures.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says you don't feel anything during gas exchange.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Air Gets In: The Diaphragm at Work

Before gas exchange can happen, air needs a way inside. That job belongs to a dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs called the diaphragm — think of it as a trampoline stretched under your rib cage. When you breathe in, the diaphragm pulls downward and flattens out. This creates extra space in your chest, and air rushes in to fill it, the same way air rushes into a balloon when you stretch it open.

The air travels down your trachea, the main airway in your throat, which works like a hallway leading into your lungs. The trachea splits into two smaller tubes called bronchi — one for each lung. Each bronchus then branches again and again into even tinier tubes called bronchioles, like a tree trunk splitting into thinner and thinner branches. At the very end of those branches sit the tiny air sacs where the real work begins.
stimulus.content_text: How Air Gets In: The Diaphragm at Work Before gas exchange can happen, air needs a way inside. That job belongs to a dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs called the diaphragm — think of it as a trampoline stretched under your rib cage. When you breathe in, the diaphragm pulls downward and flattens out. This creates extra space in your chest, and air rushes in to fill it, the same way air rushes into a balloon when you stretch it open. The air travels down your trachea, the main airway in your throat, which works like a hallway leading into your lungs. The trachea splits into two smaller tubes called bronchi — one for each lung. Each bronchus then branches again and again into even tinier tubes called bronchioles, like a tree trunk splitting into thinner and thinner branches. At the very end of those branches sit the tiny air sacs where the real work begins.

prompt._: Based on the section 'How Air Gets In: The Diaphragm at Work,' which key detail best supports the idea that the diaphragm causes air to enter the lungs?

choices.text: Tiny air sacs sit at the ends of the smallest branches
choices.feedback: The tiny air sacs are where gas exchange happens deep inside the lungs, not what causes air to enter the body.

choices.text: The trachea splits into two bronchi, one for each lung
choices.feedback: This tells us how air moves through the lungs after it enters, not what causes air to come in.

choices.text: The bronchi branch into smaller tubes called bronchioles
choices.feedback: Bronchioles are tubes inside the lungs that air travels through after it has already entered, so this doesn't explain how air gets in.

choices.text: The diaphragm flattens and creates extra space in the chest
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the diaphragm flattening creates extra chest space, which makes air rush in.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Three Hundred Million Tiny Balloons

Those air sacs are called alveoli, and your lungs hold about three hundred million of them. A single alveolus is so small you would need a microscope to see it clearly. But together, they add up to something enormous. If you could peel all your alveoli apart and lay them flat, they would cover an area about the size of a tennis court. That huge surface gives oxygen and carbon dioxide plenty of room to move.

Each alveolus is wrapped in a net of the thinnest blood vessels in your body, called capillaries. Picture a tiny grape covered in a web of thread — that's roughly what an alveolus and its capillaries look like. Both the alveolus wall and the capillary wall are only one cell thick. That extreme thinness matters because gases can slip through easily.
