If you could peel all your alveoli apart and lay them flat, they would cover an area about the size of a tennis court. That huge surface gives oxygen and carbon dioxide plenty of room to move. Each alveolus is wrapped in a net of the thinnest blood vessels in your body, called capillaries. Picture a tiny grape covered in a web of thread — that's roughly what an alveolus and its capillaries look like. Both the alveolus wall and the capillary wall are only one cell thick. That extreme thinness matters because gases can slip through easily.

prompt._: According to the passage, why can gases slip through the walls of the alveoli and capillaries easily?

choices.text: because the walls are covered in three hundred million sacs
choices.feedback: Three hundred million is the number of alveoli in your lungs, not a description of the walls or a reason gases move through them.

choices.text: because the walls are only one cell thick
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the walls are only one cell thick, and that extreme thinness is exactly why gases can pass through easily.

choices.text: because the walls are wrapped in a net of blood vessels
choices.feedback: Being wrapped in blood vessels describes what the alveolus looks like, but the passage never says that's why gases can slip through.

choices.text: because the walls are about the size of a tennis court
choices.feedback: The tennis court size describes how much surface area all the alveoli would cover if laid flat, not why gases can slip through the walls.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Swap: How Diffusion Works

You might think your lungs actively grab oxygen out of the air, the way your hand grabs a pencil. Actually, the swap happens all on its own through a process called diffusion — the natural movement of a substance from where there is a lot of it to where there is less of it. Imagine opening a bottle of perfume in the corner of a room. The scent spreads on its own until it fills the whole space. No fan needed.

But the blood arriving at the alveoli has almost no oxygen left because it just delivered its supply to cells all over your body. So oxygen flows naturally from the oxygen-rich air into the oxygen-poor blood. Carbon dioxide works the same way in reverse. The returning blood is loaded with carbon dioxide picked up from your cells, but the air inside the alveolus has very little. So carbon dioxide flows from the blood into the air sac — no pushing required.

prompt._: To help explain the meaning of the word diffusion, the author compares it to perfume because both...

choices.text: spread out naturally on their own.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says perfume spreads on its own with no fan needed, just like diffusion moves naturally without any pushing — both happen all by themselves.

choices.text: grab oxygen actively from the air.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says diffusion is NOT like actively grabbing — it happens on its own without any effort.

choices.text: move from areas of less to more.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because diffusion moves from where there is MORE of a substance to where there is LESS, which is the opposite of what this option says.

choices.text: require a fan to fill up the room.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states 'No fan needed,' meaning perfume spreads without any help from a fan.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Delivery Trucks in Your Blood

Oxygen doesn't travel through your blood alone. It hitches a ride on a protein inside red blood cells called hemoglobin. Hemoglobin works like a delivery truck: it picks up oxygen at the lungs, carries it through your arteries, and drops it off at cells in your muscles, brain, heart, and every other organ. When the delivery is done, the same blood picks up carbon dioxide waste and carries it back to the lungs.

It just has less oxygen and more carbon dioxide than the air you breathed in.
stimulus.content_text: Delivery Trucks in Your Blood Oxygen doesn't travel through your blood alone. It hitches a ride on a protein inside red blood cells called hemoglobin. Hemoglobin works like a delivery truck: it picks up oxygen at the lungs, carries it through your arteries, and drops it off at cells in your muscles, brain, heart, and every other organ. When the delivery is done, the same blood picks up carbon dioxide waste and carries it back to the lungs. It just has less oxygen and more carbon dioxide than the air you breathed in.

prompt._: In the section 'Delivery Trucks in Your Blood,' how does the author define what hemoglobin does?

choices.text: by listing the chemicals that make up hemoglobin
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions any chemicals or ingredients that make up hemoglobin.

choices.text: by describing a problem that hemoglobin solves
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author doesn't describe a problem — the passage just explains how hemoglobin carries oxygen through the body.

choices.text: by putting the steps of breathing in time order
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage is not listing the steps of breathing in order; it is explaining the job of hemoglobin using a comparison.

choices.text: by comparing hemoglobin to a delivery truck
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author directly says 'Hemoglobin works like a delivery truck,' using that comparison to explain exactly what hemoglobin does.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Carbon Dioxide Has to Go

Your cells produce carbon dioxide as waste whenever they turn food into energy, a process called cellular respiration — like the exhaust that comes out of a car's tailpipe after burning fuel. If carbon dioxide built up in your blood, it would make the blood too acidic, meaning too much like vinegar or lemon juice for your cells to work properly. Gas exchange prevents that by constantly refreshing your blood with new oxygen and sweeping carbon dioxide away.

With every single breath, millions of alveoli trade gases, keeping your blood full of oxygen and your cells powered up. You will take around twenty thousand breaths today. Each one keeps that invisible exchange running — the same quiet process that has been keeping you alive since the moment you were born.

If carbon dioxide built up in your blood, it would make the blood too acidic, meaning too much like vinegar or lemon juice for your cells to work properly. Gas exchange prevents that by constantly refreshing your blood with new oxygen and sweeping carbon dioxide away. With every single breath, millions of alveoli trade gases, keeping your blood full of oxygen and your cells powered up. You will take around twenty thousand breaths today. Each one keeps that invisible exchange running — the same quiet process that has been keeping you alive since the moment you were born.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that breathing helps keep your blood safe for your cells?

choices.text: Alveoli trade gases during every single breath.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it tells us alveoli trade gases, but it doesn't explain the connection to keeping blood safe from too much carbon dioxide.

choices.text: People take about twenty thousand breaths each day.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because knowing we take twenty thousand breaths a day doesn't explain how breathing actually protects the blood.

choices.text: Cells make carbon dioxide when they turn food into energy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only explains that cells make carbon dioxide as a waste product — it doesn't tell us how the blood is kept safe.

choices.text: Gas exchange sweeps carbon dioxide away from the blood.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says gas exchange sweeps carbon dioxide away, which directly prevents the blood from becoming too acidic and harmful to cells.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast the article makes between the air in the alveoli and the blood arriving there?

choices.text: The air moves through the trachea, but the blood moves through the bronchioles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blood travels through capillaries, not bronchioles — bronchioles are tiny air tubes, not blood vessels.

choices.text: The air stays in one place, but the blood spreads through the whole chest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says air stays in one place; it describes air traveling all the way through the airways to reach the alveoli.

choices.text: The air has more carbon dioxide, but the blood has less carbon dioxide.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's the blood, not the air, that is loaded with carbon dioxide when it arrives at the alveoli.

choices.text: The air has more oxygen, but the blood has less oxygen.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the blood arriving at the alveoli has almost no oxygen, while the air there is oxygen-rich — that contrast is what drives diffusion.

prompt._: According to the article, what causes the blood returning to the lungs to be loaded with carbon dioxide?

choices.text: The tiny alveoli trade gases to keep the blood full of fresh oxygen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the alveoli are where carbon dioxide is removed from the blood and breathed out, not the reason the blood is full of carbon dioxide in the first place.

choices.text: The body's cells produce carbon dioxide when turning food into energy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that cells produce carbon dioxide as waste when turning food into energy, and the blood carries that waste back to the lungs.

choices.text: The protein hemoglobin drops off its supply of oxygen at the body's organs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hemoglobin dropping off oxygen explains why returning blood is low on oxygen, not why it is loaded with carbon dioxide.

choices.text: The dome-shaped diaphragm flattens out to make extra space in the chest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the diaphragm flattening out is about bringing air into the lungs, which has nothing to do with carbon dioxide building up in the blood.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best describes how the author organizes the process of breathing in?

choices.text: First the diaphragm pulls downward, next oxygen moves into the blood, and finally air travels down the throat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it skips air traveling down the throat and jumps straight to oxygen moving into the blood, leaving out an important middle step.

choices.text: First air travels down the throat, next oxygen moves into the blood, and finally the diaphragm pulls downward.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because air can't travel down the throat before the diaphragm pulls down — the diaphragm has to move first to pull the air in.

choices.text: First oxygen moves into the blood, next the diaphragm pulls downward, and finally air travels down the throat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oxygen can't move into the blood first — that's the last step, and it can only happen after air has already traveled all the way to the tiny air sacs.

choices.text: First the diaphragm pulls downward, next air travels down the throat, and finally oxygen moves into the blood.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows the diaphragm moves first to bring air in, then air travels down the throat, and finally oxygen passes into the blood — exactly in that order.

prompt._: The article compares how diffusion works in the lungs to how diffusion works with perfume. What main idea do both examples support?

choices.text: The lungs need a fan to push gases through thin walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article specifically says 'no fan needed' and 'no pushing required' when describing how diffusion works.

choices.text: Substances move on their own from more to less of them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the perfume and lung examples show that substances naturally move from where there is more of them to where there is less, without any pushing.

choices.text: Gas exchange is a process the body controls with muscles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because diffusion happens automatically on its own — no muscles are in charge of it.

choices.text: Oxygen and carbon dioxide are similar kinds of waste gas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oxygen is something cells need for energy, while carbon dioxide is a waste product — they are not the same kind of gas.

syllabus_metadata.title: Gas Exchange: The Swap of Life

assessments.title: The Diaphragm: The Muscle That Makes You Breathe

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Muscle You Never See: How Your Body Breathes

Twenty Thousand Squeezes a Day

Right now, a dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs is pulling downward, stretching flat like a trampoline pressed toward the ground. By the time you fall asleep tonight, that muscle will have squeezed and relaxed more than twenty thousand times — all without you telling it to. This muscle is called the diaphragm, and it is the engine behind every single breath you take.
stimulus.content_text: The Muscle You Never See: How Your Body BreathesTwenty Thousand Squeezes a Day Right now, a dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs is pulling downward, stretching flat like a trampoline pressed toward the ground. By the time you fall asleep tonight, that muscle will have squeezed and relaxed more than twenty thousand times — all without you telling it to. This muscle is called the diaphragm, and it is the engine behind every single breath you take.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the diaphragm different from a muscle you control on purpose?

choices.text: It stretches only when you are sleeping
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the diaphragm is already working right now and keeps going all day, not only while you sleep.

choices.text: It moves up and down twenty times a day
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the diaphragm moves more than twenty thousand times a day, not just twenty times.

choices.text: It is located inside the chest area
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says that being inside the chest is what makes the diaphragm different from muscles you control on purpose.

choices.text: It squeezes and relaxes without you telling it to
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the diaphragm squeezes and relaxes 'all without you telling it to,' which is exactly what makes it different from muscles you control on purpose.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How the Diaphragm and Rib Muscles Work Together to Fill Your Lungs

Your lungs are soft and spongy, but they have no muscles of their own. They cannot pull air in by themselves. Instead, they depend on the diaphragm and a set of smaller muscles called the intercostal muscles — the thin bands of muscle stretched between each of your ribs, like rungs on a ladder. Together, these muscles do all the heavy lifting of breathing.

The diaphragm sits right below your lungs. It separates your chest cavity — the space inside your rib cage where your lungs and heart live — from your abdomen, the area below that holds your stomach and intestines. When you breathe in, the diaphragm contracts and flattens downward. At the same time, the intercostal muscles pull your rib cage up and outward. These two movements stretch the chest cavity open, giving your lungs more room to expand.

You might think your lungs suck air in the way a vacuum cleaner sucks up dust. Actually, something different happens. When your chest cavity expands, the air pressure inside your lungs drops below the air pressure outside your body. Air always flows from areas of higher pressure to areas of lower pressure. Think of pulling back the plunger on a syringe — you are not grabbing the air, you are making extra space, and air rushes in on its own to fill it. So air streams in through your nose or mouth, travels down your trachea — the tough, ringed tube in your throat sometimes called the windpipe — and enters your lungs.

Inside the lungs, the trachea splits into two tubes called bronchi, one for each lung. These bronchi branch again and again into smaller tubes called bronchioles, like a tree trunk splitting into thinner branches. At the very ends sit tiny air sacs called alveoli. Your lungs contain about 300 million of them, each wrapped in a net of the tiniest blood vessels in your body. This is where the real exchange happens.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does air rush into the lungs when you breathe in?

choices.text: Because the bronchi push the air downward
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because bronchi are simply tubes that air passes through after entering the lungs — they play no role in pulling air into the body.

choices.text: Because the chest cavity gets bigger
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage explains that when the chest cavity expands, air pressure inside drops and air rushes in from outside to fill the extra space.

choices.text: Because the lungs squeeze the air inward
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage specifically says lungs have no muscles of their own and cannot pull air in by themselves.

choices.text: Because the alveoli pull the air inside
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because alveoli are tiny air sacs where oxygen passes into the blood, not structures that pull air into the lungs.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Gas Exchange and What Your Cells Do with Oxygen

Oxygen from the air you breathed in passes through the paper-thin walls of the alveoli and slips into your blood. At the same time, carbon dioxide — a waste gas your cells have produced — moves from the blood into the alveoli. This two-way trade is called gas exchange. Your blood then carries the fresh oxygen to cells all over your body. Those cells use the oxygen to release energy from food, a process scientists call cellular respiration. Breathing moves air in and out of your lungs; cellular respiration is what your cells do with that oxygen once it arrives.

The air you breathe out is not pure carbon dioxide — it still contains nitrogen and some leftover oxygen, just with more carbon dioxide than before.

Air flows out through the airways and exits through your nose or mouth. At rest, you repeat this cycle about twelve to twenty times every minute. Each breath moves roughly half a liter of air — about enough to fill a small water bottle.

At the same time, carbon dioxide — a waste gas your cells have produced — moves from the blood into the alveoli. This two-way trade is called gas exchange. Your blood then carries the fresh oxygen to cells all over your body. Those cells use the oxygen to release energy from food, a process scientists call cellular respiration. Breathing moves air in and out of your lungs; cellular respiration is what your cells do with that oxygen once it arrives. The air you breathe out is not pure carbon dioxide — it still contains nitrogen and some leftover oxygen, just with more carbon dioxide than before. Air flows out through the airways and exits through your nose or mouth. At rest, you repeat this cycle about twelve to twenty times every minute. Each breath moves roughly half a liter of air — about enough to fill a small water bottle.

prompt._: According to the text, after blood carries oxygen to the cells, what do the cells do next with that oxygen?

choices.text: They move it back into the alveoli.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's carbon dioxide (a waste gas) that moves into the alveoli, not the oxygen that was just delivered to the cells.

choices.text: They send it out through the nose or mouth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because air exits through the nose or mouth as part of breathing, which is a separate process from what the cells do with oxygen.

choices.text: They trade it for nitrogen in the lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions cells trading oxygen for nitrogen — that idea is not in the text at all.

choices.text: They use it to release energy from food.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that cells 'use the oxygen to release energy from food,' which is called cellular respiration.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Your Brain Keeps the Rhythm — and You Can Too

A part of your brain called the brainstem controls your breathing automatically. It monitors the level of carbon dioxide in your blood. When carbon dioxide builds up, the brainstem fires signals through nerves to the diaphragm and intercostal muscles, telling them to contract. You inhale. Oxygen enters the blood, carbon dioxide leaves, and the brainstem detects the drop. The signals slow, and you exhale. This constant cycle of sensing and adjusting is called a feedback loop — your body checking its own work and correcting it, like a thermostat switching a heater on and off to hold a steady temperature.

Even though breathing runs on autopilot, you can override it. You can hold your breath underwater, blow out birthday candles, or take a slow deep breath before a race. Singers, swimmers, and flute players train their diaphragms the way athletes train their legs — with practice and control.

But right now it is working — pulling flat, springing up, pulling flat again — delivering oxygen to every cell in your body. That invisible muscle beneath your ribs is the reason any of it is possible.

Singers, swimmers, and flute players train their diaphragms the way athletes train their legs — with practice and control. But right now it is working — pulling flat, springing up, pulling flat again — delivering oxygen to every cell in your body. That invisible muscle beneath your ribs is the reason any of it is possible.

prompt._: The author compares a "feedback loop" to a thermostat switching a heater on and off. Based on this comparison, what does a feedback loop do?

choices.text: It directs the oxygen to flow to the brainstem.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the feedback loop sends nerve signals to the breathing muscles, not oxygen to the brainstem.

choices.text: It corrects the body to keep conditions steady.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a feedback loop is the body 'checking its own work and correcting it,' just like a thermostat keeps temperature steady.

choices.text: It forces the lungs to hold breath underwater.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because holding your breath underwater is an example of overriding the automatic system, not what a feedback loop does.

choices.text: It trains the muscles to work harder for sports.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because training muscles for sports is about practice and control, which the passage talks about separately from the feedback loop.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the difference between the roles of the brainstem and the diaphragm?

choices.text: The brainstem protects the soft and spongy lungs, but the diaphragm protects the stomach and the intestines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says the brainstem protects the lungs or that the diaphragm protects the stomach; the diaphragm simply separates the chest from the abdomen.

choices.text: The brainstem expands the space in the chest, but the diaphragm monitors the blood for carbon dioxide.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the jobs backwards — it's the diaphragm that expands the chest space, and it's the brainstem that monitors carbon dioxide in the blood.

choices.text: The brainstem decides when you need to breathe, but the diaphragm does the physical work of moving air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article shows the brainstem sends signals deciding when to breathe, while the diaphragm is the muscle that physically contracts to move air in and out.

choices.text: The brainstem carries fresh oxygen to the cells, but the diaphragm carries carbon dioxide out of the body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carrying oxygen to cells is the blood's job, and moving carbon dioxide out happens through the alveoli and airways — not through the brainstem or diaphragm.

prompt._: According to the text, what causes the brainstem to signal the diaphragm to contract?

choices.text: The air pressure inside the chest cavity drops too low.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a drop in air pressure inside the chest cavity is what happens during a breath to let air rush in, not the signal that tells the brain to start breathing.

choices.text: The lungs run out of fresh oxygen to send to the body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the brainstem tracks carbon dioxide levels, not oxygen levels, to decide when to send a signal to breathe.

choices.text: The intercostal muscles pull the rib cage up and out.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the intercostal muscles pulling the rib cage up and out is part of what happens while you inhale, not the trigger that causes the brainstem to send its signal.

choices.text: The cells release carbon dioxide waste into the blood.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the brainstem monitors carbon dioxide in the blood, and when it builds up (from cells releasing it as waste), the brainstem fires signals to the diaphragm to contract.

prompt._: Which choice shows the breathing process in the correct order described in the article?

choices.text: First air leaves the nose or mouth, next the chest cavity expands, then the lungs fill with air
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because air leaving through the nose or mouth is exhaling, which happens at the end of a breath cycle, not the beginning.

choices.text: First the diaphragm flattens, next air moves through the trachea, then oxygen enters the blood
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the article: the diaphragm flattens first, then air travels through the trachea, and finally oxygen enters the blood at the alveoli.

choices.text: First the alveoli trade gases, next the diaphragm flattens, then air enters the trachea
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the diaphragm flattening is the very first step that starts a breath — it has to happen before air even reaches the alveoli.

choices.text: First oxygen enters the blood, next the brainstem checks carbon dioxide, then the diaphragm flattens
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oxygen entering the blood happens after you breathe in, so it cannot be the first step in the breathing process.

prompt._: Compared to the lungs, which cannot move air on their own, what does the article show is most important about the diaphragm and the other muscles described?

choices.text: They produce the oxygen that cells need for energy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oxygen comes from the air around us, not from the muscles — the muscles just help move that air into the lungs.

choices.text: They do all the physical work that makes breathing possible
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the lungs can't move air on their own, so the diaphragm and intercostal muscles do all the physical work that makes breathing happen.

choices.text: They send signals that tell the brain when to breathe
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the brainstem sends signals to the diaphragm and muscles, so the brain controls the muscles, not the other way around.

choices.text: They protect the lungs from germs and keep the airways clean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says the diaphragm or intercostal muscles protect the lungs from germs — that job isn't mentioned at all.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Diaphragm: The Muscle That Makes You Breathe

assessments.title: Keeping Your Lungs Healthy

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Respiratory System: A Tennis Court Inside Your Chest

Fifteen Thousand Breaths a Day

Right now, without thinking about it, you just took a breath. You will do it again in a few seconds. By the time you go to sleep tonight, your body will have breathed in and out about fifteen thousand times. Each breath pulls air through your nose or mouth, down your windpipe, and into your two lungs. But here is where things get interesting — your lungs are not hollow bags that fill up like balloons. They are soft, spongy organs packed with about 300 million tiny air sacs called alveoli. Picture a bunch of grapes at the end of a branching stem. Each tiny "grape" is one air sac, and each one is wrapped in the thinnest blood vessels your body has.

If you could unfold and flatten every single one of those 300 million alveoli, they would spread out to cover an entire tennis court.
stimulus.content_text: The Respiratory System: A Tennis Court Inside Your ChestFifteen Thousand Breaths a Day Right now, without thinking about it, you just took a breath. You will do it again in a few seconds. By the time you go to sleep tonight, your body will have breathed in and out about fifteen thousand times. Each breath pulls air through your nose or mouth, down your windpipe, and into your two lungs. But here is where things get interesting — your lungs are not hollow bags that fill up like balloons. They are soft, spongy organs packed with about 300 million tiny air sacs called alveoli. Picture a bunch of grapes at the end of a branching stem. Each tiny "grape" is one air sac, and each one is wrapped in the thinnest blood vessels your body has. If you could unfold and flatten every single one of those 300 million alveoli, they would spread out to cover an entire tennis court.

prompt._: According to the text, lungs are not hollow bags that fill up like balloons, but instead they are what?

choices.text: Tiny grapes growing on a branching stem
choices.feedback: Grapes on a branching stem is a helpful picture the author uses to describe the shape of the alveoli, but it is not the description of what lungs actually are.

choices.text: Thin blood vessels covering a large court
choices.feedback: Blood vessels wrap around the air sacs inside the lungs, but the passage never says the lungs themselves are made of blood vessels.

choices.text: Spongy organs packed with tiny air sacs
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says lungs are 'soft, spongy organs packed with about 300 million tiny air sacs,' which is exactly what option B says.

choices.text: Hollow windpipes pulling air from the nose
choices.feedback: The windpipe is the tube that moves air from your nose or mouth down into your lungs — it is a different body part, not the lungs.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Gas Exchange Actually Works

Actually, the air around you is mostly nitrogen — about 78 percent. Only about 21 percent is oxygen. When you exhale, your breath still contains oxygen, just a little less of it, along with more carbon dioxide than before. Your lungs swap some oxygen for some carbon dioxide with every single breath.

This swap is called gas exchange — the process where oxygen passes through the paper-thin walls of the alveoli into your bloodstream, and carbon dioxide passes the other direction, out of your blood and into the air you breathe out. Think of it like a trading post: blood drops off carbon dioxide waste and picks up fresh oxygen.

Here is the part that surprises many people. Oxygen does not just stay in your lungs. It hitches a ride on your blood, traveling through blood vessels to reach every cell in your body — your brain cells, your muscle cells, even the cells in your toes. That is why lung damage does not just make breathing harder. It can starve your whole body of the oxygen it needs.

When you exhale, your breath still contains oxygen, just a little less of it, along with more carbon dioxide than before. Your lungs swap some oxygen for some carbon dioxide with every single breath. This swap is called gas exchange — the process where oxygen passes through the paper-thin walls of the alveoli into your bloodstream, and carbon dioxide passes the other direction, out of your blood and into the air you breathe out. Think of it like a trading post: blood drops off carbon dioxide waste and picks up fresh oxygen. Here is the part that surprises many people. Oxygen does not just stay in your lungs. It hitches a ride on your blood, traveling through blood vessels to reach every cell in your body — your brain cells, your muscle cells, even the cells in your toes. That is why lung damage does not just make breathing harder. It can starve your whole body of the oxygen it needs.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why can lung damage affect your whole body and not just your breathing?

choices.text: Because the lungs hold all of the body's carbon dioxide waste inside them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says carbon dioxide is breathed out of the body, not stored inside the lungs.

choices.text: Because carbon dioxide must be carried from the lungs to all body cells
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carbon dioxide is a waste product that moves out of the blood and into the air you exhale — it is oxygen, not carbon dioxide, that is carried to body cells.

choices.text: Because oxygen travels through the blood to reach every cell in the body
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that oxygen travels through the blood to reach every cell in the body, which is why lung damage can affect the whole body.

choices.text: Because the lungs contain nitrogen that the rest of the body needs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions nitrogen as part of the air around us, but never says body cells need nitrogen delivered by the lungs.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Tiny Brooms and Sticky Mucus

Your airways have their own cleaning crew. The tubes leading to your lungs are lined with cilia, microscopic hairs that wave back and forth like tiny brooms. They sweep dust, germs, and mucus up and out of your lungs before those particles cause trouble. A thin layer of sticky mucus traps the invaders, and the cilia push them toward your throat so you can cough or swallow them away. Drinking plenty of water keeps that mucus thin, which helps the cilia do their job.
stimulus.content_text: Tiny Brooms and Sticky Mucus Your airways have their own cleaning crew. The tubes leading to your lungs are lined with cilia, microscopic hairs that wave back and forth like tiny brooms. They sweep dust, germs, and mucus up and out of your lungs before those particles cause trouble. A thin layer of sticky mucus traps the invaders, and the cilia push them toward your throat so you can cough or swallow them away. Drinking plenty of water keeps that mucus thin, which helps the cilia do their job.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after mucus traps dust and germs?

choices.text: The cilia trap them in a sticky layer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the jobs — the mucus does the trapping, and the cilia do the pushing.

choices.text: The cilia push them toward your throat.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'the cilia push them toward your throat so you can cough or swallow them away.'

choices.text: The throat sweeps them out with tiny hairs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the tiny hairs called cilia are found lining the airways, not the throat.

choices.text: The mucus carries them deeper into your lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says particles are pushed OUT of your lungs toward your throat, not deeper into your lungs.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Smoking Does to Lungs

Cigarette smoke contains thousands of harmful chemicals. When smoke enters the airways, it paralyzes the cilia and eventually destroys them. Without those tiny brooms, dirt and germs pile up in the lungs. Infections become more likely. Smoke also attacks the alveoli themselves, tearing apart those delicate air sacs. Once alveoli are destroyed, they do not grow back. This leads to a disease called emphysema — a condition where so many air sacs are gone that the lungs can no longer move enough oxygen into the blood. Every breath becomes a struggle. Smoking is also one of the leading causes of lung cancer.
stimulus.content_text: What Smoking Does to Lungs Cigarette smoke contains thousands of harmful chemicals. When smoke enters the airways, it paralyzes the cilia and eventually destroys them. Without those tiny brooms, dirt and germs pile up in the lungs. Infections become more likely. Smoke also attacks the alveoli themselves, tearing apart those delicate air sacs. Once alveoli are destroyed, they do not grow back. This leads to a disease called emphysema — a condition where so many air sacs are gone that the lungs can no longer move enough oxygen into the blood. Every breath becomes a struggle. Smoking is also one of the leading causes of lung cancer.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are cilia similar to brooms?

choices.text: They cause infections in the lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cilia actually help prevent infections by sweeping out dirt and germs; infections happen when cilia are gone.

choices.text: They move oxygen into the blood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says alveoli (air sacs) move oxygen into the blood, not cilia.

choices.text: They tear apart delicate air sacs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cigarette smoke tears apart the delicate air sacs — cilia don't do that.

choices.text: They sweep dirt out of the lungs.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage calls cilia 'tiny brooms' and explains they keep dirt and germs from piling up, just like a broom sweeps away mess.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Pollution and Asthma

Smoke from wildfires, vehicle exhaust, and factory emissions release tiny particles into the air. These particles can settle deep in the lungs and cause inflammation — swelling and redness, like what happens around a splinter in your finger, except it is happening inside your airways. Over time, breathing polluted air regularly can lead to serious breathing problems, especially for children and older adults. On days when air quality is poor, staying indoors helps protect your lungs.

Some people have a condition called asthma that makes breathing even harder. During an asthma episode, the muscles around the airways tighten, the airway lining swells, and extra mucus clogs the passages. Less air gets through. People with asthma may wheeze, cough, or feel short of breath, especially during exercise, in cold air, or near triggers like pollen and pet dander. Asthma cannot be cured, but medicine and avoiding triggers help many children with asthma stay active and healthy.

Over time, breathing polluted air regularly can lead to serious breathing problems, especially for children and older adults. On days when air quality is poor, staying indoors helps protect your lungs. Some people have a condition called asthma that makes breathing even harder. During an asthma episode, the muscles around the airways tighten, the airway lining swells, and extra mucus clogs the passages. Less air gets through. People with asthma may wheeze, cough, or feel short of breath, especially during exercise, in cold air, or near triggers like pollen and pet dander. Asthma cannot be cured, but medicine and avoiding triggers help many children with asthma stay active and healthy.

prompt._: In the section "Pollution and Asthma," what does the word "inflammation" mean?

choices.text: swelling and redness inside the airways
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines inflammation as 'swelling and redness' happening inside the airways, right after the bold word appears.

choices.text: tight muscles around the airways
choices.feedback: Tight muscles around the airways is something that happens during an asthma episode, but the passage uses that detail to describe asthma, not to define inflammation.

choices.text: less air moving through the lungs
choices.feedback: Less air moving through the lungs is a result of asthma symptoms, not the definition of inflammation that the passage gives us.

choices.text: extra mucus inside the passages
choices.feedback: Extra mucus clogging the passages is another thing that happens during asthma, but the passage never connects mucus to the meaning of inflammation.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Keeping Your Lungs Strong

When you run, swim, or ride your bike, your breathing rate climbs. Your lungs work harder. Over time, regular exercise strengthens the muscles that expand your chest and improves how well your body uses oxygen. Washing your hands prevents respiratory infections — illnesses that attack the breathing system, like colds and the flu. Covering your mouth when you cough or sneeze keeps those germs from reaching someone else's lungs.

Every few seconds, without a single conscious thought, your lungs trade gases with the air, and your blood carries oxygen to 37 trillion cells.

Over time, regular exercise strengthens the muscles that expand your chest and improves how well your body uses oxygen. Washing your hands prevents respiratory infections — illnesses that attack the breathing system, like colds and the flu. Covering your mouth when you cough or sneeze keeps those germs from reaching someone else's lungs. Every few seconds, without a single conscious thought, your lungs trade gases with the air, and your blood carries oxygen to 37 trillion cells.

prompt._: Based on 'Keeping Your Lungs Strong,' which detail from the passage best supports the idea that exercise makes the lungs work better over time?

choices.text: Your blood carries oxygen to 37 trillion cells in your body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's a fact about how blood delivers oxygen to cells, but it doesn't say anything about exercise making the lungs work better.

choices.text: Regular exercise strengthens the muscles that expand your chest.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that regular exercise strengthens chest muscles and improves how the body uses oxygen, which shows the lungs getting better over time.

choices.text: Your breathing rate climbs when you run or swim.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a faster breathing rate just describes what happens during exercise, not that the lungs actually get stronger or work better over time.

choices.text: Washing your hands prevents illnesses like colds and the flu.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because washing hands helps prevent illness, which is about staying healthy — not about exercise improving lung strength.

prompt._: According to the text, how does the job of mucus in a healthy person contrast with what mucus does during an asthma episode?

choices.text: It helps trap dust and germs, but during asthma, it clogs the breathing passages.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says mucus traps germs in healthy airways, but during asthma, extra mucus clogs the breathing passages and makes it hard to breathe.

choices.text: It helps destroy harmful viruses, but during asthma, it paralyzes the waving cilia.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's cigarette smoke that paralyzes the cilia, not mucus — the passage clearly says smoke is what damages those tiny brooms.

choices.text: It helps shrink the tiny air sacs, but during asthma, it swells up like balloons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says mucus shrinks air sacs or swells like balloons — those details simply don't appear anywhere in the text.

choices.text: It helps make fresh oxygen gas, but during asthma, it makes extra carbon dioxide.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mucus doesn't make oxygen or carbon dioxide — the passage explains that gas exchange in the alveoli handles oxygen and carbon dioxide.

prompt._: Based on the text, why does the destruction of alveoli cause a person's entire body to suffer from a lack of oxygen?

choices.text: Because the tiny brooms stop sweeping harmful dust and germs out of the lungs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cilia stopping is about germs and dust piling up in the lungs, which is a different problem from alveoli being destroyed.

choices.text: Because the blood cannot pick up enough oxygen to deliver to all the cells.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says oxygen hitches a ride on blood to reach every cell, so when alveoli are destroyed and can't pass enough oxygen into the blood, the whole body suffers.

choices.text: Because the airways become swollen and clogged with too much extra sticky mucus.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because swollen airways and extra mucus describe what happens during an asthma episode, not the destruction of alveoli.

choices.text: Because the lungs shrink and can no longer hold fifteen thousand daily breaths.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the lungs shrink — it says the delicate air sacs are torn apart and cannot grow back.

prompt._: Which option shows the sequence of what happens in the body after a person breathes in cigarette smoke, based on the article?

choices.text: The alveoli are destroyed, the cilia stop working, and then germs build up in the lungs.
choices.feedback: A has the order backwards — the article says the cilia stop working first, then germs build up, and then the alveoli are destroyed, not the other way around.

choices.text: Less oxygen moves into the blood, the cilia trap the smoke, and then the alveoli stay healthy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because smoke destroys the cilia so they cannot trap anything, and the alveoli are definitely damaged by smoke according to the article.

choices.text: The cilia stop working, germs and dirt build up, and then less oxygen moves into the blood.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's sequence exactly: smoke stops the cilia, germs and dirt pile up, and eventually less oxygen gets into the blood.

choices.text: Germs and dirt build up, the cilia grow back, and then breathing becomes easier again.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that destroyed alveoli and cilia do not grow back, so breathing does not become easier again.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the sections about smoking and pollution?

choices.text: Both explain that exercise is the best way to cure serious lung diseases caused by air.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says exercise can cure diseases like emphysema or lung damage from pollution.

choices.text: Both explain that lung problems mostly happen because the body does not make enough mucus.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither section is about the body not making enough mucus — they are about harmful things in the air damaging the lungs.

choices.text: Both show that harmful things in the air can damage the lungs and make breathing harder.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both sections explain how harmful things in the air (smoke and pollution) damage the lungs and cause breathing problems.

choices.text: Both show that all breathing problems happen only to people who already have asthma.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows that smoking and pollution can harm anyone's lungs, not only people who already have asthma.

assessments.title: Atoms: The Tiniest Building Blocks

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Tiny Builders of Everything

Smaller Than You Can Imagine

So what exactly is an atom? Everything you can touch, pour, breathe, or taste is made of matter — the physical stuff that takes up space and has weight. Your desk, a glass of milk, the air in your lungs — all matter. And if you could zoom in on any piece of matter with the most powerful tool ever built, you would eventually reach its tiniest building blocks: atoms.

An atom is the smallest piece of an element that still behaves like that element. An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom — think of it as a single flavor of matter.

Line up about ten million atoms side by side, and they would stretch across just one millimeter — roughly the thickness of a dime. You will never see a single atom with your eyes alone. But scientists have learned an astonishing amount about what is hiding inside them.
stimulus.content_text: The Tiny Builders of EverythingSmaller Than You Can Imagine So what exactly is an atom? Everything you can touch, pour, breathe, or taste is made of matter — the physical stuff that takes up space and has weight. Your desk, a glass of milk, the air in your lungs — all matter. And if you could zoom in on any piece of matter with the most powerful tool ever built, you would eventually reach its tiniest building blocks: atoms. An atom is the smallest piece of an element that still behaves like that element. An element is a pure substance made of only one kind of atom — think of it as a single flavor of matter. Line up about ten million atoms side by side, and they would stretch across just one millimeter — roughly the thickness of a dime. You will never see a single atom with your eyes alone. But scientists have learned an astonishing amount about what is hiding inside them.

prompt._: According to the text, which statement shows the contrast between how small atoms are and how much scientists know about them?

choices.text: Atoms are made only of air, but scientists can still breathe them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says atoms make up everything — desks, milk, and air — not just air.

choices.text: Atoms are too small to see, but scientists have learned a lot about them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states atoms are too small to see with your eyes, yet scientists have learned an astonishing amount about them — that's the contrast!

choices.text: Atoms are bigger than matter, but scientists can still study matter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says atoms are the tiniest building blocks of matter, which means atoms are smaller than matter, not bigger.

choices.text: Atoms are too heavy to lift, but scientists can still measure them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about atoms being heavy or too heavy to lift.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What's Inside an Atom

Every atom contains three types of even smaller particles. At the very center sits a tightly packed core called the nucleus — the atom's headquarters, like the pit at the center of a peach. The nucleus is extremely tiny compared to the whole atom, but it holds almost all of the atom's mass, which is the amount of matter packed into an object. A bowling ball has more mass than a balloon, even if they are the same size.

Two kinds of particles live inside the nucleus. Protons carry a positive electrical charge. Think of charge as an invisible force — positive and negative charges pull toward each other the way opposite ends of two magnets snap together. Neutrons sit right next to the protons but carry no charge at all. They are neutral, which is how they got their name. Neutrons add mass to the atom without changing its charge.

Zooming outward from the nucleus, you find electrons. These particles carry a negative charge, and they move at immense speed around the nucleus. You might picture them circling in neat loops, the way planets orbit the Sun. Many textbook diagrams even draw it that way. But that picture is wrong. Electrons actually zip around in a blurry, cloud-like zone. They do not follow a single clean path. Imagine a bee buzzing wildly around a flower — you know the bee is nearby, but you cannot predict exactly where it will be at any moment. To get a sense of the distances involved: if the nucleus were a marble sitting at center court of a basketball arena, the electrons would be buzzing somewhere near the outer walls of the building.

The nucleus is extremely tiny compared to the whole atom, but it holds almost all of the atom's mass, which is the amount of matter packed into an object. A bowling ball has more mass than a balloon, even if they are the same size. Two kinds of particles live inside the nucleus. Protons carry a positive electrical charge. Think of charge as an invisible force — positive and negative charges pull toward each other the way opposite ends of two magnets snap together. Neutrons sit right next to the protons but carry no charge at all. They are neutral, which is how they got their name. Neutrons add mass to the atom without changing its charge. You might picture them circling in neat loops, the way planets orbit the Sun. Many textbook diagrams even draw it that way. But that picture is wrong. Electrons actually zip around in a blurry, cloud-like zone. They do not follow a single clean path. Imagine a bee buzzing wildly around a flower — you know the bee is nearby, but you cannot predict exactly where it will be at any moment. To get a sense of the distances involved: if the nucleus were a marble sitting at center court of a basketball arena, the electrons would be buzzing somewhere near the outer walls of the building.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did scientists name the neutral particles in the nucleus 'neutrons'?

choices.text: because they carry no electrical charge at all
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says neutrons carry no charge and are 'neutral,' which is exactly where their name comes from.

choices.text: because they sit at the very center of the nucleus
choices.feedback: The passage says the nucleus sits at the center, but that's not the reason neutrons got their name.

choices.text: because they are the largest particles inside the atom
choices.feedback: The passage never says neutrons are the largest particles inside the atom, so this is not why they got their name.

choices.text: because they move around the nucleus in a cloud
choices.feedback: Moving in a cloud describes electrons, not neutrons, so this has nothing to do with how neutrons got their name.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Protons Tell You the Element

The number of protons inside an atom's nucleus decides which element that atom belongs to. Scientists call this number the atomic number. Every carbon atom has exactly six protons. Every oxygen atom has exactly eight. Every gold atom has seventy-nine. Change the number of protons, and you have a completely different element.

In a neutral atom, the number of electrons matches the number of protons exactly. The positive charges and negative charges balance perfectly, so the atom has no overall charge.

When atoms connect to each other, they form bonds — links that hold atoms together the way a clasp holds two ends of a bracelet. Atoms bonded in different arrangements create different materials. That is why a diamond and pencil graphite — the soft, dark mineral inside your pencil — can both be pure carbon yet look and feel completely different. One is the hardest natural material on Earth, and the other leaves soft, dark streaks on paper. Yet both are made of the exact same thing: carbon atoms, arranged in different patterns. Same atoms, different structure.

Every carbon atom has exactly six protons. Every oxygen atom has exactly eight. Every gold atom has seventy-nine. Change the number of protons, and you have a completely different element. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons matches the number of protons exactly. The positive charges and negative charges balance perfectly, so the atom has no overall charge. When atoms connect to each other, they form bonds — links that hold atoms together the way a clasp holds two ends of a bracelet. Atoms bonded in different arrangements create different materials. That is why a diamond and pencil graphite — the soft, dark mineral inside your pencil — can both be pure carbon yet look and feel completely different. One is the hardest natural material on Earth, and the other leaves soft, dark streaks on paper. Yet both are made of the exact same thing: carbon atoms, arranged in different patterns. Same atoms, different structure.

prompt._: According to the text, what must happen before you can have a completely different element?

choices.text: The number of protons must change
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says directly that changing the number of protons gives you a completely different element.

choices.text: The atoms must form bonds with each other
choices.feedback: Atoms forming bonds creates different materials, like diamond and graphite, but the passage explains those are still the same element — carbon — just arranged differently.

choices.text: The electrons must move to a new orbit
choices.feedback: Electrons moving to a new orbit is not mentioned in the passage as something that changes the element — only the number of protons does that.

choices.text: The positive and negative charges must balance
choices.feedback: Balanced positive and negative charges describe a neutral atom, but the passage never says that balancing charges creates a new element.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
From an Ancient Guess to Modern Science

More than two thousand years ago, a Greek thinker named Democritus guessed that matter might be made of tiny particles too small to cut apart. He called them atomos, a Greek word meaning "uncuttable." But Democritus had no experiments to prove it. His idea stayed just a guess for centuries.

It was not until the 1800s that scientists like John Dalton began testing the idea with real experiments. Over the next two hundred years, researchers discovered protons, neutrons, and electrons. You might think protons and neutrons are the smallest things that exist, but scientists have found that protons and neutrons themselves are made of even tinier particles called quarks. The deeper scientists look, the more there is to find.

Every material you will ever hold started with atoms bonding together in a particular pattern. The difference between a glittering jewel and a pencil streak comes down to how those atoms connect.
stimulus.content_text: From an Ancient Guess to Modern Science More than two thousand years ago, a Greek thinker named Democritus guessed that matter might be made of tiny particles too small to cut apart. He called them atomos, a Greek word meaning "uncuttable." But Democritus had no experiments to prove it. His idea stayed just a guess for centuries. It was not until the 1800s that scientists like John Dalton began testing the idea with real experiments. Over the next two hundred years, researchers discovered protons, neutrons, and electrons. You might think protons and neutrons are the smallest things that exist, but scientists have found that protons and neutrons themselves are made of even tinier particles called quarks. The deeper scientists look, the more there is to find. Every material you will ever hold started with atoms bonding together in a particular pattern. The difference between a glittering jewel and a pencil streak comes down to how those atoms connect.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are Democritus and John Dalton similar?

choices.text: They both proved their ideas using real experiments.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says Democritus had no experiments to prove his idea — only Dalton used real experiments.

choices.text: They both lived in Greece more than two thousand years ago.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only Democritus lived in ancient Greece; Dalton lived in the 1800s, which is much more recent.

choices.text: They both discovered quarks inside protons and neutrons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says scientists discovered quarks after Dalton, and Democritus never knew about quarks at all.

choices.text: They both thought matter was made of tiny particles.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows both men believed matter was made of tiny particles — Democritus guessed it and Dalton tested that same idea.

prompt._: According to the article, how is the way electrons actually move different from the way many textbook diagrams show them?

choices.text: They travel in straight lines instead of curving near the nucleus.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions electrons moving in straight lines — that idea doesn't appear anywhere in the passage.

choices.text: They spin in place inside the nucleus instead of moving around it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because electrons are found outside the nucleus, not spinning inside it — that's where protons and neutrons live.

choices.text: They move slowly through empty space instead of staying close by.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says electrons move at 'immense speed,' which is the opposite of slowly.

choices.text: They zip around in a blurry cloud instead of following neat loops.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states electrons move in a 'blurry, cloud-like zone' rather than the neat circular loops shown in many textbook diagrams.

prompt._: Based on the article, why can a diamond and pencil graphite look and feel so different even though they are both made of carbon atoms?

choices.text: because their atoms have different electrical charges
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both diamond and graphite are made of the same carbon atoms, so their atoms have the same electrical charges, not different ones.

choices.text: because their atoms are bonded in different patterns
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that atoms bonded in different arrangements create different materials, and that diamond and graphite are both carbon atoms arranged in different patterns.

choices.text: because their atoms have different numbers of protons
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both diamond and graphite are made of carbon atoms, which always have the same number of protons — if the proton number changed, it would be a completely different element, not carbon.

choices.text: because their atoms are different sizes and weights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions carbon atoms being different sizes or weights in diamond versus graphite — that idea doesn't appear anywhere in the passage.

prompt._: In what order does the author explain information in the first two sections?

choices.text: The author starts with the history of atoms and then lists everyday objects.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the history of atoms (Democritus and John Dalton) doesn't appear until the fourth section, not at the beginning.

choices.text: The author starts with the parts of a nucleus and then zooms out to everyday objects.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the author starts with big, everyday objects and moves inward — not the other way around from the nucleus outward.

choices.text: The author starts with everyday objects and then zooms in to the parts of an atom.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the first section opens with familiar things like desks and milk, then zooms in to atoms, and the second section zooms in even further to the parts inside an atom.

choices.text: The author starts with different types of elements and then explains how they connect.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the first two sections don't focus on different elements or how they connect; that topic comes up later in the third section.

prompt._: Which statement best explains how the author uses comparisons to support the main idea of the article?

choices.text: By comparing a bowling ball to a balloon to show their heavy mass, and comparing ancient thinkers to modern scientists to show their long history.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the bowling ball and balloon comparison explains mass, and the ancient vs. modern scientists comparison explains history — neither one supports the main idea about atoms as tiny builders.

choices.text: By comparing atoms to everyday items to show their tiny size, and comparing carbon materials to show how atoms build different things.
choices.feedback: This is correct because both comparisons directly support the main idea: atoms are unimaginably tiny AND they are the building blocks that create different materials.

choices.text: By comparing the weight of matter to empty space to show its physical form, and comparing the nucleus to a peach pit to show its solid center.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because comparing weight to empty space and the nucleus to a peach pit are details about what matter and atoms look like inside, not about the main idea of atoms building all materials.

choices.text: By comparing protons to snapping magnets to show their strong charge, and comparing moving electrons to buzzing bees to show their wild speed.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while the magnet and bee comparisons are real, they only explain details about protons and electrons — not the article's main idea about atoms building everything.

assessments.title: Elements: Nature's Ingredients

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The 118 Ingredients of Everything

A Nugget of Pure Gold

In 1799, a twelve-year-old boy named Conrad Reed found a heavy, yellow rock in a creek in North Carolina. It turned out to be a seventeen-pound lump of gold. That gold was pure — nothing but gold all the way through. Scrape it, melt it, or crush it into dust, and every tiny piece is still gold. Gold is one of nature's building blocks, and scientists call these building blocks elements.
stimulus.content_text: The 118 Ingredients of EverythingA Nugget of Pure Gold In 1799, a twelve-year-old boy named Conrad Reed found a heavy, yellow rock in a creek in North Carolina. It turned out to be a seventeen-pound lump of gold. That gold was pure — nothing but gold all the way through. Scrape it, melt it, or crush it into dust, and every tiny piece is still gold. Gold is one of nature's building blocks, and scientists call these building blocks elements.

prompt._: According to the text, how is pure gold different from other kinds of material?

choices.text: It is made of only gold.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states pure gold is 'nothing but gold all the way through,' so every piece of it is only gold.

choices.text: It is found only in creeks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only tells us where Conrad found one piece of gold — it never says gold is found only in creeks.

choices.text: It is made of many mixed parts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says pure gold is 'nothing but gold all the way through,' meaning it is NOT made of many mixed parts.

choices.text: It is heavier than all rocks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says gold is heavier than all rocks, only that Conrad's gold nugget was heavy.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Makes an Element an Element

An element is a substance made of only one kind of atom — the smallest possible particle of that element, far too tiny to see even with most microscopes. Think of an atom as a single LEGO brick. One gold atom is the smallest piece of gold that can exist. Pile billions of gold atoms together, and you get a gold nugget like the one Conrad found.

Iron, carbon, hydrogen, and helium are all elements too.

Sometimes atoms of the same element link up in groups called molecules — clusters of atoms bonded together, like LEGO bricks snapped side by side. The oxygen gas you breathe is actually two oxygen atoms bonded into one molecule. But the element is still just oxygen.
stimulus.content_text: What Makes an Element an Element An element is a substance made of only one kind of atom — the smallest possible particle of that element, far too tiny to see even with most microscopes. Think of an atom as a single LEGO brick. One gold atom is the smallest piece of gold that can exist. Pile billions of gold atoms together, and you get a gold nugget like the one Conrad found. Iron, carbon, hydrogen, and helium are all elements too. Sometimes atoms of the same element link up in groups called molecules — clusters of atoms bonded together, like LEGO bricks snapped side by side. The oxygen gas you breathe is actually two oxygen atoms bonded into one molecule. But the element is still just oxygen.

prompt._: According to the passage, what causes a molecule to form?

choices.text: Several LEGO bricks snapping side by side.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — LEGO bricks are just a fun comparison the author uses to explain atoms and molecules, not an actual example of molecules forming.

choices.text: Atoms of the same element linking together.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says 'atoms of the same element link up in groups called molecules,' which tells us that atoms linking together is what creates a molecule.

choices.text: Different kinds of elements mixing together.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says molecules form when atoms of the *same* element link up, not when different kinds of elements mix together.

choices.text: Billions of gold atoms piling into a nugget.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says billions of gold atoms piling together makes a gold nugget, which is a different idea from how molecules form.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Chart That Reveals Hidden Patterns

Scientists have discovered about 118 elements so far. Some, like oxygen and iron, are common and familiar. Others, like oganesson and tennessine, are extremely rare and exist only briefly in laboratories. To keep track of all these elements, scientists organize them in a chart called the periodic table.

Actually, every element is placed in a specific spot because of what it is and how it behaves.

A Russian chemist named Dmitri Mendeleev created the first version in 1869. He arranged the elements by atomic weight — a measure of how heavy each element's atoms are compared to the lightest element, hydrogen. Then he noticed something astonishing. Elements with similar behavior appeared at regular intervals, forming columns on his chart. One column held shiny metals that reacted violently with water. Another column held invisible gases that almost never react with anything at all. The pattern repeated like a rhythm in a song.

Mendeleev was so confident in his pattern that he left gaps in the table for elements nobody had found yet. He even predicted what those missing elements would look like and how they would behave. When scientists later discovered them, his predictions turned out to be remarkably accurate.

Today the periodic table is organized by atomic number instead of weight. The atomic number is the count of protons — tiny particles packed inside an atom's center, called the nucleus. Hydrogen has one proton, so it is element number one. Helium has two protons, so it is element number two. The table continues all the way up to element 118.

Each element also has a short symbol of one or two letters. Gold is Au, from the Latin word aurum, meaning gold.

Others, like oganesson and tennessine, are extremely rare and exist only briefly in laboratories. To keep track of all these elements, scientists organize them in a chart called the periodic table. Actually, every element is placed in a specific spot because of what it is and how it behaves. A Russian chemist named Dmitri Mendeleev created the first version in 1869. He arranged the elements by atomic weight — a measure of how heavy each element's atoms are compared to the lightest element, hydrogen. Then he noticed something astonishing.
