Another column held invisible gases that almost never react with anything at all. The pattern repeated like a rhythm in a song. Mendeleev was so confident in his pattern that he left gaps in the table for elements nobody had found yet. He even predicted what those missing elements would look like and how they would behave. When scientists later discovered them, his predictions turned out to be remarkably accurate. Today the periodic table is organized by atomic number instead of weight. The atomic number is the count of protons — tiny particles packed inside an atom's center, called the nucleus. Hydrogen has one proton, so it is element number one. Helium has two protons, so it is element number two. The table continues all the way up to element 118. Each element also has a short symbol of one or two letters. Gold is Au, from the Latin word aurum, meaning gold.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Mendeleev do first that allowed him to notice that elements with similar behavior appeared at regular intervals?

choices.text: He left gaps in the table for missing elements
choices.feedback: Leaving gaps came after Mendeleev already noticed the pattern, so it was a result of his discovery, not the first step that caused it.

choices.text: He counted the protons inside each atom's nucleus
choices.feedback: Counting protons is how scientists organize the periodic table today, but Mendeleev lived before scientists even knew about protons.

choices.text: He gave each element a symbol of one or two letters
choices.feedback: The passage mentions element symbols as a separate fact about the periodic table and never connects them to Mendeleev or his discovery.

choices.text: He arranged the elements by their atomic weight
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly says Mendeleev arranged elements by atomic weight first, and that step is what led him to notice the repeating pattern.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Elements Inside You and Around You

Your body is a collection of elements working together. Oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen make up about 96 percent of you. Carbon forms the framework of proteins, fats, and even your DNA. Iron in your blood carries oxygen to your muscles, and calcium gives your bones their strength.

Look around your room, and you will spot elements everywhere. Aluminum shapes the soda can on your desk. Copper carries electricity through the wires in your walls. Silicon — a hard, grayish element found in sand — powers the chip inside your computer. Helium, the lightweight gas, makes balloons float toward the ceiling.
stimulus.content_text: Elements Inside You and Around You Your body is a collection of elements working together. Oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen make up about 96 percent of you. Carbon forms the framework of proteins, fats, and even your DNA. Iron in your blood carries oxygen to your muscles, and calcium gives your bones their strength. Look around your room, and you will spot elements everywhere. Aluminum shapes the soda can on your desk. Copper carries electricity through the wires in your walls. Silicon — a hard, grayish element found in sand — powers the chip inside your computer. Helium, the lightweight gas, makes balloons float toward the ceiling.

prompt._: The author uses the word "carries" to describe both iron and copper. Based on the text, how is the meaning of "carries" similar in both examples?

choices.text: It means the element floats upward toward the ceiling.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because floating upward describes helium and balloons, not iron or copper.

choices.text: It means the element lifts and holds a heavy object.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither iron nor copper is described as lifting or holding a heavy object — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

choices.text: It means the element builds a hard, solid structure.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'carries' is not about building a hard structure — that's what calcium does when it gives bones their strength.

choices.text: It means the element moves something to a place.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both iron and copper move something (oxygen and electricity) from one place to another, which is exactly what 'carries' means in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
When Elements Team Up

When two or more elements bond together, they form a new substance called a compound. Here is the surprising part: water seems simple, but it is not an element. Water is a compound made of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom. Table salt seems like a single, pure substance too, but it is a compound of sodium and chlorine. Compounds look and behave completely differently from the elements that make them up. Hydrogen is a gas. Oxygen is a gas. Yet together, they form liquid water.
stimulus.content_text: When Elements Team Up When two or more elements bond together, they form a new substance called a compound. Here is the surprising part: water seems simple, but it is not an element. Water is a compound made of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom. Table salt seems like a single, pure substance too, but it is a compound of sodium and chlorine. Compounds look and behave completely differently from the elements that make them up. Hydrogen is a gas. Oxygen is a gas. Yet together, they form liquid water.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a compound?

choices.text: a substance that is made of only one kind of atom
choices.feedback: This is actually the definition of an element, not a compound — a compound is made of two or more kinds of atoms bonded together.

choices.text: a gas that is created when hydrogen and oxygen are separated
choices.feedback: This is wrong because compounds are created when elements join together, and the passage never says a gas is created when hydrogen and oxygen are separated.

choices.text: a new substance formed when two or more elements bond together
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says exactly this: when two or more elements bond together, they form a new substance called a compound.

choices.text: another word for any element found in nature
choices.feedback: This is wrong because compounds and elements are different things — the whole passage explains how compounds are formed FROM elements.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why 118 Elements Matter to You

Scientists use their knowledge of elements to develop new medicines, build stronger bridges, and search for signs of life on other planets. When astronomers study distant stars, they read the light those stars give off — and that light reveals which elements are present, even from billions of miles away. The same 118 elements that make up your bones, your bicycle, and the air in your lungs also make up every star, every planet, and every moon in the universe. You are literally built from the same stuff as the stars.
stimulus.content_text: Why 118 Elements Matter to You Scientists use their knowledge of elements to develop new medicines, build stronger bridges, and search for signs of life on other planets. When astronomers study distant stars, they read the light those stars give off — and that light reveals which elements are present, even from billions of miles away. The same 118 elements that make up your bones, your bicycle, and the air in your lungs also make up every star, every planet, and every moon in the universe. You are literally built from the same stuff as the stars.

prompt._: Which detail from the text gives the best evidence that elements are found everywhere in the universe?

choices.text: Astronomers read the light given off by distant stars.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because reading starlight tells us how astronomers do their work, but it doesn't directly show that the same elements are found everywhere.

choices.text: The exact same elements make up bones and planets.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that the exact same 118 elements make up your bones AND every star, planet, and moon — proving elements are found throughout the entire universe.

choices.text: Scientists search for signs of life on other planets.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because searching for signs of life tells us what scientists are curious about, not that elements themselves are found everywhere in the universe.

choices.text: Scientists use their knowledge to build stronger bridges.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because building stronger bridges is something that happens on Earth, so it doesn't give evidence that elements are found everywhere in the universe.

prompt._: Based on the text, how do elements like oganesson contrast with elements like carbon and aluminum?

choices.text: Oganesson is discovered in distant stars, while carbon and aluminum are found inside the human body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions oganesson being discovered in stars — it says oganesson exists briefly in laboratories.

choices.text: Oganesson forms strong chemical compounds, while carbon and aluminum remain as pure, single atoms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says oganesson forms strong compounds or that carbon and aluminum stay as pure single atoms — that's not information the text gives us.

choices.text: Oganesson exists for a short time in labs, while carbon and aluminum make up everyday objects.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states oganesson exists only briefly in labs, while carbon and aluminum are described as common elements found in everyday objects like your body and soda cans.

choices.text: Oganesson is a heavy metal found in creeks, while carbon and aluminum act as invisible gases.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because oganesson is not described as a heavy metal found in creeks, and carbon and aluminum are solid materials, not invisible gases.

prompt._: Based on the article, why does knowing about elements help astronomers study distant stars?

choices.text: because elements in stars create gravity that pulls light toward Earth
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions elements in stars creating gravity that pulls light toward Earth — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

choices.text: because stars give off light that reveals which elements they contain
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly states that stars give off light, and that light reveals which elements are present inside the star.

choices.text: because the periodic table lists stars alongside the 118 elements
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the periodic table is only a chart that organizes the 118 elements — it does not list stars.

choices.text: because astronomers use compounds instead of elements to read light
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says astronomers read the light from stars to find elements, not compounds.

prompt._: Which sequence best summarizes the text's main idea about how nature's building blocks create new substances?

choices.text: First, compounds make up individual elements, and then different elements bond together to form atoms.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because compounds are made FROM elements, not the other way around — elements don't come from compounds.

choices.text: First, atoms make up individual elements, and then different elements bond together to form compounds.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly shows that atoms build elements, and then elements bond together to form compounds like water and table salt.

choices.text: First, molecules make up individual elements, and then different elements break apart to form atoms.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says molecules are atoms bonded together within an element, and it never describes elements breaking apart to form atoms.

choices.text: First, atoms make up individual compounds, and then different compounds bond together to form elements.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because atoms make up elements, not compounds, and compounds are formed when elements join together.

prompt._: Which idea does the author support by comparing the elements in your body with the elements in stars and planets?

choices.text: Everything in space is made of heavier elements than living things on Earth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the weight of space elements to Earth elements — that idea isn't in the article at all.

choices.text: Only a few special elements are important enough to appear in many places.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author says all 118 elements matter and are used in medicines, bridges, and space exploration — not just a few special ones.

choices.text: Elements in the human body are different from elements found in rocks and air.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite: the elements in your body, like oxygen and carbon, are the same elements found in rocks, air, and even distant stars.

choices.text: The same set of elements can be found in people, objects, and the universe.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the same 118 elements make up your body, everyday objects, and everything in the universe, showing they appear everywhere.

syllabus_metadata.title: Elements: Nature's Ingredients

assessments.title: Molecules and Compounds: Atoms Join Forces

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
When Atoms Team Up: Molecules and Compounds

A Dangerous Recipe for Something Safe

Put these two gases in a room together, and you have the ingredients for an explosion. But somehow, two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom create water — the exact liquid firefighters spray to put fires out. That strange fact sits at the heart of how atoms build the world around you.
stimulus.content_text: When Atoms Team Up: Molecules and CompoundsA Dangerous Recipe for Something Safe Put these two gases in a room together, and you have the ingredients for an explosion. But somehow, two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom create water — the exact liquid firefighters spray to put fires out. That strange fact sits at the heart of how atoms build the world around you.

prompt._: According to the text, how are hydrogen and oxygen different from the water they create?

choices.text: They are gases that can cause an explosion, but water is a liquid used to fight fires.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly says hydrogen and oxygen are gases that can cause an explosion, while water is a liquid used to fight fires.

choices.text: They are liquids that can cause an explosion, but water is a liquid used to fight fires.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes hydrogen and oxygen as gases, not liquids.

choices.text: They are gases that can cause an explosion, but water is a gas used to fight fires.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us water is a liquid, not a gas.

choices.text: They are atoms that stay apart, but water is a compound that stays together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the atoms do bond together to form water, so they don't stay apart.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Atoms That Stick Together

Most atoms do not drift around alone. They connect to other atoms, and when two or more atoms attach to each other, they form a molecule — a tiny cluster of atoms joined together, like LEGO bricks snapped into a small shape. Molecules are the building blocks of nearly everything you see, eat, drink, and breathe.

But how do atoms hold on to each other? They form a chemical bond, a strong connection made when atoms share or swap even tinier particles called electrons. Think of a chemical bond like two friends gripping the same jump rope handle — neither one lets go easily, and together they act as a unit.

Some molecules are made of atoms from the same element. The oxygen you breathe right now is actually two oxygen atoms bonded together. Scientists write this as O₂. The "O" stands for oxygen, and the small "2" tells you there are two atoms in each molecule. Nitrogen gas, the most common gas in the air around you, is two nitrogen atoms bonded together, written as N₂. These molecules contain only one element, so they are still considered elements — not compounds.

You might think that every molecule is a compound. Actually, a molecule only counts as a compound when it contains atoms of different elements. O₂ is a molecule, yes, but it is not a compound because both atoms are oxygen.
stimulus.content_text: Atoms That Stick Together Most atoms do not drift around alone. They connect to other atoms, and when two or more atoms attach to each other, they form a molecule — a tiny cluster of atoms joined together, like LEGO bricks snapped into a small shape. Molecules are the building blocks of nearly everything you see, eat, drink, and breathe. But how do atoms hold on to each other? They form a chemical bond, a strong connection made when atoms share or swap even tinier particles called electrons. Think of a chemical bond like two friends gripping the same jump rope handle — neither one lets go easily, and together they act as a unit. Some molecules are made of atoms from the same element. The oxygen you breathe right now is actually two oxygen atoms bonded together. Scientists write this as O₂. The "O" stands for oxygen, and the small "2" tells you there are two atoms in each molecule. Nitrogen gas, the most common gas in the air around you, is two nitrogen atoms bonded together, written as N₂. These molecules contain only one element, so they are still considered elements — not compounds. You might think that every molecule is a compound. Actually, a molecule only counts as a compound when it contains atoms of different elements. O₂ is a molecule, yes, but it is not a compound because both atoms are oxygen.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is O₂ NOT considered a compound even though it is a molecule?

choices.text: because both of its atoms are the same element
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states a compound must have atoms of different elements, and O₂ only has oxygen atoms.

choices.text: because its atoms do not form a chemical bond
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly explains that atoms in O₂ are bonded together through a chemical bond.

choices.text: because it is a gas instead of a solid or liquid
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says nothing about a substance's state (gas, liquid, or solid) affecting whether it is a compound.

choices.text: because it contains too few atoms to be a compound
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a minimum number of atoms being required to make something a compound.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Compounds: New Substances with New Rules

A compound is a substance made of two or more different elements chemically bonded together — like a recipe that mixes different ingredients to create something entirely new. Water is one of the most familiar compounds on Earth. Each water molecule has two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom. Scientists write its chemical formula as H₂O. The "H" stands for hydrogen, the small "2" means two hydrogen atoms, and the "O" stands for one oxygen atom.

You already know hydrogen burns and oxygen helps things burn. Yet water puts out fire. The bond between those atoms creates a completely different substance with completely different properties.

Table salt gives another striking example. Salt is a compound made of sodium and chlorine, and its scientific name is sodium chloride. Sodium is a soft, silvery metal that reacts violently when it touches water. Chlorine is a poisonous, greenish gas with a sharp smell. Neither one is safe to eat on its own. But when sodium and chlorine atoms bond, the result is the harmless, tasty salt you sprinkle on popcorn. The atoms didn't just sit next to each other. They formed bonds and became an entirely new substance.
stimulus.content_text: Compounds: New Substances with New Rules A compound is a substance made of two or more different elements chemically bonded together — like a recipe that mixes different ingredients to create something entirely new. Water is one of the most familiar compounds on Earth. Each water molecule has two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom. Scientists write its chemical formula as H₂O. The "H" stands for hydrogen, the small "2" means two hydrogen atoms, and the "O" stands for one oxygen atom. You already know hydrogen burns and oxygen helps things burn. Yet water puts out fire. The bond between those atoms creates a completely different substance with completely different properties. Table salt gives another striking example. Salt is a compound made of sodium and chlorine, and its scientific name is sodium chloride. Sodium is a soft, silvery metal that reacts violently when it touches water. Chlorine is a poisonous, greenish gas with a sharp smell. Neither one is safe to eat on its own. But when sodium and chlorine atoms bond, the result is the harmless, tasty salt you sprinkle on popcorn. The atoms didn't just sit next to each other. They formed bonds and became an entirely new substance.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after sodium and chlorine atoms bond together?

choices.text: They turn into a poisonous gas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because chlorine is the poisonous gas on its own — once it bonds with sodium, it becomes safe table salt, not a poisonous gas.

choices.text: They just sit next to each other.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says 'The atoms didn't just sit next to each other' — they formed bonds and became an entirely new substance.

choices.text: They react violently with the water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sodium reacts violently with water by itself, but that is not what happens after sodium and chlorine bond together.

choices.text: They become a harmless, tasty salt.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that when sodium and chlorine bond, the result is 'the harmless, tasty salt you sprinkle on popcorn.'

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Compounds Are Not Mixtures

It's easy to assume that stirring salt into water creates a new compound. After all, the salt seems to disappear. But the salt only dissolved — it broke into tiny pieces too small to see. You can boil the water away and find the salt sitting at the bottom of the pot, unchanged. That makes salt water a mixture, a combination where the substances sit together but do not form new chemical bonds. In a true compound, atoms are bonded so tightly that you cannot simply pull them apart by boiling or stirring.
stimulus.content_text: Compounds Are Not Mixtures It's easy to assume that stirring salt into water creates a new compound. After all, the salt seems to disappear. But the salt only dissolved — it broke into tiny pieces too small to see. You can boil the water away and find the salt sitting at the bottom of the pot, unchanged. That makes salt water a mixture, a combination where the substances sit together but do not form new chemical bonds. In a true compound, atoms are bonded so tightly that you cannot simply pull them apart by boiling or stirring.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are compounds and mixtures different from each other?

choices.text: Compounds are made of tiny pieces, but mixtures are made of large pieces
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about mixtures being made of large pieces — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

choices.text: Compounds always involve water, but mixtures never involve water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses salt water as an example of a mixture, which means mixtures can definitely involve water.

choices.text: Compounds have atoms bonded tightly together, but mixtures do not
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that compounds have atoms bonded tightly together, while mixtures are just substances combined without forming new chemical bonds.

choices.text: Compounds can be separated by boiling, but mixtures cannot be taken apart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect and backwards — the passage shows that mixtures like salt water CAN be separated by boiling, not compounds.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Fixed Recipes, Every Single Time

Atoms bond together in exact ratios — specific amounts that never change, like a recipe that must be followed precisely. Every molecule of water always has exactly two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. Every molecule of carbon dioxide — the gas you breathe out — always has one carbon atom bonded to two oxygen atoms, written as CO₂. Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air and use it, along with sunlight and water, to make their own food. If you changed the ratio of atoms, you would get a different substance entirely.

Water has just three atoms. Others are far more complex. A single molecule of table sugar contains twelve carbon atoms, twenty-two hydrogen atoms, and eleven oxygen atoms. Scientists write it as C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁. Simple or complex, every compound follows its own exact formula.
stimulus.content_text: Fixed Recipes, Every Single Time Atoms bond together in exact ratios — specific amounts that never change, like a recipe that must be followed precisely. Every molecule of water always has exactly two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. Every molecule of carbon dioxide — the gas you breathe out — always has one carbon atom bonded to two oxygen atoms, written as CO₂. Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air and use it, along with sunlight and water, to make their own food. If you changed the ratio of atoms, you would get a different substance entirely. Water has just three atoms. Others are far more complex. A single molecule of table sugar contains twelve carbon atoms, twenty-two hydrogen atoms, and eleven oxygen atoms. Scientists write it as C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁. Simple or complex, every compound follows its own exact formula.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information in the section 'Fixed Recipes, Every Single Time'?

choices.text: by listing events in the order they happen over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't describe events happening in a time order, like first, next, and finally.

choices.text: by describing a problem with atoms and then a solution
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author never presents a problem with atoms or offers a solution to fix it.

choices.text: by giving a definition and then supporting it with examples
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author defines ratios as exact recipes, then supports that idea with examples like water, carbon dioxide, and table sugar.

choices.text: by comparing molecules to see which ones are the biggest
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage isn't comparing molecules to rank them by size — it's showing that every molecule has its own exact formula.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Language Every Scientist Shares

When a chemist in Japan writes H₂O, a chemist in Brazil knows exactly which atoms are involved and how many. This shared language lets researchers across the globe trade discoveries and build on each other's work.

Chemistry is not hidden in a lab somewhere. It is sitting on your dinner table.
stimulus.content_text: A Language Every Scientist Shares When a chemist in Japan writes H₂O, a chemist in Brazil knows exactly which atoms are involved and how many. This shared language lets researchers across the globe trade discoveries and build on each other's work. Chemistry is not hidden in a lab somewhere. It is sitting on your dinner table.

prompt._: In the section "A Language Every Scientist Shares," how does the author support the point that scientists have a shared language?

choices.text: By explaining that researchers travel to different countries to share their discoveries.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says researchers travel to different countries — it says they share written formulas, not that they visit each other.

choices.text: By describing how chemistry is found on dinner tables instead of inside laboratories.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the dinner table detail is about chemistry being part of everyday life, not about how scientists communicate with each other.

choices.text: By showing that chemists in different countries understand the same written formula.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly shows that a chemist in Japan and a chemist in Brazil both understand the written formula H₂O, which proves scientists share a common language.

choices.text: By listing the specific atoms that are needed to create a simple glass of water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses H₂O as an example of a shared formula, but it never lists or explains the specific atoms needed to make water.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the main difference between a compound and a mixture?

choices.text: A compound is always a safe liquid, but a mixture is a dangerous gas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because compounds are not always safe liquids — sodium and chlorine are both dangerous on their own, and the article never says all compounds are liquids or all mixtures are gases.

choices.text: A compound has only one single element, but a mixture has many elements.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's backwards — a compound is made of two or more *different* elements bonded together, not just one single element.

choices.text: A compound is easily boiled apart, but a mixture stays locked together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the opposite — mixtures can be separated by boiling or stirring, while compounds have atoms bonded so tightly they cannot be pulled apart that easily.

choices.text: A compound creates a new substance, but a mixture just sits together.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that in a compound, atoms bond tightly to form a brand-new substance, while in a mixture the substances just sit together without bonding and can be separated.

prompt._: How does the author use a cause-and-effect structure to explain what happens when atoms form a compound?

choices.text: By detailing how atoms follow exact recipes to cause green plants to make their food.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because plants making food from carbon dioxide is just a small supporting detail in the passage, not the main cause-and-effect idea used to explain how compounds are formed.

choices.text: By explaining how someone stirs ingredients together to cause a mixture to boil away completely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stirring ingredients together creates a mixture, not a compound, and the passage says you can boil water away to find the salt unchanged — nothing disappears completely.

choices.text: By showing how atoms share tiny electrons to cause them to act as one unit.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sharing electrons explains how chemical bonds form, which is about molecules in general, not the cause-and-effect of what happens specifically when a compound is created.

choices.text: By describing how dangerous elements bond together to cause the creation of safe new substances.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author clearly shows that dangerous elements (like sodium and chlorine) bonding together causes the creation of safe new substances (like table salt), which is a cause-and-effect structure about compounds.

prompt._: According to the article, what is the difference between a molecule and a compound?

choices.text: A molecule is made only in a lab, but a compound is made only when substances dissolve in water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says molecules are made only in labs, and it actually explains that dissolving creates a mixture, not a compound.

choices.text: A molecule is any substance that can dissolve in water, but a compound is any substance that cannot.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never connects molecules or compounds to whether they can dissolve in water — that's about mixtures, not molecules.

choices.text: A molecule is always a compound, but a compound is not always a mixture of different elements.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the opposite — not every molecule is a compound, only those with atoms from different elements.

choices.text: A molecule is any group of bonded atoms, but a compound must have atoms from different elements.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says a molecule is any group of bonded atoms, while a compound specifically requires atoms from two or more different elements — like how O₂ is a molecule but not a compound.

prompt._: According to the text, how is a molecule of water similar to a molecule of table sugar?

choices.text: They both are compounds that follow an exact recipe of bonded atoms.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that both water and table sugar are compounds that always follow an exact, fixed recipe of chemically bonded atoms that never changes.

choices.text: They both are molecules that contain carbon atoms chemically bonded to oxygen atoms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because water is made of only hydrogen and oxygen atoms, with no carbon atoms at all, so water and sugar do not share that similarity.

choices.text: They both are elements that consist of only one single type of atom.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both water and sugar are compounds made of different kinds of atoms bonded together, which is the opposite of an element that has only one type of atom.

choices.text: They both are mixtures that can be easily separated by boiling the water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because water and sugar are compounds with atoms bonded tightly together, not mixtures — the passage says mixtures can be separated by boiling, but true compounds cannot.

syllabus_metadata.title: Molecules and Compounds: Atoms Join Forces

assessments.title: Mixtures and Solutions

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Mixtures and Solutions: The Science of Stuff That Doesn't Fully Combine

A Handful of Trail Mix

Reach into a bag of trail mix and pull out a single handful. Right there in your palm, you can spot raisins, peanuts, chocolate chips, and pretzels — all jumbled together but completely themselves. Nothing has changed about any piece just because it is sitting next to the others. You could sort every ingredient back into its own pile if you wanted to.
stimulus.content_text: Mixtures and Solutions: The Science of Stuff That Doesn't Fully CombineA Handful of Trail Mix Reach into a bag of trail mix and pull out a single handful. Right there in your palm, you can spot raisins, peanuts, chocolate chips, and pretzels — all jumbled together but completely themselves. Nothing has changed about any piece just because it is sitting next to the others. You could sort every ingredient back into its own pile if you wanted to.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens to the ingredients in trail mix even though they are all jumbled together?

choices.text: They slowly take on the flavor of each other
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about flavors mixing or ingredients taking on each other's taste.

choices.text: They become impossible to sort into piles
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — you could sort every ingredient back into its own pile if you wanted to.

choices.text: They blend into one new ingredient over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that nothing changes about any piece, so the ingredients never blend into one new ingredient.

choices.text: They stay completely themselves without changing
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses the exact words 'completely themselves' to show that every ingredient stays the same even when jumbled together.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Mixtures You Cannot Pick Apart

Trail mix is easy to sort because you can see each piece. But imagine mixing a spoonful of salt with a spoonful of sand. Both are small white or tan grains. You could not separate them with your fingers. Does that mean they have become one new substance? No — you just need a smarter way to separate them.

Two physical steps — filtering and evaporation — can pull the mixture apart. First, dissolve the salt in water, leaving the sand behind. Now pour the wet mixture through a filter — a material with tiny holes that catches solid pieces, like a strainer catching pasta. The sand stays in the filter while the salty water passes through. Next, heat the salty water until it evaporates — turning from liquid into gas, the way a puddle vanishes on hot pavement. The water disappears into the air, and the salt is left behind.

A magnet can separate certain mixtures, too. Picture a cup of sand with tiny bits of iron mixed in. Run a magnet over the surface. The iron pieces leap up and cling to the magnet while the sand stays still. No chemical reaction needed — just a physical pull.
stimulus.content_text: Mixtures You Cannot Pick Apart Trail mix is easy to sort because you can see each piece. But imagine mixing a spoonful of salt with a spoonful of sand. Both are small white or tan grains. You could not separate them with your fingers. Does that mean they have become one new substance? No — you just need a smarter way to separate them. Two physical steps — filtering and evaporation — can pull the mixture apart. First, dissolve the salt in water, leaving the sand behind. Now pour the wet mixture through a filter — a material with tiny holes that catches solid pieces, like a strainer catching pasta. The sand stays in the filter while the salty water passes through. Next, heat the salty water until it evaporates — turning from liquid into gas, the way a puddle vanishes on hot pavement. The water disappears into the air, and the salt is left behind. A magnet can separate certain mixtures, too. Picture a cup of sand with tiny bits of iron mixed in. Run a magnet over the surface. The iron pieces leap up and cling to the magnet while the sand stays still. No chemical reaction needed — just a physical pull.

prompt._: Which detail best supports the main idea that physical actions cause mixtures to separate?

choices.text: Heating salty water causes the liquid to evaporate and leave salt.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that heating water (a physical action called evaporation) separates the salt from the water, which directly supports the main idea.

choices.text: Mixing salt and sand causes the grains to form a brand new substance.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — mixing salt and sand does NOT form a brand new substance.

choices.text: Sorting a trail mix causes the tiny pieces to become easier to see.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sorting trail mix is just about seeing the pieces more easily, not about how physical actions cause separation.

choices.text: Using a strong magnet causes a chemical reaction to move the iron.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says a magnet separates iron with no chemical reaction — it is a physical pull, not a chemical one.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Solutions: A Special Kind of Mixture

Drop a spoonful of sugar into a glass of warm water and stir. The sugar seems to vanish, but it has not disappeared. Every single particle of sugar is still there. The sugar has broken into pieces so tiny that your eyes cannot see them, and those pieces have spread evenly throughout the water. That is why every sip tastes equally sweet, whether you drink from the top or the bottom of the glass.

This even, invisible blend is called a solution — a special type of mixture in which one substance dissolves completely into another. In your sugar water, the sugar is the solute — the substance that dissolves, like a guest moving into a new home. The water is the solvent — the substance that does the dissolving, like the home that takes the guest in. Water can dissolve more substances than any other common liquid, which is why scientists call it the "universal solvent."

Here is the surprising part: dissolving is a physical change, not a chemical one. The sugar particles are still sugar. Leave your sugar water in a sunny spot. The water evaporates, and dry sugar crystals appear — the same sugar you started with. If a chemical reaction had happened, you would not be able to recover the original substance so easily.

Every single particle of sugar is still there. The sugar has broken into pieces so tiny that your eyes cannot see them, and those pieces have spread evenly throughout the water. That is why every sip tastes equally sweet, whether you drink from the top or the bottom of the glass. This even, invisible blend is called a solution — a special type of mixture in which one substance dissolves completely into another. In your sugar water, the sugar is the solute — the substance that dissolves, like a guest moving into a new home. The water is the solvent — the substance that does the dissolving, like the home that takes the guest in. Water can dissolve more substances than any other common liquid, which is why scientists call it the "universal solvent." Here is the surprising part: dissolving is a physical change, not a chemical one. The sugar particles are still sugar. Leave your sugar water in a sunny spot. The water evaporates, and dry sugar crystals appear — the same sugar you started with. If a chemical reaction had happened, you would not be able to recover the original substance so easily.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after the water evaporates from the sugar water?

choices.text: The water becomes sweeter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because once the water evaporates, there is no water left in the glass to taste sweet.

choices.text: Dry sugar crystals appear.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that when the water evaporates, dry sugar crystals appear — proving the sugar was there all along.

choices.text: Tiny sugar pieces vanish.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that the tiny sugar pieces spread evenly through the water instead of vanishing.

choices.text: A chemical reaction begins.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us that dissolving is a physical change, not a chemical reaction.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Solutions Are Everywhere

Solutions are not just something you make in a kitchen. The air you breathe is a solution of gases — mainly nitrogen, which makes up about 78 percent of air, and oxygen, which makes up about 21 percent. Ocean water is a solution of water and dissolved salts, about 3.5 percent salt by weight. Even the blood moving through your body is a solution carrying dissolved nutrients — substances your cells need for energy and growth — along with gases and waste.

Because ocean water is a solution, its salt can be separated by evaporation. Workers in coastal countries fill wide, shallow pools with ocean water and let the sun evaporate the water over days or weeks. What remains is a layer of salt crystals, pulled from a solution by nothing more than sunlight and patience.

But not everything dissolves in everything. Pour oil into water and shake hard. The oil breaks into tiny droplets for a moment, then floats right back to the top. The tiny particles in oil and water do not attract each other strongly enough to blend. That is why salad dressing separates if you let it sit for just a few minutes.

Even the blood moving through your body is a solution carrying dissolved nutrients — substances your cells need for energy and growth — along with gases and waste. prompt._: Based on the passage, how is a mixture of oil and water different from ocean water?

choices.text: Its parts do not stay blended.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says oil floats back to the top after shaking, which means its parts do not stay blended — unlike ocean water, where the salt stays dissolved.

choices.text: Its parts are made of nitrogen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because nitrogen is found in air, not in oil or ocean water.

choices.text: Its parts separate in sunlight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is ocean water, not oil and water, that separates in sunlight through evaporation.

choices.text: Its parts carry cell nutrients.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blood is what carries nutrients to cells, not oil and water or ocean water.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why This Matters to You

The difference between a mixture and a solution comes down to one key idea: how evenly the parts blend together. In a solution, particles spread so evenly that a single drop contains the same ratio of solute to solvent as the entire container. Once you understand that, you start noticing mixtures and solutions everywhere: in the soap in your shower and in the soil beneath your feet.
stimulus.content_text: Why This Matters to You The difference between a mixture and a solution comes down to one key idea: how evenly the parts blend together. In a solution, particles spread so evenly that a single drop contains the same ratio of solute to solvent as the entire container. Once you understand that, you start noticing mixtures and solutions everywhere: in the soap in your shower and in the soil beneath your feet.

prompt._: Based on the text, what is the meaning of the word "solution"?

choices.text: A material that is made entirely of soap and soil.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because soap and soil are just real-life examples in the passage, not the definition of a solution.

choices.text: A container that holds only a single drop of liquid.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions a single drop only to show how evenly a solution is mixed, not to say a solution is a container for one drop.

choices.text: A mixture where the parts do not blend together evenly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes the opposite of a solution — the passage says solutions blend evenly, not unevenly.

choices.text: A blend where the particles are spread out completely evenly.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says particles in a solution spread out completely evenly, which is exactly what this answer describes.

prompt._: Based on the text, how is a handful of trail mix different from a glass of sugar water?

choices.text: Trail mix requires heavy magnets to separate, while sugar water requires filters to pull apart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says magnets separate iron from sand, and filters separate sand from salty water — these methods are not used for trail mix or sugar water.

choices.text: Trail mix forms a completely new substance, while sugar water keeps its original properties.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says trail mix does NOT form a new substance — each ingredient stays exactly the same as before it was mixed.

choices.text: Trail mix is separated by extreme heating, while sugar water is separated by using fingers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says trail mix can be sorted by hand (fingers), not by heating, and sugar water is separated by evaporation, not fingers.

choices.text: Trail mix contains visible pieces, while sugar water has invisible pieces that blend evenly.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states trail mix has visible pieces you can see and sort, while sugar water has particles too tiny to see that spread evenly throughout the liquid.

prompt._: Based on the article, why can workers in coastal countries get salt back from ocean water by letting it sit in the sun?

choices.text: Because the sun causes a chemical reaction that creates new salt crystals
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that evaporation is a physical change, not a chemical reaction — no new substance is being created.

choices.text: Because the salt floats to the top of the water like oil does
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because salt dissolves evenly into water and stays mixed throughout — it does not float to the top the way oil does.

choices.text: Because the sun causes the salt to dissolve faster in the water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sun's role here is to evaporate the water away, not to speed up how fast the salt dissolves.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that sunlight evaporates the water from the shallow pools, leaving salt crystals behind.

choices.text: Solutions that appear in everyday life, then mixtures that you can pick apart, then magnets that pull substances apart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article introduces simple mixtures before solutions, and the magnet example appears in the middle of the article, not at the end.

choices.text: Solutions that are easy to see, then mixtures that are hard to sort, then chemical changes that transform substances
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never describes chemical changes that transform substances — it actually explains that dissolving is a physical change.

prompt._: Which sentence best summarizes the article's main idea comparing mixtures and solutions?

choices.text: Both are chemical changes of materials, but only solutions have parts that change into new matter.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article specifically says dissolving is a physical change, not a chemical change, so nothing turns into new matter.

choices.text: Both are natural combinations of liquids, but only solutions have parts that float right to the top.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because mixtures and solutions aren't only liquids — the article gives examples like trail mix (solid) and air (gas).

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains both are physical blends, but solutions are unique because their parts spread completely and evenly throughout, like sugar in water.

syllabus_metadata.title: Mixtures and Solutions

assessments.title: Physical Changes vs. Chemical Changes

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Same Stuff or New Stuff? Physical and Chemical Changes

A Campfire's Secret

You could wait a thousand years, and that ash will never become a log again. Something happened inside the wood that changed it into completely different stuff. But earlier that day, you sliced a piece of cheese for a sandwich. The cheese changed shape, yet it was still cheese. Both events changed matter, but they changed it in very different ways.
stimulus.content_text: Same Stuff or New Stuff? Physical and Chemical ChangesA Campfire's Secret You could wait a thousand years, and that ash will never become a log again. Something happened inside the wood that changed it into completely different stuff. But earlier that day, you sliced a piece of cheese for a sandwich. The cheese changed shape, yet it was still cheese. Both events changed matter, but they changed it in very different ways.

prompt._: According to the text, how is slicing cheese different from burning a log?

choices.text: The cheese turned into completely different stuff after being sliced
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the cheese did NOT turn into completely different stuff, it was still cheese after being sliced.

choices.text: The cheese and the log both changed into different materials
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the log changed into a different material (ash); the cheese stayed cheese, just in a different shape.

choices.text: The cheese changed shape but was still cheese afterward
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states the cheese 'changed shape, yet it was still cheese,' which is exactly what makes it different from the burning log.

choices.text: The cheese could never go back to its original form
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes the log, not the cheese — the passage says the ash will 'never become a log again,' not the cheese.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Physical Changes: A New Look, the Same Stuff

A physical change alters the form or appearance of a substance without creating anything new. Think of it like rearranging furniture in your room. The room looks different, but you still have the same couch, the same desk, the same bed.

Ice melting into water is a classic example. Solid ice and liquid water look and feel different, but they are made of the same tiny particles called molecules — the smallest pieces of a substance that still act like that substance, the way a single LEGO brick is the smallest unit that is still recognizably LEGO. Water molecules stay water molecules whether they are frozen solid or flowing as a liquid. Pop that water back into the freezer, and you get ice again. Most physical changes can be reversed like this.

Dissolving sugar in water is a physical change, too. Actually, the sugar molecules are still there, just spread out among the water molecules. Boil the water away, and sugar crystals reappear on the bottom of the pot.

Here's a surprising part: physical changes can be enormous and dramatic. A giant machine at a junkyard crushes a car into a flat metal cube. That is loud, violent, and impressive — but the metal is still the same metal. The size or drama of a change does not tell you whether it is physical or chemical.
stimulus.content_text: Physical Changes: A New Look, the Same Stuff A physical change alters the form or appearance of a substance without creating anything new. Think of it like rearranging furniture in your room. The room looks different, but you still have the same couch, the same desk, the same bed. Ice melting into water is a classic example. Solid ice and liquid water look and feel different, but they are made of the same tiny particles called molecules — the smallest pieces of a substance that still act like that substance, the way a single LEGO brick is the smallest unit that is still recognizably LEGO. Water molecules stay water molecules whether they are frozen solid or flowing as a liquid. Pop that water back into the freezer, and you get ice again. Most physical changes can be reversed like this. Dissolving sugar in water is a physical change, too. Actually, the sugar molecules are still there, just spread out among the water molecules. Boil the water away, and sugar crystals reappear on the bottom of the pot. Here's a surprising part: physical changes can be enormous and dramatic. A giant machine at a junkyard crushes a car into a flat metal cube. That is loud, violent, and impressive — but the metal is still the same metal. The size or drama of a change does not tell you whether it is physical or chemical.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is melting ice called a physical change?

choices.text: Because the water molecules stay the same
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that water molecules stay water molecules whether frozen or liquid, meaning the substance never changes.

choices.text: Because the liquid cannot freeze again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says you can put water back in the freezer to get ice again, showing the change can be reversed.

choices.text: Because the ice turns into a new substance
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ice and liquid water are made of the same molecules — no new substance is created.

choices.text: Because the change is easy to see
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that how visible or dramatic a change is does not determine whether it is physical or chemical.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Chemical Changes: Brand-New Substances

A chemical change produces one or more entirely new substances with different properties. When wood burns, it reacts with oxygen in the air and transforms into ash, carbon dioxide gas, and water vapor. Those are not just different shapes of wood. They are different materials altogether. You cannot un-burn a log.

Chemical changes can also be quiet and slow. A shiny iron nail left outside in the rain gradually turns reddish-brown. The iron reacts with oxygen and water to form iron oxide — the scientific name for rust. Iron oxide has a different color, a different texture, and different properties than the original iron. That nail didn't just change its look. It became a new substance.
stimulus.content_text: Chemical Changes: Brand-New Substances A chemical change produces one or more entirely new substances with different properties. When wood burns, it reacts with oxygen in the air and transforms into ash, carbon dioxide gas, and water vapor. Those are not just different shapes of wood. They are different materials altogether. You cannot un-burn a log. Chemical changes can also be quiet and slow. A shiny iron nail left outside in the rain gradually turns reddish-brown. The iron reacts with oxygen and water to form iron oxide — the scientific name for rust. Iron oxide has a different color, a different texture, and different properties than the original iron. That nail didn't just change its look. It became a new substance.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the correct order of events that happens when an iron nail is left outside in the rain?

choices.text: The nail changes color, then reacts with oxygen and water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the reddish-brown color change happens as a result of the chemical reaction, meaning the reaction comes first, not the color change.

choices.text: The nail reacts with oxygen and water, then turns reddish-brown
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the iron reacts with oxygen and water, and that reaction is what produces the reddish-brown rust — reaction first, color change second.

choices.text: The nail turns reddish-brown, then loses its shiny appearance
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not describe losing shininess and turning reddish-brown as two separate steps in that order — they are both part of the same change caused by the reaction.

choices.text: The nail forms rust, then reacts with oxygen and water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rust (iron oxide) is formed as a result of the reaction with oxygen and water, so the reaction must come first, not after.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Clues That a Chemical Change Happened

Scientists watch for several clues. A color change is one sign, like that nail turning reddish-brown as rust forms. But be careful — not every color change means a chemical change. Mix red paint and blue paint and you get purple, but no new substance is created. That is just a physical mixture. Clues are hints, not proof, so scientists often check more than one.

The production of gas is another clue. Drop a fizzing tablet into a glass of water. Bubbles of carbon dioxide rush to the surface. Those bubbles tell you a chemical reaction is happening. But remember, boiling water also makes bubbles, and boiling is a physical change. One clue alone is not enough.

A temperature change can also signal a chemical change. A burning match releases heat. A cold pack — the kind you squeeze when you twist your ankle — absorbs heat during a chemical reaction and turns icy cold.

Finally, the formation of a precipitate is a clue. A precipitate is a solid that appears when two liquids are mixed, like a clump forming out of nowhere in a clear solution. Imagine stirring two clear liquids together and suddenly seeing cloudy chunks appear at the bottom. That new solid did not exist before. A chemical reaction created it.

But be careful — not every color change means a chemical change. Mix red paint and blue paint and you get purple, but no new substance is created. That is just a physical mixture. Clues are hints, not proof, so scientists often check more than one. The production of gas is another clue. Drop a fizzing tablet into a glass of water. Bubbles of carbon dioxide rush to the surface. Those bubbles tell you a chemical reaction is happening. But remember, boiling water also makes bubbles, and boiling is a physical change. One clue alone is not enough. A temperature change can also signal a chemical change. A burning match releases heat. A cold pack — the kind you squeeze when you twist your ankle — absorbs heat during a chemical reaction and turns icy cold. Finally, the formation of a precipitate is a clue. A precipitate is a solid that appears when two liquids are mixed, like a clump forming out of nowhere in a clear solution. Imagine stirring two clear liquids together and suddenly seeing cloudy chunks appear at the bottom. That new solid did not exist before. A chemical reaction created it.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is a precipitate different from the substances used to create it?

choices.text: It forms a cold chunk, while the starting substances are warmer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because coldness is a clue for temperature change (like a cold pack), not for the formation of a precipitate.

choices.text: It forms a new solid, while the starting substances are liquids.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states a precipitate is a solid that forms when two liquids are mixed, so the starting substances are liquids and the result is a new solid.

choices.text: It forms a gas bubble, while the starting substances are solids.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a precipitate is a solid, not a gas bubble, and the starting substances in this clue are liquids, not solids.

choices.text: It forms a clear pool, while the starting substances are cloudy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says the two starting liquids are clear, and the precipitate is the cloudy solid that appears.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Reversible or Permanent?

One key difference between the two types of change is whether you can undo them. Freeze water, melt it, freeze it again — you can repeat that cycle forever. Once bread is toasted, you cannot un-toast it. Once a match has burned, the original wood and chemicals are gone for good.
stimulus.content_text: Reversible or Permanent? One key difference between the two types of change is whether you can undo them. Freeze water, melt it, freeze it again — you can repeat that cycle forever. Once bread is toasted, you cannot un-toast it. Once a match has burned, the original wood and chemicals are gone for good.

prompt._: According to the text, what does it mean when a change is described as 'reversible'?

choices.text: a change that creates a brand new substance
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because creating a brand new substance describes a permanent change, like burning a match, not a reversible one.

choices.text: a change that is permanent and cannot stop
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being permanent and unable to stop is the opposite of reversible — that describes changes like toasting bread that cannot be undone.

choices.text: a change that can be undone and repeated
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that water can be frozen, melted, and frozen again — meaning the change can be undone and repeated.

choices.text: a change that happens very slowly over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about how fast or slow a reversible change happens.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why This Matters at Breakfast and Beyond

You witness both kinds of changes before you even leave for school. Butter melting on hot toast is a physical change — the butter is still butter. That golden-brown surface formed through a chemical reaction between heat and the bread's sugars. Engineers use this same knowledge to build bridges that resist rust. Chefs use it to know exactly when a cake is done. The difference is not about size or drama. It is about whether the stuff itself stays the same.
stimulus.content_text: Why This Matters at Breakfast and Beyond You witness both kinds of changes before you even leave for school. Butter melting on hot toast is a physical change — the butter is still butter. That golden-brown surface formed through a chemical reaction between heat and the bread's sugars. Engineers use this same knowledge to build bridges that resist rust. Chefs use it to know exactly when a cake is done. The difference is not about size or drama. It is about whether the stuff itself stays the same.

prompt._: Which detail from the text provides the best evidence that a chemical change happens when bread is toasted?

choices.text: A golden-brown surface forms on the bread.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the golden-brown surface forms through a chemical reaction between heat and the bread's sugars.

choices.text: The butter melts on the hot toast.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us that butter melting is a physical change, not a chemical change.

choices.text: The heat changes the size of the bread.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions heat changing the size of the bread.

choices.text: The bread remains the exact same stuff.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stuff staying the same describes a physical change, which is the opposite of a chemical change.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is a car being crushed at a junkyard different from an iron nail rusting in the rain?

choices.text: The car turns into a completely new substance, while the nail only changes its shape.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the car stays the same metal (physical change), while the nail becomes a new substance called iron oxide (chemical change).

choices.text: The car remains the exact same metal, while the nail becomes a brand-new material.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the crushed car is still the same metal, while the rusting nail becomes iron oxide, a brand-new material with different properties.

choices.text: The car goes through a slow and quiet change, while the nail goes through a loud one.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article describes the car crushing as loud and dramatic, and rusting as the slow, quiet change — the opposite of what this answer says.

choices.text: The car changes its color and its properties, while the nail only changes its size.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's the nail that changes color and properties as it turns to rust, not the car, which only changes its shape.

prompt._: Based on the article, what causes a fizzing tablet in water to be a chemical change, while dissolving sugar in water is only a physical change?

choices.text: The tablet creates a loud and violent reaction, while the sugar molecules slowly freeze into ice.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that the size or drama of a change doesn't tell you if it's physical or chemical, and sugar dissolving has nothing to do with freezing into ice.

choices.text: The tablet changes the water's temperature, while the sugar molecules turn into a cloudy solid.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says sugar turns into a cloudy solid — a cloudy solid forming is actually a clue for a chemical change called a precipitate, not what happens with sugar.

choices.text: The tablet permanently changes the water's color, while the sugar molecules boil into a hot vapor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says the fizzing tablet permanently changes the water's color, and sugar molecules spreading out in water is not the same as boiling into vapor.

choices.text: The tablet produces a completely new gas, while the sugar molecules just spread out in the water.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the fizzing tablet produces carbon dioxide gas (a new substance), while sugar molecules just spread out in the water without creating anything new.

prompt._: Which sequence best shows how the author organizes ideas to explain the difference between physical and chemical changes?

choices.text: First showing campfire and cheese examples, then defining the two kinds of changes, and last giving clues and everyday examples
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage truly starts with the campfire and cheese hook, then defines both types of changes, and finishes with clues and breakfast examples — matching this order perfectly.

choices.text: First giving clues like bubbles and color change, then defining the two kinds of changes, and last showing campfire and cheese examples
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the clues like bubbles and color changes come near the end of the article, not at the beginning — the campfire and cheese examples actually come first.

choices.text: First explaining how to reverse changes, then giving rust and melting examples, and last defining molecules and texture
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article begins with examples, not with how to reverse changes, and explaining molecules is just a small detail in the middle of the article.

choices.text: First defining the two kinds of changes, then ending with the campfire story, and last explaining how to reverse every change
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the campfire story opens the article, not closes it, and the passage never says every change can be reversed.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes the author's main comparison between physical and chemical changes?

choices.text: Physical changes only happen to liquids like water, while chemical changes only happen to solid objects like wood or metal.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage gives examples of both types of changes happening to solids, liquids, and gases — not just liquids for physical changes or solids for chemical changes.

choices.text: Physical changes usually happen in loud and dramatic ways, while chemical changes mostly occur in quiet and slow ways.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage specifically says chemical changes can be quiet and slow (like rust forming), and physical changes can be loud and dramatic (like a car being crushed in a junkyard).

choices.text: Physical changes always allow you to undo the process, while chemical changes only occur when heat is added to a material.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage shows that some physical changes like toasting bread cannot be undone, and chemical changes don't always require heat — rust forms from iron reacting with oxygen and water.

choices.text: Physical changes just rearrange the same materials, while chemical changes make brand-new stuff no matter how they look.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage's main point is that physical changes keep the same materials while chemical changes create entirely new substances, no matter how big or small the change looks.

syllabus_metadata.title: Physical Changes vs. Chemical Changes

assessments.title: States of Matter: Solid, Liquid, Gas

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Solid, Liquid, Gas: The Three States of Matter

A Pot on the Stove

A single ice cube sits in a metal pot on the stove. It is hard, cold, and shaped like a little block. Within minutes, the ice softens, then collapses into a puddle of water. The water begins to bubble and churn, and a cloud of steam rises into the air. That one ice cube just passed through all three common states of matter — solid, liquid, and gas — right before your eyes.
stimulus.content_text: Solid, Liquid, Gas: The Three States of MatterA Pot on the Stove A single ice cube sits in a metal pot on the stove. It is hard, cold, and shaped like a little block. Within minutes, the ice softens, then collapses into a puddle of water. The water begins to bubble and churn, and a cloud of steam rises into the air. That one ice cube just passed through all three common states of matter — solid, liquid, and gas — right before your eyes.

prompt._: According to the passage, how does the ice cube contrast with what it becomes after it collapses?

choices.text: It starts as a puddle of water and turns into a gas
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ice cube starts as a solid block, not a puddle of water.

choices.text: It starts as bubbling water and collapses into ice
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the bubbling water happens after the ice melts — the ice cube never starts as bubbling water.

choices.text: It starts as a hard block and collapses into a puddle
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly says the ice is 'hard' and shaped 'like a little block' before it 'collapses into a puddle of water.'

choices.text: It starts as a cloud of steam and turns into a block
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the ice starts as a hard block and ends as water and steam — not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Particles: The Tiny Building Blocks of All Matter

Everything around you — your desk, your drink, the air you breathe — is made of particles, tiny pieces of matter far too small to see. Think of particles like grains of sand on a beach. One grain is too small to notice from far away, but billions of them together form something you can hold and shape. In every state of matter, these particles are always moving. What changes from state to state is how fast they move and how close together they stay.
stimulus.content_text: Particles: The Tiny Building Blocks of All Matter Everything around you — your desk, your drink, the air you breathe — is made of particles, tiny pieces of matter far too small to see. Think of particles like grains of sand on a beach. One grain is too small to notice from far away, but billions of them together form something you can hold and shape. In every state of matter, these particles are always moving. What changes from state to state is how fast they move and how close together they stay.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what causes tiny particles to become an object you can hold?

choices.text: The particles stop moving completely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that particles are always moving — they never stop completely.

choices.text: Billions of particles change states.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say particles need to change states in order to form an object you can hold.

choices.text: Billions of particles group together.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says billions of particles together form something you can hold and shape, just like grains of sand forming a beach.

choices.text: The particles grow into larger pieces.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says particles grow larger; instead, many tiny particles group together.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
How Solids, Liquids, and Gases Behave Differently

In a solid, particles are packed tightly in a fixed arrangement, like students standing shoulder to shoulder in a crowded elevator. They vibrate in place, but they do not wander. Because the particles stay locked in position, a solid has a definite shape and a definite volume — the amount of space something takes up. A rock stays the shape of a rock whether it sits on a table or on the ground.

In a liquid, particles are close together but are not locked in place. They slide past one another, like people weaving through a crowd. That sliding motion is why liquids flow and pour. A liquid keeps the same volume — a cup of water is always a cup of water — but it has no shape of its own. Pour water into a round bowl and it curves to match. Pour the same water into a tall glass and it stretches upward instead.

In a gas, particles spread far apart and zoom in every direction. Picture a handful of bouncy balls launched inside a gymnasium — they ricochet off walls, off the ceiling, off each other. Gas particles behave the same way. A gas has no fixed shape and no fixed volume. It expands to fill whatever space is available. Open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a room, and the gas molecules — the specific particles that make up that substance — carry the scent to every other corner.

You might think that because gas is invisible, it is not real matter. Actually, gas still has mass, which means it still has weight you can measure. Blow up a balloon and place it on a sensitive scale. It weighs slightly more than an empty one. The air inside is real material — you just cannot see it.

They vibrate in place, but they do not wander. Because the particles stay locked in position, a solid has a definite shape and a definite volume — the amount of space something takes up. They slide past one another, like people weaving through a crowd. That sliding motion is why liquids flow and pour. Pour the same water into a tall glass and it stretches upward instead. In a gas, particles spread far apart and zoom in every direction.
