A gas has no fixed shape and no fixed volume. It expands to fill whatever space is available. Open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a room, and the gas molecules — the specific particles that make up that substance — carry the scent to every other corner. You might think that because gas is invisible, it is not real matter. Actually, gas still has mass, which means it still has weight you can measure. Blow up a balloon and place it on a sensitive scale. It weighs slightly more than an empty one. The air inside is real material — you just cannot see it.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happens in order after you open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a room?

choices.text: The scent stays in the corner and then slowly disappears completely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the gas molecules carry the scent to every other corner, meaning it spreads out instead of staying put or disappearing.

choices.text: The liquid perfume pours out and flows across the floor of the room
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage is describing perfume as a gas spreading through the air, not a liquid pouring across the floor.

choices.text: The gas molecules carry the scent to every other corner of the room
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that gas molecules 'carry the scent to every other corner' after the bottle is opened.

choices.text: The perfume particles lock into place and hold a definite shape
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because locking into a fixed shape is what solids do — the passage clearly says gases have no fixed shape at all.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
How Heat Causes Matter to Change States

What makes matter shift from one state to another? Energy, which you often feel as heat. Add heat to a solid, and its particles vibrate faster and faster. Add enough, and the particles break free from their fixed spots and begin to slide. The solid melts and becomes a liquid. The particles themselves do not change. They are the same tiny pieces of matter they always were. They simply move differently and arrange themselves in a new way.

Add still more heat to a liquid, and the particles speed up until they escape into the air. At lower temperatures, this happens slowly at the surface and is called evaporation. When you heat water on a stove until it reaches its boiling point, the change happens rapidly throughout the whole liquid, with bubbles of gas forming inside the water. This faster process is called boiling. Either way, the liquid turns into water vapor — water in its invisible gas form. The water has not disappeared. Every single molecule still exists, just spread out so far that your eyes cannot detect them.

The reverse changes happen too. When a gas cools, its particles slow down and drift closer together. The gas becomes a liquid again through condensation. You see condensation when water droplets form on the outside of a cold glass on a warm day. Cool a liquid enough, and its particles lock back into fixed positions. This is freezing — a liquid becoming a solid.

Add heat to a solid, and its particles vibrate faster and faster. Add enough, and the particles break free from their fixed spots and begin to slide. The solid melts and becomes a liquid. The particles themselves do not change. They are the same tiny pieces of matter they always were. They simply move differently and arrange themselves in a new way. This faster process is called boiling. Either way, the liquid turns into water vapor — water in its invisible gas form. The water has not disappeared. Every single molecule still exists, just spread out so far that your eyes cannot detect them. The reverse changes happen too. When a gas cools, its particles slow down and drift closer together. The gas becomes a liquid again through condensation. You see condensation when water droplets form on the outside of a cold glass on a warm day. Cool a liquid enough, and its particles lock back into fixed positions. This is freezing — a liquid becoming a solid.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what does the word 'condensation' mean compared to the word 'evaporation'?

choices.text: a slower version of the same process of liquid turning into gas
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because condensation and evaporation are not the same process at different speeds — they are opposites, with evaporation turning liquid into gas and condensation turning gas back into liquid.

choices.text: a faster version of boiling that happens inside the water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because bubbles forming inside water describes boiling, not condensation.

choices.text: the opposite process in which a gas turns back into a liquid
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says condensation is a 'reverse change' where a gas cools and becomes a liquid again, which is the exact opposite of evaporation where a liquid turns into a gas.

choices.text: the same process of particles speeding up and escaping into air
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because condensation involves particles slowing down and moving closer together, not speeding up and escaping into the air.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Water: The Shape-Shifter Found in All Three States

Water is one of the few substances found naturally in all three states on Earth. Water vapor drifts invisibly through the air above your head right now. Every rainstorm, every snowfall, every foggy morning is matter changing states on a massive scale.
stimulus.content_text: Water: The Shape-Shifter Found in All Three States Water is one of the few substances found naturally in all three states on Earth. Water vapor drifts invisibly through the air above your head right now. Every rainstorm, every snowfall, every foggy morning is matter changing states on a massive scale.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the author mean by calling water a 'shape-shifter'?

choices.text: a substance that changes between solid and liquid only
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions water vapor (a gas) in addition to solid and liquid forms, so water changes between more than just two states.

choices.text: a substance that moves between places but keeps one form
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'shape-shifter' is about water changing its form or state, not about moving from place to place while staying the same.

choices.text: a substance found naturally in all three states on Earth
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says water is found naturally in all three states on Earth, which is exactly what 'shape-shifter' means — it shifts between solid, liquid, and gas.

choices.text: a substance that shifts into two different states on Earth
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says water exists in all three states, not just two different states.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how does the shape of the ice cube contrast with the shape of the steam?

choices.text: The ice cube is made of many tiny particles, but the steam is made of one continuous material.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that both ice and steam are made of the same tiny particles; the particles just move differently in each state.

choices.text: The ice cube changes its shape very quickly, but the steam stays locked in one position.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — solids like ice stay locked in one position, while gases like steam move freely and spread out.

choices.text: The ice cube has a fixed and definite shape, but the steam expands to fill empty space.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says solids have a definite shape that stays fixed, while gases like steam expand to fill whatever space is available.

choices.text: The ice cube takes the exact shape of the pot, but the steam keeps the shape of a block.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's liquid water — not the ice cube — that takes the shape of its container, and steam definitely does not keep a block shape.

prompt._: Which cause-and-effect relationship does the author use to organize the explanation of how matter changes?

choices.text: Adding or removing heat causes the tiny particles to change speed.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author repeatedly uses the cause-and-effect pattern of adding or removing heat to explain how particles speed up or slow down, which is what causes matter to change states.

choices.text: Moving a liquid into a new container causes its total volume to change.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that a liquid always keeps the same volume — pouring it into a new container only changes its shape, not how much there is.

choices.text: Melting a solid block of ice causes new energy to be created as heat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says heat is added to cause melting — energy is used up, not created.

choices.text: Grouping billions of particles together causes a new substance to form.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses the example of billions of particles to explain why matter is visible, not to say that grouping particles creates a new or different substance.

prompt._: Based on the passage, which sequence best summarizes what happens to particles when a solid is heated until it becomes a gas?

choices.text: They zoom in all directions, then they slide past each other, and finally they vibrate in place.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it lists the steps backwards — zooming in all directions is what gas particles do, and vibrating in place is what solid particles do, so this is the opposite of what happens when something heats up.

choices.text: They vibrate faster, then they slide past each other, and finally they escape into the air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes exactly this sequence: particles vibrate faster in a solid, then slide past each other as a liquid, then escape into the air as a gas.

choices.text: They grow larger in size, then they change their shape, and finally they become new materials.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us the particles themselves do not change — they are 'the same tiny pieces of matter they always were' and do not grow larger or become new materials.

choices.text: They vibrate in place, then they stop moving entirely, and finally they disappear completely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says particles are always moving and never disappear — even as a gas, 'every single molecule still exists.'

prompt._: How does the author use the examples of a balloon on a scale and water droplets on a cold glass to support the same point about matter?

choices.text: Both examples explain that heat must be added for matter to change states
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the water droplets on the cold glass form when things cool down, not when heat is added.

choices.text: Both examples demonstrate that gases move faster than liquids do
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither example talks about or compares how fast gas and liquid particles move.

choices.text: Both examples show that matter still exists even when it changes form
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the balloon shows invisible air is still real matter with weight, and the water droplets show that invisible water vapor is still real matter that reappears when cooled.

choices.text: Both examples prove that matter is always visible to the human eye
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both examples actually involve matter you cannot see — invisible air in the balloon and invisible water vapor in the air around the glass.

assessments.title: Static Electricity: Sparks and Shocks

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Science of the Zap: Understanding Static Electricity

A Spark in the Dark

Your feet slide across the carpet. Your hand reaches for the metal doorknob. Then — ZAP. A tiny blue spark jumps from your fingertip, and a sharp sting shoots through your hand. That little bolt was not magic. It was static electricity, and the same invisible force that stung your finger also powers bolts of lightning that split the sky.
stimulus.content_text: The Science of the Zap: Understanding Static ElectricityA Spark in the Dark Your feet slide across the carpet. Your hand reaches for the metal doorknob. Then — ZAP. A tiny blue spark jumps from your fingertip, and a sharp sting shoots through your hand. That little bolt was not magic. It was static electricity, and the same invisible force that stung your finger also powers bolts of lightning that split the sky.

prompt._: According to the text, how does the author contrast the spark from your finger with what it might seem like?

choices.text: It seems like static electricity but is really magic
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the ideas backwards — the passage says the spark seems like magic but is really static electricity, not the other way around.

choices.text: It seems like magic but is really static electricity
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'That little bolt was not magic. It was static electricity,' showing the author contrasts the idea of magic with the real explanation.

choices.text: It seems like a big shock but is really lightning
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the spark seems like a big shock — it says it seems like magic.

choices.text: It seems like a spark but is really just carpet dust
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carpet dust is never mentioned anywhere in the passage.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Inside the Atom: Where Charges Live

To understand that spark, you need to zoom in — way in — to the building blocks of all matter. Every object around you is made of tiny particles called atoms. Inside each atom sits a dense center called the nucleus, the atom's core, like the pit of a peach. The nucleus holds particles called protons, and each proton carries a positive electric charge. Buzzing around the nucleus are even tinier particles called electrons, each carrying a negative charge.

They can be knocked loose and passed from one object to another. Most of the time, an atom has the same number of protons and electrons. The positive and negative charges cancel each other out, so the atom is neutral — balanced, like a seesaw with equal weight on both sides.
stimulus.content_text: Inside the Atom: Where Charges Live To understand that spark, you need to zoom in — way in — to the building blocks of all matter. Every object around you is made of tiny particles called atoms. Inside each atom sits a dense center called the nucleus, the atom's core, like the pit of a peach. The nucleus holds particles called protons, and each proton carries a positive electric charge. Buzzing around the nucleus are even tinier particles called electrons, each carrying a negative charge. They can be knocked loose and passed from one object to another. Most of the time, an atom has the same number of protons and electrons. The positive and negative charges cancel each other out, so the atom is neutral — balanced, like a seesaw with equal weight on both sides.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is an atom considered neutral most of the time?

choices.text: because protons push electrons away from the nucleus
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions protons pushing electrons away — in fact, electrons buzz around the nucleus freely.

choices.text: because the nucleus blocks the charges from escaping the atom
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says electrons can be knocked loose and passed from one object to another, which means charges can escape the atom.

choices.text: because the positive and negative charges cancel each other out
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the positive charges from protons and negative charges from electrons cancel each other out, making the atom neutral and balanced.

choices.text: because electrons are too tiny to carry any real charge
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states that electrons carry a negative charge, so their size doesn't stop them from having a real charge.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Rubbing, Stealing, and Building Up Charge

Rub a balloon on your hair. As the rubber slides against your strands, friction — the gripping force between two surfaces rubbing together — pulls electrons off your hair and onto the balloon. Your hair loses electrons and becomes positively charged. The balloon gains those extra electrons and becomes negatively charged.

Here is the key: no new charge was created. Not a single electron appeared out of thin air. The same electrons simply moved from your hair to the balloon, the way coins move from one pocket to another. The total amount of charge stayed exactly the same.

Now your hair and the balloon carry opposite charges. Objects with opposite charges pull toward each other, so your hair stands on end and reaches for the balloon. Press that negatively charged balloon against a wall, and it sticks. The balloon's extra electrons attract the positive charges near the wall's surface, holding it in place.

What about objects with the same charge? They push apart. This pushing is called repulsion — the force that shoves same-charged objects away from each other, like two magnets flipped the wrong way. Rub two balloons on your hair and hold them side by side. Both carry extra electrons, so both are negative. They swing away from each other. The rule is simple: opposite charges attract, and like charges repel.

As the rubber slides against your strands, friction — the gripping force between two surfaces rubbing together — pulls electrons off your hair and onto the balloon. Your hair loses electrons and becomes positively charged. The balloon gains those extra electrons and becomes negatively charged. Here is the key: no new charge was created. Not a single electron appeared out of thin air. The same electrons simply moved from your hair to the balloon, the way coins move from one pocket to another. The total amount of charge stayed exactly the same. Now your hair and the balloon carry opposite charges. Objects with opposite charges pull toward each other, so your hair stands on end and reaches for the balloon. Press that negatively charged balloon against a wall, and it sticks. The balloon's extra electrons attract the positive charges near the wall's surface, holding it in place. What about objects with the same charge? They push apart. This pushing is called repulsion — the force that shoves same-charged objects away from each other, like two magnets flipped the wrong way. Rub two balloons on your hair and hold them side by side. Both carry extra electrons, so both are negative. They swing away from each other. The rule is simple: opposite charges attract, and like charges repel.

prompt._: According to the text, why does your hair stand on end and reach for the balloon after you rub the balloon on your hair?

choices.text: because your hair and the balloon carry opposite charges
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states that your hair becomes positive and the balloon becomes negative, and opposite charges pull toward each other, which is why your hair reaches for the balloon.

choices.text: because repulsion pushes your hair toward the balloon
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because repulsion pushes same-charged objects away from each other — it doesn't pull your hair toward the balloon.

choices.text: because friction creates brand-new electrons on the balloon
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says 'no new charge was created' — electrons just moved from your hair to the balloon, they weren't brand new.

choices.text: because the balloon and your hair both become negative
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the balloon becomes negative; your hair loses electrons and becomes positively charged.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Doorknob Spark

Back to that carpet shock. As you shuffle across the floor, friction between your socks and the carpet strips electrons from the carpet fibers and piles them onto your body. With every step, you collect more. You are building up a negative charge, and you cannot feel it happening. Then your hand gets close to the metal doorknob. Metal is a conductor — a material that lets electric charges flow through it easily, like water through an open pipe. The excess electrons on your skin leap through the air to the metal in a tiny spark. That sudden jump is called a discharge, meaning the built-up charge releases all at once. You feel the sting, and the charges balance out again.
stimulus.content_text: The Doorknob Spark Back to that carpet shock. As you shuffle across the floor, friction between your socks and the carpet strips electrons from the carpet fibers and piles them onto your body. With every step, you collect more. You are building up a negative charge, and you cannot feel it happening. Then your hand gets close to the metal doorknob. Metal is a conductor — a material that lets electric charges flow through it easily, like water through an open pipe. The excess electrons on your skin leap through the air to the metal in a tiny spark. That sudden jump is called a discharge, meaning the built-up charge releases all at once. You feel the sting, and the charges balance out again.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares a conductor to 'water through an open pipe.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word conductor?

choices.text: a material that creates new electric charges inside
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says a conductor creates new charges — it only allows existing charges to move through it.

choices.text: a material that stops charges from moving at all
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a conductor lets charges flow easily, which is the opposite of stopping them.

choices.text: a material that lets charges move through it easily
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a conductor 'lets electric charges flow through it easily,' and the water-in-a-pipe comparison shows how smoothly that movement happens.

choices.text: a material that stores charges until they are needed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes charges flowing through a conductor and releasing, not being stored inside it.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Lightning: Static Electricity on a Giant Scale

Lightning is the most dramatic discharge on Earth. Inside a storm cloud, tiny ice crystals and water droplets slam into each other. All that friction separates charges. The top of the cloud becomes positively charged, and the bottom becomes negatively charged. The difference in charge between the cloud's base and the ground grows enormous.

When the difference becomes too great, electricity rips through the air. A single bolt heats the surrounding air to about fifty thousand degrees Fahrenheit — roughly five times hotter than the surface of the Sun.
stimulus.content_text: Lightning: Static Electricity on a Giant Scale Lightning is the most dramatic discharge on Earth. Inside a storm cloud, tiny ice crystals and water droplets slam into each other. All that friction separates charges. The top of the cloud becomes positively charged, and the bottom becomes negatively charged. The difference in charge between the cloud's base and the ground grows enormous. When the difference becomes too great, electricity rips through the air. A single bolt heats the surrounding air to about fifty thousand degrees Fahrenheit — roughly five times hotter than the surface of the Sun.

prompt._: According to the text, what is lightning?

choices.text: the top part of a cloud with positive charge
choices.feedback: The positive charge at the top of the cloud is something that happens inside the cloud, not a description of what lightning is.

choices.text: the most dramatic discharge on Earth
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage states directly that 'Lightning is the most dramatic discharge on Earth.'

choices.text: the hot air around the surface of the Sun
choices.feedback: The passage mentions the Sun's surface to compare temperatures, but lightning is not the hot air around the Sun — that detail is just used to show how incredibly hot a lightning bolt gets.

choices.text: a storm cloud full of ice and water
choices.feedback: A storm cloud full of ice and water is where lightning comes from, but the passage never says that's what lightning is.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Nuisance, Tool, and Everyday Wonder

Static electricity can be annoying. It glues your socks to your shirts in the dryer. It makes dust cling to your television screen. But people have also put it to work. Photocopiers and laser printers use carefully controlled static charges to attract ink onto paper in exact patterns, building up each letter and image dot by dot.

So the next time a spark bites your fingertip or lightning cracks overhead, remember: you are watching electrons on the move. The same tiny particles that make your hair stand up with a balloon also light up the sky during a thunderstorm. Electricity is not something that only lives inside wires and batteries. It is woven into every object you touch — waiting, sometimes, for just the right shuffle across the carpet.
stimulus.content_text: Nuisance, Tool, and Everyday Wonder Static electricity can be annoying. It glues your socks to your shirts in the dryer. It makes dust cling to your television screen. But people have also put it to work. Photocopiers and laser printers use carefully controlled static charges to attract ink onto paper in exact patterns, building up each letter and image dot by dot. So the next time a spark bites your fingertip or lightning cracks overhead, remember: you are watching electrons on the move. The same tiny particles that make your hair stand up with a balloon also light up the sky during a thunderstorm. Electricity is not something that only lives inside wires and batteries. It is woven into every object you touch — waiting, sometimes, for just the right shuffle across the carpet.

prompt._: Which detail does the author use as evidence to show that people have put static electricity to work?

choices.text: Balloons use it to flash lightning in the clouds.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage connects balloons to making your hair stand up, not to lightning, and lightning is a natural event, not something people control.

choices.text: Batteries use it to trap electrons in the wires.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says batteries use static electricity to trap electrons — batteries are just mentioned as a place where electricity normally lives.

choices.text: Televisions use it to pull dust onto the screen.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage mentions dust clinging to TV screens as an annoying problem, not as an example of people putting static electricity to work.

choices.text: Printers use it to attract ink onto the paper.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that printers use static charges to attract ink onto paper, which is an example of people using static electricity as a useful tool.

prompt._: Based on the text, how is the cause of a doorknob spark different from the cause of lightning?

choices.text: A doorknob spark is caused by protons moving to metal, while lightning is caused by electrons moving down to the ground.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says electrons move, not protons, and it doesn't describe electrons moving 'down to the ground' as the cause of lightning.

choices.text: A doorknob spark is caused by opposite charges pushing apart, while lightning is caused by like charges pulling toward each other.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both sparks are caused by opposite charges attracting each other, not like charges pushing apart.

choices.text: A doorknob spark is caused by socks rubbing a carpet, while lightning is caused by ice and water rubbing in clouds.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage specifically says socks rubbing carpet causes the doorknob spark, while ice and water droplets rubbing inside a cloud cause lightning.

choices.text: A doorknob spark is caused by creating brand new charges, while lightning is caused by destroying old charges in a cloud.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that no new charges are ever created — electrons just move from one place to another.

prompt._: According to the passage, what causes both a balloon to stick to a wall and ink to stick to paper in a printer?

choices.text: The pulling force between opposite electric charges.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says opposite charges attract, and uses that same idea to explain both the balloon sticking to the wall and ink being pulled onto paper in a printer.

choices.text: The creation of new electrons inside the materials.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states that no new electrons are ever created — the same electrons just move from one place to another.

choices.text: The pushing force between objects with the same charge.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because pushing apart (repulsion) happens when charges are the same — that's the opposite of sticking or attracting, so it can't explain why things stick together.

choices.text: The sudden jump of extra electrons through the air.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because electrons jumping through the air describes a discharge, like the doorknob spark, not the slow, steady sticking of a balloon to a wall or ink to paper.

prompt._: Which choice shows the order the article uses to explain how a tiny spark and a lightning bolt both happen?

choices.text: first atoms become neutral, then electrons disappear, then sparks form in metal
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says electrons move from place to place, not that they disappear, and neutral atoms are where everything starts, not a step that leads directly to sparks.

choices.text: first rubbing moves electrons, then charge builds up, then a discharge happens
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article consistently shows that rubbing moves electrons first, then charge builds up, and finally a discharge (spark or lightning) happens — this matches both the carpet shock and the lightning examples.

choices.text: first lightning heats the air, then clouds form, then ice crystals start rubbing
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — in the article, ice crystals rubbing together is what starts the process inside a storm cloud, and the air heating up is what happens after the lightning bolt is already released.

choices.text: first charges jump away, then rubbing builds them up, then objects become charged
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts the discharge (charges jumping away) first, but the article clearly shows that rubbing and building up charge must happen before any spark or discharge occurs.

prompt._: How do the descriptions of the doorknob spark and the lightning bolt support the main idea of the article?

choices.text: They show that static electricity happens when built-up charges are released all at once.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the doorknob spark and the lightning bolt are examples of built-up charges releasing all at once, which the article calls a discharge — and that is the main idea connecting all the examples.

choices.text: They demonstrate that static electricity happens because conductors pull electrons off the air.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says conductors let charges flow through them easily, but it never says conductors pull electrons out of the air.

choices.text: They explain that static electricity happens only when objects rub against a carpet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because static electricity builds up in many situations, like inside storm clouds where ice crystals collide, not only when something rubs against a carpet.

choices.text: They prove that static electricity happens because protons move from one object to another.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly explains that electrons move between objects, not protons, which stay locked inside the nucleus.

syllabus_metadata.title: Static Electricity: Sparks and Shocks

assessments.title: Current Electricity: A River of Charge

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The River Inside Your Walls: How Current Electricity Works

A Stack of Metal That Started Everything

In the year 1800, an Italian scientist named Alessandro Volta stacked small discs of zinc and copper into a tower. Between each pair of metal discs, he placed a piece of cardboard soaked in saltwater. Then he connected a wire from the top of the stack to the bottom. Electric current flowed. For the first time in history, a person had built a device that produced a steady stream of electricity — not just a quick spark, but a reliable flow. Volta had invented the first true battery. His work was so important that we still honor him today: the unit of voltage is called the volt.
stimulus.content_text: The River Inside Your Walls: How Current Electricity WorksA Stack of Metal That Started Everything In the year 1800, an Italian scientist named Alessandro Volta stacked small discs of zinc and copper into a tower. Between each pair of metal discs, he placed a piece of cardboard soaked in saltwater. Then he connected a wire from the top of the stack to the bottom. Electric current flowed. For the first time in history, a person had built a device that produced a steady stream of electricity — not just a quick spark, but a reliable flow. Volta had invented the first true battery. His work was so important that we still honor him today: the unit of voltage is called the volt.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was Volta's battery different from earlier ways of making electricity?

choices.text: it produced a quick spark instead of a steady flow
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes the old way of making electricity (a quick spark), not what Volta's battery did.

choices.text: it produced a steady flow instead of a quick spark
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Volta's battery made 'a reliable flow' of electricity, 'not just a quick spark.'

choices.text: it stored electricity rather than creating any new charge
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the battery produced electricity, and never mentions storing electricity instead of creating it.

choices.text: it ran on saltwater rather than on metal discs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Volta's battery actually used both saltwater-soaked cardboard AND metal discs together — they were all part of the same invention.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Makes Current Electricity Different

You already know about static electricity — the zap you feel after shuffling across a carpet. Static electricity builds up in one spot and then discharges, meaning it releases all at once in a sudden burst, like a balloon popping. Current electricity is completely different. It flows steadily through materials, delivering energy the whole time. The lights in your classroom, the refrigerator in your kitchen, and the tablet you use at school all run on current electricity.

Current electricity is the steady movement of tiny particles called electrons through a conductor — a material that lets electric charges pass through it easily, the way a hallway lets you walk from one room to another. Metals like copper and aluminum are excellent conductors. That is why the wires inside your walls are made of copper.
stimulus.content_text: What Makes Current Electricity Different You already know about static electricity — the zap you feel after shuffling across a carpet. Static electricity builds up in one spot and then discharges, meaning it releases all at once in a sudden burst, like a balloon popping. Current electricity is completely different. It flows steadily through materials, delivering energy the whole time. The lights in your classroom, the refrigerator in your kitchen, and the tablet you use at school all run on current electricity. Current electricity is the steady movement of tiny particles called electrons through a conductor — a material that lets electric charges pass through it easily, the way a hallway lets you walk from one room to another. Metals like copper and aluminum are excellent conductors. That is why the wires inside your walls are made of copper.

prompt._: Based on the text, why are the wires inside your walls made of copper?

choices.text: Because it builds up static electricity in one spot.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because building up static electricity in one spot describes static electricity, not what copper does.

choices.text: Because it releases energy in a sudden, quick burst.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because releasing energy in a sudden burst describes how static electricity discharges, not how copper wire works.

choices.text: Because it lets electric charges pass through easily.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says copper is an excellent conductor, meaning it lets electric charges pass through easily.

choices.text: Because it creates the tiny particles called electrons.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says copper creates electrons — electrons are tiny particles that already exist and simply move through copper.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
How a Battery Pushes Charges Along

A battery creates current using chemical reactions inside its case. These reactions cause electrons to pile up at one end, called the negative terminal — the connection point marked with a minus sign. The other end, the positive terminal, has fewer electrons. When you connect a wire between the two terminals, electrons flow from the negative side, through the wire, and into the positive side.

The wire is already packed with its own electrons. Think of a garden hose already full of water — when you turn on the faucet, the pressure pushes the water that is already inside, and all the water moves together. A battery's voltage works the same way, pushing electrons that are already in the wire.

Voltage is the force that pushes charges through a conductor. The higher the voltage, the harder the push. Scientists measure the flow of charges in units called amperes, often shortened to amps. If voltage is like water pressure, then amperage is like the amount of water rushing through.
stimulus.content_text: How a Battery Pushes Charges Along A battery creates current using chemical reactions inside its case. These reactions cause electrons to pile up at one end, called the negative terminal — the connection point marked with a minus sign. The other end, the positive terminal, has fewer electrons. When you connect a wire between the two terminals, electrons flow from the negative side, through the wire, and into the positive side. The wire is already packed with its own electrons. Think of a garden hose already full of water — when you turn on the faucet, the pressure pushes the water that is already inside, and all the water moves together. A battery's voltage works the same way, pushing electrons that are already in the wire. Voltage is the force that pushes charges through a conductor. The higher the voltage, the harder the push. Scientists measure the flow of charges in units called amperes, often shortened to amps. If voltage is like water pressure, then amperage is like the amount of water rushing through.

prompt._: In the section "How a Battery Pushes Charges Along," what happens after a wire is connected between a battery's negative terminal and positive terminal?

choices.text: Electrons pile up at both ends of the battery at the same time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says electrons pile up only at the negative terminal, not at both ends of the battery.

choices.text: Electrons flow from the negative side through the wire to the positive side.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that electrons flow from the negative side, through the wire, and into the positive side when the wire is connected.

choices.text: Electrons flow from the positive side through the wire to the negative side.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the direction backwards — the passage clearly states electrons flow from the negative side to the positive side, not the other way around.

choices.text: Electrons stop moving in the wire until the voltage gets higher.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says electrons wait or stop — they flow through the wire as soon as it connects the two terminals.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Loop That Must Not Break

Current electricity needs a complete loop to flow. Electrons leave one terminal of the battery, travel through the wire and through a device like a light bulb, and then return to the other terminal. This complete loop is called a circuit — picture a circular racetrack where cars go around and come back to where they started.

You might think that current shoots out of both ends of a battery and crashes together inside the bulb. Actually, current flows in one direction around the entire loop. You might also think the bulb "uses up" the electrons, like a car burning fuel. But electrons are never consumed. They keep flowing round and round. What the bulb uses is the energy the electrons carry. Picture a water wheel in a river — the water spins the wheel, giving up some of its energy, but the water itself flows on. Electrons deliver energy to the bulb and keep moving.

If the loop breaks, the current stops instantly. That is why a flashlight goes dark when a battery is missing or inserted backward. The circuit is incomplete, so electrons have no path to follow.

This complete loop is called a circuit — picture a circular racetrack where cars go around and come back to where they started. You might think that current shoots out of both ends of a battery and crashes together inside the bulb. Actually, current flows in one direction around the entire loop. You might also think the bulb "uses up" the electrons, like a car burning fuel. But electrons are never consumed. They keep flowing round and round. What the bulb uses is the energy the electrons carry. Picture a water wheel in a river — the water spins the wheel, giving up some of its energy, but the water itself flows on. Electrons deliver energy to the bulb and keep moving. If the loop breaks, the current stops instantly. That is why a flashlight goes dark when a battery is missing or inserted backward. The circuit is incomplete, so electrons have no path to follow.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares a circuit to a circular racetrack. What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word 'circuit'?

choices.text: a circuit is a device that stores extra electrons
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a circuit is a path that electrons flow through, not a device that holds or stores extra electrons.

choices.text: a circuit is a path where energy gets used up
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says electrons are never used up or consumed, only the energy they carry gets used by the bulb.

choices.text: a circuit is a loop that brings electrons back around
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the racetrack comparison shows that a circuit is a loop where electrons travel all the way around and return to where they started, just like cars on a racetrack.

choices.text: a circuit is a wire that makes electrons speed up
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions electrons speeding up — the racetrack comparison is about going in a loop, not about speed.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Two Kinds of Current

Direct current, called DC, moves in one direction only. Every battery you have ever used produces DC. Alternating current, called AC, rapidly switches direction — back and forth, many times per second. The electricity in your home's outlets is AC. Power companies use alternating current because it can transmit — travel from one place to another — over very long distances without losing as much energy along the way.
stimulus.content_text: Two Kinds of Current Direct current, called DC, moves in one direction only. Every battery you have ever used produces DC. Alternating current, called AC, rapidly switches direction — back and forth, many times per second. The electricity in your home's outlets is AC. Power companies use alternating current because it can transmit — travel from one place to another — over very long distances without losing as much energy along the way.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word transmit mean?

choices.text: To travel from one place to another
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses dashes right after 'transmit' to define it as 'travel from one place to another.'

choices.text: To move in only one single direction
choices.feedback: This describes how direct current (DC) moves, not what the word transmit means.

choices.text: To lose energy along a long distance
choices.feedback: This gets it backwards — the passage says AC can transmit over long distances without losing as much energy, so losing energy is not the definition of transmit.

choices.text: To switch direction back and forth
choices.feedback: This describes how alternating current (AC) works, not what the word transmit means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A World Wired Together

Today, a single power plant can send alternating current across hundreds of miles of wire to light up an entire city, while a tiny battery in your flashlight sends direct current through a few inches of metal to brighten a dark room. The scale is wildly different, but the idea is exactly the same: electrons moving in a loop, carrying energy to wherever you need it. Every time you flip a switch, you are completing a circuit that Alessandro Volta could only have dreamed of.
stimulus.content_text: A World Wired Together Today, a single power plant can send alternating current across hundreds of miles of wire to light up an entire city, while a tiny battery in your flashlight sends direct current through a few inches of metal to brighten a dark room. The scale is wildly different, but the idea is exactly the same: electrons moving in a loop, carrying energy to wherever you need it. Every time you flip a switch, you are completing a circuit that Alessandro Volta could only have dreamed of.

prompt._: Which detail from the article best supports the author's point that a huge power plant and a tiny battery work in the same way?

choices.text: They both use a few inches of metal to brighten a dark room.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because using a few inches of metal to brighten a dark room describes only the flashlight battery, not the large power plant.

choices.text: They both were built by a famous inventor named Alessandro Volta.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Alessandro Volta was a historical inventor the author mentions as a fun reference, but he did not build the power plant or the battery described in the passage.

choices.text: They both send alternating current across hundreds of miles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the power plant uses alternating current across hundreds of miles — the battery uses direct current through just a few inches of metal.

choices.text: They both rely on electrons moving in a loop to carry energy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that both the power plant and the battery work by 'electrons moving in a loop, carrying energy,' which is the shared idea the author is pointing out.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the alternating current from a power plant different from the direct current in a flashlight?

choices.text: It builds up in a single spot instead of flowing steadily through a wire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because building up in one spot describes static electricity, not alternating current.

choices.text: It switches direction back and forth instead of moving in only one direction.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says alternating current 'rapidly switches direction — back and forth,' while direct current 'moves in one direction only.'

choices.text: It consumes the moving electrons instead of returning them safely to the source.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that electrons are never consumed — they keep flowing around the circuit and only deliver their energy.

choices.text: It travels through a short piece of metal instead of crossing hundreds of miles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the details backwards — AC from the power plant travels hundreds of miles, while DC in the flashlight travels through just a short piece of metal.

prompt._: Based on the article, what causes electrons from a battery to flow steadily and deliver energy to a bulb?

choices.text: Static electricity builds up and releases them in a sudden burst.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because static electricity builds up and releases in a sudden burst, which is the opposite of the steady flow that powers a light bulb.

choices.text: Alternating current switches their direction back and forth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because alternating current (AC) comes from wall outlets, not batteries, and a battery produces direct current (DC) that flows in only one direction.

choices.text: The light bulb pulls them directly from the positive terminal.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because electrons flow from the negative terminal through the wire and bulb — the bulb doesn't pull electrons from the positive terminal.

choices.text: Chemical reactions push them through a complete, unbroken loop.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that chemical reactions in a battery push electrons through a complete circuit loop, delivering energy to devices like bulbs.

prompt._: Which sequence best describes how the author organizes the main ideas in the passage?

choices.text: By defining what static electricity is, explaining how chemical reactions happen inside a case, and describing the copper wires inside your walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because static electricity, chemical reactions, and copper wires are small supporting details, not the main ideas that organize the whole passage.

choices.text: By describing how water flows through a hose, explaining how a water wheel spins, and comparing a circular racetrack to a circuit.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the hose, water wheel, and racetrack are just helpful comparisons the author uses to explain bigger ideas, not the main ideas of the passage.

choices.text: By describing the wires inside your walls, explaining how a flashlight uses energy, and comparing a tiny battery to a power plant.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the flashlight and power plant comparison only appears briefly at the end, and wall wires are just a minor detail, so these don't describe how the whole passage is organized.

choices.text: By sharing the history of the first battery, explaining how electrons move in a complete loop, and describing two types of current.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it accurately names the three main topics the author covers in order: Volta's battery, how electrons flow in a circuit loop, and the two types of current.

prompt._: How do the comparisons to a water hose and a river support the main idea of the article?

choices.text: By showing that current electricity is pushed forward by a force and keeps flowing without being used up.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the hose comparison shows voltage pushes electrons forward, and the water wheel comparison shows electrons keep flowing without being used up — both support the main idea of steady, continuous current.

choices.text: By explaining that current electricity builds up in a single spot and releases its energy all at once.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because building up in one spot and releasing all at once describes static electricity, which the article says is the *opposite* of current electricity.

choices.text: By describing how current electricity switches directions back and forth rapidly to travel over long distances.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because switching directions to travel long distances describes alternating current (AC), not what the water hose or river comparisons are about.

choices.text: By proving that current electricity travels in a straight line until it crashes together inside a machine.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article directly tells us that current does NOT crash together — it flows in one direction around a complete loop.

syllabus_metadata.title: Current Electricity: A River of Charge

assessments.title: Circuits: The Path of Electricity

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Loop That Lights Up: How Circuits Work

A Flip, a Click, and a Mystery

You flip a switch on your bedroom wall, and the ceiling light blazes on. That tiny motion — less than a centimeter of movement in your finger — controls enough energy to fill a room with light. But the electricity did not appear out of thin air. And that path has rules.
stimulus.content_text: The Loop That Lights Up: How Circuits WorkA Flip, a Click, and a Mystery You flip a switch on your bedroom wall, and the ceiling light blazes on. That tiny motion — less than a centimeter of movement in your finger — controls enough energy to fill a room with light. But the electricity did not appear out of thin air. And that path has rules.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the contrast between the small action and its result when you flip a light switch?

choices.text: A tiny finger movement controls enough energy to light a room
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a tiny finger movement of less than a centimeter controls enough energy to fill a room with light — that's exactly the contrast the question is asking about.

choices.text: A slow turn of the switch gradually brightens the ceiling light
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes a quick flip of the switch, not a slow turn, and never mentions the light gradually getting brighter.

choices.text: A quick flip of the switch creates electricity out of thin air
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the electricity did NOT appear out of thin air — that's the opposite of what the passage says.

choices.text: A large push on the switch sends a small spark to the bulb
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says you push hard on the switch, and it doesn't mention a small spark traveling to the bulb.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Is a Circuit?

The path electricity follows is called a circuit, a word that means "to go around." A circuit is a complete, closed loop through which electric current can flow. Think of a circular track at a running meet. Runners leave the starting line, race around the track, and return to where they started. Electric current does the same thing. It leaves the power source, moves through a wire, does its job, and comes back.

The energy gets used, but the charges themselves loop back around, ready to go again. You might think a light bulb "uses up" electricity the way a campfire uses up wood. They pass through the bulb, give up some of their energy as light and heat, and keep moving. Here is the surprising part: the current that returns to the battery is the same amount that left it.
stimulus.content_text: What Is a Circuit? The path electricity follows is called a circuit, a word that means "to go around." A circuit is a complete, closed loop through which electric current can flow. Think of a circular track at a running meet. Runners leave the starting line, race around the track, and return to where they started. Electric current does the same thing. It leaves the power source, moves through a wire, does its job, and comes back. The energy gets used, but the charges themselves loop back around, ready to go again. You might think a light bulb "uses up" electricity the way a campfire uses up wood. They pass through the bulb, give up some of their energy as light and heat, and keep moving. Here is the surprising part: the current that returns to the battery is the same amount that left it.

prompt._: According to the text, why does a light bulb produce light and heat?

choices.text: The current races around a circular track.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the circular track is only a comparison used to explain how a circuit works, not an explanation for why a bulb produces light and heat.

choices.text: The battery creates a new power source.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the battery creates a new power source; it is simply where the current starts and ends its loop.

choices.text: The bulb uses up all of the electricity.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says electricity is NOT used up — the same amount of current that leaves the battery returns to it.

choices.text: Charges give up some of their energy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that charges pass through the bulb and give up some of their energy as light and heat.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Three Parts Every Circuit Needs

Every working circuit needs at least three parts: a power source, a conductor, and a load.

The power source provides the push that moves the charges. A battery or a wall outlet can serve as the power source. Scientists call this push voltage — the force that shoves charges forward, the way a pump pushes water through a hose. But here is something that surprises many people: the battery does not create new charges and send them into the wire. The metal wire is already packed with tiny charges. The battery just gives them a push, exactly like a water pump moves water that is already sitting inside the pipes.

The conductor is any material that lets charges move through it easily. Copper wire is the most common conductor in circuits because copper lets charges slide through with very little resistance — like a smooth water slide instead of a bumpy gravel road.

The load is the device that puts the electrical energy to work. A light bulb, a motor, or a buzzer can all be loads. Without a load, the energy has nowhere to go and nothing useful happens.

Connect all three in a closed loop, and current flows. Break the loop anywhere, and the circuit is open — meaning current stops completely. This is exactly what happens when a light bulb burns out. A tiny wire inside the bulb snaps, the loop breaks, and the light goes dark. A switch works on this same idea. It is really just a tiny gate in the circuit. Flip it off, and you pull apart a small piece of the loop. A gap opens, current stops, and the light goes out. Flip it on, and the gap closes so current flows again.

Scientists call this push voltage — the force that shoves charges forward, the way a pump pushes water through a hose. But here is something that surprises many people: the battery does not create new charges and send them into the wire. The metal wire is already packed with tiny charges. The battery just gives them a push, exactly like a water pump moves water that is already sitting inside the pipes. The conductor is any material that lets charges move through it easily. Copper wire is the most common conductor in circuits because copper lets charges slide through with very little resistance — like a smooth water slide instead of a bumpy gravel road. The load is the device that puts the electrical energy to work. A light bulb, a motor, or a buzzer can all be loads. Without a load, the energy has nowhere to go and nothing useful happens. Connect all three in a closed loop, and current flows. Break the loop anywhere, and the circuit is open — meaning current stops completely. This is exactly what happens when a light bulb burns out. A tiny wire inside the bulb snaps, the loop breaks, and the light goes dark. A switch works on this same idea. It is really just a tiny gate in the circuit. Flip it off, and you pull apart a small piece of the loop. A gap opens, current stops, and the light goes out. Flip it on, and the gap closes so current flows again.

prompt._: According to the text, what series of events explains why a light bulb goes dark when it burns out?

choices.text: A power battery dies, the push ends, and the tiny charges vanish.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly explains that the battery doesn't create or destroy charges, so charges cannot vanish when a bulb burns out.

choices.text: A tiny wire snaps, the loop breaks, and the light goes dark.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'a tiny wire inside the bulb snaps, the loop breaks, and the light goes dark,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: A small switch flips, a gap opens, and the current stops flowing.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because flipping a switch is a separate example the passage gives — it does not describe what happens when a light bulb burns out.

choices.text: A copper wire bends, the resistance grows, and the electrical energy escapes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions copper wire bending, resistance growing, or electrical energy escaping — none of that is in the text.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Series Circuits: One Path, One Problem

There are two main ways to arrange a circuit. The first is a series circuit, where all the parts line up along a single path, like beads on one string. If you wire three light bulbs in series, the current must pass through the first bulb, then the second, then the third before returning to the battery.

The same current flows through every bulb in the series, so they all glow equally. But they do glow dimmer than a single bulb would. The current has to push through three loads instead of one, so each bulb receives less energy. If one bulb burns out, the entire loop breaks and every bulb goes dark. Finding the single dead bulb that killed the whole string was a frustrating chore.
stimulus.content_text: Series Circuits: One Path, One Problem There are two main ways to arrange a circuit. The first is a series circuit, where all the parts line up along a single path, like beads on one string. If you wire three light bulbs in series, the current must pass through the first bulb, then the second, then the third before returning to the battery. The same current flows through every bulb in the series, so they all glow equally. But they do glow dimmer than a single bulb would. The current has to push through three loads instead of one, so each bulb receives less energy. If one bulb burns out, the entire loop breaks and every bulb goes dark. Finding the single dead bulb that killed the whole string was a frustrating chore.

prompt._: When the author compares the parts of a series circuit to 'beads on one string,' what does this comparison help the reader understand about the word 'series'?

choices.text: that the parts are colorful and decorative
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions color or decoration — the bead comparison is about the shape of the path, not how things look.

choices.text: that the parts follow a single path
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because beads on a string follow one single line, just like the parts in a series circuit all connect along one path.

choices.text: that the parts can be easily removed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't say anything about removing parts easily — in fact, if one bulb burns out, the whole circuit stops working.

choices.text: that the parts are very close together
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison is about following one path in order, not about how close together the parts are.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Parallel Circuits: Many Paths, More Power

The second arrangement is a parallel circuit. In a parallel circuit, each load sits on its own separate branch. Current leaves the battery and splits, with some flowing through one branch and some through another. Each bulb gets a full share of the battery's push, so the bulbs glow brighter than they would in a series circuit.

If one bulb burns out, current still flows through the other branches. Most of the wiring in your home uses parallel circuits. That is why you can turn off a lamp in your bedroom without shutting off the lights in the kitchen.
stimulus.content_text: Parallel Circuits: Many Paths, More Power The second arrangement is a parallel circuit. In a parallel circuit, each load sits on its own separate branch. Current leaves the battery and splits, with some flowing through one branch and some through another. Each bulb gets a full share of the battery's push, so the bulbs glow brighter than they would in a series circuit. If one bulb burns out, current still flows through the other branches. Most of the wiring in your home uses parallel circuits. That is why you can turn off a lamp in your bedroom without shutting off the lights in the kitchen.

prompt._: According to the text, what is a parallel circuit?

choices.text: a circuit with all loads in one line
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because having all loads in one line describes a series circuit, not a parallel circuit.

choices.text: a circuit with each load on its own branch
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states clearly that in a parallel circuit, each load sits on its own separate branch.

choices.text: a circuit with every load on the same branch
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because in a parallel circuit, each load is on its own separate branch, not all on the same branch.

choices.text: a circuit with current in only one branch
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because parallel circuits have current flowing through multiple branches, not just one.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Circuits Are Everywhere

The circuit inside a flashlight is small enough to hold in your hand. The circuit that powers a city stretches across thousands of kilometers of wire. Yet both follow the same rule: a closed loop with a source, a conductor, and a load.

assessments.title: Conductors, Insulators, and Switches

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Conductors, Insulators, and Switches: The Team Inside Every Wire

A Copper Highway Hidden in Your Walls

Right now, behind the walls of your home, thin copper wires stretch for hundreds of feet — carrying invisible energy to every lamp, refrigerator, and screen in the building. Pull the plastic coating off any power cord (don't actually do this!), and you will find a shiny metal core. That tiny thread of metal is doing enormous work. But the metal alone is not enough. It takes three partners working together — conductors, insulators, and switches — to bring electricity safely from a power source to the devices you use every day.
stimulus.content_text: Conductors, Insulators, and Switches: The Team Inside Every WireA Copper Highway Hidden in Your Walls Right now, behind the walls of your home, thin copper wires stretch for hundreds of feet — carrying invisible energy to every lamp, refrigerator, and screen in the building. Pull the plastic coating off any power cord (don't actually do this!), and you will find a shiny metal core. That tiny thread of metal is doing enormous work. But the metal alone is not enough. It takes three partners working together — conductors, insulators, and switches — to bring electricity safely from a power source to the devices you use every day.

prompt._: In the article, the author describes a thin thread of metal inside a power cord and says it is 'doing enormous work.' What contrast does the author make about this copper wire?

choices.text: It is hidden inside walls but easy to find and remove.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the wires are hidden in walls and even tells you NOT to pull off the coating, so they are not easy to remove.

choices.text: It is old technology but powers modern devices.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about the wire being old technology or compares it to modern devices.

choices.text: It is very small but carries a huge amount of energy.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author contrasts the wire being a 'tiny thread of metal' with it 'doing enormous work,' showing something very small carrying a huge amount of energy.

choices.text: It is made of copper but looks like shiny silver metal.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage does call the metal 'shiny,' it never says the copper looks like silver or compares the two metals.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Conductors: Materials That Let Current Through

Before we talk about those partners, you need to understand what is actually moving. Electric current is the flow of tiny particles called electrons — imagine a long line of marbles rolling through a tube, each one bumping the next one forward. Those electrons travel through materials made of atoms, the incredibly small building blocks that make up everything around you. Think of atoms as Lego bricks so tiny that millions of them could fit on the period at the end of this sentence.

A conductor is a material that lets electric current flow through it easily. Inside a metal like copper, electrons can hop freely from atom to atom, like kids passing a ball quickly down a line. Copper is one of the best conductors on Earth, which is why most electrical wires have a copper core. Aluminum and silver also conduct very well. Gold is an outstanding conductor too, and it is used in the tiny connectors of phones and computers because it does not corrode — meaning it does not rust or break down the way iron does when it gets wet.

You might think a light bulb "uses up" the electricity that flows into it, the way a car uses up gasoline. But actually, current flows through the bulb and back to the power source in a complete loop. The bulb grabs some of the energy the current carries and turns it into light and heat, but the current itself keeps moving — like a river spinning a water wheel while the water flows right past.

Those electrons travel through materials made of atoms, the incredibly small building blocks that make up everything around you. The bulb grabs some of the energy the current carries and turns it into light and heat, but the current itself keeps moving — like a river spinning a water wheel while the water flows right past.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why is gold used in the tiny connectors of phones and computers?

choices.text: because it carries more electrons than any other metal on Earth
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares how many electrons different metals carry, so this idea is not supported by anything in the text.

choices.text: because it is the easiest metal for factories to shape into small parts
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about how easy gold is for factories to shape into small parts.

choices.text: because it conducts electricity well and does not corrode over time
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage specifically says gold is 'an outstanding conductor' and 'does not corrode,' which are exactly the two reasons it is used in phone and computer connectors.

choices.text: because it is cheaper than copper and easier to find underground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say gold is cheaper or easier to find than copper — it only explains that gold resists corrosion.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Insulators: Materials That Block the Flow

An insulator is a material that strongly resists the flow of electric current. Rubber, glass, plastic, and wood are common insulators. Inside an insulator, the electrons are held tightly by their atoms. They cannot hop from one atom to the next, so current has an extremely hard time passing through.

Insulators protect you every single day. Look at any power cord in your home. The copper wire inside carries the current, and the plastic coating wrapped around it keeps that current safely contained. Without the plastic insulation, touching the wire while current flowed through it could give you a dangerous shock.

Under normal conditions, insulators do their job well. But extremely powerful electricity can force its way through almost anything. Lightning is a perfect example — air is normally a great insulator, yet a bolt of lightning rips right through it to reach the ground. So insulators are more like very strong shields than perfectly solid walls.
