Insulators protect you every single day. Look at any power cord in your home. The copper wire inside carries the current, and the plastic coating wrapped around it keeps that current safely contained. Without the plastic insulation, touching the wire while current flowed through it could give you a dangerous shock. Under normal conditions, insulators do their job well. But extremely powerful electricity can force its way through almost anything. Lightning is a perfect example — air is normally a great insulator, yet a bolt of lightning rips right through it to reach the ground. So insulators are more like very strong shields than perfectly solid walls.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the order of steps that happens inside a power cord to keep you safe from electric current?

choices.text: Plastic coating carries the current, then copper wire contains it safely
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two materials switched — the passage clearly says the copper wire carries the current, not the plastic coating.

choices.text: Copper wire carries the current, then plastic coating keeps it contained
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches exactly what the passage says: the copper wire carries the current and the plastic coating keeps it safely contained.

choices.text: Plastic coating blocks the atoms, then copper wire stops the electrons
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never describes plastic blocking atoms or copper stopping electrons — those steps are not in the passage at all.

choices.text: Copper wire melts the insulation, then plastic coating rebuilds around it
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions wire melting or plastic rebuilding — this is completely made up and not supported by anything in the text.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Switches: Your Finger on the Circuit

Current must travel in a complete loop — from a power source, through wires and a device, and back again. This loop is called a circuit. If any part of that loop breaks, current stops everywhere, just as water stops flowing through a garden hose if you cut it in half.

A switch is a device that opens or closes a gap in a circuit. When you flip a light switch to the "on" position, a small piece of metal inside slides into place and completes the loop. Current flows, and the light turns on. When you flip the switch off, that metal piece pulls away, the loop breaks, and current stops.

You use switches dozens of times each day. Pressing the button on a flashlight moves a tiny metal contact to connect or disconnect the battery from the bulb. The power button on a computer is a switch. Even tapping your tablet screen opens and closes circuits inside the device. Without switches, the only way to stop current would be to pull wires apart by hand — messy, slow, and dangerous.
stimulus.content_text: Switches: Your Finger on the Circuit Current must travel in a complete loop — from a power source, through wires and a device, and back again. This loop is called a circuit. If any part of that loop breaks, current stops everywhere, just as water stops flowing through a garden hose if you cut it in half. A switch is a device that opens or closes a gap in a circuit. When you flip a light switch to the "on" position, a small piece of metal inside slides into place and completes the loop. Current flows, and the light turns on. When you flip the switch off, that metal piece pulls away, the loop breaks, and current stops. You use switches dozens of times each day. Pressing the button on a flashlight moves a tiny metal contact to connect or disconnect the battery from the bulb. The power button on a computer is a switch. Even tapping your tablet screen opens and closes circuits inside the device. Without switches, the only way to stop current would be to pull wires apart by hand — messy, slow, and dangerous.

prompt._: In the section, the author compares a circuit to a garden hose that is cut in half. Based on this comparison, what does the phrase 'the loop breaks' most likely mean?

choices.text: the device attached to the circuit turns on
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because when the loop breaks, the device actually turns OFF, not on — current needs a complete loop to make a device work.

choices.text: the power source runs out of energy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the power source running out of energy is a different problem — the passage is talking about the path being interrupted, not the battery or power being used up.

choices.text: the wires in a circuit get physically damaged
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'the loop breaks' doesn't mean the wires are physically damaged or destroyed — it just means the path is no longer connected, like when a switch pulls a metal piece away.

choices.text: the path for current is no longer complete
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the garden hose comparison shows that when the path is cut or interrupted, nothing can flow through it anymore — just like current stops when the circuit loop is no longer complete.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Three Partners, One Job

Next time you plug in a lamp, look closely at the cord. Copper conductor on the inside. Plastic insulator on the outside. A switch on the lamp or the wall. Each one has a different job, and none of them works well alone. Remove the conductor, and current has no path. Remove the insulator, and the current becomes dangerous. Remove the switch, and you lose all control.

A switch on the lamp or the wall. Each one has a different job, and none of them works well alone. Remove the conductor, and current has no path. Remove the insulator, and the current becomes dangerous. Remove the switch, and you lose all control.

choices.text: A conductor lets current move, while an insulator keeps current from moving out.

assessments.title: Magnets and Electromagnetism

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Invisible Force: How Electricity and Magnetism Work Together

A Compass Goes Haywire

In 1820, a Danish scientist named Hans Christian Oersted stood in front of his students with a simple electric circuit and a compass. He switched on the current. The compass needle jumped. He switched it off. The needle swung back. On, jump. Off, swing back. Something invisible was reaching out from that wire and pushing the needle around.

Here is why that mattered: a compass needle is a tiny magnet. It only responds to magnetic forces. Yet an electric current — the flow of charged particles through a wire — was making it move. You might think electricity and magnetism are completely separate things, like heat and sound. Actually, they are deeply connected. Oersted had just proved it. Electric current creates a magnetic field — the invisible area of force that surrounds every magnet. You cannot see a magnetic field, but you can picture it as a bubble of pulling power wrapped around the wire.

He switched on the current. The compass needle jumped. He switched it off. The needle swung back. On, jump. Off, swing back. Something invisible was reaching out from that wire and pushing the needle around. Here is why that mattered: a compass needle is a tiny magnet. It only responds to magnetic forces. Yet an electric current — the flow of charged particles through a wire — was making it move. You might think electricity and magnetism are completely separate things, like heat and sound. Actually, they are deeply connected. Oersted had just proved it. Electric current creates a magnetic field — the invisible area of force that surrounds every magnet. You cannot see a magnetic field, but you can picture it as a bubble of pulling power wrapped around the wire.

prompt._: According to the section "A Compass Goes Haywire," which pair of things does the author contrast as seeming separate but actually being connected?

choices.text: electricity and heat
choices.feedback: Heat is mentioned in the passage only as an example of something that seems unrelated to sound, not as something connected to electricity.

choices.text: magnets and compasses
choices.feedback: Magnets and compasses are not contrasted as separate things; the passage actually explains that a compass needle is itself a tiny magnet.

choices.text: sound and charged particles
choices.feedback: Sound and charged particles are never paired together as a contrast in the passage — sound is only used as part of a comparison example.

choices.text: electricity and magnetism
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly states that electricity and magnetism seem separate but are actually deeply connected, which Oersted proved with his experiment.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Building a Magnet You Can Switch Off

Scientists got excited fast. If a straight wire creates a weak magnetic field, what happens when you coil the wire into loops? The field gets stronger. What if you wrap those coils around a bar of iron? Even stronger. This device is called an electromagnet — a magnet made by running electric current through a coil of wire wrapped around an iron core. Think of it like a volume knob for magnetism. More coils and more current turn the volume up.

Here is the surprising part: the moment you stop the current, the magnetism disappears. The iron bar goes back to being an ordinary piece of metal. You might expect that once iron becomes magnetic, it stays that way forever. That is true for permanent magnets, the kind stuck to your refrigerator. But an electromagnet only works while electricity flows through it. Turn the current on, and you have a magnet. Turn it off, and you don't.

That on-off ability makes electromagnets incredibly useful. In junkyards, giant electromagnets dangle from cranes. An operator sends current through the coil, and the magnet grabs an entire car. Then the operator cuts the current, and the car drops onto a pile of scrap metal. Electric doorbells use tiny electromagnets too. When you press the button, current flows through a small coil. The magnetic field pulls a metal hammer against the bell — ding! Hospital machines called MRI scanners (short for Magnetic Resonance Imaging) use extremely powerful electromagnets to take detailed pictures of the inside of your body, all without surgery.
stimulus.content_text: Building a Magnet You Can Switch Off Scientists got excited fast. If a straight wire creates a weak magnetic field, what happens when you coil the wire into loops? The field gets stronger. What if you wrap those coils around a bar of iron? Even stronger. This device is called an electromagnet — a magnet made by running electric current through a coil of wire wrapped around an iron core. Think of it like a volume knob for magnetism. More coils and more current turn the volume up. Here is the surprising part: the moment you stop the current, the magnetism disappears. The iron bar goes back to being an ordinary piece of metal. You might expect that once iron becomes magnetic, it stays that way forever. That is true for permanent magnets, the kind stuck to your refrigerator. But an electromagnet only works while electricity flows through it. Turn the current on, and you have a magnet. Turn it off, and you don't. That on-off ability makes electromagnets incredibly useful. In junkyards, giant electromagnets dangle from cranes. An operator sends current through the coil, and the magnet grabs an entire car. Then the operator cuts the current, and the car drops onto a pile of scrap metal. Electric doorbells use tiny electromagnets too. When you press the button, current flows through a small coil. The magnetic field pulls a metal hammer against the bell — ding! Hospital machines called MRI scanners (short for Magnetic Resonance Imaging) use extremely powerful electromagnets to take detailed pictures of the inside of your body, all without surgery.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why can a junkyard crane drop a car after picking it up with an electromagnet?

choices.text: The coils of wire around the iron core start to lose their shape
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the coils losing their shape; the electromagnet stops working only when the electric current is cut off.

choices.text: The iron core in the electromagnet becomes a permanent magnet over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the iron core goes back to being an ordinary piece of metal when the current stops — it never becomes a permanent magnet.

choices.text: The car becomes too heavy for the magnetic field to hold
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the car being too heavy — the car drops because the magnetism disappears when the current is turned off.

choices.text: The operator cuts the electric current so the magnetism disappears
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the operator 'cuts the current' and the car drops, since an electromagnet loses its magnetism the moment electricity stops flowing.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Faraday Flips the Discovery Around

Eleven years after Oersted's surprise, an English scientist named Michael Faraday asked a bold question. If electricity can create magnetism, can magnetism create electricity? In 1831, he found the answer by pushing a magnet through a coil of wire. Current began to flow — but only while the magnet was moving. The instant Faraday held the magnet still inside the coil, the current stopped. Pull the magnet out, and current flowed again, this time in the opposite direction.

The key was motion. A magnet just sitting next to a wire does nothing special. But a changing magnetic field — one that is growing, shrinking, or shifting direction — pushes electrons through the wire and creates current. Faraday called this electromagnetic induction, which means "using a changing magnetic field to produce electric current." Think of it like wind and a pinwheel. Still air does nothing. Moving air makes the pinwheel spin.
stimulus.content_text: Faraday Flips the Discovery Around Eleven years after Oersted's surprise, an English scientist named Michael Faraday asked a bold question. If electricity can create magnetism, can magnetism create electricity? In 1831, he found the answer by pushing a magnet through a coil of wire. Current began to flow — but only while the magnet was moving. The instant Faraday held the magnet still inside the coil, the current stopped. Pull the magnet out, and current flowed again, this time in the opposite direction. The key was motion. A magnet just sitting next to a wire does nothing special. But a changing magnetic field — one that is growing, shrinking, or shifting direction — pushes electrons through the wire and creates current. Faraday called this electromagnetic induction, which means "using a changing magnetic field to produce electric current." Think of it like wind and a pinwheel. Still air does nothing. Moving air makes the pinwheel spin.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened right after Faraday held the magnet still inside the coil?

choices.text: The coil became a magnet
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the coil turning into a magnet at any point.

choices.text: The wire started to move
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about the wire moving — only the magnet moved in Faraday's experiment.

choices.text: The current reversed its direction
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the current reversing direction happened when Faraday pulled the magnet out of the coil, not when he held it still.

choices.text: The current stopped flowing
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states, 'The instant Faraday held the magnet still inside the coil, the current stopped.'

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Where Your Electricity Really Comes From

Electromagnetic induction is the idea behind every electric generator — a machine that turns motion into electric current. Inside a generator, magnets spin near coils of wire, or coils spin around magnets. That constant motion keeps the magnetic field changing, which keeps current flowing through the wire.

But trace those wires backward. They lead to power lines. The power lines lead to a power plant. And inside that plant, something is spinning magnets — rushing steam, falling water, or blowing wind.

Transformers — devices that raise or lower the push of electric current for long-distance travel through power lines — also depend on electromagnetic induction. Without Faraday's discovery, there would be no practical way to generate electricity or deliver it to your home.
stimulus.content_text: Where Your Electricity Really Comes From Electromagnetic induction is the idea behind every electric generator — a machine that turns motion into electric current. Inside a generator, magnets spin near coils of wire, or coils spin around magnets. That constant motion keeps the magnetic field changing, which keeps current flowing through the wire. But trace those wires backward. They lead to power lines. The power lines lead to a power plant. And inside that plant, something is spinning magnets — rushing steam, falling water, or blowing wind. Transformers — devices that raise or lower the push of electric current for long-distance travel through power lines — also depend on electromagnetic induction. Without Faraday's discovery, there would be no practical way to generate electricity or deliver it to your home.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are electric generators and transformers similar?

choices.text: Both depend on the same scientific idea.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that generators use electromagnetic induction, and then says transformers 'also depend on electromagnetic induction,' meaning both rely on the same scientific idea.

choices.text: Both turn constant motion into electric current.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because turning motion into electric current describes only generators, not transformers — transformers raise or lower the push of electricity instead.

choices.text: Both travel long distances through power lines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's the power lines that travel long distances, not the generators or transformers themselves.

choices.text: Both use rushing steam to spin magnets.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rushing steam is just one example of what spins magnets inside a generator, and the passage never mentions steam being connected to transformers at all.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Two Forces, One Story

So electricity and magnetism are not two separate forces living in two separate worlds. They are partners. A flowing current wraps itself in a magnetic field. A changing magnetic field kicks electrons into motion. Oersted noticed the first half by accident. Faraday chased down the second half on purpose. Together, their discoveries power everything from the tiny speaker in your earbuds to the massive generators lighting whole cities.
stimulus.content_text: Two Forces, One Story So electricity and magnetism are not two separate forces living in two separate worlds. They are partners. A flowing current wraps itself in a magnetic field. A changing magnetic field kicks electrons into motion. Oersted noticed the first half by accident. Faraday chased down the second half on purpose. Together, their discoveries power everything from the tiny speaker in your earbuds to the massive generators lighting whole cities.

choices.text: two forces that scientists discovered

choices.text: two forces that move whole cities

choices.text: two forces that work together
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that electricity and magnetism help each other — a current makes a magnetic field, and a magnetic field moves electrons — which is exactly what partners do.

choices.text: two forces that stay in separate worlds

prompt._: Based on the article, how was Michael Faraday's discovery different from Hans Christian Oersted's discovery?

choices.text: Oersted used electricity to create magnetism, while Faraday used moving magnets to create electricity.

choices.text: Oersted showed that a compass needle always moves, while Faraday showed that a compass needle stays still.

prompt._: Based on the article, why can a power plant make electricity for homes?

choices.text: Because moving magnets near coils create electric current

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only Faraday's experiment involved a moving magnet — Oersted worked with electric current and a compass, not a moving magnet.

choices.text: Both showed that electricity and magnetism can affect each other.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Oersted showed electricity creates magnetism, and Faraday showed magnetism creates electricity — both proved these two forces affect each other.

assessments.title: Electrical Safety

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Staying Safe Around Electricity

A Spark You Never See Coming

A girl plugs in a hair dryer, reaches for it with a wet hand, and feels a sharp jolt shoot up her arm. Electricity found a path through the water on her skin and into her body. She is fine — this time. But every year, thousands of people are hurt by electrical accidents that a few simple rules could prevent.

To understand those rules, you need one big idea: electricity always needs a complete loop to flow. That loop is called a circuit — like a racetrack where cars must travel all the way around to keep moving. If the track has a gap, the cars stop. Current flows from a power source, through a wire, through a device, and back again. When your body accidentally becomes part of that loop, you get a shock.
stimulus.content_text: Staying Safe Around ElectricityA Spark You Never See Coming A girl plugs in a hair dryer, reaches for it with a wet hand, and feels a sharp jolt shoot up her arm. Electricity found a path through the water on her skin and into her body. She is fine — this time. But every year, thousands of people are hurt by electrical accidents that a few simple rules could prevent. To understand those rules, you need one big idea: electricity always needs a complete loop to flow. That loop is called a circuit — like a racetrack where cars must travel all the way around to keep moving. If the track has a gap, the cars stop. Current flows from a power source, through a wire, through a device, and back again. When your body accidentally becomes part of that loop, you get a shock.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happens when a circuit has a gap, in contrast to when the loop is complete?

choices.text: The current stops flowing through the loop
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses the racetrack comparison to explain that if the track has a gap, the cars (current) stop moving.

choices.text: The current flows faster through the gap
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says current flows faster when there is a gap — in fact, it says the opposite, that current stops.

choices.text: The current finds a new path around the gap
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not mention current finding a new path around a gap — that idea simply isn't in the text.

choices.text: The current builds up and causes a shock
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes shocks happening when your body completes a loop, not when there is a gap in the circuit.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Water: A Dangerous Partner

You have probably heard that water and electricity are a dangerous mix. That is true — but you might think all water conducts electricity equally. Actually, perfectly pure water is a poor conductor, meaning it does not let current pass through easily. A conductor is any material that carries electricity well, the way a metal slide carries heat from the sun to your legs. What makes everyday water so dangerous? Tap water, pool water, and bathwater contain tiny dissolved minerals and salts that turn them into excellent conductors.

This is why you should never use a plugged-in device near a bathtub, sink, or pool. If an electrical device falls into that water, current races through it instantly. Even touching an outlet with damp hands can create a path for electricity through your body. Keep your hands dry around anything electrical.
stimulus.content_text: Water: A Dangerous Partner You have probably heard that water and electricity are a dangerous mix. That is true — but you might think all water conducts electricity equally. Actually, perfectly pure water is a poor conductor, meaning it does not let current pass through easily. A conductor is any material that carries electricity well, the way a metal slide carries heat from the sun to your legs. What makes everyday water so dangerous? Tap water, pool water, and bathwater contain tiny dissolved minerals and salts that turn them into excellent conductors. This is why you should never use a plugged-in device near a bathtub, sink, or pool. If an electrical device falls into that water, current races through it instantly. Even touching an outlet with damp hands can create a path for electricity through your body. Keep your hands dry around anything electrical.

prompt._: Based on the text, why does everyday water carry electricity so well?

choices.text: Because it has tiny minerals and salts.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that tiny dissolved minerals and salts in everyday water make it an excellent conductor of electricity.

choices.text: Because it acts like a hot metal slide.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the hot metal slide is only used as an example to explain what a conductor is, not to explain why water carries electricity.

choices.text: Because it is perfectly pure and clean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says perfectly pure water does NOT carry electricity well — it's actually a poor conductor.

choices.text: Because it comes from a sink or bathtub.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the location of the water (sink or bathtub) doesn't make it conduct electricity — it's the minerals and salts dissolved in the water that do that.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Damaged Cords and Hidden Dangers

Look at the cord on any lamp or charger. You will see plastic wrapped around the metal wire inside. That plastic coating is called insulation — it works like the rubber sole of a shoe, blocking electricity from escaping. But if a cord is cracked, frayed (meaning its fibers are torn and splitting apart, like the end of a worn-out rope), or chewed by a pet, the bare wire underneath is exposed. Touching it can deliver a serious shock.

If you spot a damaged cord, tell an adult right away. Never try to fix it yourself. And when you unplug something, grip the hard plastic plug — not the cord. Yanking the cord can tear the wires hidden inside.

That plastic coating is called insulation — it works like the rubber sole of a shoe, blocking electricity from escaping. But if a cord is cracked, frayed (meaning its fibers are torn and splitting apart, like the end of a worn-out rope), or chewed by a pet, the bare wire underneath is exposed. Touching it can deliver a serious shock. If you spot a damaged cord, tell an adult right away. Never try to fix it yourself. And when you unplug something, grip the hard plastic plug — not the cord. Yanking the cord can tear the wires hidden inside.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happens to the wires inside a cord after someone yanks the cord instead of gripping the plug?

choices.text: The wires hidden inside can get torn
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'yanking the cord can tear the wires hidden inside,' which is exactly what option B says.

choices.text: The cord becomes too short to reach
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions the cord becoming too short to reach anything.

choices.text: The plug breaks off from the outlet
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say the plug breaks off from the outlet — it talks about damage happening to the wires inside the cord.

choices.text: The plastic insulation melts from the heat
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about insulation melting from heat when you yank a cord.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Short Circuits, Fuses, and Circuit Breakers

Sometimes damaged insulation lets two wires touch each other inside a cord or wall. Electricity takes a shortcut through this shorter path. This is called a short circuit. The shorter path has very little resistance — the force that slows current down, the way thick mud slows a bicycle tire. With almost nothing to slow it, current surges. The wires heat up fast, throw off sparks, and can start a fire.

Buildings fight back with safety devices. A fuse is a thin strip of metal designed to melt when too much current flows through it. Once it melts, the circuit breaks — like a bridge that collapses on purpose to stop a flood from crossing. A circuit breaker does the same job but uses a switch that flips off automatically. An adult can reset it after finding and fixing the problem.

Electricity takes a shortcut through this shorter path. This is called a short circuit. The shorter path has very little resistance — the force that slows current down, the way thick mud slows a bicycle tire. With almost nothing to slow it, current surges. The wires heat up fast, throw off sparks, and can start a fire. Buildings fight back with safety devices. A fuse is a thin strip of metal designed to melt when too much current flows through it. Once it melts, the circuit breaks — like a bridge that collapses on purpose to stop a flood from crossing. A circuit breaker does the same job but uses a switch that flips off automatically. An adult can reset it after finding and fixing the problem.

prompt._: In the section, the author compares resistance to thick mud slowing a bicycle tire. Based on this comparison, what does the word 'resistance' mean?

choices.text: a force that slows the flow of current down
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says resistance is 'the force that slows current down,' and the mud-and-tire comparison shows exactly what slowing down looks like.

choices.text: a force that creates extra electricity in wires
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because resistance does not create extra electricity — it actually slows electricity down instead of making more of it.

choices.text: a material that melts when it gets too hot
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because melting when it gets too hot describes a fuse, not resistance.

choices.text: a shortcut that electricity takes through wires
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a shortcut that electricity takes describes a short circuit, not resistance.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Power Lines and Lightning

Overhead power lines carry electricity at extremely high voltage — the push that drives current through a wire, the way water pressure drives water through a hose. The voltage in a power line is hundreds of times stronger than what comes out of your wall outlet. Never climb a utility pole, fly a kite near power lines, or touch a fallen wire. If you see a downed line, stay far away and call for help.

Lightning is a massive spark of electricity jumping between clouds and the ground. During a storm, move indoors right away. If you cannot reach a building, stay away from tall objects like trees, poles, and fences. Lightning tends to strike the tallest thing in an area. Avoid open fields, hilltops, and water. If you are swimming, get out the moment you hear thunder. A car with a metal roof is a surprisingly safe shelter — the metal shell guides lightning around the outside and into the ground, keeping the people inside protected.

If you are swimming, get out the moment you hear thunder. A car with a metal roof is a surprisingly safe shelter — the metal shell guides lightning around the outside and into the ground, keeping the people inside protected.

prompt._: According to the text, what is voltage?

choices.text: the amount of electricity stored inside a battery
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions batteries or storing electricity when it defines voltage, so this is incorrect.

choices.text: the flow of water moving through a garden hose
choices.feedback: Water moving through a hose is used as a comparison to help you picture how voltage works, but it is not the definition of voltage itself.

choices.text: the spark of electricity that jumps between clouds
choices.feedback: This describes lightning, not voltage — the passage explains lightning separately as a spark jumping between clouds and the ground.

choices.text: the push that drives current through a wire
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage states directly that voltage is 'the push that drives current through a wire.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Invisible River in Your Walls

Electricity flows through your home like an invisible river — silent, useful, and powerful. You flip a switch and a room fills with light. But rivers deserve respect. That knowledge turns you from someone who just uses electricity into someone who truly understands it — and that understanding is the best safety tool you will ever own.
stimulus.content_text: The Invisible River in Your Walls Electricity flows through your home like an invisible river — silent, useful, and powerful. You flip a switch and a room fills with light. But rivers deserve respect. That knowledge turns you from someone who just uses electricity into someone who truly understands it — and that understanding is the best safety tool you will ever own.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that learning about electricity protects you?

choices.text: Electricity flows like an invisible river.
choices.feedback: This describes electricity like a river, which is a cool comparison, but it doesn't say anything about learning keeping you safe.

choices.text: Understanding is the best safety tool.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'understanding is the best safety tool you will ever own,' which clearly connects learning to protection.

choices.text: You turn into someone who just uses power.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — the author says understanding electricity moves you beyond just using it, not that you stay someone who 'just uses power.'

choices.text: A room fills with light from a wall switch.
choices.feedback: This just shows that electricity powers lights — it's about what electricity does, not about how understanding it protects you.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is insulation different from a conductor?

choices.text: Insulation creates a complete electrical loop, but a conductor breaks the circuit apart.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because insulation does not create a circuit loop — the article says a circuit is a complete loop of wires and a power source, not insulation.

choices.text: Insulation is found in everyday tap water, but a conductor is found in plastic cords.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says tap water is an example of a conductor (due to dissolved minerals), and insulation is the plastic coating on cords, not something in water.

choices.text: Insulation blocks electricity from escaping, but a conductor carries electricity well.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that insulation blocks electricity from escaping and that a conductor carries electricity well — they do opposite jobs.

choices.text: Insulation lets current pass through easily, but a conductor stops the flow of current.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the definitions completely backwards — the article says insulation blocks current and conductors let current pass through easily.

prompt._: Based on the article, how does a melted fuse prevent a short circuit from starting a fire?

choices.text: It guides the extra current to the ground to protect the people.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because guiding electricity to the ground describes how a car's metal roof protects people during lightning, not how a fuse works.

choices.text: It creates a gap in the loop to stop the current's flow.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that when a fuse melts, it breaks the circuit — creating a gap that stops current from flowing, just like a gap in a racetrack stops the cars.

choices.text: It wraps a layer of plastic around the wires to insulate them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wrapping plastic around wires describes insulation on a cord, which is a completely different safety feature from a fuse.

choices.text: It adds resistance to the circuit to slow the extra current down.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because adding resistance to slow current is not what a fuse does; the article actually says a short circuit is dangerous because there is very little resistance.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the correct sequence of events that causes a damaged cord to start a fire?

choices.text: The metal melts, the circuit breaks, the current surges, and the wires heat up.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because metal melting describes what happens in a fuse as a safety response, not the cause of a fire from a damaged cord.

choices.text: The plastic cracks, two wires touch, the current surges, and the wires heat up.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly describes this exact sequence: damaged plastic exposes wires, the wires touch, current surges with little resistance, and the wires heat up and spark.

choices.text: The cord is pulled, the plug breaks, the current stops, and the sparks fly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pulling a cord and breaking a plug does not describe the short circuit process that leads to a fire.

choices.text: The wires heat up, the plastic cracks, two wires touch, and the current surges.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — the wires heating up is the result of the current surge, not the starting point of the problem.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the first section with the main idea of the last section?

choices.text: The first section warns about the hidden dangers of broken cords, while the last section warns about the extreme power of outdoor utility lines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because damaged cords are the main idea of the third section, not the first or last sections.

choices.text: The first section describes how people get shocked by everyday water, while the last section describes how to use electricity to light a room.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the first section focuses on circuits and loops, not on water dangers, which are covered in the second section.

choices.text: The first section explains how electricity moves around a physical racetrack, while the last section explains how electricity flows through an actual invisible river.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the racetrack and invisible river are just comparisons used to explain ideas — the sections are really about circuits and the value of understanding electricity, not about actual tracks or rivers.

choices.text: The first section explains how electricity travels in a continuous loop, while the last section explains why understanding this flow helps keep you safe.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the first section teaches that electricity travels in a loop (a circuit), and the last section says understanding that flow is your best safety tool.

assessments.title: Layers of the Earth

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Geology: The Earth's Surface and What Lies Beneath

Thinner Than You Think

Right now, you are sitting on a shell of rock thinner than the skin on an apple. Seriously. If you shrank the Earth down to the size of an apple, the ground beneath your feet — every mountain, every valley, every ocean floor — would be thinner than that red peel you bite through without thinking. Below that thin shell, our planet is a ball of superheated rock and metal stretching nearly four thousand miles down to its center. The Earth is built in layers, like a giant jawbreaker candy, and each layer is made of different materials at different temperatures.
stimulus.content_text: Geology: The Earth's Surface and What Lies BeneathThinner Than You Think Right now, you are sitting on a shell of rock thinner than the skin on an apple. Seriously. If you shrank the Earth down to the size of an apple, the ground beneath your feet — every mountain, every valley, every ocean floor — would be thinner than that red peel you bite through without thinking. Below that thin shell, our planet is a ball of superheated rock and metal stretching nearly four thousand miles down to its center. The Earth is built in layers, like a giant jawbreaker candy, and each layer is made of different materials at different temperatures.

prompt._: According to the text, unlike the thin shell of rock on the surface, what is the layer below it made of?

choices.text: A ball of superheated rock and metal
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that below the thin shell is 'a ball of superheated rock and metal' stretching thousands of miles deep.

choices.text: A layer of red apple skin and peel
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because apple skin is only used as a comparison to describe how thin the rock shell is — it's not a real layer inside the Earth.

choices.text: A core of completely frozen solid ice
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions ice, and in fact says the inside of the Earth is superheated, meaning extremely hot — the opposite of frozen!

choices.text: A shell of mountains and ocean floors
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because mountains and ocean floors are actually part of the thin shell on the surface, not the layer below it.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Crust: Where We Live

The outermost layer is the crust — the solid, rocky surface where people, animals, and plants live. Under the oceans, the crust is only about three miles thick, made of a dense, dark rock called basalt. Under the continents, the crust is thicker, averaging about twenty-five miles. Continental crust is made of lighter rock like granite, the speckled stone you might have seen in kitchen countertops.

The crust is not one smooth piece. It is cracked into enormous slabs called tectonic plates — giant puzzle pieces of rock that fit together to cover the whole planet. These plates are always moving, though very slowly. Some push together, crumpling the crust upward into mountain ranges. Some pull apart, creating deep valleys and ocean ridges. When plates suddenly slip or collide, the ground shakes — that is an earthquake. The crust may be the layer you know best, but it is the thinnest and coolest part of a scorching planet.
stimulus.content_text: The Crust: Where We Live The outermost layer is the crust — the solid, rocky surface where people, animals, and plants live. Under the oceans, the crust is only about three miles thick, made of a dense, dark rock called basalt. Under the continents, the crust is thicker, averaging about twenty-five miles. Continental crust is made of lighter rock like granite, the speckled stone you might have seen in kitchen countertops. The crust is not one smooth piece. It is cracked into enormous slabs called tectonic plates — giant puzzle pieces of rock that fit together to cover the whole planet. These plates are always moving, though very slowly. Some push together, crumpling the crust upward into mountain ranges. Some pull apart, creating deep valleys and ocean ridges. When plates suddenly slip or collide, the ground shakes — that is an earthquake. The crust may be the layer you know best, but it is the thinnest and coolest part of a scorching planet.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what causes an earthquake?

choices.text: Granite forming under the continents
choices.feedback: Granite forming under continents just describes what the crust is made of — the passage never connects granite to earthquakes.

choices.text: Plates slowly moving under the oceans
choices.feedback: Plates moving slowly under the oceans creates ocean ridges and valleys, not earthquakes — the passage says it has to be a sudden slip or collision.

choices.text: Plates suddenly slipping or colliding
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly states 'When plates suddenly slip or collide, the ground shakes — that is an earthquake.'

choices.text: Mountains rising high above the crust
choices.feedback: Mountains rising is caused by plates pushing together and crumpling the crust upward, which is a different event from an earthquake.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Mantle: Slow-Moving Rock

Below the crust lies the mantle, the thickest layer of all. It stretches about 1,800 miles deep and makes up most of Earth's volume. You might picture a sea of glowing liquid magma down there, but the mantle is actually mostly solid rock. Here is the surprising part: even though it is solid, it can flow very slowly over millions of years, like thick taffy being pulled in slow motion.

Temperatures in the mantle range from about 1,600 degrees Fahrenheit near the top to about 4,000 degrees near the bottom. That slow, creeping movement is what pushes the tectonic plates above it. The plates ride on the mantle the way crackers float on thick, barely bubbling soup. When mantle rock rises close enough to the surface, it can melt into magma and feed volcanoes.

You might picture a sea of glowing liquid magma down there, but the mantle is actually mostly solid rock. Here is the surprising part: even though it is solid, it can flow very slowly over millions of years, like thick taffy being pulled in slow motion. Temperatures in the mantle range from about 1,600 degrees Fahrenheit near the top to about 4,000 degrees near the bottom. That slow, creeping movement is what pushes the tectonic plates above it. The plates ride on the mantle the way crackers float on thick, barely bubbling soup. When mantle rock rises close enough to the surface, it can melt into magma and feed volcanoes.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the correct order of steps that leads to volcanoes being fed by magma?

choices.text: Mantle rock melts into magma, then it rises close to the surface
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the order — the passage says the rock rises first and melts second, not the other way around.

choices.text: Magma feeds volcanoes, then mantle rock rises, then the rock melts
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it lists the steps in reverse order — magma feeding volcanoes is actually the last thing that happens, not the first.

choices.text: Mantle rock rises close to the surface, then it melts into magma
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches exactly what the passage says: mantle rock rises close to the surface first, and then it melts into magma that feeds volcanoes.

choices.text: Tectonic plates push mantle rock up, then magma forms underground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says tectonic plates push mantle rock upward — that idea is not supported by the text.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Core: Metal, Not Fire

At the center of the Earth sit two layers: the outer core and the inner core. You might imagine flames or lava down there, but the core contains neither. It is made of metal — mostly iron and nickel.

The outer core is about 1,400 miles thick, and its metal is liquid. This liquid metal churns and swirls constantly. That swirling motion generates Earth's magnetic field — an invisible force that surrounds the planet like a giant shield. The magnetic field is what makes a compass needle point north. It also stretches far into space, pushing away harmful radiation from the sun.

Deeper still, about 3,950 miles beneath your feet, sits the inner core. Temperatures there reach about 9,800 degrees Fahrenheit — nearly as hot as the surface of the sun. Yet the inner core is completely solid. The pressure from all the rock and metal above it is so intense that iron atoms are squeezed together and cannot flow.

You might imagine flames or lava down there, but the core contains neither. It is made of metal — mostly iron and nickel. The outer core is about 1,400 miles thick, and its metal is liquid. This liquid metal churns and swirls constantly. That swirling motion generates Earth's magnetic field — an invisible force that surrounds the planet like a giant shield. The magnetic field is what makes a compass needle point north. It also stretches far into space, pushing away harmful radiation from the sun. Deeper still, about 3,950 miles beneath your feet, sits the inner core. Temperatures there reach about 9,800 degrees Fahrenheit — nearly as hot as the surface of the sun. Yet the inner core is completely solid. The pressure from all the rock and metal above it is so intense that iron atoms are squeezed together and cannot flow.

prompt._: The author uses the phrase "like a giant shield" to describe the magnetic field. Based on the text, how are the magnetic field and a shield similar?

choices.text: They both make compasses point north.
choices.feedback: Making a compass point north is something the magnetic field does, but that has nothing to do with what a shield does.

choices.text: They both reach extremely high heat.
choices.feedback: Extreme heat describes the inner core, not the magnetic field or a shield, so this doesn't explain the comparison at all.

choices.text: They both push away harmful things.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the magnetic field pushes away harmful radiation, and a shield's job is to push away harmful things — that's exactly what makes the comparison work.

choices.text: They both swirl around constantly.
choices.feedback: The liquid metal in the outer core swirls around, but a shield doesn't swirl, so this isn't why the magnetic field is compared to a shield.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
How Scientists See the Invisible

No one has ever visited these deep layers. The deepest hole ever drilled, the Kola Superdeep Borehole in Russia, reached only about seven and a half miles down — not even through the continental crust. So how do scientists know what is inside?

They listen to earthquakes. When an earthquake strikes, it sends seismic waves — vibrations that travel through the Earth the way sound waves travel through air. Different waves move at different speeds through solid rock and liquid metal. Some bend. Some bounce. Some stop completely when they hit liquid. By tracking how these waves travel, scientists have mapped every hidden layer, like doctors using an X-ray to see bones inside your body.
stimulus.content_text: How Scientists See the Invisible No one has ever visited these deep layers. The deepest hole ever drilled, the Kola Superdeep Borehole in Russia, reached only about seven and a half miles down — not even through the continental crust. So how do scientists know what is inside? They listen to earthquakes. When an earthquake strikes, it sends seismic waves — vibrations that travel through the Earth the way sound waves travel through air. Different waves move at different speeds through solid rock and liquid metal. Some bend. Some bounce. Some stop completely when they hit liquid. By tracking how these waves travel, scientists have mapped every hidden layer, like doctors using an X-ray to see bones inside your body.

prompt._: According to the text, what are seismic waves?

choices.text: liquid metals found inside the Earth's layers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because liquid metal describes what is inside the Earth's layers, not what seismic waves are — seismic waves actually travel through liquid metal.

choices.text: vibrations that travel through the Earth
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines seismic waves as 'vibrations that travel through the Earth,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: X-ray pictures taken of the Earth's core
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses X-rays as a comparison to explain how seismic waves work, but X-ray pictures are not the same thing as seismic waves.

choices.text: deep holes that scientists drill into the crust
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because deep holes refer to things like the Kola Superdeep Borehole that scientists drill into the ground, which is a completely different thing from seismic waves.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Planet That Builds and Breaks

Every mountain, beach, and valley you have ever seen exists because of what happens below the crust. Deep heat moves the mantle. The mantle pushes the plates. The plates build mountains, open oceans, and shake the ground. Liquid iron in the outer core wraps the whole planet in a magnetic shield. You live on the thinnest layer of a four-thousand-mile-deep machine — and that machine is still running.
stimulus.content_text: A Planet That Builds and Breaks Every mountain, beach, and valley you have ever seen exists because of what happens below the crust. Deep heat moves the mantle. The mantle pushes the plates. The plates build mountains, open oceans, and shake the ground. Liquid iron in the outer core wraps the whole planet in a magnetic shield. You live on the thinnest layer of a four-thousand-mile-deep machine — and that machine is still running.

prompt._: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that Earth's surface features are caused by forces deep underground?

choices.text: People live on the thinnest layer of a four-thousand-mile-deep machine.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it tells us how deep Earth is, but it doesn't explain what underground forces create surface features.

choices.text: Liquid iron in the outer core creates a magnetic shield around the planet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the magnetic shield protects the planet, but it doesn't explain how surface features like mountains and oceans are formed.

choices.text: Every mountain, beach, and valley you have ever seen exists on the crust.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only tells us that surface features exist on the crust, not what underground forces cause them to form.

choices.text: The mantle pushes the plates, which build mountains and open oceans.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it directly shows how a deep underground force — the mantle — pushes plates that then build mountains and open oceans on the surface.

prompt._: How is the inner core different from the outer core, according to the article?

choices.text: The inner core is cooler than the outer core and closer to the surface.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the inner core is deeper inside the Earth and reaches nearly 9,800 degrees Fahrenheit, making it hotter than the outer core, not cooler.

choices.text: The inner core is made of rock, while the outer core is made of metal.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says both the inner and outer core are made of metal — mostly iron and nickel — not rock.

choices.text: The inner core creates the magnetic field, while the outer core does not.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says it is the outer core's churning liquid metal that generates Earth's magnetic field, not the inner core.

choices.text: The inner core is solid, while the outer core is liquid.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly states the outer core is liquid metal that swirls around, while the inner core is completely solid due to the intense pressure pressing down on it.

prompt._: Based on the article, how does the heat deep inside the Earth cause mountains to form on the surface?

choices.text: The heat melts the crust into magma, which bubbles up to build tall peaks.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the article says magma can feed volcanoes, not that melting crust bubbles up to build mountain ranges.

choices.text: The heat causes the ground to shake, which cracks the crust into giant pieces.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — earthquakes happen when plates suddenly slip or collide, but shaking the ground and cracking the crust is not how mountains are formed.

choices.text: The heat creates a magnetic field, which pulls heavy rocks upward into mountains.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the magnetic field is created by swirling liquid metal in the outer core and protects us from radiation, but it has nothing to do with pulling rocks up into mountains.

prompt._: In what order does the author organize the information about Earth's layers across the passage?

choices.text: By describing the deepest center first and moving up to the surface.

prompt._: Comparing the sections about the mantle and the outer core, which statement best describes a main idea that both sections share?

choices.text: Both sections explain how movement inside Earth creates forces that affect the surface.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both sections show how movement inside Earth — the flowing mantle pushing plates, and the swirling outer core creating a magnetic field — creates forces that affect the surface and beyond.

choices.text: Both sections explain that their layers are the thickest parts of the planet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the mantle is described as the thickest layer — the outer core is never called the thickest part of the planet.

choices.text: Both sections focus on how scientists use seismic waves to study hidden layers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because seismic waves are only explained in the 'How Scientists See the Invisible' section, not in the mantle or outer core sections.

choices.text: Both sections describe layers that are completely liquid and flow very quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the mantle is mostly solid rock, not liquid, and it moves extremely slowly over millions of years.

syllabus_metadata.title: Layers of the Earth

assessments.title: Plate Tectonics: A Moving Puzzle

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Moving Earth Beneath Your Feet

A Planet Cracked Into Pieces

Stamp your foot on the ground. It feels rock-solid, like one enormous slab of stone stretching in every direction. But here is the surprising part: Earth's surface is not one solid piece like an eggshell. Instead, it is cracked into many large sections that are slowly drifting apart, crunching together, and sliding past one another — right now, as you read this sentence.

Others sit beneath the ocean floor. Every single one of them is moving. About fifteen major plates exist, along with several smaller ones. These sections are called tectonic plates — giant slabs of rock that make up Earth's outer shell, called the crust.

Underneath the crust lies a thick, hot layer of rock called the mantle. Even though the mantle is made of rock, it is so hot that parts of it flow very slowly. The tectonic plates float on top of this flowing rock, the way crackers float on a bowl of hot, thick soup.
stimulus.content_text: The Moving Earth Beneath Your FeetA Planet Cracked Into Pieces Stamp your foot on the ground. It feels rock-solid, like one enormous slab of stone stretching in every direction. But here is the surprising part: Earth's surface is not one solid piece like an eggshell. Instead, it is cracked into many large sections that are slowly drifting apart, crunching together, and sliding past one another — right now, as you read this sentence. Others sit beneath the ocean floor. Every single one of them is moving. About fifteen major plates exist, along with several smaller ones. These sections are called tectonic plates — giant slabs of rock that make up Earth's outer shell, called the crust. Underneath the crust lies a thick, hot layer of rock called the mantle. Even though the mantle is made of rock, it is so hot that parts of it flow very slowly. The tectonic plates float on top of this flowing rock, the way crackers float on a bowl of hot, thick soup.

prompt._: According to the passage, how does Earth's surface seem different from how it actually is?

choices.text: It seems like dry land but is actually floating on top of water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the plates float on the mantle, which is hot flowing rock, not on water.

choices.text: It seems like it is moving but is actually standing perfectly still
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the tectonic plates are always moving — drifting, crunching, and sliding — right now.

choices.text: It seems thin like an eggshell but is actually thick like a mantle
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses the eggshell comparison to explain what Earth's surface is NOT like, not to say it looks thin.

choices.text: It seems like one solid piece but is actually cracked into moving sections
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the ground feels like one solid piece when you stamp your foot, but it is actually cracked into many moving tectonic plates.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Slower Than Your Fingernails

You might think that moving plates would cause the ground to lurch under your shoes. Actually, the plates creep along at only about one to four inches per year. That is roughly the speed your fingernails grow. You would never feel it. But over millions of years, those tiny movements add up to enormous changes — oceans open, mountains rise, and entire continents drift thousands of miles from where they started.
stimulus.content_text: Slower Than Your Fingernails You might think that moving plates would cause the ground to lurch under your shoes. Actually, the plates creep along at only about one to four inches per year. That is roughly the speed your fingernails grow. You would never feel it. But over millions of years, those tiny movements add up to enormous changes — oceans open, mountains rise, and entire continents drift thousands of miles from where they started.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why do tectonic plates cause enormous changes like rising mountains and opening oceans?

choices.text: because small movements add up over millions of years
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that tiny movements 'add up to enormous changes' over millions of years.

choices.text: because the plates suddenly lurch and shift all at once
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the plates 'creep along' slowly, not that they suddenly lurch or shift all at once.

choices.text: because the plates move fast enough for people to feel them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the plates move so slowly that 'you would never feel it.'

choices.text: because fingernails and plates push with great force
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses fingernails only to compare the speed of movement, not to say that fingernails or plates push with great force.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Scientist Nobody Believed

In 1912, a German scientist named Alfred Wegener stared at a map of the world and noticed something strange. The eastern coastline of South America and the western coastline of Africa fit together like two pieces of a jigsaw puzzle. He dug deeper. Fossils of a small reptile called Mesosaurus had been found in both South America and Africa — but Mesosaurus could not have swum across an entire ocean. Identical plant fossils appeared on continents separated by thousands of miles of water.

Wegener proposed a bold idea: all the continents were once joined in a single supercontinent he called Pangaea, a Greek word meaning "all lands." Over hundreds of millions of years, Pangaea broke apart and the pieces drifted to where they sit today.

Most scientists rejected his idea. Wegener could not explain what force pushed the continents around. He died in 1930, still waiting for proof. It did not arrive until the 1960s, when scientists explored the ocean floor and discovered underwater mountain ridges. Hot rock called magma — melted rock from deep inside the Earth — was rising up through cracks in those ridges and pushing the plates apart. This rising magma, combined with the slow flow of the mantle underneath, is the engine that drives the plates.
stimulus.content_text: The Scientist Nobody Believed In 1912, a German scientist named Alfred Wegener stared at a map of the world and noticed something strange. The eastern coastline of South America and the western coastline of Africa fit together like two pieces of a jigsaw puzzle. He dug deeper. Fossils of a small reptile called Mesosaurus had been found in both South America and Africa — but Mesosaurus could not have swum across an entire ocean. Identical plant fossils appeared on continents separated by thousands of miles of water. Wegener proposed a bold idea: all the continents were once joined in a single supercontinent he called Pangaea, a Greek word meaning "all lands." Over hundreds of millions of years, Pangaea broke apart and the pieces drifted to where they sit today. Most scientists rejected his idea. Wegener could not explain what force pushed the continents around. He died in 1930, still waiting for proof. It did not arrive until the 1960s, when scientists explored the ocean floor and discovered underwater mountain ridges. Hot rock called magma — melted rock from deep inside the Earth — was rising up through cracks in those ridges and pushing the plates apart. This rising magma, combined with the slow flow of the mantle underneath, is the engine that drives the plates.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens next in the process after hot magma rises up through cracks in the underwater ridges?

choices.text: It creates new underwater ocean fossils.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions magma creating fossils underwater — fossils were only mentioned in South America and Africa.

choices.text: It flows back deep inside the Earth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes magma rising up and pushing outward through the cracks, not flowing back down into the Earth.

choices.text: It joins the continents into Pangaea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because magma pushing plates apart is what separates continents, which is the opposite of joining them into Pangaea.

choices.text: It pushes the Earth's plates apart.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that magma rises through the cracks and then pushes the plates apart — that's the next step in the process.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Where Plates Meet: Three Kinds of Boundaries

You might think earthquakes and volcanoes can strike anywhere at random. But most happen along the edges where plates meet. These edges are called boundaries, and three types exist.

When two plates crash into each other, one may slide beneath the other and sink into the mantle. Scientists call this subduction. The collision crumples rock upward, building mountains, and triggers volcanic eruptions along the edge. The Himalayas, the tallest mountains on Earth, are still rising because the Indian plate keeps slamming into the Eurasian plate. That collision also causes powerful earthquakes in the region.

When two plates pull apart, magma rises from below to fill the gap and hardens into new crust. This is called a divergent boundary — "divergent" means moving apart, like two friends walking in opposite directions. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is a massive underwater mountain chain running down the middle of the Atlantic Ocean. Right there, new ocean floor is being created as plates separate.

When two plates slide past each other in opposite directions, the boundary is called a transform boundary. The friction — the resistance created when two surfaces grind against each other — builds up pressure. When that pressure releases suddenly, the ground shakes. The San Andreas Fault in California is a transform boundary where the Pacific plate and the North American plate grind past each other. That friction is why California experiences frequent earthquakes.

The collision crumples rock upward, building mountains, and triggers volcanic eruptions along the edge. prompt._: Based on the comparison to "two friends walking in opposite directions," what does the word "divergent" mean?

choices.text: Sliding right past each other
choices.feedback: Sliding past each other describes a transform boundary, like the San Andreas Fault in California, not a divergent boundary.

choices.text: Slamming hard into each other
choices.feedback: Slamming into each other describes a collision, which is what happens at a subduction boundary like where the Indian plate crashes into the Eurasian plate — not a divergent boundary.

choices.text: Sinking deep under each other
choices.feedback: Sinking under each other describes subduction, where one plate slides beneath another and sinks into the mantle — that's a completely different type of boundary.

choices.text: Moving away from each other
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'divergent means moving apart,' and the comparison to friends walking in opposite directions shows they are moving away from each other.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Puzzle That Never Stops

Right now, the Atlantic Ocean is growing wider by about one inch per year. Australia is drifting north. The Himalayas are inching higher. You will never feel any of it in your lifetime, but the evidence is everywhere — in the fossils of tropical plants buried in frozen Antarctica, in the earthquakes that shake the Pacific coast, in the volcanoes that ring the ocean like a necklace of fire. The ground beneath you is not a stage. It is a story still being written, one inch at a time.

The ground beneath you is not a stage. It is a story still being written, one inch at a time.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the author mean by calling the volcanoes a 'necklace of fire'?

choices.text: Volcanoes that form a ring around the ocean
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the volcanoes 'ring the ocean like a necklace,' meaning they form a circle or ring around it.

choices.text: Volcanoes that are found deep under the sea
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say the volcanoes are deep under the sea — it describes where they are located around the ocean.

choices.text: Volcanoes that melt the rocks beneath them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the 'necklace of fire' is describing the shape and location of the volcanoes, not what happens to rocks beneath them.

choices.text: Volcanoes that explode in a straight line
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage compares the volcanoes to a necklace, which is a circle shape, not a straight line.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the evidence Wegener used to study the continents different from the evidence scientists observe today?

choices.text: Wegener studied underwater mountain ridges and rising magma, while scientists today observe matching coastlines and continent shapes.
choices.feedback: B is backwards — the passage says Wegener studied matching coastlines and fossils, while scientists today discovered the underwater ridges and rising magma.

choices.text: Wegener observed flowing mantle rock and sinking plates, while scientists today focus on solid rock and outer crust.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Wegener observed flowing mantle rock or sinking plates — those are scientific discoveries that came after his time.

choices.text: Wegener measured powerful earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, while scientists today study ancient plant and reptile fossils.
choices.feedback: C is backwards — the passage never says Wegener measured earthquakes or volcanoes; those are examples of evidence we see today, not what Wegener used.

choices.text: Wegener studied matching plant and animal fossils, while scientists today measure widening oceans and rising mountains.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows Wegener used fossils and coastline shapes, while today's scientists measure things like widening oceans and growing mountains.

prompt._: Why does the author say the ground beneath you is "a story still being written"?

choices.text: because moving plates are still changing Earth little by little
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows Earth is still changing right now, with oceans growing wider, continents drifting, and mountains rising little by little.

choices.text: because earthquakes and volcanoes happen in every place on Earth
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly explains that earthquakes and volcanoes mostly happen along plate boundaries, not randomly everywhere.

choices.text: because the continents have stopped moving since Pangaea broke apart
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the continents are still moving today, even if we can't feel it.

choices.text: because scientists are still guessing where the plates are
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because scientists have already discovered and mapped the tectonic plates — they aren't just guessing.

prompt._: Which sequence of key details from the article best supports the main idea that Earth's surface is always changing?

choices.text: Continents were once joined together, then plates slowly moved apart, then oceans and mountains formed.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it traces how Earth's surface changed over time — from one supercontinent, to drifting plates, to the oceans and mountains we have today — which directly supports the main idea.

choices.text: Wegener noticed matching coastlines, then scientists rejected his idea, then he died waiting for proof.
choices.feedback: C tells the story of what happened to Wegener the scientist, not about how Earth's surface itself has been changing.

choices.text: Plates float on the mantle, then magma rises through cracks, then new crust forms at divergent boundaries.
choices.feedback: A describes the mechanics of plate movement and new crust forming, but it doesn't show the big, long-term changes to Earth's surface the way B does.

choices.text: Mountains rose first, then Pangaea formed from the pieces, then the plates stopped moving.
choices.feedback: D gets the order completely wrong — Pangaea came first and then broke apart, and the passage clearly says the plates have never stopped moving.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are the fossils found in South America and Africa similar to the fossils found in Antarctica?

choices.text: They both provide evidence that the continents have moved over millions of years.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the South America/Africa fossils and the Antarctica plant fossils are used as evidence that continents have moved over millions of years.

choices.text: They both explain why powerful earthquakes happen along the edges of tectonic plates.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because fossils don't explain earthquakes; earthquakes happen because of pressure building up where tectonic plates meet, not because of fossil evidence.

choices.text: They both show that small reptiles swam across the oceans to new lands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says Mesosaurus could NOT have swum across an entire ocean, which is actually why the fossils are such important clues.

choices.text: They both prove that the Earth's thick mantle is made of flowing rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the fossils have nothing to do with proving the mantle is made of flowing rock — that's a separate idea in the passage.

syllabus_metadata.title: Plate Tectonics: A Moving Puzzle

assessments.title: Earthquakes: When the Ground Shakes

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
When the Ground Shakes: The Science of Earthquakes

A Crack Beneath the City

At 5:12 in the morning on April 18, 1906, the city of San Francisco jolted awake. In less than a minute, one of the most powerful earthquakes in American history had torn the city apart. That earthquake happened over a hundred years ago, but the forces that caused it are still at work right now, deep beneath your feet.

To understand earthquakes, you first need to know something surprising about the ground you stand on. Earth's outer shell is not one solid piece. It is broken into enormous slabs called tectonic plates — giant puzzle pieces of rock that fit together and cover the entire planet, some wide enough to carry whole continents.

These plates are not sitting still. They drift slowly, pushed by heat rising from deep inside the Earth. They slide past each other, pull apart, or crunch together at their edges. But the plates do not glide smoothly. Friction — the gripping force between two surfaces, like the resistance you feel when you drag your sneaker across a rubber mat — locks the plates together at their borders. Pressure builds. Rocks along the boundary bend and strain, sometimes for centuries. Then, in a sudden snap, the rocks break free and lurch forward. All that stored energy bursts outward as seismic waves — vibrations that ripple through rock the way ripples spread across a pond when you toss in a stone. Those vibrations are what you feel as shaking.
