In less than a minute, one of the most powerful earthquakes in American history had torn the city apart. That earthquake happened over a hundred years ago, but the forces that caused it are still at work right now, deep beneath your feet. To understand earthquakes, you first need to know something surprising about the ground you stand on. Earth's outer shell is not one solid piece. It is broken into enormous slabs called tectonic plates — giant puzzle pieces of rock that fit together and cover the entire planet, some wide enough to carry whole continents. These plates are not sitting still. They drift slowly, pushed by heat rising from deep inside the Earth. They slide past each other, pull apart, or crunch together at their edges. But the plates do not glide smoothly. Friction — the gripping force between two surfaces, like the resistance you feel when you drag your sneaker across a rubber mat — locks the plates together at their borders. Pressure builds. Rocks along the boundary bend and strain, sometimes for centuries. Then, in a sudden snap, the rocks break free and lurch forward. All that stored energy bursts outward as seismic waves — vibrations that ripple through rock the way ripples spread across a pond when you toss in a stone. Those vibrations are what you feel as shaking.

prompt._: The text states that the 1906 earthquake happened over a hundred years ago. In contrast, what does the text say about the forces that caused it?

choices.text: They are broken into giant puzzle pieces now.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tectonic plates are the puzzle pieces, not the forces — and the plates being broken into pieces is not something that just happened 'now.'

choices.text: They only cause shaking early in the morning.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the forces only happen in the morning; that detail was about when the 1906 earthquake struck, not about the forces.

choices.text: They are still at work deep underground today.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the forces that caused the earthquake 'are still at work right now, deep beneath your feet.'

choices.text: They have completely stopped moving over time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the forces are still active, not stopped.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Where Earthquakes Begin and How They Are Measured

You might think earthquakes start at a crack you can see on the surface. Actually, the rupture begins underground, sometimes miles deep. The exact underground spot where the rock first breaks is called the focus. Directly above the focus, up on the surface, sits a point called the epicenter — the bullseye of the action. The shaking is usually strongest at the epicenter and weakens the farther you travel from it.

Not all earthquakes are equal. Some are so gentle you would never notice them. Others flatten entire neighborhoods. In 1935, scientist Charles Richter developed the Richter scale, one of the first tools used to measure earthquake strength. Today, scientists rely on the moment magnitude scale, which calculates the total energy an earthquake releases. A magnitude 2 or 3 quake is barely felt. A magnitude 5 can crack walls. A magnitude 7 or higher can cause massive destruction.

Here's the surprising part: it's easy to assume a magnitude 6 is just a little stronger than a magnitude 5. But the scale is logarithmic, meaning each whole number jump represents about thirty-two times more energy. A magnitude 7 earthquake releases roughly a thousand times more energy than a magnitude 5.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why can a magnitude 7 earthquake cause much more damage than a magnitude 5 earthquake?

choices.text: Because it releases far more energy
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly explains that because the scale is logarithmic, a magnitude 7 releases about a thousand times more energy than a magnitude 5, which is why it causes so much more damage.

choices.text: Because it starts closer to the surface
choices.feedback: The passage never says that a magnitude 7 earthquake starts closer to the surface — depth of the focus isn't connected to the magnitude number.

choices.text: Because it happens at the epicenter
choices.feedback: The epicenter is the surface point above where the earthquake starts, but the passage doesn't say higher-magnitude earthquakes happen at the epicenter while lower ones don't — all earthquakes have an epicenter.

choices.text: Because it lasts many more hours
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions how long earthquakes last, so there's no evidence that a magnitude 7 lasts many more hours.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Where Earthquakes Happen Most: The Ring of Fire

Earthquakes do not strike randomly. About ninety percent of them happen along a horseshoe-shaped zone that wraps around the edges of the Pacific Ocean. Scientists call this zone the Pacific Ring of Fire. It follows the borders of several large tectonic plates, where all that pushing and grinding creates constant stress. Countries along the Ring of Fire — Japan, Chile, Indonesia, and the western coast of the United States — experience earthquakes far more often than places in the middle of a plate.
stimulus.content_text: Where Earthquakes Happen Most: The Ring of Fire Earthquakes do not strike randomly. About ninety percent of them happen along a horseshoe-shaped zone that wraps around the edges of the Pacific Ocean. Scientists call this zone the Pacific Ring of Fire. It follows the borders of several large tectonic plates, where all that pushing and grinding creates constant stress. Countries along the Ring of Fire — Japan, Chile, Indonesia, and the western coast of the United States — experience earthquakes far more often than places in the middle of a plate.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the correct order of events that happen at the Ring of Fire?

choices.text: Earthquakes happen, plates push and grind, and stress is created.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — earthquakes are the result of the pushing and grinding, not the starting point.

choices.text: The ocean wraps around, a horseshoe forms, and earthquakes happen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the horseshoe shape of the ocean describes where the Ring of Fire is located, not the steps that cause earthquakes.

choices.text: Scientists name the zone, plates push and grind, and earthquakes happen.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because scientists naming the zone is something humans do to describe it, and it has nothing to do with causing earthquakes.

choices.text: Plates push and grind, stress is created, and earthquakes happen.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows this cause-and-effect chain: plates push and grind → stress builds up → earthquakes happen.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Reading Seismic Waves and the Scientists Who Study Them

The seismic waves from an earthquake come in different types. P-waves (primary waves) arrive first, pushing and pulling through rock like squeezing and stretching a spring. S-waves (secondary waves) come next, shaking the ground up and down or side to side. Surface waves travel along the top of the Earth and usually cause the most damage because they produce the strongest rolling and shaking.

Scientists called seismologists use sensitive instruments called seismographs to detect and record these waves. By comparing wave patterns picked up at different locations, seismologists can figure out exactly where an earthquake started, how deep its focus was, and how strong it was. You might expect that scientists can also predict the next earthquake. They cannot — not yet. But seismologists have mapped the high-risk zones, and they study every quake to learn more.

P-waves (primary waves) arrive first, pushing and pulling through rock like squeezing and stretching a spring. S-waves (secondary waves) come next, shaking the ground up and down or side to side. By comparing wave patterns picked up at different locations, seismologists can figure out exactly where an earthquake started, how deep its focus was, and how strong it was. You might expect that scientists can also predict the next earthquake. They cannot — not yet. But seismologists have mapped the high-risk zones, and they study every quake to learn more.

prompt._: Based on the comparison to a spring, what is the meaning of the word "P-waves" in the passage?

choices.text: Waves that cause the most damage to the Earth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says surface waves cause the most damage, not P-waves.

choices.text: Waves that shake the ground side to side.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because shaking the ground side to side describes S-waves, not P-waves.

choices.text: Waves that push and pull through the rock.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says P-waves move by 'pushing and pulling through rock,' and the spring comparison helps show that same push-and-pull motion.

choices.text: Waves that are built out of metal springs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the spring is just a comparison to help explain how P-waves move — P-waves are not actually built out of metal springs.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Building for the Shake

Communities along the Ring of Fire do not just wait and hope. Engineers design buildings with flexible frames that can sway during shaking instead of snapping. Schools in earthquake zones hold drills so students practice the life-saving response: drop, cover, and hold on.

The ground beneath you is never truly still — it is shifting, straining, and storing energy right now. Knowing that can make you the person in the room who understands what is happening, stays calm, and knows exactly what to do.

Schools in earthquake zones hold drills so students practice the life-saving response: drop, cover, and hold on. The ground beneath you is never truly still — it is shifting, straining, and storing energy right now. Knowing that can make you the person in the room who understands what is happening, stays calm, and knows exactly what to do.

choices.text: a way engineers design buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because designing buildings with flexible frames is the engineers' job, not what 'drop, cover, and hold on' describes.

choices.text: a plan for building flexible frames
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because flexible frames are a building design idea, while 'drop, cover, and hold on' is something people do to stay safe.

choices.text: a method for measuring ground shaking
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never talks about measuring how much the ground shakes.

choices.text: a life-saving response for earthquakes
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly calls 'drop, cover, and hold on' a 'life-saving response' that students practice during earthquake drills.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between places along the Pacific Ring of Fire and places in the middle of a tectonic plate?

choices.text: Places along the Ring of Fire have safer buildings because earthquakes begin at the surface.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly explains that earthquakes start underground at the focus, not at the surface.

choices.text: Places along the Ring of Fire have weaker shaking because they are farther from plate edges.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the Ring of Fire has more earthquakes due to being near plate borders, not weaker shaking from being farther away.

choices.text: Places along the Ring of Fire have more earthquakes because they are near plate borders.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that 90% of earthquakes happen along the Ring of Fire where tectonic plates meet, compared to calmer areas in the middle of plates.

choices.text: Places along the Ring of Fire have fewer earthquakes because their plates move more slowly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Ring of Fire actually has more earthquakes than other places, which is the opposite of what this answer says.

prompt._: Based on the article, why do schools in some places practice earthquake drills more often than schools in other places?

choices.text: Because those places have more earthquakes near plate borders
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that 90% of earthquakes happen along the Ring of Fire where plates meet, so schools in those areas need to practice drills more often.

choices.text: Because those places have stronger buildings than other places
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because flexible buildings are a safety feature engineers design, not a reason why schools in those areas practice earthquake drills more.

choices.text: Because those places can predict earthquakes before they begin
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly tells us that scientists cannot predict earthquakes yet.

choices.text: Because those places are closer to the epicenter of every earthquake
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because earthquakes happen in many different places, so schools aren't always near the same epicenter — each earthquake starts somewhere new.

prompt._: Which sequence best describes the order of steps the article explains from when an earthquake starts underground to when people respond to it?

choices.text: Engineers build flexible buildings, then tectonic plates move, then seismic waves travel through rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because engineers building flexible buildings is something people do to prepare before an earthquake, so it doesn't belong in a sequence describing what happens when an earthquake actually starts and unfolds.

choices.text: Seismologists record waves, then rocks break underground, then people drop, cover, and hold on.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts seismologists recording waves first, but the article clearly shows that rocks breaking underground is the very first step — seismologists record the waves after the earthquake has already started.

choices.text: Rocks break at the focus, then seismic waves shake the surface, then people drop, cover, and hold on.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it follows the exact order the article describes: rocks break at the focus underground, seismic waves travel up and shake the surface, and then people use the 'drop, cover, and hold on' response.

choices.text: People feel shaking at the epicenter, then rocks break at the focus, then seismologists measure the waves.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it reverses the order — people feel shaking at the epicenter only after the rocks have already broken at the focus, so the rock breaking must come first.

prompt._: Which sentence best summarizes the article?

choices.text: Scientists have learned how to predict earthquakes caused by friction between tectonic plates.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that scientists cannot yet predict earthquakes, even though they have mapped high-risk zones.

choices.text: Earthquakes starting deep underground send out strong waves that always cause massive destruction.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says many earthquakes are so small you never feel them, so earthquakes definitely do not always cause massive destruction.

choices.text: Scientists use special tools to measure the energy of earthquakes along the Pacific Ring of Fire.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only covers one small part of the article, leaving out tectonic plates, seismic waves, building safety, and the main message about being prepared.

choices.text: Understanding how shifting plates cause earthquakes helps people stay calm and know what to do.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it captures the whole article's big idea — learning about shifting plates and earthquakes helps people understand what's happening and know how to stay safe.

assessments.title: Volcanoes: Fire from Below

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Volcanoes: Fire from Below

A Mountain Explodes

At 8:32 on a Sunday morning in May 1980, the entire north face of Mount St. Helens in Washington State slid away. Seconds later, a blast of superheated rock, ash, and gas ripped sideways out of the mountain at 300 miles per hour. The explosion tore away the top 1,300 feet of the peak and flattened forests for miles in every direction. One of the most powerful volcanic eruptions in American history had just begun.

But what caused it?
stimulus.content_text: Volcanoes: Fire from BelowA Mountain Explodes At 8:32 on a Sunday morning in May 1980, the entire north face of Mount St. Helens in Washington State slid away. Seconds later, a blast of superheated rock, ash, and gas ripped sideways out of the mountain at 300 miles per hour. The explosion tore away the top 1,300 feet of the peak and flattened forests for miles in every direction. One of the most powerful volcanic eruptions in American history had just begun. But what caused it?

prompt._: According to the text, how was the blast from Mount St. Helens different from what you might expect an explosion from a mountaintop to do?

choices.text: It ripped sideways instead of shooting straight up
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the blast 'ripped sideways out of the mountain,' which is the opposite of what you'd expect from a mountaintop explosion.

choices.text: It only destroyed the bottom of the mountain
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the explosion destroyed the top of the mountain, tearing away the top 1,300 feet of the peak.

choices.text: It moved slowly down the mountain's south side
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the blast moved extremely fast at 300 miles per hour and went sideways, not slowly down the south side.

choices.text: It erupted from underground rather than the peak
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the blast came out of the mountain's north face, not from underground.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How a Volcano Is Born

Miles beneath your feet, the rock is so hot it melts. This melted rock underground is called magma — think of it as liquid stone trapped in a pressure cooker. Magma is lighter than the solid rock around it, so it pushes upward through cracks in the Earth's crust, the thin outer shell of rock we live on. When magma breaks through the surface, it gets a new name: lava. You might think they are different substances, but the difference is simply location — underground versus aboveground.

A volcano forms wherever magma finds a path to the surface. But volcanoes don't pop up just anywhere. They appear in specific zones connected to the movement of tectonic plates — enormous slabs of rock that fit together like puzzle pieces and make up the Earth's crust. Where two plates pull apart, magma rises to fill the gap. Where one plate slides beneath another, the sinking plate melts, and new magma pushes upward. That is why so many volcanoes ring the Pacific Ocean, where several plates meet.

Some volcanoes break the pattern. The Hawaiian Islands sit in the middle of a plate, not at an edge. They formed over a hot spot, a place where a column of extremely hot rock rises from the mantle — the thick, slow-moving layer between the crust and the Earth's core. As the plate drifts over the hot spot, the heat melts through the crust and builds a new volcano. Each Hawaiian island was built over that same hot spot at a different time.

As the plate drifts over the hot spot, the heat melts through the crust and builds a new volcano. Each Hawaiian island was built over that same hot spot at a different time.

prompt._: Based on the section, which statement best summarizes why so many volcanoes are found around the Pacific Ocean?

choices.text: Lava from the Pacific Ocean floor cools and builds up into volcanic islands.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not describe lava cooling on the ocean floor as the reason for Pacific volcanoes — it points to tectonic plate activity instead.

choices.text: The Pacific Ocean has many hot spots that melt through the crust over time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says hot spots explain volcanoes like Hawaii, which sit in the middle of a plate — not the ones around the Pacific Ocean's edges.

choices.text: The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean, so it has the most room for volcanoes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects the size of an ocean to the number of volcanoes — volcanoes form because of plate movement, not available space.

choices.text: Several tectonic plates meet there, creating places for magma to push up.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that many volcanoes ring the Pacific Ocean 'where several plates meet,' and plate edges are where magma pushes upward.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Three Shapes, Three Styles

If you picture a volcano, you probably imagine a tall, steep cone with an explosion at the top. Scientists group volcanoes into three main kinds based on shape and eruption style.

Shield volcanoes are wide and gently sloped, like an upside-down dinner plate. They form from thin, runny lava that flows far from the opening, called the vent, before it cools and hardens. Mauna Loa in Hawaii is a shield volcano and the largest active volcano on Earth. Its eruptions are usually quiet — rivers of glowing lava rather than violent explosions.

Cinder cone volcanoes are the smallest type. They form when chunks of lava shoot into the air and pile up around the vent. These steep little hills grow quickly but rarely reach great heights.

Composite volcanoes, also called stratovolcanoes, are built from alternating layers of lava and ash, stacked like layers in a cake. Mount St. Helens and Mount Fuji in Japan are both composite volcanoes. They produce the most explosive, violent eruptions of all three types.

Scientists group volcanoes into three main kinds based on shape and eruption style. Shield volcanoes are wide and gently sloped, like an upside-down dinner plate. They form from thin, runny lava that flows far from the opening, called the vent, before it cools and hardens. Mauna Loa in Hawaii is a shield volcano and the largest active volcano on Earth. Its eruptions are usually quiet — rivers of glowing lava rather than violent explosions. Cinder cone volcanoes are the smallest type. They form when chunks of lava shoot into the air and pile up around the vent. These steep little hills grow quickly but rarely reach great heights. Composite volcanoes, also called stratovolcanoes, are built from alternating layers of lava and ash, stacked like layers in a cake. Mount St. Helens and Mount Fuji in Japan are both composite volcanoes. They produce the most explosive, violent eruptions of all three types.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens first when a cinder cone volcano forms?

choices.text: Layers of lava and ash stack up
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because alternating layers of lava and ash describe how composite volcanoes (like Mount Fuji) are built, not cinder cones.

choices.text: The steep little hill reaches great heights
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says cinder cone volcanoes rarely reach great heights, and the hill forming comes after the lava shoots up anyway.

choices.text: Rivers of glowing lava flow quietly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because quiet rivers of glowing lava describe shield volcano eruptions, like those at Mauna Loa in Hawaii.

choices.text: Chunks of lava shoot into the air
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says cinder cones form when chunks of lava shoot into the air first, and then pile up around the vent to build the hill.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Volcanoes That Changed the Weather

Large eruptions do more than destroy nearby land. They can change weather around the world. When a volcano hurls ash and gases high into the atmosphere, those tiny particles block sunlight and cool the planet's surface. In 1815, Mount Tambora in Indonesia erupted so violently that the following year, 1816, became known as "the year without a summer." Cold temperatures and heavy rains destroyed crops across Europe and North America.

In 1815, Mount Tambora in Indonesia erupted so violently that the following year, 1816, became known as "the year without a summer." Cold temperatures and heavy rains destroyed crops across Europe and North America.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are the effects of a large volcanic eruption and the effects of blocking sunlight similar?

choices.text: Both make ash and gases rise into the atmosphere
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ash and gases rising into the atmosphere is what the volcano does, not an effect shared by both the eruption and blocked sunlight.

choices.text: Both lead to cooler temperatures on Earth's surface
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says that blocking sunlight cools the planet's surface, and the eruption caused exactly that kind of cooling in 1816.

choices.text: Both create heavy rains only in Indonesia
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the cold temperatures and heavy rains affected Europe and North America, not just Indonesia where the volcano was.

choices.text: Both cause the land near the volcano to be destroyed
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because destroying nearby land is only caused by the eruption itself — blocking sunlight doesn't destroy land near a volcano.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Builders, Not Just Destroyers

Volcanoes cause terrible damage, but they also create. Every single Hawaiian island was built entirely by volcanic eruptions over millions of years. Volcanic soil is some of the richest on Earth because lava breaks down into minerals that help plants grow. That is why farming communities thrive on the slopes of volcanoes in places like Indonesia and Central America. In Iceland, people tap into heat from volcanic hot springs — called geothermal energy — to warm their homes and generate electricity.

Scientists called volcanologists watch active volcanoes around the clock. They use instruments that detect small earthquakes, measure gases escaping from the rock, and track tiny changes in the mountain's shape. These clues can warn that an eruption is coming, giving nearby communities time to evacuate.

That is why farming communities thrive on the slopes of volcanoes in places like Indonesia and Central America. prompt._: According to the text, what is geothermal energy?

choices.text: heat from volcanic hot springs
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says people in Iceland tap into 'heat from volcanic hot springs — called geothermal energy' — that's the exact definition the text gives us.

choices.text: changes in a mountain's shape
choices.feedback: Changes in a mountain's shape are clues that scientists track to predict eruptions, which is a completely different topic from geothermal energy.

choices.text: soil made from broken lava
choices.feedback: This describes volcanic soil, which the passage talks about separately as something that helps plants grow — not geothermal energy.

choices.text: gas escaping from the rock
choices.feedback: Gas escaping from rock is something volcanologists measure to watch for eruptions, not what geothermal energy means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Planet Still Under Construction

After the 1980 eruption, the area around Mount St. Helens looked lifeless — gray ash in every direction. The volcano itself is still active, still growing.

Volcanoes are not relics of a wilder, younger Earth. They are proof that the ground beneath you is still moving, still melting, still building new land — one eruption at a time.
stimulus.content_text: A Planet Still Under Construction After the 1980 eruption, the area around Mount St. Helens looked lifeless — gray ash in every direction. The volcano itself is still active, still growing. Volcanoes are not relics of a wilder, younger Earth. They are proof that the ground beneath you is still moving, still melting, still building new land — one eruption at a time.

prompt._: Which detail does the author use as evidence to show that the Earth is still building new land?

choices.text: The ground is covered in every direction by gray ash.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because gray ash covering the ground describes the aftermath of an eruption, not the Earth creating or building new land.

choices.text: The area around the mountain looked completely lifeless.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a lifeless area covered in ash shows destruction, not evidence of the Earth building new land.

choices.text: The volcanoes are relics of a wilder and younger Earth.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually says the opposite — volcanoes are NOT relics of a wilder, younger Earth.

choices.text: The volcano itself is still active and still growing.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states the volcano is still active and growing, which is the author's evidence that Earth is still building new land.

prompt._: Based on the text, how is the effect of volcanic soil different from the effect of volcanic ash?

choices.text: Soil warms the planet's surface, while ash causes heavy summer rains.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ash cools the planet's surface by blocking sunlight, and it caused cold temperatures and heavy rains — not warmth or summer heat.

choices.text: Soil helps farmers grow crops, while ash can cause crops to die.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that volcanic soil helps plants grow and supports farming, while volcanic ash blocks sunlight and caused crops to be destroyed in 1816.

choices.text: Soil builds new ocean islands, while ash creates geothermal energy.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says lava builds islands (not soil), and geothermal energy comes from volcanic hot springs, which has nothing to do with ash.

choices.text: Soil blocks the warm sunlight, while ash provides minerals for plants.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the two things switched — the passage says ash blocks sunlight, and soil provides minerals for plants, not the other way around.

prompt._: Based on the article, how does melted rock from deep underground eventually cause farming communities to thrive?

choices.text: It pushes tectonic plates apart to create new flat land where farmers can plant their seeds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that plates pulling apart causes magma to rise and form volcanoes, not flat land for farmers to use.

choices.text: It shoots into the air as ash and blocks the sunlight to keep the crops cool.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says ash blocking sunlight cools the planet and destroys crops, like what happened in 1816 — it hurts farming, not helps it.

choices.text: It heats the underground water to create hot springs that keep the farm fields very warm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says geothermal energy from hot springs is used to warm homes and generate electricity in Iceland, not to help farm fields.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly states that lava breaks down into minerals that help plants grow, making volcanic soil some of the richest on Earth.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is the sequence of events that forms a shield volcano?

choices.text: Tectonic plates pull apart from each other, ash blocks the sunlight, and the lava cools into thick layers.
choices.feedback: C mixes up unrelated ideas from the article; tectonic plates pulling apart and ash blocking sunlight are not steps in forming a shield volcano.

choices.text: Magma pushes up through the crust, thin lava flows from the vent, and the lava cools into a wide shape.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says shield volcanoes form from thin, runny lava that flows far from the vent and cools into a wide, gently sloped shape.

choices.text: Melted rock stays trapped deep underground, the crust moves over a hot spot, and the rock slowly hardens.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because shield volcanoes are built by lava that flows above ground, not by melted rock that stays trapped deep underground.

choices.text: Chunks of lava shoot high into the air, the pieces pile up around the vent, and they form a steep hill.
choices.feedback: B describes cinder cone volcanoes, where chunks of lava shoot into the air and pile up to form a steep hill — not a shield volcano.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are the eruptions of Mount St. Helens and Mount Tambora similar?

choices.text: Both volcanoes created rich soil that helped farmers grow new crops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article talks about volcanic soil helping farmers in general, but never connects that specifically to Mount St. Helens or Mount Tambora.

choices.text: Both volcanoes blasted large amounts of ash and gas into the air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article describes both Mount St. Helens and Mount Tambora blasting ash and gases into the air during their eruptions.

choices.text: Both volcanoes caused the weather around the entire world to cool down.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only Mount Tambora's eruption is described as cooling the world's weather — the article never says Mount St. Helens had that effect.

choices.text: Both volcanoes formed over a hot spot in the middle of a plate.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hot spots are described in connection with the Hawaiian Islands, not Mount St. Helens or Mount Tambora.

assessments.title: Weathering and Erosion: Wearing Down the Land

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Taking Mountains Apart: How Weathering and Erosion Reshape the Earth

A Mountain Disappearing in Slow Motion

The Appalachian Mountains in the eastern United States once stood nearly as tall as today's Himalayas. Picture peaks covered in snow, jutting miles into the sky. Today, those same mountains barely reach one mile high. They are soft, rounded, and covered in trees. Hundreds of millions of years of wind, water, ice, and living things wore them down, grain by grain, layer by layer. Even the hardest rock on Earth is slowly coming apart.

Two forces do most of this work: weathering and erosion. You might think those words mean the same thing, but they describe two different jobs. Weathering is the breaking down of rock into smaller pieces — like smashing a cracker into crumbs on your plate. Erosion is the carrying away of those broken pieces to a new location — like brushing the crumbs off the table. One breaks. The other moves. Together, they reshape the surface of the Earth every single day.
stimulus.content_text: Taking Mountains Apart: How Weathering and Erosion Reshape the EarthA Mountain Disappearing in Slow Motion The Appalachian Mountains in the eastern United States once stood nearly as tall as today's Himalayas. Picture peaks covered in snow, jutting miles into the sky. Today, those same mountains barely reach one mile high. They are soft, rounded, and covered in trees. Hundreds of millions of years of wind, water, ice, and living things wore them down, grain by grain, layer by layer. Even the hardest rock on Earth is slowly coming apart. Two forces do most of this work: weathering and erosion. You might think those words mean the same thing, but they describe two different jobs. Weathering is the breaking down of rock into smaller pieces — like smashing a cracker into crumbs on your plate. Erosion is the carrying away of those broken pieces to a new location — like brushing the crumbs off the table. One breaks. The other moves. Together, they reshape the surface of the Earth every single day.

prompt._: According to the text, how is weathering different from erosion?

choices.text: Weathering moves broken pieces while erosion breaks rock apart
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the definitions backwards — weathering is what breaks rock apart, and erosion is what moves the pieces.

choices.text: Weathering happens only with water while erosion happens only with wind
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage lists wind, water, ice, and living things as causes of weathering, not just water alone.

choices.text: Weathering builds up mountains while erosion wears mountains down
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because both weathering and erosion wear mountains down — neither one builds them up.

choices.text: Weathering breaks rock into pieces while erosion carries pieces away
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that weathering breaks rock into smaller pieces and erosion carries those pieces away to a new location.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Weathering: Three Ways to Break a Rock

Physical weathering cracks rock apart without changing what the rock is made of. Think of snapping a stick in half. The two pieces are still wood — just smaller. One common type happens when water seeps into tiny cracks in rock and then freezes. Ice takes up more space than liquid water, so it pushes against the sides of the crack like a wedge. After hundreds of freeze-and-thaw cycles, a chunk of rock splits off. Plant roots cause physical weathering too. As a tree grows, its roots spread through cracks in stone and slowly force them apart. You may have seen sidewalks lifted and broken by tree roots. That same force works on boulders and cliff faces.

Chemical weathering actually changes what the rock is made of. Rainwater absorbs carbon dioxide from the air, which makes it slightly acidic. When this slightly acidic rainwater falls on certain rocks, especially limestone, it dissolves the minerals bit by bit. Over thousands of years, chemical weathering can hollow out caves, sinkholes, and underground rivers. Rust is chemical weathering too. When iron inside a rock meets oxygen and water, it forms iron oxide — the orange, flaky substance you see on old nails. That same reaction weakens and crumbles rock from the inside out.

Biological weathering comes from living things. Besides tree roots, burrowing animals like earthworms, moles, and ants constantly move soil and break apart rock underground. Lichens — flat, crusty organisms made from a fungus and a tiny plant-like life form called algae working together — grow on rock surfaces and release acids that slowly eat into the stone beneath them.

The two pieces are still wood — just smaller. One common type happens when water seeps into tiny cracks in rock and then freezes. Ice takes up more space than liquid water, so it pushes against the sides of the crack like a wedge. After hundreds of freeze-and-thaw cycles, a chunk of rock splits off. Plant roots cause physical weathering too. As a tree grows, its roots spread through cracks in stone and slowly force them apart. You may have seen sidewalks lifted and broken by tree roots. When this slightly acidic rainwater falls on certain rocks, especially limestone, it dissolves the minerals bit by bit. Over thousands of years, chemical weathering can hollow out caves, sinkholes, and underground rivers. Rust is chemical weathering too. When iron inside a rock meets oxygen and water, it forms iron oxide — the orange, flaky substance you see on old nails. That same reaction weakens and crumbles rock from the inside out. Biological weathering comes from living things. Besides tree roots, burrowing animals like earthworms, moles, and ants constantly move soil and break apart rock underground. Lichens — flat, crusty organisms made from a fungus and a tiny plant-like life form called algae working together — grow on rock surfaces and release acids that slowly eat into the stone beneath them.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why can freezing water cause a rock to crack?

choices.text: Because tree roots pull water out of stone
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tree roots work by growing into cracks and forcing them apart, not by pulling water out of stone.

choices.text: Because rainwater melts the rock into soil
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rainwater dissolving rock is chemical weathering, not what happens when water freezes in cracks.

choices.text: Because ice takes up more space in cracks
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that ice takes up more space than liquid water and pushes against crack walls like a wedge, breaking the rock.

choices.text: Because frozen water becomes softer than rock
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says ice is softer than rock — ice actually cracks rock by pushing hard against it.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Erosion: Carrying the Pieces Away

Once weathering breaks rock into smaller pieces, erosion takes over. Water, wind, ice, and gravity are the four main agents that move broken rock and soil from one place to another.

Water is the most powerful of all. Rivers carry huge loads of sediment — tiny bits of rock, sand, and soil suspended in moving water, like dirt swirling in a puddle. The Grand Canyon proves what water can do given enough time. Over millions of years, the Colorado River sliced through layer after layer of rock. Today the canyon drops more than a mile deep and stretches up to eighteen miles wide. Even gentle rain erodes soil, washing it downhill into streams.

Wind erosion hits hardest in dry, open areas like deserts and prairies. Wind lifts sand and dust and blasts it against rock surfaces, smoothing and sculpting them into strange shapes. The famous rock arches of the American Southwest were partly carved by wind-blown sand.

Glaciers are massive sheets of ice that creep forward under their own weight. As a glacier slides forward, it scrapes rock from the ground beneath it and shoves piles of debris ahead of it. Glaciers carved many of the valleys, lakes, and deep coastal channels called fjords found across northern parts of the world.

Gravity pulls loose rock and soil downhill. A landslide is the dramatic version — tons of earth crashing down a slope in seconds. But most gravity erosion is invisible. Soil on a gentle hillside creeps downward so slowly you would never notice.

Rivers carry huge loads of sediment — tiny bits of rock, sand, and soil suspended in moving water, like dirt swirling in a puddle. The Grand Canyon proves what water can do given enough time. Glaciers carved many of the valleys, lakes, and deep coastal channels called fjords found across northern parts of the world. Gravity pulls loose rock and soil downhill. A landslide is the dramatic version — tons of earth crashing down a slope in seconds. But most gravity erosion is invisible. Soil on a gentle hillside creeps downward so slowly you would never notice.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after weathering breaks rock into smaller pieces?

choices.text: Wind turns the pieces into rock
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wind erodes and sculpts rock into different shapes, but it cannot turn small pieces back into solid rock.

choices.text: Gravity pulls the pieces uphill
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gravity pulls loose rock and soil *downhill*, not uphill — things don't move against gravity!

choices.text: Glaciers melt into the pieces
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says glaciers melt into broken pieces — glaciers scrape and shove rock as they slide forward.

choices.text: Erosion moves the pieces away
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that after weathering breaks rock into pieces, erosion takes over and moves those pieces from one place to another.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Where Does It All Go?

Eroded material doesn't just vanish. Rivers dump their sediment into lakes, floodplains, and ocean floors. Wind drops dust hundreds of miles from where it started. This process is called deposition — the dropping off of sediment in a new place.

The ground beneath your feet may contain tiny grains that once sat near the top of a mountain. Weathering broke them free. Erosion carried them to you. The Earth is always under construction — tearing down in one place, building up in another.
stimulus.content_text: Where Does It All Go? Eroded material doesn't just vanish. Rivers dump their sediment into lakes, floodplains, and ocean floors. Wind drops dust hundreds of miles from where it started. This process is called deposition — the dropping off of sediment in a new place. The ground beneath your feet may contain tiny grains that once sat near the top of a mountain. Weathering broke them free. Erosion carried them to you. The Earth is always under construction — tearing down in one place, building up in another.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the word 'deposition' similar in meaning to the everyday phrase 'dropping off'?

choices.text: Both describe leaving something in a new location
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines deposition as 'the dropping off of sediment in a new place,' which means leaving something in a new location.

choices.text: Both describe carrying material over a long distance
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because carrying material over a long distance describes erosion, which is a different process that happens before deposition.

choices.text: Both describe the process of breaking rocks into smaller pieces
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because breaking rocks into smaller pieces is called weathering, not deposition.

choices.text: Both describe the disappearance of sediment forever
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that eroded material 'doesn't just vanish,' meaning sediment is never gone forever.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the effect of tree roots different from the effect of gravity?

choices.text: Tree roots hollow out underground caves, but gravity blasts sand against cliffs.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because caves are hollowed out by chemical weathering from acidic water, and it is wind-blown sand — not gravity — that blasts against rock surfaces.

choices.text: Tree roots split solid rocks apart, but gravity pulls broken rocks downhill.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says tree roots force cracks in rock apart (splitting solid rock), while gravity pulls loose rock and soil downhill.

choices.text: Tree roots move dirt to new places, but gravity smashes large boulders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tree roots break rocks apart through weathering, not move dirt to new places, and gravity moves material downhill rather than smashing boulders.

choices.text: Tree roots change what rocks contain, but gravity holds loose soil together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gravity doesn't hold soil together — the article clearly says gravity pulls loose rock and soil downhill.

prompt._: Based on the article, why are the Appalachian Mountains now shorter and rounder than they used to be?

choices.text: Because weathering broke their rocks apart and erosion carried the pieces away
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says wind, water, ice, and living things wore the mountains down over hundreds of millions of years through weathering and erosion.

choices.text: Because glaciers pushed the mountains into flatter land during the ice age
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says glaciers were responsible for shrinking the Appalachian Mountains.

choices.text: Because chemical weathering dissolved all the mountains into underground caves
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because chemical weathering creating caves happens underground in certain rocks like limestone, and it didn't dissolve the entire Appalachian mountain range.

choices.text: Because rivers deposited sediment on top of them and changed their shape over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because deposition means sediment is dropped somewhere new, but the Appalachians got shorter, meaning material was taken away, not piled on top.

prompt._: Which choice best shows the order the article uses to explain how a grain from a mountain can end up somewhere else?

choices.text: Rock is broken into smaller pieces, then moved away, then dropped in a new place.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's order exactly: weathering breaks rock, erosion moves it, and deposition drops it in a new place.

choices.text: Rock is moved by erosion, then broken by weathering, then lifted by glaciers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says erosion happens before weathering, but the article explains that weathering must break the rock first before erosion can carry it away.

choices.text: Rock is changed by roots, then turned into ice, then washed into a crack.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it combines random details from the article that don't describe the main journey of a rock grain from a mountain to a new location.

choices.text: Rock is dropped in a new place, then water breaks it, then wind forms a mountain.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts deposition (dropping) first, but the article clearly shows that rock must be broken by weathering before anything else can happen.

prompt._: The main idea of the article is that the Earth is 'always under construction.' Which contrast best summarizes how the author supports this main idea across the passage?

choices.text: Weathering and erosion break down and move old rock, while deposition drops those pieces to build up new areas.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's big idea: weathering and erosion break things down and move them, while deposition builds up new areas somewhere else.

choices.text: Physical weathering breaks rocks apart into smaller pieces, while chemical weathering glues those pieces back together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because chemical weathering breaks rocks apart by dissolving minerals — it never glues pieces back together.

choices.text: Weathering builds up new mountains over millions of years, while erosion slowly tears those mountains down into loose dirt.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says weathering tears mountains down, not builds them up.

choices.text: Glaciers scrape rock and soil away as they slide forward, while wind blows that rock and soil back to where it started.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says wind carries dust and sand far away and drops it in a new place, not back where it started.
