assessments.title: Fossils: Clues to the Past

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Fossils: Stone Clues to Ancient Life

Footprints Frozen in Time

Millions of years ago, a dinosaur walked along a muddy riverbank. Its heavy feet pressed deep tracks into the wet ground. The mud dried. Layers of sand and silt buried it. Minerals slowly seeped into the hardened mud and turned it to stone. Today, a scientist chips away at a rocky hillside and finds those footprints perfectly preserved. They are fossils — the preserved remains or traces of living things from the ancient past.
stimulus.content_text: Fossils: Stone Clues to Ancient LifeFootprints Frozen in Time Millions of years ago, a dinosaur walked along a muddy riverbank. Its heavy feet pressed deep tracks into the wet ground. The mud dried. Layers of sand and silt buried it. Minerals slowly seeped into the hardened mud and turned it to stone. Today, a scientist chips away at a rocky hillside and finds those footprints perfectly preserved. They are fossils — the preserved remains or traces of living things from the ancient past.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the dinosaur's footprint different today than when it was first made?

choices.text: It was made in hard stone, but today it is wet mud.
choices.feedback: B is backwards — the footprint was made in wet mud first, and became hard stone later, not the other way around.

choices.text: It was made in wet mud, but today it is hard stone.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly says the dinosaur stepped in wet mud, and minerals later turned that hardened mud into stone.

choices.text: It was made in dry sand, but today it is soft silt.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the footprint was made in dry sand or that it is soft silt today.

choices.text: It was made in wet mud, but today it is dry sand.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though the footprint did start in wet mud, the passage says it became stone, not dry sand.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How a Fossil Forms

The vast majority of living things that have ever existed left no trace at all. When most organisms die, bacteria break them down completely. For a fossil to form, something lucky has to happen fast.

The organism must be buried quickly by sediment — loose material like mud, sand, or silt that settles on the ground, the way dust settles on a shelf. This quick burial shields the remains from weather, scavengers, and decay. Over millions of years, more sediment piles on top. The weight and pressure squeeze those layers into sedimentary rock, the type of rock formed when sediment hardens into solid stone, like wet sand packed so tightly it turns rigid.

Here's the surprising part: a fossil is not the original bone. During burial, minerals dissolved in groundwater seep into the tiny spaces inside bones, teeth, or shells. Bit by bit, those minerals replace the original material and turn it to stone. This process is called petrification — the slow replacement of living material with minerals, the way water soaking into a sponge fills every tiny hole. A petrified bone keeps the exact shape and detail of the original, but it is now made entirely of rock.
stimulus.content_text: How a Fossil Forms The vast majority of living things that have ever existed left no trace at all. When most organisms die, bacteria break them down completely. For a fossil to form, something lucky has to happen fast. The organism must be buried quickly by sediment — loose material like mud, sand, or silt that settles on the ground, the way dust settles on a shelf. This quick burial shields the remains from weather, scavengers, and decay. Over millions of years, more sediment piles on top. The weight and pressure squeeze those layers into sedimentary rock, the type of rock formed when sediment hardens into solid stone, like wet sand packed so tightly it turns rigid. Here's the surprising part: a fossil is not the original bone. During burial, minerals dissolved in groundwater seep into the tiny spaces inside bones, teeth, or shells. Bit by bit, those minerals replace the original material and turn it to stone. This process is called petrification — the slow replacement of living material with minerals, the way water soaking into a sponge fills every tiny hole. A petrified bone keeps the exact shape and detail of the original, but it is now made entirely of rock.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why must an organism be buried quickly for a fossil to form?

choices.text: to protect the remains from damage and decay
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says quick burial 'shields the remains from weather, scavengers, and decay,' which protects them long enough to eventually become a fossil.

choices.text: to let the original bone stay unchanged
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that the original bone does not stay the same — minerals replace it completely through petrification.

choices.text: to turn the remains into rock right away
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the rock-forming process (petrification) takes millions of years and happens slowly, not right away after burial.

choices.text: to give bacteria more time to break it down
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because bacteria break organisms down and destroy them, so more time for bacteria would make it harder, not easier, for a fossil to form.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Just Bones

Plants, insects, fish, seashells, and even tiny bacteria have been preserved as fossils. And some fossils aren't body parts at all.

Trace fossils record evidence of what an organism did, not what it looked like. Think of them as clues left behind at a crime scene. The dinosaur footprints described above are trace fossils. So are burrows dug by ancient worms, nests built by prehistoric birds, and even coprolites — the scientific name for fossilized droppings. Coprolites may sound gross, but they tell scientists exactly what ancient animals ate.

Sometimes an organism's body decays completely but leaves a hollow shape in the surrounding rock, like pressing your hand into clay and pulling it away. That hollow shape is a mold fossil. If minerals later fill in the mold, the result is a cast fossil — a stone copy of the original organism. Leaf impressions in shale, a fine-grained sedimentary rock that splits into thin sheets, are beautiful examples of mold fossils.

In very rare cases, organisms are preserved almost entirely. Insects trapped in sticky tree resin — the thick, golden sap that oozes from certain trees — millions of years ago can be found perfectly sealed inside amber, the hardened form of that resin. Animals frozen in glaciers and creatures preserved in tar pits have also given scientists remarkably complete specimens, or individual samples, to study.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Just Bones Plants, insects, fish, seashells, and even tiny bacteria have been preserved as fossils. And some fossils aren't body parts at all. Trace fossils record evidence of what an organism did, not what it looked like. Think of them as clues left behind at a crime scene. The dinosaur footprints described above are trace fossils. So are burrows dug by ancient worms, nests built by prehistoric birds, and even coprolites — the scientific name for fossilized droppings. Coprolites may sound gross, but they tell scientists exactly what ancient animals ate. Sometimes an organism's body decays completely but leaves a hollow shape in the surrounding rock, like pressing your hand into clay and pulling it away. That hollow shape is a mold fossil. If minerals later fill in the mold, the result is a cast fossil — a stone copy of the original organism. Leaf impressions in shale, a fine-grained sedimentary rock that splits into thin sheets, are beautiful examples of mold fossils. In very rare cases, organisms are preserved almost entirely. Insects trapped in sticky tree resin — the thick, golden sap that oozes from certain trees — millions of years ago can be found perfectly sealed inside amber, the hardened form of that resin. Animals frozen in glaciers and creatures preserved in tar pits have also given scientists remarkably complete specimens, or individual samples, to study.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after an organism's body decays completely and leaves a hollow shape in rock?

choices.text: Tree resin seals the organism
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tree resin traps and preserves whole insects while they are alive or intact, which is a separate process described later in the passage.

choices.text: A trace fossil records its actions
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because trace fossils record evidence of what an organism *did* (like footprints or burrows), not what happens after a body decays in rock.

choices.text: Ice preserves the whole body
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because freezing in glaciers is another way organisms are preserved whole, and has nothing to do with a hollow shape left behind in rock.

choices.text: Minerals fill in the hollow shape
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that when minerals fill in a mold fossil, the result is a cast fossil — a stone copy of the original organism.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Fossils Reveal About Earth's Surface

Fossils do more than show us ancient animals. They reveal how Earth's surface has changed. Scientists have found fossils of seashells on mountaintops. Those shells prove that the rock beneath the peaks once sat at the bottom of an ocean. Over millions of years, the forces that push and fold Earth's crust lifted that seafloor high into the air. Erosion — the wearing away of rock by wind and water — later exposed those fossils at the surface.

By studying fossils, scientists called paleontologists — researchers who study prehistoric life through fossils — can figure out what kinds of plants and animals lived in a particular area, what the climate was like, and how species changed over time.
stimulus.content_text: What Fossils Reveal About Earth's Surface Fossils do more than show us ancient animals. They reveal how Earth's surface has changed. Scientists have found fossils of seashells on mountaintops. Those shells prove that the rock beneath the peaks once sat at the bottom of an ocean. Over millions of years, the forces that push and fold Earth's crust lifted that seafloor high into the air. Erosion — the wearing away of rock by wind and water — later exposed those fossils at the surface. By studying fossils, scientists called paleontologists — researchers who study prehistoric life through fossils — can figure out what kinds of plants and animals lived in a particular area, what the climate was like, and how species changed over time.

prompt._: How are fossils of seashells and fossils of ancient animals alike?

choices.text: Both prove that mountains were always under the sea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only gives seashell fossils as one example of a place that was once under the sea — it does not say all mountains were always underwater.

choices.text: Both give clues about what Earth was like long ago.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that both seashell fossils and animal fossils help scientists understand what Earth and its living things were like long ago.

choices.text: Both are studied only to learn about weather today.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says fossils help scientists learn about prehistoric life, ancient climates, and how species changed over time, not just today's weather.

choices.text: Both show that wind and water made the fossils.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wind and water (erosion) uncover fossils at the surface, but they are not what creates fossils in the first place.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Girl on the Cliffs

One of the most famous fossil hunters in history was Mary Anning. Born in 1799 in Lyme Regis, England, Anning began searching the sea cliffs near her home as a young girl. She discovered important fossils of marine reptiles, including the first correctly identified ichthyosaur skeleton — a dolphin-shaped reptile that swam ancient seas. Despite having little formal education, Anning's careful work and sharp observations made major contributions to paleontology. Her discoveries helped scientists understand that many species that once lived on Earth are now extinct.
stimulus.content_text: A Girl on the Cliffs One of the most famous fossil hunters in history was Mary Anning. Born in 1799 in Lyme Regis, England, Anning began searching the sea cliffs near her home as a young girl. She discovered important fossils of marine reptiles, including the first correctly identified ichthyosaur skeleton — a dolphin-shaped reptile that swam ancient seas. Despite having little formal education, Anning's careful work and sharp observations made major contributions to paleontology. Her discoveries helped scientists understand that many species that once lived on Earth are now extinct.

prompt._: According to the text, what is an ichthyosaur?

choices.text: A dolphin-shaped reptile that swam ancient seas
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says an ichthyosaur was 'a dolphin-shaped reptile that swam ancient seas.'

choices.text: A careful scientist who studied old paleontology
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes a scientist, but an ichthyosaur was actually an ancient animal, not a person.

choices.text: A famous fossil hunter who lived in Lyme Regis
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Mary Anning was the famous fossil hunter who lived in Lyme Regis — not an ichthyosaur.

choices.text: A steep sea cliff that was located near her home
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the sea cliffs were the place where Mary Anning searched for fossils, not what an ichthyosaur is.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Reading the Rocks

Today, paleontologists search for fossils on every continent, including Antarctica. Each new discovery adds another piece to the story of our planet. Somewhere inside it, pressed between layers of ancient sediment, there might be the outline of a creature that lived and breathed two hundred million years before you were born — waiting for someone to notice.
stimulus.content_text: Reading the Rocks Today, paleontologists search for fossils on every continent, including Antarctica. Each new discovery adds another piece to the story of our planet. Somewhere inside it, pressed between layers of ancient sediment, there might be the outline of a creature that lived and breathed two hundred million years before you were born — waiting for someone to notice.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that finding fossils helps us understand Earth's past?

choices.text: The detail that fossils are pressed in ancient sediment
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because being pressed in ancient sediment explains how fossils are formed and saved, but doesn't explain how they help us learn about Earth's past.

choices.text: The detail that discoveries add pieces to Earth's story
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says each discovery 'adds another piece to the story of our planet,' which shows fossils help us understand Earth's history.

choices.text: The detail that outlines are waiting to be noticed soon
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says fossils will be noticed 'soon' — it just says they are waiting to be discovered someday.

choices.text: The detail that people search for fossils in all places
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because searching in many places tells us about where scientists look, not how fossils help us understand Earth's past.

prompt._: According to the article, how is a trace fossil different from a petrified bone?

choices.text: A trace fossil is a hollow shape in rock, but a petrified bone is a solid mineral copy.
choices.feedback: This describes a mold fossil (the hollow shape) and a cast fossil (the mineral copy), not a trace fossil — those are two completely different types of fossils.

choices.text: A trace fossil shows how the climate changed, but a petrified bone shows how mountains were formed.
choices.feedback: Fossils of seashells on mountaintops help show how Earth's surface changed, but that's not what makes trace fossils different from petrified bones.

choices.text: A trace fossil is a creature trapped in sap, but a petrified bone is an animal frozen solid.
choices.feedback: A creature trapped in sap describes an insect preserved in amber, which is a rare whole-body preservation, not a trace fossil at all.

choices.text: A trace fossil shows an animal's actions, but a petrified bone is a body part turned to stone.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article says trace fossils show what an organism did (like footprints), while petrification turns actual body parts like bones into stone.

prompt._: Based on the article, what causes seashell fossils to be found high up on mountaintops today?

choices.text: Ocean water flooded the high mountains and buried the shells in deep mud.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says Earth's crust pushed the old seafloor upward, not that ocean water flooded over the mountains.

choices.text: Minerals in the mountain groundwater slowly replaced the living shells with solid stone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because petrification explains how minerals replace living material inside bones, not why shells end up high on mountains.

choices.text: Ancient animals carried the shells to the mountains before they could fully decay.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never mentions animals carrying shells to mountains — this idea does not appear anywhere in the passage.

choices.text: The shells were buried in ocean sediment that was pushed upward and eroded.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says the seafloor was lifted by forces in Earth's crust and erosion later exposed the shells on mountaintops.

prompt._: Which choice shows the correct order from an ancient animal living long ago to a paleontologist studying its fossil today?

choices.text: The animal dies, then it is buried by sediment, then rock later wears away and reveals the fossil.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it follows the passage's order exactly: the animal dies, sediment buries it and minerals turn it to stone, and then erosion wears away the rock to reveal the fossil for scientists to study.

choices.text: The animal is buried in amber, then it leaves footprints in mud, then a scientist studies the tracks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it jumbles together two separate types of fossil preservation (amber and footprints) in an order that doesn't describe how either one actually forms.

choices.text: The animal is found by a scientist, then it is buried by sediment, then minerals turn it to stone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the animal can't be found by a scientist first — it has to be buried and turned to stone over millions of years before anyone can discover it.

choices.text: The animal dies, then wind and water reveal it, then pressure turns the remains into rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the steps backwards — the sediment burial and pressure that turns remains to rock happen first, and erosion revealing the fossil comes much later.

prompt._: How are the details about trace fossils and the details about seashell fossils alike in supporting the article's main idea?

choices.text: They both describe how living things are quickly buried by wet mud.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither the trace fossil section nor the seashell section focuses on organisms being buried quickly in wet mud.

choices.text: They both reveal that every ancient creature left behind a perfect fossil.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly states that most organisms left no trace at all, so it is definitely not true that every ancient creature left a perfect fossil.

choices.text: They both explain how ancient animal bones slowly turn into solid rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because petrification (bones turning to rock) is a separate topic — trace fossils like footprints and seashell fossils on mountains are not about bones hardening into stone.

choices.text: They both show that fossils provide clues about Earth's ancient past.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both trace fossils and seashell fossils on mountaintops are examples that show fossils reveal clues about ancient life and how Earth has changed over time.

syllabus_metadata.title: Fossils: Clues to the Past

assessments.title: The Rock Cycle: Rocks That Change

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Rock Cycle: Nothing Stays the Same Forever

A Rock with a Secret Past

A chunk of granite sits on a kitchen counter, cold and still. It looks like it has been that way forever. But millions of years ago, that same material was liquid — hotter than any oven, glowing orange deep beneath the Earth's surface. Before that, it may have been the floor of an ancient ocean or erupted from a volcano. Every rock you have ever held has a story stretching back millions or even billions of years, and it has changed form many times along the way.

Rocks are constantly being created, broken apart, and rebuilt in a never-ending process called the rock cycle. There are three main types of rock, and each one forms in a different way.
stimulus.content_text: The Rock Cycle: Nothing Stays the Same ForeverA Rock with a Secret Past A chunk of granite sits on a kitchen counter, cold and still. It looks like it has been that way forever. But millions of years ago, that same material was liquid — hotter than any oven, glowing orange deep beneath the Earth's surface. Before that, it may have been the floor of an ancient ocean or erupted from a volcano. Every rock you have ever held has a story stretching back millions or even billions of years, and it has changed form many times along the way. Rocks are constantly being created, broken apart, and rebuilt in a never-ending process called the rock cycle. There are three main types of rock, and each one forms in a different way.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the granite on the kitchen counter different from what it was millions of years ago?

choices.text: It is now part of the ocean floor instead of a volcano
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions the ocean floor as something from the rock's ancient past, not what the granite is right now.

choices.text: It is now cold and still instead of hot and liquid
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the granite is now 'cold and still' but was once 'liquid' and 'hotter than any oven' millions of years ago.

choices.text: It is now deep underground instead of on the surface
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the granite is sitting on a kitchen counter, not deep underground — it's the opposite of what this answer says.

choices.text: It is now being broken apart instead of being created
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the granite on the counter is currently being broken apart.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Born from Fire: Igneous Rock

Igneous rock forms when hot, melted rock cools and hardens. The word "igneous" comes from the Latin word for fire, and that name fits perfectly. When a volcano erupts and lava flows across the ground, the lava cools and becomes igneous rock. Basalt is a common igneous rock that forms when lava cools quickly at the surface.

But not all melted rock reaches the surface. Underground, melted rock is called magma — lava that hasn't escaped yet. When magma cools slowly underground, its minerals have time to grow into large, visible crystals. This creates coarse-grained rock — rock with grains big enough to see without a magnifying glass, like salt crystals scattered through a cookie. Granite is the most familiar coarse-grained igneous rock. That kitchen countertop solidified from liquid deep within the Earth.
stimulus.content_text: Born from Fire: Igneous Rock Igneous rock forms when hot, melted rock cools and hardens. The word "igneous" comes from the Latin word for fire, and that name fits perfectly. When a volcano erupts and lava flows across the ground, the lava cools and becomes igneous rock. Basalt is a common igneous rock that forms when lava cools quickly at the surface. But not all melted rock reaches the surface. Underground, melted rock is called magma — lava that hasn't escaped yet. When magma cools slowly underground, its minerals have time to grow into large, visible crystals. This creates coarse-grained rock — rock with grains big enough to see without a magnifying glass, like salt crystals scattered through a cookie. Granite is the most familiar coarse-grained igneous rock. That kitchen countertop solidified from liquid deep within the Earth.

prompt._: Based on the text, what causes a rock like granite to have large, visible crystals?

choices.text: Magma cools slowly beneath the ground.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that magma cooling slowly underground gives minerals time to grow into large, visible crystals, which is exactly how granite forms.

choices.text: Lava cools quickly on the Earth's surface.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because lava cooling quickly on the surface creates fine-grained rocks like basalt, not coarse-grained rocks like granite.

choices.text: Rock melts completely deep inside the Earth.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says melting rock causes large crystals — it's the slow cooling of already-melted rock that does.

choices.text: Salt mixes evenly through the melted rock.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because salt is only used as a comparison to help you imagine what the crystals look like, not as something that actually mixes into the rock.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Built in Layers: Sedimentary Rock

Sedimentary rock forms when small pieces of rock, sand, mud, shells, or other materials settle in layers and are pressed together over long stretches of time. Picture a river carrying sand and silt toward the ocean. When the water slows down, those tiny particles — called sediment — sink to the bottom, like sugar settling at the bottom of a glass of water.

As more layers pile on top, the weight squeezes the lower layers together. Minerals dissolved in water act like glue, cementing the grains into solid rock. Limestone, sandstone, and shale are all common sedimentary rocks. Fossils are almost always found in sedimentary rock because the slow layering process can bury and preserve the remains of plants and animals before they decay.
stimulus.content_text: Built in Layers: Sedimentary Rock Sedimentary rock forms when small pieces of rock, sand, mud, shells, or other materials settle in layers and are pressed together over long stretches of time. Picture a river carrying sand and silt toward the ocean. When the water slows down, those tiny particles — called sediment — sink to the bottom, like sugar settling at the bottom of a glass of water. As more layers pile on top, the weight squeezes the lower layers together. Minerals dissolved in water act like glue, cementing the grains into solid rock. Limestone, sandstone, and shale are all common sedimentary rocks. Fossils are almost always found in sedimentary rock because the slow layering process can bury and preserve the remains of plants and animals before they decay.

prompt._: According to the passage, what happens right after layers of sediment pile on top of each other?

choices.text: The minerals break apart the grains of rock
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says minerals act like glue to cement grains together, which is the opposite of breaking them apart.

choices.text: The sediment is carried by a river toward the ocean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the river carrying sediment toward the ocean happens at the beginning of the process, before the layers pile up — not after.

choices.text: Fossils of plants and animals begin to decay
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the layering process actually preserves plants and animals before they decay, not causes them to decay.

choices.text: The weight squeezes the lower layers together
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that after more layers pile on top, the weight squeezes the lower layers together.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Changed Under Pressure: Metamorphic Rock

Metamorphic rock forms when existing rock — igneous, sedimentary, or even older metamorphic rock — is changed by intense heat and pressure deep within the Earth. The word "metamorphic" means "changed in form," the same root as when a caterpillar metamorphoses into a butterfly.

You might think that this heat melts the rock into liquid, just like igneous rock forms. But that is not what happens. The original rock does not melt completely. Instead, the heat and pressure rearrange its minerals into new patterns, the way squeezing a handful of clay changes its shape without turning it into liquid. Marble is a metamorphic rock that forms when limestone endures great heat and pressure. Slate forms from shale the same way. These changed rocks are often harder and denser than the rocks they came from.
stimulus.content_text: Changed Under Pressure: Metamorphic Rock Metamorphic rock forms when existing rock — igneous, sedimentary, or even older metamorphic rock — is changed by intense heat and pressure deep within the Earth. The word "metamorphic" means "changed in form," the same root as when a caterpillar metamorphoses into a butterfly. You might think that this heat melts the rock into liquid, just like igneous rock forms. But that is not what happens. The original rock does not melt completely. Instead, the heat and pressure rearrange its minerals into new patterns, the way squeezing a handful of clay changes its shape without turning it into liquid. Marble is a metamorphic rock that forms when limestone endures great heat and pressure. Slate forms from shale the same way. These changed rocks are often harder and denser than the rocks they came from.

prompt._: The passage compares the word "metamorphic" to a caterpillar becoming a butterfly. Based on this comparison, what does "metamorphic" mean?

choices.text: buried underground
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the caterpillar-butterfly comparison is about changing shape or form, not about being buried underground.

choices.text: melted into liquid
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually tells us that metamorphic rock does NOT melt into liquid — that's what makes it different from igneous rock.

choices.text: made from minerals
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while rocks do contain minerals, the comparison to a caterpillar changing into a butterfly is about changing form, not about what something is made of.

choices.text: changed in form
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'metamorphic means changed in form,' and the caterpillar becoming a butterfly is used as an example of something changing its form.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Cycle with Shortcuts

Now imagine one possible path through the rock cycle. A volcano erupts and creates new igneous rock. Over thousands of years, weathering breaks that rock into tiny fragments — the way a sidewalk cracks when water freezes inside its gaps. Then erosion, the movement of broken material by wind, water, or ice, washes those fragments into a river. The river carries them to the sea, where they settle and are buried under more layers. Pressure and natural cement turn them into sedimentary rock.

If tectonic forces — the slow, powerful movements of Earth's rocky plates — push that sedimentary rock deep underground, heat and pressure change it into metamorphic rock. If the metamorphic rock sinks even deeper and melts, it becomes magma. When that magma erupts from a volcano, the cycle begins again.

But here is the surprising part: not every rock follows this exact path. A sedimentary rock can be weathered right back into sediment without ever becoming metamorphic. An igneous rock can be buried and changed by heat without passing through the sedimentary stage first. The rock cycle has many shortcuts and detours. The only rule is that no rock stays the same forever.
stimulus.content_text: A Cycle with Shortcuts Now imagine one possible path through the rock cycle. A volcano erupts and creates new igneous rock. Over thousands of years, weathering breaks that rock into tiny fragments — the way a sidewalk cracks when water freezes inside its gaps. Then erosion, the movement of broken material by wind, water, or ice, washes those fragments into a river. The river carries them to the sea, where they settle and are buried under more layers. Pressure and natural cement turn them into sedimentary rock. If tectonic forces — the slow, powerful movements of Earth's rocky plates — push that sedimentary rock deep underground, heat and pressure change it into metamorphic rock. If the metamorphic rock sinks even deeper and melts, it becomes magma. When that magma erupts from a volcano, the cycle begins again. But here is the surprising part: not every rock follows this exact path. A sedimentary rock can be weathered right back into sediment without ever becoming metamorphic. An igneous rock can be buried and changed by heat without passing through the sedimentary stage first. The rock cycle has many shortcuts and detours. The only rule is that no rock stays the same forever.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is erosion?

choices.text: the settling of fragments under layers on the sea floor
choices.feedback: This describes what happens after erosion carries fragments to the sea, but settling under layers is not the definition of erosion.

choices.text: the slow, powerful movements of Earth's rocky plates
choices.feedback: This describes tectonic forces, which the passage defines separately as the slow, powerful movements of Earth's rocky plates.

choices.text: the cracking of rock when water freezes inside its gaps
choices.feedback: This describes weathering — the passage uses freezing water in cracks as an example of weathering, not erosion.

choices.text: the movement of broken material by wind, water, or ice
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly defines erosion as 'the movement of broken material by wind, water, or ice.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Ground Beneath Your Feet

It is not just a rock. It is a traveler that may have erupted from a volcano, rested on an ocean floor, and been squeezed miles underground — all before landing in your hand. And its journey is not over. One grain at a time, it is already becoming something new.
stimulus.content_text: The Ground Beneath Your Feet It is not just a rock. It is a traveler that may have erupted from a volcano, rested on an ocean floor, and been squeezed miles underground — all before landing in your hand. And its journey is not over. One grain at a time, it is already becoming something new.

prompt._: The author states that a rock's "journey is not over." Which detail provides the best evidence for this claim?

choices.text: It is erupting from a hot volcano.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because erupting from a volcano is a past event in the rock's history, not proof that its journey is still going on.

choices.text: It is landing right in your hand.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because landing in your hand is something that already happened — it doesn't show the journey is still continuing.

choices.text: It is resting on the ocean floor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because resting on the ocean floor is another past step in the rock's journey, not evidence that more change is still coming.

choices.text: It is already becoming something new.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the rock 'is already becoming something new,' which directly shows the journey is still happening right now.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the formation of metamorphic rock different from the formation of igneous rock?

choices.text: Metamorphic rock is built from layers of sand, while igneous rock is built from large crystals.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because sedimentary rock is the one built from layers of sand and other tiny pieces — not metamorphic rock.

choices.text: Metamorphic rock changes shape without melting, while igneous rock forms from melted liquid.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage explains that metamorphic rock changes shape without fully melting, while igneous rock forms when melted liquid rock cools and hardens.

choices.text: Metamorphic rock cools quickly on the surface, while igneous rock cools slowly under the ground.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect and actually backwards — cooling quickly at the surface and slowly underground are both ways igneous rock forms, not metamorphic rock.

choices.text: Metamorphic rock is broken apart by weathering, while igneous rock is glued together by minerals.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because weathering and mineral glue are part of how sedimentary rock forms and breaks down, not how metamorphic or igneous rock forms.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what causes a sedimentary rock on the Earth's surface to become a metamorphic rock?

choices.text: Volcanic eruptions push it to the surface where it cools quickly before being buried.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cooling quickly at the surface after a volcanic eruption describes how igneous rock like basalt forms, not metamorphic rock.

choices.text: Tectonic forces push it underground where heat and pressure rearrange its minerals.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says tectonic forces push rock deep underground where heat and pressure rearrange its minerals into metamorphic rock — without melting it completely.

choices.text: Tectonic forces push it underground where it melts completely and cools into large crystals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that metamorphic rock does NOT melt completely — the minerals are rearranged, not turned to liquid.

choices.text: Weathering breaks it into tiny fragments until they are glued together by minerals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because weathering and mineral glue describe how sedimentary rock forms, not metamorphic rock.

prompt._: How does the author organize the ideas in the first two paragraphs of the section "A Cycle with Shortcuts"?

choices.text: By describing the steps a rock goes through in order.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author describes each stage of the rock cycle in order, from volcanic eruption to igneous rock to sediment to sedimentary rock to metamorphic rock.

choices.text: By explaining what causes a volcano to erupt with lava.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author never explains what causes a volcano to erupt; the eruption is just used as the starting point of the cycle.

choices.text: By comparing how different types of rocks look and feel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the section never compares how different rocks look or feel — it focuses on the steps rocks go through, not their appearance.

choices.text: By describing a problem with broken rocks and fixing it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because there is no problem with broken rocks being fixed — the author is simply describing the natural journey a rock takes through the cycle.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the three rock types to show the main idea of the article?

choices.text: They stay mostly unchanged, but people give them different names for their uses.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the entire article is about how rocks are always changing form — that is the whole point of the rock cycle.

choices.text: They begin as sediment, but only some of them become part of a cycle.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because igneous rocks start from melted magma, not sediment, and the article shows that rocks can enter the cycle at many different points.

choices.text: They are all made in exactly the same way, but they appear in different places.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article clearly explains that each rock type forms in a completely different way — from fire, from layers, or from heat and pressure.

choices.text: They form in different ways, but each can change into another kind over time.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article describes how each rock type forms differently, but also shows how they can all change into one another through the rock cycle.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Rock Cycle: Rocks That Change

assessments.title: The Atmosphere: Earth's Blanket of Air

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Invisible Ocean Above Your Head

Standing at the Bottom of a Sea of Air

Right now, ten thousand pounds of air are pressing down on your body. You do not feel it because you have lived at the bottom of an invisible ocean your entire life. This ocean has no water. It is made of gases, and it stretches hundreds of miles above your head. Scientists call it the atmosphere — the thin shell of gases that wraps around Earth like the skin of an apple.

About seventy-eight percent of the atmosphere is nitrogen gas, and about twenty-one percent is oxygen, the gas your lungs pull in with every breath. The remaining one percent is a mix of other gases, including argon, carbon dioxide, and water vapor — water in its invisible gas form. Carbon dioxide and water vapor may seem like tiny ingredients, but they control Earth's temperature. They trap heat from the sun the way a car's closed windows trap warmth on a sunny day. Without them, Earth would freeze.
stimulus.content_text: The Invisible Ocean Above Your HeadStanding at the Bottom of a Sea of Air Right now, ten thousand pounds of air are pressing down on your body. You do not feel it because you have lived at the bottom of an invisible ocean your entire life. This ocean has no water. It is made of gases, and it stretches hundreds of miles above your head. Scientists call it the atmosphere — the thin shell of gases that wraps around Earth like the skin of an apple. About seventy-eight percent of the atmosphere is nitrogen gas, and about twenty-one percent is oxygen, the gas your lungs pull in with every breath. The remaining one percent is a mix of other gases, including argon, carbon dioxide, and water vapor — water in its invisible gas form. Carbon dioxide and water vapor may seem like tiny ingredients, but they control Earth's temperature. They trap heat from the sun the way a car's closed windows trap warmth on a sunny day. Without them, Earth would freeze.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the role of carbon dioxide and water vapor different from the role of nitrogen and oxygen?

choices.text: They trap heat and control Earth's temperature.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that carbon dioxide and water vapor trap heat and control Earth's temperature, which is a different job from what nitrogen and oxygen do.

choices.text: They help people breathe in air every day.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is oxygen — not carbon dioxide or water vapor — that the passage says your lungs pull in with every breath.

choices.text: They give the atmosphere its invisible color.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions any of the gases giving the atmosphere a color, and in fact describes the atmosphere as invisible.

choices.text: They make up a larger share of the atmosphere.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says carbon dioxide and water vapor are part of only the remaining one percent, which is actually the smallest share — much less than nitrogen or oxygen.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Five Layers, No Sharp Edges

Actually, each layer blends slowly into the next, the way fog gradually fades at its edges rather than stopping at a wall.

The layer closest to the ground is the troposphere. It reaches about seven miles up, and nearly all weather happens here. Rain, snow, thunderstorms, and wind — all of it takes place in this bottom layer. The air here is the densest because the weight of every layer above squeezes down on it, packing molecules — the tiny particles that make up gases — tightly together.

Here is something that surprises many people: mountaintops are colder than valleys, but not because they are closer to the sun. A seven-mile difference means almost nothing when the sun is ninety-three million miles away. Higher up, fewer molecules are packed together, so the thinner air holds less heat. Packed molecules warm a space the way a crowded room full of people feels warmer than an empty one.
stimulus.content_text: Five Layers, No Sharp Edges Actually, each layer blends slowly into the next, the way fog gradually fades at its edges rather than stopping at a wall. The layer closest to the ground is the troposphere. It reaches about seven miles up, and nearly all weather happens here. Rain, snow, thunderstorms, and wind — all of it takes place in this bottom layer. The air here is the densest because the weight of every layer above squeezes down on it, packing molecules — the tiny particles that make up gases — tightly together. Here is something that surprises many people: mountaintops are colder than valleys, but not because they are closer to the sun. A seven-mile difference means almost nothing when the sun is ninety-three million miles away. Higher up, fewer molecules are packed together, so the thinner air holds less heat. Packed molecules warm a space the way a crowded room full of people feels warmer than an empty one.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why are mountaintops colder than valleys?

choices.text: because wind blows all the warm air down to valleys
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions wind carrying warm air down to valleys, so there is no evidence to support this answer.

choices.text: because thinner air has fewer molecules to hold heat
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage explains that higher up, fewer molecules are packed together, so the thinner air holds less heat.

choices.text: because mountains are much closer to the sun's heat
choices.feedback: The passage directly says this is wrong — being seven miles closer to a sun that is 93 million miles away makes almost no difference.

choices.text: because the weight of the layers pushes cold air upward
choices.feedback: The passage says the weight of layers above makes the air near the ground denser and warmer, which is the opposite of what this answer claims.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Stratosphere and Its Invisible Sunscreen

Above the troposphere sits the stratosphere, stretching up to about thirty miles. Jet airplanes often cruise in its lower part because the air there is smooth and free of storms. But the stratosphere's biggest job is protecting you. It contains the ozone layer, a region where a special form of oxygen is more concentrated than anywhere else in the atmosphere.

You might imagine the ozone layer as a solid shield, like a dome over the planet. It is not. Ozone is a gas made of three oxygen atoms — atoms being the smallest building blocks of matter, like individual LEGO bricks. These ozone molecules float in the stratosphere, and as the sun's ultraviolet radiation passes through, they absorb it. Radiation is energy that travels in invisible waves, and ultraviolet radiation is the type that causes sunburn and skin damage. The ozone molecules soak up most of it before it reaches you. No solid wall needed — just billions of tiny gas particles doing their work.

Jet airplanes often cruise in its lower part because the air there is smooth and free of storms. But the stratosphere's biggest job is protecting you. It contains the ozone layer, a region where a special form of oxygen is more concentrated than anywhere else in the atmosphere. You might imagine the ozone layer as a solid shield, like a dome over the planet. It is not. Ozone is a gas made of three oxygen atoms — atoms being the smallest building blocks of matter, like individual LEGO bricks. These ozone molecules float in the stratosphere, and as the sun's ultraviolet radiation passes through, they absorb it. Radiation is energy that travels in invisible waves, and ultraviolet radiation is the type that causes sunburn and skin damage. The ozone molecules soak up most of it before it reaches you. No solid wall needed — just billions of tiny gas particles doing their work.

prompt._: According to the passage, which sequence correctly describes how ozone molecules protect people from ultraviolet radiation?

choices.text: Ultraviolet radiation hits Earth's surface first, then ozone molecules soak it up.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the ozone layer stops the radiation high up in the stratosphere, before it ever gets down to Earth's surface.

choices.text: Ultraviolet radiation passes through the stratosphere, then ozone molecules absorb it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that ultraviolet radiation passes through the stratosphere and then ozone molecules absorb it before it reaches people.

choices.text: Ozone molecules reflect ultraviolet radiation back into space, then the radiation disappears completely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says ozone molecules absorb ultraviolet radiation, not reflect it back into space.

choices.text: Ozone molecules absorb ultraviolet radiation, then the sun sends out the radiation.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the sequence backwards — the sun sends out radiation first, and then ozone molecules absorb it, not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Where Meteors Burn and Lights Dance

The third layer, the mesosphere, extends from about thirty to fifty-three miles up. It is the coldest layer, dropping to minus one hundred thirty degrees Fahrenheit. When small chunks of rock and metal from space slam into the atmosphere at tremendous speed, they burn up here. Those bright streaks you see during a meteor shower? That is rock turning to fire in the mesosphere.

Next comes the thermosphere, reaching about three hundred seventy miles high. Its name means "heat layer," but it would not feel warm to you. The air is so incredibly thin that very few molecules exist to carry heat to your skin — like standing near a few tiny sparks that are hot but too spread out to warm you. The thermosphere is also where the northern and southern lights appear. Charged particles — tiny bits of matter carrying electrical energy — stream from the sun and crash into atmospheric gases, making them glow green, purple, and red.

Finally, the exosphere, the outermost layer, thins out until it fades into the emptiness of space. Satellites orbit Earth here, where the atmosphere is barely there at all.

The air is so incredibly thin that very few molecules exist to carry heat to your skin — like standing near a few tiny sparks that are hot but too spread out to warm you. The thermosphere is also where the northern and southern lights appear. Charged particles — tiny bits of matter carrying electrical energy — stream from the sun and crash into atmospheric gases, making them glow green, purple, and red. Finally, the exosphere, the outermost layer, thins out until it fades into the emptiness of space. Satellites orbit Earth here, where the atmosphere is barely there at all.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares the air in the thermosphere to "tiny sparks" that are "too spread out to warm you." Based on this comparison, what does the phrase "incredibly thin" mean?

choices.text: The air molecules are spaced very far apart.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the 'tiny sparks too spread out to warm you' comparison shows that the molecules in the thermosphere are spaced very far apart from each other.

choices.text: The air is filled with tiny burning meteors.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because burning meteors happen in the mesosphere, which is a completely different layer described earlier in the passage.

choices.text: The air is too cold to create any real heat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the thermosphere is actually described as a 'heat layer,' so the issue isn't coldness — it's that the molecules are too spread out to carry heat to your skin.

choices.text: The air feels like a very lightweight blanket.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never uses a blanket to describe the air — that idea doesn't come from the text at all.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Your Invisible Partner

Every breath you take, every rainstorm that waters a field, every sunset that paints the sky orange — the atmosphere makes it happen. It filters dangerous radiation, carries moisture across continents, and keeps the planet warm enough for forests, oceans, and you. The next time wind pushes against your jacket, remember: you are not just feeling air. You are feeling the bottom of an ocean that reaches to the edge of space, and you are one of the billions of living things it keeps alive.
stimulus.content_text: Your Invisible Partner Every breath you take, every rainstorm that waters a field, every sunset that paints the sky orange — the atmosphere makes it happen. It filters dangerous radiation, carries moisture across continents, and keeps the planet warm enough for forests, oceans, and you. The next time wind pushes against your jacket, remember: you are not just feeling air. You are feeling the bottom of an ocean that reaches to the edge of space, and you are one of the billions of living things it keeps alive.

prompt._: According to the text, what does the author mean by calling the atmosphere an 'ocean that reaches to the edge of space'?

choices.text: a pool of rain that collects near outer space
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions rain collecting near outer space — the 'ocean' in the text refers to air, not water.

choices.text: a deep layer of air stretching high above Earth
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares the atmosphere to an ocean to show it is a deep layer of air that stretches all the way up to the edge of space.

choices.text: a body of salty water floating above the clouds
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the atmosphere is made of air, not salty water — the author uses 'ocean' as a comparison to show how deep and surrounding the atmosphere is.

choices.text: a wave of wind that crashes onto the ground
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author is describing the atmosphere reaching high above Earth, not a wave of wind crashing onto the ground.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is the air in the troposphere different from the air in the stratosphere?

choices.text: The troposphere has stormy weather, while the stratosphere has smooth air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that all weather happens in the troposphere, while the stratosphere has smooth, storm-free air where jets like to fly.

choices.text: The troposphere has invisible gases, while the stratosphere has solid shields.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says the ozone layer is NOT a solid shield — it is made of floating gas molecules, just like the troposphere.

choices.text: The troposphere has freezing cold, while the stratosphere has trapped heat.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the mesosphere is the coldest layer, not the troposphere, and it never describes the stratosphere as a place that traps heat.

choices.text: The troposphere has glowing lights, while the stratosphere has burning rocks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because glowing lights belong to the thermosphere and burning meteors belong to the mesosphere — neither of those things happen in the troposphere or stratosphere.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what are the different effects caused by the sun's energy interacting with Earth's atmosphere?

choices.text: Nitrogen blocks the sun's bright light, oxygen pulls in its heavy heat, and rocks burn up when hit by its rays.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says nitrogen blocks sunlight or oxygen pulls in heat — those are made-up details not found in the passage.

choices.text: The troposphere pushes away the sun's storms, the stratosphere hides its bright streaks, and the exosphere catches its satellites.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the troposphere doesn't push away storms (it's where storms happen), and the stratosphere doesn't hide bright streaks — that's the mesosphere where meteors burn up.

choices.text: Water vapor traps the sun's warmth, ozone absorbs its invisible waves, and gases glow when hit by its particles.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that water vapor traps heat, ozone absorbs ultraviolet radiation, and charged particles from the sun make atmospheric gases glow.

choices.text: The bottom layer freezes the sun's water, the middle layer spreads out its tiny sparks, and the top layer stops its heavy weight.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because none of these things are described in the passage — the bottom layer doesn't freeze water, and the other details are completely made up.

prompt._: How does the author organize the descriptions of the atmospheric layers in the passage?

choices.text: By introducing the layers from the farthest in space to the closest to the ground.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author moves from the ground upward toward space, not from space downward toward the ground.

choices.text: By introducing the layers from the most dangerous region to the most safe region.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never organizes the layers by how dangerous or safe they are.

choices.text: By introducing the layers from the warmest part of the air to the coldest section.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the layers are not ordered by temperature — the mesosphere is the coldest, but it appears in the middle, not at the beginning or end.

choices.text: By introducing the layers from the closest to the ground to the farthest in space.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes the layers starting with the troposphere (closest to the ground) and ending with the exosphere (farthest out in space).

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the beginning and ending of the article?

choices.text: Both show that the atmosphere is important mainly because it gives planes and satellites a place to travel.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because planes and satellites are only small details mentioned in the middle, not the main idea of the beginning or ending.

choices.text: Both explain that the atmosphere changes weather by making meteors burn and colored lights glow.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because meteors burning and colored lights glowing are only discussed in the middle section about the mesosphere and thermosphere, not at the start or end.

choices.text: Both explain that the atmosphere is mostly empty space with only a few gases in it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the atmosphere is full of gases like nitrogen and oxygen, not mostly empty space.

choices.text: Both show that the atmosphere surrounds Earth and helps living things stay safe and alive.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the beginning and ending describe the atmosphere as surrounding Earth and keeping living things, including people, safe and alive.

assessments.title: The Water Cycle: Nature's Recycling System

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The World's Oldest Water: How Earth Recycles Every Drop

A Glass with a History

The water you drank this morning is ancient. That same water may have floated inside a cloud over the Pacific Ocean last year. A thousand years before that, a dinosaur may have splashed through it in a swamp. Earth has been recycling the same water for billions of years through a giant loop called the water cycle — the continuous movement of water between the oceans, the air, the ground, and back again. No new water is created. None is destroyed. It just keeps moving.
stimulus.content_text: The World's Oldest Water: How Earth Recycles Every DropA Glass with a History The water you drank this morning is ancient. That same water may have floated inside a cloud over the Pacific Ocean last year. A thousand years before that, a dinosaur may have splashed through it in a swamp. Earth has been recycling the same water for billions of years through a giant loop called the water cycle — the continuous movement of water between the oceans, the air, the ground, and back again. No new water is created. None is destroyed. It just keeps moving.

prompt._: According to the passage, how is the water cycle different from a factory that makes new products?

choices.text: It moves water only between oceans and clouds
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says water moves between oceans, the air, AND the ground — not just between oceans and clouds.

choices.text: It reuses the same water instead of creating new water
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Earth recycles the same water and that no new water is ever created, which is the opposite of a factory that makes new products.

choices.text: It produces water faster than a factory makes products
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about speed or compares the water cycle to how quickly a factory produces things.

choices.text: It destroys old water before replacing it with new water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says no water is destroyed — it just keeps moving.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From Liquid to Invisible Gas

A good place to start the loop is with evaporation, the process that turns liquid water into an invisible gas called water vapor. Heat from the sun warms the surface of oceans, lakes, rivers, and even puddles on your sidewalk. That warmth gives tiny water particles called molecules — pieces so small that billions of them fit in a single drop — enough energy to break free from the surface and float upward into the air.

You might think water has to boil before it can evaporate. Actually, water doesn't need to reach anywhere near that temperature. Even on a mild day, some molecules at the surface move fast enough to escape into the air. That is why puddles dry up after a rainstorm even when the weather is cool. The water didn't disappear. It became water vapor, a gas you cannot see.

Oceans are the biggest source of evaporation because they cover about seventy percent of Earth's surface. Plants help too. They pull water up through their roots and release water vapor through tiny pores on their leaves. Scientists call this transpiration — think of plants breathing out moisture the way you breathe out warm, damp air on a cold morning.
