Heat from the sun warms the surface of oceans, lakes, rivers, and even puddles on your sidewalk. That warmth gives tiny water particles called molecules — pieces so small that billions of them fit in a single drop — enough energy to break free from the surface and float upward into the air. You might think water has to boil before it can evaporate. Actually, water doesn't need to reach anywhere near that temperature. Even on a mild day, some molecules at the surface move fast enough to escape into the air. That is why puddles dry up after a rainstorm even when the weather is cool. The water didn't disappear. It became water vapor, a gas you cannot see. Oceans are the biggest source of evaporation because they cover about seventy percent of Earth's surface. Plants help too. They pull water up through their roots and release water vapor through tiny pores on their leaves. Scientists call this transpiration — think of plants breathing out moisture the way you breathe out warm, damp air on a cold morning.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why do puddles dry up after a rainstorm even when the weather is cool?

choices.text: The water changes into vapor and moves into the air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that puddle water becomes water vapor, an invisible gas, even when the weather is cool.

choices.text: The water is pulled up through plant roots nearby.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while plants do pull water through their roots, the passage is talking about water vapor from leaves, not puddles drying up.

choices.text: The water sinks down into the ground right away.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not mention water sinking into the ground — it explains that puddle water escapes into the air as vapor.

choices.text: The water must heat up until it starts to boil.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says water does NOT need to reach boiling temperature to evaporate.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Building a Cloud

As water vapor rises, the air around it gets cooler. Cool air cannot hold as much moisture as warm air. So the vapor changes back into tiny liquid water droplets in a process called condensation. You have seen condensation yourself. On a humid day, water droplets appear on the outside of a cold glass of lemonade. The cold glass cooled the warm, moist air nearby, and vapor turned back into liquid right on the surface.

High in the sky, those tiny droplets cling to microscopic particles — bits of dust, pollen, or smoke so small they are invisible to your eyes. Billions of droplets gather together, and that gathering is what forms a cloud. Here is a surprising detail: clouds are not made of water vapor. They are made of liquid water droplets or tiny ice crystals. If clouds were pure gas, you would not be able to see them at all.
stimulus.content_text: Building a Cloud As water vapor rises, the air around it gets cooler. Cool air cannot hold as much moisture as warm air. So the vapor changes back into tiny liquid water droplets in a process called condensation. You have seen condensation yourself. On a humid day, water droplets appear on the outside of a cold glass of lemonade. The cold glass cooled the warm, moist air nearby, and vapor turned back into liquid right on the surface. High in the sky, those tiny droplets cling to microscopic particles — bits of dust, pollen, or smoke so small they are invisible to your eyes. Billions of droplets gather together, and that gathering is what forms a cloud. Here is a surprising detail: clouds are not made of water vapor. They are made of liquid water droplets or tiny ice crystals. If clouds were pure gas, you would not be able to see them at all.

prompt._: According to the text, what causes water vapor to change into liquid droplets after it rises?

choices.text: The cool air cannot hold as much moisture as warm air.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that cool air cannot hold as much moisture as warm air, which is what forces the rising water vapor to turn back into liquid droplets.

choices.text: The microscopic dust particles pull the rising water vapor together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because microscopic dust particles only give the droplets something to cling to after condensation has already happened — they don't cause the vapor to change into liquid.

choices.text: The warm, moist air touches the outside of a cold glass.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the cold glass example is just a real-life comparison to help explain condensation — it describes what happens on the ground, not what causes clouds to form in the sky.

choices.text: The pure gas in the sky freezes into tiny ice crystals.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ice crystals are something clouds can be made of, but the passage never says they cause water vapor to change into droplets.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
When Water Falls

Inside a cloud, droplets bump into each other and merge. They grow larger and heavier. Eventually, they become too heavy for the air to hold up. They fall. This falling water is called precipitation. Temperature decides what form it takes. When the air is warm enough, precipitation falls as rain. When temperatures drop below freezing, water droplets freeze into ice crystals and fall as snow. Sleet forms when rain freezes on its way down. Hail forms inside towering thunderstorm clouds, where ice pellets collect extra layers before finally dropping to the ground.
stimulus.content_text: When Water Falls Inside a cloud, droplets bump into each other and merge. They grow larger and heavier. Eventually, they become too heavy for the air to hold up. They fall. This falling water is called precipitation. Temperature decides what form it takes. When the air is warm enough, precipitation falls as rain. When temperatures drop below freezing, water droplets freeze into ice crystals and fall as snow. Sleet forms when rain freezes on its way down. Hail forms inside towering thunderstorm clouds, where ice pellets collect extra layers before finally dropping to the ground.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what is one difference between how sleet and hail form?

choices.text: Sleet freezes as it falls, but hail collects extra layers.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states sleet freezes as it falls down, while hail builds up extra layers inside a thunderstorm cloud — two totally different processes.

choices.text: Sleet forms as ice crystals, but hail forms as heavy drops.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because ice crystals are how snow forms, not sleet — the passage describes sleet as rain that freezes on its way down.

choices.text: Sleet grows by bumping drops, but hail grows by melting ice.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says sleet grows from bumping drops or that hail forms by melting ice — those details simply aren't in the passage.

choices.text: Sleet falls from warm air, but hail falls from freezing air.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says sleet comes from warm air — sleet actually forms when rain freezes, which means the air is cold.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Where the Water Goes Next

Once precipitation hits the ground, it takes different paths. Some flows over the surface as runoff, rushing downhill into streams and rivers that carry it back toward the ocean. Some soaks into the soil and becomes groundwater, filling tiny gaps between rocks and soil particles. Groundwater can travel deep underground into layers of rock called aquifers — natural underground storage tanks that hold water for years, decades, or even thousands of years. Other groundwater slowly seeps until it reaches a lake, river, or the ocean, completing the loop.

Plants absorb some groundwater through their roots and release it through transpiration, sending it right back into the sky.
stimulus.content_text: Where the Water Goes Next Once precipitation hits the ground, it takes different paths. Some flows over the surface as runoff, rushing downhill into streams and rivers that carry it back toward the ocean. Some soaks into the soil and becomes groundwater, filling tiny gaps between rocks and soil particles. Groundwater can travel deep underground into layers of rock called aquifers — natural underground storage tanks that hold water for years, decades, or even thousands of years. Other groundwater slowly seeps until it reaches a lake, river, or the ocean, completing the loop. Plants absorb some groundwater through their roots and release it through transpiration, sending it right back into the sky.

prompt._: According to the passage, what are aquifers?

choices.text: underground layers of rock that store water
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines aquifers as underground layers of rock that act as natural storage tanks for water.

choices.text: streams and rivers that carry water to the ocean
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because streams and rivers are part of runoff, which is a different path water takes after hitting the ground.

choices.text: roots that plants use to absorb groundwater
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because roots are how plants absorb groundwater, not a type of underground water storage.

choices.text: tiny gaps between rocks and soil particles
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because tiny gaps between rocks and soil particles are where groundwater collects first, not what aquifers are.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Engine and the Pull

Two forces keep the water cycle running. The sun provides the energy that lifts water into the atmosphere through evaporation. Gravity pulls precipitation back to the surface and tugs rivers downhill toward the ocean. Sun up, gravity down — over and over, endlessly. This cycle drives the weather you feel every day. Evaporation and condensation build the clouds that bring storms. Precipitation fills the rivers and reservoirs — large natural or human-made lakes that store drinking water for towns and cities.

The rain falling on your garden today could evaporate tomorrow, form a cloud next week, and fall as snow on a distant mountain next month. Every glass connects you to the entire planet — and to every rainstorm, river, and cloud that has ever existed.
stimulus.content_text: The Engine and the Pull Two forces keep the water cycle running. The sun provides the energy that lifts water into the atmosphere through evaporation. Gravity pulls precipitation back to the surface and tugs rivers downhill toward the ocean. Sun up, gravity down — over and over, endlessly. This cycle drives the weather you feel every day. Evaporation and condensation build the clouds that bring storms. Precipitation fills the rivers and reservoirs — large natural or human-made lakes that store drinking water for towns and cities. The rain falling on your garden today could evaporate tomorrow, form a cloud next week, and fall as snow on a distant mountain next month. Every glass connects you to the entire planet — and to every rainstorm, river, and cloud that has ever existed.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that the water cycle connects different places on Earth?

choices.text: Gravity pulls precipitation down to the surface and tugs rivers toward the ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes how gravity moves water downward, not how the water cycle links different places on Earth together.

choices.text: Precipitation fills the large reservoirs that store drinking water for towns and cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes how reservoirs store water for towns, which is helpful but doesn't show water traveling between different places on Earth.

choices.text: Rain falling on a garden today can fall as snow on a mountain.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it shows water starting in one place (a garden) and ending up somewhere far away (a distant mountain), which directly supports the idea that the water cycle connects different places.

choices.text: The sun provides the energy that lifts water into the atmosphere through evaporation.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it explains how the sun causes evaporation, which is one part of the cycle, but doesn't show water connecting different locations.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is water vapor different from the clouds in the sky?

choices.text: Water vapor is an invisible gas, but clouds are made of visible liquid or ice.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says water vapor is an invisible gas, while clouds are made of visible liquid droplets or ice crystals.

choices.text: Water vapor is made of tiny droplets, but clouds are made of an invisible gas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the article says water vapor is the invisible gas, and clouds are made of tiny liquid droplets or ice crystals.

choices.text: Water vapor is created by plants, but clouds are created only by the oceans.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says both plants and oceans produce water vapor, not just plants, and clouds form from vapor that can come from many sources.

choices.text: Water vapor falls to the ground, but clouds float high up in the atmosphere.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because water vapor rises up into the atmosphere, not falls to the ground — precipitation is what falls.

prompt._: According to the article, why does water that falls as precipitation eventually return to the sky?

choices.text: The sun warms the water on surfaces and plants release it as vapor.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article explains that the sun causes evaporation from surfaces and that plants release water vapor through transpiration, both sending water back into the sky.

choices.text: Wind blows the water from rivers directly up into the atmosphere.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says the sun provides the energy that lifts water into the atmosphere through evaporation, not wind blowing it upward.

choices.text: Rivers carry the water to the ocean where it freezes into clouds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while rivers do carry water to the ocean, the article says ocean water evaporates into vapor — it does not freeze into clouds at the ocean.

choices.text: Gravity pulls the water underground where it heats up and rises as steam.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gravity pulls water downward, and the article never mentions underground water turning into steam to rise.

prompt._: How does the author organize the information in the sections from "From Liquid to Invisible Gas" to "Where the Water Goes Next"?

choices.text: By describing the different places where water can be stored.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because although aquifers and groundwater are mentioned, the sections are not organized around storage locations — they follow the journey water takes.

choices.text: By explaining the problems water causes and how to fix them.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the author never describes water as causing problems or offers any solutions — it's purely an explanation of how the water cycle works.

choices.text: By showing the order of the steps water takes as it moves.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the sections follow the water cycle in order — evaporation, then condensation and clouds, then precipitation falling, then where the water goes next.

choices.text: By comparing the different types of storms water can create.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not compare different types of storms; it focuses on the steps water takes, not storm comparisons.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the beginning and ending of the article?

choices.text: Both show that the same water keeps traveling around Earth again and again.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both the beginning and ending describe how the same water keeps cycling around Earth endlessly, from ancient dinosaur swamps to today's rainstorms.

choices.text: Both argue that underground water is more important than ocean water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never compares underground water and ocean water or says one is more important than the other.

choices.text: Both describe how clouds are made from dust, pollen, and smoke.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because dust, pollen, and smoke helping form clouds is only mentioned in the middle section, not at the beginning or end.

choices.text: Both explain that water stays in one place until people move it.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the whole article explains that water is always moving and recycling, never staying in one place.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Water Cycle: Nature's Recycling System

assessments.title: Cloud Watching: Types of Clouds

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Reading the Sky: What Clouds Tell You About the Weather

How a Cloud Is Born

Every cloud begins as invisible water vapor — water in the form of a gas, mixed into the air around you. When warm air rises and cools, that invisible gas condenses, meaning it changes from a gas into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals, the way moisture appears on the outside of a cold glass of lemonade. Those billions of tiny droplets clustered together are what you see as a cloud. Here is the surprising part: a cloud is not made of water vapor at all. Water vapor is invisible. The white puff you see is actually liquid water or solid ice, floating in the sky.

You might think clouds float because they are lighter than air, like helium balloons. Actually, cloud droplets are heavier than the air around them. They are just so tiny — smaller than the period at the end of this sentence — that they fall slowly. Rising currents of air, called updrafts (winds that blow upward), keep pushing them back up. It is like tossing a feather over a fan. The feather is heavier than air, but the moving air holds it aloft.
stimulus.content_text: Reading the Sky: What Clouds Tell You About the WeatherHow a Cloud Is Born Every cloud begins as invisible water vapor — water in the form of a gas, mixed into the air around you. When warm air rises and cools, that invisible gas condenses, meaning it changes from a gas into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals, the way moisture appears on the outside of a cold glass of lemonade. Those billions of tiny droplets clustered together are what you see as a cloud. Here is the surprising part: a cloud is not made of water vapor at all. Water vapor is invisible. The white puff you see is actually liquid water or solid ice, floating in the sky. You might think clouds float because they are lighter than air, like helium balloons. Actually, cloud droplets are heavier than the air around them. They are just so tiny — smaller than the period at the end of this sentence — that they fall slowly. Rising currents of air, called updrafts (winds that blow upward), keep pushing them back up. It is like tossing a feather over a fan. The feather is heavier than air, but the moving air holds it aloft.

prompt._: According to the text, how is water vapor different from the white clouds you see in the sky?

choices.text: Water vapor floats high in the sky, but clouds stay close to the ground.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares where water vapor and clouds are located in the sky.

choices.text: Water vapor is invisible gas, but clouds are liquid water or solid ice.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states water vapor is invisible gas, while the white clouds you see are made of liquid water or solid ice.

choices.text: Water vapor is made of ice crystals, but clouds are made of gas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because water vapor is a gas, not ice crystals — the passage says water vapor is 'water in the form of a gas.'

choices.text: Water vapor is heavier than air, but clouds are lighter than air.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says cloud droplets are heavier than air, not lighter — it's updrafts that keep them floating up.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Cumulus and Stratus: Puffy Heaps and Gray Blankets

Cumulus clouds are the puffy, white clouds that look like scoops of mashed potatoes piled on a plate. The word cumulus comes from a Latin word meaning "heap." These clouds have flat bottoms and rounded, billowing tops. They form on warm days when the sun heats the ground and warm air rises quickly. If they keep growing taller through the afternoon, they can turn into storm clouds that drop heavy showers.

Stratus clouds spread across the sky in a smooth, flat layer, like a gray bedsheet pulled from one horizon to the other. Stratus means "layer" in Latin. These clouds form when a large body of moist air is gently lifted over cooler air beneath it. A stratus sky makes the whole day feel gloomy. These clouds usually bring only light rain or drizzle, not heavy downpours. Here is something you may not expect: fog is actually a stratus cloud that forms right at ground level. If you have ever walked through fog, you have walked through a cloud.

The word cumulus comes from a Latin word meaning "heap." These clouds have flat bottoms and rounded, billowing tops. They form on warm days when the sun heats the ground and warm air rises quickly. If they keep growing taller through the afternoon, they can turn into storm clouds that drop heavy showers. Stratus clouds spread across the sky in a smooth, flat layer, like a gray bedsheet pulled from one horizon to the other. Stratus means "layer" in Latin. These clouds form when a large body of moist air is gently lifted over cooler air beneath it. A stratus sky makes the whole day feel gloomy. These clouds usually bring only light rain or drizzle, not heavy downpours. Here is something you may not expect: fog is actually a stratus cloud that forms right at ground level. If you have ever walked through fog, you have walked through a cloud.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why might cumulus clouds turn into storm clouds?

choices.text: because moist air is gently lifted over cooler air
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says moist air being gently lifted over cooler air is how stratus clouds form, not cumulus clouds.

choices.text: because they spread out in a flat layer across the sky
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — spreading out in a flat layer describes stratus clouds, which are the smooth, gray blanket clouds, not cumulus clouds.

choices.text: because they keep growing taller through the afternoon
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says cumulus clouds can turn into storm clouds when they keep growing taller through the afternoon.

choices.text: because the sun stops heating the ground below them
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says the sun heating the ground is what helps cumulus clouds form and grow, so the sun stopping would actually work against them.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Cirrus: Ice Curls in the Sky

Look high above — higher than any other cloud. Cirrus clouds float at altitudes (heights above the ground) of twenty thousand feet or more. The air up there is bitterly cold, so cirrus clouds are made entirely of ice crystals instead of liquid water. High-altitude winds stretch those crystals into thin, wispy streaks that look like white hair blowing in the breeze. Cirrus means "curl of hair" in Latin. Cirrus clouds usually appear during fair weather. But when they thicken and spread across the sky, rain or snow often arrives within a day or two.
stimulus.content_text: Cirrus: Ice Curls in the Sky Look high above — higher than any other cloud. Cirrus clouds float at altitudes (heights above the ground) of twenty thousand feet or more. The air up there is bitterly cold, so cirrus clouds are made entirely of ice crystals instead of liquid water. High-altitude winds stretch those crystals into thin, wispy streaks that look like white hair blowing in the breeze. Cirrus means "curl of hair" in Latin. Cirrus clouds usually appear during fair weather. But when they thicken and spread across the sky, rain or snow often arrives within a day or two.

prompt._: According to the text, what usually happens after cirrus clouds thicken and spread across the sky?

choices.text: The clouds turn into liquid water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says cirrus clouds are made of ice crystals *instead of* liquid water — they don't turn into liquid water.

choices.text: Rain or snow often arrives
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states directly that when cirrus clouds thicken and spread, rain or snow often arrives within a day or two.

choices.text: Fair weather usually continues
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says fair weather is what happens when cirrus clouds first appear, not after they thicken and spread.

choices.text: The winds stop high above
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the winds stopping when cirrus clouds thicken and spread.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Cumulonimbus: The Storm Giant

The most powerful cloud on Earth is the cumulonimbus. Picture a cumulus cloud that kept growing until it punched sixty thousand feet into the sky — taller than two Mount Everests stacked on top of each other. Its top flattens into an anvil shape when it hits the upper atmosphere. Inside, violent updrafts and downdrafts (winds that blow downward) churn the air like a giant washing machine. This churning produces heavy rain, lightning, hail, fierce winds, and sometimes tornadoes. When you spot a dark, towering cloud with a flat, spreading top, head indoors.
stimulus.content_text: Cumulonimbus: The Storm Giant The most powerful cloud on Earth is the cumulonimbus. Picture a cumulus cloud that kept growing until it punched sixty thousand feet into the sky — taller than two Mount Everests stacked on top of each other. Its top flattens into an anvil shape when it hits the upper atmosphere. Inside, violent updrafts and downdrafts (winds that blow downward) churn the air like a giant washing machine. This churning produces heavy rain, lightning, hail, fierce winds, and sometimes tornadoes. When you spot a dark, towering cloud with a flat, spreading top, head indoors.

prompt._: Based on the comparison to a giant washing machine, what does the word "churn" mean in the passage?

choices.text: To blow the wind straight down to Earth
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blowing wind downward describes a downdraft, which the passage already explains separately — that's not the meaning of 'churn.'

choices.text: To flatten the top of the tall cloud
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the flat anvil top is caused by the cloud hitting the upper atmosphere, which is a different process from the churning happening inside the cloud.

choices.text: To wash the dirt out of the clouds
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though washing machines wash things, the passage is describing how air moves inside a cloud, not any kind of cleaning.

choices.text: To mix the air around very powerfully
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the washing machine comparison shows that 'churn' means mixing the air around very powerfully, and the passage connects this churning to all the powerful storm activity inside the cloud.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Naming System That Stuck and the Water Cycle It Reveals

In 1803, an English chemist named Luke Howard created a system for naming clouds using Latin words. He chose cumulus for "heap," stratus for "layer," and cirrus for "curl of hair." Scientists still use his system today. They combine his root words to describe other cloud types. A cirrostratus, for example, is a high, thin layer cloud. A nimbostratus is a thick, rain-producing layer cloud — the prefix or suffix "nimbus" means rain. Howard's simple naming code lets meteorologists around the world describe the same cloud in the same language.

This system connects to something bigger: the water cycle. Water evaporates from oceans, lakes, and rivers, rises as vapor, condenses into clouds, and falls back to Earth as rain or snow. Clouds are the visible stage of that endless loop. Every cloud you see is water on its way somewhere. They are messages written in water and ice — and now you know how to read them.

A cirrostratus, for example, is a high, thin layer cloud. A nimbostratus is a thick, rain-producing layer cloud — the prefix or suffix "nimbus" means rain. Howard's simple naming code lets meteorologists around the world describe the same cloud in the same language. This system connects to something bigger: the water cycle. Water evaporates from oceans, lakes, and rivers, rises as vapor, condenses into clouds, and falls back to Earth as rain or snow. Clouds are the visible stage of that endless loop. Every cloud you see is water on its way somewhere. They are messages written in water and ice — and now you know how to read them.

prompt._: In the article 'Cloud Watching: Types of Clouds,' what does the word part 'nimbus' mean?

choices.text: It means water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though clouds carry water, the passage clearly states that 'nimbus' means rain, not water.

choices.text: It means rain.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the prefix or suffix 'nimbus' means rain, and gives nimbostratus as an example of a rain-producing cloud.

choices.text: It means cloud.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says 'nimbus' means cloud — that meaning isn't mentioned anywhere in the text.

choices.text: It means heap.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'heap' is the meaning of 'cumulus,' which is a different word part from Luke Howard's naming system.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is a stratus cloud different from a cumulonimbus cloud?

choices.text: A stratus cloud is flat and brings light rain, while a cumulonimbus cloud is tall and brings heavy storms.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly says stratus clouds are flat and bring light rain or drizzle, while cumulonimbus clouds are extremely tall and bring violent storms with lightning, hail, and tornadoes.

choices.text: A stratus cloud forms high in the cold atmosphere, while a cumulonimbus cloud forms right down at ground level.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the details switched around — cirrus clouds form high in the cold atmosphere, and the article actually says fog (a stratus cloud) forms right at ground level.

choices.text: A stratus cloud is made of solid ice crystals, while a cumulonimbus cloud is made of invisible water vapor.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stratus clouds are made of liquid water droplets, not ice crystals — the article says it's cirrus clouds, high up in the cold sky, that are made of ice crystals.

choices.text: A stratus cloud has a puffy and rounded top, while a cumulonimbus cloud looks like a smooth gray bedsheet.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the cloud descriptions — the article says cumulus clouds have puffy, rounded tops, and stratus clouds are the ones that look like a smooth gray blanket across the sky.

prompt._: Based on the article, what effect do upward-moving winds, or updrafts, have on clouds?

choices.text: They change invisible water vapor into liquid droplets near the ground.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because changing water vapor into liquid droplets is condensation, which happens when warm air rises and cools — it is not something updrafts do on their own.

choices.text: They gently lift moist air to create flat gray blankets of fog.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because stratus clouds and fog form when moist air is gently lifted over cooler air, which is a different process from updrafts pushing air strongly upward.

choices.text: They stretch freezing ice crystals into thin streaks across the sky.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says high-altitude winds — not updrafts — stretch ice crystals into thin, wispy streaks to form cirrus clouds.

choices.text: They keep droplets floating and help puffy storm clouds grow tall.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says updrafts push tiny droplets back up to keep them floating, and rising warm air also helps cumulus clouds grow tall enough to become storm clouds.

prompt._: According to the article, what is the chronological order of events that leads to a cloud forming in the sky?

choices.text: Water vapor condenses, then warm air rises, then droplets cluster together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because condensation doesn't happen first — warm air has to rise and cool down before water vapor can condense into droplets.

choices.text: Droplets cluster together, then water vapor condenses, then warm air rises and cools.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts droplets clustering together first, but droplets can't even exist until after the water vapor condenses.

choices.text: Water vapor condenses, then droplets cluster together, then warm air rises and cools.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it leaves out the important first step of warm air rising and cooling, which is what triggers condensation in the first place.

choices.text: Warm air rises and cools, then water vapor condenses, then droplets cluster together.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order in the passage: warm air rises and cools, then water vapor condenses, then the droplets cluster together to form a cloud.

prompt._: Which statement best compares the main idea of the first section with the main idea of the last section?

choices.text: The first explains how clouds form, while the last shows how clouds fit into a larger water cycle.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the first section explains how clouds form from condensing water vapor, and the last section zooms out to show how clouds are part of the bigger water cycle.

choices.text: The first explains why clouds are dangerous, while the last explains why clouds are safe to watch.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never argues that clouds are dangerous or safe as its main point in either section.

choices.text: The first explains how clouds are named, while the last explains how clouds grow into storms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the first section explains how clouds form from water vapor, not how clouds are named — naming is covered in the last section, but it's not the main idea there.

choices.text: The first explains why fog stays on the ground, while the last explains why cirrus clouds are made of ice.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because fog and cirrus ice crystals are small details from the middle sections, not the main ideas of the first and last sections.

assessments.title: Weather Fronts: When Air Masses Collide

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Weather Fronts: The Invisible Battle Lines in the Sky

A Sudden Shift and the Air Masses Behind It

At 2:00 in the afternoon, you are outside in a T-shirt, sweating under a blazing sun. By 3:30, the sky has turned the color of a bruise. Wind whips through the trees, thunder cracks, and rain hammers the sidewalk. By 5:00, the storm is gone. The air feels ten degrees cooler and completely dry. Two invisible giants of air crashed into each other right over your head.

An air mass is an enormous bubble of air — sometimes stretching across half a continent — that has roughly the same temperature and humidity (the amount of moisture, or water vapor, mixed into the air) throughout. Think of it as a giant blanket that picks up the personality of whatever surface it sits on. Air that parks over a tropical ocean for days becomes warm and moist. Air that lingers over the Arctic becomes bitterly cold and dry.

These air masses don't stay put forever. Wind pushes them across the planet. When a warm air mass and a cold air mass collide, the boundary between them is called a weather front. You can't see a front the way you see a wall or a fence. It is invisible. But you can absolutely feel it — in the sudden temperature drop, the shift in wind, and the clouds that pile up along the boundary. Fronts are responsible for most of the day-to-day weather changes you experience.

By 3:30, the sky has turned the color of a bruise. Wind whips through the trees, thunder cracks, and rain hammers the sidewalk. By 5:00, the storm is gone. The air feels ten degrees cooler and completely dry. Two invisible giants of air crashed into each other right over your head. Think of it as a giant blanket that picks up the personality of whatever surface it sits on. Air that parks over a tropical ocean for days becomes warm and moist. Air that lingers over the Arctic becomes bitterly cold and dry. These air masses don't stay put forever. Wind pushes them across the planet. When a warm air mass and a cold air mass collide, the boundary between them is called a weather front. You can't see a front the way you see a wall or a fence. It is invisible. But you can absolutely feel it — in the sudden temperature drop, the shift in wind, and the clouds that pile up along the boundary. Fronts are responsible for most of the day-to-day weather changes you experience.

prompt._: The text says that air over a tropical ocean becomes warm and moist. According to the text, how is air over the Arctic different?

choices.text: It becomes cold and dry.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Arctic air becomes 'bitterly cold and dry,' which matches exactly.

choices.text: It becomes warm and dry.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Arctic air is cold, not warm — warm air comes from tropical oceans.

choices.text: It becomes warm and moist.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because warm and moist describes tropical ocean air, which is the opposite of Arctic air.

choices.text: It becomes cold and moist.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Arctic air is dry, not moist — moist air comes from sitting over water.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Cold Fronts: Fast and Fierce

A cold front forms when a cold air mass charges forward and shoves into warmer air. Here's something that surprises many people: the front is named for the air mass that is doing the pushing, not just because the temperature drops. Cold air is denser — packed more tightly together, like a crowd squeezed into a small room — so it acts like a wedge. It slides under the lighter warm air and forces it upward fast.

As that warm air shoots up, it cools. Its moisture condenses, meaning the invisible water vapor turns into tiny visible water droplets, like the beads that form on a cold glass of lemonade. Those droplets build into tall, towering clouds. The result? Intense precipitation — rain, snow, sleet, or hail that falls from clouds. A cold front's rain is like dumping a bucket of water: fast and heavy. Thunderstorms and downpours can strike. But the storms pass quickly, sometimes in under an hour. After the front rolls through, the sky clears and the air turns cooler and drier.
stimulus.content_text: Cold Fronts: Fast and Fierce A cold front forms when a cold air mass charges forward and shoves into warmer air. Here's something that surprises many people: the front is named for the air mass that is doing the pushing, not just because the temperature drops. Cold air is denser — packed more tightly together, like a crowd squeezed into a small room — so it acts like a wedge. It slides under the lighter warm air and forces it upward fast. As that warm air shoots up, it cools. Its moisture condenses, meaning the invisible water vapor turns into tiny visible water droplets, like the beads that form on a cold glass of lemonade. Those droplets build into tall, towering clouds. The result? Intense precipitation — rain, snow, sleet, or hail that falls from clouds. A cold front's rain is like dumping a bucket of water: fast and heavy. Thunderstorms and downpours can strike. But the storms pass quickly, sometimes in under an hour. After the front rolls through, the sky clears and the air turns cooler and drier.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does the warm air rise when a cold front moves in?

choices.text: Because the cold air slides under it like a wedge
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says cold air 'acts like a wedge' and slides under the warm air, forcing it upward.

choices.text: Because the rain behind the front pushes it higher
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rain is something that happens after the warm air rises, so it can't be the reason the air goes up in the first place.

choices.text: Because the warm air becomes heavier than the cold air
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — warm air is lighter than cold air, which is why it gets pushed up.

choices.text: Because the clouds above it pull it upward
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says clouds pull air upward; the clouds only form after the warm air has already risen.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Warm Fronts and Stationary Fronts: Slower and Steadier

A warm front forms when a warm air mass slides up and over a retreating cold air mass. You might think a warm front would behave just like a cold front in reverse — same storms, just warmer. Actually, the two are very different. Because warm air is lighter, it doesn't slam into the cold air. Instead, it glides gently up and over, like a ramp. This gradual rise creates wide, flat, layered clouds that can stretch for hundreds of miles.

Warm fronts move more slowly than cold fronts, so their gray, drizzly weather hangs around longer.

Sometimes two air masses meet and neither one can push the other aside. When this happens, a stationary front forms. Picture two arm wrestlers locked in place, neither winning. The front parks itself over an area and can sit there for days. The weather it brings is dreary — cloudy skies, drizzle, and fog — but usually not severe. Eventually, a change in wind nudges one air mass forward, and the stationary front becomes either a cold front or a warm front, depending on which air mass finally wins.

You might think a warm front would behave just like a cold front in reverse — same storms, just warmer. Actually, the two are very different. Because warm air is lighter, it doesn't slam into the cold air. Instead, it glides gently up and over, like a ramp. This gradual rise creates wide, flat, layered clouds that can stretch for hundreds of miles. Warm fronts move more slowly than cold fronts, so their gray, drizzly weather hangs around longer. Sometimes two air masses meet and neither one can push the other aside. When this happens, a stationary front forms. Picture two arm wrestlers locked in place, neither winning. The front parks itself over an area and can sit there for days. The weather it brings is dreary — cloudy skies, drizzle, and fog — but usually not severe. Eventually, a change in wind nudges one air mass forward, and the stationary front becomes either a cold front or a warm front, depending on which air mass finally wins.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after a stationary front sits over an area for days?

choices.text: A change in wind pushes one air mass forward
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that a change in wind nudges one air mass forward, ending the stationary front.

choices.text: Both air masses rise up and form layered clouds
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because wide, layered clouds are described as forming with warm fronts, not as a result of a stationary front ending.

choices.text: The front creates severe thunderstorms and heavy rain
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says stationary fronts bring dreary weather like drizzle and fog, but 'usually not severe.'

choices.text: The warm air mass always wins and moves ahead
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the front becomes a cold or warm front 'depending on which air mass finally wins,' meaning either one could move ahead.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Reading Weather Maps and Why Fronts Matter to You

Meteorologists — scientists who study weather — track fronts using weather maps. Cold fronts appear as blue lines with small triangles pointing in the direction the cold air is moving. Warm fronts appear as red lines with small half circles, also pointing in the direction of movement. Stationary fronts use alternating blue triangles and red half circles on opposite sides of the line, showing that neither air mass is advancing.

A blue triangle line heading your way means storms may hit fast, but cooler, clearer skies will follow. A red half-circle line creeping closer means a long stretch of clouds and gentle rain before warmer air settles in.

Every soccer game canceled for lightning, every rainy recess, every crisp morning after a summer storm — weather fronts caused those moments. Once you know what a front is and how it behaves, a weather forecast stops being a mystery. It becomes a story you can read, written in clouds, wind, and rain.

choices.text: It brings completely still air instead of moving storms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions still or calm air as something that follows a cold front.

choices.text: It brings warmer, gentler rain instead of cold storms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because warmer air and gentle rain are signs of a warm front moving in, which is the opposite of a cold front.

choices.text: It brings cooler, clearer skies instead of fast storms.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says a cold front brings fast storms first, then 'cooler, clearer skies will follow' after it passes.

choices.text: It brings long, cloudy stretches instead of clear skies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because long stretches of clouds and gentle rain are what the passage describes for a warm front, not a cold front.

prompt._: How does the type of clouds produced by a cold front contrast with the type of clouds produced by a warm front?

choices.text: Cold fronts create clouds that last for days, while warm fronts create clouds that pass quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the timing — the passage says cold front storms pass quickly, while warm front drizzly weather 'hangs around longer.'

choices.text: Cold fronts create clouds only over oceans, while warm fronts create clouds only over land.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions clouds forming only over oceans or only over land — that detail simply isn't in the text.

choices.text: Cold fronts create tall, towering clouds, while warm fronts create wide, flat, layered clouds.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that cold fronts produce 'tall, towering clouds' while warm fronts produce 'wide, flat, layered clouds.'

assessments.title: Weather Instruments and Forecasting

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Reading the Sky: How Meteorologists Predict the Weather

A Forecast That Saved a City

In late October 2012, meteorologists stared at their computer screens and saw something terrifying. A massive storm called Hurricane Sandy was spinning toward the East Coast. Days before it made landfall on October 29, weather scientists warned millions of people to leave their homes. They didn't guess. They didn't just look out the window. They used instruments, satellites, and supercomputers to see the storm coming while it was still far out at sea. This science of studying and predicting weather is called meteorology, and the scientists who practice it are called meteorologists.

Meteorologists study day-to-day weather, which is different from climate — the average weather patterns of a place measured over many years. Seattle getting more rain than Phoenix most years is climate. Real forecasting requires measuring the atmosphere with precise tools — and letting powerful computers crunch the numbers. You might think meteorologists predict weather by watching clouds drift across the sky. Clouds do offer clues, but that alone would be like trying to solve a jigsaw puzzle with only three pieces.
stimulus.content_text: Reading the Sky: How Meteorologists Predict the WeatherA Forecast That Saved a City In late October 2012, meteorologists stared at their computer screens and saw something terrifying. A massive storm called Hurricane Sandy was spinning toward the East Coast. Days before it made landfall on October 29, weather scientists warned millions of people to leave their homes. They didn't guess. They didn't just look out the window. They used instruments, satellites, and supercomputers to see the storm coming while it was still far out at sea. This science of studying and predicting weather is called meteorology, and the scientists who practice it are called meteorologists. Meteorologists study day-to-day weather, which is different from climate — the average weather patterns of a place measured over many years. Seattle getting more rain than Phoenix most years is climate. Real forecasting requires measuring the atmosphere with precise tools — and letting powerful computers crunch the numbers. You might think meteorologists predict weather by watching clouds drift across the sky. Clouds do offer clues, but that alone would be like trying to solve a jigsaw puzzle with only three pieces.

prompt._: According to the text, how is climate different from day-to-day weather?

choices.text: Climate is the use of powerful computers to crunch the numbers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because using powerful computers to crunch numbers is a weather forecasting tool, not what climate means.

choices.text: Climate is the act of watching clouds drift across the sky.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because watching clouds drift across the sky is something people might think meteorologists do, but the passage says that alone isn't enough — and it has nothing to do with defining climate.

choices.text: Climate is the average weather measured over many years.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that climate is 'the average weather patterns of a place measured over many years,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: Climate is the science of predicting storms while out at sea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because predicting storms out at sea describes meteorology, not the definition of climate.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Classic Instruments

One of the oldest weather tools is the thermometer, which measures air temperature. Inside a glass tube, a liquid — usually mercury or colored alcohol — expands when heated and shrinks when cooled. Picture a snake stretching taller in warm sun and curling shorter in cold shade. As the liquid rises or falls, you read the temperature in degrees Fahrenheit or Celsius on a scale printed alongside.

A barometer measures atmospheric pressure — the weight of all the air above you pressing down on Earth's surface. Imagine balancing a tall, invisible stack of pillows on your head. Some days the stack is heavier; some days it is lighter. A common barometer uses mercury in a glass tube. When air pressure increases, it pushes the mercury higher. When pressure drops, the mercury sinks. Rising pressure usually means fair skies. Falling pressure often warns that storms are approaching.

An anemometer measures wind speed. Most anemometers have three or four small cups attached to arms that spin around a pole — like a tiny merry-go-round caught in the breeze. The faster the wind blows, the faster the cups whirl. A sensor counts the spins and converts them into miles per hour. A wind vane, sometimes shaped like an arrow or a rooster on a rooftop, swings freely to show which direction the wind is blowing from.

A rain gauge collects and measures precipitation — any water that falls from the sky, including rain and melted snow. The simplest rain gauge is a clear cylinder with measurement lines on the side. After a storm, you read the water level to find out exactly how much rain fell. Farmers, city planners, and scientists all depend on this information.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why might a barometer warn that a storm is coming?

choices.text: Because the cups spin faster when rain starts falling
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because spinning cups are part of an anemometer that measures wind speed, not a barometer.

choices.text: Because the liquid expands when the air gets warmer
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because liquid expanding when air gets warmer describes how a thermometer works, not a barometer.

choices.text: Because the mercury sinks when air pressure drops
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that falling pressure makes the mercury sink, and falling pressure warns that storms are approaching.

choices.text: Because the mercury rises when the weather turns stormy
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says rising mercury means fair skies, not stormy weather.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Eyes in the Sky and Beyond

Traditional instruments still matter, but modern forecasting reaches far higher. Weather satellites orbit Earth and photograph cloud patterns stretching across entire continents. Doppler radar works like an echo. It sends out pulses of energy that bounce off raindrops and snowflakes, then measures what comes back. This tells meteorologists where precipitation is falling and how fast a storm is moving. Twice every day, stations around the world launch weather balloons carrying instruments up through the atmosphere, measuring temperature, humidity — the amount of moisture in the air — pressure, and wind at different altitudes, or heights above the ground.

All this data flows into powerful computer models. These models use math to simulate how the atmosphere will behave in the coming hours and days. By comparing what the instruments measure right now with what the computer predicts, meteorologists build the forecasts you see on TV or your phone.

It sends out pulses of energy that bounce off raindrops and snowflakes, then measures what comes back. This tells meteorologists where precipitation is falling and how fast a storm is moving. Twice every day, stations around the world launch weather balloons carrying instruments up through the atmosphere, measuring temperature, humidity — the amount of moisture in the air — pressure, and wind at different altitudes, or heights above the ground. All this data flows into powerful computer models. These models use math to simulate how the atmosphere will behave in the coming hours and days. By comparing what the instruments measure right now with what the computer predicts, meteorologists build the forecasts you see on TV or your phone.

prompt._: According to the text, why do meteorologists compare what instruments measure now with what the computer predicts?

choices.text: to photograph clouds over continents
choices.feedback: Photographing clouds over continents is something weather satellites do, but the passage never links that to the comparison between instrument measurements and computer predictions.

choices.text: to measure humidity at high altitudes
choices.feedback: Weather balloons do measure humidity at high altitudes, but that's a separate job — the passage doesn't connect it to why meteorologists compare instrument readings with computer predictions.

choices.text: to build the forecasts people see
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage states directly that meteorologists compare current instrument data with computer predictions specifically to build the forecasts people see on TV or their phones.

choices.text: to decide when to launch weather balloons
choices.feedback: The passage says balloons are launched twice a day, but it never says comparing instruments and computer predictions is how meteorologists decide when to launch them.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Forecasts Sometimes Miss

Here's the surprising part: even with satellites, radar, and supercomputers, forecasts are sometimes wrong. That doesn't mean the science is broken. It means the atmosphere is wildly complicated. Think of dropping a marble at the top of a rocky hill. Tiny bumps you can't even see send it rolling in a different direction each time. The atmosphere works the same way. A small temperature shift over the ocean can change what happens three days later. Meteorologists can't measure every molecule of air on the planet.

Still, today's five-day forecast is as accurate as a one-day forecast was thirty years ago. Every new satellite, every faster computer, and every extra weather balloon makes predictions a little sharper.

So the next time a forecast calls for sun and you feel a raindrop on your nose, don't blame the meteorologist. Blame the marble on the hill — and be amazed that, most of the time, scientists can tell you what the sky will do before it does it.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the marble analogy shows how tiny, unseen changes cause unpredictable results, which is exactly what 'wildly complicated' means for the atmosphere.

choices.text: Classic tools are no longer used by scientists, while modern tools are used to build the forecasts we see.

assessments.title: Climate vs. Weather and Severe Storms

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Weather, Climate, and the Atmosphere's Most Dangerous Storms

Weather vs. Climate: Understanding the Difference

On January 22, 2020, Miami, Florida, dropped to 40°F. People wore winter coats and wrapped scarves around their faces. Some visitors from up north laughed — 40 degrees felt like a warm day to them. But here is the thing: that single cold morning did not turn Miami into a cold city. The very next week, temperatures climbed back into the 70s. That chilly January day was weather. Miami's year-round warmth is its climate.

Weather describes the conditions of the atmosphere at a specific place and time. When you look out your window and see rain, fog, sunshine, or snow, you are observing weather. It can shift from hour to hour. Your morning might start cold and cloudy, then turn warm and sunny by lunch. Weather is the atmosphere's mood today.

Climate is the average weather pattern of a region measured over a long stretch of time — usually thirty years or more. If you live in a desert climate, your closet is full of light clothes. If you live near a tropical rainforest, you probably own a lot of rain gear.

A single freezing morning in Florida does not mean Florida's climate has changed, just as one warm afternoon in Alaska does not make Alaska tropical. Climate is built from thousands of days, not just one.

If you live near a tropical rainforest, you probably own a lot of rain gear. A single freezing morning in Florida does not mean Florida's climate has changed, just as one warm afternoon in Alaska does not make Alaska tropical. Climate is built from thousands of days, not just one.

prompt._: According to the text, what contrast does the author make about how climate is built?

choices.text: It is built from the atmosphere's daily mood, not a pattern.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the 'atmosphere's mood today' as weather, not climate.

choices.text: It is built from a single freezing morning, not many years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the meaning — a single freezing morning is an example of weather, and the passage says that is NOT how climate is built.

choices.text: It is built from hour-to-hour weather shifts, not an average.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hour-to-hour shifts describe weather, while climate is the long-term average pattern — the opposite of what this answer says.

choices.text: It is built from thousands of days, not just one day.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'Climate is built from thousands of days, not just one,' which is exactly what this answer says.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Tornadoes and Hurricanes: Two Kinds of Violent Storms

Most weather is mild. But sometimes the atmosphere produces severe storms with vast power. Two of the most dangerous types are tornadoes and hurricanes. Actually, they are completely different — in size, in where they form, and in how long they last.

A tornado is a violently spinning column of air that stretches from a thunderstorm down to the ground. Picture a giant invisible drill dropping from the clouds. Tornadoes form over land when warm, moist air near the surface collides with cold, dry air above. This collision creates instability — a condition where the atmosphere becomes unbalanced, like a tower of blocks stacked too high. The warm air shoots upward. If shifting winds cause that rising air to rotate, a funnel cloud forms. The moment that funnel touches the ground, it becomes a tornado.

Tornadoes can spin with winds over 300 miles per hour. They rip apart buildings, uproot trees, and hurl cars. Scientists measure their strength using the Enhanced Fujita Scale, a rating system that ranks tornadoes from EF0 (winds of 65 to 85 mph) to EF5 (winds above 200 mph). The central United States, an area often called Tornado Alley, sees more tornadoes than anywhere else on Earth. But most tornadoes are small — sometimes only a few hundred yards wide — and they usually last just minutes.

Hurricanes are enormous rotating storm systems that form over warm ocean water — at least 80°F. A hurricane begins as a cluster of thunderstorms over tropical seas. The warm water heats the air above it, and that air rises fast. Surrounding air rushes in to fill the gap and starts to spiral because of Earth's rotation. As the system grows stronger, wind speeds climb. When sustained winds — winds that blow steadily, not just in gusts — reach 74 miles per hour, the storm earns the name hurricane.

The strongest hurricanes bring winds above 157 mph. They also push walls of ocean water onto shore, called storm surges — sudden, dangerous rises in sea level that can flood entire neighborhoods in minutes. Hurricanes can stretch hundreds of miles wide and last for days or even weeks.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does the air above warm ocean water begin to spiral during the formation of a hurricane?

choices.text: because Earth's rotation causes the rushing air to spin

choices.text: because cold, dry air collides with the warm air above
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because cold, dry air colliding with warm air is the cause of tornadoes, not hurricanes.

choices.text: because thunderstorms push the air in a circular pattern
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says thunderstorms are where a hurricane begins, but never says they push air in a circular pattern.

choices.text: because the warm water causes winds to blow in gusts
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage connects gusts to the definition of sustained winds, not to why the air begins to spiral.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Staying Safe: Watches, Warnings, and Why This Knowledge Matters

The National Weather Service tracks both tornadoes and hurricanes. It issues two levels of alert. A watch means conditions could produce severe weather — time to pay attention and prepare. A warning means severe weather has been spotted or is about to strike — time to act. For a tornado warning, move to a basement or an interior room away from windows. For a hurricane warning, families near the coast may need to evacuate to higher ground, because storm surges and flooding are the deadliest threats.

Every forecast you hear uses the ideas in this article. When a meteorologist says your city will have rain tomorrow, that is weather. When scientists say your region is getting warmer over decades, that is climate. And when an alert flashes across a screen telling your family to take shelter, knowing the difference between a watch and a warning could protect the people you love. The atmosphere surrounds you every second. Learning to read its signals is one of the most practical things science can teach you.

For a hurricane warning, families near the coast may need to evacuate to higher ground, because storm surges and flooding are the deadliest threats. When scientists say your region is getting warmer over decades, that is climate. And when an alert flashes across a screen telling your family to take shelter, knowing the difference between a watch and a warning could protect the people you love. The atmosphere surrounds you every second. Learning to read its signals is one of the most practical things science can teach you.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after a watch is issued if the danger becomes more serious?

choices.text: A climate report is issued, and people need to prepare
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a climate report describes long-term patterns over decades and has nothing to do with the watch-and-warning alert system.

choices.text: A warning is issued, and people need to act
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says a warning comes after conditions get serious enough that severe weather has been spotted, and it means it's time to act.

choices.text: A watch is issued, and people need to evacuate
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a watch is the first level of alert, so it cannot come after itself, and evacuation is linked to hurricane warnings, not watches.

choices.text: A forecast is issued, and people need to wait
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a forecast is just a weather prediction, not an emergency alert that follows a watch.

prompt._: According to the article, why does a hurricane warning require a different safety plan than a tornado warning?

choices.text: A hurricane warning requires escaping rising ocean water, while a tornado warning requires hiding from violently spinning winds.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article says hurricane warnings require evacuating from deadly storm surges (rising ocean water), while tornado warnings require sheltering indoors away from the violently spinning winds.

choices.text: A hurricane warning requires hiding from violently spinning winds, while a tornado warning requires escaping rising ocean water.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the dangers — it's hurricanes that bring storm surges (rising ocean water), not tornadoes, and tornadoes that bring violently spinning winds, not hurricanes.

choices.text: A hurricane warning requires preparing for a storm lasting minutes, while a tornado warning requires preparing for a storm lasting weeks.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the timing backwards — the article says tornadoes usually last just minutes, while hurricanes can last days or even weeks.

choices.text: A hurricane warning requires preparing before a storm is spotted, while a tornado warning requires acting after a storm is spotted.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes what a watch means (prepare before a storm is spotted), not a warning — both a tornado warning and a hurricane warning mean the storm has been spotted or is about to strike.

prompt._: How does the author use cause and effect across the article to help readers understand why severe storms lead to different safety responses?

choices.text: Hurricanes spin because of colliding air masses, so families should move to a basement
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because hurricanes spin due to Earth's rotation and warm ocean air rising, and moving to a basement is the safety advice for tornadoes, not hurricanes.

choices.text: Tornadoes form over warm oceans, so people must evacuate to higher ground before winds arrive
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tornadoes form over land when warm and cold air collide, not over warm oceans — and evacuating to higher ground is the advice for hurricanes, not tornadoes.

choices.text: Both storms form the same way over land, so the same warning system works for both
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article clearly explains that tornadoes and hurricanes form in completely different ways — one over land and one over warm ocean water.

choices.text: Each storm's causes shape its dangers, so each storm requires its own safety plan
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article shows that how each storm forms creates its own unique dangers, and those different dangers lead to different safety actions like sheltering for tornadoes and evacuating for hurricanes.

prompt._: Which sentence best summarizes the article?

choices.text: The article defines weather and climate, describes severe storms, and explains safety alerts.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it accurately covers all three main topics: weather vs. climate, severe storms, and safety alerts.

choices.text: The article describes a cold morning, explains wind speeds, and tells people to hide in basements.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it picks small details from the article instead of summarizing the big, important ideas.

choices.text: The article argues that climate is changing, explains how tornadoes form, and lists safety rules.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article does not argue that climate is changing — it only explains what climate is.

choices.text: The article measures different climates, compares winter storms, and predicts future weather events.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never compares winter storms or makes any predictions about future weather.

prompt._: Which comparison best shows how the author uses examples to explain the difference between weather and climate?

choices.text: Weather is shown by a tornado touching the ground, while climate is shown by a hurricane growing over warm ocean water.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tornadoes and hurricanes are used to explain two types of dangerous storms, not to explain the difference between weather and climate.

choices.text: Weather is shown by one cold morning in Miami, while climate is shown by Miami's usual warmth over many years.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly uses Miami's single cold morning as an example of weather and Miami's usual warmth over many years as an example of climate.

choices.text: Weather is shown by winds reaching 74 miles per hour, while climate is shown by winds above 200 miles per hour.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because those wind speeds describe how strong storms are measured, not the difference between weather and climate.

choices.text: Weather is shown by a watch being issued, while climate is shown by a warning being issued afterward.

assessments.title: Benjamin Banneker: Astronomer and Mathematician

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Benjamin Banneker: The Man Who Built a Clock from Wood

A Wooden Clock That Amazed a Nation

A young man in Maryland borrowed a pocket watch, turned it over in his hands, and carefully took it apart. Then he carved each piece out of wood and built his own clock from scratch. That wooden clock kept accurate time for decades. The man who built it was Benjamin Banneker.

Banneker was born in 1731 in Baltimore County, Maryland. He was a free Black man. You might think that before the Civil War, all Black people in America were enslaved. Actually, small communities of free Black people existed in Maryland and several other states. Banneker's family was part of one of those communities. His grandmother, Molly Welsh, was an Englishwoman who had come to America as an indentured servant — a person who works for someone else for several years to pay off a debt, like repaying the cost of a journey with labor instead of money. Banneker's mother was a free Black woman, and his father had once been enslaved but purchased his own freedom. Being free gave Banneker more chances to learn than most Black people had in the 1700s, but he still faced constant racial prejudice.
stimulus.content_text: Benjamin Banneker: The Man Who Built a Clock from WoodA Wooden Clock That Amazed a Nation A young man in Maryland borrowed a pocket watch, turned it over in his hands, and carefully took it apart. Then he carved each piece out of wood and built his own clock from scratch. That wooden clock kept accurate time for decades. The man who built it was Benjamin Banneker. Banneker was born in 1731 in Baltimore County, Maryland. He was a free Black man. You might think that before the Civil War, all Black people in America were enslaved. Actually, small communities of free Black people existed in Maryland and several other states. Banneker's family was part of one of those communities. His grandmother, Molly Welsh, was an Englishwoman who had come to America as an indentured servant — a person who works for someone else for several years to pay off a debt, like repaying the cost of a journey with labor instead of money. Banneker's mother was a free Black woman, and his father had once been enslaved but purchased his own freedom. Being free gave Banneker more chances to learn than most Black people had in the 1700s, but he still faced constant racial prejudice.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Banneker's situation different from that of most Black people in the 1700s?

choices.text: He lived in a larger community than most Black people did
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never compares the size of Banneker's community to the communities of other Black people.

choices.text: He had fewer chances to learn than most Black people had
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it is the opposite of what the passage says — Banneker had more chances to learn, not fewer.

choices.text: He had more chances to learn than most Black people had
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that being free gave Banneker more chances to learn than most Black people had in the 1700s.

choices.text: He faced more racial prejudice than most Black people did
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Banneker faced racial prejudice, but it never says he faced more of it than most Black people did.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Self-Taught Mind

As a boy, Banneker attended a small Quaker school where he learned reading, writing, and arithmetic. But he only went for a few years. When people call Banneker "self-taught," they don't mean he had zero education. They mean he took that small foundation and built a mountain on top of it — mostly alone, mostly from books, and mostly through sheer stubbornness. He figured it out by looking, thinking, and trying.

In his fifties, Banneker's curiosity pulled him toward the sky. A neighbor lent him books on astronomy and mathematics. Astronomy is the study of objects in space — stars, planets, and moons. Banneker taught himself to predict eclipses, those dramatic moments when the Moon slides in front of the Sun and blocks its light, or when Earth's shadow darkens the Moon. He tracked the movements of planets across the night sky. He did all of this from his family's farm, using borrowed books and his own sharp mind.
stimulus.content_text: A Self-Taught Mind As a boy, Banneker attended a small Quaker school where he learned reading, writing, and arithmetic. But he only went for a few years. When people call Banneker "self-taught," they don't mean he had zero education. They mean he took that small foundation and built a mountain on top of it — mostly alone, mostly from books, and mostly through sheer stubbornness. He figured it out by looking, thinking, and trying.
