Banneker taught himself to predict eclipses, those dramatic moments when the Moon slides in front of the Sun and blocks its light, or when Earth's shadow darkens the Moon. He tracked the movements of planets across the night sky. He did all of this from his family's farm, using borrowed books and his own sharp mind.

prompt._: Based on the text, why was Benjamin Banneker able to teach himself how to predict eclipses?

choices.text: Because he used borrowed books and his own sharp mind.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Banneker learned astronomy 'using borrowed books and his own sharp mind,' which is exactly how he taught himself to predict eclipses.

choices.text: Because he traveled far away from his family's farm.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Banneker did all of his astronomy work from his family's farm, so traveling away from it had nothing to do with his learning.

choices.text: Because he studied at a Quaker school for many years.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Banneker only went to the Quaker school for a few years, which gave him a small foundation — not enough on its own to teach him astronomy.

choices.text: Because he watched the Moon slide in front of the Sun.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because watching the Moon slide in front of the Sun is an example of what an eclipse is, not the reason Banneker was able to predict one.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Almanac and the New Capital

Banneker put his astronomical knowledge to practical use. He created an almanac, a yearly book packed with useful information — a calendar, weather predictions, tide charts, and tables showing when the Sun and Moon would rise and set. Banneker published his almanac every year from 1792 to 1797. Farmers used it to plan their planting. Readers across the country admired how accurate his calculations were.

In 1791, Banneker received an important invitation. The United States needed a new capital city, and a surveying team was measuring the land where Washington, D.C., would be built. Surveying means carefully measuring distances and angles on the ground to create a precise map. Major Andrew Ellicott led the team. It's easy to assume one brilliant person designed the whole city alone. But building a capital takes many people with different skills. Banneker's job was to help with the astronomical observations needed to set exact boundary lines. He recorded the positions of stars and used mathematics to turn those observations into measurements on the ground.

He created an almanac, a yearly book packed with useful information — a calendar, weather predictions, tide charts, and tables showing when the Sun and Moon would rise and set. Banneker published his almanac every year from 1792 to 1797. Farmers used it to plan their planting. Readers across the country admired how accurate his calculations were. In 1791, Banneker received an important invitation. The United States needed a new capital city, and a surveying team was measuring the land where Washington, D.C., would be built. Surveying means carefully measuring distances and angles on the ground to create a precise map. Major Andrew Ellicott led the team. It's easy to assume one brilliant person designed the whole city alone. But building a capital takes many people with different skills. Banneker's job was to help with the astronomical observations needed to set exact boundary lines. He recorded the positions of stars and used mathematics to turn those observations into measurements on the ground.

prompt._: According to the text, why did Banneker record the positions of stars when helping to survey Washington, D.C.?

choices.text: to create a calendar showing when the Sun would rise
choices.feedback: Showing when the Sun would rise was part of his almanac, not the reason he recorded stars while helping survey Washington, D.C.

choices.text: to publish the information in his yearly almanac
choices.feedback: His almanac was a separate project; the passage does not say he used his surveying star observations for the almanac.

choices.text: to turn those observations into measurements on the ground
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says he recorded star positions and used math to turn those observations into measurements on the ground.

choices.text: to predict the weather for the surveying team
choices.feedback: Predicting weather was something Banneker did for his almanac, not why he recorded star positions while surveying.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Letter That Demanded Respect

Banneker did something else that took a different kind of skill: courage. He sent a copy of his almanac to Thomas Jefferson, who was then Secretary of State. In a letter, Banneker directly challenged Jefferson's published claim that Black people might be less intelligent than white people. Banneker pointed to his own work as evidence. He urged Jefferson to look past prejudice and recognize that all people are born with equal ability. Jefferson wrote back politely and called the almanac a sign of real talent. Whether Jefferson truly changed his thinking, though, historians still debate.
stimulus.content_text: A Letter That Demanded Respect Banneker did something else that took a different kind of skill: courage. He sent a copy of his almanac to Thomas Jefferson, who was then Secretary of State. In a letter, Banneker directly challenged Jefferson's published claim that Black people might be less intelligent than white people. Banneker pointed to his own work as evidence. He urged Jefferson to look past prejudice and recognize that all people are born with equal ability. Jefferson wrote back politely and called the almanac a sign of real talent. Whether Jefferson truly changed his thinking, though, historians still debate.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how were Banneker's letter and almanac alike?

choices.text: Both showed his courage and his talent.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the letter took courage and Jefferson called the almanac a sign of real talent, so both showed Banneker's bravery and skill.

choices.text: Both proved Jefferson had changed his mind.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says historians still debate whether Jefferson truly changed his thinking, so we can't say it was proven.

choices.text: Both explained why Black people were less able.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Banneker argued that all people are born with equal ability, which is the opposite of saying Black people were less able.

choices.text: Both asked Jefferson to write back politely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Jefferson chose to write back politely on his own — Banneker never asked him to do that.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Survived the Fire

Banneker spent his final years on his farm, still watching the stars, still writing. He died in 1806. On the day of his funeral, a fire swept through his house and destroyed his famous wooden clock, along with many of his papers and notebooks. Decades of observations, gone in minutes. But enough of his published work survived — his almanacs, his letters, the records of his surveying work — to keep his name alive.

Today, schools, parks, and monuments across America carry the name Benjamin Banneker. He had a few years of school, a pile of borrowed books, and a mind that refused to stop asking questions. That wooden clock still ticking after decades tells you everything you need to know — not just about Banneker, but about what curiosity can build when no one bothers to tell it to stop.

But enough of his published work survived — his almanacs, his letters, the records of his surveying work — to keep his name alive. assessments.title: Elizabeth Blackwell: America's First Woman Doctor

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Elizabeth Blackwell: The Woman Who Knocked on Thirty Doors

A Conversation That Started Everything

A dying woman lay in bed and whispered something that would echo for over a century. She told her friend Elizabeth Blackwell that her illness felt embarrassing to discuss with a male doctor. If only a woman physician had been available, she said, she might have sought help sooner. Elizabeth Blackwell listened. And then she made a decision that most people in the 1840s thought was impossible.

She would become a doctor.
stimulus.content_text: Elizabeth Blackwell: The Woman Who Knocked on Thirty DoorsA Conversation That Started Everything A dying woman lay in bed and whispered something that would echo for over a century. She told her friend Elizabeth Blackwell that her illness felt embarrassing to discuss with a male doctor. If only a woman physician had been available, she said, she might have sought help sooner. Elizabeth Blackwell listened. And then she made a decision that most people in the 1840s thought was impossible. She would become a doctor.

prompt._: According to the text, how did Elizabeth Blackwell's decision to become a doctor contrast with what most people in the 1840s thought?

choices.text: She decided to do it so that others would remember her for a century.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Elizabeth made her decision so others would remember her — being remembered for a century is not her reason.

choices.text: She decided to do it even though others thought it was impossible.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states Elizabeth decided to become a doctor even though most people in the 1840s thought it was impossible.

choices.text: She decided to do it because others thought it was a perfect idea.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says people thought it was impossible, not that it was a perfect idea.

choices.text: She decided to do it after others said it was highly embarrassing.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the embarrassment in the passage was about the sick woman talking to a male doctor, not about Elizabeth's decision.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Twenty-Nine Rejection Letters

Blackwell was born in England in 1821 and moved to the United States as a child. She was intelligent, well-read, and determined. But in the 1840s, every medical school in America admitted only men. Women who wanted to heal the sick were expected to do so at home, not in a hospital or a classroom.

Blackwell applied anyway. She sent her application to one school, then another, then another. Each time, the answer came back: no. Some schools rejected her simply because she was a woman. Others gave her strange advice — they suggested she disguise herself as a man to sneak in. Blackwell refused. She would enter medicine as herself, or not at all.

After twenty-nine rejections, Geneva Medical College in upstate New York finally accepted her. Here's the surprising part: the school's all-male students actually voted on whether to let her in. Most of them thought the whole thing was a joke. They voted yes, laughing. But Blackwell took their vote seriously, packed her bags, and showed up for class.
stimulus.content_text: Twenty-Nine Rejection Letters Blackwell was born in England in 1821 and moved to the United States as a child. She was intelligent, well-read, and determined. But in the 1840s, every medical school in America admitted only men. Women who wanted to heal the sick were expected to do so at home, not in a hospital or a classroom. Blackwell applied anyway. She sent her application to one school, then another, then another. Each time, the answer came back: no. Some schools rejected her simply because she was a woman. Others gave her strange advice — they suggested she disguise herself as a man to sneak in. Blackwell refused. She would enter medicine as herself, or not at all. After twenty-nine rejections, Geneva Medical College in upstate New York finally accepted her. Here's the surprising part: the school's all-male students actually voted on whether to let her in. Most of them thought the whole thing was a joke. They voted yes, laughing. But Blackwell took their vote seriously, packed her bags, and showed up for class.

prompt._: Which detail from the section best supports the main idea that Blackwell's determination caused her to succeed in getting into medical school?

choices.text: She refused to disguise herself and kept applying until a school accepted her.
choices.feedback: This is correct because it shows both her determination (refusing to disguise herself, keep applying) and her success (a school finally accepted her).

choices.text: She was born in England in 1821 and moved to the United States as a child.
choices.feedback: This is just a background detail about where Blackwell was born — it has nothing to do with her determination or getting into medical school.

choices.text: Women who wanted to heal the sick were expected to do so at home.
choices.feedback: This describes what society expected of women at the time, which is a challenge she faced, not evidence of her determination leading to success.

choices.text: The male students at Geneva Medical College voted yes as a joke.
choices.feedback: This tells us the students voted yes as a joke, which was lucky for Blackwell, but it doesn't show her determination causing her success.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
First in the United States — But Not the World

In 1849, Elizabeth Blackwell graduated at the top of her class. She became the first woman to earn a medical degree in the United States. You might think she was the first woman to practice medicine anywhere, but that's not quite right. Women had been healers, midwives, and medical practitioners in cultures around the world for thousands of years. What made Blackwell's achievement specific was that she earned an official degree from an American medical school — a place that had locked its doors to women since it opened.

It's easy to assume that after Blackwell graduated, other women could simply walk into medical school and sign up. Actually, the opposite happened. Most schools stayed closed to women for decades. Blackwell's success did not flip a switch. It lit a match. The full fire would take many more women and many more years to build.
stimulus.content_text: First in the United States — But Not the World In 1849, Elizabeth Blackwell graduated at the top of her class. She became the first woman to earn a medical degree in the United States. You might think she was the first woman to practice medicine anywhere, but that's not quite right. Women had been healers, midwives, and medical practitioners in cultures around the world for thousands of years. What made Blackwell's achievement specific was that she earned an official degree from an American medical school — a place that had locked its doors to women since it opened. It's easy to assume that after Blackwell graduated, other women could simply walk into medical school and sign up. Actually, the opposite happened. Most schools stayed closed to women for decades. Blackwell's success did not flip a switch. It lit a match. The full fire would take many more women and many more years to build.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after Elizabeth Blackwell graduated from medical school?

choices.text: Other women easily signed up for classes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — most schools did NOT let women simply sign up after Blackwell graduated.

choices.text: Blackwell became the first woman healer.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage tells us that women had been healers and medical practitioners around the world long before Blackwell — she was not the first woman healer.

choices.text: Most schools stayed closed for decades.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that most schools stayed closed to women for decades after Blackwell's graduation.

choices.text: Women began working as local midwives.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions midwives as something women had already been doing for thousands of years before Blackwell, not something that happened after she graduated.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Building Something New

Even with her degree, Blackwell faced prejudice — unfair treatment based on who someone is rather than what they can do, like a coach benching a talented player just because of the player's name. Hospitals refused to hire her. Some patients did not want a woman treating them.

So Blackwell built her own hospital. In 1857, she founded the New York Infirmary for Indigent Women and Children. An infirmary is a place where sick people receive medical care, like a small hospital. Indigent means very poor — too poor to pay for a doctor. This hospital served patients other doctors ignored, and it was staffed entirely by women physicians.

Blackwell did not do this alone. Her sister Emily also became a doctor and helped run the infirmary alongside her. A supportive faculty member at Geneva had spoken up for Elizabeth's admission years earlier. Even trailblazers need allies — people who believe in the mission and share the work.

Blackwell also founded a medical college specifically for women. She believed the best argument for women in medicine was more women in medicine. Her graduates spread across the country, treating patients and proving their skill in community after community. Slowly, over decades, attitudes began to shift.

prompt._: The passage says an infirmary is 'a place where sick people receive medical care, like a small hospital.' How is an infirmary compared to a hospital in this sentence?

choices.text: It is a larger version of a hospital
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage uses the word 'small,' which means an infirmary is smaller than a hospital, not larger.

choices.text: It is the opposite of a hospital
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because an infirmary does the same job as a hospital — caring for sick people — so it is not the opposite.

choices.text: It is a smaller type of hospital
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says an infirmary is 'like a small hospital,' meaning it is a smaller version of a hospital.

choices.text: It is a nickname for any hospital
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage is defining what an infirmary is, not saying that every hospital can be called an infirmary.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Voice Across the Ocean

Later in life, Blackwell returned to England. She continued to advocate for women in medicine and public health. To advocate means to speak up loudly in support of something — the way you might stand up for a friend on the playground, except Blackwell did it through lectures, books, and public speeches. She inspired a new generation of women to pursue careers in science and medicine.
stimulus.content_text: A Voice Across the Ocean Later in life, Blackwell returned to England. She continued to advocate for women in medicine and public health. To advocate means to speak up loudly in support of something — the way you might stand up for a friend on the playground, except Blackwell did it through lectures, books, and public speeches. She inspired a new generation of women to pursue careers in science and medicine.

prompt._: According to the text, how does the author organize the information to explain what it means to advocate?

choices.text: By defining the word and providing an example
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author defines 'advocate' and then gives a relatable example about standing up for a friend on the playground.

choices.text: By comparing two different types of medical jobs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions or compares different types of medical jobs.

choices.text: By listing events in the order they happened
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage isn't organized around a sequence of events happening in order — it's focused on explaining one vocabulary word.

choices.text: By stating a problem and offering a solution
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't present a problem and then offer a solution — it's explaining what a word means.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Thirty Doors Matter

Today, women make up a large percentage of medical school students and practicing physicians across the United States. That reality did not appear overnight. It was built one rejection letter, one hospital, one graduating class at a time. The next time you visit a doctor's office and see a woman in a white coat, remember: someone had to knock on thirty doors before the first one opened. And then she held it open — not just for herself, but for every woman who walked through after her.

That reality did not appear overnight. It was built one rejection letter, one hospital, one graduating class at a time. The next time you visit a doctor's office and see a woman in a white coat, remember: someone had to knock on thirty doors before the first one opened. And then she held it open — not just for herself, but for every woman who walked through after her.

prompt._: The author makes the point that having many women doctors today took a long time to happen. Which detail from the text supports this point?

choices.text: The first woman doctor held the door open for other women.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it describes what things look like today, not how slowly or gradually the change happened over time.

choices.text: You might see a woman in a white coat at the doctor's office.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because seeing a woman doctor at an office is just an observation about today, and it says nothing about how long the change took.

choices.text: The change was built one rejection letter and hospital at a time.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because 'built one rejection letter, one hospital, one graduating class at a time' clearly shows the change happened slowly, step by step, over a long period.

prompt._: How does the way other people reacted to Blackwell's medical career CONTRAST with the way she responded to those reactions?

choices.text: People asked her to teach, and she chose to treat patients instead
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never mentions people asking Blackwell to teach instead of treating patients — that contrast simply doesn't happen in the story.

choices.feedback: This is correct because people blocked her path by rejecting her applications and refusing to hire her, but she responded by creating her own hospital and medical college instead of accepting defeat.

choices.feedback: This is wrong because Blackwell never quietly gave up — she kept fighting even after 29 rejections and built her own hospital when others turned her away.

prompt._: Based on the text, what caused Elizabeth Blackwell to open her own hospital and medical college?

choices.text: A sick friend asked her to build a special place just for women.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was a dying friend's words that inspired Blackwell to become a doctor, not to build a hospital — those are two different events in the story.

choices.text: She needed a place to practice before moving back to England.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Blackwell founded the hospital and medical college in New York years before she ever returned to England.

choices.text: Other hospitals and schools refused to let women work or learn.

prompt._: Which sequence of events shows how the author organizes Elizabeth Blackwell's story?

choices.text: She decides to become a doctor, earns her degree, and opens a hospital.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the order the article tells the story: deciding to become a doctor, graduating with her degree in 1849, and then opening her hospital in 1857.

prompt._: According to the article, how does Blackwell's graduation compare with the broader change for women in medicine?

choices.text: Her graduation opened every other medical school to women within a few years.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says most medical schools stayed closed to women for decades after Blackwell graduated, not just a few years.

choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says the Geneva vote was the turning point for women in medicine overall.

choices.text: Her graduation started a slow change that took many more women and decades.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says Blackwell's graduation 'lit a match' but the full change took many more women and many more decades to happen.

choices.text: Her graduation ended prejudice because hospitals began hiring women right away.

assessments.title: Charles Drew: Pioneer of Blood Banking

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Charles Drew and the Blood That Couldn't Wait

A Problem Measured in Minutes

A soldier lies wounded on a battlefield in 1940. A doctor rushes over, but the nearest supply of stored blood has already gone bad. Whole blood — the kind flowing through your veins right now — spoils in just days, even when kept cold. Imagine milk left in the refrigerator too long. Without a fresh supply and the right blood type, the doctor can do nothing.

This scene happened thousands of times during World War II. Wounded soldiers needed blood transfusions — a medical procedure where blood from a healthy person is put into the body of someone who has lost too much of their own. But getting the right blood to the right patient in time seemed impossible on a battlefield far from any hospital. One surgeon believed he could fix this. His name was Charles Drew.
stimulus.content_text: Charles Drew and the Blood That Couldn't WaitA Problem Measured in Minutes A soldier lies wounded on a battlefield in 1940. A doctor rushes over, but the nearest supply of stored blood has already gone bad. Whole blood — the kind flowing through your veins right now — spoils in just days, even when kept cold. Imagine milk left in the refrigerator too long. Without a fresh supply and the right blood type, the doctor can do nothing. This scene happened thousands of times during World War II. Wounded soldiers needed blood transfusions — a medical procedure where blood from a healthy person is put into the body of someone who has lost too much of their own. But getting the right blood to the right patient in time seemed impossible on a battlefield far from any hospital. One surgeon believed he could fix this. His name was Charles Drew.

prompt._: According to the passage, how is whole blood different from what doctors needed on the battlefield?

choices.text: It spoils too quickly instead of lasting long enough
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states whole blood 'spoils in just days,' meaning it went bad before it could reach wounded soldiers on the battlefield.

choices.text: It flows through veins instead of through medical tubes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only mentions blood flowing through veins to explain what whole blood is, not to describe a problem with using it.

choices.text: It requires a hospital instead of a battlefield clinic
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says whole blood can only be used in a hospital.

choices.text: It comes from healthy people instead of wounded soldiers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says transfusions use blood from healthy people, which is exactly what doctors were trying to provide — that's not the problem.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From Star Athlete to Star Scientist

Charles Drew was born in Washington, D.C., in 1904. He was African American, and he grew up in a time when Black Americans faced unfair barriers in almost every part of life, including education. He earned a scholarship to Amherst College in Massachusetts, where he became a star football player. He then studied medicine at McGill University in Montreal, Canada, graduating near the top of his class.

Drew later went to Columbia University in New York, where he focused on a question that haunted him: How could doctors store blood safely for longer periods? The answer he found would end up saving millions of lives.
stimulus.content_text: From Star Athlete to Star Scientist Charles Drew was born in Washington, D.C., in 1904. He was African American, and he grew up in a time when Black Americans faced unfair barriers in almost every part of life, including education. He earned a scholarship to Amherst College in Massachusetts, where he became a star football player. He then studied medicine at McGill University in Montreal, Canada, graduating near the top of his class. Drew later went to Columbia University in New York, where he focused on a question that haunted him: How could doctors store blood safely for longer periods? The answer he found would end up saving millions of lives.

prompt._: According to the text, what was the result of Charles Drew finding the answer to storing blood safely?

choices.text: He earned a special college scholarship.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Drew earned his scholarship to Amherst College before he ever studied blood storage — it was not a result of his discovery.

choices.text: He became a famous star football player.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Drew became a star football player at Amherst College early in his life, long before his work on storing blood.

choices.text: He ended up saving millions of lives.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says his answer to storing blood safely 'would end up saving millions of lives' — those are almost the exact words from the text!

choices.text: He graduated near the top of his class.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because graduating near the top of his class happened when he finished medical school at McGill University, which was before his blood storage research.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Separating Blood Into Something That Lasts

You might think blood is just one red liquid, the same all the way through. Actually, blood is more like a soup. It has solid pieces — red blood cells and white blood cells — floating in a golden-yellow liquid called plasma. Think of it this way: plasma is the broth, and the blood cells are the chunks of vegetables and meat.

Here is the problem with whole blood. Those blood cells break down quickly, even in a refrigerator. Stored whole blood lasts only about a week. But Drew discovered that plasma, separated from the cells, could be dried into a powder. That dried plasma could sit on a shelf for months. When a doctor needed it, the powder could be reconstituted — mixed back together with sterile water, the way you add water to powdered juice mix. The plasma was ready to use again.

Plasma carries proteins, nutrients, and antibodies — tiny particles your body makes to fight off germs and disease. A patient receiving plasma got those benefits without needing a perfect blood-type match. Drew's discovery meant doctors could prepare huge supplies of plasma ahead of time and ship them across oceans without spoiling. For the first time, large-scale blood banking actually worked.

It has solid pieces — red blood cells and white blood cells — floating in a golden-yellow liquid called plasma. Think of it this way: plasma is the broth, and the blood cells are the chunks of vegetables and meat. Here is the problem with whole blood. Those blood cells break down quickly, even in a refrigerator. Stored whole blood lasts only about a week. But Drew discovered that plasma, separated from the cells, could be dried into a powder. That dried plasma could sit on a shelf for months. When a doctor needed it, the powder could be reconstituted — mixed back together with sterile water, the way you add water to powdered juice mix. The plasma was ready to use again. Plasma carries proteins, nutrients, and antibodies — tiny particles your body makes to fight off germs and disease. A patient receiving plasma got those benefits without needing a perfect blood-type match. Drew's discovery meant doctors could prepare huge supplies of plasma ahead of time and ship them across oceans without spoiling. For the first time, large-scale blood banking actually worked.

prompt._: According to the passage, why did Drew separate plasma from blood cells before drying it into a powder?

choices.text: because plasma needed antibodies added to it before it could be shipped
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says plasma already contains antibodies on its own — no one had to add them before shipping.

choices.text: because whole blood cells break down quickly and cannot be stored for long
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that blood cells break down quickly and whole blood only lasts about a week, which is exactly why Drew separated the plasma first.

choices.text: because dried blood cells could be mixed back together with sterile water
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's the dried plasma powder — not blood cells — that gets mixed back with sterile water to be used again.

choices.text: because plasma had to match a patient's blood type before it could be used
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the opposite: one benefit of plasma is that patients could receive it without needing a perfect blood-type match.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Blood for Britain, Blood for America

In 1940, German bombs were falling on British cities. Drew was asked to direct the Blood for Britain program, which collected plasma in the United States and shipped it across the Atlantic Ocean to British hospitals. Drew organized the entire operation — how to collect donations, process them, dry the plasma, and transport it safely. The program succeeded, and it saved lives an ocean away.

Next, Drew was appointed to lead the American Red Cross blood bank program. This program collected blood from American donors to supply the U.S. Military. Under Drew's leadership, workers built efficient systems for collecting, processing, and distributing blood on a massive scale. The methods Drew created became the foundation of how blood banks work today.
stimulus.content_text: Blood for Britain, Blood for America In 1940, German bombs were falling on British cities. Drew was asked to direct the Blood for Britain program, which collected plasma in the United States and shipped it across the Atlantic Ocean to British hospitals. Drew organized the entire operation — how to collect donations, process them, dry the plasma, and transport it safely. The program succeeded, and it saved lives an ocean away. Next, Drew was appointed to lead the American Red Cross blood bank program. This program collected blood from American donors to supply the U.S. Military. Under Drew's leadership, workers built efficient systems for collecting, processing, and distributing blood on a massive scale. The methods Drew created became the foundation of how blood banks work today.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are blood banks today similar to the programs Charles Drew started?

choices.text: They use the methods he first created.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the methods Drew created became the foundation of how blood banks work today.

choices.text: They supply blood only to the military.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Drew's Red Cross program supplied blood to the U.S. Military, the passage does not say that is all blood banks do today.

choices.text: They collect donations to stop bombings.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because blood collection is about saving lives through medicine, not stopping bombings — the bombing was just the reason Britain needed help at that time.

choices.text: They ship most plasma across the ocean.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because shipping plasma across the ocean was only part of the special 1940 Blood for Britain program, not something today's blood banks are known for.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Science Against Prejudice and Teaching the Next Generation

Despite everything Drew had accomplished, he faced the ugly reality of racial discrimination. The U.S. Military ordered that donated blood be segregated — separated and labeled by the race of the donor. Blood from Black donors was kept apart from blood from white donors.

But blood types do not follow racial lines. Plasma from any healthy donor can safely be given to any patient, regardless of race. He called the policy harmful and based on ignorance, not science. Speaking out against segregation risked his career, but he refused to stay silent.

Drew left the Red Cross and returned to Howard University in Washington, D.C. There, he taught surgery and trained a new generation of African American doctors. At a time when most medical schools refused to admit Black students, Drew helped his students build the skills and confidence to become excellent surgeons themselves.

Tragically, Charles Drew died in 1950 at just forty-five years old, from injuries in a car accident in North Carolina. Charles Drew didn't just solve a medical problem. He proved that the truth inside our veins is the same, no matter who we are.

The U.S. Military ordered that donated blood be segregated — separated and labeled by the race of the donor. Blood from Black donors was kept apart from blood from white donors. But blood types do not follow racial lines. Plasma from any healthy donor can safely be given to any patient, regardless of race. He called the policy harmful and based on ignorance, not science. Speaking out against segregation risked his career, but he refused to stay silent. Drew left the Red Cross and returned to Howard University in Washington, D.C. There, he taught surgery and trained a new generation of African American doctors. At a time when most medical schools refused to admit Black students, Drew helped his students build the skills and confidence to become excellent surgeons themselves. Tragically, Charles Drew died in 1950 at just forty-five years old, from injuries in a car accident in North Carolina. Charles Drew didn't just solve a medical problem. He proved that the truth inside our veins is the same, no matter who we are.

prompt._: In the sentence 'The U.S. Military ordered that donated blood be segregated — separated and labeled by the race of the donor,' what does the word 'segregated' mean?

choices.text: mixed together in one place
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'segregated' actually means the opposite of mixing — it means keeping things separated into different groups.

choices.text: thrown away after donation
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the donated blood was kept and used for patients, not thrown away.

choices.text: tested for safety before use
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the military was testing blood for safety — it says they were separating it by the race of the donor.

choices.text: divided into groups by race
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains 'segregated' right away by saying 'separated and labeled by the race of the donor,' and it describes Black donors' blood being kept apart from white donors' blood.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is giving a patient whole blood different from giving them plasma?

choices.text: Whole blood lasts for several months, while plasma spoils in just a few days.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says whole blood spoils in about a week, while dried plasma can last for months on a shelf.

choices.text: Whole blood needs a specific type match, while plasma works for any patient.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article says whole blood requires the right blood type match, while plasma can be given to any patient regardless of their blood type.

choices.text: Whole blood is dried into a powder, while plasma is kept as a golden liquid.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article explains that plasma is the one that gets dried into a powder, not whole blood.

choices.text: Whole blood is used for soldiers, while plasma is used for hospital patients.
choices.feedback: The article never says whole blood is only for soldiers and plasma is only for hospital patients — both were used to help people in different situations.

prompt._: Which sentence best describes the cause-and-effect structure used in the first three sections of the article?

choices.text: The author explains that whole blood spoiled quickly, which caused Drew to figure out how to dry plasma.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows that whole blood spoiling quickly was the cause, and that problem led Drew to discover how to dry plasma so it could last for months — a direct cause-and-effect relationship across the first three sections.

choices.text: The author states that doctors built massive blood banks overseas, which caused whole blood cells to break down fast.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the cause and effect completely backwards — blood cells breaking down fast was the problem Drew was trying to solve, not something that happened after blood banks were built.

choices.text: The author shows that wounded soldiers needed blood transfusions, which caused the military to separate donated blood by race.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the military's decision to separate blood by race happens later in the article, in section five, and it was not caused by soldiers needing blood transfusions.

choices.text: The author describes how Drew grew up facing unfair rules, which caused him to become a star college athlete.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while Drew did face unfair barriers growing up, those barriers didn't cause him to become an athlete — becoming an athlete and facing barriers were separate parts of his life story.

prompt._: Which sequence of events best summarizes Charles Drew's work with blood banking?

choices.text: He built large blood banks, learned to dry plasma, and then noticed whole blood spoiled quickly.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the steps are in the wrong order — Drew had to discover how to dry plasma first before he could build large blood banks.

choices.text: He played college football, studied medicine in Canada, and then moved to a school in New York.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it only talks about Drew's early life — football and school — and leaves out all of his important blood banking discoveries.

choices.text: He noticed whole blood spoiled quickly, learned to dry plasma, and then built large blood banks.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it accurately shows the order of Drew's work: spotting the spoiling problem, discovering dried plasma, and then building large blood banks.

choices.text: He separated whole blood into plasma, shipped it to Germany, and then stopped teaching new doctors.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Drew shipped plasma to Britain (not Germany), and he did continue teaching the next generation of doctors at Howard University.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were the challenges Charles Drew faced as a young student similar to the challenges he faced while working for the U.S. Military?

choices.text: He was forced to leave his job to teach at a college.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because leaving to teach at Howard University happened after his time with the Red Cross, so it was not a challenge he faced as a young student.

choices.text: He needed to travel across the ocean to find new work.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Drew never had to travel across the ocean to find work — he directed the Blood for Britain program from the United States.

choices.text: He struggled to discover a way to store blood safely.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because figuring out how to store blood was a scientific challenge Drew worked on at Columbia University, not something he faced as a young student or in the military.

choices.text: He had to deal with unfair rules based on his race.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both as a young student and while working for the military, Drew faced unfair rules and barriers because of his race.

assessments.title: Michael Faraday: Master of Electricity

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Michael Faraday: The Bookbinder Who Lit Up the World

A Boy Surrounded by Books

A young boy stands in the back of a London bookshop, gluing leather covers onto stacks of pages. His name is Michael Faraday. His family is so poor they sometimes cannot afford a full loaf of bread. He has almost no formal schooling. But the books piled around him are about to change his life — and, eventually, yours.

His job was to stitch and bind books for customers. His father was a blacksmith who struggled to feed the family. But Faraday did more than bind those books. He even started running simple experiments in the back of the shop, using supplies he scraped together himself. Faraday was born in 1791 in England. At fourteen, Michael became an apprentice to a bookbinder — a young worker learning a trade, the way you might learn a craft by working alongside an expert.

You might think that the greatest scientists all came from wealthy families and attended the best schools. Faraday proves that idea wrong. He taught himself science one borrowed book at a time.

His name is Michael Faraday. His family is so poor they sometimes cannot afford a full loaf of bread. He has almost no formal schooling. But the books piled around him are about to change his life — and, eventually, yours. His job was to stitch and bind books for customers. His father was a blacksmith who struggled to feed the family. But Faraday did more than bind those books. He even started running simple experiments in the back of the shop, using supplies he scraped together himself. Faraday was born in 1791 in England. At fourteen, Michael became an apprentice to a bookbinder — a young worker learning a trade, the way you might learn a craft by working alongside an expert. You might think that the greatest scientists all came from wealthy families and attended the best schools. Faraday proves that idea wrong. He taught himself science one borrowed book at a time.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Michael Faraday do in the bookshop that was different from his regular job of binding books?

choices.text: He attended the best schools in the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states Faraday had almost no formal schooling and learned on his own.

choices.text: He sold loaves of bread to customers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions bread only to show how poor his family was, not because Faraday sold it.

choices.text: He worked as a blacksmith with his dad.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says his father was the blacksmith, not Michael.

choices.text: He ran simple experiments in the shop.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says Faraday ran simple experiments in the back of the shop, which was extra — his real job was just binding books.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Bold Letter That Changed Everything

At age twenty, Faraday attended a series of public lectures by the famous chemist Humphry Davy at the Royal Institution in London. Faraday sat in the audience and took careful, detailed notes. Then he did something daring. He bound those notes into a neat book and mailed them to Davy along with a letter asking for a job.

Davy was impressed. He hired Faraday as his laboratory assistant. Suddenly, the bookbinder's apprentice was working in one of the finest science laboratories in the world. In chemistry, Faraday discovered benzene — a liquid chemical that factories later used to make plastics, dyes, and medicines. He also worked on improving laboratory burners used for heating chemicals during experiments.

Davy was impressed. He hired Faraday as his laboratory assistant. Suddenly, the bookbinder's apprentice was working in one of the finest science laboratories in the world. In chemistry, Faraday discovered benzene — a liquid chemical that factories later used to make plastics, dyes, and medicines. He also worked on improving laboratory burners used for heating chemicals during experiments.

choices.text: Faraday discovered a new liquid chemical.
choices.feedback: Faraday did discover benzene, but that happened later while working in the lab — it wasn't the result of mailing the letter.

choices.text: Faraday started teaching public lectures.
choices.feedback: It was actually Davy who gave the public lectures — Faraday was the one sitting in the audience listening.

choices.text: Davy learned how to bind books neatly.
choices.feedback: The passage says Davy was impressed by Faraday's careful notes, not that he wanted to learn how to bind books.

choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Davy was impressed by the mailed book of notes and hired Faraday as his laboratory assistant.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Magnet, the Wire, and the Electric Spark

Faraday's biggest discovery came in 1831, and it connects to something you use every single day. It's easy to assume that magnets and electricity have nothing to do with each other. A magnet sticks to your refrigerator. Electricity flows through wires. They seem completely separate. Here's the surprising part: Faraday proved they are deeply connected.

He discovered electromagnetic induction — the process of creating electricity by moving a magnet near a wire. Picture a coil of wire, which is wire wrapped in loops like a spring. When Faraday pushed a magnet through that coil, an electric current began flowing through the wire. Motion plus magnetism made electricity. He also discovered the reverse: sending electric current through a wire creates a magnetic force that can push things and cause motion.

You might think that electricity simply lives inside the wall outlet, waiting for you to plug something in. Actually, that electricity has to be made somewhere. Power plants use Faraday's discovery every day. They spin enormous magnets inside giant coils of wire. That spinning motion generates the electric current that travels through power lines to your home, your school, and every building around you. The machine that does this is called an electric generator — a device that converts motion into electricity, the way a water wheel converts flowing water into spinning energy.

The reverse works too. An electric motor takes electrical energy and turns it into motion — spinning washing machines, lifting elevators, and moving electric cars. Every motor traces back to Faraday's discovery.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that magnets spin inside coils first, and that motion creates the electric current that then travels through power lines.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Seeing the Invisible

Faraday also introduced the idea of lines of force — imaginary lines that map out the invisible fields around magnets and electric charges. If you have ever sprinkled iron filings near a magnet and watched them arrange into curved patterns, you have seen exactly what Faraday described. Years later, the physicist James Clerk Maxwell used Faraday's idea to write the mathematical equations that explain how light, electricity, and magnetism all work together.
stimulus.content_text: Seeing the Invisible Faraday also introduced the idea of lines of force — imaginary lines that map out the invisible fields around magnets and electric charges. If you have ever sprinkled iron filings near a magnet and watched them arrange into curved patterns, you have seen exactly what Faraday described. Years later, the physicist James Clerk Maxwell used Faraday's idea to write the mathematical equations that explain how light, electricity, and magnetism all work together.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how are the patterns of iron filings similar to Faraday's lines of force?

choices.text: They both show the shape of invisible fields.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says lines of force map invisible fields, and iron filings arrange into curved patterns that show those same invisible fields.

choices.text: They both are made of tiny pieces of metal.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because lines of force are described as imaginary lines, so they are definitely not made of tiny pieces of metal like iron filings are.

choices.text: They both were created by James Clerk Maxwell.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says Faraday introduced the idea of lines of force, and Maxwell came along years later to build on Faraday's work.

choices.text: They both explain how light and magnets work.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it was Maxwell's mathematical equations — not the iron filings or lines of force — that explained how light, electricity, and magnetism work together.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Simple Man of Science

Faraday became one of the most respected scientists of his time. The queen offered him a knighthood — a high honor that would have put "Sir" before his name. He was also offered the presidency of the Royal Society, Britain's most famous scientific organization. He turned both down. He preferred to stay in his laboratory, run experiments, and give public lectures that inspired young people to explore science. He worked at the Royal Institution for more than forty years.

Michael Faraday died in 1867. He never attended college. He never learned advanced mathematics. But tonight, when you flip a light switch, a generator somewhere will spin a magnet inside a coil of wire — and the electricity that reaches your lamp will exist because a poor boy in a bookshop refused to stop reading.

assessments.title: Woodblock Prints: Pictures for the People

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Art of Japan: Prints for the People

A Workshop in Old Japan and the Floating World

A sharp blade bites into a block of cherry wood. Tiny curls of pale wood fall to the floor. In a small workshop in Japan, around the year 1800, a carver hunches over his work, cutting away everything that should stay white. He leaves behind thin raised lines — the skeleton of a picture. When he finishes, a printer rolls ink across those raised lines, lays down a sheet of paper, and rubs the back with a smooth, flat tool called a baren. He peels the paper away. An image appears: a crashing wave, a snowy mountain, a street packed with people. This is the art of Japanese woodblock printing.

These prints belong to a tradition called ukiyo-e, which means "pictures of the floating world." The "floating world" was Japan's lively, ever-changing culture of entertainment and beauty — kabuki actors in wild costumes, sumo wrestlers, and crowded streets glowing with lanterns. You might picture traditional Japanese art as quiet and simple, like a Zen garden with raked sand. But ukiyo-e prints were the opposite — bursting with color, packed with detail, and full of action.

Here's the surprising part: ukiyo-e prints were not made for rich nobles — people called aristocrats, meaning those born into powerful, wealthy families. Instead, these prints were cheap enough for shopkeepers, craftspeople, and farmers to buy. Because the carved block could be inked and pressed again and again, like a rubber stamp, a printer could make hundreds of copies of the same image. For the first time in Japanese history, ordinary people could hang art on their walls.

prompt._: According to the text, how were ukiyo-e prints different from art for aristocrats?

choices.text: They were cheap enough for ordinary people.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the prints were 'cheap enough for shopkeepers, craftspeople, and farmers to buy,' making them affordable for ordinary people.

choices.text: They were printed only one time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that the carved block could be inked and pressed again and again, allowing printers to make hundreds of copies.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How Woodblock Prints Were Made

Actually, creating a woodblock print took a team of three specialists. First, an artist drew the design on thin paper. Next, a carver glued that drawing face-down onto a smooth cherry wood block and carefully cut away every area meant to stay blank, leaving only the drawn lines raised up. Then a printer took over, inking those raised lines and pressing paper onto the block.

Color prints were even more complex. The team carved a separate block for each color. The printer pressed the same sheet of paper onto one block after another, adding a new color each time. A single finished print might need ten or more carved blocks working together.

Next, a carver glued that drawing face-down onto a smooth cherry wood block and carefully cut away every area meant to stay blank, leaving only the drawn lines raised up. Then a printer took over, inking those raised lines and pressing paper onto the block. Color prints were even more complex. The team carved a separate block for each color. The printer pressed the same sheet of paper onto one block after another, adding a new color each time. A single finished print might need ten or more carved blocks working together.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states 'the team carved a separate block for each color,' meaning more colors means more blocks.

choices.text: because the printer pressed the paper onto the block many times
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pressing paper onto multiple blocks is what happens because there are many blocks, not the reason the blocks were needed in the first place.

choices.text: because each specialist on the team carved their own block
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the carver carved blocks — the passage never says each team member made their own block.

choices.text: because the carver cut away the blank areas on every block
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because cutting away blank areas describes how a block is made, not why you would need more blocks.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Hokusai and Hiroshige: Two Masters of Ukiyo-e

The most famous ukiyo-e artist is Katsushika Hokusai, who lived from 1760 to 1849. His print The Great Wave off Kanagawa is one of the most recognized images on Earth. A giant, curling wave rises like a claw, about to crash down on tiny fishing boats. Behind the chaos, the snow-covered peak of Mount Fuji sits perfectly still. The contrast between the violent water and the serene mountain — serene meaning calm and peaceful, like a sleeping cat — fills the image with drama. This print belongs to a series called Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, in which Hokusai showed Japan's most famous mountain from dozens of angles and across different seasons.

Another beloved ukiyo-e artist is Utagawa Hiroshige, who lived from 1797 to 1858. Hiroshige was famous for his scenes of nature and travel, especially prints showing life along the Tokaido road. This great highway connected the city of Edo (modern-day Tokyo) to Kyoto. His prints captured rain slanting across bridges, snow settling on rooftops, moonlight silvering a river, and mist swallowing distant hills. Hiroshige's atmosphere — the mood or feeling a scene creates, like the hush you feel in a forest — gave his work a quiet, poetic beauty.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the giant wave about to do after it rises like a claw?

choices.text: Swallow up the distant green hills
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — mist swallowing distant hills is also from Hiroshige's work, not from the Great Wave description.

choices.text: Sit perfectly still behind the chaos
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says Mount Fuji 'sits perfectly still behind the chaos,' not the wave.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Shock to European Eyes and Art That Belongs to Everyone

In the mid-1800s, Japanese woodblock prints reached Europe, and Western artists were stunned. The bold outlines, flat patches of bright color, and unusual angles looked nothing like traditional European painting. French painters known as Impressionists — artists who tried to capture quick moments of light and color rather than perfect detail — studied these prints closely. So did Post-Impressionists, the next wave of artists who pushed even further from tradition. Claude Monet collected Japanese prints and built a Japanese-style garden at his home in Giverny, France. Vincent van Gogh admired Hiroshige so deeply that he painted his own copies of Hiroshige's prints, brushstroke by brushstroke.

Today, The Great Wave appears on phone cases, T-shirts, and posters around the world. That wave started as ink on a carved block of cherry wood, printed cheaply so a Japanese shopkeeper could pin it to a wall. It was never locked behind glass in a palace. It was made for everyone — and that is exactly why, more than two hundred years later, it still belongs to everyone.

prompt._: How is the way people use the image of The Great Wave today similar to how it was used in old Japan?

choices.text: It is still studied by French painters to learn unusual angles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because French painters studied Japanese prints back in the 1800s, and the passage does not say they are still doing this today.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because The Great Wave today appears on modern products like phone cases and T-shirts, not printed by teams using carved cherry wood blocks.

assessments.title: Screens, Scrolls, and Painted Beauty

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Art of Japan: Golden Screens, Unrolling Stories, and the Power of Empty Space

A Room That Glows

A single candle flickers in a dark room. Its light catches a wall of gold and bounces back, filling the space with a warm, shimmering glow. Painted cherry blossoms seem to float across the golden surface. A crane spreads its wings. Pine branches bend in a wind you cannot feel. You are sitting inside a work of art.

This was an ordinary evening in a wealthy Japanese home hundreds of years ago. In traditional Japanese houses and palaces, people did not hang framed pictures on their walls the way many Western homes do today. Instead, art surrounded them — painted on folding screens, rolled up in scrolls, and brushed in ink on paper. These art forms turned living spaces into places of beauty and quiet thought.
stimulus.content_text: Art of Japan: Golden Screens, Unrolling Stories, and the Power of Empty SpaceA Room That Glows A single candle flickers in a dark room. Its light catches a wall of gold and bounces back, filling the space with a warm, shimmering glow. Painted cherry blossoms seem to float across the golden surface. A crane spreads its wings. Pine branches bend in a wind you cannot feel. You are sitting inside a work of art. This was an ordinary evening in a wealthy Japanese home hundreds of years ago. In traditional Japanese houses and palaces, people did not hang framed pictures on their walls the way many Western homes do today. Instead, art surrounded them — painted on folding screens, rolled up in scrolls, and brushed in ink on paper. These art forms turned living spaces into places of beauty and quiet thought.

prompt._: According to the text, how was art in traditional Japanese homes different from art in many Western homes today?

choices.text: It was carved into golden candle holders.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything being carved into candle holders — that detail does not appear anywhere in the text.

choices.text: It was placed inside heavy wooden frames.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says framed pictures are found in Western homes, which is actually the opposite of how Japanese homes displayed art.

choices.text: It was painted on screens and scrolls.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that art in traditional Japanese homes was 'painted on folding screens, rolled up in scrolls, and brushed in ink on paper.'

choices.text: It was painted directly onto the walls.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage describes painted cherry blossoms and cranes, these were on screens and scrolls, not painted directly onto the walls.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Byobu: Screens of Wind and Gold

Japanese folding screens are called byobu, which means "protection from wind." A byobu is a set of large panels connected by hinges — flexible joints that let the panels swing open and closed, like the cover of a book. The screens stood on the floor. People folded them up or carried them from room to room to block drafts and divide big spaces into smaller, cozier areas.

But over the centuries, byobu became far more than useful furniture. Artists covered them with magnificent paintings of mountains, flowers, birds, and scenes from old stories and legends. Many screens were painted over backgrounds of gold leaf — sheets of real gold hammered so thin they were almost see-through, then pressed carefully onto the surface. When candlelight or lamplight danced across that gold, the whole room seemed to glow from within. Artists painted cherry blossoms, pine trees, and cranes over the gold, creating scenes where nature seemed to shimmer with an almost magical light.

People folded them up or carried them from room to room to block drafts and divide big spaces into smaller, cozier areas. But over the centuries, byobu became far more than useful furniture. Artists covered them with magnificent paintings of mountains, flowers, birds, and scenes from old stories and legends. Many screens were painted over backgrounds of gold leaf — sheets of real gold hammered so thin they were almost see-through, then pressed carefully onto the surface. When candlelight or lamplight danced across that gold, the whole room seemed to glow from within. Artists painted cherry blossoms, pine trees, and cranes over the gold, creating scenes where nature seemed to shimmer with an almost magical light.

prompt._: Based on the text, why did a room with a byobu seem to glow from within?

choices.text: Because artists painted bright cherry blossoms.
choices.feedback: Cherry blossoms were part of the paintings on the screen, but the passage says it was the gold leaf reflecting light that made the room glow, not the blossoms.

choices.text: Because candlelight danced across the gold.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says that when candlelight danced across the gold leaf, the whole room seemed to glow from within.

choices.text: Because the large panels blocked cold drafts.
choices.feedback: Blocking cold drafts was the original job of byobu screens, but it has nothing to do with why a room would glow with light.

choices.text: Because the flexible hinges swung wide open.
choices.feedback: The hinges let the panels swing open and closed, but the passage never connects the hinges to any kind of glowing or light effect.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Scroll Paintings: Art You Unroll Like a Story

But Japanese scroll paintings worked completely differently. They were not framed or hung up for everyone to see. Instead, you held them in your hands and unrolled them slowly, a little at a time.

A hand scroll could stretch several feet long. You unrolled it from right to left, and the scene changed as you went — mountains gave way to rivers, rivers to villages, villages to distant forests. Viewing a hand scroll felt more like watching a slow, silent film than looking at a single picture. Each section surprised you with something new.

Hanging scrolls, called kakemono, were different. These vertical paintings hung on a wall inside a special alcove — a small, recessed area — called a tokonoma. Families changed the hanging scroll with the seasons. Cherry blossoms appeared in spring. A rushing waterfall cooled the summer. Red and orange leaves colored the fall. A snowy scene marked winter. The tokonoma was like a tiny gallery that refreshed itself four times a year.

These vertical paintings hung on a wall inside a special alcove — a small, recessed area — called a tokonoma. Families changed the hanging scroll with the seasons. Cherry blossoms appeared in spring. A rushing waterfall cooled the summer. Red and orange leaves colored the fall. A snowy scene marked winter. The tokonoma was like a tiny gallery that refreshed itself four times a year.

prompt._: According to the text, why did viewing a hand scroll feel like watching a slow, silent film?

choices.text: Because it was painted inside a small alcove
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the small alcove (tokonoma) is where hanging scrolls were displayed, not hand scrolls.

choices.text: Because families replaced it with each new season
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because changing with the seasons describes hanging scrolls (kakemono), not hand scrolls.

choices.text: Because the scene changed as you unrolled it bit by bit
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly explains that the scene kept changing as you unrolled the scroll, just like how a film keeps showing you new things as it plays.

choices.text: Because the whole painting stayed in one place on the wall
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hand scrolls were NOT hung on a wall — you held them in your hands and unrolled them, which is the opposite of staying in one place.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Sumi-e: Saying More with Less

You may have seen the famous Japanese woodblock print of a giant wave about to crash over tiny boats. That print, by an artist named Hokusai, is one style of Japanese art. But a very different tradition, called sumi-e, or ink wash painting, takes the opposite approach. Instead of bold colors and packed details, sumi-e uses only black ink.

Artists mixed the ink with different amounts of water to create tones ranging from the palest gray to the deepest black. Then, with just a few confident brushstrokes, they captured the essence of a bamboo stalk, a misty mountain, or a bird perched on a branch. No extra lines. No fussing. Sumi-e values suggestion over explanation — the artist paints just enough for your imagination to fill in the rest.

Here is the surprising part: the blank areas of a sumi-e painting are not mistakes. The artist did not forget to finish. That empty space has a name — ma — and it is just as carefully planned as the ink itself. Ma gives your eye a place to rest and your mind room to wander. In many Western paintings, artists fill every inch of the canvas with color and detail. In sumi-e, what the artist leaves out matters as much as what the artist puts in.

prompt._: Which phrase from the passage best helps a reader understand that the word "values" means "likes better when comparing choices"?

choices.text: different amounts of water
choices.feedback: This phrase is about how artists prepare their ink, so it doesn't help us understand what sumi-e prefers or values.

choices.text: the deepest black
choices.feedback: This phrase describes a shade of ink color, not a preference or comparison between two choices.

choices.text: the opposite approach
choices.feedback: This phrase tells us sumi-e is different from other art styles, but it doesn't show us that sumi-e is choosing or preferring one thing over another.

choices.text: suggestion over explanation
choices.feedback: This is correct because the word 'over' shows a comparison and a preference — sumi-e likes suggestion more than explanation, which matches the meaning of 'values' as liking something better when comparing choices.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Art That Breathes with You

Screens you can move. Scrolls you can unroll. Paintings that change with the seasons. Ink drawings that leave space for your own thoughts. Japanese art was never meant to sit behind glass in a museum. It was meant to live alongside people — to shift, glow, and breathe inside the rooms where families gathered, talked, and dreamed.

A Japanese ink painter would tell you that emptiness is not nothing. It is an invitation.

A Japanese ink painter would tell you that emptiness is not nothing. It is an invitation.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never suggests the empty space is a request for another artist to add more ink.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the empty space is a deliberate artistic choice, not a sign that the painting is unfinished.

choices.text: a blank space that is a mistake in the artwork
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage treats empty space as something meaningful and on purpose, not as an error.

choices.text: Sumi-e artists paint on gold leaf while Western painters use plain paper
