assessments.title: Japanese Gardens: Art in Nature

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Art of Japan: Gardens Made of Silence and Stone

A Garden Where Nothing Is an Accident

Fifteen stones sit on a rectangle of white gravel in Kyoto, Japan. No flowers. No grass. No water. Just stones, gravel, and raked lines curving like frozen waves. Visitors have been staring at this garden for over five hundred years — and here is the strange part. No matter where you stand, you can only see fourteen of the fifteen stones at once. One always hides behind the others. That hidden stone is said to represent an idea: that no one can ever see everything perfectly. This is the Ryoan-ji temple garden, and it is one of the most famous works of art in the world. Not a painting. Not a sculpture. A garden.
stimulus.content_text: Art of Japan: Gardens Made of Silence and StoneA Garden Where Nothing Is an Accident Fifteen stones sit on a rectangle of white gravel in Kyoto, Japan. No flowers. No grass. No water. Just stones, gravel, and raked lines curving like frozen waves. Visitors have been staring at this garden for over five hundred years — and here is the strange part. No matter where you stand, you can only see fourteen of the fifteen stones at once. One always hides behind the others. That hidden stone is said to represent an idea: that no one can ever see everything perfectly. This is the Ryoan-ji temple garden, and it is one of the most famous works of art in the world. Not a painting. Not a sculpture. A garden.

prompt._: According to the passage, what is the contrast between the total number of stones in the garden and what a visitor can see?

choices.text: There are fourteen stones, but visitors imagine one more.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it flips the numbers — the passage says there are fifteen real stones, not fourteen, and visitors don't imagine an extra one.

choices.text: There are fifteen stones, but one is always hidden.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly tells us there are fifteen stones but only fourteen can ever be seen at once — one always hides behind the others.

choices.text: There are fifteen stones, but they look like waves.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the wave-like lines describe the raked gravel, not the number of stones a visitor can see.

choices.text: There are fourteen stones, but they blend into gravel.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the stones blending into the gravel or being hard to see for that reason.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Designed to Look Undesigned

Japanese gardens look wild and natural, as if wind and rain arranged every rock and branch. Every stone, every curve of water, every leaning tree has been placed on purpose. Garden designers spend years planning where a single boulder will sit. Trees are trimmed to look as though storms shaped them over decades. Moss-covered stones rest beside quiet ponds, creating scenes that seem untouched by human hands — but every detail is deliberate.

This is very different from many European gardens, which use symmetrical designs — meaning both sides match evenly, like a butterfly's wings. European gardens often feature geometric shapes, patterns built from straight lines, circles, and perfect squares, like tiles on a floor. Japanese gardens reject that kind of order. They aim for the beauty of a mountain forest or a rocky shoreline — places that feel peaceful because nothing looks forced.
stimulus.content_text: Designed to Look Undesigned Japanese gardens look wild and natural, as if wind and rain arranged every rock and branch. Every stone, every curve of water, every leaning tree has been placed on purpose. Garden designers spend years planning where a single boulder will sit. Trees are trimmed to look as though storms shaped them over decades. Moss-covered stones rest beside quiet ponds, creating scenes that seem untouched by human hands — but every detail is deliberate. This is very different from many European gardens, which use symmetrical designs — meaning both sides match evenly, like a butterfly's wings. European gardens often feature geometric shapes, patterns built from straight lines, circles, and perfect squares, like tiles on a floor. Japanese gardens reject that kind of order. They aim for the beauty of a mountain forest or a rocky shoreline — places that feel peaceful because nothing looks forced.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why do Japanese gardens feel peaceful?

choices.text: Because both sides match evenly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because matching both sides evenly describes European gardens, not Japanese gardens — Japanese gardens actually reject that kind of order.

choices.text: Because people avoid planning them
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Japanese gardens actually involve a huge amount of planning, like spending years deciding where a single boulder should sit.

choices.text: Because storms shaped the trees
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because storms didn't really shape the trees — gardeners just trim the trees to make them look that way.

choices.text: Because nothing looks forced
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Japanese gardens feel peaceful 'because nothing looks forced,' making everything feel natural and calm.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Every Rock Tells a Story

In a Japanese garden, nothing is just decoration. Each element carries meaning. Rocks and stones stand for mountains. They represent strength and permanence — the quality of lasting a very long time, the way a mountain remains while rivers and seasons change around it. Water, whether a still pond or a trickling stream, stands for purity and the flow of life. Bridges connect one part of the garden to another, but they also symbolize journeys — the path from one stage of life to the next.

Cherry blossoms signal spring. Thick green leaves fill summer. Fiery red maples blaze through autumn. In winter, bare branches hold caps of snow. Walk through a Japanese garden, and you walk through time itself.
stimulus.content_text: Every Rock Tells a Story In a Japanese garden, nothing is just decoration. Each element carries meaning. Rocks and stones stand for mountains. They represent strength and permanence — the quality of lasting a very long time, the way a mountain remains while rivers and seasons change around it. Water, whether a still pond or a trickling stream, stands for purity and the flow of life. Bridges connect one part of the garden to another, but they also symbolize journeys — the path from one stage of life to the next. Cherry blossoms signal spring. Thick green leaves fill summer. Fiery red maples blaze through autumn. In winter, bare branches hold caps of snow. Walk through a Japanese garden, and you walk through time itself.

prompt._: According to the text, which event happens in the garden right after the thick green leaves of summer?

choices.text: Red maples blaze through autumn.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage lists autumn — with its fiery red maples — as the season that comes right after summer.

choices.text: Cherry blossoms signal the spring.
choices.feedback: Cherry blossoms signal spring, which comes before summer, not after it.

choices.text: Bare branches hold caps of snow.
choices.feedback: Bare branches with snow describe winter, which comes after autumn, not directly after summer.

choices.text: Trickling streams flow with life.
choices.feedback: Trickling streams are mentioned as a symbol of life's flow, but the passage never connects them to a season that follows summer.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Gardens Without a Single Drop of Water

The Ryoan-ji garden belongs to a special type called karesansui, which means "dry mountain and water." These Zen rock gardens contain no plants and no water at all. Instead, they use carefully raked gravel and a few precisely placed stones. The gravel is raked into lines and swirls that suggest flowing rivers or ocean waves — water made entirely of stone dust.

These gardens were not built for decoration. They were created at Zen Buddhist temples as places for meditation — the practice of sitting quietly and focusing your mind, the way you might close your eyes and listen to nothing but your own breathing. Monks would sit at the edge of the garden and gaze at the stones and gravel for long stretches of time. Even the raking itself was a form of meditation. Monks dragged wooden rakes slowly through the gravel, creating each line with full attention. The simple, still scene helped calm their thoughts.

These Zen rock gardens contain no plants and no water at all. Instead, they use carefully raked gravel and a few precisely placed stones. The gravel is raked into lines and swirls that suggest flowing rivers or ocean waves — water made entirely of stone dust. These gardens were not built for decoration. They were created at Zen Buddhist temples as places for meditation — the practice of sitting quietly and focusing your mind, the way you might close your eyes and listen to nothing but your own breathing. Monks would sit at the edge of the garden and gaze at the stones and gravel for long stretches of time. Even the raking itself was a form of meditation. Monks dragged wooden rakes slowly through the gravel, creating each line with full attention. The simple, still scene helped calm their thoughts.

prompt._: In the passage, the author compares meditation to 'the way you might close your eyes and listen to nothing but your own breathing.' How does this comparison help the reader understand the meaning of the word meditation?

choices.text: It shows that meditation is a type of exercise that makes your body stronger
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the comparison says nothing about exercise or making your body stronger — it's about being quiet and still, not physical activity.

choices.text: It proves that meditation requires closing your eyes at all times
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because closing your eyes is just used as an example to help explain the feeling of meditation; the passage never says you must always close your eyes to meditate.

choices.text: It connects meditation to a familiar experience of being quiet and focused
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author connects meditation to the familiar feeling of closing your eyes and focusing on your breathing, which helps you understand that meditation is about being quiet and focused.

choices.text: It explains that meditation means the same thing as falling asleep
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because listening to your breathing while being calm and focused is not the same as falling asleep — meditation is about paying attention, not drifting off.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Forest That Fits on a Table

The art of bonsai — growing miniature trees in small containers — is closely connected to Japanese garden traditions. A bonsai pine tree might stand only two feet tall yet look exactly like a centuries-old tree clinging to a cliff. Here is the surprising part: a bonsai tree is not a special tiny species. A bonsai maple is the same kind of tree as the full-sized maple in your yard. Growers keep it small through years of careful pruning — cutting back branches and roots the way a barber trims hair — along with wiring branches into graceful shapes and keeping the tree in a shallow pot. Some bonsai trees have been passed down through families for generations. They are living works of art that grow more beautiful with age.

A bonsai pine tree might stand only two feet tall yet look exactly like a centuries-old tree clinging to a cliff. Here is the surprising part: a bonsai tree is not a special tiny species. A bonsai maple is the same kind of tree as the full-sized maple in your yard. Growers keep it small through years of careful pruning — cutting back branches and roots the way a barber trims hair — along with wiring branches into graceful shapes and keeping the tree in a shallow pot. Some bonsai trees have been passed down through families for generations. They are living works of art that grow more beautiful with age.

choices.text: by contrasting pruning with other methods used to grow bonsai trees
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't contrast pruning with other methods; instead, it mentions wiring and shallow pots as methods used alongside pruning.

choices.text: by listing the steps of pruning in the order they happen over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't give a step-by-step list of pruning in the order it happens.

choices.text: by comparing pruning to a familiar action and describing what it involves
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author compares pruning to a barber trimming hair, a familiar action, and then describes what pruning involves — cutting branches and roots.

choices.text: by explaining a problem that pruning is designed to solve for growers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a problem that pruning is designed to solve.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Art You Can Walk Through

A painter uses brushes. A sculptor uses chisels. A Japanese garden designer uses moss, stone, water, and living wood. The gardens they create are never finished, because the trees keep growing and the seasons keep turning. Every visit looks different from the last. That is something no painting can do. These gardens prove that art does not have to hang on a wall. Sometimes it grows under your feet, shifts with the wind, and hides one stone just to remind you that you can never quite see it all.
stimulus.content_text: Art You Can Walk Through A painter uses brushes. A sculptor uses chisels. A Japanese garden designer uses moss, stone, water, and living wood. The gardens they create are never finished, because the trees keep growing and the seasons keep turning. Every visit looks different from the last. That is something no painting can do. These gardens prove that art does not have to hang on a wall. Sometimes it grows under your feet, shifts with the wind, and hides one stone just to remind you that you can never quite see it all.

prompt._: Which detail does the author use to support the idea that Japanese gardens are never finished?

choices.text: A painter uses brushes and a sculptor uses chisels.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because comparing painters and sculptors introduces the topic but has nothing to do with why gardens are never finished.

choices.text: The trees keep growing and the seasons keep turning.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses 'because' right before this detail, making it the direct reason the gardens are never finished.

choices.text: The gardens prove that art does not hang on walls.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because this is the author's big conclusion about art in general, not a supporting detail about why gardens keep changing.

choices.text: A garden designer uses moss, stone, and living wood.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because listing the materials a garden designer uses tells us what gardens are made of, not why they are never finished.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is a major difference between a karesansui garden and the other Japanese gardens described?

choices.text: A karesansui garden changes colors with the seasons, while other gardens remain exactly the same forever.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it's the regular Japanese gardens that change with the seasons — the article describes cherry blossoms, summer leaves, fall maples, and winter snow in those gardens, not in karesansui gardens.

choices.text: A karesansui garden serves mostly as decoration, while other gardens provide a place for quiet meditation.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has it backwards — the article says karesansui gardens were created for meditation, not decoration.

choices.text: A karesansui garden includes straight lines and squares, while other gardens look completely wild and natural.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because straight lines and geometric shapes describe European gardens, not any type of Japanese garden.

choices.text: A karesansui garden contains only stones and gravel, while other gardens feature living plants and water.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article directly says karesansui gardens have no plants or water, while other Japanese gardens feature living things like moss, trees, ponds, and seasonal flowers.

prompt._: Which statement best describes how the author uses cause and effect in the article?

choices.text: The author describes how storms and wind cause trees to grow into graceful shapes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article explains that gardeners deliberately trim trees to look storm-shaped — the storms don't actually cause the shapes.

choices.text: The author explains how European gardens caused Japanese designers to copy symmetry.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says Japanese gardens reject European-style symmetry rather than copying it.

choices.text: The author shows how monks caused the gravel to turn into real flowing water.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the raked gravel only represents or suggests water — it never becomes real flowing water.

choices.text: The author shows how careful design choices cause visitors to feel peace and calm.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article repeatedly shows how purposeful design choices, like raked gravel and placed stones, cause visitors and monks to feel peaceful and calm.

prompt._: Which sentence best describes the main idea that the article builds from beginning to end?

choices.text: Japanese gardens are carefully designed art that changes over time.
choices.feedback: This is correct because every section of the article shows that Japanese gardens are carefully designed on purpose AND keep changing with seasons and growth.

choices.text: Japanese gardens use rocks and gravel instead of flowers and water.
choices.feedback: This is just one detail about dry rock gardens, not the big idea the whole article is building.

choices.text: Japanese gardens were created by monks for quiet meditation.
choices.feedback: Meditation is only talked about in one section, so it can't be the main idea that runs through the whole article.

choices.text: Japanese gardens copy the geometric patterns found in nature.
choices.feedback: This is actually the opposite of what the article says — Japanese gardens reject geometric patterns, while European gardens use them.

prompt._: Based on the article, how is the design of a bonsai tree similar to the design of a Japanese garden?

choices.text: Both are built entirely out of stone and raked white gravel.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because bonsai trees are living trees grown in small pots, and they have nothing to do with stone or raked gravel.

choices.text: Both are shaped by humans to look like wild, ancient nature.
choices.feedback: This is correct because both Japanese gardens and bonsai trees are carefully shaped by humans to look naturally wild and ancient, even though every detail is deliberately planned.

choices.text: Both are designed with straight lines to create perfect order.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article says Japanese gardens reject straight lines and geometric shapes — that style belongs to European gardens, not Japanese ones.

choices.text: Both are left completely untouched to grow without human help.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because both Japanese gardens and bonsai trees require years of careful human work — the article makes clear that every detail in both is deliberate, not left alone.

syllabus_metadata.title: Japanese Gardens: Art in Nature

assessments.title: Thomas Cole and the Hudson River School

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Wild Mountains on Canvas: Thomas Cole and the Birth of American Art

A Painter Climbs Into the Wilderness

A young man stands alone on a rocky ridge in the Catskill Mountains of New York. He pulls out a sketchbook and starts to draw. The year is 1825, and this man — Thomas Cole — is about to prove that America can make great art.

Back then, most Americans believed only Europe had paintings worth admiring. The masterpieces of Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Rembrandt hung in European museums. If you wanted to see serious art, you crossed the Atlantic Ocean. America had forests and rivers, sure — but no art tradition anyone took seriously.
stimulus.content_text: Wild Mountains on Canvas: Thomas Cole and the Birth of American ArtA Painter Climbs Into the Wilderness A young man stands alone on a rocky ridge in the Catskill Mountains of New York. He pulls out a sketchbook and starts to draw. The year is 1825, and this man — Thomas Cole — is about to prove that America can make great art. Back then, most Americans believed only Europe had paintings worth admiring. The masterpieces of Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Rembrandt hung in European museums. If you wanted to see serious art, you crossed the Atlantic Ocean. America had forests and rivers, sure — but no art tradition anyone took seriously.

prompt._: According to the passage, what contrast existed between Europe and America in 1825?

choices.text: Europe had famous paintings worth admiring, but America had no respected art tradition.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that people believed only Europe had paintings worth admiring, while America had no respected art tradition.

choices.text: Europe had beautiful natural scenery, but America had only cities and towns.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage talks about Europe having famous art, not beautiful natural scenery — it's actually America that had the forests and rivers.

choices.text: Europe had many young artists like Thomas Cole, but America had very few painters.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions how many painters or young artists lived in Europe compared to America.

choices.text: Europe had both art and nature, but America had only forests and rivers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only describes Europe as having great art, not both art and nature — nature is what America had.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Hudson River School — Not What You Think

Cole had moved from England to the United States as a teenager. He fell in love with the wild scenery of New York — the steep cliffs along the Hudson River, the thick forests of the Catskills. He hiked deep into these places, camped beside streams, and filled sketchbooks with what he saw. Then he returned to his studio and turned those sketches into enormous oil paintings.

His work attracted other painters who shared his vision. Together, they became known as the Hudson River School. It was simply a group of artists united by one idea: the American wilderness deserved to be celebrated on canvas. Think of it like a team — not a place, but people working toward the same goal.

Cole founded this movement in the 1820s, making it the first major art movement born in America. Long before modern art galleries filled New York City, these painters were already creating a powerful American art tradition.
stimulus.content_text: The Hudson River School — Not What You Think Cole had moved from England to the United States as a teenager. He fell in love with the wild scenery of New York — the steep cliffs along the Hudson River, the thick forests of the Catskills. He hiked deep into these places, camped beside streams, and filled sketchbooks with what he saw. Then he returned to his studio and turned those sketches into enormous oil paintings. His work attracted other painters who shared his vision. Together, they became known as the Hudson River School. It was simply a group of artists united by one idea: the American wilderness deserved to be celebrated on canvas. Think of it like a team — not a place, but people working toward the same goal. Cole founded this movement in the 1820s, making it the first major art movement born in America. Long before modern art galleries filled New York City, these painters were already creating a powerful American art tradition.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did other painters join Thomas Cole to form the Hudson River School?

choices.text: Because they shared his vision of celebrating the wilderness.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says the painters were 'united by one idea' — that the American wilderness deserved to be celebrated, which is exactly Cole's vision.

choices.text: Because they needed to move from England to the United States.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because moving from England is only mentioned about Thomas Cole, not about the other painters who joined the movement.

choices.text: Because they hoped to open modern art galleries in the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says modern art galleries came to New York long after the Hudson River School painters were already creating their work.

choices.text: Because they wanted to build a new art school in New York.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says the Hudson River School was not a place — it was a group of people working toward the same goal.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Paintings That Glowed Like Real Light

Cole's canvases glowed. He painted towering cliffs bathed in golden sunset light. He captured mist-filled valleys and pristine lakes — water so clean and untouched it looked like glass. Ancient trees stretched across his scenes, their branches untrimmed by any human hand. Pristine means completely pure and unspoiled, like a fresh snowfall before anyone walks through it.

His paintings showed the grandeur of America's wild places — their enormous, size and beauty. Grandeur is the feeling you get when you stand at the edge of the Grand Canyon or look up at a mountain so tall its peak disappears into clouds. Cole captured that feeling in paint.

When Americans saw his work, something shifted inside them. The wild mountains and rivers of their own country, they realized, were just as worthy of great art as the cathedrals and palaces of Europe.
stimulus.content_text: Paintings That Glowed Like Real Light Cole's canvases glowed. He painted towering cliffs bathed in golden sunset light. He captured mist-filled valleys and pristine lakes — water so clean and untouched it looked like glass. Ancient trees stretched across his scenes, their branches untrimmed by any human hand. Pristine means completely pure and unspoiled, like a fresh snowfall before anyone walks through it. His paintings showed the grandeur of America's wild places — their enormous, size and beauty. Grandeur is the feeling you get when you stand at the edge of the Grand Canyon or look up at a mountain so tall its peak disappears into clouds. Cole captured that feeling in paint. When Americans saw his work, something shifted inside them. The wild mountains and rivers of their own country, they realized, were just as worthy of great art as the cathedrals and palaces of Europe.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened when Americans saw Thomas Cole's work?

choices.text: They walked through a fresh snowfall.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because fresh snowfall is only mentioned in the passage as an example to help explain the meaning of the word 'pristine,' not as something Americans did.

choices.text: They felt something shift inside them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses the exact words 'something shifted inside them' to describe how Americans felt when they saw Cole's paintings.

choices.text: They painted towering cliffs at sunset.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Thomas Cole was the artist who painted towering cliffs — the passage never says Americans started painting them too.

choices.text: They traveled to see the palaces of Europe.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Americans felt proud of their own country's wild places, not that they traveled to see European palaces.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Art With a Warning Inside It

Actually, Cole used his art to send urgent messages. His most ambitious work is a series of five paintings called The Course of Empire. Each painting shows the exact same place, but at a different moment in time.

In the first painting, the land is wild and untouched. In the middle paintings, a great city rises on that same spot — marble columns, crowded harbors, golden statues. Then, in the final paintings, the city crumbles. Nature slowly takes the land back.

Cole was making an argument with paint instead of words. He warned that if people kept building without caring for the natural world, they would destroy the very beauty that made America special. His brush was doing the work of a speech.
stimulus.content_text: Art With a Warning Inside It Actually, Cole used his art to send urgent messages. His most ambitious work is a series of five paintings called The Course of Empire. Each painting shows the exact same place, but at a different moment in time. In the first painting, the land is wild and untouched. In the middle paintings, a great city rises on that same spot — marble columns, crowded harbors, golden statues. Then, in the final paintings, the city crumbles. Nature slowly takes the land back. Cole was making an argument with paint instead of words. He warned that if people kept building without caring for the natural world, they would destroy the very beauty that made America special. His brush was doing the work of a speech.

prompt._: In the passage, the author says Cole's brush was 'doing the work of a speech.' Based on this comparison, what does the phrase mean?

choices.text: Writing long words directly on the canvas.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cole didn't write words on his canvas — he painted pictures to share his message.

choices.text: Painting a portrait of a famous speaker.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cole wasn't painting a portrait of a speaker — he was painting landscapes that showed the rise and fall of a city.

choices.text: Sharing a strong message through his art.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that Cole used his paintings to warn people and make an argument, which is exactly what a speech does.

choices.text: Creating a beautiful but totally silent scene.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the whole point is that the paintings were NOT just silent and pretty — they carried a strong warning and argument inside them.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Movement Spreads Across the Country

Cole inspired a whole generation of painters to follow his path. Frederic Edwin Church traveled to South America and the Arctic, painting volcanoes and icebergs. Albert Bierstadt headed west to capture the towering peaks of the Rocky Mountains and the glowing valleys of Yosemite. Asher B. Durand painted quiet forest scenes closer to home.

The Hudson River School painters shared a deep belief. They saw untouched forests, rushing rivers, and silent mountains as sources of reverence — a feeling of deep respect and awe, like the hush that falls over you inside a place that feels sacred. They believed wild places could refresh a person's spirit the way sleep refreshes a tired body. Their paintings encouraged Americans to value and protect those places.
stimulus.content_text: A Movement Spreads Across the Country Cole inspired a whole generation of painters to follow his path. Frederic Edwin Church traveled to South America and the Arctic, painting volcanoes and icebergs. Albert Bierstadt headed west to capture the towering peaks of the Rocky Mountains and the glowing valleys of Yosemite. Asher B. Durand painted quiet forest scenes closer to home. The Hudson River School painters shared a deep belief. They saw untouched forests, rushing rivers, and silent mountains as sources of reverence — a feeling of deep respect and awe, like the hush that falls over you inside a place that feels sacred. They believed wild places could refresh a person's spirit the way sleep refreshes a tired body. Their paintings encouraged Americans to value and protect those places.

prompt._: According to the text, what does reverence mean?

choices.text: a plan to protect wild places
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because protecting wild places is what the painters hoped people would do, but that is not the definition of reverence.

choices.text: a wish to travel far away
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects reverence to a wish to travel — that's something the painters did, but it's not what the word means.

choices.text: a way to paint mountains and rivers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because painting mountains and rivers describes the artists' work, not the meaning of the word reverence.

choices.text: a feeling of deep respect and awe
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines reverence right in the text as 'a feeling of deep respect and awe,' which matches this answer exactly.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The Easel in the Mountains

Thomas Cole died in 1848 at just forty-seven years old. But the places he painted along the Hudson River and in the Catskill Mountains are now protected parks and historic sites. His paintings hang in major museums across the country.

Here is what matters most: the next time you visit a national park, or notice a "protected wilderness" sign along a trail, you are standing inside an idea that a painter helped plant. Cole didn't just put wild places on canvas. He convinced a young nation that those places were worth saving — not with laws or speeches, but with light, color, and the careful stroke of a brush.
stimulus.content_text: The Easel in the Mountains Thomas Cole died in 1848 at just forty-seven years old. But the places he painted along the Hudson River and in the Catskill Mountains are now protected parks and historic sites. His paintings hang in major museums across the country. Here is what matters most: the next time you visit a national park, or notice a "protected wilderness" sign along a trail, you are standing inside an idea that a painter helped plant. Cole didn't just put wild places on canvas. He convinced a young nation that those places were worth saving — not with laws or speeches, but with light, color, and the careful stroke of a brush.

prompt._: The author believes that Thomas Cole's paintings helped save wild places. Which detail from the text provides the best evidence for this idea?

choices.text: Thomas Cole died at just forty-seven years old.
choices.feedback: This tells us how old Cole was when he died, but it gives us no information about his paintings saving wild places.

choices.text: His paintings hang in major museums across the country.
choices.feedback: His paintings being in museums shows he was famous, but it doesn't tell us anything about protecting wild places.

choices.text: He put many wild places on his painting canvas.
choices.feedback: This only says Cole painted wild places, but it doesn't show that those places were actually protected or saved because of his paintings.

choices.text: The places he painted are now protected parks.
choices.feedback: This is correct because it directly shows that the wild places Cole painted are now protected, which is the best proof that his paintings helped save them.

prompt._: Which statement best shows a contrast between how people first viewed American wilderness and how they later viewed it after seeing Cole's paintings?

choices.text: At first, people saw wild places as famous artworks, but later they saw them as ordinary travel spots.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because wild places were never called famous artworks in the passage — this answer mixes up ideas that don't appear in the story at all.

choices.text: At first, people saw wild places as dangerous land, but later they saw them as places to build bigger cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says people thought wild places were dangerous, and Cole's warning was against building too much, not in favor of it.

choices.text: At first, people saw wild places as empty land, but later they saw them as places worth admiring and saving.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows people went from ignoring American wilderness as unimportant to seeing it as beautiful and worth saving, thanks to Cole's paintings.

choices.text: At first, people saw wild places as better than Europe, but later they saw them as less important than museums.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the story — Americans started by thinking Europe was better, and Cole's work made them value their own land more, not less.

prompt._: What was the main reason Thomas Cole's paintings helped protect wild places in America?

choices.text: They made people think the Hudson River School was an actual school building.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage explains that the Hudson River School was simply a group of painters who shared the same vision, not an actual school building.

choices.text: They made people believe Europe had the best artists and museums.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Cole's whole goal was the opposite — he wanted Americans to stop thinking only Europe had great art and to appreciate the beauty of their own country.

choices.text: They made people want to build more cities in the wilderness.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Cole's paintings warned people NOT to build cities in the wilderness — his 'Course of Empire' series showed how building without caring for nature leads to destruction.

choices.text: They made people see American nature as beautiful and worth saving.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Cole's paintings made Americans realize their wild places were worth celebrating and saving, and those places are now protected parks today.

prompt._: Which choice shows the order of events in Thomas Cole's story in the article?

choices.text: He moved to America, then painted wild places, then inspired other artists.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows Cole moved to America, then painted wild places, and then inspired other artists like Church and Bierstadt — in that exact order.

choices.text: He painted in museums, then explored the mountains, then started a group of artists.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cole explored mountains and sketched outdoors before creating his paintings — he never started by painting inside museums.

choices.text: He painted warning pictures, then moved from England, then inspired other artists.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cole moved from England to America first, and only later created his warning paintings like The Course of Empire.

choices.text: He inspired other artists, then painted wild places, then his paintings went to museums.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Cole inspired other artists while he was alive and working, not after his paintings ended up in museums.

prompt._: Which statement best compares what Americans believed about art before Cole's paintings with what they came to believe after seeing his work?

choices.text: They first thought art should copy European styles, then decided American art should look different.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Americans wanting to copy European styles or change how their art looked.

choices.text: They first ignored all paintings in museums, then started visiting parks to see real scenery.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Americans ignored museums or switched from paintings to visiting parks.

choices.text: They first thought only Europe had great art, then realized American wilderness deserved it too.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states Americans first believed only Europe had great art, but Cole's paintings made them see their own wilderness deserved it too.

choices.text: They first admired paintings of wild places, then preferred paintings that warned about cities.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Americans didn't start out loving wild-place paintings — appreciating those paintings was actually the new belief, not the old one.

assessments.title: Frederic Church: Painting the Sublime

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Frederic Church: The Painter Who Brought the World to America

A Painting That Roared

In 1857, a five-foot-wide painting hung in a New York gallery, and people paid money just to stand in front of it. The painting showed Niagara Falls — not from a safe distance on the shore, but from the very edge of the thundering water. Viewers felt the spray on their faces. They heard the roar in their imaginations. Some brought opera glasses — small binoculars used at theaters — to study every tiny detail of mist and foam. The artist behind this sensation was Frederic Edwin Church, and he painted the natural world like no one before him.
stimulus.content_text: Frederic Church: The Painter Who Brought the World to AmericaA Painting That Roared In 1857, a five-foot-wide painting hung in a New York gallery, and people paid money just to stand in front of it. The painting showed Niagara Falls — not from a safe distance on the shore, but from the very edge of the thundering water. Viewers felt the spray on their faces. They heard the roar in their imaginations. Some brought opera glasses — small binoculars used at theaters — to study every tiny detail of mist and foam. The artist behind this sensation was Frederic Edwin Church, and he painted the natural world like no one before him.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Church's painting of Niagara Falls different from a view from the shore?

choices.text: It showed the falls from high above the clouds.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the view was from the edge of the water, not from high above the clouds.

choices.text: It showed the falls from the very edge of the water.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the painting showed Niagara Falls 'from the very edge of the thundering water,' not from a safe distance on the shore.

choices.text: It showed the falls from a quiet place in the city.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a quiet city place — the painting showed the powerful, roaring falls up close.

choices.text: It showed the falls from far away across the river.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the painting showed the falls from very close up at the edge, which is the opposite of far away across a river.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Student of the Hudson River School

Church was born in Hartford, Connecticut, in 1826, and he showed artistic talent from a young age. At eighteen, he traveled to the Catskill Mountains to study with Thomas Cole. Cole was the founder of the Hudson River School, a group of American painters famous for capturing the beauty of wild places — towering mountains, deep forests, and winding rivers. Cole believed that American nature deserved the same respect that European artists gave to ancient ruins and cathedrals. He passed that belief on to his only formal student.

Church learned to love the American wilderness from Cole. But the two painters had very different styles. Cole's work was often moody and philosophical — meaning it explored deep ideas about life and time, the way a poem might make you think about something bigger than yourself. Church's paintings, by contrast, exploded with light, color, and sharp detail. Where Cole whispered, Church shouted.
stimulus.content_text: A Student of the Hudson River School Church was born in Hartford, Connecticut, in 1826, and he showed artistic talent from a young age. At eighteen, he traveled to the Catskill Mountains to study with Thomas Cole. Cole was the founder of the Hudson River School, a group of American painters famous for capturing the beauty of wild places — towering mountains, deep forests, and winding rivers. Cole believed that American nature deserved the same respect that European artists gave to ancient ruins and cathedrals. He passed that belief on to his only formal student. Church learned to love the American wilderness from Cole. But the two painters had very different styles. Cole's work was often moody and philosophical — meaning it explored deep ideas about life and time, the way a poem might make you think about something bigger than yourself. Church's paintings, by contrast, exploded with light, color, and sharp detail. Where Cole whispered, Church shouted.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Thomas Cole focus on painting wild American places?

choices.text: He felt American nature deserved the same respect as European ruins.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Cole believed American nature deserved the same respect as European ruins and cathedrals, which is exactly why he painted wild places.

choices.text: He wanted to use bright light and very sharp details in his artwork.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because using bright light and sharp details describes how Church painted, not Cole.

choices.text: He thought the deep forests looked exactly like the ancient cathedrals.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Cole never said forests looked like cathedrals — he only said both things deserved equal respect from artists.

choices.text: He needed a beautiful outdoor location to teach his new young students.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Cole picking outdoor locations as a place to teach his students.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Sketches First, Paintings Later

You might think that a painter stands in front of a waterfall or a volcano and paints the finished picture right there. Actually, Church worked in two stages. First, he traveled to wild and dramatic places. He hiked, climbed, and sketched everything he saw in small notebooks. Then he carried those sketchbooks home to his studio, where he spent weeks or months turning quick pencil studies into enormous, glowing oil paintings. The sketches were his notes. The studio was where the real magic happened.

And Church did not limit his travels to America. He was an adventurer. The German scientist Alexander von Humboldt had explored South America decades earlier and written about its wonders. Church read those writings and felt pulled south. He journeyed to Ecuador and Colombia, where he found erupting volcanoes, roaring waterfalls, thick tropical forests, and sunsets that blazed with color. It's easy to assume that a famous American painter only painted American scenes. But Church carried his brushes across the globe.

Actually, Church worked in two stages. First, he traveled to wild and dramatic places. He hiked, climbed, and sketched everything he saw in small notebooks. Then he carried those sketchbooks home to his studio, where he spent weeks or months turning quick pencil studies into enormous, glowing oil paintings. The sketches were his notes. The studio was where the real magic happened. And Church did not limit his travels to America. He was an adventurer. The German scientist Alexander von Humboldt had explored South America decades earlier and written about its wonders. Church read those writings and felt pulled south. He journeyed to Ecuador and Colombia, where he found erupting volcanoes, roaring waterfalls, thick tropical forests, and sunsets that blazed with color. It's easy to assume that a famous American painter only painted American scenes. But Church carried his brushes across the globe.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Church do right after he sketched in his notebooks during his travels?

choices.text: He traveled to Ecuador to find erupting volcanoes
choices.feedback: This is wrong because traveling to Ecuador was part of Church's adventures overall, not the specific next step after he finished sketching in his notebooks.

choices.text: He painted finished pictures at the waterfall
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says Church worked in two stages and did NOT paint finished pictures on location — that's actually what most people wrongly assume he did.

choices.text: He read the writings of Alexander von Humboldt
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Church read Humboldt's writings before he traveled south, not as a step that followed his sketching.

choices.text: He carried his sketchbooks home to his studio
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states that after sketching, Church 'carried those sketchbooks home to his studio' to create his big paintings.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
From Icebergs to Ancient Ruins

Church also sailed to the Arctic. There he painted icebergs glowing blue and white against dark, stormy skies. He traveled to the Middle East, where he captured the ancient carved city of Petra and the dry hills of the Holy Land. Everywhere he went, he searched for nature at its most powerful and strange. His finished paintings brought those faraway places back to American audiences who would never see them in person.

Church's largest works hung in darkened rooms with special lighting and heavy curtains, almost like a stage set for a play. People bought tickets to enter. The experience felt closer to attending a show than strolling through a gallery. Crowds lined up. Critics praised the work. Church's painting of Niagara became one of the most celebrated American artworks of the entire century.
stimulus.content_text: From Icebergs to Ancient Ruins Church also sailed to the Arctic. There he painted icebergs glowing blue and white against dark, stormy skies. He traveled to the Middle East, where he captured the ancient carved city of Petra and the dry hills of the Holy Land. Everywhere he went, he searched for nature at its most powerful and strange. His finished paintings brought those faraway places back to American audiences who would never see them in person. Church's largest works hung in darkened rooms with special lighting and heavy curtains, almost like a stage set for a play. People bought tickets to enter. The experience felt closer to attending a show than strolling through a gallery. Crowds lined up. Critics praised the work. Church's painting of Niagara became one of the most celebrated American artworks of the entire century.

prompt._: When the author compares Church's painting displays to 'a stage set for a play,' what does the phrase 'stage set' help the reader understand?

choices.text: that the paintings were fictional stories acted out by performers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the paintings were real artworks, not stories acted out by performers — no actors or fictional stories are mentioned in the passage.

choices.text: that the paintings were meant to be watched from far away
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't say anything about viewers needing to stand far away from the paintings to see them.

choices.text: that the displays were designed to create a dramatic experience
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the darkened rooms, special lighting, and curtains were all carefully set up to give viewers a dramatic, powerful experience, just like a stage set does for a play.

choices.text: that the rooms were built inside actual theater buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says the rooms were inside actual theater buildings — it only compares the feeling of the display to a theater experience.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Home That Was Also a Masterpiece

Later in life, Church built a spectacular home called Olana on a hilltop above the Hudson River in New York. He designed it himself, inspired by the architecture he had seen in the Middle East. The house features Persian-style arches — curved doorways and windows shaped like those found in ancient Iran — along with colorful tiles and wide views of the valley below. Church treated the house and the land around it as one more work of art. He planted trees and shaped paths so that every view from every window looked like a painting. Today, Olana is a museum and historic site. Visitors walk through the same rooms Church designed and look out at the same river valley he loved.

He designed it himself, inspired by the architecture he had seen in the Middle East. The house features Persian-style arches — curved doorways and windows shaped like those found in ancient Iran — along with colorful tiles and wide views of the valley below. Church treated the house and the land around it as one more work of art. He planted trees and shaped paths so that every view from every window looked like a painting. Today, Olana is a museum and historic site. Visitors walk through the same rooms Church designed and look out at the same river valley he loved.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the meaning of the phrase "Persian-style" arches?

choices.text: Historic rooms and halls designed to be a museum.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Olana being a museum with historic rooms describes what the house is like today, not the meaning of 'Persian-style arches.'

choices.text: Beautiful paths and trees shaped to look like paintings.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because planting trees and shaping paths describes how Church designed the land around Olana, not what Persian-style arches are.

choices.text: Curved doors and windows like those in ancient Iran.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly explains that Persian-style arches are 'curved doorways and windows shaped like those found in ancient Iran.'

choices.text: Colorful tiles and wide views of the valley below.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because colorful tiles and wide views are other features of Olana, not the meaning of 'Persian-style arches.'

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Windows to the World

Think about what Church gave his audiences. In the 1800s, there were no movies, no television, no internet. Most people would never see a volcano, an iceberg, or Niagara Falls in person. Church's enormous, glowing canvases were the closest thing to stepping through a window into another world. He did not just paint pictures. He built experiences — and he proved that an artist with a sketchbook and a bold imagination could carry the entire Earth into a single room.
stimulus.content_text: Windows to the World Think about what Church gave his audiences. In the 1800s, there were no movies, no television, no internet. Most people would never see a volcano, an iceberg, or Niagara Falls in person. Church's enormous, glowing canvases were the closest thing to stepping through a window into another world. He did not just paint pictures. He built experiences — and he proved that an artist with a sketchbook and a bold imagination could carry the entire Earth into a single room.

assessments.title: John James Audubon: Painting America's Birds

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Man Who Painted Every Bird in America

A Wild Turkey, Life-Sized

Picture a painting of a wild turkey so large it fills a sheet of paper three feet tall. Every feather is visible — the bronze shimmer on its chest, the dark bars on its wings, the red skin hanging from its throat. The bird looks ready to strut right off the page. No camera captured this image. In the early 1800s, cameras did not yet exist. Instead, a man named John James Audubon painted it by hand, using watercolors — pigments mixed with water that dry into soft, rich color — along with pastel chalks and graphite pencil. He did the same for 434 more species, or distinct types, of North American birds.
stimulus.content_text: The Man Who Painted Every Bird in AmericaA Wild Turkey, Life-Sized Picture a painting of a wild turkey so large it fills a sheet of paper three feet tall. Every feather is visible — the bronze shimmer on its chest, the dark bars on its wings, the red skin hanging from its throat. The bird looks ready to strut right off the page. No camera captured this image. In the early 1800s, cameras did not yet exist. Instead, a man named John James Audubon painted it by hand, using watercolors — pigments mixed with water that dry into soft, rich color — along with pastel chalks and graphite pencil. He did the same for 434 more species, or distinct types, of North American birds.

prompt._: According to the passage, how is the way Audubon created his bird images different from how images are captured today?

choices.text: He painted them by hand instead of using a camera
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Audubon 'painted it by hand' with watercolors, chalks, and pencil, while today we use cameras to capture images.

choices.text: He used a camera that was new in the 1800s
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that cameras did not yet exist in the early 1800s, so Audubon could not have used one.

choices.text: He copied pictures from other artists' paintings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions Audubon copying other artists — he created his own original paintings of birds.

choices.text: He sketched them from memory using only pencils
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Audubon used watercolors and pastel chalks in addition to graphite pencil, not just pencils alone.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From France to the American Wild

Audubon was born in 1785 in what is now Haiti and grew up in France. Even as a boy, he spent hours outdoors watching birds and sketching them. When he moved to the United States as a young man, the sheer variety of birds astonished him. Forests, wetlands, prairies, and coastlines were packed with hundreds of species that no one had ever accurately drawn. Audubon set himself an enormous goal: paint every single one.

That goal swallowed decades of his life. He traveled from the steamy swamps of Florida to the cold, rocky shores of Labrador, a remote region in northeastern Canada. He waded through marshes, paddled rivers, and hiked through thick forests. He watched how birds flew, what they ate, how they built nests, and how they treated each other. You might think he painted from memory back at a desk. Actually, Audubon worked directly from what he saw in the wild. Sometimes he used freshly collected birds, positioning them with thin wires so their bodies held lifelike poses. This let him capture exact details — the curve of a talon, the angle of a wing mid-flight — that no artist working from imagination could match.
stimulus.content_text: From France to the American Wild Audubon was born in 1785 in what is now Haiti and grew up in France. Even as a boy, he spent hours outdoors watching birds and sketching them. When he moved to the United States as a young man, the sheer variety of birds astonished him. Forests, wetlands, prairies, and coastlines were packed with hundreds of species that no one had ever accurately drawn. Audubon set himself an enormous goal: paint every single one. That goal swallowed decades of his life. He traveled from the steamy swamps of Florida to the cold, rocky shores of Labrador, a remote region in northeastern Canada. He waded through marshes, paddled rivers, and hiked through thick forests. He watched how birds flew, what they ate, how they built nests, and how they treated each other. You might think he painted from memory back at a desk. Actually, Audubon worked directly from what he saw in the wild. Sometimes he used freshly collected birds, positioning them with thin wires so their bodies held lifelike poses. This let him capture exact details — the curve of a talon, the angle of a wing mid-flight — that no artist working from imagination could match.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why did Audubon use thin wires with the birds?

choices.text: To show where the birds lived in nature
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the wires were used to pose the birds for painting, not to show information about where they lived in nature.

choices.text: To keep the birds from flying away
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the birds Audubon used with wires were freshly collected, meaning they were already not flying away.

choices.text: To make the birds hold lifelike poses
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says he used wires to position the birds so they held lifelike poses, helping him paint accurate details.

choices.text: To help the birds build strong nests
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions wires being used for nests — Audubon used the wires for painting, not for helping birds build anything.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Art That Moved and Breathed

Before Audubon, most bird illustrations looked stiff and flat. Earlier naturalists — people who study plants and animals through careful observation, like scientists with sketchbooks — drew birds standing rigidly from the side, as if pinned to a board. Audubon did something different. He showed a hawk diving toward its prey. He painted a heron standing in shallow water with a fish clamped in its beak. He placed a pair of cardinals among blossoms. His paintings crackled with energy and motion.

He also painted every bird at its actual size. A tiny warbler fit easily on a page, but a great blue heron stretched across an enormous sheet. This life-sized scale made viewers feel as if the real bird stood before them.

Here is what made Audubon's work truly unusual: it served two purposes at once. His paintings were beautiful enough to hang in an art gallery, yet accurate enough for scientists to identify species by their markings, proportions, and feather patterns. Most art does not double as science. Most science does not double as art. Audubon's work did both.

Audubon did something different. He showed a hawk diving toward its prey. He painted a heron standing in shallow water with a fish clamped in its beak. He placed a pair of cardinals among blossoms. His paintings crackled with energy and motion. He also painted every bird at its actual size. A tiny warbler fit easily on a page, but a great blue heron stretched across an enormous sheet. This life-sized scale made viewers feel as if the real bird stood before them. Here is what made Audubon's work truly unusual: it served two purposes at once. His paintings were beautiful enough to hang in an art gallery, yet accurate enough for scientists to identify species by their markings, proportions, and feather patterns. Most art does not double as science. Most science does not double as art. Audubon's work did both.

prompt._: According to the text, why did bird illustrations look stiff and flat before Audubon changed how they were painted?

choices.text: Artists placed the birds among bright and colorful blossoms.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because placing birds among blossoms was something Audubon did — it was part of his lively, artistic approach.

choices.text: Naturalists drew the birds standing rigidly from the side.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says earlier naturalists drew birds 'standing rigidly from the side, as if pinned to a board,' which is exactly why the illustrations looked stiff and flat.

choices.text: Painters shrank the birds to fit easily on a small page.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because shrinking birds to fit a page is the opposite of Audubon's method — he painted every bird at its true, life-sized scale.

choices.text: Scientists painted the birds flying quickly toward their prey.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because painting birds flying toward prey describes Audubon's exciting new style, not what artists did before him.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Printing the Impossible Book

Audubon's masterwork is called The Birds of America. It contains 435 hand-colored prints — images printed in black ink from engraved metal plates, then carefully painted by hand one by one. Each print measures roughly three feet by two feet.

No American publisher would take on such an expensive project. So Audubon sailed to England, where he found a printer willing to produce the giant plates. To raise money, he gave lectures and sold subscriptions — advance payments from buyers who agreed to receive the prints in batches over time, like signing up for a magazine before it arrives. Alongside the prints, Audubon wrote detailed descriptions of each bird's habits, habitat, and behavior. Scientists relied on these written observations for years.

Today, only about 120 complete copies of the original edition survive. A single set has sold at auction for more than eleven million dollars.

No American publisher would take on such an expensive project. So Audubon sailed to England, where he found a printer willing to produce the giant plates. To raise money, he gave lectures and sold subscriptions — advance payments from buyers who agreed to receive the prints in batches over time, like signing up for a magazine before it arrives. Alongside the prints, Audubon wrote detailed descriptions of each bird's habits, habitat, and behavior. Scientists relied on these written observations for years. Today, only about 120 complete copies of the original edition survive. A single set has sold at auction for more than eleven million dollars.

prompt._: The author compares subscriptions to signing up for a magazine. Based on this comparison, what does the word 'subscriptions' most likely mean?

choices.text: payments made before items are received
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says subscriptions are advance payments, meaning buyers paid before getting their prints, just like paying for a magazine before it arrives.

choices.text: payments made to cover printing supplies
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects the subscription payments to covering printing supplies.

choices.text: payments made after all items are delivered
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly says buyers paid in advance — before receiving the prints — not after.

choices.text: payments made to enter a drawing contest
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a contest or drawing of any kind.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Birds Still Need a Champion

Audubon died in 1851, but his name still flies. The National Audubon Society, a conservation organization dedicated to protecting nature, was founded in 1905 in his honor. It works to defend birds and their habitats across the Americas. His original paintings still hang in museums, and his prints appear in books, calendars, and living rooms around the world.

Next time you spot a cardinal on a branch or a hawk circling overhead, remember: there was a time when no one had bothered to paint that bird as it truly looked, alive and in motion. Audubon did — with watercolor, wire, and a stubbornness that lasted a lifetime. Every detailed feather in his work is a reminder that seeing something clearly is its own kind of discovery.

His original paintings still hang in museums, and his prints appear in books, calendars, and living rooms around the world. Next time you spot a cardinal on a branch or a hawk circling overhead, remember: there was a time when no one had bothered to paint that bird as it truly looked, alive and in motion. Audubon did — with watercolor, wire, and a stubbornness that lasted a lifetime. Every detailed feather in his work is a reminder that seeing something clearly is its own kind of discovery.

choices.text: a group that paints birds

choices.text: a group that studies museums
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions museums as places that display Audubon's paintings, not as something connected to what a conservation organization does.

choices.text: a group that sells calendars
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because calendars are mentioned as places where Audubon's prints appear, not as something related to the definition of a conservation organization.

prompt._: Based on the article, how did Audubon's experience in America contrast with his experience in England?

choices.text: America supplied the special watercolors, but England supplied the metal wires for posing.
choices.feedback: The article never says America supplied his watercolors or that England supplied the wires he used to pose birds — those details aren't connected to either country.

choices.text: America had the wild birds to paint, but England had the printer to publish his book.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly states Audubon painted birds across America, but had to sail to England to find a printer willing to publish his enormous book.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused Audubon's goal of painting every bird to take decades to complete?

choices.text: He had to wait for scientists to write the detailed descriptions of each bird.

choices.text: He had to observe hundreds of birds in the wild and raise money to print them.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly shows that observing hundreds of birds across many wild places AND raising money to print the book were both major challenges that took Audubon decades to overcome.

choices.text: He spent years trying to find an art gallery that would hang his giant artwork.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage says his paintings were gallery-worthy, it never says finding a gallery was a problem that delayed his work.

prompt._: Which sequence best describes the order of major steps Audubon followed to share his bird paintings with the world?

choices.text: He wrote descriptions of birds, then began painting them for the first time.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Audubon began by watching and painting birds, and his written descriptions came alongside the paintings, not before them.

choices.text: He painted birds in the wild, then traveled to England to have them printed.
choices.feedback: This is correct — Audubon painted birds in the wild first, then traveled to England to find a printer for his work.

choices.text: He sold subscriptions in America, then started observing birds in the wild.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Audubon spent decades observing birds in the wild first, and only sold subscriptions later to raise money for printing.

choices.text: He printed the book in America, then sailed to England to sell it.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says no American publisher would help, so Audubon sailed to England to print the book, not the other way around.

prompt._: Which comparison best supports the article's main idea about what made Audubon's project so unusual?

choices.text: It combined the protection of wild bird habitats with the collection of rare species, whereas most scientists only focused on writing.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because protecting habitats was the work of the National Audubon Society founded after his death, not what made Audubon's paintings unusual.

choices.text: It combined the beauty of fine art with the exact details needed for science, whereas most work only served one purpose.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that Audubon's work was both beautiful enough for art galleries and accurate enough for scientists, which the article calls 'truly unusual' — that's the main idea.

choices.text: It combined the use of metal printing plates with hand-painted watercolor paints, whereas most artists only used simple graphite pencils.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the printing and painting methods are small technical details, not the big reason the article says Audubon's project was so special.

choices.text: It combined the study of living American birds with his childhood memories of France, whereas most naturalists only studied local species.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Audubon combined memories of France with his American bird studies — that connection isn't in the article.

assessments.title: Drawing in Perspective: Making Flat Art Look Deep

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Trick That Makes Flat Paper Look Deep

A Hallway on Paper

Stand at one end of your school hallway and look straight down to the other end. The floor tiles seem to shrink. The walls appear to lean toward each other. The far door looks tiny compared to the one right next to you. Your brain reads all of these clues and tells you: that door is far away. The paper is as thin as a leaf. So how does a flat image trick your eyes into seeing distance?

Artists figured out the answer hundreds of years ago. During the 1400s in Florence, Italy, an architect named Filippo Brunelleschi studied buildings and roads to discover exact rules for showing depth on a flat surface. The technique is called perspective drawing — a method of using lines, sizes, and placement to create the illusion (a picture that fools your eyes into seeing something that isn't really there) of three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface.
stimulus.content_text: The Trick That Makes Flat Paper Look DeepA Hallway on Paper Stand at one end of your school hallway and look straight down to the other end. The floor tiles seem to shrink. The walls appear to lean toward each other. The far door looks tiny compared to the one right next to you. Your brain reads all of these clues and tells you: that door is far away. The paper is as thin as a leaf. So how does a flat image trick your eyes into seeing distance? Artists figured out the answer hundreds of years ago. During the 1400s in Florence, Italy, an architect named Filippo Brunelleschi studied buildings and roads to discover exact rules for showing depth on a flat surface. The technique is called perspective drawing — a method of using lines, sizes, and placement to create the illusion (a picture that fools your eyes into seeing something that isn't really there) of three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface.

prompt._: According to the text, how does the far door look compared to the door right next to you?

choices.text: It looks the same.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that the doors look very different in size, not the same.

choices.text: It looks much wider.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the far door looking wider — it says it looks tiny, not wide.

choices.text: It looks very tiny.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states, 'The far door looks tiny compared to the one right next to you.'

choices.text: It looks much taller.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about the far door appearing taller than the nearby door.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
One Point, One Trick

The simplest version is one-point perspective. Picture a straight road stretching away from you into the distance. The left edge angles to the right. The right edge angles to the left. Both edges aim toward a single spot far away. That spot is called the vanishing point — an imaginary helper dot that artists place on their paper to guide where lines should go. It is not a real object floating in the air. You will never walk up and touch it. It is simply a tool, like a ruler or a compass, that keeps your drawing organized.

The vanishing point always sits on the horizon line, an imaginary straight line drawn across the page at the viewer's eye level. Think of it as the place where the ground meets the sky when you look straight ahead.

Now imagine fence posts lining that road. The posts closest to you are tall. The ones farther away are shorter. The spaces between them squeeze together. Each post recedes — moves back, like a person walking away from you and getting smaller with every step. This steady shrinking is what makes the drawing feel deep and real.
