The right edge angles to the left. Both edges aim toward a single spot far away. That spot is called the vanishing point — an imaginary helper dot that artists place on their paper to guide where lines should go. It is not a real object floating in the air. You will never walk up and touch it. It is simply a tool, like a ruler or a compass, that keeps your drawing organized. The vanishing point always sits on the horizon line, an imaginary straight line drawn across the page at the viewer's eye level. Think of it as the place where the ground meets the sky when you look straight ahead. Now imagine fence posts lining that road. The posts closest to you are tall. The ones farther away are shorter. The spaces between them squeeze together. Each post recedes — moves back, like a person walking away from you and getting smaller with every step. This steady shrinking is what makes the drawing feel deep and real.

prompt._: The main idea of this section is that artists use specific tricks to make flat drawings look deep. How does the example of the shrinking fence posts support this main idea?

choices.text: It explains why artists must draw a straight road.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the fence post example is about showing depth, not about explaining why artists have to draw a road.

choices.text: It proves that the vanishing point is a real object.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states the vanishing point is NOT a real object, so the fence posts cannot prove the opposite.

choices.text: It describes how a ruler creates the horizon line.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects rulers to the horizon line — the horizon line is described as imaginary, not drawn with a ruler.

choices.text: It shows what causes the picture to feel deep.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses the shrinking fence posts as a direct example of what creates the feeling of depth in a flat drawing.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Just "Higher Up"

That helps a little, but it is only part of the trick. If you draw a tree near the top of your paper but keep it the same size as a tree at the bottom, the picture will look strange and flat. Perspective requires two things working together: objects get smaller as they move toward the vanishing point, and the lines along edges angle inward toward that same point. Size and direction both matter.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Just "Higher Up" That helps a little, but it is only part of the trick. If you draw a tree near the top of your paper but keep it the same size as a tree at the bottom, the picture will look strange and flat. Perspective requires two things working together: objects get smaller as they move toward the vanishing point, and the lines along edges angle inward toward that same point. Size and direction both matter.

prompt._: According to the text, what comes after drawing an object higher on the paper to help show perspective?

choices.text: Moving the object to the bottom
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because moving an object to the bottom would actually make it look closer, not farther away, which is the wrong direction for perspective.

choices.text: Making the object smaller
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that objects must get smaller as they move toward the vanishing point, working together with their position on the paper.

choices.text: Coloring the object more brightly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions color or brightness as part of creating perspective — it only talks about size and the direction of lines.

choices.text: Keeping the object the same size
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says that keeping an object the same size makes the picture look strange and flat — the opposite of good perspective.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Two Vanishing Points, Two Walls

Two-point perspective uses two vanishing points instead of one. Both sit on the horizon line, but they are spread far apart — one to the left and one to the right. This method is perfect for drawing a building seen from its corner. Imagine standing at the corner of a brick school. One wall stretches away to your left. The other wall stretches away to your right. The lines along each wall aim toward different vanishing points. The result is a realistic view that shows two sides of the building at once.

To try it yourself, draw a horizon line, mark two dots near the edges, and draw a vertical line between them for the building's corner. Connect the top and bottom of that line to each vanishing point, and you will see the walls angle away just like the real building does.
stimulus.content_text: Two Vanishing Points, Two Walls Two-point perspective uses two vanishing points instead of one. Both sit on the horizon line, but they are spread far apart — one to the left and one to the right. This method is perfect for drawing a building seen from its corner. Imagine standing at the corner of a brick school. One wall stretches away to your left. The other wall stretches away to your right. The lines along each wall aim toward different vanishing points. The result is a realistic view that shows two sides of the building at once. To try it yourself, draw a horizon line, mark two dots near the edges, and draw a vertical line between them for the building's corner. Connect the top and bottom of that line to each vanishing point, and you will see the walls angle away just like the real building does.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is drawing the left wall similar to drawing the right wall?

choices.text: Both require connecting the corner line to a vanishing point.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says to connect the top and bottom of the corner line to each vanishing point, which is how both the left wall and the right wall are drawn.

choices.text: Both require placing a new dot in the middle of the horizon line.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only tells you to mark two dots near the edges of the horizon line, not to add a new dot in the middle.

choices.text: Both require aiming the lines toward the exact same vanishing point.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that each wall aims toward a different vanishing point — that's the whole point of two-point perspective.

choices.text: Both require drawing a vertical line across the horizon line.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the vertical line represents the building's corner and is drawn between the two vanishing points, not across the horizon line.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Moving the Horizon Changes Everything

Actually, it moves depending on where your eyes are. If you are standing on a sidewalk, the horizon line sits roughly in the middle. If you are lying on the grass looking up at a skyscraper, the horizon line drops near the bottom, and the building towers above you. If you are on a rooftop looking down, the horizon line rises near the top, and the street spreads out below. Moving that single line changes the entire mood of your drawing.
stimulus.content_text: Moving the Horizon Changes Everything Actually, it moves depending on where your eyes are. If you are standing on a sidewalk, the horizon line sits roughly in the middle. If you are lying on the grass looking up at a skyscraper, the horizon line drops near the bottom, and the building towers above you. If you are on a rooftop looking down, the horizon line rises near the top, and the street spreads out below. Moving that single line changes the entire mood of your drawing.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the horizon line?

choices.text: a special tool used for drawing buildings
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the horizon line is not a physical tool you use to draw — it is a line that appears in your drawing based on your eye level.

choices.text: a line that always stays in the middle
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows the horizon line can be at the middle, bottom, or top depending on where you are standing or lying.

choices.text: a line that sits where your eyes are looking
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the horizon line 'moves depending on where your eyes are,' meaning it sits at your eye level.

choices.text: a type of shading that adds depth to art
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions shading — the horizon line is about eye level, not adding shadows or depth through shading.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Lines That Pull You In

Start with a simple exercise. Draw a horizon line. Place one vanishing point on it. Then draw lines radiating (spreading outward like the spokes of a bicycle wheel) from that point. Add a road between two of those lines. Sketch a row of houses along the road, making each one a bit smaller as it nears the vanishing point. Within minutes, your flat sheet of paper will feel like a window you could climb through.

The same rules Brunelleschi figured out in Florence over six hundred years ago are still at work today — on your screen, in your favorite books, and now on your own drawing paper.

Then draw lines radiating (spreading outward like the spokes of a bicycle wheel) from that point. Add a road between two of those lines. choices.text: By stating that they spread outward like bicycle spokes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because bicycle spokes are used to explain what 'radiating' means, not to show that Brunelleschi's rules are still being used.

choices.text: They both move objects closer to the viewer's eyes.

syllabus_metadata.title: Drawing in Perspective: Making Flat Art Look Deep

assessments.title: Proportion, Shading, and Form

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Making Flat Drawings Look Real: Proportion and Shading

A Head Too Big

A fifth grader once drew a portrait of her dad — careful eyes, detailed nose, perfect smile. But the whole class laughed when she held it up. His head was the size of a watermelon on a stick-figure body. That "something off" feeling has a name: proportion.
stimulus.content_text: Making Flat Drawings Look Real: Proportion and ShadingA Head Too Big A fifth grader once drew a portrait of her dad — careful eyes, detailed nose, perfect smile. But the whole class laughed when she held it up. His head was the size of a watermelon on a stick-figure body. That "something off" feeling has a name: proportion.

prompt._: According to the text, what detail contrasts with the portrait's careful eyes and perfect smile?

choices.text: A perfectly drawn head on a stick-figure body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the head was not perfectly drawn — it was far too large, which is the whole problem the passage describes.

choices.text: A watermelon-sized nose on a perfectly drawn face.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not say anything strange about the nose; the oversized head was the only thing that looked wrong.

choices.text: A watermelon-sized head on a stick-figure body.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the head was 'the size of a watermelon on a stick-figure body,' which is the detail that stood out against the careful eyes and perfect smile.

choices.text: A stick-figure head on a watermelon-sized body.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the details — the passage says the head was watermelon-sized and the body was stick-figure, not the other way around.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Proportion: How Parts Compare

Proportion is the size relationship between different parts of a subject. Think of it like a recipe. A cookie needs the right amount of flour compared to sugar. Dump in five cups of flour and a pinch of sugar, and the cookie won't taste right. In drawing, if the head is huge and the legs are tiny, the figure won't look right either.

Your head is smaller than your torso. Proportion is about getting those differences correct.

For centuries, artists have used the head as a measuring tool for drawing people. The average human body stands about seven to eight heads tall. Picture stacking seven or eight copies of someone's head from their feet to the top of their skull. That's how tall the whole figure should be. The shoulders stretch about two to three head-widths across. The arms, hanging at the sides, reach down to about mid-thigh.

A head too large turns a person into a cartoon. Arms too short make the figure look stiff and strange. However, when you measure each part against the others and check those size relationships, your drawings start to look balanced and believable.
stimulus.content_text: Proportion: How Parts Compare Proportion is the size relationship between different parts of a subject. Think of it like a recipe. A cookie needs the right amount of flour compared to sugar. Dump in five cups of flour and a pinch of sugar, and the cookie won't taste right. In drawing, if the head is huge and the legs are tiny, the figure won't look right either. Your head is smaller than your torso. Proportion is about getting those differences correct. For centuries, artists have used the head as a measuring tool for drawing people. The average human body stands about seven to eight heads tall. Picture stacking seven or eight copies of someone's head from their feet to the top of their skull. That's how tall the whole figure should be. The shoulders stretch about two to three head-widths across. The arms, hanging at the sides, reach down to about mid-thigh. A head too large turns a person into a cartoon. Arms too short make the figure look stiff and strange. However, when you measure each part against the others and check those size relationships, your drawings start to look balanced and believable.

prompt._: According to the section, what happens to a drawn figure when the arms are made too short compared to the rest of the body?

choices.text: The figure appears too tall and thin
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions a figure looking too tall or thin when the arms are too short.

choices.text: The figure looks stiff and strange
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states 'Arms too short make the figure look stiff and strange.'

choices.text: The figure turns into a cartoon character
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a head that is too large makes a figure look like a cartoon — not arms that are too short.

choices.text: The figure loses its shading and form
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not talk about shading or form at all — that idea comes from somewhere else, not this passage.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Shading: Turning Flat into Solid

Now imagine you've drawn a circle. It sits flat on the page like a coin. But add shading, and that circle becomes a ball you could almost roll off the table. The same trick turns a flat rectangle into a solid block.

Shading is the technique of drawing areas of light and dark to make flat shapes look three-dimensional — meaning they appear to have depth, height, and width, like real objects you can hold. The direction and intensity of a light source determine where highlights and shadows appear. For example, when light hits a ball from the upper left, the upper-left surface glows bright. That bright spot is called the highlight. The area on the opposite side becomes the darkest zone, known as the core shadow — the deepest band of darkness on the object itself.

Between the highlight and the core shadow, the tone shifts gradually from light to dark. Many students make a mistake here. It's easy to assume shading means filling in one area with a single dark tone, like coloring inside the lines with one shade of gray. But real shadows don't work that way. They fade smoothly, the way the sky shifts from bright blue near the sun to deeper blue overhead. Your shading should fade, too. In addition, shadows are almost never pure black — pressing your pencil as hard as you can usually looks unnatural. Sometimes light bounces off a nearby surface and creates a thin strip of reflected light along the dark edge. That faint glow makes the object look truly solid.
stimulus.content_text: Shading: Turning Flat into Solid Now imagine you've drawn a circle. It sits flat on the page like a coin. But add shading, and that circle becomes a ball you could almost roll off the table. The same trick turns a flat rectangle into a solid block. Shading is the technique of drawing areas of light and dark to make flat shapes look three-dimensional — meaning they appear to have depth, height, and width, like real objects you can hold. The direction and intensity of a light source determine where highlights and shadows appear. For example, when light hits a ball from the upper left, the upper-left surface glows bright. That bright spot is called the highlight. The area on the opposite side becomes the darkest zone, known as the core shadow — the deepest band of darkness on the object itself. Between the highlight and the core shadow, the tone shifts gradually from light to dark. Many students make a mistake here. It's easy to assume shading means filling in one area with a single dark tone, like coloring inside the lines with one shade of gray. But real shadows don't work that way. They fade smoothly, the way the sky shifts from bright blue near the sun to deeper blue overhead. Your shading should fade, too. In addition, shadows are almost never pure black — pressing your pencil as hard as you can usually looks unnatural. Sometimes light bounces off a nearby surface and creates a thin strip of reflected light along the dark edge. That faint glow makes the object look truly solid.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens on a ball after the highlight appears but before the core shadow?

choices.text: The tone shifts gradually from light to dark
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage says 'the tone shifts gradually from light to dark' in the area between the highlight and the core shadow.

choices.text: The entire surface turns one shade of gray
choices.feedback: The passage says filling in one area with a single dark tone is actually a common mistake, because real shadows fade smoothly instead.

choices.text: A strip of reflected light shows up along the edge
choices.feedback: Reflected light appears along the dark edge of the object, not in the middle zone between the highlight and core shadow.

choices.text: The highlight moves to the opposite side
choices.feedback: The highlight stays where the light hits the object; it does not move to the opposite side, which is where the core shadow forms.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Three Ways to Shade

Hatching means drawing parallel lines close together, like the strings on a harp. The closer you pack the lines, the darker that area looks. Cross-hatching layers one set of lines over another in a different direction — imagine a window screen where horizontal and vertical wires overlap. Those extra layers create even darker values, which is the art word for how light or dark a tone is.

Blending uses a soft tool — your fingertip, a tissue, or a rolled paper tool called a blending stump — to smear pencil marks together. This creates smooth gradients, meaning gentle shifts from light to dark with no visible lines, like the shadow under your chin right now. Similarly, try squinting at an object near a lamp — squinting helps you see big shapes of light and dark without getting distracted by details.
stimulus.content_text: Three Ways to Shade Hatching means drawing parallel lines close together, like the strings on a harp. The closer you pack the lines, the darker that area looks. Cross-hatching layers one set of lines over another in a different direction — imagine a window screen where horizontal and vertical wires overlap. Those extra layers create even darker values, which is the art word for how light or dark a tone is. Blending uses a soft tool — your fingertip, a tissue, or a rolled paper tool called a blending stump — to smear pencil marks together. This creates smooth gradients, meaning gentle shifts from light to dark with no visible lines, like the shadow under your chin right now. Similarly, try squinting at an object near a lamp — squinting helps you see big shapes of light and dark without getting distracted by details.

prompt._: Based on the passage, how is cross-hatching similar to a window screen?

choices.text: Both use soft tools to smear pencil marks smoothly together.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because using soft tools to smear pencil marks together describes blending, a completely different shading technique.

choices.text: Both feature parallel strings that never touch each other.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because parallel lines that never touch describes hatching, and a window screen actually has wires that cross and overlap.

choices.text: Both have lines or wires crossing in different directions.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly compares cross-hatching to a window screen, explaining that both have lines or wires that overlap in different directions.

choices.text: Both create gentle shifts from light to dark with no lines.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because gentle shifts from light to dark with no visible lines describes gradients, which are created by blending, not cross-hatching.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
When Proportion Meets Shading

Proportion and shading are partners. A face drawn with correct proportions already looks believable. Add carefully shaded cheekbones, a nose casting a small shadow, and a chin catching the light, and that face looks like it could blink. A still life — a drawing of everyday objects arranged together, like fruit on a table — therefore looks so solid you want to reach in and grab the apple.

Leonardo da Vinci practiced these same two skills over five hundred years ago, filling notebooks with studies of light on faces and measurements of the human body. Today's comic book artists, movie concept designers, and medical illustrators still rely on them every single day. They are the language your pencil uses to tell someone's eyes, "This is real."
stimulus.content_text: When Proportion Meets Shading Proportion and shading are partners. A face drawn with correct proportions already looks believable. Add carefully shaded cheekbones, a nose casting a small shadow, and a chin catching the light, and that face looks like it could blink. A still life — a drawing of everyday objects arranged together, like fruit on a table — therefore looks so solid you want to reach in and grab the apple. Leonardo da Vinci practiced these same two skills over five hundred years ago, filling notebooks with studies of light on faces and measurements of the human body. Today's comic book artists, movie concept designers, and medical illustrators still rely on them every single day. They are the language your pencil uses to tell someone's eyes, "This is real."

choices.text: a drawing of people moving quickly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says a still life shows people moving — in fact, 'still' means not moving at all!

choices.text: a drawing of light shining on faces
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because light shining on faces is about shading, which is a different drawing skill mentioned in the passage.

choices.text: a drawing of body measurements in notebooks
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because body measurements in notebooks describes Leonardo da Vinci's studies, not the definition of a still life.

choices.text: a drawing of everyday objects arranged together
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines a still life as 'a drawing of everyday objects arranged together, like fruit on a table.'

prompt._: Which choice best shows the contrast between proportion and shading in the article?

choices.text: Proportion fixes size mistakes, while shading adds light and dark.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly says proportion is about size relationships between parts, while shading is about using light and dark to make shapes look real.

choices.text: Proportion uses crossed lines, while shading measures body parts.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because crossed lines describe cross-hatching, which is a shading method, not something proportion does.

choices.text: Proportion makes shadows darker, while shading widens the shoulders.

choices.text: Proportion blends pencil marks, while shading stacks heads for height.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the two ideas — blending belongs to shading, and stacking heads belongs to proportion.

prompt._: Which statement best describes how the author uses cause and effect throughout the article?

choices.text: By showing how fixing sizes and adding shadows causes flat shapes to look like solid, real objects.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the entire article shows that fixing size relationships (proportion) and adding light and dark areas (shading) causes flat drawings to look like solid, believable, real objects.

choices.text: By showing how mixing the right amounts of flour and sugar causes a drawing to look perfectly balanced.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the flour and sugar example is just a helpful comparison to explain what proportion means — it's not a cause-and-effect relationship about drawing.

prompt._: Which choice best summarizes the article by showing the order of ideas from start to finish?

choices.text: Artists first press hard for dark shadows, next add reflected light, and then fix body measurements.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article actually warns students NOT to press hard for dark shadows, calling it unnatural.

choices.text: Artists begin with hatching and blending, then measure body parts, and last draw still lifes.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because hatching and blending are taught in the middle of the article, not at the beginning.

choices.text: Artists first check size relationships, next add light and dark, and then make drawings look real.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches the article's order: proportion first, then shading, then how both skills make drawings look real.

choices.text: Artists begin by drawing comic books, then study Leonardo, and last learn what proportion means.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article begins with proportion and a story about a student's drawing, not with comic books or Leonardo da Vinci.

prompt._: Based on the article, how are a drawing with a head that is too large and a drawing with pure black shadows similar?

assessments.title: Pitch and Rhythm: The Building Blocks of Music

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Elements of Music: The Building Blocks of Every Song

A Whistle, a Rumble, and Everything Between

A tiny bird outside your window trills a bright, sharp note. Down the street, a truck engine growls low and heavy. Your doorbell rings two clear tones. Every one of these sounds has something in common with your favorite song — they all have pitch.

Pitch is how high or low a sound is. A whistle has a high pitch. Thunder has a low pitch. You might think that a high-pitched sound is the same as a loud sound, but those are actually two different things. Pitch tells you how high or low a sound is. Volume tells you how loud or soft it is. A flute can play a high note very softly, like a whisper. A tuba can blast a low note so loud it shakes the floor. Pitch and volume work separately.

So what makes one pitch higher than another? Every sound starts with vibrations — the fast back-and-forth shaking of an object, like a guitar string buzzing after you pluck it. Faster vibrations create higher pitches. Slower vibrations create lower pitches. Think of it like a jump rope. Spin the rope quickly, and it hums at a high pitch. Spin it slowly, and the sound drops lower. A small flute pushes air into fast vibrations and produces high-pitched sounds. A large tuba moves air more slowly and produces deep, low-pitched sounds.

You use pitch every day without thinking about it.

Spin it slowly, and the sound drops lower. A small flute pushes air into fast vibrations and produces high-pitched sounds. A large tuba moves air more slowly and produces deep, low-pitched sounds. You use pitch every day without thinking about it.

prompt._: According to the text, which statement shows the contrast between pitch and volume?

choices.text: Pitch and volume both tell how high or low a sound is.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pitch and volume do NOT both tell how high or low a sound is — that is only what pitch does.

choices.text: Pitch tells how high or low a sound is, while volume tells how loud or soft it is.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it matches exactly what the passage says: pitch = high or low, and volume = loud or soft.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it mixes up the definitions — pitch actually tells how high or low a sound is, and volume tells how loud or soft it is, not the other way around.

choices.text: Pitch and volume both tell how loud or soft a sound is.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because pitch and volume do NOT both tell how loud or soft a sound is — that is only what volume does.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Pitches in a Row: How Melody Works

When a composer — a person who writes music — arranges a series of pitches one after another, the result is a melody. A melody is a string of pitches set in a pattern that your ear recognizes as a tune. Think of the opening of "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star." Those notes step up and down in a specific order. That order is the melody. It's the part of a song you hum in the shower or sing along with in the car.

That's where the second building block comes in.

A melody is a string of pitches set in a pattern that your ear recognizes as a tune. Think of the opening of "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star." Those notes step up and down in a specific order. That order is the melody. It's the part of a song you hum in the shower or sing along with in the car. That's where the second building block comes in.

prompt._: Based on the passage, why does a melody sound like a tune you can recognize?

choices.text: because the pitches are arranged in a specific order
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says a melody is pitches arranged in a specific order or pattern, which is exactly what makes it a tune your ear can recognize.

choices.text: because the pitches are repeated over and over again
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage talks about pitches being in a specific order, not about them being repeated over and over again.

choices.text: because the pitches are sung by a composer
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says a composer writes the music, but it doesn't say the composer has to sing it — a melody can be played or sung by anyone.

choices.text: because the pitches are played at different volumes
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions volume or how loud or soft the pitches are — that has nothing to do with why a melody sounds like a recognizable tune.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Pulse and the Pattern: Rhythm and Beat

Put your hand on your chest. Feel your heartbeat? That steady thump-thump-thump never speeds up or slows down on its own. In music, this steady pulse is called the beat. It's the constant tick that keeps the music moving forward, like a clock that never stops.

Now here's where many people get confused. The beat and the rhythm are not the same thing. Rhythm is the pattern of long and short sounds — plus silences — layered on top of the beat. Imagine the beat as a sidewalk made of evenly spaced squares. Rhythm is the way you walk on that sidewalk. You might take two quick steps on one square and one long step across the next. The sidewalk stays even, but your footsteps create a pattern. That pattern is rhythm.

Try this: clap a steady beat to "Happy Birthday." Now sing the song and clap only when a word lands.

Rhythm gives music its energy and personality. A waltz flows in a graceful one-two-three pattern. A march pounds a strong one-two, one-two beat. Jazz often swings with off-beat rhythms that make your foot tap in unexpected places. Measures — small groups of beats, like paragraphs in a story — help organize rhythm into sections that musicians can follow.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Pitch and Rhythm on Paper

Musicians read pitch and rhythm through a system called notation — written symbols that work like a recipe for sound. The main tool is the staff, a set of five horizontal lines with four spaces between them. Each line and space stands for a different pitch. Notes placed higher on the staff sound higher. Notes placed lower sound lower.

The shape of each note tells the musician how long to hold it. A whole note, an open circle, lasts the longest. A half note lasts half as long. A quarter note lasts half as long again. Small symbols called rests tell the musician when to stay silent. Silence matters in music just as much as sound — it creates space, surprise, and breathing room.

Notes placed lower sound lower. The shape of each note tells the musician how long to hold it. A whole note, an open circle, lasts the longest. A half note lasts half as long. A quarter note lasts half as long again. Small symbols called rests tell the musician when to stay silent. Silence matters in music just as much as sound — it creates space, surprise, and breathing room.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because rests show when to be silent, but that is not what connects notation to a recipe.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because open circles are whole notes in music notation, and recipes do not use open circles at all.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Two Building Blocks, Infinite Songs

The rhythm tells you when to sing them and how long to hold each one. Change the rhythm of a melody you know, and the song can sound completely different — even though every note stays the same.

A symphony orchestra, a pop song on the radio, a bird calling from a branch — they all run on pitch and rhythm. Once you can hear those two elements at work, you stop just listening to music. You start understanding it, the way a mechanic hears an engine and knows exactly what each part is doing.

A symphony orchestra, a pop song on the radio, a bird calling from a branch — they all run on pitch and rhythm. Once you can hear those two elements at work, you stop just listening to music. You start understanding it, the way a mechanic hears an engine and knows exactly what each part is doing.

assessments.title: Dynamics and Timbre: Color and Expression

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Dynamics and Timbre: The Secret Powers Behind Every Sound

A Whisper That Stopped a Concert Hall

Two thousand people sat frozen in their seats. The entire orchestra had just been thundering — drums crashing, trumpets blaring, every instrument at full blast. Then the music dropped to almost nothing. A lone flute played a melody so quiet that audience members held their breath. That whisper-soft moment hit harder than all the noise before it. The secret behind that power? Two elements of music called dynamics and timbre.
stimulus.content_text: Dynamics and Timbre: The Secret Powers Behind Every SoundA Whisper That Stopped a Concert Hall Two thousand people sat frozen in their seats. The entire orchestra had just been thundering — drums crashing, trumpets blaring, every instrument at full blast. Then the music dropped to almost nothing. A lone flute played a melody so quiet that audience members held their breath. That whisper-soft moment hit harder than all the noise before it. The secret behind that power? Two elements of music called dynamics and timbre.

prompt._: According to the passage, what contrast does the author describe between the orchestra and the lone flute?

choices.text: The orchestra played quickly while the flute played slowly
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions how fast or slow either the orchestra or the flute played.

choices.text: The orchestra played loudly while the flute played softly
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage uses words like 'thundering' and 'full blast' for the orchestra, and 'almost nothing' and 'whisper-soft' for the flute, showing a loud versus soft contrast.

choices.text: The orchestra played deeply while the flute played sharply
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never describes the orchestra's sound as 'deep' or the flute's sound as 'sharp.'

choices.text: The orchestra played briefly while the flute played longer
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage does not compare how long the orchestra played versus how long the flute played.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Dynamics: More Than Just Loud and Soft

Dynamics means how loud or soft music is played. Think of dynamics as a volume dial that the composer controls. But dynamics is not just "loud" or "soft" like a light switch flipped on or off. It includes every shade of volume in between, plus the gradual changes from one level to another.

Composers write dynamic markings using Italian words — because many rules for writing music were developed in Italy hundreds of years ago. Pianissimo, written as pp, means very soft. Piano, written as p, means soft. Mezzo piano means moderately soft, and mezzo forte means moderately loud. Forte, written as f, means loud. Fortissimo, written as ff, means very loud. These markings appear right on the sheet music, telling performers exactly how much sound to produce at each moment.

You might think dynamics just means picking a volume and staying there. Actually, some of the most exciting dynamic markings describe movement. A crescendo — a gradual increase in volume, like a wave building toward shore — tells musicians to get louder bit by bit. A decrescendo, also called a diminuendo, means gradually getting softer, like that wave pulling back out to sea.

Dynamics shape the emotions in music. A lullaby stays gentle and soft, wrapping you in comfort. A battle march booms with energy and power. And here is the surprising part: soft music can be just as thrilling as loud music. A sudden drop from fortissimo to pianissimo can shock an audience like a plot twist in your favorite book. Softness creates suspense. It pulls you closer. It makes you listen harder.

But dynamics is not just "loud" or "soft" like a light switch flipped on or off. It includes every shade of volume in between, plus the gradual changes from one level to another. Piano, written as p, means soft. Mezzo piano means moderately soft, and mezzo forte means moderately loud. Forte, written as f, means loud. Fortissimo, written as ff, means very loud. A decrescendo, also called a diminuendo, means gradually getting softer, like that wave pulling back out to sea. Dynamics shape the emotions in music. A lullaby stays gentle and soft, wrapping you in comfort. A battle march booms with energy and power. And here is the surprising part: soft music can be just as thrilling as loud music. A sudden drop from fortissimo to pianissimo can shock an audience like a plot twist in your favorite book. Softness creates suspense. It pulls you closer. It makes you listen harder.

prompt._: Based on the passage, what is the main reason dynamics are important in music?

choices.text: Because dynamics help composers shape the emotions listeners feel
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states that 'dynamics shape the emotions in music,' giving examples like suspense, comfort, and energy to show how volume affects how listeners feel.

choices.text: Because dynamics make all music sound louder and more exciting
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage specifically says soft music can be just as thrilling as loud music, so dynamics are not just about making things louder.

choices.text: Because dynamics keep the music at one steady volume level
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that dynamics include gradual changes like crescendo and decrescendo, which is the opposite of staying at one steady volume.

choices.text: Because dynamics tell performers which Italian words to use
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Italian words are just the labels composers use to write dynamic markings — they're a tool, not the reason dynamics are important.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Timbre: Why a Trumpet Doesn't Sound Like a Guitar

Now picture this. A piano plays the note middle C. A violin plays the exact same note at the exact same volume. You would never mix up those two sounds. The piano's note rings out and fades quickly. The violin's note can be sustained — held out long and steady — with a warm, singing quality. Same note. Same loudness. Completely different sound. So what makes them different?

The answer is timbre, pronounced TAM-ber. Timbre is the special quality that makes one instrument or voice sound different from another. It is sometimes called tone color because it works like color in a painting — it adds richness and variety. A flute's tone color is bright and clear. A cello's tone color is deep and rich. Your own voice has a timbre that no one else on Earth shares exactly.

It's easy to confuse timbre with pitch — how high or low a note is. But they are completely different things. Pitch tells you which note is being played. Timbre tells you who is playing it. Each instrument creates sound in its own way — by striking strings, pulling a bow across strings, blowing air through a tube, or hitting a stretched drumhead. Those different methods produce different patterns in the sound waves that reach your ears. Think of it like handwriting: two people can write the same word, but their handwriting looks completely different. Timbre is an instrument's handwriting.

This is exactly why an orchestra sounds so rich. When violins, flutes, trumpets, and drums play together, each instrument adds its own tone color. Composers pick specific instruments for specific moments because of their timbres. A solo oboe might carry a sad melody because its reedy, expressive tone color fits the feeling. A trumpet might announce a bold theme because its bright, powerful timbre sounds like strength and celebration.

This is exactly why an orchestra sounds so rich. When violins, flutes, trumpets, and drums play together, each instrument adds its own tone color. A trumpet might announce a bold theme because its bright, powerful timbre sounds like strength and celebration.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sound wave patterns are produced as a result of the playing method, so the playing method must come first, not last.

choices.text: A playing method is used, sound wave patterns are produced, and the waves reach your ears.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Your Ears Already Know This

You use dynamics and timbre every day. When you whisper a secret, you are choosing piano. When you cheer at a soccer game, you are choosing forte. When you recognize your mom's voice from another room — before you see her — you are identifying her timbre. Music takes these things you already understand and organizes them into art. The next time you hear a song, listen past the melody. Notice where the music gets louder and where it pulls back. Notice which instruments are playing and what colors they add. You are not just hearing sounds anymore. You are reading the composer's message.

choices.text: A loud and powerful sound

assessments.title: Texture: Layers of Sound

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Layers of Music: Understanding Texture

A Single Voice in a Gym

Picture this: a girl stands alone in an empty gymnasium and sings "Happy Birthday." Her voice bounces off the walls. There is no piano, no guitar, no one else singing along. Just one melody floating through the air. Now imagine the whole school joins in, clapping and strumming guitars while she sings. The song sounds completely different — fuller, richer, almost like a warm blanket of sound wrapping around the room. The melody hasn't changed. So what did?

The answer is texture. In music, texture describes how many layers of sound are happening at once and how those layers relate to each other. Think of it like fabric. A single silk thread is smooth and thin. Weave hundreds of threads together, and you get something thick and complex. Music works the same way. A solo voice is one kind of texture. A voice plus instruments is another. Multiple melodies tangling together is yet another.

You might think texture means how music literally "feels" — smooth, scratchy, loud, or soft. But volume has its own name: dynamics. Speed is called tempo. The special quality of a sound — like the difference between a flute and a trumpet on the same note — is called timbre (pronounced TAM-ber). Texture is specifically about melodic lines and how they work together. It is one of several building blocks musicians call the elements of music.

Texture is specifically about melodic lines and how they work together. It is one of several building blocks musicians call the elements of music.

prompt._: According to the text, which pair of music ideas is contrasted by saying one is about layers of sound and the other is about loudness?

choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage explains that texture is about layers of sound, and then says loudness has its own name — dynamics — showing these two ideas are being contrasted.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
One Sound: Monophonic Texture

The simplest texture is monophonic, which means "one sound." Monophonic texture is a single melody with no accompaniment — no background instruments or extra notes supporting it. That girl singing alone in the gym? Monophonic. A single flute playing a tune by itself? Also monophonic. The sound is pure and clean, like a single colored pencil line on white paper.

But monophonic means one melody, not one voice. As long as everyone sings the same tune together, there is still only one melodic line. The sound gets louder, sure — but the texture stays the same.

A single flute playing a tune by itself? Also monophonic. The sound is pure and clean, like a single colored pencil line on white paper. But monophonic means one melody, not one voice. As long as everyone sings the same tune together, there is still only one melodic line. The sound gets louder, sure — but the texture stays the same.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that singing the same tune together keeps the texture monophonic, which supports the main idea about a single melody.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Melody Plus Support: Homophonic Texture

Most songs you hear on the radio use homophonic texture, which means "same sound." In homophonic texture, one main melody rides on top while chords — groups of notes played together — support it from underneath. Think of a singer and a guitar player. The singer carries the tune you hum along to. The guitar strums chords beneath it, like a floor holding up a dancer. The melody is the star. The chords are the stage.

A pianist creates homophonic texture all by herself. Her right hand plays the melody while her left hand plays chords. Pop songs, folk songs, and hymns almost always use this texture. It sounds full and warm, and your ear can follow the melody easily because the chords stay in the background.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Many Melodies at Once: Polyphonic Texture

The most complex texture is polyphonic, which means "many sounds." In polyphonic texture, two or more independent melodies play at the same time. Each melody has its own rhythm and moves in its own direction, but they are designed to sound good together.

How is this different from homophonic? In homophonic texture, the chords just support one melody — they don't have their own tune. In polyphonic texture, every voice or instrument carries its own separate melody. Nobody is just playing background support.

You may have sung a round — a song where groups start the same melody at different times. "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" is a famous example. When the second group begins singing while the first group is still going, two versions of the melody overlap. That overlap creates polyphonic texture. The composer Johann Sebastian Bach, who lived in the 1700s during a period called the Baroque era, was a master of polyphony. He wrote pieces called fugues — musical puzzles where several melodies chase each other, entering one at a time and weaving in and out like runners on crisscrossing paths.

How is this different from homophonic? In homophonic texture, the chords just support one melody — they don't have their own tune. In polyphonic texture, every voice or instrument carries its own separate melody. Nobody is just playing background support. When the second group begins singing while the first group is still going, two versions of the melody overlap. That overlap creates polyphonic texture. The composer Johann Sebastian Bach, who lived in the 1700s during a period called the Baroque era, was a master of polyphony. He wrote pieces called fugues — musical puzzles where several melodies chase each other, entering one at a time and weaving in and out like runners on crisscrossing paths.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Texture Shifts Tell a Story

Great composers don't pick one texture and stick with it. They change the texture to shape how the music feels. A piece might open with a single instrument playing a lonely monophonic melody. Then other instruments slide in with chords, building homophonic warmth. Finally, each instrument launches its own melody, and the music swells into a polyphonic climax. These shifts create surprise, tension, and release — like chapters in a story.

One melody alone? Monophonic. A melody with chords underneath? Homophonic. Multiple melodies moving independently? Polyphonic. You are not just hearing the music anymore. You are reading its architecture — and once you see the structure, you can never quite un-hear it.

assessments.title: High and Low: The Four Vocal Ranges

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Vocal Ranges: The Four Voices That Build a Choir

A Sound You Can Feel

A man steps onto a dark stage, opens his mouth, and sings a note so low you feel it rumble in your chest like distant thunder. Moments later, a woman's voice soars above his — bright, clear, and so high it seems to float near the ceiling. Same song. But their voices live in completely different neighborhoods of sound.

That difference is called vocal range — how high or how low a person's voice can sing. Think of a piano keyboard. A single hand covers only a section of keys. Each singer's voice works the same way, covering a specific section of musical notes from its lowest to its highest. You might think range means how loud someone sings, but that's actually volume. Range is only about pitch — the highness or lowness of a note.

That difference is called vocal range — how high or how low a person's voice can sing. Think of a piano keyboard. A single hand covers only a section of keys. Each singer's voice works the same way, covering a specific section of musical notes from its lowest to its highest. You might think range means how loud someone sings, but that's actually volume. Range is only about pitch — the highness or lowness of a note.

prompt._: According to the text, what is the difference between vocal range and volume?

choices.text: Range is about the keys on a piano keyboard, but volume is about the rumble in your chest.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the piano keyboard is just an example the passage uses to explain range, and the rumble in your chest is just a description from the story, not the definition of volume.

choices.text: Range is about how loud someone sings, but volume is about the highness or lowness of a note.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it flips the two definitions — the passage says volume is about loudness and range is about pitch, not the other way around.

choices.text: Range is about the highness or lowness of a note, but volume is about how loud someone sings.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that range is about pitch (highness or lowness) and that volume is about how loud someone sings.

choices.text: Range is about the low sound of a man, but volume is about the high sound of a woman.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the man and woman in the passage are just examples to introduce the topic — they are not the definitions of range or volume.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Female Voices: Soprano and Alto

Soprano is the highest of the four main vocal ranges. If you press the keys on the right side of a piano, you hear the bright, sparkling notes where sopranos sing. In a choir — a group of singers performing together — sopranos usually carry the melody. The melody is the main tune, the part you'd hum on the way home. In opera, a type of musical theater where the entire story is sung instead of spoken, the soprano often plays the leading female character. Her voice rings out over a full orchestra with powerful power.

Alto is the lower female voice, sometimes called contralto. If the soprano is sunlight on the surface of a lake, the alto is the warm water just beneath. Alto voices have a rich, mellow sound. In a choir, altos sing harmony — notes that are different from the melody but blend with it to make the music sound fuller and deeper. Imagine singing "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" in a round with a friend. That layered effect is a simple kind of harmony. Altos create that layered feeling beneath the soprano melody, and a skilled alto singing alone can stop a room.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Male Voices: Tenor and Bass

Tenor is the higher male voice. Tenors can reach notes that other male singers cannot, and their sound is often bright and ringing — like a trumpet. In opera, the tenor frequently plays the romantic hero. Famous tenor songs, called arias (solo pieces sung during an opera), are some of the most beloved pieces in classical music. In a choir, tenors sing a line that weaves between the higher female voices and the lower male voices, connecting the two groups like a bridge.

The four vocal categories are not sealed boxes — they overlap, like colors blending on a painting. Some women's alto voices dip into tenor notes, and some trained men can reach into the alto range.

Bass is the lowest male voice. Press the keys on the far left of a piano, and you'll hear where bass singers live. A bass voice is deep, powerful, and resonant — meaning it vibrates and fills a space the way a large bell fills a tower with sound. In a choir, the bass part anchors everything. It holds up the other voices the way the foundation of a building holds up the walls and roof. In opera, bass singers often portray kings, villains, or wise old characters. Their deep voices carry a natural sense of authority and weight.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Four Parts, One Sound

In a four-part choir, each vocal range sings its own line of music. Soprano takes the highest notes. Alto sings harmony just below. Tenor sits above bass. Bass holds the bottom. When all four parts join together, the blend creates a rich, full sound greater than any single voice alone.

Bass holds the bottom. When all four parts join together, the blend creates a rich, full sound greater than any single voice alone.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Your Voice Is Still Growing — and It Has a Place

Right now, most fourth graders sing in the soprano or alto range. Young voices can hit surprisingly high notes with a pure, clear tone. But your voice won't stay exactly where it is. Inside your throat sit your vocal cords — two small bands of tissue that vibrate when air passes over them, like a guitar string vibrating when you pluck it. During puberty, these cords grow longer and thicker, especially in boys. After this shift, males usually develop tenor or bass voices, while females settle into soprano or alto ranges.

See if you can pick out the sopranos floating on top, the altos warming the middle, the tenors connecting high to low, and the basses rumbling underneath. Each voice alone tells only part of the story. Together, they speak in a language no single voice could ever produce on its own. Your voice, wherever it lands, has a place in that conversation.

assessments.title: The Piano: King of Instruments

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Keyboard Instruments: One Family, Many Voices

A Surprise Inside the Piano

Lift the lid of a grand piano, and you will see something that looks nothing like what you expected. There are no speakers. No electronics. Instead, you will find over two hundred tightly stretched steel strings and a row of small felt-covered hammers, all waiting to spring into action. When a pianist presses a key, a hammer flies upward and strikes the strings — the same basic motion as a mallet hitting a drum. Here's the surprising part: because the sound comes from striking, many musicians classify the piano as a percussion instrument, not a string instrument. It has strings inside, yes, but the hammers do the work. The piano is really a bit of both.

But the piano is only one member of a much larger family called keyboard instruments. A keyboard instrument is any instrument played by pressing a row of keys with your fingers. The piano, the harpsichord, the organ, and the modern synthesizer all belong to this family. They share that familiar row of black and white keys, but they create sound in completely different ways.

They share that familiar row of black and white keys, but they create sound in completely different ways.

choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tightly stretched strings are mentioned as part of what's inside the piano, but the passage never says that's the reason it's classified as a percussion instrument.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How the Piano Was Born

Around the year 1700, an Italian instrument maker named Bartolomeo Cristofori grew frustrated with the harpsichord. It made sound by plucking strings with tiny quill picks, almost the way you might pluck a guitar string with your fingernail.

Cristofori wanted something better. He built a keyboard instrument that used hammers to strike the strings instead of plucking them. Press a key gently, and the hammer taps the string softly, producing a quiet note. Press a key firmly, and the hammer hits harder, producing a loud note. Cristofori called his invention the pianoforte, an Italian word meaning "soft-loud." Over time, people shortened the name to just piano.

It made sound by plucking strings with tiny quill picks, almost the way you might pluck a guitar string with your fingernail. Cristofori wanted something better. He built a keyboard instrument that used hammers to strike the strings instead of plucking them. Press a key gently, and the hammer taps the string softly, producing a quiet note. Press a key firmly, and the hammer hits harder, producing a loud note. Cristofori called his invention the pianoforte, an Italian word meaning "soft-loud." Over time, people shortened the name to just piano.

choices.text: Cristofori invented the harpsichord before he invented the piano.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage only says Cristofori was frustrated with the harpsichord, not that he was the one who invented it.

choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that Cristofori's frustration with the harpsichord is what motivated him to invent the piano.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions how popular the harpsichord was in Italy.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Inside the Piano

A standard piano has eighty-eight keys — fifty-two white and thirty-six black. Those keys span more than seven octaves. An octave is the distance from one note to the next note with the same letter name, like from one C up to the next C — eight white keys apart, like climbing eight rungs of a ladder.

When a hammer strikes a string, the string vibrates back and forth very quickly. Those vibrations pass into a large wooden soundboard — a flat panel that amplifies the sound, meaning it boosts the vibrations so they fill a room instead of fading away. The soundboard works like a megaphone for each vibrating string: it doesn't change the note, but it makes it louder and richer.

The soundboard works like a megaphone for each vibrating string: it doesn't change the note, but it makes it louder and richer.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Makes the Piano Special

A pianist can play ten notes at once — one with each finger. The right hand can carry a singing melody while the left hand plays supporting chords or a deep bass line. This creates harmonies, meaning two or more notes sounding together in a pleasing way, like voices blending in a choir. A violin plays one note at a time. A trumpet plays one note at a time. The piano can do the work of a whole group — it can perform alone or accompany an entire orchestra.

That power attracted composers across every style of music. Classical masters like Mozart, Beethoven, Chopin, and Debussy wrote masterpieces for it. Jazz pianists like Duke Ellington and Thelonious Monk used it for improvisational music — music made up on the spot, the way you might invent a story as you tell it. Pop and rock artists from Elton John to Alicia Keys have made the piano a star on stage.

The piano is also one of the best tools for learning music. Its layout is visual and logical. Notes go higher as you move right and lower as you move left. You can see scales, chords, and intervals — the distances between notes, like steps on a staircase — laid out right in front of you.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Rest of the Keyboard Family

You might think that every keyboard instrument works like the piano, with hammers hitting strings inside. Actually, each member of the family has its own way of making sound. A pipe organ has no strings at all. Instead, pressing a key opens a valve that sends air rushing through a metal or wooden pipe. Different pipes produce different pitches, and a large church organ can have thousands of them, filling an entire building with sound.

A synthesizer uses electronic circuits or computer software to generate sound waves. It can imitate a piano, a violin, or a trumpet — or create sounds no acoustic instrument could ever make. You hear synthesizers in movie soundtracks, pop songs, and video game music every day.

All these instruments share one thing: that row of keys under your fingertips.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Piano: King of Instruments

assessments.title: Organ and Harpsichord: Keyboards of the Past

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Before the Piano: The Pipe Organ and the Harpsichord

A Giant Instrument with No Strings at All

Thirty-three thousand pipes stand inside a single building in Atlantic City, New Jersey. Together, they form one instrument — the pipe organ at Boardwalk Hall, one of the largest musical instruments ever built.

It makes sound by pushing air through pipes of different sizes and materials. When a player presses a key, a valve opens. Pressurized air rushes into a specific pipe and vibrates inside it, creating a musical tone. Long, wide pipes produce deep, booming low notes that you can feel in your chest.

Metal pipes ring out with a bright, clear tone. Wooden pipes give a warmer, softer sound — more like a whisper than a shout. This variety is part of what makes the organ so powerful. A single organ can sound like a gentle flute one moment and a thundering wall of sound the next.
stimulus.content_text: Before the Piano: The Pipe Organ and the HarpsichordA Giant Instrument with No Strings at All Thirty-three thousand pipes stand inside a single building in Atlantic City, New Jersey. Together, they form one instrument — the pipe organ at Boardwalk Hall, one of the largest musical instruments ever built. It makes sound by pushing air through pipes of different sizes and materials. When a player presses a key, a valve opens. Pressurized air rushes into a specific pipe and vibrates inside it, creating a musical tone. Long, wide pipes produce deep, booming low notes that you can feel in your chest. Metal pipes ring out with a bright, clear tone. Wooden pipes give a warmer, softer sound — more like a whisper than a shout. This variety is part of what makes the organ so powerful. A single organ can sound like a gentle flute one moment and a thundering wall of sound the next.

prompt._: According to the text, which contrast between metal pipes and wooden pipes is stated?

choices.text: Metal pipes sound bright, while wooden pipes sound softer.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that metal pipes have a 'bright, clear tone' while wooden pipes give a 'warmer, softer sound.'

choices.text: Metal pipes use air, while wooden pipes use strings.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both metal and wooden pipes use air to make sound, and the passage never mentions strings being used in any pipes.

choices.text: Metal pipes make low notes, while wooden pipes make high notes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says low notes come from long, wide pipes — it never connects low or high notes specifically to metal or wooden pipes.

choices.text: Metal pipes are larger, while wooden pipes are smaller.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never compares the sizes of metal pipes and wooden pipes to each other.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Playing with Hands, Feet, and Knobs

An organist doesn't just sit and press keys. Large organs have several keyboards stacked one above another, called manuals — separate keyboards that each control a different group of pipes. Think of it like having several instruments built into one, each with its own voice. The organist's hands jump between manuals to switch between those voices during a single piece of music.

Below the keyboards, a row of wide pedals stretches across the floor. The organist plays bass notes with both feet, pressing these pedals like extra keys. That means an organist can play three parts at once — two hands on the manuals and feet on the pedals.

On either side of the keyboards, you'll find rows of knobs called stops — controls that select which sets of pipes will sound. Pulling a stop is like opening a door to a new group of pipes. By choosing different combinations, the organist changes the instrument's tone color, which is the particular quality or character of a sound — the difference between a flute's breathy voice and a trumpet's bold blast. One organ can produce dozens of tone colors.

Large organs have several keyboards stacked one above another, called manuals — separate keyboards that each control a different group of pipes. Think of it like having several instruments built into one, each with its own voice. The organist's hands jump between manuals to switch between those voices during a single piece of music. Below the keyboards, a row of wide pedals stretches across the floor. The organist plays bass notes with both feet, pressing these pedals like extra keys. That means an organist can play three parts at once — two hands on the manuals and feet on the pedals. On either side of the keyboards, you'll find rows of knobs called stops — controls that select which sets of pipes will sound. Pulling a stop is like opening a door to a new group of pipes. By choosing different combinations, the organist changes the instrument's tone color, which is the particular quality or character of a sound — the difference between a flute's breathy voice and a trumpet's bold blast. One organ can produce dozens of tone colors.

choices.text: to switch between the stacked manuals

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Harpsichord: A Keyboard That Plucks

While the organ filled cathedrals, another keyboard instrument filled the homes and courts of Europe from the 1500s to the 1700s. The harpsichord looks a bit like a small piano, with a wooden case and black-and-white keys. But open the lid and watch what happens inside, and you'll see something completely different.

A piano makes sound when tiny hammers strike strings. A harpsichord uses a small pick called a plectrum — a thin piece of material, like a tiny guitar pick — that plucks the string when you press a key. This plucking creates a bright, sparkling tone, crisp and quick, very different from the rich, lingering sound of a piano.

The plectrum plucks the string the same way every time. To get around this, builders added a second keyboard or extra sets of strings that the player could switch on for a fuller, louder sound. But smooth, gradual changes from soft to loud?

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Bach: Master of Both Instruments

Johann Sebastian Bach, who lived from 1685 to 1750, was one of the greatest keyboard musicians who ever lived. He played both the organ and the harpsichord with exceptional skill. He composed hundreds of works for keyboard instruments during the Baroque era — a time from roughly 1600 to 1750 when music was dramatic, decorative, and full of energy.

One of Bach's most famous collections is the Well-Tempered Clavier. It contains preludes — short pieces that introduce a mood, like an opening scene in a movie — and fugues, pieces where a melody enters one voice at a time and weaves around itself, like runners joining a race one by one. Bach wrote a prelude and fugue in every major and minor key. His music let the harpsichord's bright clarity and the organ's enormous power each shine.

It contains preludes — short pieces that introduce a mood, like an opening scene in a movie — and fugues, pieces where a melody enters one voice at a time and weaves around itself, like runners joining a race one by one. Bach wrote a prelude and fugue in every major and minor key. His music let the harpsichord's bright clarity and the organ's enormous power each shine.

prompt._: The author compares fugues to "runners joining a race one by one." Based on this comparison, what does the word fugues mean in the passage?

choices.text: Music where parts start at different times.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage explains that in a fugue, each voice (or part) enters one at a time, just like runners joining a race one by one — so it's music where parts start at different times.

choices.text: Music that introduces a feeling or a mood.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because introducing a feeling or mood describes a prelude, which the passage explains separately from fugues.

choices.text: Music that is played very fast like a race.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the race comparison is about parts entering one by one, not about the speed of the music.

choices.text: Music created for two different keyboards.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never connects fugues to two keyboards — that idea isn't mentioned anywhere in the description of fugues.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why These Instruments Still Matter

By the late 1700s, the piano began replacing the harpsichord. The piano could do what the harpsichord could not — play softly and loudly with a simple change in touch. Today, musicians who specialize in Baroque music still perform on harpsichords so audiences can hear the exact sound that Bach and the composers of his time intended. The organ, meanwhile, continues to fill churches and concert halls around the world.

Every keyboard instrument you see today — from a digital piano in a classroom to a synthesizer on a stage — carries a piece of this history. The organ showed that air and pipes could create a hundred voices from one seat. The harpsichord showed that a plucked string could sparkle with life. These instruments didn't just come before the piano. They taught musicians what a keyboard could be.

assessments.title: The Four Sections of the Orchestra

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Orchestra: One Hundred Musicians, One Sound

A Wall of Sound from a Single Stage

Eighty-seven musicians sit perfectly still. A woman in a black suit steps onto a raised platform, lifts a thin white stick called a baton — a lightweight wand used to direct the musicians — and flicks it downward. In that instant, every instrument on stage comes alive at once. Violins soar. Trumpets blaze. Drums thunder. This is a full symphony orchestra, and the enormous sound it creates comes from a careful plan. Every musician sits in a specific spot. Every instrument belongs to one of four families. Nothing about an orchestra is random.

You might think the conductor — the person holding the baton — is just waving a stick for show. Actually, the conductor has one of the hardest jobs on stage. With close to a hundred people playing at once, someone must keep the beat steady, signal each section when to start, and decide whether the music should grow louder or softer. The conductor reads a score, a written plan that shows every note for every instrument on the same page. Think of the conductor as the coach of a giant sports team. The players have the skills, but the coach shapes the game plan.

The conductor reads a score, a written plan that shows every note for every instrument on the same page. Think of the conductor as the coach of a giant sports team. The players have the skills, but the coach shapes the game plan.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the conductor different from the musicians on stage?

choices.text: The conductor sits in a specific spot with a violin
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the musicians each sit in a specific spot, not the conductor, who stands on a raised platform at the front.

choices.text: The conductor plays the loudest instrument in the orchestra
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the conductor doesn't play an instrument — they hold a thin stick called a baton to direct the musicians.

choices.text: The conductor reads the score and directs the group
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the conductor reads a score and is responsible for keeping the beat, signaling sections, and shaping how the music sounds.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because performing the notes is the musicians' job — the conductor's job is to guide and direct everyone else.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Strings and Woodwinds: Melody, Harmony, and Unique Voices

Each string instrument makes sound when a musician pulls a bow across tightly stretched strings or plucks them with a finger. The vibrating strings produce warm, singing tones that can whisper or roar.

Violins usually carry the melody, the main tune you hum after the concert. Violas add harmony — extra notes that blend with the melody the way backup singers blend with a lead vocalist. Cellos sing in a rich middle range, and the tall double basses rumble at the very bottom, like distant thunder holding everything up. Because there are so many string players, their combined sound forms the foundation the rest of the orchestra builds on.

Sitting just behind the strings are the woodwinds: flutes, oboes, clarinets, and bassoons. Most woodwinds produce sound when the player sends air over a thin piece of cane called a reed — a small, flexible strip that vibrates the way a blade of grass buzzes when you blow on it between your thumbs. But here is the surprise: the flute uses no reed at all. A flute player blows across a small opening, much like blowing across the top of a bottle. That difference gives the flute its bright, airy voice.

The oboe sounds sharp and expressive, almost like a duck with perfect pitch. The clarinet is smooth and versatile, meaning it can play many different styles — slow and dreamy one moment, fast and jazzy the next. The bassoon adds a deep, reedy warmth. When a woodwind plays a solo melody, it stands out the way a single color stands out in a painting.
