prompt._: Based on the passage, why does the flute have a bright, airy voice?

choices.text: because it uses a thin reed to make sound
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says most woodwinds use a reed, but the flute is the surprise exception that uses NO reed at all.

choices.text: because the player plucks the strings gently
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — plucking strings is something string instrument players do, and has nothing to do with how a flute works.

choices.text: because the player blows across an opening
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly states that blowing across a small opening is what gives the flute its bright, airy voice.

choices.text: because it sits just behind the strings
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — sitting behind the strings describes where the woodwinds are located, not why the flute sounds the way it does.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Brass and Percussion: Power, Rhythm, and the Orchestra's Backbone

Behind the woodwinds sit the brass instruments: trumpets, French horns, trombones, and tubas. That tiny vibration travels through coiled metal tubing and comes out the other end as a bold, ringing tone. Trombones slide between notes with a rich, powerful voice. Tubas deliver the deepest brass notes, supporting the section from below. When the full brass section plays at top volume, the sound hits your chest like a wave.

At the very back of the stage, you find the percussion section. Percussion instruments are struck, shaken, or scraped. Timpani — large kettledrums shaped like giant copper bowls — can rumble softly or explode with dramatic force. The snare drum adds a crisp, rattling rhythm. Cymbals crash and shimmer. Bass drums, triangles, xylophones, and tambourines each add their own spark. Percussion players must be exact, because even one misplaced beat rings out over the entire orchestra. Softer instruments like strings sit up front so the audience can hear them, while louder sections like brass and percussion sit farther back so they do not overpower everyone else.

At the very back of the stage, you find the percussion section. Percussion instruments are struck, shaken, or scraped. Bass drums, triangles, xylophones, and tambourines each add their own spark. Percussion players must be exact, because even one misplaced beat rings out over the entire orchestra. Softer instruments like strings sit up front so the audience can hear them, while louder sections like brass and percussion sit farther back so they do not overpower everyone else.

prompt._: According to the text, what happens after a vibration travels through the coiled metal tubing of a brass instrument?

choices.text: It slides between notes with a rich voice.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because sliding between notes describes how trombones move between pitches, not what happens when a vibration travels through the metal tubing.

choices.text: It comes out as a bold, ringing tone.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that the vibration 'comes out the other end as a bold, ringing tone' after traveling through the coiled metal tubing.

choices.text: It delivers the deepest notes from below.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because delivering the deepest notes from below describes the tuba's role in the brass section, not what happens to a vibration inside the tubing.

choices.text: It hits your chest like a strong wave.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the chest-hitting wave describes what it feels like when the whole brass section plays loudly together, not what a single vibration does inside the instrument.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Orchestra's Sound Beyond the Concert Hall

A full symphony orchestra can have between 70 and 100 musicians. Strings vibrate strings. Woodwinds vibrate air through reeds or across openings. Brass players vibrate their lips. Percussionists strike, shake, or scrape. These four methods create four different palettes of sound — sets of tonal colors a composer can mix, the way a painter mixes colors on a board.

The sweeping music in your favorite movie or the dramatic soundtrack in a video game — that is often an orchestra. Those musicians sitting in careful rows are not a relic of the past. They are the hidden voice behind some of the most powerful sounds in your everyday life.

assessments.title: The Conductor: Leading the Orchestra

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Person Facing the Wrong Way

One Hundred Musicians, One Thin Stick

Have you ever noticed something strange at an orchestra concert? A single person stands at the front of the stage, back turned to a thousand audience members, waving a thin stick through the air. Just two hands, a stick, and a hundred musicians watching every flick of those fingers. That person is the conductor, and the thin stick is called a baton — a lightweight wand, no heavier than a pencil, that helps the musicians see the conductor's movements from far away.
stimulus.content_text: The Person Facing the Wrong WayOne Hundred Musicians, One Thin Stick Have you ever noticed something strange at an orchestra concert? A single person stands at the front of the stage, back turned to a thousand audience members, waving a thin stick through the air. Just two hands, a stick, and a hundred musicians watching every flick of those fingers. That person is the conductor, and the thin stick is called a baton — a lightweight wand, no heavier than a pencil, that helps the musicians see the conductor's movements from far away.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the baton different from what you might expect based on its importance to the orchestra?

choices.text: It is very lightweight and no heavier than a pencil
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says the baton is 'lightweight' and 'no heavier than a pencil,' which is surprisingly light for something so important.

choices.text: It is very long so it reaches across the whole stage
choices.feedback: The passage never says the baton is very long or that it reaches across the stage — that detail is not in the text.

choices.text: It is brightly colored so musicians can see it easily
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about the baton being brightly colored — that detail isn't in the text.

choices.text: It is made of heavy wood so it lasts a long time
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say the baton is made of heavy wood — in fact, it says the opposite, that it is lightweight.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Homework Before Rehearsal

Here's something you might not expect: a conductor's work starts long before anyone picks up an instrument. First, the conductor studies the musical score — a written document that shows every note for every instrument in the entire piece. Think of it like a giant blueprint of a building, except instead of walls and windows, it maps out sounds. The conductor reads this blueprint and makes big decisions. Should this section feel slow and sad? Should that passage burst with energy? Where should the music get louder, and where should it pull back to a whisper?

That is why the same piece of music can sound fresh every time a new conductor leads it.

During rehearsals, the conductor works with each of the four main sections of the orchestra — strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion — helping every group understand the plan for the piece.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Speaking Without Words

Now here's where things get really interesting. Once the concert begins, the conductor communicates without saying a single word. The right hand usually holds the baton and beats the basic pulse of the music — a steady pattern of downward and upward strokes that the musicians follow. This sets the tempo, which means the speed of the music. A conductor might start a passage slowly, then gradually speed up to build energy, or pull the tempo back to create a moment of calm.

The left hand does something different — and you can try imagining these gestures yourself! A gently rising palm might ask the strings to play more softly. A sudden clenched fist might signal the brass to cut off a note sharply. A wide, sweeping motion might coax the whole orchestra into a crescendo — a gradual increase in volume, like a wave building before it crashes on shore. The conductor also controls dynamics, which means how loud or soft the music is played. One moment the orchestra might play so quietly you hold your breath. The next, it might explode into a fortissimo — a passage played at full, thundering volume!

The conductor also gives cues — small signals that tell a specific section or a solo player exactly when to start playing. In a piece where dozens of instruments enter at different moments, these cues keep everyone perfectly together. Every tilt of the head, every raised eyebrow, carries meaning. Can you imagine trying to keep track of all that at once?

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Concertmaster and the Single Note

The conductor does not work alone — and you might be surprised by what happens before the conductor even walks onstage! The concertmaster is the lead first violinist, seated closest to the conductor. Before the conductor appears, the concertmaster stands and nods to the principal oboe player — the top-ranked oboist in the orchestra. The oboe then sounds a single note: an A. Its tone is steady and clear, easy for every instrument to hear across a crowded stage. Every musician tunes to that one note, adjusting their instrument until they all match. Pretty important job for one little note!

The concertmaster also passes along bowing instructions — directions about whether the string players should pull their bows up or down at certain moments, so all the bows move together like a flock of birds turning at once. During the performance, the concertmaster watches the conductor closely and helps the other violinists follow along.

choices.text: They move in the same direction at once.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Not Every Group Needs One

Here is something surprising — not every group of musicians uses a conductor! Small ensembles called chamber groups — maybe four or five players — often perform without one. They watch and listen to each other instead. But as orchestras grew to include sixty, eighty, even a hundred musicians, someone had to stand at the center and unify all those separate voices into one sound. Can you imagine trying to keep a hundred people in sync without someone leading the way?

So next time you watch an orchestra, keep your eyes on the conductor. That person is listening to a hundred musicians at once and hearing what you cannot yet hear — the piece the music is still becoming. Now that's something worth turning your back to the audience for!

assessments.title: George Frideric Handel and the Messiah

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Handel's Messiah: Twenty-Four Days That Still Echo

A Composer Locked in His Room

In the autumn of 1741, George Frideric Handel shut himself inside his London rooms and started writing music at a furious pace. For roughly twenty-four days, he barely stopped. Page after page filled with notes — not just a melody line for one instrument, but a full score, meaning he wrote the parts for chorus, solo singers, and orchestra all at once on the same pages. Imagine juggling dozens of different voices and instruments in your head simultaneously, then writing them all down together. That is what Handel did.

Actually, Handel had spent decades studying, practicing, and composing before this moment. Handel worked faster than almost anyone — but only because he had trained longer than almost anyone, too.

When he finally set down his pen, he had created one of the most celebrated works in the history of Western music: Messiah.

For roughly twenty-four days, he barely stopped. Page after page filled with notes — not just a melody line for one instrument, but a full score, meaning he wrote the parts for chorus, solo singers, and orchestra all at once on the same pages. Imagine juggling dozens of different voices and instruments in your head simultaneously, then writing them all down together. That is what Handel did. Actually, Handel had spent decades studying, practicing, and composing before this moment. Handel worked faster than almost anyone — but only because he had trained longer than almost anyone, too. When he finally set down his pen, he had created one of the most celebrated works in the history of Western music: Messiah.

prompt._: According to the text, how was Handel's score for Messiah different from just a melody line for one instrument?

choices.text: It held parts for the orchestra but left out the chorus sections
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states the chorus was included in the score, so nothing was left out.

choices.text: It included parts for chorus, solo singers, and orchestra all at once
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Handel wrote parts for the chorus, solo singers, AND orchestra all at once — not just a single melody line.

choices.text: It featured one instrument playing the same melody over and over
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — Handel wrote many different parts for many different performers, not one instrument repeating the same melody.

choices.text: It contained notes for only the solo singers to perform alone
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the score included much more than just solo singers — it also had parts for the chorus and the orchestra.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From Germany to England

Handel was born in Germany in 1685 — the same year as the great Johann Sebastian Bach. As a young man, he traveled to Italy to study opera and quickly became known for his dramatic musical style. He moved to London, threw himself into the city's busy musical life, and eventually became a British citizen.

Handel composed operas, orchestral works, and royal celebration music for the English court. King George I enjoyed Handel's Water Music so much that he reportedly asked for it to be played three times during a single boat trip on the River Thames. Picture a king floating down a river, demanding an encore — and then another encore. Handel's music earned him fame across the continent and made him one of the most celebrated composers in all of Europe.

Handel composed operas, orchestral works, and royal celebration music for the English court. King George I enjoyed Handel's Water Music so much that he reportedly asked for it to be played three times during a single boat trip on the River Thames. Picture a king floating down a river, demanding an encore — and then another encore. Handel's music earned him fame across the continent and made him one of the most celebrated composers in all of Europe.

choices.text: because his music was enjoyed and celebrated by important people
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows King George I loved his music so much he asked for it three times, and says Handel became one of the most celebrated composers in Europe.

choices.feedback: Traveling to Italy helped Handel learn about opera, but the passage doesn't connect that trip to why he became famous all across Europe.

choices.feedback: Becoming a British citizen tells us Handel made London his home, but it doesn't explain why people throughout Europe celebrated his music.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What Is an Oratorio?

Handel's Messiah is an oratorio — a large musical work for chorus, solo singers, and orchestra that tells a story without costumes or staging. It's easy to assume that an oratorio is just another word for an opera, but the two are quite different. In an opera, singers wear costumes, act out roles, and perform on a decorated stage. In an oratorio, the singers simply stand before the audience and let the music carry the drama. No wigs. No sword fights. Just voices and instruments.

Messiah tells the story of the life of Jesus Christ, from prophecies — predictions made long before an event happens — about his coming, through his life, to his resurrection, or return to life after death. Handel set passages from the Bible to soaring music. The oratorio is divided into three parts, each building on the last. It combines powerful choral singing, expressive solo arias — songs performed by a single singer, like a musical spotlight on one voice — and rich orchestral accompaniment.

It combines powerful choral singing, expressive solo arias — songs performed by a single singer, like a musical spotlight on one voice — and rich orchestral accompaniment.

choices.text: His life, prophecies, and his resurrection
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it puts 'his life' first, but the passage clearly says prophecies come before his life.

choices.text: His resurrection, his life, and prophecies
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it starts with the resurrection, but the passage tells us the resurrection is the last part of the story, not the first.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Packed House in Dublin

Messiah premiered — was performed for the very first time — in Dublin, Ireland, in April 1742. It was an immediate hit. Audiences were stunned by the beauty and emotional force of the music. The concert was so popular that organizers had a problem: too many people, not enough space. Ladies were asked to wear dresses without hoops, and gentlemen were asked to leave their swords at home, just so more bodies could squeeze into the hall.

Over the centuries, Messiah has become one of the most frequently performed large choral works in the world. You can hear it in concert halls every holiday season, from London to Los Angeles.

Ladies were asked to wear dresses without hoops, and gentlemen were asked to leave their swords at home, just so more bodies could squeeze into the hall. Over the centuries, Messiah has become one of the most frequently performed large choral works in the world. You can hear it in concert halls every holiday season, from London to Los Angeles.

choices.text: It is still performed mostly in the city of Dublin.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Messiah is performed from London to Los Angeles, not mostly in Dublin.

choices.text: It is still played for crowds without hoop dresses.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the no-hoop-dress rule was a one-time solution for a crowded hall in 1742, and the passage never says this continues today.

choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Messiah is one of the most performed large choral works, which means there are other large choral works besides it.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Hallelujah Chorus

Of all the movements in Messiah, none is more famous than the "Hallelujah" chorus. This magnificent piece builds layer upon layer of voices singing the word "Hallelujah" in powerful, joyful harmonies. Trumpets blaze. Timpani — large kettle-shaped drums that sound like rolling thunder — pound underneath. The music surges with triumph and celebration. Even people who have never heard the full oratorio recognize this chorus instantly.

Legend says that King George II was so moved by the "Hallelujah" chorus that he stood up during the performance. Because etiquette — the rules of polite behavior, especially around royalty — required everyone to stand when the king stood, the entire audience rose to its feet. Whether or not the story is perfectly true, it started a tradition that lives on today. Audiences at performances of Messiah still stand during the "Hallelujah" chorus as a sign of respect and awe.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Music That Refuses to Stay in the Past

Handel finished Messiah nearly three hundred years ago. Yet every December, thousands of choirs around the world open those same pages and sing those same notes. Audiences still rise to their feet during the "Hallelujah" chorus — not because a king tells them to, but because the music tells them to.

choices.text: He studied opera in Italy, premiered Messiah in Dublin, and wrote it in London.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it says Handel wrote Messiah after the Dublin premiere, which makes no sense — he had to finish writing it in London before it could be performed in Dublin.

choices.text: He studied opera in Italy, wrote Messiah in London, and premiered it in Dublin.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly shows Handel studied opera in Italy as a young man, then wrote Messiah in London in 1741, and then it premiered in Dublin in April 1742 — exactly that order.

prompt._: Based on the article, what is one way Handel's Water Music and his oratorio Messiah are similar?

choices.text: Both pieces of music caused the entire audience to stand in respect.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because only the 'Hallelujah' chorus from Messiah is connected to the tradition of audiences standing — the passage never says this happened at a Water Music performance.

choices.text: Both pieces of music were written to tell stories from the Bible.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Water Music was composed for a royal boat trip on the River Thames, not to tell stories from the Bible.

choices.text: Both pieces of music were first performed on a river in London.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Messiah was first performed in Dublin, Ireland, not on a river in London.

choices.text: Both pieces of music were highly admired by an English king.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows King George I loved Water Music and King George II was deeply moved by the 'Hallelujah' chorus, meaning both pieces were highly admired by English kings.

assessments.title: Franz Joseph Haydn: Father of the Symphony

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Franz Joseph Haydn: The Father of the Symphony

A Loud Surprise

A soft, sweet melody floated from the stage — gentle enough to make your eyelids heavy. Every instrument in the orchestra slammed a massive chord at once. Up at the front, the composer Franz Joseph Haydn may have been hiding a grin. He had written that shock into the music on purpose. Welcome to Symphony No. 94, better known as the "Surprise Symphony." Haydn reportedly added that sudden blast to wake up anyone dozing off — his playful way of reminding listeners to pay attention.

That moment tells you something important about Haydn. He was brilliant, funny, and full of surprises. He was also the person most responsible for turning the symphony — a large piece of music written for a full orchestra — into the powerful art form it is today.
stimulus.content_text: Franz Joseph Haydn: The Father of the SymphonyA Loud Surprise A soft, sweet melody floated from the stage — gentle enough to make your eyelids heavy. Every instrument in the orchestra slammed a massive chord at once. Up at the front, the composer Franz Joseph Haydn may have been hiding a grin. He had written that shock into the music on purpose. Welcome to Symphony No. 94, better known as the "Surprise Symphony." Haydn reportedly added that sudden blast to wake up anyone dozing off — his playful way of reminding listeners to pay attention. That moment tells you something important about Haydn. He was brilliant, funny, and full of surprises. He was also the person most responsible for turning the symphony — a large piece of music written for a full orchestra — into the powerful art form it is today.

prompt._: According to the passage about Haydn, what contrast does the text describe between the melody at the beginning and the sound that follows it?

choices.text: a fast melody followed by a slow melody
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never describes the tempo (speed) of the music — it only talks about how loud or soft the sounds were.

choices.text: a soft melody followed by a massive chord
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says the music began with a 'soft, sweet melody' and was then followed by every instrument slamming 'a massive chord at once.'

choices.text: a happy melody followed by a sad melody
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never uses the words happy or sad to describe any part of the music.

choices.text: a long melody followed by a short chord
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage gives no information about how long the melody was or how short the chord was.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
From a Village Choir to the Streets of Vienna

Haydn was born in 1732 in a tiny Austrian village. His family did not have much money, but they loved music. At just eight years old, he traveled to Vienna to sing in the choir at St. Stephen's Cathedral. For years, his voice filled that enormous church.

Then came a tough change. As a teenager, his voice deepened and cracked the way all boys' voices do. He could no longer hit the high notes the choir needed. Suddenly, he was out of a job in a big, expensive city. Haydn scraped by however he could. He gave music lessons. He played violin in street bands. He studied composition — the art of creating and writing down original music, the way an author writes a story but with notes instead of words. Nobody hired him to compose. He taught himself, piece by piece, late into the night.
stimulus.content_text: From a Village Choir to the Streets of Vienna Haydn was born in 1732 in a tiny Austrian village. His family did not have much money, but they loved music. As a teenager, his voice deepened and cracked the way all boys' voices do. He could no longer hit the high notes the choir needed. Suddenly, he was out of a job in a big, expensive city. Haydn scraped by however he could. He gave music lessons. He played violin in street bands. He studied composition — the art of creating and writing down original music, the way an author writes a story but with notes instead of words. Nobody hired him to compose. He taught himself, piece by piece, late into the night.

prompt._: Which detail from the text best supports the main idea that growing older caused Haydn's singing career to end?

choices.text: "He taught himself, piece by piece, late into the night."
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it describes what Haydn did after his singing career ended, not why growing older caused it to end.

choices.text: "He could no longer hit the high notes the choir needed."
choices.feedback: This is correct because it directly shows that his voice changing as he got older — a sign of growing up — was the exact reason he could no longer do his job in the choir.

choices.text: "His family did not have much money, but they loved music."
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it's about his family's finances when he was young, not about how getting older ended his singing career.

choices.text: "Suddenly, he was out of a job in a big, expensive city."
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it tells us the consequence of losing his choir job, but doesn't explain that his voice changing with age was the cause.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Lucky Job with a Wealthy Family

You might think that famous composers worked alone, free to create whatever they wanted. Actually, most composers in Haydn's time worked for wealthy families, much like a chef works for a restaurant. In 1761, Haydn landed the job of a lifetime. The Esterházy family, one of the richest noble families in the Austro-Hungarian Empire, hired him as their court musician — a personal composer and music director who lived and worked on a noble family's estate. For nearly thirty years, Haydn composed music for their private concerts, operas, and church services.

This job gave Haydn something rare and precious: his own orchestra to experiment with. He could write a new piece, hear his musicians play it the same week, and fix whatever didn't sound right. Think of it like a baker who owns a kitchen and can test recipes every single day. This constant cycle of composing, listening, and improving made Haydn's music stronger and bolder over time.

The Esterházy family, one of the richest noble families in the Austro-Hungarian Empire, hired him as their court musician — a personal composer and music director who lived and worked on a noble family's estate. For nearly thirty years, Haydn composed music for their private concerts, operas, and church services. This job gave Haydn something rare and precious: his own orchestra to experiment with. He could write a new piece, hear his musicians play it the same week, and fix whatever didn't sound right. Think of it like a baker who owns a kitchen and can test recipes every single day. This constant cycle of composing, listening, and improving made Haydn's music stronger and bolder over time.

prompt._: According to the text, what did Haydn do after he heard his musicians play a new piece?

choices.text: He wrote another piece right away.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says Haydn fixed what didn't sound right after listening — it doesn't say he jumped straight into writing another piece.

choices.text: He led concerts for the Esterházy family.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because leading concerts was a general part of Haydn's job, not the specific step he took after listening to a new piece.

choices.text: He fixed what did not sound right.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states he would 'fix whatever didn't sound right' after hearing his musicians play a new piece.

choices.text: He moved to a wealthy family's estate.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because moving to the Esterházy estate happened when Haydn first got the job in 1761, not after hearing his musicians play.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Building the Symphony

Here's the surprising part: Haydn did not invent the symphony. Short symphonies already existed before he was born. But those early symphonies were brief and simple — more like appetizers than full meals. Haydn expanded and refined the form. He stretched it into a larger work with four separate movements — distinct sections of a longer piece, like chapters in a book. Each movement has its own speed and mood. One might be fast and exciting. Another might be slow and tender. Together, the movements create a complete musical journey.

Over his long career, Haydn composed an astonishing 104 symphonies. He also wrote 68 string quartets — pieces for just four instruments: two violins, a viola, and a cello. Imagine four friends having a conversation, each voice weaving around the others. Haydn proved that this tiny group could produce music as rich and emotional as a full orchestra. On top of all that, he wrote operas, piano sonatas — pieces written for a solo instrument, usually in several movements — and choral works for singers.

He also wrote 68 string quartets — pieces for just four instruments: two violins, a viola, and a cello. Imagine four friends having a conversation, each voice weaving around the others. Haydn proved that this tiny group could produce music as rich and emotional as a full orchestra. On top of all that, he wrote operas, piano sonatas — pieces written for a solo instrument, usually in several movements — and choral works for singers.

prompt._: When the author compares movements to 'chapters in a book,' what does this comparison help the reader understand about the word movements?

choices.text: Movements are the speeds at which musicians play their instruments
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because while the passage does mention that each movement has its own speed, movements themselves are the sections of music, not the speeds at which they are played.

choices.text: Movements are physical actions performers make on stage
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because movements are not physical actions on stage — the passage clearly defines them as sections of music, not something performers do with their bodies.

choices.text: Movements are different symphonies written by the same composer
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because movements are sections inside one symphony, not different symphonies — the passage says Haydn wrote 104 symphonies, but each symphony has its own movements within it.

choices.text: Movements are separate sections within a larger piece of music
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly defines movements as 'distinct sections of a longer piece,' and compares them to chapters in a book to help us understand they are separate parts within one bigger work.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Fame in London and a Famous Student

Later in life, Haydn traveled to London, where English audiences went wild for his music. He composed twelve magnificent symphonies just for those London visits. By the time he returned to Vienna, he was the most celebrated composer in all of Europe.

Haydn also mentored — guided and taught — younger composers. His most famous student was Ludwig van Beethoven, who would go on to write some of the most powerful symphonies the world has ever heard. The structures Haydn developed became the blueprint other composers followed for generations. He gave them a framework, and they filled it with their own ideas.

He composed twelve magnificent symphonies just for those London visits. By the time he returned to Vienna, he was the most celebrated composer in all of Europe. Haydn also mentored — guided and taught — younger composers. His most famous student was Ludwig van Beethoven, who would go on to write some of the most powerful symphonies the world has ever heard. The structures Haydn developed became the blueprint other composers followed for generations. He gave them a framework, and they filled it with their own ideas.

prompt._: According to the passage, what does the word 'mentored' mean?

choices.text: performed alongside and copied
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Haydn performed alongside or copied other composers.

choices.text: guided and taught
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines 'mentored' as 'guided and taught' right after the word, using dashes to show the meaning.

choices.text: competed against and challenged
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Haydn was helping and supporting younger composers, not competing against or challenging them.

choices.text: praised and celebrated
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because being praised and celebrated is what happened to Haydn in London, not what he did for his students.

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Haydn Still Matters

He started as a kid with a good voice and ended up shaping how orchestras tell stories with sound. Every symphony performed tonight — in Vienna, in London, in your own town — still carries his fingerprints.
stimulus.content_text: Why Haydn Still Matters He started as a kid with a good voice and ended up shaping how orchestras tell stories with sound. Every symphony performed tonight — in Vienna, in London, in your own town — still carries his fingerprints.

choices.text: By explaining that he started out with a very good singing voice.

assessments.title: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart: A Musical Genius

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Mozart: The Boy Who Practiced and the Man Who Never Stopped

Five Years Old at the Keyboard

A five-year-old boy sat at a harpsichord — a keyboard instrument that plucks its strings instead of striking them, like a piano's cousin with a brighter, thinner voice. He was not just pressing keys for fun. He was writing down original music on paper, note by note. Grown musicians crowded around him, stunned. The year was 1761, the city was Salzburg, Austria, and the boy was Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart.

You might think Mozart's talent appeared out of nowhere, like magic. Actually, there was someone standing right behind him every single day: his father. Leopold Mozart was a skilled violinist and composer who spotted his son's unusual ear for music early and built a strict training program around it. Young Wolfgang practiced for hours each day. Leopold taught him music theory, composition, and how different instruments worked. Mozart was gifted, yes — but his gift was shaped by years of intense, daily work.
stimulus.content_text: Mozart: The Boy Who Practiced and the Man Who Never StoppedFive Years Old at the Keyboard A five-year-old boy sat at a harpsichord — a keyboard instrument that plucks its strings instead of striking them, like a piano's cousin with a brighter, thinner voice. He was not just pressing keys for fun. He was writing down original music on paper, note by note. Grown musicians crowded around him, stunned. The year was 1761, the city was Salzburg, Austria, and the boy was Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. You might think Mozart's talent appeared out of nowhere, like magic. Actually, there was someone standing right behind him every single day: his father. Leopold Mozart was a skilled violinist and composer who spotted his son's unusual ear for music early and built a strict training program around it. Young Wolfgang practiced for hours each day. Leopold taught him music theory, composition, and how different instruments worked. Mozart was gifted, yes — but his gift was shaped by years of intense, daily work.

prompt._: According to the passage, how was a harpsichord different from a piano?

choices.text: It had more keys and a deeper, richer voice
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage describes the harpsichord's voice as 'brighter and thinner,' which is the opposite of deeper and richer, and nothing is said about it having more keys.

choices.text: It struck its strings harder to make louder sounds
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says the harpsichord plucks its strings — it does not strike them at all, let alone harder.

choices.text: It was smaller and easier for children to play
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions the harpsichord's size or suggests it was easier for children to play.

choices.text: It plucked its strings instead of striking them
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage states clearly that a harpsichord 'plucks its strings instead of striking them,' which is exactly how it differs from a piano.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Performing for Kings and Queens

By age six, Mozart was touring the royal courts of Europe. People called him a prodigy — a child who shows virtuosic skill at a very young age, the way some kids can solve adult-level math problems before they finish first grade. He played for kings, queens, and emperors. He could hear a piece of music once and play it back perfectly from memory. He could improvise, which means making up music on the spot, the way you might make up a story without planning it first. An audience member would hum a melody, and Mozart would instantly turn it into something dazzling.
stimulus.content_text: Performing for Kings and Queens By age six, Mozart was touring the royal courts of Europe. People called him a prodigy — a child who shows virtuosic skill at a very young age, the way some kids can solve adult-level math problems before they finish first grade. He played for kings, queens, and emperors. He could hear a piece of music once and play it back perfectly from memory. He could improvise, which means making up music on the spot, the way you might make up a story without planning it first. An audience member would hum a melody, and Mozart would instantly turn it into something dazzling.

prompt._: According to the text, what caused people to call Mozart a prodigy?

choices.text: He traveled to perform for many kings and queens.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because touring and performing for royalty is something Mozart did, but the passage says he was called a prodigy because of his incredible skills, not because of his travels.

choices.text: He made up long stories without planning them first.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because making up stories is only used as a comparison to help explain what improvising music means — it was never something Mozart was known for doing.

choices.text: He solved difficult math problems at a young age.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because solving math problems is just an example the author uses to explain what a prodigy means — the passage never says Mozart solved math problems.

choices.text: He showed amazing musical skills at a young age.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly says people called Mozart a prodigy for showing amazing musical skills, like playing music from memory and improvising, at a very young age.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Than One Kind of Music

As Mozart grew up, his music grew with him. It's easy to assume a composer writes only one type of music — maybe just songs for piano, or just big orchestra pieces. Mozart wrote in almost every form that existed. In his short life, he composed over 600 works. That included 41 symphonies, which are long pieces written for a full orchestra. He also wrote 27 piano concertos — works where a solo piano and an orchestra take turns and play together, like a conversation between one voice and a whole group.

He composed famous operas like The Magic Flute and Don Giovanni, where singers act out stories on stage while an orchestra plays beneath them. His operas captured every human feeling you can name: comedy, jealousy, love, forgiveness, grief. His music had surprising harmonic twists that made it sound both perfectly simple and endlessly deep. He wrote chamber music — pieces for small groups of musicians performing in a room rather than a concert hall. He wrote serenades, which are lighter pieces often performed outdoors. He even wrote church music and a Requiem, a serious musical work performed for the dead, which he left unfinished when he died.

His music can sound so smooth and natural that you might think it poured out of him without effort. Here's the surprising part: Mozart himself said otherwise. When he dedicated a set of six string quartets — pieces for two violins, a viola, and a cello — to his friend Joseph Haydn, he called them the result of "long and laborious effort." Even Mozart had to wrestle with notes, cross things out, and try again.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened before Mozart gave six string quartets to Joseph Haydn?

choices.text: He performed outdoors with an orchestra.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because performing outdoors describes serenades, which is a different type of music and has nothing to do with the string quartets for Haydn.

choices.text: He finished a serious work for the dead.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the Requiem was left unfinished when Mozart died, and the passage never connects it to the string quartets he gave to Haydn.

choices.text: He wrote church music for singers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because writing church music is a separate detail in the passage and is not connected to what Mozart did before giving the string quartets to Haydn.

choices.text: He worked hard on the pieces.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because Mozart himself said the six string quartets took 'long and laborious effort,' meaning he worked hard on them before dedicating them to Haydn.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Friendship Between Two Greats

Joseph Haydn was twenty-four years older than Mozart, but the two treated each other as equals. They played string quartets together at private gatherings. Each man studied the other's compositions carefully. When Haydn heard Mozart's six quartets, he told Leopold Mozart something exceptional: "Your son is the greatest composer known to me." Their respect for each other pushed both of them to write even better music. Great composers, like great athletes, get stronger when they have someone worthy to measure themselves against.
stimulus.content_text: A Friendship Between Two Greats Joseph Haydn was twenty-four years older than Mozart, but the two treated each other as equals. They played string quartets together at private gatherings. Each man studied the other's compositions carefully. When Haydn heard Mozart's six quartets, he told Leopold Mozart something exceptional: "Your son is the greatest composer known to me." Their respect for each other pushed both of them to write even better music. Great composers, like great athletes, get stronger when they have someone worthy to measure themselves against.

prompt._: The author compares composers to athletes to help explain the phrase "measure themselves against." Based on this comparison, what does the phrase mean?

choices.text: To win a prize for being the best
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never mentions winning a prize; it focuses on how comparing yourself to someone equally skilled helps you improve.

choices.text: To practice on the exact same team
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Haydn and Mozart were not on the same team — they were individual composers who learned from each other.

choices.text: To check who is physically stronger
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'measure themselves against' is about comparing musical talent, not physical strength.

choices.text: To compare abilities with an equal
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows Haydn and Mozart comparing their musical abilities with each other as equals, which made both of them better composers.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Thirty-Five Years and Six Hundred Works

Mozart died in 1791. He was only thirty-five years old. Scholars still debate the exact cause of his death. He was buried in an unmarked grave in Vienna — a quiet ending for someone whose music would echo for centuries.

In just three decades of composing, Mozart filled the world with more music than most composers create in twice the time. His work is still performed in concert halls, played on phones, and hummed by people who may not even know his name. The next time you hear a piece of classical music that makes you feel something — joy, sadness, excitement — there is a real chance Mozart wrote it. And behind every note was not just a gift he was born with, but thousands of hours of practice, teaching, and hard work that started when he was barely old enough to reach the keys.

He was buried in an unmarked grave in Vienna — a quiet ending for someone whose music would echo for centuries. In just three decades of composing, Mozart filled the world with more music than most composers create in twice the time. His work is still performed in concert halls, played on phones, and hummed by people who may not even know his name. The next time you hear a piece of classical music that makes you feel something — joy, sadness, excitement — there is a real chance Mozart wrote it. And behind every note was not just a gift he was born with, but thousands of hours of practice, teaching, and hard work that started when he was barely old enough to reach the keys.

prompt._: Read this sentence from the passage: 'In just three decades of composing, Mozart filled the world with more music than most composers create in twice the time.' What does the word 'composing' mean in this sentence?

choices.text: Creating new music
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because composing means creating new music, and the passage shows Mozart did this by producing hundreds of original works during his lifetime.

choices.text: Playing an instrument
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because playing an instrument means performing music that already exists, not writing brand-new music.

choices.text: Practicing the keys
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because practicing the keys means rehearsing on a piano or keyboard, which is different from the act of creating new music.

choices.text: Humming a melody
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because humming a melody is something a listener might do, not what a composer does when building a new piece of music.

prompt._: How does the way people saw Mozart's music contrast with what Mozart himself said about his work?

choices.text: People thought he only wrote operas, but Mozart said he wrote symphonies too.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says people believed Mozart only wrote operas — it actually lists many kinds of music he composed.

choices.text: People thought his music was too simple, but Mozart said it was complex.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because people thought his music sounded naturally simple, not that it was too simple, and Mozart never described it as complex.

choices.text: People thought his music was boring, but Mozart believed it was exciting.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says people thought Mozart's music was boring — they were actually amazed and moved by it.

choices.text: People thought his music came easily, but Mozart said it took hard effort.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says listeners thought his music flowed out easily, but Mozart himself called his work 'long and laborious effort,' showing a clear contrast.

prompt._: Based on the article, what caused Mozart to become such a skilled and wide-ranging composer?

choices.text: He had years of training and daily practice starting in early childhood
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article describes how Mozart practiced for hours each day from a very young age and received thorough training from his father in music theory and composition.

choices.text: He studied other composers' work and copied their styles of writing
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says Mozart copied other composers; his relationship with Haydn was about mutual respect and inspiration, not imitation.

choices.text: He traveled to royal courts and learned by performing for audiences
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because performing for royal courts was something Mozart did, but the article points to his early training and daily practice — not his performances — as what made him skilled.

choices.text: He was born with natural talent that made practice unnecessary for him
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article directly says Mozart's gift was 'shaped by years of intense, daily work,' meaning practice was essential, not unnecessary.

prompt._: How does the author organize the events of Mozart's life in the article?

choices.text: By describing the events of his life in the order that they happened
choices.feedback: This is correct because the author starts with Mozart as a five-year-old, moves through his childhood performances, his adult composing, and ends with his death at thirty-five — all in time order.

choices.text: By explaining the problems he faced and how he finally solved them
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article doesn't set up big problems for Mozart to solve; it focuses on the events of his life and his music.

choices.text: By comparing his musical skills to the skills of other famous composers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article doesn't spend time comparing Mozart's skills to lots of other composers — it mostly follows his own life story.

choices.text: By listing the different types of instruments he learned how to play
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never gives a list of instruments Mozart learned — it talks about the types of music he wrote instead.

prompt._: Which statement best summarizes how the author compares Mozart's childhood to his adulthood?

choices.text: He learned from his father as a child but from nobody as an adult.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage mentions his friendship with Haydn, where each man 'studied the other's compositions carefully,' showing Mozart kept learning from others as an adult too.

choices.text: He wrote simple music as a child but composed complex works as an adult.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage doesn't say his childhood music was simple — in fact, he was stunning grown musicians with his original compositions at age five.

choices.text: He practiced daily as a child and continued working hard as an adult.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage gives clear evidence of daily practice as a child and continued hard work as an adult, showing that effort was a constant throughout his life.

choices.text: He played for kings as a child but only for friends as an adult.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says Mozart stopped performing for royalty and only played for friends as an adult — that detail isn't in the passage.

assessments.title: American Folk Songs and Spirituals

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Songs Nobody Wrote Down

Music Before the Page

A woman bends over a row of cotton plants, her hands moving fast, her voice rising into the hot air. Nearby, other workers join in, their voices layering over hers, building a song that has no author and no sheet music. It lives only in their throats and memories. Hundreds of miles away, a mother rocks a baby in a cabin tucked into the Appalachian hills, singing a melody her own grandmother sang to her — but with new words, because she has slipped her baby's name into the verse.

Each singer reshapes the song to fit their own life. A sailor might tuck in a line about storms at sea. You might think every song has a single songwriter, the way pop songs and classical pieces do. These are folk songs — songs created by ordinary people and passed along by voice, not by paper. That is why many folk songs exist in dozens of versions, each one a little different from the last.

Hundreds of miles away, a mother rocks a baby in a cabin tucked into the Appalachian hills, singing a melody her own grandmother sang to her — but with new words, because she has slipped her baby's name into the verse. Each singer reshapes the song to fit their own life. A sailor might tuck in a line about storms at sea. You might think every song has a single songwriter, the way pop songs and classical pieces do. These are folk songs — songs created by ordinary people and passed along by voice, not by paper. That is why many folk songs exist in dozens of versions, each one a little different from the last.

prompt._: According to the text, how are folk songs different from pop songs and classical pieces?

choices.text: Folk songs are performed only by trained professional singers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows that ordinary people like field workers and mothers are the ones who sing and pass along folk songs.

choices.text: Folk songs are passed along by voice rather than by paper
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states that folk songs are 'passed along by voice, not by paper,' unlike pop songs and classical pieces.

choices.text: Folk songs are always sung the same way each time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage explains that folk songs change with each singer — people add new words and lines to fit their own lives.

choices.text: Folk songs are written down on sheet music by famous composers
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says folk songs have no sheet music and no single author — that's the opposite of what A says.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Blend of Many Cultures

It is easy to assume that American folk music grew from one single tradition. Actually, it grew from many. Settlers from the British Isles — England, Ireland, and Scotland — carried ballads across the ocean. A ballad is a long story-song, like a movie told entirely through melody and words. These ballads described heroes, tragedies, and wild adventures.

Enslaved Africans brought something different: powerful rhythms and a style called call-and-response singing. In call-and-response, one singer calls out a line, and a group answers back — like a musical conversation bouncing between a leader and a crowd. This style turned singing into something shared, something that pulled people together even in the worst conditions.

Native American peoples had their own deep musical traditions. Their songs connected to the natural world — rivers, animals, seasons — and to ceremonies that marked important moments in life. Drums and voice carried these songs through generations.

Over time, these traditions mixed wherever people met. In the Appalachian Mountains, Scottish fiddle tunes blended with African banjo playing. On the docks of New Orleans, French melodies tangled with African rhythms. No single culture created American folk music. Many cultures did, together, like rivers feeding into the same wide stream.

These ballads described heroes, tragedies, and wild adventures. Enslaved Africans brought something different: powerful rhythms and a style called call-and-response singing. prompt._: Based on the section "A Blend of Many Cultures," why did American folk music develop many different sounds?

choices.text: Because one old tradition stayed the strongest over time
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage clearly states that no single culture or tradition was the strongest — many traditions contributed equally.

choices.text: Because many cultural traditions mixed when people met
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage shows that British, African, and Native American musical traditions blended together wherever different groups of people came into contact.

choices.text: Because people met after the music had already blended
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because it has the order backwards — the music blended because people met, not after the blending had already happened on its own.

choices.text: Because songs about nature replaced other kinds of music
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because nature songs were only part of Native American traditions, and the passage never says they replaced other kinds of music.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Spirituals: Songs with Secret Maps

Among the most powerful folk songs in American history are spirituals — religious songs created by enslaved African Americans. Spirituals expressed faith, sorrow, and a fierce longing for freedom. Enslaved people were often forbidden to read, write, or gather freely. But they could sing. And so they poured their deepest feelings into music.

A song like "Nobody Knows the Trouble I've Seen" spoke openly about suffering. "Swing Low, Sweet Chariot" expressed a wish for a better life beyond the cruelty of slavery. You might think these were only about religion. Here's the surprising part: some spirituals carried hidden meanings that could save lives.

"Follow the Drinking Gourd" may have contained secret directions for escaping to freedom. The "drinking gourd" referred to the Big Dipper, a constellation — a group of stars that forms a recognizable pattern in the night sky, like a giant connect-the-dots picture. The Big Dipper's stars point toward the North Star, which guided escaped people northward. The "chariot" in "Swing Low, Sweet Chariot" may have been a code word for conductors — the brave people who led others along the Underground Railroad's secret escape routes, the way a train conductor guides passengers along a track. Singing these songs out loud raised no suspicion. To enslavers, they sounded like simple hymns. To those who knew the code, they were maps drawn in melody.

prompt._: According to the text, what happened after people recognized the "drinking gourd" in the sky?

choices.text: They used the North Star to head north.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the Big Dipper points to the North Star, which 'guided escaped people northward' toward freedom.

choices.text: They sang louder so enslavers would hear them.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because enslaved people sang these songs quietly as secret codes — they definitely did not want enslavers to catch on to the hidden meanings.

choices.text: They waited for conductors to bring a chariot.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the 'chariot' was a code word for the brave people called conductors, not a real chariot that someone would bring.

choices.text: They drew maps of the stars on paper.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions drawing maps on paper, and it even tells us that enslaved people were often forbidden to read and write.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Listening with Purpose

When you hear a folk song or a spiritual today, try listening with more than your ears. Pay attention to the words. What story are they telling? Who might have first sung them, and why? Listen to the melody. Is it slow and heavy, like someone carrying a weight? Or is it quick and bright, like feet moving toward something hopeful?

Try listening for layers. In a call-and-response song, notice how the group's answer changes the feeling of the leader's line. In a ballad, follow the story the way you would follow a chapter book — verse by verse, waiting to see what happens next. The more closely you listen, the more a song reveals.

No famous composer wrote these songs. No orchestra performed them first. They were born in fields, cabins, and church gatherings, shaped by the hands and voices of people whose names we will never know. Yet their music outlasted palaces and monuments. It turns out a song doesn't need a stage to be powerful. It just needs a voice — and someone willing to listen.

In a ballad, follow the story the way you would follow a chapter book — verse by verse, waiting to see what happens next. The more closely you listen, the more a song reveals. It turns out a song doesn't need a stage to be powerful. It just needs a voice — and someone willing to listen.

prompt._: The author compares a "ballad" to a "chapter book." Based on this comparison, what does the word "ballad" most likely mean?

choices.text: A song that is read silently alone.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because songs are listened to out loud, not read silently, and the passage never suggests that.

choices.text: A song that tells a story in parts.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage compares a ballad to a chapter book that you follow 'verse by verse,' meaning it tells a story in parts.

choices.text: A song that is about a heavy weight.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'slow and heavy, like someone carrying a weight' describes a type of melody, not the meaning of a ballad.

choices.text: A song that has a leader and group.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because a leader and group describe a call-and-response song, which the passage talks about separately from a ballad.

prompt._: Based on the article, how were ballads different from spirituals?

choices.text: Ballads told stories of adventures, while spirituals shared feelings and secret codes.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly says ballads told stories of adventures and heroes, while spirituals expressed deep feelings and some contained hidden codes that could guide people to freedom.

choices.text: Ballads were written down on paper, while spirituals were passed along entirely by memory.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article explains that both ballads and spirituals were part of the folk music tradition, meaning neither was written down — they were both passed along by voice and memory.

choices.text: Ballads were sung by large crowds, while spirituals were only sung by mothers to babies.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never says spirituals were only sung by mothers to babies — that detail was about folk songs in general, not spirituals specifically.

choices.text: Ballads used powerful drum rhythms, while spirituals used fast banjo playing and fiddles.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because drums and banjo playing are described as part of African and Appalachian musical traditions, not as features that separate ballads from spirituals.

prompt._: Why were folk songs and spirituals able to survive for so long even though no one wrote them down?

choices.text: Because the songs were simple enough that no one ever changed them
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually says singers were always changing the songs — adding new words and lines — so the songs were not kept the same.

choices.text: Because enslaved people were required to sing them every day
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says enslaved people were often forbidden to gather freely, so they were not required to sing every day.

choices.text: Because famous composers eventually turned them into classical music pieces
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions famous composers turning folk songs into classical music pieces.

choices.text: Because ordinary people kept reshaping and passing them along by voice
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage explains that ordinary people kept these songs alive by singing and reshaping them and passing them along by voice from person to person.

prompt._: Which sequence best describes how the article is organized from beginning to end?

choices.text: It starts with how spirituals were created, then explains folk songs, then describes cultures.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article doesn't start with spirituals — spirituals are explained in the third section, not the beginning.

choices.text: It starts with one culture's music, then compares it only to British ballads, then ends.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article celebrates how many cultures — not just one — blended together to create American folk music.

choices.text: It starts with how to listen to music, then defines folk songs, then lists famous composers.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article starts with folk songs, not listening tips, and it clearly states these songs had no famous composers.

choices.text: It starts with what folk songs are, then shows where they came from, then explains spirituals.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article first defines folk songs, then explains the many cultures that shaped American folk music, and then focuses on spirituals and their hidden meanings.

prompt._: Which statement best explains how the main idea of the article is developed?

choices.text: The author shows how to sing in a call-and-response style, describes the stars in the Big Dipper constellation, and lists famous composers who wrote folk music.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never teaches you how to sing call-and-response, and it clearly states that folk songs had no famous composers — not a list of them.

choices.text: The author tells a story about a mother singing to her baby, explains the history of the banjo, and gives secret directions for escaping along the Underground Railroad.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the mother and baby are just a small example at the start, and the article never actually gives you the real escape directions from the Underground Railroad.

choices.text: The author compares folk songs to modern pop music, explains how to write a traditional ballad, and describes the best way to play different instruments.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article never explains how to write a ballad or compares folk songs to modern pop music in any real detail.

choices.text: The author explains how folk songs were passed down by voice, describes the different cultures that shaped them, and reveals the powerful messages they carried.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it accurately captures all three main parts of the article: folk songs spread by voice, many cultures shaping them, and the powerful messages they carried.

assessments.title: Patriotic Songs: Music of a Nation

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Songs and Listening

A Flag in the Smoke

September 14, 1814. A lawyer named Francis Scott Key stands on the deck of a ship in Baltimore Harbor, squinting through thick smoke. All night long, British warships have bombarded — fired cannons and rockets at, the way a storm hurls hail against a window — Fort McHenry. Explosions shook the darkness for hours. Now, as pale morning light creeps across the water, Key stares toward the fort. Is the American flag still there? Then the smoke shifts. He sees it — torn, blackened, but flying. Key grabs a pen and begins writing a poem on the back of a letter.

You might think Key also wrote the melody that millions of Americans sing today. His words were later matched to a tune that already existed — a popular British song called "To Anacreon in Heaven." The poem and the borrowed melody grew together into "The Star-Spangled Banner." Here's the surprising part: it did not become the official national anthem until 1931, more than a hundred years after Key wrote those words.

All night long, British warships have bombarded — fired cannons and rockets at, the way a storm hurls hail against a window — Fort McHenry. Explosions shook the darkness for hours. Now, as pale morning light creeps across the water, Key stares toward the fort. Is the American flag still there? Then the smoke shifts. He sees it — torn, blackened, but flying. Key grabs a pen and begins writing a poem on the back of a letter. You might think Key also wrote the melody that millions of Americans sing today. His words were later matched to a tune that already existed — a popular British song called "To Anacreon in Heaven." The poem and the borrowed melody grew together into "The Star-Spangled Banner." Here's the surprising part: it did not become the official national anthem until 1931, more than a hundred years after Key wrote those words.

prompt._: According to the text, how is the origin of the melody of 'The Star-Spangled Banner' different from the origin of its words?

choices.text: The melody came from a song that already existed
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage clearly states the words were matched to 'a tune that already existed,' a popular British song called 'To Anacreon in Heaven.'

choices.text: The melody was created by British soldiers during the war
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the melody was a popular British song, not something created by soldiers during the battle.

choices.text: The melody was written by Key after the words were finished
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Key only wrote the words — the passage says the melody already existed and was matched to his poem later.

choices.text: The melody was composed when it became the national anthem
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the melody was already around long before 1931, when 'The Star-Spangled Banner' officially became the national anthem.

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Patriotic Songs Sound Like

Patriotic songs — songs that express love of country — are not just about their words. The music itself carries feeling. The next time you hear "The Star-Spangled Banner," listen for its wide leaps in melody, the pattern of pitches that move up and down like a roller coaster track. The notes jump high on "rocket's red glare" and soar even higher on "land of the free." Those big leaps make the song feel dramatic and bold. They also make it one of the hardest songs to sing well.

Now compare that to "America the Beautiful." Its melody moves in smoother, gentler steps, like walking up a hill instead of climbing a cliff. The tempo — the speed of the music, like a heartbeat that can race or slow down — is steady and calm. That smoothness gives the song a warm, peaceful feeling, even before you hear a single word. Composers choose these musical ingredients on purpose. Fast tempos and loud dynamics — the volume level of music, from a whisper to a shout — can stir excitement. Slow tempos and soft dynamics can bring comfort.
