The notes jump high on "rocket's red glare" and soar even higher on "land of the free." Those big leaps make the song feel dramatic and bold. They also make it one of the hardest songs to sing well. Now compare that to "America the Beautiful." Its melody moves in smoother, gentler steps, like walking up a hill instead of climbing a cliff. The tempo — the speed of the music, like a heartbeat that can race or slow down — is steady and calm. That smoothness gives the song a warm, peaceful feeling, even before you hear a single word. Composers choose these musical ingredients on purpose. Fast tempos and loud dynamics — the volume level of music, from a whisper to a shout — can stir excitement. Slow tempos and soft dynamics can bring comfort.

prompt._: Based on the section, what is the main reason composers choose different musical ingredients like tempo and dynamics?

choices.text: to make patriotic songs harder for people to sing
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because making a song hard to sing is just a side effect of 'The Star-Spangled Banner's' big leaps, not a goal composers aim for.

choices.text: to make every patriotic song sound exactly the same
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage shows that composers make songs sound different on purpose — 'The Star-Spangled Banner' is dramatic and bold, while 'America the Beautiful' is smooth and peaceful.

choices.text: to create specific feelings in the listener on purpose
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says composers choose musical ingredients 'on purpose' to create feelings like excitement, comfort, drama, and peace in the listener.

choices.text: to copy the feelings that the words already express
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage says music creates feelings 'even before you hear a single word,' meaning the music creates feelings on its own, not just by copying the words.

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A View from the Mountaintop

"America the Beautiful" began as a poem, too. In 1893, a teacher named Katharine Lee Bates climbed to the top of Pikes Peak in Colorado. From 14,000 feet up, she looked out over sweeping plains, purple mountain ranges, and a sky that seemed to stretch forever. She wrote a poem describing "spacious skies," "amber waves of grain," and "purple mountain majesties." The poem was later set to music, and many Americans feel it captures the spirit of the country even better than the national anthem.

Another beloved patriotic song has a surprising connection to a foreign country. "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" uses the exact same melody as the British anthem "God Save the King." Samuel Francis Smith wrote new American words for that familiar British tune in 1831. The song opens, "My country, 'tis of thee, sweet land of liberty, of thee I sing." Two nations, once at war, sharing a single melody — a reminder that American culture grew partly from British roots, even as the two countries built separate identities.
stimulus.content_text: A View from the Mountaintop "America the Beautiful" began as a poem, too. In 1893, a teacher named Katharine Lee Bates climbed to the top of Pikes Peak in Colorado. From 14,000 feet up, she looked out over sweeping plains, purple mountain ranges, and a sky that seemed to stretch forever. She wrote a poem describing "spacious skies," "amber waves of grain," and "purple mountain majesties." The poem was later set to music, and many Americans feel it captures the spirit of the country even better than the national anthem. Another beloved patriotic song has a surprising connection to a foreign country. "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" uses the exact same melody as the British anthem "God Save the King." Samuel Francis Smith wrote new American words for that familiar British tune in 1831. The song opens, "My country, 'tis of thee, sweet land of liberty, of thee I sing." Two nations, once at war, sharing a single melody — a reminder that American culture grew partly from British roots, even as the two countries built separate identities.

prompt._: According to the text, which sequence of events explains how the song "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" was created?

choices.text: A musician combined two different national anthems and then created a single new melody.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because no one combined two different anthems — Smith kept the British melody exactly as it was and only changed the words.

choices.text: A writer took a familiar British melody and then added new American words to it.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage says Samuel Francis Smith took the British melody from 'God Save the King' and wrote new American words for it.

choices.text: A teacher climbed a tall mountain peak and then wrote a beautiful poem about the scenery.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because climbing a mountain and writing a poem describes how 'America the Beautiful' started, not 'My Country, 'Tis of Thee.'

choices.text: A poet wrote a brand new American tune and then shared it with the famous British king.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Smith did not create a brand new American tune; he used the already-existing British melody.

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Battlefields

Patriotic songs don't only belong to moments of war or crisis. You probably hear them at baseball games, school assemblies, and Fourth of July celebrations. You might sing them in your school choir or hum them without thinking. These songs show up wherever Americans gather.

But they have mattered deeply during hard times, too. During the Civil War, soldiers on both sides sang patriotic songs around campfires to lift their spirits. During the two World Wars, patriotic music strengthened the morale — the courage and confidence of a group, like the energy a team feels when fans cheer — of troops overseas and comforted families waiting at home. After national tragedies, communities have gathered to sing together, finding strength in their shared voices.
stimulus.content_text: More Than Battlefields Patriotic songs don't only belong to moments of war or crisis. You probably hear them at baseball games, school assemblies, and Fourth of July celebrations. You might sing them in your school choir or hum them without thinking. These songs show up wherever Americans gather. But they have mattered deeply during hard times, too. During the Civil War, soldiers on both sides sang patriotic songs around campfires to lift their spirits. During the two World Wars, patriotic music strengthened the morale — the courage and confidence of a group, like the energy a team feels when fans cheer — of troops overseas and comforted families waiting at home. After national tragedies, communities have gathered to sing together, finding strength in their shared voices.

prompt._: The author compares "morale" to "the energy a team feels when fans cheer." Based on this comparison, what does the word "morale" mean?

choices.text: A quiet calmness found in a safe place.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because morale is an energizing, uplifting feeling like a cheering crowd, not a quiet or calm state.

choices.text: A sudden excitement about winning a game.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because morale isn't about the excitement of winning a game — it's a deeper feeling of courage and confidence that lasts through hard times.

choices.text: A shared feeling of courage and hope.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage defines morale as 'courage and confidence of a group,' which matches a shared feeling of courage and hope.

choices.text: A deep sadness felt during a hard time.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because morale is the opposite of sadness; it's what helps people feel strong and hopeful during difficult moments.

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Listening with Purpose

Patriotic songs are not just history lessons set to music. They are carefully built combinations of melody, tempo, and dynamics designed to move you. The next time one of these songs plays, try an experiment. Are the dynamics loud and powerful, or soft and gentle? Notice how those choices make you feel before you even think about the meaning of the lyrics.

When thousands of voices rise together at a stadium or a ceremony, each person is doing something humans have done for centuries — turning shared feeling into sound. That sound doesn't just float away. It connects every voice in the crowd into something larger than any one person singing alone.
stimulus.content_text: Listening with Purpose Patriotic songs are not just history lessons set to music. They are carefully built combinations of melody, tempo, and dynamics designed to move you. The next time one of these songs plays, try an experiment. Are the dynamics loud and powerful, or soft and gentle? Notice how those choices make you feel before you even think about the meaning of the lyrics. When thousands of voices rise together at a stadium or a ceremony, each person is doing something humans have done for centuries — turning shared feeling into sound. That sound doesn't just float away. It connects every voice in the crowd into something larger than any one person singing alone.

prompt._: According to the text, what are dynamics?

choices.text: the words of the song
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the words of a song are called lyrics, which the passage mentions separately from dynamics.

choices.text: the tune of the song
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the tune of a song is called melody, and the passage lists melody, tempo, and dynamics as three separate parts of music.

choices.text: the loudness of the music
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes dynamics as being 'loud and powerful, or soft and gentle,' which means how loud or quiet the music is.

choices.text: the speed of the music
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the speed of music is called tempo — the passage lists tempo and dynamics as two different things.

prompt._: Which contrast between "The Star-Spangled Banner" and "America the Beautiful" is supported by the article?

choices.text: One was written during a war, while the other was written by a soldier in a camp.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because 'America the Beautiful' was written by a teacher named Katharine Lee Bates on top of a mountain, not by a soldier in a camp.

choices.text: One became the official national anthem quickly, while the other became official much later.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article only mentions when 'The Star-Spangled Banner' became the official anthem (1931), and never discusses 'America the Beautiful' becoming an official anthem at all.

choices.text: One has a dramatic, hard-to-sing melody, while the other has a smoother, calmer melody.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the article clearly states that 'The Star-Spangled Banner' has dramatic, hard-to-sing leaps in melody, while 'America the Beautiful' has a smoother, calmer melody and steady tempo.

choices.text: One uses a borrowed British tune, while the other uses the same tune as "God Save the King."
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the article says it is 'My Country, 'Tis of Thee' — not 'America the Beautiful' — that uses the same melody as the British song 'God Save the King.'

prompt._: According to the article, what caused both Francis Scott Key and Katharine Lee Bates to write the poems that became famous songs?

choices.text: They saw a powerful and inspiring sight.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because both Key and Bates were inspired by a powerful sight — Key saw the flag surviving the battle, and Bates saw the breathtaking view from Pikes Peak.

choices.text: They listened to a beautiful British tune.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither poet was inspired by a British tune; the British melodies were added to their poems later by other people.

choices.text: They needed to create a national anthem.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because Key was not trying to write a national anthem — his poem didn't even become the official anthem until more than 100 years later.

choices.text: They wanted to comfort soldiers in a war.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because neither poet wrote their poem with the goal of comforting soldiers — that's a different way patriotic songs were used, mentioned later in the article.

prompt._: Which sequence best summarizes the text's main idea about how people experience patriotic songs?

choices.text: People read the historical words, learn facts about a battle, and then write a new melody.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage focuses on how music makes you feel emotions, not on learning historical facts from lyrics.

choices.text: People gather at a sporting event, listen to a slow tempo, and then feel a sudden excitement.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage actually says slow tempos bring comfort and calm, not sudden excitement, so this sequence gets the details mixed up.

choices.text: People hear the musical elements, feel an emotion from the sound, and then connect with others.
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage describes how musical elements create feelings, and then shared singing connects people together — exactly the sequence of hear, feel, and connect.

choices.text: People sing a familiar British tune, change the original lyrics, and then perform at a ceremony.
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because borrowing a British tune is just one small historical detail in the passage, not the main idea about how people experience patriotic songs.

prompt._: How does the author use the examples of 'The Star-Spangled Banner,' 'America the Beautiful,' and 'My Country, 'Tis of Thee' to support the idea that patriotic songs are 'carefully built combinations of melody, tempo, and dynamics designed to move you'?

choices.text: By describing how each song uses different musical choices to create different feelings
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the author carefully describes how each song uses different musical choices — like big leaps versus smooth steps — to create different feelings in the listener.

choices.text: By explaining that all three songs started as poems and were later set to borrowed melodies
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because even though some songs did start as poems, that detail is about their history, not about how melody, tempo, and dynamics are used to move you.

choices.text: By proving that older songs are always more dramatic than newer patriotic songs
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because the passage never says older songs are more dramatic than newer ones — that idea isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: By showing that all three songs were written during wars and use loud dynamics to stir excitement
choices.feedback: This option is incorrect because not all three songs were written during wars, and the passage describes both loud and soft dynamics — not just loud ones.

syllabus_metadata.title: Patriotic Songs: Music of a Nation
